Doug Ford wants 'a detailed plan' for proposed federal pharmacare program

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-wants-detailed-plan-proposed-federal/docview/2942216814/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+wants+%27a+detailed+plan%27+for+proposed+federal+pharmacare+program&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is leaving the door open to joining the Trudeau government's proposed pharmacare program, but is still waiting for more details, says Premier Doug Ford.
His remarks come a week after the plan to begin universal pharmacare with coverage for diabetes and birth control was unveiled in Ottawa by federal Health Minister Mark Holland.
"I know Quebec and Alberta just immediately said no, but I'm always open to reviewing it and if it's going to help the people of Ontario, I'm all in," Ford said Friday in Milton.
Echoing concerns previously expressed by Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones, Ford said more information is needed on how the proposal would work for the province.
"The feds have to give us more details. It was like, 'We're doing this and we'll get to the details later,' and that's fine. I'm fine, but let's see the details," the premier added.
"We want a detailed plan, not a hokey-pokey one."
Ontario has a provincial pharmacare system called OHIP+ that provides more than 5,000 medications to people aged 24 or younger who do not have private coverage, such as through an employer.
Holland's proposed pharmacare legislation, developed under a confidence and supply agreement with Jagmeet Singh's federal New Democrats to support Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's minority Liberal government, has not yet been passed in Parliament.
It is unclear whether it can get to a final vote before the end of June because of a busy legislative schedule in Ottawa.
Holland has put the initial cost of diabetes and contraceptive coverage at $1.5 billion annually and his timeline suggests it would not begin before spring of 2025, months before the next scheduled federal election.
The Parliamentary Budget Officer has estimated a complete national pharmacare program could cost $11.2 billion in its first year.
Although Alberta's United Conservative government initially balked at the concept, saying it was not consulted on the plan and considered it to be trampling on provincial jurisdiction over health care, its health minister later took a more conciliatory tone on social media.
"We expect the federal government will invest in Alberta's pharmacare program to enhance the existing comprehensive programs we have available to Albertans," Adriana LaGrange wrote on X.
Quebec has said it has its own drug program.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford attend an announcement at Seneca College in King City, Ont., on Feb. 9, 2024.

Subject: Diabetes; Prime ministers

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Document 2 of 500

How Canada's attempts at reform made foreign caregivers even more vulnerable

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-canadas-attempts-at-reform-made-foreign/docview/2942216512/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+Canada%27s+attempts+at+reform+made+foreign+caregivers+even+more+vulnerable&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Connie Alinton worked in a seafood factory in the Philippines, canning sardines.
But she needed to pay for the care of her mother's heart-enlargement problem and to support her siblings, so when an opportunity came to work in Canada in 2013, she jumped on it.
A decade after coming to this country, the Toronto home support worker and nanny, who also has a college degree in mathematics, has been trapped in a seemingly never-ending bureaucratic nightmare to become a permanent resident.
It's an unintended consequence of the successive reforms meant to improve a caregiver program in this country that was once rife with abuse and exploitation.
"Life is hard back home," says the 48-year-old Alinton. "My elderly mom depends on me. My younger sister and her three children depend on me. My brother's autistic son depends on me."
In freeing foreign caregivers from the previous live-in work requirements in 2014, then prime minister Stephen Harper's Conservative government imposed new language and post-secondary education criteria, as well as an annual cap on the number of permanent resident applications under the program.
Care workers were assessed for permanent residence criteria before they began working in Canada. Once they got their work permit and accumulated two years of care experience, they could apply for permanent status.
However, many of those who were already in Canada or who had come through different avenues but ended up working as caregivers have been stuck, unable to meet the new language requirement or to afford to go back to school to upgrade their education.
In Alinton's case, she has failed the English test seven times.
"I studied hard for the test even after my body was so tired after working long days," said Alinton, who has paid more than $2,000 on her meagre incomes for those standardized language tests. "But I got very nervous during the test. It's so frustrating."
Meanwhile, new applications under the Liberals' latest policy iterations are processed at a glacial pace, partially due to the disruptions from the pandemic. It's driving some employers to bring in overseas caregivers on visitor visas who may not have the qualifications for permanent residence down the road.
As the Immigration Department is reviewing the soon to-be-expired caregiver immigration pilots, foreign domestic workers and their supporters are calling for an overhaul of the convoluted pathways, which have seen many falling through the cracks, with no prospect of permanent residence, while enduring prolonged family separation and potential abuse.
Specifically, they want Immigration Minister Marc Miller to remove the post-secondary education and language requirements as well as the annual caps for migrant caregivers to become permanent residents, barriers they say have left the migrant workers in perpetual immigration limbo and facing exploitative conditions.
"The curtailment of what was once a program which provided access to working-class care workers in the Global South to come and work and get status is increasingly driving those workers into even more precarious work," said Mary Gellatly, a community legal worker with Toronto's Parkdale Community Legal Services.
"We've got the pilot program ending in June. So it's a real opportunity to create a program of equality for migrant care workers. Let's seize that opportunity and learn from the problems."
For decades, Canada has relied on foreign caregivers to look after our children and elderly.
To attract foreign workers to do a job that few Canadians were willing to, the Live-in Caregiver Program, which ran from 1992 to 2014, provided what's called an automatic pathway to permanent residence, affording foreign caregivers permanent status here in exchange for the work they put in and the sacrifices they made.
The scheme has gone through multiple changes since 2014. The former Conservative government eliminated the requirement that workers live with their employers after extensive media reports of caregivers being exploited through long work hours without overtime or being asked to perform jobs beyond their caregiving duties, because they felt beholden to their employers.
The new language and education requirements were expected to draw caregivers of a higher calibre, who would be more likely to stand firm on workers' rights and better integrate into the society and switch careers when they became permanent residents. Those changes were also intended to discourage Canadians from misusing the program to bring extended families as caregivers to Canada for permanent residence.
Since coming into power in 2015, the Liberals have reduced the minimum caregiving work hours from 24 months to 12 months, but have not removed the cap or the language and education standards.
Since the Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government launched its own five-year caregiver pilot programs in 2019, officials have received a total of 18,739 applications for permanent residence from caregivers, including 11,789 in the child-care and 6,950 in the home-support worker streams.
To date, immigration officials have processed only 4,024 of all applications, approving 2,438 and refusing 1,586 cases, while 1,021 applicants have withdrawn from the process. According to the department, they still have 108 applications submitted under the previous caregiver programs that are pending a decision.
Those numbers do not even include the many foreign caregivers, such as Alinton, who have been issued work permits to care for children and elderly Canadians but can't apply for permanent residence because they don't meet the language and education requirements.
Every time Alinton loses her job, she needs to find a new employer who is willing to pay a $1,000 government fee and sponsor her work permit to stay in the country. Some employers would ask her to cover the government cost or work for cash.
She was once out of status for two years and had to survive on savings, incomes from occasional cleaning jobs and friends' support.
"It's easy to find a caregiving job if you're willing to work under the table, but it's not easy to find an employer willing to sponsor you," lamented Alinton, who was again in limbo after the 95-year-old woman in her care recently died. "I always worry what if I can't find another employer. What would happen to my family back home?"
'I cannot wait any longer'
June Doliente came to Canada in 2019 from the Philippines after a stint working in Hong Kong, expecting she would get her permanent residence after two years. Due to COVID-19, she was unable to sit for the English exam until 2021, before she could apply for permanent residence.
She applied when the caregiver programs opened for application on Jan. 1, 2022, but the 2,750 cap for child-care stream was filled quickly and she had to wait another year. She only managed to get through in January 2023. But it wasn't until June that she got a notice acknowledging receipt of her application.
Today, she's still waiting for an update. The Immigration Department said the current processing times for caregivers in various streams range from 31 to 36 months from the moment an application is received and stamped.
"I cannot wait any longer. I've been here for more than four years. I missed my kids," said the 42-year-old single mother of six children, who has a Bachelor of Science degree in nursing from back home.
"People don't know about our struggle. We came from a third world country. We may have our degrees back home, but life is very difficult. We come here to give a better future for our kids. It's not just for us. We sacrifice everything for our kids."
Despite the reforms to untie caregivers from the live-in conditions and allow them upgrade their education without applying for a study permit, few care workers in reality have been able to live out or enrol in school due to financial and employment obligations, says Jhoey Dulaca, a co-ordinator with the Migrant Workers Alliance for Change.
"We have lots and lots of care workers in Canada, but many of them are trapped here, some losing their status through the cracks," said Dulaca. "We don't want the government to close the program, but they need to make it work for the caregivers."
Neither are the caregiver programs working for employers with their lengthy processing times, says Manuela Gruber Hersch, a co-founder of the Association of Caregivers and Nanny Agencies Canada. She said Canadians are not going to wait years to bring in overseas caregivers and many have turned to those who don't have work authorization, fuelling the undocumented workforce.
"The annual quota is also way too low. It's not meeting the demands of employers," said Hersch. "People are hiring individuals on visitors' visas. We are seeing it all the time because they need the caregivers now."
Although the federal government has launched the $10-a-day child care program, she said this won't help families where parents are shift workers, let alone those looking for someone to care for a frail elderly person.
Immigration officials said their survey for the program review has received more than 5,000 responses from caregivers and the evaluation team is currently planning focus groups with caregivers across Canada.
"Future policy decisions on caregiver programming will be based on the lessons learned through the recent Home Child Care Provider Pilot and Home Support Worker Pilot, and previous caregiver programming," said an Immigration Department spokesperson. "Results from the evaluation may inform future policy decisions."
CAPTION: June Doliente and her six children, from left to right (back row) Yuri, 19; Zoe, 21; Zion, 18; and (front row) Archie, 11; Seth, 10; and Bryle, 13.Connie Alinton arrived in Alberta in 2013 from the Philippines as a low-skilled temporary foreign worker before she moved to Ontario as a nanny in 2016. She has been caught up in the multiple changes made to Canada's caregiver immigration program.

Subject: Language; Migrant workers; Working hours; Pilot projects; Personal profiles; Employment; Families & family life; Canadians; Developing countries--LDCs; Children & youth; Employers; Caregivers; Prime ministers; Education; Immigration

Business indexing term: Subject: Migrant workers Working hours Employment Developing countries--LDCs Employers

The reality of the Liberals' pharmacare bill

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/reality-liberals-pharmacare-bill/docview/2942216509/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+reality+of+the+Liberals%27+pharmacare+bill&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On Feb. 29, Canada's Health Minister Mark Holland introduced a pharmacare bill in Parliament, seemingly laying down the foundation for a new national universal single payer pharmacare plan. NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh declared victory. After fifty years of efforts, he boasted that a first big step had been taken toward giving all Canadians equal access to medicines regardless of employment status or financial means.
The deal starts small, just two classes of drugs - diabetes medication and contraception - are covered free of charge, and only if the provinces who will administer the program sign on.
But already a fight is brewing between public-system advocates who want a single-payer public program established and expanded to include a vast list of medicines, and the pharmaceutical industry, health insurance firms, pharmacists and the business community who fear a multibillion-dollar change to their bottom lines, and the impact on patients and government deficits.
Navigating between the two sides is the Liberal government, which succumbed to NDP pressure in order to stave off a federal election.
In this week's episode of "It's Political," just how committed is the Liberal government to public pharmacare? Federal Health Minister Mark Holland joins us.
But first we dig deep into the debate over drug coverage in Canada with Dalhousie University Political Science Professor Katherine Fierlbeck, Canada Research Chair in Health Justice Dr. Nav Persaud, former Ontario health minister and former chair of the Federal Advisory Council on Pharmacare Dr. Eric Hoskins, Pharmacist Kristen Watt, Ontario Pharmacists Association CEO Justin Bates, Canadian Life and Health Insurance Association President Stephen Frank, Canada Canada Research Chair in Access to Medicines at the University of British Columbia Michael Law, Queen's University Assistant Professor Dr. Samantha Buttemer and Canadian Chamber of Commerce Vice President of Policy and Government Relations Matthew Holmes.
Listen here and follow or subscribe at Apple Podcasts, Spotify, Amazon Music, Google Podcasts or wherever you listen to your favourite podcasts.
Some of the clips this week were sourced from the CBC, CPAC, Global, and the Canadian Press Gallery.
This episode of "It's Political" was produced by Althia Raj and Michal Stein, and mixed by Sean Pattendon. Our theme music is by Isaac Joel.
Suggested readings:
Politicians come and go, but the clock is now ticking on long-promised pharmacare
A Prescription for Canada: Achieving Pharmacare For All
National Pharmacare: Laying the Groundwork
Cost Estimate of a Single-payer Universal Drug Plan
NDP insiders say pharmacare battle was the closest they've ever come to abandoning Justin Trudeau's Liberals
The Trudeau government has unveiled its pharmacare strategy. Here's what you need to know
CAPTION: Minister of Health Mark Holland speaks about new national pharmacare legislation during a press conference in Ottawa on Feb. 29.

International Women's Day, climate change and the blame game

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/international-womens-day-climate-change-blame/docview/2942216497/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=International+Women%27s+Day%2C+climate+change+and+the+blame+game&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Gender-based violence is an epidemic
Woman found dead at home in Vaughan was a Toronto school teacher, March 6
International Women's Day has come as another tragic death of a woman, with a man arrested, has happened in Vaughan. These acts are often described as "national occurrences," but there is no national, provincial or municipal action plan to end violence against women. Why are politicians not responding or speaking up?
Diane Sullivan, Toronto
Blame climate change for good weather
Toronto had its warmest winter ever. Here's what we can expect in March, March 5
I was interested to read the choice of words to describe our warmest ever winter. It was reported that climate change and El Nino are "to blame," a somewhat negative term, as opposed to using a more positive phrase like we have climate change and El Nino "to thank" for the warmer temperatures. I think this change of tone - to something more serious - is appropriate. Because even though Canadians will invariably tell you that they "love" this warmer weather, a lot of us know that it should not be happening and that this persistent unusually warm weather is slowly damaging our ability to continue to live on this planet.
George Parker, Cobourg, Ont.
To paraphrase John Vailant, the author of "Fire Weather: A True Story from a Hotter World," perhaps we should be saying that this winter was the coolest of our future. Some people are happy with more time to suntan but perhaps they should acknowledge that those warmer temperatures come with a host of downsides including drier ground conditions that act as fuel for fires, more drought which affects both food affordability and availability, and more pests depleting our boreal forest which until now has been helping to clean our air. Enjoy your suntan, but just remember, short term gain for long term pain.
Heather McNie, Sydenham, Ont.
As another record gets broken, we must realize that while it can feel wonderful, it's pretty bad for climate reasons when spring flowers are up from mid-January. What will the summer be like?
Hamish Wilson, Toronto
Cancel World Cup games in Toronto
The World Cup's swelling price tag: Editorial, March 6
Much has been written concerning the upcoming 2026 FIFA World Soccer games and the immense costs to the city. Cancel these games! As to the contract, I wonder what the undisclosed terms are that former mayor John Tory entered into. These terms need to be fully disclosed to the public. The $380 million costs for the city are excessive. The six games to be held here are not reputed to be top-flight teams. So much for world TV coverage and the fame for Toronto. Big deal! If a portion of this cost was just put into advertising about Toronto, I am certain it will provide a better result. The $380 million - watch it grow - can be put to much better use improving our infrastructure in the city, without requiring these games. This whole thing is a gross boondoggle. I trust Mayor Olivia Chow has the foresight and courage to cancel it.
Gabe Knapp, Toronto
The blame game
Federal procurement department punishes companies that worked on ArriveCan app, March 6
I am sorry to say that banning limited companies like GC Strategies, Dalian Enterprises and Coradix Technology Consulting is the appearance of doing something about the problem when in reality it does nothing. Companies come and go like the fashion in hats. It is the actual people who own the companies that need to be named and shamed and then banned from any form of government compensation. The CEO working for the Department of National Defence should be fired and every effort should be made to claw back as much public money as possible. Only then will confidence and justice be restored.
Kurt Crist, Consecon, Ont.
If, that is if federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is elected as prime minister, will he still blame Justin Trudeau for every mistake he and the Conservative party make? Let's hear more about policy.
Diane Herechuk, Hamilton, Ont.
Russia, the United States and Ontario
What's all the fuss about like-minded judges? Just take a look at Russia and the United States, two nations renowned for their adherence to freedom, justice and protection of all their people. First, consider Putin's Russia. Can anyone seriously say that like-minded judges have caused harm to any citizen or foreigner? Second, look south of our border to the United States. See what great things have transpired there with the appointment of three Supreme Court justices by Trump and the appointment/election of other state judges? All women (ladies of the world, please read "The Handmaid's Tale"), minorities, gays, trans people, refugees, and marginalized people can sleep at night knowing they are living in that shining city on the hill where they are valued, protected, and subjected to fair and impartial justice. Don't we want to be part of that heroic movement? Don't we want Premier Doug Ford to join that august body of great leaders? Why shouldn't judges be beholden to the government in power? What could possibly go wrong?
Barbara Pleva, Toronto
Drone show a bust
We headed to Casa Loma with great enthusiasm for the free Drone show on Wednesday night. Thousands of people showed up, climbing up the staircase to Austin Terrace for the publicized event. There was no security, police or staff doing crowd control. When word got out that it was better to view from the Toronto Archives, there was snarling traffic and mass confusion, causing a delay due to safety concerns with all the pedestrian traffic. In true Toronto fashion, we all waited patiently in the cold until 9 p.m. when it started to rain and the show was cancelled due to the safety concerns and regulations set out by Transport Canada. Some people came from the suburbs and paid for parking. What should have been a wonderful event was a disappointment and embarrassment. Happy 190, Toronto!
Larry Peloso, Toronto
CAPTION: People jog near Toronto's waterfront on during unseasonably warm weather.

Subject: Tournaments & championships; Ocean currents; Women; El Nino; Soccer; Climate change

Location: Russia; United States--US

Document 5 of 500

Canada needs to show leadership of ending famine in Gaza

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canada-needs-show-leadership-ending-famine-gaza/docview/2942081663/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+needs+to+show+leadership+of+ending+famine+in+Gaza&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I challenge anyone to watch the video of a dying boy in a Gaza hospital aired this week on CBC News without flinching.
This is a test you don't want to pass. Certainly I didn't. The first time I looked I turned away. I had to go back a second time to watch the entire report on the boy, 9-year-old Yazan al-Kafarna, who was dying because the food and medicine he needed weren't available.
His limbs were shrunken and his eyes were sunk deep into his head. He looked more dead than alive, like a survivor of a concentration camp. And indeed on Monday he died.
It's no secret there's a hunger crisis right now among the 2.3 million people in Gaza. It's going on in full view of the world and aid agencies are sounding the alarm as loud as they can.
They're telling us children are dying of hunger in hospitals. That people in the north, where the crisis is worst, are resorting to eating animal fodder. Emergency rations are being dropped from the sky and on our screens we can see desperate people stampeding food trucks. In one terrible incident more than a hundred were killed, some by Israeli army bullets, some trampled underfoot in the rush.
But while there's plenty of noise there's a lot less action. There are angels-on-the-head-of-a-pin debates about when the crisis might be officially designated a "famine" and after all what's a "famine" really? Israel says it's allowing trucks full of food to go in but aid groups, such as Oxfam, say Israel is using hunger as "collective punishment" against Palestinians.
Canada, meanwhile, just seems confused. It "paused" its contributions to the main organization that provides relief for Palestinians, UNRWA, when Israel charged that a dozen of the agency's employees (it has 13,000 in Gaza) took part in the Oct. 7 massacre of Israeli civilians.
That decision was wrong, as I've written before. What those UNRWA employees did was appalling but crippling the only organization with the resources to provide food, medicine and shelter in the midst of a war that is costing thousands of civilian lives is also appalling.
For a moment this week it looked like the government was about to reverse course on that. The minister of international development, Ahmed Hussen, scheduled a news conference and CBC News confidently reported (surely on the basis of a leak from the government) that he would announce Canada was resuming support for UNRWA. Suddenly, though, that was cancelled and a new line appeared. Now, it seems, "no decision" has been made.
So where does Canada stand? Somewhere in the muddy middle of the road. The Liberals are divided on the issue; on Thursday two of their MPs publicly called on the government not to reverse course on funding UNRWA. And my guess is the government also doesn't want to get out of step with its allies, who are waiting for an official UN report before making a decision. But that report isn't due until late April. How many more Palestinian children will waste away and die in the meantime?
Everyone's praising the late Brian Mulroney these days for bravely taking a stand against South African apartheid in defiance of Washington and London. The Trudeau government should learn from Mulroney and raise its voice against the hunger crisis going on right now in Gaza, even if that means getting ahead of where some of its allies are prepared to go. That might ruffle feathers but it's the right thing to do.
On another matter entirely: In my last column I may have left the impression that Justin Trudeau is no better than Doug Ford when it comes to letting partisan considerations affect judicial appointments.
That isn't true. The Trudeau government inherited a flawed system for naming judges and made it considerably better, i.e. less affected by purely party-political factors. The government has worked to make the judiciary more diverse - appointing a lot more women, in particular - but that's something else again.
Ford, on the other hand, inherited a pretty good appointment system in Ontario and seems determined to break it. Now he's vowing to "quintuple down" (can you do that?) in naming "tough on crime" judges in tune with his party. That's very different and not in a good way.
CAPTION: In this image grab from an AFPTV video, a U.S. aircraft carrying food parcels flies above a beach in the Gaza Strip before dropping the humanitarian aid attached to parachutes, on Saturday.Palestinians run toward parachutes attached to food parcels, air-dropped from U.S. aircrafts on a beach in the Gaza Strip on Saturday.

Document 6 of 500

Ramadan heralds a political awakening for Canadian Muslims

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ramadan-heralds-political-awakening-canadian/docview/2942081661/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ramadan+heralds+a+political+awakening+for+Canadian+Muslims&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The coming days will mark the beginning of the Muslim holy month of Ramadan, a period of fasting and spiritual growth that is one of Islam's five pillars. As in years past, nearly 2 million Canadian Muslims will gather nightly with family and friends to break fast, give thanks and reflect on how to awaken themselves spiritually.
This Ramadan, however, many of the dinner table conversations are also likely to focus on a political awakening. With war raging in the Middle East and an eye to federal and provincial elections, Canadian Muslims have discovered their voice, a development that politicians ignore at their peril.
Muslims are not a monolithic group, neither ethnically nor ideologically. They hail from countries as far-flung as India, Somalia, and Bosnia. Visit any Canadian mosque and you are likely to hear a variety of languages, from Farsi to Turkish to Malay. Some Muslims are secular and liberal in their outlook, while others adopt a more socially conservative approach to life.
Yet one thing that appears to unite most across ethnicity and ideology is sympathy for the plight of Palestinians. While there isn't much publicly available research into the attitudes of Canadian Muslims, the Council on American Islamic Relations (CAIR) surveyed 525 self-identifying American Muslims in May and June of 2022 and such a survey done in Canada would likely yield similar results.
The CAIR survey found that 91 per cent described the occupation of Palestinian territories by Israel as a top foreign policy issue, with a further 89 per cent of respondents saying that American foreign policy was tilted too far toward Israel.
We recently saw this sentiment deliver real-world political consequences for President Biden when over 100,000 Michigan Democrats marked "uncommitted" on their primary ballot rather than support the incumbent after an organized campaign by Arab American groups.
As significant as this is, it's worth pointing out that Muslims represent only about 2.5 per cent of Michigan's population. As of the 2021 census, that number is double in Canada (5 per cent) and even higher in the electorally significant Greater Toronto (10 per cent) and Montreal (9 per cent) areas.
Since 2015, the Canadian Muslim Vote - a national, non-partisan organization aiming to increase civic engagement and education within the Islamic community - has worked to increase Muslim turnout in three federal and two Ontario elections.
All this adds up to the fact that Canadian Muslims now represent a significant political force, a fact that some in Canada's political class have been slow to recognize.
But with this growing significance comes responsibility:
 It is a responsibility to recognize that Canada is a liberal democracy with diverse viewpoints, some of which don't always align with the majority of Canadian Muslims.
 It is a responsibility to recognize that just as it is wrong to equate sympathy for the Palestinian cause with support for Hamas, it is wrong for protesters to target Jewish neighbourhoods, schools, and synagogues.
 And finally, it's a responsibility to recognize that proper understanding between communities comes through dialogue, not ultimatums. There should be no litmus tests for elected officials wishing to address Muslim congregations. Those with divergent opinions should be engaged, not frozen out.
Canada has come a long way since the post-9/11 explosion of Islamophobia. While we are by no means a utopia of tolerance, there are now more than a dozen members of Parliament of Muslim faith across several parties. In recent years, Canada has had Muslim ministers of transportation, immigration, international development, gender equity, economic development, democratic institutions and justice. Ontario recently saw its first Muslim attorney general seek the leadership of a significant provincial party.
Canada's Muslim community will only increase in size over the coming years. This is a reality that our political elite cannot ignore. Whether this reality leads to further community integration into mainstream politics will depend on the words and actions of both community leaders and Canada's mainstream political parties in the months and years ahead.
But one thing is clear: Canada's Muslims have found their political voice.
CAPTION: Since 2015,the Canadian Muslim Vote- a national, non-partisan organization aiming to increase civic engagement and education within the Islamic community - has worked to increase Muslim turnout in three federal and two Ontario elections.
CREDIT: Omar Khan Contributor

Subject: Provincial elections; Election results; Community; Ramadan; Muslims; Politics; Foreign policy

Location: United States--US; Canada; Michigan; Israel

Document 7 of 500

Toronto signed a secret deal that would have let it back out of hosting the 2026 World Cup

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-signed-secret-deal-that-would-have-let/docview/2942081446/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+signed+a+secret+deal+that+would+have+let+it+back+out+of+hosting+the+2026+World+Cup&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto signed a secret agreement that gave it the right to withdraw from hosting the 2026 FIFA World Cup if it didn't receive financial support from senior levels of government by mid-2020, internal emails obtained by the Star show.
But for reasons that remain unclear, the city didn't trigger the deal, leaving it on the hook for the skyrocketing costs of games.
A draft of the deal is contained in emails obtained by the Star through a freedom of information request. They provide the fullest picture yet of how the city joined the bid to host the global soccer tournament in 2018, despite not meeting the key council condition of securing provincial and federal funding beforehand.
The withdrawal agreement was intended to give the city a legal off-ramp to avoid being burdened by a disproportionate share of World Cup costs if Queen's Park and Ottawa didn't pledge funding.
Despite not reaching funding deals with the other governments by the June 30, 2020 deadline, Toronto didn't back out. The emails don't explain why, although city officials say they have long been confident the money would flow.
However, six years after signing up to host, it's still not clear how much financial support the other governments will provide Toronto, which is grappling with a major COVID-driven budget crisis. In that time, the city's estimated World Cup costs have grown to $380 million, from $45 million.
Uncertainty about how much Toronto will have to pay prompted Mayor Olivia Chow to tell reporters last month that she had been "saddled" with the World Cup after her election last year.
Toronto will hold six of the 104 matches being staged by 16 cities across Canada, the U.S. and Mexico. While Chow described it as an exciting opportunity to showcase Toronto on the global stage, she criticized the process by which the city agreed to host under her predecessor, John Tory.
"Would I want to see $380 million being spent on it? No. Would I have signed the deal (for which) none of the provincial or the federal government contributions had been locked down? No," she said.
Premier Doug Ford's government announced in February it would conditionally contribute $97 million to the event, but rejected the city's request to increase that contribution after the tournament price tag grew. Toronto says it expects the federal government to make a funding decision "in the coming months," although it's not clear how much Ottawa will provide. Federal policy is expected to limit its contribution to 35 per cent of total costs.
In 2016, a special panel struck by then-mayor John Tory made clear that Toronto should only bid on a "mega-event" like the World Cup if it first confirmed federal and provincial financial support. In a subsequent report to council about the World Cup, staff said those commitments were "essential" to reducing the city's financial risk.
But when staff went to council in early 2018 seeking authority to sign on to the joint North American World Cup bid, they didn't have much time. The deadline for potential host cities to submit documents to FIFA, soccer's global governing body, was weeks away on March 16.
On Feb. 1 2018, council gave staff the green light to join the bid "subject to" the city confirming financial support from the senior governments. The report warned that if the city couldn't execute formal funding agreements by the deadline, it "would either submit documents with caveats around financial support or withdraw."
The emails, which are correspondence from senior municipal bureaucrats in 2018, show that Tory and city staff pushed Queen's Park and Ottawa to sign funding agreements as the submission deadline approached.
But by late February, Mike Williams, the city's general manager of economic development and culture, acknowledged in an email that locking down financial commitments in time was "probably impossible." On March 1 he reported that then-premier Kathleen Wynne had given the provincial ministry of sport "no direction ... about whether the province is supportive."
"This is all becoming a big rush," Williams wrote.
With just days to go, there was a breakthrough. On March 11 Williams told the mayor's office and others that the Canadian Soccer Association (CSA), one of the three FIFA member associations spearheading the North American bid, agreed to sign a "collateral agreement" with Toronto.
A draft of the agreement included in the emails shows it would allow the city to join the bid, but give it the option of pulling out without penalty by June 30, 2020 if one or both of the senior governments hadn't committed to paying tournament security costs - which would be expensive and outside the city's control - and provided other hosting guarantees. It included a clause specifying that the agreement was to be kept secret.
After signing the withdrawal agreement and joining the bid, staff didn't publicly report to council about the World Cup again until 2022, by which time costs had ballooned to almost $300 million. A staff report in March of that year referenced "mitigating mechanisms" the city put in place in order to join the bid, but didn't provide details of the withdrawal agreement.
Council voted in April 2022 to proceed with hosting, despite the lack of funding commitments, and in June 2022 FIFA officially announced Toronto and Vancouver as the two Canadian host cities. Montreal had withdrawn a year earlier after the Quebec government said it couldn't justify the rising costs.
Asked this week why Toronto stayed in the bid past the June 2020 withdrawal deadline, Sharon Bollenbach, executive director of the city's World Cup secretariat, said since the early stages "city staff had indication that both the provincial and federal governments supported Toronto's bid."
Discussions with both governments "led staff to be confident that suitable funding arrangements would be secured" and talks to finalize agreements are ongoing, she said.
As to the secrecy around the withdrawal agreement, Bollenbach said the city "remains committed to a transparent process," and "the mayor and council are briefed via staff reports or as may be requested."
A spokesperson for the CSA said the confidentiality clause was "standard wording ... applicable to any commercial agreements."
There remains strong support at city hall for the World Cup, which Toronto expects to generate more than $392 million in local GDP, create more than 3,500 jobs, and deliver spinoff benefits in areas from recreational sports to volunteerism to Indigenous reconciliation.
"I just think it's going to bring an energy to this city we've never seen before," said Coun. Jaye Robinson, council's World Cup lead, who predicted the anticipated influx of more than 300,000 visitors will help Toronto finally recover from the pandemic.
But Gord Perks, the lone councillor who voted against joining the bid in 2018, said revelations about the withdrawal agreement show there's been a troubling lack of transparency around Toronto's expensive hosting plans. He predicted costs will only rise before Canada's national team kicks off the city's first match on June 12, 2026.
"Council was told there was a guard rail around the costs. It doesn't look as if that was actually true," Perks said.
CAPTION: Toronto signed a secret deal that would allow it to back out of hosting the 2026 World Cup if funding wasn't secured from senior levels of government. Then-mayor John Tory announced the city's successful bid in 2018.

Document 8 of 500

2 adults, 4 children found dead in Ottawa-area home, police say

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/2-adults-4-children-found-dead-ottawa-area-home/docview/2938516740/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=2+adults%2C+4+children+found+dead+in+Ottawa-area+home%2C+police+say&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ottawa police say four children and two adults are dead and a seventh person is injured after what the police chief is describing as an attack by a "lone actor" in an Ottawa suburb Wednesday night.
Police Chief Eric Stubbs initially used the phrase "mass shooting" in an interview with media, but a police spokesperson says he misspoke and a weapon has not been confirmed.
Police remain at the scene of a townhouse in Barrhaven late Thursday morning, as the forensics team in hazmat suits continue their investigation.
On Thursday morning, five marked police cars were parked on the street and in driveways near the home, which is a middle unit in a row of brick townhouses.
At around 9 a.m., investigators began loading something into a van parked in the driveway, while other officers blocked the view of the front door with a white sheet. A stretcher sat behind the van.
One man was arrested very shortly after police arrived last night, and officials say there is no ongoing threat to public safety. A seventh person was taken to hospital with serious but not life-threatening injuries.
Evidence of the prior evening's crime linger in the area with blood smeared on the door of the neighbouring house and on the sidewalk.
Mark Wong lives a few doors down from where the homicide took place. He said he was watching a movie at around 11 p.m. when he heard a scream and saw a man covered in blood run from the house to a police car just down the street. Wong said a group of police cruisers soon arrived shortly after.
Wong said he didn't know the family but always saw the man, around late 30s or early 40s, wash his car out front. "He always looked nice and smile," Wong said. Wong said two elementary schools are located nearby and people and kids always out and about walking their dogs.
The victims have not been identified but Sri Lanka's high commission in Ottawa says they were a family of Sri Lankan nationals.
Sri Lanka's high commission in Ottawa confirmed Thursday morning that the people who died were not Canadian citizens.
The high commission said the father survived but his wife and children died, adding it is in touch with their family members in the country's capital of Colombo.
Stubbs says police are still trying to determine the relationship between the suspect and the victims but it did not appear to be a case of domestic violence.
A neighbourhood stunned
Police were called to the home before 11 p.m. Wednesday, and discovered what police called "a horrific scene."
News of the multiple deaths was a shock for the community who describe the neighbourhood as very peaceful, safe and quiet.
Shanti Ramesh, who lives across the street, was alerted to a commotion late Wednesday. From her balcony, she saw a man sitting on the driveway of the home and yelling before two police officers arrived and carried him away.
Several more police cruisers and an ambulance arrived on the scene shortly afterward, Ramesh said.
Sayed Ahmadi said he arrived home at almost midnight on Wednesday evening and wasn't able to park his car because of the police presence. He said he didn't know the family and said they were new to the neighbourhood. On Thursday morning, blood spatter was still visible just outside his car.
He said he remembers seeing a man washing his car in the townhouse's driveway and kids aged about 7 or 8.
"This is very sad," Hamdi said.
Barrhaven is a fast-growing suburb about 20 kilometres south of the city's downtown core. The townhouse is located on Berrigan Drive, a busy through street located within a block of two different elementary schools.
Neighbours, many of them walking their kids to school, were horrified by the scene unfolding before them.
Ahmed Saed was walking his son to the school about a block away from the home Thursday. He said he lives nearby and walks his son past the home every morning.
"This is unbelievable for the whole neighbourhood," Saed said.
Sharlene Rollins, who lives down the street from the house and can see it from her back window, said she ran into other parents while walking her kids to school.
"You could feel that we didn't really want to chat about it with the kids around," Rollins told the Star.
Stubbs said police were still confirming the identities of all the victims.
Sharlene Hunter, a spokesperson for the Ottawa Catholic School Board, said she had not been told if the children attended one of the board's schools but said students and staff will be provided with any assistance or support they need.
"We at the Ottawa Catholic School Board want to express our deep sorrow for the tragic events that occurred in the Barrhaven community," she said.
"Our thoughts and prayers are with all affected by this loss."
Katie McNelly, who lives a short drive away, said she considered keeping her kids home until the police made clear there was no ongoing threat. She said she is in disbelief that it happened.
"What could cause someone or people to do this, you know, annihilate six people that had their lives to lead," she said. "I'll definitely be hugging my family a bit tight tonight."
Police said investigative teams remain at the site and asked anyone with information to contact the homicide unit.
Wilson Lo, the city councillor for the area, said he woke up to a phone call sharing the news and a number of "concerned and anxious" residents have contacted him.
"There is certainly a level of shock from residents," Lo told the Star. "These are people's neighbours, somebody's friends, somebody's classmates at school."
He said a full update from police is expected at a 1:30 p.m. press conference.
Ottawa Mayor Mark Sutcliffe called the news distressing for all of the city's residents.
"I was devastated to learn of the multiple homicide in Barrhaven, one of the most shocking incidents of violence in our city's history," Sutcliffe said in a post on X.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford also offered his condolences.
"This morning's news is heartbreaking," he said on X. "My thoughts are with the family and friends of the six victims and the entire Ottawa community who is reeling from this terrible tragedy."
With files from The Canadian Press.

Document 9 of 500

Preventing fatalities more important than saving a few minutes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/preventing-fatalities-more-important-than-saving/docview/2938516736/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Preventing+fatalities+more+important+than+saving+a+few+minutes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: 'This is for you, Billy': Family of cyclist killed in Scarborough speaks at memorial ride, March 3
I have lived and worked in Scarborough for most of my life and I know how frustrating it can be to drive there. I also know how reckless some Scarborough cyclists are, especially those riding on the sidewalks.
However, I am concerned that this article's question, "how (do we) make streets safer for cyclists without worsening congestion for drivers" treats the two road concerns as equal. This sort of false equivalency keeps us from protecting our cyclists and achieving our Vision Zero goals.
A driver's trip taking a few more minutes because of bike lanes or traffic should be considered far less significant than ensuring the safety and lives of bike riders.
Complaining drivers need to remember that with Bill Petropoulos, we are once again mourning a rider's actual death, a mortality that was seemingly senseless and avoidable. Preventing future fatalities is infinitely more important than saving drivers a few minutes on their commute.
Craig Leach, Scarborough
Students should discover interests before heading to collegeHigh school graduates have more university options than they may realize, March 2
I hate that parents push their children into careers based on prestige or money. This can lead to a lifetime of unhappiness. In my professional development teaching, I make students do the Holland (RIASEC) interest survey. R's generally like to work with things more than with people. I'slike to understand things rather than persuade others. A's like to work with self-expression more than rules, S's like to help people more than engage in technology. E's like to persuade or direct others rather than work on complicated topics. Finally, C's like to work indoors and to organize things.
Generally, people have one or two letters, which can help to direct them to specific career areas. Commonly, there are niches for people who like to work between data and things, or people and data or people and ideas, or ideas and things.
Unfortunately, this is not taught in high school, when students should be learning about their interests before following their passion in college or university with their parent's support.
Derrick Rancourt, Calgary
Trustees should be treated like MPPs and vice-versaMisbehaving school board trustees could lose 25% of pay under provincial proposal, Feb. 29
Trustees are way underpaid as is and they provide a vital service to public education - assisting parents and students.
How about MPPs, especially cabinet ministers, losing 25 per cent of their pay for their stupid decisions?
Also, minimum qualifications for integrity commissioners are a must. Otherwise, how can anyone trust their decisions?
Joel Hertz, Former YRDSB Trustee Vaughan-Thornhill, Toronto

Something special, something missedSpecial Olympics gala, hosted by Devo Brown, raises $275,000, March 2
I was disappointed in the Saturday Culture section which highlighted Motionball, the fundraising organization for the Special Olympics. There was a group of athletes surrounding Tessa Virtue, who was identified by name. It struck me that these special athletes deserved to be identified by name as well.
Well done to all these athletes!
Terri Proteau, Georgetown
Ford needs to talk to Harper's justice adviserHe helped shape Stephen Harper's 'tough on crime' agenda. It's why he says Doug Ford is wrong to stack the courts, March 1
Here's why a Stephen Harper loyalist regrets the tough-on-crime politics he once practised, Jan. 28, 2020
Law professor Benjamin Perrin, once the lead criminal justice adviser to former prime minister Stephen Harper, whose federal Conservative government dramatically increased the number of mandatory minimum sentences, says Ford's intention to appoint tough "like-minded judges" who will put even more people in jail is "deeply disturbing."
Once himself an ardent believer that people convicted of crimes were being punished too leniently, Perrin's views on criminal justice have changed dramatically over the years as he researched the root causes of crime, as well as solutions.
This perfectly illustrates Conservative ideological thinking. Pass laws in favour of mandatory minimum sentences, and then, only after-the-fact, do the research.
Clyde McDonald, Bracebridge
Supply management in our dairy industry worksWhy the steep rise in the cost of milk? March 2
I agree with a recent letter writer that milk prices are being raised by the retailers and want to remind my fellow Canadians that supply management in our dairy industry works.
It provides affordable milk products for consumers at retail prices most often less than prices in the USA, with no taxpayer provided subsidies. It makes the dairy industry in Canada sustainable and profitable as it employs 200,000 in the supply chain.
Don't believe the gaslighting our Conservative politics and think tanks bombard us with. They are blatantly false. I check USDA monthly, and our fluid dairy products are as a rule less expensive than the average USA prices in Canadian dollars and allowing that four litres is three per cent more than a U.S. gallon and 500g is 10 per cent more the 16 ounces or a pound.
Code Clements, Cherry Grove, Alta.
Suppliers need to make a profit
Why the steep rise in the cost of milk? March 2
Consumers need to understand the science behind retail product pricing, and that it has almost nothing to do with input costs. Grocery marketing is not a lot different than housing marketing. Products are on offer, and the consumer chooses whether to pay the on-offer fare, or not. Yes, it is the consumer who sets the price when the product is purchased. Not unreasonably, merchants want to sell products at the highest possible price, and if that price doesn't cover all associated costs, then the evidence is that selling the product is not profitable, and thus the product leaves the market.
There is nothing in a grocery store that is essential, there are alternative products and alternative vendors. We have deluded ourselves in believing that brand A is better than brand B. Informing that belief is the objective of advertising and loyalty programs.
Governments cannot regulate consumer level pricing and expect suppliers to deliver the goods if there is no profit.
Brian Williams, Belleville
Moving trucks onto Hwy 407 could slash travel times Ford government won't end tolls for trucks on Hwy 407, March 4
Premier Doug Ford has let the people of Ontario down again. Not allowing trucks to use Hwy 407 on some negotiated toll structure.
The lobby group Environmental Defense tabled a study by Eunomia that concluded that lifting truck tolls would move 12,000 to 21,000 trucks a day off Hwy 401 and slash travel times across the GTA by 80 minutes.
The transportation industry is the largest employer in Ontario and moves our city. Ford should re-think his decision.
Mark Borkowski, Toronto
CAPTION: A plaque is put on the ghost bike for Bill Petropoulos, the cyclist killed in Scarborough on Feb. 26.

Subject: Prices; Crime; Fatalities; Students; Milk; Consumers; Trustees; Suppliers; Profits; Dairy industry

Business indexing term: Subject: Consumers Trustees Suppliers Profits Dairy industry

Location: United States--US

People: Harper, Stephen

Document 10 of 500

National pharmacare not a sprint, but a marathon we might not finish

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/national-pharmacare-not-sprint-marathon-we-might/docview/2938516719/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=National+pharmacare+not+a+sprint%2C+but+a+marathon+we+might+not+finish&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ottawa's pharmacare initiative, unveiled last week, holds some promise of improvement in Canadian health care. But it will be a long time before it proves itself to have been a breakthrough in strengthening Canada's social safety net.
The Liberals have been promising national pharmacare since 2019. Five years later, they have presented a mini-pharmacare scheme that they say puts Canada on track to achieving genuine comprehensive pharmacare at some distant point.
But for now, the Liberals are averse to a comprehensive single-payer system like Medicare.
In 2019, Dr. Eric Hoskins, an esteemed former Ontario health minister, delivered a report that was emphatic in calling for a pharmacare system much like Medicare, a comprehensive single-payer system to replace today's patchwork of drug coverage.
Excluding drugs administered in hospital, pharmacare spending is covered by government insurance plans, employer schemes and private plans. And an estimated 14 per cent of drug expenditures are made out of pocket.
The mini-pharmacare plan tabled in Parliament last week by Mark Holland, the federal health minister, maintains that patchwork system.
To start, Holland's Bill C-64 proposed pharmacare legislation is expected to cost the federal and provincial governments about $1.5 billion per year, or just 0.4 per cent of total projected 2023 health-care spending in Canada of $344 billion.
The Parliamentary Budget Office (PBO) calculated last year that genuine public comprehensive pharmacare would cost the public sector an estimated $13.4 billion per year by 2027-28 in addition to what it already spends on drug coverage.
Bill C-64, which still requires legislative passage, covers only two major drug categories, diabetes and reproductive medications including birth control.
To the credit of the Liberal and NDP negotiators of this proposed legislation, they recognized that the out-of-pocket cost of reproductive health medications and devices is effectively a tax on women.
But while contraception and diabetes are worthy categories to have chosen, any pharmacare initiative that doesn't cover meds beyond that, including drugs for cancer treatment, heart conditions and risk of stroke, is so far from comprehensive that it almost seems not worth the bother.
And bother there will be. Once Bill C-64 becomes law, the pharmacare legislation requires Ottawa to negotiate its terms with 13 provinces and territories.
In those negotiations, tight-fisted provinces will balk at covering meds they deem non-essential. Or they will provide the same partial coverage that characterizes their existing drug plans.
And probably some provinces will try to impose co-pays that violate the principle of universal access.
Provinces withholding complete coverage will cite the majority of Canadians covered by existing public or employer drug plans. But those plans provide only partial coverage, and at the whim of the jurisdiction or the employer or private plan that provides the coverage.
It will be another year or so before the 14 governments including Ottawa settle on exactly which diabetes and contraceptive meds are covered and to what degree.
Bill C-64, once it receives royal assent, requires that a committee of experts be recruited by the federal health minister within 30 days to start work on options for "the operation and financing of national, universal, single-payer pharmacare" of far greater scope than the latest pharmacare initiative.
The feds already have in hand Hoskins' detailed blueprint for doing that. And Ottawa will not be obliged to act on the recommendations of the duplicative report called for by Bill C-64, enabling the government to kick the ball down the field.
And all bets on enhanced pharmacare are off if the Conservatives take power in Ottawa next year, as widely expected. The Tories are uninterested in expanding pharmacare.
Hoskins is confident the provinces will eventually sign on to Bill C-64, despite stated concerns about it last week in Quebec and Alberta. And that future governments will be obliged to maintain the new coverage.
"I think it will be difficult, if not impossible, to take these critically important medications away from four million diabetics and millions of women who both need and deserve them," Hoskins told the Star last week.
It will take time, though, for Hoskins' wider vision to be realized.
For some pharmacare advocates, the latest initiative qualifies as a promising start.
The Heart and Stroke Foundation responded to Bill C-64's announcement last week with praise for an initiative that provides "7.5 million uninsured and underinsured people in Canada access to prescription drugs for diabetes and contraception."
But the foundation calculates that 16 per cent of people with heart conditions, which fall outside this initiative's scope, have difficulty affording their blood pressure, cholesterol and heart disease prescriptions.
Advocates for Canadians suffering from Alzheimer's, severe arthritis, acute depression and other debilitating medical conditions are aligned with the Heart and Stroke Foundation in seeking far more extensive pharmacare coverage sooner than later.
For them and the millions of Canadians they represent, this first step only marks the start of what is sure to be a long marathon.
CAPTION: Any pharmacare initiative that doesn't cover meds for cancer treatment, heart conditions and risk of stroke is so far from comprehensive that it almost seems not worth the bother, David Olive writes.

Subject: Diabetes; Stroke; Legislation; Prescription drugs; Heart; Birth control; Cancer therapies; Medicare

Business indexing term: Subject: Medicare

Document 11 of 500

After backlash, Ford government backs down and allows female MPPs to speak about International Women's Day

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/after-backlash-ford-government-backs-down-allows/docview/2938516647/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=After+backlash%2C+Ford+government+backs+down+and+allows+female+MPPs+to+speak+about+International+Women%27s+Day&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The women won.
Bowing to pressure, the Ford government granted Liberal, Green and Independent MPPs time to speak about International Women's Day in the Ontario legislature after initially denying them the opportunity.
On Wednesday, government House Leader Paul Calandra said no to a motion that would have allowed the group of female MPPs five minutes - in total - to address the importance of the day. But after an outcry - which was backed by the Official Opposition NDP - and a news conference Thursday morning by seven MPPs to denounce the move, the Tories backed off.
The group is now seeking an apology.
"This government thinks they can squash our spirits and silence us," said Liberal MPP Mary-Margaret McMahon (Beaches-East York).
"And you know what? We're not going to take it lying down. We're going to stand up for ourselves. We're going to stand up for young women and girls everywhere," she said. "And we all know that famous quote, 'Well-behaved women seldom make history.' So look out."
Independent MPP Bobbi Ann Brady (Haldimand-Norfolk) said, "International Women's Day is not a partisan celebration, and parliamentary privilege should afford us all equal opportunity if we choose to speak about the contributions women in our lives have made."
Speaking to reporters Thursday, Calandra - who is also the minister of municipal affairs and housing - said he routinely says no to non-government unanimous consent motions and was not aware of the group's request.
"It was not ever an intention not to allow them to speak," he said, adding "it's important that they have an opportunity to speak."
The group, however, said there were communications a week ago, and produced an email sent to his office with the request.
McMahon also alleged that Calandra made light of the situation on Wednesday, making a crying gesture "and then he opened up his desk and handed a box of tissues ... It was absolutely deplorable, demeaning and disgraceful."
There is no video available of that alleged gesture.
Calandra told reporters that interaction "was on a separate issue" with Liberal House Leader John Fraser and "had nothing to do" with the women's request.
Fraser, however, said he did not engage with Calandra other than to look at him when the request was denied.
Charmaine Williams, the associate minister of Women's Social and Economic Opportunity, told the Star she was pleased to see "all members speaking for International Women's Day."
After the original decision, "there's always time for sober second thoughts, and we had one, and we have an opportunity for everybody to share this International Women's Day."
The day is celebrated on Friday, but given the legislature is not sitting, the MPPs spoke Thursday afternoon in the house.
"I'm grateful for the chance to speak on International Women's Day despite the government's attempt to prevent the Independent members from speaking and representing their constituents," said Liberal MPP Stephanie Bowman (Don Valley West) urging the government to apologize.
"They misuse their power to silence opposition, contradicting the democratic principles they were elected to uphold."
Scarborough-Guildwood Liberal MPP Andrea Hazell said, "We must always find opportunities to impact the lives of women and young girls by showing up for them and giving them a shot at having equal opportunities."
But she also addressed PC MPPs, saying "to the women in the governing party, I sincerely hope you felt our pain and raised your voices to the men who stood in our way because loyalty to your party should never come second to standing up for all our women and our young girls."
CAPTION: Liberal MPP Andrea Hazell (Scarborough-Guildwood) joined MPP Stephanie Bowman (Don Valley West) to speak on behalf of a group of MPPs who wanted to address International Women's Day in the Ontario legislature

Subject: Press conferences; Women

Document 12 of 500

Ontario legislature honours Mulroney: Political leaders of all stripes remember former prime minister as a seminal Canadian figure

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-legislature-honours-mulroney/docview/2938227467/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+legislature+honours+Mulroney%3A+Political+leaders+of+all+stripes+remember+former+prime+minister+as+a+seminal+Canadian+figure&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Crusader against apartheid in South Africa, champion of the environment countering acid rain pollution, tax reformer and free trader.
Former prime minister Brian Mulroney was remembered at Queen's Park Wednesday as a seminal Canadian figure.
Political leaders of all stripes paid tribute to Mulroney, who died Friday at 84, for his nine years as prime minister between 1984 and 1993.
"Prime minister Mulroney had a massive impact on Ontario and Canada - his vision, his leadership profoundly shaped our nation," said Premier Doug Ford, who counts Treasury Board President Caroline Mulroney, the late prime minister's eldest child, as a key member of his cabinet.
"He championed economic and tax reforms to build a stronger economy. He's the reason that today we do over $1.2 trillion in two-way trade with the United States every year," said the premier.
Ford hailed Mulroney for influencing world leaders like British prime minister Margaret Thatcher and U.S. president Ronald Reagan "to play a leading role in ending South Africa's racist apartheid system and in the international response to the Ethiopian famine."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles touted Mulroney's lead on economic sanctions against the all-white South African regime that oppressed the Black majority and his calls for the release of imprisoned anti-apartheid leader Nelson Mandela.
Mandela visited Canada four months after he was freed from prison in 1990 to thank Mulroney.
"While there's no question that the fight for liberation in South Africa was won by South Africans, by people organizing and resisting, sanctions were an absolutely critical factor in applying international pressure to the apartheid state," said Stiles, whose parents were aid workers in Africa.
"Personally, I saw another side to that role that Canada played, because Canada also used, under prime minister Mulroney, our international development heft to support the front-line states in their resistance against apartheid as well," she said.
Liberal leader John Fraser said it was Mulroney's battle against then Grit leader John Turner over free trade with the U.S. "that got me off the couch in 1988 to go and knock on doors in Ottawa South.
"That was the spark that led me here some 25 years later," said Fraser, who had opposed the Progressive Conservative prime minister's push for more open trade stateside.
"Since then, I've come closer to his position on free trade and that's the thing about political legacies - our victories, our mistakes, our missed opportunities are always looked at through the lens of the present," he said.
"There is a legacy that is more important - and that is the mark that you leave on people's hearts."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner praised Mulroney for his work to curb acid rain, which was such an environmental threat decades ago.
"I want to highlight prime minister Mulroney's game-changing work on environmental issues," said Schreiner.
"Securing a treaty on acid rain with the U.S. made our lakes and rivers cleaner, (and) making Canada the first industrialized country to ratify the convention on biological diversity is even more relevant today," he said.
"International co-operation can help us fight existential threats."
Mulroney will lie in state on Parliament Hill before a state funeral March 23 in Montreal.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Apartheid; Leadership; Free trade; Acid rain; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership Free trade
