Document 28 of 500

Tory tensions flare after byelection win: New Durham MP rails against 'liberal elites' - including Ford minister

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tory-tensions-flare-after-byelection-win/docview/2937587487/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tory+tensions+flare+after+byelection+win%3A+New+Durham+MP+rails+against+%27liberal+elites%27+-+including+Ford+minister&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There appears to be no love lost between Premier Doug Ford's governing Progressive Conservatives and Pierre Poilievre's federal Opposition Conservatives.
Tensions between the two Tory parties, smouldering for months, have flared up in recent weeks.
In his victory speech after Monday's Durham byelection, newly elected federal Conservative Jamil Jivani lashed out against "liberal elites," including those running the show at Queen's Park.
"And when I say 'liberal elites,' I am talking about Justin Trudeau and the Liberal party, but not just them," Jivani, who worked for Ford as an adviser from 2019 until 2022, told supporters.
"I'm also talking about the liberal elites who run big banks and big telecommunications companies driving up the cost of everything," said the former talk-radio host, who was sacked from Bell Media in 2021.
"I am also talking about the liberal elites who run the Ontario Ministry of Education in this province," he thundered after winning a byelection with 57.4 per cent of the vote to 22.5 per cent for the Liberals, 10.4 per cent for the NDP, 4.4 per cent for the People's Party of Canada and 2.2 per cent for the Greens.
Jivani's attack against Tory Education Minister Stephen Lecce's department comes seven weeks after Poilievre blindsided Ford by poaching cabinet minister Parm Gill to run federally in Milton.
The premier had no notice that his red tape reduction minister, who has yet to be replaced, would be immediately jumping ship even though a federal election is not expected until October 2025.
That sparked some uncomfortable conversations between officials with both parties, insiders confide.
Asked Tuesday about Jivani's broadside, Lecce tried to shrug it off.
"I'm a minister focused on getting deals. That's what I've done today and I want to celebrate the fact that kids are in school," he said after making an announcement at Queen's Park about a tentative agreement with the province's Catholic teachers.
Privately, some Ford Tories were seething.
"His opponent is Justin Trudeau; he may have forgotten that," said one senior provincial insider, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations.
Even though Jivani worked as Ford's "community opportunities advocate" - a job the premier created for him - he was very critical of Lecce and his ministry during the COVID-19 pandemic, especially around school closures.
He frequently castigated him in National Post opinion columns, and in June 2022 took to social media to say the ministry "continues to be led by an incompetent minister ... who refuses to adequately acknowledge the harmful impact of his decisions on the most vulnerable children. Ontario deserves better."
In one 2021 column - published while he was still on Ford's payroll - Jivani derided Lecce as "a woke liberal in conservative clothing who has turned his back on parents."
"Lecce offers the kind of virtue signalling that Ontarians came to expect from the previous Liberal government, and very little in terms of real solutions that would benefit children," he wrote of his fellow Tory.
Still, publicly, there were some perfunctory messages of congratulations from provincial Tories for the incoming Durham MP on social media.
Business Minister Todd McCarthy, who represents Durham provincially, said he did not have time to help Jivani on the byelection.
"I'm very busy with my ministry and my job representing the province, but I look forward to working with him jointly representing the riding," said McCarthy.
Behind the scenes, Ford's Tories, who work closely with Trudeau's governing Liberals on numerous issues, have kept their distance from Poilievre's surging party.
The premier has repeatedly instructed elected officials and staffers not to work on federal byelections because he does not want to undermine the many projects he is doing with the prime minister.
Last June, Ford forbade his team from helping Tory candidate Arpan Khanna win the federal byelection in Oxford.
As retribution, the federal party did not dispatch volunteers to help Progressive Conservative standard-bearer Sean Webster, who lost to Liberal Karen McCrimmon in Kanata-Carleton a month later.
"Imagine how much 80 interns could have done in Kanata-Carleton?" a senior federal Conservative told the Star that night.
"We were always going to win Oxford, so them not helping us didn't have an impact. You don't think we could have helped them find 651 votes?" said the federal insider, referring to the Liberal margin in Kanata-Carleton.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie and Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau Stephanie Levitz Deputy Toronto Star

Subject: Conservatism; Social networks; Elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Social networks

People: Trudeau, Justin; Ford, Doug

Document 29 of 500

Prison service says MP's rink story untrue

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/prison-service-says-mps-rink-story-untrue/docview/2937587170/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Prison+service+says+MP%27s+rink+story+untrue&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's prison service says there has been no functioning ice hockey rink for the past two winters at an institution where a Conservative MP says notorious inmates can play "taxpayer-funded serial killer pickup hockey."
Frank Caputo toured the La Macaza Institution last month - and says that, after visiting Paul Bernardo's own cell, he even came face-to-face with the serial rapist and convicted murderer.
Caputo decried the availability of recreational facilities for vicious criminals, but Correctional Service Canada says there is currently no working hockey rink or tennis court at the prison, located about 190 kilometres from Montreal.
And the agency says its understanding is the MP did not "interact" with the infamous convict.
Bernardo was transferred to the medium-security prison last year from the penitentiary in Ontario where he lived for decades.
His move set off a political firestorm for the Liberals, as Tories demanded he be returned to serve out his indeterminate life sentence in maximum-security conditions.
Bernardo was imprisoned for the sexual assault and murder of 15-year-old Kristen French and 14-year-old Leslie Mahaffy, in the early 1990s near St. Catharines.
He was also convicted of manslaughter in the December 1990 death of 15-year-old Tammy Homolka, the younger sister of his then wife, Karla Homolka. Bernardo, who was designated a dangerous offender, has admitted to sexually assaulting 14 other women.
The prison service has said procedures were followed and Bernardo met the criteria to be reclassified as a medium-security inmate.
Caputo, a Conservative MP from B.C. who serves as a party critic in Parliament on justice matters, released a video on social media detailing a tour he took of the institution. In the video, Caputo said he was provided access to Bernardo's cell while the inmate was away.
He said that, after a "couple of minutes," he turned around to see Bernardo, whose likeness was "unmistakable. Just seeing him, coming eye to eye with him, I had a physical reaction," he said.
The correctional service confirmed Caputo and union representatives visited the prison in February and officials granted the MP's request to access Bernardo's cell while the inmate wasn't in. However, said spokesperson Kevin Antonucci, "it is our understanding that participants did not interact with Paul Bernardo during their visit."
The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Stephanie Taylor The Canadian Press

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+When+does+ordinary+become+a+good+thing+in+a+car%3F+Should+this+thing+be+called+the+Honda+Common+Sense%3F+Brian+Mulroney%3A+How+do+you+remember+the+former+prime+minister%3F+Is+Liberty+Village+full%3F+Residents+push+back+against+proposed+43-storey+tower%2C+A+transparent+police+force%3F+In+Toronto+that%27s+fantasy%2C+not+reality%2C+A+transparent+police+force%3F+In+Toronto+that%27s+fantasy%2C+not+reality%2C+Ford+is+supposed+to+represent+all+Ontarians%2C+not+just+the+like-minded%2C+March+2&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Review should mention it's the most stolen car
When does ordinary become a good thing in a car? Should this thing be called the Honda Common Sense? March 4
The fact that this vehicle is one of the most stolen of all cars and insurance rates are starting to skyrocket when your vehicle is stolen is completely absent from this and other reviews.
It is time journalists start publishing what steps - if any - manufacturers are taking with their products to combat theft when reviewing automobiles.
I am aware that increased thefts create a boost in replacement car sales and where is the insurance industry on this? I understand that some repeatedly stolen models are no longer being insured by some companies in the U.S. and they are advertising this. A direct impact on sales will make manufactures more attentive to designed in anti-theft measures.
I recently bought a new car and deliberated on a used model which was less of a theft target and finally bought a new vehicle that is way down on the theft target list.
If more consumers did this, anti-theft changes would be made.
Paul Einarson, Race Director - British Auto Racing Club, Milton
Mulroney a good leader, but free trade has hurt us
Brian Mulroney: How do you remember the former prime minister? March 1
It is not nice to rain on somebody's parade especially with their passing, but the overwhelming adulation of former prime minister Brian Mulroney omits the reality that this country has gone downhill ever since he forced free trade down our throats against the will of the majority of Canadians.
Everything the opposing forces, the Council of Canadians, the labour movement, and many economists, forecast has come to be. Our health system, our education system, and social services more generally have all plummeted. Working people are working longer many with little or no security. Wages have not kept up with inflation. We have a drug and crime infested society. We clearly have become a vassal of U.S. foreign policy and we no longer control our economy which is largely foreign owned. The free trade agreements were not fair-trade agreements and together have taken the country backwards.
There was of course an upside; the rich got a hell of a lot richer.
But with his passing we should give Mulroney his due. He showed strong leadership when standing up to U.S. president Ronald Reagan and U.K. prime minister Margaret Thatcher with his support of ending apartheid in South Africa and befriending Nelson Mandela. Mulroney was also a leading voice in addressing the ozone environmental crisis. He was a strong leader and I only wish there was someone of his strength and calibre that would undo the negative effects of free trade and restore Canada as a sovereign nation.
Ron Brydges, St. Catharines
Green space is key to livable communities
Is Liberty Village full? Residents push back against proposed 43-storey tower, March 4
Providing sufficient public green space is fundamental to creating livable communities for the millions of new residents our existing cities can accommodate. Yet, at the doorstep of Liberty Village, the Doug Ford government is privatizing the massive existing public green space at Ontario Place, which so many have pointed out is critical to ensuring "room to breathe" given most new housing in the city is predominantly multi-storey condos.
The failure of the Ford government to recognize this most basic of community building principles will diminish the future livability of Toronto.
Victor Doyle, Toronto
Argument misses tenet of police governance
A transparent police force? In Toronto that's fantasy, not reality, March 2
Shawn Micallef raises some interesting points regarding the expectation of privacy vs. transparency, but he misses a fundamental tenet of police governance. Regardless of the fact that the TPS budget primarily comes from municipal taxes, the employer of TPS members, including the chief, is the Police Services Board. Except in specific circumstances, discussions about that employer/employee relationship are confidential - just like any other employment relationship.
Doug Lewis, Clarington
To serve and protect who? And in what order?
A transparent police force? In Toronto that's fantasy, not reality, March 2
Shawn Micallef's article is a very good effort at demonstrating how frustrating it can be when dealing with Toronto police. It is really quite simple: their motto "to serve and protect" should read, "to serve and protect first other police officers, second their union and third the public."
David Gladstone, Toronto
Judges should
be apolitical
Ford is supposed to represent all Ontarians, not just the like-minded, March 2
There are three letters that I'd like to address, those of Larry Rayner, Claire Hoy and Marvin Zuker, a judge.
Both Rayner and Hoy conveniently neglected to mention that the system as it was set up, and as it existed before Doug Ford became premier, though perhaps imperfect, was acceptable to the legal profession and all political parties.
As to Rayner's comment about there being no outrage when former premiers Dalton McGuinty and Kathleen Wynne appointed judges, that was likely because they followed the procedure acceptable to those involved. And Hoy's comment, about it only becoming controversial when appointees lean right instead of left, has absolutely no validity based on fact. It is not the judges who have been appointed that is the issue. It is the method by which they are appointed.
Both Hoy and Rayner would learn something from the third letter by Marvin Zuker, himself a judge, appointed by the Conservative government of Bill Davis.
Zuker, in his own words regarding his appointment states, "If Ford had been at the helm, I would definitely be employed somewhere else."
He went on to say; "It's a sad day when so many incredibly competent and qualified lawyers who are not of like mind to the Ford government."
No doubt this is a sentiment that is completely beyond the understanding of Rayner or Hoy, as well as the Ford government and its current attorney general. Given the complete silence of the remaining cabinet members and the PC party caucus, therein lies the real tragedy.
Edward Seymour, Hamilton

Document 31 of 500

Chow inherits rising tab of beautiful game

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/chow-inherits-rising-tab-beautiful-game/docview/2937587053/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chow+inherits+rising+tab+of+beautiful+game&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's not uncommon for a political leader to discover as they settle into office that their predecessors have bequeathed to them a vexing problem or two.
For Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, the World Cup deal agreed to by John Tory, is just such a matter - a big-ticket commitment on which the meter continues to run.
As it stands, the cost of hosting six 2026 World Cup games in Toronto has soared to an eye-popping $380 million - or about $60 million a match.
Given the problems facing this city, it's a safe guess Chow could happily have found other uses for the money.
But the decision made during Tory times leaves the mayor with little choice but to work to keep costs in line, squeeze the largest possible help from the federal and provincial governments, and make the most of the opportunity of hosting the biggest event of the most popular game in the world.
There will be tangible benefits, such as infrastructure improvements. And the games are sure to be a tourist draw, meaning hotels, restaurants and souvenir vendors will be hopping.
There are also intangible benefits, including the buzz that comes with being a centre of such a huge global event, and the fact the diverse nature of Toronto ensures there are passionate backers of just about every nation competing and that every victory will see a flag-waving, horn-honking parade up Yonge Street.
Critics may say that would have happened if the games - co-hosted by Canada, the United States and Mexico, with 10 games in Canada and six in Toronto - were being played anywhere in the world and watched on TV, as most Canadians (given that the affluent are likely to dominate the seats) will still be doing.
It must be doubly vexing to Chow that the terms of the contracts require the City of Toronto not to disclose their contents.
As critics have said, secrecy is often a breeding ground for waste, abuse and corruption.
That being the case, with the watchdogs watching, the frugal fuming and soccer fans beside themselves with excitement, the mayor must weave a Lionel Messi-like course.
The clever ball-handling was evident in her comments in the wake of the latest cost update. Chow said she was excited to bring the world to Toronto.
The city's estimate that hosting the World Cup games would generate at least $392 million in GDP for Toronto and $120 million in tax revenue for the region "is certainly good for the country and good for the businesses and excellent for soccer fans."
The city will "do what we can," Chow said to reduce costs and find new sources of revenue to underwrite the event.
For leaders, the issue of hosting mega-events is inherently fraught, involving as it does the incendiary issues of big money, national pride, global competition, glory and heartbreak. Both cost and value need to be considered and weighed.
Over the next two years, Chow will need to cluck in sympathy with those opposed to a governing ethos of bread and circuses, or, to be frank, anything to do with the governing soccer body of FIFA, or to joining a recent list of host regimes such as Qatar and Russia.
At the same time, she mustn't come off as a killjoy incapable of understanding just how much this particular game means to so many.
Chow also knows that in the heat of competition, things can change.
The Ontario government said, after the report of ballooning costs, that it won't help cover the higher bill by increasing the $97-million commitment announced in December.
But if bet-happy sports fans know anything, it's this. Premier Doug Ford has demonstrated a world-class talent for reversing himself.
Seeing how 2026 is an Ontario election year, and knowing the premier's style of folksy appeal, and given his fondness for spectacle, Chow can reasonably bank on the fact he'll change his mind and pony up.
CREDIT: ;

Document 32 of 500

Tory MP Jamil Jivani slams 'liberal elites' at Queen's Park, ruffling feathers in Ford government

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/tory-mp-jamil-jivani-slams-liberal-elites-at/docview/2938023378/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tory+MP+Jamil+Jivani+slams+%27liberal+elites%27+at+Queen%27s+Park%2C+ruffling+feathers+in+Ford+government&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There appears to be no love lost between Premier Doug Ford's governing Progressive Conservatives and Pierre Poilievre's federal opposition Conservatives.
Tensions between the two Tory parties, smouldering for months, have flared up in recent weeks.
In his victory speech after Monday's Durham byelection, newly elected federal Conservative Jamil Jivani lashed out against "liberal elites," including those running the show at Queen's Park.
"And when I say 'liberal elites,' I am talking about Justin Trudeau and Liberal party, but not just them," Jivani, who worked for Ford as an adviser from 2019 until 2022, told supporters.
"I'm also talking about the liberal elites who run big banks and big telecommunications companies driving up the cost of everything," said the former talk-radio host, who was sacked from Bell Media in 2021.
"I am also talking about the liberal elites who run the Ontario Ministry of Education in this province," he thundered after winning a byelection with 57.4 per cent of the vote to 22.5 per cent for the Liberals, 10.4 per cent for the NDP, 4.4 per cent for the PPC and 2.2 per cent for the Greens.
Jivani's attack against Tory Education Minister Stephen Lecce's department comes seven weeks after Poilievre blindsided Ford by poaching cabinet minister Parm Gill to run federally in Milton.
The premier had no notice that his then-red tape reduction minister, who has yet to be replaced, would be immediately jumping ship even though a federal election is not expected until October 2025.
That sparked some uncomfortable conversations between officials with both parties, insiders confide.
Asked Tuesday about Jivani's broadside, Lecce tried to shrug it off.
"I'm a minister focused on getting deals. That's what I've done today and I want to celebrate the fact that kids are in school," he said after making an announcement at Queen's Park about a tentative agreement with the province's Catholic teachers.
Privately, some Ford Tories were seething.
"His opponent is Justin Trudeau, he may have forgotten that," said one senior provincial insider, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations.
Even though Jivani worked as Ford's "community opportunities advocate" - a job the premier created for him - he was very critical of Lecce and his ministry during the COVID-19 pandemic, especially around school closures.
He frequently castigated him in National Post opinion columns, and in June 2022 took to social media to say the ministry "continues to be led by an incompetent minister ... who refuses to adequately acknowledge the harmful impact of his decisions on the most vulnerable children. Ontario deserves better."
In one 2021 column - published while he was still on Ford's payroll - Jivani derided Lecce as "a woke liberal in conservative clothing who has turned his back on parents."
"Lecce offers the kind of virtue signalling that Ontarians came to expect from the previous Liberal government, and very little in terms of real solutions that would benefit children," he wrote of his fellow Tory.
Still, publicly, there were some perfunctory messages of congratulations from provincial Tories for the incoming Durham MP on social media.
Business Minister Todd McCarthy, who represents Durham provincially, said he did not have time to help Jivani on the byelection.
"I'm very busy with my ministry and my job representing the province, but I look forward to working with him jointly representing the riding," said McCarthy.
Behind the scenes, Ford's Tories, who work closely with Trudeau's governing Liberals on numerous issues, have kept their distance from Poilievre's surging party.
The premier has repeatedly instructed elected officials and staffers not to work on federal byelections because he does not want to undermine the many projects he is doing with the prime minister.
Last June, Ford forbade his team from helping Tory candidate Arpann Khanna win the federal byelection in Oxford.
As retribution, the federal party did not dispatch volunteers to help Progressive Conservative standard-bearer Sean Webster, who lost to Liberal Karen McCrimmon, in Kanata-Carleton a month later.
"Imagine how much 80 interns could have done in Kanata-Carleton?" a senior federal Conservative told the Star that night.
"We were always going to win Oxford, so them not helping us didn't have an impact. You don't think we could have helped them find 651 votes?" said the federal insider, referring to the Liberal margin in Kanata-Carleton.
CAPTION: Jamil Jivani, then Ontario'sAdvocate for Community Opportunitiesspeaks as Premier Doug Ford looks on during the Queen's Park daily COVID-19 briefing on June 4, 2020.

Document 33 of 500

Progressive Conservative candidate for Milton byelection once sought federal Liberal nomination

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/progressive-conservative-candidate-milton/docview/2938023356/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Progressive+Conservative+candidate+for+Milton+byelection+once+sought+federal+Liberal+nomination&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's candidate in the upcoming Milton byelection has a Liberal past.
Zee Hamid, named the Progressive Conservative standard-bearer on Sunday, has donated $3,508 to the rival Liberals since 2014 - including the local riding association and former leader Steven Del Duca's 2020 campaign to helm the party, according to Elections Ontario records.
A tech industry professional and father of six, Hamid served on Milton council for 12 years and ran unsuccessfully for the Liberal nomination ahead of the 2015 federal election.
Hamid and the PC media office did not reply to interview requests Tuesday from the Star.
A key Ford cabinet member brushed aside questions Tuesday about Hamid's apparent political conversion in a swing riding where the Conservatives are concerned Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie may run for a seat in the legislature.
"We have a lot of things in common. He wants to get more homes built faster, so I'm very excited about the byelection coming up," said Housing Minister Paul Calandra.
A date has not yet been set for a vote to replace former Conservative MPP and red tape reduction minister Parm Gill, who abruptly quit in January to return to federal politics. Gill won the riding by just 1,680 votes over Liberal Sameera Ali in 2022.
Hamid was also a delegate for Del Duca - now mayor of Vaughan - at the 2020 Liberal leadership convention and served as a campaign captain in the Milton area, helping propel Del Duca to a first ballot victory.
"He's a kick-ass candidate," said a source close to Del Duca who spoke privately to discuss internal deliberations. "The Tories are lucky to have him. He'll bring in people from all sides."
Hamid ran for Milton mayor in the 2022 municipal election, losing by fewer than 1,000 votes to veteran incumbent Gordon Krantz, who has held the post since 1980.
Crombie, the former three-term mayor of Mississauga, has said she is considering whether to run in Milton but no final decision has been made.
"I can't speak to why they chose him (Hamid) as a candidate ... what I do know is in byelections, they're very local races," said Liberal MPP John Fraser (Ottawa South), who heads the party's nine-member caucus in the legislature.
When Hamid ran for mayor of Milton in the last municipal election, he was endorsed by Milton Liberal MP and Olympic gold medallist Adam van Koeverden.
"He's experienced, extremely capable, and one of the most present and accessible leaders in Milton. It's a privilege to endorse his candidacy for Mayor of Milton," van Koeverden wrote in an Instagram post with a picture of them in a smiling selfie.
"Milton deserves strong local representation at Queen's Park," Hamid said in a statement Sunday in which Ford described him as "a champion for Milton."
The New Democrats and Green Party have not yet nominated candidates in the riding.

CAPTION: Zee Hamid poses in downtown Milton during his mayoral campaign on Sept. 27, 2022.

State funeral for Brian Mulroney to be held March 23

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/state-funeral-brian-mulroney-be-held-march-23/docview/2937369611/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=State+funeral+for+Brian+Mulroney+to+be+held+March+23&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Former prime minister Brian Mulroney will lie in state on Parliament Hill before a state funeral to be held in his home of Montreal, the federal government announced Tuesday.
In a statement, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's office said the funeral will take place on March 23, after Mulroney's casket is available for public viewing in Ottawa and Montreal.
"These events will give Canadians an opportunity to salute Mr. Mulroney's contributions to public life and offer their condolences. More details will be provided in due course," the Prime Minister's Office said.
Mulroney, who had been treated for prostate cancer last year, died after a fall in Palm Beach, Florida. His death was announced Feb. 29 by his daughter Caroline Mulroney, an Ontario cabinet minister.
Since then, friends and former political rivals alike have paid tribute to the former Progressive Conservative leader who governed from 1984 to 1993.
During two terms in office, he fought apartheid, brought Canada into a North American free trade pact, passed significant environmental protections, and tried repeatedly but unsuccessfully to win Quebec's signature on the Canadian Constitution.
He befriended American politicians, championed the release of jailed South African apartheid opponent Nelson Mandela, and helped create the Francophonie.
State funerals are elaborate affairs where the remains of the prime minister lie in state to allow dignitaries and ordinary Canadians to pay respects. They usually include sombre processions and church funerals, tailored to a family's wishes.
A state funeral was held for past prime ministers Pierre Elliot Trudeau and John Turner, governors general Ramon Hnatyshyn and Roméo LeBlanc, and were offered by the governments of the day for "eminent Canadians" like former NDP leaders Jack Layton and Ed Broadbent, and former finance minister Jim Flaherty.
The Mulroney funeral is being organized by the federal heritage department and the former prime minister's family.
CAPTION: Former Prime Minister Brian Mulroney stands during question period in the House of Commons on Parliament Hill, in Ottawa on Nov. 6, 2017.

Subject: Apartheid; Decades; Funerals; Prime ministers

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; Canada

People: Mulroney, Brian

Document 35 of 500

The uncertainty of the pharmacare pledge

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/uncertainty-pharmacare-pledge/docview/2937369591/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+uncertainty+of+the+pharmacare+pledge&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you're one of the 3.7 million Canadians living with diabetes, the federal government's first step toward a national pharmacare program is welcome news indeed. It is similarly welcome news for the estimated nine million people of reproductive age who rely on contraceptive drugs.
Diabetes medication and contraception will be covered for all Canadians under a deal cobbled together by the federal Liberals and New Democrats. A number of types of insulin, a drug used by type 1 and type 2 diabetics, which the federal government says can cost patients between $900 and $1,700 per year, will be covered.
Maybe some day. There is less here than meets the eye.
The pharmacare framework fell something short of "historic," as it architects shouted in unveiling the proposed legislation. It may prove to be historic at some point far down the road, but like so much in this plan, it's impossible to tell. The plan carries with it a heavy load of what-if, maybe and we'll see.
It is not a single-payer pharmacare program and it may or may not be palatable to provinces. The only certainty at this point is that the Liberal minority government has bought some time and Jagmeet Singh and his New Democrats will make single-payer, universal pharmacare a key plank in the next election.
The first 'what if' stems from the fact that health care is a provincial responsibility. Ottawa will have to negotiate 13 agreements and in some provincial capitals anything that comes its way from the federal Liberals is immediately viewed with suspicion, if not outright hostility. Quebec, as it routinely does, has opted out of the federal plan and Alberta initially said it was not interested, but after that initial rejection, Danielle Smith's government softened its tone and appeared more interested in getting a fair share of available federal funding. Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones has been non-committal.
The second 'maybe' revolves around the prohibitive cost of a complete pharmacare plan for this Liberal government which lacks the fiscal room for such an investment, estimated by Parliamentary Budget Officer to be $11.2 billion for the first year. Yves Giroux found total prescription drug spending in this country totalled $36.6 billion in 2021-22. Of that amount, 46 per cent was covered by governments, 40 per cent by private insurers and 14 per cent was paid-out-of-pocket. By starting with diabetes and contraception, federal Health Minister Mark Holland shared an estimated price tag of $1.5 billion annually, but he made it clear that price was not set in stone.
And the 'we'll see' revolves around timing and politics.
Holland hopes this pilot program will be in place next year, an election year. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, who has maintained a healthy polling lead over Trudeau and Singh, is unlikely to be prepared to commit to a multi-billion dollar expansion to this plan. A new government could decide federal coverage should be more targeted to filling gaps for those with no coverage, or do nothing.
Singh is gambling that this tentative first step to pharmacare will be so wildly popular that voters will be demanding its expansion by the fall of 2025. At very least he will argue that the Liberals will not commit to a full pharmacare plan -- they have promised one and reneged many times over -- and it may be difficult for Poilievre to roll back diabetes and contraception coverage if this program is up and running before the election.
Regardless, Singh will point to victories from his pact with Justin Trudeau which include dental care, the start of a pharmacare program and labour reforms protecting federal workers.
The real question is whether voters will give him - or Trudeau - any credit for these programs or whether the NDP will be judged to be merely the enabler of deeply unpopular government. Polling data indicates Singh is being blamed for Trudeau's sins more than he is credited with pushing for these programs. The voters' verdict awaits him next year and a single-payer universal pharmacare program may yet again be dead on arrival. But New Democrats deserve credit for pushing a reluctant Liberal government at least this far.
CAPTION: NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh speaks about drug coverage for Canadians. The NDP has made pharmacarea central pledge of their parliamentary alliance with the minority Liberal government.

Subject: Diabetes; Prescription drugs; Voters; Birth control

People: Singh, Jagmeet

Document 36 of 500

Vote! It's the only way to bring about change

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/vote-only-way-bring-about-change/docview/2937369590/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Vote%21+It%27s+the+only+way+to+bring+about+change&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Take it from me, a millionaire, Canada must lead on wealth taxes, March 1
I regularly tell our various elected representatives that I never asked for my taxes to be cut. Rather, raise my taxes, but be sure to also raise the taxes of the rich.
Over recent decades, the tax rates levied on the rich have been reduced. Why?
Poverty has increased. Housing is at a crisis level.
Non-profit and co-op housing programs were cancelled, leaving low- and middle-income people with nowhere to live.
Calling all voters - please VOTE. You and the millionaire down the street each have one vote. We are many votes - so use them! That's the only way we will bring about change.
Kate Chung, Toronto
When will Freeland live up to her reputation and tax the superrich?
Take it from me, a millionaire, Canada must lead on wealth taxes, March 1
As Claire Trottier points out, everyone, even the superrich, acknowledge that her group needs to pay more taxes. In this country we have four income tax brackets for people earning up to $246,752, and only one tax rate for everybody above that rate. Fairness says that the more you make, the more you pay - up to $246,752. At that point "fairness" says that you should pay the same tax rate, no matter how high your income goes.
Before entering politics, Chrystia Freeland was a renowned author. Her book "Plutocrats: The Rise of the New Global Super-Rich and the Fall of Everyone Else," explains to us the ethical, political and economic dangers of extreme wealth. She talks about social responsibility, fairness and the need for a more equitable society.
When she entered politics, I was thrilled that finally we had a politician with full grasp of the problems with income inequality. I was disappointed when her presence in Ottawa seemed to have no effect on this ongoing concern. When she was appointed Finance Minister, I was delighted. Finally, a true believer was placed in a position with the ability to something about it. Yet again, disappointment.
How can we ever hope to mitigate this problem when a champion of taxing the superrich, with the ability to do something about it, sits on her hands and refuses to do anything about it.
Ken Caudle, Brampton
Investing in Venture funds would benefit lower income earners more than wealth tax
Take it from me, a millionaire, Canada must lead on wealth taxes, March 1
Claire Trottier and her wealthy friends are joining the growing calls for a wealth tax. I have a suggestion: Instead of calling for more taxes, put your and your friend's money into Venture funds to improve productivity and training. In the long run, that will be more beneficial to Canadians, by creating more wealth for lower and middle-income earners.
Her suggestion that "all of society would benefit from a wealth tax," is pure nonsense. While income is taxed yearly, wealth is maintained mainly in stock and bond markets, real estate, and luxury items such as art, so to pay the wealth tax, money would have to be pulled from these areas. If a large number of rich people suddenly pull money out of the markets to pay their wealth tax, that hurts all Canadians, through lower returns on their investments (RRSP's, TFSA's, mutual funds etc.), and more importantly, reduction in the value of private and public pension programs. One final suggestion to Ms. Trottier and her friends - there is nothing stopping you from sending more money to the government at tax time, to allay your guilt.
Alan McDonald, Trenton
Bill 21 is discriminatoryQuebec Appeal Court Bill 21 ruling fuels debate on notwithstanding clause, March 2
It is disappointing but not surprising to read that the Quebec Court of Appeal upheld Bill 21, finding the secular law constitutional.
I am not a legal expert, but as a Muslim and a proud Canadian I am certain that law is discriminatory and harmful to the progress of Quebec and Canada.
True secularism is not about divorcing and masking the rich customs, beliefs, freedoms, and values of people, which this bill is advocating for.
I agree, public places should not be a place of preaching, of any religion, but simply putting on a hijab or a turban or a cross is not preaching, it is about the identity of a person.
Today is a sad day, as it ensures a sizable population of people will have to recluse themselves of serving their province and their nation in public service positions, and for that, the entire country stands to lose.
Sinwan Basharat, Ottawa, Ont.

Three ideal candidates for the judge selection committeeDoug Ford is 'tripling down' on wanting 'like-minded' judges. Here's how to understand the controversy rocking Ontario courts, Feb. 27
Recently, I attended the Toronto Legal Awards ceremony. For the first time, all three of the winners of the awards were women. Three strong, compassionate and caring women; The Honourable Gloria J. Epstein, Jessica Brant and Rebecca Durcan. Each acceptance speech had an enduring theme of treating people with kindness.
Might I recommend to Premier Doug Ford to drop his pale, stale and male members for the judge selection committee and include anyone of these women instead. Perhaps the Premier did not study statistics, but his rhetoric in the house is glaringly void of statistics about repeat offenders and their bail conditions.
Premier Ford, try practicing kindness instead of your partisan genuflecting to the lowest denominator.
Joan Honsberger, Thornhill
Pay increase is not the same as a raiseOntario's family doctors say province's 2.8% raise is 'enough for coffee and a bagel,' March1
Will everyone please stop using the term "raise" when it does not apply?
The only true measure of an increase or decrease in salary is based on the concept of real wages. Inflation was 3.9 increase in Canada last year. If doctors are getting a 2.8 per cent "increase" then they are not getting a raise. They are receiving a 1.1 per cent pay cut. Let's be honest. The latest deal with physicians runs to the end of March of this year so I am using last year's data to make these estimates. Just because the number on your paycheque is larger this year than in previous years does not mean you are the recipient of a raise.
I recommend everyone use the Bank of Canada inflation calculator online to see whether they are getting raises or cuts.
Timothy Kwiatkowski, London
Don't blame Chow for World Cup costsNo joy in Toronto hosting the World Cup as Ford is 'tripling down' another blunder, March 1
Star letter writer Djanjka Gajdel berated city hall over hosting the World Cup and spending money better used for our city's numerous issues. However, their use of the phrase "our lack of authentic leadership" should be directed to the person who signed on to this deal, John Tory. Some may even consider it a poisoned pill left by Tory knowing that people not paying attention will blame current Mayor Olivia Chow for this debacle.
T.C. Wilkins, Toronto
CAPTION: Austrian Marlene Engelhorn, who inherited from her family who owns the Germany's chemical giant BASF, poses with a placard reading "Tax the rich!" at the entrance of the Congress center on the opening of the annual meeting of the World Economic Forum (WEF) in Davos, on January 15, 2024.

Subject: Tournaments & championships; Taxes; Tax rates; Wealth tax

Document 37 of 500

Predictable, legitimate protesting should not have led to Justin Trudeau-Giorgia Meloni dinner cancellation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/predictable-legitimate-protesting-should-not-have/docview/2937267998/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Predictable%2C+legitimate+protesting+should+not+have+led+to+Justin+Trudeau-Giorgia+Meloni+dinner+cancellation&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto police say they're "reviewing" what happened during a protest on Saturday night that led to cancellation of a dinner Justin Trudeau was to host for the visiting prime minister of Italy, Giorgia Meloni.
But if there's a review to be done, it should focus on why a few hundred noisy demonstrators were allowed to derail a meeting between two G7 leaders, much to the embarrassment of Canada.
The dinner, including guests from the Italian-Canadian community, was to take place at the Art Gallery of Ontario. It should have been obvious that an event featuring two such controversial leaders would be targeted by protesters, especially since pro-Palestinian demonstrators have been taking every opportunity to go into the streets and make their views known.
And in this case it was a legitimate time and place to protest. They weren't demonstrating outside a Jewish-owned business, a Jewish community centre, Mount Sinai Hospital or a synagogue in Thornhill, as happened on Sunday.
All those should be out of bounds for protests about Israel's war against Hamas in Gaza and the thousands of civilian deaths it's caused. They cross the line between legitimate protest and antisemitism and must not be tolerated.
But demonstrating outside a meeting of two prime ministers? That's to be expected and should be allowed, as long as it stays within the law. And in fact, while the roughly 400 demonstrators were noisy and disruptive, police said there no arrests or injuries on Saturday night.
You'd think security forces - police and the RCMP detail responsible for the prime minister's security - would make sure an event like that was held at a venue that wouldn't be so vulnerable to a completely predictable protest.
You'd think they'd make sure entrances could be controlled and there'd be a safe way to get the leaders in and out. You'd think they'd be ready for a few hundred screaming demonstrators who've been turning up all round the city.
Instead, it was the demonstrators who got their way by shutting down the meeting. Toronto police say it was the RCMP who made the call because it wasn't safe to move Trudeau and Meloni into the building.
Surely we can do better. Protesters have a right to protest, but they don't have a right to dictate who can meet and when. Security forces should be better prepared and not cave in at the first sign of trouble. Why that didn't happen is worth "reviewing."
Judging judges
On another matter entirely: I got quite a lot of pushback last week for criticizing Doug Ford's comments about wanting more "like-minded" judges who believe in "keeping the bad guys in jail."
Some readers pointed out that the Trudeau Liberals have a habit of appointing judges who've donated to their party. There's truth in that, and it's reprehensible. But Justin Trudeau wasn't out proclaiming he'd "quadruple down" on bending the courts to his will. Ford was.
But there was one criticism that should not go unchallenged - the notion that Ford's desire for "tough" judges is no different from the Liberals' goal of promoting diversity on the bench. One is just the conservative agenda, the other the liberal one, goes this argument, which has been extensively aired in the National Post.
The comparison is ridiculous and patronizing. There's a suggestion that judges who aren't white men will be either less qualified or inclined to decide cases in a particular way - presumably "softer" on crime in accord with some liberal/Liberal ideology.
There's zero evidence for either of these points. Various studies have been done on how female judges differ from male ones, but there are few firm conclusions. There's certainly nothing to suggest that female or non-white judges are tougher or less tough. "It's not a good predictor," says a defence lawyer I know who's in court almost every day.
What the Trudeau Liberals have done is change the law to, for example, make it easier to get convictions for sexual assault. But all judges - wherever they fall on the toughness scale - must apply those rules. It has nothing to do with their gender or skin colour.
CAPTION: Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, right, meets with Prime Minister of Italy Giorgia Meloni in Toronto on Saturday.In a joint statement, the two prime ministers say the agreement will further deepen their countries' political, economic, and strategic ties by setting out plans to collaborate over the next three to five years in priority areas.Protestors wave flags and sing as police line the entrance to the Art Gallery of Ontario, where a cancelled event for Canada's Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Italy's Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni, was to take place on Saturday.

Subject: Sex crimes; Demonstrations & protests; Prime ministers; Art galleries & museums

Business indexing term: Subject: Art galleries & museums

Location: Italy; Canada

People: Meloni, Giorgia; Trudeau, Justin

Title: Predictable, legitimate protesting should not have led to Justin Trudeau-Giorgia Meloni dinner cancellation

Document 38 of 500

An uncertain pledge on pharmacare

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/uncertain-pledge-on-pharmacare/docview/2937267988/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=An+uncertain+pledge+on+pharmacare&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 39 of 500

Tories defeat motion to halt 407 tolls for trucks: NDP say move would ease congestion and pollution, but minister points out highway is privately owned

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tories-defeat-motion-halt-407-tolls-trucks/docview/2937267906/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tories+defeat+motion+to+halt+407+tolls+for+trucks%3A+NDP+say+move+would+ease+congestion+and+pollution%2C+but+minister+points+out+highway+is+privately+owned&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives have put the brakes on a New Democrat push to scrap tolls for transport trucks on Hwy. 407.
The Tories used their majority in the legislature Monday to defeat a motion from NDP Leader Marit Stiles seeking to exempt commercial truckers from tolls.
Her move was designed to lure thousands of trucks daily to Hwy. 407 from heavily congested Hwy. 401 and easing traffic for all drivers - and to mock a government omnibus bill introduced two weeks ago that bans tolls on all roads except the 407.
"This government has a chance right now, right here in front of them to make life cheaper and easier for people in Ontario," Stiles said.
"Anybody you know who has been near the 407 knows that it's very deeply underutilized," she told reporters, calling her idea "a common sense" solution.
Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria brushed off suggestions the government is not helping motorists, noting it has removed tolls for all drivers on Hwy. 412 and Hwy. 418 in Durham region before the 2022 election and cut the gas tax by 10 cents a litre.
"Every step of the way, we have been there for truck drivers," he told MPPs before the vote.
The government's Bill 162, the Get It Done Act, would "prohibit tolls from being charged for travel on a highway where the road authority is the Crown, unless the toll is authorized by an Act."
That specifically excludes Hwy. 407, leased to a private consortium by Progressive Conservative premier Mike Harris before the 1999 election.
Given that most of the 407 is run by a private company under lease from the province, it would likely take a new agreement to exempt transport trucks from tolls and compensatory payments from the government.
Sarkaria, who last month defended the legislation that protects the tollway, acknowledged it would be difficult to scrap the tolls, saying, "Look, the 407 is a private entity."
He was referring to how the toll costs have shot up on the 108-km private section of the highway from the Queen Elizabeth Way in Burlington to Brock Road in Pickering.
Queen's Park owns and operates the remaining 43 km of Hwy. 407 from Brock Road east to Orono.
It can cost as much as $70.66 to drive 151 km from the QEW to Hwys. 35/115 near Orono.
Stiles said it is "malarkey" that the government would propose the omnibus bill and not take tolls off an existing highway like the 407.
Stiles said that original 407 lease agreement made by the Harris government was a "bad deal."
The lobby group Environmental Defence said a study by global transport analysts at Eunomia concluded lifting truck tolls on Hwy. 407 would move between 12,000 and 21,000 trucks a day off Hwy. 401 and slash truck travel times across the GTA by 80 minutes.
CAPTION: Lobby group Environmental Defence said a study by global transport analysts concluded lifting truck tolls on Hwy. 407 would move between 12,000 and 21,000 trucksa day off of Hwy. 401. But the Ford government said scrapping tolls would be difficult since the highway is privately run.R.J. JohnstonToronto Starfile photo
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson and Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau

Ford names local councillor as PC candidate in Milton

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-names-local-councillor-as-pc-candidate/docview/2937267634/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+names+local+councillor+as+PC+candidate+in+Milton&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has tapped local councillor Zee Hamid to be the Progressive Conservative candidate in Milton.
Ford, leery of new Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie getting a seat in the legislature, has moved quickly to find a successor to former cabinet minister Parm Gill, who jumped ship last month for Pierre Poilievre's federal Conservatives.
No byelection has yet been set for Milton and the premier has until July to call one.
The premier hasn't even scheduled a byelection for Lambton-Kent-Middlesex after former minister Monte McNaughton resigned in September, although one must be called there by the end of this month.
But up in the polls - and with the Greenbelt land swap scandal fading from the headlines for now - Ford is eager to talk about a key Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area swing riding.
He is mindful that Gill only won the riding by 1,680 votes over Liberal runner-up Sameera Ali in a provincewide Tory landslide.
"Zee Hamid is a champion for Milton who will help us get it done by building Highway 413 and extending two-way, all-day GO train service along the Milton line," the premier said Sunday.
"While Bonnie Crombie and the Liberals support a carbon tax and oppose Highway 413, Zee is ready to hit the ground running to deliver on Milton's priorities," he said of the three-term Milton councillor.
Crombie has not yet ruled out seeking a seat in Milton.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Take+it+from+me%2C+a+millionaire%2C+Canada+must+lead+on+wealth+taxes%2C+Take+it+from+me%2C+a+millionaire%2C+Canada+must+lead+on+wealth+taxes%2C+Take+it+from+me%2C+a+millionaire%2C+Canada+must+lead+on+wealth+taxes%2C+Doug+Ford+is+%27tripling+down%27+on+wanting+%27like-minded%27+judges.+Here%27s+how+to+understand+the+controversy+rocking+Ontario+courts%2C+Feb.+27+Ontario%27s+family+doctors+say+province%27s+2.8%25+raise+is+%27enough+for+coffee+and+a+bagel%2C%27+March1+No+joy+in+Toronto+hosting+the+World+Cup+as+Ford+is+%27tripling+down%27+another+blunder%2C&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Voting is the
only way to bring about change
Take it from me, a millionaire, Canada must lead on wealth taxes, March 1
I regularly tell our various elected representatives that I never asked for my taxes to be cut. Rather, raise my taxes, but be sure to also raise the taxes of the rich.
Over recent decades, the tax rates levied on the rich have been reduced. Why?
Poverty has increased. Housing is at a crisis level.
Non-profit and co-op housing programs were cancelled, leaving low- and middle-income people with nowhere to live.
Calling all voters - please VOTE. You and the millionaire down the street each have one vote. We are many votes - so use them! That's the only way we will bring about change.
Kate Chung, Toronto
Will Freeland ever actually tax the super-rich?
Take it from me, a millionaire, Canada must lead on wealth taxes, March 1
As Claire Trottier points out, everyone, even the super-rich, acknowledge that her group needs to pay more taxes. In this country we have four income tax brackets for people earning up to $246,752, and only one tax rate for everybody above that rate. Fairness says that the more you make, the more you pay - up to $246,752. At that point "fairness" says that you should pay the same tax rate, no matter how high your income goes.
Before entering politics, Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland was a renowned author. Her book "Plutocrats: The Rise of the New Global Super-Rich and the Fall of Everyone Else," explains to us the ethical, political and economic dangers of extreme wealth. She talks about social responsibility, fairness and the need for a more equitable society.
When she entered politics, I was thrilled that finally we had a politician with full grasp of the problems with income inequality. I was disappointed when her presence in Ottawa seemed to have no effect on this ongoing concern. When she was appointed finance minister, I was delighted. Finally, a true believer was placed in a position with the ability to something about it. Yet again, disappointment.
How can we ever hope to mitigate this problem when a champion of taxing the superrich, with the ability to do something about it, sits on her hands and refuses to do anything about it.
Ken Caudle, Brampton
Venture funds would benefit low income earners
Take it from me, a millionaire, Canada must lead on wealth taxes, March 1
Claire Trottier and her wealthy friends are joining the growing calls for a wealth tax. I have a suggestion: Instead of calling for more taxes, put your and your friend's money into Venture funds to improve productivity and training. In the long run, that will be more beneficial to Canadians, by creating more wealth for lower and middle-income earners.
Her suggestion that "all of society would benefit from a wealth tax," is pure nonsense. While income is taxed yearly, wealth is maintained mainly in stock and bond markets, real estate, and luxury items such as art, so to pay the wealth tax, money would have to be pulled from these areas. If a large number of rich people suddenly pull money out of the markets to pay their wealth tax, that hurts all Canadians, through lower returns on their investments (RRSP's, TFSA's, mutual funds etc.), and more importantly, reduction in the value of private and public pension programs.
One final suggestion to Ms. Trottier and her friends - there is nothing stopping you from sending more money to the government at tax time, to allay your guilt.
Alan McDonald, Trenton
Ideal picks for judge selection committee
Doug Ford is 'tripling down' on wanting 'like-minded' judges. Here's how to understand the controversy rocking Ontario courts, Feb. 27
Recently, I attended the Toronto Legal Awards ceremony. For the first time, all three of the winners of the awards were women. Three strong, compassionate and caring women; The Honourable Gloria J. Epstein, Jessica Brant and Rebecca Durcan. Each acceptance speech had an enduring theme of treating people with kindness.
Might I recommend to Premier Doug Ford to drop his pale, stale and male members for the judge selection committee and include anyone of these women instead. Perhaps the Premier did not study statistics, but his rhetoric in the house is glaringly void of statistics about repeat offenders and their bail conditions.
Premier Ford, try practising kindness instead of your partisan genuflecting to the lowest denominator.
Joan Honsberger, Thornhill
Pay increase
is not the same thing as a raise
Ontario's family doctors say province's 2.8% raise is 'enough for coffee and a bagel,' March1
Will everyone please stop using the term "raise" when it does not apply?
The only true measure of an increase or decrease in salary is based on the concept of real wages. Inflation was 3.9 increase in Canada last year. If doctors are getting a 2.8 per cent "increase" then they are not getting a raise. They are receiving a 1.1 per cent pay cut. Let's be honest. The latest deal with physicians runs to the end of March of this year so I am using last year's data to make these estimates. Just because the number on your paycheque is larger this year than in previous years does not mean you are the recipient of a raise.
I recommend everyone use the Bank of Canada inflation calculator online to see whether they are getting raises or cuts.
Timothy Kwiatkowski,
London, Ont.
Don't blame Chow for rising World Cup costs
No joy in Toronto hosting the World Cup as Ford is 'tripling down' another blunder, March 1
Star letter writer Djanjka Gajdel berated city hall over hosting the World Cup and spending money better used for our city's numerous issues. However, their use of the phrase "our lack of authentic leadership" should be directed to the person who signed on to this deal, John Tory. Some may even consider it a poisoned pill left by Tory knowing that people not paying attention will blame current Mayor Olivia Chow for this debacle.
T.C. Wilkins, Toronto

Document 42 of 500

Ottawa girds for Trump's possible return: Federal officials believe he would likely act on campaign threat to slap 10% tariff on all imports

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ottawa-girds-trumps-possible-return/docview/2937267421/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+girds+for+Trump%27s+possible+return%3A+Federal+officials+believe+he+would+likely+act+on+campaign+threat+to+slap+10%25+tariff+on+all+imports&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A re-elected Donald Trump would likely act on his campaign warning to slap a 10 per cent tariff on all imports, and threaten several key Canadian economic sectors, senior Trudeau government ministers admit.
And while they said it doesn't matter to Trudeau's newly relaunched "Team Canada" effort who sits in the White House as president, the top diplomat and two politicians leading Canada's offence-defence strategy outlined to the Star's editorial board exactly how Ottawa is moving to counter that threat.
Kirsten Hillman, Canadian ambassador in Washington, Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne and International Trade Minister Mary Ng said the strategy draws in part on the 2016-2019 playbook Canada used to fight Trump's aggressive renegotiation of the North American Free Trade Agreement.
But this time it's different, said Hillman, because there is no specific renegotiation on the horizon - despite a 2026 mandatory review of the rewritten NAFTA deal - bringing with it specific trade questions on the table.
Instead, she said, Canada is lining up "strategic allies" to push back against any protectionist or isolationist measures, no matter which party is in the White House.
Still, Hillman and Ng said Canadian diplomats in the U.S. are taking Trump's campaign rhetoric seriously.
"What I think we've learned from the last Trump administration is that he will say things that seem outrageous, but you should listen to him when he says them because he will do them," said Hillman.
"And I think that that is something we know very deeply, and we should and we need to understand that. And then we need to understand how we will react to that situation."
Canadian officials have reached out to unions, business leaders, and state lawmakers "across the aisle" in the U.S. and are travelling beyond Washington, New York, L.A. and San Francisco to go to Arizona, Nevada, Arkansas and Louisiana - "all places where Canadian companies are ... putting huge amounts of money into projects in those states, which are largely Republican states."
She said it is "an exercise in illustrating to Americans across the country, why they need us and for what, because that's - to be crass about it - that's leverage" at a future time when things aren't working out.
That much is similar to Trudeau government's NAFTA renegotiation strategy where Canada sought allies in the U.S.
"What's different this time than last time," Hillman said, is that Republican and Trump-friendly think tanks have issued a series of policy papers and policy advice for the Trump team "or whoever the nominee is," and that is providing a road map for the Trudeau government to track.
"I think there's two tomes that are about 900 pages each. We've gone through all of them," she said.
Canadian diplomats have met the authors of those policy pieces to discuss where the positions are relevant to the Canada-U.S. relationship, and are clearly highlighting the consequences that could result "if a policy is implemented in a certain way without thinking about the facts on the ground about the Canada-U.S. relationship."
Hillman added "we're also reaching out to Republicans across the country who we know are close to the leadership and close to the former president Trump who may well have a very good chance of being in his cabinet, and we're refreshing those relationships and again emphasizing the stuff that's going well."
"It's not a PR exercise, because it is a truly substantive exercise that can make a big difference," she said.
Hillman said that kind of legwork was vital during the first Trump term after he imposed tariffs on Canadian steel and aluminum products. She said Canada had the Coors brothers, "who are not Democrats, on side with us, going to the president, talking to the White House ... in very detailed ways around the implications of that (tariff) policy on their business. That isn't just profits for their board, but it's beer cans for Americans ... and coke cans and ... it's very personal."
Ng said Canadian steel, aluminum and softwood lumber business leaders are particularly concerned about Trump's threats and "we should take it seriously."
Ottawa is fielding thousands of calls and offers from Canadian business and union leaders who want to help make Canada's case south of the border, she said.
However, the ministers confirmed the Trudeau government has not specifically reached out to Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre to engage him on its "Team Canada" effort, though Hillman engaged with Alberta Premier Danielle Smith when she recently travelled to Washington, and Champagne said he has spoken to Premier Doug Ford about the strategy.
"Every voice matters" said Champagne, adding they would "be happy to hear from" Poilievre.
Meanwhile, the Trudeau government is aggressively pursuing more "diversification" of trade with other countries and allies in Europe, Asia, the Indo-Pacific and what Ng called "frontier economies."
"We sort of want our cake and eat it too. And that's the work we've been pursuing," she said.
Champagne underscored that "Canada is the only G7 country which has a free-trade agreement with all the other G7 nations. That give us preferential market access to 1.5 billion consumers. So I'd say Canada has a lot of options."
But he said the "asymmetrical relationship" between Canada and the U.S. is still Canada's most integrated and strategically important one.
"We understand the integrated nature of our relationship but down south of the border, I'm not sure they always think that way."
The two ministers and the ambassador named by Trudeau to lead the latest Team Canada effort say the goal is to ensure the "integrated" economic ties are understood in the U.S., but also the bilateral security ties, and the role of Canada in securing North American supply chains, recently disrupted by the pandemic, the war in Ukraine and now the Middle East.
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star

Document 43 of 500

PM security fail an embarrassment

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pm-security-fail-embarrassment/docview/2937267412/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PM+security+fail+an+embarrassment&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto police say they're "reviewing" what happened during a protest on Saturday night that led to cancellation of a dinner Justin Trudeau was to host for the visiting prime minister of Italy, Giorgia Meloni.
But if there's a review to be done, it should focus on why a few hundred noisy demonstrators were allowed to derail a meeting between two G7 leaders, much to the embarrassment of Canada.
The dinner, including guests from the Italian-Canadian community, was to take place at the Art Gallery of Ontario. It should have been obvious that an event featuring two such controversial leaders would be targeted by protesters, especially since pro-Palestinian demonstrators have been taking every opportunity to go into the streets and make their views known.
And in this case it was a legitimate time and place to protest. They weren't demonstrating outside a Jewish-owned business, a Jewish community centre, Mount Sinai Hospital or a synagogue in Thornhill, as happened on Sunday.
All those should be out of bounds for protests about Israel's war against Hamas in Gaza and the thousands of civilian deaths it's caused. They cross the line between legitimate protest and antisemitism and must not be tolerated.
But demonstrating outside a meeting of two prime ministers? That's to be expected and should be allowed, as long as it stays within the law. And in fact, while the roughly 400 demonstrators were noisy and disruptive, police said there no arrests or injuries on Saturday night.
You'd think security forces - police and the RCMP detail responsible for the prime minister's security - would make sure an event like that was held at a venue that wouldn't be so vulnerable to a completely predictable protest.
You'd think they'd make sure entrances could be controlled and there'd be a safe way to get the leaders in and out. You'd think they'd be ready for a few hundred screaming demonstrators who've been turning up all round the city.
Instead, it was the demonstrators who got their way by shutting down the meeting. Toronto police say it was the RCMP who made the call because it wasn't safe to move Trudeau and Meloni into the building.
Surely we can do better. Protesters have a right to protest, but they don't have a right to dictate who can meet and when. Security forces should be better prepared and not cave in at the first sign of trouble. Why that didn't happen is worth "reviewing."
On another matter entirely: I got quite a lot of pushback last week for criticizing Doug Ford's comments about wanting more "like-minded" judges who believe in "keeping the bad guys in jail."
Some readers pointed out that the Trudeau Liberals have a habit of appointing judges who've donated to their party. There's truth in that, and it's reprehensible. But Trudeau wasn't out proclaiming he'd "quadruple down" on bending the courts to his will. Ford was.
But there was one criticism that should not go unchallenged - the notion that Ford's desire for "tough" judges is no different from the Liberals' goal of promoting diversity on the bench. One is just the conservative agenda, the other the liberal one, goes this argument, which has been extensively aired in the National Post. The comparison is ridiculous and patronizing. There's a suggestion that judges who aren't white men will be either less qualified or inclined to decide cases in a particular way - presumably "softer" on crime in accord with some liberal/Liberal ideology.
There's zero evidence for either of these points. Various studies have been done on how female judges differ from male ones, but there are few firm conclusions. There's certainly nothing to suggest that female or non-white judges are tougher or less tough. "It's not a good predictor," says a defence lawyer I know who's in court almost every day.
What the Trudeau Liberals have done is change the law to, for example, make it easier to get convictions for sexual assault. But all judges - wherever they fall on the toughness scale - must apply those rules. It has nothing to do with their gender or skin colour.
Andrew Phillips is a Toronto-based staff columnist for the Star's Opinion page. Reach him via email: aphillips@thestar.ca
CAPTION: Protesters wave flags as police line the entrance to the Art Gallery of Ontario on Saturday. Demonstrating outside a meeting of two prime ministers should be allowed, as long as it stays within the law, writes Andrew Phillips.Cole BurstonTHE CANADIAN PRESS

CREDIT: Andrew Phillips Toronto Star OPINION Andrew Phillips is a Toronto-based staff columnist for the Star's Opinion page. Reach him via email: aphillips@thestar.ca;
