Document 55 of 500

Ford is supposed to represent all Ontarians, not just the like-minded

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-is-supposed-represent-all-ontarians-not-just/docview/2933809874/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+is+supposed+to+represent+all+Ontarians%2C+not+just+the+like-minded&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: 'I am going to make sure we have like-minded judges,' Doug Ford doubles down amid Ontario court outcry, Feb. 26
"Justices of the peace and judges, they're letting criminals out," said Premier Doug Ford. Therein lies the crux of the problem with Ford wanting judges appointed who think like him. What qualifications does Ford have to render such a judgment? Does he have anything other than a gut feeling?
Judges are required to study the laws of the land, laws passed by Parliaments in Canada, and before that, Westminster. They are required to weigh evidence, including testimony. Precedent and prior rulings from higher courts also play a part. All of these are input to a judge's decision making, and sentencing, if found guilty.
Our constitution says everyone is equal, which means equal before the law. Political beliefs, party membership, race, gender, etc. are to make no difference.
If Ford manages to place judges on the bench who are "like-minded" to himself, he will have mortally wounded our system of justice.
Ford may have been elected by a lot of people who are "like-minded," but when he took office, he swore an oath to run our government for everyone, not just his electors. That includes selecting the best candidates to be judges on merit, not biases.
David Kister, Kingston
Non-political justice system works
I have never heard a political leader say, "I'm appointing like-minded people that believe in what we believe in," or anything that mixes politics with judicial appointments.
We have all seen what happens in the U.S. where they talk about Republican judges versus Democratic judges. This brings a whole new dimension into the law system involving blame and lack of impartiality for which party picked that judge.
The justice system I grew up with appeared to be non-political and this is a system that works. Judge choices should be part of the mainstream and not on the political fringes of left and right-minded extremes. One would like to keep out judges that show obvious prejudice against any elements of society. We should pick the best possible candidates for the judgeship and not be restricted by party guidelines.
The PC's are deviating from long accepted principles of independence for rendering true justice.
Greg Prince, Toronto
A sad day for many competent qualified lawyers
My lucky day came near the end of May 1978 when the Conservative Bill Davis government and more specifically Roy McMurtry appointed me a judge.
If Ford had been at the helm, I would definitely be employed somewhere else.
It's a sad day for so many incredibly competent and qualified lawyers who are not of a like mind to the Ford government.
Marvin Zuker, North York

So what sort of judges will there be?
Does that mean that they will be thugs, liars and bullies?
Paul Roslin, Etobicoke
Your bias is showingWhy Doug Ford's plan for "like-minded" judges is a terrible move, Feb. 27
There's no doubt where Doug Ford stands - and it's not good, Feb. 27
Where was the Star's outrage when premiers Dalton McGuinty and Kathleen Wynn, previous majority Ontario Liberal government leaders, made appointments of magistrates and judges to the bench?
I do not recall either the Federation of Ontario Law Associations or the Advocates Society being elected by a people's majority and are therefore in no legal position to determine the appointments in question.
I do recall Premier Doug Ford's Conservative government being elected by a provincial majority to "legally" make said judicial appointments, similar to what the previous Liberal majority governments of Ontario did.
Larry Rayner, Innisfil ON
So qualification is no longer a top requirement?
We can assume from now on that the first criterion for a potential candidate for an appointment will be: Are you a member of the Party?
The second criterion may be the donor records of the candidate. Expertise or experience with dull, drab law stuff may be a distant third.
This all will sound very familiar to people I know who came here from Eastern Europe.
Graeme Elliott, Toronto
Ford is protecting his future
We did not elect Premier Doug Ford to upend traditional independent tribunals, councils, and other oversight bodies to protect him from future ramifications of his disastrous expensive and indecisive policies.
Michael Gilmor, Ajax
Ford tripling down on judge recruitment
After reading this article and listening to Premier Doug Ford in the legislature, I'm reminded of the famous line, by the Peanuts character Lucy: "If you can't be right, be wrong at the top of your voice."
David Christiani, Toronto
Politicians have always made partisan judicial appointments
Who are we trying to fool here? Both federal and provincial governments of all political stripes have been making partisan judicial appointments forever. It only becomes controversial when the appointees lean right instead of left.
Claire Hoy, Toronto
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford has blamed "massive crime waves across our cities" on what he sees as too many lenient judges.

Subject: Political appointments; Law; Judicial appointments; Politics

Construction firms that built critical Ontario infrastructure projects during COVID say the province stiffed them

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/construction-firms-that-built-critical-ontario/docview/2933809851/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Construction+firms+that+built+critical+Ontario+infrastructure+projects+during+COVID+say+the+province+stiffed+them&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government has been hit with scores of claims from construction companies that say they were left on the hook for money they spent to keep critical public infrastructure projects moving ahead during the pandemic.
Several of the firms have taken the province to court, where they are seeking hundreds of millions of dollars in damages.
So far, the province has reached settlements for 67 different claims, government officials said. However, the government will not say how much it paid in settlements or how many other claims it's facing.
Despite declaring a province-wide state of emergency in March 2020 to slow the spread of COVID-19, several companies say the government refused to admit that the pandemic constituted an emergency for their projects - what the firms allege was a breach of contract. This emergency status would have entitled the companies to more money and time.
The province has argued in court that project agreements made construction companies responsible for added costs. As they negotiate claims, government officials say they are trying to shield taxpayers from runaway COVID-related expenses.
Details of settlements are 'confidential'
Michael Lindsay, president and CEO of the provincial agency Infrastructure Ontario, said details about the settlements are "commercially sensitive" and "confidential."
Lindsay said the pandemic presented "unprecedented realities" that project contracts did not fully anticipate. He said he is confident that a review of the agency's response to COVID claims would show it is protecting taxpayers, while "acting reasonably to address the validated costs" experienced by the construction firms.
The public has a "right to know" how much the pandemic added to construction costs, said Matti Siemiatycki, director of the Infrastructure Institute at University of Toronto.
Temporarily withholding settlement details may protect the province's position in negotiating ongoing claims, but that information should ultimately be released, Siemiatycki said.
"Having transparency is important, so that our community understands the costs of these mega projects, and can understand how public money is being spent," he said.
Major P3 projects account for 17 settlements
Most of the 67 COVID-related claims were settled through dispute resolution mechanisms built into the projects' agreements, Infrastructure Ontario said.
Seventeen of those settlements stemmed from major public-private partnerships (P3).
In P3 projects, a consortium of private companies inks a deal with the government, and typically delivers private financing, as well as project maintenance and operation. Governments have often touted the P3 model as a way to shift risks associated with large-scale projects from taxpayers to the private sector.
But those risks do not include "extraordinary circumstances like the COVID-19-pandemic," said Jean-Louis Servranckx, president and CEO of Aecon Group, which is part of a consortium of companies suing the province.
The consortium had been hired in 2019 to widen a stretch of Hwy. 401, from Mississauga to Milton. The $640-million contract included an "emergency provision" that was standard across all of Ontario's major P3 projects and should have been triggered by the pandemic, entitling contractors to more time and money to implement COVID-safety protocols and navigate supply-chain hiccups, the lawsuit claims.
"Nearly four years after the outbreak of the pandemic, we are still seeking fair and reasonable compensation for these costs," Servranckx told the Star.
The province had deemed P3 projects "essential," and released COVID guidelines for construction sites that mandated the sanitization of equipment, the social distancing of workers and other safety protocols.
However, to avoid triggering the emergency provision in its contracts, and "minimize its exposure to P3 contractors," the province decided to refuse to acknowledge the existence of an emergency for its projects, alleges the consortium, which also includes developers Amico and Parsons.
"In formulating and implementing this strategy ... the Ontario Crown ... breached its obligations of honest and good faith performance," the lawsuit alleges.
Infrastructure Ontario has not yet filed a statement of defence in the Hwy. 401 case, and declined to comment on the allegations, which have not been tested in court.
Eglinton Crosstown LRT project sparked court action
The companies building the Eglinton Crosstown LRT, another P3 project, have also taken the province to court. That consortium, called Crosslinx Transit Solutions, includes Aecon, which is also on the Hwy. 401 project, as well as ACS-Dragados, EllisDon and SNC-Lavalin.
In the fall of 2020, Crosslinx took legal action against Infrastructure Ontario and Metrolinx over their alleged repeated refusal to declare an emergency for the project. The consortium said the pandemic had left it grappling with new safety protocols, supply chain problems and absenteeism.
The provincial agencies disputed the claims. They argued that the $8.25-billion project agreement made Crosslinx responsible for risks associated with the pandemic. The agencies said they didn't declare an emergency on the project because Crosslinx was already required to maintain a safe and healthy workplace, including implementing COVID protocols.
The case wound its way through various levels of court. In December 2021, the provincial agencies announced they had reached a $325-million settlement to resolve various disputes with Crosslinx related to the project, including COVID costs.
After it was sued by another consortium, the province came out swinging.
The lawsuit stemmed from a $616-million contract to widen a stretch of Hwy. 427, which wasn't completed until September 2021 - a year behind schedule. The companies blamed the "long and torturous road to opening" on a series of alleged missteps by the government, including its "repeated" refusal to declare an emergency for the project during the pandemic.
Infrastructure Ontario attributed the delay to the companies' "own poor execution and poor progress" and issued a counterclaim against the consortium for $11 million, court documents state.
The province and the companies agreed to take the dispute to arbitration, which "remains ongoing," a spokesperson for Industry Ontario said.
An 'expensive' lesson?
While the province's alleged refusal to declare an emergency on projects has rankled contractors, from the perspective of taxpayers, "the government did the right thing," said James McKellar, professor emeritus at the Schulich School of Business.
Rather than giving contractors carte blanche on COVID costs, McKellar characterized this approach as "a big stick" that would have made contractors "come to the table a little more humble."
NDP infrastructure critic Jennifer French (Oshawa) said the P3 claims - and the unknown amount spent to settle them - could be an "expensive" lesson in the dangers of moving away from traditional public financing.
"We're now finding out that the risk is actually multiplying and falling back on us," she said. "It's very frustrating as a member of the public just to watch so many dollars disappear ... We are not allowed to know where. We're not allowed to know how much."
In 2014, the province's auditor general found that Infrastructure Ontario's reliance on public-private partnerships had cost $8-billion more than traditional public financing. But the agency's then-CEO pushed back against the finding, saying that number did not account for cost overruns and delays that large-scale publicly funded projects often incur.
Even before the pandemic, Infrastructure Ontario's Lindsay said the province had been reviewing its contract models and making adjustments. Since COVID struck, he said the agency has made changes to all its contracts, including for P3 projects, "to provide a more predictable way to deal with the risks and the costs associated with pandemics moving forward."
CAPTION: Several construction firms have taken the province to court, where they are seeking hundreds of millions of dollars in damages.In December 2021, Infrastructure Ontario and Metrolinx announced they had reached a$325-million settlementto resolve various disputes with Crosslinx related to the Eglinton Crosstown LRT, including COVID costs.Ontario Premier Doug Ford seen visiting a construction site in February 2021.

Document 57 of 500

Mulroney will get state funeral: Tributes to former prime minister continue to flow from across the political spectrum

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mulroney-will-get-state-funeral/docview/2933744231/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mulroney+will+get+state+funeral%3A+Tributes+to+former+prime+minister+continue+to+flow+from+across+the+political+spectrum&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Former prime minister Brian Mulroney will receive a full state funeral, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said Friday.
"We're working with the family closely to ensure that all of their wishes are respected and that it be the right and fitting tribute to him," Trudeau said. "There will be opportunities for Canadians to express their gratitude and share tributes to the former prime minister as the coming days and weeks unfold."
Flags on Parliament Hill flew at half-staff, and the House of Commons adjourned to mark Mulroney's death. Formal tributes from MPs are expected when Parliament resumes during the week of March 18.
Reflections from across the political spectrum and around the world flowed Friday, the morning after Mulroney died at 84.
Speaking in Sudbury, Trudeau said Mulroney set an example that modern politicians could learn from.
"The reality is, he was a statesman who was totally devoted to Canada," he said.
"He was an excellent politician, but at the same time, there was a great deal of respect for the role and responsibilities that he had, and for those who were serving, even if they were members of a different party.
"This is a reminder that politics can and must be rooted in service and respect."
Trudeau said Mulroney "stickhandled" the relationship with the U.S. to advance environmental protections, and was rightly recognized as "one of the greenest prime ministers Canada has ever had."
Trudeau said today's Conservatives could take a lesson from Mulroney's record, and that "just being a conservative doesn't automatically mean that you don't care about the environment. There is a choice that the current Conservative party is making that they would be good to reflect upon, because Brian Mulroney understood that caring for the environment, caring for the planet, caring for the people who live on it is part of building a strong economy for the future."
Premier Doug Ford, whose cabinet includes Mulroney's daughter, Caroline, said Friday, "I loved the prime minister ... I'm going to miss him dearly."
Ford said Mulroney charmed his mother when he called and sang "Happy Birthday" to her. "My mum absolutely loved him and so he gave her a CD of all the songs that he sang."
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh recalled Mulroney's kind words upon the recent death of former NDP leader Ed Broadbent.
Mulroney "was actually in the hospital himself, dealing with the health crisis, and the thought of someone dealing with a health crisis taking the time to share some kind words about a political opponent really is a testament to the kind of person the prime minister literally was," Singh said Friday.
James Baker, a former U.S. secretary of state, said in a written tribute in the Globe and Mail that "Canada punched well above its weight on the world stage" when Mulroney was prime minister.
"Regardless of how one gauges the ups and downs of his public life, Mr. Mulroney built a considerable legacy that continues to benefit his country and the world today, particularly through his advancement of free trade," Baker wrote.
The George and Barbara Bush Foundation called Mulroney "a valued ally and close confidant" of the late president George H.W. Bush. "Their friendship continued long after they both exited the world stage," as the Mulroneys visited the Bush family in Maine, the foundation said in a written statement, noting that "of all his many friends, PM Mulroney was one of the few President Bush asked to give a eulogy at his funeral."
Lucien Bouchard, who quit Mulroney's cabinet in the throes of failed constitutional talks and returned to Quebec to form the separatist Bloc Québécois, told Radio-Canada that they had been estranged for years, but last month shared dinner with their wives.
"We had reconciled, but very late. Too late. So many years lost," Bouchard said. "We felt very strong for what we thought and fought for, so it can explain things, but at the end of the day it's a lot of years lost for a marvellous friendship."
Bob Rae, a former NDP premier and Liberal MP, recalled that when his brother died, "Brian Mulroney was the first political leader to call, as he was when I was elected premier in 1990, when I was defeated, and at so many other moments. He was my friend, despite our differences."
Conservative MP Michael Chong said he joined the Progressive Conservative party when Mulroney was leader "because I believed in the Canada he was fighting for and still do. I'll be forever grateful for the advice and time he gave me over many years."
Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly welled up Friday as she recalled "Brian was a friend" who "was there" when times were tough for her.
Joly said she consulted him when she became foreign affairs minister, and that he aided the government on the renegotiation of NAFTA and on lifting the steel and aluminum tariffs. She called him every time before she headed to Washington, and said he knew people on the Republican and Democrat side of Congress.
Mulroney's political career and how he dealt with opponents highlights just how toxic current political debates have grown, she added.
"You don't need to the hate the person in front of you. You just need to agree to disagree," Joly said. "And I think this is something that we should all bear in mind, because politics is, particularly these years, becoming more toxic and polarized.
Former prime minister Stephen Harper called Mulroney a "historic " and "transformational" figure in Canada's modern era.
"Prime Minister Mulroney fought to secure the Free Trade Agreement with the United States, from which Canada has benefitted immensely ever since," Harper said in a written statement.
"Prime Minister Mulroney stood for freedom and democracy on the world stage, in his principled opposition to apartheid in South Africa, his enduring support for Israel, and his advocacy of independence for Ukraine and the other European nations long under the yoke of Soviet communism."
And like former prime minister Jean Chrétien, Harper hailed Mulroney's historic political accomplishment as "the first Canadian Conservative leader to win back-to-back majority mandates since Sir John A. Macdonald, including the largest electoral victory in Canadian history."
Former prime minister Joe Clark hailed Mulroney as "one of Canada's great change-makers."
Mulroney was "determined to move the country forward and with an instinct to respect differences and an extraordinary capacity to reconcile.
"He was bold, a big dreamer, and an unparalleled negotiator and persuader, both at home in Canada, and in the larger world," Clark said in a statement posted on X, formerly Twitter.
"Often then we were allies, sometimes competitors and then became colleagues in and beyond national government. I am grateful for his extraordinary service, and his friendship, and mourn his loss."
CAPTION: A photograph and book of condolences for members of Parliament to sign sits in front of the official portrait of former prime minister Brian Mulroney, in the antechamber to the House of Commons, as Canadians mourn his death.Justin Tang THE CANADIAN PRESS
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star

Judges should be apolitical: 'I am going to make sure we have like-minded judges,' Doug Ford doubles down amid Ontario court outcry, Feb. 26 Why Doug Ford's plan for "like-minded" judges is a terrible move, Editorial, Feb. 27 There's no doubt where Doug Ford stands - and it's not good, Phillips, Feb. 27

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/judges-should-be-apolitical/docview/2933744082/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Judges+should+be+apolitical%3A+%27I+am+going+to+make+sure+we+have+like-minded+judges%2C%27+Doug+Ford+doubles+down+amid+Ontario+court+outcry%2C+Feb.+26+Why+Doug+Ford%27s+plan+for+%26quot%3Blike-minded%26quot%3B+judges+is+a+terrible+move%2C+Editorial%2C+Feb.+27+There%27s+no+doubt+where+Doug+Ford+stands+-+and+it%27s+not+good%2C+Phillips%2C+Feb.+27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: 'I am going to make sure we have like-minded judges,' Doug Ford doubles down amid Ontario court outcry, Feb. 26
"Justices of the peace and judges, they're letting criminals out," said Premier Doug Ford. Therein lies the crux of the problem with Ford wanting judges appointed who think like him. What qualifications does Ford have to render such a judgment? Does he have anything other than a gut feeling?
Judges are required to study the laws of the land, laws passed by parliaments in Canada, and before that, Westminster. They are required to weigh evidence, including testimony. Precedent and prior rulings from higher courts also play a part. All of these are input to a judge's decision making, and sentencing, if found guilty.
Our constitution says everyone is equal, which means equal before the law. Political beliefs, party membership, race, gender, etc., are to make no difference.
If Ford manages to place judges on the bench who are "like-minded" to himself, he will have mortally wounded our system of justice.
Ford may have been elected by a lot of people who are "like-minded," but when he took office, he swore an oath to run our government for everyone, not just his electors. That includes selecting the best candidates to be judges on merit, not biases.
David Kister, Kingston
I have never heard a political leader say, "I'm appointing like-minded people that believe in what we believe in," or anything that mixes politics with judicial appointments.
We have all seen what happens in the U.S., where they talk about Republican judges versus Democratic judges. This brings a whole new dimension into the law system, involving blame and lack of impartiality for which party appointed that judge.
The justice system I grew up with appeared to be non-political and this is a system that works. Judge appointments should be part of the mainstream and not on the political fringes of left- or right-minded extremes. We should keep out judges who show obvious prejudice against any elements of society. We should pick the best possible candidates and not be restricted by party guidelines.
The PCs are deviating from long-accepted principles of independence for rendering true justice.
Greg Prince, Toronto

My lucky day came near the end of May 1978 when the Conservative Bill Davis government and, more specifically, Roy McMurtry appointed me a judge.
If Ford had been at the helm, I would definitely be employed somewhere else.
It's a sad day for so many incredibly competent and qualified lawyers who are not of a like mind to the Ford government.
Marvin Zuker, North York
Why Doug Ford's plan for "like-minded" judges is a terrible move, Editorial, Feb. 27
There's no doubt where Doug Ford stands - and it's not good,
Phillips, Feb. 27
Where was the Star's outrage when premiers Dalton McGuinty and Kathleen Wynn, previous majority Ontario Liberal government leaders, made appointments of magistrates and judges to the bench?
I do not recall either the Federation of Ontario Law Associations or the Advocates Society being elected by a people's majority and are therefore in no legal position to determine the appointments in question.
I do recall Premier Doug Ford's Conservative government being elected by a provincial majority to "legally" make said judicial appointments, similar to what the previous Liberal majority governments of Ontario did.
Larry Rayner, Innisfil, Ont.

We can assume from now on that the first criterion for a potential candidate for an appointment will be: Are you a member of the party?
The second criterion may be the donor records of the candidate. Expertise or experience with dull, drab law stuff may be a distant third.
This all will sound very familiar to people I know who came here from Eastern Europe.
Graeme Elliott, Toronto

We did not elect Premier Doug Ford to upend traditional independent tribunals, councils and other oversight bodies to protect him from future ramifications of his disastrous expensive and indecisive policies.
Michael Gilmor, Ajax

After reading this article and listening to Premier Doug Ford in the legislature, I'm reminded of the famous line, by the Peanuts character Lucy: "If you can't be right, be wrong at the top of your voice."
David Christiani, Toronto

Who are we trying to fool here? Both federal and provincial governments of all political stripes have been making partisan judicial appointments forever. It only becomes controversial when the appointees lean right instead of left.
Claire Hoy, Toronto

Subject: Political appointments; Law; Judicial appointments; Politics

Title: Judges should be apolitical:   'I am going to make sure we have like-minded judges,' Doug Ford doubles down amid Ontario court outcry, Feb. 26 Why Doug Ford's plan for "like-minded" judges is a terrible move, Editorial, Feb. 27 There's no doubt where Doug Ford stands - and it's not good, Phillips, Feb. 27

Document 59 of 500

Pondering the planet on aprehistoric landscape

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pondering-planet-on-aprehistoric-landscape/docview/2933743572/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pondering+the+planet+on+aprehistoric+landscape&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=L.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In western Newfoundland's Gros Morne National Park, the red mesa of the Tablelands protrudes like a wound amid the 1,805-square-kilometre swath of emerald boreal forest and blue fiords. Its presence in the landscape provokes a head-scratching dissonance - and that's because it's a geologic anomaly. This part of Gros Morne holds the key to the park's 1987 designation as a UNESCO World Heritage site.
From a wind-pummelled ridge, my eyes follow the sweep of umber rocks down toward the marked Tablelands Trail in the distance. My husband and I left the trail's boardwalk behind earlier that morning, putting our navigation skills to use on the 14-kilometre off-trail trek, picking our way through loose rock and scrambling up the steep, sun-baked slopes of Winter House Brook Canyon.
Although we've reached the peak of the trail, we're actually standing on the Earth's mantle (the layer between the planet's crust and molten core). Nearly 500 million years ago, two tectonic plates collided here, thrusting up rocks that were once deep beneath the ancient Iapetus Ocean, a precursor to the Atlantic.
The site was instrumental in proving the theory of plate tectonics - garnering the park UNESCO status - which only gelled in scientific communities in the late 1960s. "The smoking gun of plate tectonics and continental drift was finding little bits of oceans preserved on continents or between continents, and that's what the Tablelands is," says Andrew Kerr, adjunct professor of earth sciences at Memorial University of Newfoundland, in St. John's.
Treading across the subterranean depths of an ancient ocean up on the Tablelands' barren plateau is as otherworldly as it sounds. In fact, the area serves as a Mars analog site for researchers at NASA and the Canadian Space Agency. The peridotite rocks here, which are green beneath their oxidized red husk, are also found on Mars, so looking at the Tablelands' ecosystem gives scientists insight into potential life on other planets. The austere terrain is laden with toxic metals, so not much survives up here beyond a patchwork of grassy bog, murky ponds and the odd carnivorous plant.
Although the upper plateau doesn't cover a huge expanse of land, it's easy to get turned around in the monochromatic rubble, and we have to consult our GPS a couple of times as we plod along the outer rim. But soon we fall into the slow, hypnotic rhythm that comes with walking for long periods, where time seems to bend and stretch. Coupled with the imperceptible centimetres-per-year pace of our shifting continents embedded in the ancient rocks beneath my feet, I feel tuned into a powerful, primordial frequency.
This sense of deep time nearly defies human comprehension, but what I can wrap my mind around continues to make my experience of the landscape here so much richer.
"That's part of why we put Parks Canada's signature red Adirondack chairs in spots like Green Gardens, to encourage people to do more than snap a quick picture, (and) to sit and contemplate the place," says Rob Hingston, a geologist and Parks Canada visitor experience officer for Western Newfoundland and Labrador.
On another morning, we make our way to the coastal Green Gardens trail to see it for ourselves. The path snakes down (and mercilessly up on the way back) through a fragrant spruce and pine forest, finally opening onto a lush green cliffside above the sea, where lashing winds roar in off the Gulf of St. Lawrence.
I follow a small footpath dotted with purple wildflowers to the edge of the bluff and peer over, greeted by a popcorn-like cliff face of grey pillow basalt and, in the turquoise shallows, towering sea stacks. These elements make for a striking tableau, and they're also further evidence of plate tectonics.
These volcanic rocks at Green Gardens are 600 million years old, predating the nearby Tablelands, and were formed when continents pulled apart to create the Iapetus Ocean. "So, we have evidence of an ocean forming here, and then evidence of an ocean closing at the Tablelands," says Hingston. "We know an event must have happened to stack all of these rocks close together, and that was a continental collision."
At another geological site in the park, Green Point, travellers can see layered shale that would have formed in the depths of the Iapetus Ocean, and Gros Morne Mountain, a monumental fist of quartzite and limestone, was once an ancient tropical beach. It's here at the park's namesake mountain, which means "great sombre" in French, that we spend our last day in Newfoundland.
The knee-knackering scramble up dusty scree to reach the mountain's summit and the bone-rattling, boulder-strewn descent on the other side make for a challenging 17 kilometres. But at the peak, there's a worthy reward: a vast slate-coloured Arctic tundra that camouflages ptarmigan and Arctic hare, and a bird's-eye view of sapphire fiords and perfect jewel-like lakes.
In another 250 million years, this landscape will have shifted again; that's when scientists predict a new supercontinent will have formed. There's a melancholic relief in knowing Earth's movement continues on, centimetre by centimetre, year by year, outside of human time and influence.
Heading down the mountain, I look up to see Gros Morne live up to its name as a brooding sky gathers. It starts to rain, and I listen to my footsteps on the dark, slick trail.
Just the facts
To reach Gros Morne National Park by air, the closest airport is Deer Lake Regional Airport, about a 30-minute drive from the park's southern edge. Travellers can hike on their own, or on a guided tour with a company like Gros Morne Adventures.
The summertime, from about June to September, is generally considered the ideal window for hiking. (Gros Morne Mountain is closed until the end of June to protect wildlife during a critical seasonal period.) Visit pc.gc.ca/grosmorne for detailed trip-planning tips from Parks Canada.
Chloe Berge travelled as a guest of Go Western
Newfoundland, which did not review or approve this article.
CREDIT: Chloe Berge Special to the Star Chloe Berge travelled as a guest of Go Western Newfoundland , which did not review or approve this article .

Document 60 of 500

Ontario courts trouble with its judiciary

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-courts-trouble-with-judiciary/docview/2933743056/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+courts+trouble+with+its+judiciary&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Judging by his recent comments, Ontario Premier Doug Ford doesn't even understand part of democracy.
Witness his defence of his appointing two of his former staff members to the Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee, which vets and recommends judicial candidates: At a news conference last week, Ford opined that "it's part of democracy" for governments to appoint "like-minded" judges.
You won't find that part of democracy in any civics text. But in the face of withering criticism, the premier insisted that he was tripling and quadrupling down, even after it was revealed Wednesday that the man he appointed chair of the committee is a lobbyist for a gun manufacturer.
By way of explanation, Ford suggested that "like-minded" means being tough on crime, as he condemned judges who grant bail to those accused of serious offences. He neglected to mention, however, that judges, and justices of the peace, are already tough on people accused of crimes, as nearly 80 per cent of inmates in Ontario's prisons are awaiting trial.
Also missing from his pop-up civics lecture was any mention of the Constitution, including its guarantees of the presumption of innocence and the right not to be denied bail without just cause. Those rights are, it seems, mysteriously absent from Ford's conception of democracy.
That's not the worst of it. The premier's recent musing isn't a one-off, of-the-cuff remark; it is, rather, part of his government's continuing campaign to erode judicial independence.
The campaign was first revealed in 2019, when Attorney General Doug Downey told TVO that he wanted to select judges who "reflect some of the values that I have," and "sometimes only I can assess that."
Downey offered those comments in support of proposed changes to the Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee, changes that would grant the attorney general greater control over committee appointments and much greater latitude in selecting judicial candidates.
Downey's proposals met with widespread condemnation, including from the architect of the committee's judicial selection process, who said the changes risked needlessly politicizing judicial appointments. But the attorney general nonetheless pushed through the changes in 2021.
That still wasn't enough, though. Upon the impending retirement of the Chief Justice of Ontario, Downey decided in 2022 that he needed to be personally involved in vetting her replacement, rather than, as was customary, allowing the outgoing chief to vet candidates and make recommendations.
The custom existed for the same reason as the JAAC: To maintain judicial independence and ensure sufficient separation between two coequal branches of government. Yet by insinuating himself into the process, Downey required members from one branch of government to make entreaties directly to another branch - a gross violation of both judicial independence and the separation of powers.
Indeed, Downey's and Ford's efforts over the past five years amount to a sustained attack on judicial independence, which holds that judges must make their decisions free from outside pressures, especially pressure from the government.
Since the government is one of the main litigants in court, including in bail hearings, the public must have confidence that judges are guided by the evidence and the law, rather than by the whims of the government of the day.
Yet Ford's comments, and measures like those imposed by Ford and Downey, only serve to dilute that confidence. Even if every judge they appoint is beyond reproach, there will remain the suspicion that judges were selected not on merit, but on their allegiance to the government's interests.
And once trust in judicial independence is lost, so too is faith in the rule of law - the principle that the law applies equally to everyone, including the government itself - since the spectre of bias is ever present when "like-minded" judges adjudicate disputes between the government and its citizens.
This is precisely why we have independent, arms-length committees like the JAAC.
Or to put it in words the premier might understand: It isn't part of democracy for governments to appoint like-minded judges; it's part of democracy to maintain safeguards that prevent governments from doing so.
CREDIT: ;

Document 61 of 500

Ford wrong to stack courts, ex aide says: Former Harper adviser suggests premier doesn't get public safety

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-wrong-stack-courts-ex-aide-says/docview/2933743012/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+wrong+to+stack+courts%2C+ex+aide+says%3A+Former+Harper+adviser+suggests+premier+doesn%27t+get+public+safety&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's intention to appoint tough "like-minded judges" who will put even more people in jail is a "deeply disturbing" stance that fails to grasp what's really needed to keep Ontarians safe, says University of British Columbia law professor Benjamin Perrin.
And Perrin would know a thing or two about a "tough on crime" approach with a heavy focus on incarceration.
He was once the lead criminal justice adviser to former prime minister Stephen Harper, whose federal Conservative government dramatically increased the number of mandatory minimum sentences, among other measures that drove up the number of incarcerated people - only for many of those laws to later be struck down as unconstitutional.
"If the goal here is to increase the safety of Ontarians, this is completely the wrong way to do it and it's just political grandstanding and bluster," Perrin said in an interview this week.
He spoke to the Star as a crisis of constitutional proportions is erupting in Ontario as Ford "quadruples" down on wanting judges who think like him and rule the way he wants. Advocacy group Democracy Watch announced Thursday it would be launching a legal challenge over the appointments system. A number of major legal organizations have decried Ford's intentions as nothing short of a "substantial threat" to the independence of the courts and the public's confidence in the justice system; a move that risks tainting the integrity of future judges and calling their decisions into question.
In short, it's a threat to the rule of law.
Perrin said he was alarmed to hear that Ford is intent on "stacking the judiciary" with lawyers who believe in throwing more people in jail.
"To suggest that a series of appointments will be made to fundamentally alter the way that the judiciary makes decisions to suit a premier's particular political agenda is deeply disturbing," Perrin said.
"Imagine a premier saying, 'We're only going to appoint judges who believe in a pro-life agenda,' or only a pro-choice agenda, or only advance the interests of real estate developers."
Once himself an ardent believer that people convicted of crimes were being punished too leniently, Perrin's views on criminal justice have changed dramatically over the years as he researched the root causes of crime, as well as solutions.
"We have very clear research dating back for decades now that consistently shows that incarceration actually increases reoffending," he said. "When people are sent to prison, they tend to come out worse than they entered. Incarcerating someone is often seen as making them a better criminal."
He pointed to the lack of services and programming for people who are incarcerated, particularly in pretrial detention in provincial jails, which does little to help rehabilitate people, most of whom will inevitably be released.
What's really needed to increase public safety, Perrin said, are widespread access to mental health and substance use disorder supports, vocational training, affordable housing and a guaranteed minimum income, which would largely be provincial responsibilities.
"The research is really, really clear that when people have that chance, the majority of people do better," he said.
As the Star first reported last Thursday, the Ford government placed two of the premier's ex-senior staffers on the arm's-length committee that vets applications and recommends candidates for the Ontario Court of Justice.
Former deputy chief of staff Matthew Bondy is now the chair of the 13-person Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee (JAAC), while ex-director of stakeholder relations Brock Vandrick has also been a public member of the committee since December.
Global News also reported Wednesday that Bondy and Vandrick are registered to lobby the Ontario government, with Bondy lobbying for a gun manufacturer, among other firms. The Tories said both had been vetted for potential conflicts, and are not lobbying the attorney general, to whom the JAAC makes recommendations on judges.
Ford has been unrepentant about putting Bondy and Vandrick on the committee and wanting tough, like-minded judges. At Queen's Park on Wednesday, he repeated a familiar talking point that far too often, judges and justices of the peace in Ontario are releasing accused violent people on bail pending their trials, who then go on to reoffend.
"I'm not gonna double down, I'm not gonna triple down, I'm gonna quadruple down" to make sure violent criminals go to jail, Ford said in response to NDP Leader Marit Stiles, who asked during question period if he would rescind the appointments of Bondy and Vandrick.
There is a lack of data nationwide when it comes to bail, something the federal government acknowledged last year when it nonetheless implemented changes to make it more difficult for people accused of certain crimes to get bail.
About 80 per cent of people incarcerated in Ontario's overcrowded jails are on remand - meaning people who have been denied bail and are awaiting trial or sentencing - a number that has steadily increased over the years.
CREDIT: Jacques Gallant Courts and Justice Reporter

Document 62 of 500

Construction firms say province stiffed them during the pandemic: Government says it tried to shield taxpayers from runaway costs on key infrastructure projects

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/construction-firms-say-province-stiffed-them/docview/2933742503/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Construction+firms+say+province+stiffed+them+during+the+pandemic%3A+Government+says+it+tried+to+shield+taxpayers+from+runaway+costs+on+key+infrastructure+projects&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government has been hit with scores of claims from construction companies that say they were left on the hook for money they spent to keep critical public infrastructure projects moving ahead during the pandemic.
Several of the firms have taken the province to court, where they are seeking hundreds of millions of dollars in damages.
So far, the province has reached settlements for 67 different claims, government officials said. However, the government will not say how much it paid in settlements or how many other claims it's facing.
Despite declaring a province-wide state of emergency in March 2020 to slow the spread of COVID-19, several companies say the government refused to admit that the pandemic constituted an emergency for their projects - what the firms allege was a breach of contract. This emergency status would have entitled the companies to more money and time.
The province has argued in court that project agreements made construction companies responsible for added costs. As they negotiate claims, government officials say they are trying to shield taxpayers from runaway COVID-related expenses.
Michael Lindsay, president and CEO of the provincial agency Infrastructure Ontario, said details about the settlements are "commercially sensitive" and "confidential."
Lindsay said the pandemic presented "unprecedented realities" that project contracts did not fully anticipate. He said he is confident that a review of the agency's response to COVID claims would show it is protecting taxpayers, while "acting reasonably to address the validated costs" experienced by the construction firms.
But the public has a "right to know" how much the pandemic added to construction costs, said Matti Siemiatycki, director of the Infrastructure Institute at University of Toronto.
Temporarily withholding settlement details may protect the province's position in negotiating ongoing claims, but that information should ultimately be released, he said.
"Having transparency is important, so that our community understands the costs of these mega projects, and can understand how public money is being spent."
Most of the 67 COVID-related claims were settled through dispute resolution mechanisms built into the projects' agreements, Infrastructure Ontario said.
Seventeen of those settlements stemmed from major public-private partnerships (P3).
In P3 projects, a consortium of private companies inks a deal with the government, and typically delivers private financing, as well as project maintenance and operation. Governments have often touted the P3 model as a way to shift risks associated with large-scale projects from taxpayers to the private sector.
But those risks do not include "extraordinary circumstances like the COVID-19-pandemic," said Jean-Louis Servranckx, president and CEO of Aecon Group, which is part of a consortium of companies suing the province.
The consortium had been hired in 2019 to widen a stretch of Hwy. 401 from Mississauga to Milton. The $640-million contract included an "emergency provision" that was standard across all of Ontario's major P3 projects and should have been triggered by the pandemic, entitling contractors to more time and money to implement COVID-safety protocols and navigate supply-chain hiccups, the lawsuit claims.
"Nearly four years after the outbreak of the pandemic, we are still seeking fair and reasonable compensation for these costs," Servranckx told the Star.
The province had deemed P3 projects "essential," and released COVID guidelines for construction sites that mandated the sanitization of equipment, the social distancing of workers and other safety protocols.
However, to avoid triggering the emergency provision in its contracts, and "minimize its exposure to P3 contractors," the province decided to refuse to acknowledge the existence of an emergency for its projects, alleges the consortium, which also includes developers Amico and Parsons.
"In formulating and implementing this strategy ... the Ontario Crown ... breached its obligations of honest and good faith performance," the lawsuit alleges.
Infrastructure Ontario has not yet filed a statement of defence in the Hwy. 401 case, and declined to comment on the allegations, which have not been tested in court.
The companies building the Eglinton Crosstown LRT, another P3 project, have also taken the province to court. That consortium, called Crosslinx Transit Solutions, includes Aecon, which is also on the Hwy. 401 project, as well as ACS-Dragados, EllisDon and SNC-Lavalin.
In the fall of 2020, Crosslinx took legal action against Infrastructure Ontario and Metrolinx over their alleged repeated refusal to declare an emergency for the project. The consortium said the pandemic had left it grappling with new safety protocols, supply chain problems and absenteeism.
The provincial agencies disputed the claims. They argued that the $8.25-billion project agreement made Crosslinx responsible for risks associated with the pandemic. The agencies said they didn't declare an emergency on the project because Crosslinx was already required to maintain a safe and healthy workplace, including implementing COVID protocols.
The case wound its way through various levels of court. In December 2021, the provincial agencies announced they had reached a $325-million settlement to resolve various disputes with Crosslinx related to the project, including COVID costs.
After it was sued by another consortium, the province came out swinging.
The lawsuit stemmed from a $616-million contract to widen a stretch of Hwy. 427, which wasn't completed until September 2021 - a year behind schedule. The companies blamed the "long and torturous road to opening" on a series of alleged missteps by the government, including its "repeated" refusal to declare an emergency for the project during the pandemic.
Infrastructure Ontario attributed the delay to the companies' "own poor execution and poor progress" and issued a counterclaim against the consortium for $11 million, court documents state.
The province and the companies agreed to take the dispute to arbitration, which "remains ongoing," a spokesperson for Industry Ontario said.
Although the province's alleged refusal to declare an emergency on projects has rankled contractors, from the perspective of taxpayers, "the government did the right thing," said James McKellar, professor emeritus at the Schulich School of Business.
Rather than giving contractors carte blanche on COVID costs, McKellar characterized this approach as "a big stick" that would have made contractors "come to the table a little more humble."
NDP infrastructure critic Jennifer French (Oshawa) said the P3 claims - and the unknown amount spent to settle them - could be an "expensive" lesson in the dangers of moving away from traditional public financing.
"We're now finding out that the risk is actually multiplying and falling back on us," she said. "It's very frustrating as a member of the public just to watch so many dollars disappear ... We are not allowed to know where. We're not allowed to know how much."
In 2014, the province's auditor general found that Infrastructure Ontario's reliance on public-private partnerships had cost $8 billion more than traditional public financing.
But the agency's then-CEO pushed back against the finding, saying that number did not account for cost overruns and delays that large-scale publicly funded projects often incur.
Even before the pandemic, Infrastructure Ontario's Lindsay said the province had been reviewing its contract models and making adjustments.
Since COVID struck, he said the agency has made changes to all its contracts, including for P3 projects, "to provide a more predictable way to deal with the risks and the costs associated with pandemics moving forward."
CREDIT: Rachel Mendleson Toronto Star

Subject: Construction costs; Public private partnerships; Consortia; Corporate presidents; Infrastructure; Breach of contract; Public officials; Litigation; Pandemics; COVID-19; Contractors

Document 63 of 500

Who said what this week

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/who-said-what-this-week/docview/2933742463/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Who+said+what+this+week&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

What Canadians are saying about former prime minister Brian Mulroney

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/what-canadians-are-saying-about-former-prime/docview/2933614454/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+Canadians+are+saying+about+former+prime+minister+Brian+Mulroney&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: From devastation to awe, Canadians reacted Thursday night to the death of former prime minister Brian Mulroney. Here's a sampling of messages from across the country:
"Brian Mulroney loved Canada. I'm devastated to learn of his passing. He never stopped working for Canadians, and he always sought to make this country an even better place to call home. I'll never forget the insights he shared with me over the years - he was generous, tireless and incredibly passionate. As we mourn his passing and keep his family and friends in our thoughts, let us also acknowledge - and celebrate - Mr. Mulroney's role in building the modern, dynamic, and prosperous country we all know today." Prime Minister Justin Trudeau
"It is with great sadness that Canadians learn of the loss of one of our greatest ever statesmen, the Right Honourable Brian Mulroney. The love and prayers of all Canadians go to his beloved children, Caroline, Ben, Mark and Nicolas, and the love of his life, his best friend and wife, Mila. From Baie-Comeau, the son of an Irish electrician, raised in a working-class community, Mr. Mulroney had achieved prodigious success in business by the time he was in his late 30s. His success in politics was even greater, winning the biggest majority in Canadian history, along with an impressive reelection. He unleashed free enterprise, crushed inflation, restored fiscal sanity and concluded one of the greatest free trade agreements the world has ever seen, which remains largely in place today. He was among the first and most strident to fight against South Africa's apartheid policy and champion the cause of Nelson Mandela. I will always be thankful for his candid advice and generous mentorship to me personally. All Canadians are grateful for his immense sacrifice and the lasting legacy he leaves us all." Progressive Conservative Party Leader Pierre Poilievre
"I am saddened to hear of the death of the Right Honourable Brian Mulroney. He made an important contribution to Canada, including protecting our environment - leading the world in tackling acid rain and banning chemicals that were destroying the ozone layer. He was also a strong opponent of apartheid, leading the efforts to sanction South Africa by Commonwealth nations. My deep sympathy to his family." NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh
"Brian Mulroney was a giant. He leaves behind an unmatched legacy as Canada's 18th prime minister that includes negotiating NAFTA and helping to end apartheid in South Africa. Canada has lost its ultimate statesman. Brian was also so generous with his time. When faced with tough decisions, l often leaned on him for advice and benefited from his experience and his political instincts. He was a role model to me and taught me countless lessons on how to be a better leader. The man had a way with words like no one else. It didn't matter if it was a private conversation or a speech to thousands, Brian had an ability to capture a crowd and keep them captivated with his endless supply of stories. Anyone who heard him speak walked away in awe.Rest in peace, my friend. Our country will miss you." Ontario Premier Doug Ford
"I was an opponent of him all my political career, but in politics, opposition is opposition. It's like playing hockey. You can fight on the ice and have a beer together after that. And we had a lot of things in common." Former prime minister Jean Chrétien
"Brian Mulroney stood up for Canada and our national interest, including long after he left public office. I will miss his wise counsel, which he generously offered, particularly during the NAFTA negotiations. He was an advocate for democracy, human rights and the first Western leader to recognize an independent Ukraine." Deputy Prime Minister and Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland
"The Right Hon. Brian Mulroney was a transformative figure in Canadian politics. I offer my sincere condolences to his family." Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie
"Mr. Mulroney will also be remembered as the first Canadian Conservative leader to win back-to-back majority mandates since Sir John A. Macdonald, including the largest electoral victory in Canadian history. Canada has lost a historic figure, and we mourn his passing with all his many friends and associates." Former prime minister Stephen Harper
"One of my favourite memories of my time in Ottawa was when former PM Mulroney came for dinner. Afterwards he looked up at 24 Sussex, smiled at me and said, 'Not bad for two electricians' kids.'" Harper's wife, Laureen Teskey Harper
"I first met Prime Minister Mulroney when I was the Progressive Conservative U of C campus club president in 1992. I was just joining politics and felt inspired by his dedication to public service. He will be remembered by all Canadians for his service, and his commitment to our country." Alberta Premier Danielle Smith
"I first met Mr. Mulroney when I was 16, protesting (!) a speech he was giving. He responded with charm and the exchange made the national news. I was able to meet him and his family a few times since and he always showed grace and a deep commitment to public service. Rest well." Former Calgary mayor Naheed Nenshi
"At a dark point in my life, he called. I'll never forget it. His compassion is something I could never repay. There are thousands of people who can say the same today, thousands who are remembering his quiet kindness, his loyalty, his steel." Labour Minister Seamus O'Regan
"Mr. Mulroney was one of the greatest prime ministers in Canadian history. He was an exceptional leader. A visionary. And a statesman whose influence had a significant impact on the international stage. I remember him as generous, passionate, and deeply attached to Canada. Our country owes Prime Minister Mulroney a profound debt of gratitude. My family and I offer our deepest condolences to his wife, Mila, his children, Caroline, Ben, Mark, and Nicolas, and their families." Former Quebec premier Jean Charest
"Prime Minister Mulroney is perhaps the last to have sincerely attempted to reconcile Quebec and Canada. He was the architect of a free trade treaty that would not have existed without the support of Quebec. His role in respecting human rights and the release of Nelson Mandela is historic. He even had the trust of René Lévesque. Respect for a great northerner and Quebecer. My thoughts to his loved ones and family." Bloc Québécois Leader Yves-François Blanchet
"In December 2023 I was going through a pretty stressful time and Brian Mulroney took the time to call me and encourage me. That was the kind of man he was. He cared about people. A true gentleman." Montreal Liberal MP Anthony Housefather
"I am so sorry to hear about Brian Mulroney's death. He believed in an ambitious Canada & made hard decisions that benefitted the country he loved. He was also a great, green Conservative PM who always gave me good counsel. Sending condolences & love to his family and friends." Former federal minister Catherine McKenna
With files from The Canadian Press

Document 65 of 500

Brian Mulroney, former prime minister who reshaped Canada, has died

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/brian-mulroney-former-prime-minister-who-reshaped/docview/2933613854/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Brian+Mulroney%2C+former+prime+minister+who+reshaped+Canada%2C+has+died&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Brian Mulroney, the prime minister whose trade talks with the United States and introduction of a new sales tax forever reshaped the Canadian economy, has died.
He was 84.
"On behalf of my mother and our family, it is with great sadness we announce the passing of my father, The Right Honourable Brian Mulroney, Canada's 18th prime minister," his daughter Caroline posted on social media late Thursday afternoon.
"He died peacefully, surrounded by family."
Mulroney became prime minister in 1984 when his Progressive Conservatives won 211 seats in the House of Commons - the largest margin of victory for a political party in Canadian history and a feat that has never been repeated.
A self-professed "failed crooner" who made $50 a pop for singing ballads at his hometown's paper mill, Mulroney's unrelenting ambition won him friends among some of the most powerful leaders of his generation, but also made enemies of countless Canadians who scorned what they saw as his obsession with his own image and that of his legacy.
By the time he resigned in 1993, the consensus he'd built between Western populists and Quebec nationalists had collapsed, and the goods and services tax his government introduced was so wildly unpopular that it led to the near-annihilation of the PC party in the subsequent election, setting in motion a realignment of conservatism in Canada that continues to this day.
The Airbus affair, an alleged kickback scheme connected to the purchase of new aircraft for Canada, would further tarnish his political legacy, and he would spend the decades after he left office working to polish his image, ever confident that history would erase the blemishes.
"One hundred years from now, what will be remembered was that it was done," he told reporters after clinching the Canada-U.S. free trade deal.
"The naysayers will be forgotten."
Martin Brian Mulroney was born on March 20, 1939, the third of six children. He was the first of them to be born in Baie Comeau, a town set up in the 1930s to house a paper mill.
In a biography by L. Ian MacDonald, Mulroney recounted a childhood spent playing sports, devouring books from the library, and making a name with mill owner Col. Robert McCormick as a boy who could sing any tune, upon request, and being paid $50 for the job.
He left Baie Comeau for high school in New Brunswick, and from there went on to St. Francis Xavier University in Antigonish, N.S.
His undergraduate years laid the groundwork for his life in politics, as he became active in a Progressive Conservative campus club and started working on regional political campaigns.
At Laval University law school, his political network grew to include Lucien Bouchard, who would later commit a Shakespearean act of political sabotage by double-crossing Mulroney during negotiations over changes to the Constitution known as the Meech Lake accord and siding with Quebec separatists.
After law school, Mulroney found work as a labour lawyer in Montreal and there, at age 33, he met then-18-year old Mila Pivnicki, spying her at the Mount Royal Tennis Club one weekday afternoon.
They would marry two years later and go on to have four children - Caroline, Ben, Mark and Nicolas - and 15 grandchildren.
Caroline Mulroney is now a minister in Ontario Premier Doug Ford's government.
In a statement Thursday, Ford said he sees some of Mulroney's best qualities in his children.
"Brian was also so generous with his time. When faced with tough decisions, I often leaned on him for advice and benefited from his experience and his political instincts," Ford said.
"He was a role model to me and taught me countless lessons on how to be a better leader."
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau also reflected on the insights Mulroney shared with him over the years.
"He never stopped working for Canadians, and he always sought to make this country an even better place to call home," Trudeau said in a written statement, calling him generous, tireless and incredibly passionate.
"As we mourn his passing and keep his family and friends in our thoughts, let us also acknowledge - and celebrate - Mr. Mulroney's role in building the modern, dynamic, and prosperous country we all know today."
By the time of the 1984 general election, the Liberals had been in power for nearly all of two decades and Mulroney - elected leader of the Progressive Conservatives in 1983 - capitalized on dissatisfaction with their track record, perhaps summing it up best in the knockout punch he landed on Liberal leader John Turner during a leaders' debates.
As Turner deflected Mulroney's attacks on his patronage appointments, Mulroney jabbed a finger across the top of the lectern and his baritone voice belted out what would become a classic political quip: " You had an option, sir."
Mulroney would go on to win a landslide victory - 211 seats, including a record 81 in Quebec, where fallout from the constitutional talks of 1982 had led to great frustration with the Liberals.
With a decisive mandate, Mulroney embarked on twin talks to reshape Canada: the Meech Lake accord and a free trade deal with the U.S.
The accord was the sequel to the repatriation of the Constitution in 1982, a bill that Quebec refused to sign at the time.
The accord, however, needed to be formally ratified by all the provinces and the subsequent three years of debate saw the national consensus around its provisions collapse as the political landscape in some of the provinces shifted toward staunch opposition to the proposals.
Among the outcomes: a resurgence of the Quebec separatist movement, and the creation of a new federal separatist party the Bloc Québécois, led by Mulroney's old friend Bouchard.
Current Quebec Premier François Legault called Mulroney a visionary Thursday because of the Canada-U.S. trade agreement, also celebrating his fight against apartheid in South Africa and his work against acid rain, one of the biggest environmental challenges of the day.
"He was a real ambassador who made Quebec and Canada shine in the world. My thoughts are with his family and friends," Legault said.
Mulroney never campaigned on free trade with the U.S.
As Opposition leader in the House of Commons, he met U.S. President Ronald Reagan and the twomen developed an affinity for one another, rooted, as Mulroney himself would later observe in his biography, in their shared Irish heritage and their conservative global outlook.
That they were allies was cemented in the so-called "Shamrock summit" of 1985, when the two men took to the stage to belt out the song "When Irish Eyes are Smiling."
Talks towards a new trade agreement began that year and concluded at the 11th-hour in 1987, when, in Mulroney's telling, he won a last-minute concession from the U.S. for a trade dispute mechanism.
The deal reshaped the Canadian economy, ushering in a boom in two-way trade and investment, but also marked the beginning of a major upheaval in Canada's manufacturing sector, and the globalization era that followed is often directly referenced as the birth moment for the modern era of populist politics.
Current Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, who also sought guidance from Mulroney, saw his economic legacy differently.
"These changes gave thousands of working-class families the same opportunities he had, that is, the chance to work hard, buy a home, and build their dreams," he said in a statement.
The 1988 election became the "Free Trade" election, and while the Liberals were opposed to the deal, Mulroney still won a second term.
Through his time in office, Mulroney attempted to rein in government spending, embarking on rounds of cuts and freezes to social spending and then ultimately did what many thought unthinkable for political survival - introduced a new tax, the Goods and Services tax.
The opposition leader at the time, Jean Chrétien, promised the bill would get quashed in the Senate, but Mulroney used an obscure constitutional provision to stack the Senate and the new tax passed.
Chrétien said Mulroney did his best to do what he thought was right for Canada, and while he was involved in many controversial files, it was better to be involved than not to be involved.
"He left a mark as prime minister, there is no doubt about that," he said Thursday.
His mark was also international.
In one of his last public appearances, Mulroney took pride in the fact that in 1991 his government was the first in the Western world to recognize Ukraine's independence from Russia, and urged the current government to stand firm against Russian tyranny.
He also led the global fight to dismantle South Africa's apartheid regime, eventually convincing even a recalcitrant British prime minister, Margaret Thatcher, to drop her opposition to imposing sanctions.
But despite his international stature, his social service cuts, the GST, the recession of the early 1990s proved too powerful a political force to keep him in office.
He resigned in 1993, and in the subsequent election, the party was destroyed in the polls - a campaign that also marked the rise of a new Conservative party, the Reform party, formed from the seeds of western discontent.
Two years after he left, a scandal exploded: Mulroney was alleged to have taken kickbacks from the sale of airplanes while he was prime minister, and the ensuing allegations would see him under scrutiny for well over 10 years, culminating in a commission of inquiry being called by the next conservative to replace him as prime minister, Stephen Harper.
That commission ultimately concluded Mulroney had acted inappropriately by taking $225,000 - but it was after he left office.
Harper - who had successfully united the tatters of Mulroney's PCs with the reformers who broke with it - paid his tribute Thursday to a man he once refused to even speak to, calling Mulroney a historic figure, an environmental champion, and a freedom fighter.
The Mulroney family did not specify the cause of death, but last year the former prime minister had been treated for prostate cancer, and also underwent a heart procedure.
His post-political life saw Mulroney play an active role in the private sector, sitting on numerous corporate boards.
He also played a role in the renegotiation of the North American Free Trade deal, acting as Canada's unofficial emissary to the political elite in Washington, and once even reprising his duet with Reagan at a fundraising gala where then-U.S. President Donald Trump was in attendance.
When asked by reporters in 2017 what he was doing at a Liberal cabinet meeting, his dedication to the country shone through:
"There's no Conservative or Liberal way to negotiate a free-trade agreement," he said.
"There's only a Canadian way."
With files from Tonda MacCharles
CAPTION: Former prime minister Brian Mulroney arrives to speak at a conference put on by the University of Ottawa Professional Development Institute and the Canada School of Public Service in Ottawa on March 5, 2019.

Subject: Law schools; Trade agreements; Biographies; Politics; Families & family life; Kickbacks; Free trade; Pulp & paper mills; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Trade agreements Free trade Pulp & paper mills

Location: United States--US; Canada; Quebec Canada

People: Mulroney, Brian

Company / organization: Name: Senate-US; NAICS: 921120

Title: Brian Mulroney, former prime minister who reshaped Canada, has died
