Document 66 of 500

'Mr. Mulroney never stopped working for Canadians: Former prime minister's death prompts outpouring of reflection

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mr-mulroney-never-stopped-working-canadians/docview/2933613847/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Mr.+Mulroney+never+stopped+working+for+Canadians%3A+Former+prime+minister%27s+death+prompts+outpouring+of+reflection&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-The death Thursday of former Progressive Conservative Prime Minister Brian Mulroney, a joyfully combative and proud partisan, drew profound reflections by friends, admirers and former political rivals alike.
Former Prime Minister Jean Chrétien told reporters on Parliament Hill Mulroney was a formidable adversary who had "a good political career, was "an important prime minister" and his family "can be proud" of him.
Chrétien said they had similar backgrounds. "We were both from rural Quebec," and Mulroney was "himself from a minority. He was an Anglo in Quebec, while I was a Franco in the rest of the land."
And throughout their many political debates, "I never had any bitter personal discussion with him," Chrétien said."He would poke me and I would poke him."
"In politics, opposition is opposition," said Chrétien, but "Like playing hockey, you can fight on the ice and take a beer together after that."
Chrétien, who succeeded John Turner as Liberal leader and faced Mulroney as Opposition leader before Mulroney left politics in 1993, said he thought the two men probably both would have liked to have faced each other directly in a federal election.
But "history" determined it was time for Mulroney to leave the political stage, Chrétien said.
Mulroney was replaced by Kim Campbell as PC leader who went on to lose the 1993 election to Chrétien. His beloved PC party was battered and bruised for years afterward, and later merged with the Canadian Alliance party in 2003.
"History will write what we've done," said Chrétien, but the former Liberal prime minister for whom electoral wins were always the big trophy hailed Mulroney's achievement in the 1980s of rebuilding the Conservative party, especially in Quebec - a feat that brought Mulroney to his sweeping 1984 majority government, the biggest in Canadian history.
One of Mulroney's later successors, Peter MacKay, the Progressive Conservative leader who founded the modern Conservative party with Stephen Harper, recalled Mulroney's retail political skills and acumen.
He said he got to know Mulroney and his young family when the Quebecer came to Nova Scotia to run in his father's riding after Elmer MacKay stepped down to make way for the then-newly elected PC leader who needed a seat in the Commons.
"He really invested in people and knew that that was the very essence of politics," MacKay said on CBC television.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau who sought Mulroney's advice on the renegotiation of NAFTA with the Trump administration said he learned of his death "with great sadness."
He said Mulroney had a "role in building the modern, dynamic, and prosperous country we all know today."
Mulroney's legacy includes nailing a Canada-U.S. trade deal and expanding it to include Mexico, enacting environmental protections, standing up against apartheid in South Africa, and trying to bridge the divide between English and French Canada through constitutional negotiations, Trudeau said.
"Mr. Mulroney never stopped working for Canadians, and he always sought to make this country an even better place to call home," Trudeau said in a written statement.
Speaking later Thursday in Thunder Bay, Trudeau spoke of how he valued Mulroney's advice during Canada's renegotiation of NAFTA from 2016-2019.
"He shaped our past, but he shapes our present and he will impact our future as well," Trudeau said. "He was an extraordinary statesman. And he will be deeply, deeply missed."
"As we mourn his passing and keep his family and friends in our thoughts, let us also acknowledge - and celebrate - Mr. Mulroney's role in building the modern, dynamic, and prosperous country we all know today."
Former Ontario premier David Peterson who knew Mulroney before politics as a young lawyer, and who went on to work with Mulroney to rewrite the Constitution in the great Meech Lake accord debates of the late 1980s expressed deep "sadness" in an interview.
"He was just the kindest and most generous man I've known, terribly Irish in his affections and his loves and his excesses and his generosity," said Peterson, now chair of Torstar.
Peterson said Mulroney was "fun and generous and inclusive. And he can always spot the person in the room that needed an encouraging word." That gift, he said, was one of the reasons Mulroney was such a successful politician.
Through the Meech Lake debates, Peterson said, Mulroney would often phone him on Sunday nights as the Ontario premier was trying to squeeze in family time with his then three young kids, chuckling as he recalled how "Brian loved to talk."
In the tense last days as the constitutional accord needed to be ratified, but failed to win approval in the Manitoba legislature due to Indigenous MP Elijah Harper's objection, and then, in Newfoundland, former premier Clyde Wells withdrew the accord for a final vote, Mulroney and Peterson were "both devastated," he said.
They believed "it was a mistake of historical proportions" by the Manitoba and Newfoundland politicians, and Peterson hailed Mulroney's "patient" leadership throughout. "People were under enormous pressure," he recalled. "It was in a way politics at its finest."
Mulroney's political legacy aside, Peterson said Canadians should remember that he exemplified "generous, decent, good, well motivated people in politics, that did not hate the people on the other side and worked very hard to get along and find common consensus."
Wells in an interview with the Star said although he and Mulroney did "not always see eye to eye, we had our differences, but we also had some pleasant exchanges too. I greatly respected him for what his objectives were and what I sensed was his dedication to the country, and I admired him for that. But I didn't see the objective to be achieved in quite the same way he did."
Wells said while he did not agree with Mulroney on the constitutional changes proposed, he admired his fight against apartheid and thought Mulroney's greatest legacy was in negotiating the Canada-U.S. free trade deal.
"I wasn't totally certain at the time that it was going to be successful, I had mixed feelings about it but I supported it in the end because I thought on the whole I was going to be beneficial, and he was absolutely right."
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre called Mulroney "one of the greatest ever statesmen" in expressing condolences to his family, and "the love of his life, his best friend and wife, Mila."
He hailed Mulroney's working-class roots as the son of an Irish electrician who went on to become a lawyer and achieved business success as the head of Iron Ore Company. And Poilievre celebrated his success in politics as "even greater."
"But it was the transformational change he brought to his nearly a decade as prime minister that is most important. He unleashed free enterprise, crushed inflation, restored fiscal sanity and concluded one of the greatest free trade agreements the world has ever seen, which remains largely in place today."
Poilievre cast Mulroney as a leader on the world stage who "stood firmly on the side of Western allies against communism and for freedom."
The Conservative leader said he "will always be thankful for his candid advice and generous mentorship to me personally. All Canadians are grateful for his immense sacrifice and the lasting legacy he leaves us all."
Mulroney's death was announced by his daughter Caroline, an Ontario cabinet minister and the only one of his four children - including Mark, Ben and Nicholas - to enter electoral politics.
The Mulroney family did not specify the cause of death, but last year the former prime minister had been treated for prostate cancer. He had been optimistic about his treatment and recovery.

Document 67 of 500

No joy in Toronto hosting the World Cup as Ford is 'tripling down' another blunder

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/no-joy-toronto-hosting-world-cup-as-ford-is/docview/2933515762/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+joy+in+Toronto+hosting+the+World+Cup+as+Ford+is+%27tripling+down%27+another+blunder&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A taxing dilemma
Toronto to receive fraction of tax revenue for World Cup, Feb. 28
I don't understand what a city gets out of hosting the World Cup, especially when many cities are involved. I usually don't even know what city they are in when I watch those games. I would like to see something of the host cities, but generally the coverage only focuses on the pitch and the game. The cameras don't pan around the city showing all the interesting sights, except perhaps for brief glimpses. Even during halftimes very little is shown or mentioned of the host city.
If you want to advertise a city, host bike races, marathon runs or other events that provide a great viewing cross-section of a city's architecture, parks, leafy boulevards, neighbourhoods, and so on. Those events might not have quite the same stature as the World Cup, but they still can attract millions of international viewers and they are far less costly to host.
Ed Janicki, Victoria, B.C.
The City of Toronto is willing to invest over $60 million per soccer game ($380 million total for six games), while neglecting the pressing homelessness issue, showcasing a stark contrast between their priorities and authentic leadership. It serves as another blatant reminder of our lack of authentic leadership and a city council that prefers the pretense of responsibility over genuine action.
Djanka Gajdel, Toronto
So Toronto taxpayers are going to be on the hook for $380 million dollars to pay for hosting six World Cup soccer games in 2026. That works out to just over $60 million for each 90 minute game, or over $666,000 for each minute played here. Surely the city can find better ways to "kick away" $380 million dollars.
Michael Cole, Toronto
I am in favour of Toronto hosting World Cup soccer games, but the estimated costs are ridiculously high and very confusing. I got 40 pages of information from the city that I tried to review. I am a CPA and based on the information that I got I am surprised this was ever approved. But we are beyond that. What is important now is that the costs are controlled. There is no reference to ticket revenue.
I think that the city's auditor general should start now. For example if they hire nine people, they have to ask if they are required and whether their salaries are reasonable. City departments should be doing some of the identified work. The city has a big planning department that should eliminate some consulting costs. The capital expenditures are also totally confusing. It is not too late to fix this and do it right.
Elmer Csaszar, Toronto
'Tripling down' on accountability for Ford
Ford is 'tripling down' on wanting 'like-minded' judges, Feb. 27
Premier Doug Ford says he is "tripling down" on appointing "like-minded judges" to the Ontario Court of Justice. However, his recent record tells us, he is "tripping down" yet another political blunder.
Raymond Peringer, Toronto
The premier couldn't sound more foolish and immature than he has these past few days in the legislature with his juvenile responses in question period. What's next, "double-dog daring" the opposition to meet him out back of Queen's Park?
It seems almost every group and association in the justice system and legal profession has serious concerns about his attempts to politicize the courts, yet he ignores them as he insults every judge who isn't a card-carrying Conservative by implying that only Conservatives interpret the law correctly. If he truly feels harsher penalties need to be delivered, he can lobby to change the laws through legislation or be proactive and provide better funding for programs that divert people from a life of crime. Every harsh sentence costs Ontario's taxpayers in incarceration costs. Putting a person in jail costs over $110,000 a year. That kind of money could go a long way to keep many people out of the criminal justice system in the first place.
Terry Kushnier, Scarborough
I have concerns regarding the alterations to the judicial appointments process. First among them, we are continuing Premier Doug Ford's mimicking of the failed Republican law and order policy, together with their partisan judicial selection process. The law and order approach has filled U.S. prisons, but has done nothing to improve public safety. Ford wants to appoint "like-minded judges." This must mean he wants judges in favour of jailing people and offering harsher penalties. It seems to me that the first person convicted by a newly appointed judge would have right to appeal on the grounds of not receiving a fair trial.
Clyde McDonald, Bracebridge, Ont.
Teachers at risk?
Ford government says solar eclipse is no excuse for teachers to work at home, Feb. 28
Toronto's Catholic elementary teachers are pushing to work from home on April 8 to "prioritize the safety" of teachers during the solar eclipse. Does this suggest Catholic teachers are stupid enough to stare into the sun? If so, are these the people we want teaching our children?
Sandy Jeffrey, Toronto
I am shocked at the brazenly disingenuous behaviour of Toronto's Catholic elementary school teachers. They are actually pushing to work from home on April 8 due to the "dangers" posed by a solar eclipse. Really? What's next? Refusing to work during when it is dark outside because of safety concerns?
It seems to me that this would be an ideal moment to teach, to enlighten students on astronomical and celestial phenomena, to help them better understand their world. In this way, you can explain how dangerous it is to look at the sun; perhaps even demonstrate a safe method for the whole class to view the eclipse. Knowledge is the best protection you can provide.
It's a weak justification to work from home.
Paul Graham, Mississauga
Pharmacare is a win
Trudeau government unveils national pharmacare bill, Feb. 29
Trudeau's Liberal government, aided by an alliance with the NDP, just announced a deal for a new national pharmacare program that will cover birth control and diabetes drugs. Birth control coverage includes pills, intrauterine devices, injections and emergency contraception. Contrast this to the assault on women's reproductive rights currently going on in the U.S. What a great day for Canada!!
Mary Christine Tucker, Midland, Ont.
CAPTION: The City of Toronto estimates it will cost $380 million to host World Cup matches in the city, an increase of $80 million compared to a 2022 forecast partly because of the addition of another match to the schedule.

Document 68 of 500

Mount Sinai Hospital climber says he'll pause his ascents, denies antisemitic motives

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mount-sinai-hospital-climber-says-hell-pause-his/docview/2933515758/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mount+Sinai+Hospital+climber+says+he%27ll+pause+his+ascents%2C+denies+antisemitic+motives&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The man who climbed up Mount Sinai Hospital, sparking outrage, accusations of antisemitism and a police investigation, has denied targeting the site due to its connections to the Jewish community, while adding that he won't be climbing buildings again anytime soon.
In a statement to the Star, the man, who dresses up in a Spider-Man costume during pro-Palestinian protests, said he had no idea what building he was climbing in the incident last month. The statement was relayed to the Star by protest organizers.
"I never intended to make a statement by climbing up the hospital foyer," said the man, who goes by spiderman4palestine on social media, but who has not been publicly identified. "It was just somewhere high up and visually appealing to me. I didn't realize it was a hospital at the moment, and had no idea that the building had historical ties to the Jewish community."
What most people saw of the incident was a video clip of a man waving a Palestinian flag atop scaffolding outside Mount Sinai, a historically Jewish hospital with the silhouette of the Star of David for a logo. As this was happening, a crowd of onlookers chanted for intifadeh.
This video spread quickly on social media, where it drew condemnation as an act of antisemitic hate.
In the statement, spiderman4palestine says he wanted only to "bring joy" by dressing up like Spider-Man and climbing buildings during protests.
"Mount Sinai Hospital was in no way targeted and presenting this as an antisemitic attack is an intentional miscategorization of what happened," said spiderman4palestine.
Noah Shack, vice-president for countering antisemitism and hate with the UJA Federation of Greater Toronto, maintained that the hospital was "specifically targeted" because of its Jewish roots and says claims to the contrary are an attempt to "gaslight the Jewish community."
"That the organizers and protesters claim a lack of awareness is immaterial," said Shack.
The actions were condemned by politicians including Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Premier Doug Ford, federal NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Mayor Olivia Chow.
"It's easy to say I do this for the wrong reasons," said spiderman4palestine. "It's easy to point a finger, and make accusations. Put yourself in my shoes for a minute. I bring joy in people's eyes in a place where we are surrounded by pain and anger amidst the ongoing genocide of the Palestinian people."
Leaders of some of Toronto's largest hospitals authored a joint letter that said the protest "ran the real risk" of disrupting hospital operations and making staff and patients unsafe.
Protest groups took umbrage with this and have made a request for it to be retracted with an apology, insisting in a letter that had there been an urgent need, demonstrators would have made space to allow access.
Spiderman4palestine said he would not be climbing any buildings during protests for the foreseeable future, though he hoped to continue appearing at protests as part of his efforts to entertain demonstrators and to bring attention to their cause through his online activity.
Police, who said they have since bolstered their presence near the hospital, would not confirm whether they plan to charge the man when asked by the Star.
CAPTION: "I never intended to make a statement by climbing up the hospital foyer," said the man whose actions caused a major controversy.

Document 69 of 500

'A permit is not a shovel in the ground': Mississauga out on millions in funding for missing provincial housing target

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/permit-is-not-shovel-ground-mississauga-out-on/docview/2933515742/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27A+permit+is+not+a+shovel+in+the+ground%27%3A+Mississauga+out+on+millions+in+funding+for+missing+provincial+housing+target&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mississauga has missed out on millions of dollars in infrastructure cash after failing to reach the province's housing target for the city.
The Mississauga News obtained a Feb. 14 letter to the city from Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra outlining that Mississauga had met less than half its housing target, which combines residential construction starts, additional unit conversions and new long-term-care beds.
Only municipalities that made 80 per cent or more of their housing target are slated to receive money from the province's $1.2-billion Building Faster Fund.
"I encourage you to work towards eligibility for future program years," Calandra said in the letter.
The Building Faster funding that municipalities get is tied to the percentage a city's housing target makes up of the overall provincial goal of 1.5 million new units and long-term-care beds over 10 years across Ontario.
If 7,480 (85 per cent of Mississauga's target) new housing construction starts, additional residential units and long-term-care beds were recorded in the city, Mississauga would have received over $25 million, according to other municipal payment rates.
In an interview, acting Mississauga Mayor Joe Horneck said the lost provincial funding isn't likely to have an immediate impact on the city's infrastructure plans and suggested that external forces like interest rates and labour shortages had more to do with fewer housing construction starts than the city's performance.
"We're not able to put a shovel in the ground," he said. "We can give out permits. We can put out approvals, but we can't make someone put a shovel in the ground and so that's a real challenge."
He added that with most of Mississauga already built out, much of the development in the city is more expensive infill projects, like condo towers.
Mississauga officials have been asking the province to reconsider how they calculate housing target scores so that city-controlled measures like issuing building permits and housing approvals be considered as part of a municipality's targets.
"A permit is not a shovel in the ground or a home to live in," said Calandra spokesperson Bianca Meta in an email to the Mississauga News.
She added that municipalities are all judged by identical criteria and the province is "very pleased with those that are meeting or exceeding targets."
Mississauga's funding refusal follows the province writing a $25-million cheque to Brampton, which reached around 85 per cent of its housing target, and a $114-million pledge to the City of Toronto for beating its provincial goal by 10,756 units.
The province has also given over $3.5 million to Brantford and Chatham-Kent in Building Faster Fund money. The province says both municipalities exceeded their targets.
The Building Faster Fund appears to be part of a trend of senior levels of government incentivizing cities to boost housing stocks and options or risk losing cash.
In October, it looked like Mississauga might miss out on over $100 million in federal housing accelerator funding after city council failed to pass a motion supporting allowing fourplex houses "in principle."
The federal money flowed after former mayor Bonnie Crombie used her enhanced powers to bring a fourplex bylaw, which was approved, back to council.
Conservative opposition leader Pierre Poilievre seems poised to continue an incentivized approach to housing in cities and has proposed rewards for municipalities that have increases in homebuilding by 15 per cent annually.
Cities that don't see that increase would see federal funding for transit and infrastructure withheld in Poilievre's proposal.
CAPTION: Mississauga Ward 6 Coun. Joe Horneck.Mississauga has missed out on provincial funding due to missing a provincial housing target.

Subject: Infrastructure; Funding; Cities; Municipalities

Business indexing term: Subject: Infrastructure

Title: 'A permit is not a shovel in the ground': Mississauga out on millions in funding for missing provincial housing target

Document 70 of 500

Brian Mulroney will have a state funeral, Justin Trudeau says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/brian-mulroney-will-have-state-funeral-justin/docview/2933515736/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Brian+Mulroney+will+have+a+state+funeral%2C+Justin+Trudeau+says&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Former prime minister Brian Mulroney will receive a full state funeral, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said Friday.
"We're working with the family closely to ensure that all of their wishes are respected and that it be the right and fitting tribute to him," Trudeau said. "We will be sharing more details of course ... But I can also let everyone know that there will be opportunities for Canadians to express their gratitude and share tributes to the former prime minister as the coming days and weeks unfold."
Flags on Parliament Hill flew at half-mast, and the House of Commons cancelled what would have been an NDP opposition day and adjourned to mark Mulroney's death.
Formal tributes from MPs are expected when parliament resumes after a scheduled constituency break, during the week of Mar. 18.
Many other reflections and tributes from across the political spectrum and around the world flowed Friday, the morning after Mulroney died at age 84.
Speaking in Sudbury, Trudeau, highlighted Mulroney's example as one modern politicians could learn from.
"The reality is he was a statesman who was totally devoted to Canada," said Trudeau, and although he had differences with his political opponents also displayed respect for them despite ideological differences.
"He was an excellent politician, but at the same time, there was a great deal of respect for the role and responsibilities that he had, and for those who were serving even if they were members of a different party. This is a reminder that politics can and must be rooted in service and respect," Trudeau said in Sudbury.
Recalling Mulroney's environmental record, Trudeau said he "stickhandled" the relationship with the U.S. to advance environmental protections, and was rightly recognized as "one of the greenest prime ministers Canada has ever had."
Trudeau said today's Conservatives could take a lesson from Mulroney's record, that "just being a conservative doesn't automatically mean that you don't care about the environment. There is a choice that the current Conservative Party is making that they would be good to reflect upon because Brian Mulroney understood that caring for the environment, caring for the planet, caring for the people who live on it is part of building a strong economy for the future.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford, whose cabinet includes Caroline Mulroney, the former prime minister's daughter who announced his death on behalf of the family, said Friday "I loved the prime minister...I'm going to miss him dearly."
He said Mulroney charmed his mother when he called and sang happy birthday to her. "My mum absolutely loved him and so he gave her a CD of all the songs that he sang."
"He even came by our caucus meetings and you know, when you have a tough day and always give him a call, he boosts you up like I've never seen before, and we just lost such a great Canadian," Ford said Friday.
NDP leader Jagmeet Singh on Friday hailed Mulroney's gesture to offer kind words upon the death of former NDP leader Ed Broadbent.
Mulroney "was actually in the hospital himself, dealing with the health crisis, and the thought of someone dealing with a health crisis taking the time to share some kind words about a political opponent really is a testament to the kind of person Prime Minister literally was," Singh said. He too hailed Mulroney's environmental legacy, and the political breakthrough in Quebec - showing that modern federal parties had to cross the linguistic divide and work to unite the country.
James Baker, a former U.S. Secretary of the Treasury and Secretary of State who counted Mulroney as a friend of 30 years, in a written tribute in the Globe and Mail said, "With Mr. Mulroney as its prime minister, Canada punched well above its weight on the world stage."
"Regardless of how one gauges the ups and downs of his public life, Mr. Mulroney built a considerable legacy that continues to benefit his country and the world today, particularly through his advancement of free trade."
Baker said that Mulroney worked former U.S. presidents Ronald Reagan and George H.W. Bush to construct the continental free trade pact that created "world's biggest and most successful trading bloc."
Foreign Affairs Minister Melanie Joly welled up as she recalled "Brian was a friend" who "was there" when times were tough for her.
Joly said she consulted him when she became foreign affairs minister, he aided the government on the renegotiation of NAFTA and on lifting the steel and aluminum tariffs. She called him every time before she headed to Washington, and said he knew people on the Republican and Democrat side of Congress.
Joly said she was texting the Mexican foreign affairs minister who was former chief of staff to Kofi Annan, former UN secretary general, and shared memories of Mulroney with her.
"It just showed how much he had friends and allies around the world."
Joly said Mulroney's political career and how he dealt with political opponents highlights just how toxic current political debates have grown.
"You don't need to the hate the person in front of you. You just need to agree to disagree," Joly said on Parliament Hill.
"And I think this is something that we should all bear in mind. Because politics is, particularly these years becoming more toxic and polarized. At the end of the day, we all have our different values and ideas. But we're all there for building a greater country."
Former Conservative prime minister Stephen Harper, travelling in India, issued a written statement extending sympathies to Mulroney's wife and family, calling him "a historic " and "transformational" figure in Canada's modern era.
Harper first arrived in Ottawa as a junior PC staffer during Mulroney's first mandate, and swiftly became disillusioned with Mulroney's political willingness to give up a deficit-cutting measure to drop the indexation of senior's pensions to the rate of inflation. Harper returned to Alberta and would go on to be a key player in the Reform Party, its successor Canadian Alliance, and ultimately the united modern federal Conservative Party that dropped the word "Progressive" from its title.
It was Harper who called the Oliphant inquiry into Mulroney's role in the Airbus affair, but Harper outlined Mulroney's many accomplishments late Thursday and laid out a political legacy Conservatives lay claim to today.
"Prime Minister Mulroney fought to secure the Free Trade Agreement with the United States, from which Canada has benefitted immensely ever since. Prime Minister Mulroney stood for freedom and democracy on the world stage, in his principled opposition to apartheid in South Africa, his enduring support for Israel, and his advocacy of independence for Ukraine and the other European nations long under the yoke of Soviet communism."
Harper said Mulroney championed "significant international environmental measures" including the Montreal Protocol on ozone-depleting substances, and the Canada-US Acid Rain Treaty."
And like former prime minister Jean Chretien, Harper hailed Mulroney's historic political accomplishment as "the first Canadian Conservative leader to win back-to-back majority mandates since Sir John A. Macdonald, including the largest electoral victory in Canadian history."
Former prime minister and PC leader Joe Clark in a statement posted on "X" hailed Mulroney as "one of Canada's great change-makers."
Mulroney was "determined to move the country forward and with an instinct to respect differences and an extraordinary capacity to reconcile. He was bold, a big dreamer, and an unparalleled negotiator and persuader, both at home in Canada, and in the larger world."
"Often then we were allies, sometimes competitors and then became colleagues in and beyond national government. I am grateful for his extraordinary service, and his friendship, and mourn his loss."
CAPTION: Former prime minister Brian Mulroney leaves Parliament Hill in 2012. His family announced late Thursday that the former Tory leader died peacefully, surrounded by loved ones.

Subject: Conservatism; Free trade; Trade agreements; Scandals; Parliaments; Decades; Prime ministers; Political parties

Business indexing term: Subject: Free trade Trade agreements

Document 71 of 500

Ford's judicial wish a risky endeavour

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-judicial-wish-risky-endeavour/docview/2933372924/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+judicial+wish+a+risky+endeavour&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On the matter of stacking the provincial judicial appointments committee with his political lapdogs in order to ensure "like-minded judges" are appointed, folks, Doug Ford has doubled down. And then he said he was "tripling down." "Quadrupling down," now, even.
Is it "the most blatant example of patronage" one professor has recently seen? Sure it is. And the premier doesn't even deny that charge. He embraces it. Others may adopt polarizing politicization, Ford was born in it, moulded by it. To him it is normal. Just listen to him talk about it: "I am going to make sure we have like-minded judges," Ford said Monday at Queen's Park.
Well, that's not how this is supposed to work, as a barrage of legal experts have told the Star. Judges are meant to be fully independent from the government of the day and from partisan political considerations. They need to be, to ensure their administration of justice isn't tainted by political pressure or partisan considerations. And, importantly, the public needs to see and believe that they aren't tainted by those things, to ensure the integrity of the court system.
The thing about Ford's comments on the issue - his matter-of-fact response to what appears to many of us to be a bombshell revelation - is that he doesn't seem to even understand the nature of the outrage. That this should be a politicized process, and that he wants judges who openly share his ideological leaning and party affiliation seem like the most natural things in the world to him. "We got elected to get like-minded people in appointments," he said. "I am not going to appoint some NDP or some Liberal. I have made it very clear where I stand with judges."
The Federation of Ontario Law Associations may say this attitude and those comments "reflect a juvenile understanding of the role of an independent judiciary." But it may be a misunderstanding shared by many Ontarians, who have followed news from U.S. courts over the past several years.
There, as I saw recently during my years as the Star's Washington Bureau Chief, judicial appointments (particularly to the Supreme Court) have been increasingly politicized for decades, and the process became dramatically and shamelessly more partisan under former president Donald Trump. The result is the courts that should be arbiters of U.S. society's disputes and neutral administrators of justice have come to be seen by Americans - and to sometimes openly behave - like just another venue for party political warfare. And if and when the Supreme Court that Trump refers to as "my justices" needs to weigh in on the validity of an election result or the guilt or innocence of a partisan political figure, it will be seen by all as a political agent pursuing political vendettas. That is disastrous to U.S. democracy.
Canadian courts are not entirely free of political considerations, and there have sometimes been complaints of partisan patronage in the process. But it has not been open or entrenched, and the processes of appointing judges have tried to minimize it, as they should. Certainly our courts have not seen the party trench warfare seen in the U.S.
No one should want that here. But it's not even clear that Ford understands the U.S. situation is not exactly how it should be.
My colleague Betsy Powell this week directed readers (including me) to a recent piece in The Conversation by University of Guelph politics professor Troy Riddell, who noted that judicial independence post-appointment and "professional norms" on the bench mean getting ideological purity from judges may be harder for Ford than it looks.
But that notion, even if true, is only partly reassuring. Independence by judges in the decisions made free from partisan considerations is important. But so is the perception of that independence. Faith in the fairness of the courts among the public is a pillar of our democratic system. It may often falter based on suspicions among the public that the fix is in. If we know for sure the fix is in - if the people making the appointments are admitting openly that they're rigging the courts to serve their partisan political goals - it is poisonous.
In the words of the statement from the Federation of Ontario Law Associations, the premier's "comments are irresponsible. They are harmful. And worse, they are contagious on the political right and pull apart at the threads of our democracy in a misleading and dangerous way."
So the premier has doubled down on those comments, and now tripled and quadrupled down.
Longtime observers of Ford will note that him doing so has not always been an indicator that a decision is final. Sometimes, the premier's quadrupling down is a preliminary step he takes before making a complete about-face. Some will recall how resolute he was about opening up the Greenbelt, or how he summoned his steely resolve on using the notwithstanding clause against education workers, or how he insisted pre-pandemic that municipal public health budgets were ripe for massive cutting. In those cases, digging in his heels was merely a prelude to turning on them.
What appeared to change his mind then was public uproar, and part of the danger here is that an Ontario public used to obsessing on news from the U.S. may not fully understand the line that is being crossed here taking us down the same dangerous path our neighbours have already tread.
Ford's comments on the issue show that so far, he doesn't even seem to understand the ways in which he's trampling on the integrity of the justice system. Perhaps if enough Ontarians understand it, and let him hear about it, there's hope that he'll pull another of his patented reversals, even after quadrupling down.
After all, he's an NFL football fan, and in that league, what comes after the fourth down is that you turn the ball over.
CAPTION: An Ontario public used to obsessing on news from the U.S. may not fully understand that the line Premier Doug Ford is crossing is taking us down the same dangerous path our neighbours have already tread, Edward Keenan writes.Richard Lautenstoronto star
CREDIT: edward Keenan Opinion

Document 72 of 500

Federal program has long way to go: Tight legislative schedule, skepticism from provinces mean patchwork of coverage may remain for a while

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/federal-program-has-long-way-go/docview/2933371985/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Federal+program+has+long+way+to+go%3A+Tight+legislative+schedule%2C+skepticism+from+provinces+mean+patchwork+of+coverage+may+remain+for+a+while&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA A national universal pharmacare program that would lay out government-funded coverage for prescription drugs in Canada remains a hope, not a strategy.
Even after Thursday's big reveal of a long-promised bill that said many of the right things in the eyes of the NDP, it is clear the patchwork of public drug coverage that is now in place across Canada will remain, possibly for a long time.
The Trudeau government's promise in Bill C-64 is to start slowly and offer drug coverage for birth control and diabetes. The bill first has to win passage in a minority Parliament - which is likely with NDP support - but it still faces a jammed legislative schedule and relatively few sitting days between now and the end of June.
Though it has big ambitions for a broader plan, it sets out a timeline that even Health Minister Mark Holland concedes may not see initial contraceptive and diabetes coverage start before spring 2025, months before a scheduled federal election.
Beyond that, it's anyone's guess.
The Conservatives who, polls show, may well form the next federal government, have vowed to cut costs, not build new national health spending programs, and have long argued they'd leave health decisions to the provinces. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre on Thursday offered no guarantees he'd honour any deal cut between "two incompetent politicians" - Justin Trudeau and Jagmeet Singh - and backed Quebec's right to opt out of any federal plan with full compensation.
And that's where some of the threads of the patchwork quilt, even if Holland can stitch one together, begin to fray.
Holland's plan assumes that provinces responsible for health care will agree once they see there is money on the table. In this case, he pegged the cost of contraceptive and diabetes coverage at $1.5 billion.
That hope worked when the Liberals negotiated the transfer of billions for $10-a-day daycare deals and health deals to hire more doctors and nurses, enhance mental health services, and modernize health data collection.
But on pharmacare, there is deep skepticism among big provinces.
Quebec has already said no thanks - it has its own program. Just hand over the money.
Alberta grumbles it has not been consulted and is not all that interested.
And Ontario, Canada's largest province, is distinctly cool.
Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones said the province is "waiting for details" from Ottawa on how a national pharmacare program would work.
"We have OHIP+, so we have a pretty robust system, particularly on the diabetes side with the assistive devices program," said Jones, referring to the provincial pharmacare system that provides more than 5,000 different medications to people 24 years or younger who are not covered by a private benefits plan.
"So we need to see and assess what the federal government is proposing and how it matches or conflicts with the existing system that is already here," she said in Toronto.
"I was very clear with the minister when we spoke that I'm not writing off anything, but I'm also not buying into something where I don't know exactly what's there and the assessment truly has to happen. I want to make sure that those very valuable programs can continue in Ontario."
Alberta's United Conservative government on Thursday appeared to soften its vehement opposition after it accused the Liberals earlier this week of ushering in a plan bent on overstepping federal jurisdiction to maintain their alliance with the NDP.
Alberta Health Minister Adriana LaGrange wrote on X, "We expect the federal government will invest in Alberta's pharmacare program to enhance the existing comprehensive programs we have available to Albertans."
B.C. and Newfoundland and Labrador have signalled they are interested in further talks, the minister said, adding he's "optimistic" about the rest.
Still, there are big objections from the national group that represents Canada's insurance industry which said Ottawa's plan puts "at risk the workplace benefit plans that 27 million Canadians count on."
The Canadian Life and Health Insurance Association in a written statement said the bill unveiled Thursday will only waste billions by spending money on drugs for people who already have coverage, rather than targeting benefits to "the relatively small number of Canadians who lack public or private drug coverage now."
For the Heart and Stroke Foundation, the proposed bill is a good first step, but not a comprehensive one to address the high cost of prescription drugs that leaves many Canadians splitting pills, skipping doses, or not filling or renewing much-needed prescriptions.
The group - which Holland once worked for - wants Ottawa to quickly expand the program "in a fiscally prudent way by adding to it a list of essential medicines, including prescription drugs for heart conditions and stroke, as recommended in the government's 2019 Hoskins Report on pharmacare."
Eric Hoskins, the former Ontario health minister who led that report and concluded a public, universal, single-payer system was necessary to replace Canada's patchwork of private and public plans, told the Star in an email that he wasn't concerned about Ottawa's approach potentially preserving gaps in the system.
"This is a big, and important, undertaking that will take years to fully implement," wrote Hoskins, who also advised the Liberal and the NDP during the negotiations.
With files from Robert Benzie
CREDIT: Raisa Patel and Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star With files from Robert Benzie

Document 73 of 500

He helped shape Stephen Harper's 'tough on crime' agenda. It's why he says Doug Ford is wrong to stack the courts

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/he-helped-shape-stephen-harpers-tough-on-crime/docview/2933371486/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=He+helped+shape+Stephen+Harper%27s+%27tough+on+crime%27+agenda.+It%27s+why+he+says+Doug+Ford+is+wrong+to+stack+the+courts&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's intention to appoint tough "like-minded judges" who will put even more people in jail is a "deeply disturbing" stance that fails to grasp what's really needed to keep Ontarians safe, says University of British Columbia law professor Benjamin Perrin.
And Perrin would know a thing or two about a "tough on crime" approach with a heavy focus on incarceration.
He was once the lead criminal justice advisor to former Prime Minister Stephen Harper, whose federal Conservative government dramatically increased the number of mandatory minimum sentences, among other measures that drove up the number of incarcerated people - only for many of those laws to later be struck down as unconstitutional.
"If the goal here is to increase the safety of Ontarians, this is completely the wrong way to do it and it's just political grandstanding and bluster," Perrin said in an interview this week.
He spoke to the Star as a crisis of constitutional proportions is erupting in Ontario as Ford "quadruples" down on wanting judges who think like him and rule the way he wants. Advocacy group Democracy Watch announced Thursday it would be launching a legal challenge over the appointments system. A number of major legal organizations have decried Ford's intentions as nothing short of a "substantial threat" to the independence of the courts and the public's confidence in the justice system; a move that risks tainting the integrity of future judges and calling their decisions into question.
In short, it's a threat to the rule of law.
Perrin said he was alarmed to hear that Ford is intent on "stacking the judiciary" with lawyers who believe in throwing more people in jail.
"To suggest that a series of appointments will be made to fundamentally alter the way that the judiciary makes decisions to suit a premier's particular political agenda is deeply disturbing," Perrin said.
"Imagine a premier saying, 'We're only going to appoint judges who believe in a pro-life agenda,' or only a pro-choice agenda, or only advance the interests of real estate developers."
Once himself an ardent believer that people convicted of crimes were being punished too leniently, Perrin's views on criminal justice have changed dramatically over the years as he researched the root causes of crime, as well as solutions.
"We have very clear research dating back for decades now that consistently shows that incarceration actually increases reoffending," he said. "When people are sent to prison, they tend to come out worse than they entered. Incarcerating someone is often seen as making them a better criminal."
He pointed to the lack of services and programming for people who are incarcerated, particularly in pre-trial detention in provincial jails, which does little to help rehabilitate people, most of whom will inevitably be released.
What's really needed to increase public safety, Perrin said, are widespread access to mental health and substance use disorder supports, vocational training, affordable housing and a guaranteed minimum income, which would largely be provincial responsibilities.
"The research is really, really clear that when people have that chance, the majority of people do better," he said.
As the Star first reported last Thursday, the Ford government placed two of the premier's ex-senior staffers on the arm's-length committee that vets applications and recommends candidates for the Ontario Court of Justice.
Former deputy chief of staff Matthew Bondy is now the chair of the 13-person Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee (JAAC), while ex-director of stakeholder relations Brock Vandrick has also been a public member of the committee since December.
Global News also reported Wednesday that Bondy and Vandrick are registered to lobby the Ontario government, with Bondy lobbying for a gun manufacturer, among other firms. The Tories said both had been vetted for potential conflicts, and are not lobbying the attorney general, to whom the JAAC makes recommendations on judges.
WATCH: Doug Ford defends 'patronage' appointments of ex-staffers, says he wants 'like-minded' Ontario judges
Ford has been unrepentant about putting Bondy and Vandrick on the committee and wanting tough, like-minded judges. At Queen's Park on Wednesday, he repeated a familiar talking point that far too often, judges and justices of the peace in Ontario are releasing accused violent people on bail pending their trials, who then go on to re-offend.
"I'm not gonna double down, I'm not gonna triple down, I'm gonna quadruple down" to make sure violent criminals go to jail, Ford said in response to NDP Leader Marit Stiles, who asked during question period if he would rescind the appointments of Bondy and Vandrick.
There is a lack of data nationwide when it comes to bail, something the federal government acknowledged last year when it nonetheless implemented changes to make it more difficult for people accused of certain crimes to get bail.
About 80 per cent of people incarcerated in Ontario's overcrowded jails are on remand - meaning people who have been denied bail and are awaiting trial or sentencing - a number that has steadily increased over the years.
The office of Ontario Court Chief Justice Sharon Nicklas declined to comment on Ford's stance this week, while the Association of Ontario Judges has not issued any public statements on the matter.
Attorney General Doug Downey has accused the former Liberal government of appointing a number of party donors to the bench. He went further during question period on Thursday, saying the Liberals filled two associate chief justice positions when they weren't open yet, on the same day that former premier Dalton McGuinty stepped down.
"I will take no lessons from these people on how the system should work," Downey said.
Perrin was a lifelong conservative who volunteered for the Reform Party before he could even vote, and worked in campaign war rooms and on policy development before becoming Harper's criminal justice advisor for 2012-13. He strongly supported the increase of mandatory minimum sentences and other Criminal Code changes designed to send more people to prison - "very much along the tough-on-crime agenda that both federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and Doug Ford are advancing today," he said.
"There are no fresh ideas, there are no new ideas, we're seeing a rehashing of old, discredited tough-on-crime ideas."
At the end of the day, judges are still required to follow the law, Perrin said. The Charter of Rights and Freedoms is very clear: people accused of crimes have the right to be tried by an "independent and unbiased tribunal," and they have the right not to be denied reasonable bail.
"And I am concerned with Premier Ford's statements that he's looking to appoint judges who will be more minded to incarcerate people, because that suggests he would be appointing biased judges," Perrin said.
"That undermines our constitutional rights and freedoms as Canadians."
CAPTION: "If the goal here is to increase the safety of Ontarians, this is completely the wrong way to do it and it's just political grandstanding and bluster," saysUniversity of British Columbia law professor Benjamin Perrin, a former advisor to Stephen Harper, left, about the crisis brewing over comments made by Premier Doug Ford.

Title: He helped shape Stephen Harper's 'tough on crime' agenda. It's why he says Doug Ford is wrong to stack the courts

Document 74 of 500

'A real ambassador': Prime minister's talks with U.S. and new sales tax reshaped economy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/real-ambassador/docview/2933371413/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27A+real+ambassador%27%3A+Prime+minister%27s+talks+with+U.S.+and+new+sales+tax+reshaped+economy&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Brian Mulroney, the prime minister whose trade talks with the United States and introduction of a new sales tax forever reshaped the Canadian economy, has died.
He was 84.
"On behalf of my mother and our family, it is with great sadness we announce the passing of my father, The Right Honourable Brian Mulroney, Canada's 18th prime minister," his daughter Caroline posted on social media late Thursday afternoon.
"He died peacefully, surrounded by family."
Mulroney became prime minister in 1984 when his Progressive Conservatives won 211 seats in the House of Commons - the largest margin of victory for a political party in Canadian history and a feat that has never been repeated.
A self-professed "failed crooner" who made $50 a pop for singing ballads at his hometown's paper mill, Mulroney's unrelenting ambition won him friends among some of the most powerful leaders of his generation, but also made enemies of countless Canadians who scorned what they saw as his obsession with his own image and that of his legacy.
By the time he resigned in 1993, the consensus he'd built between Western populists and Quebec nationalists had collapsed, and the goods and services tax his government introduced was so wildly unpopular that it led to the near-annihilation of the PC party in the subsequent election, setting in motion a realignment of conservatism in Canada that continues to this day.
The Airbus affair, an alleged kickback scheme connected to the purchase of new aircraft for Canada, would further tarnish his political legacy, and he would spend the decades after he left office working to polish his image, ever confident that history would erase the blemishes.
"One hundred years from now, what will be remembered was that it was done," he told reporters after clinching the Canada-U.S. free trade deal.
"The naysayers will be forgotten."
Martin Brian Mulroney was born on March 20, 1939, the third of six children. He was the first of them to be born in Baie Comeau, Que., a town set up in the 1930s to house a paper mill.
He left Baie Comeau for high school in New Brunswick, and from there went on to St. Francis Xavier University in Antigonish, N.S.
His undergraduate years laid the groundwork for his life in politics, as he became active in a Progressive Conservative campus club and started working on regional political campaigns.
At Laval University law school, his political network grew to include Lucien Bouchard, who would later commit a Shakespearean act of political sabotage by double-crossing Mulroney during negotiations over changes to the Constitution known as the Meech Lake accord and siding with Quebec separatists.
After law school, Mulroney found work as a labour lawyer in Montreal and there, at age 33, he met then-18-year old Mila Pivnicki, spying her at the Mount Royal Tennis Club one weekday afternoon.
They would marry two years later and go on to have four children - Caroline, Ben, Mark and Nicolas - and 15 grandchildren.
Caroline Mulroney is now a minister in Ontario Premier Doug Ford's government.
In a statement Thursday, Ford said he sees some of Mulroney's best qualities in his children.
"Brian was also so generous with his time. When faced with tough decisions, I often leaned on him for advice and benefited from his experience and his political instincts," Ford said.
"He was a role model to me and taught me countless lessons on how to be a better leader."
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau also reflected on the insights Mulroney shared with him over the years.
"He never stopped working for Canadians, and he always sought to make this country an even better place to call home," Trudeau said in a written statement, calling him generous, tireless and incredibly passionate.
"As we mourn his passing and keep his family and friends in our thoughts, let us also acknowledge - and celebrate - Mr. Mulroney's role in building the modern, dynamic, and prosperous country we all know today."
By the time of the 1984 general election, the Liberals had been in power for nearly all of two decades and Mulroney - elected leader of the Progressive Conservatives in 1983 - capitalized on dissatisfaction with their track record, perhaps summing it up best in the knockout punch he landed on Liberal leader John Turner during a leaders' debates.
As Turner deflected Mulroney's attacks on his patronage appointments, Mulroney jabbed a finger across the top of the lectern and his baritone voice belted out what would become a classic political quip: " You had an option, sir."
Mulroney would go on to win a landslide victory - 211 seats, including a record 81 in Quebec, where fallout from the constitutional talks of 1982 had led to great frustration with the Liberals.
With a decisive mandate, Mulroney embarked on twin talks to reshape Canada: the Meech Lake accord and a free trade deal with the U.S.
The accord was the sequel to the repatriation of the Constitution in 1982, a bill that Quebec refused to sign at the time.
The accord, however, needed to be formally ratified by all the provinces and the subsequent three years of debate saw the national consensus around its provisions collapse as the political landscape in some of the provinces shifted toward staunch opposition to the proposals.
Among the outcomes: a resurgence of the Quebec separatist movement, and the creation of a new federal separatist party the Bloc Québécois, led by Mulroney's old friend Bouchard.
Mulroney never campaigned on free trade with the U.S.
As Opposition leader in the House of Commons, he met U.S. president Ronald Reagan and the two men developed an affinity for one another, rooted, as Mulroney himself would later observe in his biography, in their shared Irish heritage and their conservative global outlook.
That they were allies was cemented in the so-called "Shamrock summit" of 1985, when the two men took to the stage to belt out the song "When Irish Eyes are Smiling."
Talks towards a new trade agreement began that year and concluded at the 11th-hour in 1987, when, in Mulroney's telling, he won a last-minute concession from the U.S. for a trade dispute mechanism.
The deal reshaped the Canadian economy, ushering in a boom in two-way trade and investment, but also marked the beginning of a major upheaval in Canada's manufacturing sector, and the globalization era that followed is often directly referenced as the birth moment for the modern era of populist politics.
Current Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, who also sought guidance from Mulroney, saw his economic legacy differently.
"These changes gave thousands of working-class families the same opportunities he had, that is, the chance to work hard, buy a home, and build their dreams," he said in a statement.
The 1988 election became the "Free Trade" election, and while the Liberals opposed the deal, Mulroney still won a second term.
Through his time in office, Mulroney attempted to rein in government spending, embarking on rounds of cuts and freezes to social spending and then ultimately did what many thought unthinkable for political survival - introduced a new tax, the Goods and Services tax.
The opposition leader at the time, Jean Chrétien, promised the bill would get quashed in the Senate, but Mulroney used an obscure constitutional provision to stack the Senate and the new tax passed.
Chrétien said Mulroney did his best to do what he thought was right for Canada, and while he was involved in many controversial files, it was better to be involved than not to be involved.
"He left a mark as prime minister, there is no doubt about that," he said Thursday.
His mark was also international.
In one of his last public appearances, Mulroney took pride in the fact that in 1991 his government was the first in the Western world to recognize Ukraine's independence from Russia, and urged the current government to stand firm against Russian tyranny.
He also led the global fight to dismantle South Africa's apartheid regime, eventually convincing even a recalcitrant British prime minister, Margaret Thatcher, to drop her opposition to imposing sanctions.
But despite his international stature, his social service cuts, the GST, the recession of the early 1990s proved too powerful a political force to keep him in office.
He resigned in 1993, and in the subsequent election, the party was destroyed in the polls - a campaign that also marked the rise of a new Conservative party, the Reform party, formed from the seeds of western discontent.
Two years after he left, a scandal exploded: Mulroney was alleged to have taken kickbacks from the sale of airplanes while he was prime minister, and the ensuing allegations would see him under scrutiny for well over 10 years, culminating in a commission of inquiry being called by the next conservative to replace him as prime minister, Stephen Harper.
That commission ultimately concluded Mulroney had acted inappropriately by taking $225,000 - but it was after he left office.
The Mulroney family did not specify the cause of death, but last year the former prime minister had been treated for prostate cancer, and underwent a heart procedure.
His post-political life saw Mulroney play an active role in the private sector, sitting on numerous corporate boards.
He also played a role in the renegotiation of the North American Free Trade deal, acting as Canada's unofficial emissary to the political elite in Washington, and once even reprising his duet with Reagan at a fundraising gala where then-U.S. president Donald Trump was in attendance.
When asked by reporters in 2017 what he was doing at a Liberal cabinet meeting, his dedication to the country shone through:
"There's no Conservative or Liberal way to negotiate a free-trade agreement," he said.
"There's only a Canadian way."
With files from
Tonda MacCharles

Full text: World Cup's taxing dilemma
Toronto to receive fraction of tax revenue for World Cup, Feb. 28
I don't understand what a city gets out of hosting the World Cup, especially when many cities are involved. I usually don't even know what city they are in when I watch those games. I would like to see something of the host cities, but generally the coverage only focuses on the pitch and the game.
The cameras don't pan around the city showing all the interesting sights, except perhaps for brief glimpses. Even during halftimes very little is shown or mentioned of the host city.
If you want to advertise a city, host bike races, marathon runs or other events that provide a great viewing cross-section of a city's architecture, parks, leafy boulevards, neighbourhoods, and so on.
Those events might not have quite the same stature as the World Cup, but they still can attract millions of international viewers and they are far less costly to host.
Ed Janicki, Victoria
The City of Toronto is willing to invest more than $60 million per soccer game ($380 million total for six games), while neglecting the pressing homelessness issue, showcasing a stark contrast between their priorities and authentic leadership.
It serves as another blatant reminder of our lack of authentic leadership and a city council that prefers the pretence of responsibility over genuine action.
Djanka Gajdel, Toronto
So Toronto taxpayers are going to be on the hook for $380 million to pay for hosting six World Cup soccer games in 2026. That works out to just over $60 million for each 90-minute game, or over $666,000 for each minute played here. Surely the city can find better ways to "kick away" $380 million.
Michael Cole, Toronto
I am in favour of Toronto hosting World Cup soccer games, but the estimated costs are ridiculously high and very confusing. I got 40 pages of information from the city that I tried to review. I am a CPA and based on the information that I got I am surprised this was ever approved.
But we are beyond that. What is important now is that the costs are controlled. There is no reference to ticket revenue.
I think that the city's auditor general should start now. For example if they hire nine people, they have to ask if they are required and whether their salaries are reasonable. City departments should be doing some of the identified work. The city has a big planning department that should eliminate some consulting costs. The capital expenditures are also totally confusing. It is not too late to fix this and do it right.
Elmer Csaszar, Toronto
'Tripling down' on accountability
Ford is 'tripling down'
on wanting 'like-minded'
judges, Feb. 27
Premier Doug Ford says he is "tripling down" on appointing "like-minded judges" to the Ontario Court of Justice. However, his recent record tells us, he is "tripping down" yet another political blunder.
Raymond Peringer, Toronto
The premier couldn't sound more foolish and immature than he has these past few days in the legislature with his juvenile responses in question period.
What's next, "double-dog daring" the opposition to meet him out back of Queen's Park?
It seems almost every group and association in the justice system and legal profession has serious concerns about his attempts to politicize the courts, yet he ignores them as he insults every judge who isn't a card-carrying Conservative by implying that only Conservatives interpret the law correctly.
If he truly feels harsher penalties need to be delivered, he can lobby to change the laws through legislation or be proactive and provide better funding for programs that divert people from a life of crime.
Every harsh sentence costs Ontario's taxpayers in incarceration costs. Putting a person in jail costs more than $110,000 a year. That kind of money could go a long way to keep many people out of the criminal justice system in the first place.
Terry Kushnier, Scarborough
I have concerns regarding the alterations to the judicial appointments process. First among them, we are continuing Ford's mimicking of the failed Republican law and order policy, together with their partisan judicial selection process.
The law-and-order approach has filled U.S. prisons, but has done nothing to improve public safety. Ford wants to appoint "like-minded judges." This must mean he wants judges in favour of jailing people and offering harsher penalties. It seems to me that the first person convicted by a newly appointed judge would have right to appeal on the grounds of not receiving a fair trial.
Clyde McDonald,
Bracebridge, Ont.
Solar eclipse risk for teachers?
Ford government says solar eclipse is no excuse for teachers to work at home, Feb. 28
Toronto's Catholic elementary teachers are pushing to work from home on April 8 to "prioritize the safety" of teachers during the solar eclipse. Does this suggest Catholic teachers are stupid enough to stare into the sun? If so, are these the people we want teaching our children?
Sandy Jeffrey, Toronto
I am shocked at the brazenly disingenuous behaviour of Toronto's Catholic elementary school teachers. They are actually pushing to work from home on April 8 due to the "dangers" posed by a solar eclipse.
Really? What's next? Refusing to work during when it is dark outside because of safety concerns?
It seems to me that this would be an ideal moment to teach, to enlighten students on astronomical and celestial phenomena, to help them better understand their world.
In this way, you can explain how dangerous it is to look at the sun; perhaps even demonstrate a safe method for the whole class to view the eclipse. Knowledge is the best protection you can provide.
It's a weak justification to work from home.
Paul Graham, Mississauga
Pharmacare
is a victory
Trudeau government
unveils national pharmacare bill, Feb. 29
Trudeau's Liberal government, aided by an alliance with the NDP, just announced a deal for a new national pharmacare program that will cover birth control and diabetes drugs. Birth control coverage includes pills, intrauterine devices, injections and emergency contraception.
Contrast this to the assault on women's reproductive rights currently going on in the U.S. What a great day for Canada!
Mary Christine Tucker,
Midland, Ont.
