Document 76 of 500

'Extraordinary statesman': Friends and former political rivals alike reflect on leader's character and legacy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/extraordinary-statesman/docview/2933368910/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Extraordinary+statesman%27%3A+Friends+and+former+political+rivals+alike+reflect+on+leader%27s+character+and+legacy&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA The death Thursday of former Progressive Conservative prime minister Brian Mulroney, a joyfully combative and proud partisan, drew profound reflections by friends, admirers and former political rivals alike.
Former prime minister Jean Chrétien told reporters on Parliament Hill Mulroney was a formidable adversary who had "a good political career, was "an important prime minister" and his family "can be proud" of him.
Chrétien said they had similar backgrounds. "We were both from rural Quebec," and Mulroney was "himself from a minority. He was an Anglo in Quebec, while I was a Franco in the rest of the land."
And throughout their many political debates, "I never had any bitter personal discussion with him," Chrétien said. "He would poke me and I would poke him."
"In politics, opposition is opposition," said Chrétien, but "Like playing hockey, you can fight on the ice and take a beer together after that."
One of Mulroney's later successors, Peter MacKay, the Progressive Conservative leader who founded the modern Conservative party with Stephen Harper, recalled Mulroney's retail political skills and acumen.
He said he got to know Mulroney and his young family when the Quebecer came to Nova Scotia to run in his father's riding after Elmer MacKay stepped down to make way for the then-newly elected PC leader who needed a seat in the Commons.
"He really invested in people and knew that that was the very essence of politics," MacKay said on CBC television.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, who sought Mulroney's advice on the renegotiation of NAFTA with the Trump administration, said he learned of his death "with great sadness."
He said Mulroney had a "role in building the modern, dynamic, and prosperous country we all know today."
Mulroney's legacy includes nailing a Canada-U.S. trade deal and expanding it to include Mexico, enacting environmental protections, standing up against apartheid in South Africa, and trying to bridge the divide between English and French Canada through constitutional negotiations, Trudeau said.
"Mr. Mulroney never stopped working for Canadians, and he always sought to make this country an even better place to call home," Trudeau said in a written statement.
Speaking later Thursday in Thunder Bay, Trudeau spoke of how he valued Mulroney's advice during Canada's renegotiation of NAFTA from 2016-2019.
"He shaped our past, but he shapes our present and he will impact our future as well," Trudeau said. "He was an extraordinary statesman. And he will be deeply, deeply missed."
Former Ontario premier David Peterson who knew Mulroney before politics as a young lawyer, and who went on to work with Mulroney to rewrite the Constitution in the great Meech Lake accord debates of the late 1980s expressed deep "sadness" in an interview.
"He was just the kindest and most generous man I've known, terribly Irish in his affections and his loves and his excesses and his generosity," said Peterson, now chair of Torstar.
Peterson said Mulroney was "fun and generous and inclusive. And he can always spot the person in the room that needed an encouraging word." That gift, he said, was one of the reasons Mulroney was such a successful politician.
Through the Meech Lake debates, Peterson said, Mulroney would often phone him on Sunday nights as the Ontario premier was trying to squeeze in family time with his then three young kids, chuckling as he recalled how "Brian loved to talk."
In the tense last days as the constitutional accord needed to be ratified, but failed to win approval in the Manitoba legislature due to Indigenous MP Elijah Harper's objection, and then, in Newfoundland, former premier Clyde Wells withdrew the accord for a final vote, Mulroney and Peterson were "both devastated," he said.
They believed "it was a mistake of historical proportions" by the Manitoba and Newfoundland politicians, and Peterson hailed Mulroney's "patient" leadership throughout. "People were under enormous pressure," he recalled. "It was in a way politics at its finest."
Mulroney's political legacy aside, Peterson said Canadians should remember that he exemplified "generous, decent, good, well motivated people in politics, that did not hate the people on the other side and worked very hard to get along and find common consensus."
Wells in an interview with the Star said although he and Mulroney did "not always see eye to eye, we had our differences, but we also had some pleasant exchanges too. I greatly respected him for what his objectives were and what I sensed was his dedication to the country, and I admired him for that. But I didn't see the objective to be achieved in quite the same way he did."
Wells said while he did not agree with Mulroney on the constitutional changes proposed, he admired his fight against apartheid and thought Mulroney's greatest legacy was in negotiating the Canada-U.S. free trade deal.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre called Mulroney "one of the greatest ever statesmen" in expressing condolences to his family, and "the love of his life, his best friend and wife, Mila."
He hailed Mulroney's working-class roots as the son of an Irish electrician who went on to become a lawyer and achieved business success as the head of Iron Ore Company.
And Poilievre celebrated his success in politics as "even greater."
Poilievre cast Mulroney as a leader on the world stage who "stood firmly on the side of western allies against communism and for freedom."
The Conservative leader said he "will always be thankful for his candid advice and generous mentorship to me personally. All Canadians are grateful for his immense sacrifice and the lasting legacy he leaves us all."
CAPTION: Clockwise from top: Brian Mulroney and wife Mila greet Pope John Paul II in Ottawa in 1984. He shares a toast with Queen Elizabeth II in 1987. He applauds South Africa's Nelson Mandela in the House of Commons in 1990. Mulroney joins U.S. president Ronald Reagan, German chancellor Helmut Kohl and U.K. prime minister Margaret Thatcher in Toronto in 1988. Brian Mulroney and Jean Chretien at an Ottawa event in 2002. Recalling the legacy of his political adversary, Chretien said Thursday, "in politics, opposition is opposition," but "like playing hockey, you can fight on the ice and take a beer together after that."File photos from The Canadian Press, Toronto Star and the Prime Minister's OfficeTOM HANSONTHE CANADIAN PRESS file photo
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star

Document 77 of 500

Closing schools for the eclipse unfair, Lecce says: Working families need more notice to arrange child care

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/closing-schools-eclipse-unfair-lecce-says/docview/2933368860/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Closing+schools+for+the+eclipse+unfair%2C+Lecce+says%3A+Working+families+need+more+notice+to+arrange+child+care&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Closing schools on the day of the solar eclipse would be "unfair" to working families and won't be tolerated, says Education Minister Stephen Lecce.
While deeming April 8 a professional activity day is fine - as long as parents are given notice - Lecce said boards shouldn't be shutting down scheduled classes if they've opted to keep it a regular learning day.
Lecce also slammed the Toronto Elementary Catholic Teachers union for pushing to have a virtual P.A. day so members can work from home for "safety" reasons, something their board has already rejected.
"I think every citizen on Earth will be showing up to work - every front-line officer, every front-line worker, from nurses to transit workers - will be going to work, and I expect the same for our educators," Lecce said at Queen's Park on Thursday when asked by reporters about schools closings.
"I've expressed a great deal of concern that the instinct of that local union is to try to keep their teachers home when every single person in the economy is going to work," Lecce said, adding he has indicated to all 72 school boards across the province "that educators will show up, work with our kids and support them as they do every day, and that extends on the day of the solar eclipse."
The minister also said he's not in favour of "the unilateral closure of schools that really sets back working parents, particularly the most vulnerable families, single parents, who don't have the luxury of a child-care space. I find it very unfair on them to close schools. When we have a (professional activity day) with notice, we can tolerate that. What I won't tolerate is indiscriminately closing schools, affecting working families and frankly, reducing the ability of a child to learn in a dynamic way about science and astronomy and exciting things that are happening."
Some boards, like the York Region District School Board, have opted to end the school day early rather than send kids home in the midst of the solar eclipse.
Liberal MPP John Fraser said he agrees there is no reason to close schools. "We've had solar eclipses before," he told reporters. "It's not the end times."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said "schools can take measures if there are any health and safety concerns and use it as a learning opportunity for students."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said she "would like to understand a little bit better what their concerns are. But you know ... it's pretty tough to be a teacher right now or any education worker in our schools."
Last weekend, Toronto Elementary Catholic Teachers (TECT) sent a memo to members saying in anticipation of the upcoming solar eclipse, the professional activity day was changed from April 19 to April 8 to better ensure student safety.
"TECT supported this change and also advocated that the April 8 P.A. day be virtual to prioritize the safety of our members," the memo said. "At this time, the board has not extended similar protective measures for our teachers."
School boards across the province have swapped April's professional activities to the day of the eclipse so that students aren't headed for home around the time skies are dark or when looking at the sky could damage their eyes.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy and Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 78 of 500

Ontario invests $1.6B in CAMH expansion: Project includes research centre, secure space for 214 in-patients

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-invests-1-6b-camh-expansion/docview/2933368778/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+invests+%241.6B+in+CAMH+expansion%3A+Project+includes+research+centre%2C+secure+space+for+214+in-patients&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mindful of the toll that mental health and addiction take on Ontario society and the economy, the province is spending $1.6 billion on new Centre for Addiction and Mental Health facilities.
"Without health, you're not going to have a healthy economy," Associate Minister for Mental Health Michael Tibollo said Thursday at the official groundbreaking for the expansion of the Queen Street West campus.
"Over 200,000 people every week are not going to work because of a mental health or addiction issue," said Tibollo. "These are significant, staggering numbers and they need to be addressed."
To that end, Premier Doug Ford said Queen's Park would spend more than $1.6 billion toward the design and construction of two massive new buildings at the Centre for Addiction and Mental Health.
That will help bankroll a "discovery centre" for research and a "secure care and recovery" building for an additional 214 in-patients with complex mental illness who are under court-ordered care and require enhanced treatment and higher security for their own safety and for that of the public.
"This expansion is a major step forward in improving mental health care here in Toronto and throughout our province," said Ford.
Dr. Aristotle Voineskos, CAMH vice-president of research and director of the Campbell Family Mental Health Research Institute, said the discovery centre "isn't just a building."
"It's a beacon of hope, a magnet that will attract the best and brightest minds from around the world to CAMH and this great province to transform mental-health care," said Voineskos.
"Hope is the most powerful thing in the world and that's what research gives us," he said.
With private donors expected to foot the rest of the tab for the two buildings, famed architect Bruce Kuwabara said the new campus is a reminder of the progress made on mental health and addiction over the past few decades.
"Twenty years ago, no one would put their name on a building for mental health," said Kuwabara, whose firm designed the curvy research centre under construction at Queen and Shaw streets.
He pointed out that when the facility was founded in the 19th century, its 27-acre site was "as far away from downtown Toronto as you could possibly be - out of sight, out of mind."
CAMH president and CEO Sarah Downey stressed that "the physical transformation of our Queen Street West site is turning what was once a walled institution into a symbol of hope for the future of mental-health care.
"With the construction of the secure care and recovery building and discovery centre, we will continue to provide the highest quality care to our most complex patients and accelerate research discoveries and developments that will improve the lives of those living with mental illness," said Downey.
"Because mental health is health."
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 79 of 500

Doug Ford's pursuit of 'like-minded judges' risks taking us down the same disastrous path as the U.S.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-pursuit-like-minded-judges-risks/docview/2933366680/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+pursuit+of+%27like-minded+judges%27+risks+taking+us+down+the+same+disastrous+path+as+the+U.S.&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On the matter of stacking the provincial judicial appointments committee with his political lapdogs in order to ensure "like-minded judges" are appointed, folks, Doug Ford has doubled down. And then he said he was "tripling down." "Quadrupling down," now, even.
Is it "the most blatant example of patronage" one professor has recently seen? Sure it is. And the premier doesn't even deny that charge. He embraces it. Others may adopt polarizing politicization, Ford was born in it, moulded by it. To him it is normal. Just listen to him talk about it: "I am going to make sure we have like-minded judges," Ford said Monday at Queen's Park.
Well, that's not how this is supposed to work, as a barrage of legal experts have told the Star. Judges are meant to be fully independent from the government of the day and from partisan political considerations. They need to be, to ensure their administration of justice isn't tainted by political pressure or partisan considerations. And, importantly, the public needs to see and believe that they aren't tainted by those things, to ensure the integrity of the court system.
WATCH: Doug Ford defends 'patronage' appointments of ex-staffers, says he wants 'like-minded' Ontario
The thing about Ford's comments on the issue - his matter-of-fact response to what appears to many of us to be a bombshell revelation - is that he doesn't seem to even understand the nature of the outrage. That this should be a politicized process, and that he wants judges who openly share his ideological leaning and party affiliation seem like the most natural things in the world to him. "We got elected to get like-minded people in appointments," he said. "I am not going to appoint some NDP or some Liberal. I have made it very clear where I stand with judges."
The Federation of Ontario Law Associations may say this attitude and those comments "reflect a juvenile understanding of the role of an independent judiciary." But it may be a misunderstanding shared by many Ontarians, who have followed news from U.S. courts over the past several years.
There, as I saw recently during my years as the Star's Washington Bureau Chief, judicial appointments (particularly to the Supreme Court) have been increasingly politicized for decades, and the process became dramatically and shamelessly more partisan under former president Donald Trump. The result is the courts that should be arbiters of U.S. society's disputes and neutral administrators of justice have come to be seen by Americans - and to sometimes openly behave - like just another venue for party political warfare. And if and when the Supreme Court that Trump refers to as "my justices" needs to weigh in on the validity of an election result or the guilt or innocence of a partisan political figure, it will be seen by all as a political agent pursuing political vendettas. That is disastrous to U.S. democracy.
Canadian courts are not entirely free of political considerations, and there have sometimes been complaints of partisan patronage in the process. But it has not been open or entrenched, and the processes of appointing judges have tried to minimize it, as they should. Certainly our courts have not seen the party trench warfare seen in the U.S.
No one should want that here. But it's not even clear that Ford understands the U.S. situation is not exactly how it should be.
My colleague Betsy Powell this week directed readers (including me) to a recent piece in The Conversation by University of Guelph politics professor Troy Riddell, who noted that judicial independence post-appointment and "professional norms" on the bench mean getting ideological purity from judges may be harder for Ford than it looks.
But that notion, even if true, is only partly reassuring. Independence by judges in the decisions made free from partisan considerations is important. But so is the perception of that independence. Faith in the fairness of the courts among the public is a pillar of our democratic system. It may often falter based on suspicions among the public that the fix is in. If we know for sure the fix is in - if the people making the appointments are admitting openly that they're rigging the courts to serve their partisan political goals - it is poisonous.
In the words of the statement from the Federation of Ontario Law Associations, the premier's "comments are irresponsible. They are harmful. And worse, they are contagious on the political right and pull apart at the threads of our democracy in a misleading and dangerous way."
So the premier has doubled down on those comments, and now tripled and quadrupled down.
Longtime observers of Ford will note that him doing so has not always been an indicator that a decision is final. Sometimes, the premier's quadrupling down is a preliminary step he takes before making a complete about-face. Some will recall how resolute he was about opening up the Greenbelt, or how he summoned his steely resolve on using the notwithstanding clause against education workers, or how he insisted pre-pandemic that municipal public health budgets were ripe for massive cutting. In those cases, digging in his heels was merely a prelude to turning on them.
What appeared to change his mind then was public uproar, and part of the danger here is that an Ontario public used to obsessing on news from the U.S. may not fully understand the line that is being crossed here taking us down the same dangerous path our neighbours have already tread.
Ford's comments on the issue show that so far, he doesn't even seem to understand the ways in which he's trampling on the integrity of the justice system. Perhaps if enough Ontarians understand it, and let him hear about it, there's hope that he'll pull another of his patented reversals, even after quadrupling down.
After all, he's an NFL football fan, and in that league, what comes after the fourth down is that you turn the ball over.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford has quadrupled down on his comments about stacking the provincial judicial appointments committee with his political lapdogs.

Subject: Patronage; Political appointments; Judicial appointments; Politics; Judges & magistrates

Location: United States--US

Title: Doug Ford's pursuit of 'like-minded judges' risks taking us down the same disastrous path as the U.S.

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Mar 1, 2024

Document 80 of 500

In photos: A look back at Brian Mulroney's life in politics

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/photos-look-back-at-brian-mulroneys-life-politics/docview/2933366637/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=In+photos%3A+A+look+back+at+Brian+Mulroney%27s+life+in+politics&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Brian Mulroney became prime minister in 1984, he did so with a whopping majority of seats in the House of Commons and a margin of victory that has yet to be matched.
And the population will be divided on whether that is even among the most significant moments of his political career. Mulroney, who died Thursday at 84, would be prime minister until 1993; his tenure is rememberd for its significant political legacy, from environmental advocacy, to relationships with international leaders such as U.S presidents Ronald Reagan and George H.W. Bush, and economic policies that were divisive at the time but shaped Canada's economy for years to come.
We look back at some of the biggest moments of his career and life.
Brian Mulroney becomes prime minister
Brian Mulroney and Mila wave from the stage on election night Sept. 4, 1984. Mulroney became prime minister when his Progressive Conservatives won 211 seats in the House of Commons - the largest margin of victory for a political party in Canadian history and a feat that has never been repeated.
Mulroney greets the Pope
Pope John Paul II on arrival in Ottawa from Vancouver on Sept. 19, 1984. He was greeted by Mulroney and wife Mila at Uplands airport.
Mulroney and the Queen
Queen Elizabeth II and Prime Minister Brian Mulroney in Quebec City, Oct. 23, 1987. Following the Queen's death in 2022, Mulroney recalled a moment when he and his wife Mila were preparing to host a small dinner for the Queen during a visit to Canada, and how the monarch requested that Mulroney's young children also be there.
"What struck me was the kindness and the interest that she lavished on the children," he said. "She treated them with the same kind of respect that she would the chancellor of the exchequer in London."
Meech Lake accord
Mulroney shakes hands with former Quebec premier Robert Bourassa in 1987. With a decisive mandate, Mulroney embarked on twin talks to reshape Canada: the Meech Lake accord and a free trade deal with the U.S. The accord was the sequel to the repatriation of the Constitution in 1982, which Quebec had refused to sign.
Among the outcomes: a resurgence of the Quebec separatist movement, and the creation of a new federal separatist party the Bloc Québécois, led by Mulroney's old friend Lucien Bouchard.
Free trade deal with the United States
Though Mulroney had not campaigned on free trade with the U.S., talks towards a new trade agreement began in 1985, and concluded at the 11th-hour in 1987, when, in Mulroney's telling, he won a last-minute concession from the U.S. for a trade dispute mechanism, and signed off on the deal being pushed through Congress.
Introduction of a national sales tax
Mulroney reviews a budget with then-finance minister Michael Wilson in 1991. Through his time in office, Mulroney attempted to rein in government spending, embarking on rounds of cuts and freezes to social spending and then ultimately did what many thought unthinkable for political survival - introduced a new tax, the Goods and Services tax or GST.
Signing of North American Free Trade Agreement
Prime Minister Brian Mulroney signs the North American Free Trade Agreement during a signing ceremony in Ottawa on Dec. 17, 1992. In 2018, Mulroney played a role in the renegotiation of the NAFTA deal, acting as Canada's unofficial emissary to the political elite in Washington.
Inducted into the Order of Canada
Mulroney (middle) stands proud with hockey legends Jean Beliveau (left) and Maurice Richard (right) after being invested into the Order of Canada at a ceremony in Ottawa in 1998.
-with files from the Star Archive and Stephanie Levitz
CAPTION: Brian Mulroney election.JPGBrian Mulroney and pope.JPGBrian Mulroney and queen.JPGBrian Mulroney MeechBrian Mulroney and ronald reagan.JPGmulroney GST.JPGmulroney signs nafta.JPGBrian Mulroney Order of CanadaFormer prime minister Brian Mulroney is shown on Parliament Hill on June 6, 2012. Mulroney died Thursday.

Subject: North American Free Trade Agreement; Free trade; Politics; Prime ministers

Document 81 of 500

Closing schools for the eclipse would be unfair to working families, Ford's education minister says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/closing-schools-eclipse-would-be-unfair-working/docview/2933614492/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Closing+schools+for+the+eclipse+would+be+unfair+to+working+families%2C+Ford%27s+education+minister+says&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Closing schools on the day of the solar eclipse would be "unfair" to working families and won't be tolerated, says Education Minister Stephen Lecce.
While deeming April 8 a professional activity day is fine - as long as parents are given notice - Lecce said boards shouldn't be shutting down scheduled classes if they've opted to keep it a regular learning day.
Lecce also slammed the Toronto Elementary Catholic Teachers union for pushing to have a virtual P.A. day so members can work from home for "safety" reasons, something their board has already rejected.
"I think every citizen on Earth will be showing up to work - every front-line officer, every front-line worker, from nurses to transit workers - will be going to work, and I expect the same for our educators," Lecce said at Queen's Park on Thursday when asked by reporters about schools closings.
"I've expressed a great deal of concern that the instinct of that local union is to try to keep their teachers home when every single person in the economy is going to work," Lecce said, adding he has indicated to all 72 school boards across the province "that educators will show up, work with our kids and support them as they do every day and that extends on the day of the solar eclipse."
The minister also said he's not in favour of "the unilateral closure of schools that really sets back working parents, particularly the most vulnerable families, single parents, who don't have the luxury of a child-care space. I find it very unfair on them to close schools. When we have a (professional activity day) with notice, we can tolerate that. What I won't tolerate is indiscriminately closing schools, affecting working families and frankly, reducing the ability of a child to learn in a dynamic way about science and astronomy and exciting things that are happening."
Some boards, like the York Region District School Board, have opted to end the school day early rather than send kids home in the midst of the solar eclipse.
Liberal MPP John Fraser said he agrees there is no reason to close schools.
"We've had solar eclipses before," he told reporters. "It's not the end times."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said "schools can take measures if there are any health and safety concerns and use it as a learning opportunity for students."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said she "would like to understand a little bit better what their concerns are. But you know ... it's pretty tough to be a teacher right now or any education worker in our schools."
Last weekend, Toronto Elementary Catholic Teachers, also known as TECT, sent a memo to members saying "in anticipation of the upcoming solar eclipse, the (professional activity) day was changed from April 19 to April 8 to better ensure student safety. TECT supported this change and also advocated that the April 8 P.A. day be virtual to prioritize the safety of our members. At this time, the board has not extended similar protective measures for our teachers."
School boards across the province have swapped April's professional activities to the day of the eclipse so that students aren't headed for home around the time skies are dark or when looking at the sky could damage their eyes.
In the Thames Valley public board in the London area, a recent public survey showed about half of respondents said they were unhappy about a scheduled P.A. day at the start of the next school year, right after Labour Day.
The survey, which was small with less than 200 taking part, found that respondents believed it caused child-care issues for parents.
The board, however, cannot declare a professional activity day before Labour Day based on collective agreements with educators - and hasn't had one for the past five years - and has said that the health and safety issues covered are essential for teachers to know before classes resume. Parents are to be notified as soon as the education ministry approves the board's 2024-25 school year calendar.
CAPTION: Ontario Education Minister Stephen Lecceslammed the Toronto Elementary Catholic Teachers union for pushing to have a virtual P.A. day so members can work from home for "safety" reasons during the solar eclipse.Elementary teachers at the Toronto Catholic have asked to work from home during the April 8 solar eclipse saying there is a safety risk going to work.

Subject: Parents & parenting; Families & family life; Teachers; Education; Learning; Elementary schools; Solar eclipses; Labor unions; School boards; School closures

Document 82 of 500

Will pharmacare become an empty dream for Trudeau's government?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/will-pharmacare-become-empty-dream-trudeaus/docview/2933613953/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+pharmacare+become+an+empty+dream+for+Trudeau%27s+government%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-A national universal pharmacare program that would lay out government-funded coverage for prescription drugs in Canada remains a hope, not a strategy.
Even after Thursday's big reveal of a long-promised bill that said many of the right things in the eyes of the NDP, it is clear the patchwork of public drug coverage that is now in place across Canada will remain, possibly for a long time.
The Trudeau government's promise in Bill C-64 is to start slowly and offer drug coverage for birth control and diabetes. The bill first has to win passage in a minority Parliament - which is likely with NDP support - but it still faces a jammed legislative schedule and relatively few sitting days between now and the end of June.
Though it has big ambitions for a broader plan, it sets out a timeline that even Health Minister Mark Holland concedes may not see initial contraceptive and diabetes coverage start before spring 2025, months before a scheduled federal election.
Beyond that, it's anyone's guess.
The Conservatives who, polls show, may well form the next federal government, have vowed to cut costs, not build new national health spending programs, and have long argued they'd leave health decisions to the provinces. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre on Thursday offered no guarantees he'd honour any deal cut between "two incompetent politicians" - Justin Trudeau and Jagmeet Singh - and backed Quebec's right to opt out of any federal plan with full compensation.
And that's where some of the threads of the patchwork quilt, even if Holland can stitch one together, begin to fray.
Holland's plan assumes that provinces responsible for health care will agree once they see there is money on the table. In this case, he pegged the cost of contraceptive and diabetes coverage at $1.5 billion.
That hope worked when the Liberals negotiated the transfer of billions for $10-a-day daycare deals and health deals to hire more doctors and nurses, enhance mental health services, and modernize health data collection.
But on pharmacare, there is deep skepticism among big provinces.
Quebec has already said no thanks - it has its own program. Just hand over the money.
Alberta grumbles it has not been consulted and is not all that interested.
And Ontario, Canada's largest province, is distinctly cool.
Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones said the province is "waiting for details" from Ottawa on how a national pharmacare program would work.
"We have OHIP+, so we have a pretty robust system, particularly on the diabetes side with the assistive devices program," said Jones, referring to the provincial pharmacare system that provides more than 5,000 different medications to people 24 years or younger who are not covered by a private benefits plan.
"So we need to see and assess what the federal government is proposing and how it matches or conflicts with the existing system that is already here," she said in Toronto.
"I was very clear with the minister when we spoke that I'm not writing off anything, but I'm also not buying into something where I don't know exactly what's there and the assessment truly has to happen. I want to make sure that those very valuable programs can continue in Ontario."
Alberta's United Conservative government on Thursday appeared to soften its vehement opposition after it accused the Liberals earlier this week of ushering in a plan bent on overstepping federal jurisdiction to maintain their alliance with the NDP.
Alberta Health Minister Adriana LaGrange wrote on X, "We expect the federal government will invest in Alberta's pharmacare program to enhance the existing comprehensive programs we have available to Albertans."
B.C. and Newfoundland and Labrador have signalled they are interested in further talks, the minister said, adding he's "optimistic" about the rest.
Still, there are big objections from the national group that represents Canada's insurance industry which said Ottawa's plan puts "at risk the workplace benefit plans that 27 million Canadians count on."
The Canadian Life and Health Insurance Association in a written statement said the bill unveiled Thursday will only waste billions by spending money on drugs for people who already have coverage, rather than targeting benefits to "the relatively small number of Canadians who lack public or private drug coverage now."
For the Heart and Stroke Foundation, the proposed bill is a good first step, but not a comprehensive one to address the high cost of prescription drugs that leaves many Canadians splitting pills, skipping doses, or not filling or renewing much-needed prescriptions.
The group - which Holland once worked for - wants Ottawa to quickly expand the program "in a fiscally prudent way by adding to it a list of essential medicines, including prescription drugs for heart conditions and stroke, as recommended in the government's 2019 Hoskins Report on pharmacare."
Eric Hoskins, the former Ontario health minister who led that report and concluded a public, universal, single-payer system was necessary to replace Canada's patchwork of private and public plans, told the Star in an email that he wasn't concerned about Ottawa's approach potentially preserving gaps in the system.
"This is a big, and important, undertaking that will take years to fully implement," wrote Hoskins, who also advised the Liberal and the NDP during the negotiations.
He said the first step is "such a powerful and impactful way to start" that he has no doubt provinces and territories will sign on.
Hoskins said the significance of Thursday's bill is that the government has enshrined its intentions in legislation, which makes it more difficult for a future government to roll it back.
"If a future government wants to roll back pharmacare, they will have to amend or repeal the legislation through votes and a full, public legislative process in Parliament. I think it will be difficult, if not impossible, to take these critically important medications away from 4 million diabetics and millions of women who both need and deserve them."
However, Patrick Fafard, a professor who teaches at the University of Ottawa's schools of medicine, and public and international affairs, said the bill really only makes it "a little bit politically harder" to turn back.
Fafard agreed the Liberals' intentions are ambitious. And real savings may be found if governments agree on - as the bill sets out - "a pan-Canadian strategy regarding the appropriate use of prescription drugs and related products." At the same time, he said, the bill stipulates only that the health minister "may, after discussions with the provinces and territories," request a report within three years on the progress to advance a national drug strategy.
"The legislation as written - 'may'- seems pretty permissive. So a future Conservative government could simply do nothing so as not to draw attention to what it's not doing or not progressing," he said.
With files from Robert Benzie, Queen's Park bureau chief

CAPTION: Federal Health Minister Mark Holland rises during Question Period in the House of Commons on Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Feb. 29, 2024.

Subject: Provinces; Diabetes; Legislation; Prescription drugs; Cost control; Insurance industry

Document 83 of 500

Ford has forced our universities into bankruptcy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-has-forced-our-universities-into-bankruptcy/docview/2933058669/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+has+forced+our+universities+into+bankruptcy&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Post-secondary chaos comes courtesy of Ford, Feb. 13
It is important to inform the people of Ontario how the Doug Ford government has forced our universities into bankruptcy. Ford has no interest in post-secondary education or education period. Since he has been in power, all education institutions have had their funds from the government reduced at a time when they should be increased to maintain our competitiveness in the real world.
Ontario had a world-class education system until Ford took power. He froze tuition and greatly reduced funding from the government.
All the students will view this tuition freeze as a good move by the government. However, it does not cost the government anything. The universities and colleges are left with no money to replace what is lost with a tuition freeze. Thus, the government gets the votes. Ford says the universities must look for efficiencies. The only option open to post-secondary institutions is to cut courses. This is what happened at Laurentian University in Sudbury after it went bankrupt. No math courses, no philosophy courses, etc.
Where does Ford think that we will get our engineers, planners, architects, scientists, lawyers, teachers, doctors, the list goes on?
Last year a panel set up by the Premier on Post Secondary Education advised Ford to stop cutting tuition and stop freezing funding.
Please continue to inform the people of Ontario how the Ford government is ruining post secondary education.
Sandra and Jim Hill, Guelph
Substance use is a health condition and should be treated as such'We can't wait a month': Belleville mayor calls out province for slow response to drug emergency, Feb. 20
Belleville needs business case for new health hub during opioid crisis, province says, Feb. 21
While the initial funding provided by the Ontario government will help start to address the toxic drug supply in Belleville, alone it is not enough.
Mayor Neil Ellis is right; his community cannot wait for funding. There were 35 overdoses within six days that resulted in two deaths. On average, seven people die from the toxic drug supply every day in Ontario. How many more will die before Premier Doug Ford does the right thing?
Substance use is a health condition and should be treated as such. Belleville has made a plea for help to keep their citizens alive. What is needed is a harm reduction strategy across the province, including funding for supervised consumption services (SCS) sites where people can consume drugs in a safe environment.
A new study published in The Lancet this month shows that in Toronto neighbourhoods where these sites were located, drug related deaths "decreased significantly."
The government must act swiftly to address this crisis and help Ontarians. The time to act is now!
Dr. Doris Grinspun, Registered Nurses' Association of Ontario (RNAO), Toronto
Look to Europe for affordable and sustainable health-care models
How much could a Canadian pharmacare plan save you? We looked in this 84-year-old's medicine cabinet, Feb. 23
I've always known that the way health care in Canada was rolled out in the 1960s was unsustainable and eventually, unaffordable. But I was shocked when I saw Dr. Malik's list of drugs. My first reaction was to be thankful that I don't have conditions that require those drugs but who knows, as I age, maybe I will eventually. If and when that happens, yes, I will benefit from the coverage that pharmacare will offer.
However, I'm also alarmed at the cost to the government and taxpayers. Many questions come to mind:
1. How many of those drugs were on the market when government health-care plans were introduced in the 1960s?
2. What was the life expectancy in Canada in the 1960s versus now?
3. The population of Canada has grown by 10,000,000 people since the inception of government health-care plans. Did we anticipate the needs of population growth?
4. How many new diagnostic tools and equipment - all of which are hugely expensive - exist today?
n Ontario, around 40 per cent of our tax dollars go to health care. Without debating what's federal and what's provincial, how can we afford more? It's clear that our government plans were introduced for the economic climate of the 1960s with no regard for population growth and advances in medicine.
The NDP and Jagmeet Singh are quick to point out that among countries with universal health care, Canada is the only one in the world without some sort of pharmacare coverage but what they omit is that Canada is also the only country with universal health care that doesn't allow public and private systems to coexist. At the mere mention of the word "private" most Canadians faint and immediately look at the state of health care in the U.S. to draw conclusions. We must stop equating private health care with the U.S. and look to Europe for affordable and sustainable models for both the government and the population.
We simply need to change the way we deliver health care.
Claude Gannon, Markham
What party is willing to take one for the team?Doug Ford's Tories are surging at the expense of Marit Stiles' New Democrats, poll suggests, Feb. 26
Recent polls show that Premier Doug Ford's PCs are treading water comfortably at 41 per cent. The remaining 59 per cent is spread amongst the three other parties, all fighting one another for a piece of the left-of-centre pie.
If two of these three parties were to take-one-for-the-team by stepping aside, we Ontarians could all win.
Jack Drury, Toronto
Poll shows voters not rewarding NDP for its good work
Doug Ford's Tories are surging at the expense of Marit Stiles' New Democrats, poll suggests, Feb. 26
So, the latest polls suggest that Doug Ford is extending his lead and the NDP are falling. Don't people remember that it was the NDP that kept the Greenbelt fiasco in the news until the Tories had to back down. So why is NDP support falling?
Gordon Robinson, Pickering
Here's the next step for fellow animal lovers
Stewart's raw grief reveals a universal truth, Feb. 28
I truly loved Vinay's heartfelt and tender column dealing with the bond shared between humans and their pets. We are a nation of animal lovers. What we forget though - because we are conditioned to do so - is that all animals are gifted with emotional intelligence. There are countless online videos of pigs - who have the same intelligence as dogs - sharing joyful interactions with their human friends. And yet, the vast majority of such animals are never allowed to express their feelings to us, or even their fellow beings as they are caged and treated as numbers, only to see the light of day in the truck on their way to the horrors of the slaughterhouse floor.
The next step for animal lovers then, is to recognize this paradox and turn away from exploiting all animals toward a compassionate plant-based system that is better for human and environmental health, and certainly better for the other species with whom we share the planet.
Cheryl Simpson, Stratford
CAPTION: Jill Dunlop, Ontario Minister of Colleges and Universities, speaks during a press conference at Queen's Park in Toronto, on Feb 26.

Ontario invests $1.6B in new buildings at CAMH

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-invests-1-6b-new-buildings-at-camh/docview/2933058592/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+invests+%241.6B+in+new+buildings+at+CAMH&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mindful of the toll that mental health and addiction take on Ontario society and the economy, the province is spending $1.6 billion on new CAMH facilities.
"Without health, you're not going to have a healthy economy," Associate Minister for Mental Health Michael Tibollo said Thursday at the official groundbreaking for the expansion of the Queen Street West campus.
"Over 200,000 people every week are not going to work because of a mental health or addiction issue," said Tibollo.
"These are significant, staggering numbers and they need to be addressed," he said.
To that end, Premier Doug Ford said Queen's Park would spend more than $1.6 billion toward the design and construction of two massive new buildings at the Centre for Addiction and Mental Health.
That will help bankroll a "discovery centre" for research and a "secure care and recovery" building for an additional 214 in-patients with complex mental illness who are under court-ordered care and require enhanced treatment and higher security for their own safety and for that of the public.
"This expansion is a major step forward in improving mental health care here in Toronto and throughout our province," said Ford.
Dr. Aristotle Voineskos, CAMH vice-president of research and director of the Campbell Family Mental Health Research Institute, said the discovery centre "isn't just a building."
"It's a beacon of hope, a magnet that will attract the best and brightest minds from around the world to CAMH and this great province to transform mental health care," said Voineskos.
"Hope is the most powerful thing in the world and that's what research gives us," he said.
With private donors are expected to foot the rest of the tab for the two buildings, famed architect Bruce Kuwabara said the new campus is a reminder of the progress made on mental health and addiction over the past few decades.
"Twenty years ago, no one would put their name on a building for mental health," said Kuwabara, whose firm designed the curvy research centre under construction at Queen and Shaw Streets.
He pointed out that when the facility was founded in the 19th century, its 27-acre site was "as far away from downtown Toronto as you could possibly be -- out of sight, out of mind."
CAMH president and CEO Sarah Downey stressed that "the physical transformation of our Queen Street West site is turning what was once a walled institution into a symbol of hope for the future of mental health care.
"With the construction of the secure care and recovery building and discovery centre, we will continue to provide the highest quality care to our most complex patients and accelerate research discoveries and developments that will improve the lives of those living with mental illness," said Downey.
"Because mental health is health."
CAPTION: Associate Minister of Mental Health and Addictions Michael Tibollo, shown in Peterborough, Ont. on May 2, 2023.

Subject: Addictions; Mental health care; Mental disorders

Title: Ontario invests $1.6B in new buildings at CAMH

Document 85 of 500

Doug Ford is wrong about wanting 'like-minded' tough-on-crime judges, says 'tough' ex-Ontario judge

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-is-wrong-about-wanting-like-minded/docview/2933058532/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+is+wrong+about+wanting+%27like-minded%27+tough-on-crime+judges%2C+says+%27tough%27+ex-Ontario+judge&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is wrong if he thinks appointing "tough" judges will solve violent crime, says a veteran Ontario jurist with a reputation for being a tough judge.
"We want judges who care about the community, give to the community; (who are) decisive, and fearless," said Norm Douglas, a former prosecutor and Ontario criminal court judge who retired in 2021 after nearly a half-century in the criminal justice system.
"You don't want 'Judge Judy' on the bench," he said, referencing the tough, moralizing style of the American TV character.
Ford has touched off a firestorm with his public comments responding to a Star exclusive on the appointment of two of his former staffers to the provincial committee responsible for selecting judges for the Ontario Court of Justice. Asked to justify what critics had called "blatant" patronage appointments, Ford explained Monday that he wants "like-minded" judges who share his belief that the "massive crime waves across our cities" are caused by judges releasing too many criminals on bail.
The committee ultimately sends a ranked list of recommendations to the attorney general, who makes the final decision on which judges to appoint.
Earlier in his career, Douglas, 77, earned the nickname "Stormin' Norman" from defence lawyers for his aggression as a prosecutor, which included serving as senior counsel and director of criminal prosecutions for Ontario. In many ways, it's a resume that made him exactly the kind of "tough-on-crime" judge Ford says he wants to appoint - but even so, Douglas doesn't buy the suggestion that there's an epidemic of violence due to judges granting bail too often.
"You can't take a broad brush and say the system is wrong because a judge or justice of the peace let this guy out on bail, and he should never have been let out, so why don't we toughen up the system," he said. "You don't know the details." (Ontario bail hearings are, with few exceptions, subject to publication bans that prohibit journalists from reporting the rationale behind any bail decision.)
In his recently published book, "You Be the Judge," Douglas set out to show the complexities involved in a judge's decision-making process, including summaries of some of his cases. Readers are invited to decide on their own before Douglas reveals his own verdict and reasoning. The book, he says, includes cases that demonstrate his reputation for toughness was undeserved.
But a stiff sentence is sometimes called for, he explains, such as when he "whacked a guy" with a 14-year prison sentence for sexually abusing his stepchildren. Nevertheless, Douglas says he was never under the illusion that such a punishment would solve "the problem of stepfathers' abusing children."
And details matter in court cases. Douglas once sentenced an 84-year-old man to six months of jail time for writing graffiti on walls in Guelph. Some might regard that as harsh - jail time for a non-violent crime is unusual for someone that old - but the man adored Hitler and wrote things such as "kill Jews" and other reprehensible comments.
Judges are also not the only ones responsible for the perceived leniency that's now become the target of politicians like Ford, Douglas added in an interview Tuesday in a Collingwood coffee shop.
His book includes a case of a man he prosecuted for sexually torturing and killing a neighbour. He was convicted and handed a life sentence, but Douglas was shocked to later learn the man was released on parole after 10 years before going on to kill a 21-year-old criminology student working in a halfway house.
"He should never have been released," Douglas said - but the decision was made by a federal parole board, and had nothing to do with how tough the judge was.
Douglas also takes exception to Ford endorsing the "politicization" of the appointment process. Such an American-style system would lead to governments of different stripes getting elected and making appointments of their own "like-minded people."
The problem with appointments based on political leanings is that it leads to the erosion of judicial independence and increases the possibility of miscarriages of justice, he said.
The central occupation of the justice system is to avoid convicting an innocent person, but that "is more likely to happen with a tough prosecutor and a tough judge than it is with anyone else," Douglas noted. Nor do we want judges who might convict in a high-profile case under pressure from political masters, he added.
"We can't be governed by what the community thinks of us. That's not why we're appointed, we're not politicians."
Nevertheless, as University of Guelph politics professor Troy Riddell wrote this week, Ford might find choosing judges who decide cases consistently in a certain direction is a difficult task. "Not only do judges have guarantees of independence but, once appointed, professional norms tend to lead judges to impartially apply the law (as best as they understand it) to the facts," wrote Riddell in an article published by The Conversation.
Riddell's article included a link to research on judicial behaviour that shows in cases where judges have latitude, such as in granting bail, "one cannot assume former prosecutors or Conservative appointees are going to be "tough on crime" as envisioned by Premier Ford."
As for the system that selects Ontario judges, Douglas, who became a judge in 1994 after being approved by the judicial advisory committee, says it works and has produced an excellent bench.
He also notes this.
This "tough Crown attorney, Stormin' Norman, when I was appointed to be a judge, you know the (Ontario) government in power when I was appointed? The New Democrats," he says with a smile.
CAPTION: Former Ontario judge Norman Douglas is seen here in a 2011 file photo.Premier Doug Ford has blamed "massive crime waves across our cities" on what he sees as too many lenient judges.

Subject: Criminal sentences; Committees; Political appointments; Court hearings & proceedings; Violent crime; Books; Decision making

Document 86 of 500

Leap Day deserves to be a stat holiday

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/leap-day-deserves-be-stat-holiday/docview/2932837029/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Leap+Day+deserves+to+be+a+stat+holiday&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=C.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There is no point in getting an extra 1,440 minutes if you are locked in routine.
Thursday is LEAP DAY. I capitalized it to make it sound more dramatic since Thursday will also be just another Regular Day. There will be no leap parties, leap concerts, leap food, leap TV specials or leap sales on Amazon.
You will just leap once again into the ho-hum.
A leap day is about as exciting as rush hour.
And this quadrennial plot twist to the Gregorian calendar is hardest on service journalism. How do you find "fun facts" on a cultural quirk that is devoid of meaning?
Ah, the Earth's axial rotation and orbital revolution can be roughly but not precisely measured in an uneven number of days per year! Ah, the Summer Olympics are held on leap years! Ah, those born on Feb. 29 areleaplings!
Even the leapling Tony Robbins can't give a motivational speech on why any of this matters in the hurly-burly of 2024.
NBC's "Today" recently tried to stoke the leaper madness with, "25 Leap Year Activities To Celebrate February's Bonus Day." It was like reading a grocery list you find in a Sobeys parking lot.
The generic suggestions included "get organized," "get outside," "make a special meal" and "play leapfrog." Yes, that seems sensible. After I'm done work on Thursday, I will ask my wife to bend over while resting her hands on her knees. Then I will press down on the small of her back and catapult the full weight of my body over hers and hopefully stick the landing without ripping out her earrings with my rickety knees.
Then we will crawl into the kitchen to "shake up a leap day cocktail" and prepare a "special meal" while wearing neck braces and reeking of Tiger Balm. Before bedtime, hopefully, we can also "make a time capsule" or "reminisce about past leap years."
Honey, remember Feb. 29, 2020? Just before the lockdown? Me neither!
Leap Day needs a drastic overhaul. Leap Day needs to be a national holiday.
The Romans didn't think this through. Screw you, Julius Caesar, or whoever came up with this astronomical hack. There is no better reason for a day off than an extra day. But right now, Leap Day is just an extra day of work.
It's like getting a free lottery ticket and discovering the grand prize is you pay Doug Ford five bucks. It makes no sense to pretend Leap Day is a bonus. Don't invite me over for dinner and ask me to cook. Don't give me an extended warranty and charge me for the floor mats.
Other countries at least try to make Leap Day seem special. As the Washington Post reported Wednesday: "In Ireland, for example, women are encouraged to propose to their partners on leap days, flipping traditional gender roles. In parts of China, children give their parents gifts. In some countries, leap days are popular days for weddings."
Imagine only having to remember your anniversary every four years.
But on Thursday, my wife won't ask me to renew our wedding vows. My daughters won't lavish me with presents. It will be just a Regular Thursday. And why must this "extra day" be shoehorned into the winter doldrums?
Why can't there be an April 31 or Oct. 32? Why can't there be one day every four years in which Kanye's new wife, Bianca Censori, does not go commando in see-through leggings while strolling European streets looking like a zombie with a Skittles fetish?
I am this close to buying that poor woman underwear from the Bay.
An "extra day" in 2024 sounds good until you realize it's a grand illusion. There is no "extra" to be found anywhere these days. Come Friday, you can't even get a plastic fork in your food delivery order. The culture is now all about the clawback. We do more for less.
But what is the one thing we all complain about? Not having enough time.
Life has never been busier. That's why Leap Day should be a statutory holiday.
We deserve to get paid to do nothing for one more day.
Give us a bit more breathing room to catch up on life. Give us a few more hours to calculate the hike to our property taxes. Give us a bonus day to stay abreast of the dystopian circus that is American politics as do-nothing Republicans turn their backs on Ukraine while parroting Russian talking points.
Give us 1,440 minutes to wonder why Mitch McConnell announced Wednesday he's stepping down when he never once stepped up for democracy. Give us a bonus day to make sense of the revolting antisemitism on display around the world in recent months, including here after a man was charged with vandalizing a statue of Al Waxman in Bellevue Square Park with a pro-Hamas message.
But as it stands, Leap Day is a total waste of time for the rest of us. That's why you read Leap Day suggestions that include, "Visit a distant relative." Yes. Hop on a transatlantic flight. Surprise your great aunt while scrambling to get back home before your lunch break ends.
Make Leap Day a national holiday. We need time to make sense of the madness.
CAPTION: The Romans didn't think this through, Vinay Menon writes. Screw you, Julius Caesar, or whoever came up with this astronomical hack. There is no better reason for a day off than an extra day. But right now, Leap Day is just an extra day of work.
CREDIT: Vinay Menon Toronto Star OPINION

Title: Leap Day deserves to be a stat holiday

Document 87 of 500

Where will MPPs go while Queen's Park gets repaired?: New chambers expected to be in place nearby following 2026 election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/where-will-mpps-go-while-queens-park-gets/docview/2932836982/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Where+will+MPPs+go+while+Queen%27s+Park+gets+repaired%3F%3A+New+chambers+expected+to+be+in+place+nearby+following+2026+election&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's the $1-billion-renovation question and only a handful of people know the answer.
Where will the Ontario government operate from once the provincial legislature is closed for restoration the year after next?
"Not for announcement today," Legislative Affairs Minister Paul Calandra told reporters Tuesday at Queen's Park.
"We've been very clear that we are moving forward with a decant of the building so that we can begin to restore and deal with the health and safety issues that are inherent across the building," said Calandra.
Affectionately known as "the pink palace," the Legislative Assembly building opened in 1893 and is showing its age.
The ancient heating and cooling systems need replacing as does the plumbing - the tap water is unsafe to drink due to lead pipes - and there is asbestos insulation and exposed wiring throughout the building.
That's why the plan is for MPPs to soon be gathering to conduct the province's business in a new temporary chamber in an as-yet-undisclosed location near the existing legislature.
Calandra, noting an all-party committee is studying the move, said the aim is to have the new locale in place after the June 2026 election.
"That is where we are going for sure," the minister said, adding the legislative assembly staff had looked into whether it was possible to remain at Queen's Park during renovations that will take years to complete.
"Because of the way the systems are connected here, you can't do one half of the building and renovate it and do another half of the building," he said. "So the only way you can accomplish this is with a full decant of the building."
MPPs on the committee examining the move confirmed the members have been touring sites, including a vacant Bay Street office building two weeks ago, but said they are sworn to secrecy due to commercial sensitivities.
All discussions about potential locations are held behind closed doors with no media present and no public transcripts.
Sources told the Star that 500,000-square-feet of space is being sought so the temporary lodgings would have the same number of offices as the existing legislature.
That is key because Premier Doug Ford, official Opposition Leader Marit Stiles, Speaker Ted Arnott, dozens of MPPs, scores of journalists in the Queen's Park press gallery as well as hundreds of political aides, librarians and legislative staffers currently work from Queen's Park offices.
The Star - like other major media outlets such as CBC, the Globe and Mail, The Canadian Press, the Toronto Sun, CityNews, Global, Radio-Canada and CTV - has a four-person bureau in the legislative building.
But Calandra warned the legislative committee last November it may not be possible to have the 5,000-square-foot legislative chambers, where MPPs debate, in the same location as offices, committee rooms, caucus rooms, the library, Hansard and translation services and cafeterias.
The minister also cautioned about soaring bills.
"In the Netherlands, the cost of renovations has doubled as a result of the war in Ukraine," Calandra, who has been looking at similar restoration projects around the world, said at the time.
Last summer, the Ministry of Legislative Affairs put out a request for proposals for an interim legislature that could be used for a minimum of six years.
Sixteen bids were submitted and that has been narrowed down to short list of about four undisclosed options.
Security - and secure parking for elected officials and staff - is a major concern. Currently, visitors to Queen's Park must go through airport-style security screening with bags X-rayed before being allowed in.
Such measures mean any shared tenancy with another organization or business has effectively been ruled out.
Another MPP said the panel is looking at the four-tower Macdonald Block complex across the street from the legislature, which is owned by the government.
That sprawling space, at the southwest corner of Bay and Wellesley streets, is in the midst of its own $1.5-billion renovation that, as the Star revealed last October, will not be completed on schedule or on budget.
It was supposed to be finished this spring, but the COVID-19 pandemic and elevator engineering headaches have kept ministers, political staff and thousands of civil servants in leased office space throughout downtown Toronto.
The Macdonald Block work, which was first announced eight years ago, began in 2019 and there is no completion date in sight.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie and Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Committees; Legislatures; Renovation & restoration; Building construction

Document 88 of 500

Here's why Doug Ford can get away with squeezing Ontario's colleges and universities

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/heres-why-doug-ford-can-get-away-with-squeezing/docview/2932836746/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here%27s+why+Doug+Ford+can+get+away+with+squeezing+Ontario%27s+colleges+and+universities&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The problem with Premier Doug Ford's pocketbook populism is that it's so infectious.
It catches on with voters. And latches on to opposition politicians.
Ford led the way by cutting and then freezing tuition for campuses across Ontario after taking power in 2018. Now, New Democrats and Liberals are parroting his tuition position - freeze for the foreseeable future - lest they be outflanked.
Ford sets the affordability agenda and his rivals flatter the premier by aping him. Students approve, parents applaud and politicians play along to harvest every vote.
In their shared political playbook, post-secondary populism can't be beat. Too bad it leaves Ontario's colleges and universities holding the bag.
You don't need a PhD in math to figure out that someone pays a price for a freeze and a free ride. The problem with pocketbook populism is that it's a shell game, not a serious strategy.
Ford understands that. Just look at his growing lead in the latest public opinion polls, where voters are rewarding him for his generosity and grandiosity.
Refund licence plate fees! Banish more road tolls! Cut gas taxes!
Few complain because - here's the magic - our roads still get built even if the taxes and tolls aren't collected. That's because the funding is found, magically, from another budgetary stash of cash - albeit at the expense of something else.
Now consider the higher education equation: Ford cuts funding by 10 per cent, then freezes it every year thereafter - all the while refusing to make up the difference.
Then his Progressive Conservative government goads colleges and universities into recruiting foreign students with extraordinarily extortionary tuition rates to bolster their bottom line. Until the resulting chaos forced the federal government to clamp down on this costly free for all, cutting off campuses from their newest addiction.
After five years of policy bankruptcy, Ford's Tories landed on the Canadian tradition of a commission. They empowered and empanelled a blue-ribbon panel, presumably to provide political cover to do what must one day - some day - be done.
Predictably, the panel urged the government to raise tuition rates amid rising inflation and/or bolster government funding to make up the difference. This week, the Ford government decided to ignore its own experts, proclaiming that tuition shall remain in the deep freeze now and for years to come.
"I will not apologize for continuing to freeze tuition," Colleges and Universities Minister Jill Dunlop proclaimed, echoing the premier's impromptu talking points earlier this month.
This is not the time, the Tories say, for a tuition increase. If not now in 2024, neither was it time in 2019, 2020, 2021, 2022 or 2023; nor will be 2025 or 2026 - you get the idea.
Which raises a reasonable question on tuition: Is there ever a good time for a politician to allow for an increase, if only to keep up with inflation?
Answer: Former NDP premier Bob Rae (later a Liberal) headed a similar tuition panel appointed by the Liberal premier of the day, Dalton McGuinty, two decades ago. It concluded that students so clearly benefit from the public investment in their education that they should also contribute their fair share - with the caveat that student aid should remove barriers to accessibility.
The last Liberal government of Kathleen Wynne built on that principle by boosting student aid to the point that those in need would effectively be spared out of pocket tuition, with more generous grants in lieu of loans. The Tories trashed that formula in favour of lower tuition for all - rich and poor - at the expense of campus operating budgets.
This week, Dunlop 's ministry had fun with figures by coughing up - and then dressing up - roughly $1 billion in one-time funding, stretched over three years, spread across so many areas that it amounts to a rounding error and a strategic error.
It allocates barely $700 million - or roughly $233 million a year - to be divvied up among colleges and universities in a post-secondary system with 47 campuses. That's a fraction of the nearly $2.5 billion funding infusion recommended by the government's own expert panel, which also called for a five per cent tuition increase that the Tories are ignoring.
This stingy strategy comes mere weeks after campuses discovered that the cash cow foisted upon them by the Ford government - high foreign student fees - can no longer be milked so murkily.
Are the premier and his Progressive Conservatives hostile to higher education? Or merely fixated on the bottom line?
In truth, their policy is neither idiocy nor ideology, merely expediency. There are no votes to be had in higher tuition for higher education, and there are no votes to be lost in starving the system of proper funding to keep it afloat (which is why the previous Liberal governments of Wynne and McGuinty also froze the base operating grants to colleges and universities year after year).
A government that dares to shortchange health care will hear about it from doctors and nurses, patients and their families; a politician who squeezes primary or secondary schools will face protests from teachers and parents.
But colleges and universities are the sacrificial lambs of Ontario politics: Underfunded, frozen and fleeced by all parties all the time.
(Disclosure: I'm a senior fellow at Toronto Metropolitan University and at the University of Toronto's Munk School of Global Affairs and Public Policy.)
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks in the Ontario legislature on Feb. 26, 2024.

Subject: Tuition; Higher education; Colleges & universities; Voters; Funding; Politics; Populism; College campuses
