Document 89 of 500

Trudeau government to unveil national pharmacare bill today

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-government-unveil-national-pharmacare/docview/2932836740/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+government+to+unveil+national+pharmacare+bill+today&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - The Liberal government's plan to kick-start a national pharmacare program is set to be unveiled today, ending months of negotiations with the NDP over whether the proposed system would meet the New Democrats' demands and maintain the parliamentary alliance between the two parties.
Health Minister Mark Holland will introduce long-awaited legislation outlining a framework for a prescription drug coverage program, as is required under the governing pact that sees the NDP prop up Justin Trudeau's minority Liberal government.
In December, after the parties failed to agree on what form such a program should take, the NDP extended its end-of-year deadline to pass the bill. The New Democrats have insisted that the program should be a publicly delivered, single-payer system, something the cash-strapped Liberal government has been wary of supporting.
That extension, which expires Friday, came with strings attached: the Liberals needed to deliver more than just a piece of legislation sketching out a vision for a universal program, the NDP said.
The extra negotiations resulted in a deal reached last week that would see diabetes drugs and some related medical equipment, along with prescription birth control, covered across the country - if provinces and territories opt in.
That buy-in got off to a rocky start when Alberta Health Minister Adriana LaGrange rejected news of the yet-to-be-released plan this week, arguing that the province had not been consulted. Quebec has also signalled it wants Ottawa to stay out of the province's own drug coverage system.
"Alberta, like every province, should probably wait and see what the legislation is and what the scheme is before they make a decision," NDP health critic Don Davies told the Star on Wednesday.
"I think it's absurd that anybody would reject out of hand a proposal that they don't understand or haven't seen yet."
NDP deputy leader Alexandre Boulerice said it will be up to provincial governments to explain to their citizens why they prematurely opted out.
"I'm really sad today and surprised they are trying to close the door," Boulerice said. "They will see what is the real offer on the table. It's going to be really hard to say no."
Holland suggested Tuesday that provinces did have a hand in shaping his government's thinking.
"As I've had conversations with my provincial and territorial counterparts, I've been taking their feedback, their concerns, their issues, sort of holding them in my mind as I'm having conversations with other parties and with the New Democrats," he told reporters.
"Certainly now, I can come back to them and say here's some things that you expressed concern about, here are some things that you wanted to see if we were to move forward on foundational pharmacare legislation, and potentially some other actions, to demonstrate that that process of listening to them was included in the negotiations."
Thursday's bill is expected to outline more details about what types of drugs and devices the federal government will cover within the two agreed-upon classes.
Sources have previously told the Star that several types of insulin, plus birth control options like pills, intrauterine devices, injections and emergency contraception, will fall under the proposed plan.
The legislation is also expected to reference recommendations urging Canada to adopt a single-payer system, which the Parliamentary Budget Officer estimated would cost federal and provincial governments $11.2 billion in 2024-25, and up to $13.4 billion in 2027-28. The PBO also found that cost savings on drug expenditures would reach $2.2 billion by 2027-28.
It remains unclear, however, just how strongly the bill will commit to implementing a single-payer model.
"The NDP has been simply unceasing in our insistence that we build a system that constructs a single-payer formulary and a formula for pharmacare," Davies said.
"And I can tell you that the legislation does that."
Eric Hoskins, a former Ontario health minister who was also involved in the discussions, told the Star it's not surprising that provinces would need time to jump aboard.
"With medicare in the late 60s, it took about five or six years before all the provinces and territories had signed on," said Hoskins, who led a pharmacare advisory council convened by the federal government. It issued a 2019 report recommending that a public, universal, single-payer system replace Canada's existing patchwork of private and public plans.
"When we wrote our report, and we looked at this issue, like how to implement it ... and also respect provincial-territorial jurisdiction and health care, we also came to a similar recommendation that it be an opt in," Hoskins said.
As for whether the potentially languid pace of provinces signing on would compromise Canada's plans for buying a large volume of drugs in order to lower costs, Hoskins said it would be worth the wait.
"We may not get the maximum benefit from bulk purchasing at the beginning," he said.
"But over time, if it means that a few years from now, at least on these two drug classes, if it takes a few years for all the provinces and territories to come on board ... that's a really short period of time compared to how long we've been waiting to get around to this."
CAPTION: Health Minister Mark Holland speaks with reporters in the foyer of the House of Commons on Feb. 28, 2024.

Full text: Ford has forced our universities into bankruptcy
Post-secondary chaos comes courtesy of Ford, Feb. 13
Doug Ford government has forced Ontario's universities into bankruptcy. He has no interest in post-secondary education or education period. Since he has been in power, all education institutions have had their funds from the government reduced at a time when they should be increased to maintain our competitiveness in the real world.
Ontario had a world-class education system until Ford took power. He froze tuition and greatly reduced funding from the government.
All the students will view this tuition freeze as a good move by the government. However, it does not cost the government anything. The universities and colleges are left with no money to replace what is lost with a tuition freeze. Thus, the government gets the votes. Ford says the universities must look for efficiencies. The only option open to post-secondary institutions is to cut courses. This is what happened at Laurentian University in Sudbury after it went bankrupt. No math courses, no philosophy courses, etc.
Where does Ford think we will get our engineers, planners, architects, scientists, lawyers, teachers, doctors, etc., from?
Sandra and Jim Hill, Guelph
Treat substance use as health condition
'We can't wait a month': Belleville mayor calls out province for slow response to drug emergency, Feb. 20
Belleville needs business case for new health hub during opioid crisis, province says, Feb. 21
While the initial funding provided by the Ontario government will help start to address the toxic drug supply in Belleville, alone it is not enough.
Mayor Neil Ellis is right; his community cannot wait for funding. There were 35 overdoses within six days that resulted in two deaths. On average, seven people die from the toxic drug supply every day in Ontario. How many more will die before Premier Doug Ford does the right thing?
Substance use is a health condition and should be treated as such. Belleville has made a plea for help to keep their citizens alive. What is needed is a harm reduction strategy across the province, including funding for supervised consumption services sites where people can consume drugs in a safe environment.
A new study published in The Lancet this month shows that in Toronto neighbourhoods where these sites were located, drug related deaths "decreased significantly."
Ontario's government must act swiftly to address this crisis and help. The time to act is now!
Dr. Doris Grinspun, Registered Nurses' Association of Ontario (RNAO), Toronto
Poll shows voters are not rewarding NDP's good work
Doug Ford's Tories are surging at the expense of Marit Stiles' New Democrats, poll suggests, Feb. 26
So, the latest polls suggest that Doug Ford is extending his lead and the NDP are falling. Don't people remember that it was the NDP that kept the Greenbelt fiasco in the news until the Tories had to back down. So why is NDP support falling?
Gordon Robinson, Pickering
Here's the next step for fellow animal lovers
Stewart's raw grief reveals a universal truth, Feb. 28
I truly loved Vinay's heartfelt and tender column dealing with the bond shared between humans and their pets. We are a nation of animal lovers. What we forget though - because we are conditioned to do so - is that all animals are gifted with emotional intelligence. There are countless online videos of pigs - who have the same intelligence as dogs - sharing joyful interactions with their human friends. And yet, the vast majority of such animals are never allowed to express their feelings to us, or even their fellow beings as they are caged and treated as numbers, only to see the light of day in the truck on their way to the horrors of the slaughterhouse floor.
The next step for animal lovers then, is to recognize this paradox and turn away from exploiting all animals toward a compassionate plant-based system that is better for human and environmental health, and certainly better for the other species with whom we share the planet.
Cheryl Simpson, Stratford, Ont.
Sustainable health-care models do exist
How much could a Canadian pharmacare plan save you? We looked in this 84-year-old's medicine cabinet, Feb. 23
I've always known that the way health care in Canada was rolled out in the 1960s was unsustainable and eventually, unaffordable. But I was shocked when I saw Dr. Malik's list of drugs.
My first reaction was to be thankful that I don't have conditions that require those drugs but who knows, as I age, maybe I will eventually. If and when that happens, yes, I will benefit from the coverage that pharmacare will offer.
However, I'm also alarmed at the cost to the government and taxpayers. Many questions come to mind:
How many of those drugs were on the market when government health-care plans were introduced in the 1960s?
What was the life expectancy in Canada in the 1960s versus now?
The population of Canada has grown by 10,000,000 people since the inception of government health-care plans. Did we anticipate the needs of population growth?
How many new diagnostic tools and equipment - all of which are hugely expensive - exist today?
n Ontario, around 40 per cent of our tax dollars go to health care. Without debating what's federal and what's provincial, how can we afford more? It's clear that our government plans were introduced for the economic climate of the 1960s with no regard for population growth and advances in medicine.
The NDP and Jagmeet Singh are quick to point out that among countries with universal health care, Canada is the only one in the world without some sort of pharmacare coverage but what they omit is that Canada is also the only country with universal health care that doesn't allow public and private systems to coexist. At the mere mention of the word "private" most Canadians faint and immediately look at the state of health care in the U.S. to draw conclusions. We must stop equating private health care with the U.S. and look to Europe for affordable and sustainable models for both the government and the population.
We simply need to change the way we deliver health care.
Claude Gannon, Markham

Document 91 of 500

Former judge slams Ford's comments: Appointing 'like-minded' officials will lead to erosion of judicial independence, says retired jurist

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/former-judge-slams-fords-comments/docview/2932835902/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+judge+slams+Ford%27s+comments%3A+Appointing+%27like-minded%27+officials+will+lead+to+erosion+of+judicial+independence%2C+says+retired+jurist&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is wrong if he thinks appointing "tough" judges will solve violent crime, says a veteran Ontario jurist with a reputation for being a tough judge.
"We want judges who care about the community, give to the community; (who are) decisive, and fearless," said Norm Douglas, a former prosecutor and Ontario criminal court judge who retired in 2021 after nearly a half-century in the criminal justice system.
"You don't want 'Judge Judy' on the bench," he said, referencing the tough, moralizing style of the American TV character.
Ford has touched off a firestorm with his public comments responding to a Star exclusive on the appointment of two of his former staffers to the provincial committee responsible for selecting judges for the Ontario Court of Justice. Asked to justify what critics had called "blatant" patronage appointments, Ford explained Monday that he wants "like-minded" judges who share his belief that the "massive crime waves across our cities" are caused by judges releasing too many criminals on bail.
The committee ultimately sends a ranked list of recommendations to the attorney general, who makes the final decision on which judges to appoint.
Earlier in his career, Douglas, 77, earned the nickname "Stormin' Norman" from defence lawyers for his aggression as a prosecutor, which included serving as senior counsel and director of criminal prosecutions for Ontario. In many ways, it's a resume that made him exactly the kind of "tough-on-crime" judge Ford says he wants to appoint - but even so, Douglas doesn't buy the suggestion that there's an epidemic of violence due to judges granting bail too often.
"You can't take a broad brush and say the system is wrong because a judge or justice of the peace let this guy out on bail, and he should never have been let out, so why don't we toughen up the system," he said. "You don't know the details." (Ontario bail hearings are, with few exceptions, subject to publication bans that prohibit journalists from reporting the rationale behind any bail decision.)
In his recently published book, "You Be the Judge," Douglas set out to show the complexities involved in a judge's decision-making process, including summaries of some of his cases. Readers are invited to decide on their own before Douglas reveals his own verdict and reasoning. The book, he says, includes cases that demonstrate his reputation for toughness was undeserved.
But a stiff sentence is sometimes called for, he explains, such as when he "whacked a guy" with a 14-year prison sentence for sexually abusing his stepchildren. Nevertheless, Douglas says he was never under the illusion that such a punishment would solve "the problem of stepfathers' abusing children."
And details matter in court cases. Douglas once sentenced an 84-year-old man to six months of jail time for writing graffiti on walls in Guelph. Some might regard that as harsh - jail time for a non-violent crime is unusual for someone that old - but the man adored Hitler and wrote things such as "kill Jews" and other reprehensible comments.
Judges are also not the only ones responsible for the perceived leniency that's now become the target of politicians like Ford, Douglas added in an interview Tuesday in a Collingwood coffee shop.
His book includes a case of a man he prosecuted for sexually torturing and killing a neighbour. He was convicted and handed a life sentence, but Douglas was shocked to later learn the man was released on parole after 10 years before going on to kill a 21-year-old criminology student working in a halfway house.
"He should never have been released," Douglas said - but the decision was made by a federal parole board, and had nothing to do with how tough the judge was.
Douglas also takes exception to Ford endorsing the "politicization" of the appointment process. Such an American-style system would lead to governments of different stripes getting elected and making appointments of their own "like-minded people."
The problem with appointments based on political leanings is that it leads to the erosion of judicial independence and increases the possibility of miscarriages of justice, he said.
The central occupation of the justice system is to avoid convicting an innocent person, but that "is more likely to happen with a tough prosecutor and a tough judge than it is with anyone else," Douglas noted. Nor do we want judges who might convict in a high-profile case under pressure from political masters, he added.
"We can't be governed by what the community thinks of us. That's not why we're appointed, we're not politicians."
Nevertheless, as University of Guelph politics professor Troy Riddell wrote this week, Ford might find choosing judges who decide cases consistently in a certain direction is a difficult task. "Not only do judges have guarantees of independence but, once appointed, professional norms tend to lead judges to impartially apply the law (as best as they understand it) to the facts," wrote Riddell in an article published by The Conversation.
Riddell's article included a link to research on judicial behaviour that shows in cases where judges have latitude, such as in granting bail, "one cannot assume former prosecutors or Conservative appointees are going to be "tough on crime" as envisioned by Premier Ford."
As for the system that selects Ontario judges, Douglas, who became a judge in 1994 after being approved by the judicial advisory committee, says it works and has produced an excellent bench.
He also notes this.
This "tough Crown attorney, Stormin' Norman, when I was appointed to be a judge, you know the (Ontario) government in power when I was appointed? The New Democrats," he says with a smile.
CREDIT: Betsy Powell Courts Reporter

Subject: Criminal sentences; Committees; Political appointments; Court hearings & proceedings; Violent crime; Decision making; Judges & magistrates

Document 92 of 500

How premier squeezes higher education

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/how-premier-squeezes-higher-education/docview/2932835873/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+premier+squeezes+higher+education&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The problem with Premier Doug Ford's pocketbook populism is that it's so infectious.
It catches on with voters. And latches on to opposition politicians.
Ford led the way by cutting and then freezing tuition for campuses across Ontario after taking power in 2018. Now, New Democrats and Liberals are parroting his tuition position - freeze for the foreseeable future - lest they be outflanked.
Ford sets the affordability agenda and his rivals flatter the premier by aping him. Students approve, parents applaud and politicians play along to harvest every vote.
In their shared political playbook, post-secondary populism can't be beat. Too bad it leaves Ontario's colleges and universities holding the bag.
You don't need a PhD in math to figure out that someone pays a price for a freeze and a free ride. The problem with pocketbook populism is that it's a shell game, not a serious strategy.
Ford understands that. Just look at his growing lead in the latest public opinion polls, where voters are rewarding him for his generosity and grandiosity.
Refund licence plate fees! Banish more road tolls! Cut gas taxes!
Few complain because - here's the magic - our roads still get built even if the taxes and tolls aren't collected. That's because the funding is found, magically, from another budgetary stash of cash - albeit at the expense of something else.
Now consider the higher education equation: Ford cuts funding by 10 per cent, then freezes it every year thereafter - all the while refusing to make up the difference.
Then his Progressive Conservative government goads colleges and universities into recruiting foreign students with extraordinarily extortionary tuition rates to bolster their bottom line. Until the resulting chaos forced the federal government to clamp down on this costly free for all, cutting off campuses from their newest addiction.
After five years of policy bankruptcy, Ford's Tories landed on the Canadian tradition of a commission. They empowered and empanelled a blue-ribbon panel, presumably to provide political cover to do what must one day - some day - be done.
Predictably, the panel urged the government to raise tuition rates amid rising inflation and/or bolster government funding to make up the difference. This week, the Ford government decided to ignore its own experts, proclaiming that tuition shall remain in the deep freeze now and for years to come.
"I will not apologize for continuing to freeze tuition," Colleges and Universities Minister Jill Dunlop proclaimed, echoing the premier's impromptu talking points this month.
This is not the time, the Tories say, for a tuition increase. If not now in 2024, neither was it time in 2019, 2020, 2021, 2022 or 2023; nor will be 2025 or 2026 - you get the idea.
Which raises a reasonable question on tuition: Is there ever a good time for a politician to allow for an increase, if only to keep up with inflation?
Answer: Former NDP premier Bob Rae (later a Liberal) headed a similar tuition panel appointed by the Liberal premier of the day, Dalton McGuinty, two decades ago. It concluded that students so clearly benefit from the public investment in their education that they should also contribute their fair share - with the caveat that student aid should remove barriers to accessibility.
The last Liberal government of Kathleen Wynne built on that principle by boosting student aid to the point that those in need would effectively be spared out of pocket tuition, with more generous grants in lieu of loans. The Tories trashed that formula in favour of lower tuition for all - rich and poor - at the expense of campus operating budgets.
This week, Dunlop's ministry had fun with figures by coughing up - and then dressing up - roughly $1 billion in one-time funding, stretched over three years, spread across so many areas that it amounts to a rounding error and a strategic error.
It allocates barely $700 million - or roughly $233 million a year - to be divvied up among colleges and universities in a post-secondary system with 47 campuses. That's a fraction of the nearly $2.5-billion funding infusion recommended by the government's own expert panel, which also called for a five per cent tuition increase that the Tories are ignoring.
This stingy strategy comes mere weeks after campuses discovered that the cash cow foisted upon them by the Ford government - high foreign student fees - can no longer be milked so murkily.
Are the premier and his Progressive Conservatives hostile to higher education? Or merely fixated on the bottom line?
In truth, their policy is neither idiocy nor ideology, merely expediency. There are no votes to be had in higher tuition for higher education, and there are no votes to be lost in starving the system of proper funding to keep it afloat (which is why the previous Liberal governments of Wynne and McGuinty also froze the base operating grants to colleges and universities year after year).
A government that dares to shortchange health care will hear about it from doctors and nurses, patients and their families; a politician who squeezes primary or secondary schools will face protests from teachers and parents.
But colleges and universities are the sacrificial lambs of Ontario politics: Underfunded, frozen and fleeced by all parties all the time.
(Disclosure: I'm a senior fellow at Toronto Metropolitan University and at the University of Toronto's Munk School of Global Affairs and Public Policy.)
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

NDP MP Daniel Blaikie calls it quits

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ndp-mp-daniel-blaikie-calls-quits/docview/2933170126/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+MP+Daniel+Blaikie+calls+it+quits&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-New Democrat MP Daniel Blaikie is set to resign at the end of March to work with Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew, the NDP announced Wednesday.
Blaikie's exit comes as the NDP has struggled to advance in the polls, despite a supply and confidence agreement with Justin Trudeau's Liberals that has bought the party legislative influence and advanced progress on key priorities such as dental care, pharmacare and legislation banning the use of replacement workers.
Now, nearly a fifth of the 25-member caucus is set to step down from federal politics, or will have their seat lost in a riding redistribution by the next election. Longtime B.C. MPs Randall Garrison and Richard Cannings announced they would not be seeking reelection last year, while Assistant Deputy Speaker and Northern Ontario MP Carol Hughes will lose her riding in the upcoming redrawing of the electoral map. It's unclear if Hughes will seek reelection elsewhere.
In a statement Wednesday afternoon, the Manitoba MP said he would be resigning from federal politics to work as a senior adviser for Kinew starting in April.
"As someone who became a father and an MP around the same time, one of the things I learned during the pandemic was how much it means to me to spend more time at home with my family while our kids are young," Blaikie said.
Though the NDP has been trying to gain ground in B.C., Alberta and Southwestern and Northern Ontario in an attempt to capitalize on what they see as rising discontent with the Liberals and fears that a Conservative government would usher in cuts to social services. But polls show the party has been unable to make significant gains with voters, stalling at around 19 per cent in federal voting intention since the 2021 election.
While the party has made advancements with disaffected Liberal voters, it has struggled to shake off its association with the Trudeau government in Western provinces, where the Conservatives are targeting key NDP ridings.
Mélanie Richer, the party's former director of communications, told the Star she's not concerned several MPs will not be running for the New Democrats in the next election, set for 2025.
"It's always easier to get re-elected for sure, but this is an opportunity for the party to get new people and new perspectives," Richer said. "Being able to point to actual results does move people in a real way."
But losing Blaikie, the party's finance critic, is a "huge loss," she said.
In a statement, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh said Blaikie championed "fairness for workers and real accountability to ensure wealthy corporations and CEOs are paying what they owe" in his time as an MP.
"I know his family will be very happy to have him closer to home as he continues to take on this important work in his new role," Singh said.
With files from Raisa Patel.
CAPTION: NDP MP Daniel Blaikie speaks during a press conference on Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Sept. 21, 2023.

Document 94 of 500

Ford 'tripling down' on judge recruitment: Critics warn premier's stance a 'threat' to judicial independence

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-tripling-down-on-judge-recruitment/docview/2932383709/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+%27tripling+down%27+on+judge+recruitment%3A+Critics+warn+premier%27s+stance+a+%27threat%27+to+judicial+independence&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is "tripling down" on his stance that he wants to appoint "like-minded judges" to the Ontario Court of Justice.
His comments have thrown the province's judicial appointment process under the spotlight, with critics warning his words represent a "substantial threat" to the independence of the courts.
"I'm tripling down now," Ford said during question period at Queen's Park Tuesday, after NDP Leader Marit Stiles said he had "doubled down" on his position in the legislature Monday.
"We're going to triple down on making sure communities are safe. We're going to triple down on getting judges that believe in throwing someone in jail when they kick the doors in, put a gun to people's heads, terrorizing their kids," the premier continued.
"I'm sick and tired of judges letting these people out on bail. We're going to hire tough judges, tough (justices of the peace.) That's what we're doing."
Ford's comments are in reaction to a Star exclusive on Thursday that his government has appointed two of his ex-senior staffers to the committee that recommends Ontario judges.
Ford's former deputy chief of staff Matthew Bondy has been the chair of the Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee (JAAC) since Feb. 1, while his former director of stakeholder relations Brock Vandrick has been a public member of the committee since December.
Here's what you need to know to understand the controversy:
Who is eligible to become a provincial court judge?
The eligibility criteria are simple and straightforward: you must be a lawyer who has been a member of the bar for at least 10 years, and who promises to follow any courses as mandated by the chief justice of the court.
Who handles lawyers' applications to become judges?
The Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee (JAAC) was created in the 1980s as an arm's-length, non-partisan body that vets applications for the bench and makes recommendations on possible judges to the Attorney General of Ontario, who appoints new judges.
Who gets to sit on the appointments committee?
The JAAC is made up of 13 members: two provincial court judges appointed by the Ontario Court chief justice, and a third judge appointed by the Ontario Judicial Council. The Attorney General appoints seven members of the public. And three legal organizations - the Law Society of Ontario, the Federation of Ontario Law Associations, and the Ontario Bar Association - each have a lawyer representative on the committee. As public members, Bondy and Vandrick were both directly appointed by Attorney General Doug Downey.
What has changed under the Ford government?
Downey made significant changes to the JAAC in 2021, sparking the ire of legal groups who said they were unnecessary and would risk opening the door to patronage appointments to the bench.
Downey now has the power to appoint almost all of the JAAC's members. He took away the ability of the three legal organizations to appoint their own representative; they must now each submit a shortlist of three candidates to Downey for his selection.
Downey also increased the number of judicial candidates that appear on the ranked shortlist submitted to him by the JAAC. Previously, the shortlist contained just two names, which the attorney general of the day could send back and request the next two. Downey increased the number to six, and is still permitted to send the list back and ask for the next six.
Advocates warned that by increasing the number of candidates on the shortlist, the provincial government would have greater access to the entire application pool, running the risk that lower-quality candidates get appointed.
On Monday, Downey's spokesperson told the Star the appointments process "is separate from the total independence judges have from government once they are appointed to their positions."
Who's upset, and why?
Legal organizations have expressed grave concerns that Ford's comments impact the independence of the courts and will harm the public's confidence in the justice system. They have also argued his comments could lead people to question the integrity of judges appointed by the Ford government going forward.
Two major legal organizations, the Advocates' Society and the Criminal Lawyers' Association, communicated those concerns directly to Ford in separate letters Monday, copying Downey.
The Federation of Ontario Law Associations, which has a representative on the JAAC, has said that Ford's comments "reflect a juvenile understanding" of the independence of the courts. The comments also suggest "that appointed judges were politically biased and take partisan positions that parallel Doug Ford's policies," the federation said.
CREDIT: Jacques Gallant Courts and Justice Reporter

Full text: Prescription costs need some serious scrutiny
How much could a Canadian pharmacare plan save you? We looked in this 84-year-old's medicine cabinet, Feb. 23
It sucks getting older when your health is good, never mind aging and requiring prescription medications.
But one has to wonder exactly what the profit margins are for Big Pharma on some of these drugs. The story has always been that high costs are necessary to cover the years of research. Understandable, but is Big Pharma maybe getting a little too out of hand knowing there is no watchdog, reaping gigantic profits?
Whether it's a drug plan (government or private) or individuals picking up the costs, there seems to be the need for some serious scrutiny. For example, one gentleman is paying $9,947 per month for 30 tablets (cancer) covered by the Ontario Drug Benefit; $608 for two injections monthly not covered by ODB so the manufacturer reduced the cost to $300, just for him.
Our taxes pay for any government drug plan, and we should be very concerned.
Gail Bennett, Toronto
CBC should co-operate rather than compete
CBC needs to change to capture a broader and younger international audience. Here's how, Feb. 25
I agree with Glenn O'Farrell about our need for the CBC as a reliable source of news and public affairs information and think his idea of co-operation with other media rather than competition makes a lot of sense. I would add that there are many Canadian films in existence that need wider viewership which CBC could provide. We can also use some TV programs on Canadian visual, film, and performing artists.
Barbara Sternberg, Toronto
More hospices would help many communities
A new tool could help predict death sooner and improve care for the dying, Feb. 26
Lucky are the communities with a local hospice facility. Hospice community volunteers could at the least have provided Katrina Prescott a few hours of respite weekly - and when her doctor deemed it time she could have seen her mother in a home-like setting, in a private room with compassionate care from an expert medical team.
Katrina could have said goodbye as a daughter, heard the stories, held the hand and felt hospice care envelope her as well as her mother. The volunteers would have provided cups of tea, meals and concern for her well-being.
The hospice where I volunteer has a Murphy bed in each room plus shower capability for families and all this at absolutely no cost.
Elizabeth Priddle, Barrie
No profit at food-court mall McDonald's
When a mall McDonald's closes, more than food disappears, Feb. 25
There is a reason why McDonald's has closed several of its mall food-court outlets: they are no longer profitable. Seniors only purchase coffee, never food like breakfast sandwiches and muffins. They often seek free refills. Then they gather in large numbers and monopolize tables for hours on end, despite prominent signs asking that patrons sit for 30 minutes. This deters other customers who wish to purchase and enjoy a meal.
I perfectly agree that seniors need to get out and have social interaction, but food-courts are not the right venue.
James Phillips, Toronto
Proposed change would worsen Avenue Road
There is no reason to fear a safer Avenue Road, Feb. 18
This article is misleading. It ignores traffic congestion for which Toronto is ranked third worst in the world. Contrary to the wishes of local residents, Toronto proposes to restrict the flow of traffic on Avenue Road (between Bloor Street and St. Clair Avenue West) by cutting traffic lanes, extending sidewalks and installing bike lanes. Driving is already a nightmare as one-by-one our arterial roadways are being decimated. These unnecessary initiatives are taking place in a time of record tax increases and supposed fiscal restraint.
The South Hill community would be disproportionately impacted. It is ridiculous to compare our winding, single-lane, north-south streets with Avenue Road. If these proposals are enacted more vehicles will seek to escape the worsening congestion on Avenue Road and as "alternative measures" have not and cannot prevent it, the traffic will end up moving into our residential neighbourhood.
Avenue Road has no unusual safety problems. If there is speeding, simply lower speed limits, install photo radar and create "higher fine" zones.
Pedestrian traffic on the sidewalks is light and the sidewalks are more than adequate.
The city should listen to local residents and not to special interest groups willing to throw South Hill "under the bus."
Stop this folly, leave Avenue Road alone, and spend our tax dollars where they are really needed.
Paul Lechtzier, South Hill District Homeowners' Association, Toronto
What qualifies
as affordable housing?
I have been looking at renting a two-bedroom apartment - most have one bathroom. I don't know what Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow and Premier Doug Ford classify as affordable, but this is what I've found. One place is $2,600 a month plus hydro plus parking with no air. Another is $2,695 plus hydro plus parking with air. The third one is $2,600 plus hydro plus parking with air. How are families able to afford these prices? Parking ranges from $65 a month outside to $100 plus for inside.
Please someone tell me what they classify as affordable.
Anna May Bourgeois, Etobicoke
Avro Arrow was an absolute miracle
I was there when the Avro Arrow landed at CFB Trenton, Feb. 24
I was also with the RCAF at that time as a pilot in western Canada. We all marvelled at the Avro Arrow and hoped one day we would fly it. Alas it didn't happen. But for history lovers and Arrow followers, there is an exact replica at Edenvale Aerodrome, in Stayner, Ont., near Barrie. And they allow public viewings of the aircraft. It is still an absolute miracle.
David Kirk, Orangeville, Ont.

Document 96 of 500

Minister rips claims Ontario was in dark: Miller points to meetings between federal, provincial governments on foreign student cap

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/minister-rips-claims-ontario-was-dark/docview/2932383528/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Minister+rips+claims+Ontario+was+in+dark%3A+Miller+points+to+meetings+between+federal%2C+provincial+governments+on+foreign+student+cap&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Claims Ontario was blindsided by a cap on international student permits are "complete garbage," federal Immigration Minister Marc Miller said Tuesday as he threatened to go as far as shutting down institutions abusing the program if provinces don't pull their weight.
The latest round of finger-pointing over management of pressures on the immigration system comes a day after Ontario's universities' minister Jill Dunlop said the cap was imposed "without consultation and without warning, and without regard to the impact it would have on students and their families."
Miller scoffed, noting rounds of meetings between federal and provincial officials, and public statements from him both flagging issues with the program and coming changes.
He also pointed to the province's own auditor general's reports about the post-secondary sector's high reliance on international students, and Ontario also knowing about the high numbers of people with student permits making claims for asylum.
Considering all that, Dunlop's claim was completely false, and rubbish, he said in French, and then in English, "complete garbage."
"It's beneath me to share text messages with journalists, but the reality is that there was communication, it just was never followed up on," he told reporters on his way into the federal government's weekly cabinet meeting.
Dunlop's office did not return a request for comment made by the Star.
The spat between Miller and Premier Doug Ford's government is the latest in rising tensions between different levels of government on the state of Canada's immigration system, not just for students but for those also coming to Canada in record numbers to seek asylum.
The cost of assisting refugee claimants is another sore spot between Parliament Hill and Queen's Park - as well as Toronto city hall - and the tension around international students follows a tense round of talks on the refugee question last month.
The financial risks facing Ontario's post-secondary sector were already well documented ahead of the imposition of the cap.
On Monday, the Ford government announced a three-year, $1-billion funding boost for the province's colleges and universities, a pledge linked to a government-commissioned report last year on the financial sustainability of the sector.
The report also repeatedly raised the issue of how much schools depend on international student tuition - which is higher than domestic students - to remain afloat, a risk also raised by auditor general Bonnie Lysyk in 2022.
Dunlop, however, repeatedly dodged questions about how the province will now also make up for the coming shortfall of revenue with the cut to international student permits.
In January, Miller had announced that overall, Ottawa is cutting student permit applications by 35 per cent, with individual caps established for each province and territory.
For Ontario, that cap could see permits slashed by 50 per cent as more international students flock to the province than anywhere else.
The reduction in permits flowed from numerous issues being flagged with the student permit program.
Among them, "diploma mills" giving people ways to get a study permit to get into Canada, but never making them attend a single class, fraud rings exploiting students and growing numbers of stories about inadequate housing and social support shortages for newcomer students, leaving some dependent on social housing and food banks to get by, have also attracted attention.
That situation was exacerbated by high inflation rates and the current housing crunch nationwide, raising political concerns Canada's open arms stance to newcomers was being undermined by a system failing to support them; data from Abacus suggests immigration is rising as an issue in the minds of voters.
While the federal government ultimately makes the call on who is allowed into Canada, it has never before capped international student permits as long as the applicant could show they'd been accepted by an approved school. Still, some 40 per cent of applications are rejected annually.
What more Ottawa could do is under consideration, Miller said Tuesday.
A new model for how schools are recognized to accept international students could help and, if it doesn't, Ottawa could step in he said - though there are jurisdictional questions to consider.
"We will be able to separate the wheat from the chaff and perhaps even if, if provinces don't assume their responsibility, shut down institutions ourselves if they don't do a good enough job," he said.
CREDIT: Stephanie Levitz Deputy Toronto Star

Document 97 of 500

Doug Ford's "Get It Done" Act worsens housing crisis with sneaky new push for sprawl

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-get-done-act-worsens-housing-crisis/docview/2932383419/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+%26quot%3BGet+It+Done%26quot%3B+Act+worsens+housing+crisis+with+sneaky+new+push+for+sprawl&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford claims his new so-called "Get it Done Act" will make it easier to build houses in Ontario, but in reality it's a disingenuous piece of work that quietly slips in changes that will make Ontario's housing crisis worse.
Hidden in the middle of this messy omnibus bill is language that will require many Ontario municipalities to allow new development on previously protected farmland, forests, river valleys and wetlands.
It's clear that Ford's government intends to continue leaning into the repeatedly debunked myth that we don't have enough land already set aside in Ontario for housing development.
The premier used this falsehood to justify his scandal-plagued plan to open up Greenbelt land for development, which he had to reverse after a major public outcry induced by his previous commitment to protect it and by the whiff of corruption.
Ontario is losing 319 acres of farmland every day to development and sprawl. Yet studies show that there is ample serviced or service-ready land within municipal boundaries across the province.
Last year, Waterloo Region planner Kevin Eby used the government's own flawed, sprawl friendly land assessment approach to show that well over 2 million homes can be built on land already set aside for development.
But by once again pushing the myth that we need to pave more nature and farmland, the government sends a strong signal to municipalities and developers that will lead both them and the rest of us in the wrong direction when it comes to housing.
Ford's new bill pushes cities, towns and builders to divert hard-to-get skilled labour and construction materials away from building affordable, diverse housing units where people want to live and work.
Instead, if the bill is passed we'll see builders encouraged to put up more large, expensive houses that are unaffordable to average Ontario families. Mini-monster homes in spread out neighbourhoods require huge public investments in sewers, power lines, roads and highways far away from where most people live and work.
The last thing Ontarians need in a housing crisis is a new law that supports building the wrong type of housing in the wrong places at the wrong prices.
One thing most Ontarians do agree on is that we need more affordable housing, for well over a million new households in the coming decade. To achieve this, we need to ensure we're getting the most bang for the buck out of our construction workers and materials.
Unfortunately, Ford's new law diverts these resources away from where they're needed most. Every worker building a large, expensive single-family oversized home in a new suburb is not building an affordable home in the right location.
If Ford actually wants to "get it done" and encourage more affordable home construction he can remove the section in his proposed legislation that requires municipalities to support wasteful construction.
Then, he can replace it with new policies proposed by housing specialists to promote sensible intensification. This would make it easy for municipalities and developers to build affordable projects within urban boundaries and get us the most number of homes in the shortest time.
This is common sense that is backed by decades of experience and hard data. Even his hand-picked Housing Affordability Task Force was heading in this direction. It's not too late for Premier Ford to amend his bill so it encourages urban affordable housing rather than paving farmland and green space.
CAPTION: "It's clear that Ford's government intends to continue leaning into the repeatedly debunked myth that we don't have enough land already set aside in Ontario for housing development," write Anne Golden and David Crombie.
CREDIT: David Crombie and Anne Golden Contributors

Subject: Affordable housing; Housing developments; Roads & highways

Document 98 of 500

Listen to local residents and leave Avenue Road alone: letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/listen-local-residents-leave-avenue-road-alone/docview/2932382112/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Listen+to+local+residents+and+leave+Avenue+Road+alone%3A+letters&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There is no reason to fear a safer Avenue Road, Feb. 18
This article is misleading. It ignores traffic congestion for which Toronto is ranked third worst in the world.Contrary to the wishes of local residents, Toronto proposes to restrict the flow of traffic on Avenue Road (between Bloor Street and St. Clair Avenue West) by cutting traffic lanes, extending sidewalks and installing bike lanes. Driving is already a nightmare as one-by-one our arterial roadways are being decimated. These unnecessary initiatives are taking place in a time of record tax increases and supposed fiscal restraint.
The South Hill community would be disproportionately impacted. It is ridiculous to compare our winding, single-lane, north-south streets with Avenue Road. If these proposals are enacted more vehicles will seek to escape the worsening congestion on Avenue Road and as "alternative measures" have not and cannot prevent it, the traffic will end up moving into our residential neighbourhood.
Avenue Road has no unusual safety problems. If there is speeding, simply lower speed limits, install photo radar and create "higher fine" zones.
Pedestrian traffic on the sidewalks is light and the sidewalks are more than adequate.
The city should listen to local residents and not to special interest groups willing to throw South Hill "under the bus." Stop this folly, leave Avenue Road alone, and spend our tax dollars where they are really needed.
Paul Lechtzier, South Hill District Homeowners' Association, Toronto
Cost of prescription medications needs serious scrutinyHow much could a Canadian pharmacare plan save you? We looked in this 84-year-old's medicine cabinet, Feb. 23
No one can deny that it sucks getting older when your health is good, never mind aging and requiring prescription medications.
But one has to wonder exactly what the profit margins are for Big Pharma on some of these drugs. The story has always been that high costs are necessary to cover the years of research. Understandable, but is Big Pharma maybe getting a little too out of hand knowing there is no watchdog, reaping gigantic profits?
Whether it's a drug plan (government or private) or individuals picking up the costs, there seems to be the need for some serious scrutiny. For example, one gentleman is paying $9,947 per month for 30 tablets (cancer) covered by the Ontario Drug Benefit; $608 for two injections monthly not covered by ODB so the manufacturer reduced the cost to $300, just for him.
Our taxes pay for any government drug plan, and we should be very concerned.
Gail Bennett, Toronto
Take solace Canadians -- the country is on the mendThink we're nothing like Americans? Some troubling findings in Canada are emerging, poll suggests, Feb.24
Given the negative attitudes expressed by Conservative voters towards politics and national institutions, as expressed in Susan Delacourt's column, one might conclude that Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre's constant cry of "everything is broken" has had the desired effect.
While no system or government is mistake-proof, maybe they wouldn't be so unhappy if they remembered that Canada procured more than enough COVID vaccines when needed, that inflation is going down, employment is up, federal, provincial and municipal governments are working together to build more housing, and transit, we now have at least the start of a national dental insurance program and a limited national drug program, and our democratic institutions are relatively intact, especially compared to many other countries.
There now, doesn't that feel better?
Carol Libman, Toronto
More community hospices would help A new tool could help predict death sooner and improve care for the dying, Feb. 26
Lucky are the communities with a local hospice facility.Hospice community volunteers could at the least have provided Katrina Prescott a few hours of respite weekly -- and when her doctor deemed it time she could have seen her mother in a home-like setting, in a private room with compassionate care from an expert medical team.
Katrina could have said goodbye as a daughter, heard the stories, held the hand and felt hospice care envelope her as well as her mother.The volunteers would have provided cups of tea, meals and concern for her well-being.
The hospice where I volunteer has a murphy bed in each room plus shower capability for families and all this at absolutely no cost.
Elizabeth Priddle, Barrie
CBC co-operation rather than competition makes senseCBC needs to change to capture a broader and younger international audience. Here's how, Feb. 25
I agree with Glenn O'Farrell about our need for the CBC as a reliable source of news and public affairs information and think his idea of cooperation with other media rather than competition makes a lot of sense. I would add that there are many Canadian films in existence that need wider viewership which CBC could provide . We can also use some TV programs on Canadian visual, film, and performing artists.
Barbara Sternberg, Toronto
Food-court mall McDonald's locations are not profitable When a mall McDonald's closes, more than food disappears, Feb. 25
There is a reason why McDonald's has closed several of its mall food-court outlets: they are no longer profitable. Seniors only purchase coffee, never food like breakfast sandwiches and muffins. They often seek free refills. Then they gather in large numbers and monopolize tables for hours on end, despite prominent signs asking that patrons sit for 30 minutes. This deters other customers who wish to purchase and enjoy a meal.
I perfectly agree that seniors need to get out and have social interaction, but food-courts are not the right venue.
James Phillips, Toronto
What classifies as affordable housing?
I have been looking at renting a two-bedroom apartment -- most have one bathroom. I don't know what Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow and Premier Doug Ford classify as affordable, but this is what I've found. One place is $2,600 a month plus hydro plus parking with no air. Another is $2,695 plus hydro plus parking with air. The third one is $2,600 plus hydro plus parking with air.How are families able to afford these prices? Parking ranges from $65 a month outside to $100 plus for inside.
Please someone tell me what they classify as affordable.
Anna May Bourgeois, Etobicoke
Avro Arrow was an absolute miracleI was there when the Avro Arrow landed at CFB Trenton, Feb. 24
I was also with the RCAF at that time as a pilot in western Canada. We all marvelled at the Avro Arrow and hoped one day we would fly it. Alas it didn't happen. But for history lovers and Arrow followers, there is an exact replica at Edenvale Aerodrome, in Stayner, Ont. near Barrie. And they allow public viewings of the aircraft. It is still an absolute miracle.
David Kirk, Orangeville
CAPTION: Avenue Road south of Chaplin Crescent in 2021.

Ford's sneaky act pushes sprawl

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-sneaky-act-pushes-sprawl/docview/2932382001/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+sneaky+act+pushes+sprawl&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

'Complete garbage': Trudeau's immigration minister dismisses Ford government claim it was in the dark about international student cap

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/complete-garbage-trudeaus-immigration-minister/docview/2932568810/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Complete+garbage%27%3A+Trudeau%27s+immigration+minister+dismisses+Ford+government+claim+it+was+in+the+dark+about+international+student+cap&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Claims Ontario was blindsided by a cap on international student permits are "complete garbage," federal Immigration Minister Marc Miller said Tuesday as he threatened to go as far as shutting down institutions abusing the program if provinces don't pull their weight.
The latest round of finger-pointing over management of pressures on the immigration system comes a day after Ontario's universities' minister Jill Dunlop said the cap was imposed "without consultation and without warning, and without regard to the impact it would have on students and their families."
Miller scoffed, noting rounds of meetings between federal and provincial officials, and public statements from him both flagging issues with the program and coming changes.
He also pointed to the province's own auditor general's reports about the post-secondary sector's high reliance on international students, and Ontario also knowing about the high numbers of people with student permits making claims for asylum.
Considering all that, Dunlop's claim was completely false, and rubbish, he said in French, and then in English, "complete garbage."
"It's beneath me to share text messages with journalists, but the reality is that there was communication, it just was never followed up on," he told reporters on his way into the federal government's weekly cabinet meeting.
Dunlop's office did not return a request for comment from the Star.
The spat between Miller and Premier Doug Ford's government is the latest in rising tensions between different levels of government on the state of Canada's immigration system, not just for students but for those also coming to Canada in record numbers to seek asylum.
The cost of assisting refugee claimants is another sore spot between Parliament Hill and Queen's Park - as well as Toronto city hall - and the tension around international students follows a tense round of talks on the refugee question last month.
The financial risks facing Ontario's post-secondary sector were already well documented ahead of the imposition of the cap. On Monday, the Ford government announced a three-year, $1-billion funding boost for the province's colleges and universities, a pledge linked to a government-commissioned report last year on the financial sustainability of the sector.
The report also repeatedly raised the issue of how much schools depend on international student tuition - which is higher than domestic students - to remain afloat, a risk also raised by auditor general Bonnie Lysyk in 2022.
Dunlop, however, repeatedly dodged questions about how the province will now also make up for the coming shortfall of revenue with the cut to international student permits.
In January, Miller had announced that overall, Ottawa is cutting student permit applications by 35 per cent, with individual caps established for each province and territory.
For Ontario, that cap could see permits slashed by 50 per cent as more international students flock to the province than anywhere else.
The reduction in permits flowed from numerous issues being flagged with the student permit program.
Among them, "diploma mills" giving people ways to get a study permit to get into Canada, but never making them attend a single class, fraud rings exploiting students, and growing numbers of stories about inadequate housing and social support shortages for newcomer students, leaving some dependent on social housing and food banks to get by, have also attracted attention.
That situation was exacerbated by high inflation rates and the current housing crunch nationwide, raising political concerns that Canada's open arms stance to newcomers was being undermined by a system failing to support them; data from Abacus suggests immigration is rising as an issue in the minds of voters.
While the federal government ultimately makes the call on who is allowed into Canada, it has never before capped international student permits as long as the applicant could show they'd been accepted by an approved school. Still, some 40 per cent of applications are rejected annually.
What more Ottawa could do is under consideration, Miller said Tuesday.
A new model for how schools are recognized to accept international students could help, and if it doesn't, Ottawa could step in he said - though there are jurisdictional questions to consider.
"We will be able to separate the wheat from the chaff and perhaps even if, if provinces don't assume their responsibility, shut down institutions ourselves if they don't do a good enough job," he said.
CAPTION: Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Minister Marc Miller arrives to a cabinet meeting on Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Feb. 27, 2024.

Subject: Foreign students; Permits; Immigration

Location: Canada

Title: 'Complete garbage': Trudeau's immigration minister dismisses Ford government claim it was in the dark about international student cap

Document 101 of 500

Where will MPPs go when Queen's Park closes for repairs?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/where-will-mpps-go-when-queens-park-closes/docview/2932568277/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Where+will+MPPs+go+when+Queen%27s+Park+closes+for+repairs%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's the $1-billion-renovation question and only a handful of people know the answer.
Where will the Ontario government operate from once the provincial legislature is closed for restoration the year after next?
"Not for announcement today," Legislative Affairs Minister Paul Calandra told reporters Tuesday at Queen's Park.
"We've been very clear that we are moving forward with a decant of the building so that we can begin to restore and deal with the health and safety issues that are inherent across the building," said Calandra.
Affectionately known as "the pink palace," the Legislative Assembly building opened in 1893 and is showing its age.
The ancient heating and cooling systems need replacing as does the plumbing - the tap water is unsafe to drink due to lead pipes - and there is asbestos insulation and exposed wiring throughout the building.
That's why the plan is for MPPs to soon be gathering to conduct the province's business in a new temporary chamber in an as-yet-undisclosed location near the existing legislature.
Calandra, noting an all-party committee is studying the move, said the aim is to have the new locale in place after the June 2026 election.
"That is where we are going for sure," the minister said, adding the legislative assembly staff had looked into whether it was possible to remain at Queen's Park during renovations that will take years to complete.
"Because of the way the systems are connected here, you can't do one half of the building and renovate it and do another half of the building," he said.
"So the only way you can accomplish this is with a full decant of the building."
MPPs on the committee examining the move confirmed the members have been touring sites, including a vacant Bay Street office building two weeks ago, but said they are sworn to secrecy due to commercial sensitivities.
All discussions about potential locations are held behind closed doors with no media present and no public transcripts.
Sources told the Star that 500,000-square-feet of space is being sought so the temporary lodgings would have the same number of offices as the existing legislature.
That is key because Premier Doug Ford, Official Opposition Leader Marit Stiles, Speaker Ted Arnott, dozens of MPPs, scores of journalists in the Queen's Park press gallery as well as hundreds of political aides, librarians and legislative staffers currently work from Queen's Park offices.
The Star - like other major media outlets such as CBC, the Globe and Mail, The Canadian Press, the Toronto Sun, CityNews, Global, Radio-Canada and CTV - has a four-person bureau in the legislative building.
But Calandra warned the legislative committee last November it may not be possible to have the 5,000-square-foot legislative chambers, where MPPs debate, in the same location as offices, committee rooms, caucus rooms, the library, Hansard and translation services and cafeterias.
The minister also cautioned about soaring bills.
"In the Netherlands, the cost of renovations has doubled as a result of the war in Ukraine," Calandra, who has been looking at similar restoration projects around the world, said at the time.
Last summer, the Ministry of Legislative Affairs put out a request for proposals for an interim legislature that could be used for a minimum of six years.
Sixteen bids were submitted and that has been narrowed down to short list of about four undisclosed options.
Security - and secure parking for elected officials and staff - is a major concern. Currently, visitors to Queen's Park must go through airport-style security screening with bags X-rayed before being allowed in.
Such measures mean any shared tenancy with another organization or business has effectively been ruled out.
Another MPP said the panel is looking at the four-tower Macdonald Block complex across the street from the legislature, which is owned by the government.
That sprawling space, at the southwest corner of Bay and Wellesley Streets is in the midst of its own $1.5-billion renovation that, as the Star revealed last October, will not be completed on schedule or on budget.
It was supposed to be finished this spring, but the COVID-19 pandemic and elevator engineering headaches have kept ministers, political staff and thousands of civil servants in leased office space throughout downtown Toronto.
The Macdonald Block work, which was first announced eight years ago, began in 2019 and there is no completion date in sight.
Challenges to updating the brutalist Macdonald Block, which only dates back to the 1960s, have officials well aware that 131-year-old legislature could be even more daunting.
Officials at Queen's Park are also mindful of complexities of the $5-billion, decade-long renovation of Centre Block on Parliament Hill that is supposed to be finished in either 2030 or 2031.
CAPTION: MPPs are shown in the Ontario Legislature on Sept. 17, 2018.

Document 102 of 500

Doug Ford wants 'like-minded' judges. Here's how to understand the controversy rocking Ontario courts

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-wants-like-minded-judges-heres-how/docview/2932167584/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+wants+%27like-minded%27+judges.+Here%27s+how+to+understand+the+controversy+rocking+Ontario+courts&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's comments this week that he wants to appoint "like-minded judges" to the Ontario Court of Justice has thrown the province's judicial appointment process under the spotlight, with critics warning his words represent a "substantial threat" to the independence of the courts.
Ford's comments were in reaction to a Star exclusive on Thursday that his government has appointed two of his ex-senior staffers to the committee that recommends Ontario judges.
Ford's former deputy chief of staff Matthew Bondy has been the chair of the Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee (JAAC) since Feb. 1, while his former director of stakeholder relations Brock Vandrick has been a public member of the committee since December.
Here's what you need to know to understand the controversy:
Who is eligible to become a provincial court judge?The eligibility criteria are simple and straightforward: you must be a lawyer who has been a member of the bar for at least 10 years, and who promises to follow any courses as mandated by the chief justice of the court.
What does the Ontario Court of Justice do?The Ontario Court of Justice handles the bulk of the province's criminal caseload, as well as a large number of family matters. It has courthouses across the province.
Who handles lawyers' applications to become judges? The Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee (JAAC) was created in the 1980s as an arm's-length, non-partisan body that vets applications for the bench and makes recommendations on possible judges to the Attorney General of Ontario.
Who gets to sit on the appointments committee? The JAAC is made up of 13 members: two provincial court judges appointed by the Ontario Court chief justice, and a third judge appointed by the Ontario Judicial Council. The Attorney General appoints seven members of the public. And three legal organizations - the Law Society of Ontario, the Federation of Ontario Law Associations, and the Ontario Bar Association - each have a lawyer representative on the committee. As public members, Bondy and Vandrick were both directly appointed by Attorney General Doug Downey.
But it's the attorney general who appoints the judges? Yes, Downey must make his selection from a ranked shortlist of candidates presented to him by the JAAC. He then takes his selections to cabinet for final approval.
What has changed under the Ford government? Downey made significant changes to the JAAC in 2021, sparking the ire of legal groups who said they were unnecessary and would risk opening the door to patronage appointments to the bench.
Downey now has the power to appoint almost all of the JAAC's members. He took away the ability of the three legal organizations to appoint their own representative; they must now each submit a shortlist of three candidates to Downey for his selection.
Downey also increased the number of judicial candidates that appear on the ranked shortlist submitted to him by the JAAC. Previously, the shortlist contained just two names, which the attorney general of the day could send back and request the next two. Downey increased the number to six, and is still permitted to send the list back and ask for the next six.
Advocates warned that by increasing the number of candidates on the shortlist, the provincial government would have greater access to the entire application pool, running the risk that lower-quality candidates get appointed.
On Monday, Downey's spokesperson told the Star the appointments process "is separate from the total independence judges have from government once they are appointed to their positions."
How is this different than what happens with federal appointments?The federal government is responsible for appointing judges to superior courts and courts of appeal across the country. Recommendations on judicial candidates to the federal justice minister are made by similar advisory committees that exist in every province and territory.
Those seven-person committees are chaired by a judge appointed by the chief justice of the province, and also include a representative of the province's law society, a representative of the province's attorney general, a representative of the Canadian Bar Association, and three representatives appointed by the federal government.
The federal Liberals have faced criticism over the perception they have favoured party donors in their appointments.
According to data reported by the National Post last year, 81 per cent of the approximately 1,300 judicial and tribunal appointments made by the federal government since 2016 had not donated to any party.
But of the remaining number who did donate to a party - 240 individuals - 76 per cent had donated to the Liberals at least once in the decade leading up to their appointment.
Why is Ford making these changes?The premier has been clear that he wants judges to keep people in jail for longer. His long-standing talking point on the justice system is that far too often, judges and justices of the peace in Ontario release accused violent criminals on bail pending their trials, who then go on to reoffend.
"Every single appointment I can, to find tough judges, tough (justices of the peace) to keep guys in jail ... I'm going to do it," Ford said on Friday.
There is a lack of data nationwide regarding bail, something the federal government acknowledged last year when it nonetheless implemented changes to make it more difficult for people accused of certain crimes to get bail.
Who's upset, and why?Legal organizations have expressed grave concerns that Ford's comments impact the independence of the courts and will harm the public's confidence in the justice system. They have also argued his comments could lead people to question the integrity of judges appointed by the Ford government going forward.
Two major legal organizations, the Advocates' Society and the Criminal Lawyers' Association, communicated those concerns directly to Ford in separate letters Monday, copying Downey.
The Federation of Ontario Law Associations, which has a representative on the JAAC, has said that Ford's comments "reflect a juvenile understanding" of the independence of the courts. The comments also suggest "that appointed judges were politically biased and take partisan positions that parallel Doug Ford's policies," the federation said.
CAPTION: Attorney General Doug Downey speaks in the house on Monday.Premier Doug Ford answers questions in Queen's Park on Monday.

Subject: Federal government; Committees; Appointments & personnel changes; Law; Attorneys general; Judicial appointments; Associations; Judges & magistrates
