Document 103 of 500

Why Doug Ford's plan for "like-minded" judges is a terrible move

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-doug-fords-plan-like-minded-judges-is/docview/2932167531/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+Doug+Ford%27s+plan+for+%26quot%3Blike-minded%26quot%3B+judges+is+a+terrible+move&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is not a subtle or nuanced man. He is a pugnacious fellow who tends to see the world as divided between good guys and bad guys, hard workers and idlers, friends and foes.
He's a man, moreover, whose tongue is often guided by his temper, by emotion.
Unhappily, all these characteristics have been on display in Ford's outburst about a troubling decision to appoint two former senior staffers to a 13-person panel that makes recommendations on appointing judges.
Matthew Bondy, Ford's former deputy chief of staff, has been chair of the Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee since Feb. 1. Brock Vandrick, former director of stakeholder relations, has been a public member of the committee since December.
No one is naïve. It is the perk and prerogative of governments to appoint people to all sorts of bodies, agencies and commissions. We understand that governments typically fill such vacancies with allies and supporters, seeking to reward friends and tilt the policy direction of such bodies.
As much as Ford built his image denouncing the so-called "gravy train," he has made use of it as enthusiastically as anyone.
So, it wasn't the fact alone of the appointments -- though denounced by critics as "blatant patronage" -- that was so unsettling. It was the naked politicizing of what must be a non-partisan model for judicial appointments and the crassness of Ford's justification for it.
"We got elected to get like-minded people in appointments," he told reporters. "I'm not going to appoint some NDP or some Liberal.
"Every single appointment I can, to find tough judges, tough (justices of the peace) to keep guys in jail. . . I'm going to do it.
"I'm appointing like-minded people that believe in what we believe in - keeping the bad guys in jail."
For those folks who thought that Ford must have obviously misspoke when he impugned the independence of the judiciary and was keen to clarify his damaging comments, well, think again. On Monday, he doubled down.
"I am going to make sure we have like-minded judges," the premier said during question period at Queen's Park.
The Federation of Ontario Law Associations said the comments reflected "a juvenile understanding of the role of an independent judiciary and erodes confidence in the justice system.
"The justice system is not a playground for politicians to bully," it said. "The rule of law is upheld when judges are placed by merit, not by fiat."
Former premier Kathleen Wynne scoffed at Ford's claim that he was elected to appointment "like-minded" people.
"That is categorically NOT what a premier, any premier, is elected to do," Wynne said in a post to X, formerly Twitter. "Intentionally tainting the judicial process is reprehensible, but sadly not surprising for this man."
Michael Spratt, an Ottawa defence lawyer, told the Star's Jacques Gallant that Ford's comments "can be charitably described as one of the most disturbing quotes I've heard about our justice system."
It's worthwhile he said, if considering what "like-minded" people to Ford might look like, to review the evidence of how Ford himself thinks.
The premier is happy to invoke the notwithstanding clause in the Charter of Rights and Freedoms to override constitutional rights when passing legislation; has under-resourced courts and contributed to the backlog of cases; and has "funneled millions of dollars to law enforcement while cutting millions of dollars from civil liberties organizations, legal aid and community legal clinics," Spratt said.
Ford's comments are the worst kind of political pandering, intended to incite an emotional response of anger and scapegoating rather than offering a grasp or analysis of complicated issues.
His remarks go at the independence of the judiciary that is a cornerstone of our justice system. That the courts operate separately from the government is essential to their fair-minded application of justice and critically, vital to our collective faith that judicial decisions are based on evidence and law, not simply the result of "like-minded" appointees doling out decisions they think will make the premier happy.
By suggesting the appointments process is just one big pork barrel, his comments do a great injustice to the integrity of the justices now on the bench and future appointees.
As the Federation of Law Associations properly said, his comments were irresponsible and harmful.
It's not for nothing, after all, that the legitimacy of the United States Supreme Court has been undercut by the partisanship and dubious quality of appointments in recent years.
Ford's comments "are contagious on the political right and pull apart at the threads of our democracy in a misleading and dangerous way," the federation said.
There is more at stake here than who gets a seat on the gravy train. With his ill-considered appointments and incendiary comments, the premier is following an appalling example and playing a disturbing kind of politics.
CAPTION: In question period Monday at Queen's Park, Premier Doug Ford doubled down on his comments that he wants "like-minded" judges appointed to the bench.

Subject: Political appointments; Law; Judicial appointments; Partisanship; Judiciary; Judges & magistrates

Title: Why Doug Ford's plan for "like-minded" judges is a terrible move

Document 104 of 500

The data machine behind your grocery list: How loyalty programs track habits, predict next purchases

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/data-machine-behind-your-grocery-list/docview/2931931270/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+data+machine+behind+your+grocery+list%3A+How+loyalty+programs+track+habits%2C+predict+next+purchases&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Diana Skakavac scrolls grocery store loyalty apps, she's always looking for her "unicorn": personalized offers for Tide laundry powder.
"My grandma is a very strict user of the Tide powder. She always has me purchase that for her," said the Toronto woman behind the @HaveCouponsWillTravel account on Instagram.
But no matter how often Skakavac picks up Tide powder, the grocers never seem to reward her for the behaviour.
"My friend and I used to joke about that. She got one (Tide offer) like two or three years ago and she held onto that thing for dear life because for some reason, they never come around."
Why Skakavac never scores Tide discounts lies in the mysterious nature of grocer loyalty programs, which pump out a plethora of personalized incentives every week designed to lure in shoppers.
Retailers tell customers their offers are determined by how they shop. That's true, but it's a bit more complicated than simply granting rewards based on past purchases.
"It's like a constant test-and-learn process," said Grant Packard, an associate professor of marketing at York University in Toronto, who has advised Loblaw Cos. Ltd., Indigo Books & Music Inc. and Cineplex Inc. on their loyalty programs.
The more people shop, the more the technology powering the apps learns - and it's not only about what you reach for at the supermarket. They learn how price sensitive you are, how likely you are to try something new or switch to a competitor brand and even whether you have moved in with a partner or family member or are expecting a baby.
"We can throw hundreds of variables into this black box and let it tell us what they're going to do next without a human having to look at something and try to figure it out," Packard said.
While technology and machine learning do much of the data gathering, humans are still involved in deciding what offers shoppers get.
Packard said companies take insights the technology has gathered and order systems to prioritize offers that will often influence three things: how often people shop, how much they buy and their willingness to try new products.
"They could lure you to the store sooner than you would have gone, saying you can only have this promotion if you come by the end of Thursday ... or they can influence your basket size, saying that you only get this (deal) if you buy this," said Ceren Kolsarici, an associate professor of marketing and analytics at Queen's University in Kingston, Ont.
Instead of the Tide offers she covets, Skakavac occasionally gets promotions for laundry detergent from No Name, Loblaw's house brand. (She figures she doesn't get Tide offers because manufacturer Procter& Gamble focuses its promotional efforts elsewhere.)
How many offers shoppers get can vary. There is "no hard and fast number" of offers Loblaw gives individuals every week because how often and at how many of the company's banners people shop at varies, said Ryan Markle, senior vice-president of loyalty at Loblaw, which runs the PC Optimum program.
At Metro Inc., which runs the Moi program in some provinces, Alain Tadros said, "I wouldn't want to specify for competitive reasons on the number of offers ... but it varies from six to 10 offers per week per banner."
However, the vice-president and chief marketing officer said Moi's goal is not to change behaviour but to retain customers.
"Rewarding you for the stuff that you buy - not that you might buy - will actually make you a more loyal customer," Tadros said.
Eventually, apps might get an even better read on customers.
"The retail industry speaks a lot about personalization, but they're not at the level of personalization that we could be," Tadros said. "In the future, it's being able to even target specials and personalized pricing."
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Though Diana Skakavac habitually buys Tide laundry detergent for herself and her grandmother, she says grocery chain loyalty programs never reward her for her consistent purchases. Instead, she says she occasionally gets promotions for detergent from a grocer's house brand.Chris YoungTHE CANADIANPRESS
CREDIT: Tara Deschamps The Canadian Press

Document 105 of 500

Doug Ford's honesty is unique but his judgment on judges is dangerous

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-honesty-is-unique-his-judgment-on/docview/2931931250/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+honesty+is+unique+but+his+judgment+on+judges+is+dangerous&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One of the things about Doug Ford that makes him different from a lot of politicians is that he generally says what he thinks - both for better and for worse.
That quality was on full display when the premier was asked last Friday whether he was OK with naming two of his former staffers to a committee that helps to select new judges, a move criticized as outright patronage.
Another politician might have fuzzed up his answer, given the importance of maintaining both the appearance and the reality of judicial independence, public confidence in the justice system, stuff like that. Not Ford.
"100 per cent I am," he said proudly. "We got elected to get like-minded people in appointments ... I'm not going to appoint some NDP or some Liberal. I've made it very clear where I stand with judges ... The justices of the peace and judges, they're letting criminals out ... I'm putting like-minded people that believe in what we believe in, keeping the bad guys in jail."
The premier's office made an attempt to deny the obvious - that the premier thinks it's legitimate for his government to arrange things so we get more judges and JPs who think like him, who won't be "letting the criminals out," who are "tough on crime" and so on.
It claimed he was just talking about people named to the selection committee, not the judges themselves. Until Monday, when Ford tossed away that fig leaf and said directly: "I am going to make sure we have like-minded judges."
So now there's no doubt where the premier stands, and it's not good. The Advocates Society, a national legal organization, calls his comments a "substantial threat to the independence of Ontario's judges." The Federation of Ontario Law Associations says they undermine "public confidence in the administration of justice in a dangerous and anti-democratic way." And they reflect a "juvenile understanding of the role of an independent judiciary."
Now, there's a school of thought that maintains this is much ado about not much, that governments from time immemorial have named judges loyal to the party in power and in tune with their thinking. It's only an issue now, goes this line, because it's Conservatives who are pushing their ideology. You can read that version in, for example, the Toronto Sun.
The trouble with this argument is that it ignores a decades-long attempt in Ontario - under Liberal, NDP and Conservative governments - to get away from that discredited old tradition of stuffing the judiciary with party hacks more noted for their partisan loyalty than their legal acumen.
A little bit of history: back in 1988 the Liberal government of David Peterson (now, full disclosure, chair of Torstar) set up a pilot project to develop a non-partisan model for appointing provincial judges.
It created the Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee (JAAC), which was eventually made permanent by Bob Rae's NDP government in 1995. That committee, expanded over the years to include representatives of legal associations and the general public, was maintained by the Conservative and Liberal governments that followed. It was universally hailed as the "gold standard" in Canada for making sure judges were named for their professional, not their political, qualifications.
Until that is, the Ford government was elected in 2018. Almost immediately it started talking about wanting more "like-minded" judges and in 2021 Attorney General Doug Downey put through changes to the way the JAAC operates to make it easier for the government to get its own choices named to the bench.
The premier's latest comments are another big step along that road. His government clearly isn't content to name judges and JPs who simply apply the law. Based on Ford's own comments it wants them to rule not just on the facts and the law but on some preconceived notion of being tough on crime.
Lawyers are right to raise the alarm, but they're not the only ones who should be concerned. Ontario has long had an independent, non-political judiciary - or at least the best version of that in Canada. The last thing we need is to taint that system with politics, patronage and outright bias.
CAPTION: "Ontario has long had an independent, non-political judiciary - or at least the best version of that in Canada. The last thing we need is to taint that system with politics, patronage and outright bias," writes Andrew Phillips.

Subject: Patronage; Politics; Judiciary

Location: Canada

$1B boost not enough, schools say: Funding falls short of what province's own expert panel recommended

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/1b-boost-not-enough-schools-say/docview/2931931229/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%241B+boost+not+enough%2C+schools+say%3A+Funding+falls+short+of+what+province%27s+own+expert+panel+recommended&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A three-year, $1-billion funding boost from the Ford government isn't enough to put the province's colleges and universities on solid financial footing as they grapple with rising costs amid an extended tuition freeze and a looming cap on international students, the schools say.
"It's a good start, but not enough," Marketa Evans, president and CEO of Colleges Ontario, said in an interview, adding that schools do appreciate "some predictability that's coming in the next three years."
At the same time, she added, more needs to be done because "the future of the high-quality programs offered to students remains at risk."
Steve Orsini, president and CEO of the Council of Ontario Universities, said "while providing short-term relief, (the funding) falls far short of what the government's own expert panel found was urgently required."
Schools - which he has repeatedly warned are "at a breaking point" - now "face an eight-year long tuition freeze without adequate multi-year base funding, which continues to undermine the sustainability of the sector," he added.
Colleges and Universities Minister Jill Dunlop made the announcement Monday at Queen's Park about the one-time $903-million fund - $203 million of it set aside for schools in the greatest financial need - as well as other monies for capital repairs, research and to conduct reviews to ensure schools are operating as efficiently as possible.
Dunlop brushed aside concerns that the nearly $1.3 billion is well below the immediate 10 per cent boost in provincial grants and a five per cent tuition fee increase recommended by an expert blue-ribbon panel she appointed last year.
"What I will not apologize for is the tuition freeze" that will be extended for the next three years for Ontario students, she told a news conference. "We are having an affordability crisis in this province and across Canada. We are not going to put this on the backs of students."
The province will, however, allow a five per cent tuition increase this fall for students from other provinces.
Dunlop defended the funding increase as the biggest "in over a decade" and maintained "this is going to ensure predictability and sustainability for institutions here in Ontario."
But Nigmendra Narain, president of the Ontario Confederation of University Faculty Associations, called it "a one-time drop in the bucket."
Dunlop evaded questions on how colleges and universities will make up the looming loss of revenue from international students and other funding gaps when many are running deficits and facing other financial challenges, but pointed to $15 million within the new funding to help schools "find efficiencies."
Opposition critics said they support the tuition freeze because of the financial crush facing students, but accused Dunlop of leaving universities and colleges in a precarious position by not providing more cash.
"It is clear that this Conservative government does not in any way understand the severity of the crisis," said New Democrat MPP Peggy Sattler (London West), noting some schools are "on the brink" and facing the prospect of cutting low enrolment programs, library hours, student success advisers and other supports.
"Ten of our 23 universities are currently in deficit," she added, citing a warning from Queen's University that it may have to make cuts in the faculties of arts, social sciences and humanities.
Liberal MPP Adil Shamji (Don Valley East) said he is not surprised Dunlop's actions fell short of the blue ribbon panel's advice.
"They put out these panels and try and stack them with their friends, and then hope that some recommendations come out that align with their recommendations," Shamji told reporters. "When they don't, they ignore them."
Ontario's reputation for quality post-secondary education is "under threat" because of the shortfall in funding, said Green Leader Mike Schreiner.
"The Ford government needs to go back to university or college to study math," Schreiner said.
The funding, first reported by the Star, also came with news that the province is introducing legislation that would require schools to publish all fees and even textbook prices so students have a clear idea of their costs.
A year after Doug Ford was first elected premier, he slashed tuition by 10 per cent and has frozen tuition fees since.
That, combined with stagnant funding, inflation, unexpected COVID-19 costs as well as salary increases due to now-unconstitutional provincial legislation, has left almost half of the province's universities running deficits and warning of cuts to stay afloat.
On top of that, the schools are now facing the loss of some lucrative international student tuition revenue as the federal government cuts their numbers for this fall - a move that will disproportionately hit Ontario, given the explosion in foreign students studying here in recent years - blaming a housing shortage and questions about the quality of some of the programs run by public and private college partnerships.
Last year, Dunlop asked a panel of experts to look at the sustainability of the post-secondary sector.
In total, its recommendations came with an estimated price tag of $2.5 billion over three years for the province's 24 publicly assisted colleges and 23 universities.
The province says it is still considering the recommendations of the blue ribbon panel.
Monday's announcements includes $10 million for northern and small universities and colleges, $15 million for third-party efficiency reviews, $65.4 million for research, $100 million for STEM (science/technology/engineering/math) student spots, and $167.4 million for capital repairs.
Some $23 million has been set aside for student mental health supports.
The government will also allow colleges to offer applied masters degrees in areas where there is demand, such as manufacturing, artificial intelligence and animation.
CAPTION: Colleges and Universities Minister Jill Dunlop brushed aside concerns that the fund announced Monday is below the 10 per cent boost in grants recommended by a blue-ribbon panel she appointed last year.Arlyn McAdoreyTHE CANADIAN PRESS
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy and Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Tuition; Foreign students; Corporate presidents; Colleges & universities; Funding; Sustainability

Business indexing term: Subject: Corporate presidents Colleges & universities

Document 107 of 500

Dangers of a 'like-minded' judiciary

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/dangers-like-minded-judiciary/docview/2931930992/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dangers+of+a+%27like-minded%27+judiciary&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is not a subtle or nuanced man. He is a pugnacious fellow who tends to see the world as divided between good guys and bad guys, hard workers and idlers, friends and foes.
He's a man, moreover, whose tongue is often guided by his temper, by emotion. Unhappily, all these characteristics have been on display in Ford's outburst about a troubling decision to appoint two former senior staffers to a 13-person panel that makes recommendations on appointing judges.
Matthew Bondy, Ford's former deputy chief of staff, has been chair of the Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee since Feb. 1. Brock Vandrick, former director of stakeholder relations, has been a public member of the committee since December.
No one is naïve. It is the perk and prerogative of governments to appoint people to all sorts of bodies, agencies and commissions. We understand that governments typically fill such vacancies with allies and supporters, seeking to reward friends and tilt the policy direction of such bodies.
As much as Ford built his image denouncing the so-called "gravy train," he has made use of it as enthusiastically as anyone.
So, it wasn't the fact alone of the appointments - though denounced by critics as "blatant patronage" - that was so unsettling. It was the naked politicizing of what must be a non-partisan model for judicial appointments and the crassness of Ford's justification for it. "We got elected to get like-minded people in appointments," he said. "I'm not going to appoint some NDP or some Liberal.
"Every single appointment I can, to find tough judges, tough (justices of the peace) to keep guys in jail ... I'm going to do it.
"I'm appointing like-minded people that believe in what we believe in - keeping the bad guys in jail."
For those who thought that Ford must have obviously misspoke when he impugned the independence of the judiciary and was keen to clarify his damaging comments, well, think again. On Monday, he doubled down.
"I am going to make sure we have like-minded judges," the premier said during question period at Queen's Park.
The Federation of Ontario Law Associations said the comments reflected "a juvenile understanding of the role of an independent judiciary and erodes confidence in the justice system. "The justice system is not a playground for politicians to bully," it said. "The rule of law is upheld when judges are placed by merit, not by fiat."
Former premier Kathleen Wynne scoffed at Ford's claim that he was elected to appointment "like-minded" people. "That is categorically NOT what a premier, any premier, is elected to do," Wynne said in a post to X. "Intentionally tainting the judicial process is reprehensible, but sadly not surprising for this man."
Michael Spratt, an Ottawa defence lawyer, told the Star's Jacques Gallant that Ford's comments "can be charitably described as one of the most disturbing quotes I've heard about our justice system."
It's worthwhile he said, if considering what "like-minded" people to Ford might look like, to review the evidence of how Ford himself thinks. The premier is happy to invoke the notwithstanding clause in the Charter of Rights and Freedoms to override constitutional rights when passing legislation; has under-resourced courts and contributed to the backlog of cases; and has "funnelled millions of dollars to law enforcement while cutting millions of dollars from civil liberties organizations, legal aid and community legal clinics," Spratt said.
Ford's comments are the worst kind of political pandering, intended to incite an emotional response of anger and scapegoating rather than offering a grasp or analysis of complicated issues.
His remarks go at the independence of the judiciary that is a cornerstone of our justice system. That the courts operate separately from the government is essential to their fair-minded application of justice and critically, vital to our collective faith that judicial decisions are based on evidence and law, not simply the result of "like-minded" appointees doling out decisions they think will make the premier happy.
By suggesting the appointments process is just one big pork barrel, his comments do a great injustice to the integrity of the justices now on the bench and future appointees.
As the Federation of Law Associations properly said, his comments were irresponsible and harmful. It's not for nothing, after all, that the legitimacy of the U.S. Supreme Court has been undercut by the partisanship and dubious quality of appointments in recent years. Ford's comments "are contagious on the political right and pull apart at the threads of our democracy in a misleading and dangerous way," the federation said.
There is more at stake here than who gets a seat on the gravy train. With his ill-considered appointments and incendiary comments, the premier is following an appalling example and playing a disturbing kind of politics.
CREDIT: ;

Subject: Law; Judicial appointments; Partisanship; Judiciary; Judges & magistrates

Document 108 of 500

Ontario leads jump in number of EI recipients: Rising unemployment drives 14.8% increase in aid across Canada last year

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-leads-jump-number-ei-recipients/docview/2931930910/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+leads+jump+in+number+of+EI+recipients%3A+Rising+unemployment+drives+14.8%25+increase+in+aid+across+Canada+last+year&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The number of Canadians receiving Employment Insurance (EI) benefits soared in 2023 amid increasing unemployment rates, according to recent data from Statistics Canada.
People in Ontario receiving EI - which provides temporary income support to unemployed workers - jumped by 34,000 in 2023 - a whopping 32 per cent increase, the highest of any province.
And despite claims from a new taxpayer-funded advertising campaign by Premier Doug Ford's government that Ontario is "a place where more people are going to work than ever before," more than 140,000 Ontarians were on EI benefits in December, according to Statistics Canada, compared with approximately 106,000 people a year before who were receiving income support. Across Canada, EI recipients rose 14.8 per cent in 2023.
"This is the result of higher unemployment over the course of 2023," said David Macdonald, a senior economist with the Canadian Centre for Policy Alternatives. "As a result, you see more people applying for and getting EI."
While the unemployment rate fell 0.1 per cent in January, the overall labour market has been losing steam as the economy slowed in 2023 under pressure of high interest rates, with the unemployment rate climbing 0.8 percentage points from April to November, according to Statistics Canada.
At the same time, population growth driven by immigration has also contributed to the rising unemployment rate. From 2022 to 2023, Canada took in some 1.13 million immigrants, the highest such figure on record, and almost half a million more than the previous year.
Douglas Porter, chief economist at BMO, pointed out that in Ontario, the increase in EI recipients grew even faster than the unemployment rate. In Ontario, the unemployment rate was 6.3 per cent in December according to Statistics Canada, up from 5.3 per cent the year prior.
Porter and Macdonald said EI applicants outpacing unemployment could indicate who is currently unemployed or losing their jobs.
For example, to be eligible for EI benefits, applicants must have worked between 420 and 700 insurable employment hours during the qualifying period.
"You need a fair amount of hours to get into the system," Macdonald said. "This may be a reflection of longer-employed people or more securely employed people losing work over the course of 2023 and therefore getting into the system."
More than 300,000 full-time jobs in Ontario have disappeared since July, according to data from Statistics Canada. Meanwhile, part-time jobs are on the rise.
In April, Canada's economy added 41,000 new jobs mostly in part-time work and in January, the slight uptick in employment was largely driven by an increase in part-time work, Statistics Canada said.
Macdonald said the number of Canadians seeking EI benefits will likely continue to increase.
The Royal Bank of Canada in a report from May said that the unemployment rate is projected to climb to 6.6 per cent by early 2024.
"I don't think there's any particular slowing in the rise of the unemployment rate," he said. "As a result you're going to see more people seeking employment insurance benefits as they lose their jobs."
CREDIT: Ghada Alsharif Toronto Star

Subject: Unemployment; Economic conditions; Employment; Part time employment

Document 109 of 500

Ford may hold key to clearing intersections

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-may-hold-key-clearing-intersections/docview/2931929754/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+may+hold+key+to+clearing+intersections&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Those who drive and those who prefer to get around on foot or by bike don't usually see eye-to-eye in traffic-mad Toronto, but here is something that can unite us: we should all join hands and acknowledge that the drivers who "block the box" are the absolute worst.
For other drivers, the "blocking-the-box" traffic offence - pulling into an intersection when there's not enough room on the other side - causes time-sucking life-draining gridlock, where traffic can't move.
And, for pedestrians and cyclists, it's even worse because box-blockers make things downright dangerous. It often leads to scary situations where people need to squeeze through small gaps between vehicles that have blocked the crosswalks and bike lanes.
So let's shake on it. Making box blockers pay - and pay big - should be something we can all support.
But, alas, I regret to inform you box-blockers in Toronto are not being made to pay. Both the penalties and the enforcement are a joke.
A new report to Toronto city hall's infrastructure committee confirms the prescribed ticket for those caught blocking the box at most intersections remains just $90. In community safety zones, generally located near schools, the fine increases - but only a bit - to $120.
That's an amount that is wildly out of step with the harm this traffic offence is causing. As a point of comparison, under Ontario's Highway Traffic Act you could face a ticket for about the same amount for "improper bicycle lighting," "improper mudguards" or "operate air cushioned vehicle."
It also could soon be in spitting range of the cost of your average Toronto parking ticket.
Should city council approve recommended increases to the cost of parking tickets next month, the standard ticket for not paying for on-street parking will increase from $30 to $75.
That could create a weird reality where parking in the middle of an intersection will be only marginally more expensive than not paying for parking in a designated on-street parking spot.
Don't get me wrong here. I don't want to downplay the importance of paying for parking, bike lights, mudguards and not driving your hovercraft down the street, but blocking the box feels like a much more serious and - at least in the case of the hovercraft thing - more common offence, and the fine should be set accordingly.
Toronto's transportation department agrees. The report, set to be considered at today's meeting of the infrastructure committee and by city council next month, calls for the fine to be increased to $450 for those ticketed for blocking the box at most intersections, and $500 for those who do it in a community safety zone. That's a steep increase. It's also a justified one.
But there are a couple of snags.
First, Toronto, as is too often the case, doesn't have the power to increase these fines. Instead, the city must send an application to Ontario Attorney General Doug Downey justifying the hike. If the AG agrees, the application goes to a judge for final approval.
The city plans to send in its application following the development of an "enforcement strategy" with the police.
Which brings us to the second snag: for this offence, the city is unable to make use of the most effective enforcement mechanism it has available.
In recent years, the city has vastly expanded the number of red-light cameras at intersections. There are now nearly 300 cameras snapping photos of red-light runners in all corners of the city.
But these cameras can't currently be used to ticket people for blocking the box, even though it should be technically feasible.
The challenge is that, unlike speeding tickets and red-light tickets issued by cameras, provincial legislation requires charges for blocking the box to be levied against the person driving at the time of the offence, not the owner of the vehicle. Since the cameras are not smart enough to know who's behind the wheel at any given moment, automated enforcement can't be used.
This isn't an impassable deal-breaker. The report acknowledges provincial legislation could change this.
It should. And soon. With moves like nixing the cost of vehicle registration and making plate renewal automatic, Premier Doug Ford has done a lot of favours for motorists. Allowing for automated enforcement of box-blocking could be another boon for drivers - the good ones, anyway - and also benefit nondrivers, too.
Because, if you lend your car to someone and that person gets caught on camera blocking an intersection, they'll either offer to pay the ticket or you'll think twice about letting that person use your car again. Either outcome is fair.
And because automated enforcement like photo radar for speeding and red-light cameras has proven to be way more effective at laying charges than police officers.
And because blocking the box is a dangerous, congestion-causing behaviour that Queen's Park and city hall should finally slam the brakes on.
$90
Set fine for drivers who "block the box" by pulling into an intersection when there's not enough room on the other side.
A new city
hall report recommends increasing the fine to $450,
a move
that must
be approved
by the province.
CREDIT: Matt Elliott OPINION

Subject: Fines & penalties; Automobiles; Cameras; Automation; Enforcement; Traffic violations; Traffic control

Business indexing term: Subject: Automation

Location: Toronto Ontario Canada; Canada

Title: Ford may hold key to clearing intersections

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: A.4

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Feb 27, 2024

Section: News

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Document 110 of 500

Why Doug Ford may hold the key to unsnarling Toronto's jammed intersections

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-doug-ford-may-hold-key-unsnarling-torontos/docview/2931927590/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+Doug+Ford+may+hold+the+key+to+unsnarling+Toronto%27s+jammed+intersections&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Because if you lend your car to someone and that person gets caught on camera blocking an intersection, they'll either offer to pay the ticket or you'll think twice about letting that person use your car again. Either outcome is fair.
And because automated enforcement like photo radar for speeding and red-light cameras has proven to be way more effective at laying charges than police officers.
And because blocking the box is a dangerous, congestion-causing behaviour that Queen's Park and city hall should finally slam the brakes on.
CAPTION: City staff recommend that council hike the penalty for blocking intersections from $90 to $450. Trouble is, the province has to approve the move.

Subject: Fines & penalties; Automobiles; Cameras; Automation; Councils; Enforcement

Business indexing term: Subject: Automation

Document 111 of 500

Ford taints judicial process

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-taints-judicial-process/docview/2931927569/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+taints+judicial+process&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One of the things about Doug Ford that makes him different from a lot of politicians is that he generally says what he thinks - both for better and for worse.
That quality was on full display when the premier was asked last Friday whether he was OK with naming two of his former staffers to a committee that helps to select new judges, a move criticized as outright patronage.
Another politician might have fuzzed up his answer, given the importance of maintaining both the appearance and the reality of judicial independence, public confidence in the justice system, stuff like that. Not Ford.
"One hundred per cent I am," he said proudly.
"We got elected to get like-minded people in appointments ... I'm not going to appoint some NDP or some Liberal. I've made it very clear where I stand with judges ... The justices of the peace and judges, they're letting criminals out ... I'm putting like-minded people that believe in what we believe in, keeping the bad guys in jail."
The premier's office made an attempt to deny the obvious - that the premier thinks it's legitimate for his government to arrange things so we get more judges and JPs who think like him, who won't be "letting the criminals out," who are "tough on crime" and so on.
It claimed he was just talking about people named to the selection committee, not the judges themselves. Until Monday, when Ford tossed away that fig leaf and said directly: "I am going to make sure we have like-minded judges."
So now there's no doubt where the premier stands, and it's not good. The Advocates Society, a national legal organization, calls his comments a "substantial threat to the independence of Ontario's judges." The Federation of Ontario Law Associations says they undermine "public confidence in the administration of justice in a dangerous and anti-democratic way." And they reflect a "juvenile understanding of the role of an independent judiciary."
Now, there's a school of thought that maintains this is much ado about not much, that governments from time immemorial have named judges loyal to the party in power and in tune with their thinking. It's only an issue now, goes this line, because it's Conservatives who are pushing their ideology. You can read that version in, for example, the Toronto Sun.
The trouble with this argument is that it ignores a decades-long attempt in Ontario - under Liberal, NDP and Conservative governments - to get away from that discredited old tradition of stuffing the judiciary with party hacks more noted for their partisan loyalty than their legal acumen.
A little bit of history: back in 1988 the Liberal government of David Peterson (now, full disclosure, chair of Torstar) set up a pilot project to develop a non-partisan model for appointing provincial judges.
It created the Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee (JAAC), which was eventually made permanent by Bob Rae's NDP government in 1995. That committee, expanded over the years to include representatives of legal associations and the general public, was maintained by the Conservative and Liberal governments that followed. It was universally hailed as the "gold standard" in Canada for making sure judges were named for their professional, not their political, qualifications.
Until that is, the Ford government was elected in 2018. Almost immediately it started talking about wanting more "like-minded" judges and in 2021, Attorney General Doug Downey put through changes to the way the JAAC operates to make it easier for the government to get its own choices named to the bench.
The premier's latest comments are another big step along that road. His government clearly isn't content to name judges and JPs who simply apply the law. Based on Ford's own comments it wants them to rule not just on the facts and the law but on some preconceived notion of being tough on crime.
Lawyers are right to raise the alarm, but they're not the only ones who should be concerned. Ontario has long had an independent, non-political judiciary - or at least the best version of that in Canada. The last thing we need is to taint that system with politics, patronage and outright bias.
Andrew Phillips is a Toronto-based staff columnist for the Star's Opinion page. Reach him via email: aphillips@thestar.ca
CREDIT: Andrew Phillips OPINION Andrew Phillips is a Toronto-based staff columnist for the Star's Opinion page. Reach him via email: aphillips@thestar.ca;

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Doug+Ford+defends+%27patronage%27+appointments+of+ex-staffers%2C+says+he+wants+%27like-minded+people%27+selecting+Ontario+judges%2C+Ageism+in+Toronto+restaurants%2C+Poll+finds+most+Ontarians+think+they%27re+good+drivers%2C+When+a+neighbourhood+mall+McDonald%27s+closes%2C+more+than+food+disappears%2C+When+a+neighbourhood+mall+McDonald%27s+closes%2C+more+than+food+disappears%2C+Ontario%27s+Black+History+Curriculum+Gets+an+F%2C&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Judges who think like Ford are the last thing we need
Doug Ford defends 'patronage' appointments of ex-staffers, says he wants 'like-minded people' selecting Ontario judges, Feb. 23
Aside from Premier Doug Ford's foolish politicizing the appointment of Ontario judges, what kind of "like-minded" judge candidates can we expect? College dropouts? Rash decision makers who will have their in-haste, poorly conceived judgments promptly reversed on appeal?
Preferential treatment for those close to the judge? Jurists with little regard for the environment or the constitution?
The last thing we need are "Ford like-minded justices."
Craig Moore, Hamilton
Share restaurant experiences to change attitudes
Ageism in Toronto restaurants, Feb. 26
As a senior, I support Cate White's outrage at being asked to move away from the window table while dining in a restaurant. But I suggest the opposite of boycotting the restaurant.
First take photos of yourself and your senior coterie smiling and having a good time at the restaurant. Post them all over social media, including Yelp. Make a point of telling seniors to go and sit in the seats in front of the window. Also take videos of the staff redirecting you away from the window seats and post those them with sad, outraged emojis on social media. I predict that the restaurant will never ask a senior to move away from a window table again.
Moses Shuldiner, Toronto
Entitled drivers think rules apply to all but them
Poll finds most Ontarians think they're good drivers, Feb. 26
The results of this poll are laughable. I drive a school bus in Brampton, and I see it all on a daily basis: not stopping at a red light before turning right; or yielding to pedestrians; or stopping when a school bus has the red lights flashing and the stop sign deployed; ignoring the 40 km/h limit in a school zone; parking in no parking or no stopping zones near schools.
All of these are basic rules, supposedly taught in driving schools and supposedly covered in driving tests. It is my opinion that the thought process is that all of the above are "not for me, but everybody else."
John MacFarlane, Brampton
We need more community spaces for seniors
When a neighbourhood mall McDonald's closes, more than food disappears, Feb. 25
Karon Liu hit the nail on the head. What seniors need is community space to meet, to walk and exercise, to share coffee. But Toronto city planners are not planning with seniors' social and mental health in mind. In Toronto Seniors Strategy the word community is used once to describe leaders.
Community and exercise are keys to living longer, healthier lives according to researcher and writer, Dan Buettner in his book, "Blue Zones." In the five areas of the world where people regularly live healthy lives into their 100s, they have daily opportunities to experience community and to exercise.
Developers of Etobicoke's 32-acre Cloverdale Mall say they are creating community space but there is none for seniors to meet indoors in winter. Etobicoke has the highest percentage of seniors in the city, yet the planning seems only for younger, fitter people.
Tom Riley Park is nine acres divided by railway overpasses into three sections. The smallest is less than one acre and holds a seniors' community garden and a picnic area. It is tranquil, attracting readers and picnickers. It is across the street from high rises that are 75 per cent seniors. The city proposes to put in a basketball court. When asked where the players would come from, staff indicated the seniors' buildings.
Staff really need to start getting to know their seniors better. Maybe they think we won't be around much longer.
My grandfather lived to 105. If I follow suit, I have another 32 years to keep reminding them.
Gail Rutherford, Etobicoke
McDonald's closure brings back memories
When a neighbourhood mall McDonald's closes, more than food disappears, Feb. 25
The fast-food industry's leading innovator has regrettably shut down a growing number of stores around GTA malls, leaving customers with many questions while lamenting the loss of an institution that they came to embrace and identify as a place to gather with family and friends for a coffee or bite to eat.
The recent closing of the McDonald's restaurant at Bridlewood Mall evokes some personal memories for this customer. My parents Mary and Eugene often visited this store during their retirement years conveniently located in the mall across the street from where they lived. When Eugene passed away in 2008, Mary's health began to decline yet she was fortunate enough to meet some wonderful friends who made her feel special and instilled in her the confidence and strength to cope with her loss. This store became her saving grace until she passed away in 2015 with only a few remaining friends who drop by the mall.
McDonald's recipe for success is more than serving millions of burgers and fries to its customers, it's also the social connection it served my mother and countless seniors.
Robert Ariano, Scarborough
We are all
the same -
only different
Ontario's Black History Curriculum Gets an F, Feb. 22
I agree with Tyler Robinson that young students need to be exposed to a curriculum that begins in junior kindergarten.
While teaching a JK class early in my career, a pupil asked me if I was a giant. I am male, 6-feet-2 and about 200 pounds, so it was easy to see from their perspective I was a giant.
I developed a lesson entitled "We are All the Same Only we're Different." It was easy to establish the first part as being in the Catholic system we had learned that we were all created by God, so we were all the same - human beings. Since children at this age interpret their world in a concrete form, we could see we were different. We conducted a review of all things that made us different. Hair colour, height, body shape, eye colour, shape of eyes, names and colour of skin. We even took a picture of the class grouping each within some of the parameter's we had set. It was a visual that the children could relate to. Soon the pupils did not relate to each other by a distinctive trait but simply as another human being in their class. They came to realize we are all the same only different.
So, if we are all the same, why are some of us treated so differently?
Terry Middlemiss, Hamilton

Document 113 of 500

Officer pleads guilty in Taser case: Toronto constable faces misconduct charges for using excessive force on Black U of T student

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/officer-pleads-guilty-taser-case/docview/2931927526/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Officer+pleads+guilty+in+Taser+case%3A+Toronto+constable+faces+misconduct+charges+for+using+excessive+force+on+Black+U+of+T+student&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One of three Toronto police officers involved in the 2021 "mistaken identity" arrest and Tasering of a Black University of Toronto student, which included that officer placing a knee to the student's neck, has pleaded guilty to using unnecessary force, a professional police misconduct charge.
Const. Seth Rietkoetter, who has been a police officer for 17 years, pleaded guilty Monday at a Toronto police tribunal hearing over the takedown of Hasani O'Gilvie, who was on his way to class when he was mistaken by police for what the tribunal heard was a Black man of similar description with a violent history.
"I would like to offer my most sincere apologies to Mr. O'Gilvie, and in (his) absence, to his mother," Rietkoetter, 42, said, addressing Christine Stought-O'Gilvie, a teacher with the Toronto District School Board, who made a point of attending in person for a son who's still recovering from what happened on a hot August day three summers ago.
"I hope this is something that both you and your son are able to overcome. I recognize that this has had a significant impact on you, your son (and) the greater community," said Rietkoetter, who also apologized to the police service for bringing it into "disrepute." He asked that his career not be judged by "this very brief moment."
Lawyer Noah Schachter, acting as a prosecutor for police, and Rietkoetter's lawyer, Sandy Khehra, are jointly calling for a penalty of a one-year-long demotion from 1st class to 2nd class constable, though Schachter told tribunal hearing officer Supt. Shane Branton that dismissal was "in the range of options."
The tribunal heard that this was not Rietkoetter's first time in trouble at the tribunal and that if he landed there again, the police service would "seek dismissal," said Schachter.
After hearing submissions, Branton reserved his decision on penalty to a later date.
In submissions made on behalf of O'Gilvie and his mother, lawyer David Shellnutt took issue with an agreed statement of facts that said O'Gilvie was initially resisting arrest while being Tasered multiple times - if anything, he said his client was "overpowered" and his arms ended up under his body because that was how officers "tackled and pinned him to the ground."
"He had his hands up ... He was scared, tasers were pointed at him."
Shellnut also highlighted the lack of acknowledgment in the agreed statement of facts that O'Gilvie is Black and that anti-Black racism has been acknowledged as a problem within Toronto police.
"As we have repeatedly stated, PC Rietkoetter's use of force against Mr. O'Gilvie represents the human face of the disproportionate use of force," said Shellnutt.
The incident has had a "broader impact" on the relationship between police and the city's Black communities, added Shellnutt, noting that the high-profile killing of George Floyd by a Minneapolis officer who also used a knee-to-neck manoeuvre - "That is the very same manoeuvre that was employed against Mr. O'Gilvie."
Rietkoetter's lawyer countered that race was not a factor in his "involvement."
It was unclear Monday what may come from police act charges laid against the other two officers. A schedule indicated tribunal hearings are to take place this week for Cons. Jilliane Baquiran and Sgt. Rachel Saliba, beginning Tuesday.
O'Gilvie was on his way to class on Aug. 12, 2021, when he identified himself to police verbally, was thrown to the ground, had a knee placed on his neck and Rietkoetter used his Taser - which was set to "drive-stun" mode - five times. He was then handcuffed, put in the back of a police car and then let go after his identification confirmed he was exactly who he said he was.
The use of drive-stun mode is controversial. It involves an officer holding their Taser directly against an individual and discharging it in a manner that causes pain but does not incapacitate them.
Police had been looking for a Black man of similar description after his mother reported he had taken her phone.
O'Gilvie, who has no criminal record and is now close to completing his university degree, and his mother have sued Toronto police over the incident, which was documented on police body-worn cameras.
Police footage from what police documents described as a case of "mistaken identity" has yet to be publicly released and scrutinized, and may never be - unless there is a full police tribunal hearing or Shellnutt is successful in an appeal of a police decision to deny access to the footage in a freedom of information request.
That appeal is pending; the lawsuit has since been settled, the terms of which are private.
The police video evidence mirrors O'Gilvie's claims and the public would likely find it "quite disturbing," Shellnut, who has reviewed the video, told the Star in a statement last year.
A police complaint investigation determined misconduct charges were warranted against all three officers involved in the arrest. All three were initially charged for allegedly not informing O'Gilvie of the reason for his arrest, nor of his right to a lawyer, and for unlawful or unnecessary arrest.
According to police tribunal charge documents, Rietkoetter faced an additional misconduct charge for excessive force using his Taser five times when O'Gilvie "was not resisting, and his hands were behind his back" and for "deliberately" placing a knee on his neck in a manner that "served no legitimate purpose."
He pleaded guilty to the latter charge, though the agreed statement of fact deemed the first three hits with the taser to be justifiable. The remaining two disciplinary charges against Rietkoetter are to be withdrawn in the plea deal.
It was also agreed that Rietkoetter did not inform O'Gilvie why he was kept in detention after his ID proved who he was, nor was he informed of his right to counsel.
The tribunal heard of mitigating factors in Rietkoetter's life, including that he was suffering from undiagnosed post-traumatic stress disorder at the time of the incident, for which he is now getting treatment.
On the aggravating side for Rietkoetter was a "serious" misconduct case that involved a "criminal finding of guilt," said Schachter.
For Stought-O'Gilvie, she welcomed the opportunity of seeing the officers in person "who caused my son, our family, such pain."
"I see the remorse shown (by Rietkoetter), and despite my pain and the unjustified suffering endured by my son, I wish no ill-will," she wrote. "My intent as the mother of Hasani has always been to seek justice for the wrongs done to my son."
She added: "I implore the police officers of Toronto to examine their biases, and we all do have them, whether we want to admit it or not ... They will recognize that when they encounter future 'Hasanis,' they will treat them with dignity and understand that not all young Black men have criminalistic intent."
CREDIT: Jim Rankin Toronto Star

Document 114 of 500

York workers eye higher pay: 3,000 staff walk off job as union pushes university for wage increase, job security

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/york-workers-eye-higher-pay/docview/2931927500/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=York+workers+eye+higher+pay%3A+3%2C000+staff+walk+off+job+as+union+pushes+university+for+wage+increase%2C+job+security&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hundreds of education workers gathered outside of York University subway station Monday, waving signs and chanting, "More wages now!"
The workers were among 3,000 from York University now on strike after a deal between CUPE 3903, the union representing the school's contract instructors, teaching assistants and graduate assistants, and the university was not reached over the weekend.
"We're struggling to make ends meet," CUPE 3903's communications officer Erin McIntosh told the Star. "Basic needs like housing and food have skyrocketed in price, but the wages for people who do the majority of teaching at York have been falling further and further behind. The employer is still making an offer that is far below the rising cost of living and forcing workers into a strike position."
McIntosh said that the union is asking for decent wages, job security and workplace protections for contract faculty and graduate student workers. She added that she believes that quality education is at stake for both undergraduate and graduate students.
"Close to 70 per cent of our members rely on food banks to feed themselves because their wages have simply fallen against inflation so dramatically," Fred Hahn, president of CUPE, said to the Star before addressing the crowd.
Zoe Newman, a faculty member on contract who's been at the bargaining table for more than eight months, echoes McIntosh's and Hahn's concerns about affordability. Workers are facing a "crisis," she said, adding that many staff are working multiple jobs.
"We would love to get the strike resolved as quickly as possible," she said from the protest.
A line-up for picket pay snaked around the rally. According to the union, workers are entitled to up to $15 an hour while on strike.
In response to the strike, the university said it had hoped to achieve a negotiated collective agreement over the weekend with the union prior to their planned strike.
"With that goal in mind, we offered two consecutive proposals on Feb. 7 and 21, which addressed crucial items, including increase in rates of pay. Thus far, none of these proposals have been responded to at the bargaining table," the university told the Star.
The university added that it will continue to ensure that student learning needs remain the top priority.
"Contingency plans for all other aspects of university operations are in place and will be activated as required and we will continue to work toward a swift, equitable, and sustainable agreement with CUPE 3903," the university said.
However, Reesa Barrett, a fourth-year kinesiology and health science student, said her professors and teaching assistants haven't offered much insight or notice about the strike.
"All I know is we pay way too much tuition for them to not be paying education workers better," she said, while walking by the rally Monday.
Fourth-year business student Sumar Busari wonders how long the strike will last, and if final exams and graduation will be impacted.
"(It) is stressful for me and other students who have already secured full-time employment starting immediately after graduation," Busari said.
The last time York University academic workers went on strike was in 2018 when Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative government legislated them back to work.
At the time of that strike, the province passed Bill 124 restricting wage increases to one per cent per year. This time around, Newman said workers are asking for a minimum of a four per cent increase.
In 2015, 3,700 teaching assistants and contract professors at the university voted to go on strike, shooting down an offer that didn't provide the wage hikes and job security that was sought. The strike lasted around a month before a deal was made that allowed classes to resume after being cancelled.
The deal froze tuition for the union's teaching and graduate assistants and the university also paid union members 100 per cent back pay for the strike period.
With files from Asma Sahebzada, Nawa Tahir and Abhiraj Lamba.
CREDIT: Fatima Raza and Jermaine Wilson Toronto Star With files from Asma Sahebzada, Nawa Tahir and Abhiraj Lamba.

Document 115 of 500

Ford reiterates call for 'like-minded judges': Premier's comments come amid outcry from opposition and legal experts

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-reiterates-call-like-minded-judges/docview/2931927455/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+reiterates+call+for+%27like-minded+judges%27%3A+Premier%27s+comments+come+amid+outcry+from+opposition+and+legal+experts&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is doubling down on his desire to have "like-minded" judges in Ontario courts, a stance described by legal groups as a "substantial threat" to the independence of the courts and the public's confidence in the justice system.
"I am going to make sure we have like-minded judges," Ford said Monday during question period at Queen's Park.
The premier's comments confirm and go further than already controversial remarks he made to reporters late last week - when he said he wanted "like-minded people in appointments" - in response to a Star exclusive that two of his ex-senior staffers have been appointed to the arm's-length committee that vets and recommends judges for the Ontario Court of Justice.
His former deputy chief of staff Matthew Bondy is now the chair of the Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee (JAAC), while his former director of stakeholder relations Brock Vandrick has also been a public member of the 13-person committee since December.
"We got elected to get like-minded people in appointments," Ford said Friday. "I'm not going to appoint some NDP or some Liberal."
His own office insisted Friday that Ford said he wanted like-minded people selecting judges, and not actually like-minded judges. But on Monday, Ford could not have been more clear: He wants to appoint judges who think like him and his government.
Ford was responding in the legislature to a question from Liberal MPP John Fraser, who asked if he would rescind the appointments of Bondy and Vandrick to the committee.
"We don't elect judges," Fraser told Ford. "We're not the U.S. Politicians don't get to stack the courts, premier. This is so Canadians all have trust in the judicial system."
Speaking to reporters afterward, NDP Leader Marit Stiles warned that Ford was continuing along a path toward "politicization of the judiciary."
"It's a very scary prospect," she said. "A lot of Ontarians will find this quite chilling."
The appointments of Bondy and Vandrick follow significant changes that the Ford government has already made to the process for naming judges, which critics warned would open the door for patronage appointments to the bench.
Ford said on Friday he was "100 per cent" OK with the pair being on the committee. He later added: "Every single appointment I can, to find tough judges, tough (justices of the peace) to keep guys in jail ... I'm going to do it."
The Advocates' Society, the national organization representing lawyers who appear in court, told Ford in a letter Monday - prior to his comments at Queen's Park - that they had serious concerns with what he said on Friday.
"Your comments - and the approach to judicial appointments that your comments convey - pose a substantial threat to the independence of Ontario's judges from the government and to public confidence in the administration of justice in this province," society president Dominique Hussey told Ford, copying his attorney general, Doug Downey.
In their own letter to Ford, also copying Downey, the Criminal Lawyers' Association echoed the Advocates' Society's concerns, and told Ford his comments "confirmed your government's intentions to politicize the process.
"Your ill-conceived public comments about appointing 'like-minded' judges will have the unintended effect of tarnishing the integrity of your future appointments," wrote association president Boris Bytensky.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said he was "surprised and shocked" that Ford doubled down on what he had said Friday.
"It's dangerous to have American-style politicization of our judiciary system," said Schreiner. "I mean, judges are there to apply the law, not do the premier's bidding."
The attorney general rejected NDP calls on Monday to remove Bondy and Vandrick from the committee, describing the party as "Liberals in sandals" who don't approve of his choices.
"Shocking that a conservative government wants to hear from conservative voices," Downey said in question period, responding to NDP justice critic Kristyn Wong-Tam.
He said he takes his responsibility to appoint judges seriously, and that his selections are based on the "collective advice" of the appointments committee.
"We have a gold standard of appointments of judges, and that standard has continued," Downey said. "Only because the Toronto Star wrote a story are the NDP now paying attention. I am happy to go through every single one of the judicial appointments and you will see that they are in fact qualified."
Downey's spokesperson told the Star on Monday that "this appointments process is separate from the total independence judges have from government once they are appointed to their positions." Andrew Kennedy also stated that under the previous Liberal government, "members of the committee had donated thousands of dollars to political parties, including to the Liberals themselves."
Hussey said in her letter to Ford that judges must be qualified, unbiased, impartial and reflective of Canadian society, and appointed through a "robust and transparent" non-partisan process.
"The fact that a candidate holds the same beliefs as the government of the day, or is a member of the party in power, should never factor in their selection as a judge," Hussey wrote.
She said judges are supposed to apply the law to the facts of each case. "It is not to implement wider government policies, such as being 'tough' or 'soft' on crime."
To suggest otherwise, Hussey wrote, "undermines the judiciary's independence as the third, equal branch of government. Moreover, to suggest otherwise weakens public confidence in the independence of Ontario's judges and their ability to make fair, impartial decisions."
Downey's office has maintained that Bondy and Vandrick were appointed for their public policy experience and volunteer activities.
Downey sparked the ire of legal groups in 2021 when he made major changes to the appointments committee that none of them had asked for.
He now has the power to appoint nearly all of the committee's members, and he also increased the number of candidates the committee recommends to him for each judicial vacancy.
Advocates warned that by increasing the number, the government would have greater access to the entire application pool, running the risk that lower-quality candidates get appointed to the bench.
The Federation of Ontario Law Associations, which has a representative on the JAAC, said last week that Ford's comments "reflect a juvenile understanding" of the independence of the courts.
The comments also suggest "that appointed judges were politically biased and take partisan positions that parallel Doug Ford's policies," the federation said.
With files from Robert Benzie
CREDIT: Jacques Gallant Courts and Justice Reporter With files from Robert Benzie
