Document 116 of 500

Toronto cop admits misconduct for Tasering, kneeing neck of U of T student in 'mistaken identity' case

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-cop-admits-misconduct-tasering-kneeing/docview/2932278270/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+cop+admits+misconduct+for+Tasering%2C+kneeing+neck+of+U+of+T+student+in+%27mistaken+identity%27+case&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One of three Toronto police officers involved in the 2021 "mistaken identity" arrest and Tasering of a Black University of Toronto student, which included that officer placing a knee to the student's neck, has pleaded guilty to using unnecessary force, a professional police misconduct charge.Const. Seth Rietkoetter, who has been a police officer for 17 years, pleaded guilty Monday at a Toronto police tribunal hearing over the takedown of Hasani O'Gilvie, who was on his way to class when he was mistaken by police for what the tribunal heard was a Black man of similar description with a violent history."I would like to offer my most sincere apologies to Mr. O'Gilvie, and in (his) absence, to his mother," Rietkoetter, 42, said, addressing Christine Stought-O'Gilvie, a teacher with the Toronto District School Board, who made a point of attending in person for a son who's still recovering from what happened on a hot August day three summers ago."I hope this is something that both you and your son are able to overcome. I recognize that this has had a significant impact on you, your son (and) the greater community," said Rietkoetter, who also apologized to the police service for bringing it into "disrepute." He asked that his career not be judged by "this very brief moment."Lawyer Noah Schachter, acting as a prosecutor for police, and Rietkoetter's lawyer, Sandy Khehra, are jointly calling for a penalty of a one-year-long demotion from 1st class to 2nd class constable, though Schachter told tribunal hearing officer Supt. Shane Branton that dismissal was "in the range of options."The tribunal heard that this was not Rietkoetter's first time in trouble at the tribunal and that if he landed there again, the police service would "seek dismissal," said Schachter.After hearing submissions, Branton reserved his decision on penalty to a later date.In submissions made on behalf of O'Gilvie and his mother, lawyer David Shellnutt took issue with an agreed statement of facts that said O'Gilvie was initially resisting arrest while being Tasered multiple times - if anything, he said his client was "overpowered" and his arms ended up under his body because that was how officers "tackled and pinned him to the ground.""He had his hands up ... He was scared, tasers were pointed at him."Shellnut also highlighted the lack of acknowledgment in the agreed statement of facts that O'Gilvie is Black and that anti-Black racism has been acknowledged as a problem within Toronto police."As we have repeatedly stated, PC Rietkoetter's use of force against Mr. O'Gilvie represents the human face of the disproportionate use of force," said Shellnutt.The incident has had a "broader impact" on the relationship between police and the city's Black communities, added Shellnutt, noting that the high-profile killing of George Floyd by a Minneapolis officer who also used a knee-to-neck manoeuvre - "That is the very same manoeuvre that was employed against Mr. O'Gilvie."Rietkoetter's lawyer countered that race was not a factor in his "involvement."It was unclear Monday what may come from police act charges laid against the other two officers. A schedule indicated tribunal hearings are to take place this week for Cons. Jilliane Baquiran and Sgt. Rachel Saliba, beginning Tuesday.O'Gilvie was on his way to class on Aug. 12, 2021, when he identified himself to police verbally, was thrown to the ground, had a knee placed on his neck and Rietkoetter used his Taser - which was set to "drive-stun" mode - five times. He was then handcuffed, put in the back of a police car and then let go after his identification confirmed he was exactly who he said he was.The use of drive-stun mode is controversial. It involves an officer holding their Taser directly against an individual and discharging it in a manner that causes pain but does not incapacitate them.Police had been looking for a Black man of similar description after his mother reported he had taken her phone.O'Gilvie, who has no criminal record and is now close to completing his university degree, and his mother have sued Toronto police over the incident, which was documented on police body-worn cameras.Police footage from what police documents described as a case of "mistaken identity" has yet to be publicly released and scrutinized, and may never be - unless there is a full police tribunal hearing or Shellnutt is successful in an appeal of a police decision to deny access to the footage in a freedom of information request.That appeal is pending; the lawsuit has since been settled, the terms of which are private.The police video evidence mirrors O'Gilvie's claims and the public would likely find it "quite disturbing," Shellnut, who has reviewed the video, told the Star in a statement last year.A police complaint investigation determined misconduct charges were warranted against all three officers involved in the arrest. All three were initially charged for allegedly not informing O'Gilvie of the reason for his arrest, nor of his right to a lawyer, and for unlawful or unnecessary arrest.According to police tribunal charge documents, Rietkoetter faced an additional misconduct charge for excessive force using his Taser five times when O'Gilvie "was not resisting, and his hands were behind his back" and for "deliberately" placing a knee on his neck in a manner that "served no legitimate purpose."He pleaded guilty to the latter charge, though the agreed statement of fact deemed the first three hits with the taser to be justifiable. The remaining two disciplinary charges against Rietkoetter are to be withdrawn in the plea deal.It was also agreed that Rietkoetter did not inform O'Gilvie why he was kept in detention after his ID proved who he was, nor was he informed of his right to counsel.The tribunal heard of mitigating factors in Rietkoetter's life, including that he was suffering from undiagnosed post-traumatic stress disorder at the time of the incident, for which he is now getting treatment.On the aggravating side for Rietkoetter was a "serious" misconduct case that involved a "criminal finding of guilt," said Schachter.For Stought-O'Gilvie, she welcomed the opportunity of seeing the officers in person "who caused my son, our family, such pain.""I see the remorse shown (by Rietkoetter), and despite my pain and the unjustified suffering endured by my son, I wish no ill-will," she wrote. "My intent as the mother of Hasani has always been to seek justice for the wrongs done to my son."She added: "I implore the police officers of Toronto to examine their biases, and we all do have them, whether we want to admit it or not ... They will recognize that when they encounter future 'Hasanis,' they will treat them with dignity and understand that not all young Black men have criminalistic intent."
CAPTION: Christine Stought-O'Gilvie and husband Kenneth O'Gilvie, with son Hasani O'Gilvie in the background, near their Toronto home in a file photo. Hasani, whose face has been digitally altered to assure he is not identifiable as requested by the family, was arrested. tased and released by Toronto police in a case of mistaken identity.

Subject: Official misconduct; Criminal pleas; Nonlethal weapons; Mistaken identity

Doug Ford's call for 'like-minded' people picking judges is 'substantial threat' to Ontario courts, lawyers tell him

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-call-like-minded-people-picking-judges/docview/2931758424/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+call+for+%27like-minded%27+people+picking+judges+is+%27substantial+threat%27+to+Ontario+courts%2C+lawyers+tell+him&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's comments last week that he wants "like-minded" people picking Ontario judges pose a "substantial threat" to the independence of the courts and the public's confidence in the justice system, a national legal organization told Ford in a letter Monday.
Ford made the comments responding on Friday to a Star exclusive that two of his ex-senior staffers were appointed by his government to the arm's-length committee that vets and recommends judges for the Ontario Court of Justice.
His former deputy chief of staff Matthew Bondy is now the chair of the Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee (JAAC), while his former director of stakeholder relations Brock Vandrick has also been a public member of the 13-person committee since December.
Ford said he was "100 per cent" OK with the pair being on the committee, a move denounced by critics as patronage that risks tainting what is supposed to be an independent process.
"We got elected to get like-minded people in appointments ... I'm not going to appoint some NDP or some Liberal," Ford said.
He later added: "Every single appointment I can, to find tough judges, tough (justices of the peace) to keep guys in jail ... I'm going to do it."
While Ford's office later clarified that he was talking about wanting "like-minded" people selecting judges - and not like-minded judges - critics and advocacy groups were unconvinced.
The Advocates' Society, the national organization representing lawyers who appear in court, told Ford in a letter Monday that they had serious concerns with what he said.
"Your comments - and the approach to judicial appointments that your comments convey - pose a substantial threat to the independence of Ontario's judges from the government and to public confidence in the administration of justice in this province," society president Dominique Hussey told Ford, cc'ing his attorney general, Doug Downey.
Hussey said that judges must be qualified, unbiased, impartial, and reflective of Canadian society, and appointed through a "robust and transparent" non-partisan appointment process.
"The fact that a candidate holds the same beliefs as the government of the day, or is a member of the party in power, should never factor in their selection as a judge," Hussey wrote.
"The role of a judge is to apply the law as it stands to the facts of the case before them as they find them to be, without fear or favour; it is not to implement wider government policies, such as being 'tough' or 'soft' on crime, by means of their judgments in individual cases."
To suggest otherwise, Hussey wrote, "undermines the judiciary's independence as the third, equal branch of government. Moreover, to suggest otherwise weakens public confidence in the independence of Ontario's judges and their ability to make fair, impartial decisions."
Downey's office has maintained that Bondy and Vandrick were appointed for their public policy experience and volunteer activities.
Putting them on the committee follows significant changes that Downey has made to the JAAC that were condemned by legal associations as unnecessary and opening the door for patronage appointments to the bench, with critics charging that the changes would water down a process that was once described as the gold standard for appointing judges.
The Federation of Ontario Law Associations, which has a representative on the JAAC, said last week that Ford's comments "reflect a juvenile understanding" of the independence of the courts.
They also suggest "that appointed judges were politically biased and take partisan positions that parallel Doug Ford's policies," the federation said. "All of this undermines public confidence in the administration of justice in a dangerous and anti-democratic way."
CAPTION: Matthew Bondy, left, Ontario Premier Doug Ford's former deputy chief of staff, and Brock Vandrick, right, his former director of stakeholder relations, were recently appointed to the committee that selects Ontario judges."We got elected to get like-minded people in appointments ... I'm not going to appoint some NDP or some Liberal," Doug Ford said on Friday.

Document 118 of 500

3,000 academic workers at York University on strike

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/3-000-academic-workers-at-york-university-on/docview/2931758357/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=3%2C000+academic+workers+at+York+University+on+strike&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Three thousand academic workers at York University are now on strike as a deal with the university was not reached over the weekend.
CUPE3903's communications officer Erin McIntosh told the Star that the union was officially on strike on Monday morning.
"Academic workers have been left with no other choice but to begin a strike action today," McIntosh said. "Who can afford to work at York anymore?"
CUPE 3903, the union representing the school's contract instructors, teaching assistants and graduate assistants, had previously said picket lines were expected to go up on Monday if no deal was reached by midnight on Sunday.
Workers will be joined by faculty, other campus workers, students and labour leaders at 11 a.m. for a rally outside York University subway station.
In response to the strike, the university said it had hoped to achieve a negotiated collective agreement over the weekend with the union prior to their planned strike.
"With that goal in mind, we offered two consecutive proposals on Feb. 7 and 21, which addressed crucial items, including increase in rates of pay. Thus far, none of these proposals have been responded to at the bargaining table," the university told the Star.
The university added that will continue to ensure that student learning needs remain the top priority.
"Contingency plans for all other aspects of university operations are in place and will be activated as required and we will continue to work toward a swift, equitable, and sustainable agreement with CUPE 3903," the university said.
"We're struggling to make ends meet. Basic needs like housing and food have skyrocketed in price, but the wages for people who do the majority of teaching at York have been falling further and further behind. The employer is still making an offer that is far below the rising cost of living and forcing workers into a strike position," McIntosh said.
McIntosh added that she believes that quality education is at stake for both undergraduate and graduate students.
"We are asking for decent wages, job security and work-place protections for contract faculty and graduate student workers that provide a more equitable and accessible high quality education to all," McIntosh told the Star.
The last time York University academic workers went on strike was in 2018 when Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives government legislated them back to work.
At the time of that strike, the province passed Bill 124 restricting wage increases to one per cent per year.
CAPTION: On Friday morning, three units of the union representing contract instructors, teaching assistants and graduate assistants at York University voted in favour of taking strike action.

Document 119 of 500

Judging Ford's appointments, Black History Month observance and Tyler Bertuzzi's game

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/judging-fords-appointments-black-history-month/docview/2931758301/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Judging+Ford%27s+appointments%2C+Black+History+Month+observance+and+Tyler+Bertuzzi%27s+game&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Judging Ford's appointments
Ford says he wants 'like-minded people' selecting Ontario judges, Feb. 23
We can now add judges to Premier Doug Ford's contempt of the Constitution, the environment, the Ontario Science Centre and Ontario Place, and finally, nurses, teachers and child care workers. It seems the only people Ford doesn't have contempt for are his picks who are appointed to the Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee (JAAC).
James Wigmore, Toronto
Premier Doug Ford needs to be reminded that judicial appointments in Canada are, generally, non-political and most governments try to insulate politicians from appointments by having arms-length advisory committees. Putting your friends on these is a sad reflection on him and his understanding of ethics. The appointment of provincial judges and tribunal members should only follow recommendations from arms-length committees. Shame!
David S. Crawford, Toronto
With all the finger pointing at the Ford Conservatives, I think it's time to put the responsibility on all the people who chose not to vote. To those who did not, please do so in 2026 so both you and I get the type of government we want.
J. Armour, Mississauga
Premier Doug Ford had been searching for the gravy train for years. The crony train has arrived and the engineer is Attorney General Doug Downey. The independence of the judiciary is a fundamental principal of democracy and although sorely tested at times, it does function to the benefit of citizens most of the time. Where it doesn't work is in places like the U.S. where Republicans labour quietly and effectively to stack the entire court system with right-leaning Republican judges. When Ford became premier with a majority government, he must have thought he became president and could do as he liked. We've successfully pushed back on some of his calls but undeterred, he continues to nibble at the political landscape of Ontario so it reflects an image of the freewheeling U.S. I hope the legal profession in this province uses all the tools at its disposal to attack this latest ill conceived Ford policy and I hope the voters of this country can smell the clear odour of Conservative politics before they cast their next ballot. This may at first seem like it isn't a big deal, but great oaks from little acorns grow.
Peter Bradley, Mississauga
Black History Month deserves respect
Some public institutions seem to be going through the motions of celebrating Black History Month with lacklustre observance. This is a travesty! Black History is Canadian history! I live in Scarborough and, dropping into a city community centre, found that people highlighted in the Black History Month display were Americans. Not one Canadian! Why? Given the proximity to libraries, couldn't there be some collaboration?
I then went to my neighbourhood library to see their display and only found disappointment. There was no visible mention of Black History Month. When I asked the librarian how they marked the month, she showed me an unmarked display of children's books. I'm fully aware that library collections have been compromised because of recent computer problems, however, the lack of visibility is concerning. Is the flurry of hiring DEI (diversity, equity and inclusive) specialists mere window dressing?
That brings me to another point. Last summer when researching 1930s track star Sam Richardson, I visited Central Tech where a laneway was named after him with a plaque extolling his contribution to Canadian history. I asked a student where I could find the laneway. He didn't know, even though it is the path that leads to the school's front door. I also asked whether he knew about Richardson. He didn't. With changes to the Ontario curriculum to include Black history, let us not only teach the truth about the treatment of Black people in Canadian history, but let us also celebrate Black contribution across all sectors. Public institutions must do better in celebration of Black History Month. There is no shortage of resources.
JM Singh, Scarborough
Van attack terrorism too
Judge rules London, Ont. truck murders were terrorism, Feb. 22
Just as Ontario Superior Court Justice Renee Pomerance declared that the mass murder of three generations of a Muslim family by a white nationalist killer was "textbook" terrorism, the van driver's attack on Yonge St. in 2018 should be considered a terrorist act as well. While the van driver was not charged with terrorism, he was influenced by misogynistic "incel" ideology which is centred on hostility towards women. Many of the victims targeted and killed were women. There is no hierarchy in cases of hate.
Diane Sullivan, Toronto
Ageism in Toronto restaurants
My friends and I are all over 75, white-haired and middle class. We live in Leslieville and like to go out for lunch and dinner. We like to sit in the window, but when we make a move to do so we are redirected to seats away from the window. It didn't take long to realize that having the aged sitting in the front window was not the look these trendy and expensive restaurants desired. I guess young people don't want to eat with the aged. When this happens now, I make it clear that the only move I will be making away from my choice of seating is out the door.
Cate White, Toronto
Bertuzzi adds no value
While Bertuzzi struggles to score, other metrics show greater value, Feb. 22
What I have seen game after game is that Tyler Bertuzzi is decent in the defensive zone, but has been mostly terrible in the neutral or offensive zones. He is a poor passer of the puck, gives it away frequently and cannot create a nice play for a goal. He has also damaged William Nylander and John Tavares' game on the ice, playing on their line. If Bertuzzi made $1 million instead of the $5.5 million (U.S.) he earns, he would very likely be playing on the Leafs fourth line, would have been scratched several times or demoted to the Marlies. There have been no consequences for his poor play. There are eight players on the Leafs roster who have scored at least five goals this season, earning much less than Bertuzzi, playing significantly fewer minutes. Every one of them has been scratched at least once, or demoted to the minors. It's clear coach Sheldon Keefe has a double standard.
Juven Duarte, Toronto
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford says he is"100 per cent" OK with the appointment of his former staffers to the committee that selects Ontario judges.

Document 120 of 500

Ford government announces details of billion-dollar boost to colleges and universities

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-announces-details-billion-dollar/docview/2931524210/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+announces+details+of+billion-dollar+boost+to+colleges+and+universities&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's colleges and universities are getting a billion-dollar funding boost from the Ford government over the next three years, which is also introducing legislation that would require schools to publish all fees and even textbook prices so students have a clear idea of their costs.
Tuition fees will also be frozen for the next three years for Ontario students, but the government will allow a five per cent increase this fall for students from other provinces.
Post-secondary Minister Jill Dunlop will make the announcement Monday afternoon at Queen's Park. Premier Doug Ford said Friday it would be "fabulous" news.
Details of the new money, as first reported in the Star, include $903 million over three years, plus additional funds for student mental health and safety.
Some $200 million of the $903 million will be targeted at schools with the greatest need.
However, the funds are about half of what colleges and universities expected, based on recommendations from an expert panel set up by the province.
A year after Ford was first elected premier, he slashed tuition by 10 per cent and has frozen fees since.
That, combined with stagnant funding, inflation, unexpected COVID costs as well as salary increases due to now-unconstitutional provincial legislation, has left almost half of the province's universities running deficits and warning of cuts to stay afloat.
On top of that, the schools are now facing the loss of some lucrative international student tuition revenue as the federal government cuts their numbers for this fall - a move that will disproportionately hit Ontario give the explosion in foreign students studying here in recent years - blaming a housing shortage and questions about the quality of some of the programs run by public and private college partnerships.
Last year, Dunlop asked a panel of experts to look at the sustainability of the post-secondary sector, and they urged an immediate 10 per cent boost in government funding, with future increases set at a minimum of two per cent a year, as well as an immediate tuition bump of five per cent this fall.
In total, its recommendations came with an estimated price tag of $2.5 billion over three years for the province's 24 publicly assisted colleges and 23 universities.
The province has not implemented the panel's recommendations, but says it is still looking at them.
One secondary leader called the funding level the province is proposing "dangerous" for the sector.
The province's announcement includes $15 million for schools to undergo efficiency reviews, $65.4 million for research and $167.4 million for capital repairs.
CAPTION: Premier Doug at Toronto City Hall on Feb. 22, 2024.

Subject: Tuition; Colleges & universities; Funding

Business indexing term: Subject: Colleges & universities

Document 121 of 500

Ford's Tories surging, new poll suggests: Data shows PCs' gains coming at expense of New Democrats, while Liberals sit in between

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-tories-surging-new-poll-suggests/docview/2931524126/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+Tories+surging%2C+new+poll+suggests%3A+Data+shows+PCs%27+gains+coming+at+expense+of+New+Democrats%2C+while+Liberals+sit+in+between&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives are riding high at the apparent expense of Marit Stiles' New Democrats, a new poll suggests.
The latest Abacus Data tracking poll for the Star had the Tories up three points to 41 per cent while the NDP had dropped four points to 19 per cent.
Bonnie Crombie's Liberals remain mired in second place at 27 per cent, unchanged from last month's survey, and Mike Schreiner's Greens are up to eight per cent from five per cent in January.
"The greatest risk for the NDP and Marit Stiles is a strong Liberal party and resilient Greens," said Abacus president David Coletto.
"I think the NDP is still figuring out how to be relevant while also managing demons within its own party. The infighting within that party is likely to blame for the NDP drop," said Coletto.
That was a reference to internal tumult within the official opposition after rookie Hamilton Centre MPP Sarah Jama was defenestrated for repeated insubordination last October. She is now an Independent MPP.
There was also sobering news for the Liberals, who have not surged as much as they had hoped after Crombie, a popular former Mississauga mayor, won the leadership Dec. 2.
"It's a tough job being an opposition leader in Ontario, especially when you don't have official party status, nor a seat in the legislature," said Coletto.
The Liberals, who governed from 2003 until 2018, have nine seats in the 124-member house, three shy of recognized standing as a party in the chamber.
That means the Grits don't get much time in the legislature's question period and lack funding for staff and research - a challenge for Crombie, who is also being targeted by a multimillion-dollar PC attack ad blitz.
"She will need to start talking directly to voters through advertising to break through. Take a page from Pierre Poilievre's strategy," said Coletto, referring to the federal Conservative leader's ongoing promotional campaign.
Abacus surveyed 1,000 Ontarians from Feb. 15 until last Wednesday using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Encouraging as February's findings may be to the Tories, Coletto noted the undecided vote remains "worryingly high" for the governing party.
When asked for their "current provincial vote intention," 32 per cent of respondents said PC, 21 per cent Liberal, 15 per cent NDP, six per cent Green, four per cent another party while 22 per cent were undecided.
"High undecideds could definitely be a vulnerability if those folks become engaged and consolidate around an alternative to Ford," said Coletto, noting it was 23 per cent undecided last month.
"Ford's approval rating has dropped over the past few months but it hasn't really impacted vote intentions," the pollster noted.
"That's often the last thing to shift, but also requires voters to feel there's a better alternative. If that condition emerges, Ford could be in trouble."
Mindful of current concerns over inflation, Abacus asked respondents "between your provincial government and the federal government, which has made decisions that have most increased your cost of living?"
About two-thirds - 64 per cent - of Ontarians said the federal government, while 36 per cent said Queen's Park. (In the rest of Canada, 70 per cent pointed at Ottawa while 30 per cent blamed their respective provincial government.)
"I think Doug Ford has built his personal brand around fighting for taxpayers," said Coletto.
"He's seen as empathetic and committed to making life more affordable and so that's insulated him from some of the same forces bringing down incumbents elsewhere," he said.
"There's a subset of the electorate unhappy with the Ford government, but not ready to commit to an alternative."
Fewer than one-third - 30 per cent - had a positive impression of Ford while nearly half - 47 per cent - had a negative impression with 20 per cent neutral and two per cent didn't know enough to say.
That's a net impression rating for the premier of minus 17 per cent.
By comparison, the Liberals' Crombie was 27 per cent positive, 29 per cent negative, 28 per cent neutral and 16 per cent unsure for a net rating of minus two per cent.
Similarly, the NDP's Stiles had a 23 per cent positive, 25 per cent negative, 30 per cent neutral and 21 per cent unsure for a net rating of minus two per cent.
The Greens' Schreiner had a 19 per cent positive, 21 per cent negative, 37 per cent neutral and 24 per cent unsure for a net rating of minus two per cent.
These latest findings come against the backdrop of the Greenbelt land swap scandal that is under investigation by the RCMP.
In November, Abacus poll - conducted before Crombie won the Liberal leadership - the Tories were at 42 per cent, the NDP at 24 per cent, the Grits at 23 per cent and the Greens at seven per cent.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 122 of 500

Letters: Ford defends 'patronage' appointments, Feb. 24 Murders were terrorism, court rules, Feb. 23 Making a contribution, Feb. 23

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/2931524099/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Ford+defends+%27patronage%27+appointments%2C+Feb.+24+Murders+were+terrorism%2C+court+rules%2C+Feb.+23+Making+a+contribution%2C+Feb.+23&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Judging Ford's approach to appointments
Ford defends 'patronage'
appointments, Feb. 24
We can now add judges to Premier Doug Ford's contempt of the Constitution, the environment, the Ontario Science Centre and Ontario Place, and finally, nurses, teachers and child care workers. It seems the only people Ford doesn't have contempt for are his picks who are appointed to the Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee.
James Wigmore, Toronto
Premier Doug Ford needs to be reminded that judicial appointments in Canada are, generally, non-political and most governments try to insulate politicians from appointments by having arms-length advisory committees.
Putting your friends on these is a sad reflection on him and his understanding of ethics. The appointment of provincial judges and tribunal members should only follow recommendations from arms-length committees. Shame!
David S. Crawford, Toronto
With all the finger pointing at the Ford Conservatives, I think it's time to put the responsibility on all the people who chose not to vote. To those who did not, please do so in 2026 so both you and I get the type of government we want.
J. Armour, Mississauga
Premier Doug Ford had been searching for the gravy train for years. The crony train has arrived and the engineer is Attorney General Doug Downey. The independence of the judiciary is a fundamental principal of democracy and although sorely tested at times, it does function to the benefit of citizens most of the time.
Where it doesn't work is in places like the U.S. where Republicans labour quietly and effectively to stack the entire court system with right-leaning Republican judges. When Ford became premier with a majority government, he must have thought he became president and could do as he liked.
We've successfully pushed back on some of his calls but undeterred, he continues to nibble at the political landscape of Ontario so it reflects an image of the freewheeling U.S. I hope the legal profession in this province uses all the tools at its disposal to attack this latest ill conceived Ford policy and I hope the voters of this country can smell the clear odour of Conservative politics before they cast their next ballot.his may at first seem like it isn't a big deal, but great oaks from little acorns grow.
Peter Bradley, Mississauga
Black History Month deserves more respect
Some public institutions seem to be going through the motions of celebrating Black History Month with lacklustre observance. This is a travesty. Black History is Canadian history. I live in Scarborough and, dropping into a city community centre, found that people highlighted in the Black History Month display were Americans. Not one Canadian! Why? Given the proximity to libraries, couldn't there be some collaboration?
I then went to my neighbourhood library to see their display and only found disappointment. There was no visible mention of Black History Month. When I asked the librarian how they marked the month, she showed me an unmarked display of children's books. I'm fully aware that library collections have been compromised because of recent computer problems, however, the lack of visibility is concerning. Is the flurry of hiring DEI (diversity, equity and inclusive) specialists mere window dressing?
That brings me to another point. Last summer when researching 1930s track star Sam Richardson, I visited Central Tech where a laneway was named after him with a plaque extolling his contribution to Canadian history. I asked a student where I could find the laneway.
He didn't know, even though it is the path that leads to the school's front door. I also asked whether he knew about Richardson. He didn't. With changes to the Ontario curriculum to include Black history, let us not only teach the truth about the treatment of Black people in Canadian history, but let us also celebrate Black contribution across all sectors. Public institutions must do better in celebration of Black History Month. There is no shortage of resources.
JM Singh, Scarborough
2018 van attack also terrorism
Murders were terrorism,
court rules, Feb. 23
Just as Ontario Superior Court Justice Renee Pomerance declared that the mass murder of three generations of a Muslim family by a white nationalist killer was "textbook" terrorism, the van driver's attack on Yonge Street in 2018 should be considered a terrorist act as well. While the van driver was not charged with terrorism, he was influenced by misogynistic "incel" ideology which is centred on hostility toward women. Many of the victims targeted and killed were women. There is no hierarchy in cases of hate.
Diane Sullivan, Toronto
Ageism is on
the menu at
this restaurant
My friends and I are all over 75, white-haired and middle class. We live in Leslieville and like to go out for lunch and dinner. We like to sit in the window, but when we make a move to do so we are redirected to seats away from the window. It didn't take long to realize that having the aged sitting in the front window was not the look these trendy and expensive restaurants desired. I guess young people don't want to eat with the aged. When this happens now, I make it clear that the only move I will be making away from my choice of seating is out the door.
Cate White, Toronto
Double standard on Leafs bench
Making a contribution, Feb. 23
What I have seen game after game is that Tyler Bertuzzi is decent in the defensive zone, but has been mostly terrible in the neutral or offensive zones. He is a poor passer of the puck, gives it away frequently and cannot create a nice play for a goal. He has also damaged William Nylander and John Tavares' game on the ice, playing on their line. If Bertuzzi made $1 million instead of the $5.5 million (U.S.) he earns, he would very likely be playing on the Leafs fourth line, would have been scratched several times or demoted to the Marlies.
There have been no consequences for his poor play. There are eight players on the Leafs roster who have scored at least five goals this season, earning much less than Bertuzzi, playing significantly fewer minutes. Every one of them has been scratched at least once, or demoted to the minors. It's clear coach Sheldon Keefe has a double standard.
Juven Duarte, Toronto

Document 123 of 500

Doug Ford's Tories are surging at the expense of Marit Stiles' New Democrats, poll suggests

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-tories-are-surging-at-expense-marit/docview/2931524008/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+Tories+are+surging+at+the+expense+of+Marit+Stiles%27+New+Democrats%2C+poll+suggests&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 124 of 500

Tories' ad spending far outpaces Liberals: Opposition spent thousands more, Meta data shows

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tories-ad-spending-far-outpaces-liberals/docview/2931523649/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tories%27+ad+spending+far+outpaces+Liberals%3A+Opposition+spent+thousands+more%2C+Meta+data+shows&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Flush with cash after a record-breaking fundraising year, Pierre Poilievre and the Conservatives are vastly outspending Justin Trudeau's Liberals on advertising, Meta data shows.
But Liberal insiders say they have no plans to go on an advertising offensive until the country is closer to an election.
Poilievre and the Conservatives have spent $230,000 on advertising on Meta in the past 90 days, about four times more than Trudeau and the Liberals, who have spent $55,000 in the same time period. It's a pattern political marketing experts say reflects the two parties' approaches to advertising since Poilievre won the leadership in September 2022.
It could be paying off for the Conservatives, who have been surging in the polls since the summer. The launch of an expensive ad campaign to boost Poilievre's image has also helped the opposition leader to become more familiar to Canadians.
"There's been a successful effort to show Poilievre as a Canadian that understands local issues and represents their economic anxiety," said Cole Hogan, a digital strategist who has worked for Ontario Premier Doug Ford and former Alberta Premier Jason Kenney. "The Conservatives also have a lot more money to play with than the Liberals do."
The Tories raised $35 million in 2023, compared to about $15 million from the Liberals, according to Elections Canada filings.
Current Conservative ads mainly link Liberal policies to Canadians' struggles with affordability, through repeated attacks on carbon pricing, government spending under the Trudeau government, and the cost of housing.
There are also more targeted ads that argue Trudeau is promoting censorship, and attack gun control policies, vaccine mandates and the World Economic Forum. In one advertisement written in Chinese and English, the Tories accuse the federal government of "handing out taxpayer-funded narcotics that fuel crime, chaos, drugs and disorder in our streets," specifically targeting a safe supply program in Richmond, B.C.
At the same time, the Conservatives are still running clips from a multimillion-dollar summer ad campaign that was meant to soften Poilievre's image, including one advertisement where the Conservative leader completes a puzzle with his daughter as he argues that "everything feels broken in Canada." It's been viewed more than a million times since January, mostly in Alberta and the Atlantic Provinces, a Liberal stronghold where the Conservatives are leading in the polls.
The Conservative Party of Canada did not return a request for comment.
The Liberals, meanwhile, have directed their ad spending toward defending their record on housing and affordability, especially in Ontario and Quebec. They've also been relying on organic content - that is, regular social media content from the party and Liberal politicians - as well as attacks on Poilievre from ministers, which have escalated in recent months, said Dan Arnold, the former director of research and advertising for Trudeau.
Senior Liberals who spoke to the Star on background in order to frankly discuss strategy said they believe it would not be useful or effective to spend more money on advertising against Poilievre until the country is closer to a real vote. They said Trudeau and his ministers already draw, and will continue to highlight, contrasts between the two leaders, and underscore their differences, such as what they call lukewarm Conservative support for Ukraine, the party's lack of a climate action plan, its anti-vaccine-mandate stance, as well as Poilievre's more restrictive view of transgender rights.
"While we don't publicly discuss our advertising strategy, Pierre Poilievre and his Conservative party continue to sow fear and division in their communications and advertising to import far-right American-style politics here to Canada and push for deep cuts to the middle class," Liberal party spokesperson Parker Lund told the Star.
In the last week, Poilievre's comments on transgender kids drew ire from the Liberals, who accused him of putting vulnerable Canadians at risk. Poilievre also attacked Trudeau personally, calling the prime minister racist over the blackface scandal that surfaced in 2019.
It's moments like these that some experts say could hurt the Conservatives' efforts to soften Poilievre's image, said Éric Blais, a political marketing commentator. But for the Liberals, passively defending your record and relying on Poilievre to shoot himself in the foot is not a strategy voters will buy into, he said. What's needed, he added, is an immediate, more aggressive advertising push that aims to regain the trust of Canadians.
"It's hard, particularly when you've got ArriveCan and memories of WE Charity and memories of (SNC-Lavalin)," he said.
"Plus, an election is rarely about the past. It's about the future."
With files from
Tonda MacCharles
CREDIT: Mark Ramzy Toronto Star With files from Tonda MacCharles

Subject: Conservatism; Canadians; Vaccines; Political leadership; Advertising expenditures; Political advertising; Transgender persons; Political parties; Advertising campaigns

Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives ad spending far outpacing Justin Trudeau's Liberals, data shows

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievres-conservatives-ad-spending-far/docview/2931523529/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre%27s+Conservatives+ad+spending+far+outpacing+Justin+Trudeau%27s+Liberals%2C+data+shows&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 126 of 500

About 3,000 academic workers at York University prepare to strike on Monday

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/about-3-000-academic-workers-at-york-university/docview/2931331652/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=About+3%2C000+academic+workers+at+York+University+prepare+to+strike+on+Monday&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Three thousand academic workers at York University are preparing to go on strike Monday morning if the university doesn't come "back to the table with a real plan to address the affordability crisis," the union said.
CUPE 3903, the union representing the school's contract instructors, teaching assistants and graduate assistants, said picket lines are expected to go up on Monday if no deal is reached. Workers will be joined by faculty, other campus workers, students and labour leaders at 11 a.m. for a rally outside York University subway station.
On Friday morning, three units of the union voted in favour of taking strike action.
"Members made this very important yet difficult decision because the key bargaining issues, especially those tied to compensation and job security, are about survival," the union said. "After eight months of bargaining, this is the situation we're in: a university run by an administration that will happily give itself massive raises while our members increasingly rely on food banks."
York University's deputy spokesperson, Yanni Dagonas told the Star, "Following the CUPE 3903 vote, the university and the union met as planned for collective bargaining negotiations, however an agreement has not yet been achieved. Negotiations between the university and the union may continue throughout the weekend and into next week with the agreement of both parties"
On Sunday morning, CUPE3903's communications officer Erin McIntosh told the Star in a statement that the union's bargaining team has indicated they're available to continue bargaining into the rest of the weekend "as long as the employer had something to offer us and was serious about bargaining."
"We are serious about negotiating a deal, and we call on the employer to match our commitment and sense of urgency to reach a just settlement," McIntosh said.
As of Sunday morning, McIntosh said the university is not taking any "serious steps" to avoid a strike action. The strike is set to begin at 12:01 a.m. on Monday, unless the university and union reach an agreement before then.
The last time York University academic workers went on strike was in 2018 when Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives government legislated them back to work.
At the time of that strike, the province passed Bill 124 restricting wage increases to one per cent per year.
"That law was subsequently struck down by the courts because it was unconstitutional, but York has yet to address the imbalance it created," said the union's press release.
CAPTION: On Friday morning, three units of the union representing contract instructors, teaching assistants and graduate assistants at York University voted in favour of taking strike action.

Document 127 of 500

Is consensus on immigration fraying?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/is-consensus-on-immigration-fraying/docview/2931284086/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+consensus+on+immigration+fraying%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Anti-immigration sentiment used to be politically taboo. Election after election, a majority of voters told party leaders they wanted more immigrants, not fewer. That vision of a Canada welcoming newcomers with open arms, however, is increasingly challenged. Unless governments address a growing perception that unbridled migration is making the country worse off, we may be walking towards a darker, more divisive path, one that makes us less wealthy in the long run.
This past week, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre signalled a large reduction in migration numbers will be part of his upcoming platform.
Speaking to reporters in Kitchener, Ont., Poilievre accused Prime Minister Justin Trudeau of ruining the entire immigration system by opening "the floodgates in a way that was disconnected from the number of homes to house people, and the amount of jobs to employ them." He said he'll use a mathematical formula to link population increases to the growth in the supply of housing, so Canada's housing stock outpaces the population - something that hasn't happened since the mid-1990s.
Though his office declined to elaborate on his plans, Statistics Canada's numbers and housing forecasts from the Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation suggest that, without a huge increase in construction, Poilievre could be looking at capping annual migration, including non-permanent residents, to approximately 400,000 - a 65 per cent reduction from 2023 figures.
Just a few elections ago, that was unthinkable. During the 2015 campaign, the Liberals and NDP tried to outpromise each other on the number of Syrian refugees each was willing to welcome, while the Conservatives were lambasted for proposing a tip line urging neighbours to report "barbaric cultural practices."
In the years that followed, People's Party of Canada Leader Maxime Bernier remained the only federal leader promising to severely restrict immigration. He is no longer alone.
Public opinion surveys suggest concern over immigration has jumped in the past year.
More people now think immigration is making Canada worse off, said Abacus Data CEO David Coletto. Just under half of respondents believe their community needs fewer immigrants, and only one in four believe more immigrants are needed, he told the Star.
It is a shift from a year ago when support for immigration was at an all-time high,. Blame seems to have landed on the Liberals, not for steadily increasing permanent immigration levels, but for allowing a huge rise in non-permanent residents - 2.5 million in the past two years - that appears to have shifted the social consensus around immigration.
"It's really dangerous in politics to argue with feelings," Immigration Minister Marc Miller recently told the Star's "It's Political" podcast. "Sometimes they reflect a reality, and sometimes not."
Immigrants aren't to blame for rising interest rates or the recent affordability crisis, he said, though the spike in non-permanent residents has affected shelter costs. Still, he argued, other parts of that immigration equation can't be ignored. Newcomers nearly single-handedly have grown the Canadian economy the last few years. A large proportion of nurses and health-care workers in Canadian hospitals are recent arrivals or second-generation immigrants.
"We are an aging population; that's probably the thing that keeps me up at night," Miller said. By 2031, one in four will be over the age of 65, according to Statistics Canada. "If we want that level of services ... a free health-care system - we do need immigrants. But it has to be done in a better way than what we've done in the past."
Miller's list is long.
Statistics Canada - which failed to forecast the boom in population growth - needs to provide more accurate projections, he said. Governments at all levels must do a better job of planning for growth and co-ordinating with each other. Post-secondary schools should ensure proper housing for international students. And the provinces should shoulder more financial responsibility for migrants, he said.
"If you want the ability to select in your town, your city, this type of person, which we don't doubt the labour needs, you also have to be able to accompany them and help them with integration," Miller said, "and not expect the Government of Canada to constantly shovel money over to you every time you ask or scream and yell."
Miller also believes governments and businesses have been "a bit lazy" championing the benefits of immigration. "Every chamber of commerce that I talked to is super bullish on immigration, but they don't go out selling it."
Miller has problems to fix in his own sandbox.
This winter, he brought in changes to the international student program, doubling the amount of money prospective students need to show to come to Canada. He reduced and capped undergraduate study permits to about 360,000 - a 35 per cent drop from 2023 levels, and redistributed those permits across the country, so that B.C. and Ontario have to cut what Miller calls "garbage programs" that offered fake MBAs and a subpar education. He also banned students in public-private college partnerships from accessing postgraduate work permits, as a way of curbing a rapidly-growing sector he said is rife with fraud and abuse.
He plans to make changes in other non-permanent migration streams, too, though those will require more time. Those categories include everything from agricultural workers here for the summer, to temporary foreign workers at Tim Hortons, to the 220,000 Ukrainians who have arrived since Russia's invasion two years ago.
"No one is asking us to take away work permits for the Ukrainians that are here," he noted. "But we have to be quite clear to people that if we do do cuts, it can have a clear impact and make any recession, that we may or may not be in, worse."
The minister also floated the idea of offering low-wage workers citizenship. He noted labour needs exist across the spectrum.
This spring, Miller wants cabinet to offer undocumented workers a path to citizenship. His plan won't make everyone happy, and it is likely to be met with criticism about line-skipping and preferential treatment. "I can't deny that there's a lot of resistance within the system ... perhaps even in our own party," he said. But he feels strongly that people without status, already living here, with housing, working under the table, should be paying taxes.
Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown said he's been impressed by Miller's ability to fight the resistance within government and get results. The former Conservative MP told the Star he approached Miller in August after he was named to the portfolio. "He gave me his cellphone number, said if there's a problem, text me. I'm happy to work co-operatively."
They spent most of their time discussing international students and asylum seekers' needs. Asylum claimants used to account for two per cent of Peel Region's shelter beds, Brown said, but last fall that number jumped to more than 85 per cent. Migrants were sleeping outside. In November, after a Nigerian man died in his makeshift tent, Miller called Brown. "He essentially said, I know you're going to be hard on us," Brown recounted, "but I want you to know, we're going to fix this. Just give me time to figure it out. I'm getting a lot of pushback from my officials, but I'm going to find a way. And he did, almost a week later, he found a solution." Miller came forward with funding for 1,000 new beds and a welcome centre at Pearson Airport that Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow had pitche.
Across the country, in each of the past five months an average of 15,635 asylum claims have been filed, a third more than the same time last year. And although wait times have dropped, there are still 165,000 claims waiting to be processed.
"I don't think anyone credible can tell you they know what the solution is in the long term, given the flows," said Miller. "But there are better things that we can do."
The stakes are high. People need to feel the border is under control, the minister acknowledged, and anti-immigration sentiment is blowing across Europe and the United States. He believes "that consensus" around immigration "is still there."
"What Canadians want is governments to be more organized, more co-ordinated in their way they do immigration," he said. "I think a country like Canada is up to it."
CAPTION: Immigration Minister Marc Miller says newcomers aren't to blame for rising interest rates or the recent affordability crisis.Justin Tang The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Althia raj OPINION

Subject: Citizenship; Foreign students; Interest rates; Migration; Immigrants; Public opinion surveys; Immigration

Document 128 of 500

How much could a Canadian pharmacare plan save you: We looked in this 84-year-old retiree's medicine cabinet to find out

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/how-much-could-canadian-pharmacare-plan-save-you/docview/2931284084/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+much+could+a+Canadian+pharmacare+plan+save+you%3A+We+looked+in+this+84-year-old+retiree%27s+medicine+cabinet+to+find+out&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Momin Malik's medical history belongs in the "Guinness World Records."
The retired doctor has 34 coronary stents - equal to the current world record-holder - and has previously had six heart bypasses and five cardiac arrests. The 84-year-old also has a pacemaker, is a prostate cancer survivor and is currently being treated for lung cancer.
But his medications make an even longer list, some of which come with astronomical prices. For example, Malik's lung cancer pill costs close to $10,000 per month. In the past, the former family physician was faced with $1,500 for a shot he needed every three months for prostate cancer.
Although the Ontario Drug Benefit - a program covering select drugs for Ontarians 65-plus and others eligible - covers the majority of his costs, Malik must still pay a $100 deductible per year plus the costs of medications that aren't covered.
Last year, the Brampton resident used about 20 prescription drugs, for which his annual out-of-pocket bill would easily surpass $2,500. That's not including pharmacy dispensing fees or the costs of over-the-counter drugs such as the Aspirin he has to take daily for his heart, or his allergy medication. And it's despite Malik's decision to stop taking some of his drugs due to side effects or other reasons.
About 63 per cent of Canadians reported paying at least $100 out-of-pocket to fill prescriptions in 2020, per Angus Reid Institute numbers, with 11 per cent paying at least $1,000 that year.
In 2016/17, about four million Ontarians (mostly seniors and people on social assistance), received drugs through provincial coverage programs. That was a jump from 3.1 million a decade prior, according to a report from Ontario's auditor general, which noted the increased use of provincial programs was partly due to the aging population.
On Friday the NDP said it has reached a deal with the governing Liberals to introduce the first piece of a national pharmacare program that includes coverage for birth control and diabetes medication, a first step to potentially wider-ranging drug coverage for Malik and all Canadians.
"I can save a lot of money," said Malik, noting that many older adults can't afford to pay hundreds per month for medication - such as for an anti-cholesterol injection he was taking every two weeks, which normally cost more than $300 each.
The federal Liberals and NDP have agreed to pass legislation outlining the framework for a universal national pharmacare program by March 1. A list of essential medicines that would be covered under the plan would later be developed, according to the agreement.
"The focus in the first instance will be on prescription medicines and related testing devices ... like test strips for diabetes that are really, truly essential to high-performing health care," said Morgan, who researches pharmacare systems, speaking of the shape a pharmacare program would likely take. He is not part of the current policy discussions in the government.
Asked whether over-the-counter drugs might also be covered, Health Canada noted in early February that it's up to provinces to administer their own drug plans, which currently mostly cover prescription drugs. The department also pointed to a 2019 report by a national government advisory council that recommended pharmacare to help increase access to prescription drugs.
It's unlikely for Canada's plan to cover over-the-counter drugs even if they are prescribed, at least at the outset, said Morgan.
But on the flip side, programs such as the Ontario Drug Benefit are at risk of eroding if the federal government doesn't step up to support provinces, Morgan added. Pharmacare drug coverage would likely still be administered provincially, but with federal funds and national minimum standards for everyone, not just select groups.
"TheOntario government would run, essentially, an Ontario Drug Benefit for everybody," Morgan said.
Although it's not known what precise shape the law would take, eliminating the cost of drugs could save Canadians, especially older adults such as Malik and others with complex medical needs, a significant amount of money.
CAPTION: Dr. Momin Malik is a retired doctor who needs 19 medications, some of which are not covered by the Ontario Drug Benefit.Steve RussellToronto Star
CREDIT: Maria Iqbal Toronto Star

Subject: Diabetes; Nonprescription drugs; Older people; Lung cancer; Prescription drugs; Costs; Prostate cancer

Business indexing term: Subject: Costs

Location: Canada

Document 129 of 500

Academic workers could strike on Monda

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/academic-workers-could-strike-on-monda/docview/2931284076/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Academic+workers+could+strike+on+Monda&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Three thousand academic workers at York University are preparing to go on strike Monday morning if the university doesn't come "back to the table with a real plan to address the affordability crisis."
CUPE 3903, the union representing the school's contract instructors, teaching assistants and graduate assistants, says picket lines are expected to go up on Monday if no deal is reached. Workers will be joined by faculty, other campus workers, students and labour leaders at 11 a.m. for a rally outside York University subway station.
On Friday morning, three units of the union voted in favour of taking strike action.
"Members made this very important yet difficult decision because the key bargaining issues, especially those tied to compensation and job security, are about survival," the union said. "After eight months of bargaining, this is the situation we're in: a university run by an administration that will happily give itself massive raises while our members increasingly rely on food banks."
York University's deputy spokesperson, Yanni Dagonas, told the Star, "Following the CUPE 3903 vote, the university and the union met as planned for collective bargaining negotiations, however an agreement has not yet been achieved. Negotiations between the university and the union may continue throughout the weekend and into next week with the agreement of both parties"
The last time York University academic workers went on strike was in 2018, when Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative government legislated them back to work.
At the time of that strike, the province passed Bill 124 restricting wage increases to one per cent per year.
"That law was subsequently struck down by the courts because it was unconstitutional, but York has yet to address the imbalance it created," said the union's press release.
CREDIT: Abhiraj Lamba Toronto Star

Subject: Strikes; Collective bargaining

Document 130 of 500

Why Canada's consensus on immigration is fraying

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-canadas-consensus-on-immigration-is-fraying/docview/2931156759/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+Canada%27s+consensus+on+immigration+is+fraying&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Anti-immigration sentiment used to be politically taboo. Election after election, a majority of voters told party leaders they wanted more immigrants, not fewer. That vision of a Canada welcoming newcomers with open arms, however, is increasingly challenged. Unless governments address a growing perception that unbridled migration is making the country worse off, we may be walking towards a darker, more divisive path, one that makes us less wealthy in the long run.
This week, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre signalled a large reduction in migration numbers will be part of his upcoming platform.
Speaking to reporters in Kitchener, Ont., Poilievre accused Prime Minister Justin Trudeau of ruining the entire immigration system by opening "the floodgates in a way that was disconnected from the number of homes to house people, and the amount of jobs to employ them." He said he'll use a mathematical formula to link population increases to the growth in the supply of housing, so Canada's housing stock outpaces the population - something that hasn't happened since the mid-1990s.
Though his office declined to elaborate on his plans, Statistics Canada's numbers and housing forecasts from the Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation suggest that, without a huge increase in construction, Poilievre could be looking at capping annual migration, including non-permanent residents, to approximately 400,000 - a 65 per cent reduction from 2023 figures.
Just a few elections ago, that was unthinkable. During the 2015 campaign, the Liberals and NDP tried to outpromise each other on the number of Syrian refugees each was willing to welcome, while the Conservatives were lambasted for proposing a tip line urging neighbours to report "barbaric cultural practices" to the RCMP.
In the years that followed, People's Party of Canada Leader Maxime Bernier remained the only federal leader promising to severely restrict immigration.
He is no longer alone.
Public opinion surveys suggest concern over immigration has jumped in the past year.
More people now think immigration is making Canada worse off, said Abacus Data CEO David Coletto. Just under half of respondents believe their community needs fewer immigrants, and only one in four believe more immigrants are needed, he told the Star.
It is a dramatic shift from a year ago when support for immigration was at an all-time high, reflecting a rising trend over the past three decades. Blame seems to have landed on the Liberals, not for steadily increasing permanent immigration levels, but for allowing a huge rise in non-permanent residents - 2.5 million in the past two years - that appears to have shifted the social consensus around immigration.
"It's really dangerous in politics to argue with feelings," Immigration Minister Marc Miller recently told the Star's "It's Political" podcast. "Sometimes they reflect a reality, and sometimes not."
Immigrants aren't to blame for rising interest rates or the recent affordability crisis, he said, though the spike in non-permanent residents has affected shelter costs. Still, he argued, other parts of that immigration equation can't be ignored. Newcomers nearly single-handedly have grown the Canadian economy the last few years. A large proportion of nurses and health-care workers in Canadian hospitals are recent arrivals or second-generation immigrants.
"We are an aging population; that's probably the thing that keeps me up at night," Miller said. By 2031, one in four Canadians will be over the age of 65, according to Statistics Canada. "If we want that level of services ... a free health-care system - we do need immigrants. But it has to be done in a better way than what we've done in the past."
Miller's list is long.
Statistics Canada - which failed to forecast the boom in population growth - needs to provide more accurate projections, he said. Governments at all levels must do a better job of planning for growth and co-ordinating with each other. Post-secondary schools should ensure proper housing for international students. And the provinces should shoulder more financial responsibility for migrants, he said.
"If you want the ability to select in your town, your city, this type of person, which we don't doubt the labour needs, you also have to be able to accompany them and help them with integration," the minister said, "and not expect the Government of Canada to constantly shovel money over to you every time you ask or scream and yell."
Miller also believes governments and businesses have been "a bit lazy" championing the benefits of immigration. "Every chamber of commerce that I talked to is super bullish on immigration, but they don't go out selling it."
Miller has problems to fix in his own sandbox.
This winter, he brought in changes to the international student program, doubling the amount of money prospective students need to show to come to Canada. He reduced and capped undergraduate study permits to about 360,000 - a 35 per cent drop from 2023 levels, and redistributed those permits across the country, so that B.C. and Ontario have to cut what Miller calls "garbage programs" that offered fake MBAs and a subpar education. He also banned students in public-private college partnerships from accessing postgraduate work permits, as a way of curbing a rapidly-growing sector he said is rife with fraud and abuse.
He plans to make changes in other non-permanent migration streams, too, though those will require more time. Those categories include everything from agricultural workers here for the summer, to temporary foreign workers at Tim Hortons, to the 220,000 Ukrainians that have arrived since Russia's invasion two years ago.
"No one is asking us to take away work permits for the Ukrainians that are here," he noted. "But we have to be quite clear to people that if we do do cuts, it can have a clear impact and make any recession, that we may or may not be in, worse."
The minister also floated the idea of offering low-wage workers - and not just high-skilled migrants - citizenship. He noted labour needs exist across the spectrum.
This spring, Miller wants cabinet to offer undocumented workers a path to citizenship. His plan won't make everyone happy, and it is likely to be met with criticism about line-skipping and preferential treatment.
"I can't deny that there's a lot of resistance within the system ... perhaps even in our own party," he said. But he feels strongly that people without status, already living here, with housing, working under the table, should be paying taxes.
Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown said he's been impressed by Miller's ability to fight the resistance within government and get results. The former Conservative MP told the Star he approached Miller in August after he was named to the portfolio. "He gave me his cellphone number, said if there's a problem, text me. I'm happy to work co-operatively."
They spent most of their time discussing international students and asylum seekers' needs. Asylum claimants used to account for two per cent of Peel Region's shelter beds, Brown said, but last fall that number jumped to more than 85 per cent. Shelters were full. Migrants were sleeping outside. In November, after a Nigerian man died in his makeshift tent, Miller called to Brown as he was walking up to his press conference.
"He essentially said, I know you're going to be hard on us," Brown recounted, "but I want you to know, we're going to fix this. Just give me time to figure it out. I'm getting a lot of pushback from my officials, but I'm going to find a way. And he did, almost a week later, he found a solution." Miller came forward with funding for 1,000 new beds and a welcome centre at Pearson Airport that Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow had pitched to address the growing number of arrivals.
Across the country, in each of the past five months an average of 15,635 asylum claims have been filed, a third more than the same time last year. And although wait times have dropped, there are still 165,000 claims waiting to be processed.
"I don't think anyone credible can tell you they know what the solution is in the long term, given the flows," said Miller. "But there are better things that we can do."
The stakes are high. People need to feel the border is under control, the minister acknowledged, and anti-immigration sentiment is blowing across Europe and the United States. He believes "that consensus" around immigration "is still there."
"What Canadians want is governments to be more organized, more co-ordinated in their way they do immigration," he said. "I think a country like Canada is up to it."
CAPTION: Federal Immigration Minister Marc Miller arrives for a meeting of the Liberal caucus on Parliament Hill in Ottawa, on Feb. 14, 2024.

Subject: Citizenship; Foreign students; Migration; Immigrants; Public opinion surveys; Immigration

Business indexing term: Subject: Public opinion surveys

Location: Canada

Company / organization: Name: Statistics Canada; NAICS: 926110

Title: Why Canada's consensus on immigration is fraying
