Document 131 of 500

'Grace and gratitude:' RBC executive's life mantra has taken him around the world ...

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/grace-gratitude-rbc-executives-life-mantra-has/docview/2931156732/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Grace+and+gratitude%3A%27+RBC+executive%27s+life+mantra+has+taken+him+around+the+world+...&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mark Beckles knows a thing or two about business.
His career in financial services and risk management has taken him around the world and back again, from Barbados, where he grew up, to Toronto, which he now calls home, working as VP of social impact and innovation at RBC.
In his youth, however, he travelled mostly in his imagination.
Every summer, his uncle Tony would visit from Canada, toting an armful of Toronto Public Library books for Beckles to read: history, political science, geography and more. Beckles devoured them; they allowed him to travel without ever leaving home.
It was through those books that Beckles learned about South African apartheid, which sparked a lifelong interest in politics, civil rights, and social cohesion.
Met Nelson Mandela several times"Little would I know that in my adult life, I would then have the opportunity to work with the Nelson Mandela Children's Fund, travel to South Africa, and meet Nelson Mandela a number of times," Beckles said.
But that would come later.
He got his start in insurance, moving to Canada to work for Zurich. He returned to the Caribbean and joined CGM Insurance Brokers Limited. In every role, he tried to embody "grace and gratitude in everything," a phrase his mother emphasized.
"One can be a strong leader, and have a strong presence, and still demonstrate empathy, care, (and) the ability to say 'thank you,'" Beckles said. "Those things don't weaken you. I think one of the great challenges that leaders struggle with today is the notion that being human is a weakness, being humane is a weakness. I actually see that as an intangible and immeasurable strength."
Beckles eventually struck out on his own, founding Allied Insurance Brokers Limited, which he grew to a value of $2.5 million and sold.
He left the corporate space to pursue his long-standing passion for philanthropy. He first served as vice president of World Relief Canada, then later became the chief executive of the Nelson Mandela Children's Fund, which works to change the way society treats its children and youth. He met his hero Mandela, who he felt epitomized "grace and gratitude."
Ran for John Tory's provincial PC partyIn 2007, while CEO of the Fund, Beckles ran for the Progressive Conservative party under John Tory's leadership in the riding of Brampton West, losing to Liberal Vic Dhillon.
"I don't think (the loss) was any reflection whatsoever on him," said John Tory, former mayor of Toronto and Beckles' friend. "I think it was just timing."
He added, "I was proud to have him as a candidate because he's an accomplished business person and a person that is deeply committed to the community." Tory said that if Beckles runs again, he suspects he would win.
The two have known each other for 20 years, starting out as colleagues in the political space, but became fast friends.
"You couldn't not be this man's friend," Tory said. "He's just a friendly, open, collegial person ... He naturally gravitates to people, and they gravitate to him."
And Beckles' community involvement and business acumen haven't gone unnoticed. He received the Harry Jerome Award for Professional Excellence in 2015, the Barbados 50 Independence Honours for Community Service in 2016, and the African Canadian Achievement Award in 2017. In 2022, Afroglobal Television named him among the 100 Most Influential Black Canadians.
"He's so respected, not only in Brampton, or in Ontario, or in Canada, but he's highly respected in Barbados and South Africa," said Phyl Durdey, who met Beckles 20 years ago when the two were neighbours in Brampton. Durdey considers Beckles his brother. "It's an honour to know him."
'He knows what he's talking about'Durdey has always been impressed by Beckles' business savvy.
"I always say when I grow up, I want to be more like him," Durdey joked. Beckles and his wife, Wendy, helped Durdey get his successful aircraft mechanic training program, Flightline Training Services, up and running.
Whenever he faced an entrepreneurial challenge, Durdey knew he could call on his friend for advice. "He knows what he's talking about. He's smart, educated. He's right on point."
No matter how high Beckles has climbed in business and philanthropy, dining with Mandela and other leaders, he has remained grounded, Durdey added.
"Mark is Mark ... He's never forgotten where he came from, and he never will."
Indeed, Beckles has never forgotten his upbringing, and those books Uncle Tony bought him from abroad. It showed him the value of educating and empowering young people.
Beckles has continued his youth advocacy at RBC, climbing the ranks to become senior director of youth strategy and innovation and, finally, VP of social impact and innovation. Today, he heads up RBC's 'Future Launch,' a $500 million-philanthropic strategy to prepare youth for the working world.
And Beckles has two key tips for youth looking to make their mark in that world.
'Automation is changing the way we work'First, he recommends looking at data to figure out which sectors will be booming. Then, find out what skills they need to succeed in those jobs.
"The reality is that automation is changing the way we work," Beckles said. "Technology is going to eliminate tons of jobs. But technology is also going to create incrementally more jobs than it eliminates."
Second, young people need to start networking during college and university, not after, when they're looking for a job.
"That network ... (is) going to advocate for you when you graduate," he added. Beckles, who has three children in their twenties, said, "I don't think that young people need to be as despondent (about) the future as sometimes they are. That said: (adults) have to do a better job of helping young people understand where the opportunities of the future are and getting them on paths to prosperity."
Canada could do a much better job at educating its youth, Beckles added, and the importance of education cannot be overstated. "Education is the part that unlocks the potential of a nation."
The fact that Canada lacks a national education policy means the country is reliant on immigration to fill labour shortages, he said.
When he's not advocating for the needs of youth, Beckles unwinds at home jamming out on one of several instruments he plays: drums, bass guitar, or the piano.
With all those instruments, he could form a one-man band.
"I just need a couple more pairs of hands."
CAPTION: Mark Beckles, speaking at a panel discussion at the Los Angeles Times Studio in RBC House, Toronto, last September advises youth to start networking in university."That network... (is) going to advocate for you when you graduate."MarkBeckleslistens to John Tory's concession speech on TV in 2007 in his losing bid to become a PC MPP for Brampton-West."One can be a strong leader, and have a strong presence," says Mark Beckles, "and still demonstrate empathy, care, (and) the ability to say 'thank you.'"
CREDIT: Marisa Coulton Special to the Star

Subject: Leadership; Appointments & personnel changes; Children & youth; Automation; Education; Social impact; Employment

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership Appointments & personnel changes Automation Employment

Location: South Africa; Canada; Barbados

People: Mandela, Nelson (1918-2013)

Title: 'Grace and gratitude:' RBC executive's life mantra has taken him around the world ...

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Feb 24, 2024

Section: business

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document type: News

ProQuest document ID: 2931156732

Document URL: https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/grace-gratitude-rbc-executives-life-mantra-has/docview/2931156732/se-2?accountid=15090

Copyright: Copyright 2024 Toronto Star Newspapers Limited. All Rights Reserved.

Last updated: 2024-02-24

Document 132 of 500

RBC exec lives by 'grace and gratitude'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/rbc-exec-lives-grace-gratitude/docview/2931110225/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=RBC+exec+lives+by+%27grace+and+gratitude%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

He received the Harry Jerome Award for Professional Excellence in 2015, the Barbados 50 Independence Honours for Community Service in 2016 and the African Canadian Achievement Award in 2017. And Beckles has two key tips for youth looking to make their mark in that world.
First, he recommends looking at data to figure out which sectors will be booming. Then, find out what skills they need to succeed in those jobs.
"The reality is that automation is changing the way we work," Beckles said. "Technology is going to eliminate tons of jobs. But technology is also going to create incrementally more jobs than it eliminates."
Second, young people need to start networking during college and university, not after, when they're looking for a job.
"That network ... (is) going to advocate for you when you graduate," he added. Beckles, who has three children in their 20s, said, "I don't think that young people need to be as despondent (about) the future as sometimes they are. That said: (adults) have to do a better job of helping young people understand where the opportunities of the future are and getting them on paths to prosperity."
Canada could do a much better job at educating its youth, Beckles added, and the importance of education cannot be overstated. "Education is the part that unlocks the potential of a nation."
The fact that Canada lacks a national education policy means the country is reliant on immigration to fill labour shortages, he said.
When he's not advocating for the needs of youth, Beckles unwinds at home jamming out on one of several instruments he plays: drums, bass guitar or the piano.
With all those instruments, he could form a one-man band.
"I just need a couple more pairs of hands."
CREDIT: Marisa Coulton Special to the Star

Subject: Leadership; Children & youth; Social impact; Education; Employment

Document 133 of 500

Are millennials ready to rule Canada?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/are-millennials-ready-rule-canada/docview/2931110153/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Are+millennials+ready+to+rule+Canada%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Attention, boomers: it's time. We're going to need you to hand over your classic rock radio stations, and turn over your classic cars. You don't have to cancel the annual winter trip to Palm Springs or Fort Lauderdale, to Mexico, or the Caribbean, but jeez, we barely even have Canadian winters anymore. That's not good news, so don't ask.
But your time in the sun, figuratively, is over. This week, Statistics Canada released a report that showed millennials now outnumber baby boomers in this country; it is the first time boomers have not been our dominant generation since 1958. Boomers have shaped Canada in the postwar era, and dominated society like no generation before or since. The last boomer won't turn 65 for another six years, but this is a seismic shift.
They are the richest generation, with something like a trillion dollars in assets, though much is concentrated toward the top. Their tastes - well, American boomer tastes, as much as anything - have shaped the culture, back when it was still a monoculture. Just about anyone over 60 can easily discuss the M*A*S*H finale, for example; they can commiserate over the end of "The Graduate" or the end of The Beatles. The current generation will have about five memes and Taylor Swift in common, at best.
The reason Canadian millennials finally outnumber boomers is a modern inevitability: immigration. Basically, Canada upped immigration targets to counter an aging population and declining birth rates, and, at the same time, a combination of frugal education funding and laissez-faire regulation meant Ontario's colleges started to cram as many non-permanent residents into their classrooms - and into Canada - as possible. And a lot of those non-permanent residents stayed.
Statistics Canada missed the bulge as it happened, and Canada added a stunning 457,354 millennial permanent and non-permanent residents from 2022 to 2023, in a country whose official annual immigration target for permanent residents in 2024 is 485,000. Meanwhile, the influx of non-permanent residents has nearly doubled since mid-2022. And as a result of all that, Canada got slightly younger for the first time since 1958.
And that's good! Or it should be good, anyway. At the other end of the scale, Japan and China are headed for demographic disaster: Japan's population is forecast to drop from 123 million to as low as 80 million by 2060. China? 1.426 billion to 800 million by the end of the century, even assuming a rise in fertility.
You think the labour market has staff shortages now? You hate it when your plane doesn't have a crew, or the restaurant is scraping by with one hassled server, or you're stranded on a hallway gurney at the ER, waiting for a nurse? Just imagine if a third of the population disappeared.
This is one reason the Trudeau government raised immigration targets: demographics are the math of people. One federal source said the projections indicate that fewer than about 400,000 new Canadians per year means we are more or less setting a time bomb in our economy and society, in a world where so many countries should be competing for immigrants. We probably should, as economist Mike Moffatt and journalist Cara Stern recently wrote, restrict some immigration in response to the housing crisis. Not throttling it, of course. But moderating the flow.
But other than African nations, the combination of aging populations and declining birth rates is a near-global phenomenon, and immigration is needed across the globe. And in Canada, we still have a brand to sell. Great!
Only a few problems. After a few decades of Canadian complacency and creeping frugality - when governments seemed to think we don't need to fix health care, we don't need any more public housing, we can download costs to the provinces, etc. - the bills are currently coming due on housing, health care, long-term care and the environment, for a start. Ah, nuts.
So millennials are inheriting a kingdom in relative disrepair. Millennials are deemed to have been born between 1981 and 1996, and so are about 28 to 43. They won't be as generationally dominant as boomers, but if you are a millennial you may be experiencing waits at the ER, and you may not have a family doctor. Municipal budgets were strafed by COVID and the unhoused population is swelling in towns big and small; cereal and guacamole both cost $10, and the summers are smoky.
And above all, if you're lucky enough to own property you might have a variable mortgage, and if you don't your rent is so high it makes that guacamole price tag really hurt. The generation has been split down the middle by the housing crisis: if you are over 35 you are far more likely to own a house than if you are under 35. The elevator doors closed on many in that generation, and have stayed shut for those who came after. And legal weed only goes so far.
And one result of all this has been a tilt of the federal polls toward the federal Conservatives, partially driven by millennials. The federal Conservatives will not fix the housing crisis, of course; no one government can, and their plans are deemed by experts to be even less adequate than those of the federal Liberals. Do these federal Conservatives seem terribly well-suited to the idea of demographic decision-making, and big, serious thoughts?
But right now they're the natural alternative. So get ready for a shift from classic rock to whatever we listened to in the early 2000s - it was uneven - and maybe for a shift to culture war Canada, where the problems might not get fixed but at least you'll have something irrelevant to blame. Demographics are destiny, some say. We'll find out about ours.
CREDIT: Bruce Arthur Toronto Star OPINION

Subject: Birth rate; Population; Millennials; Baby boomers; Immigration

Location: China; Canada; Japan

Company / organization: Name: Statistics Canada; NAICS: 926110

Document 134 of 500

Ontario repeals wage cap law: Bill 124 limited pay increases for most public-sector workers to 1% per year

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-repeals-wage-cap-law/docview/2931110104/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+repeals+wage+cap+law%3A+Bill+124+limited+pay+increases+for+most+public-sector+workers+to+1%25+per+year&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An ill-fated attempt by Premier Doug Ford to cap the wages of public sector workers has been wiped off the books.
His government has followed through with a promise to repeal its controversial Bill 124 public sector wage restraint legislation, cementing a win for thousands of unionized workers.
Twice struck down by the courts as unconstitutional for restricting collective bargaining rights, the 2019 law limited pay increases for most public sector workers - including hospital nurses, teachers and civil servants - to one per cent annually for three years. The legislation was revoked through an order-in-council, the government said Friday in a terse one-paragraph statement from the office of Treasury Board President Caroline Mulroney. "Repealing the bill will solve the inequality of workers created by the recent court decision," the statement said.
While public sector workers awaited the court decision, labour arbitrators were awarding retroactive pay increases higher than one per cent to some whose bargaining units had "reopener" clauses in their contracts with employers. They include some teachers, nurses, personal support workers, lab technicians and dietary aides.
Many other workers have not been able to make those gains.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy has said the cost of reopeners and wage catch-ups have now been "incorporated" into the government's spending plans.
"After wasting years and countless dollars fighting workers in court, it's time to start fixing the mess Bill 124 created in our hospitals, schools and public services," said New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles, who pressed Premier Doug Ford to apologize earlier this week.
He did not, telling the legislature "we think the world of the nurses."
The Progressive Conservative government signalled earlier this month it would axe Bill 124 after losing an appeal of a 2022 Superior Court decision that struck it down.
"Ontario has not been able to explain why wage restraint could not have been achieved through good-faith bargaining," Ontario's Court of Appeal ruled Feb. 12.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 135 of 500

Ford defends 'patronage' appointments: Premier wants 'like-minded people' selecting Ontario judges

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-defends-patronage-appointments/docview/2931110019/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+defends+%27patronage%27+appointments%3A+Premier+wants+%27like-minded+people%27+selecting+Ontario+judges&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford said Friday he is "100 per cent" OK that his former senior staffers are now in charge of selecting judges in Ontario, doubling down on a move that has been described as "blatant patronage" that risks tainting and politicizing what is supposed to be an independent process.
As the Star exclusively reported Thursday, Ford's former deputy chief of staff, Matthew Bondy, is now the chair of the Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee (JAAC), while Ford's former director of stakeholder relations, Brock Vandrick, has been on the committee since December.
Both men were appointed by Attorney General Doug Downey as public members.
The committee is an arm's-length body that screens applications and makes recommendations to Downey on appointing judges to the Ontario Court of Justice, which handles the bulk of the province's criminal caseload and a large number of family matters.
Speaking at an unrelated announcement in Brampton, Ford said Friday he wants to see "like-minded appointments," and tough jurists on the bench, comments that were met with alarm by legal experts who said they reflected a desire to appoint partisan judges.
Asked by a reporter if he was "OK" with Bondy and Vandrick being on the committee, Ford was unrepentant. "100 per cent I am," the premier said. "We got elected to get like-minded people in appointments ... I'm not going to appoint some NDP or some Liberal."
He went on to say, "every single appointment I can, to find tough judges, tough (justices of the peace) to keep guys in jail ... I'm going to do it.
"I'm appointing like-minded people that believe in what we believe in - keeping the bad guys in jail," he said.
Later Friday, Ford's office told the Star he was not suggesting he wants "like-minded" judges, but rather that he wanted like-minded people on the committee.
"I think it can be charitably described as one of the most disturbing quotes I've heard about our justice system," said Ottawa criminal defence lawyer Michael Spratt, a vocal critic of the government on judicial appointments.
"There's only one reason why you would appoint like-minded people to the committee, and that would be because you want to appoint judges who think like you."
Putting Bondy and Vandrick on the committee could erode public confidence in the justice system, experts have said.
University of Waterloo political science professor Emmett Macfarlane described the moves as "blatant examples of patronage" on Thursday, while Downey's office maintains the pair were appointed due to their public policy experience and volunteer activities.
The naming of the ex-Ford staffers to the committee follows significant changes that Downey has made to the JAAC that were condemned by legal associations as unnecessary and opening the door for patronage appointments to the bench, with critics charging that the changes would water down a process that was once described as the gold standard for appointing judges.
The Federation of Ontario Law Associations, which has a representative on the JAAC, said Ford's comments doubled down "on the position that judges be appointed for political and not merit-based reasons," and his statements "reflect a juvenile understanding" of the independence of the courts.
"The justice system is not a playground for politicians to bully," the association said.
"The rule of law is upheld when judges are placed by merit, not by fiat."
The association said Ford's comments also suggest "that appointed judges were politically biased and take partisan positions that parallel Doug Ford's policies ... All of this undermines public confidence in the administration of justice in a dangerous and anti-democratic way."
Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie urged Ford on Friday to reverse the appointments of Bondy and Vandrick, saying, "We do not want U.S.-style politicization" of Ontario courts.
"Let's just be clear with what Premier Ford is saying here: he deliberately appointed former Conservative staffers to the non-partisan committee that helps pick judicial nominees expressly to achieve desired partisan outcomes," Crombie tweeted.
"These are his own words - and it is entirely unacceptable."
Ford said Friday he's been clear about his position on jurists in Ontario.
"Justices of the peace and judges, they're letting the criminals out," he said.
One of his long-standing talking points on the justice system is that, far too often, jurists let out accused criminals on bail pending trial, and they then go on to reoffend. His comments have come in the wake of several high-profile incidents of people out on bail in Ontario allegedly committing a violent offence.
There is a lack of data nationwide when it comes to issues with bail, something the federal government acknowledged last year when it nonetheless implemented changes to make it harder for certain individuals to be released.
CREDIT: Jacques Gallant Courts and Justice Reporter

Document 136 of 500

Province to boost funding for colleges, universities

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-boost-funding-colleges-universities/docview/2931109963/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+to+boost+funding+for+colleges%2C+universities&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford says a "fabulous" announcement is coming Monday with regards to funding for colleges and universities.
As first reported by the Star, sources say the government is set to provide more than a billion dollars over the next three years, with the money targeted at schools with the greatest need.
The funds, however, are about half of what colleges and universities had hoped for, based on recommendations from an expert panel set up by the province.
Colleges and Universities Minister Jill Dunlop is set to provide further details on Monday.
"The government will be providing over $1.2 billion in new direct financial support," a government source has told the Star, speaking confidentially in order to discuss the plans, including $903 million over three years as well as funds for student mental health and safety.
"The institutions with the highest need will be eligible to receive a portion of that funding that meets the blue-ribbon panel recommendation."
Speaking at an unrelated event on Friday in Brampton, Ford said the federal government "blindsided" the province with its announcement to cap the number of international students, a move he said was like taking "a sledgehammer to the system."
In 2019, the Ford government slashed tuition by 10 per cent and has since implemented a freeze. That, combined with stagnant funding, inflation, unexpected COVID costs as well as salary increases due to now-unconstitutional provincial legislation, has left almost half of the province's universities running deficits and warning of program cuts.
On top of that, colleges and universities are now dealing with the loss of international student tuition revenue as the federal government chops down their numbers this fall - a move that will disproportionately hit Ontario given the explosion in foreign students studying here in recent years - amid a housing shortage and questions about the quality of some of the programs run by public and private college partnerships.
"Don't get me wrong - there's some bad actors in the private sector colleges," Ford said Friday. "But there's some good schools as well. And I just believe in working within the ministry to shoo out the bad actors and encourage the good colleges and universities to keep moving forward because everywhere we go, anywhere in the world ... our number one selling feature are the colleges and universities and the students that are coming out of (those schools)."
Last year, Dunlop tasked a panel of experts to look at the post-secondary sector, and they recommended an immediate 10 per cent boost in government grants, with future increases set at a minimum of two per cent a year, as well as an immediate tuition bump of five per cent this fall, among other increases.
In total, its recommendations came with an estimated price tag of $2.5 billion over three years for the province's 24 publicly assisted colleges and 23 universities.
The funding to be announced is not enough, one post-secondary leader has warned, calling the situation "dangerous" and one that will put schools in further financial jeopardy.
Ford has already ruled out a tuition fee increase this fall.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 137 of 500

How much could a Canadian pharmacare plan save you? We looked in this 84-year-old's medicine cabinet

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-much-could-canadian-pharmacare-plan-save-you/docview/2931178536/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+much+could+a+Canadian+pharmacare+plan+save+you%3F+We+looked+in+this+84-year-old%27s+medicine+cabinet&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Momin Malik's medical history belongs in the "Guinness World Records."
The retired doctor has 34 coronary stents - equal to the current world record-holder - and has previously had six heart bypasses and five cardiac arrests. The 84-year-old also has a pacemaker, is a prostate cancer survivor and is currently being treated for lung cancer.
But his medications make an even longer list, some of which come with astronomical prices. For example, Malik's lung cancer pill costs close to $10,000 per month. In the past, the former family physician was faced with $1,500 for a shot he needed every three months for prostate cancer.
While the Ontario Drug Benefit - a program covering select drugs for Ontarians 65-plus and others eligible - covers the majority of his costs, Malik must still pay a $100 deductible per year plus the costs of medications that aren't covered.
Last year, the Brampton resident used approximately 20 prescription drugs, for which his annual out-of-pocket bill would easily surpass $2,500. That's not including pharmacy dispensing fees or the costs of over-the-counter drugs such as the Aspirin he has to take daily for his heart, or his allergy medication. And it's despite Malik's decision to stop taking some of his drugs due to side effects or other reasons.
Approximately 63 per cent of Canadians reported paying at least $100 out-of-pocket to fill prescriptions in 2020, per Angus Reid Institute numbers, with 11 per cent paying at least $1,000 that year.
In 2016/17, about four million Ontarians (mostly seniors and people on social assistance), received drugs through provincial coverage programs. That was a jump from 3.1 million a decade prior, according to a report from Ontario's auditor general, which noted the increased use of provincial programs was partly due to the aging population.
On Friday the NDP said it has reached a deal with the governing Liberals to introduce the first piece of a national pharmacare program that includes coverage for birth control and diabetes medication, a first step to potentially wider-ranging drug coverage for Malik and all Canadians.
"I can save a lot of money," said Malik, noting that many older adults can't afford to pay hundreds per month for medication - such as for an anti-cholesterol injection he was taking every two weeks which normally cost more than $300 each.
The federal Liberals and NDP have agreed to pass legislation outlining the framework for a universal national pharmacare program by March 1. A list of essential medicines which would be covered under the plan would later be developed, according to the agreement.
"The focus in the first instance will be on prescription medicines and related testing devices ... like test strips for diabetes that are really, truly essential to high performing health care," said Morgan, who researches pharmacare systems, speaking of the shape a pharmacare program would likely take. He is not part of the current policy discussions in the government.
Asked whether over-the-counter drugs might also be covered, Health Canada noted in early February that it's up to provinces to administer their own drug plans, which currently mostly cover prescription drugs. The department also pointed to a 2019 report by a national government advisory council which recommended pharmacare to help increase access to prescription drugs.
It's unlikely for Canada's plan to cover over-the-counter drugs even if they are prescribed, at least at the outset, said Morgan.
But on the flip side, programs such as the Ontario Drug Benefit are at risk of eroding if the federal government doesn't step up to support provinces, Morgan added. Pharmacare drug coverage would likely still be administered provincially, but with federal funds and national minimum standards for everyone, not just select groups.
"The Ontario government would run, essentially, an Ontario Drug Benefit for everybody," Morgan said.
Though it's not known what precise shape the law would take, eliminating the cost of drugs could save Canadians, especially older adults such as Malik and others with complex medical needs, a significant amount of money.
Here's a breakdown of Malik's medications from last year and their approximate prices (Prices may include a markup for the pharmacy to store the medication in its inventory, but do not include dispensing fees).
CAPTION: In 2016/17, about four million Ontarians (mostly seniors and people on social assistance), received drugs through provincial coverage programs. That was a jump from 3.1 million a decade prior, according to a report from Ontario's auditor general, which noted the increased use of provincial programs was partly due to the aging population.Dr. Momin Malik is an 84-year-old retired doctor with a complex medical history. He currently takes 19 medications, some of which are covered by the Ontario Drug Benefit, and some which are not.

Document 138 of 500

Doug Ford government officially repeals provincial wage restraint law Bill 124

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-government-officially-repeals/docview/2930691083/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+government+officially+repeals+provincial+wage+restraint+law+Bill+124&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An ill-fated attempt by Premier Doug Ford to cap the wages of public sector workers has been wiped off the books.
His government has followed through with a promise to repeal its controversial Bill 124 public sector wage restraint legislation, cementing a win for thousands of unionized workers.
Twice struck down by the courts as unconstitutional for restricting collective bargaining rights, the 2019 law limited pay increases for most public sector workers - including hospital nurses, teachers and civil servants - to one per cent annually for three years.
The legislation was revoked through an order-in-council, the government said Friday in a terse one-paragraph statement from the office of Treasury Board President Caroline Mulroney.
"Repealing the bill will solve the inequality of workers created by the recent court decision," the statement said.
While public sector workers awaited the court decision, labour arbitrators were awarding retroactive pay increases higher than one per cent to some whose bargaining units had "reopener" clauses in their contracts with employers. They include some teachers, nurses, personal support workers, lab technicians and dietary aides.
Many other workers have not been able to make those gains.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy has said the cost of reopeners and wage catch-ups have now been "incorporated" into the government's spending plans.
"After wasting years and countless dollars fighting workers in court, it's time to start fixing the mess Bill 124 created in our hospitals, schools and public services," said New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles, who pressed Premier Doug Ford to apologize earlier this week.
He did not, telling the legislature "we think the world of the nurses."
The Progressive Conservative government signalled earlier this month it would axe Bill 124 after losing an appeal of a 2022 Superior Court decision that struck it down.
"Ontario has not been able to explain why wage restraint could not have been achieved through good-faith bargaining," Ontario's Court of Appeal ruled Feb. 12.
It has been blamed for an exodus of health-care workers during the COVID-19 pandemic, where nurses and personal support workers put in long hours and their own health on the line and felt disrespected.
The bill did not apply to police and firefighters, which are both male-dominated fields.
The government's move is one of several recent climbdowns from signature policies, such as its broken plan to open more of the Greenbelt to housing development.
The $8.28-billion land swap scandal is under criminal investigation by the RCMP for "irregularities" that include concerns developers were tipped off in advance.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford during Question Period in the Ontario Legislature on Feb. 20.

Subject: Court decisions; Scandals; Collective bargaining; Public sector; Nurses

Business indexing term: Subject: Collective bargaining Public sector

Title: Doug Ford government officially repeals provincial wage restraint law Bill 124

Document 139 of 500

International student cap 'blindsided' province, Doug Ford says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/international-student-cap-blindsided-province/docview/2930690884/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=International+student+cap+%27blindsided%27+province%2C+Doug+Ford+says&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal government "blindsided" the province with its announcement to cap the number of international students, a move Premier Doug Ford said was like taking "a sledgehammer to the system."
Speaking in Brampton at an unrelated news conference, Ford was also asked about a Star report that the government is set to provide more than a billion dollars in funding to colleges and universities over the next three years, which have been seeking additional funding to help deal with ongoing financial struggles.
Colleges and Universities Minister Jill Dunlop will make a "fabulous" announcement Monday, Ford said.
A government source has told the Star the money will help those schools with the greatest need.
"The government will be providing over $1.2 billion in new direct financial support," the source told the Star, speaking confidentially in order to discuss the plans. "As part of this funding, a new $903-million-dollar fund will be made available to all public colleges and universities" over the three years, the source said, with additional money for student mental health and safety.
The money, however, is far less than schools hoped for, based on recommendations from an expert panel set up by the province that had urged roughly double that in spending over the same time period.
But the government source said "the institutions with the highest need will be eligible to receive a portion of that funding that meets the blue-ribbon panel recommendation."
The overall funding will be contingent on schools proving they are operating as efficiently as possible, other sources told the Star.
In 2019, the Ford government slashed tuition by 10 per cent and then implemented a freeze. That, combined with stagnant funding and inflation, has left almost half of the province's universities running deficits and warning of program cuts.
And now, both colleges and universities are facing the loss of international student tuition revenue as the federal government chops down their numbers - a move that will disproportionately hit Ontario - amid a housing shortage and questions about the quality of some of the programs run by public and private college partnerships.
"Don't get me wrong - there's some bad actors in the private sector colleges," Ford said Friday. "But there's some good schools as well. And I just believe in working within the ministry to shoo out the bad actors and encourage the good colleges and universities to keep moving forward because everywhere we go, anywhere in the world ... our number one selling feature are the colleges and universities and the students that are coming out of (those schools)."
Last year, Dunlop appointed an expert blue-ribbon panel to look at the future of the post-secondary sector. It recommended an immediate 10 per cent boost in government grants, with future increases set at a minimum of two per cent a year, as well as an immediate tuition bump of five per cent this fall, among other increases.
In total, its recommendations came with an estimated price tag of $2.5 billion over three years for the province's 24 publicly assisted colleges and 23 universities.
The report noted a number of auditor general reports warning of low provincial funding for post-secondary institutions - among the lowest in the country - and the risk of reliance on international students as a revenue generator given they pay three to four times the tuition of domestic students.
Schools themselves have said that with no tuition increases, combined with inflation, unexpected COVID-19 costs as well as salary increases for staff in light of the province's unconstitutional wage cap legislation, they are in a financially precarious situation.
"What they are doing is very dangerous for the sector," warned one post-secondary veteran has warned.
Ford has already ruled out a tuition fee increase this fall, and sources have told the Star that is likely to be extended.
Steve Orsini, president and CEO of the Council of Ontario Universities, has said schools are at a "breaking point" that the government must "urgently" address, with 10 of the 23 projecting deficits of a total of $175 million.
CAPTION: Ford has already ruled out a tuition fee increase this fall, and sources have told the Star that is likely to be extended.

Document 140 of 500

Teacher unions slam province: Leaders blame Ford government for staffing shortage felt across Ontario

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/teacher-unions-slam-province/docview/2930423560/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Teacher+unions+slam+province%3A+Leaders+blame+Ford+government+for+staffing+shortage+felt+across+Ontario&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario schools can't recruit or retain enough teachers thanks to a "mess" created by the Ford government, Ontario's four teachers' unions charge.
Union leaders were responding to comments made Wednesday by Education Minister Stephen Lecce, who said the province is considering "every available option" to address a teacher shortage which has left school boards across Ontario scrambling.
They blamed the government for using "Band-Aid" solutions to fill growing gaps, such as extending the number of days retired teachers can work or allowing student-teachers to be at the front of the classroom.
Ontario English Catholic Teachers' Association president René Jansen in de Wal said Lecce could do more to collaborate with educators. "There are things he could do right now that would be far more effective, without anyone's permission ... and he's not doing them," Jansen in de Wal said.
"The Ontario Teachers' Federation has made recommendations to him ... and he doesn't want to act on those things."
In a statement, Lecce noted that teacher shortages are a national problem. Since 2018, he said his government has added nearly 3,000 teachers and "reduced red tape" for new teachers entering the classroom. But NDP Leader Marit Stiles said the shortage "isn't coming out of nowhere" and teachers under Premier Doug Ford's leadership are overburdened.
"People are leaving because they're exhausted, they're disrespected. We need smaller class sizes; we need more educational assistance in our schools. We probably do need to look at other avenues like reducing the number of years in teachers' college," Stiles said.
Teachers need to feel valued, Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation (OSSTF) president Karen Littlewood said.
"In every area of the province, there are multiple schools every day who have unqualified individuals in them who are covering classes," Littlewood said, referring to educational support workings filling in.
New teachers aren't filling full-time positions because they need to commit to one school board early in their careers and transferring to another board later is difficult, Littlewood said. Qualified technology and science teachers are leaving for non-teaching jobs, which offer higher pay and less stress, she said.
CAPTION: Ontario Education Minister Stephen Lecce says since 2018, his government has added nearly 3,000 teachers and "reduced red tape" for new educators entering the classroom. Union leaders, however, blamed the government for using "Band-Aid" solutions to fill growing gaps.Richard Lautens Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Sophia Coppolino Special to the Star

Document 141 of 500

City gets $114M for hitting housing target: New-home starts last year exceeded objective by 51%

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/city-gets-114m-hitting-housing-target/docview/2930422728/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City+gets+%24114M+for+hitting+housing+target%3A+New-home+starts+last+year+exceeded+objective+by+51%25&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto will receive $114 million in new housing cash, the province announced Thursday - a reward for beating its targets for Ontario's 10-year housing goal.
At a news conference at city hall, Premier Doug Ford, alongside Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow and Housing Minister Paul Calandra, lauded the province's largest city, noting Toronto exceeded last year's targets by 51 per cent on the road to the provincially outlined goal of adding 285,000 homes in the city by 2031.
"I'm pleased to announce that Toronto has knocked it out of the park," Ford said, noting the new provincial funding could be used not only for homes, but for infrastructure that could "lay the groundwork" for more housing.
Chow couldn't say exactly how the money would be spent, but called it a "welcomed boost" given the need.
"Is it enough? No. Can we do more? Absolutely. But you have to start. You have to build, and build and build mixed-income (housing) everywhere."
The money comes from a three-year provincial program, through which the province has vowed to give $1.2 billion to cities that meet or exceed their housing targets. Across Ontario, the province has outlined specific goals for its largest and fastest-growing municipalities, as it aims to add 1.5 million homes by 2031.
Although housing starts have risen in Ontario lately, the government's fall economic statement showed it has so far fallen short of the required pace.
According to the Canada Mortgage and Housing Corp., there were 30,516 housing starts within the city of Toronto in 2023. On Thursday, Chow and Ford each suggested the number was higher, citing 31,656 starts. The province's measure includes more than just newly built homes, counting new or upgraded long-term-care beds, as well as units created by renovation or conversions.
Although CMHC has warned that home construction lagged countrywide in January, Toronto bucked the trend, with The Canadian Press reporting its annual rate of housing starts went up 179 per cent.
But the city remains in a severe home affordability crunch, with Chow noting the subsidized housing wait-list is now more than 85,000 households long.
The mayor pointed to the story of Aryna, a seven-year-old child profiled by the Star as part of its the Kids Aren't Alright series. Aryna's family relies on a food bank as rent eats up most of their income.
"People in our city are finding it harder than ever to find a home they can afford," Chow said.
As the province revealed the new funding for Toronto, Calandra acknowledged some cities have struggled with using home starts as a measure of progress, with parts of York Region and Windsor needing to build infrastructure first.
However, Ford offered sharp words for cities falling short of their targets, taking particular aim at Mississauga - where former mayor Bonnie Crombie is expected to face Ford in the next election as leader of the Ontario Liberals.
Mississauga is one of 24 Ontario municipalities that, according to the province's tracking, did not meet its provincial housing targets for 2023. Seven municipalities are considered to be on track, hitting at least 80 per cent of their goals, while Toronto and 18 other municipalities were considered to be ahead of schedule.
CREDIT: Victoria Gibson Toronto Star

Document 142 of 500

Ex-Ford staffers now pick judges: 'Blatant examples of patronage' among charges as critics fear perception of politicized process

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ex-ford-staffers-now-pick-judges/docview/2930422714/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ex-Ford+staffers+now+pick+judges%3A+%27Blatant+examples+of+patronage%27+among+charges+as+critics+fear+perception+of+politicized+process&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The inclusion of two of Premier Doug Ford's former senior staffers on the committee that recommends which lawyers should become Ontario judges is raising concerns that the independent process has been tainted and politicized.
Ford's former deputy chief of staff Matthew Bondy has been the chair of the Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee (JAAC) since Feb. 1. It's an arm's-length body that screens applications and makes recommendations to the attorney general on who should be appointed to the Ontario Court of Justice.
Bondy is one of seven public members appointed to the 13-person committee by Attorney General Doug Downey, who ultimately appoints almost all of the committee members following changes made by the Ford government in 2021.
Brock Vandrick, Ford's former director of stakeholder relations, has also been a public member since December.
Legal experts and law associations have expressed concern that the appointments of ex-senior staffers in the current premier's office will lead to the perception that the appointment process for judges has been politicized, and will erode public trust in the justice system.
"This casts a cloud over the JAAC's mandate to ensure judges will be appointed free from partisan political considerations," said a statement from the Federation of Ontario Law Associations, which has a representative on the committee.
"JAAC's independence and the public's confidence is eroded when political influence appears to be driving the process by which our judges are selected. We call on the government to be respectful of the importance of this committee and the hard work it is called on to do for Ontarians."
The Ontario Court of Justice is one of the busiest courts in Canada, handling the bulk of the province's criminal caseload, as well as a large number of family matters.
"I'm not sure I've seen such blatant examples of patronage recently," University of Waterloo political science professor Emmett Macfarlane said.
The naming of Bondy and Vandrick to the committee follows significant changes that Downey has made to the JAAC that were condemned by legal associations as unnecessary and opening the door for patronage appointments to the bench, with critics charging that the changes would water down a process that was once described as the gold standard for appointing judges.
It also follows Downey's run-in with the association that represents provincial court judges. In 2022, the association rang alarm bells over his new process for selecting the court's next chief justice, describing it as an "aberration" that violated the separation of powers between the executive and judicial branches of government.
"This is exactly what we feared would happen when the attorney general decided to make changes to what was once one of the best judicial appointment processes in the world," said George Thomson, a former judge and ex-deputy attorney general of Ontario and Canada, responding to the appointments of Bondy and Vandrick.
"It seems clear that a decision has been made to politicize the formerly independent judicial appointments committee."
Downey's spokesperson, Andrew Kennedy, said Bondy and Vandrick "were chosen due to their extensive public policy experience and volunteer activities. The committee, not individual members, recommends candidates to the attorney general following an in-depth application process."
Bondy, who declined a request for comment, is currently vice-president for national public affairs at Enterprise Canada, while Vandrick is senior vice-president at Wellington Dupont Public Affairs. He did not return a request for comment.
While emphasizing he doesn't know Bondy or Vandrick, the perception caused by their appointments to the committee is that "this is a resting sinecure for political staffers," said Frank Addario, a former president of the Criminal Lawyers' Association and former vice-president of the Canadian Civil Liberties Association.
"Even if the individuals on the JAAC, or the output of the JAAC, are high-quality, it will be tainted by the perception that it's controlled by the premier's office and the attorney general's office for political ends," he said.
"We had a great non-partisan model for almost 35 years; it had tremendous output in terms of quality and there was not even a whiff of the 'gravy train.' By conspicuously politicizing it, they're debasing the currency of the judicial appointments."
Macfarlane said that the Ford government "is not alone in allowing partisanship" to affect the membership of bodies that are supposed to be providing independent advice to government, including on the appointment of judges.
Given that the government is accountable for the appointment of judges, "some might argue having people who have served as political staff on these committees is appropriate," he continued. "But I think there is a legitimate concern that explicit partisan considerations might politicize judicial appointments in ways we would want to avoid."
He pointed to the United States, where he said the public perception of the legitimacy of its Supreme Court appears to be at an all-time low, "largely as a result of the extreme partisanship around its appointments, which invariably leads to lower-quality candidates."
Downey sparked the ire of most of Ontario's main legal organizations after becoming attorney general in 2019, when he began to contemplate major changes to the judicial appointment process that none of them had asked for.
He increased the number of judicial candidates that he's allowed to see from the committee. Advocates warned that the change gives the government wider access to the entire application pool, running the risk that lower-quality candidates get appointed to the bench.
Downey also gave himself more power over the composition of the appointments committee, which includes three provincial court judges, three lawyers representing legal organizations, and seven public members named by him.
Downey took away the ability of the legal organizations - the Law Society of Ontario, the Ontario Bar Association, and the Federation of Ontario Law Associations - to name their own representatives. They must now each submit a list of three candidates for his selection.
There is currently no representative on the committee from the Law Society. The legal regulator refused to tell the Star if they have even submitted a list of candidates to Downey, saying the process is "ongoing and confidential." Downey's office confirmed, however, that the Law Society did send a list, and that he's reviewing it.
The presence of former political staffers on the committee does not "automatically jeopardize" the principles of having high-quality judges and non-partisan decision-making, the president of the Ontario Bar Association said in a statement.
Kelly McDermott said such individuals "may provide an understanding of the diversity and complexity of Ontario ... The measure of whether appointments to the JAAC compromise fundamentals will be the quality, diversity, and timeliness" of appointments to the bench.
"And we will be watching this closely."
CREDIT: Jacques Gallant Courts and Justice Reporter

Subject: Committees; Patronage; Political appointments; Law; Attorneys general; Judicial appointments; Associations; Partisanship; Politics; Judges & magistrates

Location: Canada

Document 143 of 500

Why are Ford's Tories obsessed with Crombie?: Liberal leader is under attack by Conservatives in the legislature

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-are-fords-tories-obsessed-with-crombie/docview/2930422664/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+are+Ford%27s+Tories+obsessed+with+Crombie%3F%3A+Liberal+leader+is+under+attack+by+Conservatives+in+the+legislature&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Call it the Conservatives' Crombie compulsion.
Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives have spent the early days of the new legislative session obsessed with rookie Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie.
Complementing their current multimillion-dollar attack advertising blitz - paid for by the PC party, not the provincial treasury - the Tories used the legislature's daily question period to blast Crombie's record as a three-term Mississauga mayor and one-term Liberal MP.
The governing party even inserted a section in omnibus legislation specifically designed to paint her into a corner over the federal Liberals' carbon pricing regimen.
Under the Get It Done Act, tabled Tuesday, a future premier would be required to hold a referendum before imposing any carbon tax.
Although Crombie dismissed that as a "stunt," Ford's Tories were delighted by her repeated refusal to say whether she supported Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's carbon levy during a live interview with Vassy Kapelos on CTV Power Play that day.
"As I said, there are many tools to combat climate change," the Liberal leader told Kapelos, declining to elaborate on what action a provincial Liberal government would take if elected in 2026.
"We're going to do our homework - as we should - before we announce any new policies," she said.
Replied Kapelos: "The question I had posed to you was how long do you plan to consult before you come to a decision on where you stand? Is it until the next election?"
Crombie would only say, "We will come to the right decision ..."
In the legislature, the Tories crowed about her stance.
"One party has remained steadfast in their support for the federal carbon tax, and that's Bonnie Crombie and the Liberal Party of Ontario," said Energy Minister Todd Smith.
Smith did not mention the two carbon pricing schemes the Tories have introduced - the 2022 emission performance standards program that targets industrial polluters and his own clean energy credit registry cap-and-trade system he brought in last year.
Associate Housing Minister Rob Flack signalled Wednesday that Crombie's tenure as Mississauga mayor would also be relitigated in the coming months and years.
"Mayor Crombie had the worst housing record in the province of Ontario. Mayor Crombie's housing record was abysmal," Flack told the legislature, pointing to two high-density housing projects Mississauga council had opposed.
"Ontario families cannot afford Bonnie Crombie," he said.
Crombie countered that when she was mayor, her council "dramatically improved zoning laws to add unlimited density in our downtown, to build beautiful complete communities on our waterfront by reclaiming old industrial lands, and to allow fourplexes."
At a Toronto city hall news conference on Thursday, Ford, whose Tories lead the Liberals in every major public-opinion poll, railed against Crombie, insisting he is not afraid of facing off against her in the next election.
"The mayor failed, Bonnie Crombie failed. She had the lowest housing starts of anyone in Ontario - that's unacceptable," the premier told reporters.
"Bonnie Crombie raised taxes to the highest they've ever been in Mississauga," he continued, as Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow stood patiently behind him.
"She is running to be the premier and we need (a) clear and decisive decision from her: Is she for taxing people, is she for the carbon tax or isn't she?" said Ford, adding Crombie can't "dodge and weave" now that she's moved "into the big leagues."
For her part, the Liberal leader said the premier is scrambling to "deflect attention" from the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal now under investigation by the RCMP.
"This preoccupation with me - some call it an obsession - is just weird, it's odd," Crombie said in an interview.
"I'm not going to play by his rules. I'm not going to be pushed around. He wants to box me in, but I'm going to be setting my own agenda," she said.
"He may be used to weak opposition, but there's a new sheriff in town."
A senior Conservative source, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations, suggested Crombie and Ford "live rent-free in each other's heads" because they each recognize the electoral threat the other poses.
There are almost a dozen Peel Region Tory MPPs skittish about facing off against a Liberal party led by a former mayor who won 78.5 per cent of the popular vote in the 2022 Mississauga election.
When the Liberals were in power from 2003 until 2018 under premiers Dalton McGuinty and Kathleen Wynne, those kind of suburban ridings were reliably Grit.
Since then, those seats have been key to successive Tory victories.
While most Ontarians may not be thinking about the June 2026 election, Ford certainly is.
"People will have a decision in a couple of years," the premier said Thursday. " And it's going to be a very clear decision."
CAPTION: In addition to an attack advertising blitz, Ontario Conservatives have used the daily question period to blast Bonnie Crombie's record as a three-term Mississauga mayor and one-term MP.Steve RussellToronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 144 of 500

The Ontario government will provide more than a billion dollars ...[Derived Headline]

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-government-will-provide-more-than-billion/docview/2930422657/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Ontario+government+will+provide+more+than+a+billion+dollars+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government will provide more than a billion dollars in funding to colleges and universities over the next three years, the Star has learned.
A government source told the Star the money will help those schools with the greatest need.
"The government will be providing over $1.2 billion in new direct financial support," the source said. "As part of this funding, a new $903-million dollar fund will be made available to all public colleges and universities" over the three years, the source added.
The announcement is expected to be made next week by Colleges and Universities Minister Jill Dunlop.
The money, however, would be far less than schools hoped for, based on recommendations from an expert panel set up by the province.
Sources were speaking confidentially in order to discuss the planned announcement.
The government source said "the institutions with the highest need will be eligible to receive a portion of that funding that meets the blue-ribbon panel recommendation."
Other sources said they also expect no tuition fee increases for up to three years - well past the next provincial election.
The announcement of the new funding is expected to include additional grants for student mental health and safety.
The overall funding will be contingent on schools proving they are operating as efficiently as possible, the sources said.
In 2019, the Ford government slashed tuition by 10 per cent and then implemented a freeze. That, combined with stagnant funding and inflation, has left almost half of the province's universities running deficits and warning of program cuts.
And now, both colleges and universities are facing the loss of international student tuition revenue as the federal government clamps down on their numbers.
Last year, Dunlop appointed an expert blue-ribbon panel to look at the future of the post-secondary sector, which recommended an immediate 10 per cent boost in government grants, with future increases set at a minimum of two per cent a year, as well as an immediate tuition bump of five per cent this fall, among other increases.
In total, its recommendations came with an estimated price tag of $2.5 billion over three years for the province's 24 publicly assisted colleges and 23 universities.
The report noted a number of auditor general reports warning of low provincial funding for post-secondary institutions - among the lowest in the country - and the risk of reliance on international students as a revenue generator given they pay three to four times the tuition of domestic students.
Schools themselves have said that with no tuition increases, combined with inflation, unexpected COVID-19 costs as well as salary increases for staff in light of the province's unconstitutional wage cap legislation, they are in a financially precarious situation.
"What they are doing is very dangerous for the sector," warned one post-secondary veteran.
Premier Doug Ford has been clear he doesn't support any tuition increase for domestic students this year, saying the current affordability crisis makes it unfair.
That's something NDP Leader Marit Stiles agrees with, but says that means the government has to step up.
"Our colleges and universities are deeply underfunded, funded at a lower rate by this government than any other province in Canada," she said. "The obvious answer is let's start to invest ... at a significant rate if you're going to make up the difference and not see post-secondary institutions diminished."
Steve Orsini, president and CEO of the Council of Ontario Universities, has repeatedly warned that schools need the grants and tuition increases recommended by Dunlop's own appointed experts.
The panel also noted that government per-funding student for college students in 2021-22 was $6,891 in Ontario compared to an average of $15,615 nationally, and $11,471 for universities compared to $20,772 across Canada.
The report also noted the risk of relying on international student revenue. Then, last month, the federal government announced cuts to the number of new study permits issued to foreign students by 35 per cent, with spots to be allotted based on each province's population. That move will disproportionately hit Ontario, where more than half of Canada's current international students study.
Almost half of Ontario's universities are now in the red, with schools warning that student services will face cuts if the government does not provide a bump in funding and also allow tuition to rise as the panel recommended.
Orsini has said the government must "urgently" address universities' financial situations, with 10 of the 23 projecting deficits of a total of $175 million.
"Universities are at a breaking point now with years of declining real, per-student funding," Orsini has said, adding that they are already looking for - and implementing - efficiencies.
The blue-ribbon panel did not find that salaries were out of line, but did say there was "considerable room" for savings in administrative costs.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 145 of 500

Bill aims to overturn gas rate ruling: Critics of province's move say energy board decision would encourage installation of heat pumps

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/bill-aims-overturn-gas-rate-ruling/docview/2930422625/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bill+aims+to+overturn+gas+rate+ruling%3A+Critics+of+province%27s+move+say+energy+board+decision+would+encourage+installation+of+heat+pumps&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's government is having gas pains in its bid to keep new houses affordable.
Critics say natural gas customers will get them, too, after Energy Minister Todd Smith introduced legislation to overturn an Ontario Energy Board (OEB) ruling on Enbridge gas rates - something industry observers called an "unprecedented" move.
The bill is intended to reverse a December decision by the arms-length board that developers should pay the $4,400 cost of connecting new homes to gas lines up front instead of amortizing the cost over 40 years and spreading it among all users, as has long been the standard.
"The OEB strayed out of their lane on this," the minister said on Wednesday, calling the decision "disappointing" and noting it was opposed by one member of the board.
Smith maintained the upfront cost of $4,400 - growing much higher in rural areas with longer connection distances - would add to housing prices at a time when many people are struggling to make down payments.
"As the province navigates difficult headwinds like high interest rates and inflationary pressures on the costs of building materials ... it's important we don't allow new costs to be piled on top of that."
But critics said the energy board ruling was forward-looking and would encourage homebuilders to install heat pumps as the world increasingly moves away from fossil fuels to fight climate change.
"It was about reducing rates for existing gas users and saving money for new homeowners who would pay less if they have a heat pump instead of a gas furnace," said Keith Brooks of the interest group Environmental Defence.
In its ruling, the board said amortizing the cost of a connection over 40 years would leave homeowners with a "stranded asset" as many get off natural gas with the province moving toward electrification to reduce greenhouse gas emissions. Heat pumps tend to be more energy-efficient than fossil fuel-based systems, are powered by electricity, and provide heating in cold weather and cooling on hot days.
Paying the cost of a gas connection up front would motivate homebuilders "to choose the most cost-effective, energy-efficient choice," the board said.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said Smith's legislation is "outrageous" and New Democrat MPP Peter Tabuns (Toronto-Danforth) said Smith is missing a golden opportunity to address "the realities of climate change."
"We the people of Ontario, who are ratepayers, are going to be subsidizing Enbridge's expansion of fossil gas infrastructure to install heating systems that will be obsolete," Schreiner told reporters.
"We should be installing the cleanest and lowest-cost options for heating and cooling new homes, even if that makes some of the Ford government's well-connected friends at Enbridge and in the property development industry unhappy," Keith Stewart of Greenpeace said in a statement.
Smith insisted the board's decision - reached after months of hearings - was "rushed" and "irrational" without broad-based consultation involving more from the building industry and without input from Ontario's Independent Electricity System Operator about the impact on electricity use and available supply.
That could be "significant," Smith said, noting about 70 per cent of Ontarians still use natural gas to heat their homes and water.
He announced the government will be appointing a new chair to the board this spring. The post is now vacant.
"I'll expect our appointee to help ensure that the board conducts appropriate consultation before reaching any decisions ... we have to make sure this doesn't happen again."
That smacks of "political interference," said Brooks and Schreiner.
"Appoint a qualified expert so the board can maintain its independence. It's an independent entity designed to protect the people of Ontario," the Green leader added.
Presuming the legislation is passed, the Enbridge application will go back before the board for reconsideration, Smith said, acknowledging the 40-year amortization period could be "reduced" but not committing to accepting that.
"I don't want to get into hypotheticals."
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau
