Poilievre's image, political theatre at Queen's Park and Toronto's parking fines

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/poilievres-image-political-theatre-at-queens-park/docview/2930422542/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre%27s+image%2C+political+theatre+at+Queen%27s+Park+and+Toronto%27s+parking+fines&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Poilievre needs a new image
Online harms bill coming next week, Feb. 21
Federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is seen by some as a scrappy tough guy, but remove the "s" and that is exactly what the rest of us think of him. For Canada to move ahead and put the things that need addressing into action, the opposition party needs to replace him with a positive head, if they can find one in there somewhere.
Chris Andrews, Vaughan
Recently the prime minister made comments about the online harms bill, an important piece of legislation as we have a track record to look at the devastation online harassment and hate have caused, including suicide. Taking action is worthy of prompt consideration. Yet it didn't take long for Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre to give his views, even though the bill has yet to be revealed in content or advanced detail. He found it appropriate to decry the prime minister's character and say that Trudeau should "ask himself why he was such a hateful racist," obliquely referring to the incidents of the prime minister wearing blackface who, with embarrassed acknowledgment, did apologize. I expect that Poilievre does have his own remedies to the online issues. Too bad we don't hear them. We only hear his diatribe and acidity, his way of belittling and denying his opponent(s).
Two bits of retrospection here: "Have you no decency, sir?" (Robert Welch, 1954) and "he has not one single redeeming defect"(Gladstone comment directed at political opponent Disraeli). The Welch comment is an assessment of character and the Gladstone comment shows what can be done with the language to fire off a nuanced zinger with class and style. Learn from it.
Paul Simpson, Stratford, Ont.
We don't have time for political theatre
Doug Ford's omnibus bill is political theatre: Editorial, Feb. 22
You really have to hand it to the provincial Conservatives. Their naming of legislative bills is often the complete opposite of the bill's intention. If you're going to stretch the truth, pull hard! But seriously, the ethically-challenged Conservatives understand the attention span of an electorate that likes to read only headlines before moving on to the funnies. Fewer and fewer read the whole article to realize the audacious deceit that the spin doctors have spun. There is a solution. If we cannot muster the courage for electoral reform, perhaps the next government, surely not to be Conservative, should enact legislation to outlaw spin doctors, thus putting the Conservative party on the hook to pay off a myriad of broken lifetime, personal service contracts. How do they sleep?
David Ottenbrite, Cambridge, Ont.
Despite the bill's title, Ontarians cannot really "get it done" completely until it votes these scoundrels out of office. Their abuse of parliamentary oversight is simply breathtaking.
Greg Sorbara, Toronto
It is refreshing to see that finally light is being shone on the vile practice of omnibus bills used by both the federal and provincial governments. While these bill are useful for amending several bills at one time for housekeeping issues or other issues that have a common element, using them to pass very different pieces of legislation is abhorrent. This soup to nuts approach is a deliberate tactic to suppress opposition to a specific policy. While the government side of the chamber will favour both the soup and the nuts, those on the other side of the aisle may like the soup but not the nuts. Thus, they are placed in a "damned if you do and damned if you don't situation." They are being forced either to pass bills that would fail if tabled on their own or defeat bills that have extreme merit. The cutesy titles now being given to bills only add to my annoyance. While I got a chuckle with titles quoted in the editorial, I think "Poke the Opposition in the Eye Act" is too polite for these times. I suggest "A Middle Finger to the Opposition Act" is more descriptive.
Tom Driedger, Etobicoke
Upping parking fines is murky territory
Toronto parking fines could more than double, Feb. 21
Looks like the mayor and council is considering the increase of parking fines from $30 to $75. Be assured that this will happen and probably by the summer. It will be just one more nail in the coffin to ensure that people across the GTA will no longer be coming downtown to support stores and merchants.
Marty Fruchtman, Toronto
The proposed fine increases are a step in the right direction but the new amounts still seem inadequate to me. The fine for TTC fare evasion can reach $425. Why shouldn't parking fines reach similar levels? It would only be fair.
John Gavin, Toronto
Help family doctors feel valued
As family doctors our prescription for residents is to not set up a practice in Ontario, Feb. 20
As a patient and caregiver, I want to make it clear how much I value family doctors. We need more young, innovative family docs in Ontario, not fewer. There are challenges in family care - big challenges - but there are solutions to those challenges - leaving or discouraging new family doctors from setting up a practice will not get us to solutions and will cause harm. I don't understand how the suggested "prescription" in this opinion piece will lead to any meaningful system change.
I offer another prescription - talk to the patients you care for, or will care for one day. They will not only express their gratitude and tell you about their dependence on family doctors, they will offer insights about the existing strengths of the system and about how we can leverage communities to advocate for primary care.
Please stay.
Maggie Keresteci, Elmira, Ont.
We need you, millennials
Millennials will finally outnumber boomers. We are going to need them and their contributions since we are unprepared for the "grey tsunami," including not enough hospital beds, long-term-care beds, community care staff, primary care physicians and nurses. We better start planning now before the millennials start to retire. Learn from past mistakes? Sadly, I doubt it. We had 60 years to plan for the current crisis and did nothing to be prepared for our aging generation. Good luck millennials! Over to you.
William Kennedy, Burlington, Ont.
CAPTION: Asked about online harms bill, federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre said Prime Minister Justin Trudeau "should look into his own heart and ask himself why he was such a hateful racist."

Document 147 of 500

Are millennials ready to rule Canada? The transition will be anything but smooth

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/are-millennials-ready-rule-canada-transition-will/docview/2930420879/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Are+millennials+ready+to+rule+Canada%3F+The+transition+will+be+anything+but+smooth&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Attention, boomers: it's time. We're going to need you to hand over your classic rock radio stations, and turn over your classic cars. You don't have to cancel the annual winter trip to Palm Springs or Fort Lauderdale, to Mexico, or the Caribbean, but jeez, we barely even have Canadian winters anymore. That's not good news, so don't ask.
But your time in the sun, figuratively, is over. This week, Statistics Canada released a report that showed millennials now outnumber baby boomers in this country; it is the first time boomers have not been our dominant generation since 1958. Boomers have shaped Canada in the postwar era, and dominated society like no generation before or since. The last boomer won't turn 65 for another six years, but this is a seismic shift.
They are the richest generation, with something like a trillion dollars in assets, though much is concentrated toward the top. Their tastes - well, American boomer tastes, as much as anything - have shaped the culture, back when it was still a monoculture. Just about anyone over 60 can easily discuss the M*A*S*H finale, for example; they can commiserate over the end of "The Graduate" or the end of The Beatles. The current generation will have about five memes and Taylor Swift in common, at best.
The reason Canadian millennials finally outnumber boomers is a modern inevitability: immigration. Basically, Canada upped immigration targets to counter an aging population and declining birthrates, and, at the same time, a combination of frugal education funding and laissez-faire regulation meant Ontario's colleges started to cram as many non-permanent residents into their classrooms - and into Canada - as possible. And a lot of those non-permanent residents stayed.
Statistics Canada missed the bulge as it happened, and Canada added a stunning 457,354 millennial permanent and non-permanent residents from 2022 to 2023, in a country whose official annual immigration target for permanent residents in 2024 is 485,000. Meanwhile, the influx of non-permanent residents has nearly doubled since mid-2022. And as a result of all that, Canada got slightly younger for the first time since 1958.
And that's good! Or it should be good, anyway. At the other end of the scale, Japan and China are headed for demographic disaster: Japan's population is forecast to drop from 123 million to as low as 80 million by 2060. China? 1.426 billion to 800 million by the end of the century, even assuming a rise in fertility.
You think the labour market has staff shortages now? You hate it when your plane doesn't have a crew, or the restaurant is scraping by with one hassled server, or you're stranded on a hallway gurney at the ER, waiting for a nurse? Just imagine if a third of the population disappeared.
This is one reason the Trudeau government raised immigration targets: demographics are the math of people. One federal source said the projections indicate that fewer than about 400,000 new Canadians per year means we are more or less setting a time bomb in our economy and society, in a world where so many countries should be competing for immigrants. We probably should, as economist Mike Moffatt and journalist Cara Stern recently wrote, restrict some immigration in response to the housing crisis. Not throttling it, of course. But moderating the flow.
But other than African nations, the combination of aging populations and declining birthrates is a near-global phenomenon, and immigration is needed across the globe. And in Canada, we still have a brand to sell. Great!
Only a few problems. After a few decades of Canadian complacency and creeping frugality - when governments seemed to think we don't need to fix health care, we don't need any more public housing, we can download costs to the provinces, etc. - the bills are currently coming due on housing, health care, long-term care and the environment, for a start. Ah, nuts.
So millennials are inheriting a kingdom in relative disrepair. Millennials are deemed to have been born between 1981 and 1996, and so are about 28 to 43. They won't be as generationally dominant as boomers, but if you are a millennial you may be experiencing waits at the ER, and you may not have a family doctor. Municipal budgets were strafed by COVID and the unhoused population is swelling in towns big and small; cereal and guacamole both cost $10, and the summers are smoky.
And above all, if you're lucky enough to own property you might have a variable mortgage, and if you don't your rent is so high it makes that guacamole price tag really hurt. The generation has been split down the middle by the housing crisis: if you are over 35 you are far more likely to own a house than if you are under 35. The elevator doors closed on many in that generation, and have stayed shut for those who came after. And legal weed only goes so far.
And one result of all this has been a tilt of the federal polls toward the federal Conservatives, partially driven by millennials. The federal Conservatives will not fix the housing crisis, of course; no one government can, and their plans are deemed by experts to be even less adequate than those of the federal Liberals. Do these federal Conservatives seem terribly well-suited to the idea of demographic decision-making, and big, serious thoughts?
But right now they're the natural alternative. So get ready for a shift from classic rock to whatever we listened to in the early 2000s - it was uneven - and maybe for a shift to culture war Canada, where the problems might not get fixed but at least you'll have something irrelevant to blame. Demographics are destiny, some say. We'll find out about ours.
CAPTION: A screen grab from the Canadian game show "Battle of the Generations" hosted by Lilly Singh.

Subject: Population; Millennials; Baby boomers; Decades; Immigration

Document 148 of 500

Are Doug Ford's Conservatives obsessed with Bonnie Crombie?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/are-doug-fords-conservatives-obsessed-with-bonnie/docview/2930420868/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Are+Doug+Ford%27s+Conservatives+obsessed+with+Bonnie+Crombie%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Call it the Conservatives' Crombie compulsion.
Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives have spent the early days of the new legislative session obsessed with rookie Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie.
Complementing their current multimillion-dollar attack advertising blitz - paid for by the PC party, not the provincial treasury - the Tories used the legislature's daily question period to blast Crombie's record as a three-term Mississauga mayor and one-term Liberal MP.
The governing party even inserted a section in omnibus legislation specifically designed to paint her into a corner over the federal Liberals' carbon pricing regimen.
Under the Get It Done Act, tabled Tuesday, a future premier would be required to hold a referendum before imposing any carbon tax.
While Crombie dismissed that as a "stunt," Ford's Tories were delighted by her repeated refusal to say whether she supported Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's carbon levy during a live interview with Vassy Kapelos on CTV Power Play that day.
"As I said, there are many tools to combat climate change," the Liberal leader told Kapelos, declining to elaborate on what action a provincial Liberal government would take if elected in 2026.
"We're going to do our homework - as we should - before we announce any new policies," she said.
Replied Kapelos: "The question I had posed to you was how long do you plan to consult before you come to a decision on where you stand? Is it until the next election?"
Crombie would only say, "We will come to the right decision..."
In the legislature, the Tories crowed about her stance.
"One party has remained steadfast in their support for the federal carbon tax, and that's Bonnie Crombie and the Liberal Party of Ontario," said Energy Minister Todd Smith.
Smith did not mention the two carbon pricing schemes the Tories have introduced - the 2022 emission performance standards program that targets industrial polluters and his own clean energy credit registry cap-and-trade system he brought in last year.
Associate Housing Minister Rob Flack signalled Wednesday that Crombie's tenure as Mississauga mayor would also be relitigated in the coming months and years.
"Mayor Crombie had the worst housing record in the province of Ontario. Mayor Crombie's housing record was abysmal," Flack told the legislature, pointing to two high-density housing projects Mississauga council had opposed.
"Ontario families cannot afford Bonnie Crombie," he said.
Crombie countered that when she was mayor, her council "dramatically improved zoning laws to add unlimited density in our downtown, to build beautiful complete communities on our waterfront by reclaiming old industrial lands, and to allow fourplexes."
At a Toronto city hall news conference on Thursday, Ford, whose Tories lead the Liberals in every major public-opinion poll, railed against Crombie, insisting he is not afraid of facing off against her in the next election.
"The mayor failed, Bonnie Crombie failed. She had the lowest housing starts of anyone in Ontario - that's unacceptable," the premier told reporters.
"Bonnie Crombie raised taxes to the highest they've ever been in Mississauga," he continued as Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow stood patiently behind him.
"She is running to be the premier and we need (a) clear and decisive decision from her: is she for taxing people, is she for the carbon tax or isn't she?" said Ford, adding Crombie can't "dodge and weave" now that she's moved "into the big leagues."
For her part, the Liberal leader said the premier is scrambling to "deflect attention" from the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal now under investigation by the RCMP.
"This preoccupation with me - some call it an obsession - is just weird, it's odd," Crombie said in an interview.
"I'm not going to play by his rules. I'm not going to be pushed around. He wants to box me in, but I'm going to be setting my own agenda," she said.
"He may be used to weak opposition, but there's a new sheriff in town."
A senior Conservative source, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations, suggested Crombie and Ford "live rent-free in each other's heads" because they each recognize the electoral threat the other poses.
There are almost a dozen Peel Region Tory MPPs skittish about facing off against a Liberal party led by a former mayor who won 78.5 per cent of the popular vote in the 2022 Mississauga election.
When the Liberals were in power from 2003 until 2018 under premiers Dalton McGuinty and Kathleen Wynne, those kind of suburban ridings were reliably Grit.
Since then, those seats have been key to successive Tory victories.
While most Ontarians may not be thinking about the June 2026 election, Ford certainly is.
"People will have a decision in a couple of years," the premier said Thursday. " And it's going to be a very clear decision."
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie takes questions from the media at Queens Park on Dec. 5, 2023.

Document 149 of 500

Toronto nets provincial cash after exceeding its housing target by 51 per cent

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-nets-provincial-cash-after-exceeding/docview/2930965188/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+nets+provincial+cash+after+exceeding+its+housing+target+by+51+per+cent&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto will receive $114 million in new housing cash, the province announced Thursday - a reward for beating its targets for Ontario's 10-year housing goal.
In a press conference at city hall, Premier Doug Ford, alongside Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow and Ontario Housing Minister Paul Calandra, lauded the province's largest city, noting Toronto exceeded last year's targets by 51 per cent on the road to the provincially outlined goal of 285,000 homes by 2031.
"I'm pleased to announce that Toronto has knocked it out of the park," Ford said, noting the new provincial funding could be used not only for homes, but for infrastructure that could "lay the groundwork" for housing.
Chow said she couldn't immediately say exactly how the money would be spent, but called it a "welcomed boost" given the need.
"Is it enough? No. Can we do more? Absolutely. But you have to start. You have to build, and build, and build mixed income everywhere."
The money comes from a three-year provincial program, through which the province has vowed to disperse $1.2 billion to cities that meet and exceed their housing targets. Across Ontario, the province has outlined specific goals for its largest and fastest-growing municipalities as it aims to see 1.5 million new homes by 2031.
While housing starts have risen in Ontario lately, the government's fall economic statement showed it has so far fallen short of the required pace.
According to the Canada Mortgage and Housing Corp., there were 30,516 housing starts within the city of Toronto in 2023. On Thursday, Chow and Ford each suggested the number was higher, citing 31,656 starts. The province's measure includes more than just newly built homes, counting new or upgraded long-term care beds as well as units created by renovation or conversions.
While CMHC has warned that home construction lagged countrywide in January, Toronto bucked the trend, with The Canadian Press reporting its annual rate of housing starts went up 179 per cent.
But even as construction has picked up, the city remains in a severe home affordability crunch, with Chow on Thursday noting the city's swollen subsidized housing wait-list, which is more than 85,000 households long.
In her remarks, the mayor pointed to the story of Aryna, a seven-year-old child profiled by the Star as part of its the Kids Aren't Alright series. Aryna's family relies on a food bank as rent eats up most of their income.
"People in our city are finding it harder than ever to find a home they can afford," Chow said.
As the province revealed the new funding for Toronto, Calandra acknowledged some cities have struggled with using home starts as a measure of progress, with parts of York Region and Windsor needing to build infrastructure first.
However, Ford offered sharp words for cities falling short of their targets, taking particular aim at Mississauga - where former mayor Bonnie Crombie is expected to face Ford in the next election as leader of the Ontario Liberals.
Mississauga is one of 24 Ontario municipalities that, according to the province's tracking, did not meet its provincial housing targets for 2023. Seven municipalities are considered to be on track, hitting at least 80 per cent of their goals, while Toronto and 18 other municipalities were considered to be ahead of schedule.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford congratulates Mayor Olivia Chow at Toronto City Hall after the city exceeded its provincially set housing targets by 51 per cent.

Document 150 of 500

In move called 'blatant' patronage, ex-Doug Ford staffers appointed to committee that selects Ontario judges

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/move-called-blatant-patronage-ex-doug-ford/docview/2930964923/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=In+move+called+%27blatant%27+patronage%2C+ex-Doug+Ford+staffers+appointed+to+committee+that+selects+Ontario+judges&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The inclusion of two of Premier Doug Ford's former senior staffers on the committee that recommends which lawyers should become Ontario judges is raising concerns that the independent process has been tainted and politicized.
Ford's former deputy chief of staff Matthew Bondy has been the chair of the Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee (JAAC) since Feb. 1. It's an arm's-length body that screens applications and makes recommendations to the attorney general on who should be appointed to the Ontario Court of Justice.
Bondy is one of seven public members appointed to the 13-person committee by Attorney General Doug Downey, who ultimately appoints almost all of the committee members following changes made by the Ford government in 2021.
Brock Vandrick, Ford's former director of stakeholder relations, has also been a public member since December.
Legal experts and law associations have expressed concern that the appointments of ex-senior staffers in the current premier's office will lead to the perception that the appointment process for judges has been politicized, and will erode public trust in the justice system.
"This casts a cloud over the JAAC's mandate to ensure judges will be appointed free from partisan political considerations," said a statement from the Federation of Ontario Law Associations, which has a representative on the committee.
"JAAC's independence and the public's confidence is eroded when political influence appears to be driving the process by which our judges are selected. We call on the government to be respectful of the importance of this committee and the hard work it is called on to do for Ontarians."
The Ontario Court of Justice is one of the busiest courts in Canada, handling the bulk of the province's criminal caseload, as well as a large number of family matters.
"I'm not sure I've seen such blatant examples of patronage recently," University of Waterloo law professor Emmett Macfarlane said.
The naming of Bondy and Vandrick to the committee follows significant changes that Downey has made to the JAAC that were condemned by legal associations as unnecessary and opening the door for patronage appointments to the bench, with critics charging that the changes would water down a process that was once described as the gold standard for appointing judges.
It also follows Downey's run-in with the association that represents provincial court judges. In 2022, the association rang alarm bells over his new process for selecting the court's next chief justice, describing it as an "aberration" that violated the separation of powers between the executive and judicial branches of government.
"This is exactly what we feared would happen when the attorney general decided to make changes to what was once one of the best judicial appointment processes in the world," said George Thomson, a former judge and ex-deputy attorney general of Ontario and Canada, responding to the appointments of Bondy and Vandrick.
"It seems clear that a decision has been made to politicize the formerly independent judicial appointments committee."
Downey's spokesperson, Andrew Kennedy, said Bondy and Vandrick "were chosen due to their extensive public policy experience and volunteer activities. The committee, not individual members, recommends candidates to the attorney general following an in-depth application process."
Bondy, who declined a request for comment, is currently vice-president for national public affairs at Enterprise Canada, while Vandrick is senior vice-president at Wellington Dupont Public Affairs. He did not return a request for comment.
While emphasizing he doesn't know Bondy or Vandrick, the perception caused by their appointments to the committee is that "this is a resting sinecure for political staffers," said Frank Addario, a former president of the Criminal Lawyers' Association and former vice-president of the Canadian Civil Liberties Association.
"Even if the individuals on the JAAC, or the output of the JAAC, are high-quality, it will be tainted by the perception that it's controlled by the premier's office and the attorney general's office for political ends," he said.
"We had a great non-partisan model for almost 35 years; it had tremendous output in terms of quality and there was not even a whiff of the 'gravy train.' By conspicuously politicizing it, they're debasing the currency of the judicial appointments."
Macfarlane said that the Ford government "is not alone in allowing partisanship" to affect the membership of bodies that are supposed to be providing independent advice to government, including on the appointment of judges.
Given that the government is accountable for the appointment of judges, "some might argue having people who have served as political staff on these committees is appropriate," he continued. "But I think there is a legitimate concern that explicit partisan considerations might politicize judicial appointments in ways we would want to avoid."
He pointed to the United States, where he said the public perception of the legitimacy of its Supreme Court appears to be at an all-time low, "largely as a result of the extreme partisanship around its appointments, which invariably leads to lower-quality candidates."
Downey sparked the ire of most of Ontario's main legal organizations after becoming attorney general in 2019, when he began to contemplate major changes to the judicial appointment process that none of them had asked for.
He increased the number of judicial candidates that he's allowed to see from the committee. Advocates warned that the change gives the government wider access to the entire application pool, running the risk that lower-quality candidates get appointed to the bench.
Downey also gave himself more power over the composition of the appointments committee, which includes three provincial court judges, three lawyers representing legal organizations, and seven public members named by him.
Downey took away the ability of the legal organizations - the Law Society of Ontario, the Ontario Bar Association, and the Federation of Ontario Law Associations - to name their own representatives. They must now each submit a list of three candidates to him for his selection.
There is currently no representative on the committee from the Law Society. The legal regulator refused to tell the Star if they have even submitted a list of candidates to Downey, saying the process is "ongoing and confidential." Downey's office confirmed, however, that the Law Society did send a list, and that he's reviewing it.
The presence of former political staffers on the committee does not "automatically jeopardize" the principles of having high-quality judges and non-partisan decision-making, the president of the Ontario Bar Association said in a statement.
Kelly McDermott said such individuals "may provide an understanding of the diversity and complexity of Ontario ... The measure of whether appointments to the JAAC compromise fundamentals will be the quality, diversity, and timeliness" of appointments to the bench.
"And we will be watching this closely."
CAPTION: Matthew Bondy, left, Ontario Premier Doug Ford's former deputy chief of staff, and Brock Vandrick, right, his former director of stakeholder relations, were recently appointed to the committee that selects Ontario judges.Attorney General Doug Downey recently appointed both former Doug Ford staffers to the selection committee.

Document 151 of 500

Ford government to provide $1 billion in funding to universities, colleges: sources

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-provide-1-billion-funding/docview/2930964901/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+to+provide+%241+billion+in+funding+to+universities%2C+colleges%3A+sources&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government will provide more than a billion dollars in funding to colleges and universities over the next three years, the Star has learned.
A government source told the Star the money will help those schools with the greatest need.
"The government will be providing over $1.2 billion in new direct financial support," the source said. "As part of this funding, a new $903-million dollar fund will be made available to all public colleges and universities" over the three years, the source added.
The announcement is expected to be made next week by Colleges and Universities Minister Jill Dunlop.
The money, however, would be far less than schools hoped for, based on recommendations from an expert panel set up by the province.
Sources were speaking confidentially in order to discuss the planned announcement.
The government source said "the institutions with the highest need will be eligible to receive a portion of that funding that meets the blue-ribbon panel recommendation."
Other sources said they also expect no tuition fee increases for up to three years - well past the next provincial election.
The announcement of the new funding is expected to include additional grants for student mental health and safety.
The overall funding will be contingent on schools proving they are operating as efficiently as possible, the sources said.
In 2019, the Ford government slashed tuition by 10 per cent and then implemented a freeze. That, combined with stagnant funding and inflation, has left almost half of the province's universities running deficits and warning of program cuts.
And now, both colleges and universities are facing the loss of international student tuition revenue as the federal government clamps down on their numbers.
Last year, Dunlop appointed an expert blue-ribbon panel to look at the future of the post-secondary sector, which recommended an immediate 10 per cent boost in government grants, with future increases set at a minimum of two per cent a year, as well as an immediate tuition bump of five per cent this fall, among other increases.
In total, its recommendations came with an estimated price tag of $2.5 billion over three years for the province's 24 publicly assisted colleges and 23 universities.
The report noted a number of auditor general reports warning of low provincial funding for post-secondary institutions - among the lowest in the country - and the risk of reliance on international students as a revenue generator given they pay three to four times the tuition of domestic students.
Schools themselves have said that with no tuition increases, combined with inflation, unexpected COVID-19 costs as well as salary increases for staff in light of the province's unconstitutional wage cap legislation, they are in a financially precarious situation.
"What they are doing is very dangerous for the sector," warned one post-secondary veteran.
Premier Doug Ford has been clear he doesn't support any tuition increase for domestic students this year, saying the current affordability crisis makes it unfair.
That's something NDP Leader Marit Stiles agrees with, but says that means the government has to step up.
"Our colleges and universities are deeply underfunded, funded at a lower rate by this government than any other province in Canada," she said. "The obvious answer is let's start to invest ... at a significant rate if you're going to make up the difference and not see post-secondary institutions diminished."
Steve Orsini, president and CEO of the Council of Ontario Universities, has repeatedly warned that schools need the grants and tuition increases recommended by Dunlop's own appointed experts.
The panel also noted that government per-funding student for college students in 2021-22 was $6,891 in Ontario compared to an average of $15,615 nationally, and $11,471 for universities compared to $20,772 across Canada.
The report also noted the risk of relying on international student revenue. Then, last month, the federal government announced cuts to the number of new study permits issued to foreign students by 35 per cent, with spots to be allotted based on each province's population. That move will disproportionately hit Ontario, where more than half of Canada's current international students study.
Almost half of Ontario's universities are now in the red, with schools warning that student services will face cuts if the government does not provide a bump in funding and also allow tuition to rise as the panel recommended.
Orsini has said the government must "urgently" address universities' financial situations, with 10 of the 23 projecting deficits of a total of $175 million.
"Universities are at a breaking point now with years of declining real, per-student funding," Orsini has said, adding that they are already looking for - and implementing - efficiencies.
The blue-ribbon panel did not find that salaries were out of line, but did say there was "considerable room" for savings in administrative costs.
CAPTION: Ontario's Minister of Colleges and Universities Jill Dunlop during question period at the Ontario legislature on Nov. 28, 2023.

Subject: Tuition; Government grants; Foreign students; Cost control; Colleges & universities; Funding

Doug Ford's government is to blame for Ontario's teacher recruitment and retention crisis, unions charge

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-government-is-blame-ontarios-teacher/docview/2930964896/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+government+is+to+blame+for+Ontario%27s+teacher+recruitment+and+retention+crisis%2C+unions+charge&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario schools can't recruit or retain enough teachers thanks to a "mess" created by the Ford government, Ontario's four teachers' unions charge.
Union leaders were responding to comments made Wednesday by Education Minister Stephen Lecce, who said the province is considering "every available option" to address a teacher shortage which has left school boards across Ontario scrambling.
They blamed the government for using "Band-Aid" solutions to fill growing gaps, such as extending the number of days retired teachers can work or allowing student-teachers to be at the front of the classroom.
Ontario English Catholic Teachers' Association President René Jansen in de Wal said Lecce could do more to collaborate with educators.
"There are things he could do right now that would be far more effective, without anyone's permission ... and he's not doing them," Jansen in de Wal said. "The Ontario Teachers' Federation has made recommendations to him ... and he doesn't want to act on those things."
In a statement, Lecce noted that teacher shortages are a national problem. Since 2018, he said his government has added nearly 3,000 teachers and "reduced red tape" for new teachers entering the classroom.
But NDP Leader Marit Stiles said the shortage "isn't coming out of nowhere" and teachers under Premier Doug Ford's leadership are overburdened.
"People are leaving because they're exhausted, they're disrespected. We need smaller class sizes; we need more educational assistance in our schools. We probably do need to look at other avenues like reducing the number of years in teachers' college," Stiles said.
Teachers need to feel valued, Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation (OSSTF) President Karen Littlewood said.
"In every area of the province, there are multiple schools every day who have unqualified individuals in them who are covering classes," Littlewood said, referring to educational support workings filling in.
New teachers aren't filling full-time positions because they need to commit to one school board early in their careers and transferring to another board later is difficult, Littlewood said.
Qualified technology and science teachers are leaving for non-teaching jobs, which offer higher pay and less stress, Littlewood said.
She said some high schools can't run university prerequisites every semester because there aren't enough qualified teachers.
CAPTION: Ontario's Education Minister Stephen Lecce speaks to reporters outside the Ontario legislature on Feb. 20, 2024.
CREDIT: Sophia Coppolino Special to the Star

Subject: Teachers; School boards

Title: Doug Ford's government is to blame for Ontario's teacher recruitment and retention crisis, unions charge

Document 153 of 500

Doug Ford's omnibus bill is political theatre

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-omnibus-bill-is-political-theatre/docview/2929976204/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+omnibus+bill+is+political+theatre&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There once existed a general expectation that bills introduced in parliaments and legislatures bore neutral titles seeking to indicate their content and purpose.
That quaint custom seems to have passed. In contemporary practice, the titling of legislation is, as often as not, an act of blatant political propaganda or provocation.
To wit, the Ontario government's Getting It Done Act introduced this week at Queen's Park, an omnibus bill that is the namesake of the PC government's 2022 campaign slogan.
Subtle, the Doug Ford government is not.
It's not unreasonable to say that Ontarians might have gleaned a better understanding of the contents of this unholy mishmash had the province named it the Crazy Clown Car Act, or the Empty Political Gestures Act, or, as a political historian once joked, the Poke the Opposition in the Eye Act.
In the era of constant campaigning, it appears governments now see even the simple act of titling bills as just another opportunity for partisan messaging.
But there's more to the matter than mere mischievous wordplay. At core in this bill, the Ford government is again flaunting its chronic disregard for transparency and accountability.
The employment of omnibus bills - large pieces of legislation into which many diverse policy issues and manoeuvres are packed - is almost by definition designed to hasten the process, reduce scrutiny and limit debate, thereby minimizing public uproar and resistance.
The guiding imperatives of such bills are speed and limited opposition involvement.
Which, for a government whose signature characteristic has been half-baked policy rashness and behind-the-scenes decision-making, is on the face of things alarming.
As NDP Leader Marit Stiles noted, "where the government has gone wrong over and over again is not listening to people.
"They've spent more time in the last year reversing course and they haven't actually come up with practical solutions that will make life easier for people," she said.
In that, she is not wrong.
Granted, Ford's gambit is not as egregious, say, as the infamous "Bully Bill" of the Mike Harris government almost 30 years ago, a behemoth of more than 200 pages that contained massive structural implications for a wide range of public institutions in one fell swoop. Still it is worrying.
It is a mélange of fluff and the seriously consequential. It is part arrogant time-machine -- trying to control what future governments can do, particularly on highway tolls and carbon pricing, while revisiting previous efforts on the home-building front that ended in disaster.
For instance, it would shorten environmental assessments to speed construction of new highways and hydro transmission lines, allow for quicker expropriations before such assessments were completed, and change some urban boundaries.
Into the hopper were thrown elements that would require a referendum on any future provincial carbon pricing program, ban new tolls on Ontario highways and, tossing the merely administrative in with the potentially dramatic, automatically renew vehicle registrations for owners with no outstanding fines.
On the face of it, Ford's initiative violates parliamentary norms in seeking to bind the hands of future governments in a piece of legislation so convoluted as to defy coherent parliamentary review.
The fundamental purpose of a parliament or legislature is not, after all, efficiency and productivity in racing through matters as fast as possible, but in scrutiny and accountability to craft the best possible policy.
But there are so many components to Ford's bill that MPPs cannot possibly decide on them all in a single vote -- a reality that adds to an inexorable shift of power from legislators to the executive.
In all, there is no discernible unifying theme in the Getting It Done Act other than the self-interested goal of advancing the re-election prospects of the Ford government.
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner said this week Ford's omnibus effort is "performative politics at its worst" and the bill should have been called the "Get it Done Wrong Act."
In that, he is quite right.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford's government introduced an omnibus bill this week meant to expedite environmental assessments and bind future governments on highway tolls and carbon pricing.

Subject: Legislation; Carbon; Politics; Government; Roads & highways; Accountability

Title: Doug Ford's omnibus bill is political theatre

Rome burns while Ford fiddles with licence plates

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/rome-burns-while-ford-fiddles-with-licence-plates/docview/2929976198/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Rome+burns+while+Ford+fiddles+with+licence+plates&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Rome burns while Ford fiddles with licence platesDoug Ford hopes to finally 'Get It Done' when legislature resumes Tuesday, Feb. 20
As Ford and his PC gang return to Queen's Park this week and we read the list of "goodies" in his Get It Done Act grab bag, the irony is almost too much to bear! Ford uses the term "Get It Done" to mean things he is doing "for" us ... while I see it as underscoring that what he is "getting done" is the ongoing degradation of our province and the complete destruction of our cherished institutions, such as health care, education, and the greenbelt.
Every day, I read of new and crazy ideas being imposed upon Ontarians. The latest was a 24-hour-long policy announcement, reversed the very next day, requiring LCBO customers to be carded before entry. This was followed by a cancellation of the need to renew license plate registration on a regular basis. Why? Because his elimination of license plate renewal fees was another of his poorly implemented ideas. He just eliminated the fees, with no thought or plan regarding continuing to ensure vehicles were legally on the road.
We are facing a tsunami of aging baby boomers. Where is Ford's plan to address this huge need? Rome burns while Ford continues to fiddle with licence plates.
I am further upset to see Ford's current wave of TV ads, touting his wonderful job for Ontario. The one on the state of health care is particularly dishonest and revolting. It shows spacious, uncrowded hospitals, where patients and staff are smiling and laughing together, and no one is in distress. These ads are complete lies and should be ruled illegal to air. The text superimposed on the ad says that we should see all the ways that the current government is "connecting us to care" not providing care but connecting us to it - code for increasing privatization.
Ontarians need to speak up and object to these policies and propaganda. We must not allow him to continue his hatchet job on our Ontario and certainly not allow him another four years to continue the dismantling of our beloved province.
Pam Tomasevic, Mississauga
Wider sidewalks along Avenue Road would address safety issuesThere is no reason to fear a safer Avenue Road, Feb. 18
The perilous section of Avenue Road between Bloor Street and St. Clair Avenue must change.
The only thing to fear is the present six-lane perilous section of Avenue Road between St. Clair Avenue and Bloor Street. It is currently a major safety concern due to its narrow sidewalks - many too narrow to safely accommodate wheelchairs or strollers.
There are logical fact-based arguments that this must change. The local councillors Dianne Saxe and Josh Matlow know it must change. Let us hope city recommendations soon address these safety problems and present options to make this a more people-friendly, climate-friendly, and inclusive transportation corridor that will be safe for drivers, walkers and wheelers alike.
Mary Helen Spence, Toronto
Transforming Avenue Road is long overdue
There is no reason to fear a safer Avenue Road, Feb. 18
I lived on Avenue Road near Dupont in the 1990s and regularly walked along it to get to work or for errands. At one point, I wrote to my Metro councillor in the hope that planned roadworks might narrow such a dangerous and intimidating street for pedestrians, with its speeding cars and narrow sidewalks, and she wrote back that, much as she'd like to see it, it could never happen. So it's heartening to see, three decades later, that this necessary transformation is within grasp. A two-kilometre six-lane highway is not just an anomaly in the city, but serves no real purpose now that the streets north and south of it are all four lanes. It's time to start making it a normal downtown main street.
Dylan Reid, Toronto
Proposed changes to Avenue Road unnecessary
There is no reason to fear a safer Avenue Road, Feb. 18
These proposed changes are unnecessary and unrealistic. To refer to Avenue Road.as "high speed" is a gross misstatement. Far more accurate would be its description as "typical Toronto gridlock," particularly for northbound traffic. And where are all the pedestrians who need safer sidewalks? Except for the first few blocks north of Bloor, there is more than ample space for the relatively small amount of pedestrian usage. This is the typical answer to solving a Toronto "problem": reduce the lanes by one third, and presto, problem solved. This is nothing but foolishness.
David S. Wilson, Toronto
Focus on the benefits change brings
There is no reason to fear a safer Avenue Road, Feb. 18
I get it. People don't like changes to where they live. But many changes must be made for my infant grandson to be able to enjoy a decent climate when he grows up.
I live on a street that recently installed a contra flow bike lane. When the public consultation was taking place, some of us were concerned about the increased bicycle, moped and scooter traffic that would follow. (We've all experienced some daredevils on two wheels.)
Now, pretty well all of us are pleased to be part of the solution, rather than the problem.
Change can often improve what we had thought was the best of all possible worlds.
John Liss, Toronto
Carbon tax just right for the tax averse
Rebranding the carbon tax, Feb. 14
I hate taxes. I spend ages looking for deductions on my T1 return. I shop duty-free. And I moved to Alberta where we have no PST.
So I love the Canadian consumer carbon tax. Every quarter, the feds e-transfer me the amount that they think I am going to spend on it. Then I do things (Weather stripping! Buy groceries local! Walk the kids to school!) to not pay it and keep the money.
A program that lets thrifty Canadians stick it to The Man plus fight climate change is good under any name. Keep the Tax.
Tracy Duncan, Calgary
When personhood gets earlier and earlierWarnings of the impact of fertility treatments in Alabama rush in after frozen embryo ruling, Feb. 20
In the wake of Alabama's Supreme Court ruling that frozen embryos are children, will the next step be to grant personhood to the gleam in a father's eye?
Glen Loucks, Toronto

Debate over Canada's flag
Birthday of Canadian flag is a reminder of what truly matters, Feb. 15
I remember the excitement of Canada having its own flag. I was sitting in my Grade 5 class when our teacher tasked us with designing a new flag. The idea was a flag a child could draw. I still remember some disappointment that my design (and hundreds of others who had the same design) wasn't chosen. The Toronto Star published a multitude of them in full colour. The new design with the red maple leaf and the two bars, which represented sea to sea. But the seas are blue, not red. It also seemed as if the colours of part founders, France and Scotland, along with Quebec, were left out. We did calm down and moved along. But, just saying.
Jim Skeels, Toronto
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford during question period in the Ontario Legislature on Feb. 20, 2024.

Full text: Good morning. This is the Thursday, Feb. 22 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on admissions at Ontario's toughest university programs, refugee applications for Gazans and Pierre Poilievre's anti-trans comments.
DON'T MISS:
He had a near-perfect average but still couldn't get in. How Ontario's most competitive university programs decide who makes the cut
Dante Capobianco had more than a 99.5 per cent average - he was also a math mentor, an executive of the social justice club and a AA hockey player. So when he wasn't accepted into the software engineering program at the University of Waterloo he was shocked and dismayed. Only 5 per cent of roughly 2,000 hopefuls had made the cut. The Star took an inside look at the admission process at some of Ontario's most competitive undergraduate programs and found intimidating odds, but also a holistic approach, Janet Hurley reports. Here's a breakdown of what it takes to get into eight of the province's toughest programs.
By the numbers: Take a look at the admissions data from the most competitive university programs in Ontario.Meanwhile: Canada's new restrictions on study permits will blunt efforts to diversify the international student body, experts say. Here's how it will change where students are coming from.
Immigration lawyers want a judicial review of the refugee application process for Gazans
Families who applied to Canada's refugee application program for their loved ones in Gaza have fallen into a "communication black hole," facing "radio silence" from Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC), immigration lawyers say. They're launching a legal challenge against the federal government, arguing the program has been plagued by erroneous rejections and a lack of transparency, Ben Cohen reports. Here's a closer look at what one lawyer called "an incredibly dysfunctional system."
Wait, what? Israel and Egypt have thus far refused to let anyone approved by Canada out of Gaza - but the scope of the legal action is more narrow. Participating lawyers told the Star they want better communication from the IRCC and for applicants rejected over filing errors to have their submissions reopened. More: "The amount of stress and panic this has caused is unimaginable," said Toronto-based lawyer Hana Marku. "Regardless of whether they are accepted or rejected, people just need answers at this point."Meanwhile: Palestinians in Gaza face a worsening humanitarian catastrophe without desperately needed aid. This explainer lays out how the situation grew so dire.
Pierre Poilievre's attack on transgender people puts vulnerable Canadians at risk, Justin Trudeau says
As U.S. lawmakers restrict drag performances, pronoun changes and health services for transgender people, similar ideas are gaining prominence around Canada. On Wednesday, Canada's Conservative leader said he supports banning trans women - which he called "biological males" - from female bathrooms, change rooms, shelters and sports, Tonda MacCharles reports. Earlier this month, Poilievre said he opposes puberty blockers for minors. Among criticism he received was Justin Trudeau accusing him of trying to create divisions while ignoring the risks posed to vulnerable LGBTQ youth. Here's what else Poilievre has said on the topic - and how it adds to the policies some provinces are enacting.
Word from the NDP: Noting that Poilievre's statement came in response to a question about women's safety, Jagmeet Singh said Poilievre is "not serious" about addressing domestic violence, abusive male coaches in sports, or discriminatory pay practices in sport.Linda McQuaig's take: Poilievre wants to look tough - but he bows to Donald Trump.WHAT ELSE:

Document 156 of 500

Justin Trudeau claims eight out of 10 Canadian households get more back from rebates than they pay in carbon pricing. Is that true?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-claims-eight-out-10-canadian/docview/2929400506/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+claims+eight+out+of+10+Canadian+households+get+more+back+from+rebates+than+they+pay+in+carbon+pricing.+Is+that+true%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-In the lead-up to the next federal election, the Star is taking a look at the leaders of Canada's major federal parties and fact checking some of their most common political slogans and talking points.
Today, the Star is diving into a claim often touted by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau: that eight out of 10 Canadian households get more money back through the carbon rebate than they pay under the federal carbon price.
It's a claim the Trudeau Liberals deploy frequently in defence of the highly-divisive policy, particularly as a rebuttal to Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's relentless crusade against the carbon levy.
But is the prime minister providing the full picture? Here's what we know.
What's Trudeau talking about?To start, let's look at the policies behind the prime minister's claims.
Carbon pricing is one of many tools that can be used in the fight against climate change, and is built on the idea that imposing costs on pollution will deter people and businesses from burning fossil fuels and help reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
In Canada, provinces and territories can come up with their own pricing scheme. But if they don't, or if their plan fails to meet certain standards, they must adopt the federal pricing system.
There's two parts to Ottawa's system. The first is a charge on fossil fuels like gasoline and natural gas. That "fuel charge" trickles down to consumers when they pay their home gas bill and fill up their cars. It can also show up in other areas like food costs, though those impacts are less clear.
The second component of the federal carbon pricing scheme is aimed at industries that produce heavy emissions, but that's not what Trudeau is focusing on here.
The rebates Trudeau is referencing used to be called Climate Action Incentive payments, a jargony mouthful the Liberals last week rebranded as the Canada Carbon Rebate.
According to the government, ninety per cent of the money collected from the carbon price goes back to people in the form of quarterly rebates. If you spend less on the so-called carbon tax (like reducing how much you spend on gas), the more likely it is your rebate will cover what you spent, and even exceed it.
The price on carbon will increase each year until 2030, and the rebates increase alongside it.
OK, OK. Just tell me if Trudeau is telling the truth.In the words of Canada's Parliamentary Budget Officer (PBO), Yves Giroux, Trudeau's claim is "accurate, but it's incomplete."
The PBO is an independent and non-partisan office that provides economic and financial analysis to the government.
And it was a March 2023 report from the PBO that came to the conclusion Trudeau is citing, though other economic analyses have also arrived at similar conclusions.
But there are some big caveats at play.
Giroux concluded that most households in Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba, Ontario, Prince Edward Island, and Newfoundland and Labrador will get more from the rebates than the amount they pay in the federal carbon price and related GST.
But that conclusion only considers fiscal factors - that is, the actual amount of money Canadians directly or indirectly pay because of the carbon tax, such as buying a tank of gas, paying a household gas bill, or getting something at the grocery store.
The PBO took data on consumption patterns from Statistics Canada, and then subtracted the amount an average family in each province gets from the rebates.
So if you're only looking at the money, and not considering anything else, it's true that most Canadian households that pay the federal fuel charge are seeing a gain.
Some experts caution against interpreting that finding as Canadians making a material amount of extra cash.
"Most people come out about even because the reality is, whether you're a few dollars up or a few dollars down, it is pretty close to a neutral effect for the average person," said Michael Bernstein, the executive director of Clean Prosperity.
Is Trudeau wrong once you include other factors?If you take in the wider picture, yes. The issue here is that while the Liberals are citing a correct conclusion, they're not sharing necessary context about it.
Indeed, the PBO came to an entirely different conclusion when he added the economic impacts of the federal fuel charge - like reductions in employment, income and profits - to his analysis.
"If you think about the transportation sector, they will presumably be paying a higher proportion than other sectors because they rely more heavily on fossil fuels," Giroux told the Star. "That will affect the economic output of the sectors that are more heavily exposed to the carbon tax."
Under those conditions, which Giroux said in his report provides "a more complete picture" of the household impact of the carbon tax, "the net cost increases for all households, reflecting the overall negative economic impact of the federal fuel charge."
It's that framing the Liberals are downplaying, as the Conservatives trumpet it as proof that the politically-charged policy is misguided.
How did the Liberals respond to the PBO's wider analysis?They didn't think it was entirely fair.
That's because Giroux's report didn't factor in the costs of climate change, which prompted one Liberal MP to tell Giroux he was "deeply perplexed" and "shocked" by the PBO's decision.
Giroux told the Star it's "very difficult" to estimate the economic impact of climate change in the short-term. Giroux did, in 2022, analyze the long-term impacts of climate change on Canada's economy and estimated that even if all countries around the world meet their climate commitments, changes in weather patterns could slash Canada's real GDP by 5.8 per cent - $145 billion in 2021 dollars - by 2100.
Giroux said it's hard to look at a period of just a few years and determine what environmental events were caused by climate change alone.
And he said estimating the cost of doing nothing technically falls outside his mandate.
"We're providing information on the cost of what the government is putting forward ... and it's very difficult to cost or to estimate the cost of doing nothing, which is not government policy," Giroux said.
Trevor Tombe, an economist at the University of Calgary, who has previously come to some similar conclusions as the PBO, said the data Statistics Canada collects allows economists to estimate how the carbon tax is affecting Canadians.
But it's harder, Tombe said, to look at the future impacts of climate change because there are simply too many unknown and constantly evolving factors.
So what does this all mean?
"It's up to the government to explain and justify its own actions," said Giroux.
And if it doesn't, the PBO said, Canadians will need to inform themselves.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, right, tours a housing facility as he announces funding for housing in Edmonton on Feb. 21, 2024.

Cap on students will hurt economy, province suggests

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/cap-on-students-will-hurt-economy-province/docview/2929400462/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Cap+on+students+will+hurt+economy%2C+province+suggests&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ottawa did not consult with the provinces before announcing caps on international students, and it's not just colleges and universities that will feel the impact - Ontario's economy will too, says Post-Secondary Minister Jill Dunlop.
"We're very disappointed with the federal government," Dunlop said in the legislature on Wednesday.
She said she's heard from fellow MPPs and other ministries about "the impact this is going to have on our economy" and that the tourism sector in particular "is going to be devastated without these students," given the number of post-graduation work permits will drop.
"That's why, when we're working with the federal government on the allocation, we will be looking at the labour market needs across regions in Ontario as a number-one priority."
Also, "ensuring that there is guaranteed housing for international students coming to our campus will be a priority, and ensuring that the economic needs of each region are a concern as well. There is going to be an impact across Ontario and the federal government did absolutely no consultations with the provinces or with the sector."
Last month, the federal immigration minister - feeling the pressure of the growing housing crisis - chopped the number of new study permits issued to international students by 35 per cent, with spots to be allotted based on each province's population. Ontario, where more than half of Canada's current international students study, will see a 50-per-cent drop.
New Democrat MPP Peggy Sattler (London West), her party's post-secondary critic, said "colleges and universities are bracing for the impact of a 50-per-cent reduction in international study permits at a time when the sector is already at a (financial) breaking point."
She said the province has seen decades of "chronic underfunding by this government and the Liberals before them, which has put our post-secondary institutions starved for resources and now they are on the brink," with 10 of 23 schools running deficits.
Premier Doug Ford has already ruled out a tuition increase this fall for domestic students - as recommended by the government's blue ribbon panel - but it remains unknown if Ontario will boost funding for colleges and universities as the panel also urged.
"We will be announcing our path forward shortly," Dunlop said.
Liberal MPP Adil Shamji (Don Valley East), his party's colleges and universities critic, said schools "have been suffering for years" and the government should have already increased funding.
"There's not a moment to lose," he said, adding schools were forced to turn to international students to make up for gaps in funding - they pay three to four times the tuition of domestic students - but that they "should not be the piggy bank."
"What I really worry about is with a cap on international students, without the kind of funding that universities are asking, without the support that they need, what we're going to see is universities have no choice but to either cut back on programs, cut back on staff, or cut back on student services."
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

PCs plan to enshrine anti-harassment law

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pcs-plan-enshrine-anti-harassment-law/docview/2929400181/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PCs+plan+to+enshrine+anti-harassment+law&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ford government says new legislation that could see local politicians thrown out of office for workplace harassment should be introduced before the summer.
Speaking to reporters at Queen's Park on Wednesday, Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra called it "a very important piece of legislation. It has to be constitutional, but has to actually achieve the results that I think that everybody's asking for. So absolutely, we are going to bring something forward and continue to work" with municipalities.
Asked if the bill could come before the legislature rises in June, Calandra said: "I don't see why not."
The Ford government previously voted down a private member's bill from Liberal MPP Stephen Blais (Orléans), saying it would leave voters to decide who to remove from office at election time.
But with more than 200 municipalities supporting the idea and the non-partisan advocacy group The Women of Ontario Say No keeping the issue in the spotlight, last month Calandra changed course, promising the government would create "airtight" legislation.
Municipalities across the province have struggled with situations where elected officials have been found to have committed workplace harassment, but there is little that can be done beyond docking pay.
Emily McIntosh, of The Women of Ontario Say No, said removal from office is a must in any legislation. "The premise is simple," she said. "You should go to work and you should not be subjected to abuse. We are asking municipally elected representatives to be held to the very same standard as every other worker in Ontario. Elected representatives are not above the people - they are of the people."
Blais recounted his own days on Ottawa council, where fellow politician Rick Chiarelli was found to have harassed and abused staff and job applicants for years.
His bill would have allowed for politicians to be removed from office after findings of inappropriate behaviour by an integrity commissioner and a judge's review.
"We absolutely need this change," added Aurora Coun. Rachel Gilliland. "No council is immune to it."
David Arbuckle, who is executive director of the Association of Municipal Managers, Clerks and Treasurers of Ontario, said "while most councillor-staff relationships and interactions are positive, respectful and focused on collaboration to achieve community goals, our members are telling us that the overall relationship between elected officials and professional municipal staff is increasingly confrontational" and that can impact staff recruitment and retention.
Farah Grozelle is a small-business owner in Bradford who has been working with The Women of Ontario Say No.
"A majority government can pass legislation in less than four weeks," she said. "It is now the winter of 2024, and here we are without accountability structures in place with respect to the safety and well-being of individuals and communities. I know we can do better, now."
Leaders of the opposition parties have also thrown their support behind such legislation.
"We need to close these loopholes," NDP Leader Marit Stiles told a press conference Wednesday morning at Queen's Park with Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner.
"We need transparency, accountability for those who do the wrong thing," Crombie added. "We know politics can be a nasty business, but it doesn't have to be."
McIntosh said she'd also like to eventually see legislation covering provincial politicians.
With files from Robert Benzie and Rob Ferguson
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau Lauren Nicol Special to the Star With files from Robert Benzie and Rob Ferguson
