Full text: Carbon levy
a good fit for
the tax averse
Rebranding the carbon tax, Feb. 14
I hate taxes. I spend ages looking for deductions on my T1 return. I shop duty-free. And I moved to Alberta where we have no PST. So I love the Canadian consumer carbon tax. Every quarter, the feds e-transfer me the amount that they think I am going to spend on it. Then I do things (Weather stripping! Buy groceries locally! Walk the kids to school!) to not pay it and keep the money.
A program that lets thrifty Canadians stick it to The Man plus fight climate change is good under any name. Keep the Tax.
Tracy Duncan, Calgary
Rome burns as Ford fiddles with licence plates
Doug Ford hopes to finally 'Get It Done' when legislature resumes Tuesday, Feb. 20
As Ford and his PC gang return to Queen's Park this week and we read the list of "goodies" in his Get It Done Act grab bag, the irony is almost too much to bear! Ford uses the term "Get It Done" to mean things he is doing "for" us ... while I see it as underscoring that what he is "getting done" is the ongoing degradation of our province and the complete destruction of our cherished institutions, such as health care, education and the Greenbelt.
Every day, I read of new and crazy ideas being imposed upon Ontarians. The latest was a 24-hour-long policy announcement, reversed the very next day, requiring LCBO customers to be carded before entry. This was followed by a cancellation of the need to renew licence plate registration on a regular basis. Why? Because his elimination of licence plate renewal fees was another of his poorly implemented ideas. He just eliminated the fees, with no thought or plan regarding continuing to ensure vehicles were legally on the road.
We are facing a tsunami of aging baby boomers. Where is Ford's plan to address this huge need? Rome burns while Ford continues to fiddle with licence plates.
I am further upset to see Ford's current wave of TV ads, touting his wonderful job for Ontario. The one on the state of health care is particularly dishonest and revolting. It shows spacious, uncrowded hospitals, where patients and staff are smiling and laughing together, and no one is in distress. These ads are complete lies and should be ruled illegal to air. The text superimposed on the ad says that we should see all the ways that the current government is "connecting us to care" not providing care but connecting us to it - code for increasing privatization.
Ontarians need to speak up and object to these policies and propaganda. We must not allow him to continue his hatchet job on our Ontario and certainly not allow him another four years to continue the dismantling of our beloved province.
Pam Tomasevic, Mississauga
Wider sidewalks would address safety issues
There is no reason to fear a safer Avenue Road, Feb. 18
The only thing to fear is the present six-lane perilous section of Avenue Road between St. Clair Avenue and Bloor Street. It is currently a major safety concern due to its narrow sidewalks - many too narrow to safely accommodate wheelchairs or strollers.
There are logical fact-based arguments that this must change. The local councillors Dianne Saxe and Josh Matlow know it must change. Let us hope city recommendations soon address these safety problems and present options to make this a more people-friendly, climate-friendly, and inclusive transportation corridor that will be safe for drivers, walkers and wheelers alike.
Mary Helen Spence, Toronto
Transforming Avenue Road is long overdue
There is no reason to fear a safer Avenue Road, Feb. 18
I lived on Avenue Road near Dupont in the 1990s and regularly walked along it to get to work or for errands. At one point, I wrote to my Metro councillor in the hope that planned roadworks might narrow such a dangerous and intimidating street for pedestrians, with its speeding cars and narrow sidewalks, and she wrote back that, much as she'd like to see it, it could never happen. So it's heartening to see, three decades later, that this necessary transformation is within grasp. A two-kilometre six-lane highway is not just an anomaly in the city, but serves no real purpose now that the streets north and south of it are all four lanes. It's time to start making it a normal downtown main street.
Dylan Reid, Toronto
Changes to thoroughfare unnecessary
There is no reason to fear a safer Avenue Road, Feb. 18
These proposed changes are unnecessary and unrealistic. To refer to Avenue Road as "high speed" is a gross misstatement. Far more accurate would be its description as "typical Toronto gridlock," particularly for northbound traffic. And where are all the pedestrians who need safer sidewalks? Except for the first few blocks north of Bloor, there is more than ample space for the relatively small amount of pedestrian usage. This is the typical answer to solving a Toronto "problem": reduce the lanes by one third, and presto, problem solved. This is nothing but foolishness.
David S. Wilson, Toronto
Focus on benefits change can bring
There is no reason to fear a safer Avenue Road, Feb. 18
I get it. People don't like changes to where they live. But many must be made for my grandson to be able to enjoy a decent climate when he grows up.
I live on a street that recently installed a contra flow bike lane. When the public consultation was taking place, some of us were concerned about the increased bicycle, moped and scooter traffic that would follow. (We've all experienced daredevils on two wheels.)
Now, pretty well all of us are pleased to be part of the solution, rather than the problem.
Change can often improve what we had thought was the best of all possible worlds.
John Liss, Toronto
Personhood starting earlier and earlier
Warnings of the impact of fertility treatments in Alabama rush in after frozen embryo ruling, Feb. 20
In the wake of Alabama's Supreme Court ruling that frozen embryos are children, is the next step to grant personhood to the gleam in a father's eye?
Glen Loucks, Toronto

Document 160 of 500

'We knew it was going to be bafflegab': Sault Ste. Marie residents without clinic disappointed by Tories' reaction

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-knew-was-going-be-bafflegab/docview/2929386758/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27We+knew+it+was+going+to+be+bafflegab%27%3A+Sault+Ste.+Marie+residents+without+clinic+disappointed+by+Tories%27+reaction&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As 10,000 residents of Sault Ste. Marie prepare to be dropped by their longtime medical clinic in a stunning move symptomatic of Ontario's chronic doctor shortage, their prospects for finding physicians look bleak.
Community representatives came to the legislature Wednesday seeking help, but left disappointed after Premier Doug Ford and Health Minister Sylvia Jones touted recent investments in health care without offering practical solutions.
"We knew it was going to be bafflegab," Algoma Steel retiree Burnie Thorp said after New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles pressed the government on their behalf.
In the legislature's daily question period, Ford and Jones cited $110 million announced Feb. 1 to expand 78 primary care teams in the province - including two in the Sault Ste. Marie area - helping 328,000 more patients get family doctors.
"We are committed to making sure the people of Sault Ste. Marie and across Ontario have access to primary care," Jones said.
Her office later told the Star the funding for the two Sault Ste. Marie clinics would help 4,550 patients get access to care.
That's fewer than half the 10,000 people being cut loose by the Group Health Centre founded by the United Steelworkers union before the advent of medicare.
About 2.3 million Ontarians do not have a primary care physician and the Ontario Medical Association says that number could double in the next two years given a growing population and doctor retirements.
The Group Health Centre - a major medical provider serving 60,000 patients in Sault Ste. Marie, a remote northern city of 73,000 - is struggling with a shortage of doctors because of retirements and departures. There are 31 physicians left.
Centre officials announced in January they had no choice but to remove 10,000 patients from its roster effective the end of May, a move Ontario Medical Association president Dr. Andrew Park called "unprecedented."
"The people in Sault Ste. Marie didn't hear from this minister any sympathy for the crisis that they're facing," Stiles told reporters following question period.
"People have been waiting for the government to swoop in and help figure this out and they don't seem to be focused on it at all."
Raising concerns in the legislature, Stiles was heckled by Sault Ste. Marie MPP Ross Romano, a Progressive Conservative, who accused her of mischaracterizing the situation.
"I will not take lessons from the member from Sault Ste. Marie, who has failed his constituents," she shot back.
Thorp said he never imagined he'd be on the hunt for a primary care clinic at the age of 77 after 35 years of going to the Group Health Centre, which has several locations offering a wide variety of services.
"I might look a little old to be an orphan, but I am an orphan ... you would expect to have health care continuing through your lifetime," added Thorp, president of the Steelworkers Organization of Active Retirees.
"We are obviously people who pay taxes. We have paid for this health care and so we think it is our right."
Thorp said he has been told he will be provided with a year's worth of prescription renewals before he is taken off the roster.
"That's taken a little bit of the pressure off, but around December I'm going to start thinking, 'Holy crap, where am I going to get these prescriptions?'"
Mike Da Prat, president of Steelworkers local 2251 in the Soo, who is also being taken off the clinic roster, told a news conference he was given a list of walk-in clinics in the area to try.
The Group Health Centre said it had been approaching the government for help over the last few months before issuing an open letter to the community stating patients whose doctors have "recently retired or left their practices" will have to seek treatment elsewhere. "We recognize the profound effect this will have."
Because older doctors are retiring, there will be more impact on older patients, said Da Prat.
The health minister's office said it got just over a week's notice of the plans to chop patients from the roster, leaving little time to find replacement physicians.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Belleville needs business case for health hub

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/belleville-needs-business-case-health-hub/docview/2929385145/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Belleville+needs+business+case+for+health+hub&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An eastern Ontario city beleaguered by opioids must come up with a business plan if it wants $2 million to fund a health and social services hub, Premier Doug Ford's government said Wednesday.
Progressive Conservative MPP Todd Smith, who represents the area, said he has made it clear to the mayor of Belleville that the province needs a costing and business plan before moving forward.
"I can't go into (the) Treasury Board and just say I need two million bucks," Smith told The Canadian Press. "There has to be a costing estimate, there has to be a business plan around that, and so that's what the organizations involved are working to do right now."
Belleville declared a state of emergency this month after first responders were called out to 17 drug overdoses in 24 hours.
Hastings Prince Edward Public Health said Health Canada testing has confirmed that the drugs police found in Belleville linked to the spate of overdoses included an opioid, a benzodiazepine (a depressant like Valium) and xylazine, a tranquilizer used by veterinarians.
Fentanyl, the opioid involved in the vast majority of drug overdose deaths in Ontario, is often combined with benzodiazepines. The addition of xylazine is a more recent phenomenon.
Naloxone can reverse an opioid overdose, but does nothing for benzodiazepines and xylazine.
Mayor Neil Ellis said Tuesday the province did not commit to the $2-million funding request to help kick-start construction of a centralized hub that would offer addictions and mental-health services and money to open a detox centre.
Ellis said the city will go it alone if the province won't step up.
"We can't wait a month, we can't wait three months," Ellis said. "Our hope was to have this up and running by December this year."
The city is also working on a "mental health and addictions strategy" with the local Canadian Mental Health Association office.
CREDIT: The Canadian Press

Document 162 of 500

Are most Canadians gaining from carbon tax?: Trudeau's claim is accurate, but it's also incomplete

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/are-most-canadians-gaining-carbon-tax/docview/2929385128/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Are+most+Canadians+gaining+from+carbon+tax%3F%3A+Trudeau%27s+claim+is+accurate%2C+but+it%27s+also+incomplete&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the lead-up to the next federal election, the Star is taking a look at the leaders of Canada's major federal parties and fact checking some of their most common political slogans and talking points.

Today, the Star is diving into a claim often touted by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau: that eight out of 10 Canadian households get more money back through the carbon rebate than they pay under the federal carbon price.
It's a claim the Trudeau Liberals deploy frequently in defence of the highly-divisive policy, particularly as a rebuttal to Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's relentless crusade against the carbon levy.
But is the prime minister providing the full picture? Here's what we know.
What's Trudeau talking about?
Carbon pricing is one of many tools that can be used in the fight against climate change, and is built on the idea that imposing costs on pollution will deter people and businesses from burning fossil fuels and help reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
There's two parts to Ottawa's system. The first is a charge on fossil fuels like gasoline and natural gas. That "fuel charge" trickles down to consumers when they pay their home gas bill and fill up their cars. It can also show up in other areas like food costs, although those impacts are less clear.
The second component is aimed at industries that produce heavy emissions, but that's not what Trudeau is focusing on here.
The rebates Trudeau is referencing used to be called Climate Action Incentive payments, a jargony mouthful the Liberals last week rebranded as the Canada Carbon Rebate.
According to the government, 90 per cent of the money collected from the carbon price goes back to people in the form of quarterly rebates. If you spend less on the so-called carbon tax (like reducing how much you spend on gas), the more likely it is your rebate will cover what you spent, and even exceed it.
The price on carbon will increase each year until 2030, and the rebates increase alongside it.
Is Trudeau telling the truth?
In the words of Canada's Parliamentary Budget Officer (PBO), Yves Giroux, Trudeau's claim is "accurate, but it's incomplete."
The PBO is an independent and non-partisan office that provides economic and financial analysis to the government.
And it was a March 2023 report from the PBO that came to the conclusion Trudeau is citing. But there are some big caveats at play.
Giroux concluded that most households in Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba, Ontario, Prince Edward Island, and Newfoundland and Labrador will get more from the rebates than the amount they pay in the federal carbon price and related GST.
But that conclusion only considers fiscal factors - that is, the actual amount of money Canadians directly or indirectly pay because of the carbon tax.
The PBO took data on consumption patterns from Statistics Canada, and then subtracted the amount an average family in each province gets from the rebates.
So if you're only looking at the money, and not considering anything else, it's true that most Canadian households that pay the federal fuel charge are seeing a gain.
"Most people come out about even, because the reality is, whether you're a few dollars up or a few dollars down, it is pretty close to a neutral effect for the average person," said Michael Bernstein, the executive director of Clean Prosperity.
Is Trudeau wrong once you
include other factors?
If you take in the wider picture, yes. The issue here is that while the Liberals are citing a correct conclusion, they're not sharing necessary context about it.
Indeed, the PBO came to an entirely different conclusion when he added the economic impacts of the federal fuel charge - like reductions in employment, income and profits - to his analysis.
"If you think about the transportation sector, they will presumably be paying a higher proportion than other sectors because they rely more heavily on fossil fuels," Giroux told the Star. "That will affect the economic output of the sectors that are more heavily exposed to the carbon tax."
Under those conditions, which Giroux said in his report provides "a more complete picture" of the household impact of the carbon tax, "the net cost increases for all households, reflecting the overall negative economic impact of the federal fuel charge."
It's that framing the Liberals are downplaying, as the Conservatives trumpet it as proof that the politically-charged policy is misguided.
How did the Liberals respond
to the PBO's wider analysis?
They didn't think it was entirely fair.
That's because Giroux's report didn't factor in the costs of climate change.
Giroux told the Star it's "very difficult" to estimate the economic impact of climate change in the short-term. He did, in 2022, analyze the long-term impacts of climate change on Canada's economy and estimated that even if all countries around the world meet their climate commitments, changes in weather patterns could slash Canada's real GDP by 5.8 per cent - $145 billion in 2021 dollars - by 2100.
Giroux said it's hard to look at a period of just a few years and determine what environmental events were caused by climate change alone.
And he said estimating the cost of doing nothing technically falls outside his mandate.
"We're providing information on the cost of what the government is putting forward ... and it's very difficult to cost or to estimate the cost of doing nothing, which is not government policy," Giroux said.
CREDIT: Raisa Patel Toronto Star

Subject: Rebates; Economic impact; Financial analysis; Costs; Fossil fuels; Emissions; Carbon; Prime ministers; Households; Climate change; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Rebates Economic impact Financial analysis Costs Environmental tax

Document 163 of 500

Omnibus bill is political theatre

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/omnibus-bill-is-political-theatre/docview/2929384859/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Omnibus+bill+is+political+theatre&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There once existed a general expectation that bills introduced in parliaments and legislatures bore neutral titles seeking to indicate their content and purpose.
That quaint custom seems to have passed. In contemporary practice, the titling of legislation is, as often as not, an act of blatant political propaganda or provocation.
To wit, the Ontario government's Getting It Done Act introduced this week at Queen's Park, an omnibus bill that is the namesake of the PC government's 2022 campaign slogan.
Subtle, the Doug Ford government is not.
It's not unreasonable to say that Ontarians might have gleaned a better understanding of the contents of this unholy mishmash had the province named it the Crazy Clown Car Act, or the Empty Political Gestures Act, or, as a political historian once joked, the Poke the Opposition in the Eye Act.
In the era of constant campaigning, it appears governments now see even the simple act of titling bills as just another opportunity for partisan messaging.
But there's more to the matter than mere mischievous wordplay. At core in this bill, the Ford government is again flaunting its chronic disregard for transparency and accountability.
The employment of omnibus bills - large pieces of legislation into which many diverse policy issues and manoeuvres are packed - is almost by definition designed to hasten the process, reduce scrutiny and limit debate, thereby minimizing public uproar and resistance.
The guiding imperatives of such bills are speed and limited opposition involvement.
Which, for a government whose signature characteristic has been half-baked policy rashness and behind-the-scenes decision-making, is on the face of things alarming.
As NDP Leader Marit Stiles noted, "where the government has gone wrong over and over again is not listening to people.
"They've spent more time in the last year reversing course and they haven't actually come up with practical solutions that will make life easier for people," she said.
In that, she is not wrong.
Granted, Ford's gambit is not as egregious, say, as the infamous "Bully Bill" of the Mike Harris government almost 30 years ago, a behemoth of more than 200 pages that contained massive structural implications for a wide range of public institutions in one fell swoop. Still it is worrying.
It is a mélange of fluff and the seriously consequential. It is part arrogant time-machine - trying to control what future governments can do, particularly on highway tolls and carbon pricing, while revisiting previous efforts on the home-building front that ended in disaster.
For instance, it would shorten environmental assessments to speed construction of new highways and hydro transmission lines, allow for quicker expropriations before such assessments were completed, and change some urban boundaries.
Into the hopper were thrown elements that would require a referendum on any future provincial carbon pricing program, ban new tolls on Ontario highways and, tossing the merely administrative in with the potentially dramatic, automatically renew vehicle registrations for owners with no outstanding fines.
On the face of it, Ford's initiative violates parliamentary norms in seeking to bind the hands of future governments in a piece of legislation so convoluted as to defy coherent parliamentary review.
The fundamental purpose of a parliament or legislature is not, after all, efficiency and productivity in racing through matters as fast as possible, but in scrutiny and accountability to craft the best possible policy.
But there are so many components to Ford's bill that MPPs cannot possibly decide on them all in a single vote - a reality that adds to an inexorable shift of power from legislators to the executive.
In all, there is no discernible unifying theme in the Getting It Done Act other than the self-interested goal of advancing the re-election prospects of the Ford government.
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner said this week Ford's omnibus effort is "performative politics at its worst" and the bill should have been called the "Get it Done Wrong Act."
In that, he is quite right.
CREDIT: ;

Subject: Legislation; Politics; Accountability

Location: Ontario Canada; Canada

Cataract procedures drawing billing complaints: Patients told they agreed to pay for non-insured service

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/cataract-procedures-drawing-billing-complaints/docview/2929384802/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Cataract+procedures+drawing+billing+complaints%3A+Patients+told+they+agreed+to+pay+for+non-insured+service&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Ontario prepares to expand the number of private clinics that perform publicly funded cataract surgeries, documents show those procedures form one of the most common complaints the province receives from patients about unfair billing practices.
The Canadian Press submitted a freedom-of-information request for copies of complaints made under the law banning medical professionals from charging patients for services covered by the Ontario Health Insurance Plan.
From 2020 to 2022 there were 68 complaints, with 15 of them about charges for eye exams and 13 of them related to cataract procedures. Other complaints involved a range of medical procedures including blood tests and prenatal testing.
While the absolute numbers are relatively small, NDP health critic France Gelinas said the true number of patients with similar complaints is likely much higher, because not many people know how to go through the process.
The ministry's response to many of the patients who complained about charges for cataract surgery was to tell them the records showed they had actually consented to paying for non-insured services.
In response to one person who complained that they had paid $2,400 for "cataract extraction with intraocular implant service covered by Ministry of Health," the ministry wrote that records showed the patient had agreed to non-insured extras. They received a $200 credit for the cost of a medically necessary lens, but the premium lens they received had to be paid for out of pocket.
"According to the records provided, you had consented to paying for and receiving these services," the ministry told the patient. "The (Commitment to the Future of Medicare Act) does not apply to uninsured services, and any costs associated with these services are the responsibility of the patient."
Gelinas said this shows that patients receiving publicly funded surgeries in private clinics are being upsold and are not always clear on what charges they will have to pay out of pocket.
"If the people went through the process of finding which number to call (to complain), of leaving a message, of waiting three weeks for somebody to call them back, of waiting another three months for them to do their investigation to get an answer ... you didn't have consent," she said.
The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Allison Jones The Canadian Press

Document 165 of 500

Ontario patients pleaded for help after 10,000 were dumped by their clinic. They say Doug Ford left them disappointed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-patients-pleaded-help-after-10-000-were/docview/2930046179/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+patients+pleaded+for+help+after+10%2C000+were+dumped+by+their+clinic.+They+say+Doug+Ford+left+them+disappointed&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As 10,000 residents of Sault Ste. Marie prepare to be dropped by their longtime medical clinic in a stunning move symptomatic of Ontario's chronic doctor shortage, their prospects for finding physicians look bleak.
Community representatives came to the legislature Wednesday seeking help, but left disappointed after Premier Doug Ford and Health Minister Sylvia Jones touted recent investments in health care without offering practical solutions.
"We knew it was going to be bafflegab," Algoma Steel retiree Burnie Thorp said in an interview after New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles pressed the government on their behalf.
In the legislature's daily question period, Ford and Jones cited $110 million announced Feb. 1 to expand 78 primary care teams in the province - including two in the Sault Ste. Marie area - helping 328,000 more patients get family doctors.
"We are committed to making sure the people of Sault Ste. Marie and across Ontario have access to primary care," Jones said.
Her office later told the Star the funding for the two Sault Ste. Marie clinics would help 4,550 patients get access to care.
That's fewer than half the 10,000 people being cut loose by the Group Health Centre founded by the United Steelworkers union before the advent of medicare.
About 2.3 million Ontarians do not have a primary care physician and the Ontario Medical Association says that number could double in the next two years given a growing population and doctor retirements.
The Group Health Centre - a major medical provider serving 60,000 patients in Sault Ste. Marie, a remote northern city of 73,000 - is struggling with a shortage of doctors because of retirements and departures. There are 31 physicians left.
Centre officials announced in January they had no choice but to remove 10,000 patients from its roster effective the end of May, a move Ontario Medical Association president Dr. Andrew Park called "unprecedented."
"The people in Sault Ste. Marie didn't hear from this minister any sympathy for the crisis that they're facing," Stiles told reporters following question period.
"People have been waiting for the government to swoop in and help figure this out and they don't seem to be focused on it at all."
Raising concerns in the legislature, Stiles was heckled by Sault Ste. Marie MPP Ross Romano, a Progressive Conservative, who accused her of mischaracterizing the situation.
"I will not take lessons from the member from Sault Ste. Marie, who has failed his constituents," she shot back.
Thorp said he never imagined he'd be on the hunt for a primary care clinic at the age of 77 after 35 years of going to the Group Health Centre, which has several locations offering a wide variety of services.
"I might look a little old to be an orphan, but I am an orphan ... you would expect to have health care continuing through your lifetime," added Thorp, president of the Steelworkers Organization of Active Retirees.
"We are obviously people who pay taxes. We have paid for this health care and so we think it is our right."
Thorp said he has been told he will be provided with a year's worth of prescription renewals before he is taken off the roster.
"That's taken a little bit of the pressure off, but around December I'm going to start thinking, 'Holy crap, where am I going to get these prescriptions?'"
Mike Da Prat, president of Steelworkers local 2251 in the Soo, who is also being taken off the clinic roster, told a news conference he was given a list of walk-in clinics in the area to try.
The Group Health Centre said it had been approaching the government for help over the last few months before issuing an open letter to the community stating patients whose doctors have "recently retired or left their practices" will have to seek treatment elsewhere.
"We recognize the profound effect this will have."
Because older doctors are retiring, there will be more impact on older patients, said Da Prat.
The health minister's office said it got just over a week's notice of the plans to chop patients from the roster, leaving little time to find replacement physicians.
CAPTION: Minister of Health Sylvia Jones during question period in the Ontario legislature on Feb. 20, 2024.

Document 166 of 500

'Very disappointed': Ford government says international student cap will hurt economy, calls out Ottawa

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/very-disappointed-ford-government-says/docview/2930046168/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Very+disappointed%27%3A+Ford+government+says+international+student+cap+will+hurt+economy%2C+calls+out+Ottawa&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ottawa did not consult with the provinces before announcing caps on international students, and it's not just colleges and universities that will feel the impact - Ontario's economy will too, says Post-Secondary Minister Jill Dunlop.
"We're very disappointed with the federal government," Dunlop said in the legislature on Wednesday.
She said she's heard from fellow MPPs and other ministries about "the impact this is going to have on our economy" and that the tourism sector in particular "is going to be devastated without these students" given the number of post-graduation work permits will drop.
"That's why, when we're working with the federal government on the allocation, we will be looking at the labour market needs across regions in Ontario as a number one priority."
Also, "ensuring that there is guaranteed housing for international students coming to our campus will be a priority, and ensuring that the economic needs of each region are a concern as well. There is going to be an impact across Ontario and the federal government did absolutely no consultations with the provinces or with the sector."
Last month, the federal immigration minister - feeling the pressure of the growing housing crisis - chopped the number of new study permits issued to international students by 35 per cent, with spots to be allotted based on each province's population. Ontario, where more than half of Canada's current international students study, will see a 50 per cent drop.
New Democrat MPP Peggy Sattler (London West), her party's post-secondary critic, said "colleges and universities are bracing for the impact of a 50 per cent reduction in international study permits at a time when the sector is already at a (financial) breaking point."
She said the province has seen decades of "chronic underfunding by this government and the Liberals before them, which has put our post-secondary institutions starved for resources and now they are on the brink," with 10 of 23 schools running deficits.
Premier Doug Ford has already ruled out a tuition increase this fall for domestic students - as recommended by the government's blue ribbon panel - but it remains unknown if Ontario will boost funding for colleges and universities as the panel also urged.
"We will be announcing our path forward shortly," Dunlop said.
Liberal MPP Adil Shamji (Don Valley East), his party's colleges and universities critic, said schools "have been suffering for years" and the government should have already increased funding.
"There's not a moment to lose," he said, adding schools were forced to turn to international students to make up for gaps in funding - they pay three to four times the tuition of domestic students - but that they "should not be the piggy bank."
"What I really worry about is with a cap on international students, without the kind of funding that universities are asking, without the support that they need, what we're going to see is universities have no choice but to either cut back on programs, cut back on staff, or cut back on student services."
CAPTION: Jill Dunlop, Ontario's minister of colleges and universities, attends question period at the Ontario Legislature on Nov. 28, 2023.

Subject: Foreign students; Colleges & universities; Funding

Business indexing term: Subject: Colleges & universities

Title: 'Very disappointed': Ford government says international student cap will hurt economy, calls out Ottawa

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Document 167 of 500

Ford government says law to punish local politicians who harass staff coming soon

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-says-law-punish-local-politicians/docview/2930046096/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+says+law+to+punish+local+politicians+who+harass+staff+coming+soon&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ford government says new legislation that could see local politicians thrown out of office for workplace harassment should be introduced before the summer.
Speaking to reporters at Queen's Park on Wednesday, Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra called it "a very important piece of legislation. It has to be constitutional, but has to actually achieve the results that I think that everybody's asking for. So absolutely, we are going to bring something forward and continue to work" with municipalities.
Asked if the bill could come before the legislature rises in June, Calandra said: "I don't see why not."
The Ford government previously voted down a private member's bill from Liberal MPP Stephen Blais (Orléans), saying it would leave voters to decide who to remove from office at election time.
But with more than 200 municipalities supporting the idea and the non-partisan advocacy group The Women of Ontario Say No keeping the issue in the spotlight, last month Calandra changed course, promising the government would create "airtight" legislation.
Municipalities across the province have struggled with situations where elected officials have been found to have committed workplace harassment, but there is little that can be done beyond docking pay.
Emily McIntosh, of The Women of Ontario Say No, said removal from office is a must in any legislation.
"The premise is simple," she said. "You should go to work and you should not be subjected to abuse. We are asking municipally elected representatives to be held to the very same standard as every other worker in Ontario. Elected representatives are not above the people - they are of the people."
Blais recounted his own days on Ottawa council, where fellow politician Rick Chiarelli was found to have harassed and abused staff and job applicants for years.
His bill would have allowed for politicians to be removed from office after findings of inappropriate behaviour by an integrity commissioner and a judge's review.
"We absolutely need this change," added Aurora Coun. Rachel Gilliland. "No council is immune to it."
David Arbuckle, who is executive director of the Association of Municipal Managers, Clerks and Treasurers of Ontario, said "while most councillor-staff relationships and interactions are positive, respectful and focused on collaboration to achieve community goals, our members are telling us that the overall relationship between elected officials and professional municipal staff is increasingly confrontational" and that can impact staff recruitment and retention.
Farah Grozelle is a small-business owner in Bradford who has been working with The Women of Ontario Say No.
"A majority government can pass legislation in less than four weeks," she said. "It is now the winter of 2024, and here we are without accountability structures in place with respect to the safety and well-being of individuals and communities. I know we can do better, now."
Leaders of the opposition parties have also thrown their support behind such legislation.
"We need to close these loopholes," NDP Leader Marit Stiles told a press conference Wednesday morning at Queen's Park with Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner.
"We need transparency, accountability for those who do the wrong thing," Crombie added. "We know politics can be a nasty business, but it doesn't have to be."
McIntosh said she'd also like to eventually see legislation covering provincial politicians.
With files from Robert Benzie and Rob Ferguson
CAPTION: Emily McIntosh is shown at the Barrie City Hall on March 18, 2023.
CREDIT: Lauren Nicol, Special to the Star

Subject: Legislation; Politics; Politicians; Accountability

Document 168 of 500

Increasing corporate taxes would restore wealth to governments

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/increasing-corporate-taxes-would-restore-wealth/docview/2929126521/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Increasing+corporate+taxes+would+restore+wealth+to+governments&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tapeworm economy arrives in Ontario, Feb. 20
Congratulations to Armine Yalnizyan on her outstanding and succinct description of the tapeworm economy. The term could not be more apt. Her article and its solutions should be implemented immediately with one major addition - raising corporate taxes.
This attack on economies around the world began 30 years ago with U.S. President Ronald Reagan and Margaret Thatcher, former Prime Minister of the United Kingdom, who ushered in globalization which disemboweled North American workforces and slashed corporate taxes ushering in "the age of austerity."
Ever since governments have been cutting taxes on corporations that Yalnizyan points out have grown like a tapeworm ever since. Corporate taxes plummeted, profits soared and labour rates for workers remained stubbornly stagnant amounting to a slave labour society.
It is time to reverse 30 years of austerity and restore proper funding to the services that we all cherish in Ontario. An immediate increase in corporate taxes of 20 per cent would make corporations pay what we all pay. This increase would restore wealth to governments not the corporate sector.
We must cut out the tapeworm to bring our economy back to health.
Untreated, a tapeworm will eventually kill its host.
Robert Bahlieda, Newmarket
Doug Ford has indeed swallowed the tapeworm
If anyone is wondering which path Ontario is treading and the continuing impact that will have on ordinary citizens, look no further than Armine Yalnizyan's column.
Premier Doug Ford has indeed swallowed the tape worm and we will all pay for it.
He will sail along in blissful ignorance because he doesn't read and therefore learns nothing from the follies of others.
Despite this, we need voices like hers to allow us to make an informed choice at the next election.
Peter Bradley, Mississauga
Put car in garage to deter theftA strategy to foil car thieves. But will it focus on the remedy? Feb. 20
One thing that may help to reduce the number of car thefts would be for those folks who have a garage to actually put their car in it. There sure are a whole lot of car owners who leave their car in the driveway when it could be securely locked in their garage.
Perhaps auto insurance companies could offer a price reduction incentive to those who do garage their vehicle. It could be like the snow tire incentive that they already offer.
Those insurance companies who sell both auto and home insurance could also offer a cost reduction for houses that have a car garage and a shed to put your other stuff in.
Those incentives will put many vehicles beyond the reach of would-be thieves.
Roger Hilderley, Stratford
Replace car fobs with keys
Now that the summit for car theft is over and all these brilliant minds have "said all the right things" where to now?
It seems to me that what has precipitated the epidemic of car thefts is electronics. Here's a novel idea. Replace all the new doodads with keys. Keys to unlock the doors, keys to start your car and then lock the steering wheel when removed, eliminate the fobs.
Larry Snow, Oakville
Leave the early years aloneWhy Ontario's kindergarten reforms worry some experts, Feb. 11
As a retired educator, I am very concerned about Doug Ford government's direction in reforming the kindergarten program. Play is the "work" of young children. Friedrich Fröbel, the coiner of "kindergarten" believed that this was a place where children could develop and flourish freely through self-directed play under the guidance, not direction, of the teacher. Downloading specific expectations on young minds with short attention spans is inane. "
Highly trained, motivated and caring early childhood educators accept each child as they are, then guide and nurture them during their time together. They understand child development and carefully design meaningful, often literature-based, activities that promote literacy, numeracy and social development; critical building blocks necessary for success in reading, mathematics and life. These teachers understand and recognize key stages in child development and use observation to regularly evaluate/celebrate a child's success and report to parents. Formal instruction has no place in these wonderful, child-centred, activity-based environments. Each child is unique. Development cannot be rushed.
Is Education Minister Stephen Lecce aware that junior and senior kindergarten are optional years of schooling? Attendance isn't mandatory until the year in which a child turns six years of age. Besides, given their track record with the Greenbelt and Ontario Place, we should be concerned whether this change is the best pedagogy for four- and five-year-olds.
It would be best if the entire Ford government took a deep breath, sat in a circle on the floor with a glass of milk and a healthy snack and listened to an early year's teacher read Robert Fulgham's "All I Really Needed To Know I Learned in Kindergarten," followed by Robert Kraus's "Leo the Late Bloomer." They might learn something.
Robert Parker, St. Catharines
Put Eglinton Crosstown at the top of the 'get it done' list
Doug Ford hopes to 'Get It Done' when legislature resumes, Feb. 20
On Family Day I drove along Eglinton Street East and the tracks of the Crosstown LRT were covered with snow and all the stations empty and barricaded. I hope Premier Doug Ford will apply his "Get It Done" mantra to getting the Eglinton Crosstown LRT running by this Spring. The LRT was supposed to be carrying passengers in 2020!
Ian Darragh, Toronto
Since when do terrorists respect the law?
Judge orders Scarborough Canadian Tire terrorist to sign terrorism peace bond, Feb. 15
A convicted terrorist with schizophrenia must sign a "peace bond" or return to prison? I didn't know that terrorists were respecters of peace by choice or will. The signing of such a paper certainly makes me feel a lot safer. About as safe as I feel with the wishy-washy bylaws against savage dogs referenced in the same edition of the Star.
Anita Dermer, Toronto
Navalny's death makes it clear we should increase support of Ukraine
Canada to send more than 800 drones to Ukraine, Feb. 19
According to the article, support for Ukraine is dropping. Given the recent death of Russia's top opposition leader Alexei Navalny, it's obvious we should be increasing support. Russia is run by a dictator who considers the country and its neighbours to be his personal property. He has not only looted the Russian economy, but he has also invaded some of his neighbours. His intentions are quite clear and if he isn't stopped in Ukraine, he will likely continue with other countries. It's a similar situation to what happened with Hitler, prior to the Second World War.
Incidentally, when I was young, one of my friends was Ukrainian. His parents experienced firsthand what Russia had done to their country, back in the 1930s. We're seeing it again today.
Ukraine needs our help to stop Vladimir Putin.
James Knott, Mississauga
CAPTION: In September1983, then U.S. President Ronald Reagan shakes hands with then British Prime Minister Margaret Thatcher at the White House in Washington.

Document 170 of 500

Ford refuses to apologize: Nurse, teacher, public servant wages were capped under controversial law

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-refuses-apologize/docview/2928800326/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+refuses+to+apologize%3A+Nurse%2C+teacher%2C+public+servant+wages+were+capped+under+controversial+law&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was supposed to be a fresh start, but the past kept coming back to haunt Premier Doug Ford as MPPs returned to the legislature following the winter break.
A defiant Ford refused to apologize to thousands of nurses, teachers and other public servants who had their wages capped under his controversial Bill 124.
While he is backtracking on the law deemed unconstitutional by the courts last week, NDP Leader Marit Stiles said he has some explaining to do to workers for limiting their raises to one per cent annually.
"Will he apologize to Ontario's hard-working nurses, PSWs, teachers, educational assistants and all the public sector workers for suppressing their wages with Bill 124?" demanded Stiles.
"Look what we've done for the health-care workers, which we think the world of - we think the world of the nurses," shot back Ford, declining to express any contrition.
The forthcoming repeal of Bill 124 came against the backdrop of the Progressive Conservatives tabling separate omnibus legislation - dubbed the Get It Done Act after the 2022 campaign slogan.
It is designed to reboot a PC government that spent the past six months under a cloud due to the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal now under investigation by the RCMP.
The bill fast-tracks construction of highways and other infrastructure by streamlining environmental assessments, bans tolls on all roads except Hwy. 407, eliminates the need for licence plate renewals, undoes the government's unilateral changes to a dozen cities' official plans and attempts to force a future premier to hold a referendum on any carbon-pricing scheme.
Mocked by Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie as "another attempt to get it undone," the legislation corrects some previous mistakes the Tories made.
"This government is one that just reverses its decisions and its proposals. They need to do a lot more homework," said Crombie, who won the Liberal leadership on Dec. 2.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner, whose caucus doubled in size with new MPP Aislinn Clancy taking her seat in the house Tuesday, derided Ford's legislation as the "Getting It Done Wrong Act."
"It's performative politics at its worst, distracting from the Ford government's failure in addressing the housing crisis, and the fact that they've made access to health care worse, people are sick and tired of having to wait for long times and emergency departments not having access to primary health care, not having access to mental health care," said Schreiner.
The Liberals made sport of former Ford cabinet minister Parm Gill suddenly resigning four weeks ago to join federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's team.
With a Milton byelection looming later this year to fill Gill's seat, the provincial Tories are once again promising to bring all-day GO trains there.
"The Milton service is only happening because one of their MPPs wanted to escape from the premier," said Liberal MPP Andrea Hazell (Scarborough-Guildwood).
"It appears that the only way to get this government to invest in a riding is to have the PC MPP jump ship from their caucus," said Hazell.
Crombie, who does not have a seat in the legislature, said she has not ruled out running in Milton.
"I'm taking a very serious look at it. I'm very familiar with their issues and I think very strongly about the need for all day GO train service on the Milton line," said the former Mississauga mayor.
When asked about the referendum portion of the bill - which is the Tories' attempt to tie Crombie, a former Liberal MP, to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's carbon tax - she dismissed it as a "stunt ... to deflect from what's going on with the Conservative caucus with the reversals, the scandals, the insider deals."
Ontarians were exempt from paying the federal levy until Ford cancelled the previous provincial Liberal government's cap-and-trade alliance with Quebec and California in 2018, which costs the treasury $2 billion in lost revenue annually.
The Tories bristled when asked if there would be any retroactive plebiscites on the two carbon pricing schemes they introduced in 2022 and last year - the emission performance standards program that targets industrial polluters and the clean energy credit registry cap-and-trade system.
"We've worked exclusively on removing carbon taxes," insisted Government House Leader Paul Calandra before fleeing reporters to avoid answering more questions about the government's own greenhouse gas reduction efforts.
According to the Get It Done Act, any future premier would have to hold a referendum that "authorizes the carbon pricing program" before implementing it.
"A referendum question must be clear, concise and impartial in its wording and must be capable of being answered in the affirmative or the negative," the legislation says.
But the bill suggests no plebiscite would be binding on a new administration.
"A referendum shall not be interpreted to require the executive council of a subsequent government formed by another party to establish a carbon pricing program or give authority to establish a carbon pricing program as described."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford backed down on wage restraint legislation after the courts found last week that Bill 124 infringed upon Charter rights.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie AND ROB FERGUSON Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Leadership; Legislation; Referendums; Emissions trading; Carbon; Nurses; Roads & highways

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership Emissions trading

Location: Ontario Canada; Canada

People: Ford, Doug

Title: Ford refuses to apologize:   Nurse, teacher, public servant wages were capped under controversial law

Document 171 of 500

Doug Ford refuses to apologize over wage-capping legislation, as MPPs return to Queen Park

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-refuses-apologize-over-wage-capping/docview/2929195614/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+refuses+to+apologize+over+wage-capping+legislation%2C+as+MPPs+return+to+Queen+Park&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was supposed to be a fresh start, but the past kept coming back to haunt Premier Doug Ford as MPPs returned to the legislature following the winter break.
A defiant Ford refused to apologize to thousands of nurses, teachers and other public servants who had their wages capped under his controversial Bill 124.
While he is backtracking on the law deemed unconstitutional by the courts last week, NDP Leader Marit Stiles said he has some explaining to do to workers for limiting their raises to one per cent annually.
"Will he apologize to Ontario's hard-working nurses, PSWs, teachers, educational assistants and all the public sector workers for suppressing their wages with Bill 124?" demanded Stiles.
"Look what we've done for the health-care workers, which we think the world of - we think the world of the nurses," shot back Ford, declining to express any contrition.
The forthcoming repeal of Bill 124 came against the backdrop of the Progressive Conservatives tabling separate omnibus legislation - dubbed the Get It Done Act after the 2022 campaign slogan.
It is designed to reboot a PC government that spent the past six months under a cloud due to the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal now under investigation by the RCMP.
The bill fast-tracks construction of highways and other infrastructure by streamlining environmental assessments, bans tolls on all roads except Hwy. 407, eliminates the need for licence plate renewals, undoes the government's unilateral changes to a dozen cities' official plans and attempts to force a future premier to hold a referendum on any carbon-pricing scheme.
Mocked by Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie as "another attempt to get it undone," the legislation corrects some previous mistakes the Tories made.
"This government is one that just reverses its decisions and its proposals. They need to do a lot more homework," said Crombie, who won the Liberal leadership on Dec. 2.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner, whose caucus doubled in size with new MPP Aislinn Clancy taking her seat in the house Tuesday, derided Ford's legislation as the "Getting It Done Wrong Act."
"It's performative politics at its worst, distracting from the Ford government's failure in addressing the housing crisis, and the fact that they've made access to health care worse, people are sick and tired of having to wait for long times and emergency departments not having access to primary health care, not having access to mental health care," said Schreiner.
The Liberals made sport of former Ford cabinet minister Parm Gill suddenly resigning four weeks ago to join federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's team.
With a Milton byelection looming later this year to fill Gill's seat, the provincial Tories are once again promising to bring all-day GO trains there.
"The Milton service is only happening because one of their MPPs wanted to escape from the premier," said Liberal MPP Andrea Hazell (Scarborough-Guildwood).
"It appears that the only way to get this government to invest in a riding is to have the PC MPP jump ship from their caucus," said Hazell.
Crombie, who does not have a seat in the legislature, said she has not ruled out running in Milton.
"I'm taking a very serious look at it. I'm very familiar with their issues and I think very strongly about the need for all day GO train service on the Milton line," said the former Mississauga mayor.
When asked about the referendum portion of the bill - which is the Tories' attempt to tie Crombie, a former Liberal MP, to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's carbon tax - she dismissed it as a "stunt ... to deflect from what's going on with the Conservative caucus with the reversals, the scandals, the insider deals."
Ontarians were exempt from paying the federal levy until Ford cancelled the previous provincial Liberal government's cap-and-trade alliance with Quebec and California in 2018, which costs the treasury $2 billion in lost revenue annually.
The Tories bristled when asked if there would be any retroactive plebiscites on the two carbon pricing schemes they introduced in 2022 and last year - the emission performance standards program that targets industrial polluters and the clean energy credit registry cap-and-trade system.
"We've worked exclusively on removing carbon taxes," insisted Government House Leader Paul Calandra before fleeing reporters to avoid answering more questions about the government's own greenhouse gas reduction efforts.
According to the Get It Done Act, any future premier would have to hold a referendum that "authorizes the carbon pricing program" before implementing it.
"A referendum question must be clear, concise and impartial in its wording and must be capable of being answered in the affirmative or the negative," the legislation says.
But the bill suggests no plebiscite would be binding on a new administration.
"A referendum shall not be interpreted to require the executive council of a subsequent government formed by another party to establish a carbon pricing program or give authority to establish a carbon pricing program as described in the referendum question."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford during question period in the Ontario Legislature on Feb. 20, 2024.

Ancient Indigenous remains found in Ontario + Inside Toronto and Ottawa's public funding fight

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ancient-indigenous-remains-found-ontario-inside/docview/2928619393/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ancient+Indigenous+remains+found+in+Ontario+%2B+Inside+Toronto+and+Ottawa%27s+public+funding+fight&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Tuesday, Feb. 20 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on ancient Indigenous remains in southern Ontario, Toronto's fight for federal refugee funding and your iPhone addiction.
DON'T MISS:
This man fell into a hole and raised questions about how Toronto builds on Indigenous lands
John Brown was walking home on Withrow Avenue when he stepped on the edge of his neighbour's garden and felt the earth crumble beneath him, Emily Fagan and Jermaine Wilson report. In a pit six feet below ground, he found a bone that turned out to be among centuries-old Indigenous remains. Similar discoveries have grown more frequent across southern Ontario with increasing housing developments and infrastructure projects. So, what will be done with the remains? "The Indigenous voice is really written out" of the process, says the archeological supervisor for Six Nations of the Grand River. Here's what Toronto is - and isn't - doing about its history of building over sacred Indigenous places.
Go deeper: Withrow Avenue runs on land that was once an Indigenous burial site and village. As the City of Toronto was built, First Nations were not consulted about development on culturally significant areas.By the numbers: Ontario's chief coroner, Dr. Dirk Huyer, said his provincial office receives 30 to 50 reports every year of discoveries of buried human remains. Each report is unique and the outcomes can vary, he said.
Inside Toronto city hall's fight for federal refugee funding
Earlier this month, Toronto city hall and the Trudeau government finally clinched a deal to give the city $162 million in federal support for asylum seekers, David Rider reports. But it took a public war to get there - including Toronto's budget chief announcing a "federal impacts levy" in January and a Liberal MP later taking to radio to accuse Mayor Olivia Chow of "lying" about federal support. So what finally turned the tide? Here's what city and federal insiders told the Star.
Context: At the Jan. 10 budget launch, Olivia Chow's budget chief Coun. Shelley Carroll announced the city would have to impose a six-per-cent property tax surcharge branded the "federal impacts levy" - unless Ottawa paid outstanding 2023 refugee costs and pledged 2024 support.Ottawa's reaction: The proposed levy angered the Liberals, whose government was already low in opinion polls and who had flowed billions more to Toronto than their Conservative predecessors.
Smartphones are the new cigarettes. But will we ever be able to put them down?
Before it was known that cigarettes definitively and unequivocally caused cancer, smoking seemed like a permanent fixture of Canadian life, Richard Warnica writes. Prior to 1966, roughly half of Canada's population 15 and older smoked regularly. Fast forward to 2022, and barely one in 10 Canadians 15 and older smoked regularly. Could smartphones be the next big industry to face a reckoning? Here's a look back at the rise and fall of the cigarette industry - and why we may end up viewing our phone addictions with similar disbelief.
By the numbers:In 2022, more than 95 per cent of Canadians between ages 15 and 54 used a smartphone to access the internet. Even seniors are doing it, with 37 per cent of Canadians 75 and older using smartphones that year.Go deeper: Most debate and litigation around harmful smartphone use so far has focused on social media apps. But if apps are nicotine, smartphones are cigarettes - perfectly formulated to keep consumers hooked on dopamine-driven feedback loops, Warnica writes.WHAT ELSE:
It's now looking like we won't see an interest-rate cut until June. Why does the Bank of Canada keep us guessing?Here's what happened to overdose deaths in Toronto neighbourhoods with safe consumption sites.Israel is ordering new evacuations in northern Gaza, where the UN says one in six children are malnourished.Alexei Navalny's widow is the latest woman to take up the fight of her silenced spouse, Allan Woods writes.Canada is sending more than 800 drones to Ukraine. Here's the latest in the war.One in four ALS patients opt for MAID because the care isn't there.In Thorncliffe Park, an elevator ride is a journey of its own - and rush hour means gridlock.The tapeworm economy has arrived in Ontario - and it's threatening to eat us alive. Here are five things never to say to someone who lost their job - and what will help them instead.As Scotiabank Arena celebrates 25 years, here are 25 highlights from the Raptors and Leafs home.The TTC signals there's little room on transit for student field trips - or new ideas.Watching Auston Matthews score is like watching hockey history unfold in real time.POV:
Israel's operation is a war on children, a Canadian surgeon who helped in Gaza hospitals writes.
CLOSE-UP:
MANITOBA: A portrait of Louis Riel and his councillors that hangs in the Manitoba legislature now recognizes the Métis leader as the province's first premier. Here's why the government says it's taking this historic step.

Document 173 of 500

Inside the city's battle for refugee funding: Public ght didn't stop backroom progress on deal

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/inside-citys-battle-refugee-funding/docview/2928511144/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Inside+the+city%27s+battle+for+refugee+funding%3A+Public+ght+didn%27t+stop+backroom+progress+on+deal&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The public war between Toronto city hall and the Trudeau government over refugee funding, which ended in peace and cash in time for passage of the city's 2024 budget, was not as it seemed, say city and federal insiders.
The Star spoke to city and federal officials to understand how they went from apparent warfare in mid-January - when a Liberal MP took to radio airwaves to accuse Mayor Olivia Chow of "lying" about federal support, sometimes rebutted in real time by Chow's budget chief - to Feb. 2, when they all stood smiling on a stage proclaiming mutual admiration and peace in our time.
Behind the war of words in the headlines lay long-simmering tensions, backroom negotiations - and a personal attempt to dial down the heat.
Federal-city friction preceded Chow taking office July 12. Her predecessor, John Tory, had pressed Ottawa in vain to continue pandemic relief funding into 2023.
Amid a surge in refugee costs, Coun. Jennifer McKelvie, acting as mayor between Tory and Chow, announced that asylum seekers looking for emergency beds in Toronto's non-refugee-specific shelters would be directed to the federal government.
That was quickly reversed but "there was quite a bit of tension," when she took office, Chow said in an interview. Good news when Ottawa approved $97 million in refugee funding, requested months earlier, soured when city officials notified Chow, and she relayed to Ottawa, that a surge in asylum seekers would require another $103 million. "I think in some ways the feds thought, 'Why isn't she being grateful?' "
Deputy Prime Minister and Toronto MP Chrystia Freeland met the mayor frequently, but the relationship took time to jell. On Dec. 19, Chow bemoaned her inability to secure ongoing federal refugee funding. "I don't have as good, close a working relationship with the federal government (as with Ontario Premier Doug Ford) but I can develop that," Chow said. Freeland declined comment for this story.
Liberal MP Julie Dzerowicz (Davenport) said in an interview: "It just took some time for the new (city-federal) relationships to develop." In January, at the height of city-federal tensions, Chow invited Freeland to a hockey game. Freeland was busy but invited Chow to a dinner party at her home that included mutual friends. Chow said it was a nice, relaxed night with a "fabulous" home-cooked meal.
Insiders say Freeland's choice last fall of Andrew Bevan as her new chief of staff helped keep negotiations for a 2024 refugee funding agreement on track despite political conflict. Chow and her chief of staff Michal Hay both know Bevan, a former chief of staff to Kathleen Wynne when she was premier and 2023 mayoral campaign manager for Mitzi Hunter. Bevan and Hay worked well together, sources say, ensuring political movement continued alongside negotiations between city and federal staff, even when it publicly appeared impossible that there would be a refugee funding deal in time for Feb. 14 city budget passage.
Coun. Shelley Carroll, Chow's budget chief, did not want to repeat a practice of building into the budget a placeholder amount sought from other governments. She says Ottawa didn't seem to understand the budget time constraints of Ontario's new "strong mayor" system. She made a decision that triggered that open warfare - announcing at the Jan. 10 budget launch that, unless Ottawa paid outstanding 2023 refugee costs and pledged '24 support, the city would impose a six per cent property tax surcharge branded the "federal impacts levy."
"I know it comes across as a threat," Carroll told the Star, "but it really is me doing my job. For the budget to be balanced, by law, I need to attach a funding source to everything we do. That $250-million worth of shelter actions didn't have a funding source."
Carroll's provocative federal impacts levy was inspired by Guelph Mayor Cam Guthrie. His city's 8.52 per cent property tax hike for this year includes 2.69 per cent branded a "provincial impacts levy" to alert residents to provincial downloading and underfunding. "I noticed that Toronto got an FIL and we a PIL," Guthrie told the Star. "I say it's a hard PIL to swallow."
Carroll said she fully briefed Chow two days before the budget launch, calling her consent for the levy "courageous." Chow recalled seeing the six per cent levy in the slide deck but said the "federal impacts" branding was not "red-flagged." Chow revealed to the Star it was her decision to not simply raid finite city reserve funds to replace the $250 million, avoid the levy threat and just hope the feds came through. City staff offered the option, the mayor said, but she didn't want to risk efforts to improve the city's credit rating by raiding reserves. "I made it very clear that it's either on the property tax or (the feds) pay. I don't want to delay facing what we need to face," said Chow, who chose in her comments not to highlight the federal impacts levy.
The FIL angered Liberal MPs who saw it as unnecessary given assurances a refugee funding deal was close at hand. Their government, already low in opinion polls, was under attack after flowing billions more dollars to Toronto than their Conservative predecessors.
Liberal MP Julie Dabrusin (Toronto-Danforth) says: "Where I was in the spectrum was, this needs to get figured out, right?" Caucus colleague Yvan Baker chose war, going on radio to accuse Chow of "lying" and trying to "shake down" his government. To cool tempers, Chow rewrote the script for her Jan. 16 telephone townhall on the budget, replacing demands for refugee money with praise for her federal government partners.
Background talks finally settled, in early February, on a deal with more than $162 million in federal supports for asylum seekers, including $40 million toward this year's refugee costs with a promise of talks on more to come. Toronto would also get $19.75 million through a program that helps low-income residents cover rent payments. Politicians on both sides signed off. But the Liberals waited until after Chow unveiled her proposed budget Feb. 1 with a $250-million hole, but no mention of the "federal impacts levy," to hold the celebratory news conference, said a source with knowledge of the deliberations.
Chow looked into the news cameras and said the Trudeau government has "fully delivered for Toronto," smiling broadly as Freeland said the money builds on her government's record as "the City of Toronto's greatest partner." Baker clapped and smiled. He regrets nothing about his attacks, he told the Star, saying he was correct and believes it had an impact. Most insiders, however, said neither Carroll's threatened levy nor Baker's accusations had a significant impact on the drive to a deal.
With files from Ben Spurr
CAPTION: Deputy Prime Minister and University- Rosedale MP Chrystia Freeland met with Mayor Olivia Chow frequently, but the relationship reportedly took time to jell.Andrew Francis WallaceToronto Star File Photo

Title: Inside the city's battle for refugee funding:   Public ght didn't stop backroom progress on deal

Inside Toronto city hall's fight for federal refugee funding. 'There was quite a bit of tension'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/inside-toronto-city-halls-fight-federal-refugee/docview/2928510962/se-2?accountid=15090
