Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Inside+Toronto+city+hall%27s+fight+for+federal+refugee+funding.+%27There+was+quite+a+bit+of+tension%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The public war between Toronto city hall and the Trudeau government over refugee funding, which ended in peace and cash in time for passage of the city's 2024 budget, was not as it seemed, say city and federal insiders.
The Star spoke to city and federal officials to understand how they went from apparent warfare in mid-January - when a Liberal MP took to radio airwaves to accuse Mayor Olivia Chow of "lying" about federal support, sometimes rebutted in real time by Chow's budget chief - to Feb. 2, when they all stood smiling on a stage proclaiming mutual admiration and peace in our time.
Behind the war of words in the headlines lay long-simmering tensions, backroom negotiations - and a personal attempt to dial down the heat.
Present tense
Federal-city friction preceded Chow taking office July 12. Her predecessor, John Tory, had pressed Ottawa in vain to continue pandemic relief funding into 2023. Amid a surge in refugee costs, Coun. Jennifer McKelvie, acting as mayor between Tory and Chow, announced that asylum seekers looking for emergency beds in Toronto's non-refugee-specific shelters would be directed to the federal government.
That was quickly reversed but "there was quite a bit of tension," when she took office, Chow said in an interview. Good news when Ottawa approved $97 million in refugee funding, requested months earlier, soured when city officials notified Chow, and she relayed to Ottawa, that a surge in asylum seekers would require another $103 million. "I think in some ways the feds thought, 'Why isn't she being grateful?'"
Breaking bread
Deputy Prime Minister and Toronto MP Chrystia Freeland met the mayor frequently, but the relationship took time to gel. On Dec. 19 Chow bemoaned her inability to secure ongoing federal refugee funding. "I don't have as good, close a working relationship with the federal government (as with Ontario Premier Doug Ford) but I can develop that," Chow said. Freeland declined comment for this story.
Liberal MP Julie Dzerowicz (Davenport) said in an interview: "It just took some time for the new (city-federal) relationships to develop." In January, at the height of city-federal tensions, Chow invited Freeland to a hockey game. Freeland was busy but invited Chow to a dinner party at her home that included mutual friends. Chow said it was a nice, relaxed night with a "fabulous" home-cooked meal.
Hail to the chiefs
Insiders say Freeland's choice last fall of Andrew Bevan as her new chief of staff helped keep negotiations for a 2024 refugee funding agreement on track despite political conflict. Chow and her chief of staff Michal Hay both know Bevan, a former chief of staff to Kathleen Wynne when she was premier and 2023 mayoral campaign manager for Mitzi Hunter. Bevan and Hay worked well together, sources say, ensuring political movement continued alongside negotiations between city and federal staff, even when it publicly appeared impossible that there would be a refugee funding deal in time for Feb. 14 city budget passage.
Warning shot
Coun. Shelley Carroll, Chow's budget chief, did not want to repeat a practice of building into the budget a placeholder amount sought from other governments. She says Ottawa didn't seem to understand the budget time constraints of Ontario's new "strong mayor" system. She made a decision that triggered that open warfare - announcing at the Jan. 10 budget launch that, unless Ottawa paid outstanding 2023 refugee costs and pledged 2024 support, the city would impose a six-per-cent property tax surcharge branded the "federal impacts levy." "I know it comes across as a threat," Carroll told the Star, "but it really is me doing my job. For the budget to be balanced, by law, I need to attach a funding source to everything we do. That $250 million worth of shelter actions didn't have a funding source."
Going Guelph
Carroll's provocative federal impacts levy was inspired by Guelph Mayor Cam Guthrie. His city's 8.52-per-cent property tax hike for this year includes 2.69 per cent branded a "provincial impacts levy" to alert residents to provincial downloading and underfunding. "I noticed that Toronto got an FIL and we a PIL," Guthrie told the Star. "I say it's a hard PIL to swallow."
War and peace
Carroll said she fully briefed Chow two days before the budget launch, calling her consent for the levy "courageous." Chow recalled seeing the six-per-cent levy in the slide deck but said the "federal impacts" branding was not "red-flagged." Carroll says the mayor might have been surprised by how "plain-spoken" she was at the announcement. Chow revealed to the Star that it was her decision to not simply raid finite city reserve funds to replace the $250 million, avoid the levy threat, and just hope the feds came through. City staff offered the option, the mayor said, but she didn't want to risk efforts to improve the city's credit rating by raiding reserves. "I made it very clear that it's either on the property tax or (the feds) pay. I don't want to delay facing what we need to face," said Chow, who chose in her comments to not to highlight the federal impacts levy.
The FIL angered Liberal MPs who saw it as unnecessary given assurances that a refugee funding deal was close at hand. Their government, already low in opinion polls, was under attack after flowing billions more dollars to Toronto than their Conservative predecessors. Reactions ranged from wanting to just get the deal done to open warfare.
Liberal MP Julie Dabrusin (Toronto-Danforth) says: "Where I was in the spectrum was, this needs to get figured out, right?" Caucus colleague Yvan Baker chose war, going on radio to accuse Chow of "lying" and trying to "shake down" his government. To cool tempers, Chow rewrote the script for her Jan. 16 telephone townhall on the budget, replacing demands for refugee money with praise for her federal government partners.
The deal is done
Background talks finally settled, in early February, on a deal with more than $162 million in federal supports for asylum seekers, including $40 million toward this year's refugee costs with a promise of talks on more to come. Toronto would also get $19.75 million through a program that helps low-income residents cover rent payments. Politicians on both sides signed off. But the Liberals waited until after Chow unveiled her proposed budget Feb. 1 with a $250-million hole, but no mention of the "federal impacts levy", to hold the celebratory news conference, said a source with knowledge of the deliberations. That "repaired any damage that might have been caused by actions on any side."
Chow looked into the news cameras and said the Trudeau government has "fully delivered for Toronto," smiling broadly as Freeland said the money builds on her government's record as "the City of Toronto's greatest partner." Baker, standing behind Chow, clapped and smiled. He regrets nothing about his attacks, he told the Star, saying he was correct and believes it had an impact. Most insiders, however, said neither Carroll's threatened levy nor Baker's accusations had a significant impact, in the end, on the drive to a deal.
With files from Ben Spurr
CAPTION: It was all smiles when Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow and Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland announced funding for refugee supports in the city.

Document 175 of 500

Doug Ford hopes to finally 'Get It Done' when legislature resumes Tuesday

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-hopes-finally-get-done-when-legislature/docview/2928510806/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+hopes+to+finally+%27Get+It+Done%27+when+legislature+resumes+Tuesday&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Some 628 days after being re-elected on a campaign slogan to "Get It Done," Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives are finally enshrining that promise in legislation.
As MPPs return to Queen's Park on Tuesday for the spring session, Ford will table the Get It Done Act, an omnibus bill the Tories hope will expedite infrastructure construction, address cost-of-living concerns - and deflect attention from their Greenbelt land swap scandal.
In hearkening back to his June 2, 2022 victory, the premier also signalled he is looking ahead to the 2026 election and the threat posed by new Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie.
As part of the Get It Done bill, any future government would be required to hold a referendum before implementing a new provincial carbon pricing program.
"People know where I stand on the carbon tax. I've opposed it from the very beginning. My record couldn't be more different from Bonnie Crombie," Ford said last week in Mississauga.
"She's the queen of carbon tax," he said, in apparent reference to Crombie once being a Liberal MP, although years before Prime Minister Justin Trudeau was elected in 2015 and imposed carbon pricing.
Ford did not mention that Ontarians were exempt from paying the federal levy until he cancelled the previous provincial Liberal government's cap-and-trade alliance with Quebec and California in 2018, costing the provincial treasury $2 billion annually.
Nor did he acknowledge that his Tory government slapped an industrial carbon tax on big polluters - known as the emission performance standards program - in 2022 and last year launched the clean energy credit registry that is effectively a cap-and-trade system.
Noting no opposition party is proposing a provincial carbon tax, Crombie said Monday that "Desperate Doug is pulling out all the stops to distract from his failures, flip-flops, scandals and the RCMP's criminal investigation."
"That's why he is spending all of his time talking about me. He seems to have conceded the next election and wants to bind what the next Liberal government may or may not even do," said the Grit leader, who is not an MPP and has no immediate plans to seek a seat in the legislature.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles told the Star's editorial board that Ford is flailing as he scrambles to shift the focus away from the $8.28-billion Greenbelt debacle currently under RCMP investigation, among other controversies.
"They keep making these mistakes. So much money and so much time is wasted. It's heartbreaking actually," said Stiles, pointing to the Tories' U-turn last week on the Bill 124 wage cap for public servants after losing a court challenge.
"When I think of the public service, think of how many people are wasting their time putting in policies that (Ford) is just going to flip-flop on again," the New Democratic chief said, shaking her head.
"There's a cost to all of this - for all of us."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner dismissed Ford's proposed referendum bill as "a performative, political moment to distract from the failures in housing and health care."
"We're talking about a federal carbon tax that isn't even provincial jurisdiction, for one thing, and, two, he knows as well as anyone he can do whatever he wants, it's not going to prevent a future government from overturning the legislation," said Schreiner.
"So it's purely performative and not even in his jurisdiction," he said.
Schreiner will have company on his benches Tuesday for the first time in the history of the Green Party of Ontario.
That's because he will be joined by a second Green MPP, Aislinn Clancy, who easily won the Nov. 30 Kitchener Centre byelection in a riding that had been held by the New Democrats.
Aside from the carbon tax referendum scheme, the Get It Done Act will also freeze any possible increase for drivers' licence fees, eliminate the need for licence plate renewals, outlaw the possibility of new tolls on all freeways except Hwy. 407 and streamline environmental assessments so highways, sewers and electricity infrastructure can be built faster.
The licence plate change comes after the Star revealed last week there are more than one million expired plates on Ontario roads.
In the wake of the renewal fee of up to $120 per vehicle being scrapped in 2022, many motorists incorrectly believed they didn't keep plates up to date, leading to a surge in fines of as much as $1,000 for driving with invalid plates.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford delivers remarks at the opening ceremonies of the Canadian International Auto Show in Toronto on Feb. 16, 2024.

Subject: Jurisdiction; Referendums; Emissions trading; Scandals; Carbon; Roads & highways; Environmental tax

Ford government set to return with Get It Done Act: Leaders dismiss premier's legislation as a 'performative' distraction

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-government-set-return-with-get-done-act/docview/2928510800/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 177 of 500

Clear sign city's reserved image is history

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/clear-sign-citys-reserved-image-is-history/docview/2928144710/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Clear+sign+city%27s+reserved+image+is+history&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With the new Cadillac Fairview skyscraper at Front and Simcoe streets, Toronto has lost one of its last remaining downtown surface parking lots. A moment of silence for those who mourn such things. Perhaps a plaque should even be installed marking this great change.
Before cars became king, downtown Toronto had hardly an empty lot. Like many cities, the urban fabric was later hollowed out for bigger roads and endless parking, one of the great ironies of the 20th century: cars devoured the cities they were supposed to help people get to. Unlike other cities, Toronto's relentless real estate boom that began at the end of the recession in the 1990s has filled many of them in. We're lucky in that respect.
A requiem for a lost parking lot may not be why most people took note of 160 Front St. over the last few weeks, though. Rather, it is lit up like a bright electric dagger stuck into the heart of downtown Toronto at night.
The edges of the building are outlined with lights from sidewalk to its curved roof, as are some of the floors. The TD bank logo is currently shining bright on east and west facing screens at the top, while screens on the north and south side show different messages. Last week noted Black History Month, and this week a dragon marked Chinese New Year, though they are so high I had to use my phone camera to zoom in to see it.
The lights have sparked divergent opinions: some like it, some very much hate it. I took an informal, totally unscientific poll on social media and it was nearly 50-50 for and against. It's a classic, subjective question of personal preference.
I am a person of common tastes. I like bright lights, I love Las Vegas even though I don't gamble, and think some over the top artifice and decoration is sometimes fantastic (while still loving minimalism elsewhere too). I keep asking myself if this building is too much, too bright, too impossible to ignore? It's one thing when something sinks into the background, but this building insists upon itself, boldness that seems antithetical to the usual Toronto reserve.
Other cities are more comfortable with this kind of thing. One of the world's tallest buildings, Taipei 101, lights up like a torch in various colours. In Hong Kong, the facade of the 108-storey International Commerce Centre has two of its facades animated by lights, sometimes showing the time of day and other images as if it was a giant screen. When I was in Hong Kong I found I couldn't take my eyes off it. In fact, an entire portion of the Hong Kong skyline participates in a "Symphony of Light" show every night. It isn't subtle.
Yet as out of place in Toronto as 160 Front seems, there are precedents here. A number of residential towers have coloured lights on top as design elements. Aura, the tallest residential building in Canada at Yonge and Gerrard, has white, icicle-like lights "dripping" from the roofline, though sometimes they don't all work. Next door to 160 Front itself another Cadillac Fairview tower, the RBC Centre, has blue light accents from top to bottom. There's also the venerable Canada Life weather beacon on University Avenue at Queen Street and, on a much smaller scale, an older building on the northeast corner of College Street and Dovercourt Road recently received a restoration that added an illuminated green clock face and low-key white lights along the roof line. Toronto really isn't that different from other, brighter places, though our wattage is much lower.
The City of Toronto has said it is also taking note of the lights on 160 Front, including and importantly how they might affect migratory birds. However, "architectural lighting" like this does not fall under Toronto's sign bylaw which regulates where signs can go, especially "third party" signage where the business being advertised is not located on the same property. TD is a tenant at 160, as is Cadillac Fairview's corporate parent, the Ontario Teachers' Pension Plan.
Third-party sign regulations are sometimes loosened, like when city council permitted video billboards along the Gardiner Expressway, or how Metrolinx and Pearson Airport have installed huge ones where their properties intersect with 400-series highways, the latter two agencies tending to do as they please. It's all more automobile irony: why allow driver distractions like this at exactly the moment they should be paying the most attention to driving?
The tower at 160 Front likely won't distract drivers but it's impossible to miss. When the CN Tower turned on its new colourful and animated lights in 2007, it caused a similar buzz, but that eventually subsided and the lights have become widely accepted. The difference is the CN Tower is not a purely commercial building as 160 Front is, perhaps making it easier to accept.
As a person of common tastes, I don't hate these new lights, but I don't know if I like them either. Your opinion will surely vary too.
CAPTION: TD Terrace on Front Street, left, joins the RBC Centre and the CN Tower in adding brightness to the city's skyline. This building insists upon itself, boldness that seems antithetical to the usual Toronto reserve, writes Shawn Micallef.Shawn Micallef
CREDIT: Shawn Micallef OPINION

Document 178 of 500

Here's what happened to overdose deaths in Toronto neighbourhoods with safe consumption sites

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/heres-what-happened-overdose-deaths-toronto/docview/2928015650/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here%27s+what+happened+to+overdose+deaths+in+Toronto+neighbourhoods+with+safe+consumption+sites&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As opioid overdoses continue to ravage Ontario, Toronto neighbourhoods with supervised consumption sites have seen dramatic decreases in drug fatalities, a new study shows.
The study, published this month in The Lancet, found a 67 per cent reduction in overdose deaths in neighbourhoods within 500 metres of supervised consumption sites after they opened. That reduction in mortality rippled as far as five kilometres from the sites.
It is one of the first papers to study overdose deaths in communities surrounding consumption sites, controversial spaces where people can use drugs under supervision. The results come amid a provincial pause on consumption site approvals, and provide new evidence supporting controlled drug use as an effective tool in the battle against a public health crisis that has affected tens of thousands of Canadians.
"These sites are doing what they're supposed to be doing, which is preventing deaths," said Indhu Rammohan, a research assistant at Li Ka Shing Knowledge Institute's Centre on Drug Policy Evaluation and the study's lead author. "And potentially also doing that in a way that we haven't really thought of before, which is at the community level or population level."
Annual opioid overdose deaths in Toronto have skyrocketed in recent years, increasing more than 250 per cent between 2015 and 2022. Across Canada, more than 40,000 people have died from overdoses since 2016 - one of the leading causes of death in the country.
The study's authors used records from the Office of the Chief Coroner of Ontario to measure fatal overdose rates between May 1, 2017, and December 2019, before and after the implementation of nine consumption sites in Ontario. The researchers analyzed 787 fatal overdoses and found the mortality rate decreased to 2.7 deaths per 100,000 from 8.1 in neighbourhoods that implemented the sites.
There are 10 supervised consumption sites across Toronto where people can bring their own drugs to use under the supervision of health professionals. The sites teach safer consumption practices and provide access to clean needles and other drug-use materials - as well as Naloxone, the life-saving drug that can reverse the effects of an opioid overdose.
The sites have been controversial, with some residents and businesses questioning their impact on neighbourhoods. The first sites opened in Toronto in 2017, but concern over their services ramped up last July after Karolina Huebner-Makurat was killed by a stray bullet fired outside South Riverdale Community Health Centre (SRCHC).
This week, the Star's Betsy Powell reported that a proposed class-action lawsuit was filed in Ontario Superior Court alleging the SRCHC was failing to follow city and provincial rules in its operation of the site and is responsible for Leslieville's "rapid" deterioration since the site's inception in 2017. The suit, which is seeking unspecified damages, alleges the centre has failed to enforce a zero-tolerance drug-trafficking policy.
The new research suggests the consumption sites increase the protection to drug users over time, said Daniel Werb, director of the Centre on Drug Policy Evaluation and a co-author of the Lancet study.
"The effect was so immense that you saw two-thirds reduction in mortality and that it was so spread out geographically, you know, which was pretty amazing," he said.
"What we know about them, whether you like it or not, is that they produce a sense of community among clients and the broader network of people who visit them and are served by them ... From a public health perspective, that's a good thing."
Sarah Larney, an associate professor at the Université de Montréal and an expert in harm reduction, said the study is particularly timely given that it was conducted when fentanyl and other highly potent synthetic opioids had overtaken heroin as the drug of choice for many users.
"Because fentanyl is so much stronger than other opioids, then that tolerance and withdrawal cycle is also very strong," said Larney, who was not associated with the Lancet study. "It's very difficult to manage that without actually continuing to take opioids."
The Investigative Journalism Bureau and the Toronto Star have previously reported on the increasingly toxic illicit drug supply and the emergence of ultra-potent synthetic opioids in Toronto and beyond.
Kimberly Mitchell, who has used supervised consumption sites and now advocates for drug users, credits the sites as a factor in her recovery. She says that having a safe place to use drugs helped lift the burden of physical addiction and allowed users to focus on their mental health.
"They're non-judgmental, right?" Mitchell said. "So you go there, and just talk to them. I've seen so many lives saved. I was the worst of the worst, and now I can function."
In October, the province announced it was pausing approval of any new supervised consumption sites and treatment facilities, citing "public safety" concerns - including the death of Huebner-Makurat - while it conducts a "critical incident review."
A spokesperson for the Ministry of Health said the province has invested $525 million in 2019 on addictions and treatment services - including short-term accommodations for those brought to hospital in crisis and increased access to medications for people looking to kick their opioid addictions.
The spokesperson added that the 2023-24 budget includes a five per cent increase "in base funding for all community based mental health and addiction organizations."
France Gélinas, MPP for Nickel Belt and NDP health critic, called the results of the Lancet study "powerful," and said she hopes it will inform policy and the review that is currently underway.
"This is the strongest body of evidence that we get to work with ... So does that give me hope? Yes, absolutely."
Gélinas approaches the overdose crisis with the fatigue and perspective that comes from deep personal loss.
The son of a close family friend is currently in the hospital following an overdose. And two and a half months ago, she buried her nephew, who died from an overdose, she said.
"When will this end? ... This is too hard," she said in a recent interview. "Why is it that we see young people needing our help and we turn our backs on them?"
Sam Mortimer, a public health nurse at Public Health Sudbury and Districts, said the new study has implications for communities in northern Ontario, where funding for these sites is strained.
Sudbury's supervised consumption site, The Spot, has stopped receiving funds from the city and currently operates on donations while it awaits a funding decision from the province. Mortimer said that if sites such as this lose funding, it would be "devastating" for Sudbury and other northern Ontario communities.
"Then we're looking at preventable deaths, substance-use related harms and strained emergency and health care, like social services."
According to Public Health Sudbury and Districts, 29 non-fatal overdose events occurred at The Spot, and 645 referrals were made to off-site health-care services since it opened in late September 2022.
In the fall of 2020, Rammohan, then studying for her master's in science in health policy, made a weekly trip to the chief coroner's office in Toronto from her parents' home near Grimsby, where she would sift through hundreds of files containing the intimate details of Ontarians who died of an overdose. The reports included the circumstances surrounding a person's death, including hospital admission records, police reports and interviews with family, friends or whoever had found them.
Rammohan became a witness to the tragic - and often untimely - deaths of Ontarians swept up in an ever-expanding overdose crisis.
Those hours at the coroner's office became the foundation of her research into supervised consumption sites. But it came with a mental toll, she said.
"It was heavy. It was ... the nature of people's lives ending and descriptions of how they were found," she recalls. "In many cases people were alone in their last minutes and they were found later.
"That was difficult. ... You can only read so many of those stories."
CAPTION: Indhu Rammohan, a research assistant at the Centre on Drug Policy Evaluation at the Li Ka Shing Knowledge Institute and the lead author of the study.Dan Werb is photographed on Victoria Street. Werb is one of the authors of a recent scientific paper in The Lancet looking at neighbourhoods where safe injection sites are located.Dan Werb is photographed on Victoria Street. Werb is one of the authors of a recent scientific paper in The Lancet looking at neighbourhoods where safe injection sites are located.Dan Werb
CREDIT: Scott Martin and Declan Keogh, Investigative Journalism Bureau and

Subject: Public health; Drug overdose; Fatalities; Mental health; Addictions; Neighborhoods; Drug policy; Mortality; Funding; Fentanyl; Narcotics

People: Werb, Dan

Company / organization: Name: Knowledge Institute; NAICS: 541611, 611430

Title: Here's what happened to overdose deaths in Toronto neighbourhoods with safe consumption sites

Document 179 of 500

New funding will help tackle health care system woes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/new-funding-will-help-tackle-health-care-system/docview/2927856733/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+funding+will+help+tackle+health+care+system+woes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: To his credit, Ontario Premier Doug Ford looks to have accepted a couple of home truths.
One, Ontarians prefer results to rancour between levels of government. Two, they want the health-care crisis addressed in a big way and in a hurry.
The recently announced agreement with the federal government that will see a $3.1-billion increase in health-care funding over three years is good news.
The money should enable the Ford government to create new primary-care teams to increase access to family doctors, see the addition of hundreds of family physicians and nurse practitioners and thousands of new nurses and personal support workers.
The funding is part of an $8.4-billion injection over 10 years agreed to between Ottawa and the Ford government.
Under that deal, Ontario agrees to address federal priorities by improving access to family health services, including in rural and remote areas; boosting the health workforce; providing better access to timely mental health, substance abuse and addictions services; and allowing individuals access to their electronic health information shared between health professionals.
The balance of the promised money will be contingent on meeting timelines and targets.
The federal government had allotted $25 billion over 10 years for bilateral deals with provinces - Ontario is the fifth to sign on - to address the four federal priorities.
Reports from across the health-care spectrum have revealed crises in pediatric care, long-term care, emergency care, mental-health care and access to family doctors.
The Star's Kenyon Wallace put a human face on the data with a report on Sunday from the emergency room at Toronto Western Hospital that compellingly illustrated the anxiety and suffering of patients and the extraordinary workload and risk of burnout placed on health-care professionals.
A system at the breaking point is the result of decades of failure by successive provincial and federal governments of various parties to make the necessary investments in infrastructure and personnel to meet the needs of an aging and growing population.
As of 2023, Wallace reported, Canada had one of the lowest hospital-bed-to-patient ratios in the OECD (Organization for Economic Co-ordination and Development).
In health care, the system is interconnected and problems inevitably pile up downstream.
Too few family doctors means more people showing up in ERs. Too few beds results in extended wait times to see a doctor and delays in getting patients to surgical or medical wards. Rather than being back on the road, paramedics who bring patients in must stay with them until they can be seen.
As Ontario's acting auditor general recently reported, the rising delays can mean a matter of life or death.
"When emergency departments are unable to operate efficiently and effectively, patients may not receive timely and high-quality care, which can lead to negative or even fatal health outcomes," wrote Nick Stavropoulos.
In the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic, targeting mental-health care is particularly laudable.
Dr. Eileen de Villa, Toronto's medical officer of health, has reported that student mental health declined sharply in the aftermath of pandemic, with visits to Toronto ERs related to self-harm among children and youth rising 30 per cent in the first year afterwards.
Prior to the pandemic, a strained health-care system was already struggling to meet demand, with huge backlogs and waiting lists. The social isolation and stresses of COVID-19 only made it worse.
Studies in the Canadian Medical Association Journal found the number of Canadian adolescents reporting self-harm or suicidal thoughts rose significantly. "If a kid seeks help, we can't wait 10 months to get a kid care," Dr. Natasha Saunders, a pediatrician at the Hospital for Sick Children, said last year.
Ford's office recently said the premier is focused on delivering on commitments in policy areas that matter to Ontarians. Not much matters more than health care.
To be sure, a complex, long-underfunded system will not be fixed in one fell swoop. Some of the remedies funded by the new cash -- like new training spots for doctors -- will only be felt years from now. So this funding -- and the balance which is owed -- is only part of the solutions needed to relieve the pressures on a strained system.
But the federal-provincial agreement is an important part of the long-term effort to rebuild capacity and prepare for the future.
CAPTION: Ontario and the federal government have reached agreement that would see $3.1 billion invested in Ontario's health care system. Ontario says the new funding will mean new family physicians and nurse practitioners, as well as nurses and personal support workers.

Subject: Hospitals; Emergency medical care; Nurse practitioners; Mental health; Health care policy; Funding; Pandemics; Physicians; COVID-19; Self destructive behavior

Document 180 of 500

Love it or hate it, this boldly lit new tower is a clear sign Toronto's reserved image is history

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/love-hate-this-boldly-lit-new-tower-is-clear-sign/docview/2927856714/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Love+it+or+hate+it%2C+this+boldly+lit+new+tower+is+a+clear+sign+Toronto%27s+reserved+image+is+history&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With the new Cadillac Fairview skyscraper at Front and Simcoe streets, Toronto has lost one of its last remaining downtown surface parking lots. A moment of silence for those who mourn such things. Perhaps a plaque should even be installed marking this great change.
Before cars became king, downtown Toronto had hardly an empty lot. Like many cities, the urban fabric was later hollowed out for bigger roads and endless parking, one of the great ironies of the 20th century: cars devoured the cities they were supposed to help people get to. Unlike other cities, Toronto's relentless real estate boom that began at the end of the recession in the 1990s has filled many of them in. We're lucky in that respect.
A requiem for a lost parking lot may not be why most people took note of 160 Front St. over the last few weeks, though. Rather, it is lit up like a bright electric dagger stuck into the heart of downtown Toronto at night.
The edges of the building are outlined with lights from sidewalk to its curved roof, as are some of the floors. The TD bank logo is currently shining bright on east and west facing screens at the top, while screens on the north and south side show different messages. Last week noted Black History Month, and this week a dragon marked Chinese New Year, though they are so high I had to use my phone camera to zoom in to see it.
The lights have sparked divergent opinions: some like it, some very much hate it. I took an informal, totally unscientific poll on social media and it was nearly 50-50 for and against. It's a classic, subjective question of personal preference.
I am a person of common tastes. I like bright lights, I love Las Vegaseven though I don't gamble, and think some over the top artifice and decoration is sometimes fantastic (while still loving minimalism elsewhere too). I keep asking myself if this building is too much, too bright, too impossible to ignore? It's one thing when something sinks into the background, but this building insists upon itself, boldness that seems antithetical to the usual Toronto reserve.
Other cities are more comfortable with this kind of thing. One of the world's tallest buildings, Taipei 101, lights up like a torch in various colours. In Hong Kong, the facade of the 108-storey International Commerce Centre has two of its facades animated by lights, sometimes showing the time of day and other images as if it was a giant screen. When I was in Hong Kong I found I couldn't take my eyes off it. In fact, an entire portion of the Hong Kong skyline participates in a "Symphony of Light" show every night.It isn't subtle.
Yet as out of place in Toronto as 160 Front seems, there are precedents here. A number of residential towers have coloured lights on top as design elements. Aura, the tallest residential building in Canada at Yonge and Gerrard, has white, icicle-like lights "dripping" from the roofline, though sometimes they don't all work. Next door to 160 Front itself another Cadillac Fairview tower, the RBC Centre, has blue light accents from top to bottom.
There's also the venerable Canada Life weather beacon on University Avenue at Queen Street and, on a much smaller scale, an older building on the northeast corner of College Street and Dovercourt Road recently received a restoration that added an illuminated green clock face and low-key white lights along the roof line. Toronto really isn't that different from other, brighter places, though our wattage is much lower.
The City of Toronto has said it is also taking note of the lights on 160 Front, including and importantly how they might affect migratory birds. However, "architectural lighting" like this does not fall under Toronto's sign bylaw which regulates where signs can go, especially "third party" signage where the business being advertised is not located on the same property. TD is a tenant at 160, as is Cadillac Fairview's corporate parent, the Ontario Teachers' Pension Plan.
Third-party sign regulations are sometimes loosened, like when city council permitted video billboards along the Gardiner Expressway, or how Metrolinx and Pearson Airport have installed huge ones where their properties intersect with 400-series highways, the latter two agencies tending to do as they please. It's all more automobile irony: why allow driver distractions like this at exactly the moment they should be paying the most attention to driving?
The tower at 160 Front likely won't distract drivers but it's impossible to miss. When the CN Tower turned on its new colourful and animated lights in 2007, it caused a similar buzz, but that eventually subsided and the lights have become widely accepted. The difference is the CN Tower is not a purely commercial building as 160 Front is, perhaps making it easier to accept.
As a person of common tastes, I don't hate these new lights, but I don't know if I like them either. Your opinion will surely vary too.
CAPTION: TD Terrace at 160 Front St., left, joins the RBC Centre and the CN Tower in adding brightness to the city's skyline.

Subject: Cities; Roads & highways

Location: China; Canada; Hong Kong China

Company / organization: Name: Cadillac Fairview Corp Ltd; NAICS: 237210

Document 181 of 500

Safe consumption sites saving lives: New study shows dramatic drop in overdoses within 500 metres of supervised spaces

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/safe-consumption-sites-saving-lives/docview/2927815659/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Safe+consumption+sites+saving+lives%3A+New+study+shows+dramatic+drop+in+overdoses+within+500+metres+of+supervised+spaces&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As opioid overdoses continue to ravage Ontario, Toronto neighbourhoods with supervised consumption sites have seen dramatic decreases in drug fatalities, a new study shows.
The study, published this month in the Lancet, found a 67 per cent reduction in overdose deaths in neighbourhoods within 500 metres of supervised consumption sites after they opened. That reduction in mortality rippled as far as five kilometres from the sites.
It is one of the first papers to study overdose deaths in communities surrounding consumption sites, controversial spaces where people can use drugs under supervision. The results come amid a provincial pause on consumption site approvals, and provide new evidence supporting controlled drug use as an effective tool in the battle against a public health crisis that has affected tens of thousands of Canadians.
"These sites are doing what they're supposed to be doing, which is preventing deaths," said Indhu Rammohan, a research assistant at Li Ka Shing Knowledge Institute's Centre on Drug Policy Evaluation and the study's lead author. "And potentially also doing that in a way that we haven't really thought of before, which is at the community level or population level."
Annual opioid overdose deaths in Toronto have skyrocketed in recent years, increasing more than 250 per cent between 2015 and 2022. Across Canada, more than 40,000 people have died from overdoses since 2016 - one of the leading causes of death in the country.
The study's authors used records from the Office of the Chief Coroner of Ontario to measure fatal overdose rates between May 1, 2017, and December 2019, before and after the implementation of nine consumption sites in Ontario. The researchers analyzed 787 fatal overdoses and found the mortality rate decreased to 2.7 deaths per 100,000 from 8.1 in neighbourhoods that implemented the sites.
There are 10 supervised consumption sites across Toronto where people can bring their own drugs to use under the supervision of health professionals. The sites teach safer consumption practices and provide access to clean needles and other drug-use materials - as well as Naloxone, the life-saving drug that can reverse the effects of an opioid overdose.
The sites have been controversial, with some residents and businesses questioning their impact on neighbourhoods. The first sites opened in Toronto in 2017, but concern over their services ramped up last July after Karolina Huebner-Makurat was killed by a stray bullet fired outside South Riverdale Community Health Centre (SRCHC).
This week, the Star's Betsy Powell reported that a proposed class-action lawsuit was filed in Ontario Superior Court alleging the SRCHC was failing to follow city and provincial rules in its operation of the site and is responsible for Leslieville's "rapid" deterioration since the site's inception in 2017. The suit, which is seeking unspecified damages, alleges the centre has failed to enforce a zero-tolerance drug-trafficking policy.
The new research suggests the consumption sites increase the protection to drug users over time, said Daniel Werb, director of the Centre on Drug Policy Evaluation and a co-author of the Lancet study.
"The effect was so immense that you saw two-thirds reduction in mortality and that it was so spread out geographically, you know, which was pretty amazing," he said.
"What we know about them, whether you like it or not, is that they produce a sense of community among clients and the broader network of people who visit them and are served by them ... From a public health perspective, that's a good thing."
Sarah Larney, an associate professor at the Université de Montréal and an expert in harm reduction, said the study is particularly timely given that it was conducted when fentanyl and other highly potent synthetic opioids had overtaken heroin as the drug of choice for many users.
"Because fentanyl is so much stronger than other opioids, then that tolerance and withdrawal cycle is also very strong," said Larney, who was not associated with the Lancet study. "It's very difficult to manage that without actually continuing to take opioids."
The Investigative Journalism Bureau and the Toronto Star have previously reported on the increasingly toxic illicit drug supply and the emergence of ultra-potent synthetic opioids in Toronto and beyond.
Kimberly Mitchell, who has used supervised consumption sites and now advocates for drug users, credits the sites as a factor in her recovery. She says having a safe place to use drugs helped lift the burden of physical addiction and allowed users to focus on their mental health.
"They're non-judgmental, right?" Mitchell said. "So you go there, and just talk to them. I've seen so many lives saved. I was the worst of the worst, and now I can function."
In October, the province announced it was pausing approval of any new supervised consumption sites and treatment facilities, citing "public safety" concerns - including the death of Huebner-Makurat - while it conducts a "critical incident review."
A spokesperson for the Ministry of Health said the province had invested $525 million in 2019 on addictions and treatment services - including short-term accommodations for those brought to hospital in crisis and increased access to medications for people looking to kick their opioid addictions.
The spokesperson added that the 2023-24 budget includes a five per cent increase "in base funding for all community based mental health and addiction organizations."
France Gélinas, MPP for Nickel Belt and NDP health critic, called the results of the Lancet study "powerful," and said she hopes it will inform policy and the review that is currently underway.
"This is the strongest body of evidence that we get to work with ... So does that give me hope? Yes, absolutely."
Gélinas approaches the overdose crisis with the fatigue and perspective that comes from deep personal loss.
The son of a close family friend is currently in the hospital following an overdose. And two and a half months ago, she buried her nephew, who died from an overdose, she said.
"When will this end? ... This is too hard," she said in a recent interview. "Why is it that we see young people needing our help and we turn our backs on them?"
Sam Mortimer, a public health nurse at Public Health Sudbury and Districts, said the new study has implications for communities in northern Ontario, where funding for these sites is strained.
Sudbury's supervised consumption site, the Spot, has stopped receiving funds from the city and currently operates on donations while it awaits a funding decision from the province. Mortimer said that if sites such as this lose funding, it would be "devastating" for Sudbury and other northern Ontario communities.
"Then we're looking at preventable deaths, substance-use related harms and strained emergency and health care, like social services."
According to Public Health Sudbury and Districts, 29 non-fatal overdose events occurred at the Spot, and 645 referrals were made to off-site health-care services since it opened in late September 2022.
In the fall of 2020, Rammohan, then studying for her master's in science in health policy, made a weekly trip to the chief coroner's office in Toronto from her parents' home near Grimsby, where she would sift through hundreds of files containing the intimate details of Ontarians who died of an overdose. The reports included the circumstances surrounding a person's death, including hospital admission records, police reports and interviews with family, friends or whoever had found them.
Rammohan became a witness to the tragic - and often untimely - deaths of Ontarians swept up in an ever-expanding overdose crisis.
Those hours at the coroner's office became the foundation of her research into supervised consumption sites. But it came with a mental toll, she said.
"It was heavy. It was ... the nature of people's lives ending and descriptions of how they were found," she recalls. "In many cases people were alone in their last minutes and they were found later.
"That was difficult ... You can only read so many of those stories."
CREDIT: Scott Martin and Declan Keogh Investigative Journalism Bureau Kenyon Wallace Toronto Star

Subject: Public health; Drug overdose; Fatalities; Mental health; Addictions; Neighborhoods; Funding; Mortality; Drug policy; Fentanyl; Narcotics

Document 182 of 500

Ford's Tories want to fast-track projects: Critics warn Greenbelt shows government can't be trusted with 'harmful' policies

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-tories-want-fast-track-projects/docview/2927815516/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+Tories+want+to+fast-track+projects%3A+Critics+warn+Greenbelt+shows+government+can%27t+be+trusted+with+%27harmful%27+policies&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Still stinging from the Greenbelt land swap fiasco, the Progressive Conservatives insist streamlining approvals for more infrastructure projects will not be harmful to the environment.
"We're taking a hard look at our policies and processes and have found ways to make them smarter and more efficient," Environment Minister Andrea Khanjin told reporters Friday at a Brampton hydro corridor. "We've consulted extensively with municipalities, Indigenous communities and key stakeholders on proposals to move more projects to a streamlined environmental process.
"This means that more projects such as new highways, railways and electricity transmission lines like the ones behind us here could see up to four years taken off their building timelines, which is a massive improvement that saves time and money," she said.
The changes will take effect next Thursday two days after being included in omnibus legislation being tabled when the house resumes after the winter break.
They are designed to fast-track construction of projects such as Hwy. 413, the 60-kilometre freeway between Milton and Vaughan and the 16.2-km Bradford Bypass linking Hwys. 400 and 404.
Noting similarly streamlined environmental assessments for public transit projects have been in place since 2008 - under changes made by the previous Liberal government - Khanjin emphasized oversight was not being undermined. "This is going to help streamline low-risk projects," said Khanjin, adding the federal government, Quebec and British Columbia have similar policies.
The minister emphasized there would still be more comprehensive environmental assessments for complex projects like large landfill sites and electricity generation facilities.
But pointing to the $8.28-billion Greenbelt scandal - in which Premier Doug Ford opened up 7,400 acres of protected land to housing development before cancelling that controversial move, which has triggered an RCMP investigation - critics are wary.
"The premier and his government have learned nothing from their Greenbelt scheme and keep pushing ahead with policies that they know are harmful," said NDP MPP Sandy Shaw (Hamilton West-Ancaster-Dundas).
"This government's disastrous track record on the environment speaks for itself. Today's announcement raises serious questions about the future of environmental protection in our province," said Shaw.
Environmental Defence's Phil Pothen said the best indicator of future behaviour is past behaviour.
"We've seen how a gap in process will be used by this government," said Pothen, land use and land development program manager for the environmental group.
Last August, separate reports from the auditor general and integrity commissioner found certain property developers with Tory connections were "favoured" when parcels of Greenbelt land were unexpectedly opened to build housing in late 2022. The following month, Ford cancelled the swap and enshrined in legislation protections for the two-million acre Greenbelt around the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area.
But Pothen warned that watering down environmental assessments for highways and hydro corridors will hasten "sprawl development."
"The public understands why they should take this seriously. It's not fantastical to imagine how (the streamlined assessments) will be used," he said. "This is a planned curtailment of full-scale environmental assessments."
Colin Best, president of the Association of Municipalities of Ontario and a Halton regional councillor, countered that, saying "modernized environmental assessment processes are a critical enabler for housing, streamlining processes for municipalities to help them build infrastructure like roads, sewers, and water systems more quickly."
"The initiatives announced today take a risk-based approach to environmental management, removing red tape for straightforward municipal infrastructure projects and focusing resources where they are needed most," said Best.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks at the Canadian International Auto Show at the Metro Toronto Convention Centre on Friday. The Progressive Conservatives insist streamlining approvals for more infrastructure projects will not be harmful to the environment.Steve RussellToronto Star
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 183 of 500

More funding is needed for health-care ills

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/more-funding-is-needed-health-care-ills/docview/2927815114/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=More+funding+is+needed+for+health-care+ills&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: To his credit, Ontario Premier Doug Ford looks to have accepted a couple of home truths. One, Ontarians prefer results to rancour between levels of government. Two, they want the health-care crisis addressed in a big way and in a hurry.
The recently announced agreement with the federal government that will see a $3.1-billion increase in health-care funding over three years is good news.
The money should enable the Ford government to create new primary-care teams to increase access to family doctors, see the addition of hundreds of family physicians and nurse practitioners and thousands of new nurses and personal support workers.
The funding is part of an $8.4-billion injection over 10 years agreed to between Ottawa and the Ford government.
Under that deal, Ontario agrees to address federal priorities by improving access to family health services, including in rural and remote areas; boosting the health workforce; providing better access to timely mental health, substance abuse and addictions services; and allowing individuals access to their electronic health information shared between health professionals.
The balance of the promised money will be contingent on meeting timelines and targets.
The federal government had allotted $25 billion over 10 years for bilateral deals with provinces - Ontario is the fifth to sign on - to address the four federal priorities.
Reports from across the health-care spectrum have revealed crises in pediatric care, long-term care, emergency care, mental-health care and access to family doctors.
The Star's Kenyon Wallace put a human face on the data with a report on Sunday from the emergency room at Toronto Western Hospital that compellingly illustrated the anxiety and suffering of patients and the extraordinary workload and risk of burnout placed on health-care professionals.
A system at the breaking point is the result of decades of failure by successive provincial and federal governments of various parties to make the necessary investments in infrastructure and personnel to meet the needs of an aging and growing population.
As of 2023, Wallace reported, Canada had one of the lowest hospital-bed-to-patient ratios in the OECD (Organization for Economic Co-ordination and Development).
In health care, the system is interconnected and problems inevitably pile up downstream.
Too few family doctors means more people showing up in ERs. Too few beds results in extended wait times to see a doctor and delays in getting patients to surgical or medical wards. Rather than being back on the road, paramedics who bring patients in must stay with them until they can be seen.
As Ontario's acting auditor general recently reported, the rising delays can mean a matter of life or death.
"When emergency departments are unable to operate efficiently and effectively, patients may not receive timely and high-quality care, which can lead to negative or even fatal health outcomes," wrote Nick Stavropoulos.
In the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic, targeting mental-health care is particularly laudable.
Dr. Eileen de Villa, Toronto's medical officer of health, has reported that student mental health declined sharply in the aftermath of pandemic, with visits to Toronto ERs related to self-harm among children and youth rising 30 per cent in the first year afterwards.
Prior to the pandemic, a strained health-care system was already struggling to meet demand, with huge backlogs and waiting lists. The social isolation and stresses of COVID-19 only made it worse.
Studies in the Canadian Medical Association Journal found the number of Canadian adolescents reporting self-harm or suicidal thoughts rose significantly. "If a kid seeks help, we can't wait 10 months to get a kid care," Dr. Natasha Saunders, a pediatrician at the Hospital for Sick Children, said last year.
Ford's office recently said the premier is focused on delivering on commitments in policy areas that matter to Ontarians. Not much matters more than health care.
To be sure, a complex, long-underfunded system will not be fixed in one fell swoop. Some of the remedies funded by the new cash - like new training spots for doctors - will only be felt years from now. So this funding - and the balance which is owed - is only part of the solutions needed to relieve the pressures on a strained system.
But the federal-provincial agreement is an important part of the long-term effort to rebuild capacity and prepare for the future.
CREDIT: ;

Subject: Hospitals; Emergency medical care; Mental health; Funding; Health care; Pandemics; Physicians; Self destructive behavior; COVID-19

Business indexing term: Subject: Hospitals Physicians

People: Ford, Doug

Title: More funding is needed for health-care ills

Document 184 of 500

Ottawa may walk back parts of net-zero plan: Minister considers allowing new gas plants to operate with no limits past 2045 target

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ottawa-may-walk-back-parts-net-zero-plan/docview/2927815101/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+may+walk+back+parts+of+net-zero+plan%3A+Minister+considers+allowing+new+gas+plants+to+operate+with+no+limits+past+2045+target&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ottawa is proposing to water down its clean electricity regulations in the face of intense opposition from some provinces by further opening the door for natural gas power plants to keep burning well beyond the promised deadline for net-zero power in 2035.
In response to objections brought by provinces and electrical grid operators, Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault is now considering allowing new gas plants to operate with no limits past 2045, as well as loosening restrictions on existing plants.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau "made a commitment to a net-zero grid in 2021," said Keith Brooks, programs director at Environmental Defence. "But provinces like Ontario and Saskatchewan, they have gone ahead (and started building new gas plants) in a direct challenge to federal regulations, and now the federal government seems to be waffling."
In a statement Friday, Guilbeault said the changes are meant to "enhance the flexibility" of electricity operators in the provinces while "maintaining" Canada's objectives to reduce emissions causing the climate crisis.
While these changes will likely still mean reduced carbon emissions from electricity generation in provinces like Alberta, Saskatchewan and Nova Scotia, which rely on fossil fuels for the bulk of their power, they could be so loose as to allow emissions to actually rise in provinces like Ontario, which currently derives about 10 per cent of its power from natural gas, critics allege.
"If these regulations allow new plants to be contracted, constructed and run completely unmitigated out past 2045, and the existing fleet is allowed to continue to operate for its 20-year end of life period as well, there's no limit on emissions for quite some time," said Brooks.
"It's important that this 20-year lifespan not be extended," he said. "Otherwise, it's going to be a huge problem."
According to a summary of consultations released Friday, the changes that Ottawa is considering include:
Allowing greater use of natural gas power when electricity demand spikes;
Lifting a hard cap of 450 hours per year for natural gas plants without means to prevent emissions, and replacing it with a limit based on annual emissions;
Allowing for the "limited use" of offsets for gas plants that exceed their emissions limits;
"Slightly" extending the exemption for gas plants currently being planned and built, which aren't subject to the regulations until 20 years after they come online. This originally applied to plants that came online before 2025; the government is proposing to extend that by an unspecified amount of time.
While some climate experts welcomed the changes, others raised concerns that they would allow emitting power plants to continue without restraints in the midst of the climate crisis.
New Democrat MP Laurel Collins, the NDP's climate critic in Parliament, called the changes "disappointing" because they weaken what she sees as a critical climate policy for the federal government.
"This is them, yet again, creating more loopholes," Collins said. "This is a critical piece of our climate policy and getting clean electricity by 2035 ... It is essential if we have any hope of meeting our climate targets."
It's not yet clear how these changes would impact overall emissions from the electricity sector, which the Liberal government in Ottawa has pledged to reduce to "net zero" by 2035. The original version of the regulations already fell short of that goal. Guilbeault told the Star last year that there would have been fewer than 10 megatonnes of "residual" emissions from the country's power grids by 2035 under the first proposal - about 20 per cent of overall electricity emissions based on 2021 figures.
The federal government maintains it can still attain net-zero emissions from electricity by 2035 through other policies outside of these regulations, which have sparked heated criticism from provinces like Ontario, Alberta and Saskatchewan. They argue the regulations will drive up electricity bills and possibly strain power grids.
In a written statement, Dustin Duncan, the minister responsible for Saskatchewan's provincial power utility, told the Star that Friday's proposed changes are "an admission" the federal regulations "are unaffordable and technically and logistically unattainable." Duncan said the regulations infringe on provincial authorities and should still be scrapped entirely.
The Ontario government welcomed the changes but indicated it's still not satisfied over concerns the regulations could affect the reliability of the province's power grids.
"More work is still needed to ensure these regulations address all of the concerns identified by Ontario's expert system planners," read a statement from Ontario Energy Minister Todd Stone's spokesperson, Palmer Lockridge.
Alberta's environment minister, Rebecca Schulz, added in her own statement that the regulations are still not "feasible or realistic" based on the 2035 timeline for implementation.
Why is natural gas used?
Natural gas was once considered a transition fuel because it produces less carbon when burned. It was favoured as a way for heavy industry like power generation to reduce emissions and stop burning coal. But research over the last few years has shown that because natural gas is mostly made up of the particularly potent greenhouse gas methane, leaks during extraction and along the pipeline network make the gas worse for the climate than coal.
While efforts are underway to plug those leaks, satellite monitoring and flyover measurements show they are far greater than has generally been understood.
All of this adds up to an argument to phase out natural gas as quickly as possible, rather than prolong its use, environmentalists say.
Meanwhile, the grid operator in Ontario has contracted three new gas plants, in St. Clair, Windsor and Napanee, none of which would be built fast enough to be grandfathered into the regulations as they were originally proposed, and would have had to cease operation by 2035, except during peak or emergency periods.
These plants, and others announced in Saskatchewan, could be allowed to run for a full 20 years under the proposed changes to the regulations, a prospect that worries clean energy advocates.
"Allowing unabated gas-fired generators to come online after Jan. 1, 2025 (the previous cut-off date) risks locking in gas emissions and higher electricity costs for decades," said Scott MacDougall, director of electricity at the Pembina Institute.
Other clean energy advocates said the changes were necessary to ensure the targets were achievable in every province, and that the electricity regulations were never going to achieve net zero on their own.
"The clean electricity regulations were never a silver bullet for this," said Evan Pivnick, clean energy program manager at Clean Energy Canada.
CREDIT: Marco Chown Oved and Alex Ballingall Toronto Stars

Subject: Provinces; Electricity; Climate policy; Power plants; Emissions; Environmental policy; Clean technology; Industrial plant emissions; Federal regulation; Natural gas

Business indexing term: Subject: Power plants

Location: Saskatchewan Canada; Canada

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: A.14

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Feb 17, 2024

Section: News

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Trudeau government considers walking back a key environmental pledge on power generation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-government-considers-walking-back-key/docview/2927876472/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+government+considers+walking+back+a+key+environmental+pledge+on+power+generation&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 186 of 500

Ford government wants to fast-track construction projects but critics warn Greenbelt shows it can't be trusted

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-wants-fast-track-construction/docview/2927876471/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+wants+to+fast-track+construction+projects+but+critics+warn+Greenbelt+shows+it+can%27t+be+trusted&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Still stinging from the Greenbelt land swap fiasco, the Progressive Conservatives insist streamlining approvals for more infrastructure projects will not be harmful to the environment.
"We're taking a hard look at our policies and processes and have found ways to make them smarter and more efficient," Environment Minister Andrea Khanjin told reporters Friday at a Brampton hydro corridor.
"We've consulted extensively with municipalities, Indigenous communities and key stakeholders on proposals to move more projects to a streamlined environmental process," said Khanjin.
"This means that more projects such as new highways, railways and electricity transmission lines like the ones behind us here could see up to four years taken off their building timelines, which is a massive improvement that saves time and money," she said.
The changes will take effect next Thursday two days after being included in omnibus legislation being tabled when the house resumes after the winter break.
They are designed to fast-track construction of projects such as Hwy. 413, the 60-km freeway between Milton and Vaughan and the 16.2-km Bradford Bypass linking Hwys. 400 and 404.
Noting similarly streamlined environmental assessments for public transit projects have been in place since 2008 - under changes made by the previous Liberal government - Khanjin emphasized oversight was not being undermined.
"This is going to help streamline low-risk projects," said Khanjin, adding the federal government, Quebec and British Columbia have similar policies.
The minister emphasized there would still be more comprehensive environmental assessments for complex projects like large landfill sites and electricity generation facilities.
But pointing to the $8.28-billion Greenbelt scandal - in which Premier Doug Ford opened up 7,400 acres of protected land to housing development before cancelling that controversial move, which has triggered an RCMP investigation - critics are wary.
"The premier and his government have learned nothing from their Greenbelt scheme and keep pushing ahead with policies that they know are harmful," said NDP MPP Sandy Shaw (Hamilton West-Ancaster-Dundas).
"This government's disastrous track record on the environment speaks for itself. Today's announcement raises serious questions about the future of environmental protection in our province," said Shaw.
Environmental Defence's Phil Pothen said the best indicator of future behaviour is past behaviour.
"We've seen how a gap in process will be used by this government," said Pothen, land use and land development program manager for the environmental group.
Last August, separate reports from the auditor general and integrity commissioner found certain property developers with Tory connections were "favoured" when parcels of Greenbelt land were unexpectedly opened to build housing in late 2022.
The following month, Ford, whose popularity plummeted over public concern about the land deal, cancelled the swap and enshrined in legislation protections for the two-million acre Greenbelt around the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area.
But Pothen warned that watering down environmental assessments for highways and hydro corridors will hasten "sprawl development."
"The public understands why they should take this seriously. It's not fantastical to imagine how (the streamlined assessments) will be used," he said.
"This is a planned curtailment of full-scale environmental assessments."
Colin Best, president of the Association of Municipalities of Ontario and a Halton regional councillor, countered that, saying "modernized environmental assessment processes are a critical enabler for housing, streamlining processes for municipalities to help them build infrastructure like roads, sewers, and water systems more quickly."
"The initiatives announced today take a risk-based approach to environmental management, removing red tape for straightforward municipal infrastructure projects and focusing resources where they are needed most," said Best.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford delivers remarks at the opening ceremonies of the Canadian International Auto Show in Toronto on Feb. 16, 2024.

Subject: Infrastructure; Roads & highways

Business indexing term: Subject: Infrastructure

Title: Ford government wants to fast-track construction projects but critics warn Greenbelt shows it can't be trusted
