Document 187 of 500

Here's how many international student applications Ontario universities want

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/heres-how-many-international-student-applications/docview/2927649637/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here%27s+how+many+international+student+applications+Ontario+universities+want&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's universities say more than one-third of the province's international student applications should go to them, maintaining they are "not the cause" of the current troubles and "should not be asked to shoulder the consequences" of government clampdowns.
"Universities have been very careful and responsible in taking on new students," said Steve Orsini, president and CEO of the Council of Ontario Universities, adding foreign undergraduate student enrolment has held steady over the past four years at around 23,000 despite explosive growth in public and private colleges.
"We've been doing it for decades - we've been very responsible and managing the growth. So when there's a gold rush for international students, universities didn't play that game. We were very measured and responsible to ensure that our students were put first" with proper services such as housing and mental health supports, said Orsini in releasing the council's proposal in response to the federal government's recent announcement curbing student visas and leaving it to provinces to divide them up.
The council is asking that about 82,250 of 235,000 eligible undergraduate applications be given to the province's 23 publicly assisted universities, and be allocated based on enrolment.
Amid all the uncertainty - and the possibility of students ultimately choosing to study elsewhere - Orsini said the universities are hoping that translates into about 28,000 approvals, which will keep international undergraduate enrolment stable at under 20 per cent.
''This will ensure we can continue to recruit the best and the brightest talent from across the globe," the council's proposal says.
It is unclear how colleges will react to the universities' proposal, given the clawback in the number of foreign student spots and the sector's increasing reliance on the fees those students pay. Many public colleges also partner with private colleges to offer diplomas that comes with a post-graduation work permit, which Ottawa has now put an end to.
Ontario has since announced it will impose a moratorium on new public-private college partnerships.
Immigration Minister Marc Miller has singled out the Wild West landscape in Ontario on several occasions, highlighting the concerns raised in a 2021 auditor general's report with respect to the fast-growing public-private college partnerships.
The report found Ontario's public colleges, especially those outside of the GTA, have become increasingly dependent on international students as a source of revenue and "some of these partnerships did not uphold enrolment requirements." It said their quality assurance and student support processes should be strengthened.
"We have a challenge in Ontario in particular," Miller told a news conference last year. "What we are seeing in the ecosystem is one that has been chasing after short-term gain without looking at the long-term pain. And we need to reverse that trend."
Miller more recently announced Ottawa's plan to decrease the number of new study permits by 35 per cent across the country, but not impact the number of applicants in master's and doctoral programs.
"We're very concerned with having everyone lumped into the same cap," said Orsini. Universities weren't the cause of the housing crisis but "they're collateral damage now, and we really want the province to tread carefully on how they apply the cap to ensure they don't hurt the good performers."
According to the latest Statistics Canada data, 31.9 per cent of post-secondary international students across the country were enrolled in short-term programs in 2021-2022, almost 48 per cent in undergraduate programs and 20.3 per cent in postgraduate studies.
In Ontario, 46.5 per cent of post-secondary international students were in short-term programs, while 40.3 per cent were enrolled in undergraduate programs and 13.1 per cent in postgraduate studies.
Kitchener's Conestoga College, for example, saw 12,822 new study permits in 2021, jumping to 20,905 in 2022 and to 30,395 in 2023 - up 137 per cent over the three years - and now has the largest number of international students anywhere in the country.
The Council of Ontario Universities is also calling on the Ford government to allow tuition to increase by five per cent this fall, and boost funding by 10 per cent as recommended by the province's own blue-ribbon panel.
Shortly after taking office, Premier Doug Ford slashed tuition by 10 per cent and has banned any increases since for domestic students.
Ford has already ruled out a fee hike for this fall, saying students can't afford it.
Between that, stagnant provincial funding amid inflation on top of unexpected COVID-19 costs, Ontario universities say they are in financial crisis and need the additional revenue from a tuition increase, as well as more government help - an amount estimated to bring in about $1.9 billion over three years.
Ten of the province's 23 publicly assisted universities are running deficits totalling more than $175 million this year.
"Ontario's universities are asking the federal and provincial governments to work together to take a more focused and targeted approach. We support enhancing the integrity of the international recruitment process, but are calling on both levels of government to focus their efforts on those organizations that have rapidly expanded international student enrolment without student supports," the council said.
Post-secondary schools have come to rely on tuition from international students, which can be three or four times the rate of domestic students - at an average of $32,000 a year - to make up funding gaps.
While the university council supports Ottawa's move to limit international student numbers amid a housing crisis, "we are extremely disappointed with the approach taken. Applying a large, blunt cap poses significant risks to all post-secondary institutions - not just those that the federal government is specifically concerned about. This blunt cap is already undermining Canada's global reputation for attracting talent," the council said.
CAPTION: Outside of the law school at Toronto Metropolitan University.

Subject: Tuition; Foreign students; Private colleges & universities; Councils; Private schools; Graduate studies; Funding

Company / organization: Name: Council of Ontario Universities; NAICS: 813910

Title: Here's how many international student applications Ontario universities want

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Feb 16, 2024

Section: politics/provincial

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document type: News

Document 188 of 500

Disappointment in the property tax hike, Ford reversals, carbon tax rebrand and Rielly suspension

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/disappointment-property-tax-hike-ford-reversals/docview/2927458710/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Disappointment+in+the+property+tax+hike%2C+Ford+reversals%2C+carbon+tax+rebrand+and+Rielly+suspension&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Upped property taxes unwelcome
Police funding increase, big tax hike finalized in Mayor Chow's first budget, Feb. 14
I am disappointed that Mayor Olivia Chow caved to the cops. They complained before they needed more money to improve response times of 20 minutes. They got it and response times went longer, not shorter. Maybe they should be looking at inefficiencies in their organization, rather than just expecting another handout will solve everything.
This property tax increase won't sit well with many, but if former mayors Rob Ford and John Tory had the guts to at least put a 1 per cent increase through each year over the past ten years, we might not have ended up with such a large increase and the city might not have become so run down. Ford and Tory governed with a short-term gain long-term pain approach.
Gail Bennett, Toronto
Columnist Edward Keenan seems to suggest that Toronto homeowners have happily accepted Mayor Olivia Chow's outrageous tax hike of 9.5 per cent without protest. Nothing could be further from the truth! There were intense letter-writing campaigns and calls made by residents to city councillors and the mayor, demanding a reduction to the proposed hike. That is probably the reason for the reduction to 9.5 per cent from what was first proposed. Many owners are seething and counting down the days until the next municipal election.
Maria Christopoulos, Toronto
Think before you act, Ford
Ontario government scrapping licence plate renewals, Feb. 13
Must we really live with Premier Doug Ford's almost daily, hairbrained changes to programs that worked well before, kept some control and brought money into the province. Is the next step no license plates? Ford seems to have no understanding of the consequences of his decisions. The loss of revenue does not seem to matter, but it's OK to spend massive amounts on court battles. Fixing the mess he created in health care and education is not important. He should also remember that he is employed by us. He would be fired in the business world.
Helga Weick, Toronto
Targeted hate is everywhere
Toronto police increase presence around Mount Sinai Hospital amid debate over pro-Palestinian protesters, Feb. 14
The claims of the Mount Sinai Hospital protest organizers that they were not targeting the hospital would be risible if they were not so polar opposite to the truth. I suppose the graffiti on the downtown Jewish Community Centre, the blocking of a bridge that is the gateway to a predominantly Jewish neighbourhood and the vandalism and arson at a Jewish food store were a pause too, just like at the hospital. And now, they have the chutzpah to demand an apology from Canadian politicians for calling out their antisemitism. It is the protest organizers who should apologize and vow to stop intimidating the Jewish community.
Dr. Philip Berger, Toronto
Introduce national pharmacare now
Kudos to the Heart and Stroke Foundation and the Canadian Cancer Society for urging the federal government to introduce national pharmacare "as soon as possible."To back up their argument they present the results of a Leger poll that found 22 per cent of Canadians report splitting pills, skipping doses or not even having their prescriptions dispensed due to costs. The result of not taking medications can lead to serious consequences including more trips to doctors, emergency rooms and possible hospitalization, not to mention loss of work and personal distress. These all represent cost to society, much of it hidden.
Pharmacare eventually became part of Liberal platforms but, of course, a truly universal single-payer plan has not materialized. It is reassuring to see the increased political pressure, up to date data and national health organizations advocating for change. The use of the words "as soon as possible" are prophetic. There doesn't appear to be much time left for our federal government to finally make our national health plan complete.
Bill Wensley, Cobourg, Ont.
Mixed thoughts on carbon tax
Liberals bet a new name for carbon pricing will help them sell the rebate, Feb. 14
As Canadians struggle with affordability and climate concerns, the increasing carbon rebate is a win-win. The rebate payments will total $1,120 for a family of four in Ontario. That is a significant boost for household budgets. Pricing carbon pollution is the most effective way to reduce greenhouse gas emissions, as first proposed by Stephen Harper and supported by thousands of economists worldwide. As the Parliamentary Budget Office has reported, 80 per cent of Canadians get more money back in the carbon rebate than they pay. What's not to like about that?
Doug Pritchard, Toronto
Rebranding carbon tax is like putting lipstick on a pig. The federal Liberals under Prime Minister Justin Trudeau have had their 15 minutes of fame, now let's have that spring election.
Wayne Robertson, Chatham, Ont.
Discipline for Rielly was overkill
Leafs defenceman tried to stand up for his team. He got a five-game suspension for not being good at it: Arthur, Feb. 13
The NHL failed to look at the big picture when handing out the five-game suspension to Morgan Rielly. Rielly did not target the head or neck with his cross-check, but initial contact was with the well padded and protected shoulder area, with his stick subsequently riding up. In a sport that encourages and promotes emotional play and physicality, Rielly's response to a totally hot dog play by the Ottawa rookie was more akin to a dose of comeuppance rather than an act of retribution. The player was not injured, nor has he missed any playing time.
Rielly arguably is one of the most, if not the most, revered and virtuous player in the entire league! Until very recently, he had zero penalties which is incredulous, especially for a defenceman where on many nights the refs call the most miniscule infractions. He's even married to someone with the last name. They are Canada's sweetheart couple! By all means, give him a couple of games for a momentary lack of restraint. But five games? For shame!!
Philip Adams, Toronto
CAPTION: Mayor Olivia Chow presents her first city budget with a proposed 9.5 per cent property tax hike.

Why Ontario doctors are fleeing family practice + the fight among colleges for international students gets ugly

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-ontario-doctors-are-fleeing-family-practice/docview/2927458425/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+Ontario+doctors+are+fleeing+family+practice+%2B+the+fight+among+colleges+for+international+students+gets+ugly&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Friday, Feb. 16 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on Ontario's family doctor shortage, the fight over international students and an extortion wave terrorizing Peel's South Asian community.
DON'T MISS"Beyond crisis levels." Here's why Ontario doctors are fleeing family medicine
It was hard for Dr. Natalie Leahy to walk away after 16 years as a family doctor and even harder to say goodbye to the 1,200 patients with her Oshawa practice. But a combination of heavy workloads, financial pressures and the ever-increasing burden of administrative tasks had led to burnout, Leahy told the Star's Megan Ogilvie. She's just one in a wave of family doctor resignations, adding to an acute shortage that's left 2.3 million Ontarians without a family physician - a figure forecasted to rise to 4.4 million by 2026. A recent survey of 1,300 doctors found 65 per cent planned to either leave the profession or reduce their hours within the next five years. Here's what you need to know.
Silver lining? In an effort to address the shortage, the Ontario government recently announced $110 million in funding for primary care teams to help close the care gap. Critics have called the effort "as useful as an umbrella in a hurricane" given the scope of the problem.What we know: The Star spent 12 hours shadowing staff in the ER at Toronto Western Hospital. We witnessed doctors combat crowding, chaos and threats all while doling out life-saving treatments. Here's what it's like working overnight in the ER."Gloves truly off now." The fight among college presidents over international students is getting ugly
As soon as Ottawa announced it was capping international student permits, experts began predicting brawls between Ontario's colleges - and disputes have now boiled over into full public display. Conestoga College president John Tibbits responded to criticism from Sault College president David Orazietti, who singled out Conestoga for scrutiny in a recent podcast interview. Tibbits told Guelph Today: "Why are his goddamn students in Toronto? Why not up there? Talk about a whore, I mean, he's taking a percentage of the profits of an operation. I can't stand the guy by the way." Take a closer look inside the rising tensions among Ontario's colleges.
Context: Tibbits' comments came after his college released a report unapologetically boasting about its growth plan, in what it says are efforts to combat labour pressures from Canada's declining birth rate and aging workforce. Conestoga currently welcomes the largest number of international students in the country.Meanwhile: Ontario's 23 publicly assisted universities have also chimed into the debate, saying more than one-third of the province's international students should go to them and claiming they are "not the cause" of Canada's current troubles.Pay $200,000 or "we will come and shoot you." A GTA businessmen described "terrorizing" extortion attempts
Security footage appears to show a masked gunman emptying a handgun magazine into a row of parked SUVs. Two more shooters follow, firing indiscriminately at vehicles in a Caledon car dealer's lot. Elsewhere, a man pulls up to a Brampton restaurant and empties a gas can near the window before setting the area ablaze. These are just two examples of a wave of violent crime and threats "terrorizing" Peel region's South Asian community. Impacted business owners tell the Star's Jason Miller they're being extorted for hundreds of thousands of dollars and are facing threats of property damage or death. Why is this happening?
More: Peel police's new 23-member extortion task force has investigated 29 incidents - several including shootings - since late last year. Last week, the team announced five arrests and the seizure of firearms, ammunition, cash, computers and approximately 50 cell phones linked to alleged extortion attempts.Word from local leaders: In a recent letter to Canada's Minister of Public Safety, the mayors of Brampton and Surrey expressed their "deep concern" about the extortion wave and asked the minister to "do everything he can to protect our communities."WHAT ELSE
Doug Ford's government is aiming to ban most highway tolls, freeze driver's licence fees and eliminate plate registration.Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre says he'd cut "wasteful" foreign aid and hike defence spending after Justin Trudeau made Canada "weak."Here's how the war in Gaza brought this man's Canadian dream crashing down.Four patients died as oxygen ran out in a Gaza hospital seized by Israeli forces.Leslieville locals have filed a class action lawsuit against the supervised injection site where a woman was shot and killed.The tip of the iceberg? Experts are warning the plunge in Canadian Tire profits points to an economic storm ahead.Toronto cops have been charged with assault with a weapon and suspended with pay over a daylight shooting.Russia's prison service says that imprisoned opposition leader Alexei Navalny has died this morning at 47.A verdict is expected today in Donald Trump's civil fraud trial, capping a busy week of court action.Despite the noise over Joe Biden and Donald Trump, age and dementia do not go hand in hand.A couple built a multiplex six months ago. So why did it take Toronto Hydro until this week to agree to hook it up?He was cut from his Hamilton high school team. Now, he's an NBA megastar.POV
It's hard not to think that the Liberals have given up.

Document 190 of 500

Is it time for the Liberals and NDP to break up?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-time-liberals-ndp-break-up/docview/2927101552/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+it+time+for+the+Liberals+and+NDP+to+break+up%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The deal between Justin Trudeau's Liberals and Jagmeet Singh's New Democrats has already lasted longer than most minority governments in Canadian history.
Within a matter of days, that deal will either collapse or carry on - depending on whether the two sides can come to some kind of agreement to bring pharmacare to Canada.
Two questions hang over that fork in the road for the two parties. Can the deal last? Beyond that, though, should the deal continue?
On the first question, cautious optimism surrounds the whole debate over pharmacare, for both sides, for now.
As the Star reported on Wednesday, Liberals and New Democrats appear to be circling around some kind of consensus on which drugs to include in whatever pharmacare deal they hammer out before their self-imposed, March 1 deadline. Diabetes drugs and birth-control pills seem to have made the cut.
It also appears that both sides agreed that full pharmacare - as the NDP envisions it and the Liberals have on-and-off promised - won't be coming to Canada in the near future.
"We're not saying the money's got to be in the budget. That's never been our ask. And we've never said that," Singh said during a tough-talking news conference earlier this week. "But we do expect legislation that lays the foundation, and that's what we're asking for."
Singh also threw a bucket of cold water over what the Liberals have called a major problem - the possible damage to Canada's credit rating if Trudeau introduces a whole new social program at this point in the mandate.
"There's lots of ways to clarify for credit-rating agencies that this is something that's going to require additional work - (that) it's going to require additional steps to happen."
New Democrats are saying that if the supply and confidence agreement ends over an impasse on pharmacare, they're going to be arguing it's the Liberals who walked away from the deal, not them.
That's where we come to the more strategic considerations, for both sides, on whether they want the deal to continue - the should-it-last question.
For Liberals, this is mostly a no-brainer. The collapse of the deal may not precipitate an immediate election, but it makes Trudeau's minority more precarious. And judging from all of the polls, odds are that an election could turn out badly for the Liberals.
If election-avoidance is the only issue, the New Democrats would likely be similarly motivated to keep the deal going. They may have limited clout as the fourth party in the House of Commons right now, but they would have zero influence, one assumes, in a Pierre Poilievre government.
But two former leaders of the NDP have openly questioned in recent months the wisdom of the party attaching its fortunes too closely to Trudeau's Liberals.
In one of his last interviews before his death last month, Ed Broadbent told the CBC's Rosemary Barton: "Maybe if the agreement were a year shorter, it might be a little better."
This week, former leader Thomas Mulcair said on CTV that it was time for the NDP to walk away from the Liberals, too. "I think that they have to draw that line in the sand and then say the deal is done ... They've got to show autonomy from the Liberals."
Mulcair said that walking away now would position Singh perfectly for a future election, as a principled advocate for pharmacare.
The other benefit, which he didn't mention, was that if Trudeau really is going into the next election as a liability for his party, it may be best for the New Democrats to start creating some distance between their party and Trudeau's team. Poilievre and his Conservatives have spent nearly two years branding the current regime as the "Liberal-NDP" government for their own strategic reasons.
At the risk of being overly earnest, however, the best reason for keeping the deal alive is the one offered when the agreement was first unveiled in March, 2022. It was, Liberals and New Democrats said, all about getting things accomplished.
It's worth remembering the state of politics in this country as this deal was being negotiated two years ago. Ottawa was neck deep in the midst of the so-called "Freedom Convoy" occupation and it looked like Canada was hurtling into the same kind of polarized chaos we're seeing south of the border. The idea of two political rivals rising above the fray to give Canadians dental care, pharmacare and other progressive pieces of legislation seemed like an antidote, especially if you're of the view that the best reply to anti-institutional populism is to prove that institutions work for people.
If the Liberals and New Democrats can find their way back to those first principles, there may be hope for the deal to continue, beyond practical or strategic disputes.
The average duration for minority government in Canada is roughly a year and a half. The Liberal-NDP deal will be two years old next month. That in itself is an achievement, but the progress need not stop there.
CAPTION: In this file photo taken on Sept. 9, 2021, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh participate in the federal election English-language Leaders debate in Gatineau, Que. In 2022, the two leaders signed on to asupply and confidence agreement that helps keep the Liberals in power.

Title: Is it time for the Liberals and NDP to break up?

Full text: Raised property taxes unwelcome

They got it and response times went longer, not shorter. Maybe they should be looking at inefficiencies in their organization, rather than just expecting another handout will solve everything.

Ford and Tory governed with a short-term gain long-term pain approach.
Gail Bennett, Toronto

That is probably the reason for the reduction to 9.5 per cent from what was first proposed. Many owners are seething and counting down the days until the next municipal election.

Increasing carbon rebate
is a win-win
Liberals bet a new name for carbon pricing will help them sell the rebate, Feb. 14
As Canadians struggle with affordability and climate concerns, the increasing carbon rebate is a win-win. The rebate payments will total $1,120 for a family of four in Ontario. That is a significant boost for household budgets. Pricing carbon pollution is the most effective way to reduce greenhouse gas emissions, as first proposed by Stephen Harper and supported by thousands of economists worldwide. As the Parliamentary Budget Office has reported, 80 per cent of Canadians get more money back in the carbon rebate than they pay. What's not to like about that?

Toronto police increase presence around Mount Sinai Hospital amid debate over pro-Palestinian protesters, Feb. 14

Subject: Mayors; Jewish life & ethics; Carbon; Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Tax increases

Title: Letters:   Police funding increase, big tax hike finalized in Mayor Chow's first budget, Ontario government scrapping licence plate renewals, Liberals bet a new name for carbon pricing will help them sell the rebate, Leafs defenceman tried to stand up for his team. He got a five-game suspension for not being good at it: Arthur, Toronto police increase presence around Mount Sinai Hospital amid debate over pro-Palestinian protesters,

Document 192 of 500

No need for Liberals, NDP to break up

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/no-need-liberals-ndp-break-up/docview/2927101500/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+need+for+Liberals%2C+NDP+to+break+up&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The deal between Justin Trudeau's Liberals and Jagmeet Singh's New Democrats has already lasted longer than most minority governments in Canadian history.
Within a matter of days, that deal will either collapse or carry on - depending on whether the two sides can come to some kind of agreement to bring pharmacare to Canada.
Two questions hang over that fork in the road for the two parties. Can the deal last? Beyond that, though, should the deal continue?
On the first question, cautious optimism surrounds the whole debate over pharmacare, for both sides, for now.
As the Star reported on Wednesday, Liberals and New Democrats appear to be circling around some kind of consensus on which drugs to include in whatever pharmacare deal they hammer out before their self-imposed, March 1 deadline. Diabetes drugs and birth-control pills seem to have made the cut.
It also appears that both sides agreed that full pharmacare - as the NDP envisions it and the Liberals have on-and-off promised - won't be coming to Canada in the near future.
"We're not saying the money's got to be in the budget. That's never been our ask. And we've never said that," Singh said during a tough-talking news conference this week. "But we do expect legislation that lays the foundation, and that's what we're asking for."
Singh also threw a bucket of cold water over what the Liberals have called a major problem - the possible damage to Canada's credit rating if Trudeau introduces a whole new social program at this point in the mandate.
"There's lots of ways to clarify for credit-rating agencies that this is something that's going to require additional work - (that) it's going to require additional steps to happen."
New Democrats are saying that if the supply and confidence agreement ends over an impasse on pharmacare, they're going to be arguing it's the Liberals who walked away from the deal, not them.
That's where we come to the more strategic considerations, for both sides, on whether they want the deal to continue - the should-it-last question.
For Liberals, this is mostly a no-brainer. The collapse of the deal may not precipitate an immediate election, but it makes Trudeau's minority more precarious. And judging from all of the polls, odds are that an election could turn out badly for the Liberals.
If election-avoidance is the only issue, the New Democrats would likely be similarly motivated to keep the deal going. They may have limited clout as the fourth party in the House of Commons right now, but they would have zero influence, one assumes, in a Pierre Poilievre government.
But two former leaders of the NDP have openly questioned in recent months the wisdom of the party attaching its fortunes too closely to Trudeau's Liberals.
In one of his last interviews before his death last month, Ed Broadbent told the CBC's Rosemary Barton: "Maybe if the agreement were a year shorter, it might be a little better."
This week, former leader Thomas Mulcair said on CTV that it was time for the NDP to walk away from the Liberals, too. "I think that they have to draw that line in the sand and then say the deal is done ... They've got to show autonomy from the Liberals."
Mulcair said that walking away now would position Singh perfectly for a future election, as a principled advocate for pharmacare.
The other benefit, which he didn't mention, was that if Trudeau really is going into the next election as a liability for his party, it may be best for the New Democrats to start creating some distance between their party and Trudeau's team. Poilievre and his Conservatives have spent nearly two years branding the current regime as the "Liberal-NDP" government for their own strategic reasons.
At the risk of being overly earnest, however, the best reason for keeping the deal alive is the one offered when the agreement was first unveiled in March, 2022. It was, Liberals and New Democrats said, all about getting things accomplished.
It's worth remembering the state of politics in this country as this deal was being negotiated two years ago. Ottawa was neck deep in the midst of the so-called "Freedom Convoy" occupation and it looked like Canada was hurtling into the same kind of polarized chaos we're seeing south of the border. The idea of two political rivals rising above the fray to give Canadians dental care, pharmacare and other progressive pieces of legislation seemed like an antidote, especially if you're of the view that the best reply to anti-institutional populism is to prove that institutions work for people.
If the Liberals and New Democrats can find their way back to those first principles, there may be hope for the deal to continue, beyond practical or strategic disputes.
The average duration for minority government in Canada is roughly a year and a half. The Liberal-NDP deal will be two years old next month. That in itself is an achievement, but the progress need not stop there.
CAPTION: If the deal between Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh falls apart over pharmacare, the big winner could be Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, Susan Delacourt writes.Adrian Wyld THE CANADIAN PRESS file photo
CREDIT: Susan Delacourt OPINION

Business indexing term: Subject: Rating services

Document 193 of 500

Province to ban new tolls, freeze driver's licence fees: Law to prohibit levies on provincial freeways except 407

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-ban-new-tolls-freeze-drivers-licence/docview/2927101383/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+to+ban+new+tolls%2C+freeze+driver%27s+licence+fees%3A+Law+to+prohibit+levies+on+provincial+freeways+except+407&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is slamming the brakes on any rate increases for driver's licences and outlawing the possibility of new tolls on all freeways except Hwy. 407.
At the same time, Ford's Progressive Conservatives are eliminating the need for registering licence plates after the Star revealed there are more than one million expired plates on Ontario roads.
Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria said "many families are feeling the pinch in their wallets" with the rising cost of living.
"The last thing they need to see is another unnecessary fee or toll coming their way," Sarkaria told reporters Thursday in downtown Toronto.
"I am pleased to announce that our government is introducing legislation that would, if passed, ban tolls on provincial highways - that will not only apply to the DVP and Gardiner Expressway only minutes away from here, but also to the province's 400-series highways," he said.
Queen's Park is uploading the Don Valley Parkway and the Gardiner from the city as part of Ford's new deal with Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, so the Tories wanted to assure motorists it will remain free to use.
But the most expensive toll road in the province - Hwy. 407, which can cost as much as $70.66 to drive 151 kilometres from the Queen Elizabeth Way in Burlington to highways 35/115 near Orono - will remain intact on both the privately and publicly operated stretches.
"Look, the 407 is a private entity," said a defensive Sarkaria, referring to the 1999 long-term lease signed by a previous Tory government that has seen tolls skyrocket on the 108-kilometre private section of the highway.
In fact, Queen's Park owns and operates the remaining 43 kilometres of Hwy. 407 from Brock Road in Pickering east to Orono.
"We're here to talk about the province's infrastructure like the DVP and the Gardiner," he said, emphasizing the changes are aimed at prohibiting new tolls, not existing levies.
While Hwy. 407 charges are here to stay, the nuisance of renewing a licence plate will soon be gone.
After the renewal sticker fee, which cost up to $120 annually, was phased out two years ago, many motorists incorrectly assumed they didn't have to keep their plates up to date, which led to a surge in fines of as much as $1,000 for driving with invalid plates.
"We will be automating the licence plate renewal process starting this summer, which will save vehicle owners more than 900,000 hours each year," said Sarkaria.
That's an apparent reference to the amount of time 13.6 million plate-holders would spend renewing their permits online or in person at ServiceOntario outlets.
"The automatic renewal process will only be available to drivers in good standing, who do not have outstanding tickets or related fees, and municipalities will still be able to use the renewal process to collect outstanding fees," the minister said.
Motorists who owe traffic fines or tolls would not receive renewals until those are paid.
Scofflaws will be notified that they must pay penalties or their plates will not be validated.
Sarkaria said the government is also planning to make permanent the freeze on driver's licence renewal fees at $90 and the Ontario Photo Card at $35. The Tories first announced that after taking office in 2018 and the regulation took effect in 2019.
"Our freeze has saved Ontarians $22 million since 2019 and it will save drivers $66 million more this decade," he said. The minister said the changes would be included in the Get It Done Act, an omnibus bill being tabled when the legislature resumes on Tuesday after the winter break.
As first reported Monday by the Star, there were exactly 1,015,139 expired licence plates in the province last month.
That means about seven per cent of the 13.6 million plates in Ontario have not been renewed by forgetful vehicle owners who risk hefty fines.
According to the Ontario Provincial Police, charges have soared since the paid renewal stickers were eliminated.
Last year, there were 15,631 tickets issued to motorists lacking a "currently validated permit for the vehicle," compared with 5,341 charges in 2022 and 1,556 in 2021.
It is believed the COVID-19 pandemic that kept Ontarians off roads and highways could have contributed to those lower violations in 2021.
Removing the renewal charge costs Queen's Park more than $1 billion in lost revenue annually, but the Tories believe it was a key factor in their re-election in 2022 and it remains popular with voters.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Fines & penalties; Tolls; Automobile drivers; Fees & charges; Roads & highways

Here's how many international student applications Ontario universities want

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/heres-how-many-international-student-applications/docview/2927644066/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here%27s+how+many+international+student+applications+Ontario+universities+want&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's universities say more than one-third of the province's international student applications should go to them, maintaining they are "not the cause" of the current troubles and "should not be asked to shoulder the consequences" of government clampdowns.
"Universities have been very careful and responsible in taking on new students," said Steve Orsini, president and CEO of the Council of Ontario Universities, adding foreign undergraduate student enrolment has held steady over the past four years at around 23,000 despite explosive growth in public and private colleges.
"We've been doing it for decades - we've been very responsible and managing the growth. So when there's a gold rush for international students, universities didn't play that game. We were very measured and responsible to ensure that our students were put first" with proper services such as housing and mental health supports, said Orsini in releasing the council's proposal in response to the federal government's recent announcement curbing student visas and leaving it to provinces to divide them up.
The council is asking that about 82,250 of the 235,000 eligible undergraduate applications be given to the province's 23 publicly assisted universities, and be allocated based on enrolment.
Amid all the uncertainty - and the possibility of students ultimately choosing to study elsewhere - Orsini said the universities are hoping that translates into about 28,000 approvals, which will keep international undergraduate enrolment stable at under 20 per cent.
"This will ensure we can continue to recruit the best and the brightest talent from across the globe," the council's proposal says.
Immigration Minister Marc Miller has singled out the Wild West landscape in Ontario on several occasions, highlighting the concerns raised in a 2021 auditor general's report with respect to the fast-growing public-private college partnerships.
The report found Ontario's public colleges, especially those outside of the GTA, have become increasingly dependent on international students as a source of revenue and "some of these partnerships did not uphold enrolment requirements." It said their quality assurance and student support processes should be strengthened.
"We have a challenge in Ontario in particular," Miller told a news conference last year. "What we are seeing in the ecosystem is one that has been chasing after short-term gain without looking at the long-term pain. And we need to reverse that trend."
The Council of Ontario Universities is also calling on the provincial government to allow tuition to increase by five per cent this fall, and boost funding by 10 per cent as recommended by the province's own blue-ribbon panel.
Shortly after taking office, Premier Doug Ford slashed tuition by 10 per cent and has banned any increases since for domestic students.
Ford has already ruled out a fee hike for this fall, saying students can't afford it.
Between that, stagnant provincial funding as well as inflation and unexpected COVID-19 costs, Ontario universities say they are in financial crisis and need the additional revenue from a tuition increase, as well as more government help - an amount estimated to bring in about $1.9 billion over three years.
Ten of the province's 23 publicly assisted universities are running deficits totalling more than $175 million this year.
"Ontario's universities are asking the federal and provincial governments to work together to take a more focused and targeted approach. We support enhancing the integrity of the international recruitment process, but are calling on both levels of government to focus their efforts on those organizations that have rapidly expanded international student enrolment without student supports," the council said.
Post-secondary schools have come to rely on tuition from international students, which can be three or four times the rate of domestic students, to make up funding gaps.
CAPTION: Outside of the law school at Toronto Metropolitan University.

Lawsuit launched against federal government over Emergencies Act after judge ruled its use unconstitutional

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lawsuit-launched-against-federal-government-over/docview/2926810611/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Lawsuit+launched+against+federal+government+over+Emergencies+Act+after+judge+ruled+its+use+unconstitutional&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A lawsuit was launched Wednesday against the federal government, Canada's major financial institutions, police agencies and others related to the "unconstitutional misuse" of the Emergencies Act in February 2022, weeks after a landmark court ruling last month.
In January, a federal court judge concluded Ottawa acted unlawfully when it used the Emergencies Act to end weeks of pandemic-related protests, saying the move breached Canadians' Charter rights.
The Trudeau Liberals immediately said they would appeal the decision, and that using the act was justified because the threats posed to Canada by the "Freedom Convoy" amounted to a national emergency.
On Wednesday, a Calgary-based law firm filed a "notice of action" in Ontario Superior Court in Ottawa on behalf of about two dozen individuals and businesses who were "subjected to the unreasonable use of the Emergencies Act." The lawsuit is seeking compensation for each plaintiff of up to $1.75 million dollars. None of the claims have been proven in court.
"A democracy is only as strong as its commitment to the rule of law, and when a government decides that it's not bound by the law of Canada, the resulting unlawful action not only breaks the law, it undermines and degrades the very democracy that put the government in power in the first place," said a press release issued by law firm Loberg Ector and founding partner Blair Ector.
About half of the plaintiffs are individuals and businesses in Ontario, while the others reside in or are based across Canada, including Edward Cornell, a resident of Shediac Cape, N.B., whose name appears first in the court filing Edward Cornell et al v. Justin Trudeau et al.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and several cabinet ministers are named as defendants, as are others and the six big banks. The plaintiffs are asking the court to find the government violated the Charter when they "identified individuals, provided financial information, interfered with private property, and seized financial products, information services of the plaintiffs including but not limited to their bank accounts and credit cards."
The suit is also asking for a "declaration that the defendants acted in panic, political spite, and with the intention of punishing and intimidating citizens of Canada."
The federal government could not immediately be reached for comment. Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland - who is also named in the lawsuit - said following the Federal Court's ruling last month that the threats to Canada at the time were real, and the use of the act was the right thing to do.
"We believed we were doing something necessary and something legal at the time," Freeland said. "That continues to be my belief today."
Included in the news release is a link to a website called The Accountability Project (TAP), described as a federal non-profit that has a mandate to advocate for human rights, including raising funds for public interest plaintiffs and class-action law suits. The primary beneficiary of donated funds will be the plaintiffs in the Cornell et al v. Trudeau lawsuit, the website says.
CAPTION: Police line up opposite "Freedom Convoy" protesters in front of Parliament Hill in Ottawa in February, 2022.

Document 196 of 500

Toronto police increase presence around Mount Sinai Hospital amid debate over pro-Palestinian protesters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-police-increase-presence-around-mount/docview/2926810431/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+police+increase+presence+around+Mount+Sinai+Hospital+amid+debate+over+pro-Palestinian+protesters&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto police said Wednesday they have increased patrols of the area around Mount Sinai Hospital, after a man climbed up scaffolding at the hospital waving a Palestinian flag during a rally on Monday.
Leaders of the Toronto's largest hospitals say the protest ran the real risk of disrupting hospital operations and making staff and patients unsafe. In a letter published Tuesday night, hospital leaders wrote they are "continuing to work alongside local law enforcement as well as our local security teams" to ensure their buildings are safe.
Protesters, meanwhile, say their intentions have been misconstrued and are demanding comments accusing them of antisemitism be retracted.
Police said they are continuing to investigate the incident and could lay charges in the future. Police did not share whether they had any particular people they were investigating, including the man who climbed the hospital.
Debate rages over the incident at Mount Sinai. Some believe the historically Jewish hospital was "targeted" by protesters. But protester accounts describe a lone, attention-seeking man scampered up the outside of the building and a crowd of onlookers paused to watch his ascent.
"We condemn the Canadian politicians incorrectly portraying the protest as targeting the hospital," reads a joint statement Wednesday from protest organizers, including a coalition of pro-Palestinian Jews.
This, they say, is patently untrue and "encourages a racist and limited view of Palestinian liberation movements."
"There's this notion that anybody who is aligned with the (pro-Palestinian) movement needs to be feared because they might do something," said Israeli-Canadian activist and retired sociologist Sheryl Nestel.
Politicians of all stripes, including Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford, spoke out against the protesters. NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh said the hospital was "targeted because of its ties to the Jewish community in Toronto," which he said was "not OK." Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie accused protesters of "infiltrating" the hospital, an "indefensible" and "antisemitic" act, she said. Mayor Olivia Chow called what happened "unacceptable" adding that "hate has no place in our city."
"It looked like there was a large crowd that stopped specifically outside this hospital in addition to the person climbing this specific hospital," Noah Shack, vice-president for countering antisemitism and hate with the UJA Federation of Greater Toronto, said Tuesday.
"That seems to me to be a clear targeting of this hospital."
In videos circulating online of the incident at Mount Sinai, "spiderman4palestine," as he is known on social media, is seen climbing up on the hospital while a crowd outside chants for an "intefadeh," a call for an uprising for Palestinian liberation, which many Jews have seen as a call for violence against them.
Intefadeh, which comes from the Arabic word meaning "to shake off," refers to two Palestinian uprisings in the West Bank and Gaza Strip aimed at ending Israel's occupation and establishing an independent Palestinian state. Collectively, 5,000 Palestinians and some 1,400 Israelis were killed in the two conflicts.
There has been widespread debate over where the line lies between criticism of Israel and antisemitism. There are those who say conflating anti-Zionism with antisemitism has created a chill on pro-Palestinian discourse in many areas of Canadian society in recent months. It has been widely accepted, though, that holding Jews or Jewish institutions in general responsible for Israel's actions is antisemitism.
CAPTION: "Palestinian Spider-Man," or spiderman4palestine on social media, has become a "fixture" at pro-Palestinian protests in Toronto for climbing up various structures. On Monday, he was widely condemned for climbing up historically Jewish hospital Mount Sinai.

Subject: Antisemitism; Jewish people; Climbing; Demonstrations & protests; Social networks

Document 197 of 500

An "untenable" crisis - Ottawa has no good excuse for its failure to appoint judges

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/untenable-crisis-ottawa-has-no-good-excuse/docview/2926637068/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=An+%26quot%3Buntenable%26quot%3B+crisis+-+Ottawa+has+no+good+excuse+for+its+failure+to+appoint+judges&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The verdict is in - the federal government has done a lousy job at appointing judges and has no good excuse for not doing better.
In a judgment released this week, Justice Henry Brown, of the Federal Court, declared that the shortage of Superior Court and Federal Courts judges is at "both a crisis and critical level."
Sadly, that will not be news for those denied justice when the resulting court delays has pushed cases beyond 30-month time frame set by the Supreme Court and caused a stay of proceedings.
Star reporter Jacques Gallant has detailed serious criminal cases when that has happened, including the collapse of a human trafficking case this month, a firearms case and just before Christmas, a sexual assault case after the jury had already delivered its guilty verdict. The shortages impact civil trials too.
Brown was ruling on a case brought by Yavar Hameed, a human rights lawyer in Ottawa, who represents low-income and marginalized individuals. He argued that his clients faced significant delays in court proceedings and sought a judicial order compelling the government to swiftly fill judicial vacancies.
In a judgment that is both a sharp rebuke and pointed call to do better, Brown agreed, ruling that the federal government must act to fill the vacancies.
Federal Justice Minister Arif Virani responded on the social media platform X, saying there are more federally appointed judges now than ever before. And he claimed the federal Liberals were appointing judges at a faster pace than the previous Conservative government under Stephen Harper. "We are making judicial appointments at the fastest pace in history. We made 100 judicial appointments last year, a number never attained by the Conservatives," Virani said.
Given that the Liberals have been in power for almost nine years, such partisan comparisons are tiresome and pointless. Virani can cheer the current pace of appointments but the tossed trials and concerns previously raised by the country's top judge show that the government has failed in its duty to keep the judicial benches adequately staffed.
Indeed, Brown's judgment reprints verbatim the letter that Richard Wagner, chief justice of the Supreme Court of Canada, sent to Justin Trudeau last May in which he said it was imperative the prime minister act to ensure appointments are made in a timely manner.
"The positions that have been left vacant are having significant impacts on the administration of justice, the operations of our courts and the health of our judges," Wagner wrote.
At the time of his letter, there were 85 vacancies and Wagner noted that some courts were dealing with vacancy rates as high as 15 per cent "for years now." Senior judicial positions, including the chief justice positions in Alberta, Ontario and Prince Edward Island, were being filled "at a very slow pace.
"No clear explanation justifies these delays," Wagner wrote, noting that judicial vacancies are predictable, especially those caused by retirements.
"In this context, these delays in appointments send a message that this is simply not a priority for the government," he stated.
Wagner's lengthy letter to Trudeau doesn't seem to have moved the government to action. Nine months later, the number of vacancies for federally appointed judges stands at 75, prompting Brown to conclude that the government ignored Wagner's concerns.
"With the greatest respect, the Court finds the Prime Minister and Minister of Justice are simply treading water" and in the process are failing those who have "unsuccessfully sought timely justice," Brown declared.
It's essential then that the government take Brown's ruling seriously. He did not set a number on what would be a reasonable level of vacancies, though he suggested mid-40s. Nor did he set a deadline. But he left no doubt he expects action.
"How long should it take to fill a sufficient number of vacancies? In the Court's view the answer is plain and obvious: these vacancies must be materially reduced within a reasonable time to a reasonable level," he said.
Virani can pat himself on the back all he wants on the government's record. But the evidence from the courts is clear and persuasive. Whatever the government has been doing, it's not enough. The federal Liberals need to get serious about filling judicial vacancies and ensuring the timely delivery of justice in Canada.
CAPTION: Toronto's Superior Court on University Ave. The Federal Court ruled this week that the federal governmenthas caused an "untenable and appalling crisis" by not appointing enough judges.

Singh must continue to push for national pharmacare

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/singh-must-continue-push-national-pharmacare/docview/2926637008/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Singh+must+continue+to+push+for+national+pharmacare&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Jagmeet Singh says he's getting 'a lot of heat' for working with the Liberals. Here's how he answers his critics, Feb. 6

NDP leader Jagmeet Singh really needs to continue insisting the government keep its promise to implement a national pharmacare plan.
It's terrible and expensive when a federal government repeatedly promises Canadians much-needed universal albeit-generic-brand medication coverage only to have the pharmaceutical industry typically react with successful threats to abandon their Canada-based R&D, et cetera, if the government went ahead with the "pharmacare" plan.
Such universal medication coverage would negatively affect the industry's superfluously plentiful profits. The profits would still be great, just not as great. Meantime, we continue to be the world's sole nation that has universal health care but no similar coverage of prescribed medication, however necessary.
A 2019 Angus Reid study found that, over the previous year, due to medication unaffordability, almost a quarter of Canadians decided against filling a prescription or having one renewed. Resultantly, many low-income outpatients who cannot afford to fill their prescriptions end up back in the hospital system as a result, therefore costing far more for provincial and federal government health ministries than if the medication had been covered.
The Angus Reid study also found that about 90 per cent of Canadians - including three quarters of Conservative supporters specifically not known for supporting publicly-funded social programs - support a national pharmacare plan. Another 77 per cent believed this should be a high-priority matter for the federal government.
Ergo, in order for the industry to continue raking in huge profits, Canadians and their health, as both individual consumers and a taxpaying collective, must lose out big time. So, while we are envied abroad for our "universal" health-care system, full care seemingly still comes second to the big-profit interests of industry.
Frank Sterle Jr., White Rock, B.C.
Spend excess fed money on education and health care
Yesterday I read more about burned-out health workers who are leaving the public system and today the same thing with school teachers.
It looks like former Ontario Conservative leader Mike Harris's protege is dedicated to the Conservative plan to destroy public education and public health care.
In the face of huge financial needs by both of these sectors, it is despicable that Ontario Premier Doug Ford is sitting on a giant "rainy day" fund ($22.6 billion) that he received from the federal government. I think he will use that in the next election to fake a balanced budget.
Today is that "rainy day."
T.C. Wilkins, Toronto
Educate students about harms of pornography Sex-ed should teach how to sext safely, study suggests, Feb. 7
Perhaps before teaching "safe sexting" in Ontario's Health and Physical Education curriculum, educators should research the association between sexting and maladaptive sexual development. Similarly, the relationship between pornography, unhealthy sexuality and relationships could be taught.
Brian O'Sullivan, Stouffville
Criminals must pay higher price for crimes to promise harsher punishment for car thieves, Feb. 4
I was shocked to see the Federal Conservative leader actually talk about policy instead of his usual attack dog from the bully pulpit stand. It seems Pierre Poilievre is taking a page out of the Liberal soft on criminals' playbook. He says that if you steal an automobile for the third time (really, third time?) and it is part of organized crime, your slap on the wrist will be three years in jail. That would be more time than the current three months. I'm shocked that a Conservative would even bring this to our attention.
First and foremost, our vehicles are our most important possession outside of our homes. Sparing any thug whether it's their first offensive is just wrong. As a society we need to send a stern message. Vehicle theft is going to cost you jail time, starting with the first offence.
People have to feel safe in their vehicles and their homes, when these are violated, criminals must pay price, whether it's the street criminal or organized crime.
The penalties for many crimes have not evolved with our times. The current disregard, lack of respect for the rule of law is very concerning. Whether it's 200+km/h speeds on our roads, drinking and driving, street racing, home invasions, vehicle thefts or hate crimes, the current judicial system is not working.
Mike Walker, Washago
Money vs. values
Smith enters value-versus-values clash, Feb. 5
Susan Delacourt writes of how Conservatives take the side of money against the Liberals' stand for rights and values. To paraphrase Oscar Wilde, the Conservatives "know the price of everything, and the value of nothing."
Ed Butts, Guelph
Large majority of Canadian immigrants stayMore than 15 per cent of immigrants leave Canada within 20 years of arriving, StatCan report finds, Feb. 4
Since when is 85 per cent bad?
I am reading all the negative comments on immigrants to Canada leaving. Fifteen per cent over 20 years is nothing. That 85 per cent stayed is amazing to me.
Why focus on 15 per cent leaving?
David Lambert, Aurora
Province is obstructing energy regulator's path toward a green futureMinister to overrule Ontario Energy Board, says decision will raise cost of new homes, Dec. 22
Ontario's Conservative government has announced it's intent to bypass a decision made by the independent energy regulator, the Ontario Energy Board (OEB), against Enbridge's infrastructure expansion request. The OEB cited unsupportable projections by Enbridge that all new houses would become gas consumers and remain so over the next 40 years - an absurd unlikelihood given the clear science that these fossil fuels are accelerating deadly global climate collapse.
Reversing the OEB's decision is a calculated attack on Ontario consumers and the global public - adults, children, everyone. The OEB correctly concluded that removing the fossil fuel subsidy would encourage the use of electric heat pumps in new housing which would lower energy bills, improve air quality, reduce the pace of climate destabilization, accelerate energy investment transition, and provide much-needed energy efficient cooling in the summer months without the need to install expensive separate AC systems.
Taxpayers shouldn't be saddled with the costs - bad debt and increasingly deadly weather patterns - for fossil fuel assets certain to be stranded, obsolete, before midlife.
With 1.5 million new homes set to be built by 2031, the Ford government's determination to see these homes connected to gas lines, will ratchet UP GHG emissions. In contrast, encouraging new homes to use heat pumps will help Ontario reduce emissions slowing our climate's degradation.
Ontario taxpayers should ponder why the government elected to safeguard our interests is actively obstructing the independent energy board's effort to save Ontarians' money and safeguard our families' futures.
D'Arcy McLenaghen, Toronto

CAPTION: NDP leader Jagmeet Singh holds a press conference on Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Feb. 13.

Toronto approves Olivia Chow's 2024 budget + One family's struggle with infertility

Full text: Good morning. This is the Thursday, Feb. 15 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Significant snowfall is expected today; if you don't need to travel, Environment Canada is cautioning people to stay indoors.
Here's the latest on Toronto's approved 2024 budget, a tale of fertility struggles and a lawsuit over Canadian major junior leagues' "atrocious" contracts.
DON'T MISSOlivia Chow's 2024 budget has been finalized - here's what's it means for you
After weeks of debate, Toronto mayor Olivia Chow's 2024 budget - including the largest property tax increase in 25 years - was approved Wednesday evening. Chow's 9.5 per cent property tax hike proposal survived an attempt to whittle it down; it will cost the average homeowner an extra $338 a year. The budget also includes an additional $12.6 million in funds for Toronto police's $1.2 billion budget, which police officials spent weeks campaigning for. Action highlights include freezing TTC fares and investing in affordable housing, tenant protections, homeless shelters, infrastructure repair and the Scarborough busway. Take a closer look at the details and how you'll be impacted.
Go deeper: With a tax hike on the horizon, data suggests a growing number of seniors are applying for exemptions through Toronto's property tax increase cancellation program. In 2023, almost 8,300 homeowners successfully applied for the program - up 44 per cent from 2018. Check if you're eligible for an exemption.Matt Elliott's take: Toronto has been described for years as a "broken" city. Chow's budget is off to an OK start - but it falls short of the billions needed for unfunded projects and repairs.She offered to carry a baby for her sister-in-law. Then came a twist no one expected
Laura and Martin spent $100,000 trying, unsuccessfully, to have a baby. Early in their relationship, Laura met Martin's sister Kelly, and they instantly formed a bond - "we just instantly felt like we'd known each other forever," Kelly told the Star's Amy Dempsey. As the years passed, the two grew even closer through a shared struggle - both wanted a child, but neither could get pregnant. So, when Laura finally did have her miracle baby, Kelly was in the delivery room with her. And, when Kelly began giving up on her dreams of motherhood, Laura decided to give her sister-in-law the ultimate gift: a baby. This is their story.
Caitlin Dunne's take: Despite reports that roughly one in six people will be diagnosed with infertility at some point in their lives, women are still saddled with stigma and shame. Infertility should be addressed as a public health issue with the potential to impact everyone.Another angle: While our society has improved in addressing women's fertility issues more sensitively, fertility struggles take their toll on men too - but they're often left to go it alone.Canadian major junior leagues are being sued over "atrocious" contracts for teenage hockey players
Young hockey players chasing the dream of making it big are often forced into exploitative contracts, a new lawsuit alleges, referring to Canadian major junior leagues as a "cartel." The leagues' conspired to limit athletes' ability to negotiate their own contracts, capping their pay at just $250 a month despite working full time, Robert Cribb reports. The suit also named the NHL as a "co-conspirator in the unlawful conduct." Filed by the North American division of the World Association of Ice Hockey Players Unions in New York Wednesday, the suit takes aim at the Canadian Hockey League and three of its local leagues. Take a closer look at the details.
Meanwhile: Junior hockey has dominated the headlines lately, after five players from Canada's 2018 world junior men's team were charged with sexual assault.Wait, what? The sexual assault allegations pushed Hockey Canada under a harsh spotlight, resulting in a series of parliamentary hearings in 2022 - which unearthed a number of new allegations that go back decades.WHAT ELSE

Doug Ford government to ban most highway tolls, freeze driver's licence fees and eliminate plate registration

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-government-ban-most-highway-tolls/docview/2926636802/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+government+to+ban+most+highway+tolls%2C+freeze+driver%27s+licence+fees+and+eliminate+plate+registration&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is slamming the brakes on any rate increases for drivers' licences and outlawing the possibility of new tolls on all freeways except Hwy. 407.
At the same time, Ford's Progressive Conservatives are eliminating the need for registering licence plates after the Star revealed there are more than one million expired plates on Ontario roads.
Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria said "many families are feeling the pinch in their wallets" with the rising cost of living.
"The last thing they need to see is another unnecessary fee or toll coming their way," Sarkaria told reporters Thursday in downtown Toronto.
"I am pleased to announce that our government is introducing legislation that would, if passed, ban tolls on provincial highways - that will not only apply to the DVP and Gardiner Expressway only minutes away from here, but also to the province's 400 series highways," he said.
Queen's Park is uploading the Don Valley Parkway and the Gardiner from the city as part of Ford's new deal with Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow so the Tories wanted to assure motorists it will remain free to use.
But the most expensive toll road in the province - Hwy. 407, which can cost as much as $70.66 to drive 151 km from the Queen Elizabeth Way in Burlington to Hwys. 35/115 near Orono - will remain intact on both the privately and publicly operated stretches.
"Look, the 407 is a private entity," said a defensive Sarkaria, referring to the 1999 long-term lease signed by a previous Tory government that has seen tolls skyrocket on the 108-km private section of the highway.
In fact, Queen's Park owns and operates the remaining 43 km of Hwy. 407 from Brock Road in Pickering east to Orono.
"We're here to talk about the province's infrastructure like the DVP and the Gardiner," he said, emphasizing the changes are aimed at prohibiting new tolls, not existing levies.
While Hwy. 407 charges are here to stay, the nuisance of renewing a licence plate will soon be gone.
After the renewal sticker fee, which cost up to $120 annually, was phased out two years ago, many motorists incorrectly assumed they didn't have to keep their plates up to date, which led to a surge in fines of as much as $1,000 for driving with invalid plates.
"We will be automating the licence plate renewal process starting this summer which will save vehicle owners more than 900,000 hours each year," said Sarkaria.
That's an apparent reference to the amount time 13.6 million plate holders would spend renewing their permits online or in person at ServiceOntario outlets.
"The automatic renewal process will only be available to drivers in good standing, who do not have outstanding tickets or related fees and municipalities will still be able to use the renewal process to collect outstanding fees," the minister said.
But motorists who owe traffic fines or tolls would not receive renewals until those are paid.
Scofflaws will be notified that they must pay penalties or their plates will not be validated.
Sarkaria said the government is also planning to make permanent the freeze on driver's licence renewal fees at $90 and the Ontario Photo Card at $35. The Tories first announced that after taking office in 2018 and the regulation took effect in 2019.
"Our freeze has saved Ontarians $22 million since 2019 and it will save drivers $66 million more this decade," he said.
The minister said the changes would be included in the Get It Done Act, an omnibus bill being tabled when the legislature resumes after the winter break on Tuesday.
As first reported Monday by the Star, there were exactly 1,015,139 expired licence plates in the province last month.
That means about seven per cent of the 13.6 million plates have not been renewed by forgetful vehicle owners who risk hefty fines.
According to the Ontario Provincial Police, charges have soared since the paid renewal stickers were eliminated.
Last year, there were 15,631 tickets issued to motorists lacking a "currently validated permit for the vehicle" compared with 5,341 charges in 2022 and 1,556 in 2021.
It is believed the COVID-19 pandemic that kept Ontarians off roads and highways could have contributed to those lower violations in 2021.
Removing the renewal charge costs Queen's Park more than $1 billion in lost revenue annually, but the Tories believe it was a key factor in their re-election in 2022 and it remains popular with voters.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford makes an announcement and answers questions at a press conference in Mississauga on Tuesday.
