Document 201 of 500

Rental demand will slow, RBC says: Recent report expects government's temporary cap to have larger effect in Ontario and B.C.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/rental-demand-will-slow-rbc-says/docview/2926411429/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Rental+demand+will+slow%2C+RBC+says%3A+Recent+report+expects+government%27s+temporary+cap+to+have+larger+effect+in+Ontario+and+B.C.&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: RBC says a recently announced cap on new study permits won't lead to an outright decrease in demand for rental housing from international students this year.
But a new report from the bank about the cap finds the expected increase in rental demand from international students could fall by about 50 per cent as a result of the policy.
Immigration Minister Marc Miller announced a temporary cap on new study permits over the next two years, as the federal government tries to get a handle on sharp growth in international student enrolments at Canadian post-secondary institutions. The number of new visas handed out this year will be capped at 364,000, a 35 per cent decrease from the nearly 560,000 issued last year.
"Even though we're going to be limiting the number of international study permits to about half in 2024 relative to what was issued in 2023, that likely won't translate to an immediate fall in the total number of international students in Canada," said economist Rachel Battaglia, the author of the report. "And because of that, it likely won't lead to an outright decline in the number of rental units demanded by international students in Canada this year."
The Liberal government plans to allocate student permits to provinces in proportion to population share. The report says the cap will have a larger effect on rental markets in Ontario and British Columbia, given international student admissions in those provinces outweigh their share of the population.
If the cap for this year is extended beyond 2025, RBC says it would lead to a decline in the number of international students in the country and therefore relieve pressure on the rental market more meaningfully.
"In the meantime, post-secondary institutions and other stakeholders must find creative ways to grow the student housing stock amid challenges in quickly building new structures," the report says.
The Liberals' immigration policies, including the international student program, have come under heightened scrutiny as the country experiences rapid population growth.
The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Nojoud Al Mallees The Canadian Press

Subject: Foreign students; Immigration

Document 202 of 500

Partisan politics leaves tenants out in cold

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/partisan-politics-leaves-tenants-out-cold/docview/2926411150/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Partisan+politics+leaves+tenants+out+in+cold&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Amid the biggest housing and homelessness crisis in recent memory, with the premier promising to move heaven and earth (and the Greenbelt) to give people a roof over their head, his government is leaving tenants out in the cold.
Landlords, too.
The backlog at Ontario's Landlord and Tenant Board (LTB) has surged to more than 53,000 cases, compared to barely 14,000 under the previous Liberal government. And it shows no sign of abating, according to a new analysis by Tribunal Watch Ontario that documents a system out of control.
Doug Ford's Tories took power in 2018 with a hidden agenda to purge Ontario's tribunals of anyone inherited from the previous government - no matter their accumulated wisdom. Rather than approve the routine reappointments of seasoned adjudicators, each with expertise and experience, Ford's partisan and paranoid Progressive Conservative government rebuffed, rejected and refused as many as they could.
No matter how apolitical the appointments, almost all were suspect and stripped of their authority. Year after year, as the knowledge gap grew at the LTB, the waiting list exploded nearly fourfold - and the hardship for ordinary Ontarians mounted.
Today, a tenant facing a nightmarish maintenance dispute has to wait more than 14 months to have their case resolved, on average. That compares to a mere two months in 2018.
Yes, even the landlords who might be expected to be well served by Ford's Tories are left holding the bag. Getting an eviction order for nonpayment of rent used to take about a month under the previous Liberal government, but now takes nearly a year.
"There is little justice to be found for Ontarians who have to turn to Ontario's busiest tribunal," warns the report from Tribunal Watch. "The deplorable situation at the LTB is not getting better ... it is a dereliction of responsibility."
I first documented this injustice nearly four years ago, describing how the LTB and other tribunals were chronically short-staffed thanks to the self-inflicted wounds of the Ford government. To be fair, Attorney General Doug Downey inherited the chaos from his predecessor, Caroline Mulroney, who presided over the dismantling of this quasi-judiciary body in the early days of the Ford era at its worst.
In the years since, Downey has thrown more money and resources at the problem. To no avail.
Last year, Ontario's Ombudsman Paul Dubé weighed in with his own report condemning the LTB as a "moribund" tribunal that fails the fairness test. Yet even after Downey painstakingly rebuilt the tribunal's ranks - cleaning up Mulroney's mess - the backlog has only grown worse.
How can this be? Did the system get gummed up by COVID-era disputes and delays?
To the contrary, "the huge increase in the backlog cannot be blamed on COVID-19," the report argues. Evictions were frozen, and there was a 40-per-cent drop in the number of new applications at the height of the pandemic.
Landlord and tenant disputes are about peoples' lives and livelihoods, their homes and their properties. They require good judgment from trained adjudicators, not cronyism and partisan pork barrelling.
"We don't replace our judges when we have a change in government and we shouldn't replace our tribunal adjudicators either," Tribunal Watch argues. It is calling on all major political parties "to support the depoliticization of the appointment and reappointment processes for Ontario tribunals."
At the Human Rights Tribunal, the caseload has doubled under the Ford government, even while the number of full decisions has declined.
It's not just the personnel, but the process. Part of the problem has been a shift, pre-pandemic, to remote hearings instead of in-person facilitators in regional offices that have since been closed down.
The result is more waiting time, down time, and conflict time. Too often, the process goes off the rails when landlords and tenants aren't meeting and mediating in the same room, face to face.
Electronic justice also falls flat when tenants (usually lacking legal representation) are left on hold on a phone line - often disconnected - while their landlord adversaries are lawyered up and teed up in front of their computers on high-speed broadband lines. A mismatch within a morass.
After decimating the ranks of Ontario's tribunals, and then stuffing them with favoured political appointments, the government can't keep up. Throwing money at the problem hasn't helped it catch up.
One alternative would be to create a special supplementary, temporary panel, working in parallel, to take the load off the existing LTB, Tribunal Watch suggests. Nothing else seems to have worked.
"Ford says he stands up for the little guy, but apparently not the single mom in substandard housing or the small landlord who is about to lose his property because the rent hasn't been paid for months," says Kathy Laird, a human rights lawyer who authored the latest report.
"The impact is enormous in terms of personal cost - lost housing, miserable living conditions for tenants, loss of income and property, especially for small landlords."
Day after day, the premier insists his top priority is providing people with shelter. Year after year, tenants and landlords are still waiting for justice.
CAPTION: The backlog at Ontario's Landlord and Tenant Board has surged to more than 53,000 cases, compared to barely 14,000 under the previous Liberal government, Martin Regg Cohn writes.Andrew Francis WallaceToronto Star File Photo
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Subject: Political appointments; Human rights; Pandemics; Tribunals & commissions; COVID-19; Tenants; Homeless people

Location: Ontario Canada; Canada

Document 203 of 500

Police increase presence around hospital: Debate rages over incident during rally outside Mount Sinai

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/police-increase-presence-around-hospital/docview/2926411101/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Police+increase+presence+around+hospital%3A+Debate+rages+over+incident+during+rally+outside+Mount+Sinai&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto police said Wednesday they have increased patrols of the area around Mount Sinai Hospital, after a man climbed up scaffolding at the hospital waving a Palestinian flag during a rally on Monday.
Leaders of the Toronto's largest hospitals say the protest ran the real risk of disrupting hospital operations and making staff and patients unsafe. In a letter published Tuesday night, hospital leaders wrote they are "continuing to work alongside local law enforcement as well as our local security teams" to ensure their buildings are safe.
Protesters, meanwhile, say their intentions have been misconstrued and are demanding comments accusing them of antisemitism be retracted.
Police said they are continuing to investigate the incident and could lay charges in the future. Police did not share whether they had any particular people they were investigating, including the man who climbed the hospital.
Debate rages over the incident at Mount Sinai. Some believe the historically Jewish hospital was "targeted" by protesters. But protester accounts indicate a lone, attention-seeking man scampered up the outside of the building and a crowd of onlookers paused to watch his ascent.
"We condemn the Canadian politicians incorrectly portraying the protest as targeting the hospital," reads a joint statement Wednesday from protest organizers, including a coalition of pro-Palestinian Jews.
This, they say, is patently untrue and "encourages a racist and limited view of Palestinian liberation movements."
Politicians of all stripes, including Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford, spoke out against the protesters. NDP leader Jagmeet Singh said the hospital was "targeted because of its ties to the Jewish community in Toronto," which he said was "not OK." Ontario Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie accused protesters of "infiltrating" the hospital, an "indefensible" and "antisemitic" act, she said. Mayor Olivia Chow called what happened "unacceptable" adding that "hate has no place in our city."
"There's this notion that anybody who is aligned with the (pro-Palestinian) movement needs to be feared because they might do something," said Israeli-Canadian activist and retired sociologist Sheryl Nestel.
"And underlying that atmosphere of fear in anticipation of things that have not happened is anti-Palestinian racism and Islamophobia. They are almost always characterized as violent, as antisemitic, as aggressive, as mobs who terrorize."
In videos circulating online of the incident at Mount Sinai, "spiderman4palestine," as he is known on social media, is seen climbing up on the hospital while a crowd outside chants for an "intefadeh," a call for an uprising for Palestinian liberation, which many Jews have seen as a call for violence against them.
Intefadeh, which comes from the Arabic word meaning "to shake off," refers to two Palestinian uprisings in the West Bank and Gaza Strip aimed at ending Israel's occupation and establishing an independent Palestinian state. Collectively, 5,000 Palestinians and some 1,400 Israelis were killed in the two conflicts.
There has been debate over where the line lies between criticism of Israel and antisemitism. There are those who say conflating anti-Zionism with antisemitism has created a chill on pro-Palestinian discourse in many areas of Canadian society in recent months. It has been widely accepted, though, that holding Jews or Jewish institutions in general responsible for Israel's actions is antisemitism.
CREDIT: Ben Cohen Toronto Star

Document 204 of 500

Liberals rebrand carbon tax as 'rebate': Ottawa trying to counter Tories' framing of levy as driver of inflation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-rebrand-carbon-tax-as-rebate/docview/2926406182/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+rebrand+carbon+tax+as+%27rebate%27%3A+Ottawa+trying+to+counter+Tories%27+framing+of+levy+as+driver+of+inflation&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal Liberals are attempting to redefine the rebate for the price on carbon as an affordability measure, as they roll out a rebrand of their politically contentious climate policy.
On Wednesday, a group of cabinet ministers announced the "Climate Action Incentive Payment" will be renamed the "Canada Carbon Rebate," following exclusive reporting from the Star that the federal government was considering a rebrand of the program.
"If we can speak the language that people speak, because people say the words 'carbon,' they say the words, 'rebate,' right? And if we can speak that language, that's important, so people understand what's going on here," said Labour and Seniors Minister Seamus O'Regan.
It's the latest attempt for the Liberals to defend - and reframe - their flagship climate policy in the face of repeated attacks by Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives, who have blamed the levy for Canadians' affordability woes.
Carbon pricing aims to encourage consumers and companies to produce less greenhouse gas emissions by making them more expensive. Revenue from the price on carbon has been returned to Canadians through a rebate since 2018, which Ottawa is now attempting to emphasize to counter the Conservatives' narrative.
That was clear on Wednesday when it was O'Regan - and not Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault - who led the announcement. O'Regan was flanked by Guilbeault, Revenue Minister Marie-Claude Bibeau, Rural Economic Development Minister Gudie Hutchings and several Atlantic MPs as he announced how much Canadians will receive in rebates in the next year once the price increases by $15 per tonne.
"In Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba, Ontario and New Brunswick, where the federal backstop is in place, families are taking home more money next fiscal year than they did in the previous one," O'Regan told reporters. In Ontario, a family of four will receive quarterly rebate payments totalling $1,120 this year.
"Without Canada's revenue neutral carbon pricing system, the cost to taxpayers and to the Canadian economy to achieve our climate goals would be far greater," said Guilbeault.
"Wildfires, floods, droughts, more intense storms - these all come with a substantial cost."
Whether this change in approach will affect Canadians' awareness of the rebates and their approval of the Liberals' carbon pricing program remains to be seen. Experts and political insiders who previously spoke to the Star said a rebrand may not be enough to fix the government's communications on carbon pricing.
At issue, they said, is a pattern of poor messaging that has not resonated with Canadians amid a cost-of-living crisis, and a Conservative opposition that has filled the vacuum and controlled the narrative.
"When the economy is in a difficult spot, it's really difficult to be able to talk about climate," Acadia University political scientist Alex Marland told the Star. He and others said nothing short of a full-on advertising push could win back the narrative on carbon pricing, which was a prominent policy for the Liberals in the two most recent election campaigns.
It's a strategy the Tories have used in their attempts to frame the price on carbon as a driver of inflation.
"Trudeau's carbon tax on the farmer who grows the food, and the trucker who ships the food, is a tax on all who buy the food. Now he wants to quadruple the tax," Poilievre says in a recent advertisement on Meta platforms.
Recent polling for the Star by Abacus Data suggests Canadians are split on whether carbon pricing is a good policy, even in Liberal strongholds such as Ontario and the Atlantic Provinces.
And while 72 per cent of those eligible for the rebate reported being aware of it, only 48 per cent knew it had to do with carbon pricing.
On Wednesday, the Liberals pushed back, touting carbon pricing as an affordability measure meant to help Canadians in addition to being a climate policy supported by scientists and economists.
"We need people to understand that when we talk about 'axe the tax,' we're talking about 'axe the rebates' and people are relying on that rebate," O'Regan said. "That rebate is $1,200 for a family of four in Newfoundland and Labrador, $600 for a single senior. For most people that I know, that is going to stuff that they need."
The federal government has been measuring Canadians' awareness of the previously named Climate Action Incentive Payment through its polling on Canadians' attitudes toward climate change since at least early 2023, documents tabled in Parliament show.
"The previous name was a bit difficult to understand and for many people to remember," Guilbeault said. "This will likely make it easier."
CREDIT: Mark Ramzy Toronto Star

Subject: Rebates; Canadians; Climate policy; Climate action; Environmental policy; Carbon; Climate change; Environmental tax

Document 205 of 500

Doug Ford's partisan ignorance has left 53,000 tenants and landlords dangling

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-partisan-ignorance-has-left-53-000/docview/2926402607/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+partisan+ignorance+has+left+53%2C000+tenants+and+landlords+dangling&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Amid the biggest housing and homelessness crisis in recent memory, with the premier promising to move heaven and earth (and the Greenbelt) to give people a roof over their head, his government is leaving tenants out in the cold.
Landlords, too.
The backlog at Ontario's Landlord and Tenant Board (LTB) has surged to more than 53,000 cases, compared to barely 14,000 under the previous Liberal government. And it shows no sign of abating, according to a new analysis by Tribunal Watch Ontario that documents a system out of control.
Doug Ford's Tories took power in 2018 with a hidden agenda to purge Ontario's tribunals of anyone inherited from the previous government - no matter their accumulated wisdom. Rather than approve the routine reappointments of seasoned adjudicators, each with expertise and experience, Ford's partisan and paranoid Progressive Conservative government rebuffed, rejected and refused as many as they could.
No matter how apolitical the appointments, almost all were suspect and stripped of their authority. Year after year, as the knowledge gap grew at the LTB, the waiting list exploded nearly fourfold -and the hardship for ordinary Ontarians mounted.
Today, a tenant facing a nightmarish maintenance dispute has to wait more than 14 months to have their case resolved, on average. That compares to a mere two months in 2018.
Yes, even the landlords who might be expected to be well served by Ford's Tories are left holding the bag. Getting an eviction order for nonpayment of rent used to take about a month under the previous Liberal government, but now takes nearly a year.
"There is little justice to be found for Ontarians who have to turn to Ontario's busiest tribunal," warns the report from Tribunal Watch. "The deplorable situation at the LTB is not getting better ... it is a dereliction of responsibility."
I first documented this injustice nearly four years ago, describing how the LTB and other tribunals were chronically short-staffed thanks to the self-inflicted wounds of the Ford government. To be fair, Attorney General Doug Downey inherited the chaos from his predecessor, Caroline Mulroney, who presided over the dismantling of this quasi-judiciary body in the early days of the Ford era at its worst.
In the years since, Downey has thrown more money and resources at the problem. To no avail.
Last year, Ontario's Ombudsman Paul Dubé weighed in with his own report condemning the LTB as a "moribund" tribunal that fails the fairness test. Yet even after Downey painstakingly rebuilt the tribunal's ranks - cleaning up Mulroney's mess - the backlog has only grown worse.
How can this be? Did the system get gummed up by COVID-era disputes and delays?
To the contrary, "the huge increase in the backlog cannot be blamed on COVID-19," the report argues. Evictions were frozen, and there was a 40-per-cent drop in the number of new applications at the height of the pandemic.
Landlord and tenant disputes are about peoples' lives and livelihoods, their homes and their properties. They require good judgment from trained adjudicators, not cronyism and partisan pork barrelling.
"We don't replace our judges when we have a change in government and we shouldn't replace our tribunal adjudicators either," Tribunal Watch argues. It is calling on all major political parties "to support the depoliticization of the appointment and reappointment processes for Ontario tribunals."
At the Human Rights Tribunal, the caseload has doubled under the Ford government, even while the number of full decisions has declined.
It's not just the personnel, but the process. Part of the problem has been a shift, pre-pandemic, to remote hearings instead of in-person facilitators in regional offices that have since been closed down.
The result is more waiting time, down time, and conflict time. Too often, the process goes off the rails when landlords and tenants aren't meeting and mediating in the same room, face to face.
Electronic justice also falls flat when tenants (usually lacking legal representation) are left on hold on a phone line - often disconnected - while their landlord adversaries are lawyered up and teed up in front of their computers on high-speed broadband lines. A mismatch within a morass.
After decimating the ranks of Ontario's tribunals, and then stuffing them with favoured political appointments, the government can't keep up. Throwing money at the problem hasn't helped it catch up.
One alternative would be to create a special supplementary, temporary panel, working in parallel, to take the load off the existing LTB, Tribunal Watch suggests. Nothing else seems to have worked.
"Ford says he stands up for the little guy, but apparently not the single mom in substandard housing or the small landlord who is about to lose his property because the rent hasn't been paid for months," says Kathy Laird, a human rights lawyer who authored the latest report.
"The impact is enormous in terms of personal cost - lost housing, miserable living conditions for tenants, loss of income and property, especially for small landlords."
Day after day, the premier insists his top priority is providing people with shelter. Year after year, tenants and landlords are still waiting for justice.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford greets members of the public following a press conference in Mississauga, Ont. on Feb. 13, 2024.After decimating the ranks of Ontario's tribunals, and then stuffing them with favoured political appointments, the Ford government can't keep up, Martin Regg Cohn writes.

Document 206 of 500

Feds eye birth control, diabetes coverage: Contraceptives could be part of Liberal-NDP pharmacare deal, sources say

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/feds-eye-birth-control-diabetes-coverage/docview/2926402275/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feds+eye+birth+control%2C+diabetes+coverage%3A+Contraceptives+could+be+part+of+Liberal-NDP+pharmacare+deal%2C+sources+say&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal Liberals are looking at covering the cost of birth control and diabetes drugs in a national pharmacare plan as part of steps to secure ongoing NDP support for their minority government.
According to multiple sources, the range of birth control coverage could be broad, possibly including contraceptive pills, injections, intrauterine devices and so-called "morning after" medications like "Plan B" that prevent a pregnancy from occurring.
The range of diabetic treatments under discussion is also potentially broad as officials assess the "fiscal hit" of covering different medications and devices such as insulin pumps and complex blood glucose monitoring systems. Yet two sources said the notion the government would cover Ozempic - an injectable medication sometimes prescribed for Type 2 diabetes but hyped as a weight-loss drug - is unlikely. One said Ozempic was raised as a "challenge" and that the government would not want to subsidize people taking it for purposes of vanity.
But sources with knowledge of the discussions, whom the Star is not identifying because they were not authorized to speak publicly, underscored that no decisions have yet been made, with two weeks of talks still ahead.
The negotiations are part of the Liberal-NDP governing agreement, which originally tasked the Liberals with passing a bill by the end of 2023 that would outline a pharmacare framework in Canada.
When talks stalled in December on the final shape of such a program - the New Democrats are demanding a single-payer model that the fiscally constrained Liberals are wary of backing - the NDP agreed to extend the deadline for introducing the legislation until March.
That compromise came with an additional demand that fell outside the scope of the agreement: that if the Liberals wanted more time, they needed to bring more to the table.
In that spirit, the Liberals are examining covering costs for contraception and certain diabetes medications, with the NDP pushing for coverage as broad as possible.
"I don't think it's clear yet within contraception - are we talking about just birth control pills? Are we talking about birth control pills and IUDs and injections and other forms of birth control?" a senior NDP source said.
The source said the New Democrats are pushing to see broad coverage for contraception and diabetes medication, but said the Liberals are trying to keep them as restricted as possible.
However, another senior source characterized the ongoing discussions differently, saying there is an effort to analyze the fiscal cost of the proposals. "All of this is very expensive," the source said.
The NDP source pointed to British Columbia, which already covers a wide range of prescription contraceptives under its publicly funded pharmacare program.
In Ottawa, Health Minister Mark Holland offered no details.
"No Canadian should have to choose between the prescription drugs they need and putting food on the table. We continue to negotiate and have productive conversations on pharmacare as we work towards the March 1 deadline," his office said in a written statement.
In the Liberals' view, the strategy around contraceptives would have the additional benefit of being aligned with Liberal political support for women's autonomy when it comes to their reproductive health decisions, and would highlight differences with Conservatives over the issue.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has endorsed a woman's right to make decisions about abortion, but several of his caucus members are more socially conservative on that issue.
The federal New Democrats have long called for free contraception in Canada, arguing that Prime Minister Justin Trudeau cannot call himself a feminist and a supporter of gender equality without increasing access to birth control.
For now, the New Democrats appear content with two classes of drugs covered as a starting point, although the source said discussions have included a number as high as five.
It is the party's expectation that progress will be seen on drug coverage before the next election - which could come as late as October 2025 - meaning the NDP will be looking to this year's federal budget to ensure that goal is met.
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh has signalled that if he were to quit the parliamentary alliance over the Liberals' handling of drug coverage, or its failure to commit to a single-payer plan in the coming legislation, he would consider slowing or blocking government legislation rather than triggering an election.
The Liberals have shied away from a single-payer plan, a model the parliamentary budget officer last year estimated would cost federal and provincial governments $11.2 billion in 2024-25, and up to $13.4 billion in 2027-28. (The PBO also found that cost savings on drug expenditures would reach $2.2 billion by 2027-28.)
It isn't clear which other classes of drugs the Liberals and NDP are discussing.
But on Wednesday, the Heart & Stroke Foundation and the Canadian Cancer Society urged the federal government to introduce national pharmacare "as soon as possible."
In a statement, they endorsed a phased-in approach that would emphasize coverage of life-saving, essential prescription drugs first, citing a key recommendation of the 2019 report on pharmacare by former Ontario health minister Dr. Eric Hoskins. Universal coverage of essential drugs would save the health-care system an average of $1,488 per patient per year by preventing unexpected trips to the hospital, they said.
"We're advocating for this fiscally responsible option which will reduce pressure on the health-care system, including by cutting costs," said Doug Roth, chief executive officer of the Heart & Stroke Foundation.
"You cannot put a price on a life. But if we put it in terms of dollars and cents alone, controlling a condition such as high blood pressure with key prescription medications is more cost-effective than the specialized care required for more serious health consequences such as a stroke or heart attack because someone could not afford their medication."
The two organizations commissioned a poll by Leger which found 22 per cent of Canadians report splitting pills, skipping doses, or not filling or renewing a prescription due to high drug costs.
It also found 10 per cent of Canadians with chronic conditions report ending up in the emergency room due to worsening health because they were unable to afford prescription medications.
The Leger poll sampled opinions of 2,048 adult Canadians through an online survey from Jan. 24 to 29. While an online poll cannot be considered truly random, a comparative random sample of this size would have a margin of error of 2.2 per cent, 19 times out of 20.
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles and Raisa Patel Toronto Star

Document 207 of 500

Foot-dragging delays justice

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/foot-dragging-delays-justice/docview/2926402254/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Foot-dragging+delays+justice&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The verdict is in - the federal government has done a lousy job at appointing judges and has no good excuse for not doing better.
In a judgment released this week, Justice Henry Brown, of the Federal Court, declared that the shortage of Superior Court and Federal Courts judges is at "both a crisis and critical level."
Sadly, that will not be news for those denied justice when the resulting court delays has pushed cases beyond 30-month time frame set by the Supreme Court and caused a stay of proceedings.
Star reporter Jacques Gallant has detailed serious criminal cases when that has happened, including the collapse of a human trafficking case this month, a firearms case and just before Christmas, a sexual assault case after the jury had already delivered its guilty verdict. The shortages also impact civil trials too.
Brown was ruling on a case brought by Yavar Hameed, a human rights lawyer in Ottawa, who represents low-income and marginalized individuals. He argued that his clients faced significant delays in court proceedings and sought a judicial order compelling the government to swiftly fill judicial vacancies.
In a judgment that is both a sharp rebuke and pointed call to do better, Brown agreed, ruling that the federal government must act to fill the vacancies.
Federal Justice Minister Arif Virani responded on X, saying there are more federally appointed judges now than ever before. And he claimed the federal Liberals were appointing judges at a faster pace than the previous Conservative government under Stephen Harper.
"We are making judicial appointments at the fastest pace in history. We made 100 judicial appointments last year, a number never attained by the Conservatives," Virani said.
Given that the Liberals have been in power for almost nine years, such partisan comparisons are tiresome and pointless. Virani can cheer the current pace of appointments but the tossed trials and concerns previously raised by the country's top judge show that the government has failed in its duty to keep the judicial benches adequately staffed.
Indeed, Brown's judgment reprints verbatim the letter that Richard Wagner, chief justice of the Supreme Court of Canada, sent to Justin Trudeau last May in which he said it was imperative the prime minister act to ensure appointments are made in a timely manner.
"The positions that have been left vacant are having significant impacts on the administration of justice, the operations of our courts and the health of our judges," Wagner wrote.
At the time of his letter, there were 85 vacancies and Wagner noted that some courts were dealing with vacancy rates as high as 15 per cent "for years now." Senior judicial positions, including the chief justice positions in Alberta, Ontario and Prince Edward Island, were being filled "at a very slow pace.
"No clear explanation justifies these delays," Wagner wrote, noting that judicial vacancies are predictable, especially those caused by retirements.
"In this context, these delays in appointments send a message that this is simply not a priority for the government," he stated.
Wagner's lengthy letter to Trudeau doesn't seem to have moved the government to action. Nine months later, the number of vacancies for federally appointed judges stands at 75, prompting Brown to conclude that the government ignored Wagner's concerns.
"With the greatest respect, the Court finds the Prime Minister and Minister of Justice are simply treading water" and in the process are failing those who have "unsuccessfully sought timely justice," Brown declared.
It's essential then that the government take Brown's ruling seriously. He did not set a number on what would be a reasonable level of vacancies, though he suggested mid-40s. Nor did he set a deadline. But he left no doubt he expects action.
"How long should it take to fill a sufficient number of vacancies? In the Court's view the answer is plain and obvious: these vacancies must be materially reduced within a reasonable time to a reasonable level," he said.
Virani can pat himself on the back all he wants on the government's record. But the evidence from the courts is clear and persuasive. Whatever the government has been doing, it's not enough. The federal Liberals need to get serious about filling judicial vacancies and ensuring the timely delivery of justice in Canada.
CREDIT: ;

Full text: Large majority
of Canadian immigrants stay
More than 15 per cent of immigrants leave Canada within 20 years of arriving, StatCan report finds, Feb. 4
Since when is 85 per cent bad? I am reading all the negative comments on immigrants to Canada leaving. Fifteen per cent over 20 years is nothing. That 85 per cent stayed is amazing to me. Why focus on 15 per cent leaving?
David Lambert, Aurora
Educate students about the harms
of pornography
Sex-ed should teach how to sext safely, study suggests, Feb. 7
Perhaps before teaching "safe sexting" in Ontario's Health and Physical Education curriculum, educators should research the association between sexting and maladaptive sexual development. Similarly, the relationship between pornography, unhealthy sexuality and relationships could be taught.
Brian O'Sullivan, Stouffville
Criminals must pay higher price for their crimes
Poilievre to promise harsher punishment for car thieves, Feb. 4
I was shocked to see the federal Conservative leader actually talk about policy instead of his usual attack dog from the bully pulpit stand.
It seems Pierre Poilievre is taking a page out of the Liberal 'soft on criminals' playbook. He says that if you steal an automobile for the third time (really, third time?) and it is part of organized crime, your slap on the wrist will be three years in jail. That would be more time than the current three months. I'm shocked that a Conservative would even bring this to our attention.
First and foremost, our vehicles are our most important possession outside of our homes. Sparing any thug whether it's their first offensive is just wrong. As a society we need to send a stern message. Vehicle theft is going to cost you jail time, starting with the first offence.
People have to feel safe in their vehicles and their homes, when these are violated, criminals must pay a price. The penalties for many crimes have not evolved with our times. The current lack of respect for the rule of law is very concerning. Whether it's 200+km/h speeds on our roads, drinking and driving, street racing, home invasions, vehicle thefts or hate crimes, the current judicial system is not working.
Mike Walker, Washago, Ont.
Singh must push for national pharmacare
Jagmeet Singh says he's getting 'a lot of heat' for working with the Liberals. Here's how he answers his critics, Feb. 6
NDP leader Jagmeet Singh really needs to continue insisting the government keep its promise to implement a national pharmacare plan.
It's terrible and expensive when a federal government repeatedly promises Canadians much-needed universal albeit-generic-brand medication coverage only to have the pharmaceutical industry typically react with successful threats to abandon their Canada-based R&D, et cetera, if the government went ahead with the "pharmacare" plan.
Such universal medication coverage would negatively affect the industry's superfluously plentiful profits. The profits would still be great, just not as great. Meantime, we continue to be the world's sole nation that has universal health care but no similar coverage of prescribed medication.
A 2019 Angus Reid study found that, over the previous year, due to medication unaffordability, almost a quarter of Canadians decided against filling a prescription or having one renewed. Resultantly, many low-income outpatients who cannot afford to fill their prescriptions end up back in the hospital system as a result, therefore costing far more for provincial and federal government health ministries than if the medication had been covered.
The Angus Reid study also found that about 90 per cent of Canadians - including three quarters of Conservative supporters specifically not known for supporting publicly-funded social programs - support a national pharmacare plan. Another 77 per cent believed this should be a high-priority matter for the federal government.
Ergo, in order for the industry to continue raking in huge profits, Canadians and their health, as both individual consumers and a taxpaying collective, must lose out big time. So, while we are envied abroad for our "universal" health-care system, full care seemingly still comes second to the big-profit interests of industry.
Frank Sterle Jr., White Rock, B.C.
PCs obstructing path toward
a green future
Minister to overrule Ontario Energy Board, says decision will raise cost of new homes, Dec. 22
Ontario's PC government has announced it's intent to bypass a decision made by the independent energy regulator, the Ontario Energy Board (OEB), against Enbridge's infrastructure expansion request. The OEB cited unsupportable projections by Enbridge that all new houses would become gas consumers and remain so over the next 40 years - an absurd unlikelihood given the clear science that these fossil fuels are accelerating deadly global climate collapse.
Reversing the OEB's decision is a calculated attack on Ontario consumers and the global public - adults, children, everyone. The OEB correctly concluded that removing the fossil fuel subsidy would encourage the use of electric heat pumps in new housing which would lower energy bills, improve air quality, reduce the pace of climate destabilization, accelerate energy investment transition, and provide much-needed energy efficient cooling in the summer months without the need to install expensive separate AC systems.
Taxpayers shouldn't be saddled with the costs - bad debt and increasingly deadly weather patterns - for fossil fuel assets certain to be stranded, obsolete, before mid-life.
With 1.5 million new homes set to be built by 2031, the Ford government's determination to see these homes connected to gas lines, will ratchet UP GHG emissions. In contrast, encouraging new homes to use heat pumps will help Ontario reduce emissions slowing our climate's degradation.
Ontario taxpayers should ponder why the government elected to safeguard our interests is actively obstructing the independent energy board's effort to save Ontarians' money and safeguard our families' futures.
D'Arcy McLenaghen, Toronto

Document 209 of 500

Battered by Pierre Poilievre, Liberals bet a new name will help them sell carbon pricing to inflation-weary Canadians

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/battered-pierre-poilievre-liberals-bet-new-name/docview/2926812864/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Battered+by+Pierre+Poilievre%2C+Liberals+bet+a+new+name+will+help+them+sell+carbon+pricing+to+inflation-weary+Canadians&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - The federal Liberals are attempting to redefine the rebate for the price on carbon as an affordability measure, as they roll out a rebrand of their politically contentious climate policy.On Wednesday, a group of cabinet ministers announced the "Climate Action Incentive Payment" will be renamed the "Canada Carbon Rebate," following exclusive reporting from the Star that the federal government was considering a rebrand of the program."If we can speak the language that people speak, because people say the words 'carbon,' they say the words, 'rebate,' right? And if we can speak that language, that's important, so people understand what's going on here," said Labour and Seniors Minister Seamus O'Regan. It's the latest attempt for the Liberals to defend - and reframe - their flagship climate policy in the face of repeated attacks by Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives, who have blamed the levy for Canadians' affordability woes. Carbon pricing aims to encourage consumers and companies to produce less greenhouse gas emissions by making them more expensive. Revenue from the price on carbon has been returned to Canadians through a rebate since 2018, which Ottawa is now attempting to emphasize to counter the Conservatives' narrative.That was clear on Wednesday when it was O'Regan - and not Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault - who led the announcement. O'Regan was flanked by Guilbeault, Revenue Minister Marie-Claude Bibeau, Rural Economic Development Minister Gudie Hutchings and several Atlantic MPs as he announced how much Canadians will receive in rebates in the next year once the price increases by $15 per tonne."In Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba, Ontario and New Brunswick, where the federal backstop is in place, families are taking home more money next fiscal year than they did in the previous one," O'Regan told reporters. In Ontario, a family of four will receive quarterly rebate payments totalling $1,120 this year."Without Canada's revenue neutral carbon pricing system, the cost to taxpayers and to the Canadian economy to achieve our climate goals would be far greater," said Guilbeault."Wildfires, floods, droughts, more intense storms - these all come with a substantial cost."Whether this change in approach will affect Canadians' awareness of the rebates and their approval of the Liberals' carbon pricing program remains to be seen. Experts and political insiders who previously spoke to the Star said a rebrand may not be enough to fix the government's communications on carbon pricing.At issue, they said, is a pattern of poor messaging that has not resonated with Canadians amid a cost-of-living crisis, and a Conservative opposition that has filled the vacuum and controlled the narrative."When the economy is in a difficult spot, it's really difficult to be able to talk about climate," Acadia University political scientist Alex Marland told the Star. He and others said nothing short of a full-on advertising push could win back the narrative on carbon pricing, which was a prominent policy for the Liberals in the two most recent election campaigns.It's a strategy the Tories have used in their attempts to frame the price on carbon as a driver of inflation."Trudeau's carbon tax on the farmer who grows the food, and the trucker who ships the food, is a tax on all who buy the food. Now he wants to quadruple the tax," Poilievre says in a recent advertisement on Meta platforms.Recent polling for the Star by Abacus Data suggests Canadians are split on whether carbon pricing is a good policy, even in Liberal strongholds such as Ontario and the Atlantic Provinces. And while 72 per cent of those eligible for the rebate reported being aware of it, only 48 per cent knew it had to do with carbon pricing.On Wednesday, the Liberals pushed back, touting carbon pricing as an affordability measure meant to help Canadians in addition to being a climate policy supported by scientists and economists."We need people to understand that when we talk about 'axe the tax,' we're talking about 'axe the rebates' and people are relying on that rebate," O'Regan said. "That rebate is $1,200 for a family of four in Newfoundland and Labrador, $600 for a single senior. For most people that I know, that is going to stuff that they need."The federal government has been measuring Canadians' awareness of the previously named Climate Action Incentive Payment through its polling on Canadians' attitudes toward climate change since at least early 2023, documents tabled in Parliament show."The previous name was a bit difficult to understand and for many people to remember," Guilbeault said. "This will likely make it easier."Following the announcement, Poilievre and the Conservatives were once again on the attack in the House of Commons as Prime Minister Justin Trudeau promoted the rebate."The prime minister cannot defend his policy, so he is changing the name," Poilievre said. "How much did he spend on consultants to come up with the new name?"
CAPTION: Labour and Seniors Minister Seamus O'Regan speaks to reporters in the foyer of the House of Commons on Feb. 14, 2024.

Document 210 of 500

Ford government facing lawsuit over expansion of Pickering care home where dozens died during COVID

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-facing-lawsuit-over-expansion/docview/2926264201/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+facing+lawsuit+over+expansion+of+Pickering+care+home+where+dozens+died+during+COVID&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A health advocacy coalition and the daughter of a deceased long-term-care resident have launched a court challenge against the Ministry of Long-term Care's approval of the expansion of a Pickering home where the Canadian military reported disturbing conditions during the pandemic.
The challenge announced Tuesday calls for a judicial review of the ministry's decision to approve an 87-bed expansion of Orchard Villa long-term-care home and a new 30-year licence for its parent company, Southbridge Care Homes.
The home would also redevelop 131 of its 233 existing long-term care beds.
Orchard Villa was one of five homes that the military entered in April 2020, when it reported inadequate staff training and resident care within the facility. According to the recent court filing, 206 of Orchard Villa's 233 residents had COVID by spring 2020. During the first wave, 71 residents died at the home.
Orchard Villa continues to face reports of non-compliances under the Fixing Long-Term Care Act, as recently as January.
"I'm at a loss for words as to how this home can be granted a licence to go ahead with a new build," said Catherine Parkes, whose father died at Orchard Villa almost four years ago.
In April 2020, the military was called in to help Orchard Villa and four other care homes handle COVID outbreaks. The Canadian Armed Forces later released a scathing, 23-page report about conditions in some of the facilities.
The report said, among many problems noted, patients at Orchard Villa were left in soiled diapers, caregivers were burned out and the facility had cockroaches and flies.
"It's not like the military report was an aberration," said Natalie Mehra, executive director of the Ontario Health Coalition, during the news conference Tuesday, pointing to ongoing issues found in inspections at Orchard Villa. She noted Ontario law says long-term-care licences can only be issued to those whose past conduct related to the operation of a care home offers reason to believe the home will not be run in a way that is "prejudicial to the health, safety or welfare of its residents."
In an email statement, the ministry said the proposed Orchard Villa project had to undergo "a rigorous undertaking process" to prove it can meet provincial standards under the Fixing Long-term Care Act.
"All proposed license extensions must undergo a rigorous undertaking process to show they can meet these new high standards, as is the case with the proposed Pickering development," said the statement, attributed to spokesperson Daniel Strauss.
"Hundreds of people" are currently waiting for care in Pickering, the statement added, noting the province has invested $6.4 billion to build 58,000 new and upgraded long-term-care spaces.
"Our government believes in building Ontario's long-term-care capacity, not reducing it," said the email.
A Jan. 18 report from an inspection at Orchard Villa found the home continued to face problems meeting provincial regulations, including minimizing the risk of harm to residents, reporting matters appropriately, ensuring staff use safe transferring and positioning devices and techniques to help residents, among others.
The Star contacted Orchard Villa by phone and through a contact form on its website, as well as Southbridge by phone and email. Neither responded to a request for comment Tuesday afternoon.
"I acknowledge it was a tough time throughout COVID not only here, but around the world," said Premier Doug Ford in a separate news conference on Tuesday. "But we've corrected those problems and are going to continue improving the processes."
Ford noted the government has hired more nursing home inspectors and is expanding long-term-care homes to meet growing demand from aging seniors.
"There was a lot of problems with many homes across the province. We corrected those problems. We have more inspectors going in. We're modernizing all the facilities."
In January, the ministry launched a 10-person unit to investigate long-term-care homes facing allegations of resident abuse or neglect.
The CEO of a national seniors' advocacy organization also raised questions about the ministry's decision.
"I was frankly surprised that Orchard Villa was chosen for investment and expansion based on their problematic history," said Laura Tamblyn Watts of CanAge, noting the home was highlighted in recent years as one of the homes "where some of the worst outcomes happened."
"I would like to know more about why they were chosen for a renewal and expansion," she said. "It is publicly a problematic signal."
At the same time, she hoped the investment would help transform care in the home. While Tamblyn Watts said Ontario has strong long-term-care laws, the ministry needs to ensure that there are substantive consequences to failures to meet standards - something she said the province's new investigative unit could potentially help with - as well as investment in staffing.
She added that she's surprised to see investment in building larger homes when it's clear that smaller homes tend to provide better quality of care.
"People should feel confident that if their loved ones need to go into care, that this isn't a place where they're going to be harmed, this is a place where they're going to be helped," she said.
With files from Rob Ferguson
CAPTION: The Ontario Ministry of Long-term Care said the proposed Orchard Villa project had to undergo "a rigorous undertaking process" to prove it can meet provincial standards under the Fixing Long-term Care Act.

Subject: Press conferences; Long term health care; Judicial reviews

Title: Ford government facing lawsuit over expansion of Pickering care home where dozens died during COVID

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Document 211 of 500

Backlash over pro-Palestinian protesters outside Mount Sinai Hospital in Toronto

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/backlash-over-pro-palestinian-protesters-outside/docview/2926178837/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Backlash+over+pro-Palestinian+protesters+outside+Mount+Sinai+Hospital+in+Toronto&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford have spoken out against actions outside Mount Sinai Hospital on Monday night, when a man climbed atop scaffolding and held aloft a Palestinian flag during apro-Palestinian protest.
"I strongly condemn this display of antisemitism," Trudeau tweeted Tuesday. "In Toronto and across Canada, we stand with Jewish communities against this hate."
Mount Sinai Hospital has deep ties to the local Jewish community and has the Star of David in its logo. Jewish groups told the Star they believe the hospital was targeted by the protesters because of this.
Protesters told the Star this is not the case and accused the PM and others of trying to discredit their pro-Palestinian advocacy. The man acted independently, they say, and without malicious intent. Moreover, they say the hospital was not targeted. The man who climbed it regularly attends protests dressed as Spider-Man and scales structures. Protest organizers say this was part of his schtick, not an indictment of the hospital.
The protest began at the Israeli Consulate at Yonge and Bloor Street on Monday evening and was organized in opposition to Israel's bombardment of Rafah city in the Gaza Strip on Sunday night.
Protesters were passing by Mount Sinai on the way to the U.S. Consulate near Queen and University Avenue. Pro-Palestinian protests have taken this route dozens of times in recent months.
In videos circulating on online, "spiderman4palestine," as he is known on social media, is seen climbing up on the hospital while a crowd outside chants for an "intefadeh," a call for an uprising for Palestinian liberation, which many Jews have seen as a call for violence against them.
Intefadeh, which comes from the Arabic word meaning "to shake off," refers to two Palestinian uprisings in the West Bank and Gaza Strip aimed at ending Israel's occupation and establishing an independent Palestinian state. Collectively, 5,000 Palestinians and some 1,400 Israelis were killed in the two conflicts.
Jewish groups and politicians said they believe protesters picketed the hospital and that it was chosen because it is a historically Jewish organization.
"It looked like there was a large crowd that stopped specifically outside this hospital in addition to the person climbing this specific hospital," said Noah Shack, vice-president for countering antisemitism and hate with the UJA Federation of Greater Toronto.
"That seems to me to be a clear targeting of this hospital. Regardless of the intent of the individual, who climbed up to plant the Palestinian flag, the impact is pretty clear."
Gur Tsabar, a protest organizer with pro-Palestinian Jewish coalition Jews Say No to Genocide, said protesters stopped outside the hospital to gawk at the man who climbed the scaffolding, who has become a "fixture" at protests, whom people "love to see climb on everything." He called accusations of antisemitism "baseless."
"Accusations of antisemitism because a protester climbed different buildings along the rally route to proudly display a Palestinian flag is ridiculous."
There has been widespread debate over where the line lies between criticism of Israel and antisemitism. There are those who say conflating anti-Zionism with antisemitism has created a chill on pro-Palestinian discourse in many areas of Canadian society in recent months. It has been widely accepted, though, that holding Jews or Jewish institutions in general responsible for Israel's actions is antisemitism.
Rafah was the last vestige of civilian safety in the enclave and is where most of Gaza's civilians are sheltering. Israel has said it is preparing a ground invasion of Rafah. Canada has expressed concern, noting that there is nowhere left for civilians to flee the conflict.
"The civilians in Rafah are people - they are mothers, children. They have names, stories - and they exist," said Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly Monday. "Asking them to move again is unacceptable. They have nowhere to go. We need a hostage deal, for more humanitarian aid to enter Gaza and we need a sustainable ceasefire."
Speaking at a gas station in Port Credit Tuesday morning, Ford told reporters protesters should have picked a more traditional spot for their rally, such as City Hall or Queen's Park.
"I think it's absolutely terrible," he said. "The federal government came out with a law that you cannot protest on hospitals, folks, get some decency and common sense. These hospitals are there to save lives. You know, Mount Sinai did everything they could to save my brother's life."
Ford was referring to Bill C-3, an amendment to the Criminal Code made in 2021 in response to anti-vaccination protests that created new offences for intimidating health-care workers or obstructing access to health-care services.
Toronto police told the Star they are investigating the incident and will increase police presence in the area going forward.
"Interfering with the operations of a hospital is not acceptable," TPS spokesperson Stephanie Sayer told the Star in an email. "The Toronto Police Service is investigating several incidents that occurred in front of Mount Sinai Hospital and along the demonstration route."
Sayer said police did not arrest anyone Monday, possibly because officers deemed it unsafe to do so due to the large crowd, but charges could be laid at a later date, as police have done with some protesters previously. She encouraged anyone who witnessed the incident by the hospital to contact police.
Mount Sinai did not return a request for comment by publication time. The identity of the man who climbed atop Mount Sinai Hospital is not known. He did not respond to attempts to reach him through other protesters.
Emergency physician Dr. Raghu Venugopal, who practises across the street from Mount Sinai at Toronto General Hospital, voiced his displeasure with the protest on social media Tuesday.
"Ontario's hospitals are places of healing, science and with no bias toward race, religion or ethic background," he wrote. "I respect our citizen's right to protest, but never where the ill, injured and distressed seek medical care."
CAPTION: "Palestinian Spider-Man," or spiderman4palestine on social media, has become a "fixture" at pro-Palestinian protests in Toronto.Protestors block Bloor and Yonge intersection as hundreds attend a protest against the bombing of Rafah last night, where the vast majority of civilians in Gaza are sheltering.A cyclist blocks Bloor Street at Yonge with his bike."Palestinian Spider-Man," or spiderman4palestine on social media, has become a "fixture" at pro-Palestinian protests in Toronto for climbing up various structures. On Monday, he was widely condemned for climbing up a historically Jewish hospital, Mount Sinai.Protesters brought signs and chants to Yonge and Bloor on Monday.

Subject: Antisemitism; Jewish people; Criminal investigations; Climbing; Demonstrations & protests; Health care; Diplomatic & consular services; Social networks; Truces & cease fires

Business indexing term: Subject: Social networks

Location: Gaza Strip; Canada; Israel

Title: Backlash over pro-Palestinian protesters outside Mount Sinai Hospital in Toronto

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Feb 14, 2024

Section: news/gta

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Document 212 of 500

Unions warn of more labour turmoil in the coming years: Almost 15% of all workers in Ontario will see contracts expire by 2026

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/unions-warn-more-labour-turmoil-coming-years/docview/2925929214/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Unions+warn+of+more+labour+turmoil+in+the+coming+years%3A+Almost+15%25+of+all+workers+in+Ontario+will+see+contracts+expire+by+2026&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Following a year of high-profile strikes and wage gains, Canada should brace for more turmoil and work stoppages this year in both the public and private sectors, labour experts and unions say.
"Unionized workers are demonstrating a greater willingness to strike post-pandemic," said Larry Savage, a professor of labour studies at Brock University. "The combination of high inflation, low unemployment and the cost-of-living crisis have created conditions for heightened union militancy."
According to a Scotiabank report published in January, "strikes and aggressive wage settlements will persist," as some one million workers in Ontario alone - almost 15 per cent of all workers - will see their collective bargaining agreements expire between 2024 and 2026.
Expect the heat to rise as negotiations ramp up or continue for Air Canada pilots and flight attendants, Via Rail workers, LCBO workers and more than 70,000 health care workers.
Here's where major negotiations stand so far:
LCBO: Expect a 'significant battle'
The Liquor Control Board of Ontario's contract expires March 31. The Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU) - which represents around 10,000 LCBO workers in retail and warehouses - delivered a notice to bargain in January.
"We have hundreds of organizers across the province, in retail and warehouses, gearing up for what we know is going to be, a significant battle there," said OPSEU president J.P. Hornick. "They also have their wage reopener after Bill 124 has been found for the second time, to be unconstitutional."
Ontario's Bill 124, which capped public sector salary hikes to one per cent a year for three years, was found by the Court of Appeal for Ontario on Monday to have violated collective bargaining rights for workers, including nurses and teachers.
Unions hailed the ruling as a huge victory for workers' rights with Premier Doug Ford's government announcing it would repeal the law immediately.
Hornick said a major goal for OPSEU is to fight back against Ford's efforts to privatize the LCBO and alcohol sales.
"What we've seen with Ford is these attempts to ... keep nibbling around the edges of all of the public services," Hornick said.
Health care: 'A crisis unfolding'
Ontario health-care workers are already at the bargaining table and have been for several months.
Three unions - CUPE's Ontario Council of Hospital Unions, SEIU Healthcare and Unifor - are "jointly in bargaining as we speak," CUPE Ontario president Fred Hahn said. Together they represent more than 70,000 hospital workers in Ontario.
"There's a crisis unfolding in our health-care system," Hahn said.
The tri-union coalition is urging a five per cent funding increase beyond inflation from the Ford government to establish fair compensation, which they say is essential for recruiting thousands of workers and expanding hospital beds amid a 10 per cent staff turnover rate exacerbated by funding cuts and wage restraints.
The bargaining is ongoing, Hahn said.
Planes and trains:
'Big year for rail agreements'
CUPE also has an agreement with Air Canada flight attendants, which expires in 2025, but bargaining is expected to start this year.
"Our Air Canada members are just finishing a very long collective agreement," Hahn said, adding that the previous collective agreement was a decade long.
More than 10,000 Air Canada flight attendants represented by CUPE are seeking better wages and compensation for unpaid work according to Hahn.
"Flight attendants are only paid for the time that the aircraft is in the air," Hahn said. "If a flight is delayed by four hours, that means it's four hours of their day" that goes unpaid.
Air Canada pilots, represented by the Air Line Pilots Association (ALPA), are also in bargaining, calling for better pay and working conditions. Air Canada pilots last May "invoked a clause" to end their 10-year collective agreement a year early and initiate bargaining a new one over the summer. Since landing on a 10-year collective agreement in 2014, the pilots have received a two per cent pay hike each year.
Bargaining will likely continue until the summer, said Tim Perry the Canadian president of ALPA.
"We've agreed with the Air Canada management team to engage a private mediator," Perry said. "That phase will last until at least June."
Unifor will also be bargaining with Via Rail this year with some 2,400 workers' contracts expiring at the end of 2024.
"This will be a big year for rail agreements," Unifor's national president Lana Payne said in a previous interview.
Telecoms and media:
Members 'thrown out of work'
Payne also has her sights set on Canada's telecommunication and media sectors. Unifor Local 87-M represents media workers across southern Ontario, including the Toronto Star.
Bell Canada's contract with Unifor, representing nearly 4,000 crafts and services employees, is set to expire on Nov. 30. This will follow Bell Canada's announcement in February that it will slash 4,800 jobs across the country, it's largest restructuring in almost 30 years.
"Executives and shareholders are doing just fine while our members are being thrown out of work, including, once again, in the media," Payne, whose union represents 800 of the workers being laid off, said at the time.
Negotiations are also underway between the Canadian Media Guild (CMG) and the CBC "as we seek a deal before the current collective agreement expires on March 31, 2024," the CMG said in statement in January. The CMG represents some 4,000 employees at the CBC.
With files from
The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Ghada Alsharif Toronto Star With files from The Canadian Press

Subject: Agreements; Presidents; Strikes; Health care; Employees; Collective bargaining; Wages & salaries

Business indexing term: Subject: Strikes Employees Collective bargaining Wages & salaries

Document 213 of 500

Ford vows Belleville aid amid overdose crisis

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-vows-belleville-aid-amid-overdose-crisis/docview/2925929198/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+vows+Belleville+aid+amid+overdose+crisis&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An eastern Ontario city dealing with an acute overdose crisis will soon be getting provincial funding, Premier Doug Ford said Tuesday while also pledging to discuss longer-term solutions for the community.
Ford said he's going to sit down with Neil Ellis, the mayor of Belleville, Ont., to talk about ways to address the problem in the city, including through supports such as a community health hub.
"We're going to try to support them," Ford said.
"They need an influx of money right away. We're going to get that done, and then we're going to sit down and talk to them about building."
Belleville declared a state of emergency last week after a spate of overdoses in its downtown core overwhelmed first responders.
On Monday, the mayor specifically asked for funding to expand a social and health services hub, saying it needed $2 million in provincial funds to move the project forward.
Belleville also wants a detox centre to help in its ongoing battle with toxic street drugs. Ford said the province would be helping the city of about 55,000 residents.
"I always believe in the rehabilitation centres and supporting communities," Ford said, adding that police needed to pursue drug dealers distributing toxic drugs.
"We need to catch them, we need to throw them in jail and that's exactly what we're going to do."
Last week in Belleville, emergency crews responded to 17 overdoses in just 24 hours, and the majority of them occurred within a two-hour window on Feb. 6.
The sudden and overwhelming demand for ambulances and opioid overdose-reversing naloxone stretched emergency services to their "breaking point," officials said.
The city needs a well-staffed location where homeless people can get food, use a shower and access health care and substance-use support, officials said.
The opioid crisis has gripped the province for years and deaths have nearly doubled since 2019, with a significant spike since the pandemic began in 2020.
Data from Ontario's Office of the Chief Coroner shows fentanyl, a powerful opioid, contributes to the majority of opioid deaths in the province.
Last week, the coroner released data that showed a nine per cent decrease in opioid toxicity deaths in the third quarter of 2023 compared with the second quarter of that year, although deaths are 64 per cent higher than in 2019.
On average, 216 people died from an opioid overdose each month in Ontario in 2023.
Some medical professionals and advocates have called on the province to provide a "safe supply" of prescription opioids as a solution to the toxic drugs found on the streets.
CREDIT: The Canadian Press

Document 214 of 500

PM condemns actions near hospital: Trudeau calls out 'display of antisemitism' during pro-Palestinian protest outside Mount Sinai

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pm-condemns-actions-near-hospital/docview/2925929019/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PM+condemns+actions+near+hospital%3A+Trudeau+calls+out+%27display+of+antisemitism%27+during+pro-Palestinian+protest+outside+Mount+Sinai&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/
