Document 215 of 500

Province to scrap renewal of licence plates: Registration will be automatic for those 'in good standing'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-scrap-renewal-licence-plates/docview/2925928539/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+to+scrap+renewal+of+licence+plates%3A+Registration+will+be+automatic+for+those+%27in+good+standing%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford will eliminate the need to renew licence plates after the Star revealed there are more than one million expired plates on Ontario roads.
"We're getting rid of that totally - registering your vehicle," Ford told reporters Tuesday at a Mississauga gas station, where he also announced planned legislation designed to thwart a future government from imposing a provincial carbon tax.
"We did the first step, getting rid of the sticker (in 2022)," the premier said at a campaign-style event where he attacked newly elected Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, who stepped down as Mississauga mayor last month.
"Now we're getting rid of the re-registration. They'll be automatically re-registered so people won't have to worry about that at all," he said, adding details would be unveiled later this week.
"We're going to make sure that's very soon, extremely soon."
Dakota Brasier, senior media adviser to Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria, said "the process will automatically renew for those in good standing."
But motorists who owe traffic fines or unpaid tolls would not be renewed until those are paid, she said.
Scofflaws will be notified by mail or via email or text that they must pay their penalties or their plates will not be validated.
That means municipalities should not be shortchanged on revenue from unpaid parking tickets.
As first disclosed Monday by the Star, there were exactly 1,015,139 expired plates in the province last month. That means about seven per cent of the 13.6 million plates on Ontario roads have not been renewed by forgetful vehicle owners - and face Highway Traffic Act fines of up to $1,000.
Ford scrapped the annual renewal fee of up to $120 before the 2022 election, leading many motorists to assume they no longer needed to keep their plates up to date.
According to the Ontario Provincial Police, charges have soared since the paid renewal stickers were eliminated two years ago.
Last year, there were 15,631 tickets issued to motorists lacking a "currently validated permit for the vehicle," the OPP said.
That compared with 5,341 charges in 2022 and 1,556 in 2021, although the COVID-19 pandemic that kept Ontarians off roads and highways could have contributed to those lower violations.
Removing the renewal charge costs the treasury more than $1 billion in lost revenue annually.
Sarkaria's office has been urging motorists to check to see if their plates are valid at: ontario.ca/page/renew-your-licence-plate.
Those can be renewed online until the new measures take effect later this year.
All passenger vehicles under 3,000 kilograms - including cars, SUVs and vans - are covered under the policy.
Validation stickers are currently still required for heavy commercial vehicles, like large trucks and snowplows.
Because many motorists still have their old 2022 renewal stickers on their plates, there have been instances of drivers getting tickets out of province because local police believe the plates have expired.
The Canadian Automobile Association has recommended motorists keep a printout of proof of renewal, although that is not legally required.
Ford blurted out the looming plate change at a press conference where he touted forthcoming legislation to put carbon pricing on the front-burner at Queen's Park.
Under the proposed bill that will be tabled when the legislature resumes next Tuesday, any future government would have to hold a referendum before imposing any carbon tax or cap-and-trade system.
It would not impact federal carbon pricing measures.
Ironically, Ontarians did not have to pay the federal levy until Ford cancelled the province's cap-and-trade agreement with Quebec and California upon winning power in 2018.
That exposed Ontario taxpayers to the federal carbon scheme.
Speaking to the Star editorial board on Tuesday, New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles said the premier's referendum proposal is a bid to shift the spotlight from controversies like the ongoing $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal and the surprise flip-flop on the Bill 124 wage cap.
"I absolutely think that things like the announcement today are just attempts to distract from whatever the latest thing is - like their failure to continue to suppress wages of workers in Bill 124," said Stiles.
"And (the Greenbelt issue) is not gone; there's a criminal investigation underway," she said, referring to the RCMP probe.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson and Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau

Document 216 of 500

Ottawa must act fast to fill vacancies, judge says: Ruling blasts feds for 'appalling crisis' delaying Canadians' access to justice

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ottawa-must-act-fast-fill-vacancies-judge-says/docview/2925928517/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+must+act+fast+to+fill+vacancies%2C+judge+says%3A+Ruling+blasts+feds+for+%27appalling+crisis%27+delaying+Canadians%27+access+to+justice&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and his justice minister have "failed" Canadians and caused an "untenable and appalling crisis" by not appointing enough judges, the Federal Court has said in a damning judgment.
As the number of judicial vacancies remains very high across Canada, Federal Court Justice Henry Brown declared Tuesday that the federal government must appoint more people to the bench in a reasonable time to finally get this "critical situation" under control.
"Very unfortunately, the court has no reason to expect the situation will change without judicial intervention," Brown said.
The decision comes as serious criminal cases have collapsed due to delays caused by the lack of judges at the Superior Court in Toronto. Meanwhile, it's taking longer and longer to schedule trials on the civil side of the court - which handles all lawsuits such as personal injury and medical malpractice matters - leaving Ontarians with serious health and financial issues waiting years for access to justice.
Judicial appointments to provincial superior courts and courts of appeal are made by the federal cabinet - formally by the Governor General - on the recommendation of the minister of justice, currently Arif Virani. The prime minister is responsible for recommending the appointments of the chief justices and associate chief justices of those courts.
The federal Liberals have been criticized for years for their slow pace at filling judicial vacancies, while the reasons for the backlog have long been unclear.
The government did not provide an explanation or justification to Brown.
"With the greatest respect, the court finds the prime minister and minister of justice are simply treading water," Brown wrote.
"And with the greatest respect, they have also failed all those who rely on them for the timely exercise of their powers in relation to filling these vacancies. Also failed are all those who have unsuccessfully sought timely justice in the Superior Courts and Federal Courts across Canada," Brown wrote.
There are currently about 73 judicial vacancies across Canada, including 21 in the Superior Court throughout Ontario. Brown declared that the government must appoint judges "within a reasonable time" after a vacancy arises. He pointed out that in many cases, the government is aware months in advance of a judge's pending retirement and of the need to fill that position.
Should the government fail to comply, the judge left the door open to revisit the issue and implement specific timelines for appointments.
Asked about the number of vacancies, Virani told reporters in Ottawa on Tuesday he's "working to get it down as fast as possible." He said he was reviewing Brown's decision, but did not respond as to whether the government will appeal.
He argued that the government has appointed judges at record speed, and that Brown failed to mention that the Liberals have added around 115 new judicial positions across Canada over the last few years.
His office has previously said that the overall vacancy number "has only seemingly been mildly reduced" due to a high number of judges going part-time or retiring.
"This is my top priority as minister of justice ... appointing judges of the highest calibre who also reflect the diversity of this country," Virani said Tuesday.
The Prime Minister's Office deferred to Virani when asked for comment.
Conservative justice critic Rob Moore said in a statement that the government has failed to take its job seriously.
"The Liberal government's complete disregard for our justice system has brought us to this crisis point," he said.
Tuesday's decision stems from a legal challenge brought by Ottawa-based human rights lawyer Yavar Hameed, who argued that the government's slow pace in appointing more judges was delaying or denying justice to Canadians. He stated he had experienced "significant delays" in court while representing vulnerable clients, including the plaintiff in a workplace sexual harassment lawsuit whose trial was rescheduled due to the lack of judges.
"This litigation, and the issue of judicial appointments, is not some sort of theoretical issue; it is primarily about vulnerable people who need to use the courts," Hameed's lawyer, Nicholas Pope, told the Star. "When they can't use the courts, then there's no justice, and they become the casualties in all of this."
Brown accepted as expert evidence a letter sent to Trudeau last May by the chief justice of Canada and chair of the Canadian Judicial Council, Richard Wagner, who said some courts have had to deal with a 10 per cent to 15 per cent vacancy rate for years and it was impacting the timely hearing of cases. Wagner told Trudeau at the time that the "government's inertia regarding vacancies and the absence of satisfactory explanations for these delays are disconcerting."
Brown said it's his expectation that his declaration will bring the number of vacancies nationally back down to the mid-40s, the number that existed in 2016 before the Liberals revamped the judicial appointment process.
"In this manner, the court expects the untenable and appalling crisis and critical judicial vacancy situation, found by this court as identified by the chief justice and Canadian Judicial Council, will be resolved," Brown wrote.
He added that Wagner and the judicial council "have serious concerns that without concrete efforts to remedy the situation, Canada's federal judiciary will soon reach a point of no return in several jurisdictions. The consequences will make headlines and have serious repercussions on our democracy and on all Canadians."
The recent criminal cases to collapse at Toronto's Superior Court include a human trafficking case, gun charges and a prosecution for an alleged sex assault, with the judges in each one citing delays caused by the systemic vacancies.
The Supreme Court has ruled trials must be completed within a strict time limit or be tossed for violating the right of the accused to a trial within a reasonable time.
The president of the Criminal Lawyers' Association, Boris Bytensky, said the federal and provincial governments must properly resource the courts to cut down on delay, which "includes, but is not limited to, the timely appointment of judges." While the federal government appoints higher court judges, the provinces are responsible for funding the rest of the system, including hiring most prosecutors and court staff, building courthouses and providing money for legal aid.
On the civil side, litigants have seen their trials cancelled at the last minute due to the lack of judges, said Laurie Tucker, president of the Ontario Trial Lawyers Association, which represents lawyers acting for plaintiffs.
"It causes additional stress to the litigants, and financially it's really challenging," she said, adding: "This ruling is timely and important."
CREDIT: Jacques Gallant Courts and Justice Reporter

Document 217 of 500

'I'm at a loss for words': Ford government faces lawsuit over expansion of Pickering home where dozens died during COVID-19

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/im-at-loss-words/docview/2925928369/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27I%27m+at+a+loss+for+words%27%3A+Ford+government+faces+lawsuit+over+expansion+of+Pickering+home+where+dozens+died+during+COVID-19&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A health advocacy coalition and the daughter of a deceased long-term-care resident have launched a court challenge against the Ministry of Long-Term Care's approval of the expansion of a Pickering home where the Canadian military reported disturbing conditions during the pandemic.
The challenge announced Tuesday calls for a judicial review of the ministry's decision to approve an 87-bed expansion of Orchard Villa long-term-care home and a new 30-year licence for its parent company, Southbridge Care Homes.
The home would also redevelop 131 of its 233 existing long-term-care beds.
Orchard Villa was one of five homes that the military entered in April 2020, when it reported inadequate staff training and resident care within the facility. According to the recent court filing, 206 of Orchard Villa's 233 residents had COVID by spring 2020. During the first wave, 71 residents died at the home.
Orchard Villa continues to face reports of non-compliances under the Fixing Long-Term Care Act, as recently as January.
"I'm at a loss for words as to how this home can be granted a licence to go ahead with a new build," said Catherine Parkes, whose father died at Orchard Villa almost four years ago.
In April 2020, the military was called in to help Orchard Villa and four other care homes handle COVID outbreaks. The Canadian Armed Forces later released a scathing, 23-page report about conditions in some of the facilities.
The report said, among many problems noted, patients at Orchard Villa were left in soiled diapers, caregivers were burned out and the facility had cockroaches and flies.
"It's not like the military report was an aberration," said Natalie Mehra, executive director of the Ontario Health Coalition, during the news conference Tuesday, pointing to ongoing issues found in inspections at Orchard Villa. She noted Ontario law says long-term-care licences can only be issued to those whose past conduct related to the operation of a care home offers reason to believe the home will not be run in a way that is "prejudicial to the health, safety or welfare of its residents."
In an email statement, the ministry said the proposed Orchard Villa project had to undergo "a rigorous undertaking process" to prove it can meet provincial standards under the Fixing Long-term Care Act.
"All proposed license extensions must undergo a rigorous undertaking process to show they can meet these new high standards, as is the case with the proposed Pickering development," said the statement, attributed to spokesperson Daniel Strauss.
"Hundreds of people" are currently waiting for care in Pickering, the statement added, noting the province has invested $6.4 billion to build 58,000 new and upgraded long-term-care spaces.
"Our government believes in building Ontario's long-term-care capacity, not reducing it," said the email.
A Jan. 18 report from an inspection at Orchard Villa found the home continued to face problems meeting provincial regulations, including minimizing the risk of harm to residents, reporting matters appropriately, ensuring staff use safe transferring and positioning devices and techniques to help residents, among others.
The Star contacted Orchard Villa by phone and through a contact form on its website, as well as Southbridge by phone and email. Neither responded to a request for comment Tuesday afternoon.
"I acknowledge it was a tough time throughout COVID not only here, but around the world," Premier Doug Ford said in a separate news conference on Tuesday. "But we've corrected those problems and are going to continue improving the processes."
Ford noted the government has hired more nursing home inspectors and is expanding long-term-care homes to meet growing demand from aging seniors.
"There was a lot of problems with many homes across the province. We corrected those problems. We have more inspectors going in. We're modernizing all the facilities."
In January, the ministry launched a 10-person unit to investigate long-term-care homes facing allegations of resident abuse or neglect.
The CEO of a national seniors' advocacy organization also raised questions about the ministry's decision.
"I was frankly surprised that Orchard Villa was chosen for investment and expansion based on their problematic history," said Laura Tamblyn Watts of CanAge, noting the home was highlighted in recent years as one of the homes "where some of the worst outcomes happened."
"I would like to know more about why they were chosen for a renewal and expansion," she said. "It is publicly a problematic signal."
At the same time, she hoped the investment would help transform care in the home. While Tamblyn Watts said Ontario has strong long-term-care laws, the ministry needs to ensure that there are substantive consequences to failures to meet standards - something she said the province's new investigative unit could potentially help with - as well as investment in staffing.
She added that she's surprised to see investment in building larger homes when it's clear that smaller homes tend to provide better quality of care.
"People should feel confident that if their loved ones need to go into care, that this isn't a place where they're going to be harmed, this is a place where they're going to be helped," she said.
With files from Rob Ferguson
CREDIT: Maria Iqbal Toronto Star With files from Rob Ferguson

Document 218 of 500

'Workers are fed up:' Unions warn of labour turmoil as thousands of contracts due to expire

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/workers-are-fed-up-unions-warn-labour-turmoil-as/docview/2925928305/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Workers+are+fed+up%3A%27+Unions+warn+of+labour+turmoil+as+thousands+of+contracts+due+to+expire&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Following a year of high-profile strikes and wage gains, Canada should brace for more turmoil and work stoppages this year in both the public and private sectors, labour experts and unions say.
"Unionized workers are demonstrating a greater willingness to strike post-pandemic," said Larry Savage, a professor of labour studies at Brock University. "The combination of high inflation, low unemployment and the cost-of-living crisis have created conditions for heightened union militancy."
According to a Scotiabank report published in January, "strikes and aggressive wage settlements will persist," as some one million workers in Ontario alone - almost 15 per cent of all workers - will see their collective bargaining agreements expire between 2024 and 2026.
Expect the heat to rise as negotiations ramp up or continue for Air Canada pilots and flight attendants, Via Rail workers, LCBO workers, and more than 70,000 health care workers.
Here's where major negotiations stand so far:
LCBO: Expect a 'significant battle ...'
The Liquor Control Board of Ontario's contract expires March 31. The Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU) - which represents around 10,000 LCBO workers in retail and warehouses - delivered a notice to bargain in January
"We have hundreds of organizers across the province, in retail and warehouses, gearing up for what we know is going to be, a significant battle there," said OPSEU president J.P. Hornick. "They also have their wage reopener after Bill 124 has been found for the second time, to be unconstitutional."
Ontario's Bill 124, which capped public sector salary hikes to one per cent a year for three years, was found by the Court of Appeal for Ontario on Monday to have violated collective bargaining rights for workers, including nurses and teachers.
Unions hailed the ruling as a huge victory for workers' rights with Premier Doug Ford's government announcing it would repeal the law immediately.
Hornick said a major goal for OPSEU is to fight back against Ford's efforts to privatize the LCBO and alcohol sales.
"What we've seen with Ford is these attempts to ... keep nibbling around the edges of all of the public services," Hornick said.
Healthcare: 'A crisis unfolding ...'
Ontario health care workers are already at the bargaining table and have been for several months.
Three unions - CUPE's Ontario Council of Hospital Unions, SEIU Healthcare and Unifor - are "jointly in bargaining as we speak," CUPE Ontario president Fred Hahn said. Together they represent more than 70,000 hospital workers in Ontario.
"There's a crisis unfolding in our health care system," Hahn said.
The tri-union coalition is urging a five per cent funding increase beyond inflation from the Ford government to establish fair compensation, which they say is essential for recruiting thousands of workers and expanding hospital beds amid a 10 per cent staff turnover rate exacerbated by funding cuts and wage restraints.
The bargaining is ongoing, Hahn said.
Planes and trains: 'Big year for rail agreements ...'
CUPE also has an agreement with Air Canada flight attendants which expires in 2025, but bargaining is expected to start this year.
"Our Air Canada members are just finishing a very long collective agreement," Hahn said, adding that the previous collective agreement was a decade long.
More than 10,000 Air Canada flight attendants represented by CUPE are seeking better wages and compensation for unpaid work according to Hahn.
"Flight attendants are only paid for the time that the aircraft is in the air," he said. "If a flight is delayed by four hours, that means it's four hours of their day" that goes unpaid.
Air Canada pilots, represented by the Air Line Pilots Association (ALPA) are also in bargaining, calling for better pay and working conditions. Air Canada pilots last May "invoked a clause" to end their 10-year collective agreement a year early and initiate bargaining a new one over the summer. Since landing on a 10-year collective agreement in 2014, the pilots have received a two per cent pay hike each year.
Bargaining will likely continue until the summer, said Tim Perry the Canadian president of ALPA.
"We've agreed with the Air Canada management team to engage a private mediator," Perry said. "That phase will last until at least June."
Unifor will also be bargaining with Via Rail this year with some 2,400 workers' contracts expiring at the end of 2024.
"This will be a big year for rail agreements," said Unifor's national president Lana Payne in a previous interview.
Telecoms and media: Members 'thrown out of work ...'
Payne also has her sights set on Canada's telecommunication and media sectors. Unifor Local 87-M represents media workers across southern Ontario, including the Toronto Star.
Bell Canada's contract with Unifor, representing nearly 4,000 crafts and services employees, is set to expire on Nov. 30. This will follow Bell Canada's announcement in February that it will slash 4,800 jobs across the country, it's largest restructuring in almost 30 years.
"Executives and shareholders are doing just fine while our members are being thrown out of work, including, once again, in the media," said Payne at the time, whose union represents 800 of the workers being laid off.
Negotiations are also underway between the Canadian Media Guild (CMG) and the CBC "as we seek a deal before the current collective agreement expires on March 31, 2024," the CMG said in statement in January. The CMG represents some 4,000 employees at the CBC.
With files from The Canadian Press.
CAPTION: National president Lana Payne's Unifor will be involved in Via Rail and Bell Media negotiations this year.

Subject: Agreements; Presidents; Strikes; Health care; Contract negotiations; Employees; Collective bargaining; Medical personnel; Wages & salaries

Document 219 of 500

Stintz nomination causes uproar: Toronto Conservatives accuse party of 'undemocratic' meddling in Eglinton-Lawrence riding

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/stintz-nomination-causes-uproar/docview/2925928245/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Stintz+nomination+causes+uproar%3A+Toronto+Conservatives+accuse+party+of+%27undemocratic%27+meddling+in+Eglinton-Lawrence+riding&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal Conservative party's nomination of Karen Stintz in the Toronto riding of Eglinton-Lawrence has triggered an uproar among local Conservatives, prompted the executive to protest in writing to leader Pierre Poilievre, and led one board member who was shut out of the race to quit.
Lawyer Michelle Simard resigned late Monday night over what she said is an "undemocratic" move by party operatives to bypass a fair contest that should have been overseen by the riding association, not central party officials.
Eglinton-Lawrence is the second Ontario riding association executive to express grassroots party concerns and file a written objection to federal Conservative party brass over a hand-picked candidate.
In a letter dated Feb. 10, riding association president Allan Hoffman told Poilievre, deputy leader Melissa Lantsman and other top Conservative officials that the process by which Stintz, a former Toronto city councillor, was selected "created turmoil and has left a number of board members quite distraught and unhappy."
The Conservative nomination for the riding, which is currently held by Liberal MP Marco Mendicino, is one that at least four people wanted, sources said.
In his letter, Hoffman expressed concerns about the party's "conduct, behaviour and interference" in the riding's nomination process. He said senior party officials decided to close nominations within 24 hours on Feb. 8, without consulting local riding workers, and despite earlier assurances that nominations would remain open until late spring or early summer.
Other candidates were asked to withdraw or step aside, the letter said, flagging "questionable practices involving cutting access to the candidate platform portal before the deadline so that remaining candidates could not properly access it so as to complete their application."
"Taking away the constitutional and democratic rights" of the riding association or its members "is neither democratic nor grassroots," the letter said.
Hoffman asked for a meeting with the party's national council, and a reopening of the nomination period to allow "the democratic, constitutional, nomination process to play itself out," a request the riding association's board members approved at a meeting Monday night.
Their concerns are similar to those raised by Conservatives in the Wellington-Halton Hills area about the party's decision to forgo a full nomination contest and parachute in Parm Gill, a former Doug Ford cabinet minister, as the candidate for one of the two Milton ridings which will be created in an upcoming riding redistribution. Which riding Gill will run in has not been specified because, the party says, they don't exist yet.
Following Monday's emergency meeting, Simard resigned from the riding association board, alleging she had been "locked out" from the website where potential candidates could apply to run for the nomination.
"Why would I give my talent, energy, and precious time to an organization that doesn't want to follow the democratic values and rules?" she said in an interview.
Another would-be candidate, Howard Levitt, said in an interview he never got as far submitting a nomination application because "somebody from the party told me that I would not be allowed to run in that riding ... I was told that they viewed (Stintz) as the candidate."
"Do I think I'd be a better candidate by a country mile? Yes," said Levitt, a labour lawyer, prominent Jewish community activist and Financial Post columnist, who said he has more "status," "profile" and "visibility" in the riding .
Levitt said he was led to believe other candidates might have dropped out of the race in favour of him "because I had a better chance than they did of beating (Stintz) for a nomination, and they wanted more of a champion for our community, which she was not viewed as being."
Levitt acknowledged that party officials have the right to select people they deem as "star" candidates, but said "they should be open about it ... they should at least present a saleable case to the riding executives who control the funds and have a lot of volunteers among them that this is the right candidate for that riding, and not purport to have an open nomination fight and then not."
In an emailed statement, Conservative Party of Canada communications director Sarah Fischer said the request for the party's specific response to the letter and the riding's request for a reopened selection process was "ridiculous," the Star's reporting on the Wellington-Halton Hills riding association's concerns was "overtly biased," and cited the Star's "lazy reporting."
"Karen Stintz was nominated under the Party rules and in compliance with the Party Constitution," Fischer wrote.
In a followup email, Fischer wrote that "Karen Stintz was the only candidate to submit a completed application. All nomination rules and procedures under the constitution were followed."
Several local Conservatives with the riding executive declined to speak to the Star, with some fearing reprisals if they went public.
The Star contacted two other individuals said to be interested in the race, but they had not responded to requests by publication time.
Stintz was not available for comment on Tuesday afternoon.
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star

Toronto Conservatives accuse federal party of 'undemocratic' meddling in nomination of Karen Stintz

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-conservatives-accuse-federal-party/docview/2926264411/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+Conservatives+accuse+federal+party+of+%27undemocratic%27+meddling+in+nomination+of+Karen+Stintz&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - The federal Conservative party's nomination of Karen Stintz in the Toronto riding of Eglinton-Lawrence has triggered an uproar among local Conservatives, prompted the executive to protest in writing to leader Pierre Poilievre, and led one board member who was shut out of the race to quit.
Lawyer Michelle Simard resigned late Monday night over what she said is an "undemocratic" move by party operatives to bypass a fair contest that should have been overseen by the riding association, not central party officials.
Eglinton-Lawrence is the second Ontario riding association executive to express grassroots party concerns and file a written objection to federal Conservative party brass over a hand-picked candidate.
In a letter dated Feb. 10, riding association president Allan Hoffman told Poilievre, deputy leader Melissa Lantsman and other top Conservative officials that the process by which Stintz, a former Toronto city councillor, was selected "created turmoil and has left a number of board members quite distraught and unhappy."

He said senior party officials decided to close nominations within 24 hours on Feb. 8, without consulting local riding workers, and despite earlier assurances that nominations would remain open until late spring or early summer.

Hoffman asked for a meeting with the party's national council, and a reopening of the nomination period to allow "the democratic, constitutional, nomination process to play itself out," a request the riding association's board members approved at a meeting Monday night.
Their concerns are similar to those raised by Conservatives in the Wellington-Halton Hills area about the party's decision to forgo a full nomination contest and parachute in Parm Gill, a former Doug Ford cabinet minister, as the candidate for one of the two Milton ridings which will be created in an upcoming riding redistribution. Which riding Gill will run in has not been specified because, the party says, they don't exist yet.

"Why would I give my talent, energy, and precious time to an organization that doesn't want to follow the democratic values and rules?" she said in an interview.
Another would-be candidate, Howard Levitt, said in an interview he never got as far submitting a nomination application because "somebody from the party told me that I would not be allowed to run in that riding ... I was told that they viewed (Stintz) as the candidate."
"Do I think I'd be a better candidate by a country mile? Yes," said Levitt, a labour lawyer, prominent Jewish community activist and Financial Post columnist, who said he has more "status," "profile" and "visibility" in the riding .
Levitt said he was led to believe other candidates might have dropped out of the race in favour of him "because I had a better chance than they did of beating (Stintz) for a nomination, and they wanted more of a champion for our community, which she was not viewed as being."

In a followup email, Fischer wrote that "Karen Stintz was the only candidate to submit a completed application. All nomination rules and procedures under the constitution were followed."
Several local Conservatives with the riding executive declined to speak to the Star, with some fearing reprisals if they went public.
The Star contacted two other individuals said to be interested in the race, but they had not responded to requests by publication time.
Stintz was not available for comment on Tuesday afternoon.
CAPTION: Former city councillor Karen Stintz will be the Conservative candidate in Eglinton-Lawrence in the next federal election.

Document 221 of 500

Ontario misses deadline on first of 57 Soleiman Faqiri inquest recommendations

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-misses-deadline-on-first-57-soleiman/docview/2925580030/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+misses+deadline+on+first+of+57+Soleiman+Faqiri+inquest+recommendations&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Two months after a coroner's inquest ruled Soleiman Faqiri's jailhouse death a homicide, the Ontario government has failed to meet the first of dozens of recommendations meant to help prevent similar deaths.
In December, a jury in the landmark inquest asked the provincial government to develop and issue a public position statement recognizing that jails are not appropriate environments for those experiencing significant mental health issues, and do so by Feb. 10.
On Saturday, that deadline came and went.
Shortly after the jury's verdict, a spokesperson told the Star the Ministry of the Solicitor General would "carefully review the inquest recommendations," look for ways to "inform policies and procedures" and respond to the Office of the Chief Coroner.
When asked by the Star on Monday why it had not put out a public statement in line with the inquest's first recommendation, another spokesperson responded with the same comment.
The recommendation was an opportunity for the government "to stand on the right side of history," Soleiman Faqiri's brother Yusuf told the Star.
"Do we really need another mother or sister burying their loved one?" he said.
Soleiman Faqiri was beaten to death by jail guards on Dec. 15, 2016, while experiencing an acute mental health crisis due to his schizophrenia. During 11 days in solitary confinement at the Central East Correctional Centre near Lindsay, he never saw a psychiatrist nor a hospital before his death when guards pepper sprayed him, beat him, restrained him face-down, placed a spit hood over his head and shackled him.
In December, the inquest jury deemed Faqiri's death to be a homicide.
A key focus of the inquest was the question of why Faqiri was never sent to hospital despite clear evidence he was experiencing a crisis.
All 57 recommendations were directed at the Ontario government and aimed at preventing similar deaths. The recommendations cover a range of policy changes from modifying jail guards' training to how the justice system can effectively help those with mental health challenges; most fall upon the Ministry of the Solicitor General, which is responsible for Ontario's jails.
The call for a public acknowledgment was the only recommendation with a deadline.
Recognizing the problem would have been a symbolic first step, said Zahir Kolia, assistant professor in Toronto Metropolitan University's criminology department.
The missed deadline is "deeper than the government saying it doesn't care. It's almost like a recognition that these problems are so incredibly deep they don't even know how to address it," Kolia said
Prabhu Rajan, one of the inquest's lawyers, said he's disappointed in the province because every other recommendation from the jury derives from this statement of awareness.
"There were red flags everywhere in this case," he said.
The recommendations were jointly created by the jury, the Faqiri family, the inquest's lawyers, mental health advocates and the Lindsay jail's physician.
Other key asks included immediately implementing a committee that ensures inquest recommendations, which are not legally binding, are considered and renewed calls for the province to implement an independent oversight body to ensure police accountability - both of which echo recommendations from a recent inquest into the 2013 killing by Toronto police of 18-year-old Sammy Yatim.
Ontario has an unreliable track record in adopting coroner's inquest recommendations to address rising jail deaths, despite repeated calls for change from families, advocates and even correctional workers, said NDP MPP Kristyn Wong-Tam.
The failure to respond to the Faqiri inquest is demoralizing, she said. "It's justice denied once again."
According to a 2022 report by the group Tracking (In)Justice, there have been 280 deaths in Ontario jails since 2010.
The jury's call for a public acknowledgment that those experiencing mental health crises should not be in jail was echoed last week at a separate inquest into the 2016 death of 30-year-old Terry Baker at the Grand Valley Institution for Women, a federal prison.
On Friday, that inquest's jury also called upon the federal government to acknowledge that its prisons are not an appropriate setting for people with severe forms of mental illness or those at risk of self-harm.
Like the Faqiri inquest, this was also No. 1 on the jury's list of 67 recommendations. No. 2 was a call for the Correctional Service of Canada to implement dozens of recommendations still unmet from the 2013 inquest into the death of Ashley Smith, 19, who died by self-inflicted strangulation while under suicide watch at the Kitchener women's prison.
"This story is not going away," Yusuf Faqiri said. "These families won't go away."
CAPTION: The family of Soleiman Faqiri held a final vigil on Dec. 16, 2023 days afterSoleiman's 2016 jailhouse death was deemed a homicide in a coroner's inquest.

Subject: Prisons; Mental health; Inquests; Mental disorders

Document 222 of 500

'Untenable and appalling crisis': Ottawa must fill judicial vacancies plaguing lower courts, Federal Court rules

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/untenable-appalling-crisis-ottawa-must-fill/docview/2925579933/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Untenable+and+appalling+crisis%27%3A+Ottawa+must+fill+judicial+vacancies+plaguing+lower+courts%2C+Federal+Court+rules&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The long-standing failure of the federal government to appoint enough judges across the country has caused an "untenable and appalling crisis," the Federal Court said Tuesday, declaring that the Liberals must make more timely appointments.
The decision comes as a number of serious criminal cases have collapsed due to delay caused by the lack of judges at the Toronto Superior Court.
Tuesday's decision stems from a legal challenge brought by lawyer Yavar Hameed, who argued that the government's slow pace at appointing more judges was delaying or denying justice to Canadians.
There are currently about 73 judicial vacancies across the country, including 21 in Ontario.
Judges appointed to provincial superior courts and courts of appeal are made by the federal cabinet on the recommendation of the minister of justice. Reasons for the government's slow pace at appointing judges have long been unclear.
"With the greatest respect, the court finds the Prime Minister and Minister of Justice are simply treading water," Federal Court Justice Henry Brown wrote on Tuesday.
"And with the greatest respect, they have also failed all those who rely on them for the timely exercise of their powers in relation to filling these vacancies. Also failed are all those who have unsuccessfully sought timely justice in the Superior Courts and Federal Courts across Canada."
The recent criminal cases to collapse at Toronto's Superior Court include a human trafficking case, gun charges and a prosecution for an alleged sex assault, with the judges in each one citing delays caused by the systemic vacancies.
The Supreme Court has ruled trials must be completed within a strict time limit or be tossed for violating the accused's right to trial within a reasonable time.
Brown wrote that the government had provided no justification for the "untenable situation."
He declared that the government must appoint judges "within a reasonable time" after a vacancy arises, in order to "materially reduce" the total number of vacancies across the country and bring the number back down to the mid-40s - the number that existed in 2016 before the Liberals revamped the judicial appointment process.
"In this manner, the Court expects the untenable and appalling crisis and critical judicial vacancy situation found by this Court as identified by the Chief Justice and Canadian Judicial Council, will be resolved," Brown wrote.
More to come.
CAPTION: Toronto's Superior Court at 361 University Ave., where a number of recent trials have collapsed over a systemic shortage of federally appointed judges.

Subject: Political appointments; Judicial appointments; Trials; Judges & magistrates

Title: 'Untenable and appalling crisis': Ottawa must fill judicial vacancies plaguing lower courts, Federal Court rules

Document 223 of 500

Doug Ford government scrapping licence plate renewals

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-government-scrapping-licence-plate/docview/2925579931/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+government+scrapping+licence+plate+renewals&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford will eliminate the need to renew licence plates after the Star revealed there are more than one million expired plates on Ontario roads.
"We're getting rid of that totally - registering your vehicle," Ford told reporters Tuesday at a Mississauga gas station, where he also announced planned legislation designed to thwart a future government from imposing a provincial carbon tax.
"We did the first step getting rid of the sticker (in 2022)," the premier said at a campaign-style event where he attacked newly elected Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, who stepped down as Mississauga mayor last month.
"Now we're getting rid of the reregistration. They'll be automatically reregistered so people won't have to worry about that at all," he said, adding details would be unveiled later this week.
"We're going to make sure that's very soon, extremely soon."
WATCH: Ford takes digs at Bonnie Crombie during announcement
Dakota Brasier, senior media adviser to Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria, said "the process will automatically renew for those in good standing."
But motorists who owe traffic fines or unpaid tolls would not be renewed until those are paid, she said.
Scofflaws will be notified by mail or via email or text that they must pay their penalties or their plates will not be validated.
That means municipalities should not be shortchanged on revenue from parking tickets that haven't been paid.
As first disclosed Monday by the Star, there were exactly 1,015,139 expired plates in the province last month.
That means about seven per cent of the 13.6 million plates on Ontario roads have not been renewed by forgetful vehicle owners - and face Highway Traffic Act fines of up to $1,000.
Ford scrapped the annual renewal fee of up to $120 before the 2022 election, leading to many motorists assuming they no longer needed to keep their plates up to date.
According to the Ontario Provincial Police, charges have soared since the paid renewal stickers were eliminated two years ago.
Last year, there were 15,631 tickets issued to motorists lacking a "currently validated permit for the vehicle," the OPP said.
That compared with 5,341 charges in 2022 and 1,556 in 2021, although the COVID-19 pandemic that kept Ontarians off roads and highways could have contributed to those lower violations.
Removing the renewal charge costs the treasury more than $1 billion in lost revenue annually.
Sarkaria's office has been urging motorists to check to see if their plates are valid at: https://www.ontario.ca/page/renew-your-licence-plate.
Those can be renewed online until the new measures take effect later this year.
All passenger vehicles - including cars, SUVs and vans - under 3,000 kilograms are covered under the policy.
Validation stickers are currently still required for heavy commercial vehicles, like large trucks and snowplows.
Because many motorists still have their old 2022 renewal stickers on their plates, there have been instances of drivers getting tickets out of province because local police believe the plates have expired.
The Canadian Automobile Association has recommended motorists keep a printout of proof of renewal although that is not legally required.
Ford blurted out the looming plate change at a press conference where he touted forthcoming legislation to put carbon-pricing on the front-burner at Queen's Park.
Under the proposed bill that will be tabled when the legislature resumes next Tuesday, any future government would have to hold a referendum before imposing any carbon tax or cap-and-trade system.
It would not impact federal carbon pricing measures.
Ironically, Ontarians did not have to pay the federal levy until Ford cancelled the province's cap-and-trade agreement with Quebec and California upon winning power in 2018. That exposed Ontario taxpayers to the federal carbon scheme.
Speaking to the Star editorial board on Tuesday, NDP Leader Marit Stiles said the premier's referendum proposal is a bid to shift the spotlight from controversies like the ongoing $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal and the surprise flip-flop on the Bill 124 wage cap.
"I absolutely think that things like the announcement today are just attempts to distract from whatever the latest thing is - like their failure to continue to suppress wages of workers in Bill 124," said Stiles.
"And (the Greenbelt issue) is not gone, there's a criminal investigation under way," she said, referring to the RCMP probe.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford attends an announcement at Seneca College in King City, Ont., on Feb. 9, 2024.

Subject: Automobiles; Automobile drivers; Emissions trading; Carbon; Roads & highways; Vehicles; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Emissions trading Environmental tax

Title: Doug Ford government scrapping licence plate renewals

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Document 224 of 500

In a victory for nurses teachers and thousands of unionized ...[Derived Headline]

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/victory-nurses-teachers-thousands-unionized/docview/2925411539/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=In+a+victory+for+nurses+teachers+and+thousands+of+unionized+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a victory for nurses, teachers and thousands of unionized public-sector workers, Premier Doug Ford's government is backing down on another major policy - his controversial wage restraint legislation.
The sudden shift came Monday evening after Ontario's top court upheld a Superior Court ruling that Bill 124 was unconstitutional and union leaders signalled they were out of patience.
A terse statement from the office of Attorney General Doug Downey said the government "will not appeal today's Court of Appeal decision and will instead take steps to repeal Bill 124 in its entirety in the coming weeks."
The statement was unexpected.
Just three hours before it was issued, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy had told a news conference the government would "take ... time" to consider whether to seek leave to appeal the ruling to the Supreme Court of Canada.
"Every climbdown and policy reversal is proof that when we work together, we can win," NDP Leader Marit Stiles - whose party had long called for the repeal of the 2019 legislation - said on social media.
"After wasting years and countless dollars fighting workers in court, it's time to start fixing the mess Bill 124 created in our hospitals, schools and public services," Stiles said.
In its long-awaited decision Monday morning, the Court of Appeal found the Progressive Conservative government infringed upon Charter rights to collective bargaining with Bill 124.
The legislation capped wage increases for most public-sector workers at one per cent annually for three years and has been blamed for an exodus of health-care workers during the COVID-19 pandemic as they toiled long hours at the risk of their own health.
"Ontario has not been able to explain why wage restraint could not have been achieved through good-faith bargaining," judges Lise Favreau and David Doherty wrote in their majority decision.
In a dissenting opinion, a third judge on the appeal panel, William Hourigan, said courts are "public policy amateurs."
"Thus, it is not the role of judges to second-guess the policy choice made by governments," Hourigan said.
The government's reversal follows its decision in September to drop plans to open more of the ecologically sensitive Greenbelt in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area to development. The Royal Canadian Mounted Police are investigating "irregularities" in the government's original plan, including whether developers were tipped off in advance.
The attorney general's statement hinted at other measures coming soon to help those impacted by the wage restraint legislation.
"To solve for the inequality of workers created by today's court decision, the province will urgently introduce regulations to exempt non-unionized and non-associated workers from Bill 124 until it is repealed."
The challenge to Bill 124 was brought by teachers unions, the Ontario Nurses' Association, the Ontario Public Service Employees Union, Unifor and the Power Workers' Union, among others representing 2,500 bargaining units.
Among other things, they argued Bill 124 was unfair because it exempted police and firefighters - both male-dominated occupations - from the wage caps.
"Let the court's ruling be a lesson for the Ford government to never circumvent bargaining or trample on worker democratic rights again," said Karen Brown, president of the Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario.
"This government does not respect workers," said René Jansen in de Wal, president of the Ontario English Catholic Teachers' Association, calling the province's appeal of the initial ruling "reckless" and "a waste of tax dollars."
Bethlenfalvy acknowledged the legislation "continues to impact a lot of people" and said he did not have a tally of the government's legal costs.
Liberal labour critic John Fraser, MPP for Ottawa South, estimated the amount in the "millions of dollars."
"Nothing to show for it," he added.
Ontario Nurses' Association president Erin Ariss, other union leaders and opposition parties at Queen's Park before Bethlenfalvy spoke had urged the province not to attempt an appeal.
"Since Doug Ford took office in 2018, the number of nurses leaving the system has increased every year. For every 10 nurses this government says have been hired, six have left," Ariss said.
Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie congratulated the unions on a "long-overdue victory ... for the workers who play such important roles in our lives."
Even as public-sector unions awaited the ruling, labour arbitrators have been awarding some public-sector workers retroactive pay in what has been a period of high inflation because their contracts included "reopener" clauses.
The latest such award was an extra 6.5 per cent for members of the Ontario Public Service Employees Union.
Nurses, some teachers and hospital workers, including cooks, lab technicians, personal support workers and dietary aides, have also been awarded pay increases.
But thousands of public-sector workers without "reopener" clauses have no such remedy - something the unions said they intend to address through pressure on the government, although the process is unclear.
"We want to ensure everyone gets something," said Ontario Federation of Labour president Laura Walton, noting unionized workers should not have to wait until their contracts are up for renegotiation. "No worker should be left behind."
Bethlenfalvy said the cost of reopeners and catch-ups has been "incorporated" into the government's spending plans but did not provide a dollar figure, calling it a "moving" target.
High inflation in the last couple of years has worsened the impact of Bill 124 on workers, putting them further behind in terms of purchasing power and in need of a catch-up, union leaders said.
A previous Liberal government's Bill 115, which imposed wages on teachers and education workers, was struck down as unconstitutional a decade ago. As part of the remedy process, a judge instructed the government and unions to reach an agreement.
The government subsequently awarded more than $200 million to the impacted workers.
Ontario's independent Financial Accountability Office warned 18 months ago it may cost the province $8.4 billion in suppressed wages if Bill 124 was struck down. The estimate was based on the one per cent cap in the legislation and historical trends in provincial public-sector wage increases.
Monday's ruling followed the government's appeal of a 2022 decision by the Ontario Superior Court of Justice that the wage restraint legislation - passed as an austerity measure to help balance the budget before the COVID-19 pandemic emerged - violated Charter rights to collective bargaining.
"On my view of the evidence, Ontario was not facing a situation in 2019 that justified an infringement on Charter rights," Justice Markus Koehnen wrote in his original ruling. "Unlike other cases that have upheld wage restraint legislation, Bill 124 sets the wage cap at a rate below which employees were obtaining in free collective bargaining negotiations."
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson and Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 225 of 500

Chow is spending, but not enough

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/chow-is-spending-not-enough/docview/2925411385/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chow+is+spending%2C+but+not+enough&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Big number: $50 million, money in Mayor Olivia Chow's 2024 budget dedicated to a "Back on Track Fund" intended to fix Toronto's streets and public spaces
We've spent a lot of time over the last couple of years talking about how Toronto is broken. It was a recurring theme during both Toronto's regularly scheduled mayoral election in the fall of 2022 and the surprise party of a byelection we got last year.
It wasn't a niche sentiment. The Star ran a whole series around the 2022 election asking, "Can't we do better?" It told stories about things like Toronto's lack of functioning public washrooms, dead raccoons left on the street for days on end and way too many potholes. Months later, Olivia Chow was able to catapult herself into the mayor's office by talking about a lot of those same things and the need for change.
So, as Chow gets ready to present her first budget at Wednesday's meeting of city council, it's time for a broken city vibe check. Let's switch the question from "Can't we do better?" to "Are we doing better?"
The answer, reading through the budget, is that she's off to an OK start - but she's got a lot more work to do.
The central challenge Chow faced when she took office is that city hall had been left with a $1.8 billion budget hole, and an annual budgeting process that was simply not sustainable. She had to fix that. And fixing that has been - and will be - ridiculously hard, requiring frank conversations about taxes and tough negotiations with Queen's Park and Ottawa.
But even if she pulls it off, achieving sustainability for a status quo that voters were describing as a broken city is not going to be a victory. No one is going to care that the city balance sheet looks better if the city still looks shabby.
In other words, Chow can't spike the football in an end zone that's still full of litter, dead raccoons and park-goers looking frantically for a place to pee.
Instead, she needs to simultaneously fix the mistakes of the past and improve the prospects for the future. To that end, it's notable that her budget has started squirreling away funds in what she's calling a "Back on Track Fund."
Taking some of the money that's expected to be freed up once the city officially hands over the Gardiner Expressway and Don Valley Parkway to Premier Doug Ford's government at the end of this year, the fund will go toward, well, fixing stuff. A letter from Chow to city council says it'll be spent on "parks that are beautiful, welcoming places for families to enjoy. Roads that are well-maintained and not choked with gridlock. Garbage bins that aren't overflowing, and washrooms you can use when you need them."
Digging further into the budget reveals some more specifics. Chow's got $20 million over the next two years to improve outdoor recreation facilities, with an emphasis on fast-growing sports like pickleball and cricket and fixing drinking fountains.
There's another $1 million for "public space beautification" this year, including adding more litter bins and temporary public washrooms. And there's $30 million for the repair of local roads.
To skeptics who read this stuff and scoff at government spending, there's lots of precedent for extra money making for a significant improvement in quality of service. For a recent example, consider last Friday's news about how hiring small teams of workers to help direct traffic along King Street reduced average travel times for streetcars riders between Bathurst Street and Jarvis Street from between 45 and 65 minutes to between 17 and 21 minutes.
That's some real bang for your buck. And there's opportunity for more of it. The problem isn't that Chow is spending money, it's that she's not spending enough.
The parks department budget lists $630 million worth of projects that currently lack funding. The transportation department's list of unfunded projects totals a whopping $3.3 billion, with $1.6 billion needed to repair local roads and $805 million needed to fix bridges. And the TTC has a total basket case of a budget, with $16.3 billion in unfunded projects needed to keep transit on the rails into the 2030s.
Stacked up against those kinds of numbers, the math says Chow's $50-million fund isn't going to make much of a difference. But there could be a smart strategy in purposefully directing funds to some lower-cost, highly visible fixes like emptying litter bins more often, keeping water fountains functional and patching up more potholes.
Because I think people understand that big projects like building a new community centre or fixing all of Toronto's roads can't happen overnight, but what's frustrated residents in recent years is the feeling that, in addition to not delivering on big projects, the city hasn't even seemed to care too much about doing the basics to keep the city functional.
Chow's budget is constructed to be a sign to those who have grown cynical about the state of the city that help is on the way. It answers the "Can't we do better?" question with an emphatic yes. As for the "Are we doing better?" question? Well, she's working on it.
CREDIT: Matt Elliott OPINION

Subject: Local elections; Mayors; Parks & recreation areas; Budgets; Urban planning; State budgets

Business indexing term: Subject: Budgets State budgets

Location: Toronto Ontario Canada; Canada

People: Chow, Olivia

Title: Chow is spending, but not enough

Document 226 of 500

Olivia Chow's budget sets aside money to fix a broken Toronto. It's not nearly enough

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/olivia-chows-budget-sets-aside-money-fix-broken/docview/2925383085/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Olivia+Chow%27s+budget+sets+aside+money+to+fix+a+broken+Toronto.+It%27s+not+nearly+enough&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 227 of 500

Post-secondary chaos comes courtesy of Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/post-secondary-chaos-comes-courtesy-ford/docview/2925380691/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Post-secondary+chaos+comes+courtesy+of+Ford&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The education of Doug Ford comes at a high price.
Not for him - the premier is doing just fine.
But under his stewardship, post-secondary education has spiralled from crisis to catastrophe. It is a disaster of Ford's own making, with implications that go far beyond Ontario's colleges and universities.
Here's the problem with the premier's post-secondary playbook: He's been playing with other people's money - a shell game - while gambling on the outcomes.
What looked like a good deal for Ford has become a bad bet for the entire province. The chaos over surging foreign enrolments on campus, amped up by the premier, has created panicky headlines.
But if you dig a little deeper, the crisis has also created an unsavoury windfall for the province: A "head tax" on foreign students, on top of a shell game for colleges, which together buff up the provincial treasury by hundreds of millions of dollars.
That's on top of the savings for Ford's Progressive Conservatives by freezing funding for higher education in Ontario at a time of rising inflation (disclosure: I'm a senior fellow at Toronto Metropolitan University and also at the University of Toronto's Munk School of Global Affairs and Public Policy).
Upon taking power in 2018, Ford ordered every post-secondary institution to cut tuition by 10 per cent - without making up the shortfall. Those fees have been frozen ever since, while government funding stagnated year after year - inflation be damned.
Ontario's colleges, left to fend for themselves after the tuition cut and freeze, were encouraged to make up the difference by recruiting high-paying foreigners with abandon. The inflated cash flow propelled all but one of Ontario's 24 colleges into sudden surpluses worth more than $660 million in 2022.
Conveniently, that windfall benefited Ford's Tories because the colleges' balance sheets show up on the books of the provincial treasury. That's a sweet deal for Ford and his surplus-addicted finance minister, Peter Bethlenfalvy.
But the province's universities, most of which resisted the temptation to feast on foreign students, are now in dire financial straits, with 10 of 23 now running deficits. Happily for Ford's Tories, those university deficits are seemingly not the province's problem - neither fiscally nor politically. That's because any university's red ink doesn't show up on the province's balance sheet, since they are deemed autonomous institutions (unlike colleges which report directly to the government).
It's an accident of history that manifests as an accounting quirk. But it amounts to a handy political payoff for the premier.
Moreover, Ford's Tories have been shamelessly milking foreign students with a de facto "head tax," which is dressed up as an international "recovery fee" for every warm body lured to Ontario. It's not a massive amount - more than $140 million a year - but it's profiteering all the same.
Campuses are at the breaking point. Communities are at the boiling point over housing shortages exacerbated by an unplanned foreign influx.
Forced into action by the province's inaction, the federal government imposed a cap on foreign students and work permits last month. With cascading abuses, Ottawa had little choice but to act - Ontario broke the system, and now Queen's Park has to take responsibility for fixing it.
The boom will fall especially hard on Ontario, which already fills 51 per cent of the permits with only 39 per cent of the population. And it will hit universities harder than colleges and private educational institutions, which cornered an outsized share of permits driven by a strategy of greedy growth.
Will the government play hardball with universities when it comes to those scarce international permits, in order to protect the foreign cash flow of colleges who are already Ford's preferred partners for his policy of promoting the skilled trades?
Ontario had been warned for years that its campus cash flow was overly exposed to foreign risk, amid diplomatic tensions with source countries such as Saudi Arabia, China and now India. The remedy is to start paying our own way, instead of recruiting rich (or indebted) foreigners to subsidize our world class education system.
Last year, it seemed the premier had seen the light. The Tories set up a fancy-sounding Blue Ribbon Panel on Post-secondary Education that quickly focused on fixing the distorted bottom line with straightforward advice:
Stop cutting tuition and stop freezing funding.
"The sector's financial sustainability is currently at serious risk, and it will take a concerted effort to right the ship," its report warned in November.
The outside panel recommended a one-time tuition hike of five per cent in 2024-25, followed by increases of two per cent (or higher, tied to inflation) thereafter. By the panel's calculations, it would take a tuition increase of 25 per cent to make up for lost monies - politically unpalatable, so it urged the Ford government to increase funding by about 10 per cent with subsequent increases of at least two per cent (plus inflation).
Its report urged the government to confront its growing addiction by moving to "reduce or eliminate the student recovery fee for international students paid by colleges," amounting to $750 a year.
Ford's reaction? Pull out the populist playbook:
"I just don't believe this is the time to go into these (Ontario) students' pockets, especially the ones that are really struggling, and ask for a tuition increase," he told reporters last month, calling instead for more "efficiencies."
Let's not confuse efficiencies with distortions. By profiting from the penury of post-secondary institutions - boosting his own bottom line while starving universities and contorting colleges - Ford is giving the province a costly lesson about false economies.
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

ERIC Subject: Secondary Education; Postsecondary Education; Tuition; Government (Administrative Body); Universities; Foreign Students

Subject: Tuition; Foreign students; Colleges & universities; Funding; College campuses; Education; Cash flow

Title: Post- secondary chaos comes courtesy of Ford

Document 228 of 500

How shamelessly has Doug Ford ground down Ontario's colleges and universities? Let me count the ways

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-shamelessly-has-doug-ford-ground-down/docview/2925380521/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+shamelessly+has+Doug+Ford+ground+down+Ontario%27s+colleges+and+universities%3F+Let+me+count+the+ways&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Moreover, Ford's Tories have been shamelessly milking foreign students with a de facto "head tax," which is dressed up as an international "recovery fee" for every warm body lured to Ontario. It's not a massive amount - more than $140 million a year - but it's profiteering all the same.
Campuses are at the breaking point. Communities are at the boiling point over housing shortages exacerbated by an unplanned foreign influx.
Forced into action by the province's inaction, the federal government imposed a cap on foreign students and work permits last month. With cascading abuses, Ottawa had little choice but to act - Ontario broke the system, and now Queen's Park has to take responsibility for fixing it.
The boom will fall especially hard on Ontario, which already fills 51 per cent of the permits with only 39 per cent of the population. And it will hit universities harder than colleges and private educational institutions, which cornered an outsized share of permits driven by a strategy of greedy growth.
Will the government play hardball with universities when it comes to those scarce international permits, in order to protect the foreign cash flow of colleges who are already Ford's preferred partners for his policy of promoting the skilled trades?
Ontario had been warned for years that its campus cash flow was overly exposed to foreign risk, amid diplomatic tensions with source countries such as Saudi Arabia, China and now India. The remedy is to start paying our own way, instead of recruiting rich (or indebted) foreigners to subsidize our world class education system.
Last year, it seemed the premier had seen the light. The Tories set up a fancy-sounding Blue Ribbon Panel on Post-secondary Education that quickly focused on fixing the distorted bottom line with straightforward advice:
Stop cutting tuition and stop freezing funding.
"The sector's financial sustainability is currently at serious risk, and it will take a concerted effort to right the ship," its report warned in November.
The outside panel recommended a one-time tuition hike of five per cent in 2024-25, followed by increases of two per cent (or higher, tied to inflation) thereafter. By the panel's calculations, it would take a tuition increase of 25 per cent to make up for lost monies - politically unpalatable, so it urged the Ford government to increase funding by about 10 per cent with subsequent increases of at least two per cent (plus inflation).
Its report urged the government to confront its growing addiction by moving to "reduce or eliminate the student recovery fee for international students paid by colleges," amounting to $750 a year.
Ford's reaction? Pull out the populist playbook:
"I just don't believe this is the time to go into these (Ontario) students' pockets, especially the ones that are really struggling, and ask for a tuition increase," he told reporters last month, calling instead for more "efficiencies."
Let's not confuse efficiencies with distortions. By profiting from the penury of post-secondary institutions - boosting his own bottom line while starving universities and contorting colleges - Ford is giving the province a costly lesson about false economies.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford attends an announcement at Seneca College in King City, Ont., on Feb. 9, 2024.

Subject: Tuition; Foreign students; Colleges & universities; Funding; College campuses; Education; Cash flow

Business indexing term: Subject: Colleges & universities Cash flow

Full text: Ontario is on track for a $4.5-billion deficit in the fiscal year ending March 31, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy said Monday as he presented the province's third quarter results.
That figure is $1.1 billion less than the finance minister forecast in his fall economic statement in November, thanks to increased tax and other government revenue and lower debt interest.
However, the deficit projection remains more than three times higher than the $1.3-billion forecast in Bethlenfalvy's spring budget last year.
He pledged to deliver his next budget by the end of March.

Will Toronto's budget keep up with its skyrocketing repair backlog + Are you driving with an expired licence plate?
