Full text: Good morning. This is the Monday, Feb. 12 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on Toronto's draft budget, the one million Ontarians driving with expired licence plates and why syphilis is making a comeback in Ontario.
DON'T MISSOlivia Chow's budget vows to get the city "back on track" - but by one measure it's heading the wrong way
For all the investments included in Toronto mayor Olivia Chow's 2024 draft budget, by one important measure, the city is headed the wrong way - and it could mean more disruptions for users of the city's public recreation facilities, public transit or roads. Over the next decade, the amount of unfunded capital work required to keep Toronto's assets in good shape is expected to double, from $10.6 billion by the end of last year to $22.7 billion in 2033, Ben Spurr reports. Even though Toronto's draft budget will throw $1.8 billion more at the problem than was allocated last year, things are only getting worse. Here's how city spending may impact you.
By the numbers: Toronto's new budget is dedicating $26 billion to the state of good repair work - the amount of unfunded capital work required to keep its assets in good shape - over the next decade. But will it be enough to keep up with the city's projected 2033 backlog, which has swelled by $4 billion in just the last year?Context: At the start of February, Chow unveiled her proposed budget - including a 9.5 per cent property tax hike (down from the initial 10.5 per cent). The plan will see improvements to transit and public services, while largely shunning police demands. Take a look at the details.More than a million Ontario drivers with expired licence plates are risking $1,000 fines. Are you one of them?
The Star has learned more than a million Ontarians have yet to renew their licence plates and are facing fines up to $1,000 - even though doing so is now free, Robert Benzie reports. In 2022, premier Doug Ford scrapped the $120 annual licence plate renewal fee. In the time since, charges for people with expired plates have skyrocketed, from 5,341 tickets in 2022 to 15,631 in 2023, according to Ontario Provincial Police. What's the state of your plate?
Go deeper: Removing the renewal charge was a signature policy of Ford's after he campaigned on keeping costs down - but the act would cost the provincial treasury more than $1 billion a year.Wait, what? There was some confusion after licence plate renewal stickers were scrapped alongside the annual renewal fee, leading some motorists to think they no longer have to renew at all. Others, forgetting to remove the 2022 renewal sticker from their plates, wound up getting ticketed outside the province by police thinking their plates were expired.Get it done: One's licence plate expiry date is usually the birthday of the owner; motorists can check their licence plate status and renew it online.You can see here if your licence plate has expired and renew your plate online here.Ontario is dealing with syphilis rates not seen in decades
Though once commonly passed from mothers to their children during childbirth, syphilis was long considered a disease of the past. Congenital syphilis was "something you learned about but didn't see," one doctor told the Star's Megan Ogilvie. Now, he said, that's changed. In 2022, the latest data available, there were 27 cases of syphilis being passed to babies - up from five in 2020. It's the largest spike seen in Ontario in decades. The provincial trend mirrors what's happening across Canada and the rest of the world, leading experts to sound alarm bells. The surge in cases signals a dangerous gap in public health, they say. Take a closer look at what's happening, and why.
What we know: Syphilis is a sexually transmitted infection that can be preventable and easily treated with antibiotics. If left untreated, it can cause blindness and paralysis, alongside other dire consequences, from memory problems to organ damage.Across the border: Last month, the U.S. CDC reported roughly 207,000 diagnosed syphilis cases in 2022, the largest number since the 1950s. In Canada, 13,953 cases were detected the same year.WHAT ELSEPierre Poilievre claims 40 offenders were arrested 6,000 times in Vancouver in a year. But is that actually true?We spent 12 hours inside a Toronto ER. Addressing car thefts, a 'shameful' city councillor, 'Teflon Don' and the Niagara wine industry

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/addressing-car-thefts-shameful-city-councillor/docview/2925082577/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Addressing+car+thefts%2C+a+%27shameful%27+city+councillor%2C+%27Teflon+Don%27+and+the+Niagara+wine+industry&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ideas to address car theft issue
Trudeau blames Stephen Harper cuts for auto theft issue, Feb. 8
Justice Minister Arif Virani says the criminal code already contains sufficient legislation to tackle the issues of organized crime and theft, but that there was "absolutely more to do." With 90,000 cars stolen a year, I'd say so.
Car manufacturers definitely need to improve security measures to this theft problem. The adage "there is no honour among thieves" is something to think about and exploit. Offer money to anyone in organized crime or to a person with intimate knowledge for information - no testifying, no charges - just give up names, locations and intelligence about theft rings. The one billion dollars paid out by Canadians insurers, the expense of police investigations, victim trauma and court costs may make this a small price to pay.
Jim Skeels, Toronto
Car companies should provide each owner with a club steering wheel lock device as part of the purchase. It costs around $95 and prevents anyone from driving the car. We don't need more electronics or federal task forces. A car you can't drive isn't much good to a thief. The insurance companies which are currently bleeding money to cover the cost of car thefts could pay for the clubs.
Peter Bradley, Mississauga
Given all the recent focus on car thefts and potential means to prevent such I find it incredible that a major car company has introduced digital technology which turns your compatible smartphone into a digital key. The key can lock/unlock and start your car, and you can share this code by texting it to a friend. Does this not make it easy for crooks to intercept your code and, with a cellphone, drive off with your car? Can we make it any easier for them?
Maurice Sacco, Toronto
Premier on the right track
Ford's actions this week should be greeted with a sigh of relief: Keenan, Feb. 9
Premier Doug Ford's government has so often been the focus of incompetence and even corruption that he didn't wish to add regressive ideology to his resumé, especially if he wants to retain the word progressive as part of the party name. As a populist, he is always eager to do things that seem popular but turn out not to be (invading the Greenbelt, for example). Nor did bucking science and standing in the way of universal vaccination, so he didn't. He is correct to refuse to further complicate the lives of kids who feel compelled to change their gender on the grounds that such kids are already more prone to depression and suicide than their peers. He needs to be reminded that, second only to young people, older, white rural males are most prone to suicide by firearm, so he should support federal and even municipal gun control to the hilt, rather than naysaying it.
Dr. Ron Charach, Toronto
Pickering councillor's actions 'shameful'
Pickering mayor slams councillor's Black history rant as 'racist' and 'unethical,' Feb. 7
In my opinion, there has never been a more shameful politician in Pickering than Lisa Robinson. Her election can stand as a warning to everyone who shares her kind of divisive, racist, homophobic far right views. People must wonder what's going on in Pickering. Well, she was elected because two progressive candidates split the vote and she and her fringe buddies marched up the middle. One local councillor told me he could work with anyone, but not her. If you were to watch her on council, you'd know why. God help us if we make the same mistake federally.
Brian Clow, Pickering
What about the right to live?
Why is a right to die better accommodated than a right to live with quality palliative care? The Star has suggested that the lack of palliative care services may be a contributing factor to this surge. I agree and wonder why a terminal patient in Canada has a constitutional right to choose a medically assisted death but not an equivalent legal right to choose to live out their natural life pain free with quality palliative care.
MAID is readily available throughout Canada while quality palliative care is most often not equally available. Why is a right to die better accommodated than a right to live until natural death? We are told that MAID is all about compassion but I increasingly believe it is more about economics. It is much less expensive for governments to allow a person to end their life prematurely than to pay for quality palliative care or other services necessary to support those who are suffering.
Perhaps instead of looking at extending MAID we should be examining how we can reduce the number of people who feel it necessary to choose MAID by providing the support and services the lack of which have been identified as potential causes for this surge.
Greg Sheehan, Mississauga
Look out for 'Teflon Don!'
Supreme Court leans in favour of Donald Trump's bid to run again, Feb. 8
If 'Teflon Don' wins in 2024, one of his first likely tasks as president in 2025 may be to withdraw from NATO. This will force countries like Canada to fill a huge void in military personnel, equipment and funding assigned to NATO. In other words, a President Donald Trump will cost Canadians billions of dollars. Hopefully the Trudeau government is properly prepared for this undesired scenario without significantly raising our taxes!
Jim McEwen, Bowmanville, Ont.
Cheers to calling out expensive wineries
The Star letter writer hit a bullseye in her scathing indictment of the state of the Niagara wine industry. The pretentious greed of the Niagara wine industry is astounding. This should be a case study in schools of how to destroy tourism, guaranteed business these winemakers have worked so hard for so long to create. My wife and I concur with the letter writer and will not be back to sample or buy the marginal wares of Niagara. This example of greed is in contrast to stories that I read from time to time of diners that have been open for decades and are always full. How do they do it? Volume. Goodbye Niagara.
David Ottenbrite, Cambridge, Ont.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau speaks with Toronto Chief of Police Myron Demkiw before the National Summit on Combatting Auto Theft on Feb. 8, 2024 in Ottawa.

Document 232 of 500

In Danielle Smith's world, ideology trumps sound medical policy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/danielle-smiths-world-ideology-trumps-sound/docview/2925082567/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=In+Danielle+Smith%27s+world%2C+ideology+trumps+sound+medical+policy&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It isn't the argument we could have predicted making and yet here we are. When it comes to transgender rights in Canada, other leaders in the nation would be wise to take a page out of Ontario Premier Doug Ford's book.
Earlier this month, when asked if he would follow Alberta's lead on the issue of trans rights, Premier Ford responded with a definitive "no." "We have a law here," he said, "we're leaving everything alone."
Unfortunately, Alberta Premier Danielle Smith is not leaving everything alone. On the contrary, Smith, who has an ancient Sumerian symbol for "liberty" tattooed on her forearm, intends to encroach on the liberty of others: in this case, gender nonconforming youth and their parents.
Smith recently announced a host of proposed policies that seek to curb transgender rights and access to health care. They include a policy that would demand parental consent for students 15 and under who wish to change their names and pronouns at school. Students aged 16 and 17 will not need consent "but their parents will be notified."
Other policies include a ban on gender-affirming surgery for anyone under 17, as well as a ban on hormone treatment for those under 15. In Smith's Alberta, trans women will be prohibited from competing against cisgender women in single sex sports leagues. And schools, the premier said, will require "parental notification and an opt in requirement for each instance" that gender and sexual orientation are addressed in class.
Though some of Smith's proposals echo pronoun policies in Saskatchewan and New Brunswick, the Alberta premier's gender identity policy push marks the boldest effort by a Canadian leader to crackdown on transgender rights.
These policies target a tiny and profoundly marginalized group. According to one Canadian study, transgender youth are five times more likely than their peers to contemplate suicide, and seven times more likely to attempt it.According to census data, 0.0037 per cent of Albertans over 15 identify as transgender or non-binary. Yet LGBT youth represent between 25-40 per cent of Canada's homeless youth population.
This is because contrary to Smith's recent assertion that it is "rare" for parents to reject or abuse a child on account of their gender identity, it is not nearly rare enough.
It is almost certain that Smith's pronoun-consent policies will "out" children and teens to disapproving or abusive parents. And it is certain that the leader's health care bans will deny trans people the medical care they require to live in dignity.
According to the Alberta Medical Association's Section of Pediatrics, which strongly opposes the premier's policies, gender affirming care is safe for minors. "The effects of puberty-blocking agents are not irreversible," the AMA writes, "and once treatment stops, puberty goes forward. Treatment allows the patient time to determine their options without permanent effects. Puberty blocking actually has benefits for gender-divergent patients by preventing development of mature secondary sex characteristics so that, later in life, the most invasive gender-affirming surgery may not be necessary if the patient moves forward with gender-affirming care."
The premier may be correct when she asserts that internationally, "there is divergence in the medical community" regarding appropriate gender-affirming care for minors. However, if this is the case, surely medical experts are best suited to determine the appropriate treatment options for their patients: not an ideologically driven premier.
As for "bottom surgery" which Smith says she will ban for minors, there is no need for such a ban because the procedure is already off limits to minors across Canada.
In other words, Smith's policies represent a solution in search of a problem. It is telling that when the premier is asked why she wishes to implement these rules -- and why now -- that she consistently falls back on anecdotes about transgender people who regret their medical choices.
These anecdotes may be compelling, but health policy is not written in response to anecdotes. It should be dictated by facts and data. This is what transgender youth in Alberta deserve. Of course, Smith's new policies on transgender rights have nothing to do with smart health policy and everything to do with ideology.
CAPTION: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith has unveiled controversial new policies fortransgender youth that have sparked concerns among medical professionals.

Subject: Bans; LGBTQ rights; Non-binary gender; Gender reassignment surgery; Puberty; Health care; Homeless people; Gender identity; Gender-affirming care; Consent; Transgender persons

Location: Canada

Title: In Danielle Smith's world, ideology trumps sound medical policy

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Feb 12, 2024

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Doug Ford's wage restraint legislation is unconstitutional, appeal court finds

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-wage-restraint-legislation-is/docview/2925082274/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+wage+restraint+legislation+is+unconstitutional%2C+appeal+court+finds&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a victory for nurses, teachers and thousands of unionized public sector workers, Ontario's top court has upheld a ruling that Premier Doug Ford's wage restraint legislation is unconstitutional.
The Court of Appeal released a long-awaited decision Monday finding the Progressive Conservative government infringed upon Charter rights to collective bargaining with Bill 124.
The legislation capped wage increases for most public sector workers at one per cent annually for three years and has been blamed for an exodus of health-care workers during the COVID-19 pandemic as they toiled long hours at the risk of their own health.
"Ontario has failed to demonstrate that the Act is minimally impairing of the respondents' collective bargain rights," said the court's decision.
Unifor President Lana Payne hailed the decision, calling it "a victory for free and fair collective bargaining."
Even as public sector unions awaited the ruling, labour arbitrators have been awarding some public sector workers retroactive pay in what has been a period of high inflation because their contracts included "reopener" clauses.
The latest such award was an extra 6.5 per cent for members of the Ontario Public Service Employees Union.
Nurses, some teachers and hospital workers including cooks, lab technicians, personal support workers and dietary aides have also been awarded pay increases.
The provincial government had appealed a 2022 decision by the Ontario Superior Court of Justice that the wage restraint legislation - passed as an austerity measure to help balance the budget before the COVID-19 pandemic emerged - violated Charter rights to collective bargaining.
"On my view of the evidence, Ontario was not facing a situation in 2019 that justified an infringement on Charter rights," Justice Markus Koehnen wrote in his original ruling.
"Unlike other cases that have upheld wage restraint legislation, Bill 124 sets the wage cap at a rate below which employees were obtaining in free collective bargaining negotiations."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford attends an announcement at Seneca College, in King City, Ont. on Feb. 9, 2024.

Subject: Legislation; Charters; Public sector; Collective bargaining; Pandemics; COVID-19

Business indexing term: Subject: Charters Public sector Collective bargaining

Full text: Ideas to address car-theft issue
PM opens door to longer sentences for car thieves, Feb. 9
Justice Minister Arif Virani says the criminal code already contains sufficient legislation to tackle the issues of organized crime and theft, but that there was "absolutely more to do." With 90,000 cars stolen a year, I'd say so.
Car manufacturers definitely need to improve security measures. The adage "there is no honour among thieves" is something to think about and exploit. Offer money to anyone in organized crime or to a person with intimate knowledge for information - no testifying, no charges - just give up names, locations and intelligence about theft rings.
The $1 billion paid out by Canadian insurers, the expense of police investigations, victim trauma and court costs may make this a small price to pay.
Jim Skeels, Toronto
Car companies should provide each owner with a club steering wheel lock device as part of the purchase. It costs about $95 and prevents anyone from driving the car. We don't need more electronics or federal task forces. A car you can't drive isn't much good to a thief. The insurance companies that are currently bleeding money to cover the cost of thefts could pay for the clubs.
Peter Bradley, Mississauga
Given all the recent focus on car thefts and potential means to prevent such, I find it incredible that a major car company has introduced digital technology that turns your compatible smartphone into a digital key. The key can lock/unlock and start your car, and you can share this code by texting it to a friend. Does this not make it easy for crooks to intercept your code and, with a cellphone, drive off with your car? Can we make it any easier for them?
Maurice Sacco, Toronto
Premier is on
the right track
Ford's actions bring sigh of relief, Keenan, Feb. 9
Premier Doug Ford's government has so often been the focus of incompetence and even corruption that he didn't wish to add regressive ideology to his resumé. As a populist, he is always eager to do things that seem popular but turn out not to be (invading the Greenbelt, for example). Nor did bucking science and standing in the way of universal vaccination, so he didn't. He is correct to refuse to further complicate the lives of kids who feel compelled to change their gender on the grounds that such kids are already more prone to depression and suicide than their peers. He needs to be reminded that, second only to young people, older, white rural males are most prone to suicide by firearm, so he should support federal and even municipal gun control to the hilt, rather than naysaying it.
Dr. Ron Charach, Toronto
Shameful actions from councillor
Councillor slammed
for 'racist' rant, Feb. 7
In my opinion, there has never been a more shameful politician in Pickering than Lisa Robinson. Her election can stand as a warning to everyone who shares her kind of divisive, racist, homophobic, far right views. People must wonder what's going on in Pickering. Well, she was elected because two progressive candidates split the vote and she and her fringe buddies marched up the middle. One local councillor told me he could work with anyone, but not her. God help us if we make the same mistake federally.
Brian Clow, Pickering
What about
the right to live?
Why is a right to die better accommodated than a right to live with quality palliative care? The Star has suggested that the lack of palliative care services may be a contributing factor to this surge. I agree and wonder why a terminal patient in Canada has a constitutional right to choose a medically assisted death but not an equivalent legal right to choose to live out their natural life pain free with quality palliative care.
MAID is readily available throughout Canada, while quality palliative care is most often not equally available. We are told that MAID is all about compassion, but I increasingly believe it is more about economics. It is much less expensive for governments to allow a person to end their life prematurely than to pay for quality palliative care or other services necessary to support those who are suffering.
Perhaps instead of looking at extending MAID, we should be examining how we can reduce the number of people who feel it necessary to choose MAID by providing the support and services the lack of which have been identified as potential causes for this surge.
Greg Sheehan, Mississauga
Look out for 'Teflon Don!'
Supreme Court leans in
favour of Donald Trump's bid to run again, Feb. 8
If "Teflon Don" wins in 2024, one of his first likely tasks as U.S. president in 2025 may be to withdraw from NATO. This will force countries like Canada to fill a huge void in military personnel, equipment and funding assigned to NATO. In other words, a president Trump will cost Canadians billions of dollars.
Jim McEwen, Bowmanville, Ont.
There's much
to wine about
Prices in Niagara aren't
helping to boost tourism, Letter, Feb. 9
The Star letter writer hit a bullseye in her scathing indictment of the state of the Niagara wine industry. The pretentious greed of the Niagara wine industry is astounding. This should be a case study in schools of how to destroy tourism, guaranteed business these winemakers have worked so hard for so long to create. My wife and I concur with the letter writer and will not be back to sample or buy the marginal wares of Niagara. This example of greed is in contrast to stories that I read from time to time of diners that have been open for decades and are always full. How do they do it? Volume. Goodbye Niagara.
David Ottenbrite,
Cambridge, Ont.

Document 235 of 500

More than one million Ontario drivers with expired licence plates are risking $1,000 fines. Are you one of them?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/more-than-one-million-ontario-drivers-with/docview/2924895556/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=More+than+one+million+Ontario+drivers+with+expired+licence+plates+are+risking+%241%2C000+fines.+Are+you+one+of+them%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There are more than one million expired licence plates in Ontario because motorists are forgetting to renew them now that they're free, the Star has learned.
Even though Premier Doug Ford scrapped the annual renewal fee of up to $120 before the 2022 election, vehicle owners still must keep their plates up to date or face Highway Traffic Act fines of as much as $1,000.
But about seven per cent of the 13.6 million plates on Ontario roads have not been renewed by forgetful permit holders - leading to a rise in charges.
As of January, there were exactly 1,015,139 expired plates in the province.
That's an improvement over the 1,107,098 that were not up to date in December.
A spokesperson for Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria urged motorists to check at ontario.ca/page/renew-your-licence-plate to see if their plates were valid.
"We continue to encourage individuals to renew online at Ontario.ca/Renew if their licence plate is expired or cancelled," said Dakota Brasier.
Brasier said the government "has made life more affordable and convenient for over eight million vehicle owners by eliminating licence plate renewal fees" and is working toward other ways to make it easier to keep plates validated.
"Also, individuals can sign up for digital reminders to receive their renewal notices by email or text," she noted.
According to the Ontario Provincial Police, Highway Traffic Act charges have skyrocketed since the paid renewal stickers were phased out two years ago.
In 2023, there were 15,631 tickets issued to motorists lacking a "currently validated permit for the vehicle," said the OPP's Robert Simpson.
That compares with 5,341 charges in 2022 and 1,556 in 2021, though the COVID-19 pandemic that kept Ontarians at home and off the roads could be a factor in those lower numbers.
Removing the renewal charge in 2022, which costs the treasury more than $1 billion annually, has been a signature policy for Ford as his Progressive Conservative government tries to address cost-of-living challenges.
"We're on a mission to keep costs down," the premier told 1,000 Tory delegates Feb. 3 at the party's Niagara Falls policy conference.
"We cut 10.7 cents per litre off the cost of gas. We scrapped the Liberal road tolls and eliminated the licence plate sticker fee," he said.
Along with the Ministry of Transportation's digital tools, motorists can also check when their plate expires and renew them in person at ServiceOntario outlets.
All passenger vehicles - including cars, SUVs and vans - under 3,000 kg are covered under the policy and a plate can be renewed for up to two years at a time within six months of its expiry date. Plates for motorcycles and mopeds must also be kept up to date.
The expiry date is usually the birthday of the vehicle owner.
Validation stickers are still required for heavy commercial vehicles, like large trucks and snowplows.
Damaged, lost or stolen plates cost $59 to replace and that can only be done in person at a ServiceOntario counter. That's the same charge as for a new plate registration.
Because many motorists still have their old 2022 renewal stickers affixed to their plates, there have been instances of drivers getting tickets out of province because local police think the plates have expired.
The Canadian Automobile Association has recommended motorists keep a printout of proof of renewal in their glovebox even though that is not legally required in Ontario.
When the change was unveiled two years ago, the province said owners could bring their information to any ServiceOntario outlet for a printout free of charge if they need one for travel or employment.
CAPTION: Ontario drivers no longer have to pay to licence their vehicles but they do have to keep registering them. Many people have not.

Subject: Automobiles; Automobile drivers; Fees & charges; Roads & highways; Vehicles

Company / organization: Name: ServiceOntario; NAICS: 926120

Title: More than one million Ontario drivers with expired licence plates are risking $1,000 fines. Are you one of them?

Document 236 of 500

A transgender policy rooted in politics

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/transgender-policy-rooted-politics/docview/2924894817/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+transgender+policy+rooted+in+politics&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It isn't the argument we could have predicted making and yet here we are. When it comes to transgender rights in Canada, other leaders in the nation would be wise to take a page out of Ontario Premier Doug Ford's book.
When asked this month if he would follow Alberta's lead on the issue of trans rights, Premier Ford responded with a definitive "no." "We have a law here," he said, "we're leaving everything alone."
Unfortunately, Alberta Premier Danielle Smith is not leaving everything alone. On the contrary, Smith, who has an ancient Sumerian symbol for "liberty" tattooed on her forearm, intends to encroach on the liberty of others: in this case, gender-nonconforming youth and their parents.
Smith recently announced a host of proposed policies that seek to curb transgender rights and access to health care. They include a policy that would demand parental consent for students 15 and under who wish to change their names and pronouns at school. Students aged 16 and 17 will not need consent "but their parents will be notified."
Other policies include a ban on gender-affirming surgery for anyone under 17, as well as a ban on hormone treatment for those under 15. In Smith's Alberta, trans women will be prohibited from competing against cisgender women in single-sex sports leagues. And schools, the premier said, will require "parental notification and an opt in requirement for each instance" that gender and sexual orientation are addressed in class.
Although some of Smith's proposals echo pronoun policies in Saskatchewan and New Brunswick, the Alberta premier's gender-identity policy push marks the boldest effort by a Canadian leader to crack down on transgender rights.
These policies target a tiny and profoundly marginalized group. According to one Canadian study, transgender youth are five times more likely than their peers to contemplate suicide, and seven times more likely to attempt it. According to census data, 0.0037 per cent of Albertans over 15 identify as transgender or non-binary. Yet LGBTQ youth represent 25 to 40 per cent of Canada's homeless youth population.
This is because contrary to Smith's recent assertion that it is "rare" for parents to reject or abuse a child on account of their gender identity, it is not nearly rare enough.
It is almost certain that Smith's pronoun-consent policies will "out" children and teens to disapproving or abusive parents. And it is certain that the leader's health-care bans will deny trans people the medical care they require to live in dignity.
According to the Alberta Medical Association's Section of Pediatrics, which strongly opposes the premier's policies, gender-affirming care is safe for minors. "The effects of puberty-blocking agents are not irreversible," the AMA writes, "and once treatment stops, puberty goes forward. Treatment allows the patient time to determine their options without permanent effects. Puberty blocking actually has benefits for gender-divergent patients by preventing development of mature secondary sex characteristics so that, later in life, the most invasive gender-affirming surgery may not be necessary if the patient moves forward with gender-affirming care."
The premier may be correct when she asserts that internationally, "there is divergence in the medical community" regarding appropriate gender-affirming care for minors. However, if this is the case, surely medical experts are best suited to determine the appropriate treatment options for their patients, not an ideologically driven premier.
As for "bottom surgery," which Smith says she will ban for minors, there is no need for such a ban because the procedure is already off limits to minors across Canada.
In other words, Smith's policies represent a solution in search of a problem. It is telling that when the premier is asked why she wishes to implement these rules - and why now - she consistently falls back on anecdotes about transgender people who regret their medical choices.
These anecdotes may be compelling, but health policy is not written in response to anecdotes. It should be dictated by facts and data. This is what transgender youth in Alberta deserve. Of course, Smith's new policies on transgender rights have nothing to do with smart health policy and everything to do with ideology.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: ;

Subject: Bans; LGBTQ rights; Non-binary gender; Puberty; Gender reassignment surgery; Homeless people; Gender identity; Gender-affirming care; Transgender persons; Consent

Are your vehicle's licence plates up to date?: More than a million risking nes over expired permits

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/are-your-vehicles-licence-plates-up-date/docview/2924894745/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 238 of 500

Olivia Chow's budget vows to get the city 'back on track.' By one measure it's heading the wrong way

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/olivia-chows-budget-vows-get-city-back-on-track/docview/2924894721/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Olivia+Chow%27s+budget+vows+to+get+the+city+%27back+on+track.%27+By+one+measure+it%27s+heading+the+wrong+way&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Like any hockey parent, Judy Alexander is used to shuttling her kids to and from the rink a few times a week.
Her boys, 13 and 16, play competitively, which means they're on the ice so often she and her husband have to be in two places at once.
"A lot of times we can't watch the games together because one's at a practice, one's at a game," Alexander said.
It makes things easier if the rink they need to get to is close by. Scarborough Gardens, a 10-minute drive from their home in the Beach and the home rink of team one son played on, was a favourite.
But in early 2020, the city abruptly closed the 70-year-old facility when it found structural problems in the roof. An inspection eventually determined the damage was extensive enough it would be cheaper to tear down the structure than replace it.
Her son's team had to relocate to a facility in north Scarborough, forcing Alexander to spend up to 40 minutes in traffic. The smaller pool of available rinks has also left teams fighting for scarcer ice time.
When a local arena shuts down "the community feels it," Alexander said.
Hockey parents, along with anyone else who uses Toronto's public recreation facilities, takes public transit, or drives on city roads should brace for more closures and disruptions.
That's because for all the investments Mayor Olivia Chow has included in her 2024 budget to get Toronto "back on track," by one important measure the city is still headed in the wrong direction.
Toronto's state of good repair backlog - the amount of unfunded capital work required to keep its assets in good shape - is set to more than double over the next decade, from $10.6 billion by the end of last year to $22.7 billion in 2033, according to budget documents.
The parks department, which oversees arenas, is among the divisions with the largest unfunded needs, along with the city transportation and real estate divisions, the TTC and Toronto Community Housing Corporation.
Even as the city throws more money at the problem, it's getting worse. The 2024 budget would commit $26 billion to state of good repair work over the next decade, $1.8 billion more than was allocated last year. Among the funded projects is the Scarborough Gardens replacement, which is set to be complete by spring 2025.
Yet the projected backlog the city will face by 2033 has grown by $4 billion since just last year, due to aging assets, inflation, labour shortages and the city pausing some work during the pandemic to paper over operating shortfalls.
The projections don't include the planned upload of the Gardiner Expressway and Don Valley Parkway as part of the "new deal" Chow negotiated with Premier Doug Ford in November. The agreement could take at least $3 billion in capital costs off the city's books, but would still leave Toronto facing a daunting backlog.
Coun. Lily Cheng (Willowdale) called the numbers "apocalyptic," and said she worries about the state of the city her children will inherit.
Chow's 2024 budget, which will be debated by council on Wednesday, would impose a historic 9.5 per cent property tax increase on residents, including a 1.5 per cent for the city building fund dedicated to transit and housing.
But Cheng said as long as the backlog continues to grow, taxpayers won't be getting bang for their buck. "We're asking for more, but at the same time we are really just maintaining a mediocre standard," she said.
As Scarborough Gardens demonstrates, the problem snowballs the longer it goes unaddressed. By the time the roof damage was discovered, it had grown bad enough the city had to issue a $490,000 emergency contract to shore up the structure for inspections. The contract grew to $1.8 million before the city decided to rebuild the arena, at a cost of at least $32 million.
Signs of Toronto's infrastructure decay, big and small, are everywhere: in crumbling public housing, signal problems that cause subway delays and broken air conditioning that last year sent indoor temperatures at a downtown community centre soaring to 40 C.
And then there's Toronto's road network. The city transportation department reported that the city's major roads scored 57 on the pavement quality index (PQI), a measurement of a roadway's state of repair. That's well below the score of 70 that would be considered well-maintained.
At current spending levels the city can only address about one-third of its road repair needs, and staff project the condition of major roads will "deteriorate steadily," to a PQI of 49.
The TTC is projected to be biggest single driver of the backlog increase, adding $8.2 billion by 2033. The agency has warned that unless it's able to replace aging vehicles and infrastructure, system reliability will be "at risk due to possible failure." The province pledged up to $758 million for new 55 subway trains for Line 2 as part of the new deal, but the funding is contingent on a matching promise from the federal government.
Chow says her budget, the first of her tenure, will help tame the backlog. The mayor is proposing leveraging funds freed up through the new deal to create a $50 million "back on track fund," that could be used to accelerate urgent work like fixing potholes and repairing park infrastructure.
Chow said she's also asked city staff to come up with "clear criteria" to determine which repair projects are prioritized, which would take into account the lifespan of the asset and focus on repairs that would benefit the most residents and address environmental concerns. That would help ensure fixes are made before assets deteriorate beyond repair.
"We'll do it systematically," Chow said.
Coun. Dianne Saxe said given the size of the backlog, the mayor's $50-million fund is "a drop in the bucket" but "still an important drop."
The University-Rosedale representative said residents should know that the 2024 budget "is not a budget that will deliver significant improvements.
"This is a budget to hold the line and keep services running, and slightly slow the rate of deterioration."
CAPTION: A rendering of the new arena and city notice is posted outside where the Scarborough Gardens used to be on Birchmount Road.Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow gives a keynote speech at the Canadian Club at the Fairmont Royal York in Toronto on Jan. 29.An inspection eventually determined the damage to Scarborough Gardens was extensive enough it would be cheaper to tear down the structure than replace it.

Subject: Capital costs; Stadiums; Infrastructure; Roads & highways

City faces growing backlog of repair projects: Costs expected to reach $22.7B over next decade

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/city-faces-growing-backlog-repair-projects/docview/2924894633/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City+faces+growing+backlog+of+repair+projects%3A+Costs+expected+to+reach+%2422.7B+over+next+decade&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto's state of good repair backlog - the amount of unfunded capital work required to keep its assets in good shape - is set to more than double over the next decade, from $10.6 billion by the end of last year to $22.7 billion in 2033, according to budget documents.

Like any hockey parent, Judy Alexander is used to shuttling her kids to and from the rink a few times a week. Her boys, 13 and 16, play competitively, which means they're on the ice so often she and her husband have to be in two places at once.
"A lot of times we can't watch the games together because one's at a practice, one's at a game," Alexander said.
It makes things easier if the rink they need to get to is close by.
Scarborough Gardens, a 10-minute drive from their home in the Beach and the home rink of the team one son played on, was a favourite.
But, in early 2020, the city abruptly closed the 70-year-old facility when it found structural problems in the roof. An inspection eventually determined the damage was extensive enough it would be cheaper to tear down the structure than replace it.
Her son's team had to relocate to a facility in north Scarborough, forcing Alexander to spend up to 40 minutes in traffic. The smaller pool of available rinks has also left teams fighting for scarcer ice time.

But Cheng said, as long as the backlog continues to grow, taxpayers won't be getting bang for their buck. "We're asking for more but, at the same time, we are really just maintaining a mediocre standard," she said.

By the time the roof damage was discovered, it had grown bad enough the city had to issue a $490,000 emergency contract to shore up the structure for inspections. The contract grew to $1.8 million before the city decided to rebuild the arena, at a cost of at least $32 million. The replacement is set to be complete by spring 2025.
And then there's Toronto's road network. The city transportation department reported the city's major roads scored 57 on the pavement quality index (PQI), a measurement of a roadway's state of repair. That's well below the score of 70 that would be considered well-maintained.
At current spending levels, the city can only address about one-third of its road repair needs, and staff projects the condition of major roads will "deteriorate steadily," to a PQI of 49.
The TTC is projected to be biggest single driver of the backlog increase, adding $8.2 billion by 2033. The agency has warned that, unless it's able to replace aging vehicles and infrastructure, system reliability will be "at risk due to possible failure." The province pledged up to $758 million for new 55 subway trains for Line 2 as part of the new deal, but the funding is contingent on a matching promise from the federal government.

That would help ensure fixes are made before assets deteriorate beyond repair.
"We'll do it systematically," Chow said.

The University-Rosedale representative said residents should know the 2024 budget "is not a budget that will deliver significant improvements."
"This is a budget to hold the line and keep services running, and slightly slow the rate of deterioration."
CREDIT: Ben Spurr Toronto Star

Document 240 of 500

'Unfair and undemocratic': Decision to appoint former Ford minister in federal riding sparks complaints

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/unfair-undemocratic/docview/2924647382/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Unfair+and+undemocratic%27%3A+Decision+to+appoint+former+Ford+minister+in+federal+riding+sparks+complaints&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA The federal Conservative party's decision to parachute former provincial cabinet minister Parm Gill into a Milton riding is being blasted by another would-be candidate and prompted a riding association to fire off an angry letter of protest to party headquarters.
"We believe this appointment would not be in the best interest of the community or the Conservative Party of Canada. Indeed, it would be both unfair and undemocratic," says the letter addressed to national council, the party's governing body, a copy of which was obtained by the Star.
It was signed by 19 members of the board of directors of the riding currently next door - Wellington-Halton Hills - which will soon see a big chunk of its turf hived off and folded into a new Milton riding under redrawn federal boundaries effective in April.
The riding association board members said they intend to defy the decision to appoint Gill and thereby close off a contested nomination race, and plan to establish a new electoral district association for the new Milton riding to come, and "conduct a candidate selection process in a fair, open and transparent manner."
"We intend to follow the candidate selection process as laid out in the Constitution of the Conservative Party of Canada. Anyone is welcome to submit their name as a candidate and we know there are several qualified individuals interested."
The letter notes that 40 per cent of the new riding - to be called Milton East-Halton Hills South - would come from what is currently called Wellington-Halton Hills, and expresses concern that with the appointment of Gill, who does not live in that newly drawn riding, that part of the riding "may not be well represented."
"We are asking that you respect the democratic processes set out in the Constitution of the Conservative Party of Canada, as well as the wishes of the party membership," they wrote.
A board member confirmed to the Star on Friday the party has acknowledged receipt of the letter.
Sources say at least four people are interested in running to carry the banner for the Pierre Poilievre-led Conservative party in the next federal election.
With Poilievre's Conservatives hitting 43 per cent support in the most recent Abacus Data poll that was shared with the Star - putting the party in majority government territory if that level of public support holds - interest in Conservative nomination contests is running high.
Many area Conservatives believe that once Milton - now represented by Liberal Adam van Koeverden - is split into two ridings, the Conservatives have a good shot at winning both.
D'Arcy Keene, a councillor in Georgetown which will fall into the new Milton East riding, is a member of the board of directors who recused himself from the meeting where the decision to send the letter was unanimously approved and signed.
In an interview with the Star, he said he told the party's federal executive director before Christmas he intended to run for the nomination, and he queried Gill personally, after hearing rumours about a week before Gill's jump back into federal politics in late January.
Gill told him at that time "Never say never, but at this point," said Keene, Gill said he was not running. The Georgetown councillor told Gill then that he was "100 per cent" sure he was going to run for the nomination. "I don't believe in appointments. I've never believed in them," he said. "I think it's a violation of democracy."
An open nomination contest, he said, "is the ultimate grassroots participation. Like, you get a few hundred people in a room, who get to choose their candidate to run. And to take that away from people is a slap in the face."
Keene said he would "love" for Poilievre, the party leader, to step in and "see what's going on." He added perhaps the party headquarters hopes that "we'll just go away."
He said that's not going to happen.
"I'm in this to win it. I feel very passionately I'm the right guy for the job to represent my community."
Gill, formerly a Doug Ford cabinet minister and MPP for Milton and a former federal MP for Brampton, announced late last month that he was quitting Queen's Park to jump back to federal politics.
In a statement to the Star, the Conservative party's communications director Sarah Fischer responded that "Parm Gill was nominated in the riding of Milton. Michael Chong is the MP for Wellington-Halton Hills who was named as the candidate after meeting the requirements set out by National Council."
"It is bizarre that his (Chong's) EDA (electoral district association) Board would be writing the Party about a nomination in another riding - one that is currently not held by a Conservative. Or, are they asking to open up the riding of Wellington-Halton Hills so that Michael Chong can be challenged? This will not happen because Mr. Chong, as well as Mr. Gill, are already nominated."
Chong will be running in Wellington-Halton Hills North, where he lives, once the redrawn boundaries take effect. Conservative politics in the Milton riding have seen intense battles play out before, with a former deputy leader Lisa Raitt having been booted off the board of directors in a riding she once represented but lost in 2015.
Fisher said that she could not address the question of which of the two new Milton ridings Gill will carry the Conservative banner for because "new ridings technically do not exist."
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star

Subject: Conservatism; Election districts; Nominations; Boards of directors; Political parties

Business indexing term: Subject: Boards of directors

Location: Canada

Company / organization: Name: Conservative Party-Canada; NAICS: 813940

Title: 'Unfair and undemocratic':   Decision to appoint former Ford minister in federal riding sparks complaints

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: A.3

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Feb 11, 2024

Section: News

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 03190781

Document 241 of 500

Conservative riding association protests 'unfair and undemocratic' decision to parachute former Doug Ford minister into federal riding

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/conservative-riding-association-protests-unfair/docview/2924524991/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Conservative+riding+association+protests+%27unfair+and+undemocratic%27+decision+to+parachute+former+Doug+Ford+minister+into+federal+riding&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 242 of 500

Justin Trudeau's attacks on Pierre Poilievre not working, poll suggests

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeaus-attacks-on-pierre-poilievre-not/docview/2924336117/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau%27s+attacks+on+Pierre+Poilievre+not+working%2C+poll+suggests&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals keep taking punches at Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, but a new poll suggests they aren't landing.
The Abacus Data survey, provided exclusively to the Star, suggests a 19-point lead for the Conservatives over the governing Liberals, with the support of 43 per cent of those polled compared to 24 per cent.
The three-point jump for the Tories since the last time Abacus surveyed comes as its latest poll shows the government's approval rating is down by four percentage points, with 59 per cent of respondents saying they disapprove of the job Trudeau's government is doing.
In the survey, Abacus sought to probe people's perceptions of the Trudeau Liberals, putting five phrases to respondents to see which they thought best described the party - including whether the government is focused on the right priorities, listens to Canadians and is transparent and accountable. For each, more than 40 per cent said "not at all."
The poll surveyed 2,398 adults between Feb. 1 to 7. Online polls cannot be considered truly random, but a comparative random sample of this size would have a margin of error of plus or minus two percentage points 19 times out of 20.
"Overall, the results are not good and underscore just how challenging the environment is for this government and Mr. Trudeau," Abacus Data CEO David Coletto said.
From a cabinet retreat in late January through to the past week, the Trudeau Liberals have upped their attacks on Poilievre to tamp down his momentum with voters.
Just ahead of Abacus going into the field, Trudeau tried to link Poilievre to the right-wing rhetoric of U.S. TV host Tucker Carlson, and accused Poilievre of siding with apologists for Russian President Vladimir Putin.
After Alberta Premier Danielle Smith unveiled policies to restrict medical options for transgender youth, Liberals castigated Poilievre for not speaking up against them, accusing him of being indifferent to the lives of kids.
Trudeau's team also tried to undercut Poilievre's accusations that the government is responsible for high grocery prices, pointing out that the firm of his chief adviser Jenni Byrne has lobbied for Loblaws. The firm has lobbied for the grocery giant in Ontario but not specifically about food prices.
Poilievre and the Conservatives have been punching back. To the accusations on lobbying, they put together their own list of former and current members of Trudeau's inner circle who've also lobbied for the company.
They've blasted Trudeau for using transgender policies as a dangerous wedge issue, and also put the government on its backfoot over its main message for the last week - promised measures to combat auto theft.
Just before the Liberals could unveil a new plan, Poilievre unveiled his own, forcing the Liberals on the defensive.
In an interview with Citytv on Friday, Trudeau sloughed off the current polling landscape for his party in general, pointing out the next election is still a year-and-a-half away.
"We were way behind in the polls before the 2015 election where I won a majority, we were way behind in the polls before the 2019 election. Polls go up and down," he said.
The Tories did, in the end, vote against an updated free trade deal with Ukraine - that vote is the reason Trudeau accused Poilievre of being a Putin apologist. Poilievre insists it is because of the carbon price reference in the deal, and his party still backs Ukraine.
While Trudeau brought that up too in the Citytv interview, signalling his party won't let that issue drop, Coletto said the risk for the Liberals in general is that no one is listening to Trudeau anymore.
"They can't cut through because most people aren't listening anymore to Trudeau and until they start spending money on direct-to-voter advertising, that will be the case," he said.
"The fundamental problem facing the Liberals is not a popular Conservative leader they need to bring back to Earth. It's their unpopular leader who the country is largely tired of."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau attends an announcement at Seneca College, in King City, Ont. on Feb. 9, 2024.

Document 243 of 500

They couldn't say no to community: Downsizing couple and their kids loved Port Credit's waterfront - so they all decided to buy homes there

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/they-couldnt-say-no-community/docview/2924183171/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=They+couldn%27t+say+no+to+community%3A+Downsizing+couple+and+their+kids+loved+Port+Credit%27s+waterfront+-+so+they+all+decided+to+buy+homes+there&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Call it a family affair!
As Rosemary Bain prepared to retire last year from her job as an investment adviser, she and her husband, David, who had retired four years before, looked to downsize from the detached home in Mississauga where they'd raised their four children. The Bains enjoyed their proximity to Port Credit. As the couple contemplated downsizing, some of their adult kids were ready to enter the real estate market.
"To me, being close to the water was important, as I really enjoy walking along Lake Ontario," says Bain, who grew up in the Maritimes before moving to Ontario in 1966.
The Bains and three of their children decided to buy units at Brightwater, a master-planned waterfront community on 72 acres in Port Credit, on the remediated site of a former oil refinery.
When complete, the community by Port Credit West Village Partners will have 2,995 residences, including townhomes and condos, 150 affordable housing units, 300,000 square feet of retail, restaurants and office space, and a new elementary school. There'll be 18 acres of new green space, including a nine-acre waterfront park, European-style promenades, pedestrian mews and public plazas.
The development aims to set a benchmark for future waterfront community designs in Canada and globally. It has been recognized with the Award of Merit for Healthy Communities by the Canadian Institute of Planners.
"I'd been following this particular project for a number of years and really liked it," says Bain. "It's on the water, and it will be a little village within a village, with a Farmboy and LCBO, and there's a Loblaws across the street. I enjoy the walkability. And it's easy to get to downtown Toronto by GO train. All of that was very appealing."
She and her husband will soon move into a 2,400-square-foot, three-bedroom townhome. It's smaller than their detached home and will give them a turnkey lifestyle. "We are hoping to travel, and didn't want a big yard and all the work that goes with that."
Their son, Nathan, 30, a software developer, and his fiancée, Julliette Hebert, a teacher, moved into their 530-square-foot, one bedroom-plus-den condo at Brightwater last fall.
"I grew up five minutes from here and always loved Port Credit," Nathan says. "I always envisioned living here. We'd been living with Julliette's parents in Scarborough, and that was awesome, but I always wanted to come back to the west side. We really wanted to buy something. This opportunity came up and we jumped at it."
His younger brother bought the unit next door, and he's renting it out for a few years before moving in himself. Their sister has a condo on the same floor she purchased as an investment unit.
"This is the first building that people have moved into, and watching the community grow from the ground up is cool," he says. "It's going to be a great neighbourhood. We are really excited about what the developers are going to do. We have a dog we share with Julliette's parents and he loves it here. He loves the waterfront." Although they commute to their jobs - his is in Markham, hers in Scarborough - Nathan is able to work from home two days a week. He's also a musician and enjoys playing gigs close to home.
Christina Giannone, vice-president, planning and development, for Brightwater, lives in Port Credit with her young family and says Brightwater is intended to be a place for everyone. The mix of purchasers reflects that, from people born in the area who wanted to come back, as Nathan Bain does, and people, such as his parents, who are looking to downsize.
Giannone says the goal is to add to the fabric of the neighbourhood and bring additional amenities that Brightwater residents and others in the area can enjoy, with new green spaces, retail shops, public spaces and schools. She can picture her own children playing in the new parks there.
"The water and Port Credit is such a special place," Giannone says. "It's a beautiful little village and when you walk along the pier in summer, it feels like the place you go to vacation. Before Brightwater, there weren't a lot of options in Port Credit. It will be a 21st village, but connected to what makes Port Credit special."
Location Mississauga Rd., south of Lakeshore Rd. W., Mississauga
Description Waterfront community in Port Credit with five public parks, retail, restaurants, office space and new school and close to Port Credit's amenities, and to transit. Will have 2,995 residences, including townhomes and condos, and affordable units. Connected to Lake Ontario and the Waterfront Trail.
Bridge House at Brightwater Studio, one-bedroom, one-bedroom with den, two-bedroom, and two-bedroom with den. Limited selection of two- and three-bedroom townhouses. Prices from mid $600,000s. Private residents' club with 30,000 square feet of amenities, including fitness centre, sauna, rooftop lounge, swim spa, sundeck, barbecues and party rooms.
The Mason Towns Two- and three-bedroom townhomes, from 1,393 sq. ft. to 2,266 sq. ft. Starting from $1,474,900.
Brightwater Towns New luxury townhomes with quick closings, 1,945 sq. ft. to 2,430 sq. ft. From $1.96 million.
Developer Port Credit West Village Partners (Kilmer Group, DiamondCorp, Dream, Fram + Slokker)
CREDIT: Tracy Hanes Special to the Star

Document 244 of 500

Arbitrator awards 2.75% pay hike to Ontario teachers: Retroactive bump compensates for province's 1% cap

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/arbitrator-awards-2-75-pay-hike-ontario-teachers/docview/2924182926/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Arbitrator+awards+2.75%25+pay+hike+to+Ontario+teachers%3A+Retroactive+bump+compensates+for+province%27s+1%25+cap&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An arbitrator has awarded Ontario's public school teachers a 2.75-per-cent retroactive pay raise to make up for the last of three years of imposed wage caps that were deemed unconstitutional by the courts.
That raise is on top of a 0.75-per-cent bump for each of the first two years.
When the one per cent yearly wage-capped increase is factored in, and with compounding, teachers will receive about 7.41 per cent in pay raises in total for the three years.
"This should never have happened," said Karen Littlewood, president of the Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation, of Bill 124. "This decision reinforces what we have known all along - our members have been underpaid and undervalued for years while the Progressive Conservatives underfunded public education and shortchanged Ontario's students."
Karen Brown, president of the Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario, said the decision "sends a clear message to the Ford government that they must never circumvent bargaining or trample on workers' democratic rights again. Workers have a right to an unrestricted bargaining process. This is guaranteed by the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms and must be upheld."
The award also affects any future wage increases for teachers, as their base pay will now be higher.
The decision by arbitrator William Kaplan, released late Friday afternoon, comes as Ontario's Court of Appeal is set to release a ruling Monday on the Ford government's challenge of an earlier decision that Bill 124 infringed on the collective bargaining rights of public sector workers, whose unions had turned to the legal system to fight it.
The 2019 legislation set a maximum wage increase of one per cent per year for three years.
Several education unions, however, opted out of that court challenge after agreeing with the province to allow an arbitrator to rule on the back pay.
The Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario and the Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation represent about 145,000 members in total.
About 55,000 education workers, represented by CUPE's Ontario School Board Council of Unions, are also entitled to the 2.75-per-cent raise as part of a deal they negotiated with the province and school boards.
"The Ford Conservative government never should have illegally interfered in collective bargaining and suppressed the wages of Ontario's lowest-paid education workers - or any public sector workers at all," said Rod McGee, president of Ontario School Board Council of Unions.
Ontario nurses and other groups have also been awarded pay raises to make up for the three years Bill 124 affected their wages.
The education payouts alone will be costly for the provincial treasury. After the previous Liberal government forced contracts on teachers in 2012 amid an austerity push, courts ruled that move unconstitutional, which led to payouts of $103 million for public elementary teachers, $50 million for public secondary teachers and $56.7 million for CUPE school workers from provincial coffers.
The Ford government now has 60 days to provide school boards with funding to cover the retroactive pay raises.
CAPTION: Public sector workers protest in March 2022 against Ontario's move to cap wage hikes.Justin Tang THE CANADIAN PRESS file photo
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 245 of 500

No punch in attacks on Poilievre: New Abacus survey suggests Conservatives have 19-point lead over Liberals

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/no-punch-attacks-on-poilievre/docview/2924182868/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+punch+in+attacks+on+Poilievre%3A+New+Abacus+survey+suggests+Conservatives+have+19-point+lead+over+Liberals&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.19&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals keep throwing punches at Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, but a new poll suggests they aren't landing.
The Abacus Data survey, provided exclusively to the Star, suggests a 19-point lead for the Conservatives over the governing Liberals, with the support of 43 per cent of those polled compared to 24 per cent.
The three-point jump for the Tories since the last time Abacus surveyed comes as its latest poll shows the government's approval rating is down by four percentage points, with 59 per cent of respondents saying they disapprove of the job Trudeau's government is doing.
In the survey, Abacus sought to probe people's perceptions of the Trudeau Liberals, putting five phrases to respondents to see which they thought best described the party - including whether the government is focused on the right priorities, listens to Canadians and is transparent and accountable. For each, more than 40 per cent said "not at all."
The poll surveyed 2,398 adults between Feb. 1 to 7. Online polls cannot be considered truly random, but a comparative random sample of this size would have a margin of error of plus or minus two percentage points 19 times out of 20.
"Overall, the results are not good and underscore just how challenging the environment is for this government and Mr. Trudeau," Abacus Data CEO David Coletto said.
From a cabinet retreat in late January through to the past week, the Trudeau Liberals have upped their attacks on Poilievre to tamp down his momentum with voters.
Just ahead of Abacus going into the field, Trudeau tried to link Poilievre to the right-wing rhetoric of U.S. TV host Tucker Carlson, and accused Poilievre of siding with apologists for Russian President Vladimir Putin.
After Alberta Premier Danielle Smith unveiled policies to restrict medical options for transgender youth, Liberals castigated Poilievre for not speaking up against them, accusing him of being indifferent to the lives of kids.
Trudeau's team also tried to undercut Poilievre's accusations that the government is responsible for high grocery prices, pointing out that the firm of his chief adviser Jenni Byrne has lobbied for Loblaws. The firm has lobbied for the grocery giant in Ontario but not specifically about food prices.
Poilievre and the Conservatives have been punching back. To the accusations on lobbying, they put together their own list of former and current members of Trudeau's inner circle who've also lobbied for the company.
They've blasted Trudeau for using transgender policies as a dangerous wedge issue, and also put the government on its backfoot over its main message for the last week - promised measures to combat auto theft.
Just before the Liberals could unveil a new plan, Poilievre unveiled his own, forcing the Liberals on the defensive.
In an interview with Citytv on Friday, Trudeau sloughed off the current polling landscape for his party in general, pointing out the next election is still a year-and-a-half away.
"We were way behind in the polls before the 2015 election where I won a majority, we were way behind in the polls before the 2019 election. Polls go up and down," he said.
The Tories did, in the end, vote against an updated free trade deal with Ukraine - that vote is the reason Trudeau accused Poilievre of being a Putin apologist. Poilievre insists it is because of the carbon price reference in the deal, and his party still backs Ukraine.
While Trudeau brought that up too in the Citytv interview, signalling his party won't let that issue drop, Coletto said the risk for the Liberals in general is that no one is listening to Trudeau anymore.
"They can't cut through because most people aren't listening anymore to Trudeau and until they start spending money on direct-to-voter advertising, that will be the case," he said.
"The fundamental problem facing the Liberals is not a popular Conservative leader they need to bring back to Earth. It's their unpopular leader who the country is largely tired of."
CREDIT: Stephanie Levitz Deputy Toronto Star
