Arbitrator awards 2.75% pay raise to Ontario's public school teachers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/arbitrator-awards-2-75-pay-raise-ontarios-public/docview/2924525051/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Arbitrator+awards+2.75%25+pay+raise+to+Ontario%27s+public+school+teachers&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An arbitrator has awarded Ontario's public school teachers a 2.75 per cent retroactive pay raise to make up for the last of three years of imposed wage caps that were deemed unconstitutional.
That's on top of the 0.75 per cent bump awarded for each of the first two years.
The move means teachers will receive about 7.41 per cent in pay raises in total for the three years, when the one per cent yearly wage-capped increase is taken into account and the amount compounded.
"This should never have happened," said Karen Littlewood, president of the Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation, in a written statement.
"This decision reinforces what we have known all along - our members have been underpaid and undervalued for years while the Progressive Conservatives underfunded public education and shortchanged Ontario's students."
Karen Brown, president of the Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario, said the decision "sends a clear message to the Ford government that they must never circumvent bargaining or trample on workers' democratic rights again. Workers have a right to an unrestricted bargaining process. This is guaranteed by the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms and must be upheld."
The award also impacts any future wage increases for teachers as base pay will now be higher.
The decision by arbitrator William Kaplan, released late Friday afternoon, comes as Ontario's Court of Appeal is set to release its ruling Monday on the Ford government's challenge of an earlier decision that Bill 124 infringed on the collective bargaining rights of public sector workers, whose unions had turned to the legal system to fight it.
The 2019 legislation set a maximum wage increase of one per cent a year for three years.
Several education unions, however, opted out of that court challenge after agreeing with the province to allow an arbitrator to rule on the back pay.
The Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario and the Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation represent about 145,000 members.
Some are 55,000 education workers represented by CUPE's Ontario School Board Council of Unions, who are also entitled to the 2.75 as part of a deal they negotiated with the province and school boards.
Ontario nurses were also awarded pay raises to make up for the three years Bill 124 impacted their wages.
The education payouts alone will be costly for the provincial treasury.
The previous Liberal government also ran into trouble after it forced contracts on teachers in 2012 amid an austerity push, with courts later ruling that move unconstitutional. That cost provincial coffers $103 million for public elementary teachers, $50 million for public secondary teachers and $56.7 million for CUPE school workers.
Brown said that while she welcomed the arbitrator's decision, "it does not replace the loss of our members' bargaining rights. This arbitration award is a clear acknowledgment of the egregious, unlawful suppression of wages that deliberately undermined our members' rights and livelihoods. "
The government now has 60 days to provide affected school boards with funds to cover the remedy owed to eligible members. School boards must issue payments no later than 120 days after the release of this arbitration decision. The amount each member will receive will vary depending on their status during the 2019-20, 2020-21 and 2021-22 school years.
CAPTION: Karen Littlewood is president of the Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation.

Document 248 of 500

PM opens door to longer sentences for car thieves: Calls for ban on high-tech devices used by criminals

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pm-opens-door-longer-sentences-car-thieves/docview/2923773102/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PM+opens+door+to+longer+sentences+for+car+thieves%3A+Calls+for+ban+on+high-tech+devices+used+by+criminals&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal government said it would pursue all avenues to ban the high-tech devices used to steal vehicles, and opened the possibility of harsher criminal penalties for car thieves, on Thursday during a summit called to tackle auto theft in Canada.
"Organized crime is becoming more brazen, and the international black market for the stolen cars is expanding," Prime Minister Justin Trudeau told the summit, which brought together police, federal officials, mayors and industry voices from across the country.
"It's become too easy for criminals to get sophisticated electronic devices that make it simple, for example, to copy car key fobs."
But as stakeholders gathered to discuss the issue, sparring over who's to blame for the growing problem continued in the House of Commons.
"When will the prime minister abandon their soft-on-crime approach so car thefts can come down?" Conservative House Leader Andrew Scheer said.
In his opening remarks at the summit, Trudeau blamed Stephen Harper-era cuts for the issue, as he dismissed the Conservatives' plan as a "catchy slogan" and a "two-minute video" that won't fix the problem. "In the previous government, funding for border security was gutted," he said. "A long tail of cuts like this have hurt the ability of border agents to do their jobs."
This week, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre promised harsher penalties for organized-crime-related auto theft, as well as mandatory minimum sentences for three-time offenders. He also said a Tory government would hire 75 Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA) staff and purchase two dozen X-ray scanners to crack down on stolen cars at Canada's major ports.
The Liberals left Thursday's summit with only one new announcement that the government would be banning devices used to steal vehicles through hacking methods, like flippers, according to Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne. On Wednesday, Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc announced $28 million in additional funding to CBSA to tackle the export of stolen vehicles.
Justice Minister Arif Virani said the criminal code already contains sufficient legislation to tackle the issues of organized crime and theft, but that there was "absolutely more to do" to bridge the two.
The Liberals also said they would capitalize on existing tools to attack the issue, and that more announcements, including a concrete plan, should be expected in the coming weeks. Expected actions include increased collaboration among police forces, modernizing the Canadian Motor Vehicle Safety Standards and security plans at ports, the government said.
Those at the summit described the issue as wide-ranging across Canada, especially in the GTA, Ontario and Quebec, and warned against increasingly violent carjackings. The federal government estimates 90,000 cars are stolen annually, many of which end up overseas, resulting in about $1 billion in insurance costs to Canadians.
Summit participants said conversations were meaningful in bringing stakeholders together to identify the root of the problem, but called for more urgent action to co-ordinate efforts.
Brian Kingston, president of the Canadian Vehicle Manufacturers' Association, told the Star he had hoped for additional funding announcements.
"The success of the summit is going to depend on whether there will be immediate resources to take on organized crime and shutting down illegal exports," he said in an interview. Last week, the federal government announced $121 million in funding to Ontario to fight auto theft and gun crimes, though Kingston said increased funding is needed across the country.
Bryan Gast, a former OPP officer who's now vice-president of investigative services of Équité Association, told the Star more work is urgently needed to make it more difficult to steal vehicles in the first place, such as through implementing "robust anti-theft standards."
But to make a long-term impact on the issue, a national strategy is required, Toronto Police Service Chief Myron Demkiw said.
"We need all levels of government, law enforcement and private industry, including vehicle manufacturers, border and port authorities and shipping companies, working together in a co-ordinated manner," he said.
CREDIT: Mark Ramzy Toronto Star

Document 249 of 500

Another case gets tossed over lack of judges: Frustration grows with 'slow pace' of appointments to Superior Court

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/another-case-gets-tossed-over-lack-judges/docview/2923773038/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Another+case+gets+tossed+over+lack+of+judges%3A+Frustration+grows+with+%27slow+pace%27+of+appointments+to+Superior+Court&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A human trafficking case has become the latest criminal matter to collapse at Toronto Superior Court over delays partly caused by the federal government's failure to fill empty spots on the bench.
It's at least the third case to be tossed in two months in Toronto as exasperated judges at the city's criminal courthouse take aim at Ottawa's inability to appoint enough new judges.
In this latest case, delays caused by the prosecution and the lack of judges pushed GTA resident Marlon Downey's trial date beyond a strict 30-month time limit set by the Supreme Court.
His first trial on human trafficking charges - related to one complainant - as well as drug trafficking and firearm charges did not get off the ground as planned last May because the judge was elevated by the federal government to the Ontario Court of Appeal. Rather than still hear the case - as he would have been permitted to do - Justice Patrick Monahan decided to declare a mistrial, as no evidence had yet been called.
That put the case in limbo, as the resource-strapped court tried to find another judge to hear the case. Although it ended up before Justice Todd Ducharme just a week later, Ducharme did not have several weeks in a row of free time to hear the trial due to other matters, which dragged things out further.
The delay in criminal cases caused by the elevation of a trial judge "has been seriously exacerbated by the slow pace of judicial appointments by the federal government" to the Superior Court, Ducharme said in court last month, reading his decision to toss the case.
He referred to a sexual assault case stayed by his colleague Justice Maureen Forestell in December, who found that the matter took longer than it should have because the court lacked the judicial resources to hear the case in a timely manner.
"The same observation can be made about this case," Ducharme said.
The Crown is appealing his decision.
There are 21 judicial vacancies in the Superior Court throughout Ontario, including about six empty spots in Toronto. The last time federal Justice Minister Arif Virani appointed a judge to the court in Toronto was in mid-December.
The reasons for the slow appointments have long been unclear, a fact highlighted by the Chief Justice of Canada last year, who expressed concern about the mounting number of vacancies.
"I can guarantee you that there is a number of exceptional candidates from both the defence and Crown, as well as judges of the Ontario Court of Justice, who have applied to be Superior Court judges, and would make exceptional judges, and yet for reasons that I still can't understand, they're not being appointed," Downey's lawyer, Ariel Herscovitch, told the Star.
"There is clearly significant frustration among the judiciary about the volume of cases in the system and its general inability to accommodate them in a constitutionally acceptable time frame," said Boris Bytensky, president of the Criminal Lawyers' Association.
For his part, Bytensky says other factors play a larger role in creating chronic delays, such as the sheer number of cases coming through the system.
Nevertheless, it's clear that these rulings are an effort by the judiciary to get more timely appointments in Toronto, he said, "and the issue of judicial vacancies has thus become a convenient target."
A spokesperson for Virani argued that the Liberals have appointed more judges than any previous government, but said the overall number of vacancies across the country "has only seemingly been mildly reduced" due to a high number of judges going part-time or retiring.
"Our government's process has resulted in the appointments of judges of the highest quality and a bench that better reflects the country we live in," said Chantalle Aubertin.
"Minister Virani will continue to encourage qualified lawyers to apply to become judges, and he will keep making high-quality appointments expeditiously."
A series of judicial rebukes
While the federal government has been criticized on this issue for years, the judges at the Superior Court in Toronto appear to have finally had enough.
Forestell, a senior criminal judge who presides over long-trial assignment court, got the ball rolling in December when she stayed the sexual assault case. (The Crown is appealing her decision.)
"In this jurisdiction, it is becoming typical that scheduled trials are not reached because of a lack of judicial resources," she wrote.
Forestell's ruling was relied upon by her colleague Justice Andras Schreck in January when he threw out a gun possession case. "One obvious way to reduce the backlog of cases is to make sure that judicial vacancies are filled so that trials do not get adjourned," Schreck said. "The state has failed to do this."
Once Monahan declared a mistrial in the Downey human trafficking case last May, it landed in Forestell's assignment court, as she tried to figure out where it could go for a new trial.
"I wish we had a judge," she told the lawyers, according to a transcript. "We were barely covering our cases with the judges we had, and now we've lost one and we simply don't have another to step in."
The new trial ended up being squeezed in among other matters before Ducharme, and the case took longer than expected.
Other reasons for delay
While the number of vacancies remains high "and does contribute to delay at least to some unknowable extent," Bytensky argues that they are not the primary reason for the backlog.
"The single biggest contributor to our problem is the unwillingness or inability of Crown attorneys to take the clearly unpleasant but necessary steps to reduce the number of cases in the system," he said.
The president of the Ontario Crown Attorneys' Association said prosecutors are already doing that, as they're required to constantly review the strength of a case to ensure that there remains a reasonable prospect of conviction, and that it's in the public interest to proceed with the prosecution.
Betty Vavougios said Crowns are "working harder than ever," and that what's needed is for the provincial government to adequately invest in the criminal justice system: more Crown attorneys and resources to support their work, but also better facilities and better legal aid funding for defence lawyers.
"Unfortunately, it takes for something to break or for tragedy to strike until something gets done about the working conditions in our criminal justice system," she told the Star.
'Very serious' allegations
In a landmark 2016 decision known as R. v. Jordan, the Supreme Court ruled that cases in Superior Court must be heard within 30 months, otherwise they must be tossed for violating an accused person's right to a trial within a reasonable time, unless the Crown can prove there were exceptional circumstances for the delay. Defence-related delay does not count toward the limit.
In his decision to stay the human trafficking case, Ducharme deducted two months of delay caused by the lingering impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on court resources, as well as a few days of delay between Monahan's mistrial decision and the case being assigned to him.
The Crown pushed for more time to not count against the 30-month limit, given that the matter ended up being heard here and there over several months in front of Ducharme.
But the judge noted that the Crown was working under the assumption the trial would have been completed on schedule in front of Monahan, and it may not have. Ducharme found that the net delay was just over 31 months from the time of Downey's arrest in November 2020, meaning the case had to be stayed.
"The allegations against Mr. Downey, especially the allegations of human trafficking, are very serious, but the gravity of an offence without more does not rebut or justify an unreasonable delay that violates the ceiling set out in Jordan," Ducharme said.
He also faulted the Crown for not moving more expeditiously. Prior to his trial, Downey was supposed to face a preliminary inquiry in the Ontario Court of Justice, where a judge decides if there's sufficient evidence to send the case to the Superior Court.
Once that hearing was already underway, the Crown obtained a direct indictment from the attorney general, which allows for a case to bypass the preliminary inquiry and head straight to the Superior Court.
"If the request for a direct indictment had been made earlier, this case could have come to the Superior Court much earlier," avoiding any conflict with the Charter right to a trial within a reasonable time, Ducharme said.
And he made a general "observation" about Monahan's decision to declare a mistrial, while emphasizing that he meant no criticism of the judge.
When a judge is presiding over a case that might come up against the 30-month limit, "it would be helpful" if they might consider whether they should finish the case, even after their appointment to the Court of Appeal, Ducharme said.
CREDIT: Jacques Gallant Courts and Justice Reporter

Subject: Indictments; Federal government; Court hearings & proceedings; Acquittals & mistrials; Judicial appointments; Attorneys; Sex crimes; Firearms; Human trafficking; Trials; Judges & magistrates

Business indexing term: Subject: Attorneys

Document 250 of 500

How Doug Ford schooled Pierre Poilievre in the art of political redirection

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-doug-ford-schooled-pierre-poilievre-art/docview/2923772841/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+Doug+Ford+schooled+Pierre+Poilievre+in+the+art+of+political+redirection&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The scene: A suburban kitchen, circa 1987.
"Petey, what happened to that box of cookies I bought yesterday? Did you eat them?
"Who are you? Are you from the CBC?"
"Petey, I'm your mother. Did you eat the cookies?
"That's a distraction from the real issues. I have a common sense plan for this household. Cut the homework, raise the allowance, more TV time."
"Petey, what are you talking about? You're 8 years old. Did you eat the cookies?"
"Cut the homework, raise the allowance, more TV time."
"Petey, did you eat the cookies?"
"Cookies are for people over 18 who can make choices about their lives."
"Petey, I'm worried about you."
People who aren't terribly familiar with politics might think that one of the essential skills of a politician is being able to answer questions about a whole range of complicated issues, especially when they're coming thick and fast from a skeptical or even hostile news media.
But that's not it at all. The essential skill of a successful politician is not answering those questions. Or more precisely, answering only the questions you'd like to be asked while ducking the ones you know will get you in trouble.
The trick is to do it artfully, so the audience hardly even notices what's happening. And to keep smiling while the media try every gambit they know to make you answer those pesky unanswerable questions.
This is a game as old as politics itself. Politicians from the dawn of time have been changing the subject when they get a question they don't like ("Thanks for the question. What Athenians are really concerned about is ..." something else entirely.)
There's even a cottage industry of "media training," often run by ex-journalists steeped in the tricks of their craft, designed specifically to equip politicians, business executives and the like to "stay on message." I.e. to not answer questions.
In Britain, a political psychologist named Peter Bull made a special study of the peculiar skill of not answering. In his research he identified no fewer than 35 ways politicians evade a direct question and rated them on how often they managed to do it (former PM Theresa May was a champ, he found).
Which brings us to a couple of politicians who faced difficult questions this week with varying success.
First, Pierre Poilievre. It's worth rewatching the media scrum he found himself in this week on Parliament Hill as reporters tried to get him to say whether he supports Alberta Premier Danielle Smith's sweeping gender policies.
Poilievre attempted to blame his favourite punching bag ("Justin Trudeau is trying to divide and distract Canadians"). He gave vague answers ("I think we should protect the rights of parents"). But you could see he was struggling to keep his composure as he was peppered with aggressive questions.
Finally, he wilted under the pressure and coughed up an actual answer - he opposes puberty blockers for those under 18. Sure enough, that's what made the headlines and set him up for exactly the kind of attacks he was trying to avoid. For example, does he even understand the issue? What use are puberty blockers for someone who's already gone through puberty?
That was a clear fail but let's now consider how another Conservative politician, Doug Ford, handled the same question.
The premier was clearly ready for that and gave an actual answer - "No. We have a law here; we're leaving everything alone."
But look at what followed. By my count Ford spent exactly four seconds on that answer, which he practically mumbled under his breath. Then he switched the subject entirely by congratulating the Globe and Mail for a story about how Ontario is beating China on electric vehicles. It turned out the reporter who wrote the story was present, so the premier spent a full minute happily talking about EVs and Ontario industry.
From gender politics to electric vehicles in four seconds flat. From potential minefield to smiles all round. Doug Ford, you get this week's gold star in the art of political redirection.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre talks to media at a party caucus meeting on Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Wednesday. The Conservative leader eventually answered a questionfrom reporters that he tried not to during an earlier scrum.

Subject: Puberty; Politics; Politicians; Electric vehicles

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Title: How Doug Ford schooled Pierre Poilievre in the art of political redirection

Document 251 of 500

Why Doug Ford's actions this week should be greeted with a sigh of relief

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-doug-fords-actions-this-week-should-be/docview/2923772826/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+Doug+Ford%27s+actions+this+week+should+be+greeted+with+a+sigh+of+relief&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I am wary of too effusively offering faint praise to a political leader - both because it can famously be damning in the low expectations it reveals, and because it risks unjustly elevating someone for doing the bare minimum. Displaying basic human decency and employing common sense shouldn't ordinarily be reasons to celebrate.
And yet, the news this week that Ontario Premier Doug Ford would not be following Alberta Premier Danielle Smith's lead in restricting the rights of transgender people caused me to sigh in relief, and even prompted the urge to publicly thank him.
"No," he responded when a reporter asked him about it, shaking his head and answering before the question was even finished. "We're leaving everything alone." His party's insiders elaborated to my esteemed colleague Robert Benzie that his government sees this as a non-issue, and they believe there is no problem to solve here. "Let's stop pretending to import U.S.-style issues to Canada," one said.
Well, hooray for that. Maybe it shouldn't be remarkable that, as that last insider told Benzie, they are resisting "making this a controversy for people who have among the highest suicide rates." Maybe that's table stakes for basic humanity. Yet at this point in history, it is a rare stance for a populist right-wing party to take.
Following in the giant clown shoes of former U.S. president Donald Trump and his perpetually outraged army of loud and proud imbeciles, right-wing politics has more and more become a giant circus of malicious, belligerent ignorance. More than economics or small government or war and peace or any other issue, many people who typically call themselves conservatives have become a movement of distilled resentment focused on villainizing vulnerable people and gleefully bullying them. Especially, recently, trans people.
From Florida to Kentucky and Tennessee and the U.S. House of Representatives, and then to Saskatchewan and New Brunswick and Alberta, such politicians have been trumping up outrage to justify punitive laws targeting pronoun use in schools, puberty blockers, youth sports participation, drag queens and anything else related to an evolving understanding of rigidly enforced gender roles, and trying to brand anyone who disagrees with them a "groomer" preying on children.
Federal Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre may prefer not to directly confront the issue (as he thinks he can draw more support on other issues, as Susan Delacourt recently wrote), but when prompted by reporters to choose a side of this wedge to slide on, he sided with Smith on treatment of trans youth to "protect children" and - even while Smith was in town proudly talking up her policies - accused Prime Minister Justin Trudeau of ginning up the issue to create controversy.
That is the populist current in which Ford swims, and so it is refreshing, and maybe a source of welcome pride, that he seems determined to kick against it.
All the more so in combination with the announcement by his government on Thursday that the Ontario school curriculum will soon include a healthy dose of mandatory Black history lessons. Education Minister Stephen Lecce called it "long overdue," and maybe he's right about that. But it's also remarkable for the same reasons I went over above. If there's anything that has powered the U.S. right as much as delusional anti-trans moral panic, it is Trumped-up outrage over the teaching of Black history framed as opposition to "critical race theory."
If anyone were under the impression the border keeps such thought currents from infecting Canadian politics, Pickering city councillor Lisa Robinson was recently happy to demonstrate otherwise, writing a column in a local newspaper arguing against observing Black history month (and indeed, the teaching of Black history) and outlining how it is racist to call her "white priviledge" (sic) and explaining how having her pay suspended for 90 days recently by her colleagues made her a "modern day slave," demonstrating that slavery is "not a Black and white issue." She then reminded people of the era "during the world wars" in the early 1900s when, she claims, soldiers sacrificed "without thought or division based on colour" (which might have been news to the soldiers serving in the segregated Black No. 2 Construction Battalion in the First World War, as well as to the 20,000 Canadian-born citizens of Japanese descent interned in camps during World War II).
You may not be surprised to learn that the last time Robinson found herself in province-wide news, it was that time she had her pay suspended for motions she put forward that were deemed homophobic and transphobic. Because it is no surprise that those two types of opinions go together - they are the dominant grievances of the right-wing outrage machine.
Those who buy such arguments are a growing and motivated constituency for many conservative politicians. So kudos to Ford for instead announcing the kind of history education Robinson's rant so vividly (if unwittingly) demonstrates we need more of.
As was the case during the darkest days of the pandemic when Ford was loudly in favour of masks and vaccines, and as is the case in his disavowing any restrictive policies on trans people, Ford is continuing to demonstrate a welcome willingness to avoid the worst impulses that have gripped his erstwhile ideological allies across the continent.
That may be, as Benzie's analysis noted, because he's simply demonstrating an impulse to stay in the mainstream of Ontario opinion - and hence the credit lies with the people of the province more than with the premier himself. That's no less noteworthy.
Maybe reaching that bare baseline of humanity and good sense isn't exactly reason for celebration. But given the all-too-visible alternative possibilities, it can still be a source of tremendous relief.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford seemed to distance himself this week from the populist current he swims in.

Subject: Black history; Politics; Populism

Location: United States--US

Title: Why Doug Ford's actions this week should be greeted with a sigh of relief

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Feb 9, 2024

Document 252 of 500

The art of not answering questions

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/art-not-answering-questions/docview/2923772818/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+art+of+not+answering+questions&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The scene: A suburban kitchen, circa 1987.
"Petey, what happened to that box of cookies I bought yesterday? Did you eat them?
"Who are you? Are you from the CBC?"
"Petey, I'm your mother. Did you eat the cookies?
"That's a distraction from the real issues. I have a common sense plan for this household. Cut the homework, raise the allowance, more TV time."
"Petey, what are you talking about? You're eight years old. Did you eat the cookies?"
"Cut the homework, raise the allowance, more TV time."
"Petey, did you eat the cookies?"
"Cookies are for people over 18 who can make choices about their lives."
"Petey, I'm worried about you."

People who aren't terribly familiar with politics might think that one of the essential skills of a politician is being able to answer questions about a whole range of complicated issues, especially when they're coming thick and fast from a skeptical or even hostile news media.
But that's not it at all. The essential skill of a successful politician is not answering those questions. Or more precisely, answering only the questions you'd like to be asked while ducking the ones you know will get you in trouble.
The trick is to do it artfully, so the audience hardly even notices what's happening. And to keep smiling while the media try every gambit they know to make you answer those pesky unanswerable questions.
This is a game as old as politics itself. Politicians from the dawn of time have been changing the subject when they get a question they don't like ("Thanks for the question. What Athenians are really concerned about is ..." something else entirely.)
There's even a cottage industry of "media training," often run by ex-journalists steeped in the tricks of their craft, designed specifically to equip politicians, business executives and the like to "stay on message." I.e. to not answer questions.
In Britain, a political psychologist named Peter Bull made a special study of the peculiar skill of not answering. In his research he identified no fewer than 35 ways politicians evade a direct question and rated them on how often they managed to do it (former PM Theresa May was a champ, he found).
Which brings us to a couple of politicians who faced difficult questions this week with varying success.
First, Pierre Poilievre. It's worth rewatching the media scrum he found himself in this week on Parliament Hill as reporters tried to get him to say whether he supports Alberta Premier Danielle Smith's sweeping gender policies.
Poilievre attempted to blame his favourite punching bag ("Justin Trudeau is trying to divide and distract Canadians"). He gave vague answers ("I think we should protect the rights of parents"). But you could see he was struggling to keep his composure as he was peppered with aggressive questions.
Finally, he wilted under the pressure and coughed up an actual answer - he opposes puberty blockers for those under 18. Sure enough, that's what made the headlines and set him up for exactly the kind of attacks he was trying to avoid.
For example, does he even understand the issue? What use are puberty blockers for someone who's already gone through puberty?
That was a clear fail but let's now consider how another Conservative politician, Doug Ford, handled the same question.
The premier was clearly ready for that and gave an actual answer - "No. We have a law here; we're leaving everything alone."
But look at what followed. By my count Ford spent exactly four seconds on that answer, which he practically mumbled under his breath. Then he switched the subject entirely by congratulating the Globe and Mail for a story about how Ontario is beating China on electric vehicles. It turned out the reporter who wrote the story was present, so the premier spent a full minute happily talking about EVs and Ontario industry.
From gender politics to electric vehicles in four seconds flat. From potential minefield to smiles all round. Doug Ford, you get this week's gold star in the art of political redirection.
ANDREW PHILLIPS IS A TORONTOBASED STAFF COLUMNIST FOR THE STAR'S OPINION PAGE. REACH HIM VIA EMAIL: APHILLIPS@THESTAR.CA
CREDIT: Andrew Phillips OPINION;

Subject: Puberty; Politics; Politicians; Electric vehicles

Document 253 of 500

Human trafficking case collapses in latest casualty of failure to appoint enough Toronto judges

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/human-trafficking-case-collapses-latest-casualty/docview/2923772779/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Human+trafficking+case+collapses+in+latest+casualty+of+failure+to+appoint+enough+Toronto+judges&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A human trafficking case has become the latest criminal matter to collapse at Toronto Superior Court over delays partly caused by the federal government's failure to fill empty spots on the bench.It's at least the third case to be tossed in two months in Toronto as exasperated judges at the city's criminal courthouse take aim at Ottawa's inability to appoint enough new judges.In this latest case, delays caused by the prosecution and the lack of judges pushed GTA resident Marlon Downey's trial date beyond a strict 30-month time limit set by the Supreme Court.His first trial on human trafficking charges - related to one complainant - as well as drug trafficking and firearm charges did not get off the ground as planned last May because the judge was elevated by the federal government to the Ontario Court of Appeal. Rather than still hear the case - as he would have been permitted to do - Justice Patrick Monahan decided to declare a mistrial, as no evidence had yet been called.That put the case in limbo, as the resource-strapped court tried to find another judge to hear the case. Although it ended up before Justice Todd Ducharme just a week later, Ducharme did not have several weeks in a row of free time to hear the trial due to other matters, which dragged things out further.The delay in criminal cases caused by the elevation of a trial judge "has been seriously exacerbated by the slow pace of judicial appointments by the federal government" to the Superior Court, Ducharme said in court last month, reading his decision to toss the case.He referred to a sexual assault case stayed by his colleague Justice Maureen Forestell in December, who found that the matter took longer than it should have because the court lacked the judicial resources to hear the case in a timely manner."The same observation can be made about this case," Ducharme said.The Crown is appealing his decision. There are 21 judicial vacancies in the Superior Court throughout Ontario, including about six empty spots in Toronto. The last time federal Justice Minister Arif Virani appointed a judge to the court in Toronto was in mid-December. The reasons for the slow appointments have long been unclear, a fact highlighted by the Chief Justice of Canada last year, who expressed concern about the mounting number of vacancies."I can guarantee you that there is a number of exceptional candidates from both the defence and Crown, as well as judges of the Ontario Court of Justice, who have applied to be Superior Court judges, and would make exceptional judges, and yet for reasons that I still can't understand, they're not being appointed," Downey's lawyer, Ariel Herscovitch, told the Star."There is clearly significant frustration among the judiciary about the volume of cases in the system and its general inability to accommodate them in a constitutionally acceptable time frame," said Boris Bytensky, president of the Criminal Lawyers' Association.For his part, Bytensky says other factors play a larger role in creating chronic delays, such as the sheer number of cases coming through the system. Nevertheless, it's clear that these rulings are an effort by the judiciary to get more timely appointments in Toronto, he said, "and the issue of judicial vacancies has thus become a convenient target." A spokesperson for Virani argued that the Liberals have appointed more judges than any previous government, but said the overall number of vacancies across the country "has only seemingly been mildly reduced" due to a high number of judges going part-time or retiring."Our government's process has resulted in the appointments of judges of the highest quality and a bench that better reflects the country we live in," said Chantalle Aubertin."Minister Virani will continue to encourage qualified lawyers to apply to become judges, and he will keep making high-quality appointments expeditiously."A series of judicial rebukesWhile the federal government has been criticized on this issue for years, the judges at the Superior Court in Toronto appear to have finally had enough. Forestell, a senior criminal judge who presides over long-trial assignment court, got the ball rolling in December when she stayed the sexual assault case. (The Crown is appealing her decision.)"In this jurisdiction, it is becoming typical that scheduled trials are not reached because of a lack of judicial resources," she wrote.Forestell's ruling was relied upon by her colleague Justice Andras Schreck in January when he threw out a gun possession case. "One obvious way to reduce the backlog of cases is to make sure that judicial vacancies are filled so that trials do not get adjourned," Schreck said. "The state has failed to do this."Once Monahan declared a mistrial in the Downey human trafficking case last May, it landed in Forestell's assignment court, as she tried to figure out where it could go for a new trial."I wish we had a judge," she told the lawyers, according to a transcript. "We were barely covering our cases with the judges we had, and now we've lost one and we simply don't have another to step in."The new trial ended up being squeezed in among other matters before Ducharme, and the case took longer than expected. Other reasons for delayWhile the number of vacancies remains high "and does contribute to delay at least to some unknowable extent," Bytensky argues that they are not the primary reason for the backlog. "The single biggest contributor to our problem is the unwillingness or inability of Crown attorneys to take the clearly unpleasant but necessary steps to reduce the number of cases in the system," he said.The president of the Ontario Crown Attorneys' Association said prosecutors are already doing that, as they're required to constantly review the strength of a case to ensure that there remains a reasonable prospect of conviction, and that it's in the public interest to proceed with the prosecution.Betty Vavougios said Crowns are "working harder than ever," and that what's needed is for the provincial government to adequately invest in the criminal justice system: more Crown attorneys and resources to support their work, but also better facilities and better legal aid funding for defence lawyers."Unfortunately, it takes for something to break or for tragedy to strike until something gets done about the working conditions in our criminal justice system," she told the Star.'Very serious' allegationsIn a landmark 2016 decision known as R. v. Jordan, the Supreme Court ruled that cases in Superior Court must be heard within 30 months, otherwise they must be tossed for violating an accused person's right to a trial within a reasonable time, unless the Crown can prove there were exceptional circumstances for the delay. Defence-related delay does not count toward the limit.In his decision to stay the human trafficking case, Ducharme deducted two months of delay caused by the lingering impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on court resources, as well as a few days of delay between Monahan's mistrial decision and the case being assigned to him. The Crown pushed for more time to not count against the 30-month limit, given that the matter ended up being heard here and there over several months in front of Ducharme.But the judge noted that the Crown was working under the assumption that the trial would have been completed on schedule in front of Monahan, and it may not have. Ducharme found that the net delay was just over 31 months from the time of Downey's arrest in November 2020, meaning the case had to be stayed."The allegations against Mr. Downey, especially the allegations of human trafficking, are very serious, but the gravity of an offence without more does not rebut or justify an unreasonable delay that violates the ceiling set out in Jordan," Ducharme said.He also faulted the Crown for not moving more expeditiously. Prior to his trial, Downey was supposed to face a preliminary inquiry in the Ontario Court of Justice, where a judge decides if there's sufficient evidence to send the case to the Superior Court. Once that hearing was already underway, the Crown obtained a direct indictment from the attorney general, which allows for a case to bypass the preliminary inquiry and head straight to the Superior Court."If the request for a direct indictment had been made earlier, this case could have come to the Superior Court much earlier," avoiding any conflict with the Charter right to a trial within a reasonable time, Ducharme said.And he made a general "observation" about Monahan's decision to declare a mistrial, while emphasizing that he meant no criticism of the judge.When a judge is presiding over a case that might come up against the 30-month limit, "it would be helpful" if they might consider whether they should finish the case, even after their appointment to the Court of Appeal, Ducharme said.
CAPTION: It's at least the third case to be tossed in two months at the Toronto Superior Court.

Document 254 of 500

Ontario health care to get $3.1B boost: Trudeau and Ford governments finalize deal that will see new primary care teams, more staff

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-health-care-get-3-1b-boost/docview/2923765963/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+health+care+to+get+%243.1B+boost%3A+Trudeau+and+Ford+governments+finalize+deal+that+will+see+new+primary+care+teams%2C+more+staff&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Trudeau government has finalized a deal with the Ontario government to direct $3.1 billion in more money to improve health care, the Star has learned.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford are to announce the details the two governments have settled on when they meet in King City, north of Toronto, on Friday.
In exchange for the infusion of new cash, the Ford government would commit spending to create new primary care teams to boost access to family doctors.
Ontario says it will add hundreds of new family physicians and nurse practitioners, as well as thousands of new nurses and personal support workers.
It will increase enrolment in health-care education programs by 700, with a specific allotment for northern Ontario.
A portion of the money will be targeted at boosting youth mental-health services and substance abuse services in rural, remote and Indigenous communities.
As well, there will be new funding to modernize the collection of medical data, which should reduce paperwork and improve the system for Ontario patients.
It's the latest example of Trudeau's federal Liberals working closely with Ford's provincial Progressive Conservatives.
Last February, Ottawa set out the four priorities that the prime minister insisted the provinces must tackle to access the extra cash.
While some premiers - especially Quebec's François Legault - objected to strings being attached to new money, Ford welcomed the infusion of cash.
"This is good news," emphasized an official at Queen's Park on Thursday.
At the first ministers' meeting last year, Ontario reached an agreement in principle for $8.4 billion in new federal spending over 10 years.
In exchange, the province pledged to improve access to family health services, including in rural and remote areas; boost the health workforce; provide better access to timely mental health, substance use and addictions services; and allow individuals access to their electronic health information that is shared between the health professionals they consult.
However, that full amount is not on the agenda for Friday's announcement, which is only over three years for $3.1 billion, government officials say.
It will be revisited within the first three years to ensure targets and timelines are being met.
That likely will happen after a federal election that is expected within the next 20 months.
It is unclear what federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, whose party leads by double digits in public-opinion polls, would do with the accord going forward.
Poilievre has indicated he does not believe in Ottawa dictating health policy to provinces.
With the formalization of the agreement, Ontario is the fifth province to have signed a one-on-one deal with the federal Liberal government, after Alberta, British Columbia, Prince Edward Island and Nova Scotia outlined specific plans for how the money would be spent, as required by Ottawa.
A year ago, following his meeting with the premiers, Trudeau offered to significantly increase federal transfer payments to the provinces - by $17.3 billion over the next decade - which included an immediate $2 billion injection to tackle the crisis in pediatric hospitals and emergency rooms, and ongoing wait times for surgeries. Beyond that, the federal Liberal government set aside a separate $25 billion fund over 10 years for the bilateral deals to address those four federal priorities.
Ottawa had given provinces until March to reach deals to get their share of the funding.
Health Minister Mark Holland told The Canadian Press this week that so far, the agreements had invested mainly in community-based care, and provinces have agreed to make health data, including patient records, more compatible across the country.
During the COVID-19 pandemic, Ontario's health system in particular struggled to integrate a patchwork of health data collection practices under different regional health authorities.
CAPTION: In exchange for the infusion of new federal cash, Premier Doug Ford's government would commit spending to add thousands of new nurses and personal support workers.Steve Russell Toronto Star file photo

Document 255 of 500

Ford's actions bring sigh of relief: Premier deserves kudos for keeping distance from Smith, Poilievre, other right-wing populists

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-actions-bring-sigh-relief/docview/2923765623/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+actions+bring+sigh+of+relief%3A+Premier+deserves+kudos+for+keeping+distance+from+Smith%2C+Poilievre%2C+other+right-wing+populists&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I am wary of too effusively offering faint praise to a political leader - both because it can famously be damning in the low expectations it reveals, and because it risks unjustly elevating someone for doing the bare minimum. Displaying basic human decency and employing common sense shouldn't ordinarily be reasons to celebrate.
And yet, the news this week that Ontario Premier Doug Ford would not be following Alberta Premier Danielle Smith's lead in restricting the rights of transgender people caused me to sigh in relief, and even prompted the urge to publicly thank him.
"No," he responded when a reporter asked him about it, shaking his head and answering before the question was even finished. "We're leaving everything alone." His party's insiders elaborated to my esteemed colleague Robert Benzie that his government sees this as a non-issue, and they believe there is no problem to solve here. "Let's stop pretending to import U.S.-style issues to Canada," one said.
Well, hooray for that. Maybe it shouldn't be remarkable that, as that last insider told Benzie, they are resisting "making this a controversy for people who have among the highest suicide rates." Maybe that's table stakes for basic humanity. Yet at this point in history, it is a rare stance for a populist right-wing party to take.
Following in the giant clown shoes of former U.S. president Donald Trump and his perpetually outraged army of loud and proud imbeciles, right-wing politics has more and more become a giant circus of malicious, belligerent ignorance.
More than economics or small government or war and peace or any other issue, many people who typically call themselves conservatives have become a movement of distilled resentment focused on villainizing vulnerable people and gleefully bullying them. Especially, recently, trans people.
From Florida to Kentucky and Tennessee and the U.S. House of Representatives, and then to Saskatchewan and New Brunswick and Alberta, such politicians have been trumping up outrage to justify punitive laws targeting pronoun use in schools, puberty blockers, youth sports participation, drag queens and anything else related to an evolving understanding of rigidly enforced gender roles, and trying to brand anyone who disagrees with them a "groomer" preying on children.
Federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre may prefer not to directly confront the issue (as he thinks he can draw more support on other issues, as Susan Delacourt recently wrote), but when prompted by reporters to choose a side of this wedge to slide on, he sided with Smith on treatment of trans youth to "protect children" and - even while Smith was in town proudly talking up her policies - accused Prime Minister Justin Trudeau of ginning up the issue to create controversy.
That is the populist current in which Ford swims, and so it is refreshing, and maybe a source of welcome pride, that he seems determined to kick against it.
All the more so in combination with the announcement by his government on Thursday that the Ontario school curriculum will soon include a healthy dose of mandatory Black history lessons. Education Minister Stephen Lecce called it "long overdue," and maybe he's right about that. But it's also remarkable for the same reasons I went over above. If there's anything that has powered the U.S. right as much as delusional anti-trans moral panic, it is Trumped-up outrage over the teaching of Black history framed as opposition to "critical race theory."
If anyone were under the impression the border keeps such thought currents from infecting Canadian politics, Pickering city councillor Lisa Robinson was recently happy to demonstrate otherwise, writing a column in a local newspaper arguing against observing Black history month (and indeed, the teaching of Black history) and outlining how it is racist to call her "white priviledge" (sic) and explaining how having her pay suspended for 90 days recently by her colleagues made her a "modern day slave," demonstrating that slavery is "not a Black and white issue." She then reminded people of the era "during the world wars" in the early 1900s when, she claims, soldiers sacrificed "without thought or division based on colour" (which might have been news to the soldiers serving in the segregated Black No. 2 Construction Battalion in the First World War, as well as to the 20,000 Canadian-born citizens of Japanese descent interned in camps during the Second World War).
You may not be surprised to learn that the last time Robinson found herself in province-wide news, it was that time she had her pay suspended for motions she put forward that were deemed homophobic and transphobic. Because it is no surprise that those two types of opinions go together - they are the dominant grievances of the right-wing outrage machine.
Those who buy such arguments are a growing and motivated constituency for many conservative politicians. So kudos to Ford for instead announcing the kind of history education Robinson's rant so vividly (if unwittingly) demonstrates we need more of.
As was the case during the pandemic's darkest days when Ford was loudly in favour of masks and vaccines, and as is the case in his disavowing any restrictive policies on trans people, Ford is continuing to demonstrate a welcome willingness to avoid the worst impulses that have gripped his erstwhile ideological allies across the continent.
That may be, as Benzie's analysis noted, because he's simply demonstrating an impulse to stay in the mainstream of Ontario opinion - and hence the credit lies with the people of the province more than with the premier himself. That's no less noteworthy.
Maybe reaching that bare baseline of humanity and good sense isn't exactly reason for celebration. But given the all-too-visible alternative possibilities, it can still be a source of tremendous relief.
CAPTION: Refusing to follow Danielle Smith's lead in restricting the rights of transgender people, Doug Ford's PCs are resisting "making this a controversy for people who have among the highest suicide rates," a party insider said. Maybe that's table stakes for basic humanity, Edward Keenan writes, yet at this point in history, it is a rare stance to take.Chris Young THE CANADIAN PRESS file photo
CREDIT: Edward Keenan Opinion

Document 256 of 500

Justin Trudeau blames Stephen Harper cuts for auto theft issue, as Ottawa moves to ban high-tech devices used to steal cars

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-blames-stephen-harper-cuts-auto/docview/2924041387/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+blames+Stephen+Harper+cuts+for+auto+theft+issue%2C+as+Ottawa+moves+to+ban+high-tech+devices+used+to+steal+cars&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-The federal government said it would pursue all avenues to ban the high-tech devices used to steal vehicles, and opened the possibility of harsher criminal penalties for car thieves on Thursday, during a summit called to tackle auto theft in Canada.
"Organized crime is becoming more brazen, and the international black market for the stolen cars is expanding," Prime Minister Justin Trudeau told the summit, which brought together police, federal officials, mayors and industry voices from across the country.
"It's become too easy for criminals to get sophisticated electronic devices that make it simple, for example, to copy car key fobs."
But as stakeholders gathered to discuss the issue, sparring over who's to blame for the growing problem continued in the House of Commons.
"When will the prime minister abandon their soft-on-crime approach so car thefts can come down?" Conservative House Leader Andrew Scheer said.
In his opening remarks at the summit, Trudeau blamed Stephen Harper-era cuts for the issue, as he dismissed the Conservatives' plan as a "catchy slogan" and a "two-minute video" that won't fix the problem. This week, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre promised harsher penalties for organized-crime-related auto theft, as well as mandatory minimum sentences for three-time offenders. He also said a Tory government would hire 75 Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA) staff and purchase two dozen X-ray scanners to crack down on stolen cars at Canada's major ports.
The Liberals left Thursday's summit with only one new announcement that the government would be banning devices used to steal vehicles through hacking methods, like flippers, according to Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne. On Wednesday, Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc announced $28 million in additional funding to CBSA to tackle the export of stolen vehicles.
Justice Minister Arif Virani said the criminal code already contains sufficient legislation to tackle the issues of organized crime and theft, but that there was "absolutely more to do" to bridge the two.
The Liberals also said they would capitalize on existing tools to attack the issue, and that more announcements, including a concrete plan, should be expected in the coming weeks. Expected actions include increased collaboration among police forces, modernizing the Canadian Motor Vehicle Safety Standards and security plans at ports, the government said.
Those at the summit described the issue as wide-ranging across Canada, especially in the GTA, Ontario and Quebec, and warned against increasingly violent carjackings. The federal government estimates 90,000 cars are stolen annually, many of which end up overseas, resulting in about $1 billion in insurance costs to Canadians.
Summit participants said conversations were meaningful in bringing stakeholders together to identify the root of the problem, but called for more urgent action to co-ordinate efforts.
Brian Kingston, president of the Canadian Vehicle Manufacturers' Association, told the Star he had hoped for additional funding announcements.
"The success of the summit is going to depend on whether there will be immediate resources to take on organized crime and shutting down illegal exports," he said in an interview. Last week, the federal government announced $121 million in funding to Ontario to fight auto theft and gun crimes, though Kingston said increased funding is needed across the country.
Bryan Gast, a former OPP officer who's now vice-president of investigative services of Équité Association, told the Star more work is urgently needed to make it more difficult to steal vehicles in the first place, such as through implementing "robust anti-theft standards."
But to make a long-term impact on the issue, a national strategy is required, Toronto Police Service Chief Myron Demkiw said.
"We need all levels of government, law enforcement and private industry, including vehicle manufacturers, border and port authorities and shipping companies, working together in a co-ordinated manner," he said.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau speaks with Toronto Chief of Police Myron Demkiw before the National Summit on Combatting Auto Theft on Feb. 8, 2024 in Ottawa.

Trudeau and Ford governments finalize health deal with Ontario agreeing to hire more doctors and nurses

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-ford-governments-finalize-health-deal/docview/2924041330/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+and+Ford+governments+finalize+health+deal+with+Ontario+agreeing+to+hire+more+doctors+and+nurses&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-The Trudeau government has finalized a deal with the Ontario government to direct $3.1 billion in more money to improve health care, the Star has learned.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford are to announce the details the two governments have settled on when they meet in King City, north of Toronto on Friday.
In exchange for the infusion of new cash, the Ford government would commit spending to create new primary care teams to boost access to family doctors.
Ontario says it will add hundreds of new family physicians and nurse practitioners, as well as thousands of new nurses and personal support workers.
It will increase enrollment in health-care education programs by 700, with a specific allotment for Northern Ontario.
A portion of the money will be targeted at boosting youth mental health services and substance abuse services in rural, remote, and Indigenous communities.
As well, there will be new funding to modernize the collection of medical data, which should reduce paperwork and improve the system for Ontario patients.
It's the latest example of Trudeau's federal Liberals working closely with Ford's provincial Progressive Conservatives.
Last February, Ottawa set out the four priorities that the prime minister insisted the provinces must tackle to access the extra cash.
While some premiers - especially Quebec's François Legault - objected to strings being attached to new money, Ford welcomed the infusion of cash.
"This is good news," emphasized an official at Queen's Park on Thursday.
At the first ministers' meeting last year, Ontario reached an agreement in principle for $8.4 billion in new federal spending over 10 years.
In exchange, the province pledged to improve access to family health services, including in rural and remote areas; boost the health workforce; provide better access to timely mental health, substance use and addictions services; and allow individuals access to their electronic health information that is shared between the health professionals they consult.
However, that full amount is not on the agenda for Friday's announcement, which is only over three years for $3.1 billion, government officials say.
It will be revisited within the first three years to ensure targets and timelines are being met.
That likely will happen after a federal election that is expected within the next 20 months.
It is unclear what federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, whose party leads by double digits in public-opinion polls, would do with the accord going forward.
Poilievre has indicated he does not believe in Ottawa dictating health policy to provinces.
With the formalization of the agreement, Ontario is the fifth province to have signed a one-on-one deal with the federal Liberal government, after Alberta, British Columbia, Prince Edward Island and Nova Scotia outlined specific plans for how the money would be spent, as required by Ottawa.
A year ago, following his meeting with the premiers, Trudeau offered to significantly increase federal transfer payments to the provinces - by $17.3 billion over the next decade - which included an immediate $2 billion injection to tackle the crisis in pediatric hospitals and emergency rooms, and ongoing wait times for surgeries. Beyond that, the federal Liberal government set aside a separate $25 billion fund over 10 years for the bilateral deals to address those four federal priorities.
Ottawa had given provinces until March to reach deals to get their share of the funding.
Health Minister Mark Holland told The Canadian Press this week that so far, the agreements had invested mainly in community-based care, and provinces have agreed to make health data, including patient records, more compatible across the country.
During the COVID-19 pandemic, Ontario's health system in particular struggled to integrate a patchwork of health data collection practices under different regional health authorities
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau delivers opening remarks at the National Summit on Combatting Auto Theft Feb.8, 2024 in Ottawa. Trudeau is expected to join Ontario Premier Doug Ford for a health announcement in King City on Friday.

Document 258 of 500

Doug Ford is ignoring Marit Stiles. But she's more interested in someone else's attention

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-is-ignoring-marit-stiles-shes-more/docview/2923264817/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+is+ignoring+Marit+Stiles.+But+she%27s+more+interested+in+someone+else%27s+attention&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The woman in the black toque clambers onstage in the mid-winter chill, flanked by union leaders braced for battle.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles is introduced as "a friend of hospital workers" to hundreds of cheering protesters, many wearing the same black toques emblazoned with their rallying cry: "Save our Hospitals!"
Stiles starts with a chant - predictable but popular: "Hey, hey! Ho, ho! Doug Ford has got to go!"
Followed by a boast: "We, the people of Ontario, we have managed to push this government over its heels over and over again."
Followed by a promise: "Solidarity" with the workers of Ontario, "not just in good times but on picket lines too."
Know your audience.
Wrapping up her pep talk, Stiles returns to her perch at Queen's Park just up the street. Sitting in her third-floor offices, the Leader of Ontario's Official Opposition ponders her proximity to power.
She has never been so close and yet so far. Stiles sits directly above the Premier's Office, yet is running far behind the ruling Progressive Conservatives in public opinion polls (while also trailing the rival Liberals).
Can she grow her audience?
Away from the crowds and the speeches, surrounded by family photos and political mementoes, Stiles takes stock of Ontario's political realities. In an interview marking her first full year since taking over the party leadership - unopposed, untested, unknown - she talks about her determination to change how the NDP does politics.
"We are the party of working people - we have those connections to unions and labour," she tells me.
No political party can keep preaching to the converted, nor take any constituency for granted, Stiles argues. Not the NDP, nor Ford's Tories as they aim their populist, affordability appeal to working-class voters.
"We are in a really deeply divided time," Stiles says. "I think Doug Ford is making the same mistake - he has been presumptuous, his assumption that he owns the vote of working people."
Stiles claims credit for leading the charge against Ford's attempts to carve up the Greenbelt's protected lands, forcing him to backtrack and respect his previous promises. She rails against the "selling-off of Ontario" and the "piece by piece privatization" of the public sector, especially in health care.
"This is a corrupt government."
But the voting and polling tells a different story. Ford romped to a re-election victory in 2022 and his Tories maintain a commanding lead in the latest Abacus Data poll last month: 38 per cent PC versus 27 per cent Liberal and 23 per cent NDP.
After losing nine seats in the last election, the New Democrats are down another three MPPs - after a humbling byelection loss in Kitchener Centre (the riding went Green after NDP incumbent Laura Mae Lindo resigned early); and the expulsion of two others from caucus (Mike Mantha for an improper office relationship, and Sarah Jama for repeated insubordination).
Despite the downturn, Stiles is determined to lay the groundwork for fresh gains in unheld ridings (the PCs currently have 79 seats, the NDP 28, Liberals nine and Greens two). The Liberals have reclaimed second place in most polls and grabbed outsized media attention last year during a leadership race that elected Bonnie Crombie.
Now, Ford and the Tories are focusing their attacks and paid ads on Crombie. And largely ignoring Stiles.
"They can go ahead and spend their millions of dollars fighting each other, while we take a different path," she muses. "He's gonna spend a lot of time and money defining who the Liberals are, and we get to define who we are. So I'm actually fine with that."
She insists the Liberals lack credibility on public services after selling off part of Hydro One and privatizing ServiceOntario. "They have to redefine themselves, they have to establish themselves."
How will Stiles redefine and re-establish the New Democrats?
"I think politics is changing, it isn't politics as usual - people are looking for something different," she says, citing the record-low turnout of 43.5 per cent in the last election.
"Why are we seeing such a low voter turnout? I happen to believe it's really not a healthy thing to scold people, blaming people for not voting. I think the question is on us as politicians and political actors - why are you not giving people the reason that they need to feel that there's any hope and purpose in government?"
Good question. Many New Democrats and Liberals blamed their own former leaders in the last election for failing to motivate voters, allowing Ford's Tories to outperform the opposition.
Stiles is determined to learn lessons by touring the province non-stop. And consulting with her fellow NDP leaders across the country about their own campaigns.
"I don't know if I'm allowed to talk about this, but I'll tell you anyway: We've connected over dinner recently, almost all of the NDP leaders across the country and the premiers, and it's really inspiring to talk to folks like (Manitoba Premier) Wab Kinew."
Kinew's private advice?
"You have to have patience ... take the time, do the work properly, line everything up."
Her response?
"I don't have patience!"
But she is mindful that politics, especially provincially, is a long game - out of the media spotlight, out of the public eye, out of the bubble.
"I am ambitious, right? I am competitive, I am a little bit impatient," she says, laughing. "We've waited long enough - and I think the people of Ontario have waited long enough."
CAPTION: Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles scrums after Premier Doug Ford faced reporters at Queen's Park on on Sept. 5, 2023.

Subject: Privatization; Voters; Political leadership; Union leadership; Voter behavior; Political parties
