Liberals offer plan to deter auto thefts: Pledge to offer $28M to CBSA comes ahead of this week's national summit

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-offer-plan-deter-auto-thefts/docview/2923263450/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+offer+plan+to+deter+auto+thefts%3A+Pledge+to+offer+%2428M+to+CBSA+comes+ahead+of+this+week%27s+national+summit&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal government will give $28 million to the Canada Border Services Agency to prevent the export of stolen vehicles, as the Liberals deflect fierce criticism from the Conservatives, who announced their own plans to tackle Canada's auto theft crisis this week.
"The agency will explore new detection technology solutions and the use of advanced analytical tools, such as artificial intelligence," Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc told reporters Wednesday.
The announcement comes as federal officials, police and industry representatives from across Canada are set to meet in Ottawa on Thursday to discuss auto thefts, after a surge in the number of stolen vehicles in Toronto and other Canadian cities. Between 2021 and 2023, auto theft increased by 48.2 per cent in Ontario and 57.9 per cent in Quebec, according to a report released this week by Équité Association, a private group funded by insurance companies which tracks fraud and insurance crime. In Toronto alone, 12,143 vehicles were stolen in 2023.
"This new funding will also enable (CBSA) to enhance their collaboration with provincial police partners, and international law enforcement agencies to identify and pursue these car thieves," LeBlanc said.
In making the announcement, the Liberals pushed back against Conservatives' criticism they had no plan of their own, though they faced questions over whether their plan mirrored that of the Tories, which Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre announced this week.
Poilievre told a news conference in Montreal on Tuesday a Conservative government would purchase two dozen X-ray machines and hire 75 special CBSA staff to scan containers for stolen vehicles at Canada's major federal ports. He said he would save the government $33 million by cutting consultancy costs to pay for the investments.
"Mr. Poilievre doesn't have a monopoly on the idea that there's some technology that can help the (CBSA). We've been talking to CBSA officials for months about this problem," LeBlanc said. "Mr. Poilievre can make something up in front of a gate. It actually doesn't solve the problem."
Earlier Wednesday, LeBlanc criticized Poilievre's proposal to purchase X-ray machines to detect stolen vehicles, but said he would be asking technological experts at the CBSA about the issue.
"The idea that there are simple solutions, that you can X-ray every single container leaving is not realistic," LeBlanc told reporters in French.
LeBlanc also hinted at announcing increased resources for the RCMP and local police forces to fight organized criminal networks, while Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne said there will be an announcement at the summit on tackling the tools used by car thieves.
"We'll come tomorrow with some announcement also, with respect to some of the equipment that we have seen the thieves using. We're going to be seeing measures from different agencies of the government," Champagne told reporters on his way into the Liberal caucus meeting on Wednesday.
In the days leading up to the summit, Poilievre has focused much of his criticism of the Liberal government on surging auto thefts - a topic that has gone largely unmentioned in the House of Commons until this sitting. Among the Tories' proposals are tougher criminal measures against car thieves, a demand echoed Wednesday by Ontario Premier Doug Ford, whose government called for mandatory minimum penalties for violent car thefts in a letter to LeBlanc.
Much of the focus on the issue has been on the role of the CBSA in preventing organized criminal groups from exporting stolen vehicles out of the country, which industry groups and police have been warning about for years. Experts have said the issue is multi-faceted and requires a mix of strategies, including updating federal vehicle safety regulations and addressing transportation networks of stolen vehicles.
Around 1,000 stolen vehicles have been retrieved by the CBSA at the Port of Montreal in each of the last three years, but the CBSA does not have an estimate of how many vehicles slip through the cracks, government data shows.
Federal officials, the chiefs of police of some of Canada's biggest cities, provincial police forces, provincial and municipal elected officials, as well as representatives from the automobile and insurance industries are expected to be in Ottawa for the summit on Thursday.
"By bringing everybody together to discuss, ideally, diagnose the root cause of the issue, and come out of it with shared understanding of what needs to be done is important," Brian Kingston, the president of the Canadian Vehicle Manufacturers' Association, told the Star in an interview. "But if it's not accompanied by funding, and an immediate action plan that will be executed on a very discrete timeline, then it will not be effective."
CREDIT: Mark Ramzy Toronto Star

Mayor knows that renters do vote: letters

Full text: Mayor knows that renters do voteMatt Elliott: Olivia Chow is spending millions on Toronto renters in her budget, Feb. 6
It was great to read Matt Elliott's article on renters. Toronto Mayor Olive Chow is the first mayor that I know of who has championed renters, who make up approximately 50 per cent of residents in Toronto, when it comes to property taxes.
Elliott pointed out that the mayor got 45 per cent of eligible renters who supported Chow, compared to 34 per cent of voters who live in homeowners' households. The myth that renters don't vote has been blown out of the water. The big number of $49 million, the approximate value of additional spending to support people who rent their homes is a huge acknowledgment that renters (voters) have power when exercised.
John Plumadore, chair Tenants Network Toronto
Bad call to cancel green grants programFederal green grants program comes to sudden end for Ontario homeowners, Feb. 5
It's not a very wise decision to target Ontario with cancellation of this green grant/rebate program, especially with our scheduled byelection in Durham Riding on March 4.
This program has been an excellent way to spend collected carbon taxes and reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
I urge the Justin Trudeau government to reverse this decision and to announce new funding in the spring budget. Obviously, this green initiative has been hugely successful.
Ontarians should not be unfairly targeted with this cancellation.
Jim McEwen, Bowmanville
Danielle Smith's double standard is remarkable Alberta premier's policy changes around trans youth could be harmful: doctors, Feb. 2
I find it odd that during COVID, our Premier Danielle Smith espouse "my body, my choice," supporting people who were anti-vaccination and freedom fighters, who want the government to back off.
And now, the Premier is imposing rules on transgender people saying in effect "your body, OUR choice" and there is no choice.
Which is it?
The double standard and political convenience is remarkable.
The full consequences are yet to be realized. Perhaps listening to Tucker Carlson last month wasn't so benign?
Rev. John Pentland, Hillhurst United Church, Calgary
Save the South Muskoka Hospital
Muskoka needs support and input from the public, cottagers/cottage associations, tourism partners and seasonal residents. Everyone who visits, lives or holidays in beautiful Muskoka should be concerned as this could affect you or a family member. Our community no longer has a local newspaper and I feel the general public is not aware of this issue.
Muskoka Algonquin Healthcare is in discussions to reduce service at the planned replacement of the South Muskoka Hospital serving Bracebridge, Gravenhurst and other South Muskoka communities. Originally proposed as a full-service hospital, slated for 2027, it is now being downgraded to 18 beds with obstetrics and in-patient service moving to Huntsville, a reduction of approximately 70 per cent. Reduction of services is short sighted. We need a full-service hospital. Our community and population are growing. In the summer, seasonal residents almost double the population.
The reduction of service came to light in the Huntsville Doppler on Dec. 30 quoting Cheryl Harrison, CEO of MAHC, who indicated they were in confidential discussions. On Jan. 26, a response from Dr. R. Gupta, supported by local doctors, was sent to the "Muskoka Bulletin Board" on Facebook stating their objections.
The new hospital would see just 18 beds, with reduced services, and obstetrics moved to Huntsville.
Not only does this negatively affect our ability to serve the needs of our community, but it also reduces the ability to attract and retain medical staff. The previous full service two hospital plan was fully supported by Premier Doug Ford as well as Graydon Smith MPP, allocating $500 million to each hospital.
The reduction of service in this huge geographic area will have a far-reaching effect. Public meetings are being held, however, the final decision rests with MAHC Board of Directors. We urge everyone to contact their MPP, Premier, and cottage association to stop the cuts.
Mary Lynn Elliott, Bracebridge

This issue is critical. Where is Ford?A Toronto doctor offers a glimpse of emergency room chaos, Feb. 4
It is extremely frustrating and disheartening to read about the terribly long wait times for patients in the emergency departments of our hospitals. Dr. Raghu Venugopal is quoted as declaring, amongst numerous other deficiencies in the system, that the most urgent issue is the "lack of hospital beds."
Where is Premier Doug Ford and his government on this critical issue?
But then again, if Ford (or any of his family) had a medical situation necessitating a visit to an emergency department, rest assured he/she would be ushered right in for immediate treatment and would not have to spend eight hours or more in the waiting room with everyone else. Shameful.
Jeffrey Manly, Toronto
Buying votes with favours is not new'It's irresponsible': Justin Trudeau's natural resources minister is worried about a provincial rebellion brewing against Ottawa, Feb. 3
Yes, Energy and Natural Resources Minister Jonathan Wilkinson, Scott Moe's intransigence respecting the collection of carbon taxes on heating fuel may be irresponsible. He should admit publicly, however, that the Premier's reaction to the exemption of heating oil from the carbon tax was provoked by something even more cynical and irresponsible which Minister of Rural Economic Development, Rudie Hutchins, highlighted most eloquently when she said that Western and Prairie provinces should vote Liberal if they want to secure some extra carve-outs in the federal carbon pricing policy.
The last time I recall the federal government so blatantly using federal money to buy votes in favoured ridings, Prime Minister Brian Mulroney was stealing an F-18 maintenance contract from Manitoba and awarding it to his home province of Quebec. Manitoba paid him back by refusing to approve the Charlottetown Constitutional Accord.
Irresponsibility is usually reciprocal, Mr. Wilkinson.
Patrick Cowan, North York
Mayor Chow putting life experiences to good use
I recently finished reading Mayor Oliva Chow's autobiography "My Journey" and was very impressed with her story. I can see that she took all her life experiences and is putting them to good use to the benefit of the city she now governs.
Mayor Chow is a welcome change from all other politicians who have governed recently. All talk and no action.
Please, give her a chance (and time) to realize her dreams for the city she loves.
Dina Moyal, Innisfil

Document 261 of 500

Letters: Matt Elliott: Olivia Chow is spending millions on Toronto renters in her budget, Federal green grants program comes to sudden end for Ontario homeowners, Alberta premier's policy changes around trans youth could be harmful: doctors, A Toronto doctor offers a glimpse of emergency room chaos, 'It's irresponsible': Justin Trudeau's natural resources minister is worried about a provincial rebellion brewing against Ottawa,

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/2923261171/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Matt+Elliott%3A+Olivia+Chow+is+spending+millions+on+Toronto+renters+in+her+budget%2C+Federal+green+grants+program+comes+to+sudden+end+for+Ontario+homeowners%2C+Alberta+premier%27s+policy+changes+around+trans+youth+could+be+harmful%3A+doctors%2C+A+Toronto+doctor+offers+a+glimpse+of+emergency+room+chaos%2C+%27It%27s+irresponsible%27%3A+Justin+Trudeau%27s+natural+resources+minister+is+worried+about+a+provincial+rebellion+brewing+against+Ottawa%2C&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Chow knows that renters do vote
Matt Elliott: Olivia Chow is spending millions on Toronto renters in her budget, Feb. 6
It was great to read Matt Elliott's article on renters. Toronto Mayor Olive Chow is the first mayor that I know of who has championed renters, who make up approximately 50 per cent of residents in Toronto, when it comes to property taxes.
Elliott pointed out that the mayor got 45 per cent of eligible renters who supported Chow, compared to 34 per cent of voters who live in homeowners' households. The myth that renters don't vote has been blown out of the water. The big number of $49 million, the approximate value of additional spending to support people who rent their homes is a huge acknowledgment that renters (voters) have power when exercised.
John Plumadore, chair Tenants Network Toronto
Bad time to cancel green grant program
Federal green grants program comes to sudden end for Ontario homeowners, Feb. 5
It's not a very wise decision to target Ontario with cancellation of this green grant/rebate program, especially with our scheduled byelection in Durham Riding on March 4.
This program has been an excellent way to spend collected carbon taxes and reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
I urge the Justin Trudeau government to reverse this decision and to announce new funding in the spring budget. Obviously, this green initiative has been hugely successful.
Ontarians should not be unfairly targeted with this cancellation.
Jim McEwen, Bowmanville
Danielle Smith's double standard is remarkable
Alberta premier's policy changes around trans youth could be harmful: doctors, Feb. 2
I find it odd that during COVID, Premier Danielle Smith espoused "my body, my choice," supporting people who were anti-vaccination and freedom fighters, who want the government to back off.
And now, the premier is imposing rules on transgender people saying in effect "your body, OUR choice" and there is no choice.
Which is it? The double standard and political convenience is remarkable.
The full consequences are yet to be realized. Perhaps listening to Tucker Carlson last month wasn't so benign?
Rev. John Pentland, Hillhurst United Church, Calgary
Save the South Muskoka Hospital
Muskoka needs support and input from the public, cottagers/cottage associations, tourism partners and seasonal residents. Everyone who visits, lives or holidays in beautiful Muskoka should be concerned as this could affect you or a family member. Our community no longer has a local newspaper and I feel the general public is not aware of this issue.
Muskoka Algonquin Healthcare is in discussions to reduce service at the planned replacement of the South Muskoka Hospital serving Bracebridge, Gravenhurst and other South Muskoka communities. Originally proposed as a full-service hospital, slated for 2027, it is now being downgraded to 18 beds with obstetrics and in-patient service moving to Huntsville, a reduction of approximately 70 per cent. Reduction of services is short sighted. We need a full-service hospital. Our community and population are growing. In the summer, seasonal residents almost double the population.
The reduction of service came to light in the Huntsville Doppler on Dec. 30 quoting Cheryl Harrison, CEO of MAHC, who indicated they were in confidential discussions. On Jan. 26, a response from Dr. R. Gupta, supported by local doctors, was sent to the "Muskoka Bulletin Board" on Facebook stating their objections.
The new hospital would see just 18 beds, with reduced services, and obstetrics moved to Huntsville.
Not only does this negatively affect our ability to serve the needs of our community, but it also reduces the ability to attract and retain medical staff. The previous full service two hospital plan was fully supported by Premier Doug Ford as well as MPP Graydon Smith, allocating $500 million to each hospital.
The reduction of service in this huge geographic area will have a far-reaching effect. Public meetings are being held, however, the final decision rests with MAHC Board of Directors. We urge everyone to contact their MPP, premier and cottage association to stop the cuts.
Mary Lynn Elliott, Bracebridge
Where is Ford amid ER chaos?
A Toronto doctor offers a glimpse of emergency room chaos, Feb. 4
It is extremely frustrating and disheartening to read about the terribly long wait times for patients in the emergency departments of our hospitals. Dr. Raghu Venugopal is quoted as declaring, amongst numerous other deficiencies in the system, that the most urgent issue is the "lack of hospital beds."
Where is Premier Doug Ford and his government on this critical issue? But then again, if Ford (or any of his family) had a medical situation necessitating a visit to an emergency department, rest assured they would be ushered right in for immediate treatment and would not have to spend eight hours or more in the waiting room with everyone else. Shameful.
Jeffrey Manly, Toronto
Buying votes with favours is nothing new
'It's irresponsible': Justin Trudeau's natural resources minister is worried about a provincial rebellion brewing against Ottawa, Feb. 3
Yes, Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe's intransigence respecting the collection of carbon taxes on heating fuel may be irresponsible. Federal Energy and Natural Resources Minister Jonathan Wilkinson should admit publicly, however, that Moe's reaction to the exemption of heating oil from the carbon tax was provoked by something even more cynical and irresponsible which federal Minister of Rural Economic Development, Gudie Hutchings, highlighted when she said that Western and Prairie provinces should vote Liberal if they want to secure some extra carve-outs in the federal carbon pricing policy.
The last time I recall the federal government so blatantly using federal money to buy votes in favoured ridings, Brian Mulroney was stealing an F-18 maintenance contract from Manitoba and awarding it to his home province of Quebec. Manitoba paid him back by refusing to approve the Charlottetown Constitutional Accord.
Irresponsibility is usually reciprocal, Mr. Wilkinson.
Patrick Cowan, North York
CREDIT: ;

Document 262 of 500

Premier is ignoring Stiles, but she's focused on workers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premier-is-ignoring-stiles-shes-focused-on/docview/2923260541/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+is+ignoring+Stiles%2C+but+she%27s+focused+on+workers&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The woman in the black toque clambers onstage in the mid-winter chill, flanked by union leaders braced for battle.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles is introduced as "a friend of hospital workers" to hundreds of cheering protesters, many wearing the same black toques emblazoned with their rallying cry: "Save our Hospitals!"
Stiles starts with a chant - predictable but popular: "Hey, hey! Ho, ho! Doug Ford has got to go!"
Followed by a boast: "We, the people of Ontario, we have managed to push this government over its heels over and over again."
Followed by a promise: "Solidarity" with the workers of Ontario, "not just in good times but on picket lines, too."
Know your audience.
Wrapping up her pep talk, Stiles returns to her perch at Queen's Park just up the street. Sitting in her third-floor offices, the Leader of Ontario's Official Opposition ponders her proximity to power.
She has never been so close and yet so far. Stiles sits directly above the Premier's Office, yet is running far behind the ruling Progressive Conservatives in public opinion polls (while also trailing the rival Liberals).
Can she grow her audience?
Away from the crowds and the speeches, surrounded by family photos and political mementoes, Stiles takes stock of Ontario's political realities. In an interview marking her first full year since taking over the party leadership - unopposed, untested, unknown - she talks about her determination to change how the NDP does politics.
"We are the party of working people - we have those connections to unions and labour," she tells me.
No political party can keep preaching to the converted, nor take any constituency for granted, Stiles argues. Not the NDP, nor Ford's Tories as they aim their populist, affordability appeal to working-class voters.
"We are in a really deeply divided time," Stiles says. "I think Doug Ford is making the same mistake - he has been presumptuous, his assumption that he owns the vote of working people."
Stiles claims credit for leading the charge against Ford's attempts to carve up the Greenbelt's protected lands, forcing him to backtrack and respect his previous promises. She rails against the "selling-off of Ontario" and the "piece by piece privatization" of the public sector, especially in health care.
"This is a corrupt government."
But the voting and polling tells a different story. Ford romped to a re-election victory in 2022 and his Tories maintain a commanding lead in the latest Abacus Data poll last month: 38 per cent PC versus 27 per cent Liberal and 23 per cent NDP.
After losing nine seats in the last election, the New Democrats are down another three MPPs - after a humbling byelection loss in Kitchener Centre (the riding went Green after NDP incumbent Laura Mae Lindo resigned early); and the expulsion of two others from caucus (Mike Mantha for an improper office relationship, and Sarah Jama for repeated insubordination).
Despite the downturn, Stiles is determined to lay the groundwork for fresh gains in unheld ridings (the PCs currently have 79 seats, the NDP 28, Liberals nine and Greens two). The Liberals have reclaimed second place in most polls and grabbed outsized media attention last year during a leadership race that elected Bonnie Crombie.
Now, Ford and the Tories are focusing their attacks and paid ads on Crombie. And largely ignoring Stiles.
"They can go ahead and spend their millions of dollars fighting each other, while we take a different path," she muses. "He's gonna spend a lot of time and money defining who the Liberals are, and we get to define who we are. So I'm actually fine with that."
She insists the Liberals lack credibility on public services after selling off part of Hydro One and privatizing ServiceOntario. "They have to redefine themselves, they have to establish themselves."
How will Stiles redefine and re-establish the New Democrats?
"I think politics is changing, it isn't politics as usual - people are looking for something different," she says, citing the record-low turnout of 43.5 per cent in the last election.
"Why are we seeing such a low voter turnout? I happen to believe it's really not a healthy thing to scold people, blaming people for not voting. I think the question is on us as politicians and political actors - why are you not giving people the reason that they need to feel that there's any hope and purpose in government?"
Good question. Many New Democrats and Liberals blamed their own former leaders in the last election for failing to motivate voters, allowing Ford's Tories to outperform the opposition.
Stiles is determined to learn lessons by touring the province non-stop. And consulting with her fellow NDP leaders across the country about their own campaigns.
"I don't know if I'm allowed to talk about this, but I'll tell you anyway: We've connected over dinner recently, almost all of the NDP leaders across the country and the premiers, and it's really inspiring to talk to folks like (Manitoba Premier) Wab Kinew."
Kinew's private advice?
"You have to have patience ... take the time, do the work properly, line everything up."
Her response?
"I don't have patience!"
But she is mindful that politics, especially provincially, is a long game - out of the media spotlight, out of the public eye, out of the bubble.
"I am ambitious, right? I am competitive, I am a little bit impatient," she says, laughing. "We've waited long enough - and I think the people of Ontario have waited long enough."
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Subject: Privatization; Voters; Political leadership; Union leadership; Voter behavior; Political parties

Business indexing term: Subject: Privatization Union leadership

Title: Premier is ignoring Stiles, but she's focused on workers

Document 263 of 500

Chow backs MZO city staff opposed: Rexdale rezoning will convert employment land into a mosque and 220 rental housing units

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/chow-backs-mzo-city-staff-opposed/docview/2923260510/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chow+backs+MZO+city+staff+opposed%3A+Rexdale+rezoning+will+convert+employment+land+into+a+mosque+and+220+rental+housing+units&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mayor Olivia Chow backed a request for a minister's zoning order at an Etobicoke property late last year, despite city planning staff's objections to the plan, the Star has learned.
The owners of 65 Rexdale Blvd. want to rebuild an existing mosque on the site, as part of a new mixed-use development that would include 220 rental units. But the land is zoned for employment, and residential uses are prohibited. The provincial MZO would override city planning policy and allow mixed-use development, including new homes.
It's a case that pitted two of Toronto's most pressing interests against one another: the need for more housing, especially rental, and the necessity of preserving land for good jobs.
Supporters of the redevelopment say it will deliver low-cost housing to an area of the city that needs it. Up to 30 per cent of the units in the nine-storey development would be designated affordable, while rent revenue would help make the busy mosque financially sustainable.
According to sources, the mayor's office turned to city planning staff last fall and asked if they could support the expedited zoning order request. But staff said no. The sources, who the Star is not naming because they weren't authorized to speak publicly about discussions around the plan, said staff believed 65 Rexdale wasn't a good candidate for residential uses, and the request did not follow the normal planning process meant to protect employment land.
But the request went ahead anyway. While MZO asks from the city typically come to council with a report offering planning staff's analysis, this one was via a committee motion from local councillor Vince Crisanti. There were no staff recommendations on the ask, which council approved 17 to 4 on Dec. 13.
In an email, Chow's office confirmed she worked with Crisanti to get the request to council.
"Building new housing is a top priority for Mayor Chow," Arianne Robinson, Chow's press secretary, said when asked about the mayor's support. Chow has set an ambitious target of building 65,000 rental units by 2030.
Crisanti didn't return a request for comment.
The Dec. 13 council vote represents at least the fourth time Chow has supported an MZO, a sometimes controversial tool that allows the province to bypass local planning rules. The Star reported last month that Housing Minister Paul Calandra worked with the mayor on three other orders in Toronto.
The property at 65 Rexdale is in Premier Doug Ford's Etobicoke North riding. Its owner, the non-profit International Muslims Organization of Toronto (IMO), said in a public presentation last April, before Chow was elected, that the group had "political traction" at the city, and had received "assurances from the province" that the MZO would be granted.
A spokesperson for the premier declined to answer questions about whether Ford backs the MZO. A spokesperson for Calandra also wouldn't say whether the minister intends to grant the request. The Ontario PC government has mostly paused issuing MZOs until it completes a review it launched in December after facing criticism it was imposing too many.
Both the mayor's and minister's office said advancing the redevelopment wasn't related to talks between Chow and Ford over a new financial deal for the city that concluded in November.
The IMO has operated a mosque at 65 Rexdale since 1994. The building near the intersection of Islington Avenue and Hwy. 401 hosts about 1,000 worshippers for Friday prayers, and both Chow and Ford have attended events there.
As a rule, city staff are reluctant to convert employment lands to residential. The properties are often strategically located near rail lines and highways that facilitate industrial activity, and former chief planner Gregg Lintern has likened them to the Greenbelt because once they're converted, they - and the well-paying jobs they provide newcomers and others without professional qualifications - can't be replaced.
A 2022 city land needs assessment also found there was "more than enough" space for anticipated housing demand in the city's residentially-zoned land, and called for not just retention, but intensification, of employment land.
In the case of 65 Rexdale, despite its zoning, the property isn't currently being used for employment. However, Lintern warned council last July an employment conversion at one property risks setting off "unmanaged land speculation" that erodes employment zones. That's because allowing residential uses boosts a property's value, and a conversion at one site provides a precedent for other nearby landowners to make similar requests.
The Greater Toronto Airport Authority has raised concerns about the proposal at 65 Rexdale, because the property is in the Pearson operating area, a designated zone intended to create a buffer between the airport and noise-sensitive land uses like housing. Last year, staff cited similar concerns while opposing a conversion request at 2200 Islington Ave., around the corner from 65 Rexdale.
Coun. Shelley Carroll is among Chow's progressive allies who have spoken passionately about the need to preserve employment lands. In October, she described them as a "sacred boundary" that protects jobs for the city's less affluent.
In an interview, she said she was initially concerned about the 65 Rexdale plan, but voted for the MZO when she learned the property was at the "tail end" of the Pearson zone, close to neighbourhoods already zoned residential.
The city does have a formal process for approving employment conversions, called a municipal planning review, which started in 2020 and wrapped up last year. IMO didn't submit a request to that major review, which would have been the normal channel to pursue the rezoning.
In an email, the IMO told the Star it ran out of time to submit a request through the review, and sought out the MZO to "expedite the building of residential units."
As for its statement about "traction" at city hall and provincial "assurances," the group asserted the MZO plan has long had government support because it would help with the housing crisis.
IMO noted that 65 Rexdale already isn't being used for employment, and its location near transit and midrise apartments to its north make it appropriate for residential uses. There is already a library, pharmacy, and big-box retail stores nearby.
"The best use of the site is to add the residential component," it said.
The IMO has yet to submit a development application. If the MZO is granted, council has asked the minister to consider rescinding it if there isn't "reasonable progress" on the development within two years.
And while staff didn't support the MZO, they helped Crisanti craft the wording of his motion to ensure that if the order goes ahead, the development will still have to go through a site planning process that will allow staff to press for changes that address their concerns.
IMO's plan is opposed by some members of the mosque's community, who have started an online petition claiming IMO leadership is planning to "demolish" the mosque and profit from the redevelopment. The IMO rejects the allegations, and said the group's board members "have not and will not personally benefit" from the plan.
With files from Victoria Gibson.
CREDIT: Ben Spurr Toronto Star With files from Victoria Gibson.

Subject: Councils; Urban planning; Zoning; Employment; Mosques & temples; Redevelopment; Airports

Business indexing term: Subject: Employment Airports

Location: Etobicoke Ontario Canada; Canada

People: Chow, Olivia

Title: Chow backs MZO city staff opposed:   Rexdale rezoning will convert employment land into a mosque and 220 rental housing units

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: A.3

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Feb 8, 2024

Section: News

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 03190781

Language of publication: English

ProQuest document ID: 2923260510

Document 264 of 500

Mayor Olivia Chow backed MZO for Etobicoke lot despite staff objections

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mayor-olivia-chow-backed-mzo-etobicoke-lot/docview/2923260452/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mayor+Olivia+Chow+backed+MZO+for+Etobicoke+lot+despite+staff+objections&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

"The best use of the site is to add the residential component," it said.
The IMO has yet to submit a development application. If the MZO is granted, council has asked the minister to consider rescinding it if there isn't "reasonable progress" on the development within two years.
And while staff didn't support the MZO, they helped Crisanti craft the wording of his motion to ensure that if the order goes ahead, the development will still have to go through a site planning process that will allow staff to press for changes that address their concerns.
IMO's plan is opposed by some members of the mosque's community, who have started an online petition claiming IMO leadership is planning to "demolish" the mosque and profit from the redevelopment. The IMO rejects the allegations, and said the group's board members "have not and will not personally benefit" from the plan.
With files from Victoria Gibson.
CAPTION: The owners of 65 Rexdale Blvd. want to rebuild an existing mosque on site, as part of a new mixed-use development that would include 220 rental units.

Subject: Councils; Urban planning; Zoning; Employment; Mosques & temples; Redevelopment; Airports

Business indexing term: Subject: Employment Airports

Document 265 of 500

Why Ford's PCs won't follow Smith on trans rights: 'This isn't Alberta'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-fords-pcs-wont-follow-smith-on-trans-rights/docview/2923260436/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+Ford%27s+PCs+won%27t+follow+Smith+on+trans+rights%3A+%27This+isn%27t+Alberta%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The premier was shaking his head before the reporter had finished asking him the question.
"No," said Doug Ford when queried Monday about whether the Ontario government would consider "any similar action" to the controversial moves Alberta Premier Danielle Smith is making on transgender rights.
"We have a law here; we're leaving everything alone," he said firmly.
By immediately shutting down the possibility of his Progressive Conservatives following the decision of Alberta's United Conservatives to restrict youth hormone treatments and surgeries, Ford was, as is his wont, keeping in the mainstream of public opinion in Ontario.
That he did so two days before a long-scheduled private breakfast meeting with Smith - no press cameras - was purely coincidental.
That he did so 150 days after musing off the cuff to partisan supporters at a summer picnic - following Saskatchewan and New Brunswick implementing contentious trans policies - that "it's not up to the teachers ... to indoctrinate our kids" was not.
Underscoring this, Ford's office issued a statement late Tuesday that "the premier was clear that our focus remains on delivering on the commitments we made during the campaign, which doesn't include replicating the changes the Alberta government unveiled last week."
That missive followed a wide-ranging discussion on a number of key issues among Tory MPPs last Friday in Niagara Falls during debate on the government's priorities for its second mandate.
Sources told the Star that one MPP - a rare social conservative left after Ford defenestrated most of them over his insistence that all PC candidates in 2022 be vaccinated against COVID-19 - wondered to some of their colleagues where the Ontario Tories stood on Smith's trans crusade.
Patiently, Ford and his key advisers explained it does not even register among the 25 or so things Ontarians most care about.
"It was basically that 'this isn't Alberta,'" said one Tory insider, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations, when asked about the tenor of that part of the conversation.
"What is the problem we are trying to solve?" said another PC source, baffled by Smith's actions and warning Alberta's premier is fanning flames of intolerance against an already vulnerable minority.
Ontarians, the MPPs were told, are far more concerned about the high cost of living, the housing shortage, health care and education than they are about a narrow issue that affects perhaps several dozen Ontario youth.
The tone had been set last Thursday, after Smith's Alberta announcement, when Health Minister Sylvia Jones emphasized Ontario would be keeping its gender surgery policy as is - no age restrictions and a requirement for approval from two doctors.
"We're not making any changes as it relates to gender-affirming surgeries in the province of Ontario," Jones had said in Peterborough.
Similarly, Education Minister Stephen Lecce's office had said there would no policy revisions related to children choosing to use a different pronoun in school.
Speaking to journalists last summer, Lecce had said "parents must be fully involved" in any such pronoun decision, but he has no interest in legislating measures.
That's because Queen's Park believes school boards and teachers here are understanding and accommodating to trans kids and their parents and guardians. (The Toronto District School Board has rules against outing students regardless of age.)
"If we don't have examples of real issues, let's stop pretending to import U.S.-style issues to Canada," said a third Ontario Tory, referring to the culture wars that dominate political discourse stateside.
"We don't need to make this a controversy for people who have among the highest suicide rates."
Another PC insider said Smith is playing politics because she's leery of insurgent grassroots groups like Take Back Alberta, which helped to force out her more moderate predecessor, Jason Kenney, in 2022.
Ford faces no such potential threat from the right in Ontario - and after he apparently vanquished social conservatives over sex education curriculum changes in 2019 and vaccines in 2021, none is forthcoming.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Education; LGBTQ rights

Location: Ontario Canada; Canada

People: Smith, Danielle; Ford, Doug

Company / organization: Name: Progressive Conservative Party-Canada; NAICS: 813940

Document 266 of 500

Public banks should serve the public

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/public-banks-should-serve/docview/2923258989/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Public+banks+should+serve+the+public&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It seems like a good rule-of-thumb that the housing of frail and vulnerable people should not be left to those with a voracious appetite for profit.
This could be regarded as a key takeaway from the pandemic, when it emerged that death rates were four times higher in private, for-profit long-term-care homes than in municipally operated ones.
So it's curious that the Ford government, citing the need for more long-term-care facilities, recently announced plans for a new infrastructure bank that would rely on private sector financiers, rather than the tried-and-true method of publicly financing our infrastructure.
The new bank, as structured by Doug Ford's government, seems to be less about meeting public needs and more about providing lucrative opportunities for major investors.
It's likely to exacerbate the already serious problem of privatization in long-term care. In 2022 - after the pandemic disaster - the Ford government awarded contracts for 30,000 additional long-term-care beds to private operators, including chains with some of the highest pandemic death rates.
The new bank will mean that even more public money allocated for long-term care gets diverted into private profits, says Natalie Mehra, director of the Ontario Health Coalition.
Traditionally, Ontario has raised money to build infrastructure by selling bonds to the public, and this public financing method has worked well, according to Brian Lewis, former chief economist for the province.
Teaming up with the private sector is often sold to the public as a way to entice private investors to put their money into public projects, thereby saving the public money.
But this makes no sense. To believe that involving private investors saves us money, you'd have to believe that these private players - including notoriously penny-pinching private equity firms - give us their money or provide it at bargain-basement rates. (Spoiler alert: they don't.)
Rather, they insist on collecting high rates of return. This diverts a significant chunk of public money to their profits, and drives up the cost of our infrastructure.
We should have learned this lesson long ago. In 2014, Ontario's auditor general investigated 74 infrastructure projects - built as "public-private partnerships" - and concluded they had cost Ontario taxpayers $8 billion more than if the government had publicly financed the infrastructure.
Similarly, B.C.'s auditor general concluded the province paid only 4 per cent interest when it raised the money itself, compared to rates as high as 14 per cent paid to private investors on infrastructure projects.
Furthermore, introducing profits undermines the public goals of the project, says Thomas Marois, a political economist at McMaster University who studies public banks around the world.
Marois points to successful public banks like Holland's BNG, which provides low-cost financing to municipalities, explicitly declaring on its website: "Instead of maximizing profits, our priority is to maximize the social impact of our activities."
Another widely respected public bank, Germany's KfW, is notable for its democratic governance.
Marois notes that Canada has an impressive history of creating useful public banks - including the Alberta Treasury Branch, Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation, Farm Credit Canada - that don't involve private investors.
But, in 2017, the Trudeau government broke with this tradition when it created the Canada Infrastructure Bank. Designed with heavy input from Wall Street, the bank was structured to attract major investors with high rates of return, and it's done little to advance the public interest. In 2022, a parliamentary committee recommended it be abolished.
Marois argues it should instead focus on low-cost public financing, without private investors.
The Ford government is now replicating the failed privatization model in its new infrastructure bank. One telltale sign the bank will likely have a pro-corporate approach is its governance. Ford has appointed former Scotiabank CEO Brian J. Porter as chair, and the two other board members so far both have backgrounds in the financial sector.
But what we need is a public bank, governed by people representative of the community, that isn't all about maximizing profits.
Linda McQuaig, an author, journalist and activist, is a freelance contributing columnist for The Star.
CREDIT: Linda McQuaig, an author, journalist and activist, is a freelance contributing columnist for The Star.;

Subject: Privatization; Long term health care; Infrastructure; Pandemics; Profits; Rates of return

Business indexing term: Subject: Privatization Infrastructure Profits Rates of return

Location: Canada

Title: Public banks should serve the public

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: A.15

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Feb 8, 2024

Section: Opinion

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 03190781

What Doug Ford insiders say about Danielle Smith's plan for transgender rights - and what it means for Ontario

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/what-doug-ford-insiders-say-about-danielle-smiths/docview/2923473608/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+Doug+Ford+insiders+say+about+Danielle+Smith%27s+plan+for+transgender+rights+-+and+what+it+means+for+Ontario&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 269 of 500

Fewer cops, more money and why it matters: The numbers behind both sides of the Toronto police budget debate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/fewer-cops-more-money-why-matters-numbers-behind/docview/2923117419/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Fewer+cops%2C+more+money+and+why+it+matters%3A+The+numbers+behind+both+sides+of+the+Toronto+police+budget+debate&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto's police budget is again at the centre of the city's debate over spending.
It's no wonder - with more than $1 billion currently allotted for the service, the police budget is the city's single most expensive taxpayer-funded line item.
However, recent rhetoric over the state of policing flying between Mayor Olivia Chow at city hall and Chief Myron Demkiw from police headquarters, as well as the association representing officers, might be leaving taxpayers confused about what's at stake.
Residents have faced a barrage of flyers in their mailboxes and social media campaigns using alarming stock images to warn of how long they'll have to wait for help from the police. Meanwhile, Chow and her supporters on council have pushed back against the police narrative, saying there is no cut to the budget.
Here's what you need to know to understand where both sides are coming from:
Does Toronto police have fewer officers than in the past?
Yes.
The number of uniform officers - the number usually cited by police, which excludes administrative and other "civilian" staff - has fluctuated. But compared to the peak of 5,651 in 2009, the end of 2023 saw that number down to 5,127 officers on the force, according to annual statistical reports released by the service. (Police say the average number of cops deployed last year was actually lower than that before a new class of 122 recruits became available in October.)
In social media and in interviews with reporters, police have repeatedly pointed out that this means the city has just 37 more officers on the street than in 1999, despite significant population growth.
That's true. The number of cops has not kept up with the city's population.
It's worth noting that the number of officers is a fluid total that's also affected by officers leaving to work for other forces, quitting to pursue other careers or retiring.
To put it in simple terms: There was one cop for every 469 Torontonians at the peak of staffing in 2009; now, it's one for every 590.
But hasn't the police budget been increasing?
Also yes.
The police budget has been steadily increasing since 1999. Of course, you have to factor in inflation to account for some of that difference. But even when adjusted, using the Bank of Canada's inflation calculator, Toronto's gross police budget grew well above the rate of inflation before being essentially frozen in the pandemic years, like a lot of city departments.
Today, the figure being debated is nearly $1.2 billion - in 2023 dollars, that's 46 per cent more than what the city spent on policing in 1999.
This is significant growth, but it's not the biggest jump.
The Star compared police spending growth to other city departments, net any money brought in by the department in fees or outside funding. Adjusted for inflation, the police's net budget grew 32 per cent between 1999 and 2023. That's compared, for example, to a seven per cent increase for the city's shelter, support and housing division; 37 per cent for Toronto Fire Services; and an 83 per cent increase for parks and recreation.
So where is the money going?
According to police statistics, what the service is spending per officer on salaries has dramatically increased in the last 25 years - critical when you consider police consistently spend about 90 per cent of their budget on salaries and benefits.
In 2023 dollars, police spent around $161,000 on salaries and benefits per uniform officer in 1999. Last year, that figure hit $232,000 per officer (To be clear, this all-in average does not reflect a typical Toronto cop's income).
So while the budget has been increasing year-over-year, and the number of officers has not kept up with population growth, what it costs to pay for the officers the service does still have has increased a whopping 44 per cent above inflation.
Although that change accounts for much of the police budget growth, it's important to note that salary increases are dictated by the collective agreement between uniform officers and the police board, and aren't up to the chief.
In 2019, the last time a contract was settled, officers received a wage hike of 11.1 per cent over five years without major concessions. That beat what other city workers negotiated in 2016 - a five per cent increase over four years, while losing some job security and benefits.
Are police hiring new officers?
That's the plan. The police service laid out a plan in its 2024 budget request to hire four new classes of 90 recruits each. Counting cadets graduating from 2023 classes and planned separations from the service, the police service estimated the uniform strength of the force would be 5,433 by the end of 2024 - or a net increase of just over 300 officers compared to 2023.
Is the police budget being 'cut'?
No. Whichever version passes city council, the police budget is going up.
Even the controversial budget being debated by city hall allocates slightly more to police than in 2023. That total doesn't include money the city has set aside for upcoming contract negotiations with officers. It also doesn't include additional one-time funds the city is promising, like $10 million available from a reserve fund for policing special events.
However, that proposed budget is $12.6 million less than the request the police board, which oversees the force, previously approved; this difference is what police have been referring to as a "cut."
It is unusual - but not unheard of - for city hall to not approve the board-recommended budget.
During the two previous terms when John Tory was mayor, he was also an influential member of the police board, often able to iron out the budget request before it landed at city hall. Chow, meanwhile, is not on the police board.
Why is Demkiw saying police can't hire new officers?
Though city hall sets the police budget, politicians cannot direct the police on operational matters - like where to spend the money.
So how police account for $12.6 million less than they say they need is up to them. Demkiw has previously said that the reduction threatens plans to hire new recruits. How much so is not clear, given that a budget difference of that size shouldn't prevent them from hiring all four planned recruit classes.
Demkiw was asked at a press conference at police headquarters last week whether the gap would really impact the service's ability to keep the city safe.
"We will have to evaluate all our service delivery and look at what other essential services we're going to have to degrade or cut all together in order to make sure we do what we can to address our core service needs," he said.
"In particular our ability to respond to the residents of Toronto when they need us the most."
He suggested cuts could come from the neighbourhood community officer program, which he called part of their efforts at "proactive" policing.
Former mayor John Sewell, a long-time member of the Toronto Police Accountability Coalition, said he believes there is lots of room for the police to cut their budget.
"In any budget of $1.2 billion, there's bound to be one per cent of efficiencies that you can find," Sewell said.
What about crime? Isn't the city less safe than it was before?
When looking at the big picture, Toronto is much safer than it was decades ago and is relatively safe compared to other North American and international cities.
But this question often feels subjective - depending on where you live in the city and your lived experience with crime.
Statistics Canada produces an annual crime severity index, which looks at both the rate of certain reported crimes - how often a crime is occurring relative to a municipality's population - and the severity of those crimes. The severity is determined by assigning a weight to crimes based on the rate people are being sent to prison and the length of those stays.
This is one of the better ways to track crime trends over time.
In 1999, the severity index for what are considered violent crimes was 133. In 2022 - the most current figure - it was 92. By that metric, Toronto was higher than Ontario on the whole, but lower than other municipalities like Vancouver (106), Edmonton (127) and Winnipeg (204).
According to recent numbers reported by Toronto police, there have been some encouraging developments - like a drop in reported shootings last year, with the number of people killed or injured in a shooting down from a peak of 284 in 2019 to 168 last year.
Meanwhile, what Toronto police call "major crimes" - including homicides, assaults and robberies but also some crimes that Statistics Canada does not count as "violent," like auto thefts or break and enters - are at a decade high with almost 49,400 incidents reported compared to nearly 32,500 in 2014.
But that has largely been fuelled by a surge in auto thefts - a particularly high-profile trend.
The city is also seeing other concerning trends like a reported rise in youth violence. Statistics Canada reported a national rise in the number of young people under the age of 18 who were arrested for homicide - 90 in 2023, with a quarter of those from Toronto.
What's the effect on the ground?
Toronto police say it was taking over 22 minutes on average for officers to respond to "priority one" calls - the most serious of 911 emergency calls - as of the end of 2023. That's up from a recent low of about 12 minutes in 2011.
Police attribute the problem to a reduced number of uniform officers being asked to respond to more serious calls.
According to data provided to the Star by police, the number of priority one calls has gone up over 60 per cent in the last decade, but lesser priority calls have all decreased.
Police assign the priority rating based on the severity and threat of each type of call. Most serious calls - including stabbings and shootings - default to priority two and can be upgraded or downgraded at the discretion of the dispatcher.
Priorities one through three are considered emergency calls; four through six are considered non-emergency situations, such as calls for a noisy party, or an accident causing property damage.
In 1995, police established a target of responding to 85 per cent of priority one calls within six minutes. In 2019, police met that target only about 28 per cent of the time, according to a report from the city's auditor general.
Police spokesperson Stephanie Sayer said in an email that "it is difficult to establish formal targets because response times depend on a number of dynamic factors" like staffing levels and the fluctuating number of calls dispatched and their assigned priority.
"What's for certain is that an average response time of 22 minutes to priority one calls is not acceptable," she said, "and there is a direct correlation between the number of officers available to respond to a call and the time it takes to attend."
In an earlier interview with the Star, Demkiw said police have moved staff around to tackle the response-time issue. Those changes included disbanding special task forces and not filling vacancies in specialized units, like homicide and sex crimes. Demkiw noted, however, that a task force for auto thefts was recently reconstituted to deal with that growing problem.
Sewell, from TPAC, said police and Demkiw need to examine how they are deploying staff.
"He is not being a good manager," he said. "It's as simple as that."
Would more police officers make the city safer?
Research suggests the amount of money a city spends on policing isn't clearly linked to crime rates, including a recent paper in the journal Canadian Public Policy that looked at 20 major municipalities, including Toronto.
"I think a lot of people simply assume that more money for the police will mean safer communities," co-author Akwasi Owusu-Bempah, an associate professor in sociology at the University of Toronto previously told the Star.
"We're demonstrating that that's not the case."
Police, of course, say otherwise.
Demkiw stressed the importance of not just crime rates but a community's perception of safety. Despite falling rates of shootings, he said, some communities still don't feel safe at home.
"Regardless of that number," some Torontonians may take "their children to play somewhere else than their neighbourhood," he said.
The notion that "police are somehow not contributing to people's sense of safety, I can tell you firsthand, I would debate that anywhere, anytime, because I've seen it in play."
What happens next?
The budget tabled at city hall right now is the budget proposed by city staff.
Last week, Mayor Olivia Chow released her own amendments which did not change the funding allotted for policing.
The whole of city council will meet on Feb. 14 to debate the budget, including police spending, and finalize those figures. Chow can use strong mayor powers bestowed on the city by Premier Doug Ford to veto any amendments made by council.

Document 270 of 500

Toronto Islanders have been paying far less property tax than mainland homeowners and renters. A motion at city hall aims to change that

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-islanders-have-been-paying-far-less/docview/2923016112/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+Islanders+have+been+paying+far+less+property+tax+than+mainland+homeowners+and+renters.+A+motion+at+city+hall+aims+to+change+that&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto Islanders pay dramatically less in taxes than other city dwellers - something a motion headed to council aims to change.
"There's something wrong here," Coun. Jon Burnside (Ward 16 - Don Valley East), who filed the motion to be heard at council this week, told the Star. "In what world does somebody pay $1,500 annually in property taxes? The answer is on no world other than the Island."
Toronto Islanders, who own their house but lease the land under it, on average pay about $1,530 per year in property taxes, for homes with an assessment value of $241,785, according to a 2022 analysis from the City of Toronto's Revenue Services Division.
On the mainland, homeowners paid an average of roughly $4,400 annually in 2022, based on a property's assessed value as determined by the Municipal Property Assessment Corporation, an agency hired by the province to do this work.
For the past 30 years, Island life has been protected by a law that permits residents on Ward's and Algonquin Islands to hold title to their homes and lease the lots on which their houses stand until 2092.
They have direct access to municipal services including an elementary school, ferry service and fire station, which Burnside said can cost taxpayers up to 10 times the amount to operate as its mainland counterpart.
"People supporting Islanders argue, 'Well, they don't own the land,' " Burnside said. "Neither do apartment dwellers but the tax is still included in their rent."
Burnside asked city staff for those numbers, too.
Tenants in Flemingdon Park pay an average of $4,320 in property tax annually as part of their rent for a single-bedroom unit, the city estimated. Two-bedroom renters were paying $5,520 per year based on average rent of $2,295 in the same area.
The Toronto Island Community Association declined the Star's request for comment.
"We are not willing to meet and discuss this matter," chair Tony Farebrother wrote in an email.
The Toronto Islands Residential Community Trust Corporation, which manages the land and buildings associated with the community, was unavailable for comment.
Property tax is the single-biggest expense many homeowners incur, apart from a mortgage. But they pay it on faith that the tax is, as it is supposed to be, administered fairly.
Burnside would like to see the city utilize a tool in its tax arsenal to ensure Islanders aren't paying less than their fair share.
The tool, called "area rating," allows the city to employ a special tax rate to an area with unique circumstances. He's asking city council to direct the chief financial officer and treasurer to determine whether it's feasible to use this tool on the Island.
"We'll ask for the report and see where it goes," Burnside said.
Burnside said the problem with the Island rests in the fact that home prices are set by the trust and sold through a lottery system. The trust set a cap on the value of Island homes that is substantially lower than what they would fetch on an open Toronto real estate market. The assessment industry relies on open-market transaction data to set values for tax purposes.
MPAC confirmed it is responsible for assessing the homes on Toronto Island.
"Since the homes on Toronto Island are not bought and sold on the open market, we use the cost approach to value these properties. This approach involves determining the value of the land and adding the replacement cost of the buildings, adjusted for depreciation," spokesperson Paula Chung said.
Land lease values are provided to MPAC by the Toronto Island trust administrator leading up to each provincewide reassessment. The last provincial reassessment was Jan. 1, 2016. The next cycle was intended to begin four years later but Premier Doug Ford postponed it because of the pandemic.
A home's age, size, condition, construction quality, and any improvements can affect assessment value using the cost approach, Chung wrote in an email.
A street-by-street analysis of 261 Island homes completed by the City of Toronto found the current value assessment of properties in 2022 ranged from $88,000 to $715,000 with annual property tax payments as low as $556 to a maximum of $4,518.
In December 2022, the average price of a detached home in Toronto was $1.6 million. The average assessment value of residential properties in the city that year was $697,185, since the province has not yet called for a reassessment and valuations for tax purposes are still supposed to peg a home's worth as of Jan. 1, 2016 and not its value in real time. This leaves the average 2022 homeowner in Toronto paying roughly $4,406 per year.
This isn't the first time the equity of the city's tax system has come under scrutiny.
A Toronto Star investigation last year revealed that Ontario's property tax system is neither fair nor transparent.
The series' exhaustive and innovative data analysis showed that homes are routinely taxed more than they should be, while others aren't taxed enough. Worse yet, the burden of this tax inequality is shouldered by those with the least-expensive houses, while owners of some of the city's most-expensive homes, nestled in some of the toniest neighbourhoods, catch a break. A retired tennis pro, a Toronto sports team executive and a former Blue Jays infielder all own or owned homes that were assessed at significantly less than what they paid for them. Working class families, meanwhile, are often paying too much tax.
This hidden systemic unfairness exists, the reporters found, because the publicly funded agency tasked with valuating properties for tax purposes has a flawed methodology that consistently under- and over-assesses homes' worth compared to their sales prices.
Mayor Olivia Chow has called for a Toronto-wide review of the property tax system following the Star's investigation.
CAPTION: Coun. Jon Burnside wants Toronto Islanders to pay property taxes that are more in line with mainland homeowners and renters.

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto
