Document 271 of 500

What's on the line: City hall wants to give the service $1.174 billion. Police are raising the alarm. Answers to key questions in the ongoing debate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/whats-on-line/docview/2922785845/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What%27s+on+the+line%3A+City+hall+wants+to+give+the+service+%241.174+billion.+Police+are+raising+the+alarm.+Answers+to+key+questions+in+the+ongoing+debate&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto's police budget is again at the centre of the city's debate over spending.
It's no wonder - with more than $1 billion currently allotted for the service, the police budget is the city's single most expensive taxpayer-funded line item.
However, recent rhetoric over the state of policing flying between Mayor Olivia Chow at city hall and Chief Myron Demkiw from police headquarters, as well as the association representing officers, might be leaving taxpayers confused about what's at stake.
Residents have faced a barrage of flyers in their mailboxes and social media campaigns using alarming stock images to warn of how long they'll have to wait for help from the police. Meanwhile, Chow and her supporters on council have pushed back against the police narrative, saying there is no cut to the budget.
Here's what you need to know to understand where both sides are coming from:
Are there now fewer Toronto police officers than in the past?
Yes.
The number of uniform officers - the number usually cited by police, which excludes administrative and other "civilian" staff - has fluctuated.
But compared to the peak of 5,651 in 2009, the end of 2023 saw that number down to 5,127 officers on the force, according to annual statistical reports released by the service. (Police say the average number of cops deployed last year was actually lower than that before a new class of 122 recruits became available in October.)
In social media and in interviews with reporters, police have repeatedly pointed out that this means the city has just 37 more officers on the street than in 1999, despite significant population growth.
That's true. The number of cops has not kept up with the city's population.
It's worth noting that the number of officers is a fluid total also affected by officers leaving to work for other forces, quitting to pursue other careers or retiring.
To put it in simple terms: There was one cop for every 469 Torontonians at the peak of staffing in 2009; now, it's one for every 590.
But hasn't the police budget
been increasing?
Also yes.
The police budget has been steadily increasing since 1999. Of course, you have to factor in inflation to account for some of that difference. But, even when adjusted, using the Bank of Canada's inflation calculator, Toronto's gross police budget grew well above the rate of inflation before being essentially frozen in the pandemic years, like a lot of city departments.
Today, the figure being debated is nearly $1.2 billion - in 2023 dollars, that's 46 per cent more than what the city spent on policing in 1999.
This is significant growth, but it's not the biggest jump.
The Star compared police spending growth to other city departments, net any money brought in by the department in fees or outside funding.
Adjusted for inflation, the police's net budget grew 32 per cent between 1999 and 2023. That's compared, for example, to a seven per cent increase for the city's shelter, support and housing division; 37 per cent for Toronto Fire Services; and an 83 per cent increase for parks and recreation.
So where is all the money
going?
According to police statistics, what the service is spending per officer on salaries has dramatically increased in the past 25 years - critical when you consider police consistently spend about 90 per cent of their budget on salaries and benefits.
In 2023 dollars, police spent around $161,000 on salaries and benefits per uniform officer in 1999. Last year, that figure hit $232,000 per officer. (To be clear, this all-in average does not reflect a typical Toronto cop's income).
So, while the budget has been increasing year-over-year, and the number of officers has not kept up with population growth, what it costs to pay for the officers the service does still have has increased a whopping 44 per cent above inflation.
Although that change accounts for much of the police budget growth, it's important to note that salary increases are dictated by the collective agreement between uniform officers and the police board, and aren't up to the chief.
In 2019, the last time a contract was settled, officers received a wage hike of 11.1 per cent over five years without major concessions.
That beat what other city workers negotiated in 2016 - amounting to a five per cent increase over four years, while losing some job security and benefits.
Are the police going to be hiring
new officers?
That's the plan. The police service laid out a plan in its 2024 budget request to hire four new classes of 90 recruits each.
Counting cadets graduating from 2023 classes and planned separations from the service, the police service estimated the uniform strength of the force would be 5,433 by the end of 2024 - or a net increase of just over 300 officers compared to 2023.
Is the police budget
actually being 'cut'?
No. Whichever version passes city council, the police budget is going up.
Even the controversial budget being debated by city hall allocates slightly more to police than in 2023. That total doesn't include money the city has set aside for upcoming contract negotiations with officers. It also doesn't include additional one-time funds the city is promising, like $10 million available from a reserve fund for policing special events.
However, that proposed budget is $12.6 million less than the request the police board, which oversees the force, previously approved; this difference is what police have been referring to as a "cut."
It is unusual - but not unheard of - for city hall to not approve the board-recommended budget.
During the two previous terms when John Tory was mayor, he was also an influential member of the police board, often able to iron out the budget request before it landed at city hall. Chow, meanwhile, is not on the police board.
Why is Demkiw saying police
can't hire new officers?
Though city hall sets the police budget, politicians cannot direct the police on operational matters - like where to spend the money.
So how police account for $12.6 million less than they say they need is up to them. Demkiw has previously said that the reduction threatens plans to hire new recruits. How much so is not clear, given that a budget difference of that size shouldn't prevent them from hiring all four planned recruit classes.
Demkiw was asked at a news conference at police headquarters last week whether the gap would really impact the service's ability to keep the city safe.
"We will have to evaluate all our service delivery and look at what other essential services we're going to have to degrade or cut all together in order to make sure we do what we can to address our core service needs," he said, "in particular, our ability to respond to the residents of Toronto when they need us the most."
He suggested cuts could come from the neighbourhood community officer program, which he called part of their efforts at "proactive" policing.
Former mayor John Sewell, a longtime member of the Toronto Police Accountability Coalition, said he believes there is lots of room for the police to cut its budget.
"In any budget of $1.2 billion, there's bound to be one per cent of efficiencies that you can find," said Sewell.
What about crime? Isn't the city
less safe than it was before?
When looking at the big picture, Toronto is much safer than it was decades ago and is relatively safe compared to other North American and international cities.
But this question often feels subjective - depending on where you live in the city and your lived experience with crime.
Statistics Canada produces an annual crime severity index, which looks at both the rate of certain reported crimes - how often a crime is occurring relative to a municipality's population - and the severity of those crimes.
The severity is determined by assigning a weight to crimes based on the rate people are being sent to prison and the length of those stays in prison.
This is one of the better ways to track crime trends over time.
In 1999, the severity index for what are considered violent crimes was 133. In 2022 - the most current figure - it was 92. By that metric, Toronto was higher than Ontario on the whole, but lower than other municipalities like Vancouver (106), Edmonton (127) and Winnipeg (204).
According to recent numbers reported by Toronto police, there have been some encouraging developments - like a drop in reported shootings last year, with the number of people killed or injured in a shooting down from a peak of 284 in 2019 to 168 last year.
Meanwhile, what Toronto police call "major crimes" - including homicides, assaults and robberies but also some crimes that Statistics Canada does not count as "violent," like auto thefts or break and enters - are at a decade high with almost 49,400 incidents reported compared to nearly 32,500 in 2014.
But that has largely been fuelled by a surge in auto thefts - a particularly high-profile trend.
The city is also seeing other concerning trends like a reported rise in youth violence. Statistics Canada reported a national rise in the number of young people under the age of 18 who were arrested for homicide - 90 in 2023, with a quarter of those from Toronto.
What's the effect of this
on the ground?
Toronto police say it was taking over 22 minutes on average for officers to respond to "priority one" calls - the most serious of 911 emergency calls - as of the end of 2023. That's up from a recent low of about 12 minutes in 2011.
Police attribute the problem to a reduced number of uniform officers being asked to respond to more serious calls.
According to data provided to the Star by police, the number of priority one calls has gone up over 60 per cent in the past decade, but lesser priority calls have all decreased.
Police assign the priority rating based on the severity and threat of each type of call. Most serious calls - including stabbings and shootings - default to priority two and can be upgraded or downgraded at the discretion of the dispatcher.
Priorities one through three are considered emergency calls; four through six are considered non-emergency situations, such as calls for a noisy party, or an accident causing property damage.
In 1995, police established a target of responding to 85 per cent of priority one calls within six minutes. In 2019, police met that target only about 28 per cent of the time, according to a report from the city's auditor general.
Police spokesperson Stephanie Sayer said in an email "it is difficult to establish formal targets because response times depend on a number of dynamic factors" like staffing levels and the fluctuating number of calls dispatched and their assigned priority.
"What's for certain is that an average response time of 22 minutes to priority one calls is not acceptable," she said, "and there is a direct correlation between the number of officers available to respond to a call and the time it takes to attend."
In an earlier interview with the Star, Demkiw said police have moved staff around to tackle the response-time issue. Those changes included disbanding special task forces and not filling vacancies in specialized units, like homicide and sex crimes. Demkiw noted, however, that a task force for auto thefts was recently reconstituted to deal with that growing problem.
Sewell, from TPAC, said police and Demkiw need to examine how they are deploying staff.
"He is not being a good manager," he said. "It's as simple as that."
Would more police officers
make the city safer?
Research suggests the amount of money a city spends on policing isn't clearly linked to crime rates, including a recent paper in the journal Canadian Public Policy that looked at 20 major municipalities, including Toronto.
"I think a lot of people simply assume that more money for the police will mean safer communities," co-author Akwasi Owusu-Bempah, an associate professor in sociology at the University of Toronto, previously told the Star. "We're demonstrating that that's not the case."
Police, of course, say otherwise.
Demkiw stressed the importance of not just crime rates but a community's perception of safety. Despite falling rates of shootings, he said, some communities still don't feel safe at home.
"Regardless of that number," some Torontonians may take "their children to play somewhere else than their neighbourhood," he said.
The notion that "police are somehow not contributing to people's sense of safety, I can tell you firsthand, I would debate that anywhere, anytime, because I've seen it in play."
So what is going to happen
next?
The budget tabled at city hall right now is the budget proposed by city staff.
Last week, Mayor Olivia Chow released her own amendments, which did not change the funding allotted for policing.
The whole of city council will meet Feb. 14 to debate the budget, including police spending, and finalize those figures. Chow can use strong mayor powers bestowed on the city by Premier Doug Ford to veto any amendments made by council.

Document 272 of 500

Island property tax bills under scrutiny: Annual fees lower due to area's unique ownership structure

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/island-property-tax-bills-under-scrutiny/docview/2922785809/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Island+property+tax+bills+under+scrutiny%3A+Annual+fees+lower+due+to+area%27s+unique+ownership+structure&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto Islanders pay dramatically less in taxes than other city dwellers - something a motion headed to council aims to change.
"There's something wrong here," Coun. Jon Burnside (Ward 16 - Don Valley East), who filed the motion to be heard at council this week, told the Star. "In what world does somebody pay $1,500 annually in property taxes? The answer is on no world other than the Island."
Toronto Islanders, who own their house but lease the land under it, on average pay about $1,530 per year in property taxes, for homes with an assessment value of $241,785, according to a 2022 analysis from the City of Toronto's Revenue Services Division.
On the mainland, homeowners paid an average of roughly $4,400 annually in 2022, based on a property's assessed value as determined by the Municipal Property Assessment Corporation, an agency hired by the province to do this work.
For 30 years, Island life has been protected by a law that permits residents on Ward's and Algonquin Islands to hold title to their homes and lease the lots on which their houses stand until 2092.
They have direct access to municipal services including an elementary school, ferry service and fire station, which Burnside said can cost taxpayers up to 10 times the amount to operate as its mainland counterpart.
"People supporting Islanders argue, 'Well, they don't own the land,'" Burnside said. "Neither do apartment dwellers but the tax is still included in their rent."
Burnside asked city staff for those numbers, too.
Tenants in Flemingdon Park pay an average of $4,320 in property tax annually as part of their rent for a single-bedroom unit, the city estimated.
Two-bedroom renters were paying $5,520 per year based on average rent of $2,295 in the same area.
The Toronto Island Community Association declined the Star's request for comment.
"We are not willing to meet and discuss this matter," chair Tony Farebrother wrote in an email.
The Toronto Islands Residential Community Trust Corporation, which manages the land and buildings associated with the community, was unavailable for comment.
Property tax is the single-biggest expense many homeowners incur, apart from a mortgage. But they pay it on faith that the tax is, as it is supposed to be, administered fairly.
Burnside would like to see the city utilize a tool in its tax arsenal to ensure Islanders aren't paying less than their fair share.
The tool, called "area rating," allows the city to employ a special tax rate to an area with unique circumstances. He's asking city council to direct the chief financial officer and treasurer to determine whether it's feasible to use this tool on the Island.
"We'll ask for the report and see where it goes," Burnside said.
Burnside said the problem with the Island rests in the fact that home prices are set by the trust and sold through a lottery system. The trust set a cap on the value of Island homes that is substantially lower than what they would fetch on an open Toronto real estate market. The assessment industry relies on open-market transaction data to set values for tax purposes.
MPAC confirmed it is respon-sible for assessing the homes on Toronto Island.
"Since the homes on Toronto Island are not bought and sold on the open market, we use the cost approach to value these properties. This approach involves determining the value of the land and adding the replacement cost of the buildings, adjusted for depreciation," spokesperson Paula Chung said.
Land lease values are provided to MPAC by the Toronto Island trust administrator leading up to each provincewide reassessment. The last provincial reassessment was Jan. 1, 2016. The next cycle was intended to begin four years later but Premier Doug Ford postponed it because of the pandemic.
A home's age, size, condition, construction quality, and any improvements can affect assessment value using the cost approach, Chung wrote in an email.
A street-by-street analysis of 261 Island homes completed by the City of Toronto found the current value assessment of properties in 2022 ranged from $88,000 to $715,000 with annual property tax payments as low as $556 to a maximum of $4,518.
In December 2022, the average price of a detached home in Toronto was $1.6 million.
The average assessment value of residential properties in the city that year was $697,185, since the province has not yet called for a reassessment and valuations for tax purposes are still supposed to peg a home's worth as of Jan. 1, 2016 and not its value in real time. This leaves the average 2022 homeowner in Toronto paying roughly $4,406 per year.
This isn't the first time the equity of the city's tax system has come under scrutiny.
A Toronto Star investigation last year revealed that Ontario's property tax system is neither fair nor transparent.
The series' exhaustive and innovative data analysis showed that homes are routinely taxed more than they should be, while others aren't taxed enough. Worse yet, the burden of this tax inequality is shouldered by those with the least-expensive houses, while owners of some of the city's most-expensive homes, nestled in some of the toniest neighbourhoods, catch a break. A retired tennis pro, a Toronto sports team executive and a former Blue Jays infielder all own or owned homes that were assessed at significantly less than what they paid for them. Working class families, meanwhile, are often paying too much tax.
This hidden systemic unfairness exists, the reporters found, because the publicly funded agency tasked with valuating properties for tax purposes has a flawed methodology that consistently under- and over-assesses homes' worth compared to their sales prices.
Mayor Olivia Chow has called for a Toronto-wide review of the property tax system following the Star's investigation.
CREDIT: Diana Zlomislic Housing Reporter

Toronto Western Hospital getting more beds and operating rooms to help tackle wait times

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-western-hospital-getting-more-beds/docview/2923117673/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+Western+Hospital+getting+more+beds+and+operating+rooms+to+help+tackle+wait+times&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto Western Hospital is getting a new 15-storey tower with 82 beds and 20 operating rooms aimed at reducing wait times for treatment.
The $1.3-billion project at the busy University Health Network hospital, to be completed within four years, will boost the number of beds at Toronto Western to about 400 and eventually boost surgical capacity at UHN by 20 per cent, officials said Tuesday.
"This is a great investment for our patients ... for Ontario," Dr. Kevin Smith, president of UHN, told a news conference where Premier Doug Ford announced provincial funding of $794 million.
The hospital foundation is also running a fundraising campaign to cover the cost. UHN includes Toronto General Hospital.
"It will help shorten wait times," Ford said of the project for which construction is slated to begin in May.
The tower will be located on what is now a small parking lot on the north side of the hospital atrium near the southeast corner of Bathurst and Nassau Streets, with the parking spots moved underground and the existing patient drop-off lane for cars maintained.
It will include critical-care beds and three highly specialized, state-of-the-art operating rooms for complex neurological and spinal surgeries, such as severe brain trauma and complex care for strokes, allowing Toronto Western to expand its role as a leading centre for neurological care.
The funding announcement came as Health Minister Sylvia Jones prepares to take applications from medical groups to provide more diagnostic and surgical care like cataracts and hip replacements in private clinics - a plan that has generated controversy over concerns about more private involvement in health care and the prospect they will compete with hospitals for doctors, nurses and other staff.
Jones said the government needs to pursue both avenues to provide Ontario's fast-growing population with the necessary care.
"It's not an either-or," she told reporters at Toronto Western, noting it was UHN that first proposed the tower project several years ago and made a case for it.
CAPTION: A rendering of the 15-storey tower that is to be built at Toronto Western Hospital. The tower will add 82 beds and 20 operating rooms.

TTC joins single-fare payment system

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ttc-joins-single-fare-payment-system/docview/2922392576/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=TTC+joins+single-fare+payment+system&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Almost two years after the Ontario government implemented the one-fare system for transit agencies across the GTA, TTC riders will finally get to join the savings.
Starting Feb. 26, transit riders commuting on both the TTC and a municipal GTA transit system, including Brampton Transit, Durham Regional Transit, MiWay and York Region Transit, will be able to transfer for free within a two-hour window.
TTC riders who connect to and from GO Transit within a three-hour window will have their fare discounted by the equivalent of the price of a single TTC ride.
The provincial government will reimburse transit agencies for the lost revenue.
"This program will be a game changer for transit riders," Premier Doug Ford said at a press conference Monday with Mayor Olivia Chow and Vijay Thanigasalam, the province's associate minister of transportation. "That means someone living in Barrie can take a Barrie transit bus to the GO station, ride the GO train to here, Downsview Park station, and take the (TTC) to the TMU campus, all with one fare," Ford said.
"When the Ontario government and the municipal government work together, we can make life more affordable," Chow said.
The province ended local fares on trips between GO Transit and a dozen local transit agencies in March 2022, though the TTC was initially left out of the deal.
Ford's government promised in spring 2023 to expand the one-fare program to the TTC by the end of the year, though that timeline was pushed to February 2024.
The program will lead to more than eight million new rides every year, Ford said, and save the average transit rider $1,600 annually. A single TTC adult fare is $3.35. The discount applies for customers paying with a Presto fare card, including in a mobile wallet, a debit card or a credit card.
Shelagh Pizey-Allen, executive director of transit advocacy group TTCriders, cheered Monday's announcement but called it "long overdue."
"We would like to see the policy go even further by bringing in one flat fare in Toronto to unlock more transit access, whether someone is using GO, TTC, or Union Pearson Express," she said.
In a statement, the Toronto Region Board of Trade, which has also been a loud voice in support of fare integration, said it will "help address critical workforce challenges. By breaking down financial barriers to transit accessibility, this encourages more people to choose public transportation and over time, alleviate traffic congestion and unlock the true potential of the billions of dollars of transit infrastructure investment already underway."
CREDIT: Lex Harvey Toronto Star

Company / organization: Name: Greater Toronto Transit Authority; NAICS: 485111, 926120

Document 276 of 500

I was wrong about Chow

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/i-was-wrong-about-chow/docview/2922392425/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=I+was+wrong+about+Chow&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When you're wrong, you're wrong. And boy, was I ever wrong about Olivia Chow.
Back in June, just before Chow was elected mayor of Toronto, I worried she'd be left helpless in dealing with senior levels of government that control the money the city needs so badly.
With a low turnout and a split vote, went my sage argument, she'd squeak in with the support of only a small fraction of Torontonians. She'd be in an even weaker position with Ottawa and Queen's Park than John Tory, who won a smashing mandate the previous October. "The new mayor is being dealt a bad hand at the start of this game," I wrote.
Well, so much for that.
Mayor Chow may have been holding only a low pair at the federal-provincial-municipal poker table. But as any poker player knows, it's not mostly about the cards, it's how you play them. And Chow has played her hand like a Vegas veteran.
Late last year she managed to strike a historic deal with the Ford government that will free up billions in capital funding for the city by having the province take over financial responsibility for the Gardiner Expressway and Don Valley Parkway.
The "New Deal for Toronto" (capital letters and all) will bring another billion or so in capital expenses for housing and new subway cars. As well as $1.2 billion in operating expenses for transit operations and shelter spaces. As much as $9 billion over a decade.
Tory had been pushing for a big deal along these lines but it was Chow who delivered it, and only five months after she took office. The only concession she had to make was to stop fussing over Doug Ford's plans for Ontario Place - a battle the city couldn't have won in any case.
So much for the province. Things weren't going so well with the feds, with Toronto facing skyrocketing costs for dealing with the influx of refugees into the city. Here, again, the mayor played her cards like an expert.
Toronto's budget chief, Shelley Carroll, came out with a draft budget proposing a tax increase of 10.5 per cent - plus a stinger to the effect that if Ottawa didn't cover $250 million in refugee expenses then the hike for property owners would soar to 16.5 per cent. She even called that extra six-point increase the "Federal Impacts Levy."
In poker, like any other game, you hope your opponents will make their share of mistakes - and that was certainly the case this time. Liberal MPs from Toronto started whining to the media that the city's move was "outrageous" and their government wasn't getting credit for all the nice things it does for Toronto. Yvan Baker of Etobicoke Centre publicly accused Chow of conducting a "shakedown."
The mayor, smartly, stayed quiet. Note that the 10.5 per cent proposal and the six-point federal levy didn't come from her, but from her budget chief. She didn't personally endorse those numbers. Nor did she get drawn into an insult battle with the touchy Liberal MPs; she let them rail on and put their political desperation on display.
Which brings us to last Friday, when Chow and Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland were all smiles as another deal was announced - this one involving $162 million in federal cash to help Toronto house refugee claimants. With more money pledged for the rest of the year, the mayor happily agreed the city was being fully compensated for asylum expenses while Freeland was able to claim Ottawa is "Toronto's greatest partner."
Yvan Baker, let it be said, was right that Toronto was conducting a shakedown of sorts. Chow cleverly put the feds on the spot and forced them to cough up money for costs they were imposing on the city. Plus her own proposed tax increase is now just 9.5 per cent, which looks like a bargain after those bigger numbers were tossed around.
It was a master class in talking nice while acting tough, in playing it smart and getting results. I was wrong about Mayor Chow, and I'm glad I was.
ANDREW PHILLIPS IS A TORONTO-BASED STAFF COLUMNIST FOR THE STAR'S OPINION PAGE. REACH HIM VIA EMAIL: APHILLIPS@THESTAR.CA
CREDIT: Andrew Phillips OPINION ANDREW PHILLIPS IS A TORONTO-BASED STAFF COLUMNIST FOR THE STAR'S OPINION PAGE. REACH HIM VIA EMAIL: APHILLIPS@THESTAR.CA;

Subject: Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Tax increases

Title: I was wrong about Chow

Document 277 of 500

I was wrong about Olivia Chow -- and I am glad I was

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/i-was-wrong-about-olivia-chow-am-glad/docview/2922391930/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=I+was+wrong+about+Olivia+Chow+--+and+I+am+glad+I+was&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When you're wrong, you're wrong. And boy, was I ever wrong about Olivia Chow.
Back in June, just before Chow was elected mayor of Toronto, I worried she'd be left helpless in dealing with senior levels of government that control the money the city needs so badly.
With a low turnout and a split vote, went my sage argument, she'd squeak in with the support of only a small fraction of Torontonians. She'd be in an even weaker position with Ottawa and Queen's Park than John Tory, who won a smashing mandate the previous October. "The new mayor is being dealt a bad hand at the start of this game," I wrote.
Well, so much for that. Mayor Chow may have been holding only a low pair at the federal-provincial-municipal poker table. But as any poker player knows, it's not mostly about the cards, it's how you play them. And Chow has played her hand like a Vegas veteran.
Late last year she managed to strike a historic deal with the Ford government that will free up billions in capital funding for the city by having the province take over financial responsibility for the Gardiner Expressway and Don Valley Parkway.
The "New Deal for Toronto" (capital letters and all) will bring another billion or so in capital expenses for housing and new subway cars. As well as $1.2 billion in operating expenses for transit operations and shelter spaces. As much as $9 billion over a decade.
Tory had been pushing for a big deal along these lines but it was Chow who delivered it, and only five months after she took office. The only concession she had to make was to stop fussing over Doug Ford's plans for Ontario Place - a battle the city couldn't have won in any case.
So much for the province. Things weren't going so well with the feds, with Toronto facing skyrocketing costs for dealing with the influx of refugees into the city. Here, again, the mayor played her cards like an expert.
Toronto's budget chief, Shelley Carroll, came out with a draft budget proposing a tax increase of 10.5 per cent - plus a stinger to the effect that if Ottawa didn't cover $250 million in refugee expenses then the hike for property owners would soar to 16.5 per cent. She even called that extra six-point increase the "Federal Impacts Levy."
In poker, like any other game, you hope your opponents will make their share of mistakes - and that was certainly the case this time. Liberal MPs from Toronto started whining to the media that the city's move was "outrageous" and their government wasn't getting credit for all the nice things it does for Toronto. Yvan Baker of Etobicoke Centre publicly accused Chow of conducting a "shakedown."
The mayor, smartly, stayed quiet. Note that the 10.5-per-cent proposal and the 6-point federal levy didn't come from her, but from her budget chief. She didn't personally endorse those numbers. Nor did she get drawn into an insult battle with the touchy Liberal MPs; she let them rail on and put their political desperation on display.
Which brings us to last Friday, when Chow and Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland were all smiles as another deal was announced - this one involving $162 million in federal cash to help Toronto house refugee claimants. With more money pledged for the rest of the year, the mayor happily agreed the city was being fully compensated for asylum expenses while Freeland was able to claim Ottawa is "Toronto's greatest partner."
Yvan Baker, let it be said, was right that Toronto was conducting a shakedown of sorts. Chow cleverly put the feds on the spot and forced them to cough up money for costs they were imposing on the city. Plus her own proposed tax increase is now just 9.5 per cent, which looks like a bargain after those bigger numbers were tossed around.
It was a master class in talking nice while acting tough, in playing it smart and getting results. I was wrong about Mayor Chow, and I'm glad I was.
CAPTION: By advocating for the city, Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow "delivered a master class in talking nice while acting tough, in playing it smart and getting results," writes Andrew Phillips.

Subject: Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Tax increases

Full text: Save tax dollars, cut the mounted police unit
Toronto police are at 'a critical point in our history,' Chief says in defence of budget rhetoric, Jan. 30
Toronto's police chief insists his service desperately needs an additional $12.7 million to reduce response times for criminal incidents.
One way the chief might save $5.7 million from this total would be to follow U.S. cities such as Boston, San Diego, Newark and others by abandoning Toronto's beloved mounted unit. It will be a brave decision and one that will lead to screams of protest, but if taken, it might lead the chief to look for further internal savings rather using the Toronto taxpayer as a bottomless piggy bank.
H. Simmons, Toronto
Ford forgets who came up with
the carbon tax
Doug Ford takes aim at Bonnie Crombie in speech to the Tory troops in Niagara Falls, Feb. 3
One would think that, by now, Premier Doug Ford would have learned his lesson and stayed far away from ever mentioning carbon tax, after losing his court challenge and having his gas pump stickers not sticking. He still does not recognize that the carbon tax idea was a Conservative idea.
Frederic Wieler, Oakville
Conservatives' tactics could help Crombie win
Every time Ontario Premier Doug Ford insults Bonnie Crombie's Liberal leadership either personally or through Conservative party ads, I become more interested in voting for her and the Liberal party next election.
Dean Bodkin, Stouffville
Queen Crombie sounds pretty good to me
I do the work the federal government has failed to do: Explain the carbon tax, Feb. 3
In Saturday's Star, David Olive did a great job of explaining the carbon tax. He put to bed rumours that it is a cash grab. He explained that everyone gets their money back in the form of cheques from the federal government. He explained that it is used in 72 countries worldwide to make people think twice before driving or using other carbon-based machinery. It is effective in reducing carbon production.
Then in the Sunday's Star there is Premier Doug Ford calling Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie "Queen of the Carbon Tax" as a put down. Really?
If Crombie is such a queen, then she is an environmentalist, urging people to stop polluting while not costing them a dime. She sounds good to me.
Gail Rutherford, Etobicoke
Please read your Bible again,
Mr. Alexander
'We are tired of fighting':
Why the York Catholic school board's approach to inclusivity is again sparking outrage, Feb. 3
York Catholic District School Board Trustee Frank Alexander seems unaware that lesbian, gay, bisexual, trans and queer people do not choose their sexuality.
That's how they are born, just as Alexander did not choose to be born Black. He can't undo being Black, just as the millions of kind, creative, highly moral LGBTQ people can't undo loving who they love.
Read your Bible again, Mr. Alexander, especially the parts where Jesus urges his followers to be kind to everyone.
Douglas Buck, Toronto
Raptors' changes are losing this fan's attention
'He might be the nicest guy you've ever met': The private side of the Houston Rockets' Dillon (The Villain) Brooks, Feb. 2
I watched Dillon Brooks as Team Canada took the bronze medal and have followed our Canadian players in the NBA playing for their own teams.
I will always enjoy watching Canadian talent and those players who played for the Raptors like Pascal Siakam and OG Anunoby.
In fact, as a result of the recent changes to the Raptors roster, I now watch the Knicks and the Pacers more than the Raptors at least until RJ Barrett comes back to the Raptors roster healthy.
It was a big mistake to move Siakam and Anunoby before Scottie Barnes is even ready to step up at least for the Raptors fans.
Bonnie Bacvar, Toronto
Bad behaviour triple crown
for Bettman
Bettman says NHL 'committed to setting the right example,' says commenting on junior case 'would be inappropriate, Feb. 2
It is time for NHL commissioner Gary Bettman to be awarded the bad behaviour triple crown award.
He has stripped and stopped NHL players and teams from celebrating Pride events.
He did not acknowledge the Hockey Diversity Alliance this week at the all-star activities, even though the sport has a long way to go to acknowledge and deal with racism.
And now he has no comment on the alleged sexual assaults by Hockey Canada players in 2018.
David Kidd, Toronto
Ask your local MP to support electoral reform
Will Trudeau end up regretting his decision to walk away from electoral reform? Feb. 2
Want to end foreign interference in our elections? Reform our electoral system.
Motion M-86, now being debated in Parliament, proposes establishing a Citizens' Assembly on Electoral Reform, which will tackle this and other problems compromising our democracy.
Ask your MP to support it.
Don Young, Toronto
Why would we cancel a day that celebrates love?
Is it time we cancelled Valentine's Day? Feb. 5
Cancel Valentine's Day? OK, next we go after Christmas, birthdays, anniversaries and more? Writer Yuthika Girme may be the Grinch of every day of celebration. Valentine's is a celebration of love. Valentine's has important value to society. Let's promote it as it becomes increasingly eroded and dissipated by the supposed benefits of our electronic technology.
How about celebrating old school with a bunch of flowers or box of chocolates and an "I love you" note.
Richard Szpin, Pickering

Document 279 of 500

Ford hails ruling on mandate letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-hails-ruling-on-mandate-letters/docview/2922391636/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+hails+ruling+on+mandate+letters&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford says "it's good news" the Supreme Court of Canada has ruled that he does not have to release his mandate letters to cabinet ministers.
"Obviously, there was a unanimous decision and we're listening the courts so we're happy," Ford said Monday at an unrelated Downsview announcement.
It was the premier's first comment on the case since Friday's Supreme Court decision.
In a 7-0 ruling, the top court said "the mandate letters are protected from disclosure" because they "are revealing of the substance of cabinet deliberations."
The 57-page decision was written by Justice Andromache Karakatsanis, Ontario's secretary of cabinet in charge of the provincial public service from 2000 to 2002.
"(It) would increase the public pressure that stakeholders put on ministers and give rise to partisan criticism from their political opponents; this scrutiny would ultimately paralyze the collective decision-making process," Karakatsanis wrote.
"Freedom of information legislation strikes a balance between the public's need to know and the confidentiality the executive requires to govern effectively. In Canada's constitutional democracy, the confidentiality of cabinet deliberations is a precondition to responsible government because it enables collective ministerial responsibility," she continued.
"The prerogative to determine when and how to announce cabinet decisions is grounded in the harmful impact that premature disclosure of policy priorities can have on the deliberative process. The efficiency of the deliberative process justifies keeping cabinet proceedings confidential until a final decision is made and announced."
Ford's Progressive Conservative government had spent years fighting the release of the 2018 directive letters to ministers in the wake of a freedom of information request filed by CBC.
Cabinet mandate letters have traditionally been secret, though former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne released them in 2014. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau began making them public in 2015.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles blasted the Tories for feeling "that they do not owe the people of Ontario any transparency."
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson and Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau

Document 280 of 500

An optimistic turn in Toronto's budget

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/optimistic-turn-torontos-budget/docview/2922134783/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=An+optimistic+turn+in+Toronto%27s+budget&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto's budget storm clouds seem be clearing.
Indeed, the city's budget drama over the last few weeks is playing out like a TV-series script. It kicked off in dramatic fashion with the proposal for a double-digit tax hike and the possibility to go even higher if Ottawa didn't pony up, followed by bitter name-calling by Liberal MPs.
And then surprise, resolution. Federal cash pledged and back-slapping for the cameras.
Last Thursday at Scarborough Centre Station, Toronto mayor Olivia Chow unveiled her 2024 budget from behind a podium bearing the words "Back On Track." This message was as deliberate as it gets: Chow spoke in a building that once served as a stop on the recently decommissioned Scarborough Rapid Transit Line.
Her remarks, however, should give hope to Torontonians inconvenienced by the closure of that transit line. If passed at council on Feb. 14, the mayor's $17-billion budget will fully fund the estimated $67.9 million dollar Scarborough busway; a dedicated bus route that will effectively replace the rapid transit line (that is until the Scarborough subway extension is completed around 2030).
This is exceptional news for Scarborough residents who have too long been neglected by local politics. But it was not the only positive news to arise from the mayor's budget announcement, the result of a consultation process that sought input from more than 50,000 Torontonians at telephone townhalls and public meetings.
Chow also revealed that the headline-making 10.5 property tax hike proposed in the initial staff budget has been reduced to 9.5 per cent. The mayor will make up that lost revenue with a one-time withdrawal from a reserve fund. And gone, thank goodness, is the prospect of a "federal impacts levy" - a further six per cent tax increase that Toronto's budget chief Shelley Carroll warned could be necessary if the federal government did not come through with funding to help shelter asylum seekers.
Indeed, months of intergovernmental squabbling came to an abrupt end Friday, when Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland joined Chow to announce a federal commitment of more than $162 million for asylum seekers and other vulnerable Torontonians in the shelter system. Though this figure falls short of the $250 million the city said it needed for asylum seekers, Chow seemed to indicate that the full amount would come in installments.
"More newcomers will have a safe and stable place to call home while they get settled in Canada," Freeland said, at one point referring to Chow as "Toronto's wonderful mayor." Chow in turn praised the "416 caucus" for its work securing the funding.
It was a sharp turn in tone from just a few weeks ago when Toronto Liberals were openly taking potshots at the mayor.
This federal funding, along with the savings gained from the mayor's new deal with the province - which will see Ontario take on the costs of the Don Valley Parkway and the Gardiner Expressway - will greatly assist the city on a daunting mission to improve services in the face of a $1.8 billion shortfall.
Other notable elements of the mayor's budget include new funding for youth programs and infrastructure repairs. Absent is an additional $12.6 million to the police budget, despite some fierce lobbying by the force, as well as funds for a popular windrow clearing program.
Overall, this budget marks a critical step toward ensuring the fiscal sustainability of the city. But it does not solve Toronto's problems outright. The Scarborough busway is funded , for example, by reallocating funds in the city's capital plan, which will surely mean some other project gets delayed.
That said, the budget is cause for cautious optimism. Its proposed tax rate, though much higher than many Torontonians would prefer, is lower than originally feared; it is also a reflection of the fiscal reality we face. Services do not improve let alone survive through austerity. They improve through investment. Indeed, one surprising element to emerge from the city's budget consultations was a common refrain from Torontonians who said they were willing to pay a greater share for a better city.
However, we suspect their enthusiasm will disappear should they fail to see a marked improvement in their services -- and fast. Mayor Olivia Chow soon faces her biggest challenge yet: ensuring Torontonians get sufficient bang for their buck.
CAPTION: Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow unveils her budget proposal on Thursday, joined by left, Coun. Michael Thompson, Coun. Jamaal Myers, chair of the Toronto Transit Commission, and Coun. Peter Ainslie.

Subject: Tax rates; Funding; Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Tax rates Tax increases

Document 281 of 500

TTC riders finally join GTA's single-fare payment system

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ttc-riders-finally-join-gtas-single-fare-payment/docview/2922134777/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=TTC+riders+finally+join+GTA%27s+single-fare+payment+system&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Almost two years after the Ontario government implemented the one-fare system for transit agencies across the GTA, TTC riders will finally get to join the savings.
Starting Feb. 22, transit riders who transfer from GO Transit to the TTC will have their single ride TTC fares reimbursed. Transit riders commuting on both the TTC and another municipal transit agency will be able to do so without paying a second fare. The provincial government will reimburse transit agencies for the lost revenue.
"This program will be a game changer for transit riders," said Premier Doug Ford, at a press conference Monday with Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow and Vijay Thanigasalam, the province's associate minister of transportation.
"That means someone living in Barrie can take a Barrie transit bus to the GO station, ride the GO train to here, Downsview Park station, and take the (TTC) to the TMU campus, all with one fare," Ford said.
"When the Ontario government and the municipal government work together, we can make life more affordable," Chow said, thanking the premier for his "support and love for Toronto."
The province ended local fares on trips between GO Transit and a dozen local transit agencies in March 2022, though the region's largest transit system, the TTC was initially left out of the deal.
Ford's government promised in spring 2023 to expand the one-fare program to the TTC by the end of the year, though that timeline was pushed to February 2024.
The program will lead to more than 8 million new rides every year, Ford said, and save the average transit rider $1,600 annually. A single TTC adult fare is $3.35.
CAPTION: The province ended local fares on trips between GO Transit and a dozen local transit agencies in March 2022, though the region's largest transit system, the TTC was initially left out of the deal.

Company / organization: Name: Greater Toronto Transit Authority; NAICS: 485111, 926120

Document 282 of 500

Doug Ford hails Supreme Court ruling on ministers' mandate letters as 'good news'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-hails-supreme-court-ruling-on-ministers/docview/2922134626/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+hails+Supreme+Court+ruling+on+ministers%27+mandate+letters+as+%27good+news%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford says "it's good news" the Supreme Court of Canada has ruled that he does not have to release his mandate letters to cabinet ministers.
"Obviously, there was a unanimous decision and we're listening the courts so we're happy," Ford told reporters Monday at an unrelated Downsview announcement.
It was the premier's first comment on the case since Friday's Supreme Court decision.
In a 7-0 ruling, the top court said "the mandate letters are protected from disclosure" because they "are revealing of the substance of cabinet deliberations."
The 57-page decision was written by Justice Andromache Karakatsanis, Ontario's secretary of cabinet in charge of the provincial public service from 2000 to 2002.
"(It) would increase the public pressure that stakeholders put on ministers and give rise to partisan criticism from their political opponents; this scrutiny would ultimately paralyze the collective decision-making process," Karakatsanis wrote.
"Freedom of information legislation strikes a balance between the public's need to know and the confidentiality the executive requires to govern effectively. In Canada's constitutional democracy, the confidentiality of cabinet deliberations is a precondition to responsible government because it enables collective ministerial responsibility," she continued.
"The prerogative to determine when and how to announce cabinet decisions is grounded in the harmful impact that premature disclosure of policy priorities can have on the deliberative process. The efficiency of the deliberative process justifies keeping cabinet proceedings confidential until a final decision is made and announced."
Ford's Progressive Conservative government had spent years fighting the release of the 2018 directive letters to ministers in the wake of a freedom of information request filed by CBC.
Cabinet mandate letters have traditionally been secret, though former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne released them in 2014. They read like press releases and are still available online at the Ontario government's website.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau began making the letters public in 2015.
Ford reversed that practice when he toppled Wynne's Liberals in 2018.
The province's information and privacy commissioner determined CBC should have access to the documents, triggering a long and expensive legal fight with Queen's Park.
In many ways, the decision in the case - known as Attorney General for Ontario v. Information and Privacy Commissioner of Ontario, et al - was moot because the letters had been leaked to Global News last year.
All three opposition leaders have denouced Ford for not making the letters public and for fighting the CBC in court.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said if she wins the June 2026 election, she would release her ministers' mandate letters.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles blasted the Tories for feeling "that they do not owe the people of Ontario any transparency or accountability for their sketchy backroom decisions, even as they remain under RCMP criminal investigation."
Stiles was referring to the police probe of the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner warned the "disappointing ruling ... sets a poor precedent for democracy in Ontario."
CAPTION: File photo: Premier Doug Ford attends an event at Pinewood Toronto Studios on Jan. 17, 2024.

Subject: Supreme Court decisions; Scandals; Confidentiality; Freedom of information

Document 283 of 500

Pierre Poilievre to promise heftier punishment for car thieves if Conservatives win next election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievre-promise-heftier-punishment-car/docview/2922134502/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre+to+promise+heftier+punishment+for+car+thieves+if+Conservatives+win+next+election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre is promising that if his party forms government, car thieves will face more time in prison and be punished more severely if the thefts are linked to organized crime.
Poilievre will make those pledges at a campaign-style event in Brampton on Monday, the Star has learned, his latest effort to flesh out what he'd do if he becomes prime minister, and they'll be followed by more this week.
The timing is deliberate - his promises coincide with this week's national summit on auto theft taking place in Ottawa, announced by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals during their cabinet retreat last month.
Both the Liberal government and Poilievre's Conservatives are responding to what some are calling a national crisis: record numbers of vehicles are being stolen from driveways and parking lots across Canada. In Toronto alone, 12,143 were stolen in the city in 2023. The last time thefts were that high was in 2003.
A recent report from Criminal Intelligence Service Canada, which tracks organized crime in Canada, said street gangs are behind most vehicle thefts, with higher-level organized crime groups and transnational organizations involved in exporting them.
Poilievre is expected to promise sentencing reforms that would make it an "aggravating factor" if a vehicle is stolen for the benefit of organized crime, meaning someone convicted could face a harsher penalty.
If someone is convicted of stealing a vehicle for the third time, his government would increase their mandatory sentence from six months to three years, and he'll take house arrest off the table as a potential penalty for certain vehicle thefts.
While the next election isn't scheduled until 2025, it's also expected that the Conservatives will introduce a private member's bill in the House of Commons to push for the changes.
Poilievre is not alone in calling for tougher criminal penalties for car thieves.
While the Ontario government has announced tens of millions of dollars in recent months to help police combat auto theft, Ontario Premier Doug Ford has also urged the federal government to make changes to the criminal code, calling for stronger mandatory minimum penalties for violent car thefts.
"I've talked to friends that are sleeping with baseball bats beside their beds because someone is going to kick the door in, put a gun to their head and say hand over your keys," he said last week, while standing with the federal ministers of public safety and justice at a funding announcement to combat gang and gun violence.
Poilievre and his party blame the Liberals for crime rates in Canada, and last week had begun to set the stage for an attack on Trudeau for vehicle thefts as well, pressing the prime minister repeatedly on the issue during question period.
"When it comes to stopping crime, the prime minister is throwing on a cape and claiming to be a big hero on auto theft, but since he brought in catch-and-release and Netflix sentences for car thieves, auto theft has risen by 30 per cent, and that is not to mention the mismanagement of our ports," Poilievre said last Wednesday.
"Will he reverse the policies that caused the auto theft crisis instead of holding another meeting?"
In response, Trudeau accused Poilievre of having empty slogans but not solutions, and noted that border agents intercepted 1,600 vehicles last year.
Experts say solving the problem of auto thefts will take a mix of strategies, which include the existing criminal penalty structure, but also addressing issues like the transportation networks for stolen cars, and the fact that current federal vehicle safety regulations don't take into account advances in car technology that have made vehicles easier to steal.
All those issues are expected to be on the table at the coming summit.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks in the House of Commons on Feb. 1, 2024.

Subject: Fines & penalties; Organized crime; Automobile theft; Robbery; Prime ministers; Vehicles

Location: Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Document 284 of 500

Optimistic turn in city's budget debate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/optimistic-turn-citys-budget-debate/docview/2921975311/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Optimistic+turn+in+city%27s+budget+debate&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto's budget storm clouds seem be clearing.
Indeed, the city's budget drama over the last few weeks is playing out like a TV-series script. It kicked off in dramatic fashion with the proposal for a double-digit tax hike and the possibility to go even higher if Ottawa didn't pony up, followed by bitter name-calling by Liberal MPs.
And then surprise, resolution. Federal cash pledged and back-slapping for the cameras.
Last Thursday at Scarborough Centre Station, Mayor Olivia Chow unveiled her 2024 budget from behind a podium bearing the words "Back On Track." This message was as deliberate as it gets: Chow spoke in a building that once served as a stop on the recently decommissioned Scarborough Rapid Transit Line.
Her remarks, however, should give hope to Torontonians inconvenienced by the closure of that transit line. If passed at council on Feb. 14, the mayor's $17-billion budget will fully fund the estimated $67.9-million dollar Scarborough busway; a dedicated bus route that will effectively replace the rapid transit line (that is until the Scarborough subway extension is completed around 2030).
This is exceptional news for Scarborough residents who have too long been neglected by local politics. But it was not the only positive news to arise from the mayor's budget announcement, the result of a consultation process that sought input from more than 50,000 Torontonians at telephone town halls and public meetings.
Chow also revealed that the headline-making 10.5 property tax hike proposed in the initial staff budget has been reduced to 9.5 per cent. The mayor will make up that lost revenue with a one-time withdrawal from a reserve fund. And gone, thank goodness, is the prospect of a "federal impacts levy" - a further six per cent tax increase that Toronto's budget chief Shelley Carroll warned could be necessary if the federal government did not come through with funding to help shelter asylum seekers.
Indeed, months of intergovernmental squabbling came to an abrupt end Friday, when Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland joined Chow to announce a federal commitment of more than $162 million for asylum seekers and other vulnerable Torontonians in the shelter system. Though this figure falls short of the $250 million the city said it needed for asylum seekers, Chow seemed to indicate that the full amount would come in installments.
"More newcomers will have a safe and stable place to call home while they get settled in Canada," Freeland said, at one point referring to Chow as "Toronto's wonderful mayor." Chow in turn praised the "416 caucus" for its work securing the funding.
It was a sharp turn in tone from just a few weeks ago when Toronto Liberals were openly taking potshots at the mayor.
This federal funding, along with the savings gained from the mayor's new deal with the province - which will see Ontario take on the costs of the Don Valley Parkway and the Gardiner Expressway - will greatly assist the city on a daunting mission to improve services in the face of a $1.8-billion shortfall.
Other notable elements of the mayor's budget include new funding for youth programs and infrastructure repairs. Absent is an additional $12.6 million to the police budget, despite some fierce lobbying by the force, as well as funds for a popular windrow clearing program.
Overall, this budget marks a critical step toward ensuring the fiscal sustainability of the city. But it does not solve Toronto's problems outright. The Scarborough busway is funded, for example, by reallocating funds in the city's capital plan, which will surely mean some other project gets delayed.
That said, the budget is cause for cautious optimism. Its proposed tax rate, though much higher than many Torontonians would prefer, is lower than originally feared; it is also a reflection of the fiscal reality we face. Services do not improve let alone survive through austerity. They improve through investment. Indeed, one surprising element to emerge from the city's budget consultations was a common refrain from Torontonians who said they were willing to pay a greater share for a better city.
However, we suspect their enthusiasm will disappear should they fail to see a marked improvement in their services - and fast. Chow soon faces her biggest challenge yet: ensuring Torontonians get sufficient bang for their buck.
CREDIT: ;

Subject: Tax rates; Funding; Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Tax rates Tax increases

Document 286 of 500

Doug Ford takes aim at Bonnie Crombie in speech to the Tory troops in Niagara Falls

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-takes-aim-at-bonnie-crombie-speech-tory/docview/2921791094/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+takes+aim+at+Bonnie+Crombie+in+speech+to+the+Tory+troops+in+Niagara+Falls&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Niagara Falls may be the honeymoon capital, but Premier Doug Ford wasn't about to give newly elected Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie a political honeymoon.
Speaking to about 1,000 Progressive Conservatives gathered at the party's policy convention in Niagara Falls on Saturday night, Ford took aim at Crombie.
"Who you vote for, who's in government, it matters - leadership matters," the premier said, as PC cameras rolled for a new campaign-style TV ad.
"I want to talk about Bonnie Crombie just for a minute. Bonnie is the queen of the carbon tax," he said, referring to the former Mississauga mayor who was once a Liberal MP though her tenure in Ottawa predated Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Grits.
"Bonnie's never seen a tax she didn't like - during her time as mayor she jacked up property taxes and fought Highway 413," said Ford, whose Tories were re-elected in 2022 by promising to build the 60-kilometre freeway linking Highway 401 in Milton with Highway 400 in Vaughan.
"Under Bonnie's watch - as the rest of Ontario was growing and thriving - Mississauga's population shrunk for the first time in its history as people left the city because of her high-cost, high-tax policies."
The premier did not mention that Crombie, a three-term mayor, was re-elected in October 2022 with 77.1 per cent of the popular vote in Mississauga.
But his partisan speech - which criticized the previous Liberal government three times and used similar language to a current PC ad blitz attacking the new Grit chief - made clear that he viewed her as his main opponent in the June 2026 election.
Ford did not name NDP Leader Marit Stiles or the New Democrats once even though the official opposition party holds Niagara Falls, which has been represented by MPP Wayne Gates since 2014.
"The Liberals chased away 300,000 jobs, they were an absolute disaster," the premier said, decrying "15 long, long years of Liberal mismanagement" between 2003 and 2018.
"Our record is different, we're on a mission to keep costs down, we cut 10.7 cents per litre off the cost of gas, we scrapped the Liberal road tolls and eliminated the licence plate sticker fee."
Ford noted the Tories plan to spent almost $185 billion over the next decade on infrastructure, including Highway 413, the Bradford Bypass and the Garden City Skyway.
"We're leading the largest expansion in public transit in North America as we bring GO train service to Niagara, Kitchener and Bowmanville," he said.
"Shovels are in the ground on the new Ontario Line subway and the Hazel McCallion Line in Mississauga."
That latter project, an 18-kilometre LRT linking Mississauga and Brampton along Hurontario Street, has been fast-tracked by Ford amid fears Crombie could win many of the 11 local ridings the Tories won there two years ago.
While Ford touted that "across the province we're getting more homes built faster so that young families and newcomers can once again afford to buy a home," he pointedly did not broach the $8.28-billion Greenbelt scandal that is currently under RCMP investigation.
In a statement, Crombie said Ford was making "more empty promises to the people of Ontario."
"The reality is the only people who are better off after six years under this scandal-plagued government are a handful of Ford's wealthy friends," the Liberal leader said.
Mocking the Tories' 2022 election slogan, "Get It Done," Crombie said the governing party has achieved little.
"The Conservatives like to talk about getting it done but, in fact, have spent more money doing less than any government in Ontario's history," she said.
"Wait times in hospitals have skyrocketed, the dream of home ownership has slipped further away and cuts to our classrooms are leaving students behind. Meanwhile, Ford is preoccupied with outsourcing ServiceOntario to major American corporations and covering up how much it will cost you."
Emphasizing that "the people of Ontario deserve better," Crombie said her party would be criss-crossing the province for "grassroots policy discussions" later this year.
"Together, Ontario Liberals will build a team and a plan to defeat and replace this failed government with one that is squarely focused on the needs of the people of Ontario."
CAPTION: Doug Ford, seen at an event last summer, took aim at Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie last night, telling about 1,000 Progressive Conservatives in Niagara Falls that "during her time as mayor she jacked up property taxes and fought Highway 413."As the premier and his caucus met behind closed doors inside the Niagara Falls Convention Centre on Saturday, hundreds of people lined up outside to protest the provincial government.

Document 287 of 500

Ford takes aim at 'queen of the carbon tax': Premier's campaign-style speech to the Tory troops focuses on new Liberal leader Crombie

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-takes-aim-at-queen-carbon-tax/docview/2921666927/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+takes+aim+at+%27queen+of+the+carbon+tax%27%3A+Premier%27s+campaign-style+speech+to+the+Tory+troops+focuses+on+new+Liberal+leader+Crombie&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Niagara Falls Niagara Falls may be the honeymoon capital, but Premier Doug Ford wasn't about to give newly elected Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie a political honeymoon.
Speaking to about 1,000 Progressive Conservatives gathered at the party's policy convention in Niagara Falls on Saturday night, Ford took aim at Crombie.

"Under Bonnie's watch - as the rest of Ontario was growing and thriving - Mississauga's population shrunk for the first time in its history, as people left the city because of her high-cost, high-tax policies."
The premier did not mention that Crombie, a three-term mayor, was re-elected in October 2022 with 77.1 per cent of the popular vote in Mississauga.
But his partisan speech - which criticized the previous Liberal government three times and used similar language to a current PC ad blitz attacking the new Grit chief - made clear that he viewed her as his main opponent in the June 2026 election.
Ford did not name NDP Leader Marit Stiles or the New Democrats once, even though the official opposition party holds Niagara Falls, which has been represented by MPP Wayne Gates since 2014.
"The Liberals chased away 300,000 jobs, they were an absolute disaster," the premier said, decrying "15 long, long years of Liberal mismanagement" between 2003 and 2018.
"Our record is different, we're on a mission to keep costs down, we cut 10.7 cents per litre off the cost of gas, we scrapped the Liberal road tolls and eliminated the licence plate sticker fee."
Ford noted the Tories plan to spend almost $185 billion over the next decade on infrastructure, including Hwy. 413, the Bradford Bypass and the Garden City Skyway.
"We're leading the largest expansion in public transit in North America, as we bring GO train service to Niagara, Kitchener and Bowmanville," he said. "Shovels are in the ground on the new Ontario Line subway and the Hazel McCallion Line in Mississauga."
That latter project, an 18-kilometre LRT linking Mississauga and Brampton along Hurontario Street, has been fast-tracked by Ford amid fears Crombie could win many of the 11 local ridings the Tories won there two years ago.
Although Ford touted that "across the province we're getting more homes built faster so that young families and newcomers can once again afford to buy a home," he pointedly did not broach the $8.28-billion Greenbelt scandal that is currently under RCMP investigation.
In a statement, Crombie said Ford was making "more empty promises to the people of Ontario."
"The reality is the only people who are better off after six years under this scandal-plagued government are a handful of Ford's wealthy friends," the Liberal leader said.
Mocking the Tories' 2022 election slogan, "Get It Done," Crombie said the governing party has achieved little.
"The Conservatives like to talk about getting it done, but in fact, have spent more money doing less than any government in Ontario's history," she said.
"Wait times in hospitals have skyrocketed, the dream of home ownership has slipped further away and cuts to our classrooms are leaving students behind. Meanwhile, Ford is preoccupied with outsourcing ServiceOntario to major American corporations and covering up how much it will cost you."
Emphasizing that "the people of Ontario deserve better," Crombie said her party would be criss-crossing the province for "grassroots policy discussions" later this year.
"Together, Ontario Liberals will build a team and a plan to defeat and replace this failed government with one that is squarely focused on the needs of the people of Ontario."
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief
