Document 288 of 500

Justin Trudeau needs to stop his Donald Trump trash talk, former ambassador warns

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-needs-stop-his-donald-trump-trash/docview/2921534150/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+needs+to+stop+his+Donald+Trump+trash+talk%2C+former+ambassador+warns&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - A former Canadian ambassador to Washington says Justin Trudeau should ditch the anti-Trump "MAGA Conservative" rhetoric directed at the Liberals' Canadian political rivals.
David MacNaughton, who co-chaired the Ontario campaign for Trudeau's 2015 election victory, says it's neither a wise domestic political strategy nor is it smart for Canada-U.S. relations.
In an interview Friday, MacNaughton said the prime minister is taking a risk by being seen to take swipes at Donald Trump, the presumptive Republican nominee, in a U.S. presidential election year.
MacNaughton - who Trudeau hailed as key to Canada's successful renegotiation of NAFTA with the Trump administration - worries there could be "blowback" if Trump wins and decides to set his sights on Canada.
Taking indirect shots at Trump would make it a lot harder to fight against Trump's promised 10 per cent tariffs on U.S. imports, or to persuade a new Trump administration that the Trudeau government is a trusted partner on defence and security, he suggested.
"I would be trying to make sure that there weren't any huge blow-ups in our relationship with the Americans between now and our (Canadian) election," he added.
American voters go to the polls in November, while a Canadian federal election is not expected before October 2025.
Moreover, MacNaughton said there's no indication that drawing such a contrast would be a winning electoral strategy for the Liberals here at home.
Public opinion research suggests a large percentage of Canadians feel the country is taking the wrong direction or that it's time for a change in government, MacNaughton said. He drew a comparison to the electorate's mood in 2015 when people "were tired of (Conservative prime minister) Stephen Harper. They wanted a change."
At that time, MacNaughton said, Trudeau was offered all kinds of advice on what kind of campaign to run. The reality, however, was that the public mood was such that Trudeau "didn't have to be spectacular, he just had to be a change from what Stephen Harper was," he said.
"Well, you know what? We're almost in exactly the same situation."
Attacking Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre "by itself isn't going to work anyway," he said. "And secondly, trying to draw a comparison with Donald Trump ... why would you run the risk?"
In MacNaughton's opinion, the bigger challenge for the Trudeau Liberals is to persuade Canadians that they understand the world has changed dramatically since their 2015 election, and even since the most recent election in 2021.
He said they need to demonstrate they understand the economic pain and anxieties that stem from a changed global order, and are prepared to make changes to how they govern - not that they will continue to do more of the same. He suggested if Harper had admitted to mistakes and promised to govern differently in 2015, it may have posed a "dilemma" for the Trudeau campaign back then.
The Liberals have announced a revival of what they call a "Team Canada" approach to getting ready for whoever wins the next U.S. election, but MacNaughton's view, first reported by Politico, is that the Trudeau government should "do the strategy. You don't talk strategy."
When MacNaughton departed the ambassador post in 2019, Trudeau praised him as a "trusted adviser, friend, and counsellor ... whose honesty, moderation, and wisdom were pivotal to Team Canada in our successful renegotiation of NAFTA."
He said the reality now, even with a Democrat currently in the White House, is that it is a time of "increased isolationism and protectionism" and Canada is "vulnerable."
"We need to be seen by the Americans as a trusted friend, ally, partner and, you know, right now, I don't think that feeling is as strong as it has been," he said. "And that's not just this government's fault. That's been going on for some time."
MacNaughton has shared his view with senior Liberals, but is at a loss to explain why Trudeau - who has criticized Poilievre's "ideologically driven MAGA Conservatives" in Parliament - thinks the attacks will work.
"I don't understand it," he said.
The Star has reported on polling data that also suggests Canadians are not persuaded by the Liberals' Trump-Poilievre comparisons.
The Conservatives did not respond Friday to a request for comment.
Conservative MP Randy Hoback, who has long worked on Canada-U.S. issues, recently said the Canada-U.S. relationship is too critical to be jeopardized in order to score "domestic political points."
"Trudeau and his caucus must end their repeated jabs south of the border ... His actions are hazardous to our economy and to our national security," Hoback wrote in an online post.
Trudeau is not the only Canadian political leader to take shots at Trump.
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, the Liberals' governing ally in Parliament, last month warned that a Trump election victory would be bad for Canada. Trump is "openly running on an egomaniac, vengeance-filled motive to become the president, and it is incredibly disturbing to watch this," Singh said.
"Donald Trump frankly, is in a ... world of his own. The things that he has done, the things that he says, the type of person he is, there is no other comparison to someone who is as bad for democracy, as bad for people, as bad for the planet as Donald Trump."
With files from Raisa Patel
CAPTION: David MacNaughton, then Canada's ambassador to the United States, speaks to reporters at a Liberal cabinet retreat in Calgary on Jan. 22, 2017.

Former Premier David Peterson celebrates 80th birthday

Document 290 of 500

Children learn what is unteachable through play: letter

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/children-learn-what-is-unteachable-through-play/docview/2921531596/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Children+learn+what+is+unteachable+through+play%3A+letter&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford government to revamp full-day kindergarten curriculum, Jan. 23
The love of language that makes for lifelong readers and writers doesn't come from a series of lessons. It begins in the preschool years with the delight and rhythm of story time. Children learn about fractions when one child receives a bigger slice of the birthday cake than another. It is impossible to list all the science concepts children learn from mucking about in the sand box.
We have ample reason to believe in the power of play. Studies show that without play, children are robbed of healthy development that can never be recovered.
Learning, at any age, is more a matter of direct experience than direct instruction.
How many parents sit the toddler down and go through a series of lessons to teach them to walk or talk? Children learn these most complex skills through experience, and following a developmental path that is uneven but predictable.
Support, encouragement, and security from caring adults are important, but the essential ingredient is play. It is through play that children learn that which no one could teach them.
In this post pandemic time, young children need play more than ever. They have been traumatized by the lack of natural social development and in many cases are already addicted to using technology as an escape from reality. Our children deserve the right to play and learn in the best possible way. This is what will build a solid foundation for academic success. Moreover, it will provide the vehicle for them to sing, dance, laugh and cry, developing the capacity to thrive in a rapidly changing world.
Children cannot speak for themselves. They rely on us to protect their rights. Where is the leadership that ought to be promoting an understand of play and learning? Why are the teachers' federations, faculties of education and social sciences not speaking up? Our children need our leadership today as never before.
We will not move forward by going backwards.
Susanne Eden, associate professor, retired, York University, Faculty of Education, Newmarket

CAPTION: Ontario Education Minister Stephen Lecce is revamping thefull-day kindergarten curriculum, adding some 'back to basics' instruction in math and reading.

Subject: Preschool education; Kindergarten; Curricula

Document 291 of 500

I do the work the federal government has failed to do: Explain the carbon tax

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/i-do-work-federal-government-has-failed-explain/docview/2921530591/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=I+do+the+work+the+federal+government+has+failed+to+do%3A+Explain+the+carbon+tax&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: What we have here, with Canada's needlessly controversial carbon pricing system, is a failure to communicate.
As the Star reported this week, Ottawa is giving serious thought to "rebranding" its carbon pricing system, as if merely renaming it will make it less unpopular.
Seven years after it was first introduced, Ottawa's carbon pricing system is still misunderstood by a large plurality of Canadians. Many regard it as an inflationary increase in their cost of living.
It is no such thing.
For Canadians affected by the levy, as consumers of gasoline and carbon-based home heating fuels, the carbon levy is a quarterly windfall in Ottawa rebates that either match the levy or exceed it for those who reduce their carbon purchases.
Eight in 10 Canadians affected by the carbon levy actually get more money back in quarterly cash rebates from Ottawa than they spend in carbon levies, as the Parliamentary Budget Office (PBO) reported last year.
About 72 national and subnational governments worldwide have adopted carbon pricing systems. In its dashboard of those systems, the World Bank reports that they cover a total of 23 per cent of global greenhouse gas emissions.
The motive for carbon pricing, to remind, is the warning by climate scientists for decades that until we put a price on carbon pollution the fight against global warming is unwinnable no matter what other steps we take.
Ottawa created a carbon pricing system, back in 2017, that rewards Canadians for reducing their carbon footprint and doesn't punish them if choose not to change their consumer habits.
Households receive fixed annual rebates whether they reduce their car trips and turn down the thermostat or not.
The average Ontario family of four will receive roughly $1,000 in cash carbon rebates this year regardless of how much carbon it consumes.
But if that family reduces its carbon consumption, saving money upfront in doing so, a portion of those federal rebates becomes an additional source of household wealth in rebates that exceed the family's carbon levy payments.
It is akin to RSPs, by which Ottawa rewards Canadians for saving for their retirement.
But the Trudeau government never did the necessary work of explaining the role of carbon pricing in the global warming fight. Or how, more often than not, the carbon levy system increases household wealth rather than reducing it.
After failing to explain its carbon pricing when it was introduced and in the years since, the Trudeau government further botched its own initiative last fall by abruptly suspending the carbon levy on heating oil (not all heating fuel) for three years.
At a stroke, the carbon pricing system became arbitrary, subject to exemptions, or "carve-outs," which might be politically motivated.
Worse, in suggesting that the carbon levy might indeed be a hardship for some Canadians, the feds allowed that it might be an increase in cost of living for all Canadians.
Which is nonsense. The levy is "revenue neutral," returning to Canadians the money it takes, and more than that for those who reduce their carbon purchases.
A "rebranding" of the carbon levy only strengthens the hand of Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre in his successful demonization of carbon pricing.
Canada's carbon tax fiasco is an example of a global phenomenon in which leaders fail to explain major initiatives. Leaders often don't, as Franklin Roosevelt did, explain their major priorities with a 21st-century equivalent to FDR's "fireside chats."
In Canada, that malpractice dates to then-prime minister Brian Mulroney's failure to explain his introduction of the hated GST.
The GST was actually an overdue blow for transparency. It didn't raise prices. It did make public a hidden manufacturers' tax, so that Canadians now have a true sense of what they pay in sales taxes.
As prime minister, Jean Chrétien decided to keep the GST, breaking a campaign promise to scrap it.
Keep that in mind when leaders today promise to "axe the carbon tax." Especially in jurisdictions like B.C. with their own carbon pricing that no PM has the power to scrap.
In the U.S., Germany and France in recent decades, national leaders have repeatedly failed to explain laudable reforms in health care, immigration and carbon pricing, respectively.
Into the voids they thereby created stepped critics with libertarian and nativist agendas, generating misinformation that still haunts us today.
Meanwhile, the Canadian carbon pricing system carries on.
Last month, the first of this year's four quarterly Ottawa rebates were issued. Those federal cheques averaged $122 for Ontario adults and $244 for Ontario families of four.
Carbon pricing's unpopularity, based on misinformation, might help cost the federal Liberals the next election.
That needn't concern anyone.
The modest task for Canadians is to keep conserving energy as we've long done. And to keep pocketing the cash rewards from Ottawa for as long as the current carbon pricing system continues.
CAPTION: Conservative party Leader Pierre Poilievre has been pushing his "axe the tax" message over federal carbon pricing.

Subject: Canadians; Cost of living; False information; Carbon; Decades; Prime ministers; Heating; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Cost of living Environmental tax

Location: Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Title: I do the work the federal government has failed to do: Explain the carbon tax

Document 292 of 500

Regular families will never again be able to buy a house in Toronto - but we can still fix the housing crisis. Here's how

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/regular-families-will-never-again-be-able-buy/docview/2921530379/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Regular+families+will+never+again+be+able+to+buy+a+house+in+Toronto+-+but+we+can+still+fix+the+housing+crisis.+Here%27s+how&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In late July, Lindsay Dworkin, a senior project manager at a medical tech company, received a text message from her downstairs neighbour. Their landlord, the neighbour said, was putting the home Dworkin lived in with her husband and two-year-old daughter up for sale. Her family would have to find a new place to live.
In a normal city, at a normal time, that wouldn't have been a big deal. But in the Toronto of 2023 it was devastating. "I couldn't function," Dworkin said. "I had to get my parents to come over and take over child care duties."
Dworkin has a law degree. She's in her early 40s, saves fanatically, and is on the executive track in a growing and lucrative industry. But even for her, the prospect of finding a new, decent place in this city, where the average new rent for a vacated two-bedroom apartment climbed 40 per cent last year, was paralyzing.
"It's a little heartbreaking," she said. "You suddenly feel unseated and you feel like you don't have a home."
Toronto has had a housing crisis of one form or another for decades. But over the last two years, as interest rates climbed, in-migration soared and wages stayed flat, the scope and scale of that crisis has grown in unprecedented ways. It isn't just the poor or the working class anymore. It isn't new immigrants, or artists, or students. By last year, almost anyone in Toronto who didn't have family money or get into the market early enough was struggling.
"It's almost like a tide coming in," said Tim Richter, the president and CEO of the Canadian Alliance to End Homelessness. As the water climbs, wealthier and wealthier families are getting caught up in the flow.
Today that tide is higher than it has ever been. Two-income families are floundering with their mortgages. Young lawyers are turning down Toronto jobs because they can't afford Toronto homes. Professionals, like Dworkin, are considering leaving the city because the costs of renting are so high.
"We gave our real estate agent from Scarborough to Mississauga, from Richmond Hill to the lake, and the prices are all the same," she said. "You want three bedrooms? $4,000. No matter what the condition. No matter where it is."
That's the bad news. But there is good news, too.
Radical action on housing policy is now possible in ways it hasn't been for decades. In fact, it's already happening. And all for one simple reason: The tide has now risen so high, housing costs are so out of control, that they are finally affecting the kinds of people politicians can't ignore.
"I joke about it with my husband," Dworkin, a management professional with a law degree and a young family, said. "But our long-term goal is eat the rich."
Over the past several months, the Star has spoken to almost three dozen leading experts in construction, urban planning, politics, economics, and housing advocacy. Almost to a person they agreed that 2024 could be a sliding-doors moment for housing in this city.
"The time is here for us to do something," said Mayor Olivia Chow in an exclusive interview in her office last fall.
Few believe we can actually "solve" the housing crisis, certainly not in the short or medium term. But many, from construction magnates to homeless advocates, are convinced we can do better, a lot better, if we commit to dramatic changes starting right now. "We are going to have to do something radically different in how we build housing in order to meet the moment," Richter said.
Those changes have the potential to significantly increase housing affordability over the next 10 years. But they won't be easy and they will mean accepting, well, change: to the built form of this city, to our idea of where homes go and how they get built and, perhaps especially, to our collective cultural understanding of what exactly a home is. It will mean, in other words, accepting that the Toronto of 2034 will be a lot different from the one we live in today.
That's because getting there will mean accepting, among other things, that a dream that has endured for generations in this city - that if you work hard at a good job, you can own your own home, with a patch of grass out front and a mailbox on the porch - is dead, at least for anyone without generational wealth. "That is the (19)50s-'60s concept," Chow said. "That is so done."
How we got here
To understand where we're going with housing in Toronto, or at least where we could go if things go right, it helps to first understand how we got where we are. Home prices and rents tend to get treated in the public imagination like a mystical force. They rise and (rarely) fall according to whims beyond mortal understanding.
But the reality is a lot less complicated. In short, we have more people than good places to live in Toronto. For decades that imbalance has been getting worse, and that imbalance creates scarcity. Because housing is a nondiscretionary good - you have to have somewhere to live, you can't opt out because the costs are too high - that scarcity creates competition, and that competition drives relentless upward pressure on pricing.
Ontario grew by almost three million people between 2007 and 2023, according to Statistics Canada numbers analyzed by Mike Moffatt, a housing policy advocate and the senior director of policy and innovation at the Smart Prosperity Institute in Ottawa. Much of that growth was concentrated in the GTA. Over the same period, the province broke ground on projects that all together added up to just 1.2 million new housing units.
The crisis starts at the bottom, with what experts call nonmarket housing. There will always be, in any big city, a significant portion of the population who cannot afford to pay the prevailing rents. That proportion will rise and fall depending on a host of factors, including the economy, market rental costs, refugee inflows and more. But it will never be zero. And the only way to house that nonzero population, according to decades of research on chronic homelessness, is for governments to build, or fund, places for those for those people to live.
The problem in Canada is that "governments got out of the business of helping to build affordable units" decades ago, said Tyler Meredith, who was Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's lead economic adviser until 2022.
Between the mid-1960s and the mid-1980s, the Canadian government built a small empire of nonmarket homes in this country - somewhere between 20,000 and 40,000 new units a year, every year, according to Carolyn Whitzman, a housing policy researcher. But in the decades since, that boom has gone decidedly bust. Between 1993 and 2001, Canada built just 50,000 new public or nonmarket housing units, according to a recent analysis.
That creates a problem for the people at the bottom rung. It explains in large part the huge increase in chronic homelessness we've seen in Canada since the 1980s. But it also has run-on effects higher up the chain. "In a lot of ways homelessness is like a canary in the coal mine," said Richter.
Put simply, if you don't have enough nonmarket housing, some people will end up with no housing at all. They'll be in shelters, on couches or, increasingly in Toronto, in public parks. But not everyone will end up homeless. A lot of them instead will end up spending more than they can afford to rent a market unit. (Indeed, in Toronto last year, the share of rental units in arrears climbed to nearly 20 per cent, according to a CMHC report released last week.) That's a problem for them. It means more families taking on unsustainable or predatory debt or going without enough money to spend on food, clothing and other necessities. But it's also a problem for everyone else. It puts more pressure on the rental market. And the rental market, like public housing, is a mess.
The rental vacancy rate in Toronto last year was a measly 1.4 per cent, according to the CMHC, tied for the second lowest in the country and behind only Vancouver. Prices, meanwhile, are soaring. "I've always done rentals, and usually I can do maybe about 20 rental deals a year," said Samantha Fraser, a real estate agent in Toronto, in an interview last year. "This year, I've done two." (Fraser said the market has eased slightly so far in 2024, but it remains historically strained.)
Why? Well, mostly because there aren't enough good places to rent. Canada mostly stopped putting up purpose-built rental buildings after a boom in the 1960s and '70s. Fast forward through several decades of explosive population growth and you have a situation where there are far more people who want to rent than there are good rentals available. "Right now I have four separate clients who are competing for two beds with a budget of $2,800," Fraser said in the fall. "And there's barely anything that's coming up."
Move up the chain and the problem compounds. In a healthy market, some of the rental pressure would be relieved every year by renters climbing the income ladder, starting families and buying homes. But for people without family money, that is increasingly not an option. The costs of owning any kind of home in Toronto are just too far out of whack with average salaries.
In a sane market, those costs should have come down as supply exploded to keep up with demand. But thanks to decades of restrictive zoning, high development charges, limited land, political pressure and poor productivity, that explosion never happened. Instead, prices just went up and up and up. So renters, like Lindsay Dworkin, stayed put.
"My parents talk to me like, 'Really, you can't buy a house?' And I'm like, 'Guys: here's my investments. Here's what we have saved,'" she said. "I'm like, 'what was your mortgage when you bought your house?' And they're like, '300 bucks a month.' And I'm like, 'OK, cool.'"
The solution to that problem isn't rocket science. We don't have enough housing. We need to build more of it, everywhere along the scale. But doing that would mean accepting some pretty radical changes to the way this city looks and operates. For one thing, "the city has to look at giving up the idea that you can live 50 feet from College Street in a detached house and pretend that you're in the suburbs," said one longtime Toronto planner, who was granted anonymity to speak frankly about the internal workings of the city.
The city, in other words, has to convince families to give up the dream of having a single-family home inside the old city core, and, increasingly, outside it too.
But for that to work, it has to offer them something coherent and desirable in its place - something that somehow crams far more housing and people into places we aren't used to seeing housing at all.
Housing everywhere
Picture this. The year is 2034. You live two blocks from an arterial road, say Oakwood Avenue, or Gerrard Street East. You own your own home, a three-bedroom unit in a fourplex on what used to be a single-family lot. In the mornings, you walk your kids to a school on the first two floors of a purpose-built rental tower. Afterward you drop into a library built inside a 300-unit co-op before sending some mail from a post office below a social housing complex and grabbing lunch in a diner beneath an upscale condo project full of market-priced units.
To dig itself out of a decades-deep housing hole, Toronto doesn't need action on any one level, according to virtually every expert interviewed for this piece. It needs action everywhere, all at once. It needs more social housing, more co-ops, more rental housing, more condos, more market units and way, way more of what planning nerds call the "missing middle" - duplexes, triplexes, three-bedroom suites, anything, really, that can fit a middle-class family without being a stand-alone home.
But jamming all those new units into an existing city won't be easy. We can't just bulldoze the Danforth and start over. Instead, we need a total rethink of where housing can go.
Some experts believe, for example, that governments should be looking at any kind of land they own as being ripe for housing developments. "There are more post offices in Canada than there are Tim Hortons," said Moffatt. "There's nothing stopping the government from saying, 'hey, you know that post office over there? Why don't we just renovate it? We'll build off that building or tear it down and put up a six-storey midrise apartment with retail on the bottom.'"
It isn't just post offices either. It's "everything from parking lots to building on top of schools and health centres and libraries and fire halls and ambulance bays," Whitzman said.
Repurposing government land for mixed use, including housing, would help. So too would accepting far more housing on existing residential streets. "What would it take to double the number of homes on my street and in my community?" said Ene Underwood, the CEO of Habitat for Humanity in the GTA. "That's not a comfortable conversation for people. But that is the conversation we need to have."
In the old city especially, Toronto needs policies that will spur the construction of new basement suites and laneway units and full teardowns where single-family homes are turned into four units or six. It needs more small apartment blocks on existing cookie-cutter roads and far more people, twice as many people, in fact, on each and every residential street.
Even still, that would only solve half the problem. Toronto doesn't just need far more homes, it needs them incredibly quickly. The deficit built up over the past 40 years is just too big. And building fast is not something at this which this city excels.
So how will it manage? The answer, it turns out, may not come from Toronto, or even from the here and now. It may instead be found in another housing crisis, on another continent, generations ago.
All at once
In the early 1960s, Sweden's social democratic government faced a demographic and political crisis. The country's population had swelled and urbanized at an unprecedented rate in the years following the Second World War. The government was, in theory, all for that growth. It was part of the country's plan to develop a model social democracy. But there was a big problem. All those new city dwellers had nowhere to live.
Sweden had a housing crisis. And to push back against growing unrest among the nascent middle class, the Swedish government announced an outlandish plan: in just 10 years, it would build or fund one million new homes, in a country of just eight million people.
It sounded absurd. And yet, somehow, they did it.
By 1974, Sweden had built over one million new homes, including student apartments, highrise towers, lowrise apartment blocks and single-family houses. And the way they got there may hold part of the secret to solving Toronto's housing crisis by 2034.
Mass-market homes, Sweden decided, could never be built at pace and scale using traditional, on-site construction alone. So what the government did, according to Tyler Pullen, a manufactured housing expert at Berkeley University's Terner Centre for Housing Innovation, was create a program to incentivize what's become known as "manufactured" or "off-site" construction.
If Toronto's housing market looks more sane in a decade's time, many experts believe it will be in part because Canada finally made the same leap Sweden did all those years ago. Manufactured construction, where large parts of new homes and apartment complexes are built off-site, in factories, can raise construction capacity, stabilize timelines and costing and, crucially for Canada, drive productivity improvements across the sector, according to Pullen's research. "It really tends to raise the bar of the construction industry writ large," Pullen said.
Getting there won't happen overnight and will be impossible without significant government investment and support, Pullen believes. But despite a number of false starts in Canada, it could happen, some industry sources believe, if the investment and the will are there.
"I'm of the belief that if the government said, 'Hey, listen, we're going to do 150,000 homes, and we're going to do them using modular construction and we mean it,' that investment would happen pretty quick," said one senior construction industry executive who was granted anonymity to speak frankly about commercial matters. "The technology would improve pretty quick, because it's almost there. I can tell you first hand we would get the cost down."
Projects built using off-site construction are not necessarily faster than traditional builds, Pullen said. But they do add to the overall capacity of the industry. They change the workforce needs in a chronically understaffed sector and bring up productivity across the board.
But to get where we need to be in 2034, being faster and more efficient once projects start won't be enough. We also need to speed up the pre-construction phase. Doing that will mean embracing once-verboten ideas like pre-approved, off-the-shelf designs, and somehow speeding up the approval process itself, which everyone from bureaucrats to business leaders to the mayor agree is still eating up way too much time in this city.
It will also mean finding ways to make building cheaper, for developers, builders and buyers. And to do that, taxpayers are going to have to pay more, much more, all because successive generations of Toronto, Ontario and Canadian politicians have failed the heed the lessons of an American sociologist named Robert Merton.
Unintended consequences
If there is one ironclad law in politics it is that voters like having things a lot more than they like paying for them. Pressure for services is relentless, matched only by an endless desire for taxes to stay low. In Toronto, those two competing pressures have combined over time to create a situation where yes, taxes are low, services do exist, but runaway side-effects abound.
Beginning in the 1980s, the federal and Ontario governments began downloading services onto cities and towns. (Downloading, in this case, is a euphemism for "stopped paying for them.") For housing, that had a number of direct consequences. The federal government stopped paying for social housing, so social housing stopped being built. But over time, it also had a number of what Merton famously called "unanticipated consequences."
Merton believed that all social and fiscal polices have both intended and unintended outcomes. Policy analysis tends to focus overwhelmingly on the former. But the latter can have just as much, if not more impact on society.
That was certainly the case with housing and fiscal downloading. Federal and provincial governments wanted to both cut spending and taxes without suffering for it politically. They did that by telling cities to pay for those services themselves. That meant that the cities themselves had to either cut services, raise taxes or find some other way to punt the problem. Mostly they punted, in ways that would, over time, drastically fuel housing inflation.
In Toronto, successive municipal governments have kicked the cost of building and growing the city onto the development industry. In 2021, about 27 per cent of the cost of any new housing unit in Toronto came from development charges, according to an analysis by the Building Industry and Land Development Association, and those costs inevitably get passed to homeowners and renters.
Almost everyone interviewed for this piece believes that will have to change for Toronto to make any kind of dent in the housing crisis by 2034. Developers and their advocates believe it, obviously. But so too do advocates for the homeless. "This idea of growth paying for growth ... is kind of goofy," said Tim Richter. "You're heaping thousands of dollars of cost onto each unit in your housing."
The problem is that, as Chow pointed out in her interview, you can't cut development charges without making up the revenue somewhere else. "One way or another, you have to build the infrastructure," she said. The city can't raise property taxes high enough to make up the difference. It also can't approve new developments without having the money to pay for corresponding parks, community centres, roads, sewers and buses. So how do you square the circle?
The only real way, unfortunately, is to undo the original, unanticipated sins of downloading. To slash municipal development charges and spur growth, higher levels of government need to re-upload and start paying for services. And to do that, they are going to have to raise your taxes.
That's not great for the economy, or for anyone's political survival. But the economic consequences of the alternative - letting the housing crisis get worse - are far more dire, some economists believe. "Regardless of how you slice and dice, it's going to be costly from a public finance perspective," said Jimmy Jean, chief economist and strategist at the Desjardins Group. "But we have to see this as an investment."
The only other option, Jean believes, would be to slow population growth, by cutting immigration. And that would create its own problem, Jean said. "It's kind of kicking the can down the road."
(Of course, immigration is only one kind of growth. And while almost nobody who spoke to the Star for this story believes Canada should significantly reduce its long-term immigration goals, many, even most, did express support for another idea: curbing demand by reducing the number of people who come to this country temporarily - a number that has exploded in recent years.)
Change, one way or another
Getting to a livable Toronto in 2034 - one where rents are tethered somewhat to incomes, where middle-class families can afford to have kids and build lives, where parks aren't overrun by people locked out of a grossly inadequate nonmarket system - will take sustained, co-ordinated action from all three levels of government. It will require huge investments of public capital, long-term commitments to controversial and sometimes unpopular policies, and significant cultural changes: in the city government, the construction industry and among Torontonians themselves.
It would be easy to look at that list and think 'that's never going to happen.' Priorities change too fast. Politicians get scared. People and businesses are too stubborn and stuck in their ways. And besides, Toronto is still in Canada and Canada is still, well, Canadian, a country that can't seem to stop being less than the sum of its federal parts.
In her interview with the Star, Chow painted a picture of a Toronto in 2034 that looks citywide a lot like the area around St. Lawrence Market does today: There are parks and community centres, co-ops, market condos, public housing, and public transit all stacked together on top of each other. "It's a safe, livable, healthy neighbourhood that has all the amenities," she said.
But when asked why she believes, after everything she's seen in an almost 40-year political career, that can actually happen, she laughed. "I'm an eternal optimist, or else I'm in the wrong job," she said. "If we want to build housing, we have to work together."
Chow's optimism is not entirely without cause. Change is already happening.
The federal government, after years of inadequate action, has announced a flurry of meaningful housing programs since the summer (including, most recently, more than $230 million in financing for a new 500-unit rental complex in Etobicoke announced in January and another half a billion for city housing programs announced in December). Chow and Premier Doug Ford, meanwhile, agreed to a deal in November that would see the province re-upload the costs of the Don Valley Parkway and Gardiner Expressway.
Yes, the problems remain colossal. But what was surprising, reporting this story, was how many experts - in politics, industry and advocacy - believe they can be tackled. Why? Because the alternative, they said, almost too a person, was simply too dire.
Lindsay Dworkin spent most of the fall looking at rentals. By Christmas, her family was still in the same place. Dworkin feels almost existentially beaten down at this point, by her house hunt and by a city she doesn't understand anymore. She knows she's better off financially than most people in this city. But she still can't find a decent, affordable place to live.
"I think everybody kind of knows what happens when things get dire for people and their families," she said, then laughed. "Like, revolution, let's go."
The stakes really are that high. Without change Toronto will continue to shrink and grow at the same time. Families will flee. Homelessness will soar. It will, increasingly, be a city built for the very rich and the very poor, with less culture, less kids, and far fewer usable parks, where the only people who can afford to follow their dreams will be the already wealthy or those who dream of joining them.
"You're always gonna have super-rich kids that have mediocre talent that think they're incredible," Dworkin, who used to work in the music industry, said. "And they will always meet each other in the condos that their parents buy for them. And they will always put together bands. But it's not going to have the same heart and soul."
It doesn't have to be that way. The solutions are out there. All it will take is political - and public - will.
CAPTION: Mayor Olivia Chow andDeputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland at a housing announcement last fall."If we want to build housing, we have to work together," Chow told the star.An encampment at The Church of St Stephen in the Field Toronto.Housing policy advocate Mike Moffatt found that while Ontario grew by almost three million people between 2007 and 2023 - with much of that growth in the GTA - the province broke ground on just 1.2 million new housing units.Mayor Olivia Chow has pointed to a Toronto in 2034 that looks a lot like the area around St. Lawrence Market does today: parks, community centres, co-ops, market condos, public housing and public transit - all alongside each other.The Toronto that needs to exist has some housing options that we haven't been creating.
CREDIT: Richard Warnica Business Feature Writer

PM's trash talk worries former official: Ex-ambassador says Trudeau should ditch the anti-Trump rhetoric

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pms-trash-talk-worries-former-official/docview/2921462660/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PM%27s+trash+talk+worries+former+official%3A+Ex-ambassador+says+Trudeau+should+ditch+the+anti-Trump+rhetoric&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA A former Canadian ambassador to Washington says Justin Trudeau should ditch the anti-Trump "MAGA Conservative" rhetoric directed at the Liberals' Canadian political rivals.
David MacNaughton, who co-chaired the Ontario campaign for Trudeau's 2015 election victory, says it's neither a wise domestic political strategy nor is it smart for Canada-U.S. relations.
In an interview Friday, MacNaughton said the prime minister is taking a risk by being seen to take swipes at Donald Trump, the presumptive Republican nominee, in a U.S. presidential election year.
MacNaughton - who Trudeau hailed as key to Canada's successful renegotiation of NAFTA with the Trump administration - worries there could be "blowback" if Trump wins and decides to set his sights on Canada.
Taking indirect shots at Trump would make it a lot harder to fight against Trump's promised 10 per cent tariffs on U.S. imports, or to persuade a new Trump administration that the Trudeau government is a trusted partner on defence and security, he suggested.
"I would be trying to make sure that there weren't any huge blow-ups in our relationship with the Americans between now and our (Canadian) election," he added.
American voters go to the polls in November, while a Canadian federal election is not expected before October 2025.
Moreover, MacNaughton said there's no indication that drawing such a contrast would be a winning electoral strategy for the Liberals here at home.
Public opinion research suggests a large percentage of Canadians feel the country is taking the wrong direction or that it's time for a change in government, MacNaughton said. He drew a comparison to the electorate's mood in 2015 when people "were tired of (Conservative prime minister) Stephen Harper. They wanted a change."
At that time, MacNaughton said, Trudeau was offered all kinds of advice on what kind of campaign to run. The reality, however, was that the public mood was such that Trudeau "didn't have to be spectacular, he just had to be a change from what Stephen Harper was," he said.
"Well, you know what? We're almost in exactly the same situation."
Attacking Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre "by itself isn't going to work anyway," he said. "And secondly, trying to draw a comparison with Donald Trump ... why would you run the risk?"
In MacNaughton's opinion, the bigger challenge for the Trudeau Liberals is to persuade Canadians that they understand the world has changed dramatically since their 2015 election, and even since the most recent election in 2021.
He said they need to demonstrate they understand the economic pain and anxieties that stem from a changed global order, and are prepared to make changes to how they govern - not that they will continue to do more of the same. He suggested if Harper had admitted to mistakes and promised to govern differently in 2015, it may have posed a "dilemma" for the Trudeau campaign back then.
The Liberals have announced a revival of what they call a "Team Canada" approach to getting ready for whoever wins the next U.S. election, but MacNaughton's view, first reported by Politico, is that the Trudeau government should "do the strategy. You don't talk strategy."
When MacNaughton departed the ambassador post in 2019, Trudeau praised him as a "trusted adviser, friend, and counsellor ... whose honesty, moderation, and wisdom were pivotal to Team Canada in our successful renegotiation of NAFTA."
He said the reality now, even with a Democrat currently in the White House, is that it is a time of "increased isolationism and protectionism" and Canada is "vulnerable."
"We need to be seen by the Americans as a trusted friend, ally, partner and, you know, right now, I don't think that feeling is as strong as it has been," he said. "And that's not just this government's fault. That's been going on for some time."
MacNaughton has shared his view with senior Liberals, but is at a loss to explain why Trudeau - who has criticized Poilievre's "ideologically driven MAGA Conservatives" in Parliament - thinks the attacks will work.
"I don't understand it," he said.
The Star has reported on polling data that also suggests Canadians are not persuaded by the Liberals' Trump-Poilievre comparisons.
The Conservatives did not respond Friday to a request for comment.
Conservative MP Randy Hoback, who has long worked on Canada-U.S. issues, recently said the Canada-U.S. relationship is too critical to be jeopardized in order to score "domestic political points."
"Trudeau and his caucus must end their repeated jabs south of the border ... His actions are hazardous to our economy and to our national security," Hoback wrote in an online post.
Trudeau is not the only Canadian political leader to take shots at Trump.
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, the Liberals' governing ally in Parliament, last month warned that a Trump election victory would be bad for Canada. Trump is "openly running on an egomaniac, vengeance-filled motive to become the president, and it is incredibly disturbing to watch this," Singh said.
"Donald Trump frankly, is in a ... world of his own. The things that he has done, the things that he says, the type of person he is, there is no other comparison to someone who is as bad for democracy, as bad for people, as bad for the planet as Donald Trump."
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star

Document 294 of 500

Home truths: How our housing market got to its unhappy state, and how we could fix it. It might be starting to happen already

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/home-truths/docview/2921462268/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Home+truths%3A+How+our+housing+market+got+to+its+unhappy+state%2C+and+how+we+could+fix+it.+It+might+be+starting+to+happen+already&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In late July, Lindsay Dworkin, a senior project manager at a medical tech company, received a text message from her downstairs neighbour. Their landlord, the neighbour said, was putting the home Dworkin lived in with her husband and two-year-old daughter up for sale. Her family would have to find a new place to live.
In a normal city, at a normal time, that wouldn't have been a big deal. But in the Toronto of 2023 it was devastating. "I couldn't function," Dworkin said. "I had to get my parents to come over and take over child-care duties."
Dworkin has a law degree. She's in her early 40s, saves fanatically, and is on the executive track in a growing and lucrative industry. But even for her, the prospect of finding a new, decent place in this city, where the average new rent for a vacated two-bedroom apartment climbed 40 per cent last year, was paralyzing.
"It's a little heartbreaking," she said. "You suddenly feel unseated and you feel like you don't have a home."
Toronto has had a housing crisis of one form or another for decades. But over the last two years, as interest rates climbed, in-migration soared and wages stayed flat, the scope and scale of that crisis has grown in unprecedented ways. It isn't just the poor or the working class anymore. It isn't new immigrants, or artists, or students. By last year, almost anyone in Toronto who didn't have family money or get into the market early enough was struggling.
"It's almost like a tide coming in," said Tim Richter, the president and CEO of the Canadian Alliance to End Homelessness. As the water climbs, wealthier and wealthier families are getting caught up in the flow.
Today that tide is higher than it has ever been. Two-income families are floundering with their mortgages. Young lawyers are turning down Toronto jobs because they can't afford Toronto homes. Professionals, like Dworkin, are considering leaving the city because the costs of renting are so high.
"We gave our real estate agent from Scarborough to Mississauga, from Richmond Hill to the lake, and the prices are all the same," she said. "You want three bedrooms? $4,000. No matter what the condition. No matter where it is."
That's the bad news. But there is good news, too.
Radical action on housing policy is now possible in ways it hasn't been for decades. In fact, it's already happening. And all for one simple reason: The tide has now risen so high, housing costs are so out of control, that they are finally affecting the kinds of people politicians can't ignore.
"I joke about it with my husband," Dworkin, a management professional with a law degree and a young family, said. "But our long-term goal is eat the rich."
Over the past several months, the Star has spoken to almost three dozen leading experts in construction, urban planning, politics, economics and housing advocacy. Almost to a person they agreed that 2024 could be a sliding-doors moment for housing in this city.
"The time is here for us to do something," said Mayor Olivia Chow in an exclusive interview in her office last fall.
Few believe we can actually "solve" the housing crisis, certainly not in the short or medium term. But many, from construction magnates to homeless advocates, are convinced we can do better, a lot better, if we commit to dramatic changes starting right now. "We are going to have to do something radically different in how we build housing in order to meet the moment," Richter said.
Those changes have the potential to significantly increase housing affordability over the next 10 years. But they won't be easy and they will mean accepting, well, change: to the built form of this city; to our idea of where homes go and how they get built; and, perhaps especially, to our collective cultural understanding of what exactly a home is. It will mean, in other words, accepting that the Toronto of 2034 will be a lot different from the one we live in today.
That's because getting there will mean accepting, among other things, that a dream that has endured for generations in this city - that if you work hard at a good job, you can own your own home, with a patch of grass out front and a mailbox on the porch - is dead, at least for anyone without generational wealth. "That is the (19)50s-'60s concept," Chow said. "That is so done."
How we got here
To understand where we're going with housing in Toronto, or at least where we could go if things go right, it helps to first understand how we got where we are. Home prices and rents tend to get treated in the public imagination like a mystical force. They rise and (rarely) fall according to whims beyond mortal understanding.
But the reality is a lot less complicated. In short, we have more people than good places to live in Toronto. For decades that imbalance has been getting worse, and that imbalance creates scarcity. Because housing is a nondiscretionary good - you have to have somewhere to live, you can't opt out because the costs are too high - that scarcity creates competition, and that competition drives relentless upward pressure on pricing.
Ontario grew by almost three million people between 2007 and 2023, according to Statistics Canada numbers analyzed by Mike Moffatt, a housing policy advocate and the senior director of policy and innovation at the Smart Prosperity Institute in Ottawa. Much of that growth was concentrated in the GTA. Over the same period, the province broke ground on projects that all together added up to just 1.2 million new housing units.
The crisis starts at the bottom, with what experts call nonmarket housing. There will always be, in any big city, a significant portion of the population who cannot afford to pay the prevailing rents.That proportion will rise and fall depending on a host of factors, including the economy, market rental costs, refugee inflows and more. But it will never be zero. And the only way to house that nonzero population, according to decades of research on chronic homelessness, is for governments to build, or fund, places for those for those people to live.
The problem in Canada is that "governments got out of the business of helping to build affordable units" decades ago, said Tyler Meredith, who was Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's lead economic adviser until 2022.
Between the mid-1960s and the mid-1980s, the Canadian government built a small empire of nonmarket homes in this country - somewhere between 20,000 and 40,000 new units a year, every year, according to Carolyn Whitzman, a housing policy researcher. But in the decades since, that boom has gone decidedly bust. Between 1993 and 2001, Canada built just 50,000 new public or nonmarket housing units, according to a recent analysis.
That creates a problem for the people at the bottom rung. It explains in large part the huge increase in chronic homelessness we've seen in Canada since the 1980s. But it also has run-on effects higher up the chain. "In a lot of ways homelessness is like a canary in the coal mine," said Richter.
Put simply, if you don't have enough nonmarket housing, some people will end up with no housing at all. They'll be in shelters, on couches or, increasingly in Toronto, in public parks. But not everyone will end up homeless. A lot of them instead will end up spending more than they can afford to rent a market unit. (Indeed, in Toronto last year, the share of rental units in arrears climbed to nearly 20 per cent, according to a CMHC report released last week.) That's a problem for them. It means more families taking on unsustainable or predatory debt or going without enough money to spend on food, clothing and other necessities. But it's also a problem for everyone else. It puts more pressure on the rental market. And the rental market, like public housing, is a mess.
The rental vacancy rate in Toronto last year was a measly 1.4 per cent, according to the CMHC, tied for the second lowest in the country and behind only Vancouver. Prices, meanwhile, are soaring. "I've always done rentals, and usually I can do maybe about 20 rental deals a year," said Samantha Fraser, a real estate agent in Toronto, in an interview last year. "This year, I've done two." (Fraser said the market has eased slightly so far in 2024, but it remains historically strained.)
Why? Well, mostly because there aren't enough good places to rent. Canada mostly stopped putting up purpose-built rental buildings after a boom in the 1960s and '70s. Fast forward through several decades of explosive population growth and you have a situation where there are far more people who want to rent than there are good rentals available. "Right now I have four separate clients who are competing for two beds with a budget of $2,800," Fraser said in the fall. "And there's barely anything that's coming up."
Move up the chain and the problem compounds. In a healthy market, some of the rental pressure would be relieved every year by renters climbing the income ladder, starting families and buying homes. But for people without family money, that is increasingly not an option. The costs of owning any kind of home in Toronto are just too far out of whack with average salaries.
In a sane market, those costs should have come down as supply exploded to keep up with demand. But thanks to decades of restrictive zoning, high development charges, limited land, political pressure and poor productivity, that explosion never happened. Instead, prices just went up and up and up. So renters, like Lindsay Dworkin, stayed put.
"My parents talk to me like, 'Really, you can't buy a house?' And I'm like, 'Guys: here's my investments. Here's what we have saved,'" she said. "I'm like, 'what was your mortgage when you bought your house?' And they're like, '300 bucks a month.' And I'm like, 'OK, cool.'"
The solution to that problem isn't rocket science. We don't have enough housing. We need to build more of it, everywhere along the scale. But doing that would mean accepting some pretty radical changes to the way this city looks and operates. For one thing, "the city has to look at giving up the idea that you can live 50 feet from College Street in a detached house and pretend that you're in the suburbs," said one longtime Toronto planner, who was granted anonymity to speak frankly about the internal workings of the city.
The city, in other words, has to convince families to give up the dream of having a single-family home inside the old city core, and, increasingly, outside it too.
But for that to work, it has to offer them something coherent and desirable in its place - something that somehow crams far more housing and people into places we aren't used to seeing housing at all.
Housing everywhere
Picture this. The year is 2034. You live two blocks from an arterial road, say Oakwood Avenue, or Gerrard Street East. You own your own home, a three-bedroom unit in a fourplex on what used to be a single-family lot. In the mornings, you walk your kids to a school on the first two floors of a purpose-built rental tower. Afterward you drop into a library built inside a 300-unit co-op before sending some mail from a post office below a social housing complex and grabbing lunch in a diner beneath an upscale condo project full of market-priced units.
To dig itself out of a decades-deep housing hole, Toronto doesn't need action on any one level, according to virtually every expert interviewed for this piece. It needs action everywhere, all at once. It needs more social housing, more co-ops, more rental housing, more condos, more market units and way, way more of what planning nerds call the "missing middle" - duplexes, triplexes, three-bedroom suites, anything, really, that can fit a middle-class family without being a stand-alone home.
But jamming all those new units into an existing city won't be easy. We can't just bulldoze the Danforth and start over. Instead, we need a total rethink of where housing can go.
Some experts believe, for example, that governments should be looking at any kind of land they own as being ripe for housing developments. "There are more post offices in Canada than there are Tim Hortons," said Moffatt. "There's nothing stopping the government from saying, 'hey, you know that post office over there? Why don't we just renovate it? We'll build off that building or tear it down and put up a six-storey midrise apartment with retail on the bottom.'"
It isn't just post offices either. It's "everything from parking lots to building on top of schools and health centres and libraries and fire halls and ambulance bays," Whitzman said.
Repurposing government land for mixed use, including housing, would help. So too would accepting far more housing on existing residential streets. "What would it take to double the number of homes on my street and in my community?" said Ene Underwood, the CEO of Habitat for Humanity in the GTA. "That's not a comfortable conversation for people. But that is the conversation we need to have."
In the old city especially, Toronto needs policies that will spur the construction of new basement suites and laneway units and full teardowns where single-family homes are turned into four units or six. It needs more small apartment blocks on existing cookie-cutter roads and far more people, twice as many people, in fact, on each and every residential street.
Even still, that would only solve half the problem. Toronto doesn't just need far more homes, it needs them incredibly quickly. The deficit built up over the past 40 years is just too big. And building fast is not something at this which this city excels.
So how will it manage? The answer, it turns out, may not come from Toronto, or even from the here and now. It may instead be found in another housing crisis, on another continent, generations ago.
All at once
In the early 1960s, Sweden's social democratic government faced a demographic and political crisis. The country's population had swelled and urbanized at an unprecedented rate in the years following the Second World War. The government was, in theory, all for that growth. It was part of the country's plan to develop a model social democracy. But there was a big problem. All those new city dwellers had nowhere to live.
Sweden had a housing crisis. And to push back against growing unrest among the nascent middle class, the Swedish government announced an outlandish plan: in just 10 years, it would build or fund one million new homes, in a country of just eight million people.
It sounded absurd. And yet, somehow, they did it.
By 1974, Sweden had built more than one million new homes, including student apartments, highrise towers, lowrise apartment blocks and single-family houses. And the way they got there may hold part of the secret to solving Toronto's housing crisis by 2034.
Mass-market homes, Sweden decided, could never be built at pace and scale using traditional, on-site construction alone. So what the government did, according to Tyler Pullen, a manufactured housing expert at Berkeley University's Terner Centre for Housing Innovation, was create a program to incentivize what's become known as "manufactured" or "off-site" construction.
If Toronto's housing market looks more sane in a decade's time, many experts believe it will be in part because Canada finally made the same leap Sweden did all those years ago. Manufactured construction, where large parts of new homes and apartment complexes are built off-site, in factories, can raise construction capacity, stabilize timelines and costing and, crucially for Canada, drive productivity improvements across the sector, according to Pullen's research. "It really tends to raise the bar of the construction industry writ large," Pullen said.
Getting there won't happen overnight and will be impossible without significant government investment and support, Pullen believes. But despite a number of false starts in Canada, it could happen, some industry sources believe, if the investment and the will are there.
"I'm of the belief that if the government said, 'Hey, listen, we're going to do 150,000 homes, and we're going to do them using modular construction and we mean it,' that investment would happen pretty quick," said one senior construction industry executive who was granted anonymity to speak frankly about commercial matters. "The technology would improve pretty quick, because it's almost there. I can tell you first hand we would get the cost down."
Projects built using off-site construction are not necessarily faster than traditional builds, Pullen said. But they do add to the overall capacity of the industry. They change the workforce needs in a chronically understaffed sector and bring up productivity across the board.
But to get where we need to be in 2034, being faster and more efficient once projects start won't be enough. We also need to speed up the pre-construction phase. Doing that will mean embracing once-verboten ideas like pre-approved, off-the-shelf designs, and somehow speeding up the approval process itself, which everyone from bureaucrats to business leaders to the mayor agree is still eating up way too much time in this city.
It will also mean finding ways to make building cheaper, for developers, builders and buyers. And to do that, taxpayers are going to have to pay more, much more, all because successive generations of Toronto, Ontario and Canadian politicians have failed the heed the lessons of an American sociologist named Robert Merton.
Unintended consequences
If there is one ironclad law in politics it is that voters like having things a lot more than they like paying for them. Pressure for services is relentless, matched only by an endless desire for taxes to stay low. In Toronto, those two competing pressures have combined over time to create a situation where yes, taxes are low, services do exist, but runaway side-effects abound.
Beginning in the 1980s, the federal and Ontario governments began downloading services onto cities and towns. (Downloading, in this case, is a euphemism for "stopped paying for them.") For housing, that had a number of direct consequences. The federal government stopped paying for social housing, so social housing stopped being built. But over time, it also had a number of what Merton famously called "unanticipated consequences."
Merton believed that all social and fiscal polices have both intended and unintended outcomes. Policy analysis tends to focus overwhelmingly on the former. But the latter can have just as much, if not more impact on society.
That was certainly the case with housing and fiscal downloading. Federal and provincial governments wanted to both cut spending and taxes without suffering for it politically. They did that by telling cities to pay for those services themselves. That meant that the cities themselves had to either cut services, raise taxes or find some other way to punt the problem. Mostly they punted, in ways that would, over time, drastically fuel housing inflation.
In Toronto, successive municipal governments have kicked the cost of building and growing the city onto the development industry. In 2021, about 27 per cent of the cost of any new housing unit in Toronto came from development charges, according to an analysis by the Building Industry and Land Development Association, and those costs inevitably get passed to homeowners and renters.
Almost everyone interviewed for this piece believes that will have to change for Toronto to make any kind of dent in the housing crisis by 2034. Developers and their advocates believe it, obviously. But so too do advocates for the homeless. "This idea of growth paying for growth ... is kind of goofy," said Tim Richter. "You're heaping thousands of dollars of cost onto each unit in your housing."
The problem is that, as Chow pointed out in her interview, you can't cut development charges without making up the revenue somewhere else. "One way or another, you have to build the infrastructure," she said. The city can't raise property taxes high enough to make up the difference. It also can't approve new developments without having the money to pay for corresponding parks, community centres, roads, sewers and buses. So how do you square the circle?
The only real way, unfortunately, is to undo the original, unanticipated sins of downloading. To slash municipal development charges and spur growth, higher levels of government need to re-upload and start paying for services. And to do that, they are going to have to raise your taxes.
That's not great for the economy, or for anyone's political survival. But the economic consequences of the alternative - letting the housing crisis get worse - are far more dire, some economists believe. "Regardless of how you slice and dice, it's going to be costly from a public finance perspective," said Jimmy Jean, chief economist and strategist at the Desjardins Group. "But we have to see this as an investment."
The only other option, Jean believes, would be to slow population growth, by cutting immigration. And that would create its own problem, Jean said. "It's kind of kicking the can down the road."
(Of course, immigration is only one kind of growth. And while almost nobody who spoke to the Star for this story believes Canada should significantly reduce its long-term immigration goals, many, even most, did express support for another idea: curbing demand by reducing the number of people who come to this country temporarily - a number that has exploded in recent years.)
Change, one way or another
Getting to a livable Toronto in 2034 - one where rents are tethered somewhat to incomes, where middle-class families can afford to have kids and build lives, where parks aren't overrun by people locked out of a grossly inadequate nonmarket system - will take sustained, co-ordinated action from all three levels of government. It will require huge investments of public capital, long-term commitments to controversial and sometimes unpopular policies, and significant cultural changes: in the city government, the construction industry and among Torontonians themselves.
It would be easy to look at that list and think 'that's never going to happen.' Priorities change too fast. Politicians get scared. People and businesses are too stubborn and stuck in their ways. And besides, Toronto is still in Canada and Canada is still, well, Canadian, a country that can't seem to stop being less than the sum of its federal parts.
In her interview with the Star, Chow painted a picture of a Toronto in 2034 that looks citywide a lot like the area around St. Lawrence Market does today: There are parks and community centres, co-ops, market condos, public housing, and public transit all stacked together on top of each other. "It's a safe, livable, healthy neighbourhood that has all the amenities," she said.
But when asked why she believes, after everything she's seen in an almost 40-year political career, that can actually happen, she laughed. "I'm an eternal optimist, or else I'm in the wrong job," she said. "If we want to build housing, we have to work together."
Chow's optimism is not entirely without cause. Change is already happening.
The federal government, after years of inadequate action, has announced a flurry of meaningful housing programs since the summer (including, most recently, more than $230 million in financing for a new 500-unit rental complex in Etobicoke announced in January and another half a billion for city housing programs announced in December). Chow and Premier Doug Ford, meanwhile, agreed to a deal in November that would see the province re-upload the costs of the Don Valley Parkway and Gardiner Expressway.
Yes, the problems remain colossal. But what was surprising, reporting this story, was how many experts - in politics, industry and advocacy - believe they can be tackled. Why? Because the alternative, they said, almost too a person, was simply too dire.
Lindsay Dworkin spent most of the fall looking at rentals. By Christmas, her family was still in the same place. Dworkin feels almost existentially beaten down at this point, by her house hunt and by a city she doesn't understand anymore. She knows she's better off financially than most people in this city. But she still can't find a decent, affordable place to live.
"I think everybody kind of knows what happens when things get dire for people and their families," she said, then laughed. "Like, revolution, let's go."
The stakes really are that high. Without change Toronto will continue to shrink and grow at the same time. Families will flee. Homelessness will soar. It will, increasingly, be a city built for the very rich and the very poor, with less culture, less kids, and far fewer usable parks, where the only people who can afford to follow their dreams will be the already wealthy or those who dream of joining them.
"You're always gonna have super-rich kids that have mediocre talent that think they're incredible," Dworkin, who used to work in the music industry, said. "And they will always meet each other in the condos that their parents buy for them. And they will always put together bands. But it's not going to have the same heart and soul."
It doesn't have to be that way. The solutions are out there. All it will take is political - and public - will.
CREDIT: Richard Warnica business Toronto Star
