Document 296 of 500

Singh says he owes O'Toole an apology: Five things we learned about the NDP leader at the Democracy Forum

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/singh-says-he-owes-otoole-apology/docview/2921460892/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Singh+says+he+owes+O%27Toole+an+apology%3A+Five+things+we+learned+about+the+NDP+leader+at+the+Democracy+Forum&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh made his second appearance at Toronto Metropolitan University's Democracy Forum on Friday, speaking to Star columnist and TMU Senior Fellow Martin Regg Cohn about the state of federal politics today.
Here are five things the New Democratic leader revealed during the discussion.
His new daughter's name
Singh shared that his second daughter - who the NDP leader and his wife, Gurkiran Kaur Sidhu, welcomed in mid-December - is named Dani, a moniker meaning "generosity" and "benevolence."
"There is a true kind of generosity at the core of the human spirit," Singh said of the inspiration behind the name.
Singh spent much of the hour-long chat holding the two-month-old, who grew fussy after being minded by a staffer.
His two-year-old daughter, Anhad, was bestowed a name meaning "limitless," prompting Singh to occasionally refer to his governing deal with the minority Liberals as the "Anhad Accord."
(The deal was brokered, in part, due to a congratulatory call Prime Minister Justin Trudeau placed to Singh after Anhad's birth. It remains to be seen what Dani's arrival portends for the future of Canada's political landscape.)
He owes former Conservative leader Erin O'Toole an apology
When Singh first appeared at the Democracy Forum during a virtual 2021 chat, he told Cohn that there was absolutely no way his party would support a minority government led by then-Conservative leader Erin O'Toole.
When asked Friday whether he would answer similarly if the next election handed a victory to Pierre Poilievre, Singh had a sharp - and sheepish - response.
"In retrospect, Erin O'Toole seemed like a very reasonable person. I'd like to be very blunt with you," Singh said.
"He believed in science. He thought that if a health-care professional gives us good advice around the medication, we should probably listen to that health-care advice. Like, normal, standard stuff."
Singh admitted he was likely too hard on O'Toole, and said he should probably "call him up" to convey as much.
As for whether he'd have similar sober second thoughts about the prospect of a Poilievre-helmed government, Singh was unequivocal.
"No, man!" the NDP leader exclaimed.
He's trying to spin
pharmacare wins
Singh confirmed reporting from the CBC that he's pushing the government for coverage of some essential medicines in exchange for extending the deadline by which pharmacare legislation must be passed.
"Our agreement simply said that there should be progress toward pharmacare, meaning legislation that moves us towards that direction. It didn't actually require (the Liberals) to cover any medication," Singh told Cohn.
"We're going to fight to make sure there will be some medication delivered to all Canadians ... which was never actually in the agreement initially."
According to the governing pact, which could see the New Democrats prop up Justin Trudeau until 2025, the Liberals were meant to pass legislation outlining a framework for a national pharmacare program by the end of last year.
The NDP agreed in late 2023 to let the Liberals introduce the bill by March, but both parties still disagree on what such a program should look like.
He condemned a B.C. NDP
minister for her "horribly wrong" Middle East comments
The federal NDP and British Columbia NDP are usually seen pumping each other up, but that wasn't the case on Friday.
Singh was answering audience questions as B.C.'s minister of post-secondary education, Selina Robinson, fielded calls for her resignation after she said this week that the state of Israel was established on "a crappy piece of land with nothing on it."
The remarks have drawn swift criticism from those who say the comments erase Palestine's history before the state of Israel was created in 1948.
Robinson apologized for her remarks and was rebuked by Singh, who was asked to weigh in on the matter after a handful of pro-Palestinian protesters briefly disrupted the event.
"What she said was wrong ... horribly wrong," Singh said.
"She was factually wrong. It was harmful, and it was absolutely wrong to do that. There's no question about it."
He regrets riding a bike
with Doug Ford
In 2017, when Singh was the newly minted leader of the NDP and Doug Ford was not yet Ontario's premier, the duo appeared on the TVO program Political Blind Date: a show that paired political rivals to discuss the issues of the day.
During the episode, Singh took Ford on a bike ride through Toronto's streets - an activity Ford said he had not attempted in decades.
While the pair came out of the program on perfectly friendly terms, Singh had tough words for Ford during Friday's discussion.
"I kind of regret that a bit now," Singh said of the cycling jaunt. "That made him seem like a friendly fellow and he's not."
CREDIT: Raisa Patel Toronto Star

Subject: Legislation; Politics; Apologies

Location: Israel

People: Trudeau, Justin; Singh, Jagmeet

Document 297 of 500

Children learn through play: Doug Ford government to revamp full-day kindergarten curriculum, Jan. 23

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/children-learn-through-play/docview/2921460797/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Children+learn+through+play%3A+Doug+Ford+government+to+revamp+full-day+kindergarten+curriculum%2C+Jan.+23&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford government to revamp full-day kindergarten curriculum, Jan. 23
The love of language that makes for lifelong readers and writers doesn't come from a series of lessons. It begins in the preschool years with the delight and rhythm of story time. Children learn about fractions when one child receives a bigger slice of the birthday cake than another. It is impossible to list all the science concepts kids learn from mucking about in the sand box. We have ample reason to believe in the power of play. Studies show that without play, children are robbed of healthy development that can never be recovered.
Learning, at any age, is more a matter of direct experience than direct instruction.
How many parents sit the toddler down and go through a series of lessons to teach them to walk or talk? Children learn these most complex skills through experience, and following a developmental path that is uneven but predictable.
Support, encouragement, and security from caring adults are important, but the essential ingredient is play. It is through play that children learn that which no one could teach them.
In this post-pandemic time, young children need play more than ever. They have been traumatized by the lack of natural social development and in many cases are already addicted to using technology as an escape from reality. Our children deserve the right to play and learn in the best possible way. This is what will build a solid foundation for academic success. Moreover, it will provide the vehicle for them to sing, dance, laugh and cry, developing the capacity to thrive in a rapidly changing world.
Children cannot speak for themselves. They rely on us to protect their rights. Where is the leadership that ought to be promoting an understand of play and learning? Why are the teachers' federations, faculties of education and social sciences not speaking up? Our children need our leadership today as never before.

Susanne Eden, associate professor, retired, York University, Faculty of Education, Newmarket

Subject: Preschool education

Title: Children learn through play:   Doug Ford government to revamp full-day kindergarten curriculum, Jan. 23

Document 298 of 500

'Rebranding' carbon tax helps Poilievre

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/rebranding-carbon-tax-helps-poilievre/docview/2921460191/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Rebranding%27+carbon+tax+helps+Poilievre&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: What we have here, with Canada's needlessly controversial carbon pricing system, is a failure to communicate.
As the Star reported this week, Ottawa is giving serious thought to "rebranding" its carbon pricing system, as if merely renaming it will make it less unpopular.
Seven years after it was first introduced, Ottawa's carbon pricing system is still misunderstood by a large plurality of Canadians. Many regard it as an inflationary increase in their cost of living.
It is no such thing.
For Canadians affected by the levy, as consumers of gasoline and carbon-based home heating fuels, the carbon levy is a quarterly windfall in Ottawa rebates that either match the levy or exceed it for those who reduce their carbon purchases.
Eight in 10 Canadians affected by the carbon levy actually get more money back in quarterly cash rebates from Ottawa than they spend in carbon levies, as the Parliamentary Budget Office (PBO) reported last year.
About 72 national and subnational governments worldwide have adopted carbon pricing systems. In its dashboard of those systems, the World Bank reports that they cover a total of 23 per cent of global greenhouse gas emissions.
The motive for carbon pricing, to remind, is the warning by climate scientists for decades that until we put a price on carbon pollution the fight against global warming is unwinnable no matter what other steps we take.
Ottawa created a carbon pricing system, back in 2017, that rewards Canadians for reducing their carbon footprint and doesn't punish them if choose not to change their consumer habits.
Households receive fixed annual rebates whether they reduce their car trips and turn down the thermostat or not.
The average Ontario family of four will receive roughly $1,000 in cash carbon rebates this year regardless of how much carbon it consumes.
But if that family reduces its carbon consumption, saving money upfront in doing so, a portion of those federal rebates becomes an additional source of household wealth in rebates that exceed the family's carbon levy payments.
It is akin to RSPs, by which Ottawa rewards Canadians for saving for their retirement.
But the Trudeau government never did the necessary work of explaining the role of carbon pricing in the global warming fight.
Or how, more often than not, the carbon levy system increases household wealth rather than reducing it.
After failing to explain its carbon pricing when it was introduced and in the years since, the Trudeau government further botched its own initiative last fall by abruptly suspending the carbon levy on heating oil (not all heating fuel) for three years.
At a stroke, the carbon pricing system became arbitrary, subject to exemptions or "carve-outs," which might be politically motivated.
Worse, in suggesting that the carbon levy might indeed be a hardship for some Canadians, the feds allowed that it might be an increase in cost of living for all Canadians.
Which is nonsense. The levy is "revenue neutral," returning to Canadians the money it takes, and more than that for those who reduce their carbon purchases.
A "rebranding" of the carbon levy only strengthens the hand of Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre in his successful demonization of carbon pricing.
Canada's carbon tax fiasco is an example of a global phenomenon in which leaders fail to explain major initiatives.
In Canada, that malpractice dates to then-prime minister Brian Mulroney's failure to explain his introduction of the hated GST.
The GST was actually an overdue blow for transparency. It didn't raise prices.
It did make public a hidden manufacturers' tax, so that Canadians now have a true sense of what they pay in sales taxes.
As prime minister, Jean Chrétien decided to keep the GST, breaking a campaign promise to scrap it.
Keep that in mind when leaders today promise to "axe the carbon tax." Especially in jurisdictions like B.C. with their own carbon pricing that no PM has the power to scrap.
In the U.S., Germany and France in recent decades, national leaders have repeatedly failed to explain laudable reforms in health care, immigration and carbon pricing, respectively.
Into the voids they thereby created stepped critics with libertarian and nativist agendas, generating misinformation that still haunts us today.
Meanwhile, the Canadian carbon pricing system carries on.
Last month, the first of this year's four quarterly Ottawa rebates were issued. Those federal cheques averaged $122 for Ontario adults and $244 for Ontario families of four.
Carbon pricing's unpopularity, based on misinformation, might help cost the federal Liberals the next election.
That needn't concern anyone.
The modest task for Canadians is to keep conserving energy as we've long done. And to keep pocketing the cash rewards from Ottawa for as long as the current carbon pricing system continues.
CAPTION: A "rebranding" of the carbon levy only strengthens the hand of Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre in his successful demonization of carbon pricing, David Olive writes.ETHAN CAIRNSThe Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: David Olive Toronto Star OPINION

Business indexing term: Subject: Cost of living Environmental tax

Document 299 of 500

PM and Tories spar over food costs: Trudeau claims Poilievre aide lobbied for Loblaw. Conservatives dismiss comment as 'pathetic'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pm-tories-spar-over-food-costs/docview/2921460190/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PM+and+Tories+spar+over+food+costs%3A+Trudeau+claims+Poilievre+aide+lobbied+for+Loblaw.+Conservatives+dismiss+comment+as+%27pathetic%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals are trying to undermine Conservative criticism over the high cost of food by noting how the lobbying firm of one of Pierre Poilievre's top advisers works for a grocery giant that is raking in big profits.
A day after the Liberals first raised the lobbying connection in the House of Commons, Trudeau brought it up unprompted at a news conference in Waterloo on Friday. The prime minister suggested the fact that top Tory strategist Jenni Byrne's lobbying firm has worked for Loblaws casts doubt on the Conservative leader's credibility as a self-styled champion of ordinary Canadians who are struggling with rising food costs.
"One more thing," Trudeau said, while answering questions from journalists.
"Pierre Poilievre has been standing up for months now, pretending that he cares about the high grocery prices faced by Canadians, and it turns out that his top adviser is working as a lobbyist for Loblaws. I think Mr. Poilievre owes some explanations to Canadians."
The Star searched the publicly available lobbying registries for the federal government and all 10 provinces. Byrne herself is not registered as a lobbyist in any of them.
Employees at her firm, however, are registered as lobbyists for Loblaw Companies Ltd. at Queen's Park. According to the registry, six representatives from Jenni Byrne + Associates have registered to lobby Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative government on behalf of Loblaws since August 2022. The records say the goals of the lobbying effort are to reduce "red tape within the cannabis sector," and to promote "energy conservation opportunities" in Ontario. They don't mention food or grocery prices.
Sebastian Skamski, a spokesperson for Poilievre, dismissed Trudeau's comments as a desperate attempt to pin blame for rising food costs on the leader of the Official Opposition when the governing Liberals have not prevented them from increasing, despite promising last fall to pressure Loblaws and other big chains to "stabilize" prices.
The cost of groceries has increased faster than overall inflation for more than two years, according to Statistics Canada. In December 2023, the cost of store-bought food was 4.7 per cent higher than one year earlier, compared with overall inflation of 3.4 per cent.
"The Liberals failed and now claim that it's really the leader of the opposition - and not the prime minister and government of Canada - who holds all the power. This is laughable and pathetic," Skamski said in a written statement.
Byrne did not respond to a request for comment from the Star on Friday.
In an emailed statement, Simon Jefferies, a spokesperson for Byrne's company, said Byrne has never been registered to lobby for Loblaws. "The work JB+A does with Loblaws is limited to the provincial level and focused on expanded access to beer and wine and red tape reduction," Jefferies said.
Under Poilievre's leadership, the Conservatives have constantly hammered the Liberal government over rising costs.
Poilievre blames huge increases in federal spending during the COVID-19 pandemic, along with central bank measures to increase the money supply, for spurring inflation.
On grocery prices, the Conservatives argue Trudeau's carbon pricing system - a policy to reduce emissions that cause climate change by requiring all provinces to have a carbon levy or cap-and-trade system - is responsible for increasing costs of producing and transporting food.
The New Democrats have taken a different tack, and claim it is "greedy" corporations that are needlessly profiting off of struggling Canadians during this recent period of high inflation. Loblaws and other major grocers deny they are responsible for rising prices, and say the drivers of inflation are outside of their control. Yet big grocery chain profits are up along with prices. Loblaws reported more than $1 billion in operating income in the third quarter of 2023, up 7.5 per cent from the same period a year earlier.
On Friday, at an event in Toronto, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh said the fact that Byrne's firm lobbies for Loblaws means Poilievre is "never going to take on" big grocery chains.
"He's never going to take on corporate greed. He is never going to take on the real causes of the problems we're going through," Singh said.
With files from Raisa Patel
CAPTION: In Waterloo on Friday, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said the fact that the lobby firm of top Tory strategist Jenni Byrne, left, has worked for Loblaws casts doubt on Pierre Poilievre's credibility as a champion of Canadians struggling with rising food costs.Nick Iwanyshyn THE CANADIAN PRESS photos
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall Toronto Star With files from Raisa Patel

Document 300 of 500

Top court supports mandate letter secrecy: Ruling protects Ford's directives to his cabinet ministers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/top-court-supports-mandate-letter-secrecy/docview/2921458619/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Top+court+supports+mandate+letter+secrecy%3A+Ruling+protects+Ford%27s+directives+to+his+cabinet+ministers&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's cabinet secrets are safe for now, after the Supreme Court of Canada ruled his ministers' mandate letters do not have to be made public.
"The mandate letters are protected from disclosure ... The letters are revealing of the substance of cabinet deliberations," the high court ruled Friday in a unanimous 7-0 decision, warning of the risks of prematurely "publicizing cabinet's decision-making process."
"(It) would increase the public pressure that stakeholders put on ministers and give rise to partisan criticism from their political opponents; this scrutiny would ultimately paralyze the collective decision-making process," the court said.
"Freedom of information legislation strikes a balance between the public's need to know and the confidentiality the executive requires to govern effectively. In Canada's constitutional democracy, the confidentiality of cabinet deliberations is a precondition to responsible government because it enables collective ministerial responsibility," it continued.
"The prerogative to determine when and how to announce cabinet decisions is grounded in the harmful impact that premature disclosure of policy priorities can have on the deliberative process. The efficiency of the deliberative process justifies keeping cabinet proceedings confidential until a final decision is made and announced."
Justice Andromache Karakatsanis, who was Ontario cabinet secretary in charge of the provincial public service from 2000 to 2002, wrote the 57-page decision.
Ford's Progressive Conservative government had spent years fighting the release of the 2018 documents that give ministers their marching orders.
"We've been pretty clear - the premier's been pretty clear - those are captured by cabinet confidentiality and we haven't wavered from that position," Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy told reporters earlier this week.
Neither Ford nor Attorney General Doug Downey would comment Friday, but the three opposition leaders were quick to weigh in.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said if she becomes premier after the June 2026 election, she would release her ministers' mandate letters.
"All of this time, effort and millions of dollars of taxpayers' money to fight against making mandate letters public suggests they have more to hide," said Crombie.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said the Tories "feel that they do not owe the people of Ontario any transparency or accountability for their sketchy backroom decisions, even as they remain under RCMP criminal investigation."
That was a reference to the police probe of the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
"From the Greenbelt to ServiceOntario to Ontario Place, the Conservatives have never met a backroom deal they didn't like," said Stiles.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said it was "a disappointing ruling that sets a poor precedent for democracy in Ontario."
"The people of Ontario are entitled to transparency and accountability from their elected officials. Through multiple scandals and investigations, we have seen the lengths this government will go to in order to conceal its bad behaviour from the people of Ontario," said Schreiner.
While cabinet mandate letters have traditionally been kept private, former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne abandoned that practice in 2014. Reading like glorified press releases, they are still available online at the Ontario government's website.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau followed suit when he took power federally the following year.
After Ford's Conservatives defeated Wynne's Liberals in 2018, the new premier returned to the custom of sealing the documents written to 23 cabinet ministers.
CBC filed a freedom of information request for the letters that year, which the Tories denied.
The province's information and privacy commissioner determined the broadcaster should have access to the documents, leading to a lengthy - and costly - legal fight with Queen's Park that concluded with Friday morning's high court ruling.
While the decision in the case - known as Attorney General for Ontario v. Information and Privacy Commissioner of Ontario, et al - was much-anticipated, no salacious revelations were expected.
That's because the 2018 mandate letters, resplendent with lofty rhetoric about "accountability," were leaked to Global News last year, generating little buzz at the provincial legislature.
The directives to ministers from the premier are not legally binding, meaning their responsibilities could differ from what is included in the missives.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Justin Trudeau slams Pierre Poilievre for link to Loblaws lobbying

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-slams-pierre-poilievre-link/docview/2921555158/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+slams+Pierre+Poilievre+for+link+to+Loblaws+lobbying&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 302 of 500

Jagmeet Singh on his new baby's name and why he regrets biking with Doug Ford: 5 things we learned at TMU's Democracy Forum

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jagmeet-singh-on-his-new-babys-name-why-he/docview/2921555099/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jagmeet+Singh+on+his+new+baby%27s+name+and+why+he+regrets+biking+with+Doug+Ford%3A+5+things+we+learned+at+TMU%27s+Democracy+Forum&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh made his second appearance at Toronto Metropolitan University's Democracy Forum on Friday, speaking to Star columnist and TMU Senior Fellow Martin Regg Cohn about the state of federal politics today.
Here are five things the New Democratic leader revealed during the discussion.
His new daughter's name
Singh shared that his second daughter - who the NDP leader and his wife, Gurkiran Kaur Sidhu, welcomed in mid-December - is named Dani, a moniker meaning "generosity" and "benevolence."
"There is a true kind of generosity at the core of the human spirit," Singh said of the inspiration behind the name.
Singh spent much of the hour-long chat holding the two-month-old, who grew fussy after being minded by a staffer.
His two-year-old daughter, Anhad, was bestowed a name meaning "limitless," prompting Singh to occasionally refer to his governing deal with the minority Liberals as the "Anhad Accord."
(The deal was brokered, in part, due to a congratulatory call Prime Minister Justin Trudeau placed to Singh after Anhad's birth. It remains to be seen what Dani's arrival portends for the future of Canada's political landscape.)
He owes former Conservative leader Erin O'Toole an apology
When Singh first appeared at the Democracy Forum during a virtual 2021 chat, he told Cohn that there was absolutely no way his party would support a minority government led by then-Conservative leader Erin O'Toole.
When asked Friday whether he would answer similarly if the next election handed a victory to Pierre Poilievre, Singh had a sharp - and sheepish - response.
"In retrospect, Erin O'Toole seemed like a very reasonable person. I'd like to be very blunt with you," Singh said.
"He believed in science. He thought that if a health-care professional gives us good advice around the medication, we should probably listen to that health-care advice. Like, normal, standard stuff."
Singh admitted he was likely too hard on O'Toole, and said he should probably "call him up" to convey as much.
As for whether he'd have similar sober second thoughts about the prospect of a Poilievre-helmed government, Singh was unequivocal.
"No, man!" the NDP leader exclaimed.
He's trying to spin pharmacare wins
Singh confirmed reporting from the CBC that he's pushing the government for coverage of some essential medicines in exchange for extending the deadline by which pharmacare legislation must be passed.
"Our agreement simply said that there should be progress toward pharmacare, meaning legislation that moves us towards that direction. It didn't actually require (the Liberals) to cover any medication," Singh told Cohn.
"We're going to fight to make sure there will be some medication delivered to all Canadians ... which was never actually in the agreement initially."
According to the governing pact, which could see the New Democrats prop up Justin Trudeau until 2025, the Liberals were meant to pass legislation outlining a framework for a national pharmacare program by the end of last year.
The NDP agreed in late 2023 to let the Liberals introduce the bill by March, but both parties still disagree on what such a program should look like.
He condemned a B.C. NDP minister for her "horribly wrong" Middle East comments
The federal NDP and British Columbia NDP are usually seen pumping each other up, but that wasn't the case on Friday.
Singh was answering audience questions as B.C.'s minister of post-secondary education, Selina Robinson, fielded calls for her resignation after she said this week that the state of Israel was established on "a crappy piece of land with nothing on it."
The remarks have drawn swift criticism from those who say the comments erase Palestine's history before the state of Israel was created in 1948.
Robinson apologized for her remarks and was rebuked by Singh, who was asked to weigh in on the matter after a handful of pro-Palestinian protesters briefly disrupted the event.
"What she said was wrong ... horribly wrong," Singh said.
"She was factually wrong. It was harmful, and it was absolutely wrong to do that. There's no question about it."
He regrets riding a bike with Doug Ford
In 2017, when Singh was the newly-minted leader of the NDP and Doug Ford was not yet Ontario's premier, the duo appeared on the TVO program Political Blind Date: a show that paired political rivals to discuss the issues of the day.
During the episode, Singh took Ford on a bike ride through Toronto's streets - an activity Ford said he had not attempted in decades.
While the pair came out of the program on perfectly friendly terms, Singh had tough words for Ford during Friday's discussion.
"I kind of regret that a bit now," Singh said of the cycling jaunt. "That made him seem like a friendly fellow and he's not."
CAPTION: Jagmeet Singh holds his daughter Dani while speaking at the Toronto Metropolitan University Democracy Forum on Feb. 2, 2024.

Document 303 of 500

Police chief's budget ask is misleading, Star readers say

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/police-chiefs-budget-ask-is-misleading-star/docview/2921177537/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Police+chief%27s+budget+ask+is+misleading%2C+Star+readers+say&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Police chief budget spin misleading
Toronto police chief: Invest in front-line officers to keep Toronto safe, Feb. 1
Myron Demkiw, chief of the Toronto Police Service, is misleading us. He points out there are 1.4 million emergency calls to 911 a year, but does not say the majority of 911 calls do not involve violence and can be dealt with by community and social workers. He says that more officers equal less crime, but years of research has shown that the crime rate does not correlate to police spending in any consistent way.
Toronto is one of the safest cities in North America. The key to crime prevention is through reducing inequality. To reduce response times, we need better management of the 911 service, diversion of nonpolice calls and smarter deployment. But no, our police leadership doesn't want to follow evidence. If they truly cared about our safety and well-being, they would support investment in stronger community and social supports. That is how we'll get to a safer and thriving Toronto.
Anna Willats, Toronto
Rather than attempting to intimidate the residents of Toronto by writing of facts without nuance, our police chief Myron Demkiw should be looking for the means to make his force more efficient and effective. I suggest Premier Doug Ford, who can always be counted on to have an interest in city spending, be brought in to rout out the waste in the police budget.
Peter Pinch, Toronto
How can Myron Demkiw be permitted to tease about our safety if he doesn't get his way? The rest of us must live within our means and yet, this public servant sees fit to pressure tax payers for more money through the public media. This is a tactic of a dictator, not a police chief. We obviously need to find a replacement.
Dave De Sylva, Milton, Ont.
There was an interesting contrast of articles from columnist Edward Keenan and Police Chief Myron Demkiw regarding the police budget. What struck me was how the chief says how terrible things are in the service and somehow a loss of $12.6 million is a major threat to our safety. If the biggest city in the country is at risk of chaos due to the lack of $12.6 million it is time for the politicians to demand that a third-party independent forensic team review police service spending from top to bottom to see the truth of the matter.
Len Bulmer, Aurora, Ont.
Benched officers take too much budget
City budget spinning from police could be a big problem: Keenan, Jan. 31
Here we go again with all the ballyhoo about the police budget. What I would like to know is how much of the budget is slated to be paid to suspended officers who are sitting on their butts at home while collecting a full pay cheque? It is long overdue for the mayor to ask the powers to be to provide a complete breakdown of the monies paid out to suspended cops over the years and to ask how many inevitably had to leave the service yet walked away with thousands of unearned money from taxpayers.The police act is long overdue for an overhaul especially in these trying times when citizens cannot afford the basics of life.
I have said it before: if an officer is on paid leave, put their earnings into an interest generating trust account. He or she gets the money if they return to service, and if not, those funds are redirected to the police force. It is a simple solution to a long-standing problem.
Larry Snow, Oakville, Ont.
Committing to a fix without an estimate is irresponsible
Ford government wants to refurbish Pickering nuclear reactors - but can't say how much it will cost, Jan. 30
It is difficult to understand the government's commitment to refurbishing four aging nuclear reactors in Pickering given that the design and engineering work for the project is at such a preliminary stage Energy Minister Todd Smith says it would be "irresponsible" to provide a cost estimate at this time. What is more irresponsible is the government making a multibillion dollar commitment without a good understanding of full cost, timing and feasibility. Ontario electricity consumers, who will be paying for this seemingly blank cheque approach, deserve better.
Jeffrey Levitt, Toronto
Toronto's climate fight in Chow's hands
Toronto is losing its fight against climate change amid budget squeeze, Jan. 30
This recent piece on Toronto's 2024 budget says that Toronto has fallen off track to reach its stated goal of becoming carbon neutral by 2040. I would contend that the city was not really on this track to begin with. When the TransformTO Net Zero Strategy was voted on in 2021, the will was there, but the necessary money had not been allotted and has not been since. I deputed to this effect in the public budget consultations last year and the previous year, while I was living in Toronto. Nevertheless, this year's budget is far and away the most encouraging one that I have seen in recent years. I encourage Mayor Olivia Chow and all of council to keep the city's stated climate goals as a top priority, and to continue to work toward the laudable targets set forward in the TransformTO plan. All of Ontario needs to do a better job of reducing our greenhouse gas emissions. I look to Chow and her team to be leaders on this front.
Mary Blake Rose, London, Ont.
It's one thing to set a target, but council needs to put their money where their mouth is. It's time to be proactive about climate change by funding it now. Climate change is literally and figuratively a forest fire. I can't think of many fires that become cheaper to put out the longer you ignore them.
Tori Slobodzian, Calgary, Alta.
Think outside the box for refugee housing
Trudeau government announces $362.4 million in new refugee supports, Jan. 31
An immediate solution to the refugee housing issue would be to encourage and promote safe and legal rooming houses. The inspection and approval process could be improved and it could be set up so that the interests of property owners would be protected through adequate insurance and financial incentives. Ideally these rooming houses would be close to public transit. Empty buildings (such as office towers with vacancies) should also be considered for housing. The $362.4 million from the federal government for shelter refugees could be used to support these initiatives.
Brian O'Sullivan, Stouffville, Ont.
CAPTION: "Cutting $12.6 million from the Toronto Police Services Board approved budget will result in unacceptable risks to public safety," PoliceChief Myron Demkiw wrote in the Star.

Subject: Nuclear reactors; Crime prevention; Budgets; Climate change; Cost estimates; Boarding houses

Business indexing term: Subject: Budgets Cost estimates

Title: Police chief's budget ask is misleading, Star readers say

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Document 304 of 500

Province will help city pay for 2026 World Cup: But $97M in funds will come with strings attached

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-will-help-city-pay-2026-world-cup/docview/2921084831/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+will+help+city+pay+for+2026+World+Cup%3A+But+%2497M+in+funds+will+come+with+strings+attached&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's government is teaming up with Toronto to host the 2026 FIFA Men's World Cup - but the funding from Queen's Park will come with strings attached.
Deputy Minister of Tourism, Culture and Sport Sarah Harrison has confirmed in a letter to the city that Ontario has "conditionally committed" up to $97 million for the event. The letter was addressed to Toronto city manager Paul Johnson and dated Dec. 1. It was shared with the Star by a senior provincial government source.
News of the funding comes six years after city council first asked Ontario to help pick up the costs of the wildly popular international soccer tournament, and just days before FIFA is expected to announce details of the competition schedule Sunday afternoon. The games are being co-hosted by Canada, the U.S. and Mexico.
Although critics have questioned the wisdom of governments spending a projected $300 million on the event while Toronto is facing a financial crisis, Mayor Olivia Chow said Thursday she was excited to host the competition. She welcomed the provincial funding.
"I'm very pleased," Chow said at a press conference to unveil her 2024 budget. "We signed the bid,and we've been waiting for the provincial and the federal government to step up. And the province has and I believe the federal government will soon also."
The city would pay some of the costs, but has asked the federal and provincial governments to split $184 million. According to the letter, Ontario's funding is contingent on Ottawa putting up a matching contribution.
Although the mayor said she expected the federal government to come to the table, it has yet to make a formal commitment. A federal spokesperson didn't respond to the Star's request for comment by publication time.
The province is also stipulating that money it provides be "exclusively limited to investments that will build lasting public infrastructure and benefits that will serve the community long after the games are over."
The money is not to go toward any costs "where the primary beneficiary is a private, for-profit, or otherwise nonpublic entity." Some of the funding could be provided in the form of in-kind services, like policing, transportation and health care.
The deputy minister wrote that while the province is willing to help the cash-strapped municipality with its hosting obligations, it is asking the city to limit public spending on the event "to the greatest extent possible."
The letter doesn't specify which private entities Ford's government is concerned about benefiting from government funding.
Toronto has faced criticism for a hosting deal it reached with Maple Leaf Sports and Entertainment, under which the company won't be on the hook for any costs associated with the games but will be entitled to revenues. Taxpayers will also pay for upgrades to municipally-owned BMO Field, where MLSE's CFL and MLS franchises play.
The city's hosting contracts with FIFA have not been made public.
City council voted to join the bid for the World Cup in 2018, on the condition that Toronto confirm financial support from other levels of government.
Despite not executing those funding agreements, staff signed bid agreements later that year. Toronto was officially named a host city in 2022.
Staff's $300-million estimation of the tournament costs is based on Toronto staging five games. The price tag could change depending on how many games Toronto is allotted. Initially it was supposed to split 10 games with Canada's other host city, Vancouver. But FIFA has since expanded the event from 80 to 104 games.
With files from David Rider
CREDIT: Ben Spurr Toronto Star With files from David Rider

Subject: Tournaments & championships; Soccer; Funding

Location: Canada; Ontario Canada

People: Harrison, Sarah; Ford, Doug

Ontario electricity consumers, who will be paying for this seemingly blank cheque approach, deserve better.

Document 306 of 500

Ford's team braces for more MPPs jumping to federal Tories: 'Why wouldn't they think about it?' says party insider

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-team-braces-more-mpps-jumping-federal/docview/2921083361/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+team+braces+for+more+MPPs+jumping+to+federal+Tories%3A+%27Why+wouldn%27t+they+think+about+it%3F%27+says+party+insider&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Blindsided by Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre poaching one of his provincial cabinet ministers to run federally, Premier Doug Ford is girding for more departures.
As the premier gathers with Progressive Conservatives for a policy convention in Niagara Falls this weekend, he is mindful Poilievre may pilfer more of his caucus for a federal election expected next year.
Ford's turf is fertile ground for the federal Conservatives because PC MPPs represent dozens of ridings that are also held by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals.
That means veterans who have won back-to-back provincial elections - and have local name recognition - could help the rookie federal Tory leader topple Liberal incumbents, especially in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area that is crucial to a Poilievre victory.
"I wouldn't say they're jumping ship," Ford said Wednesday in Aurora when asked about the surprise departure of former minister of red tape reduction Parm Gill on Jan. 25 and the potential for others to leave.
"I think the world of Parm, I wish him all the best. He was a federal MP before," he said, referring to Gill's service in the caucus of prime minister Stephen Harper between 2011 and 2015.
Asked what he would say to any of his caucus thinking about departing for federal pastures, Ford said, "My message is to anyone - no matter when I ran my private company or run the government - no one has a handcuff on anyone."
Hours after the premier's comments - and nearly a week after Gill bolted - Poilievre issued a statement.
"Parm will be an asset to our common sense Conservative team in our mission to axe the tax, build the homes, fix the budget and stop the crime," said the federal leader.
Poilievre's enthusiasm for Gill's arrival isn't shared broadly by others within the party, some of whom deride the Ford Tories as "conservative-lite."
Several federal Conservatives admitted to the Star some confusion as to why Gill was brought on since they don't see the ideological stars aligning between the national party and its Ontario cousin.
The unexpected resignation of the Milton MPP, who only won his seat in June 2022 by 1,680 votes, has sent a jolt through Queen's Park.
Sources told the Star the provincial Tories are worried that MPPs representing ridings in Toronto, Peel, Halton and York Region, as well as some in northern Ontario, may jump to Poilievre's surging federal Conservatives.
Public opinion polls show they have a healthy lead over Trudeau's three-term Liberal government with an election scheduled to be held by October 2025.
"Why wouldn't they think about it? Pierre is going to win and they can make a lot more money" as federal representatives, said one concerned Ford Tory, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations.
Indeed, there was grumbling in PC ranks after the Star reported on Jan. 17 that the premier would keep MPPs' salaries frozen at the same level they have been since 2008.
The base salary for an MPP is $116,550, while Tory parliamentary assistants earn $133,799 and cabinet ministers make $165,851. The MPPs' pension plan was eliminated in 1996.
In contrast, MPs, who receive annual raises in April and have a pension, had base pay of $194,600 last year while federal ministers made $287,400 and Liberal MPs who serve as parliamentary secretaries earn $213,400.
Publicly at least, Ford stresses he is not worried about an exodus, emphasizing his party's resilience.
"I believe that the people of Ontario have a lot of confidence in us - all you have to do is look at the polls," the premier said at Wednesday's crime-fighting announcement with federal Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc.
That was an apparent reference to the Star's monthly tracking poll conducted by Abacus Data.
The most recent survey, released last week, had Ford's Tories holding steady at 38 per cent support, with Bonnie Crombie's Liberals at 27 per cent, Marit Stiles's New Democrats at 23 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at five per cent.
Using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform, Abacus surveyed 995 Ontarians Jan. 18 to 23. Although opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Another high-ranking provincial Tory said fears of the federal party raiding their benches are "overblown" and the premier isn't concerned about a brain drain.
While Ford, who works closely with the Trudeau Liberals and counts Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland as a personal friend, has only met Poilievre briefly, some of his cabinet ministers are supporters of the federal party.
According to Elections Canada disclosures, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy, Education Minister Stephen Lecce, Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra, Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria and Northern Development and Indigenous Affairs Minister Greg Rickford each donated money to the federal Tories last year.
Bethlenfalvy (Pickering-Uxbridge), Calandra (Markham-Stouffville) and Sarkaria (Brampton South) have Liberal MPs as their federal counterparts. Lecce (King-Vaughan) has a Tory MP in his riding.
Rickford's Kenora-Rainy River seat has slightly different boundaries, with part of his riding represented by a Tory and part by a Liberal MP. He was a minister in the Harper government.
Poilievre's inner circle is populated by people who once worked for Ford but are no longer close to the premier, including the federal leader's most influential adviser, Jenni Byrne, deputy leader Melissa Lantsman, chief of staff Ian Todd, party executive director Mike Crase, lawyer Michael Wilson and press secretary Sebastian Skamski.
Despite - or perhaps because of - those long-standing relationships, there are tensions between the federal and provincial parties.
In part, that's because those around Ford and Poilievre are well aware of their political history: Ontario voters traditionally opt for different parties to be in power at Queen's Park and Ottawa at the same time.
The phenomenon even has a name: Underhill's balance theory, named for the famed Canadian political scientist Frank Underhill, who also wrote the Regina Manifesto that led to the modern-day New Democratic Party.
Over the past 80 years, for example, different parties have been in power federally and in Ontario 85 per cent of the time.
That suggests when the Tories are in the ascendance in Ottawa, the Liberals or the NDP do better at Queen's Park and vice versa.
Time - and the next federal and provincial elections - will tell if that trend continues.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief Stephanie Levitz Deputy Toronto Star

Subject: Provincial elections; Conservatism; Prime ministers; Elections; Primaries & caucuses

Document 307 of 500

Supreme Court rules Doug Ford's mandate letters for cabinet ministers can be kept secret

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/supreme-court-rules-doug-fords-mandate-letters/docview/2921083344/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Supreme+Court+rules+Doug+Ford%27s+mandate+letters+for+cabinet+ministers+can+be+kept+secret&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's cabinet secrets are safe for now after the Supreme Court ruled his ministers' mandate letters do not have to be made public.
"The mandate letters are protected from disclosure ... The letters are revealing of the substance of cabinet deliberations," the high court ruled Friday in a unanimous decision.
Ford's Progressive Conservative government had spent years fighting the release of the 2018 documents that give ministers their marching orders.
"We've been pretty clear - the premier's been pretty clear - those are captured by cabinet confidentiality and we haven't wavered from that position," Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy had told reporters earlier this week.
The top court agreed with the province's argument Friday.
Attorney General Doug Downey was not immediately available for comment.
While cabinet mandate letters have traditionally been kept private, former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne abandoned that practice in 2014. Reading like glorified press releases, they are still available online at the Ontario government's website.
Still, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau followed suit when he took power federally the following year.
After Ford's Conservatives defeated Wynne's Liberals in 2018, the new premier returned to the custom of sealing the documents written to 23 cabinet ministers.
But CBC filed a freedom of information request for the letters that year, which the Tories denied.
The province's information and privacy commissioner determined the broadcaster should have access to the documents, leading to a lengthy - and costly - legal fight with Queen's Park that concluded with Friday morning's high court ruling.
While the decision in the case - known as Attorney General for Ontario v. Information and Privacy Commissioner of Ontario, et al - was much-anticipated, no salacious revelations were expected.
That's because the 2018 mandate letters, resplendent with lofty rhetoric about "accountability," were leaked to Global News last year, generating little buzz at the provincial legislature.
The directives to ministers from the premier are not legally binding, meaning their responsibilities could differ from what is included in the missives.
Ford's government also lost at the Ontario Superior Court of Justice in August 2020 then again at the Court of Appeal for Ontario in January 2022, so the Tories had been anticipating another defeat at the Supreme Court.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks at Pinewood Toronto Studios on Jan. 17, 2024.

Subject: Privacy

Title: Supreme Court rules Doug Ford's mandate letters for cabinet ministers can be kept secret

Document 309 of 500

Province says it will help pay for 2026 World Cup in Toronto - with strings attached

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/province-says-will-help-pay-2026-world-cup/docview/2920623584/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+says+it+will+help+pay+for+2026+World+Cup+in+Toronto+-+with+strings+attached&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's government is teaming up with Toronto to host the 2026 FIFA Men's World Cup - but the funding from Queen's Park will come with strings attached.
Deputy Minister of Tourism, Culture and Sport Sarah Harrison has confirmed in a letter to the city that Ontario has "conditionally committed" up to $97 million for the event. The letter was addressed to Toronto city manager Paul Johnson and dated Dec. 1, 2023. It was shared with the Star by a senior provincial government source.
News of the funding comes six years after city council first asked Ontario to help pick up the costs of the wildly popular international soccer tournament, and just days before FIFA is expected to announce details of the competition schedule Sunday afternoon. The games are being co-hosted by Canada, the U.S. and Mexico.
According to the letter, Ontario's funding is contingent on the federal government putting up a matching contribution. The province is also stipulating that the money it provides be "exclusively limited to investments that will build lasting public infrastructure and benefits that will serve the community long after the games are over."
The money is not to go toward any costs "where the primary beneficiary is a private, for-profit, or otherwise nonpublic entity." Some of the funding could be provided in the form of in-kind services like policing, transportation and health care.
The deputy minister wrote that while the province is willing to help the cash-strapped municipality with its hosting obligations, it is asking the city to limit public spending on the event "to the greatest extent possible."
The letter doesn't specify which private entities Ford's government is concerned about benefiting from government funding. However, Toronto has faced criticism for a hosting deal it reached with Maple Leaf Sports and Entertainment, under which the company won't be on the hook for any costs associated with the games but will be entitled to revenues. Taxpayers will also pay for upgrades to municipally-owned BMO Field, where MLSE's CFL and MLS franchises play.
The city's hosting contracts with FIFA have not been made public.
City council voted to join the bid for the World Cup in 2018, on the condition that Toronto confirm financial support from other levels of government. Despite not executing those funding agreements, staff signed bid agreements later that year. Toronto was officially named a host city in 2022.
Staff have estimated it will cost about $300 million to stage five games for the tournament, and has asked the federal and provincial governments to together shoulder $184 million.
The event's price tag could change depending on how many games Toronto is allotted. Initially it was supposed to split 10 games with Canada's other host city, Vancouver. But FIFA has since expanded the event from 80 to 104 games.
CAPTION: Under then-mayor John Tory, city council voted to join the bid for the World Cup in 2018, on the condition that Toronto confirm financial support from other levels of government.

Subject: Tournaments & championships; Councils; Costs; Soccer; Funding; Cities

Business indexing term: Subject: Costs

Ford government expands primary care teams in effort to fix doctor shortage

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-expands-primary-care-teams-effort/docview/2920622773/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+expands+primary+care+teams+in+effort+to+fix+doctor+shortage&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: PETERBOROUGH, Ont.-In a bid to blunt the doctor shortage, Ontario is pulling another 400 physicians and other medical providers into its growing network of primary care teams - a move that should give up to 328,000 people better access to treatment, says Health Minister Sylvia Jones.
Funding of $110 million is going to 78 new and expanded teams of doctors, nurse practitioners, nurses, physiotherapists, nutritionist and other medical professionals across the province - including in Sault Ste. Marie where a major clinic stunned patients last week by announcing 10,000 would be cut loose at the end of May because their doctors have retired or quit.
While it was not clear if the money earmarked for two teams in the Sault will help 10,000 stranded patients, Jones said the fundingwill "close the gap" for 1.3 million in Ontarians who do not have their own family doctor.
"We know there's still more to do," she said.
As an example, Jones noted the $3 million earmarked for the new Peterborough Community Health Centre will enable it to provide care to more than 11,300 patients needing family doctors, mental health services, help with managing chronic diseases like diabetes, and other health needs.
The family health team concept is intended to provide support to doctors so they can see more patients while providing a broader range of care through nurse practitioners, nurses and others on staff at a time when physicians are in short supply for the province's fast-growing and aging population and hospital emergency departments are overwhelmed.
"Many are overworked, some are burned out," Jones acknowledged, speaking of doctors.
CAPTION: Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones makes an announcement on health care with Premier Doug Ford in Toronto on Jan. 16, 2023.

Subject: Nurse practitioners; Primary care; Physicians

Business indexing term: Subject: Physicians

Title: Ford government expands primary care teams in effort to fix doctor shortage

Document 311 of 500

Vehicle makers must pay for anti-theft solutions, not consumers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/vehicle-makers-must-pay-anti-theft-solutions-not/docview/2920456291/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Vehicle+makers+must+pay+for+anti-theft+solutions%2C+not+consumers&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford, Justin Trudeau governments announces $121M in federal funding to fight auto theft, gun crimes, Jan. 31
Here we go again, consumers being programmed by the insurance industry, with the assistance of federal and provincial governments this time, to prepare uncalled for and outrageous increases to insurance protection based on vehicle make and model. Vehicle theft is a real problem. The result has been insurance payouts beginning to outstrip the objectives of providing for a positive return on business investment.
Fast forward to this week's proposed solutions and here is where the con really takes shape: Insurance agencies cry foul over increasing costs and the government's response is to hold yet another summit to review the problem. This problem doesn't need another summit to address a problem that is so obvious. Therefore, I offer the following solutions:
1. Review police records and investigations relating to vehicle theft in lieu of a summit. A review will identify the problem and provide credible indicators possibly identifying and providing locations of future thefts. The expense of a costly summit eliminated.
2. Mandate by government order that insurance companies return rate increases relating to vehicle theft to their clients and immediately freeze all future rate increases relating to theft.
3. Mandate by government order that all vehicle manufactures have one year to produce anti-theft devices restricting the starting and movement of vehicles by anyone other than the owner. This would include a valet key system available to owners to allow authorized access to their vehicle by individuals of their choice.
Simplistic? Perhaps, but vehicle theft requires a solution based on the KISS principle rather than ongoing discussions and corresponding insurance increases. Vehicle manufacturers must be held accountable and responsible to produce anti-theft solutions, not consumers.
Jack Wilson, Barrie

State of post-secondary education is a direct result of greedInternational student cap will be devastating, Jan. 30
Pari Johnston writes: "According to Higher Education Strategy Associates, over the past 13 years, international student fees have sustained 100 per cent of the increased operating spending by post-secondary institutions."
This abjectly immoral state of affairs is a direct result of greedy universities and privately run schools that sought to balance their books by selling Canadian work visas to foreign workers who had no other way to get work in Canada.
These schools deserve every morsel of hardship that the new immigration caps will inflict on any institution whose financial stability depends on foreign-student enrolment. For many years now, Canadian students, Canadian professors, and Canadian administrators have been subsidized by foreign students and these are precisely the groups that should suffer the pain required to correct the situation. If a few schools must declare bankruptcy, so be it.
Patrick Cowan, Toronto
Ford is manufacturing these crises
Premier Doug Ford talks about "finding efficiencies" whenever he announces cuts. It is a mantra that his ministers have learned to parrot.
Where have these "efficiencies" landed us? There are now record waiting times in ER's, schools without teaching assistants, universities and colleges threatened with deficits and closure, etc.
In each of these cases, the private sector ends up being the beneficiary. His buddies have elbowed their way into Ontario, giving us the so-called public-private partnerships for colleges, the use of private firms that provide nurses, the outsourcing of Service Ontario services to Staples, etc.
Ford is destroying Ontario with his schemes.
He starts with cutting funding, and when services suffer as a result, he announces there is a "crisis" and brings in the private sector. This is a page right out of former conservative premier Mike Harris' book on how to manufacture a crisis.
Enough is enough.
Gloria Fung, Unionville
Preventing homelessness, domestic violence will do more to help public safety than increasing police budget
Toronto police are at 'a critical point in our history,' Chief says in defence of budget rhetoric, Jan. 30
Toronto police Chief Myron Demkiw insists any deviation from the service's requested budget will "significantly impact public safety."
Research shows otherwise.
A recent letter to Toronto City Council from my United Church of Canada congregation in Toronto quotes an academic study in the Journal of Canadian Public Policy published this month. (The letter was signed by church members from more than 13 wards.)
After looking at data from Canada's 20 largest municipalities including Toronto, the study concludes that there is absolutely no correlation between increased police spending and reduced crime rates. Indeed, as our congregation advocates, what is desperately needed is to take money from the outrageously overfunded police budget and put it towards desperately underfunded budget items such as affordable housing, homelessness, mental health, domestic violence and other social services. That is what will help to reduce crime in Toronto and, indeed, is what our citizens need.
Joanne Clarke, Toronto
Accessible transit stops will help more than touristsWe enjoyed the sites but it's the kindness of others that will stay with us, Jan. 27
What a beautiful compliment from a visitor to our city to remind us just how great Toronto and the people who live here are. It is a shame though that one of our major tourist attractions - the Royal Ontario Museum - does not have an accessible transit stop.
I have seen many people with baby strollers, canes etc. trying to navigate the stairs at the Museum subway station. We are trying to get tourism back, making it user friendly would certainly help.
It will be interesting to see how we all access the Ontario Science Centre if Premier Doug Ford moves it to the waterfront.
Please take note Mayor Olivia Chow.
Susan Ditchburn, Toronto
Ed Broadbent was a statesman who earned respect'How do you sleep at night, bro?' Parliament returns with the same old toxic behaviour, Jan. 30
Althia Raj reports that while Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre had left the House of Commons chamber on Monday, before the moment of silence for the late form NDP leader Ed Broadbent, the Conservative Opposition Leader "was still in the atrium" and "could be heard laughing with a group on the other side of the curtains." I had to read that twice.
There's a time and a place for partisan politics, I suppose, but decency and gracefulness recognize no temporal or spatial bounds.
Everyone knows that Ed Broadbent was a statesman devoted to public service and had earned the respect accorded to his memory by elected parliamentarians.
Well, almost everyone.
Dale Churchward, Toronto

Document 312 of 500

Ontario's new odd couple may save Toronto

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontarios-new-odd-couple-may-save-toronto/docview/2920454133/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario%27s+new+odd+couple+may+save+Toronto&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It seems like only yesterday that Olivia Chow stood little chance of solving one of the worst budget crises in Toronto's history.
It was obvious that closing the projected gap in the city's 2024 operating budget, estimated at $1.8 billion, would require a large, unpopular hike in property taxes.
And the feds and Ontario Premier Doug Ford balked at providing Toronto with the additional funds needed to restore the city's finances.
Yet here we are, on Chow's scheduled Feb. 1 presentation of her first draft budget to city council, with a solution to the crisis plainly in sight.
If council approves Chow's turnaround budget with its higher property tax revenues in a vote set for Valentine's Day, Toronto will be on a sounder financial footing than it has been in years.
Earlier in this saga, Ford surprised Toronto with a partial bailout that considerably narrows Toronto's budget gap.
That "New Deal for Toronto" provides as much as $9 billion this decade for housing, subway cars and Toronto's other priorities, and has the province assuming financial responsibility for the Gardiner Expressway and the Don Valley Parkway.
In its own change of heart, Ottawa last month gave Toronto about $471 million to help build tens of thousands of affordable housing units over the next decade.
At this writing, Toronto is still hoping for an additional $250 million from Ottawa for refugees using Toronto's shelter system.
Without those funds, city budget staff warn the property tax increase could be as high as 16.5 per cent, rather than a widely expected 10.5 per cent.
But it's likely that the Trudeau government will eventually provide more refugee funding. It's the feds who set refugee levels and they shouldn't impose fiscal hardship on cities that provide for refugees' needs.
Assuming council approves a property tax increase of 10.5 per cent, that would boost Toronto revenues by close to $400 million. The additional monthly cost to the average homeowner would be about $31.
Chow said Monday she will propose a tax increase on multi-residential buildings of less than 3.75 per cent to protect renters from the full impact of the coming tax hikes.
The budget package Chow presents Feb. 1 will include smarter procurement practices, including greater collaboration on expenses with neighbouring Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area (GTHA) municipalities.
And it will incorporate roughly $376 million in savings - or "reductions and offsets," as the city's budget staff calls them.
Better yet, this budget will leave untapped some further economies and revenue sources.
In future council votes, Toronto can delay non-essential capital expenses and eliminate some of its many property tax waivers for eligible property owners.
Those measures are among the recommendations in a report for the city by outside consultants Ernst & Young and StrategyCorp.
Council can also vote to overhaul Toronto's underpriced parking system. A report by city staff last year said Toronto could collect as much as $490 million a year in additional funds by raising commercial parking fees.
But Toronto won't soon embrace some of the revenue-enhancing options detailed in the Ernst & Young and StrategyCorp. report. They include a municipal sales tax, widely used in major U.S. cities. And road tolls, or traffic "congestion pricing," adopted by many European cities and recently by New York.
The province has repeatedly knocked down proposals for a municipal sales tax.
Chow is generally averse to cutbacks, arguing that the city has underinvested in the past decade in civic infrastructure that has become deficient.
And Chow has tried not to alienate major constituencies, including motorists and the police force.
For instance, Chow's proposed 2024 police funding will be an expected $1.35 billion. That's a slight increase over last year, though Toronto's police chief and police union have called for a bigger increase.
This chapter in Toronto history largely turns on Chow.
It helps that she was elected on a campaign promise to raise property taxes. And that she began appealing to senior levels of government for help from the moment she was elected mayor last June. Her persistence is paying off.
As part of the New Deal for Toronto, Chow ended her opposition to Ford's plan for a mega-spa at Ontario Place. She later supported the renaming of an Etobicoke football stadium for Rob Ford, the premier's late brother and former Toronto mayor.
It is those seemingly small gestures that often tell the tale.
Before Chow was elected, Ford said a Chow mayoralty would be an "unmitigated disaster" for Toronto. But several months and a new deal later, Ford said, "I just have a soft spot for the mayor, I do. We get along very, very well."
Governments have a history of dithering and deadlock on Toronto issues. But not this time.
The unusual collaboration here is shaping up as a case study for scholars of public administration.
The result is edifying. We can only hope it continues long after the budget crisis has passed.
CREDIT: David Olive Toronto Star OPINION

Subject: Collaboration; Councils; Budgets; Sales taxes; Decades; Cities; Property taxes; Tax increases; Affordable housing

Business indexing term: Subject: Budgets Sales taxes Property taxes Tax increases Affordable housing

Location: Toronto Ontario Canada; Canada

People: Chow, Olivia

Company / organization: Name: EY Global Services Ltd; NAICS: 541211
