Document 313 of 500

Ottawa pledges $362M to help with cost of sheltering refugee claimants

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ottawa-pledges-362m-help-with-cost-sheltering/docview/2920451165/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+pledges+%24362M+to+help+with+cost+of+sheltering+refugee+claimants&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Under pressure from the City of Toronto and Quebec to foot a bigger portion of the bill for asylum seekers, the federal Liberal government announced $362.4 million in new money to shelter refugees nationwide.
Immigration Minister Marc Miller refused to reveal how much of that will go to Toronto, saying only it will be a "significant amount" and revealed in the coming days.
Meanwhile, Quebec will get $100 million or nearly 28 per cent of the new funds, he told a hastily organized news conference outside the House of Commons on Wednesday. That's less than a quarter of the money that province has demanded.
Other than Quebec's share, immediate breakdowns for the other hardest-hit cities and regions were not available.
The move represents a big shift by the federal government, which up until now has resisted demands by Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow to pony up the $250 million the city expects to spend this year to take care of refugee claimants.
The Trudeau government has argued it already supports Toronto with billions of dollars, and sends billions more to the province that should bear more responsibility.
Now, with final amounts to be determined, it's not clear what the announcement will mean for the property tax rate in Chow's budget, which she's set to launch Thursday morning in Scarborough.
In a brief statement, Chow said she was "encouraged" by the news and looked forward to working with Miller on Toronto's allocation.
However, Chow's ongoing advocacy for more federal funds had clearly irritated the Liberal government.
When she threatened to label an additional six per cent tax hike as a "federal impacts levy" that would come on top of a city staff-proposed 10.5 per cent increase, members of Trudeau's 23-strong Toronto caucus complained.
On Wednesday, when asked what had changed the federal government's mind vis-à-vis Toronto's demands, Miller pointed to the ongoing "needs that people have" and the historically high and rising numbers of refugee claimants, saying, "there is a need to be a little more co-ordinated and get money into people's pockets that are best served to house those people."
He added tersely: "I have nothing to do with a federal levy. You can call a municipal tax whatever you want. It's still a municipal tax."
Chow had already reached a "new deal" with Premier Doug Ford, whose government agreed to provide $200 million a year to cover shelter costs for homeless populations. Ford and Chow joined forces to call on the federal government to cover the cost of sheltering refugees.
The Ontario government said it was "disappointed" with Miller's announcement.
"Last year, more than half of all asylum claimants in the country came to Ontario, with even more expected this year," Labour and Immigration Minister David Piccini said in a statement.
"The funding announced by the federal government today doesn't cover the needs of Toronto, let alone all the other municipalities facing the same pressures due to the increased numbers of asylum claimants."
Miller said all levels of government have to work to settle the growing number of asylum seekers who continue to enter Canada at land borders and airports despite steps Ottawa has taken to close irregular border entry points.
The new money will flow through the interim housing assistance program that works on a reimbursement basis where communities "send us the bill and we pay for it."
But he called it a "very expensive stopgap measure," and said Ottawa and the provinces must reform it together. Overall, the federal immigration department said it provided $2 billion last year alone to support refugees and asylum seekers across the country.
But cities like Toronto and Montreal are under clear strain.
By 8:45 a.m. on Wednesday, a crowd of people was already waiting outside of Toronto's downtown street referral outreach centre - the same stretch of pavement where clusters of newcomers congregated when turned away from the city's main shelter system this past summer.
As of 4 a.m. the previous night, city data shows that zero emergency beds were available in women's or coed shelters, with a single men's emergency bed and five youth spaces unfilled by night's end. All of the city's warming centres were filled to capacity, as were women's drop-ins, with just two respite beds empty - the kind of crunch often seen this winter.
Diana Chan McNally, a community worker with All Saints Toronto, said she sees refugee claimants on a daily basis - some who've managed to secure space in Toronto's packed general shelters, while others have been sleeping on the streets. For many, this was their first experience with biting winter weather, Chan McNally said.
Loly Rico, executive director of Toronto's FCJ Refugee Centre, also sees the depth of need across the city today - when, as its intake opens each Monday, a crowd of 70 to 80 people arrive looking for aid. Rico is not optimistic Toronto will receive the full amount of funding it has requested.
"We welcome the help - the support the federal government is doing," Rico noted. "We are hoping that (the $250 million) will come, because if not, we'll go again into the situation we've seen last year."
Quebec Premier François Legault wrote the federal government in mid-January demanding Ottawa reimburse Quebec about $470 million that his province spent on shelter, education and health services to absorb asylum seekers in 2021 and 2022 and to do the same for subsequent years.
He said Quebec services were nearing a breaking point as the province received 60,000 new refugee claimants in the first 11 months of 2023.
Miller said Wednesday the record flow of migrants across the western hemisphere "is not going to change overnight."
According to data posted by the Immigration and Refugee Board, asylum seekers coming from Mexico represent the largest number of claimants Canada received last year. From January to September 2023, nearly 17,500 refugee claims were made and only about 11 per cent were accepted. Another 10 per cent were rejected. The rest were among nearly 23,000 claims from Mexico still being processed.
Earlier Wednesday, Miller said the federal government is considering a range of unspecified options to deal with the huge spike in the number of travellers arriving from Mexico and claiming refugee status.
Among those options, he said, is reimposing a visa requirement on Mexican visitors that the Harper government had imposed and the Trudeau government lifted.
With files from Ben Spurr and Rob Ferguson
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star Victoria Gibson Toronto Star With files from Ben Spurr and Rob Ferguson

Subject: Federal government; Refugees; Tax rates; Cities; Immigration; Tax increases; Shelters

Business indexing term: Subject: Tax rates Tax increases

Document 314 of 500

Those over 72 now eligible to apply for plan

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/those-over-72-now-eligible-apply-plan/docview/2920448187/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Those+over+72+now+eligible+to+apply+for+plan&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Starting Thursday, applications for Canada's dental benefit will open up to an additional group of seniors needing help with their oral health care.
If you're 72 years of age and older, and you meet a list of other eligibility criteria, you could soon start receiving coverage for a number of dental services.
The benefit, slated to cost $13 billion over five years, was first launched in December for Canadians aged 87 and up.
On Wednesday, Citizens' Services Minister Terry Beech said about 408,000 seniors have applied for the benefit so far, clocking in at an average of 20,000 new sign-ups a day.
Health Minister Mark Holland also touted the measure as the largest benefit program implemented in Canada's history. The government estimates it could capture up to nine million Canadians, though some health groups have charged that eligibility requirements around income levels leave out millions of others still deserving of aid.
The program will be further expanded to other seniors, people with disabilities, and youth under 18 over the course of the year, with remaining Canadians able to access the help in 2025. The rollout is a key requirement of the Liberal-NDP deal that could see the New Democrats prop up the minority Liberals until 2025.
Here's what you need to know.
I'm 72 or older. Do I qualify?
Like all applicants for the program, you're eligible if you don't have access to dental insurance (through your employer, your pension, a benefits company or a family member), if your family's net income is less than $90,000, if you're a Canadian resident, and if you filed your tax return last year.
If you're already covered through a provincial, territorial or other federal program, you're still eligible: your coverage will just be co-ordinated between both plans.
The federal government has the ability to verify your eligibility details, so if you falsify any information, you could be kicked off the plan and forced to pay up for the services you received.
How do I apply?
If you qualify, you'll be sent a letter as soon as Feb. 1 from Service Canada inviting you to apply. As of May, applications will switch over to an online process. That letter will include an application code and instructions on how to apply. For seniors, the application process can be completed by phone.
If your information checks out, you'll be referred to Sun Life, which will enrol you in the program and send further information, including your member ID card and the date your coverage begins.
At that point, you'll be covered for services like cleanings, X-rays, fillings, root canals, dentures and extractions. Some of those procedures, however, won't be covered until this fall. The lowest-income seniors won't have to pay for procedures, but eligible Canadians at other income levels will have to contribute a co-payment.
When can I go to the dentist?
You can start receiving services under the plan as early as May, though younger seniors might have to wait a little longer.
That's assuming the rollout goes smoothly, which is not guaranteed.
Some dental associations have raised the alarm that despite discussions with Health Canada, dentists across the country still don't have a clear picture of how the program would work for providers.
"Are (patients) going to be able to go to any dentist and access this program? Is it going to be simple for not just the patient, but the dentist?" said Dr. Brock Nicolucci, president of the Ontario Dental Association.
"You hear about health-care burnout and dentistry is not immune to this. So who's going to be doing all that if there is an extra administration burden?"
Nicolucci told the Star he's worried some providers might not be able to handle the increased strain, leaving Canadians to seek help elsewhere or forgo receiving care entirely. He said he would have "loved" to see Ottawa finalize how the program would actually work before trumpeting its launch.
On Wednesday, Holland said that while he could not promise the rollout would be "perfect," he remains committed to ensuring dentists have the answers they need.
CREDIT: Raisa Patel Toronto Star

Subject: Canadians; Dental insurance; Dentists

Business indexing term: Subject: Dentists

Location: Canada

Title: Those over 72 now eligible to apply for plan

Document 315 of 500

The Odd Couple: How Olivia Chow's unlikely alliance with Doug Ford may save Toronto from disaster

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/odd-couple-how-olivia-chows-unlikely-alliance/docview/2920444378/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Odd+Couple%3A+How+Olivia+Chow%27s+unlikely+alliance+with+Doug+Ford+may+save+Toronto+from+disaster&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It seems like only yesterday that Olivia Chow stood little chance of solving one of the worst budget crises in Toronto's history.
It was obvious that closing the projected gap in the city's 2024 operating budget, estimated at $1.8 billion, would require a large, unpopular hike in property taxes.

Chow said Monday she will propose a tax increase on multi-residential buildings of less than 3.75 per cent to protect renters from the full impact of the coming tax hikes.
Toronto budget has $376M in reductions and 'offsets'
The budget package Chow presents Feb. 1 will include smarter procurement practices, including greater collaboration on expenses with neighbouring Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area (GTHA) municipalities.
And it will incorporate roughly $376 million in savings - or "reductions and offsets," as the city's budget staff calls them.
Better yet, this budget will leave untapped some further economies and revenue sources.
In future council votes, Toronto can delay non-essential capital expenses and eliminate some of its many property tax waivers for eligible property owners.
Those measures are among the recommendations in a report for the city by outside consultants Ernst & Young and StrategyCorp.
Council can also vote to overhaul Toronto's underpriced parking system. A report by city staff last year said Toronto could collect as much as $490 million a year in additional funds by raising commercial parking fees.
This chapter in Toronto history is on Chow
But Toronto won't soon embrace some of the revenue-enhancing options detailed in the Ernst & Young and StrategyCorp. report. They include a municipal sales tax, widely used in major U.S. cities. And road tolls, or traffic "congestion pricing," adopted by many European cities and recently by New York.
The province has repeatedly knocked down proposals for a municipal sales tax.
Chow is generally averse to cutbacks, arguing that the city has underinvested in the past decade in civic infrastructure that has become deficient.
And Chow has tried not to alienate major constituencies, including motorists and the police force.
For instance, Chow's proposed 2024 police funding will be an expected $1.35 billion. That's a slight increase over last year, though Toronto's police chief and police union have called for a bigger increase.
This chapter in Toronto history largely turns on Chow.
It helps that she was elected on a campaign promise to raise property taxes. And that she began appealing to senior levels of government for help from the moment she was elected mayor last June. Her persistence is paying off.
Toronto governments usually dither. Not this time
As part of the New Deal for Toronto, Chow ended her opposition to Ford's plan for a mega-spa at Ontario Place. She later supported the renaming of an Etobicoke football stadium for Rob Ford, the premier's late brother and former Toronto mayor.
It is those seemingly small gestures that often tell the tale.
Before Chow was elected, Doug Ford said a Chow mayoralty would be an "unmitigated disaster" for Toronto. But several months and a New Deal later, Ford said, "I just have a soft spot for the mayor, I do. We get along very, very well."
Governments have a history of dithering and deadlock on Toronto issues.
But not this time.
The unusual collaboration here is shaping up as a case study for scholars of public administration.
The result is edifying. We can only hope it continues long after the budget crisis has passed.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford had said an Olivia Chow mayoralty would be an "unmitigated disaster" for Toronto. He has since changed his tune. "I just have a soft spot for the mayor, I do. We get along very, very well."

Ford dismisses need for tuition hike: Postsecondary schools haven't seen increase since 2019

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-dismisses-need-tuition-hike/docview/2920443763/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+dismisses+need+for+tuition+hike%3A+Postsecondary+schools+haven%27t+seen+increase+since+2019&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The premier says college and university students won't face a tuition hike this fall because they can't afford one - but schools say they can't afford the status quo.
After a surprise comment by Premier Doug Ford that students would not pay more for the next school year, both colleges and universities said the government needs to step up.
"We are disappointed in the government not ending the tuition freeze at this time, which has been cut and frozen for five years," Steve Orsini, president and CEO of the Council of Universities, said in an interview.
"We believe it's still not too late. With the right student support package, the government could allow increased tuition while protecting low- and moderate-income students. would target financial support for students who need it most" as a recent government-appointed panel urged, Orsini added.
Earlier Wednesday at an unrelated event, Ford was asked about the recent blue-ribbon panel looking at post-secondary finances that recommended a five per cent tuition increase this fall, as well as an immediate 10 per cent boost in funding.
"I just don't believe this is the time to go into these students' pockets - especially the ones that are really struggling - and ask for a tuition increase," said the premier, who slashed tuition 10 per cent in 2019 for Ontario students and has not allowed schools to raise rates since.
"We're going to work with our colleges and universities to support them any way we can and drive efficiencies throughout the system," Ford also said. "There's some classes out there, believe it or not, that have six or seven students. That's unacceptable. So there are areas of improvement but we're always there to support our colleges and universities."
Queen's University is among those that have started to lay off staff and look at axing low enrolment classes. Its provost recently said the school could shut down if no cuts are made, though principal Patrick Deane sent out a letter last week saying there is "no risk that Queen's in any foreseeable future will close its doors."
At York University, the subject of a recent report by Ontario's auditor general, almost one-quarter of its undergraduate programs have 20 or fewer students, and enrolment has dropped by at least 10 per cent in 58 of 135 of them.
Orsini told the Star that universities are cost-efficient - something the blue-ribbon panel also found - and are already working on areas where they can find further savings. But, he added, "it's nowhere sufficient to close the massive gap in our current funding structure. The funding system is broken, and the government's own expert panel agrees."
Marketa Evans, president and CEO of Colleges Ontario, said "we recognize Ontarians are very concerned about affordability and the high cost of living. At the same time, it's essential for Ontario to invest in its public college students. This is particularly critical as the province strives to fill the huge labour-market demand for people in key sectors."
In a statement, she said "we will continue to work with the province to determine how to best enhance Ontario's investments in its public college students" and is calling on the government to increase operating grants to schools by 10 per cent.
Almost half of Ontario's 23 publicly assisted universities are currently running deficits, totalling $175 million.
The Ontario Undergraduate Student Alliance said while a tuition freeze "would be welcome, (it) does not combat the current financial issues across the sector" including the need for more student loans and grants, as well as increased funding for universities.
Meanwhile, Ford was also asked about the recent federal announcement on a cap for international students, acknowledging there are some "bad actors" among private colleges.
Public colleges - which have taken on lucrative partnerships with private ones - and universities have seen an increase in international students, who pay four or five times the tuition of domestic students.
Many schools have done so as a way to make up for stalled government funding.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau Nicholas Keung Toronto Star

Document 317 of 500

Province misfires moving ServiceOntario outlets

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/province-misfires-moving-serviceontario-outlets/docview/2920443712/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+misfires+moving+ServiceOntario+outlets&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's not difficult to identify a common denominator in the serial policy controversies of the Ontario government.
The halving of Toronto City Council. Ontario Place. The Ontario Science Centre. Municipal boundary changes. The off-again, on-again, off-again development of the Greenbelt.
These initiatives and more have been characterized by lack of public consultation, lack of transparency, and the whiff of backroom decision-making to which certain interests are privy while the public is not.
One would have thought that after the epic recent reversals of the Greenbelt scandal -- a career and credibility eroding mess still being investigated by the RCMP -- that Premier Doug Ford would have wanted to bend over backwards, for at least a time, in the name of demonstrating fair process and inviting public involvement. There was an opportunity to show that hard lessons in governing had been learned and lasting changes made.
But no. Here we are again with the moving of some ServiceOntario outlets into the Staples chain of office supply stores.
Opposition critics are justifiably concerned, given the government's track record, about the inexplicable absence of a competitive bidding process.
"There's a lack of transparency, decisions are being made behind closed doors all the time and they're always looking to reward the big corporations," New Democrat MPP Tom Rakocevic (Humber River-Black Creek) told the Star.
Now, the initiative might be every bit as innocent as Business Services Delivery Minister Todd McCarthy described it.
McCarthy says the move, with six Service Ontario centres to open in Staples locations on Feb. 1, is part of a three-year pilot project that will save about $1 million and help determine what arrangements work best.
New outlets in Staples will be open 30 per cent longer than the locations they are replacing, he said, and the province has been using retail partners such Canadian Tire, IDA and Home Hardware for Service Ontario locations for years.
But who's to know?
The Liberals have asked Ontario's Financial Accountability Office for an analysis into the costs of retrofits in Staples centres -- to be paid for by the province -- and also whether unionized public service jobs might be lost.
Liberal MPP Stephanie Bowman (Don Valley West) said the contracts will provide significant value to large American companies with Ontario paying for renovations and delivering increased foot traffic into their outlets.
The Star's Rob Ferguson reported that there was no competitive bidding because some existing Service Ontario agreements with the private contractors that run them were expiring soon and the government wanted to avoid service gaps.
But with the Ford government, there is always an excuse for haste. And the record shows that government haste has too often resulted in reversals that end up costing the public plenty.
The Ford administration's modus operandi -- first a policy announcement, then at some unspecified future date the business case, as happened with the plan to move the Ontario Science Centre -- is too often the opposite of what most Ontarians would likely deem sensible and prudent.
The fact that this government can generate controversy in its handling of so comparatively minor a matter is concerning.
And McCarthy's hapless effort this week to explain the matter to reporters -- especially on where savings will be found and why the sole-source contracts were necessary -- hardly provided reassurance. The minister seemed faintly perplexed at the persistent questions put to him on releasing the business case.
The trouble for McCarthy and for Ford is that critics -- and a good many ordinary Ontarians -- are disinclined to give them the benefit of the doubt for the simple reason that the government, as its track record makes clear, hasn't earned it.
The benefit of trust will be won back -- assuming, after the Greenbelt fiasco, that matters aren't already too far gone -- through durable evidence that lessons have been learned and changes in procedure made.
That means slowing down when necessary, adopting competitive bidding processes as a matter of course, and letting the public in early and often on decisions. No more surprise policy reveals with little evidence to support the looming changes.
It doesn't seem that big an expectation or too difficult a task.
CAPTION: The Ontario government is moving sixServiceOntario centres to Stapleslocations but critics are raising concerns about the lack of a competitive bid process.

Subject: Bids; Competition

Ottawa announces $121M to fight guns, auto theft

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ottawa-announces-121m-fight-guns-auto-theft/docview/2920441821/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+announces+%24121M+to+fight+guns%2C+auto+theft&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With a vehicle being stolen every 14 minutes in Ontario, Queen's Park and Ottawa are revving up measures to combat auto theft and gun crimes.
Federal Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc announced Wednesday $121 million in nationwide funding over five years to battle the criminal gangs profiting from stealing cars and smuggling guns.
"People shouldn't have to live in fear of their cars getting stolen in the middle of the night or doors being kicked in at their residence and sometimes at gunpoint," Premier Doug Ford told reporters at the joint announcement at York Regional Police headquarters in Aurora.
Ford praised Ottawa for stepping up, noting the provincial government has already earmarked $51 million over three years "to help police identify and dismantle criminal networks behind auto theft rings."
"I have a message to all the criminals, we're coming after you and we're going to catch you and you're going to jail," the premier said.
Next week, LeBlanc will host a national summit on fighting auto theft in Ottawa.
The minister said the Feb. 8 confab would be a chance for governments, police and insurance companies to share best practices and and "further co-ordinate joint activities."
Federal Justice Minister Arif Virani said his constituents in Parkdale-High Park "are scared" by the alarming rise in auto theft.
According to the Insurance Board of Canada, auto insurers paid out $1.2 billion in theft claims in 2022, which is triple what was paid out in 2018 and the trend continued to climb in the first half of last year.
Last fall, Ford announced new "preventing auto thefts" grant for 21 projects using modern crime-fighting tools that focus on focused on "prevention, detection, analysis and enforcement."
These included surveillance equipment and GPS tracking devices for police forces and additional training for investigators and moves designed to "break down silos and address organized criminal activity."
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Parliament returns with the same old toxic behaviour, Jan. 30

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/2920436520/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Ford%2C+Trudeau+governments+announces+%24121M+in+federal+funding+to+fight+auto+theft%2C+gun+crimes%2C+International+student+cap+will+be+devastating%2C+Toronto+police+are+at+%27a+critical+point+in+our+history%2C%27+Chief+says+in+defence+of+budget+rhetoric%2C+We+enjoyed+the+sites+but+it%27s+the+kindness+of+others+that+will+stay+with+us%2C+%27How+do+you+sleep+at+night%2C+bro%3F%27+Parliament+returns+with+the+same+old+toxic+behaviour%2C+Jan.+30&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Vehicle makers should pay for theft solutions
Ford, Trudeau governments announces $121M in federal funding to fight auto theft, gun crimes, Jan. 31
Here we go again, consumers being programmed by the insurance industry, with the assistance of federal and provincial governments this time, to prepare uncalled for and outrageous increases to insurance protection based on vehicle make and model. Vehicle theft is a real problem. The result has been insurance payouts beginning to outstrip the objectives of providing for a positive return on business investment.
Fast forward to this week's proposed solutions and here is where the con really takes shape: Insurance agencies cry foul over increasing costs and the government's response is to hold yet another summit to review the problem. Instead, I offer the following solutions:
Review police records and investigations relating to vehicle theft in lieu of a summit. A review will identify the problem and provide credible indicators possibly identifying and providing locations of future thefts.
Mandate by government order that insurance companies return rate increases relating to vehicle theft to their clients and immediately freeze all future rate increases relating to theft.
Mandate that all vehicle manufactures have one year to produce anti-theft devices restricting the starting and movement of vehicles by anyone other than the owner. This would include a valet key system available to owners to allow authorized access to their vehicle by individuals of their choice.
Vehicle manufacturers must be held accountable and responsible to produce anti-theft solutions, not consumers.
Jack Wilson, Barrie
International student mess is
a result of greed
International student cap will be devastating, Jan. 30
Pari Johnston writes: "According to Higher Education Strategy Associates, over the past 13 years, international student fees have sustained 100 per cent of the increased operating spending by post-secondary institutions."
This abjectly immoral state of affairs is a direct result of greedy universities and privately-run schools that sought to balance their books by selling Canadian work visas to foreign workers who had no other way to get work in Canada.
These schools deserve every morsel of hardship that the immigration caps will inflict on institutions whose financial stability depends on foreign-student enrolment. If some must declare bankruptcy, so be it.
Patrick Cowan, Toronto
Premier Ford is manufacturing these crises
Premier Doug Ford talks about "finding efficiencies" whenever he announces cuts. It is a mantra that his ministers have learned to parrot. Where have these "efficiencies" landed us? There are now record waiting times in ERs, schools without teaching assistants, universities and colleges threatened with deficits and closure, etc.
In each of these cases, the private sector is the beneficiary. His buddies have elbowed their way into Ontario, giving us the so-called public-private partnerships for colleges, the use of private firms that provide nurses, the outsourcing of Service Ontario services to Staples, etc.
Ford is destroying Ontario with his schemes. He starts with cutting funding, and when services suffer as a result, he announces there is a "crisis" and brings in the private sector. This is a page right out of Mike Harris' book on how to manufacture a crisis.
Enough is enough.
Gloria Fung, Unionville
Growing police budget doesn't fix society's issues
Toronto police are at 'a critical point in our history,' Chief says in defence of budget rhetoric, Jan. 30
Toronto police Chief Myron Demkiw insists any deviation from the service's requested budget will "significantly impact public safety."
Research shows otherwise.
A recent letter to Toronto City Council from my United Church of Canada congregation in Toronto quotes an academic study in the Journal of Canadian Public Policy published this month. (The letter was signed by church members from more than 13 wards.)
After looking at data from Canada's 20 largest municipalities including Toronto, the study concludes that there is absolutely no correlation between increased police spending and reduced crime rates. Indeed, as our congregation advocates, what is desperately needed is to take money from the outrageously overfunded police budget and put it towards desperately underfunded budget items such as affordable housing, homelessness, mental health, domestic violence and other social services. That is what will help to reduce crime in Toronto and, indeed, is what our citizens need.
Joanne Clarke, Toronto
Accessible stops will help more than tourists
We enjoyed the sites but it's the kindness of others that will stay with us, Jan. 27
What a beautiful compliment from a visitor to our city to remind us just how great Toronto and the people who live here are. It is a shame though that one of our major tourist attractions - the Royal Ontario Museum - does not have an accessible transit stop.
I have seen many people with strollers, canes, etc. trying to navigate the stairs at Museum subway station. We are trying to get tourism back, making it user friendly would certainly help.
It will be interesting to see how we all access the Ontario Science Centre if Premier Doug Ford moves it to the waterfront.
Please take note Mayor Olivia Chow.
Susan Ditchburn, Toronto
Broadbent was
a statesman who earned respect
'How do you sleep at night, bro?' Parliament returns with the same old toxic behaviour, Jan. 30
Althia Raj reports that while Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre had left the House of Commons chamber on Monday, before the moment of silence for the late form NDP leader Ed Broadbent, the Conservative leader "was still in the atrium" and "could be heard laughing with a group on the other side of the curtains." I had to read that twice.
There's a time and a place for partisan politics, I suppose, but decency and gracefulness recognize no temporal or spatial bounds. Everyone knows that Ed Broadbent was a statesman devoted to public service and had earned the respect accorded to his memory by elected parliamentarians.
Well, almost everyone.
Dale Churchward, Toronto

Province misfires on ServiceOntario

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-misfires-on-serviceontario/docview/2920436071/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+misfires+on+ServiceOntario&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's not difficult to identify a common denominator in the serial policy controversies of the Ontario government.
The halving of Toronto City Council. Ontario Place. The Ontario Science Centre. Municipal boundary changes. The off-again, on-again, off-again development of the Greenbelt.

New outlets in Staples will be open 30 per cent longer than the locations they are replacing, he said, and the province has been using retail partners such as Canadian Tire, IDA and Home Hardware for Service Ontario locations for years.
But who's to know?
The Liberals have asked Ontario's Financial Accountability Office for an analysis into the costs of retrofits in Staples centres - to be paid for by the province - and also whether unionized public service jobs might be lost.
Liberal MPP Stephanie Bowman (Don Valley West) said the contracts will provide significant value to large American companies with Ontario paying for renovations and delivering increased foot traffic into their outlets.
The Star's Rob Ferguson reported that there was no competitive bidding because some existing Service Ontario agreements with the private contractors that run them were expiring soon and the government wanted to avoid service gaps.
But with the Ford government, there is always an excuse for haste. And the record shows that government haste has too often resulted in reversals that end up costing the public plenty.
The Ford administration's modus operandi - first a policy announcement, then at some unspecified future date the business case, as happened with the plan to move the Ontario Science Centre - is too often the opposite of what most Ontarians would likely deem sensible and prudent.
The fact that this government can generate controversy in its handling of so comparatively minor a matter is concerning.
And McCarthy's hapless effort this week to explain the matter to reporters - especially on where savings will be found and why the sole-source contracts were necessary - hardly provided reassurance. The minister seemed faintly perplexed at the persistent questions put to him on releasing the business case.
The trouble for McCarthy and for Ford is that critics - and a good many ordinary Ontarians - are disinclined to give them the benefit of the doubt for the simple reason that the government, as its track record makes clear, hasn't earned it.
The benefit of trust will be won back - assuming, after the Greenbelt fiasco, that matters aren't already too far gone - through durable evidence that lessons have been learned and changes in procedure made.
That means slowing down when necessary, adopting competitive bidding processes as a matter of course, and letting the public in early and often on decisions. No more surprise policy reveals with little evidence to support the looming changes.
It doesn't seem that big an expectation or too difficult a task.

Under pressure from Toronto and Quebec, Trudeau government announces $362.4 million in new refugee supports

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/under-pressure-toronto-quebec-trudeau-government/docview/2920739815/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Under+pressure+from+Toronto+and+Quebec%2C+Trudeau+government+announces+%24362.4+million+in+new+refugee+supports&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Under pressure from the City of Toronto and Quebec to foot a bigger portion of the bill for asylum seekers, the federal Liberal government announced $362.4 million in new refugee supports nationwide.
Immigration Minister Marc Miller refused to reveal how much of that will go to Toronto, saying it will be revealed in the coming days. Quebec will get $100 million of that new money, he said Wednesday at a hastily organized news conference outside the House of Commons.
Miller said all levels of government have to work to settle asylum seekers
The money will flow through the interim housing assistance program.
Other than Quebec's share, immediate breakdowns for the hardest-hit cities and regions were not available.
Still, the move represents a big shift by the federal government which up to now has resisted demands by Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow to pony up the $250 million that the city expects to spend this year to take care of refugee claimants.
The Trudeau government has repeatedly argued it already supports Toronto with billions of dollars, and sends billions more to the province which should bear more responsibility.
When Chow threatened to label an additional six per cent tax hike a "federal impacts levy" that would come on top of a city staff-proposed 10.5 per cent increase, members of Trudeau's 23-strong Toronto caucus bristled.
But Chow reached a "new deal" with Premier Doug Ford, whose government agreed to provide $200 million a year to cover shelter costs for homeless populations, with Ford and Chow joining forces to call on the federal government to cover the cost of sheltering refugees.
This is a breaking story. More to come.
CAPTION: Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Minister Marc Miller arrives to a caucus meeting on Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Jan. 31, 2024.

Canada's dental benefit is expanding. Here's what you need to know

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadas-dental-benefit-is-expanding-heres-what/docview/2920730039/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+dental+benefit+is+expanding.+Here%27s+what+you+need+to+know&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Starting Thursday, applications for Canada's dental benefit will open up to an additional group of seniors needing help with their oral health care.If you're 72 years of age and older, and you meet a list of other eligibility criteria, you could soon start receiving coverage for a number of dental services.The benefit, slated to cost $13 billion over five years, was first launched in December for Canadians aged 87 and up. On Wednesday, Citizens' Services Minister Terry Beech said about 408,000 seniors have applied for the benefit so far, clocking in at an average of 20,000 new sign-ups a day.Health Minister Mark Holland also touted the measure as the largest benefit program implemented in Canada's history. The government estimates it could capture up to nine million Canadians, though some health groups have charged that eligibility requirements around income levels leave out millions of others still deserving of aid.The program will be further expanded to other seniors, people with disabilities, and youth under 18 over the course of the year, with remaining Canadians able to access the help in 2025. The rollout is a key requirement of the Liberal-NDP deal that could see the New Democrats prop up the minority Liberals until 2025. Here's what you need to know if you're hoping to apply.I'm 72 or older. Do I qualify?Like all applicants for the program, you're eligible if you don't have access to dental insurance (through your employer, your pension, a benefits company or a family member), if your family's net income is less than $90,000, if you're a Canadian resident, and if you filed your tax return last year.If you're already covered through a provincial, territorial or other federal program, you're still eligible: your coverage will just be co-ordinated between both plans.The federal government has the ability to verify your eligibility details, so if you falsify any information, you could be kicked off the plan and forced to pay up for the services you received.How do I apply?If you qualify, you'll be sent a letter as soon as Feb. 1 from Service Canada inviting you to apply. As of May, applications will switch over to an online process. That letter will include an application code and instructions on how to apply. For seniors, the application process can be completed by phone.If your information checks out, you'll be referred to Sun Life, which will enrol you in the program and send further information, including your member ID card and the date your coverage begins.At that point, you'll be covered for services like cleanings, X-rays, fillings, root canals, dentures and extractions. Some of those procedures, however, won't be covered until this fall. The lowest-income seniors won't have to pay for procedures, but eligible Canadians at other income levels will have to contribute a co-payment.When can I start going to the dentist?You can start receiving services under the plan as early as May, though younger seniors might have to wait a little longer.That's assuming the rollout goes smoothly, which is not guaranteed.Some dental associations have raised the alarm that despite discussions with Health Canada, dentists across the country still don't have a clear picture of how the program would work for providers."Are (patients) going to be able to go to any dentist and access this program? Is it going to be simple for not just the patient, but the dentist?" said Dr. Brock Nicolucci, president of the Ontario Dental Association."You hear about health-care burnout throughout the country and dentistry is not immune to this. So who's going to be doing all that if there is an extra administration burden?"Nicolucci told the Star he's worried some providers might not be able to handle the increased strain, leaving Canadians to seek help elsewhere or forgo receiving care entirely. He said he would have "loved" to see Ottawa finalize how the program would actually work before trumpeting its launch.On Wednesday, Holland said that while he could not promise the rollout would be "perfect," he remains committed to ensuring dentists have the answers they need."We're ready imminently to be able to share information with all providers, with all dentists," Holland said."We'll be able to talk about the fruits of those negotiations very soon."
CAPTION: Minister of Health Mark Holland speaks to reporters in the foyer of the House of Commons on Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Jan. 31, 2024.

Subject: Canadians; Dental insurance; Dentists

Business indexing term: Subject: Dentists

Doug Ford, Justin Trudeau governments announces $121M in federal funding to fight auto theft, gun crimes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-justin-trudeau-governments-announces/docview/2920227715/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%2C+Justin+Trudeau+governments+announces+%24121M+in+federal+funding+to+fight+auto+theft%2C+gun+crimes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With a vehicle being stolen every 14 minutes in Ontario, Queen's Park and Ottawa are revving up measures to combat auto theft and gun crimes.
Federal Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc announced Wednesday $121 million in nationwide funding over five years to battle the criminal gangs profiting from stealing cars and smuggling guns.
"People shouldn't have to live in fear of their cars getting stolen in the middle of the night or doors being kicked in at their residence and sometimes at gunpoint," Premier Doug Ford told reporters at the joint announcement at York Regional Police headquarters in Aurora.
Ford praised Ottawa for stepping up, noting the provincial government has already earmarked $51 million over three years "to help police identify and dismantle criminal networks behind auto theft rings."
"I have a message to all the criminals, we're coming after you and we're going to catch you and you're going to jail," the premier said.
Next week, LeBlanc will host a national summit on fighting auto theft in Ottawa.
The minister said the Feb. 8 confab would be a chance for governments, police and insurance companies to share best practices and and "further coordinate joint activities."
Federal Justice Minister Arif Virani said his constituents in Parkdale-High Park "are scared" by the alarming rise in auto theft.
"That's terrifying for people," said Virani.
According to the Insurance Board of Canada, auto insurers paid out $1.2 billion in theft claims in 2022, which is triple what was paid out in 2018 and the trend continued to climb in the first half of last year.
Last fall, Ford announced new "preventing auto thefts" grant for 21 projects using modern crime-fighting tools that focus on focused on "prevention, detection, analysis and enforcement."
These included surveillance equipment and GPS tracking devices for police forces and additional training for investigators and moves designed to "break down silos and address organized criminal activity."
The Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area has become a hotbed of auto theft with gangs stealing vehicles and exporting them abroad.
CAPTION: Doug Ford praised Ottawa for stepping up during the funding announcement.

Emphasize reading and writing over math in kindergarten: letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/emphasize-reading-writing-over-math-kindergarten/docview/2920227707/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Emphasize+reading+and+writing+over+math+in+kindergarten%3A+letters&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford government to revamp full-day kindergarten curriculum, Jan. 23
As a Registered Early Childhood Educator for more than 10 years, I have seen first-hand a decrease in basic childhood skills and watched children fall through the cracks because of poor supports and wait times.
Society expects its citizens to have basic fundamental skills. Our elementary schools are in place to help children navigate life as they grow.
While I believe a majority play-based curriculum is necessary, I also think mandatory set times for direct instruction will benefit children in the long run.
However, the province needs to look at its plan a little more closely and favour reading and writing over adding in more math concepts.
The whole "here's a change we are making and it will benefit the children greatly" ideology without proper outlines or trainings set out for the educators, who this change directly impacts, is giving me pandemic déjà vu, when educators were thrown in the trenches and left to their own devices.
Education Minister Stephen Lecce please do better, not just for the children but for all educators.
Rebecca Tabone, Scarborough

Where is the outrage about the vile online threats?
Justin Trudeau adviser defends use of crude language in online exchange with right-wing group, Jan, 29


While I don't care for Supriya Dwivedi's language in her online response to Canada Proud, I applaud her gumption in giving them back a bit of their own medicine.
Now, if only the media would cover some of the vile online threats coming from groups like Canada Proud with as much outrage as Dwivedi's comments received, perhaps Canadians who are not immersed in politics, X (Twitter) or Facebook would understand where the real danger lies.
Patricia Wilmot, Toronto
If Biden orchestrated Trump's predicament, there's no way he is feeble-mindedDonald Trump is on the hook for $88.3 million in defamation damages. What happens next? Jan. 28
After just three hours, the jury awarded E. Jean Carroll $83.3 million (U.S.) in her defamation lawsuit against Donald Trump.
Trump claims that this outcome is just one part of a broader witch hunt being waged against him by President Joe Biden through a weaponized Department of Justice. Clearly there must be some truth in Trump's accusation: how can such a monumental decision be made in such a short period of time if it were not preordained?
To put Trump's accusation in context and fully appreciate the implications, we need to keep in mind all the moving parts involved in orchestrating such an outcome.
First, the case was heard in a court of law, which is governed by the third branch of the government - not the executive branch, not the congress - and the plaintiff was represented by private legal representatives, not lawyers from the Department of Justice. The jury of five men and four women was made up of public citizens, whose appointment was agreed to by the legal counsel of both sides.
But E. Jean Carroll's case is just one in the broader hunt including other civil trials as well as four criminal indictments, with 91 felony charges in all. Each of these cases involves its own collection of juries, lawyers, and courts. This is not your simple, everyday witch hunt. This is a massive mobilization of people, processes and agencies.
To organize and then successfully execute such a public-private partnership, that crosses branches of government, and swaying individual from the general public, requires some real know-how.
And yet Trump wants us to believe that Joe Biden is behind all of this; he is the mastermind of this grand scheme.
If it is true that Biden has organized all of this, then I need to reassess my opinion that he is old, frail, and in mental decline.
Bill Hertha, Thornhill
Why are so many tax breaks available for the rich?
Looking to make an RRSP contribution by Feb. 29? Maybe you shouldn't, Jan. 29
David Aston provided an insightful explanation on the pros and cons of the many "available" tax-advantaged savings programs.
I agree with his advice that the best plan for the ordinary taxpayer is to pay down their mortgage - a non-government plan.
While overtly admitting "most of us have limited funds" to use these programs, he is covertly admitting they are best suited for taxpayers who can afford to use, many, if not all, of these plans. He went to explain how these plans can benefit the wealthy.
The question to be asked is why are all these plans "available." Simply, lobbyists for the wealthy segment in our society have influenced government decisions that benefit them over others.
Shame on the wealthy for hiring these lobbyists. Shame on the government for bowing to these interests. Shame on the taxpayers who continually vote for the same political parties that ignore their interests.
Wake up and smell the withering roses.
Walter Krystia, Toronto
Renting has become too risky for small investors
Academics and urban planners have proposed many potential solutions to rectify the critical shortage of rental accommodations in major Canadian cities. Virtually all of these solutions propose building new structures with financing support from governments. Everyone I have seen will take three to five years to deliver any units.
There is a simple solution that can turn on a large number of rental units virtually overnight, all it requires is a revamping of the current tenant-landlord relationship. It is now so tilted in favour of the tenant and the dispute system so overburdened that it is no longer viable for individual homeowners or small investors to take the risk of renting.
Folks I talk to all say they would be open to converting living space in larger homes into rentals, but they all can't overcome the fear of doing the work for little or no real return on the investment.
Two of my close friends have just completed actions in civil court after the Landlord and Tenant Board failed to resolve simple issues. One case took seven years, and the other case took three years to evict deadbeat renters resulting in losses and cost exceeding $30,000 in each case.
I grew up in a family that owned a small apartment building. We lived there and it was home to five other couples. If I owed that building today, I would be looking for a buyer and get out of the rental business.
When small landlords leave the business, it opens the market to developers and large rental corporations that want to maximize rental income, reduce maintenance cost and have the resources to deal with the current broken system. Just don't expect below market rents that reward good tenants or a friendly family landlord willing to work on a break even business.
Arno Schmidt, Mississauga

Document 325 of 500

Under pressure from universities, Doug Ford says it's 'not the time' for a tuition increase

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/under-pressure-universities-doug-ford-says-not/docview/2920226916/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Under+pressure+from+universities%2C+Doug+Ford+says+it%27s+%27not+the+time%27+for+a+tuition+increase&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario college and university students won't be paying more tuition this fall, says Premier Doug Ford.
"I just don't believe this is the time to go into these students' pockets - especially the ones that are really struggling - and ask for a tuition increase," Ford said Wednesday morning.
Ford, speaking at an unrelated announcement in Aurora, was asked about a recent blue-ribbon panel looking at post-secondary finances that recommended the provincial tuition freeze be lifted and allowed to increase by five per cent for this fall.
Shortly after taking office in 2018, the Ford government slashed tuition by 10 per cent for students and has not allowed schools to raise rates since.
Universities had been hoping for some flexibility to raise fees and are also hoping for an immediate 10 per cent funding increase the panel also urged.
"We're going to work with our colleges and universities to support them any way we can and drive efficiencies throughout the system," Ford also said.
"There's some classes out there, believe it or not, that have six or seven students. That's unacceptable. So there are areas of improvement but we're always there to support our colleges and universities."
Queen's University is among those that have started to lay off staff and look at axing low enrolment classes. Its provost recently said the school could shut down if no cuts are made, though Principal Patrick Deane sent out a letter last week saying there is "no risk that Queen's in any foreseeable future will close its doors."
Ford was also asked about the recent federal announcement on a cap for international students, and he acknowledged there are some "bad actors" among private colleges.
Public colleges - which have taken on more lucrative partnerships with private ones - and universities have seen an increase in international students, who pay four or five times the tuition of domestic students. Many have done so as a way to make up for stalled government funding.
Ford also said the province didn't drop the ball in keeping international student enrolment at a reasonable rate, given the current housing crunch which has been in part blamed on the number of foreign students that has ballooned under his government.
More to come.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford says he does not want to burden students with a tuition increase this fall, despite calls from universities and colleges to allow for a fee hike to help address financial pressures.Queen's is one of 10 universities across the province running a deficit and looking to make cuts if the government doesn't allow a tuition hike or boost funding. Premier Doug Ford signalled Wednesday that he's not in favour of a tuition increase.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sikh-advocacy-group-seeks-intervener-status/docview/2920060551/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sikh+advocacy+group+seeks+intervener+status+in+inquiry%3A+World+Sikh+Organization+had+called+for+India+to+be+included+in+probe&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A prominent Sikh advocacy group wants to play a role at the foreign interference inquiry after commissioner Marie-Josée Hogue added India to the list of countries she's probing as part of her work.
The initial terms of reference for the Hogue commission named China and Russia as the states under the microscope for their actions during the 2019 and 2021 elections. But five days before the start of public hearings this week, Hogue asked the government to hand over information and documents relating to alleged interference by India as well.
Her specific reasons for doing so were not disclosed. The commission's terms allow Hogue to probe "other foreign states or non-state actors."
Sikh organizations in Canada have warned for years that India's actions weren't being taken seriously enough by the Canadian government.
In a report last spring, the B.C. and Ontario associations of gurdwaras cited numerous security briefings that explicitly named the risk of India seeking to influence the diaspora community in Canada.
"Indian officials and intelligence operatives have manufactured news, offered bribes to media outlets for favourable news coverage, amplified targeted messages to disrupt public debate, interfered in electoral processes across the country, and have attempted to manipulate Canadian policymakers," the report alleged.
"This is not a partisan issue as all parties have been impacted by Indian attempts to manipulate electoral outcomes."
Their concerns were further amplified last fall when Prime Minister Justin Trudeau revealed there was credible allegations that India was linked to the killing of a prominent Sikh leader in B.C.
The World Sikh Organization had called for India to be included in the commission's work and is pleased Hogue followed suit, the organization's legal counsel Balpreet Singh told the Star on Tuesday.
"We are looking at requesting the commission to use its discretion to grant us intervener status, given this development," Singh said.
"We are consulting with other community stakeholders and will be reaching out to the commission very shortly."
India denies it has interfered in Canadian affairs, and had initially rejected all assertions it was involved in Hardeep Singh Nijjar's death in Surrey, B.C. The refusal of the Indian government to co-operate in the investigation was cited by the Trudeau Liberals as the reason they made the allegations public. Talks between Delhi and Ottawa are ongoing.
While the formal deadline to apply for status at the commission passed in November, the commissioner can consider an application for status at any time.
Hogue was tasked last year with overseeing a public inquiry into foreign state interference in the last two elections, the latest effort by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to respond to months of media reports detailing apparent incidents of states engaging in Canadian electoral contests, but the public - and those targeted - weren't made aware until well after the fact.
Another demand for an expansion of Hogue's mandate came Tuesday from the Conservative party, which has intervener status at the inquiry.
In a letter, they requested that Hogue also include what it called Iran's campaign of intimidation, repression and interference in the scope of her work.
The party cited revelations Monday that two Canadians were charged in a plot to kill people in the U.S. who had fled Iran.
The letter also notes that in 2021, the federal body charged with election oversight said Iran was engaged in cyber activities aimed at influencing campaigns.
It also cites public statements by security officials naming Iran as an active participant in interference campaigns.
"With the many high-profile examples of Iranian interference in Canada, both in relation to our democratic processes and attempts to repress political expression in Canada, it is of the utmost importance that Iran be a subject of interest in the commission's work," says the letter, first reported by the Globe and Mail and obtained by the Star.
The Conservative party has been frustrated at being denied robust access to the proceedings; intervenors have the ability to examine exhibits entered into evidence at the commission, but none of the classified materials are available to the parties given full standing.
What can or can't be disclosed publicly over the course of the inquiry is at the heart of the first week of hearings, which on Tuesday heard from a panel of academics with extensive expertise in national security laying out what they think the challenges are in that regard.
CAPTION: Commissioner Justice Marie-Josée Hogue, who was tasked last year with overseeing a public inquiry into foreign state interference in the last two elections, can give a group intervener status at any time.Adrian Wyld THE CANADIAN PRESS
CREDIT: Stephanie Levitz Deputy Toronto Star

Document 327 of 500

Carbon price rebrand aims to improve clarity: Rebate name change may not be enough, experts say

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/carbon-price-rebrand-aims-improve-clarity/docview/2920060146/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Carbon+price+rebrand+aims+to+improve+clarity%3A+Rebate+name+change+may+not+be+enough%2C+experts+say&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal government's discussions around rebranding the rebate program for its carbon pricing system are aimed at ensuring Canadians are aware of what their rebates are for, Natural Resources Minister Jonathan Wilkinson said Tuesday.
The first public acknowledgment of a potential rebrand of the government's flagship climate policy comes as the Conservatives immediately dismissed the idea, and experts and political insiders raised questions on whether a rebrand would boost perceptions of the carbon price.
"I think the discussions around rebranding really relate to trying to ensure that it's understandable," Wilkinson said on his way into the Liberals' weekly cabinet meeting. "A lot of folks out there don't know what they're getting when they actually get the rebate."
The Star reported Monday that the federal Liberals were taking a "serious look" at how the Climate Action Incentive Payment was being branded, according to a senior government official, who was not authorized to speak on the matter.
The source said there was a multi-department effort weighing a name change for the rebate and examining how to improve labelling from banks across Canada. Any changes would likely come alongside the next quarterly rebate payment in April, they said, although discussions are in early stages and further details are unclear.
But political marketing experts and insiders who spoke to the Star, including those long associated with the Liberal party, said a rebrand may not be enough to fix the Liberals' communications on carbon pricing. At issue, they said, is a pattern of poor messaging that has not resonated with Canadians, and an opposition that has filled the vacuum and capitalized on Canadians' economic concerns to control the narrative.
Catherine McKenna, who first introduced the price on carbon as environment and climate change minister in 2018, said she had worked incessantly after introducing the policy to make sure she and MPs emphasized the rebates to Canadians. But she can't help but feel "disappointed" with how the government has since communicated the policy, she said.
"Whatever you want to call it, you have to ensure you're telling people they're getting money back."
Éric Blais, a frequent commentator on political marketing, said increased government advertising may help the Liberals, but noted Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's first-term efforts to limit partisan government advertising has hindered that possibility. The Canada Revenue Agency has not spent any money on advertising the Climate Action Incentive Payment since 2020.
He said he worries the Liberals might think a rebrand would transform negative perceptions of the policy after it has already been so successfully portrayed as a punitive tax by the Conservatives.
"If you don't brand yourself, someone else will do it for you," Blais told the Star.
Carbon pricing aims to encourage consumers and companies to produce less greenhouse gas emissions by making it more expensive. Revenue from the price on carbon is returned to Canadians through a rebate, leaving 80 per cent of those eligible with more money than they pay, according to the Parliamentary Budget Officer.
Indeed, the Conservatives quickly dismissed the idea of a rebrand as "putting lipstick on a pig."
"Now we have their big solution. They are going to give it a new name. They are going to rename the carbon tax," Poilievre said in the House of Commons on Monday. "They think people will not notice their heating bills, gas bills and grocery bills are going through the roof under this tax if they just give it a new name."
Trudeau and Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland dodged reporters' questions on the government's considerations of a rebrand on Monday, although Freeland criticized the Tories' pledge to eliminate the consumer price on carbon, saying the move would take money out of Canadians' pockets.
"That is $1,000 in Ontario and more than $1,000 in Alberta and Saskatchewan. That would hurt Canadians," Freeland said.
The office of Environment and Climate Change Minister Steven Guilbeault declined to comment Tuesday.
CREDIT: Mark Ramzy Toronto Star
