Full text: Kindergarten should prioritize reading, writing
Doug Ford government to revamp full-day kindergarten curriculum, Jan. 23
As a Registered Early Childhood Educator for more than 10 years, I have seen first-hand a decrease in basic childhood skills and watched children fall through the cracks because of poor supports and wait times.
Society expects its citizens to have basic fundamental skills. Our elementary schools are in place to help children navigate life as they grow.
While I believe a majority play-based curriculum is necessary, I also think mandatory set times for direct instruction will benefit children in the long run.
However, the province needs to look at its plan a little more closely and favour reading and writing over adding in more math concepts.
The whole "here's a change we are making and it will benefit the children greatly" ideology without proper outlines or training set out for the educators, who this change directly impacts, is giving me pandemic déjà vu, when educators were thrown in the trenches and left to their own devices.
Education Minister Stephen Lecce please do better, for the children and for all educators.
Rebecca Tabone, Scarborough
Why are there so many tax breaks open to the rich?
Looking to make an RRSP contribution by Feb. 29? Maybe you shouldn't, Jan. 29
David Aston provided an insightful explanation on the pros and cons of the many "available" tax-advantaged savings programs.
I agree with his advice that the best plan for the ordinary taxpayer is to pay down their mortgage - a non-government plan.
While overtly admitting "most of us have limited funds" to use these programs, he is covertly admitting they are best suited for taxpayers who can afford to use, many, if not all, of these plans. He explains how these plans can benefit the wealthy.
The question to be asked is why are all these plans "available." Simply, lobbyists for the wealthy segment in our society have influenced government decisions that benefit them over others.
Shame on the wealthy for hiring these lobbyists. Shame on the government for bowing to these interests. Shame on the taxpayers who continually vote for the same political parties that ignore their interests.
Walter Krystia, Toronto
Renting is now too risky for small investors
Academics and urban planners have proposed many potential solutions to rectify the critical shortage of rental accommodations in major Canadian cities. Virtually all of these solutions propose building new structures with financing support from governments. Every one I have seen will take three to five years to deliver any units.
There is a simple solution that can turn on a large number of rental units virtually overnight, all it requires is a revamping of the current tenant-landlord relationship. It is now so tilted in favour of the tenant and the dispute system so overburdened that it is no longer viable for individual homeowners or small investors to take the risk of renting.
Folks I talk to all say they'd be open to converting living space in larger homes into rentals, but can't overcome the fear of doing the work for little or no real return on the investment.
Two of my close friends have just completed actions in civil court after the Landlord and Tenant Board failed to resolve simple issues. One case took seven years, and the other took three years to evict deadbeat renters resulting in losses and cost exceeding $30,000.
I grew up in a family that owned a small apartment building. We lived there and it was home to five other couples. If I owned that building today, I'd be looking for a buyer and get out of the rental business.
When small landlords exit, it opens the market to developers and large rental corporations that want to maximize rental income, reduce maintenance cost and have the resources to deal with the current broken system. Just don't expect below market rents that reward good tenants or a friendly family landlord willing to work on a break-even business.
Arno Schmidt, Mississauga
We should be outraged by vile online threats
Justin Trudeau adviser defends use of crude language in online exchange with right-wing group, Jan, 29
While I don't care for Supriya Dwivedi's language in her online response to Canada Proud, I applaud her gumption in giving them back a bit of their own medicine.
Now, if only the media would cover some of the vile online threats coming from groups like Canada Proud with as much outrage as Dwivedi's comments received, perhaps Canadians who are not immersed in politics, X (Twitter) or Facebook would understand where the real danger lies.
Patricia Wilmot, Toronto
Trump's claims about Biden are contradictory
Donald Trump is on the hook for $88.3 million in defamation damages. What happens next? Jan. 28
After just three hours, the jury awarded E. Jean Carroll $83.3 million (U.S.) in her defamation lawsuit against Donald Trump.
He claims that this outcome is just one part of a broader witch hunt being waged against him by U.S. President Joe Biden through a weaponized Department of Justice. Clearly there must be some truth in Trump's accusation: how can such a monumental decision be made in such a short period of time if it were not preordained?
First, the case was heard in a court of law, which is governed by the third branch of the government - not the executive branch, not Congress - and the plaintiff was represented by private legal representatives, not lawyers from the Department of Justice. The jury of five men and four women was made up of public citizens, whose appointment was agreed to by the legal counsel of both sides.
But Carroll's case is just one in the broader hunt including other civil trials as well as four criminal indictments, with 91 felony charges in all. Each of these cases involves its own collection of juries, lawyers, and courts. This is not your simple, everyday witch hunt. This is a massive mobilization of people, processes and agencies.
To organize and then successfully execute such a public-private partnership, that crosses branches of government, and swaying individual from the general public, requires some real know-how.
And yet Trump wants us to believe that Joe Biden is behind all of this; he is the mastermind of this grand scheme.
If it is true that Biden has organized all of this, then I need to reassess my opinion that he is old, frail, and in mental decline.
Bill Hertha, Thornhill

Document 329 of 500

Province eyes plan to revamp Pickering reactors: Costly project raises questions as price of green energy falls

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-eyes-plan-revamp-pickering-reactors/docview/2920057553/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+eyes+plan+to+revamp+Pickering+reactors%3A+Costly+project+raises+questions+as+price+of+green+energy+falls&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario will seek approval to refurbish four aging 1980s Candu nuclear reactors at Pickering - a decade-long, multibillion-dollar project aimed at milking another 30 years of electricity from the power plant.
The move by Energy Minister Todd Smith is rekindling the debate over nuclear versus renewable energy such as wind turbines and solar panels as the best technology heading into a carbon-free future to fight climate change.
Smith admitted Tuesday there is no precise cost estimate for the nuclear refurbishment - at least not one he is willing to reveal - because design and engineering work is continuing.
"It would be irresponsible at this point in time to put a number out there," he said at the plant.
Smith's decision comes 15 years after a refurbishment at the nuclear generating station first slated to close in 2018 was rejected as too expensive by a previous Liberal government following a detailed feasibility study.
But amid soaring demand for electricity and looming federal regulations requiring carbon-free emissions from power plants, Pickering could continue producing enough power for two million homes as Ontario grows "quickly," Smith said.
"With all that growth comes new energy needs," he added. "Our province still needs this station and its workers."
He dismissed calls for the province to take the billions it will cost to refurbish Pickering and develop more wind and solar generation as the world increasingly shifts to renewable sources of electricity.
"This is the responsible, pragmatic move forward," Smith insisted of the nuclear route, citing the need for baseload power as the province becomes home to two new massive electric vehicle battery plants, clean steel manufacturing, an increasing number of electric vehicles and a surging population.
But environmental groups begged to differ, noting the lack of a firm price tag and the age of the plant and its radioactive waste that must be stored indefinitely.
"Since the idea of rebuilding Pickering was abandoned ... the price of wind and solar power with battery storage has dropped like a stone," said Keith Stewart, senior energy strategist at Greenpeace.
"We should be investing in the renewable-powered energy system of the future, rather than pouring billions more into rebuilding nuclear reactors long past their best-before date."
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner said "it makes no sense for the government to pour billions into keeping it (Pickering) operational when lower-cost, cleaner solutions are available."
Smith said Ontario's Independent Electricity System Operator, which manages day-to-day needs of the electrical grid, has determined it would take almost four times as many wind turbines in the province plus more battery storage to equal the guaranteed output from the Pickering plant's "small footprint" on the shore of Lake Ontario.
"It would be 18,000 megawatts of wind and 2,000 megawatts of battery ... we have 5,000 installed megawatts of wind in our grid right now."
Ontario gets just over half its electricity from nuclear power, providing carbon-free energy in a global race to eliminate the use of fossil fuels.
Approval for the refurbishment of the four reactors known as Pickering B is required from the Canadian Nuclear Safety Commission. The four original Pickering A reactors that began operating in 1971 are being retired at the end of this year.
The safety commission is already considering an extension of Pickering B's operating licence by one year to September 2026, months after the next provincial election. Crews would begin the 11-year refurbishment after that if approval is granted.
Premier Doug Ford's government has been considering the refurbishment plan by Crown-owned Ontario Power Generation since 2022, with OPG dusting off and updating its 2009 study.
It was widely expected the government would back the plan revived two years ago - just two years after Ford's government said Pickering would be shut down in 2025.
New Democrat MPP Peter Tabuns (Toronto-Danforth) called on Smith to release all relevant financial details as the government proceeds with $2 billion in preparatory work for the refurbishment in a bet that approval will be granted.
"We can't assess this project without those details ... we need to have a serious conversation about how we can ensure all Ontarians have access to reliable and affordable energy," said Tabuns, his party's energy critic.
Warning nuclear power is not a "silver bullet," the lobby group Clean Energy Canada said costs of wind and solar power are expected to further decline as much as 40 per cent by 2035.
"Renewables are set to be highly cost-competitive," said Rachel Doran, the group's vice-president of policy and strategy.
Nuclear plants at Darlington, east of Toronto, and Bruce, on Lake Huron, are currently being refurbished. The price tag at Darlington, for example, is $12.8 billion for four reactors.
While no estimate was provided for the Pickering project, it would create about 11,000 jobs annually until its completion in the mid-2030s and sustain 6,400 jobs a year once the plant resumes producing 2,000 megawatts of electricity, Smith said.
That refurbishment experience and a veteran workforce of crews can be applied at Pickering, said Ontario Power Generation president Ken Hartwick, pledging to meet deadlines and budgets.
"We have learned a lot about what it takes."
The province is also building a small modular nuclear reactor at Darlington, near Oshawa, with a completion date of 2028. It will provide 300 megawatts of power, one-third of the 900 megawatts produced by each of the four full-scale reactors at the neighbouring Darlington plant.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Nuclear reactors; Solar energy; Electricity; Carbon; Electric vehicles; Employment; Nuclear power plants; Cost estimates

Document 330 of 500

Colleges, universities warn of 'long-lasting consequences' from international student cap

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/colleges-universities-warn-long-lasting/docview/2920279488/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Colleges%2C+universities+warn+of+%27long-lasting+consequences%27+from+international+student+cap&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Colleges and universities are urging the federal government to continue processing international study permits and modify the hard cap on students intake, warning of the "widespread and long-lasting consequences" this may cause.
"Faced with uncertainty, these students are likely to choose alternative destinations, posing a significant risk to Canada's post-secondary sector and hindering our efforts to attract global talent for years to come," Universities Canada and Colleges and Institutes Canada said in a joint letter to Immigration Minister Marc Miller on Tuesday.
"The policy's impact extends far beyond institutions, affecting businesses and communities."
Last week, Miller announced a plan to slash the number of new study permits issued across Canada this year by 35 per cent from last year's level, to 364,000, while leaving the number of applicants accepted in master's and doctoral programs, as well as those admitted to primary and secondary schools, uncapped.
The minister also immediately imposed a requirement for all study permit applicants to attach an attestation letter issued by individual provinces in their applications to be considered. However, other than Quebec, none of the provincial governments currently have such a process in place to verify admissions and issue such a letter.
This new requirement has essentially ground the study permit processing to a halt, with some applicants already receiving refusals as a result.
"Our foremost concern is the moratorium on processing new international student study permits, especially for college and undergraduate students," said the letter by the industry groups, which, combined, represent 234 post-secondary institutions. "This sudden halt while provinces establish attestation systems is already impacting enrolments."
The international student program has been under growing scrutiny in the past few years, criticized by observers as a cash cow for the financially strapped post-secondary sector, and touted by unscrupulous agents as a shortcut for permanent residence in Canada. Through their spending and tuition, international students contribute $22 billion to the Canadian economy and support 200,000 jobs in the country.
The number of study-permit holders in Canada has tripled in the past decade, from 300,000 in 2013 to 900,000 last year. However, a growing number of students have complained about abuse and exploitation by employers and landlords and that the experience in Canada has not lived up to their expectations.
Universities Canada and Colleges and Institutes Canada said international students' contributions are vital in sustaining local labour markets, increasing diversity and meeting the demands of high-growth sectors.
"The potential consequences are difficult to overstate," they said in their letter. "The cap will have far-reaching effects on Canadian communities, given that international students play a pivotal role in bolstering the economy."
They urged the immigration minister to delay the provincial attestation letter requirement until at least March 31 or until the provinces establish the required process, while requesting "urgent consultations" to modify the cap policy, clarify the "many outstanding questions" and mitigate the negative consequences of the changes.
"As the national voices of not-for-profit higher education institutions, we emphasize our willingness to collaborate on program integrity and student well-being," said the letter signed by Pari Johnston and Philip Landon, the heads of the colleges' and universities' associations respectively.
"The global reputation of Canada as a top education destination is at stake, and careful consideration is crucial to prevent lasting consequences."
Last Friday, the Ontario government also belatedly responded to the federal changes to the international student program by unveiling new measures meant to "protect students and improve the integrity of Ontario's post-secondary education."
However, Premier Doug Ford's government has yet to commit to boosting post-secondary funding as recommended last November by a blue-ribbon panel it created to review the funding situations of public colleges and universities.
The report noted that in 2021-22, Ontario's funding per college student was $6,891 - 44 per cent of the figure for the rest of Canada ($15,615). Universities received $11,471 per student, which represented 57 per cent of the figure for the rest of Canada ($20,772). More than half of Canada's international students go to school in the province.
CAPTION: Minister of Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Marc Miller speaks to the media during the federal cabinet retreat in Montreal on Monday, Jan. 22, 2024.Canada's immigration department is denying Mexico's claim that a pact between the two countries is already curbing asylum claims in Canada, as the minister faces mounting pressure to reimpose visas for Mexican citizens.THE CANADIAN PRESS/Christinne MuschiA blunt cap on international students "would have a negative impact on Canada's reputation, Canada's ability to grow, and our ability to attract top talent into the country," said Philip Landon, president and CEO of Universities Canada.NA-INTERNATIONAL-STUDENTS-30 Pari Johnston, CEO and President of Colleges and Institutes Canada Uploaded by: NICHOLAS KEUNG

Document 331 of 500

Justin Trudeau's government may rebrand its carbon pricing program. But is it too late?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeaus-government-may-rebrand-carbon/docview/2920278737/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau%27s+government+may+rebrand+its+carbon+pricing+program.+But+is+it+too+late%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - The federal government's discussions around rebranding the rebate program for its carbon pricing system are aimed at ensuring Canadians are aware of what their rebates are for, Natural Resources Minister Jonathan Wilkinson said Tuesday.
The first public acknowledgment of a potential rebrand of the government's flagship climate policy comes as the Conservatives immediately dismissed the idea, and experts and political insiders raised questions on whether a rebrand would boost perceptions of the carbon price.
"I think the discussions around rebranding really relate to trying to ensure that it's understandable," Wilkinson said on his way into the Liberals' weekly cabinet meeting. "A lot of folks out there don't know what they're getting when they actually get the rebate."
The Star reported Monday that the federal Liberals were taking a "serious look" at how the Climate Action Incentive Payment was being branded, according to a senior government official, who was not authorized to speak on the matter.
The source said there was a multi-department effort weighing a name change for the rebate and examining how to improve labelling from banks across Canada. Any changes would likely come alongside the next quarterly rebate payment in April, they said, although discussions are in early stages and further details are unclear.
But political marketing experts and insiders who spoke to the Star, including those long associated with the Liberal party, said a rebrand may not be enough to fix the Liberals' communications on carbon pricing. At issue, they said, is a pattern of poor messaging that has not resonated with Canadians, and an opposition that has filled the vacuum and capitalized on Canadians' economic concerns to control the narrative.
Catherine McKenna, who first introduced the price on carbon as environment and climate change minister in 2018, said she had worked incessantly after introducing the policy to make sure she and MPs emphasized the rebates to Canadians. But she can't help but feel "disappointed" with how the government has since communicated the policy, she said.
"Whatever you want to call it, you have to ensure you're telling people they're getting money back," she said, calling for an "all-MP effort and "constant communications."
Éric Blais, a frequent commentator on political marketing, said increased government advertising may help the Liberals, but noted Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's first-term efforts to limit partisan government advertising has hindered that possibility. The Canada Revenue Agency has not spent any money on advertising the Climate Action Incentive Payment since 2020.
He said he worries the Liberals might think a rebrand would transform negative perceptions of the policy after it has already been so successfully portrayed as a punitive tax by the Conservatives.
"If you don't brand yourself, someone else will do it for you," Blais told the Star. "That's exactly what happened here."
Carbon pricing aims to encourage consumers and companies to produce less greenhouse gas emissions by making it more expensive. Revenue from the price on carbon is returned to Canadians through a rebate, leaving 80 per cent of those eligible with more money than they pay, according to the Parliamentary Budget Officer.
Indeed, the Conservatives quickly dismissed the idea of a rebrand as "putting lipstick on a pig."
"Now we have their big solution. They are going to give it a new name. They are going to rename the carbon tax," Poilievre said in the House of Commons on Monday. "They think people will not notice their heating bills, gas bills and grocery bills are going through the roof under this tax if they just give it a new name."
"Taking money out of the pockets of Canadians rebranded is still taking money out of their pockets," Conservative deputy leader Melissa Lantsman said Tuesday.
Trudeau and Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland dodged reporters' questions on the government's considerations of a rebrand on Monday, although Freeland criticized the Tories' pledge to eliminate the consumer price on carbon, saying the move would take money out of Canadians' pockets.
"The Conservatives want to take carbon price rebate cheques out of the bank accounts of Canadian families," Freeland said in the House of Commons. "That is $1,000 in Ontario and more than $1,000 in Alberta and Saskatchewan. That would hurt Canadians."
Exclusive polling for the Star by Abacus Data suggests Canadians are split on whether carbon pricing was a good policy, even in Liberal strongholds such as Ontario and the Atlantic Provinces. And while 72 per cent of those eligible for the Climate Action Incentive Payment reported being aware of the rebate, only 48 per cent knew it had to do with carbon pricing.
The office of Environment and Climate Change Minister Steven Guilbeault declined to comment Tuesday.
CAPTION: Energy and Natural Resources Minister Jonathan Wilkinson speaks during question period in the House of Commons on Parliament Hill in Ottawa.

Document 332 of 500

Scan your documents through a Chromebook® with the right scanner

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/scan-your-documents-through-chromebook®-with/docview/2920277805/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Scan+your+documents+through+a+Chromebook%26reg%3B+with+the+right+scanner&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ricoh ScanSnap document scanners made a name for themselves on Windows PCs and Macs, and now it's time for Chromebooks to get in on the action as well.
Simply put, select Ricoh ScanSnap devices - chiefly the iX100, iX1300, iX1500 and iX1600 - are now compatible with Chromebooks. More specifically, the compatibility is with ChromeOS, the operating system Chromebooks run on.
It's a unique mix of a desktop application with a focus on cloud-based access - all done via your Wi-Fi connection.
How the process works
There are nuances involved here that differ from Windows or macOS but the similarities actually outweigh the distinctions. For example, when you go to the Google Play Store on any Chromebook, the app you're looking for is ScanSnap Home, which is the same desktop app as on a PC or Mac. At first, ScanSnap Home on a Chromebook may look like an oversized phone app, but you have the option to expand it to cover the whole screen.
So long as the ScanSnap iX1600 is on the same Wi-Fi network as the Chromebook, it's easy enough to look for available scanners and select the iX1600 (or another compatible model) from the list. From start to finish, you should be ready to scan documents in mere minutes. But before doing that, it's best to set everything up, including logging in with your ScanSnap Cloud account to streamline access to the cloud services you want documents to go to.
Default settings largely mirror those of other platforms, and features such as duplex, auto deskew, auto page size detection, auto colour detection and blank page removal are all available.
Due to resource limitations on Chromebooks, real-time Optical Character Recognition (OCR) cannot be performed locally, however PDF's can be OCR'd after the fact by ScanSnap Cloud.Once complete, your PDFs will be fully text searchable which makes it much easier to search for any particular words within scanned documents.
Reaching for the cloud
Scanned documents appear under "Data" in the menu on the bottom row. From there, you can also see which documents are locally stored or saved to the cloud. Before they can even get to the cloud, however, you need to set up their location in the "Cloud Services" menu.
Ricoh scanners make this pretty granular, meaning you can opt to send business cards to one cloud service while receipts go to another. Hence, when you load a mix of documents, photos, business cards and receipts into the scanner they just go where they're supposed to. For example, you could have it set up to send your documents to Dropbox®, your photos to Google Photos, your business cards to Evernote® and your receipts to Quickbooks®. Once set up, your documents will automatically go to the desired cloud service regardless of whether you're scanning from your Chromebook, PC or Mac.You can also set up a local file system on your Chromebook to send scanned files wherever you please.
A new feature for ScanSnap Home is the ability to use the camera on your phone to capture an image and upload it to your preferred cloud service via ScanSnap Cloud.Simply launch the ScanSnap Home app on your smartphone and use your camera to 'scan' a document to ScanSnap Cloud.NOTE:ScanSnap Cloud routes your images to your preferred cloud service such as Google Drive, OneDrive, Evernote, Dropbox and many others.
Quality and control
With the iX1600, you can scan documents and images at 300dpi, 400dpi or 600dpi. It's great for receipts, invoices and business cards.The included image-enhancing features in the scanner can help pull out more legible text from faded documents as well. Duplexing scans both sides of any document without bleed-through, so that text and images from the other side don't "bleed" onto the scanned page.
Although the ScanSnap iX1600 isn't designated as a pure photo scanner, it can scan photos and brochures with impressive precision and colour accuracy. Chromebooks don't have all the same apps you would find on a PC or Mac, including those for editing photos, though there are plenty of alternative options available in the Play Store.
Browser-based apps have long been the backbone for ChromeOS, with the cloud as a key part of the platform. The process to scan documents with the iX1600 on a Chromebook proves to be pretty fluid and straightforward from start to finish, offering another avenue to organize various documents.
RICOH and the RICOH logo are registered trademarks of Ricoh Company, Ltd. Chromebook is the registered trademark of Google LLC. Quickbooks is the registered trademark of Intuit Inc. All other trademarks are the property of their respective owners with all rights reserved.

Sikh advocacy group seeks role at Canada's inquiry into foreign interference

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/sikh-advocacy-group-seeks-role-at-canadas-inquiry/docview/2920277675/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sikh+advocacy+group+seeks+role+at+Canada%27s+inquiry+into+foreign+interference&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - A prominent Sikh advocacy group wants to play a role at the foreign interference inquiry after commissioner Marie-Josée Hogue added India to the list of countries she's probing as part of her work.
The initial terms of reference for the Hogue commission named China and Russia as the states under the microscope for their actions during the 2019 and 2021 elections. But five days before the start of public hearings this week, Hogue asked the government to hand over information and documents relating to alleged interference by India as well.
Her specific reasons for doing so were not disclosed. The commission's terms allow Hogue to probe "other foreign states or non-state actors."
Sikh organizations in Canada have warned for years that India's actions weren't being taken seriously enough by the Canadian government.
In a report last spring, the B.C. and Ontario associations of gurdwaras cited numerous security briefings that explicitly named the risk of India seeking to influence the diaspora community in Canada.
"Indian officials and intelligence operatives have manufactured news, offered bribes to media outlets for favourable news coverage, amplified targeted messages to disrupt public debate, interfered in electoral processes across the country, and have attempted to manipulate Canadian policymakers," the report alleged.
"This is not a partisan issue as all parties have been impacted by Indian attempts to manipulate electoral outcomes."
Their concerns were further amplified last fall when Prime Minister Justin Trudeau revealed there was credible allegations that India was linked to the killing of a prominent Sikh leader in B.C.
The World Sikh Organization had called for India to be included in the commission's work and is pleased Hogue followed suit, the organization's legal counsel Balpreet Singh told the Star on Tuesday.
"We are looking at requesting the commission to use its discretion to grant us intervener status, given this development," Singh said.
"We are consulting with other community stakeholders and will be reaching out to the commission very shortly."
India denies it has interfered in Canadian affairs, and had initially rejected all assertions it was involved in Hardeep Singh Nijjar's death in Surrey, B.C. The refusal of the Indian government to co-operate in the investigation was cited by the Trudeau Liberals as the reason they made the allegations public. Talks between Delhi and Ottawa are ongoing.
While the formal deadline to apply for status at the commission passed in November, the commissioner can consider an application for status at any time.
Hogue was tasked last year with overseeing a public inquiry into foreign state interference in the last two elections, the latest effort by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to respond to months of media reports detailing apparent incidents of states engaging in Canadian electoral contests, but the public - and those targeted - weren't made aware until well after the fact.
Another demand for an expansion of Hogue's mandate came Tuesday from the Conservative party, which has intervener status at the inquiry.
In a letter, they requested that Hogue also include what it called Iran's campaign of intimidation, repression and interference in the scope of her work.
The party cited revelations Monday that two Canadians were charged in a plot to kill people in the U.S. who had fled Iran.
The letter also notes that in 2021, the federal body charged with election oversight said Iran was engaged in cyber activities aimed at influencing campaigns. It also cites public statements by security officials naming Iran as an active participant in interference campaigns.
"With the many high-profile examples of Iranian interference in Canada, both in relation to our democratic processes and attempts to repress political expression in Canada, it is of the utmost importance that Iran be a subject of interest in the commission's work," says the letter, first reported by the Globe and Mail and obtained by the Star.
The Conservative party has been frustrated at being denied robust access to the proceedings; intervenors have the ability to examine exhibits entered into evidence at the commission, but none of the classified materials available to the parties given full standing.
What can or can't be disclosed publicly over the course of the inquiry is at the heart of the first week of hearings, which on Tuesday heard from a panel of academics with extensive expertise in national security laying out what they think the challenges are in that regard.
CAPTION: Commissioner Justice Marie-Josée Hogue waits behind a curtain on Tuesday before entering the public inquiry looking into foreign interference in federal elections.

Popularity of MAID reveals society's failings: letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/popularity-maid-reveals-societys-failings-letters/docview/2919810690/se-2?accountid=15090

Full text: Surge in medically assisted deaths under Canada's MAID program outpaces every other country, Jan 27
It is very concerning to learn that more Canadians are expected to consider medically assisted death (MAID) this March, when one-in-five Canadians who suffer from mental disorder as an underlying condition will become eligible for MAID.
This sad statistic will soon balloon as 20 per cent of Canadians have mental health disorders. Canada is poised to be the global MAID leader next year. Canada is already the fastest-growing adopter in history among the 11 countries where MAID, the controversial end-of-life treatment, exists.
The head of psychiatry at Sunnybrook Hospital, Dr. Sonu Gaind, has sounded his valid concern about what the expected death surge from MAID "says about our society."
It is time we had a serious reconsideration about how Canadians should cope with this coming MAID catastrophe.
Rudy Fernandes, Global Health Strategy Inc., Mississauga
Please allow me the right to die with help if I wish
Surge in medically assisted deaths under Canada's MAID program outpaces every other country, Jan 27
My brother committed suicide after years of intolerable mental pain. He died alone and that will hurt forever. The criticism of the speed with which Canada's MAID caught up with other countries is confusing. Would Canada not have benefitted from their findings?
The argument that mental health care should be improved before MAID's criteria is extended condemns people like my brother to misery or their own life-ending solutions while politicians argue and needed funding does not appear.
Dr. Robin Stuart is right about fear mongering. She is an expert, a professional, a group that does not fare well in this age of misinformation. Living in soiled diapers, lost in the terror of a confused mind like my mother was forced to live is beyond cruel.
We are kinder to our pets. While of sound mind, I should be able to legally set the criteria that would make my life intolerable.
My greatest fear is the evangelical agenda that wishes to impose a belief I and many do not share. Please allow me the right to die with help if I wish.
Rochelle Hatton, Sudbury

Compensation required to rebuild teacher's aide's life'Untrue, contrived, and unworthy of belief': Police failed to verify 'fabricated' sex assault claim against GTA teacher's aide, judge rules, Jan. 29
Reading Wendy Gillis' report on Ashley Jansen brought tears to my eyes, especially reading that she was temporarily prohibited from being alone with her own children, and all this, because a 12-year-old falsely accused her.
This could have happened to me. I am now a retired Early Childhood Educator.
Some children unfortunately, come from extremely sad homes, where lack of respect is the norm. I was disrespected on several occasions, being kicked in the shin, which took a long time to heal and being called "white scum," but that is nothing compared to what Jansen is suffering through.
I hope she will be compensated for lost wages, and reinstated to her teaching position, and that her son will be able to get the treatment he needs. Hopefully, a "Go Fund Me" can be started.
Elizabeth Harmelink, Mississauga

The problem is not the result of one thing. There are many contributing factors, as pointed out by Cohn. If the problem is to be solved, all these factors must be acknowledged and dealt with. Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre would have us believe that there is only one cause. If that is his view, he will not fix the problem. Either he lacks the ability to fully understand the situation, or he understands it but prefers to misstate the facts and knowingly mislead the public. Whichever the case, the result is that Poilievre's approach would fail.
Maria Pede, Woodbridge
Plenty of bad actors to blame for use of Emergencies ActTrudeau government's use of Emergencies Act during 'Freedom Convoy' violated Canadians' Charter rights: Federal Court, Jan. 23
The judge's ruling that the use of the Emergencies Act violated Charter Rights was technically the correct interpretation of the law. Sadly, it may have the negative effect of encouraging further actions of this type.
Public roads and streets were cut off virtually holding local residents' hostage. These were the jurisdiction of the Ontario and local governments. Roads leading to the international border crossing were provincial roads. The Conservative governments of Ontario, Alberta and Saskatchewan gladly turned the blame on the federal government. Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre shared a beverage with the protesters sitting on the bumper of a tractor trailer.
A few of those protesters, mainly the organizers, have been charged. A few others were handed tickets by local police for trespassing on private property, publicly relieving themselves. Others disturbed the peace by constantly chanting and blowing air horns from their trucks. Some are said to have used intimidation to have business establishments provide them with toilet facilities and free food and drink. Laws were being broken, and the local governments - including Doug Ford's government - stood by, gladly handing over the messy task to the federal authorities.
I would advise the Fed bashers to take a good look in the mirror when assigning blame in this matter.
Richard Murri, Niagara Falls
Olive oil in coffee sounds dreadfulStarbucks' olive oil coffee comes to Canada this week. Should you have a cup? Jan. 29

She would invite guests to join her in a shot of olive oil. In all the Italian cooking shows I have watched over the years, I never saw someone imbibing coffee mixed with olive oil.
The concept sounds dreadful, but I guess there is one born every minute.
Catherine Hetu, Merlin, Ont.

Note that the theatres doing well - Crow's, Mirvish, and Stratford - are offering a combination of entertainment first, and then thought-provoking material.

Document 335 of 500

Ontario seeks approval to refurbish 4 Pickering nuclear reactors

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-seeks-approval-refurbish-4-pickering/docview/2919808157/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+seeks+approval+to+refurbish+4+Pickering+nuclear+reactors&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario will seek approval to refurbish four aging 1980s Candu nuclear reactors at Pickering - a decade-long project aimed at milking another 30 years of electricity from the power plant.
The move comes 15 years after a refurbishment at the nuclear generating station first slated to close in 2018 was rejected as too expensive by a previous Liberal government following a detailed feasibility study.
But amid soaring demand for electricity and looming federal regulations requiring carbon-free emissions from power plants, Pickering could continue producing enough power for two million homes as Ontario grows "quickly," Energy Minister Todd Smith said Tuesday.
"With all that growth comes new energy needs," Smith said at the plant on the shore of Lake Ontario just east of Toronto. "Our province still needs this station and its workers."
Approval for the multibillion refurbishment for the four reactors known as Pickering B is required from the Canadian Nuclear Safety Commission. The four original Pickering A reactors that began operating in 1971 are being retired at the end of this year.
The safety commission is already considering an extension of Pickering B's operating licence by one year to September 2026, four years after the next provincial election. Crews would begin the 11-year refurbishment after that if approval is granted.
Premier Doug Ford's government has been considering the refurbishment plan by Crown-owned Ontario Power Generation since 2022, with OPG dusting off and updating its 2009 study.
It was widely expected the government would back the plan despite opposition from proponents of renewable sources of energy - such as solar and wind, which are falling in price - who warn more nuclear could force electricity prices up.
Nuclear plants at Darlington and Bruce on Lake Huron are currently being refurbished. The price tag at Darlington, for example, is $12 billion. No budget was given for the Pickering project, which would create about 11,000 jobs annually until its completion in the mid-2030s and sustain 6,400 jobs a year once the plant resumes producing.
That experience and a veteran workforce of refurbishment crews can be applied at Pickering, said Ontario Power Generation president Ken Hartwick.
"We have learned a lot about what it takes."
CAPTION: Energy Minister Todd Smith addresses a news conference at the Pickering nuclear generating station in Pickering, Ont. on Jan.30, 2024.

Subject: Nuclear reactors; Electricity; Nuclear power plants

Business indexing term: Subject: Nuclear power plants

Full text: Popularity of MAID reveals society's failings
Surge in medically assisted deaths under Canada's MAID program outpaces every other country, Jan. 27
More Canadians are expected to consider medical assistance in dying (MAID) this March, when one-in-five Canadians who suffer from mental disorder as an underlying condition will become eligible for MAID.
This sad statistic will balloon as 20 per cent of Canadians have mental health disorders. Canada is poised to be the global MAID leader next year and is already the fastest-growing adopter in history among the 11 countries where MAID exists.
The head of psychiatry at Sunnybrook Hospital, Dr. Sonu Gaind, has sounded his valid concern about what the expected death surge from MAID "says about our society."
It is time we had a serious reconsideration about how Canadians should cope with this coming MAID catastrophe.
Rudy Fernandes, Global Health Strategy Inc., Mississauga
Please allow me the right to die with help if I wish
Surge in medically assisted deaths under Canada's MAID program outpaces every other country, Jan. 27
My brother committed suicide after years of intolerable mental pain. He died alone and that will hurt forever.
The criticism of the speed with which Canada's MAID caught up with other countries is confusing. Would Canada not have benefited from their findings?
The argument that mental health care should be improved before MAID's criteria is extended condemns people like my brother to misery or their own life-ending solutions while politicians argue and needed funding does not appear.
Dr. Robin Stuart is right about fear mongering. She is an expert, a professional, a group that does not fare well in this age of misinformation. Living in soiled diapers, lost in the terror of a confused mind like my mother was forced to live is beyond cruel.
We are kinder to our pets. While of sound mind, I should be able to legally set the criteria that would make my life intolerable. Please allow me the right to die with help if I wish.
Rochelle Hatton, Sudbury
Compensation required to rebuild aide's life
'Untrue, contrived, and unworthy of belief': Police failed to verify 'fabricated' sex assault claim against GTA teacher's aide, judge rules, Jan. 29
Reading Wendy Gillis' report on Ashley Jansen brought tears to my eyes, especially reading that she was temporarily prohibited from being alone with her own children, and all this, because a 12-year-old falsely accused her.
As a retired early childhood educator, this could have happened to me. I hope she is compensated for lost wages, reinstated to her teaching position, and that her son will be able to get the treatment he needs.
Elizabeth Harmelink, Mississauga
Many factors led to 'puppy mill' colleges creation
Blame Doug Ford for turning a blind eye to student immigration abuses, Jan. 26
I commend Martin Regg Cohn on his article concerning foreign students and "puppy mills." He states "There's plenty of blame to go around - federal and provincial, Liberal and Tory, public and private, educators and entrepreneurs."
The problem is not the result of one thing. There are many contributing factors, as pointed out by Cohn. If the problem is to be solved, all these factors must be acknowledged and dealt with. Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre would have us believe that there is only one cause. If that is his view, he will not fix the problem. Either he lacks the ability to fully understand the situation, or he understands it but prefers to misstate the facts and knowingly mislead the public. Whichever the case, the result is that Poilievre's approach would fail.
Maria Pede, Woodbridge
Many are to blame for use of Emergencies Act
Trudeau government's use of Emergencies Act during 'Freedom Convoy' violated Canadians' Charter rights: Federal Court, Jan. 23
The judge's ruling that the use of the Emergencies Act violated Charter Rights was technically the correct interpretation of the law. Sadly, it may have the negative effect of encouraging further actions of this type.
Public roads and streets were cut off virtually holding local residents' hostage. These were the jurisdiction of the Ontario and local governments. Roads leading to the international border crossing were provincial roads. The Conservative governments of Ontario, Saskatchewan and Alberta gladly turned the blame on Ottawa. Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre shared a beverage with the protesters sitting on the bumper of a tractor trailer.
A few of those protesters, mainly the organizers, have been charged. A few others were handed tickets by local police for trespassing on private property, publicly relieving themselves. Others disturbed the peace by constantly chanting and blowing air horns from their trucks. Some are said to have used intimidation to have business establishments provide them with toilet facilities and free food and drink. Laws were being broken, and the local governments - including Doug Ford's - stood by, gladly handing over the messy task to the federal authorities.
I'd advise Fed bashers to take a good look in the mirror when assigning blame in this matter.
Richard Murri, Niagara Falls
Olive oil in coffee sounds dreadful
Starbucks' olive oil coffee comes to Canada this week. Should you have a cup? Jan. 29
The only person I ever saw chugging olive oil was Maria Loi on the PBS show, "The Life of Loi: Mediterranean Secrets" and she was Greek. I never saw her ever put it in her coffee. She'd invite guests to join her in a shot of olive oil. In all the Italian cooking shows I've watched, I never saw someone imbibing coffee mixed with olive oil.
The concept sounds dreadful, but I guess there is one born every minute.
Catherine Hetu, Merlin, Ont.
Entertainment comes first at popular theatres
Two years after reopening from the pandemic, is Toronto's theatre sector on the brink of crisis? Jan. 28
Note that the theatres doing well - Crow's, Mirvish, and Stratford - are offering a combination of entertainment first, and then thought-provoking material. It is possible the failing theatres have forgotten that entertainment comes first?
Allan Wilson, Etobicoke

Document 337 of 500

Ford approves three MZOs - with Chow's OK

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-approves-three-mzos-with-chows-ok/docview/2919759457/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+approves+three+MZOs+-+with+Chow%27s+OK&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's government has quietly issued three minister's zoning orders for key Toronto projects with the approval of Mayor Olivia Chow, the Star has learned.
In contrast to past MZOs - a controversial tool used to override local planning decisions that has fallen out of favour due to the Greenbelt scandal - the province worked in lockstep with city hall.
MZOs have gained notoriety amid concerns from municipalities, opposition parties, community groups and environmentalists that the government was invoking too many of them.
Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra, who announced he was revisiting the use of zoning orders six weeks ago, has greenlit the three projects.
The new MZOs, which were posted online Friday, clear the way for:
The preservation and protection of air flight paths of Ornge emergency helicopters that need to land at heliports at the Hospital for Sick Children on University Avenue and St. Michael's Hospital on Queen Street West;
The expansion of the University Health Network's Toronto Western Hospital patient tower, a new 13-storey building with 100 in-patient beds and 20 operating theatres on Bathurst Street, just north of Dundas Street West;
The relocation of Iqbal Halal Foods, a popular grocery story in Thorncliffe Park displaced by Metrolinx in order to build a maintenance and storage facility for the new Ontario Line subway.
Calandra worked closely with Chow on the MZOs.
"The mayor's office was consulted on all three of these," Arianne Robinson, Chow's press secretary, confirmed Monday.
At Queen's Park, Justine Teplycky, Calandra's director of communication, said "these MZOs have now been signed and approved by the minister."
"Minister's zoning orders remain an important tool to get critical local projects moving at the pace that Ontarians need and deserve," she said.
Teplycky noted when Calandra announced he was scaling back MZOs on Dec. 13, he had promised to push forward protection of the air ambulance flight corridor and a new location for the 30-year-old Halal supermarket.
"(It was) a request received from the minister of health and endorsed by Mayor Olivia Chow related to the protection of air flight paths of Ornge emergency helicopters, patients, and crews that need to land at hospitals in downtown Toronto," she said.
"(The second was) a request endorsed by Mayor Chow and Toronto city council intended to support a new location for a regionally significant Halal grocery store in the Thorncliffe Park neighbourhood of Toronto."
City council passed a motion on Dec. 13 "to permit a grocery store on the lands at 100 Thorncliffe Park Dr., following the displacement of the grocery store at 2 Thorncliffe Park Dr., as a result of Metrolinx's expropriation of lands."
But the expansion of the Toronto Western Hospital was not mentioned last month when the Progressive Conservatives heralded a curb in MZOs.
"Additionally, an MZO request was received from the minister of health to deliver on provincial priorities," said Teplycky.
"It was supported by Mayor Chow and Toronto city council relating to the expansion of the ... UHN Toronto Western Hospital patient tower," she said.
"This MZO will facilitate an expansion of the hospital with the development of a new 13-storey building ... as well as a wastewater energy transfer facility site. This MZO has also been approved."
At the time he announced the MZO pause, Calandra warned eight orders not related to housing and 14 others connected to other developments would be revoked because there was not "substantial progress" on them.
"I've always been clear that if we do not see the results respecting the minister zoning order, our government will not hesitate to amend or revoke it," Calandra said in December. "It sends a clear message that when we issue a zoning order to support priorities like housing, long-term care, we expect to see results," he said.
In the wake of the Tories' $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal, which is now under investigation by the RCMP, the use of MZOs to expedite development has been widely criticized.
Last August, separate reports by the auditor general and integrity commissioner concluded that certain well-connected developers were "favoured" when Ford opened up 7,400 acres of protected Greenbelt land to build housing.
A month later, the premier was forced to cancel the land swap.
So far, two cabinet ministers and two top aides have parted ways with Ford over a debacle that continues to loom large.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Robert Benzie Toronto Star Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 338 of 500

Opposition raises concerns about deals: Relocation of sites took place without competitive bidding

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/opposition-raises-concerns-about-deals/docview/2919759381/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Opposition+raises+concerns+about+deals%3A+Relocation+of+sites+took+place+without+competitive+bidding&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the province puts more ServiceOntario centres in Staples stores without a competitive bidding process, opposition parties are raising concerns about the deals.
The cabinet minister in charge of public and business service delivery said the changes are part of a pilot project intended to determine what arrangements work best and noted the centres will be open 30 per cent longer than the locations they are replacing.
"Let me be clear, this is not new," said Todd McCarthy, adding the province has been using retail partners such as Canadian Tire, IDA and Home Hardware for ServiceOntario locations for years because it's more convenient for customers.
Another six ServiceOntario centres will open at Staples locations on Feb. 1 as part of a three-year pilot project with savings estimated at just under $1 million, he added.
Government officials said that's the difference between the $11.1 million in costs for operating the ServiceOntario locations they are replacing and an estimated cost of $10.2 million in the new locations.
But the change prompted complaints about "backroom deals" from opposition parties as contracts with many privately operated ServiceOntario locations approach expiry.
The Liberals wrote to Ontario's independent Financial Accountability Office requesting an analysis into how much the renovations are costing taxpayers and whether unionized jobs are being lost.
"These contracts will provide significant value to these large American companies, for example, through the Ontario taxpayer potentially paying for renovations, and by increased foot traffic in their retail stores," said Liberal MPP Stephanie Bowman (Don Valley West), an accountant. "However, the government has not been clear on the value to the Ontario taxpayer."
A senior government official told the Star, on condition of anonymity to discuss internal deliberations, that employees at ServiceOntario locations being closed and moved to Staples have been given "right of first refusal" on jobs there.
The official also said there has been no accounting for the value of the increased customer traffic that Staples could experience, but that data is being gathered on that as part of the pilot project for use in future negotiations. There was no competitive bidding because some agreements were expiring soon and the government acted to avoid service gaps.
ServiceOntario centres have, in many cases, been operated by small businesses. McCarthy defended the change, saying ServiceOntario centres inside established retailers will offer readily available parking and provide easier access to customers than stand-alone locations.
"This is an investment in customer service excellence," he said, noting minimum hours of service will be from 9 a.m. to 7 p.m. and as late as 9 p.m. in some locations.
"The government always funds the costs 100 per cent ... of retrofitting the one-time capital costs. That's to ensure common branding and consistency ... for ServiceOntario," he added.
New Democrat MPP Tom Rakocevic (Humber River-Black Creek) said paying the stores for retrofits is "the opposite of saving money."
"There's a lack of transparency, decisions are being made behind closed doors all the time and they're always looking to reward ... the big corporations," he added.
ServiceOntario centres will be opening Thursday in six Staples in Oakville, Scarborough, Newmarket, Welland, Tillsonburg and Strathroy.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 339 of 500

World Cup bills may soon merit yellow card

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/world-cup-bills-may-soon-merit-yellow-card/docview/2919759307/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=World+Cup+bills+may+soon+merit+yellow+card&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For most of the politicians and staffers who work at Toronto City Hall, now is a time of crisis. Their budget process started with a $1.8-billion gap. There's $23 billion in unfunded repair bills coming due. Residents are being asked to pay a lot more in property taxes. It's a tough year.
But a small but growing part of city hall operates in an entirely different reality. They're planning a big spectacle of a soccer event that'll kick off in just two-and-a-half years.
Yes, FIFA is still coming. The draft version of city hall's 2024 budget continues to advance significant cash for hosting an unspecified number of soccer matches as a host city for the World Cup in 2026.
On Thursday, residents will learn whether Mayor Olivia Chow has kept those costs in the version of the budget she'll be bringing forward to city council. A few days later, on Sunday, Toronto will learn exactly how many games it'll be hosting at BMO Field, as part of a special broadcast.
I don't know the details, but I can tell you now that it won't be cheap. The city has estimated the cost of serving as host city at somewhere north of $300 million, which assumed five matches in Toronto. If there are more, the cost will grow. The cost will probably grow anyway. Budgets for publicly funded sporting events tend to do that.
How much of the cost the city will need to shoulder is also up in the air. When the bid was approved by Toronto city council, it was expected that Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford would come in as full partners and together kick in two-thirds of that cost. That hasn't happened yet.
But the city has been moving forward anyway.
In the draft version of the budget - which hasn't yet been endorsed by Chow - the World Cup Secretariat, the office tasked with overseeing preparation for the event, is currently set to see its budget increase by 42 per cent this year, from $2.4 million to $3.4 million.
That budget is then projected to jump by about 10 times next year, to $34.7 million, and then double again in 2026, to $70.4 million.
And that's not the only FIFA-related cost lurking in these budget documents.
The police have a budget line for $600,000 worth of motorcycles, listed as a "FIFA requirement." Toronto's paramedics, who are otherwise dealing with major challenges keeping emergency response times low, are set to spend $207,000 on staff time to "participate in FIFA World Cup planning committees and working groups."
The proposed budget for Exhibition Place includes $70 million for BMO Field upgrades, while the parks budget includes $41 million to build a training site at Centennial Park.
The city says its share of these costs will be funded by a special reserve fund created to pay for large events, but with a balance of just $35 million at the beginning of this year, city hall's "major special event" fund is going to fall way short of what's needed. A footnote in the budget says this fund will require "replenishment" in future budgets to "fund the City's share of expenses related to hosting the 2026 FIFA World Cup."
Given the public cost, it's frustrating that much of the work to prepare for this event is happening in the shadows. The FIFA contract itself hasn't been released publicly, reportedly due to clauses in the contract that require secrecy. And under former mayor John Tory, council approved a deal to have private sector sports giant MLSE handle several aspects of event planning, without taking on any of the financial risks - or public reporting requirements.
Chow, of course, doesn't deserve blame for these circumstances. The FIFA file was something she inherited. But starting Thursday, the World Cup will be part of her budget - and she should be giving serious scrutiny to every related item in it.
If the cancellation clauses in the FIFA deal really do mean Toronto can't back out of this, Chow should be embarking on a major effort to ensure every dollar is matched by sponsorship money or dedicated tax revenue from ticket sales or other money generated by the event. A scenario where city hall is spending tens of millions on World Cup soccer while simultaneously telling people the shelters are full is not something any politician should want on their record.
And seriously, Chow needs to demand an answer now on whether Ford and Trudeau are going to be playing in this game, too.
We're getting close to full time here.
The mayor may not have asked for it, but she's got this ball anyway. Chow will need a strategy that amounts to more than kicking it down the pitch and hoping for the best.
CREDIT: Matt Elliott OPINION

Document 340 of 500

City hall plans to dramatically ramp up 2026 World Cup spending. Olivia Chow now has the ball

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/city-hall-plans-dramatically-ramp-up-2026-world/docview/2919759141/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City+hall+plans+to+dramatically+ramp+up+2026+World+Cup+spending.+Olivia+Chow+now+has+the+ball&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For most of the politicians and staffers who work at Toronto city hall, now is a time of crisis. Their budget process started with a $1.8-billion gap. There's $23 billion in unfunded repair bills coming due. Residents are being asked to pay a lot more in property taxes. It's a tough year.
But a small but growing part of city hall operates in an entirely different reality. They're planning a big spectacle of a soccer event that'll kick off in just two and a half years.
Yes, FIFA is still coming. The draft version of Toronto city hall's 2024 budget continues to advance significant cash for hosting an unspecified number of soccer matches as a host city for the World Cup in 2026.
On Thursday, residents will learn whether Mayor Olivia Chow has kept those costs in the version of the budget she'll be bringing forward to city council. A few days later, on Feb. 4, Toronto will learn exactly how many games it'll be hosting at BMO Field, as part of a special broadcast.
I don't know the details, but I can tell you now that it won't be cheap. The city has estimated the cost of serving as host city at somewhere north of $300 million, which assumed five matches in Toronto. If there are more, the cost will grow. And hell, the cost will probably grow anyway. Budgets for publicly funded sporting events tend to do that.
How much of the cost the city will need to shoulder is also up in the air. When the bid was approved by Toronto city council, it was expected that Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford would come in as full partners and together kick in two-thirds of that cost. That hasn't happened yet.
But the city has been moving forward anyway. In the draft version of the budget - which hasn't yet been endorsed by Chow - the World Cup Secretariat, the office tasked with overseeing preparation for the event, is currently set to see its budget increase by 42 per cent this year, from $2.4 million to $3.4 million.
That budget is then projected to jump by about 10 times next year, to $34.7 million, and then double again in 2026, to $70.4 million.
And that's not the only FIFA-related cost lurking in these budget documents.
The police have a budget line for $600,000 worth of motorcycles, listed as a "FIFA requirement." Toronto's paramedics, who are otherwise dealing with major challenges keeping emergency response times low, are set to spend $207,000 on staff time to "participate in FIFA World Cup planning committees and working groups." The proposed budget for Exhibition Place includes $70 million for BMO Field upgrades, while the parks budget includes $41 million to build a training site at Centennial Park.
The city says its share of these costs will be funded by a special reserve fund created to pay for large events, but with a balance of just $35 million at the beginning of this year, city hall's "major special event" fund is going to fall way short of what's needed. A footnote in the budget says that this fund will require "replenishment" in future budgets to "fund the City's share of expenses related to hosting the 2026 FIFA World Cup."
Given the public cost, it's frustrating that much of the work to prepare for this event is happening in the shadows. The FIFA contract itself hasn't been released publicly, reportedly due to clauses in the contract that require secrecy. And under former mayor John Tory, council approved a deal to have private sector sports giant MLSE handle several aspects of event-planning, without taking on any of the financial risks - or public reporting requirements.
Chow, of course, doesn't deserve blame for these circumstances. The FIFA file was something she inherited. But starting Thursday, the World Cup will be part of her budget - and she should be giving serious scrutiny to every related item in it.
If the cancellation clauses in the FIFA deal really do mean Toronto can't back out of this, Chow should be embarking on a major effort to ensure every dollar is matched by sponsorship money or dedicated tax revenue from ticket sales or other money generated by the event. A scenario where city hall is spending tens of millions on World Cup soccer while simultaneously telling people the shelters are full is not something any politician should want on their record.
And seriously, Chow needs to demand an answer now on whether Ford and Trudeau are going to be playing in this game too. We're getting close to full time here.
The mayor may not have asked for it, but she's got this ball anyway. Chow will need a strategy that amounts to more than kicking it down the pitch and hoping for the best.
CAPTION: When he was mayor, John Tory helped push the city's bid to host 2026 World Cup games, an expensive commitment to a sporting event that the current mayor now has to deal with.

Document 341 of 500

Ford government slammed for 'secrecy' over ServiceOntario deals

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-slammed-secrecy-over/docview/2919899500/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+slammed+for+%27secrecy%27+over+ServiceOntario+deals&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the province puts more ServiceOntario centres in Staples stores without a competitive bidding process, questions are growing about secrecy behind the deals.
The cabinet minister in charge of public and business service delivery repeatedly refused to release financial details Monday at a Staples in Oakville where he touted the centres will be open 30 per cent longer hours than the locations they are replacing.
"Let me be clear, this is not new," said Todd McCarthy, noting the province has been using retail partners such as Canadian Tire, IDA and Home Hardware for ServiceOntario locations for 15 years.
Another six ServiceOntario centres will open at Staples locations on Feb. 1 as part of a three-year pilot project with savings estimated at $1 million, he added.
But the move prompted complaints about "backroom deals" from opposition parties as contracts with 134 remaining privately operated ServiceOntario locations approach expiry.
The Liberals wrote to Ontario's independent Financial Accountability Office requesting an analysis into how much the renovations are costing taxpayers and whether unionized jobs are being lost.
"These contracts will provide significant value to these large American companies, for example, through the Ontario taxpayer potentially paying for renovations, and by increased foot traffic in their retail stores," said Liberal MPP Stephanie Bowman (Don Valley West), an accountant.
"However, the government has not been clear on the value to the Ontario taxpayer."
ServiceOntario centres have, in many cases, been operated by small Ontario businesses.
McCarthy defended the change, saying ServiceOntario centres inside established retailers will offer readily available parking and provide easier access to customers than stand-alone locations.
"This is an investment in customer service excellence," he said under repeated questions from reporters on the financial arrangements.
"The government always funds the costs 100 per cent ... of retrofitting the one-time capital costs. That's to ensure common branding and consistency ... for ServiceOntario," he added.
"It's well known to Ontarians, the common green signage with white letters."
New Democrat MPP Tom Rakocevic (Humber River-Black Creek) said paying the stores for retrofits is "the opposite of saving money."
"There's a lack of transparency, decisions are being made behind closed doors all the time and they're always looking to reward ... the big corporations," he added, calling on the government to release its "business case" for the shift in strategy.
"The Ford government's secrecy on the ServiceOntario scandal should tell Ontarians everything they need to know," said Green Leader Mike Schreiner. "They're pulling cost savings out of thin air and refusing to back them up with an actual business case."
ServiceOntario centres will be opening Thursday in six Staples locations in Oakville, Scarborough, Newmarket, Welland, Tillsonburg and Strathroy with more to come in Hamilton, Toronto's Leaside area and Keswick later this year.
McCarthy told The Canadian Press the government is evaluating whether the 134 ServiceOntario locations still operated by small businesses should remain or be closed and moved into retail stores, libraries or municipal offices. He also said more than 80 locations run by the government will remain in place.
CAPTION: A ServiceOntario location is shown in Whitby, Ont. on Jan. 27, 2024.
