Document 342 of 500

Doug Ford government approves three MZOs - with Olivia Chow's blessing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-government-approves-three-mzos-with/docview/2919899180/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+government+approves+three+MZOs+-+with+Olivia+Chow%27s+blessing&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's government has quietly issued three minister's zoning orders for key Toronto projects with the approval of Mayor Olivia Chow, the Star has learned.
In contrast to past MZOs - a controversial tool used to override local planning decisions that has fallen out of favour due to the Greenbelt scandal - the province worked in lockstep with city hall.
MZOs have gained notoriety amid concerns from municipalities, opposition parties, community groups and environmentalists that the government was invoking too many of them.
Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra, who announced he was revisiting the use of zoning orders six weeks ago, has greenlit the three projects.
The new MZOs, which were posted online Friday, clear the way for:
 The preservation and protection of air flight paths of Ornge emergency helicopters that need to land at heliports at the Hospital for Sick Children on University Avenue and St. Michael's Hospital on Queen Street West.
 The expansion of the University Health Network's Toronto Western Hospital patient tower, a new 13-storey building with 100 in-patient beds and 20 operating theatres on Bathurst Street, just north of Dundas Street West.
 The relocation of Iqbal Halal Foods, a popular grocery story in Thorncliffe Park displaced by Metrolinx in order to build a maintenance and storage facility for the new Ontario Line subway.
Calandra worked closely with Chow on the MZOs.
"The mayor's office was consulted on all three of these," Arianne Robinson, Chow's press secretary, confirmed Monday.
At Queen's Park, Justine Teplycky, Calandra's director of communication, said "these MZOs have now been signed and approved by the minister."
"Minister's zoning orders remain an important tool to get critical local projects moving at the pace that Ontarians need and deserve," she said.
Teplycky noted when Calandra announced he was scaling back MZOs on Dec. 13, he had promised to push forward protection of the air ambulance flight corridor and a new location for the 30-year-old Halal supermarket.
"(It was) a request received from the minister of health and endorsed by Mayor Olivia Chow related to the protection of air flight paths of Ornge emergency helicopters, patients, and crews that need to land at hospitals in downtown Toronto," she said.
"(The second was) a request endorsed by Mayor Chow and Toronto city council intended to support a new location for a regionally significant Halal grocery store in the Thorncliffe Park neighbourhood of Toronto."
City council passed a motion on Dec. 13 "to permit a grocery store on the lands at 100 Thorncliffe Park Dr., following the displacement of the grocery store at 2 Thorncliffe Park Dr., as a result of Metrolinx's expropriation of lands."
But the expansion of the Toronto Western Hospital was not mentioned last month when the Progressive Conservatives heralded a curb in MZOs.
"Additionally, an MZO request was received from the minister of health to deliver on provincial priorities," said Teplycky.
"It was supported by Mayor Chow and Toronto city council relating to the expansion of the ... UHN Toronto Western Hospital patient tower," she said.
"This MZO will facilitate an expansion of the hospital with the development of a new 13-storey building ... as well as a wastewater energy transfer facility site. This MZO has also been approved."
At the time he announced the MZO pause, Calandra warned eight orders not related to housing and 14 others connected to other developments would be revoked because there was not "substantial progress" on them.
"I've always been clear that if we do not see the results respecting the minister zoning order, our government will not hesitate to amend or revoke it," Calandra said in December.
"It sends a clear message that when we issue a zoning order to support priorities like housing, long-term care, we expect to see results," he said.
In the wake of the Tories' $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal, which is now under investigation by the RCMP, the use of MZOs to expedite development has been widely criticized.
Last August, separate reports by the auditor general and integrity commissioner concluded that certain well-connected developers were "favoured" when Ford opened up 7,400 acres of protected Greenbelt land to build housing.
A month later, the premier was forced to cancel the land swap.
So far, two cabinet ministers and two top aides have parted ways with Ford over a debacle that continues to loom large.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford and Mayor Olivia Chow speak at a news conference at Queen's Park in Toronto on Nov. 27, 2023.

Subject: Relocation; Councils; Halal food; Zoning; Cities; Grocery stores

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/2919312424/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+%27Something+had+to+be+done%2C%27+Jones+says%2C+Jan.+25+Thousands+scramble+to+pay+CEBA+loans%2C+Jan.+18+Smith+%26amp%3B+Wesson+denies+fault+in+%24150M+lawsuit%2C+Jan.+26&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Action against convoy justified
'Something had to be done,' Jones says, Jan. 25
The federal government was absolutely in their right to use the Emergencies Act to end the "Freedom Convoy" on the streets of Ottawa. I don't have too much sympathy for the protesters who had their bank accounts frozen.
The owners who were able to take those trucks off the road from their usual activities for three weeks must have been in a good financial position.
Those vehicles are extremely powerful and could have been used as weapons, running down other vehicles and pedestrians. That was a concern throughout. As well, gasoline canisters were being carted into the protest site. Horrific consequences could have resulted with all that fuel! Our government did what had to be done to put a pin in the prolonged protest.
Elspeth Dowell, Oshawa
I am quite confused about Federal Court Justice Richard Mosely's ruling that use of the Emergencies Act was an infringement on protesters' rights.
Perhaps I could expect an explanation on how harassing, intimidating citizens of downtown Ottawa for three weeks and preventing them from free movement, conducting business or simply going about their lives is not also a right guaranteed under the Charter.
Is it a right under the Charter to block a vital supply line of parts and materials at Ambassador Bridge destined for industry in Ontario causing production shutdowns and depriving Canadians of making a living?
I can't wait for a definition of "freedom of expression" related to bringing firearms to Coutts border blockade.
Bottom line is that these "protesters" enjoy a lifetime of freedom in a democratic Canadian society. This society, and indeed the world, came under attack by a deadly virus and this society and its government asked them to step up and join millions of other Canadians by masking, distancing and being vaccinated to try and slow down the spread of virus that was killing thousands of their fellow Canadians. I have only one way to describe their position against that - SHAME.
Francis J. Zita, Scarborough
The court's ruling doesn't change my opinion of the extremely yet unnecessarily disruptive "Freedom Convoy" occupation of downtown Ottawa. However, the conservative press seems to remain apologetic toward the protesters' recklessness and inconsideration toward the residents of Ottawa-Gatineau who suffered them. Some of us call it plain bullying.
This includes the blind resident left housebound due to her usual reliance on her acute hearing to guide her into and through town being hampered by the continuous gratuitous horn-blaring.
There also was the harassment of some local businesses and customers, and even the bullying of residents who chose to mask. I'd say that too many protesters were not peaceful but rather behaved like they didn't at all care about the residents' mental well-being. Then there were the protesters excreting on public and private property.
Just to think, a large number of protesters had planned to stay there for as long as (re)supplies lasted! I could have supported the protesters' general COVID cause, or at least have felt neutral, and therefore opposed the implementation of the Emergencies Act against the remaining protesters, but what they did to innocent non-violent area residents changed my mind.
Frank Sterle Jr., White Rock, B.C.
To the Star Letter writer pointing fingers at Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's leadership and praising his father Pierre, remember that it was the elder Trudeau who gave us the Charter of Rights and Freedoms and this very document enabled the so-called "Freedom Convoy" to invade Ottawa, trample the rights of citizens to have a peaceful sleep, stroll their streets and attend their businesses without being harassed, chased and spit upon, and now have the satisfaction that the Emergencies Act was deemed inappropriate. Very few politicians suit everyone.
Dale Nicol, Trenton, Ont.
Retiring to a CEBA payment
Thousands scramble to pay CEBA loans, Jan. 18
I've been a small businessman all of my life, turned 65 in November and got my first old age pension payment in December. Was it used to buy Christmas presents for the grandkids? Groceries? Rent? Nope, after considerable scrutiny of my budget, the first pension payment went to pay off the last bit of my CEBA loan.
Kind of ironic. The current government policies have raised taxes, raised rent, raised the cost of food, raised interest rates and didn't act enough on the rise in crime.
Someone should point out that they haven't raised our standard of living. I'm not saying the Liberals are out of touch with real people but, yes, I guess I am saying that.
Mark Yates, Elmira, Ont.
We don't need guns at all
Smith & Wesson denies fault in $150M lawsuit, Jan. 26
I wish the victims of the Danforth shootings and their families well in trying to get an American gun maker to take responsibility for the carnage caused by its increasingly deadly wares.
Guns are marketed and sold in the U.S. like widgets, and anything that might slow down or complicate the production line, such as smart technology, might affect profit margins and shareholder value.
That the M&P40 was stolen from a gun dealer reminds us that all crime guns start out as legal guns.
While gun makers argue that there is nothing inherently defective about their product, the idea that weapons that fire multiple rounds, weapons so lively and deadly that not even the police can finesse their use, ought to be available to random civilians, is patently absurd.
All one need do is tally exactly how many rounds have been fired in mass shootings, or fatal police-initiated actions involving a handgun, as in the Sammy Yatim tragedy, or in the firing at fleeing suspects, to prove that these weapons are best confined to military use and are anything but collectibles.
Dr. Ron Charach, Toronto

Subject: Shootings; Charters; Mass murders; Firearms

Document 344 of 500

He had 'vision and hope of justice and fairness for all': Political leaders remember former NDP chief Ed Broadbent at state funeral

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/he-had-vision-hope-justice-fairness-all/docview/2919312239/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=He+had+%27vision+and+hope+of+justice+and+fairness+for+all%27%3A+Political+leaders+remember+former+NDP+chief+Ed+Broadbent+at+state+funeral&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's first provincial First Nations premier singled out Ed Broadbent as a beacon of civility in politics Sunday as generations of political leaders gathered to bid a final goodbye to the left-leaning luminary.
Wab Kinew, elected just last year as Manitoba's new NDP premier, acknowledged a stark reality: that the former federal New Democrat leader's death on Jan. 11 at the age of 87 could well mark the end of an era.
"Mr. Broadbent's smiling, joyful legacy is an example we ought to learn from today," Kinew said at Broadbent's state funeral.
"That we can use good means to achieve good ends; that we don't have to appeal to our darkest impulses; that we can have faith in our fellow Canadians."
Federal NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh fought tears as he recounted how Broadbent, by then well-established as the party's elder statesman, helped him learn how best to manage the party's helm in 2017.
"He wanted me to do a lot more, and a lot faster - very New Democrat of him. And he also wanted to make sure we never let the Liberals off the hook," Singh said.
"We are so fortunate that he chose to spend his life in pursuit of his vision and his hope of justice and fairness for all."
Singh added, choking back tears: "Ed, we won't let you down. And you're still who I want to be when I grow up."
New Democrats from across the country gathered to remember and celebrate Broadbent as a friend, mentor and political visionary.
Before the ceremony got underway, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau described Broadbent as a social justice champion who left Canada a better place.
"He was a tireless campaigner for social justice," Trudeau said. "Canada is significantly better for his years of service both in politics and out of it."
A long queue of mourners - many in sombre colours punctuated with a flash of the party's trademark orange - reached down the street and around the corner as the doors opened.
Flags on federal buildings were flying at half-mast in advance of what officials were billing less as a funeral and more as a celebration of Broadbent's life.
The former New Democrat leader served as an MP for more than two decades, including 14 at the party's helm in the 1970s and '80s.
Broadbent's tenure as leader helped to usher in the modern-day NDP, building the foundation that allowed Jack Layton to lead the party to record results in 2011.
In that role, Broadbent faced off against four different prime ministers, including Pierre Trudeau, Brian Mulroney, John Turner and Joe Clark, who was scheduled to attend Sunday's funeral.
Others on Sunday's guest list included Gov. Gen. Mary Simon; Bob Rae, former NDP premier in Ontario and Canada's current ambassador to the UN; and British Columbia Premier David Eby.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre was notably absent from the gathering and instead held a meeting with his caucus members on Sunday. Conservative MP Colin Carrie who represents Oshawa, Ont., attended the funeral.
Broadbent represented his blue-collar hometown of Oshawa in the House of Commons for 21 years, including 14 as leader of the federal NDP, from 1975 to 1989. He briefly served as the MP for Ottawa Centre from 2004 to 2006.
Under his leadership, the NDP steadily expanded its seat count in the House - from 17 in 1974 to 43 in 1988, a record that would stand until the Layton era vaulted the party into official Opposition status 23 years later.
NDP strategist Brian Topp, who now leads the social justice institute Broadbent founded in 2011, described him as an academic and intellectual who very quickly learned the nuances of federal politics.
Broadbent also left his mark on the Charter of Rights and Freedoms after then-prime minister Pierre Trudeau reached out to the leader of the NDP to ask for his help in elevating the text of the document.
He was keenly interested in ensuring issues like equality for women, First Nations treaty rights and the West's rights over its natural resources were properly acknowledged in the document.
The day Broadbent died, the institute that bears his name cited his 2023 book, "Seeking Social Democracy," as leaving "an enduring vision and his hopes for what is to be done to build the good society for today and the future."
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh said Ed Broadbent helped him learn how best to manage the party's helm in 2017.Sean KilpatrickTHE CANADIAN PRESS
CREDIT: Nojoud Al Mallees The Canadian Press

Star readers support use of Emergencies Act to stop convoy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/star-readers-support-use-emergencies-act-stop/docview/2919312102/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Star+readers+support+use+of+Emergencies+Act+to+stop+convoy&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Something had to be done about the convoy
Ford government stands by use of Emergencies Act against 'Freedom Convoy' despite court decision, Jan. 24
The federal government was absolutely in their right to use the Emergencies Act to end the "Freedom Convoy" on the streets of Ottawa. I don't have too much sympathy for the protesters who had their bank accounts frozen. The owners who were able to take those trucks off the road from their usual activities for three weeks must have been in a good financial position. Those vehicles are extremely powerful and could have been used as weapons, running down other vehicles and pedestrians. That was a concern throughout. As well, gasoline canisters were being carted into the protest site. Horrific consequences could have resulted with all that fuel! Our government did what had to be done to put a pin in the prolonged protest.
Elspeth Dowell, Oshawa
I am quite confused about Federal Court Justice Richard Mosely's ruling that use of the Emergencies Act was an infringement on protesters' rights. Perhaps I could expect an explanation on how harassing, intimidating citizens of downtown Ottawa for three weeks and preventing them from free movement, conducting business or simply going about their lives is not also a right guaranteed under the Charter. Is it a right under the Charter to block a vital supply line of parts and materials at Ambassador Bridge destined for industry in Ontario causing production shutdowns and depriving Canadians of making a living? I can't wait for a definition of "freedom of expression" related to bringing firearms to Coutts border blockade.
Bottom line is that these "protesters" enjoy a lifetime of freedom in a democratic Canadian society. This society, and indeed the world, came under attack by a deadly virus and this society and its government asked them to step up and join millions of other Canadians by masking, distancing and being vaccinated to try and slow down the spread of virus that was killing thousands of their fellow Canadians. I have only one way to describe their position against that - SHAME.
Francis J. Zita, Scarborough
The court's ruling doesn't change my opinion of the extremely yet unnecessarily disruptive "Freedom Convoy" occupation of downtown Ottawa. However, the conservative press seems to remain apologetic towards the protesters' recklessness and inconsideration toward the residents of Ottawa-Gatineau who suffered them. Some of us call it plain bullying. This includes the blind resident left housebound due to her usual reliance on her acute hearing to guide her into and through town being hampered by the continuous gratuitous horn-blaring. There also was the harassment of some local businesses and customers, and even the bullying of residents who chose to mask. I'd say that too many protesters were not peaceful but rather behaved like they didn't at all care about the residents' mental well-being. Then there were the protesters excreting on public and private property. Just to think, a large number of protesters had planned to stay there for as long as (re)supplies lasted! I could have supported the protesters' general COVID cause, or at least have felt neutral, and therefore opposed the implementation of the Emergencies Act against the remaining protesters, but what they did to innocent non-violent area residents changed my mind.
Frank Sterle Jr., White Rock, B.C.
To the Star Letter writer pointing fingers at Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's leadership and praising his father Pierre, remember that it was the elder Trudeau who gave us the Charter of Rights and Freedoms and this very document enabled the so-called "Freedom Convoy" to invade Ottawa, trample the rights of citizens to have a peaceful sleep, stroll their streets and attend their businesses without being harassed, chased and spit upon, and now have the satisfaction that the Emergencies Act was deemed inappropriate. Very few politicians suit everyone.
Dale Nicol, Trenton, Ont.
Retiring to a CEBA payment
Thousands of Canadian small businesses scramble to pay back CEBA loans as deadline hits, Jan. 18
I've been a small businessman all of my life, turned 65 in November and got my first old age pension payment in December. Was it used to buy Christmas presents for the grandkids? Groceries? Rent? Nope, after considerable scrutiny of my budget, the first pension payment went to pay off the last bit of my CEBA loan. Kind of ironic. The current government policies have raised taxes, raised rent, raised the cost of food, raised interest rates and didn't act enough on the rise in crime. Someone should point out that they haven't raised our standard of living. I'm not saying the Liberals are out of touch with real people but, yes, I guess I am saying that.
Mark Yates, Elmira, Ont.
We don't need guns at all
Smith & Wesson denies liability in $150 million Toronto lawsuit, Jan. 25
I wish the victims of the Danforth shootings and their families well in trying to get an American gun maker to take responsibility for the carnage caused by its increasingly deadly wares. Guns are marketed and sold in the U.S. like widgets, and anything that might slow down or complicate the production line, such as smart technology, might affect profit margins and shareholder value. That the M&P40 was stolen from a gun dealer reminds us that all crime guns start out as legal guns. While gun makers argue that there is nothing inherently defective about their product, the idea that weapons that fire multiple rounds, weapons so lively and deadly that not even the police can finesse their use, ought to be available to random civilians, is patently absurd. All one need do is tally exactly how many rounds have been fired in mass shootings, or fatal police-initiated actions involving a handgun, as in the Sammy Yatim tragedy, or in the firing at fleeing suspects, to prove that these weapons are best confined to military use and are anything but collectibles.
Dr. Ron Charach, Toronto
CAPTION: Protesters participating in a cross-country truck convoy protesting measures taken by authorities to curb the spread of COVID-19 and vaccine mandates walk near Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Jan. 29, 2022.

Subject: Shootings; Charters; Mass murders; Firearms

Business indexing term: Subject: Charters

Document 346 of 500

Ottawa mulling carbon pricing rebrand: Polling shows confusion among Canadians as Liberal MPs admit communications challenge

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ottawa-mulling-carbon-pricing-rebrand/docview/2919312010/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+mulling+carbon+pricing+rebrand%3A+Polling+shows+confusion+among+Canadians+as+Liberal+MPs+admit+communications+challenge&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal government is considering a rebrand of the rebate program for its carbon pricing system in an attempt to tackle what it calls confusion and misconceptions about the scheme, the Star has learned.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals have admitted they are losing the communications battle over carbon pricing in the face of a campaign from Pierre Poilievre and the Conservatives to undermine the government's flagship climate policy.
Now, exclusive polling from Abacus Data for the Star details Canadians' confusion about the policy.
Ottawa's efforts to better communicate its carbon pricing system come after a year of relentless attacks from federal Conservatives, who routinely blame the "carbon tax" for the rising costs of fuel and food while often neglecting the rebates that Canadians receive under the program.
"The government is taking a serious look at how the climate action incentive payment is being branded," a senior government source told the Star. The multi-department effort includes weighing whether a name change for the rebate would boost perceptions, and ongoing efforts to improve banks' labelling of the quarterly payments. Any changes would come into place as the government rolls out its yearly update on the carbon price and the rebate.
During the national Liberal caucus retreat in Ottawa last week, several MPs who spoke to the Star admitted the Conservatives' attacks have made it difficult to communicate the policy in their ridings, but insisted their constituents remained on board with it.
But the Abacus pollsuggests Canadians are split on whether it's a good policy. In Ontario, where the Liberals hold 75 of 121 seats, 45 per cent of respondents said they believed it was a bad policy, compared to 37 per cent who said it was a good one. In the Atlantic Provinces, where the Liberals hold 24 out of 32 seats, 49 per cent believed it was a bad policy, while 31 per cent said it was a good one.
"It's been turned into this serious wedge issue," Milton MP Adam van Koeverden told the Star. "And there's so much misinformation out there about what it is and what it isn't."
Using online panels, Abacus surveyed a representative sample of 2,199 Canadian adults between Jan. 18 and 23. Although opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 2.09 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Liberal MPs who spoke to the Star indicated there was increasing pressure on individual MPs to dispel myths and correct the record on the carbon price with constituents.
Half of those Abacus polled who were eligible to receive the climate action incentive payment were asked about their awareness of what the rebate was for. Of those, 52 per cent did not know what it was for, even though 72 per cent reported being aware of it. Nearly half of all respondents said the carbon levy was a big factor for inflation.
"If there is a rebrand, there's an opportunity to explain it better," said one MP, who the Star granted anonymity because they are not authorized to speak on the matter.
While a rebrand could help increase awareness of the rebate, some former Liberal insiders told the Star the government must ensure it is constantly reminding people that the rebates are greater than any cost increases passed onto consumers. Research from the Parliamentary Budget Officer, supported by many independent economists, shows that eight of 10 eligible Canadians receive more money through carbon pricing rebates than they pay.
Catherine McKenna, who first introduced the carbon pricing program as Trudeau's environment and climate change minister in 2018, said the government's announcement in October of an exemption on home-heating oil gave ammunition to Conservatives' claims the carbon price was contributing to affordability issues.
"You can have the best name in the world (for the rebate), but you have to be willing to be out there constantly defending it," she told the Star.
The Canada Revenue Agency spent just over $2 million in advertising the rebate in the first two years after the price on carbon was introduced, but has not spent any money to advertise it in the three years since, which included the COVID-19 pandemic.
CREDIT: Mark Ramzy Toronto Star

Document 347 of 500

Amid confusion from Canadians, Justin Trudeau's government is considering rebrand of carbon pricing: source

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/amid-confusion-canadians-justin-trudeaus/docview/2919311814/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Amid+confusion+from+Canadians%2C+Justin+Trudeau%27s+government+is+considering+rebrand+of+carbon+pricing%3A+source&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-The federal government is considering a rebrand of the rebate program for its carbon pricing system in an attempt to tackle what it calls confusion and misconceptions about the scheme, the Star has learned.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals have admitted they are losing the communications battle over carbon pricing in the face of a campaign from Pierre Poilievre and the Conservatives to undermine the government's flagship climate policy.
Now, exclusive polling from Abacus Data for the Star details Canadians' confusion about the policy.
Ottawa's efforts to better communicate its carbon pricing system come after a year of relentless attacks from federal Conservatives, who routinely blame the "carbon tax" for the rising costs of fuel and food while often neglecting the rebates that Canadians receive under the program.
"The government is taking a serious look at how the climate action incentive payment is being branded," a senior government source told the Star. The multi-department effort includes weighing whether a name change for the rebate would boost perceptions, and ongoing efforts to improve banks' labelling of the quarterly payments. Any changes would come into place as the government rolls out its yearly update on the carbon price and the rebate.
During the national Liberal caucus retreat in Ottawa this week, several MPs who spoke to the Star admitted the Conservatives' attacks have made it difficult to communicate the policy in their ridings, but insisted their constituents remained on board with it.
But the Abacus poll suggests Canadians are split on whether it's a good policy. In Ontario, where the Liberals hold 75 of 121 seats, 45 per cent of respondents said they believed it was a bad policy, compared to 37 per cent who said it was a good one. In the Atlantic Provinces, where the Liberals hold 24 out of 32 seats, 49 per cent believed it was a bad policy, while 31 per cent said it was a good one.
"It's been turned into this serious wedge issue," Milton MP Adam van Koeverden told the Star. "And there's so much misinformation out there about what it is and what it isn't."
Using online panels, Abacus surveyed a representative sample of 2,199 Canadian adults between Jan. 18 and 23. Although opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 2.09 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Last week, Poilievre was in Prince Rupert, B.C., for his latest "axe the tax" rally. Because B.C. has its own carbon price, the federal levy does not apply.
"The Conservatives are doing their best to muddle the waters," said Vancouver Liberal MP Patrick Weiler.
Liberal MPs who spoke to the Star indicated there was increasing pressure on individual MPs to dispel myths and correct the record on the carbon price with constituents.
Half of those Abacus polled who were eligible to receive the climate action incentive payment were asked about their awareness of what the rebate was for. Of those, 52 per cent did not know what it was for, even though 72 per cent reported being aware of it. Nearly half of all respondents said the carbon levy was a big factor for inflation.
"If there is a rebrand, there's an opportunity to explain it better," said one MP, who the Star granted anonymity because they are not authorized to speak on the matter.
While a rebrand could help increase awareness of the rebate, some former Liberal insiders told the Star the government must ensure it is constantly reminding people that the rebates are greater than any cost increases passed onto consumers. Research from the Parliamentary Budget Officer, supported by many independent economists, shows that eight of 10 eligible Canadians receive more money through carbon pricing rebates than they pay.
Catherine McKenna, who first introduced the carbon pricing program as Trudeau's environment and climate change minister in 2018, said the government's announcement in October of an exemption on home-heating oil gave ammunition to Conservatives' claims the carbon price was contributing to affordability issues.
"You can have the best name in the world (for the rebate), but you have to be willing to be out there constantly defending it," she told the Star.
To Greg MacEachern, a long-time Liberal strategist and a former director of communications during the Paul Martin years, the government had lost the plot long before that.
"I don't want to be unkind," he said. "But I think it was very lackadaisical." MacEachern compared the government's approach to the rebate program to the Stephen Harper years, when the government would use giant prop cheques to emphasize how government programs helped people.
"What they were telling us was it was really tough, then, to let Canadians know what you were doing to help them, and their solution was giant props," he said. "And yet, there is now an expectation that Canadians would get direct deposits in their account, and somehow connect it to the price on carbon. That assumes a lot."
"And that's in a neutral territory," he added. "When you have an opponent, who is focusing everything through a very partisan lens, the challenge just rises exponentially."
One way the government can improve its reach to Canadians, MacEachern said, is through increased advertising of the rebate. The Canada Revenue Agency spent just over $2 million in advertising the rebate in the first two years after the price on carbon was introduced, but has not spent any money to advertise it in the three years since, which included the COVID-19 pandemic.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau makes an announcement that the government will double the carbon price rebate for rural Canadians beginning next April during a news conference in Ottawa on Oct. 26, 2023.

Document 348 of 500

Former city councillor eyes federal Conservative run

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/former-city-councillor-eyes-federal-conservative/docview/2919311705/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+city+councillor+eyes+federal+Conservative+run&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Karen Stintz, the former city councillor and TTC chair, is seeking the Conservative nomination in Eglinton-Lawrence, hoping to help party leader Pierre Poilievre break the federal Liberals' stranglehold on Toronto seats.
Stintz confirmed in an exclusive interview that she will announce her bid for the nomination Monday.
"I thought I was out of politics, I thought I was done, but when Pierre got elected (leader), he inspired me," Stintz said in a midtown coffee shop. "I think he's an incredible communicator and I think he's able to give voice to the frustrations that people were feeling but couldn't articulate."
Stintz was elected Eglinton-Lawrence city councillor in 2003 after answering a candidate recruitment ad placed by a ratepayers' group. She served 11 years, including four as TTC chair.
She ran for mayor in 2014 but dropped out and left politics before the election that saw John Tory start his first term.
Stintz has spent the past eight years as chief executive of Variety Village but is leaving the recreation centre for children with disabilities as soon as its board hires a replacement for her.
A card-carrying Conservative since Brian Mulroney's party leadership in the 1980s, Stintz calls Marco Mendicino, Eglinton-Lawrence's Liberal MP, "a great guy, he's done a great job. I just think he's in the wrong party."
She accused Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and his Liberals, whose domination of Toronto helped them cling to minority government status in the last election, of failing midtown and the rest of the city.
"There is a profound sense of letdown in this community around how the government has responded to community needs, the Jewish community in particular," she said, over Trudeau's response to the Israel-Hamas conflict. "It also has to be said the federal government really hasn't given back to Toronto what Toronto has given to it.
"That the mayor (Olivia Chow) has to fight to get money for refugees, which is clearly a federal priority ... I definitely think there is an opportunity for Toronto to make a statement to the current government about how it's not benefiting from its leadership."
Chow's city hall has been at odds with the Toronto Liberal caucus over the federal government's refusal, so far, to pay $250 million in forecast costs to shelter refugee claimants in the city this year. Chow's budget chief Coun. Shelley Carroll has floated the idea of tacking an extra six per cent on to Torontonians' property tax bill, if the refugee funding doesn't flow, and calling it the "federal impacts levy."
Stintz acknowledged that, if she wins the nomination, it won't be easy to convince some voters that Conservatives are the answer to their problems.
The party's power base is western Canada with little declared love for Toronto, and Poilievre's combative conservatism could be a tough sell.
After city staff's proposed budget was unveiled Jan. 10, Poilievre posted on X: "Incompetent NDP/Liberal city politicians raise Toronto's taxes 10.5 per cent. Just like Trudeau. Hands in your pocket. Not worth the cost. Common sense Conservatives will cut your taxes." Mayor Chow and city council will set the budget and tax hike on Feb. 14.
Days later the Conservative leader wrote on X: "Incompetent Toronto city politicians - who get big tax money from Trudeau - raise fees on homebuilding." He included a 2022 chart of charges that doesn't reflect later Ontario government legislation that cut, froze and exempted various fees that municipalities, including Toronto, can charge homebuilders.
Stintz said she found Poilievre to be focused and intelligent at a Toronto reception and hopes to get a chance to try to win over voters, including those who have their doubts.
CREDIT: David Rider Toronto Star Chief

Document 349 of 500

Former Toronto councillor Karen Stintz hoping to run for Poilievre's Conservatives in return to politics

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/former-toronto-councillor-karen-stintz-hoping-run/docview/2919501995/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+Toronto+councillor+Karen+Stintz+hoping+to+run+for+Poilievre%27s+Conservatives+in+return+to+politics&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Karen Stintz, the former city councillor and TTC chair, is seeking the Conservative nomination in Eglinton-Lawrence, hoping to help party leader Pierre Poilievre break the federal Liberals' stranglehold on Toronto seats.
Stintz confirmed in an interview that she will announce her bid for the nomination Monday.
"I thought I was out of politics, I thought I was done, but when Pierre got elected (leader), he inspired me," Stintz said in a midtown coffee shop. "I think he's an incredible communicator and I think he's able to give voice to the frustrations that people were feeling but couldn't articulate."
Stintz was elected Eglinton-Lawrence city councillor in 2003 after answering a candidate recruitment ad placed by a ratepayers' group. She served 11 years including four as TTC chair.
She ran for mayor in 2014 but dropped out and left politics before the election that saw John Tory start his first term.
Stintz has spent the past eight years as chief executive of Variety Village but is leaving the recreation centre for children with disabilities as soon as its board hires a replacement for her.
A card-carrying Conservative since Brian Mulroney's party leadership in the 1980s, Stintz calls Marco Mendicino, Eglinton-Lawrence's Liberal MP, "a great guy, he's done a great job. I just think he's in the wrong party."
She accused Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and his Liberals, whose domination of Toronto helped them cling to minority government status in the last election, of failing midtown and the rest of the city.
"There is a profound sense of letdown in this community around how the government has responded to to community needs, the Jewish community in particular," she said, over Trudeau's response to the Israel-Hamas conflict. "It also has to be said the federal government really hasn't given back to Toronto what Toronto has given to it.
"That the mayor (Olivia Chow) has to fight to get money for refugees, which is clearly a federal priority ... I definitely think there is an opportunity for Toronto to make a statement to the current government about how it's not benefiting from its leadership."
Chow's city hall has been at odds with the Toronto Liberal caucus over the federal government's refusal, so far, to pay $250 million in forecast costs to shelter refugee claimants in the city this year. Chow's budget chief Coun. Shelley Carroll has floated the idea of tacking an extra six per cent on to Torontonians' property tax bill, if the refugee funding doesn't flow, and calling it the "federal impacts levy."
Stintz acknowledged that, if she wins the nomination, it won't be easy to convince some voters that Conservatives are the answer to their problems.
The party's power base is Western Canada with little declared love for Toronto, and Polievre's combative conservatism could be a tough sell.
After city staff's proposed budget was unveiled Jan. 10, Poilievre posted on X, formerly Twitter: "Incompetent NDP/Liberal city politicians raise Toronto's taxes 10.5 per cent. Just like Trudeau. Hands in your pocket. Not worth the cost. Common sense Conservatives will cut your taxes." Mayor Chow and city council will set the budget and tax hike on Feb. 14.
Days later the Conservative leader wrote on X: "Incompetent Toronto city politicians - who get big tax money from Trudeau - raise fees on homebuilding." He included a 2022 chart of charges that doesn't reflect later Ontario government legislation that cut, froze and exempted various fees that municipalities, including Toronto, can charge homebuilders.
Stintz said she found Poilievre to be focused and intelligent at a Toronto reception and hopes to get a chance to try to win over voters, including those who have their doubts. "I look forward to working with (Poilievre) and I look forward to changing that perception of him."
CAPTION: Former city councillor Karen Stintz says she hopes to become the Conservative candidate in Eglinton-Lawrence and help Pierre Poilievre's party break into a city long dominated by the ruling Liberals.

For Ed Broadbent, socialism meant providing for average people - and fighting for the cause

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ed-broadbent-socialism-meant-providing-average/docview/2919137632/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=For+Ed+Broadbent%2C+socialism+meant+providing+for+average+people+-+and+fighting+for+the+cause&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A state funeral for Ed Broadbent, federal NDP leader for 14 years, is to be held Sunday in Ottawa. A friend pays tribute here.
Ed Broadbent's integrity, his decency and kindness have animated the outpouring of admiration from across the political spectrum since news of his passing. It is recognition he duly deserves.
I met Ed in 2013, an earnest new policy director at the nascent think tank the Broadbent Institute he then chaired. We became friends (or comrades, as we joked), and formed a bond that would eventually lead to working together on his remarkable new book, "Seeking Social Democracy: Seven Decades in the Fight for Equality."
That Ed was adored by many was clear anytime I was in public with him. On street corners, in cafes or in newsrooms, a man who had left the political limelight decades ago still engendered such warmth and excitement. Ed didn't quite understand people's desire for selfies; he much preferred to talk to them and hear their concerns and viewpoints.
I have no doubt Ed would appreciate the earnest affection and respect being expressed for his leadership style. But for Ed, the politics of personality were secondary to ideology and the importance of political ideas.
On Nov. 1, Ed gave what would turn out to be his last interview to a national audience. On CBC's "The Current," host Matt Galloway asked Ed whether he still considered himself a socialist.
"Yes," he replied bluntly.
His enduring socialist convictions should come as no surprise. Ed was remarkably consistent through nearly seven decades of interventions in public life.
He was a social democrat (or democratic socialist, he used the two terms interchangeably) because he believed that human freedom and liberty could only flourish on a foundation of substantive equality. His task as leader was to persuade citizens to vote for a social democratic party so that state power could be put in service of building a good society.
I have been reflecting since his death on what drew me so strongly to Ed's ideas when I first encountered them in the aughts.
I came of age in the '90s in what is sometimes referred to as the neoliberal age. An era when the free market and the dynamism of the private sector were assumed to be the answer to all that ails us. A time when government was cast as a wasteful and costly intruder that constrained our individual freedom, unions an anachronistic drag on economic growth.
In Ontario where I grew up, Jean Chrétien's austerity and Mike Harris's "common sense revolution" meant the gutting of health and environmental regulations, tax cuts for the wealthy, deep cuts to social assistance, health care and education, and the selling off of public assets and services to the private sector.
Questions about whether these decisions being made were broadly good, or fair, or just took a back seat to whether they were efficient, drove "growth," and whether they advanced the purchasing power of individual consumers and taxpayers.
To those in power, the disastrous effects of this program on people - poverty, inequality, homelessness, the erosion of public trust - was the inevitable if unfortunate result of a lack of personal ambition and resourcefulness.
I found this world view blinkered, selfish, disingenuous.
Ed Broadbent was like a tonic to me and many in my disillusioned generation. Here was someone who, throughout the neoliberal era, refused to accept that inequality was ineluctable.
His belief in universal social programs, his championing of unions and critique of corporate power, his emphasis on the importance of class; all were ideas that felt urgent and refreshing.
New to me, they were grounded in a long and proud tradition to which Ed devoted his life.
For Ed, democratic socialism meant waging a constant battle against the inequality-producing tendencies of the market. It meant institutions that were democratically accountable shaping markets to serve the needs of people not private interests.
Ed was clear-eyed about what was required to pursue greater equality and give people the dignity and resources necessary to enjoy their lives. On the one hand, redistribution of income and wealth through taxes and transfers. On the other, the deliberate removal of certain goods and services from the market entirely.
This process - what he called decommodification - was linked to his conception of social and economic rights. The right to affordable housing and dental care, for example, or to a pension, or even for a worker to unionize, were rights Ed believed ought to be guaranteed rights of citizenship. Being rights, not privileges, they should be available to everyone, just like voting and freedom of assembly are.
"If you have, for example, decommodified childcare or pharmacare," Ed wrote in his recent book, "then a working family doesn't have to worry about getting the income to pay for those things. This advances equality." If you provide goods and services based on a notion of rights rather than capacity to pay, then you liberate people "to make decisions in their lives - to engage in recreation, volunteer work, hobbies, or anything else they choose - because they don't have to struggle to work for an income just to meet their basic human needs."
Ed's vision, however, was far more expansive than merely liberal democracy with a generous welfare state and a dash of public intervention in the economy. True democracy required an activist state engaged in industrial policy, and radical changes to workplace power dynamics, too. Ed advocated for robust worker rights, including influence over management decision making; automatic unionization and access to collective bargaining; and a strong labour movement that could countervail the power of capital and governments.
These ideas were not mere abstractions for Ed. They were informed by constituents he represented for decades in Oshawa, autoworkers at the mercy of management decisions to relocate, trade agreements that eviscerated jobs and undercut workers' bargaining power. They were connected to his concern for individual freedom, his preoccupation with people having control over decisions that affect them where they spend the majority of their waking lives: their workplace.
Ed's roots in the working-class trade unionism of his hometown imbued a genuine quality to his class politics. He always knew who he was fighting for. And his determined pursuit of power was never untethered from those constituencies and their struggles. Perhaps that's why Ed's appeal to "ordinary Canadians" contrasts so sharply with the ersatz appeals to the working class of a figure like Pierre Poilievre.
He believed that ordinary people, poor and working-class people, knew things he did not. And thus that they were instrumental in how an NDP government under his leadership would wield power.
Ed had respect for other political traditions than his own. His engagement with the viewpoints of opponents and critics was authentic, and held true whether coming from his right or left.
He engaged friends, colleagues, activists, foes and passersby with both a curiosity and seriousness rooted in this respect.
This approach to democratic leadership is what allowed his politics to evolve. He credited social movements and caucus colleagues for stretching the horizons of his conception of rights to incorporate cultural and group rights, including linguistic rights and Indigenous rights. Over the course of his leadership, political demands from the peace movement, the queer rights struggle, women's liberation movements, Quebec nationalists and various movements for racial equality became part of the NDP's political program of fighting inequality.
Ed's evolution on rights laid the foundation for what was perhaps his most profound impact on the country - his crucial role in securing Aboriginal rights in Section 35 of the Charter of Rights.
He came to champion addressing the context-specific barriers and oppression certain groups face. He viewed it as a necessity in any serious pursuit of equality. But dismantling systems of oppression did not mean abandoning the class struggle. In this, Ed was a trenchant critic of the shallower form of identity politics en vogue today.
"Neoliberalism can incorporate a lot of rights, so long as the primacy of the market mechanism is not threatened," he wrote in his book. "I find this to be a wholly inadequate response to today's conditions ... The battles today over trans rights, or barriers to abortion access, for example, matter immensely for freedom and equality. But it remains my firm belief that substantive equality still requires a fundamental change in the distribution of power and wealth in society along class lines."
For Ed, altering the inequitable distribution of power could not be limited to politics within national borders. One of the lesser-known dimensions of Ed was that he was a deeply committed internationalist who practised solidarity. He spent nearly two decades as an active member of the Socialist International (SI), 10 of those as a vice-president working closely with its then president, former West German chancellor Willy Brandt.
At the SI, Ed was engaged in important diplomatic efforts in Central America, advancing human rights and advocating for social movements against an imposing current of repressive U.S. foreign policy often arming and propping up violent military regimes.
He also made a major impact as the founding president of the International Centre for Human Rights and Democratic Development. There, his commitment to social and economic rights and the international covenants meant to enforce them would butt up against the tidal forces of global free trade and corporate-driven international development priorities.
Under his leadership, the now-shuttered centre fought against the grain and took on thorny issues in the process, advocating for trade unionists in China, garment worker rights in South Asia, labour and environmental protections in global trade agreements, and against Israeli settlement expansion in the occupied territories in Palestine.
Ed's contributions to public life, both intellectually and as a democratic leader, have no real comparator in Canada. His is a rich legacy of socialism, pluralism and internationalism grounded in the belief that ordinary people, through their collective action, are a motive force in social change.
So while Ed most certainly deserves to be remembered for his character, his trustworthiness and his warmth, let's also remember him for his powerful values and ideals.
CAPTION: ed broadbent.JPGEd Broadbent sits quietly smoking his pipe during an open forum held by the NDP caucus in Port Coquitlam, on Oct. 18, 1980.
CREDIT: Jonathan Sas, Special to the Star

Document 351 of 500

Devastating cyberattack on Toronto Public Library shows how much it still means to the city

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/devastating-cyberattack-on-toronto-public-library/docview/2919137567/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Devastating+cyberattack+on+Toronto+Public+Library+shows+how+much+it+still+means+to+the+city&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Back in November, I was in North York and needed to get some work done so I opened my laptop at a café. Needing more time and not wanting to buy another coffee, I walked over to the North York Central Library.
One of Toronto's largest libraries, it's ingeniously concealed in a big office complex next to Mel Lastman Square thanks to architect Raymond Moriyama's excellent design. I went up to the sixth (top) floor and found a seat by the windows overlooking the square and got to work for a few hours.
If this was an actual office it would be expensive "Class A" space with the view and prime location, but it was entirely free to use. Still, I pretended I was a little executive with a killer office.
As the afternoon ticked on, that top floor filled with teenagers. School had let out and instead of roaming the streets, playing video games, smoking cigarettes, or doing whatever clichés teenagers are accused of doing, they came to the library. Hundreds of them, I'd estimate, spread out on all floors. Some alone, with a friend or even bigger groups that took over some of the private, glassed-in, rooms. There was chatter, but it looked like a lot of work was getting done, too.
It was so busy and buzzing that I had to remind myself that the Toronto Public Library had, only weeks before, suffered a debilitating ransomware cyberattack on its computer systems, shutting down some vital functions. It was so serious that even now the library is still recovering from the attack but on that day in the depths of it, you might not know something happened at first glance.
On my way out, I started to notice librarians helping people find the information and items they needed without the aid of computers. Books, journals, magazines and even newspapers: a visit to the library shows they're still a thing, and so are various other kinds of print resources, many being circulated the old school way. As of last week, the TPL says, "845,000 physical items and 2.8 million ebooks, audiobooks and magazines (have been) borrowed since the cyberattack." Others, like me, were content to just connect to the still-working Wi-Fi.
This is not to say librarians had an easy time of this. On the contrary, it's an endless headache and library employees had their personal data compromised, which may have long-lasting effects. Yet, they stayed open and carried on. The space itself matters as much as some of the services that were suspended, though they do matter too.
A few weeks earlier, I experienced the effects of the attack when I couldn't use my local library to print off a few documents, an infrequently used service but one that has let me avoid the hassle and expense of maintaining a printer for the few times electronic documents won't suffice.
That administrative inconvenience was a window into all the other crucial services the library provides people, like computers to look for and apply for jobs, programs or housing and simply communicate with people, all life-changing or life-sustaining things.
All of that happens next to people who just want some place to be that doesn't cost money, especially teens and older folk, people who are outside of the most "productive" times of life and are, in a way, left out of society. When you're making or spending money, it's easy to find places to belong.
With the work-from-home revolution, many people lucky enough to do so are discovering how nice it is to have flexibility. What many will find, as I have as a longtime freelancer, is working from home gets extremely lonely sometimes. Simply getting out of the house and being around people, even strangers, helps keep the mood and productivity up. This can happen in cafes or even food courts, but even if your coffee or drink preference is on the cheaper side, it still adds up quickly, especially over the last few years as prices have inflated.
These are so-called "third spaces": not work and not home. The library is a third space but without much fanfare, without the earnestness that sometimes comes with the creation of community spaces. Those are good things, but the library just is what it is. In the tech world or "innovation" sectors something like the library might be branded a "co-working space," but let's never do that. It's just the library.
As the city of Toronto formulates its budget priorities this week, the library is some of the best value for the money. Though increasingly a fringe sentiment, every now and then somebody asks: "what good are libraries for in a digital world?" Rob and Doug Ford tried to cut the libraries too. The ransomware attack is proof they still matter a great deal to people.
As long as the building stands, the library "works" and is undefeated.
CAPTION: The North York Central Library, pictured in 2019.As the city ofToronto formulates its budget prioritiesthis week, the library is some of the best value for the money, writes Shawn Micallef.

Subject: Librarians; Public libraries; Ransomware

Document 352 of 500

Doug Ford's Tories are ahead, but ominous signs are on the horizon, poll suggests

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-tories-are-ahead-ominous-signs-on/docview/2919137481/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+Tories+are+ahead%2C+but+ominous+signs+are+on+the+horizon%2C+poll+suggests&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's governing Progressive Conservatives appear to be holding their own, but there are some potentially ominous signs for the party, a new poll suggests.
The latest Abacus Data tracking poll found the Tories at 38 per cent with Bonnie Crombie's Liberals at 27 per cent, Marit Stiles' New Democrats at 23 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at five per cent.
While the January findings are virtually the same as the December results, trouble may be lurking for the Tories when undecided voters are factored in, said Abacus president David Coletto.
When asked for their "current provincial vote intention," 29 per cent of respondents said PC, 21 per cent Liberal, 18 per cent NDP, four per cent Green, five per cent another party while 23 per cent were undecided.
"That undecided vote is high," said Coletto, noting in his January federal poll only 15 per cent of respondents were undecided.
"Far more of those (provincial) undecideds say they voted PC last time than for the other parties," he said.
Indeed, 22 per cent of undecided voters said they cast Tory ballots in the 2022 election with 14 per cent voting Liberal, eight per cent NDP, two per cent Green, one per cent another party and 53 per cent either couldn't remember or didn't vote.
"That 22 per cent (of former Tory voters now undecided) represents five per cent of eligible voters," said Coletto.
The pollster said that means the race could dramatically tighten if those undecided voters "were to migrate to the Liberals or the New Democrats."
Using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform, Abacus surveyed 995 Ontarians Jan. 18 to 23. Although opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Fewer than one-third - 30 per cent - had a positive impression of Ford while almost half - 49 per cent - had a negative impression with 19 per cent neutral and 23 per cent didn't know enough to say.
That's a net impression rating for the premier of minus 19 per cent.
In contrast, the NDP's Stiles had a 26 per cent positive, 22 per cent negative, 29 per cent neutral and 23 per cent unsure for a net rating of plus four per cent.
Similarly, the Liberals' Crombie was 28 per cent positive, 25 per cent negative, 26 per cent neutral and 22 per cent unsure for a net rating of plus three per cent.
For the Greens' Schreiner, it was 19 per cent positive, 16 per cent negative, 40 per cent neutral and 25 per cent unsure for a net rating of plus three per cent.
Coletto said that indicates the multimillion-dollar advertising blitz the Tories launched against Crombie after she won the Dec. 2 Liberal leadership has so far not resonated with voters.
"It doesn't appear that the attack ads have yet had any impact on her brand," he said.
In a bid to negatively define Crombie, the Tories unleashed TV, radio and online ads criticizing the former Mississauga mayor after she took the Liberal helm.
That's because Ford's party fears she could pose an existential threat to their stronghold in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area over the past two elections.
Breaking down voting intentions in GTHA ridings, Coletto pointed out the Tories were at 37 per cent compared to 32 per cent for the Liberals, 23 per cent for the NDP, five per cent for the Greens and three per cent for other parties.
"It's worth noting that that's where the gap seems to have closed," he said, adding the surprise departure Thursday of former minister of red tape reduction Parm Gill in Milton will not help the provincial Tories in the 905.
Gill resigned in order to join federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's surging team for a national election widely expected next year.
Crombie, who does not have a seat in the legislature, has yet to decide if she will run in a Milton byelection that Ford must call within six months.
The Liberals believe there could be vacancies in Mississauga or Brampton if other ambitious Tory MPPs abandon Ford for Poilievre.
Coletto stressed the Tories, even after the Greenbelt land swap scandal, "are still in a strong position, though there is evidence of vulnerabilities."
In the November Abacus poll - before Crombie won the Grit leadership - the PCs were at 42 per cent, the NDP at 24 per cent, the Liberals at 23 per cent and the Greens at seven per cent.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford gives remarks during a media preview of the Bombardier Aircraft Assembly Centre in Mississauga, Ont., Jan. 22, 2024.

Document 353 of 500

10,000 Ontario patients cut loose because of staffing shortages

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/10-000-ontario-patients-cut-loose-because/docview/2919137471/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=10%2C000+Ontario+patients+cut+loose+because+of+staffing+shortages&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's growing shortage of doctors and nurse practitioners is hitting full force in Sault Ste. Marie, where a major medical centre is reluctantly cutting 10,000 patients loose at the end of May.
After months of approaches to the Ministry of Health for help, the Group Health Centre issued an open letter to the community warning patients whose primary-care providers have "recently retired or left their practices" will have to seek treatment elsewhere.
"We recognize the profound effect this will have," says the letter from the centre, which will continue to serve 50,000 patients and has been unable to recruit enough replacement medical staff in recent years.
"This has led us to the critical point we find ourselves at today."
Dr. Jodie Stewart, chief executive officer of the Algoma District Medical Group of doctors at the centre's eight locations, said the dramatic move is a last resort in the northern Ontario city of 73,000.
"We know what happens when people don't have good primary care. It's essential to a healthy community," she told the Star.
The centre, founded in 1963 and long hailed as an innovative model for offering a wide range of health-care services and professionals, has 31 doctors left, including some who have come out of retirement.
While Stewart said the clinic has submitted proposals to the Ministry of Health since last spring - seeking funding for incentive payments, and retention and recruiting of doctors and nurse practitioners - the office of Health Minister Sylvia Jones said it was only recently informed of the plan to chop patients from the roster.
"Group Health Care notified the ministry of these changes just over a week ago," said spokesperson Hannah Jensen.
"While the ministry has made several attempts to work with GHC on a possible solution, finding an immediate replacement for these patients on such short notice was not achievable."
Jensen added the government is about to launch the largest expansion of primary-care teams in the province's history, "including two new sites in Sault Ste. Marie" with details coming "soon."
"We will continue to work with partners in Sault Ste. Marie, and across the province, to ensure primary care is available to people where and when they need it."
The primary-care teams are intended to support doctors with other medical and social service professionals providing comprehensive services to patients.
Ontario Medical Association president Dr. Andrew Park, an emergency physician in London, said it was only a matter of time before the growing shortage of doctors hit hard in a big way.
"Ten thousand in one day is spectacular for all the wrong reasons," he told the Star, describing the situation in Sault Ste. Marie as "unprecedented."
"But it's going to become more common."
About 2.3 million Ontarians in a province of 15 million do not have access to a primary-care physician, with two in five people on track to be without a family doctor in the next two years, the OMA says.
Forty per cent of family doctors say they are considering retirement in the next five years, according to an OMA survey of its members.
Park said doctors are doing an average of 19 hours a week of administrative tasks such as filling out insurance forms and other paperwork, which is a "nonsensical" disincentive to stay in the field.
The situation in Sault Ste. Marie is more proof the health-care system is broken and in need of "urgent action" from Premier Doug Ford, said Liberal MPP and health critic Adil Shamji (Don Valley East), an emergency room physician who has worked in remote and northern communities.
"Rural health care is in crisis with rampant ER (emergency room) closures, staffing shortages and no access to primary care," he said, noting stranded patients have limited options.
"When a clinic like the one in Sault Ste. Marie says 'we need help and funding to keep our doors open,' the government needs to say 'yes.'"
In its open letter, the Group Health Centre further outlined its predicament.
"The rate of new (health-care) providers entering the field has not kept pace with retirements, and many existing primary-care providers face burnout," officials wrote.
"Despite many proactive efforts, including extensive recruitment initiatives (locally and internationally), securing temporary providers, delaying retirements and re-engaging retired professionals, this situation is no longer sustainable."
"Our immediate focus is to stabilize our operations and continue to provide care to 50,000 patients in our community. We look forward to working with our community partners and the Ministry of Health to build additional capacity for the future."
Sault Ste. Marie Mayor Matthew Shoemaker said on social media the news from the clinic is a discouraging "step back" for the community and called for more provincial support.
CAPTION: Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones makes an announcement on health care with Premier Doug Ford in Toronto on Jan. 16, 2023.

Subject: Health care access; Workforce planning; Nurse practitioners; Primary care; Emergency medical care; Retirement; Labor shortages; Physicians; Northern communities
