In tribute to Ed Broadbent, socialist: Longtime NDP leader had the common touch and an eye for concrete help for regular Canadians

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tribute-ed-broadbent-socialist/docview/2918963195/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=In+tribute+to+Ed+Broadbent%2C+socialist%3A+Longtime+NDP+leader+had+the+common+touch+and+an+eye+for+concrete+help+for+regular+Canadians&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A state funeral for Ed Broadbent, federal NDP leader for 14 years,
is to be held Sunday in Ottawa. His friend and co-author pays tribute.
Ed Broadbent's integrity, his decency and kindness have animated the outpouring of admiration from across the political spectrum since news of his passing. It is recognition he duly deserves.
I met Ed in 2013, an earnest new policy director at the nascent think tank the Broadbent Institute he then chaired. We became friends (or comrades, as we joked), and formed a bond that would eventually lead to working together on his remarkable new book, "Seeking Social Democracy: Seven Decades in the Fight for Equality."
That Ed was adored by many was clear anytime I was in public with him. On street corners, in cafes or in newsrooms, a man who had left the political limelight decades ago still engendered such warmth and excitement. Ed didn't quite understand people's desire for selfies; he much preferred to talk to them and hear their concerns and viewpoints.
I have no doubt Ed would appreciate the earnest affection and respect being expressed for his leadership style. But for Ed, the politics of personality were secondary to ideology and the importance of political ideas.
On Nov. 1, Ed gave what would turn out to be his last interview to a national audience. On CBC's "The Current," host Matt Galloway asked Ed whether he still considered himself a socialist.
"Yes," he replied bluntly.
His enduring socialist convictions should come as no surprise. Ed was remarkably consistent through nearly seven decades of interventions in public life.
He was a social democrat (or democratic socialist, he used the two terms interchangeably) because he believed that human freedom and liberty could only flourish on a foundation of substantive equality. His task as leader was to persuade citizens to vote for a social democratic party so that state power could be put in service of building a good society.
I have been reflecting since his death on what drew me so strongly to Ed's ideas when I first encountered them in the aughts.
I came of age in the '90s in what is sometimes referred to as the neoliberal age. An era when the free market and the dynamism of the private sector were assumed to be the answer to all that ails us. A time when government was cast as a wasteful and costly intruder that constrained our individual freedom, unions an anachronistic drag on economic growth.
In Ontario where I grew up, Jean Chrétien's austerity and Mike Harris's "Common Sense Revolution" meant the gutting of health and environmental regulations, tax cuts for the wealthy, deep cuts to social assistance, health care and education, and the selling off of public assets and services to the private sector.
Questions about whether these decisions being made were broadly good, or fair, or just took a back seat to whether they were efficient, drove "growth," and whether they advanced the purchasing power of individual consumers and taxpayers.
To those in power, the disastrous effects of this program on people - poverty, inequality, homelessness, the erosion of public trust - was the inevitable if unfortunate result of a lack of personal ambition and resourcefulness.
I found this world view blinkered, selfish, disingenuous.
Ed Broadbent was like a tonic to me and many in my disillusioned generation. Here was someone who, throughout the neoliberal era, refused to accept that inequality was ineluctable.
His belief in universal social programs, his championing of unions and critique of corporate power, his emphasis on the importance of class; all were ideas that felt urgent and refreshing.
New to me, they were grounded in a long and proud tradition to which Ed devoted his life.
For Ed, democratic socialism meant waging a constant battle against the inequality-producing tendencies of the market. It meant institutions that were democratically accountable shaping markets to serve the needs of people not private interests.
Ed was clear-eyed about what was required to pursue greater equality and give people the dignity and resources necessary to enjoy their lives. On the one hand, redistribution of income and wealth through taxes and transfers. On the other, the deliberate removal of certain goods and services from the market entirely.
This process - what he called decommodification - was linked to his conception of social and economic rights. The right to affordable housing and dental care, for example, or to a pension, or even for a worker to unionize, were rights Ed believed ought to be guaranteed rights of citizenship. Being rights, not privileges, they should be available to everyone, just like voting and freedom of assembly are.
"If you have, for example, decommodified child care or pharmacare," Ed wrote in his recent book, "then a working family doesn't have to worry about getting the income to pay for those things. This advances equality." If you provide goods and services based on a notion of rights rather than capacity to pay, then you liberate people "to make decisions in their lives - to engage in recreation, volunteer work, hobbies, or anything else they choose - because they don't have to struggle to work for an income just to meet their basic human needs."
Ed's vision, however, was far more expansive than merely liberal democracy with a generous welfare state and a dash of public intervention in the economy. True democracy required an activist state engaged in industrial policy, and radical changes to workplace power dynamics, too. Ed advocated for robust worker rights, including influence over management decision making; automatic unionization and access to collective bargaining; and a strong labour movement that could countervail the power of capital and governments.
These ideas were not mere abstractions for Ed. They were informed by constituents he represented for decades in Oshawa, autoworkers at the mercy of management decisions to relocate, trade agreements that eviscerated jobs and undercut workers' bargaining power. They were connected to his concern for individual freedom, his preoccupation with people having control over decisions that affect them where they spend the majority of their waking lives: their workplace.
Ed's roots in the working-class trade unionism of his hometown imbued a genuine quality to his class politics. He always knew who he was fighting for. And his determined pursuit of power was never untethered from those constituencies and their struggles. Perhaps that's why Ed's appeal to "ordinary Canadians" contrasts so sharply with the ersatz appeals to the working class of a figure like Pierre Poilievre.
He believed that ordinary people, poor and working-class people, knew things he did not. And thus that they were instrumental in how an NDP government under his leadership would wield power.
Ed had respect for other political traditions than his own. His engagement with the viewpoints of opponents and critics was authentic, and held true whether coming from his right or left.
He engaged friends, colleagues, activists, foes and passersby with both a curiosity and seriousness rooted in this respect.
This approach to democratic leadership is what allowed his politics to evolve. He credited social movements and caucus colleagues for stretching the horizons of his conception of rights to incorporate cultural and group rights, including linguistic rights and Indigenous rights. Over the course of his leadership, political demands from the peace movement, the queer rights struggle, women's liberation movements, Quebec nationalists and various movements for racial equality became part of the NDP's political program of fighting inequality.
Ed's evolution on rights laid the foundation for what was perhaps his most profound impact on the country - his crucial role in securing Aboriginal rights in Section 35 of the Charter of Rights.
He came to champion addressing the context-specific barriers and oppression certain groups face. He viewed it as a necessity in any serious pursuit of equality. But dismantling systems of oppression did not mean abandoning the class struggle. In this, Ed was a trenchant critic of the shallower form of identity politics en vogue today.
"Neoliberalism can incorporate a lot of rights, so long as the primacy of the market mechanism is not threatened," he wrote in his book. "I find this to be a wholly inadequate response to today's conditions ... The battles today over trans rights, or barriers to abortion access, for example, matter immensely for freedom and equality. But it remains my firm belief that substantive equality still requires a fundamental change in the distribution of power and wealth in society along class lines."
For Ed, altering the inequitable distribution of power could not be limited to politics within national borders. One of the lesser-known dimensions of Ed was that he was a deeply committed internationalist who practised solidarity. He spent nearly two decades as an active member of the Socialist International (SI), 10 of those as a vice-president working closely with its then president, former West German chancellor Willy Brandt.
At the SI, Ed was engaged in important diplomatic efforts in Central America, advancing human rights and advocating for social movements against an imposing current of repressive U.S. foreign policy often arming and propping up violent military regimes.
He also made a major impact as the founding president of the International Centre for Human Rights and Democratic Development. There, his commitment to social and economic rights and the international covenants meant to enforce them would butt up against the tidal forces of global free trade and corporate-driven international development priorities.
Under his leadership, the now-shuttered centre fought against the grain and took on thorny issues in the process, advocating for trade unionists in China, garment worker rights in South Asia, labour and environmental protections in global trade agreements, and against Israeli settlement expansion in the occupied territories in Palestine.
Ed's contributions to public life, both intellectually and as a democratic leader, have no real comparator in Canada. His is a rich legacy of socialism, pluralism and internationalism grounded in the belief that ordinary people, through their collective action, are a motive force in social change.
So while Ed most certainly deserves to be remembered for his character, his trustworthiness and his warmth, let's also remember him for his powerful values and ideals.
Jonathan Sas is a co-author with Ed Broadbent, Frances Abele and Luke Savage of "Seeking Social Democracy: Seven Decades in the Fight for Equality" available now from ECW Press.
CAPTION: Ed Broadbent's contributions to public life, both intellectually and as a democratic leader, have no real equal in Canada, writes his friend Jonathan Sas.ANDY CLARKThe Canadian Pressfile photo
CREDIT: Jonathan Sas Special to the Star Jonathan Sas is a co-author with Ed Broadbent, Frances Abele and Luke Savage of "Seeking Social Democracy: Seven Decades in the Fight for Equality" available now from ECW Press.

Subject: Working class; Political activism; Native rights; Inequality; Oppression; Democracy; Neoliberalism; Political leadership; Collective bargaining; Decision making; Social activism; Equal rights; Political parties

Document 355 of 500

Library cyberattack proves how much it still means to us

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/library-cyberattack-proves-how-much-still-means/docview/2918963164/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Library+cyberattack+proves+how+much+it+still+means+to+us&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Back in November, I was in North York and needed to get some work done so I opened my laptop at a café. Needing more time and not wanting to buy another coffee, I walked over to the North York Central Library.
One of Toronto's largest libraries, it's ingeniously concealed in a big office complex next to Mel Lastman Square thanks to architect Raymond Moriyama's excellent design. I went up to the sixth (top) floor and found a seat by the windows overlooking the square and got to work for a few hours.
If this was an actual office, it would be expensive "Class A" space with the view and prime location, but it was entirely free to use. Still, I pretended I was a little executive with a killer office.
As the afternoon ticked on, that top floor filled with teenagers. School had let out and instead of roaming the streets, playing video games, smoking cigarettes or doing whatever clichés teenagers are accused of doing, they came to the library. Hundreds of them, I'd estimate, spread out on all floors. Some alone, with a friend or even bigger groups that took over some of the private, glassed-in rooms. There was chatter, but it looked like a lot of work was getting done, too.
It was so busy and buzzing that I had to remind myself that the Toronto Public Library had, only weeks before, suffered a debilitating ransomware cyberattack on its computer systems, shutting down some vital functions. It was so serious that even now the library is still recovering from the attack, but on that day in the depths of it, you might not know something happened at first glance.
On my way out, I started to notice librarians helping people find the information and items they needed without the aid of computers. Books, journals, magazines and even newspapers: a visit to the library shows they're still a thing, and so are various other kinds of print resources, many being circulated the old-school way.
As of last week, the TPL says, "845,000 physical items and 2.8 million ebooks, audiobooks and magazines (have been) borrowed since the cyberattack." Others, like me, were content to just connect to the still-working Wi-Fi.
This is not to say librarians had an easy time of this. On the contrary, it's an endless headache, and library employees had their personal data compromised, which may have long-lasting effects.
Yet, they stayed open and carried on. The space itself matters as much as some of the services that were suspended, though they do matter, too.
A few weeks earlier, I experienced the effects of the attack when I couldn't use my local library to print off a few documents, an infrequently used service but one that has let me avoid the hassle and expense of maintaining a printer for the few times electronic documents won't suffice.
That administrative inconvenience was a window into all the other crucial services the library provides people, like computers to look for and apply for jobs, programs for housing, and to simply communicate with people, all life-changing or life-sustaining things.
All of that happens next to people who just want some place to be that doesn't cost money, especially teens and older folk, people who are outside of the most "productive" times of life and are, in a way, left out of society. When you're making or spending money, it's easy to find places to belong.
With the work-from-home revolution, many people lucky enough to do so are discovering how nice it is to have flexibility. What many will find, as I have as a longtime freelancer, is working from home gets extremely lonely sometimes. Simply getting out of the house and being around people, even strangers, helps keep the mood and productivity up. This can happen in cafés or even food courts, but even if your coffee or drink preference is on the cheaper side, it still adds up quickly, especially over the past few years as prices have inflated.
These are so-called "third spaces": not work and not home. The library is a third space, but without much fanfare, without the earnestness that sometimes comes with the creation of community spaces. Those are good things, but the library just is what it is. In the tech world or "innovation" sectors, something like the library might be branded a "co-working space," but let's never do that. It's just the library.
As the City of Toronto formulates its budget priorities this week, the library is some of the best value for the money. Though increasingly a fringe sentiment, every now and then somebody asks: "What good are libraries for in a digital world?" Rob and Doug Ford tried to cut the libraries, too. The ransomware attack is proof they still matter a great deal to people.
As long as the building stands, the library "works" and is undefeated.
CREDIT: Shawn Micallef OPINION

Subject: Librarians; Libraries; Ransomware

Business indexing term: Subject: Ransomware

Location: Toronto Ontario Canada; Canada

People: Moriyama, Raymond

Trouble lurking for Ford's Tories?: New poll suggests party in lead, but undecided voters could change things

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trouble-lurking-fords-tories/docview/2918963102/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trouble+lurking+for+Ford%27s+Tories%3F%3A+New+poll+suggests+party+in+lead%2C+but+undecided+voters+could+change+things&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's governing Progressive Conservatives appear to be holding their own, but there are some potentially ominous signs for the party, a new poll suggests.
The latest Abacus Data tracking poll found the Tories at 38 per cent with Bonnie Crombie's Liberals at 27 per cent, Marit Stiles' New Democrats at 23 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at five per cent.
While the January findings are virtually the same as the December results, trouble may be lurking for the Tories when undecided voters are factored in, said Abacus president David Coletto.
When asked for their "current provincial vote intention," 29 per cent of respondents said PC, 21 per cent Liberal, 18 per cent NDP, four per cent Green, five per cent another party while 23 per cent were undecided. "That undecided vote is high," said Coletto, noting in his January federal poll only 15 per cent of respondents were undecided.
"Far more of those (provincial) undecideds say they voted PC last time than for the other parties," he said.
Indeed, 22 per cent of undecided voters said they cast Tory ballots in the 2022 election with 14 per cent voting Liberal, eight per cent NDP, two per cent Green, one per cent another party and 53 per cent either couldn't remember or didn't vote. "That 22 per cent (of former Tory voters now undecided) represents five per cent of eligible voters," said Coletto. The pollster said that means the race could dramatically tighten if those undecided voters "were to migrate to the Liberals or the New Democrats."
Using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform, Abacus surveyed 995 Ontarians Jan. 18 to 23. Although opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Fewer than one-third - 30 per cent - had a positive impression of Ford while almost half - 49 per cent - had a negative impression with 19 per cent neutral and 23 per cent didn't know enough to say.
That's a net impression rating for the premier of minus 19 per cent.
In contrast, the NDP's Stiles had a 26 per cent positive, 22 per cent negative, 29 per cent neutral and 23 per cent unsure for a net rating of plus four per cent.
Similarly, the Liberals' Crombie was 28 per cent positive, 25 per cent negative, 26 per cent neutral and 22 per cent unsure for a net rating of plus three per cent.
For the Greens' Schreiner, it was 19 per cent positive, 16 per cent negative, 40 per cent neutral and 25 per cent unsure for a net rating of plus three per cent.
Coletto said that indicates the multimillion-dollar advertising blitz the Tories launched against Crombie after she won the Dec. 2 Liberal leadership has so far not resonated with voters. "It doesn't appear that the attack ads have yet had any impact on her brand," he said.
In a bid to negatively define Crombie, the Tories unleashed TV, radio and online ads criticizing the former Mississauga mayor after she took the Liberal helm.
That's because Ford's party fears she could pose an existential threat to their stronghold in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area over the past two elections.
Breaking down voting intentions in GTHA ridings, Coletto pointed out the Tories were at 37 per cent compared to 32 per cent for the Liberals, 23 per cent for the NDP, five per cent for the Greens and three per cent for other parties.
"It's worth noting that that's where the gap seems to have closed," he said, adding the surprise departure Thursday of former minister of red tape reduction Parm Gill in Milton will not help the provincial Tories in the 905.
Gill resigned in order to join federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's surging team for a national election widely expected next year.
Crombie, who does not have a seat in the legislature, has yet to decide if she will run in a Milton byelection that Ford must call within six months.
The Liberals believe there could be vacancies in Mississauga or Brampton if other ambitious Tory MPPs abandon Ford for Poilievre.
Coletto stressed the Tories, even after the Greenbelt land swap scandal, "are still in a strong position, though there is evidence of vulnerabilities."
In the November Abacus poll - before Crombie won the Grit leadership - the PCs were at 42 per cent, the NDP at 24 per cent, the Liberals at 23 per cent and the Greens at seven per cent.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 357 of 500

Caught between the red and the blue: Singh's NDP eyes gains in Tory stronghold, but 'uncomfortable' Liberal alliance poses challenge

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/caught-between-red-blue/docview/2918963075/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Caught+between+the+red+and+the+blue%3A+Singh%27s+NDP+eyes+gains+in+Tory+stronghold%2C+but+%27uncomfortable%27+Liberal+alliance+poses+challenge&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On the surface, it was a dream canvassing scenario.
As the NDP's Jagmeet Singh and a group of MPs and supporters knocked on doors in the highly sought-after riding of Edmonton Centre, a middle-aged man and his young adult son spotted the leader from afar and hurried across a strip mall parking lot to say hello.
The man, who preferred only to be identified by his first name, Craig, spoke to Singh and the NDP's candidate in the area about his lifelong ties to the New Democrats. His son, meanwhile, asked Singh the most-asked question that came up on doorsteps: what could the NDP leader do for people fed up with trying to find a place to live?
"I hear you, OK?" Singh said. "Housing. We're working on it."
The foursome enthusiastically snapped a photo before parting ways in the biting cold.
But as Singh turned the corner and walked out of sight, Craig had a confession to make.
"Unfortunately, I just think the country, in the state it's in right now, means I can't support the Liberals," he told the Star.
"And if the NDP are supporting the Liberals, I can't support the NDP either."
In many ways, Craig, a local tradesman who has long admired the NDP's championing of the working class, is exactly the sort of voter the New Democrats were hoping to encounter when they chose to hold their winter caucus gathering in Edmonton this past week. Growing up in Ontario, Craig's family would sometimes host notable New Democrats, like former MP Svend Robinson, when they passed through town. When it was time for him to cast his first ballot, his vote went to them.
In a province like Alberta, though, any connection to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is a curse. After the NDP struck a governing deal with him, Craig's faith faltered. And while he hasn't written Singh off completely, he's willing to turn to Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives to ease the country's burdens.
The question of how to position the NDP in such a political landscape arose at the retreat, where Singh and party insiders hashed out their political priorities before Parliament's return.
"We know people need us because our job has always been to make Ottawa work for people," Singh said at the gathering's close.
"We have shown that we can use our power to get people help."
When MPs and the party's top brass weren't stuck behind closed doors, they were trekking across Edmonton to chat with prospective voters and meet with housing experts, union groups, health-care workers and First Nations leaders. It's part of the NDP's nationwide strategy to boost its seat count by planting itself as the only answer to the much-maligned Liberals and increasingly popular Conservatives.
In Edmonton - where the Liberals hold one seat, the NDP two, and the Conservatives six - the New Democrats see a perfect opening.
"I think there's a lot that's unique about Alberta, and Edmonton in particular, right now," said Anne McGrath, who recently stepped into the new role of Singh's principal secretary.
McGrath, previously the party's national director, moved into the position so she could play a larger role in advising Singh and overseeing the party's governing agreement with the minority Liberals. The supply-and-confidence agreement, as it's known, could see the NDP prop up Trudeau's government until mid-2025 in exchange for action on progressive policies, such as national dental care and pharmacare programs.
Serving as principal secretary is also a job title she held when she worked with Alberta NDP Leader Rachel Notley, when Notley ended more than four decades of Progressive Conservative rule in 2015 by becoming the first New Democratic premier of the province.
"I do think it's very significant that every single seat in Edmonton, with no exception, is an NDP seat provincially," McGrath said of the Alberta NDP's 2023 showing, which failed to return Notley to power but saw her party take in its highest-ever share of the vote.
Indeed, Notley's successes in Edmonton have Singh's NDP casting an optimistic eye to a city that historically tends to veer blue in federal races.
"We're seeing the momentum change and we're seeing the opportunity to build in Edmonton," said Edmonton Strathcona MP Heather McPherson.
"Even in the last election, the number of people who chose to vote for New Democrats maybe only went to two seats, but the number of people - the percentage of that vote - just increased."
Edmonton Griesbach's Blake Desjarlais, the province's other NDP MP, succeeded in wresting the riding from the Conservatives in 2021. He credits the Alberta NDP both with sparking his interest in politics and with building the networks he needed to win.
Part of that network included Notley, who campaigned with Desjarlais door to door.
McGrath said she's "positive" Notley will similarly help NDP candidates during the next federal election, though Notley, who announced last week she intends to end her historic reign as Alberta NDP leader, said Wednesday she hadn't given much thought as to how she might get involved.
Despite her influence in Alberta's New Democratic circles, her impending exit likely won't affect the NDP's fortunes in a province that has a less than favourable view of the federal party, said Duane Bratt, a political science professor at Calgary's Mount Royal University.
Notley - who is adamant she will not join the federal fold - has had frosty relations with the federal party at times over differing positions on climate and energy policies. During the province's election campaign last year, Premier Danielle Smith revelled in falsely painting Notley as a leader controlled by Trudeau and Singh in an attempt to discredit her chief rival. And in late 2023, a former Alberta NDP cabinet minister launched a call for the provincial party to change its name, arguing that too many people believe - albeit incorrectly - that Singh has high levels of influence over Notley.
Those tensions are reflected in the numbers, said Abacus Data CEO David Coletto.
"Provincewide, 28 per cent of Albertans have a positive view of Jagmeet Singh. Forty-six per cent have a negative view. His negatives are higher than in any other region or province, and his positives are lower," said Coletto, who drew his findings from online surveys conducted from Oct. 23, 2023 to Jan. 9, 2024 with a representative sample of 990 Albertan adults. (A comparable margin of error of a random survey with a sample of that size would be 3.2 per cent, 19 times out of 20.)
"If you look at that and you're holding your caucus retreat in Alberta, it's the place where he's the least popular right now."
Sometimes, that's not immediately obvious.
In Edmonton Centre, a Liberal riding that has previously gone to the Conservatives but never the NDP, Singh was approached in the streets by locals eager to congratulate him on the birth of his second daughter, and he drew more than a hundred supporters to a passionate community centre town hall.
The New Democrats have high hopes that their next candidate, former Edmonton Public School Board chair Trisha Estabrooks, will turn that energy into an electoral gain.
In exclusive seat projection data shared with the Star, Abacus's modelling shows that if an election were held now, the New Democrats would hold onto McPherson's seat in Edmonton Strathcona, return Edmonton Griesbach to the Conservatives, and put up a competitive fight in Edmonton Centre - but ultimately lose it to Poilievre.
(The projections, which came from data collected from October to December 2023, are based on modelling using Abacus Data's multi-level regression and poststratification (MRP) methodology.)
"I'm not sure how the NDP's policies are any different than the Liberals because they are operating in an agreement as a coalition," said James Cumming, a Conservative who has faced off against the Liberal's Randy Boissonnault in Edmonton Centre three times, won once, and is seeking the nomination again.
Those aren't just Conservative talking points.
Estabrooks said she hears much of the same from potential voters, prompting her to refer to the Liberal-NDP deal as "an uncomfortable alliance" when she knocks on doors. "I say, give it time. Look at the gains that the NDP have made because of this agreement ... it's not going to last forever," Estabrooks told the Star, though the NDP has not yet decided whether it will renew the agreement should Trudeau form another minority government.
"I find it uncomfortable because there's so much that I disagree with with the current direction of the Liberal government. And, in politics, sometimes the nuances are lost and I hear that on the doorstep sometimes: 'Oh, you've hitched your wagon to the Liberals.' There's great disdain for that," Estabrooks said.
Many in the party say they just need more time for programs launched under the deal to expand - like dental care - and for other policy proposals, like a pharmacare framework, to materialize.
"I believe that as some of the benefits start to be more and more apparent, that there will be more understanding of the fact that you can extract things from a Liberal government without being tied to that Liberal government," McGrath said of the NDP's central challenge.
"You know, that's not the easiest thing to communicate."
But if the NDP wants to appeal to voters like Craig, the local tradesman who is exhausted by Trudeau's leadership and just wishes his son could afford a place to rent, they're going to have to convey that message as powerfully as they can.
"I know it's hard for the NDP to have a voice in Parliament ... they need to make a way of getting their voice heard," he said. "But at the same time, I just don't agree with it."
CAPTION: NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh and NDP candidate for Edmonton Centre, Trisha Estabrooks, chat with a supporter while door-knocking in the Alberta capital this past week. The party sees an opportunity to boost its seat count in the province. Alberta NDP Leader Rachel Notley's successes in Edmonton have Singh's New Democrats casting an optimistic eye to a city that historically tends to veer blue in federal races.JASON FRANSON photos The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Raisa Patel Toronto Star

Subject: Agreements; Conservatism; Negative campaigning; Dental care; Voters; Elections; Political parties

People: Singh, Jagmeet

Document 358 of 500

Ruling on Emergency Act sets a dangerous precedent

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ruling-on-emergency-act-sets-dangerous-precedent/docview/2918765655/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ruling+on+Emergency+Act+sets+a+dangerous+precedent&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Trudeau government's use of Emergencies Act violated rights, Jan. 23
It speaks to the level society has fallen to when a judge rules that this was an overreach.
The capital of the country was besieged, members of Parliament were intimidated into staying away, foreign funds were used to fund and support this occupation, main border points were closed, citizens of Ottawa were harassed mentally and physically and were afraid to leave their homes.
After days of ineffective local police action in Ottawa and the total lack of action by Doug Ford's government, the federal government was required to step in.
No one knew the level to which this protest would rise, or the level of policing required to end it. The government used reasonable and measured force to subdue it and arrest its ringleaders.
The government is correct to appeal this, and it is to be sincerely hoped that wiser judicial minds prevail.
This ruling sets a dangerous precedent which will be weaponized by right wing extremists who represent a minority view in this country but would impose it by force on the rest of us.
This ruling ill serves the citizens of Canada.
Peter Bradley, Mississauga
Relief spread through the country when the protest was dismantled
There exists a huge chasm between the law and the experience of the people especially those who lived near the place taken over by the so-called "freedom convoy," between the demonstrators on the bridges in Ontario and Alberta and the people suffering the loss of their jobs.
Ask most ordinary Canadians and they would say, "Thank goodness our federal government took action to restore their shattered lives." No other government, provincial or municipal, had the courage to stand up to the bullies imposing their will on others.
Businesses closed in Ottawa - including one that feeds the poor - for three long weeks. Sleep was a commodity not experienced for three long weeks with the blowing of the truckers' horns! Trucks prevented the ordinary citizens of Ottawa from the use of their roads.
Relief spread through the country when the protest was dismantled without any deaths.
Yet the federal judge says there was no legal justification for the use of the Emergencies Act.
Bonnie Bacvar, Toronto
Federal court leaves country open to insurrectionIf a bunch of anarchists can hold our country ransom and get away with it, we are on a very slippery slope.
I am absolutely disgusted with the Federal Court's decision. Which now leaves it open for every "crackpot" to do just that - insurrection without fear of reprisal.
David Milbourne, Toronto
Ottawa Police missed an opportunity
I am angry, of course, at the "truckers" who held central Ottawa hostage for several weeks. But I am also angry at Ottawa Police who missed an opportunity.
If I had parked my VW Golf in front of Canada's Parliament buildings, I would have been ticketed every single day. Even more likely, I would have been towed and would need to liberate my vehicle, but only after paying the parking fine, towing charges and storage charges.
If all those trucks had been ticketed, daily or more often if bylaws allow, each vehicle would be looking at thousands of dollars of parking tickets. The beauty of this is parking violations are statutory offences with set fines - no trial required.
Moreover, I am sure Ottawa has idling and noise bylaws - more statutory offences. Many of these trucks were parked right in the roadway, obstructing traffic; such fines would likely be higher.
A creative police department could dream up additional infractions linked to improper fuel storage, harassment, etc. All these would amount to serious pain for each, and every trucker involved.
I am annoyed that this simple approach was missed.
Peter Bursztyn, Barrie
Proud law firm initiated Charter challenge
We are proud to be the party that initiated the application to the Federal Court challenging the government's invocation of the Emergencies Act in response to the Freedom Convoy protest. We are pleased that the Federal Court agreed with our position that Justin Trudeau's government's action in invoking the Emergencies Act was unreasonable, outside their jurisdiction and that it was a violation of all Canadians' rights and freedoms under the Charter.
Alexander Boissonneau-Lehner, Johnstone and Cowling LLP., Toronto
Awaiting the appeal
Great! The barbarians at the gate won ... pending an appeal, of course.
Patrick Stewart, Toronto
CAPTION: Protesters participating in the truck convoy protesting vaccine mandates walk near Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Jan. 29, 2022.

Subject: Emergencies; Demonstrations & protests; Parking violations

Location: Canada

Document 359 of 500

Jagmeet Singh has a problem: 'If the NDP are supporting the Liberals, I can't support the NDP'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jagmeet-singh-has-problem-if-ndp-are-supporting/docview/2918765643/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jagmeet+Singh+has+a+problem%3A+%27If+the+NDP+are+supporting+the+Liberals%2C+I+can%27t+support+the+NDP%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: EDMONTON-On the surface, it was a dream canvassing scenario.

"And if the NDP are supporting the Liberals, I can't support the NDP either."

"Even in the last election, the number of people who chose to vote for New Democrats maybe only went to two seats, but the number of people - the percentage of that vote - just increased."

His negatives are higher than in any other region or province, and his positives are lower," said Coletto, who drew his findings from online surveys conducted from Oct. 23, 2023 to Jan. 9, 2024 with a representative sample of 990 Albertan adults. (A comparable margin of error of a random survey with a sample of that size would be 3.2 per cent, 19 times out of 20.)
"If you look at that and you're holding your caucus retreat in Alberta, it's the place where he's the least popular right now."

(The projections, which came from data collected from October to December 2023, are based on modelling using Abacus Data's multi-level regression and poststratification (MRP) methodology.)

"I find it uncomfortable because there's so much that I disagree with with the current direction of the Liberal government. And, in politics, sometimes the nuances are lost and I hear that on the doorstep sometimes: 'Oh, you've hitched your wagon to the Liberals.' There's great disdain for that," Estabrooks said.

"I believe that as some of the benefits start to be more and more apparent, that there will be more understanding of the fact that you can extract things from a Liberal government without being tied to that Liberal government," McGrath said of the NDP's central challenge.
"You know, that's not the easiest thing to communicate."

"I know it's hard for the NDP to have a voice in Parliament ... they need to make a way of getting their voice heard," he said.
"But at the same time, I just don't agree with it."

Document 360 of 500

There's much more to the Emergencies Act ruling than a rebuke of Justin Trudeau's government

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/theres-much-more-emergencies-act-ruling-than/docview/2918765638/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=There%27s+much+more+to+the+Emergencies+Act+ruling+than+a+rebuke+of+Justin+Trudeau%27s+government&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's being framed as one of the most severe rebukes the federal government has received to date: a Federal Court ruling on its use of the Emergencies Act two years ago to end the trucker convoy that occupied the capital for three weeks and saw Canada-U.S. border blockades across the country.
The decision is ammunition for Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, who can now say Prime Minister Justin Trudeau "broke the highest law in the land" and "directly violated Canadians' most essential right to freedom of thought, belief, opinion and expression."
The Liberal government escaped harsh criticism last year from Court of Appeal Justice Paul Rouleau, who headed the Public Order Emergency Commission tasked with reviewing the law's use. Now it has to contend with a judgment that says the use of the powerful law may have been justified in the eyes of many, but it wasn't legal. The government, unsurprisingly, plans to appeal.
But Federal Court Justice Richard Mosley's decision this week isn't a sweeping indictment. Rather, it's a thoughtful judgment that points out the failings in the law, and finds that on several fronts the government acted reasonably.
Here are some takeaways:
1. The Emergencies Act needs to be updated. For a public order emergency to be declared, the law states that there must be a "threat to the security of Canada" as defined by the Canadian Security Intelligence Services Act, which refers to threats or acts of "serious violence against persons or property." Mosley writes that there are "other valid reasons for declaring an emergency," such as the type of economic disruptions that resulted from the border crossing blockades. However, "the court cannot rewrite the statute and has to take the definition as it reads." If there was a broader and more flexible interpretation of the words "threats to the security of Canada," the judge says, "I would have found that the threshold was satisfied."
2. More public scrutiny should be directed at the Ontario government. For the federal government to lawfully invoke the Emergencies Act, the national emergency needed "to exceed the capacity or authority of a province to deal with it." But Mosley casts doubt on whether that was really the case. He disputes the notion that the emergency was national in scope - something the federal Liberals insist was the case. But his observation raises another question: what if a province doesn't want to respond to the threat at its doorstep?
Mosley notes that Alberta and Quebec were able to adequately respond to the blockades, so why wasn't Ontario? It was only in Ottawa that the local police were unable to deal with the protest, he writes. And it "was only true in Ontario" that the situation exceeded the capacity of a province to deal with the situation, and "that was in part due to the inability of the provincial and municipal authorities to compel tow-truck drivers to assist in the removal of the blockading vehicles," he notes. "It is not clear why that could not have been dealt with under the provincial legislation."
Was the Trudeau government forced to act because Premier Doug Ford wouldn't? And how should any new rewriting of the law account for such a scenario?
3. A noteworthy standard for provincial consultations: Mosley found that a hastily assembled First Ministers meeting, where premiers disagreed on the need for using the Emergencies Act and most felt the law wasn't required in their province, satisfied the requirement for consultation under the act. That's bound to make a few premiers unhappy.
4. More thought is required to ensure regulations apply only to those they target. The judge ruled that some of the regulations were too broad, namely the freezing of bank accounts, which applied nationwide, lacked an objective standard or an appeal mechanism, and affected joint-account holders not involved in the protests. While he also found that protesters who wanted to go to Parliament Hill and peacefully protest had their freedom of expression infringed, he disagreed that the temporary measures breached other Charter rights, such as the freedom of peaceful assembly, freedom of association, or life, liberty and the security of the person.
Mosley's conclusion should be appealed. Residents in Ottawa, for example, will be outraged if the decision stands and taxpayers are on the hook to compensate the occupiers who had their bank accounts frozen. But parts of his ruling make a lot of sense and MPs should pay heed. The next national security incident may not reflect what legislators in the 1980s planned for, and no government should be forced to respond in ways that may be in the public interest but are, in fact, illegal.
No party seems to want to engage on this front - not the Conservatives or the Bloc Québécois, who didn't support the use of the Emergencies Act, nor the New Democrats, who reluctantly went along, nor even the Liberals, who don't want to detract from their affordability agenda. But the Emergencies Act needs updating, regardless of what happens on appeal.
CAPTION: Police clear "Freedom Convoy" protesters from Wellington Street in front of Parliament Hill on Feb. 19, 2022.

The whiff of Tucker Carlson's odious views are increasingly surfacing when it comes to immigration

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/whiff-tucker-carlsons-odious-views-are/docview/2918765601/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+whiff+of+Tucker+Carlson%27s+odious+views+are+increasingly+surfacing+when+it+comes+to+immigration&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I get it. We're at the Pander-To-Tucker-Carlson-Whom-Even-Fox-News-Fired stage of politics in parts of our country. In Calgary this week, Alberta Premier Danielle Smith joined the far-right extremist, who predictably roped in, among other disinforming and hateful talking points, the so-called "great replacement" conspiracy theory.
"They're taking away your voting power by changing the population of your country, but nobody's talking about that," he said. "Canada has the highest immigration rate in the world per capita. ... If you change the population of the country, you change the country. And you dilute the voting power of the people who are vested in that country who have lived there long term. Who understand the history and culture of the country. All of a sudden, their vote means much less."
There's rich irony to these words coming from a descendent of European settlers on Indigenous lands.
The replacement theory that has gained renewed prominence in the past 15 years or so among white supremacist or alt-right xenophobes posits that one set of people (whites) is being replaced by another set of people (non-whites), and deliberately so, to further the political ambitions of "leftist" ideologues.
It doesn't take into account the geopolitics that push people out of their lands or the poverty often stemming from historical European colonization that leads them to seek opportunities elsewhere.
The French author Renaud Camus who conceptualized "replacement theory" in a 2012 book by the same name saw "veil wearers" in French banlieues as emblematic of white Europeans being "reverse colonized" by Black and brown immigrants.
In other versions, the theory comprises antisemitic ideas that Jewish elites are plotting said "replacement." Therefore, the rallying cry of white supremacist groups at the 2017 "Unite the Right" rally in Charlottesville, Virginia, was "You will not replace us." "Jews will not replace us."
In Calgary, Carlson insisted he wasn't being racist, that he'd say the same thing if immigrants were from New Zealand or Stockholm. This, from the man who once said white men "created civilization" and another time said immigration made America "poorer, dirtier and more divided."
It's tempting to simply roll one's eyes and dismiss the fraudulence of this discord-sowing bloviator. To not engage with conspiracy theories that are so blatantly warped and hateful. Rebutting them can feel like wasting time stating the obvious when subtler policies and public statements require unpacking.
But silence, instead of being golden, sometimes lets these once-fringe sentiments go unchecked and gain momentum in the mainstream. The "replacement" rallying cry by white nationalists often fuels racist violence. Worse, it has been repeated so frequently and so loudly that it is now moving into the realm of acceptability.
In his book "The Uncensored War: The Media and Vietnam," the journalist Daniel Hallin referred to three spheres of media discourse as determined by establishment actors, namely, the sphere of consensus (facts such as the Earth revolves around the sun); the sphere of legitimate controversy (issues such as taxes, abortion); and the sphere of deviance (ideas not considered for debate because they're considered settled such as torture is bad or stealing is a sin or equality is an inalienable right).
The way I see it, xenophobia, which had moved from Hallin's sphere of "consensus" in the 1900s to "deviance" in the 2000s is now being pulled back into the fold and placed under "legitimate controversy."
Like children who make pinky promises with one hand and cross fingers on the other behind their backs to cancel it out, the supposed adults in political circles who proclaim disdain for xenophobia are cheering on policies underpinned by values espoused by the likes of Tucker Carlson.
French President Emmanuel Macron who came to office on a pro-business, centrist platform, found it easy to move so far right on immigration issues that a controversial immigration law he passed in parliament in December was hailed by far-right forces. Some of the articles in the law included making family reunification harder and restricting social benefits such as housing assistance. Thankfully, a constitutional court on Thursday rejected some of these egregious articles.
Immigration is emerging as a top issue in the 2024 U.S. elections. Donald Trump, who in 2016 so charmingly called Mexican immigrants rapists and said "they're bringing crime," has now upped his toxicity to say immigrants "are poisoning the blood of our country."
In Germany, the far-right Alternative für Deutschland expanded its base in recent regional elections, riding on the fear sparked in German voters by the sudden burst of asylum-seekers.
To be sure, concerns about the rates at which newcomers come in aren't necessarily xenophobic. But hateful sentiments are being used to flip legitimate questions around a country's preparedness in terms of infrastructure and services.
"It's horrifying what's happening," Carlson said. "You do the math. ... Why do you think it's happening? Is it for economic reasons? Tell me how. ... Look at your housing prices. ... Look at your health-care system. No Canadian I meet brags about it anymore. One of the reasons is it's overburdened. Tell me why."
If Carlson had only spoken to Ontario Premier Doug Ford, he would have learnt in 2021 that there was a labour shortage and that "We're in such desperate need of people from around the world."
Or he could ask his ideological mate, Georgia Meloni, Italy's anti-immigration prime minister, why she approved the admission of 136,000 new immigrants each year and why her government believes the number should be much higher.
Not only do western nations want labour, they want cheap labour. People who will do the work at rates that those who are "vested in this country" and "who understand the history and culture of the country" won't accept.
Is the health-care system overburdened by immigrants? Fine. Take them out of our health-care system. See what happens.
Housing? What percentage of newcomers are able to afford our housing market enough to overburden it?
There is distance between "feeling threatened" based on perceptions and actually being in danger.
As more people struggle economically, people such as Carlson, a millionaire many times over, conflate the two to exploit disenchantment and create artificial culture wars that distract from questioning how wealth is rapidly disappearing into the bank accounts of a powerful few. Beware the grift.
CAPTION: Tucker Carlson, shown in this photo from last year, spoke this week in Calgary, and was joined on stage by Alberta Premier Danielle Smith.

New measures to 'protect students': Ontario restores ban on public-private college partnerships amid international student cap

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/new-measures-protect-students/docview/2918722124/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+measures+to+%27protect+students%27%3A+Ontario+restores+ban+on+public-private+college+partnerships+amid+international+student+cap&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Amid federal changes that are expected to send Ontario's international student enrolment into free fall, calls are growing for Premier Doug Ford's government to increase funding for post-secondary education.
On Friday, the Ford government rolled out new measures meant to "protect students and improve the integrity of Ontario's post-secondary education." Colleges and Universities Minister Jill Dunlop said that those will include imposing a moratorium on new public-private college partnerships.
What was missing, critics say, was a commitment from the province to boost post-secondary funding.
The public-private college partnerships are the area that has seen the fastest growth of international enrolment in recent years.
The partnerships, which allow graduates from private colleges to receive public college credentials and coveted post-graduation work permits, are sanctioned by the Ontario government. But there have been growing concerns about the quality of education delivered by some of the private partners.
Back in 2017, the former provincial Liberal government under then-premier Kathleen Wynne put a moratorium on these public-private partnerships and gave two years to the four public colleges with these arrangements to shut down the operations. Ford's government reversed course immediately after being elected the following year.
There were few details available about the province's new measures, but they include:
1. Instituting more oversight for the approval process for all post-secondary international student programs;
2. Integrating enforcement efforts across ministries to strengthen oversight of career colleges and ensuring timely responses to concerns and complaints; and
3. Requiring all colleges and universities to have a guarantee that housing options are available for incoming international students.
The province's changes come days after the federal government imposed a new cap on the number of international students to be admitted into Canada amid a package of reforms. Ontario's colleges - and possibly universities - are expected to be the biggest loser, as more than half of Canada's international students study in this province.
Ontario Chamber of Commerce president and CEO Daniel Tisch warned Friday the cap could slash study permits by half and threaten the province's reputation as a leading global destination for study and work, as well as damaging Ontario's labour market, post-secondary education, and regional economies.
"We urge federal and provincial policymakers to find a more common-sense approach, including implementing the recommendations from Ontario's Blue-Ribbon Panel report, which highlights the precarious financial state of post-secondary education," Tisch said.
Dunlop said the provincial government is still evaluating the report released in November by a blue-ribbon panel it created to look at the financial future of post-secondary institutions, urging Queen's Park to allow colleges and universities to raise tuition by five per cent this fall and boost funding to post-secondary schools by 10 per cent in 2024-25.
The Ontario Confederation of University Faculty Associations said the province has missed the mark by focusing its new measures on the oversight of post-secondary institutions, which have come to rely on international tuition revenues amid declining government funding.
"Ontario needs international students, but universities need more funding from the government to provide the support that international - and domestic - students need to succeed inside and outside the classroom," said the confederation's president Nigmendra Narain.
CREDIT: Nicholas Keung Toronto Star Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Ruling a dangerous precedent: Trudeau government's use of Emergencies Act violated rights,

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ruling-dangerous-precedent/docview/2918721885/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ruling+a+dangerous+precedent%3A+Trudeau+government%27s+use+of+Emergencies+Act+violated+rights%2C&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Trudeau government's use of Emergencies Act violated rights, Jan. 23
It speaks to the level society has fallen to when a judge rules that this was an overreach.
The capital of the country was besieged, members of Parliament were intimidated into staying away, foreign funds were used to fund and support this occupation, main border points were closed, citizens of Ottawa were harassed mentally and physically and were afraid to leave their homes.
After days of ineffective local police action in Ottawa and the total lack of action by Doug Ford's government, the federal government was required to step in.
No one knew the level to which this protest would rise, or the level of policing required to end it. The government used reasonable and measured force to subdue it and arrest its ringleaders.
The government is correct to appeal this, and it is to be sincerely hoped that wiser judicial minds prevail.
This ruling sets a dangerous precedent which will be weaponized by right wing extremists who represent a minority view in this country but would impose it by force on the rest of us.
This ruling ill serves the citizens of Canada.
Peter Bradley, Mississauga
Relief was felt when protest ended
There exists a huge chasm between the law and the experience of the people especially those who lived near the place taken over by the so-called "freedom convoy," between the demonstrators on the bridges in Ontario and Alberta and the people suffering the loss of their jobs.
Ask most ordinary Canadians and they would say, "Thank goodness our federal government took action to restore their shattered lives." No other government, provincial or municipal, had the courage to stand up to the bullies imposing their will on others.
Businesses closed in Ottawa - including one that feed the poor - for three long weeks. Sleep was a commodity not experienced for three long weeks with the blowing of the truckers horns! Trucks prevented the ordinary citizens of Ottawa from the use of their roads.
Relief spread through the country when the protest was dismantled without any deaths.
Yet the federal judge says there was no legal justification for the use of the Emergencies Act.
Bonnie Bacvar, Toronto
Ruling makes insurrection a risk
If a bunch of anarchists can hold our country ransom and get away with it, we are on a very slippery slope.
I am absolutely disgusted with the Federal Court's decision. Which now leaves it open for every "crackpot" to do just that - insurrection without fear of reprisal.
David Milbourne, Toronto
Police missed an opportunity
I am angry, of course, at the "truckers" who held central Ottawa hostage for several weeks. But I am also angry at Ottawa Police who missed an opportunity.
If I had parked my VW Golf in front of Canada's Parliament buildings, I would have been ticketed every single day. Even more likely, I would have been towed and would need to liberate my vehicle, but only after paying the parking fine, towing charges and storage charges.
If all those trucks had been ticketed, daily or more often if bylaws allow, each vehicle would be looking at thousands of dollars in parking tickets. The beauty of this is parking violations are statutory offences with set fines - no trial required.
Moreover, I am sure Ottawa has idling and noise bylaws - more statutory offences. Many of these trucks were parked right in the roadway, obstructing traffic; such fines would likely be higher.
A creative police department could dream up additional infractions linked to improper fuel storage, harassment, etc. All these would amount to serious pain for each, and every trucker involved.
I am annoyed that this simple approach was missed.
Peter Bursztyn, Barrie
Proud law firm initiated challenge
We are proud to be the party that initiated the application to the Federal Court challenging the government's invocation of the Emergencies Act in response to the Freedom Convoy protest. We are pleased that the Federal Court agreed with our position that Justin Trudeau's government's action in invoking the Emergencies Act was unreasonable, outside their jurisdiction and that it was a violation of all Canadians' rights and freedoms under the Charter.
Alexander Boissonneau-Lehner, Johnstone and Cowling LLP, Toronto
Awaiting the appeal
Great! The barbarians at the gate won ... pending an appeal, of course.
Patrick Stewart, Toronto

Document 364 of 500

Budget committee urges focus on 'affordability'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/budget-committee-urges-focus-on-affordability/docview/2918721529/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Budget+committee+urges+focus+on+%27affordability%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Councillors are asking Mayor Olivia Chow to ease the financial burden on residents who are facing a double-digit property tax increase as she prepares to introduce her version of Toronto's 2024 budget.
At a wrap-up meeting of council's budget committee Friday, chair Coun. Shelley Carroll introduced a broadly worded "consensus motion" that she and other members said reflected what they've heard from residents during public consultations on the preliminary budget city staff released two weeks ago.
While the motion stopped short of explicitly calling on the mayor to lower the 10.5 per cent tax hike included in that version of the spending plan, it asked Chow to find ways of "improving affordability" for residents.
The committee also asked the mayor to consider saving a specialized snow clearing service that has been on the chopping block, and didn't press her to meet the Toronto Police Service's demands for a higher budget increase than staff have already proposed. The mayor is set to launch her version of the budget Feb. 1.
Carroll (Ward 17, Don Valley North) said the committee's message to Chow was "make sure that affordability is top mind."
But while the budget chief acknowledged the committee "heard loud and clear that some people are very worried" about whether they can afford the historic tax increase, others said they were prepared to pay higher taxes "to get the city back on the right track."
"And so that is the sort of divided opinion within the community that the mayor is going to really have to sit down and work on," Carroll said.
Committee member Coun. Jennifer McKelvie (Ward 25, Scarborough-Rouge Park) said as far as she was concerned, addressing affordability "means getting that tax rate under double digits."
McKelvie said residents understand the need invest in the city, but a 10.5 per cent spike "is simply too high" and any increase should be spread out over years.
McKelvie also praised the committee's recommendation for Chow to find a way to continue the city's windrow removal service. The $16-million program clears the snow left by plows at the end of driveways and is only offered in some suburban areas.
Cancelling it was one of the only service cuts specified in staff's version of the $17-billion operating budget. McKelvie and other councillors outside the core opposed the cut, arguing that their constituents, particularly seniors, rely on it.
Despite fierce lobbying from police Chief Myron Demkiw and the union representing rank-and-file officers, the budget committee didn't ask Chow to boost the force's budget beyond the $1.35 billion recommended in the staff proposal.
That's a slight increase from last year, but Demkiw has been calling it a "cut" that could jeopardize public safety because it's $12.6 million less than recommended by his service's civilian oversight board.
McKelvie said she and other councillors would push to meet Demkiw's request when the budget goes to council on Feb. 14. "Improving community safety does mean giving the full ask to the Toronto police," she argued.
Going into this year's budget, Toronto was facing a $1.8-billion operating shortfall thanks to lingering impacts of the pandemic. The gap that was reduced but not fully addressed by the new deal Chow reached with Premier Doug Ford's government last fall.
The budget city staff released on Jan. 10 would make up most of the remaining shortfall through a combination of savings and the 10.5 per cent property tax hike, which would add about $370 to the average homeowner's bill. The proposal also includes about $150 million in new and enhanced initiatives in areas like transit safety, additional library hours, and emergency services.
Carroll has warned the proposed hike could jump to 16.5 per cent unless the federal government commits to paying $250 million in refugee shelter costs the city expects to incur this year.
Under the strong-mayor system imposed by the provincial government in 2022, Chow is not bound by budget committee recommendations. In a statement Friday, she said she would review the recommendations before releasing her budget - the first of her term - next Thursday.
"Over the last few years, Toronto has become a harder place to live. Let's work together to protect services, fix this financial mess and get Toronto back on track," Chow said.
CREDIT: Ben Spurr Toronto Star David Rider Toronto Star Chief

Carlson's ugly views gaining traction: Xenophobic sentiments are leaking out of far-right fringes and into the Canadian mainstream

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/carlsons-ugly-views-gaining-traction/docview/2918721241/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Carlson%27s+ugly+views+gaining+traction%3A+Xenophobic+sentiments+are+leaking+out+of+far-right+fringes+and+into+the+Canadian+mainstream&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I get it. We're at the Pander-To-Tucker-Carlson-Whom-Even-Fox-News-Fired stage of politics in parts of our country. In Calgary this week, Alberta Premier Danielle Smith joined the far-right extremist, who predictably roped in, among other disinforming and hateful talking points, the so-called "great replacement" conspiracy theory.
"They're taking away your voting power by changing the population of your country, but nobody's talking about that," he said. "Canada has the highest immigration rate in the world per capita. ... If you change the population of the country, you change the country. And you dilute the voting power of the people who are vested in that country who have lived there long term. Who understand the history and culture of the country. All of a sudden, their vote means much less."
There's rich irony to these words coming from a descendent of European settlers on Indigenous lands.
The replacement theory that has gained renewed prominence in the past 15 years or so among white supremacist or alt-right xenophobes posits that one set of people (whites) is being replaced by another set of people (non-whites), and deliberately so, to further the political ambitions of "leftist" ideologues.
It doesn't take into account the geopolitics that push people out of their lands or the poverty often stemming from historical European colonization that leads them to seek opportunities elsewhere.
The French author Renaud Camus who conceptualized "replacement theory" in a 2012 book by the same name saw "veil wearers" in French banlieues as emblematic of white Europeans being "reverse colonized" by Black and brown immigrants.
In other versions, the theory comprises antisemitic ideas that Jewish elites are plotting said "replacement." Therefore, the rallying cry of white supremacist groups at the 2017 "Unite the Right" rally in Charlottesville, Virginia, was "You will not replace us." "Jews will not replace us."
In Calgary, Carlson insisted he wasn't being racist, that he'd say the same thing if immigrants were from New Zealand or Stockholm. This, from the man who once said white men "created civilization" and another time said immigration made America "poorer, dirtier and more divided."
It's tempting to simply roll one's eyes and dismiss the fraudulence of this discord-sowing bloviator. To not engage with conspiracy theories that are so blatantly warped and hateful. Rebutting them can feel like wasting time stating the obvious when subtler policies and public statements require unpacking.
But silence, instead of being golden, sometimes lets these once-fringe sentiments go unchecked and gain momentum in the mainstream. The "replacement" rallying cry by white nationalists often fuels racist violence. Worse, it has been repeated so frequently and so loudly that it is now moving into the realm of acceptability.
In his book "The Uncensored War: The Media and Vietnam," the journalist Daniel Hallin referred to three spheres of media discourse as determined by establishment actors, namely, the sphere of consensus (facts such as the Earth revolves around the sun); the sphere of legitimate controversy (issues such as taxes, abortion); and the sphere of deviance (ideas not considered for debate because they're considered settled such as torture is bad or stealing is a sin or equality is an inalienable right).
The way I see it, xenophobia, which had moved from Hallin's sphere of "consensus" in the 1900s to "deviance" in the 2000s is now being pulled back into the fold and placed under "legitimate controversy."
Like children who make pinky promises with one hand and cross fingers on the other behind their backs to cancel it out, the supposed adults in political circles who proclaim disdain for xenophobia are cheering on policies underpinned by values espoused by the likes of Tucker Carlson.
French President Emmanuel Macron who came to office on a pro-business, centrist platform, found it easy to move so far right on immigration issues that a controversial immigration law he passed in parliament in December was hailed by far-right forces. Some of the articles in the law included making family reunification harder and restricting social benefits such as housing assistance. Thankfully, a constitutional court on Thursday rejected some of these egregious articles.
Immigration is emerging as a top issue in the 2024 U.S. elections. Donald Trump, who in 2016 so charmingly called Mexican immigrants rapists and said "they're bringing crime," has now upped his toxicity to say immigrants "are poisoning the blood of our country."
In Germany, the far-right Alternative für Deutschland expanded its base in recent regional elections, riding on the fear sparked in German voters by the sudden burst of asylum-seekers.
To be sure, concerns about the rates at which newcomers come in aren't necessarily xenophobic. But hateful sentiments are being used to flip legitimate questions around a country's preparedness in terms of infrastructure and services.
"It's horrifying what's happening," Carlson said. "You do the math. ... Why do you think it's happening? Is it for economic reasons? Tell me how. ... Look at your housing prices. ... Look at your health-care system. No Canadian I meet brags about it anymore. One of the reasons is it's overburdened. Tell me why."
If Carlson had only spoken to Ontario Premier Doug Ford, he would have learnt in 2021 that there was a labour shortage and that "We're in such desperate need of people from around the world."
Or he could ask his ideological mate, Georgia Meloni, Italy's anti-immigration prime minister, why she approved the admission of 136,000 new immigrants each year and why her government believes the number should be much higher.
Not only do western nations want labour, they want cheap labour. People who will do the work at rates that those who are "vested in this country" and "who understand the history and culture of the country" won't accept.
Is the health-care system overburdened by immigrants? Fine. Take them out of our health-care system. See what happens.
Housing? What percentage of newcomers are able to afford our housing market enough to overburden it?
There is distance between "feeling threatened" based on perceptions and actually being in danger.
As more people struggle economically, people such as Carlson, a millionaire many times over, conflate the two to exploit disenchantment and create artificial culture wars that distract from questioning how wealth is rapidly disappearing into the bank accounts of a powerful few. Beware the grift.
CAPTION: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith posted a photo after sharing the stage with with Jordan Peterson, Tucker Carlson and Conrad Black at a Calgary event on Wednesday.Danielle SmithTwitter/X
CREDIT: Shree Paradkar Toronto Star OPINION

Subject: Immigration policy; White supremacy; Conspiracy; Immigrants; Xenophobia
