Document 366 of 500

Much more to ruling than a rebuke of Trudeau government

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/much-more-ruling-than-rebuke-trudeau-government/docview/2918721146/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Much+more+to+ruling+than+a+rebuke+of+Trudeau+government&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's being framed as one of the most severe rebukes the federal government has received to date: a Federal Court ruling on its use of the Emergencies Act two years ago to end the trucker convoy that occupied the capital for three weeks and saw Canada-U.S. border blockades across the country.
The decision is ammunition for Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, who can now say Prime Minister Justin Trudeau "broke the highest law in the land" and "directly violated Canadians' most essential right to freedom of thought, belief, opinion and expression."
The Liberal government escaped harsh criticism last year from Court of Appeal Justice Paul Rouleau, who headed the Public Order Emergency Commission tasked with reviewing the law's use. Now it has to contend with a judgment that says the use of the powerful law may have been justified in the eyes of many, but it wasn't legal. The government, unsurprisingly, plans to appeal.
But Federal Court Justice Richard Mosley's decision this week isn't a sweeping indictment. Rather, it's a thoughtful judgment that points out the failings in the law, and finds that on several fronts the government acted reasonably.
Here are some takeaways:
The Emergencies Act needs to be updated. For a public order emergency to be declared, the law states that there must be a "threat to the security of Canada" as defined by the Canadian Security Intelligence Services Act, which refers to threats or acts of "serious violence against persons or property." Mosley writes that there are "other valid reasons for declaring an emergency," such as the type of economic disruptions that resulted from the border crossing blockades. However, "the court cannot rewrite the statute and has to take the definition as it reads." If there was a broader and more flexible interpretation of the words "threats to the security of Canada," the judge says, "I would have found that the threshold was satisfied."
More public scrutiny should be directed at the Ontario government. For the federal government to lawfully invoke the Emergencies Act, the national emergency needed "to exceed the capacity or authority of a province to deal with it." But Mosley casts doubt on whether that was really the case. He disputes the notion that the emergency was national in scope - something the federal Liberals insist was the case. But his observation raises another question: what if a province doesn't want to respond to the threat at its doorstep?
Mosley notes that Alberta and Quebec were able to adequately respond to the blockades, so why wasn't Ontario? It was only in Ottawa that the local police were unable to deal with the protest, he writes. And it "was only true in Ontario" that the situation exceeded the capacity of a province to deal with the situation, and "that was in part due to the inability of the provincial and municipal authorities to compel tow-truck drivers to assist in the removal of the blockading vehicles," he notes. "It is not clear why that could not have been dealt with under the provincial legislation."
Was the Trudeau government forced to act because Premier Doug Ford wouldn't? And how should any new rewriting of the law account for such a scenario?
A noteworthy standard for provincial consultations: Mosley found that a hastily assembled First Ministers meeting, where premiers disagreed on the need for using the Emergencies Act and most felt the law wasn't required in their province, satisfied the requirement for consultation under the act. That's bound to make a few premiers unhappy.
More thought is required to ensure regulations apply only to those they target. The judge ruled that some of the regulations were too broad, namely the freezing of bank accounts, which applied nationwide, lacked an objective standard or an appeal mechanism, and affected joint-account holders not involved in the protests. While he also found that protesters who wanted to go to Parliament Hill and peacefully protest had their freedom of expression infringed, he disagreed that the temporary measures breached other Charter rights, such as the freedom of peaceful assembly, freedom of association, or life, liberty and the security of the person.
Mosley's conclusion should be appealed. Residents in Ottawa, for example, will be outraged if the decision stands and taxpayers are on the hook to compensate the occupiers who had their bank accounts frozen. But parts of his ruling make a lot of sense and MPs should pay heed. The next national security incident may not reflect what legislators in the 1980s planned for, and no government should be forced to respond in ways that may be in the public interest but are, in fact, illegal.
No party seems to want to engage on this front - not the Conservatives or the Bloc Québécois, who didn't support the use of the Emergencies Act, nor the New Democrats, who reluctantly went along, nor even the Liberals, who don't want to detract from their affordability agenda.
But the Emergencies Act needs updating, regardless of what happens on appeal.
CAPTION: Police clear "Freedom Convoy" protesters in front of Parliament Hill on Feb. 19, 2022. Federal Court Justice Richard Mosley's decision is a thoughtful judgment that points out the failings in the law, and finds that on several fronts, the government acted reasonably, Althia Raj writes.Steve Russell Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Althia Raj OPINION

Pierre Poilievre poaches Doug Ford cabinet minister to run federally

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievre-poaches-doug-ford-cabinet/docview/2918611961/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre+poaches+Doug+Ford+cabinet+minister+to+run+federally&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Pierre Poilievre has poached Parm Gill - a Doug Ford cabinet minister - to run for the Conservatives federally, which could end up helping Bonnie Crombie.
Gill, the MPP for Milton and a former Brampton MP, announced Thursday he was leaving Queen's Park to jump back to federal politics.
"I have known Pierre and (spouse) Ana for many years and have seen firsthand Pierre's commitment and dedication to making our country better," he said in a statement.
Ford's office issued a brief statement thanking Gill "for his service in cabinet and as a valued member of our caucus."
"We wish him every success in this next chapter and will announce a new minister of red tape reduction at a later date."
Gill's surprise move means Ford needs a new minister of red tape reduction and must call a byelection in a swing riding within six months.
That could spell an opening for Crombie, the new Liberal leader, who stepped down as Mississauga mayor two weeks ago and has been looking for an available seat in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area.
Sources close to Crombie stressed "no decisions" have yet been made.
But the prospect of her candidacy in a riding the Tories won by 1,680 votes has some in Ford's orbit spooked.
One insider, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations, said the party would love to see a candidate the calibre of Lisa Raitt, the former Milton MP and cabinet minister, run in a byelection.
But the source conceded that wooing Raitt, now a vice chair of global investment banking at CIBC and a fixture on CBC Power and Politics and CTV Power Play, would be challenging.
"The one who could stop a Liberal win is Lisa in that riding," the Tory said.
Indeed, Gill's departure had Grits crowing.
"This is clearly another sign that Ford's team is abandoning a sinking ship," said party spokesperson Carter Brownlee.
"Ontario Liberals will have more to say on this race in the coming days. We certainly welcome this opportunity to grow our caucus."
In his resignation statement, Gill, who was previously citizenship and multiculturalism minister, said it was "a privilege" to serve in Ford's cabinet.
"I want to thank Premier Ford for his confidence in me and for helping me deliver for Milton and our great province," he said.
While Milton is currently represented federally by former Olympian Adam van Koeverden, who defeated Raitt in 2019, Gill is expected to run in a new Milton East riding being created through redistribution.
CAPTION: Parm Gill, the MPP for Milton and a former Brampton MP, announced Thursday he was leaving Queen's Park to jump back to federal politics.

Document 368 of 500

MPs need to step to help refugees

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mps-need-step-help-refugees/docview/2918481647/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MPs+need+to+step+to+help+refugees&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After working on the front-line of this crisis for seven months, it's ludicrous that very little has changed materially for the unhoused African refugees I see daily. Every single one of them has arrived in Canada from traumatic circumstances, only to find themselves in a new traumatic situation of having little or no access to safe shelter.
On the ground, we've seen a man recently arrived from Africa die in a Mississauga encampment while trying to stay warm. With the scarce resources I have available to me in my underfunded work, I've taught refugees without winter clothing how to roll up newspaper and stuff it into their clothes as insulation.
I've also taught people how to keep their fingers and toes warm, understanding that frostbite is, for people who have never experienced a Canadian winter, an abstract concept until it's a devastating result. So far, no one I've worked with has lost their digits - yet.
Somehow, none of the urgency of this situation has sunk in among Toronto's Liberal MPs. Etobicoke Centre's Yvan Baker recently accused Mayor Chow of "lying to Torontonians" about the federal government's contributions to the crisis of unsheltered refugees.
As someone deeply involved in this crisis, including raising over $80,000 to distribute basic supplies in the face of government failure, they haven't stepped up. It took the federal government until December to pay just one-third of the $97 million it announced for refugees last July.
And this $97 million isn't nearly enough: city staff estimate 40 per cent of shelter residents are refugees, which costs Toronto $250 million a year based on the per diem cost of each occupied bed. Considering that the emergency shelter budget is $638.5 million a year, the math is both simple and correct.
Yet, Toronto's Liberal MPs would rather make unseemly attempts at debunking the city's honest request for financial support rather than acknowledge refugees are, in fact, a federal responsibility. On the front-line, it's as infuriating as it is deeply dangerous to people's lives.
Indeed, amid this political quagmire, we've forgotten what a federal contribution of $250 million could do.
In the "New Deal" brokered by Mayor Olivia Chow with Premier Doug Ford, the province has agreed to give Toronto's shelter and homelessness services $200 million a year until 2026 - but only if the federal government matches their contribution.
This is to say, $250 million from the feds is needed on its own, but it would also open up an additional $200 million from the province. The city has pre-emptively allocated the latter to the delivery of emergency shelter services this year, but the former - if it arrives - would help to fund the Shelter Infrastructure Plan. Meaning, the expansion of shelter space.
With this funding, we could acquire new properties to build safer, better shelters. We could finally transition out of shelter-hotel contracts, which were created as a hotel industry bailout rather than a safety measure for unhoused people, and which have hemorrhaged public funds since 2020.
We could have more dedicated beds for refugees, including communitarian and culturally appropriate space, as well as private rooms to replace shelter-hotel beds.
Without this funding, we would see unprecedented cuts to homelessness services. Given that Toronto is in a clear crisis of homelessness - council has even declared it such - this can't happen.
Indeed, if Mayor Chow's final budget urges a "Federal Impacts Levy" - an additional 6 per cent property tax increase on top of a staff-proposed 10.5 per cent hike - it will be because our local MPs refused to fund their responsibilities, and because we cannot relegate people to the streets.
Politics will always be politics, but for those of us in the real world, we can't let squabbles borne out of the failures of federalism or partisanship supplant people's urgent and basic need for shelter.
If Toronto's Liberal MPs won't be moved by the urgings of an NDP mayor, or are afraid of the prospect of a partner in a Conservative premier, perhaps they'll be moved by the voice of someone front-line "doing the work."
You have the power to change the lives of thousands of unhoused Torontonians for the better; why wouldn't you?
Diana Chan McNally is a front-line worker supporting homeless people. Follow her on X: @Diana_C_McNally
CREDIT: Diana Chan McNally Contributor;

Document 369 of 500

Harassment law to punish politicians: Ford says legislation will force local officials out of roles if they have behaved inappropriately

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/harassment-law-punish-politicians/docview/2918481602/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Harassment+law+to+punish+politicians%3A+Ford+says+legislation+will+force+local+officials+out+of+roles+if+they+have+behaved+inappropriately&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Facing the heat from municipalities and opposition parties, the Ford government now says "airtight" legislation that could force local politicians out of office for harassing staff is in the works.
After voting down a private member's bill from Liberal MPP Stephen Blais (Orléans) last year, Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra says he is working with the attorney general on a law to address an issue that a number of municipal councils across the province have struggled with.
"I have to ensure that it's constitutional, that it does what it's supposed to do," Calandra told attendees at the annual meeting of the Rural Ontario Municipal Association earlier this week.
"I don't want to do something or bring something forward that can, in any way, make it harder for somebody to get the justice that they are seeking," he said. "I continue to work with the attorney general and continue to work with people on this one, but when we bring something forward, I want to make sure that it is tested at all levels and that it ... (fulfils) what it is that we want to accomplish with it, and that it is airtight."
Currently, local politicians can be removed from office for things such as campaign finance violations, but not for sexually harassing staff. A number of municipalities have been left without any recourse, including in Ottawa, where the city's integrity commissioner found a councillor guilty of harassing staff.
Blais introduced his bill after serving on Ottawa council with Rick Chiarelli, who former staffers said made inappropriate comments, urged them to go braless and shared sexually explicit stories. In the end, Chiarelli was docked pay, but could not be ousted.
Emily McIntosh, of the non-partisan advocacy group The Women of Ontario Say No, called efforts to create legislation "absolutely welcome news."
"When public pressure mounts, there is a tipping point, and we hope that we have hit that tipping point," said the Barrie-area advocate, adding "it would be naive to think that there wasn't support in caucus for this already. I think what this is really recognizing is that this is about all Ontarians, and this is about repairing that relationship with municipalities."
While she said new legislation is "long overdue ... this is a non-partisan effort, and we're fully committed to working with this government to see this on the table. The key element now is to make sure that this is prioritized; this is really about workplace safety."
The group was able to get 201 municipalities on side, and at the rural association meeting all three opposition leaders spoke in favour of the need for tighter code of conduct penalties.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles wore the group's "Accountability Now" T-shirt at the meeting, and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said legislation to remove a councillor after harassment is found "is a fundamental thing. We need accountability for those who do the wrong thing."
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner said without such a law, "we all lose."
"We must demand better," he added.
The Women of Ontario Say No have noted that any staffers who harass others would be fired, and that elected officials need to be held to the same standard.
"We're talking about people that have the most power in communities held to a lower standard than any other workers in Ontario," McIntosh has said.
Blais had proposed that all municipal councillors fall under workplace harassment and violence policies, allowing them to be terminated and even barred from running in the next election for violations if found guilty by an integrity commissioner. Any removal, however, would have to be approved by a judge.
His bill had the support of former premier Kathleen Wynne, who personally spoke to councils in Richmond Hill, Orangeville and Mulmur about it.
The Ford government had previously said that such a bill would give integrity commissioners more power than judges.
Apart from the individual municipalities, the Association of Municipalities of Ontario, Ontario Big City Mayors and the Rural Ontario Municipal Association had all asked for help on code of conduct compliance.
Calandra said Monday that "I have had a lot of discussions ... and I've been given a lot of advice on this from many of you" since taking on the municipal affairs portfolio last September. He said he knows there is a "desire to see something done so that people will truly feel safe in the workplace."
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Legislation; Attorneys general; Politics; Politicians; Accountability

Title: Harassment law to punish politicians:   Ford says legislation will force local officials out of roles if they have behaved inappropriately

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: A.10

Document 370 of 500

Blame Doug Ford for turning a blind eye to student immigration abuses

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/blame-doug-ford-turning-blind-eye-student/docview/2918481573/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Blame+Doug+Ford+for+turning+a+blind+eye+to+student+immigration+abuses&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Puppy mills are bad for puppies - but also people and politicians.
That's the new term for private colleges that pander to foreigners who game the system - by applying for student visas, then skipping classes and jumping the jobs queue.
According to Pierre Poilievre, the prime minister allowed "tens of thousands of students to come and go to fake colleges that the Liberal government now admits were now, quote, puppy mills."
J'accuse!
There's just one problem with the federal Conservative leader pointing his accusing finger. In point of fact, those private colleges aren't just Justin Trudeau's problem.
They are Doug Ford's puppy mills.
Which means the premier of our own province - Poilievre's fellow Tory - also owns the problem. Ontario boasts more of these problematic private colleges than anywhere else - far more than our share of Canada's population.
This week, Immigration Minister Marc Miller announced a cap on foreign student visas and new restrictions on work permits as part of a belated crackdown on abuses.
Once upon a time, shady private career schools were dismissed as "diploma mills." Today that's deemed a misnomer, given how many students are aiming at a paycheque - not a graduation certificate.
None of this passes the smell test. But it's been happening under Ford's nose, as Poilievre well knows.
Which is why it's always risky playing politics with immigration. You never know whether driving a wedge will push all the wrong buttons - as when the premier tried to excuse his Greenbelt land grab by saying he needed to satisfy housing demand for new immigrants.
There's plenty of blame to go around - federal and provincial, Liberal and Tory, public and private, educators and entrepreneurs.
What makes Poilievre's public musings so amusing - or laughably unserious - is his political gamesmanship about all those gaming the system. The Conservative leader stressed that visa seekers "are not to blame for (Miller)'s incompetence."
By posing so earnestly as a protector of foreigners, Poilievre is being too clever by half. While some innocent foreigners might be misled by middlemen, many other migrants know precisely what they're up to by leveraging student visas to get a job, not an education.
Let's not insult the intelligence of voters or visa holders about motives. Any approval process based on rules and regulations is open to manipulation - not least an immigration, accreditation and visa system anchored in an overcomplicated federal-provincial framework of overlapping jurisdictions where people fall through the cracks (and seek cover).
Post-pandemic, all that pent-up demand for catch-up visas led everyone to lower their guard, not least the previous immigration minister, Sean Fraser. As his successor, Miller repurposed the term "puppy mills" to describe the fly-by-night immigration and education workers that operate "on top of a massage parlour."
Henceforth, each province will be assigned work permits in proportion to its population. Ontario will be especially hard hit, as it already fills 51 per cent of them with less than 39 per cent of the population.
The problem has been a long time in the making, but the Ford government had eyes only for traditional puppy mills - the ones that breed puppies - when it announced a crackdown last month: The Preventing Unethical Puppy Sales Act, or PUPS Act, will impose a minimum fine of $10,000 for breeders in the business of abusing animals when it is debated in the legislature next month.
But there's been no similar alarm or alacrity so far from Ford's Tories when it comes to outfits that exploit foreigners and dupe our own governments.
While Quebec and B.C. have gotten out ahead of the student visa issue, Ontario allowed problems to fester. On Ford's watch, an entire industry has arisen - an education-immigration complex akin to the old military-industrial complex that raised alarm bells in America decades ago.
There was easy money - lots of it - to be made off of affluent foreign students, and Canadian universities or colleges understandably wanted a piece of the action. But you can have too much of a good thing when a surge of overseas students overwhelms classrooms, campuses, communities, housing and job markets.
A few universities and most colleges got greedy - counting on high-fee foreign students for one-third or even one-half of their tuition revenues. Meanwhile, Ontario's Progressive Conservative government got stingy.
Five years ago, Ford's Tories announced a 10 per cent cut in tuition for domestic students, and have kept them in the deep freeze ever since, while keeping overall subsidies unchanged even as more local students showed up in class. No wonder so many public colleges responded to those foregone revenues by counting on foreign students to make up the difference - leaving them vulnerable to precisely the kind of crackdown coming from Ottawa.
Now, Canada finds itself overshooting its targets while undermining public faith in the integrity of the immigration system and our capacity to integrate migrants. But a perfect storm does not mean the country is drowning.
We cannot be complacent, but we cannot afford to overreact - blaming every blip in the domestic housing market on foreign interlopers. There are many other factors to blame for our housing shortage, from interest rates and labour shortages to zoning roadblocks.
We set targets, see trends, change course and plug the gaps. This is a country that will always need immigrants, always look after refugees, always benefit from foreign students, always need to learn from its mistakes - federal but also provincial and, yes, institutional - without pressing buttons or yanking chains.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford gives remarks during a media preview of the Bombardier aircraft assembly centre in Mississauga on Jan. 22, 2024.

Subject: Foreign students; Passports & visas; Private colleges & universities; Private schools; Immigration

Location: Canada

Title: Blame Doug Ford for turning a blind eye to student immigration abuses

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jan 26, 2024

Section: politics/political-opinion

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document type: News

ProQuest document ID: 2918481573

Document URL: https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/blame-doug-ford-turning-blind-eye-student/docview/2918481573/se-2?accountid=15090

Ford's blind eye on student immigration

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-blind-eye-on-student-immigration/docview/2918481523/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 372 of 500

'Lost Canadians' celebrate victory: Federal government won't appeal landmark ruling on citizenship rights

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lost-canadians-celebrate-victory/docview/2918481519/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Lost+Canadians%27+celebrate+victory%3A+Federal+government+won%27t+appeal+landmark+ruling+on+citizenship+rights&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tim Setterfield, who works as an engineer for NASA, said he was thrilled when he received the citizenship certificates for his two young U.S.-born daughters last week.
The family was able to celebrate because of a court ruling last month that went against the federal government. The Ontario Superior Court of Justice ruled that Ottawa must hash out new legislation by June 19 to fix the Canadian Citizenship Act, which it ruled unconstitutional for denying automatic citizenship to children born abroad if their Canadian parents were also born overseas.
Setterfield was anxious as he worried the federal government might appeal the ruling. But after the government fought tooth and nail against a group of "lost Canadians" who challenged the "second-generation cutoff" rule - and lost - Ottawa now said it's not appealing the ruling, handed out by the Ontario Superior Court of Justice just before Christmas.
"This law, as it currently stands, has had unacceptable consequences for Canadians whose children were born outside the country," Immigration Minister Marc Miller said in a statement this week.
"People who may be impacted by this situation will no doubt have questions about what this means for them and their families. That is why we will continue to assess the impacts of the decision on existing legislation and will provide more information and confirm next steps as quickly as possible."
Setterfield said "being Canadian is a huge part of my identity." He was born in Fiji when his father was working in the tropical island nation in the mid-1980s. "I wanted (my daughters) to be able to share that identity and also have the freedom to experience living in my own country if they should choose in the future."
According to lawyer Sujit Choudhry, who represented the lost Canadians and their families, Ottawa has two options, but both could potentially take months to come through and pose risk of missing the deadline imposed by Justice Jasmine Akbarali to make the citizenship law compliant with the Constitution. The Liberal government, with the support of the New Democrats, could introduce its own legislation, but that would require hours of committee hearings, unless they manage to move it forward on an expedited basis. Or it could ask for help from the Conservatives, who have already stalled a similar bill for third reading in the House.
Bill S-245 includes an amendment to replace the citizenship cut-off rule with a "substantial connection test" to pass on citizenship if a person can prove such ties with Canada and has been physically present in Canada for at least 1,095 days.
"The (parliamentary) immigration committee met for 30 hours on Bill S-245 and heard from witnesses, including from government witnesses, on the substantial connection test," Choudhry said in an interview. "So there's no need, in our view, for those committee proceedings to be run again.
"There already have been committee hearings on precisely the provision that would be in a new bill. The committee process could be a highly abbreviated one. I'm hoping we'll get clarity from them immediately. The thing is, in the interim, people's rights are still being violated."
In 2021, 23 individuals from seven families that have been harmed by the citizenship cut-off rule sued the federal government, arguing the law discriminates against them based on their place of birth, violates their mobility and liberty rights, and disproportionately puts women at a disadvantage when they have to give birth outside of Canada due to circumstances beyond their control.
In the court decision released in December, Akbarali said Canadians who obtained their citizenship by descent are indeed treated differently by the current law and hold "a lesser class of citizenship."
The judge said the court would declare the Citizenship Act unconstitutional if the government didn't amend it within six months from the rendering of her decision, and she ordered immigration officials to offer immediate relief to three of the affected families and grant their four foreign-born children Canadian citizenship.
CREDIT: Nicholas Keung Toronto Star

Subject: Families & family life; Committees; Citizenship; Canadians; Legislation; Immigration

Canada briefs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canada-briefs/docview/2918480974/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+briefs&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario
Poilievre steals minister from Ford cabinet
Pierre Poilievre has poached Parm Gill - a Doug Ford cabinet minister - to run for the Conservatives federally, which could end up helping Bonnie Crombie. Gill, the MPP for Milton and a former Brampton MP, announced Thursday he was leaving Queen's Park to jump back to federal politics. "I have known Pierre and (spouse) Ana for many years and have seen firsthand Pierre's commitment and dedication to making our country better," he said in a statement. Ford's office issued a brief statement thanking Gill "for his service in cabinet and as a valued member of our caucus." Gill's move means Ford needs a new minister of red tape reduction and must call a byelection in a swing riding within six months.
Alberta
Singh says working with Liberals 'slimy'
Working with the Liberals on pharmacare legislation has been like wrestling eels covered in oil, as NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh describes it. Singh told a town hall meeting in Edmonton that dealing with the federal government is "not fun. They're just slimy and break their promises," Singh said this week, getting laughs from the crowd. "They say one thing and then try to get out of it, but we're not giving up and we're not backing down."
911 call taker charged with corruption
A City of Calgary employee who worked as a 911 call taker for police faces several criminal charges after a yearlong corruption investigation. Police said officers became aware, during the course of an unrelated investigation, that protected information had been shared for organized crime purposes. Mariana Buonincontri, 58, has been charged with breach of trust, fraudulent use of a computer system and mischief related to computer data.
Star staff, wire services
CREDIT: Star staff, wire services

The judge said the court would declare the Citizenship Act unconstitutional if the government didn't amend it within six months from the rendering of her decision, and she ordered immigration officials to offer immediate relief to three of the affected families and grant their four foreign-born children Canadian citizenship.
Setterfield, who works at NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory, in said has retained a strong connection with his family in Canada, where his parents and brother still live. While there wasn't a party or cake to for his two daughters becoming new Canadian citizens, Setterfield did share the news right away with their grandparents in B.C.
"They asked my oldest (who is three years and 10 months), 'What does it feel like when you get your Canadian citizenship?' And she said, unprompted, 'It felt like a birthday,' " recalled Setterfield, whose wife is American.
"We've been explaining to her that we've been fighting for her citizenship for quite some time. I think she grasps it and she strongly identifies as both American and Canadian."
CAPTION: Victoria Maruyama, who was born in Hong Kong and received her Canadian citizenship by descent, had two children with her Japanese husband, while teaching English in Japan. The children were refused Canada citizenship in 2020, but the Ontario court has ordered Ottawa to grant them their citizenship.Emma Kenyon, born in Japan, and U.S.-born husband Daniel Warelis came to Canada as babies and got their Canadian citizenship by descent through their parents. However, their first child, born in Hong Kong in 2021, didn't have the same access to citizenship by descent, though the baby ultimately was granted "discretionary citizenship" in 2022.

Subject: Families & family life; Federal government; Committees; Citizenship; Canadians; Legislation; Immigration

Location: China; Canada; Japan; Hong Kong China

Company / organization: Name: National Aeronautics & Space Administration--NASA; NAICS: 927110

Document 375 of 500

MP calls for leadership review: Newfoundland Liberal says there's 'almost a hatred out there' for Trudeau

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mp-calls-leadership-review/docview/2918134899/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MP+calls+for+leadership+review%3A+Newfoundland+Liberal+says+there%27s+%27almost+a+hatred+out+there%27+for+Trudeau&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Newfoundland MP has become the first member of the Liberal caucus to call for a review of Justin Trudeau's leadership, suggesting the prime minister's tenure has reached its "best-before date."
In an interview with Radio-Canada, Ken McDonald called for a review mechanism that would allow party members to express their views on the prime minister and permit leadership rivals to present themselves.
McDonald said there is "almost a hatred out there right now" for Trudeau.
"As a party, let's clear the air and if people are still intent on having the leader we have now, fine," he told Radio-Canada. "But at least give people the opportunity to have their say in what they think the direction the party is going."
His comments are the closest any Liberal MP has come to publicly suggesting it's time for Trudeau to step down. They also threaten to ignite questions about the prime minister's standing in his own party just days before the return of the House of Commons for the winter sitting.
On Wednesday, Liberal MPs were publicly united in backing Trudeau, although some acknowledged their leader was becoming unpopular in their ridings and their party was losing support in the polls.
"The relationship between a political leader and the people is a bit like marriage, and after quite a few years of a marriage, sometimes things don't quite look as rosy as they were at the beginning of the relationship," said Ontario MP Marcus Powlowski. "And if you ask people why is this relationship going bad, they can't point to one particular thing."
Yvonne Jones, another Atlantic MP, said she was disappointed by McDonald's comments, and suggested he would be issuing a statement to "clarify" them.
"I've been in elected politics for 30 years. Did I agree with every premier I served with, or every prime minister? Absolutely not," Jones said. "But I stand with the prime minister."
Asked if she believed McDonald should remain seated as a Liberal MP, Jones said "that's up to the caucus."
McDonald's office did not return a request for comment Wednesday.
However, one Liberal MP, who was granted anonymity to speak freely on caucus matters, said McDonald was "brave" for speaking out.
"It'd be good to create a conversation, if I'm being honest," the MP told the Star. "It's understandable why he would say that. I don't think it should be dismissed."
Although they did not take a position on the prime minister's leadership, the MP said there is concern among some caucus members that Trudeau's diminishing popularity is reflecting poorly on them. That concern, however, is unlikely to spur a mutiny against him, they said.
Still, one former Liberal MP told the Star that McDonald's comments could indicate a shift in views in caucus.
"When Ken speaks up like that, that means something's happened," said Robert-Falcon Ouellette, who was a Winnipeg MP from 2015 to 2019. "He's a very affable, quiet guy who, when I was in caucus, wouldn't really say very much.
"So when he is moving in a direction, it means that there is something going on under the ground among other members of the party."
In a recent Abacus Data poll, 59 per cent of those surveyed expressed a negative view of Trudeau - a record low.
There is no provision in the Liberal party's constitution for members to call for a review of their leader.
Amid constant opposition attacks on the government's carbon pricing system last year, McDonald broke ranks and voted with Conservatives on a motion to abolish it - a decision that prompted Trudeau's inner circle to urge him to fall in line, he told Radio-Canada.
Support for the Liberals has fallen in public opinion polls as Conservatives blamed the government for the affordability crisis facing many Canadians.
Late last year, the Liberals saw a modest rise in support after Trudeau and his ministers went on the attack against Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives, accusing them of importing American far-right politics.
But the holiday season, which featured headlines about another controversial Trudeau family vacation, brought the government's approval down to record lows, with 58 per cent of Canadians saying they disapprove of the current government.
The Prime Minister's Office did not return requests for comment on Wednesday.
With files from
Joy SpearChief-Morris
CREDIT: Mark Ramzy Toronto Star With files from Joy SpearChief-Morris

Subject: Political leadership; Prime ministers; Leadership

Document 376 of 500

'Something had to be done,' Jones says: Deputy premier defends federal government's use of law during 2022 'Freedom Convoy' protest

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/something-had-be-done-jones-says/docview/2918134877/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Something+had+to+be+done%2C%27+Jones+says%3A+Deputy+premier+defends+federal+government%27s+use+of+law+during+2022+%27Freedom+Convoy%27+protest&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's support for Ottawa triggering the Emergencies Act to end the convoy blockade remains unwavering despite a federal court ruling it was unconstitutional, says the deputy premier.
"We cannot have communities held hostage and not have access to critical infrastructure again," said Sylvia Jones, the province's solicitor general during the winter 2022 protest against COVID-19 pandemic restrictions that left downtown Ottawa effectively under siege for more than three weeks.
"We had ambulances and fire trucks who literally who could not go on certain roads, so something had to be done," Jones told reporters Wednesday in Burlington.
Her remarks came a day after Federal Court Justice Richard Mosley ruled there was no legal justification to use the Emergencies Act to quash the so-called "Freedom Convoy" protest, and that doing so had resulted in a breach of Canadians' Charter rights.
Mosley found the convoy participants' actions did not amount to a "national emergency" that would justify the use of the act, which is reserved for situations beyond the scope of existing laws and regulations.
The federal government, which invoked the act with enthusiastic support from Premier Doug Ford, is planning to appeal the ruling.
Jones, who is now health minister as well as Ford's deputy, indicated Ontario would back that.
"Some of those impacts that were happening in our community, whether it was in Ottawa or in our border communities, had very direct and serious impacts, whether it was economic or socially," she said.
"But I will leave it to the federal government on whether they wish to appeal and how they will do that."
In his decision, Mosley said civil liberties were violated because use of the act led to protesters being barred from participating in demonstrations and having their bank accounts frozen.
"While I agree that the evidence supports the conclusion that the situation was critical and required an urgent resolution by government, the evidence, in my view, does not support the conclusion that it could not have been effectively dealt with under other laws of Canada," he wrote.
But Mosley's findings are contrary to Justice Paul Rouleau's conclusions last year at a legally mandated public inquiry into the act's invocation, where Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and other senior officials testified.
Rouleau found - albeit "with reluctance" - that the federal government had met the legal threshold to impose the law.
While neither Jones nor Ford agreed to testify at the inquiry, the premier has said he stood "shoulder to shoulder" with Trudeau's decision to crack down on the convoy protest.
Asked if Queen's Park could have done more to help convoy protests in Ottawa and on a Windsor road leading to the Ambassador Bridge into the U.S. - a major trade route for supplying auto factories and other industries - Jones noted the province did take extra steps.
"We worked very closely with both municipal leaders, as well as of course enforcement whether that was OPP or city police," she said.
"And we did put some additional measures in place including some new legislation that ensured going forward we could not have blockades that were going to impact our ability to access critical infrastructure or our border communities."
Jones emphasized "we believed at the time" it was legal for the federal government to invoke the Emergencies Act.
"I continue to believe that we need to ensure that demonstrations don't impact communities in a very visceral way."
Earlier, representatives of the Canadian Civil Liberties Association - one of the groups that successfully challenged the federal government's use of the Emergencies Act - told reporters in Toronto that the court ruling was a victory for civil rights in Canada, even if they agreed the "Freedom Convoy" protests of 2022 caused "serious hardship" for communities like Ottawa.
"CCLA's concerns, our challenge that led to yesterday's decision, were about the government giving itself massive and extraordinary emergency powers that may be necessary in a serious and extreme situation of a true national emergency as defined by that law, but were unreasonable in the circumstances," said Noa Mendelsohn Aviv, the CCLA's executive director.
As for the federal government's appeal, Aviv said: "I want to be crystal clear. We will fight them tooth and nail at the Federal Court of Appeal. We will defend our historic victory for rights and leave no stone unturned."
With files from Mahdis Habibinia
CAPTION: Sylvia Jones, who is now health minister as well as Premier Doug Ford's deputy, indicated Ontario would back the federal government's appeal over the use of the Emergencies Act in 2022.Richard LautensToronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson and Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau With files from Mahdis Habibinia

Ford government stands by use of Emergencies Act against 'Freedom Convoy' despite court decision

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-stands-use-emergencies-act/docview/2918319668/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+stands+by+use+of+Emergencies+Act+against+%27Freedom+Convoy%27+despite+court+decision&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 378 of 500

Under pressure, Ford government readying law to punish local politicians who harass staff

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/under-pressure-ford-government-readying-law/docview/2918319196/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Under+pressure%2C+Ford+government+readying+law+to+punish+local+politicians+who+harass+staff&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Facing the heat from municipalities and opposition parties, the Ford government now says "airtight" legislation that could force local politicians out of office for harassing staff is in the works.
After voting down a private member's bill from Liberal MPP Stephen Blais (Orléans) last year, Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra says he is working with the attorney general on a law to address an issue that a number of municipal councils across the province have struggled with.
"I have to ensure that it's constitutional, that it does what it's supposed to do," Calandra told attendees at the annual meeting of the Rural Ontario Municipal Association earlier this week.
"I don't want to do something or bring something forward that can, in any way, make it harder for somebody to get the justice that they are seeking," he said. "I continue to work with the attorney general and continue to work with people on this one, but when we bring something forward, I want to make sure that it is tested at all levels and that it ... (fulfils) what it is that we want to accomplish with it, and that it is airtight."
Currently, local politicians can be removed from office for things such as campaign finance violations, but not for sexually harassing staff. A number of municipalities have been left without any recourse, including in Ottawa, where the city's integrity commissioner found a councillor guilty of harassing staff.
Blais introduced his bill after serving on Ottawa council with Rick Chiarelli, who former staffers said made inappropriate comments, urged them to go braless and shared sexually explicit stories. In the end, Chiarelli was docked pay, but could not be ousted.
Emily McIntosh, of the non-partisan advocacy group The Women of Ontario Say No, called efforts to create legislation "absolutely welcome news."
"When public pressure mounts, there is a tipping point, and we hope that we have hit that tipping point," said the Barrie-area advocate, adding "it would be naive to think that there wasn't support in caucus for this already. I think what this is really recognizing is that is about all Ontarians, and this is about repairing that relationship with municipalities."
While she said new legislation is "long overdue ... this is a non-partisan effort, and we're fully committed to working with this government to see this on the table. The key element now is to make sure that this is prioritized; this is really about workplace safety."
The group was able to get 201 municipalities on side, and at the rural association meeting all three opposition leaders spoke in favour of the need for tighter code of conduct penalties.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles wore the group's "Accountability Now" T-shirt at the meeting, and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said legislation to remove a councillor after harassment is found "is a fundamental thing. We need accountability for those who do the wrong thing."
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner said without such a law, "we all lose."
"We must demand better," he added.
The Women of Ontario Say No have noted that any staffers who harass others would be fired, and that elected officials need to be held to the same standard.
"We're talking about people that have the most power in communities held to a lower standard than any other workers in Ontario," McIntosh has said.
Blais had proposed that all municipal councillors fall under workplace harassment and violence policies, allowing them to be terminated and even barred from running in the next election for violations if found guilty by an integrity commissioner. Any removal, however, would have to be approved by a judge.
His bill had the support of former premier Kathleen Wynne, who personally spoke to councils in Richmond Hill, Orangeville and Mulmur about it.
The Ford government had previously said that such a bill would give integrity commissioners more power than judges.
Apart from the individual municipalities, the Association of Municipalities of Ontario, Ontario Big City Mayors and the Rural Ontario Municipal Association had all asked for help on code of conduct compliance.
Calandra said Monday that "I have had a lot of discussions ... and I've been given a lot of advice on this from many of you" since taking on the municipal affairs portfolio last September. He said he knows there is a "desire to see something done so that people will truly feel safe in the workplace."
CAPTION: Emily Mcintoshof the non-partisan advocacy group The Women of Ontario Say No at Barrie City Hall on March 18, 2023. McIntosh was the leading advocate of a group pushing to get support for a new Liberal bill that would make it easier to remove local politicians from office for harassment.Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra says he is working with the attorney general on a law to address the removal of municipal politicians.

Subject: Legislation; Attorneys general; Politics; Politicians; Advocacy; Accountability

Title: Under pressure, Ford government readying law to punish local politicians who harass staff

Liberal MP calls for leadership review of Justin Trudeau, as others acknowledge his unpopularity

Document 380 of 500

Lecce wants students engaged with democracy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lecce-wants-students-engaged-with-democracy/docview/2917776406/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Lecce+wants+students+engaged+with+democracy&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: How do we teach tolerance?
Can we educate and engage students on democracy? And arm them against demagoguery and disinformation?
Stephen Lecce knows what won't work - more of the same.
As Ontario's youngest-ever education minister, he took a fresh look at the same old high school civics course.
And remembered how forgettable it was in his student days.
Today, election turnouts are in decline while racism and religious intolerance are on the rise - anti-Black racism, antisemitism, Islamophobia. Now, Lecce wants students to engage again - as he did this week at the Democracy Forum at Toronto Metropolitan University.
Turning to students in the audience, he asked for feedback on their recent high school experience in democratic engagement. The education minister offered an educated guess:
"Is it fair to say that for most of you, Civics and Careers was a 'bird course' - you showed up, you did the minimum, and you got like a 75 to 80 per cent?" Leece probed.
"Tell me if I'm wrong."
Heads nodded in agreement.
"So that's your experience - that was mine, and we're of a different generation. And the same is (still) true."
Which proves the point. Even student progressives in our audience, who might disagree on most issues with a Progressive Conservative cabinet minister, could agree that something needs to be done for democracy in our schools.
"What was the point of civics? It wasn't meant to be a spare, it wasn't designed to be a social experience, it was really designed to get young people motivated and interested in democracy, in their government, and to understand how to navigate government," Lecce argued.
Instead of a course "heavily dosed in theory," he wanted to teach students "how to be the agent of change" with real-world case studies. Survey data shows roughly one in four young people "have confidence" in the often unreliable information they get on social media, so he wanted them to gain "usable tools about how to understand sourcing and how to be a critical thinker."
The point isn't to tell students what to think. Just to make them think.
As a student, Lecce blazed his own campaign trail - first volunteering for electioneering with the Tories at age 13, later launching his own bid to be president of the student council at Western University. He won in a landslide.
Upon graduation, he worked in the office of then-prime minister Stephen Harper, rising to become his director of communications - spinning the mainstream media but also mastering social media. Hence his healthy skepticism, especially the excesses of hateful discourse (requiring a thick skin) and harmful disinformation (requiring open eyes).
His political journey - on campus, on Parliament Hill and at Queen's Park - was transformed by his work as a volunteer election observer in Tunisia's first free and fair elections in 2014, years after the Arab Spring implanted democracy in that authoritarian country. I asked him about that experience because I'd been there before as a foreign correspondent, covering the sham elections during Tunisia's time as a police state - when the opposition leaders I'd interviewed were later imprisoned.
"I remember seeing and working with new political party aspirants, people who are running (but) have never debated, never been in a television commercial, because this is not permitted in a dictatorial regime," Lecce told us. "The people I met there were so grateful because they longed for freedom."
But after working with activists who are willing to die for democracy, it can be dispiriting to encounter so much indifference to democracy and ignorance of history at home. Hence his passion for reforming the civics curriculum, given the record low turnout - 43.5 per cent - in Ontario's last election.
"When I went abroad, I realized that we have a lot to fight for in this country with a lot to safeguard, a lot to protect ... and young people's belief that they're not reflected within their government."
As part of his pitch for teaching tolerance, Lecce has led the way in Holocaust education and informing students about the Holodomor. More than six million Jews died at the hands of Nazi Germany, and an estimated four million Ukrainians died from the starvation and repression of the old Soviet Union's Communist rulers.
"Civic education and history are actually really important ways we safeguard our democracy and our future," Lecce said.
"The Holodomor, the Holocaust, these are brutal examples of political extremism ... some of the darkest moments in human history."
Students on social media are now exposed to disinformation that attempts to "normalize" Holocaust denial, which is why Ontario has shared its pioneering curriculum reforms with provinces across Canada.
Schools need to educate and equip "civically engaged people who are motivated to stand up when the other person is oppressed."
For the minister, that has also meant stepping in when school boards have failed to rein in bigotry in their own bailiwicks - not merely in the classroom but the office - from anti-Black racism in York Region to Islamophobia in Peel.
"We did it immediately and swiftly and decisively, without apology, to send a signal culturally that the leaders, the directors, the school board, the trustees, the staff, the teachers ... that there would be zero tolerance."
Teaching tolerance and promoting pluralism starts in schools. But it also takes leadership at the top.

At our next Democracy Forum at TMU on Feb. 2, our guest will be federal NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh.
CREDIT: Martin Regg Cohn OPINION

Document 381 of 500

Reforms on international students were long past due

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/reforms-on-international-students-were-long-past/docview/2917776405/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Reforms+on+international+students+were+long+past+due&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After weeks of foreshadowing, the federal government moved this week to cap the number of international student visas over the next two years. File this policy change in the "better late than never" category.
The number of international students flowing to this country has grown by such epic proportions it is difficult to reach any conclusion other than the federal Liberals were simply sleeping on this file. There have been no shortage of red flags, from Statistics Canada reports warning of the strain on affordable housing and access to social services to provincial auditors warning of an unhealthy dependence on international student fees by post-secondary institutions which are being underfunded.
Over the past two years, the number of international students in this country jumped from 617,000 to more than a million. About a third are in public universities but the overwhelming majority are in public colleges or private schools, often offering substandard education and a backdoor route to permanent status in this country. Immigration Minister Marc Miller, in announcing he is cutting the number of study permits by 35 per cent to 364,000 this year, is right to target the shady operators who are preying on international students and not doctoral and postgraduate international students at public universities. Miller says hundreds of the private schools should be shut down.
"It is not the intention of this program to have sham commerce degrees and business degrees that are sitting on top of a massage parlour," Miller said in making his announcement.
There are a number of threads to unravel from this announcement. First and foremost, as the minister stressed, this is not an indictment of foreign students. They are hardly responsible for a housing crunch or fears over access to stretched social services. International students were more likely the victims, living in crowded, substandard housing, dealing with a much more expensive country that they had anticipated and receiving diplomas which Miller says were being churned out like "puppy mills." It was creating reputational damage to this country.
But these students would not be in this country without federal approval, so Miller is correcting a problem that his government largely created. According to a memo obtained by The Canadian Press, the Trudeau government was warned in 2022 that there was a widening gap between immigration and housing supply, largely driven by the increasing number of international students and temporary foreign workers admitted to this country.
This cap will be most acutely felt in Ontario, home to 51 per cent of international students. The Doug Ford government has twice been warned about a reliance on international student tuition fees, once in November by his own panel on colleges and universities, and in 2021 by the auditor-general who warned him not to increase a dependency on foreign student fees without a post-secondary education plan in place. The panel reported in November that the Progressive Conservatives had the lowest per student post-secondary funding in the country following a tuition cut and freeze that meant colleges and universities had reached the point at which revenue from international student tuition fees was "fundamental to the sector's financial sustainability." Now Ford is forced into some tough decisions. He will have to decide what schools can bring in international students and what schools should be eliminated, while protecting universities in financial trouble.
Finally, the Liberals - and all governments at all levels - must handle matters of immigration, including temporary foreign workers and international students, with utmost delicacy. To their credit, Canadians have held together on a consensus on the accommodation of immigrants. And to their credit, Canadian politicians have largely resisted any base urge to exploit frustration and anxiety in this country by playing the immigration card.
But the numbers are increasing. Some 500,000 immigrants will arrive next year and this country is going through a population boom during challenging economic times. Immigration will dominate much of the upcoming U.S. presidential election and delicacy is not a feature of debate to the south, particularly from Republicans.
It would not take much to bust that Canadian consensus. We trust our politicians to be vigilant on that score.
CREDIT: ;

Subject: Tuition; Foreign students; Private schools; Colleges & universities; Politics; Immigration

Letters: Norman Jewison, director of 'Fiddler on the Roof,' 'Moonstruck,' dies at age 97, Ontario needs a plan for its energy system, What cut? The Toronto police budget is going up - how much, we don't even know yet,

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/2917776094/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Norman+Jewison%2C+director+of+%27Fiddler+on+the+Roof%2C%27+%27Moonstruck%2C%27+dies+at+age+97%2C+Ontario+needs+a+plan+for+its+energy+system%2C+What+cut%3F+The+Toronto+police+budget+is+going+up+-+how+much%2C+we+don%27t+even+know+yet%2C&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: R.I.P. to a giant
of Canadian entertainment
Our Norman, only you, a Canadian superstar, would fiddle on a roof to escape the heat of the night while being struck by the power of Cosmo's moon. RIP dear Mr. Jewison.
Lynda Pogue, Georgetown
Jewison was a great director, generous man
Norman Jewison, director of 'Fiddler on the Roof,' 'Moonstruck,' dies at age 97, Jan. 23
After graduating from high school, I bummed around Europe for a few months in 1971 and ended up in London with a plane ticket home to start my motion picture degree at Ryerson. The flight was cancelled, and I couldn't fly for three days. I had no money left and slept on the seats and washed dishes in Heathrow Airport for food.
It was then I got the idea I would love to talk to Canadian film director Norman Jewison who was in London. So, I called the number in the directory. A voice answered and I asked to speak to Norman Jewison. The voice asked who I was, and I explained - a future film student - and he said, "This is Norman. Can you come to Shepperton Studios tomorrow at 1 p.m.? I have 20 minutes."
That was the first and last time I have ever panhandled for bus fare.
Norman ushered me into his huge, high-ceiling office with a garden outside eight French doors and we talked about filmmaking.
I loved "The Russians Are Coming," "In the Heat of the Night," "Fiddler on the Roof" and "Jesus Christ Superstar." I wanted to make films like that. He was warm and encouraging and funny. He introduced me to his co-producers on the upcoming "Rollerball," and writer John Patrick Shanley (who would years later win the Oscar for "Moonlight").
At the end of our fable-like two-hour meeting, he gave me a two-pound note. He said someone had given him a two-pound note when he was young and broke in London and he was paying it back. Then he had his assistant put me in his Jaguar chauffeur-driven limousine back to Heathrow.
Over the next 30 years, we met a few times, laughed about that first meeting and discussed projects. He wrote a cheque for us to finish our film "Where the Spirit Lives" about residential schools.
So, Norman died this week, a lovely, generous inspiring Canadian and an incredible film director. His main wish was that Canada develop a vibrant, successful, well-supported independent film industry with bold Canadian values and sensibilities as he showed with "The Russians are Coming" and "In the Heat of the Night."
Thank you, Norman.
Keith Ross Leckie, scriptwriter, Toronto
Hospital capacity must keep up with emissions
Ontario needs a plan for its energy system, Jan. 22
Mark Winfield's excellent and comprehensive article on the need for an evidenced-based plan for our energy system, exposes Ontario's lack of a plan to provide clean, affordable energy. He writes that emissions from gas fired plants have doubled since 2017, and are projected to increase dramatically. This very scary fact leads to the one cost, not included in the article, and that is the cost to human health from the increase in air pollution from gas-fired electricity generation.
The increase in particulate matter and other pollutants such as nitrogen oxides will lead to greater rates of asthma and bronchitis, more cancers and heart disease. Premier Doug Ford should consider building greater capacity in our hospitals and health-care facilities to prepare for our increased need for health services. Or maybe he could reconsider his plans for more gas and make clean, renewable power a central focus of our future energy needs.
Ruth Allen, Toronto
Ontario's leaders need to reform tobacco industry
Over many decades, Big Tobacco has engaged in deception and targeted marketing that has addicted youth and resulted in immense suffering, disease and premature death for so many people. The devastating effect of tobacco use in Ontario is extensive and undeniable - 1.4 million (11 per cent) still smoke tobacco, with 16,000 tobacco-related deaths and $2.2 billion in health-care costs every year.
All 10 provinces have filed lawsuits against the tobacco industry collectively seeking more than $500 billion in damages, and right now the Ontario government is negotiating a settlement with Big Tobacco.
To prevent disease and save lives, it's vital that the settlement implement strict new controls on the tobacco industry and establish substantial long-term funding for tobacco control through an independent fund. We must protect future generations.
As such, the Canadian Cancer Society urges Premier Doug Ford to use this monumental opportunity to hold Big Tobacco accountable and prevent future harm to Ontarians.
Andrea Seale, CEO, Canadian Cancer Society, Vancouver
Police are more important than bike paths
What cut? The Toronto police budget is going up - how much, we don't even know yet, Jan. 22
Matt Elliott can play with figures but the difference in the police budget means 100 fewer officers. This is almost 700 fewer officers than in 1992. It is now averaging 21 minutes for a police response to a priority call which is irresponsible. The city is now dealing with higher crime rates, more auto thefts, more disruptive demonstrations, more drug use, more gun crime, more hate crime.
If there are insufficient numbers of police then the investigative units will be cut and put the community more at risk. Police are more important than $290 million planned for bicycle paths.
Norman Gardner, former chair, Toronto Police Services Board, Toronto
Thank goodness for the kindness of strangers
My husband and I are seniors in our mid-80s and not in possession of a cellphone.
One Wednesday morning in December, we went shopping to our favourite deli store in Mississauga when we noticed "battery-loss" on our car's display. We hoped to make it home but no, our car stopped right on the Westway. My husband knocked on a few houses and on the third one, a young man opened his door, called CAA for us and made us a coffee while we were waiting in our car for CAA to come.
All I know is the man's name was Duncan.
Thank you, Duncan.
Gitta Schinkopf, Downsview
CAPTION:

Document 383 of 500

Necessary reforms on international students

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/necessary-reforms-on-international-students/docview/2917776061/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Necessary+reforms+on+international+students&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

CAPTION: Immigration Minister Marc Miller announced new measures to limit and curb the abuse of the international student program.
