Document 384 of 500

As education minister, Stephen Lecce has a lesson for students about protecting democracy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/as-education-minister-stephen-lecce-has-lesson/docview/2917776055/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=As+education+minister%2C+Stephen+Lecce+has+a+lesson+for+students+about+protecting+democracy&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: How do we teach tolerance?
Can we educate and engage students on democracy? And arm them against demagoguery and disinformation?
Stephen Lecce knows what won't work - more of the same.
As Ontario's youngest-ever education minister, he took a fresh look at the same old high school civics course. And remembered how forgettable it was in his student days.
Today, election turnouts are in decline while racism and religious intolerance are on the rise - anti-Black racism, antisemitism, Islamophobia. Now, Lecce wants students to engage again - as he did this week at the Democracy Forum at Toronto Metropolitan University.
Turning to students in the audience, he asked for feedback on their recent high school experience in democratic engagement. The education minister offered an educated guess:
"Is it fair to say that for most of you, Civics and Careers was a 'bird course' - you showed up, you did the minimum, and you got like a 75 to 80 per cent?" Leece probed.
"Tell me if I'm wrong."
Heads nodded in agreement.
"So that's your experience - that was mine, and we're of a different generation. And the same is (still) true."
Which proves the point. Even student progressives in our audience, who might disagree on most issues with a Progressive Conservative cabinet minister, could agree that something needs to be done for democracy in our schools.
"What was the point of civics? It wasn't meant to be a spare, it wasn't designed to be a social experience, it was really designed to get young people motivated and interested in democracy, in their government, and to understand how to navigate government," Lecce argued.
Instead of a course "heavily dosed in theory," he wanted to teach students "how to be the agent of change" with real-world case studies. Survey data shows roughly one in four young people "have confidence" in the often unreliable information they get on social media, so he wanted them to gain "usable tools about how to understand sourcing and how to be a critical thinker."
The point isn't to tell students what to think. Just to make them think.
As a student, Lecce blazed his own campaign trail - first volunteering for electioneering with the Tories at age 13, later launching his own bid to be president of the student council at Western University. He won in a landslide.
Upon graduation, he worked in the office of then-prime minister Stephen Harper, rising to become his director of communications - spinning the mainstream media but also mastering social media. Hence his healthy skepticism, especially the excesses of hateful discourse (requiring a thick skin) and harmful disinformation (requiring open eyes).
His political journey - on campus, on Parliament Hill and at Queen's Park - was transformed by his work as a volunteer election observer in Tunisia's first free and fair elections in 2014, years after the Arab Spring implanted democracy in that authoritarian country. I asked him about that experience because I'd been there before as a foreign correspondent, covering the sham elections during Tunisia's time as a police state - when the opposition leaders I'd interviewed were later imprisoned.
"I remember seeing and working with new political party aspirants, people who are running (but) have never debated, never been in a television commercial, because this is not permitted in a dictatorial regime," Lecce told us. "The people I met there were so grateful because they longed for freedom," he recalled.
But after working with activists who are willing to die for democracy, it can be dispiriting to encounter so much indifference to democracy and ignorance of history at home. Hence his passion for reforming the civics curriculum, given the record low turnout - 43.5 per cent - in Ontario's last election.
"When I went abroad, I realized that we have a lot to fight for in this country with a lot to safeguard, a lot to protect ... and young people's belief that they're not reflected within their government."
As part of his pitch for teaching tolerance, Lecce has led the way in Holocaust education and informing students about the Holodomor. More than six million Jews died at the hands of Nazi Germany, and an estimated four million Ukrainians died from the starvation and repression of the old Soviet Union's Communist rulers.
"Civic education and history are actually really important ways we safeguard our democracy and our future," Lecce said. "The Holodomor, the Holocaust, these are brutal examples of political extremism ... some of the darkest moments in human history."
Students on social media are now exposed to disinformation that attempts to "normalize" Holocaust denial, which is why Ontario has shared its pioneering curriculum reforms with provinces across Canada. Schools need to educate and equip "civically engaged people who are motivated to stand up when the other person is oppressed."
For the minister, that has also meant stepping in when school boards have failed to rein in bigotry in their own bailiwicks - not merely in the classroom but the office - from anti-Black racism in York Region to Islamophobia in Peel.
"We did it immediately and swiftly and decisively, without apology, to send a signal culturally that the leaders, the directors, the school board, the trustees, the staff, the teachers ... that there would be zero tolerance."
Teaching tolerance and promoting pluralism starts in schools. But it also takes leadership at the top.
* * *
At our next Democracy Forum at TMU on Feb. 2, our guest will be federal NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh.
CAPTION: Education Minister Stephen Lecce speaks to journalists at Queen's Park on Aug. 25, 2023.

Subject: Teaching; Students; Holocaust; Political activism; Racism; Curricula; Tolerance; Islamophobia; Political leadership; Xenophobia; Social networks; School boards; False information; Democracy; Education; Elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Social networks

Location: Tunisia

Document 385 of 500

Norman Jewison was generous, inspiring and an incredible film director: letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/norman-jewison-was-generous-inspiring-incredible/docview/2917775764/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Norman+Jewison+was+generous%2C+inspiring+and+an+incredible+film+director%3A+letters&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Norman Jewison, director of 'Fiddler on the Roof,' 'Moonstruck,' dies at age 97, Jan. 23
After graduating from high school, I bummed around Europe for a few months in 1971 and ended up in London with a plane ticket home to start my motion picture degree at Ryerson. The flight was cancelled, and I couldn't fly for three days. I had no money left and slept on the seats and washed dishes in Heathrow Airport for food.
It was then I got the idea I would love to talk to Canadian film director Norman Jewison who was in London. So, I called the number in the directory. A voice answered and I asked to speak to Norman Jewison. The voice asked who I was, and I explained - a future film student - and he said, "This is Norman. Can you come to Shepperton Studios tomorrow at 1p.m.? I have 20 minutes."
That was the first and last time I have ever panhandled for bus fare.
Norman ushered me into his huge, high-ceilinged office with a garden outside eight French doors and we talked about filmmaking.
I loved "The Russians Are Coming," and "In the Heat of the Night" and "Fiddler on the Roof" and "Jesus Christ Superstar." I want to make films like that! He was warm and encouraging and funny. He introduced me to his co-producers on the upcoming "Rollerball," and writer John Patrick Shanley (who would years later win the Oscar for "Moonlight") and asked if I wanted to be an intern on the "Rollerball" project. I turned him down!
At the end of our fablelike two-hour meeting, he gave me a two-pound note. He said someone had given him a two-pound note when he was young and broke in London and he was paying back. Then he had his assistant put me in his Jaguar chauffeur-driven limousine back to Heathrow.
Over the next 30 years, we met a few times, laughed about that first meeting and discussed projects. He wrote a cheque for us to finish our film "Where the Spirit Lives" (about residential schools).
So, Norman died this week, a lovely, generous inspiring Canadian and an incredible film director. His main wish was that Canada develop a vibrant, successful, well supported independent film industry with bold Canadian values and sensibilities as he showed with "The Russians are Coming" and "In the Heat of the Night."
Thank you, Norman.
Keith Ross Leckie, scriptwriter: The Avro Arrow, The Halifax Explosion, Everest, Toronto
R.I.P. Canadian superstar
Our Norman, Only you, a Canadian superstar, would fiddle on a roof to escape the heat of the night while being struck by the power of Cosmo's moon. RIP dear Mr. Jewison
Lynda Pogue, Georgetown
With the increase in gas fire emissions, we'll be needing greater hospital capacity Ontario needs a plan for its energy system, Jan. 22
Mark Winfield's excellent and comprehensive article on the need for an evidenced-based plan for our energy system, exposes Ontario's lack of a plan to provide clean, affordable energy.He writes that emissions from gas fired plants have doubled since 2017, and are projected to increase dramatically.This very scary fact leads to the one cost, not included in the article, and that is the cost to human health from the increase in air pollution from gas fired electricity generation.
The increase in particulate matter and other pollutants such as nitrogen oxides will lead to greater rates of asthma and bronchitis, more cancers and heart disease.Premier Ford should consider building greater capacity in our hospitals and health-care facilities to prepare for our increased need of health services.Or maybe he could reconsider his plans for more gas and make clean, renewable power a central focus of our future energy needs.
Ruth Allen, Toronto
Ontario's leadership needs to reform the tobacco industry
Over many decades, Big Tobacco has engaged in deception and targeted marketing that has addicted youth and resulted in immense suffering, disease and premature death for so many people. The devastating impacts of tobacco use in Ontario is extensive and undeniable - 1.4 million (11 per cent) still smoke tobacco, with 16,000 tobacco-related deaths and $2.2 billion in healthcare costs every year.
All 10 provinces have filed lawsuits against the tobacco industry collectively seeking more than $500 billion in damages, and right now the Ontario government is negotiating a settlement with Big Tobacco.
To prevent disease and save lives, it's vital that the settlement implement strict new controls on the tobacco industry and establish substantial long-term funding for tobacco control through an independent fund. We must protect future generations.
As such, the Canadian Cancer Society urges Premier Doug Ford to use this monumental opportunity to hold Big Tobacco accountable and prevent future harm to Ontarians.
Andrea Seale, CEO, Canadian Cancer Society, Vancouver
Police are more important than bike pathsWhat cut? The Toronto police budget is going up - how much, we don't even know yet, Jan. 22
Matt Elliott can play with figures but the difference in the police budget means 100 fewer officers. This is almost 700 fewer officers than in 1992. It is now averaging 21 minutes for a police response to a priority call which is irresponsible. The city is now dealing with higher crime rates, higher auto thefts, more disruptive demonstrations, more drug use, more gun crime, more hate crime.
If there are insufficient numbers of police then the investigative units will be cut and put the community more at risk. Police are more important than $290,000,000 planned for bicycle paths.
Norman Gardner, former chair, Toronto Police Services Board, Toronto
Read this if you donate to charityWe need to rethink the charitable sector, Dec. 15
Kudos to Maryann Kerr. She has written an excellent article which needs to read by anyone who donates to charity or is considering donating I would also suggest the Ted Talk by Dan Palotta: "The way we think about charity is dead wrong."
Elaine Fraser, Oshawa
Thank goodness for the kindness of strangers
My husband and I are seniors in our mid-eighties and not in possession of a cellphone.
One Wednesday morning in December, we went shopping to our favourite deli store in Mississauga when we noticed "battery-loss" on our car's display. We hoped to make it home but no, our car stopped right on The Westway. My husband knocked on a few houses and on the third one, a young man opened his door, called CAA for us and made us a coffee while we were waiting in our car for CAA to come.
All I know is the man's name was Duncan.
Thank you, Duncan.
Gitta Schinkopf, Downsview
CAPTION: Norman Jewison in 1999.

To reduce crime rates start by reducing poverty: letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/reduce-crime-rates-start-reducing-poverty-letters/docview/2917600469/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=To+reduce+crime+rates+start+by+reducing+poverty%3A+letters&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: To reduce crime rates start by reducing poverty Cutting police would make Toronto less safe, poll says, Jan. 19
Why do we think that increases to police budgets will increase public safety? Police react to situations. They normally cannot prevent them.
If we really want to reduce high crime rates, we need to start by reducing poverty and family breakdown, while increasing social cohesion and a sense of community.
Alan Trufal, Toronto
Where are the Liberal and NDP reactions to Ford's private surgical clinics?
It seems Premier Doug Ford can pretty much do whatever he wants with few limitations - even destroy public health care in Ontario by bringing in private surgical clinics that will introduce up-charges for patients while billing our publicly funded health-care system to line their pockets. This is the first step in initiating two-tiered medicine in Ontario and a U.S.-style system where profits rank first and patient care second.
What I would like to know is where are the Liberal and NDP reactions to Ford's announcement? It is time the opposing parties publicly state that if Ford brings in private clinics, they will immediately dismantle same when either party is elected. This will prevent these private surgical clinics from investing heavily in facilities, equipment and staff when they realize their venture may only have a two-year life span.
If Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie and NDP leader Marit Stiles remain passively silent on Ford's private surgical clinics, we can assume they are fully prepared to welcome them to Ontario at the expense of the public system.
J. Richard Wright, Niagara-on-the-Lake
The public wants action, not more studiesSome MPs want a study on cellphone bills, Jan. 20
The last thing we need is another study on cellphone bills just like we didn't need a study on grocery prices. What we need is action from the Liberal government. The Liberals are down in the polls and high cellphone and grocery prices are a gift to them to help them increase their popularity. Rogers and Loblaws are the bad guys: the consumers and the voters are the good guys. What could be easier for the Liberals? Corporations are making record profits and individuals and families are barely living paycheck to paycheck. Consumers and voters have been waiting patiently for some good news on these two important issues. Action not studies is what the public wants.
Charles Campisi, Oakville
SEC forced by courts to approve bitcoin ETFs
Bitcoin has just been legitimized in the U.S. Cue the ransomware criminals, Jan. 20
The recent U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission's approval of "several applications to launch exchange traded funds, tied to the spot price of bitcoin," is a bizarre decision made by U.S. courts.
The SEC was literally forced to approve bitcoin ETFs. This sad decision could well ruin the future of everyday Americans, who may succumb to the lure of the crypto-craze.
Star readers, and other well-informed Canadians, will surely concur with David Olive's warning about cryptocurrency's impact on fragile global economies: "What we can be sure of is that crypto will continue to be the financial instrument of choice for money launderers, terrorists and the ransomware criminals."
Reminds me of an eloquent sign in the front yard of a Funeral Home: "Drive carefully. We'll wait."
Rudy Fernandes, Mississauga
Harbourfront should continue to have something for everyone
Should Toronto's struggling Harbourfront Centre rethink what it offers? Here's why some say it's time to take a look, Jan. 14
It was a pleasure to see coverage of Harbourfront in Sunday's Insight section, together with provincial, national and international news; especially the picture and waterfront views of former Mayor David Crombie. His Honour was always a gifted wordsmith and analyst of urban social conditions.
As a former federal minister with urban responsibilities including Toronto's Harbourfront, two ideas quickly come to mind concerning any Harbourfront review.
First, don't throw the baby out with the bathwater. Second, keep in mind the Shakespeare success formula of something for everyone, both residents and visitors, continuing the public enjoyment of the Toronto shoreline.
Paul Cosgrove, former Scarborough Mayor, Metro Executive, M.P., and Federal Minister of Public Works
This is no way to look after vulnerable seniors
Seniors home to shut, not renovate, Jan. 22
If there was ever an argument to stop using private money to build and run seniors' homes, this is it.
We don't have hospital buildings in private hands (yet) because hospitals need to be there providing a service for generations of citizens and not to be abandoned to build condos.
This is the folly of private health care.
For profit business is simply that, for profit, and when there is a more lucrative or less burdensome opportunity the owners will take it.
That is no way to look after vulnerable seniors, and to add insult to injury the province is waiving the closing notice period.
Once again, Ford's false commitment to public health care exposed for what it is.
Peter Bradley, Mississauga
Cut speed limit on 400 series highways to east traffic jams and accidentsUrgent action needed for highway safety, Jan. 20
As letter writer Travis McDougall of the Truckers for Safer Highways states, urgent action for safety on our highways is desperately needed. I suggest the quickest, most effective, cost-efficient measure would be the reduction and enforcement of the speed limit for all vehicles on GTA 400 series highways to 80 km/h. This would mean that a one-hour trip at 100 km/h would only take an extra 12 minutes at 80 km/h. However, the chances of not being stuck in massive traffic jams for accident cleanups would be much reduced. As well, there will be significant fuel savings which will translate into dollar savings and less carbon emissions. Our vehicles are certainly capable of much higher speed, but it's obvious that our drivers are not since the number of accidents is way too high. As well, injuries in an 80 km/h incident will be less devastating.
Too many crashes are the result of driver distraction or inattention. Touchscreen controls, now so common in newer vehicles, take eyes off the road and add to the danger. Yes, urgent action is indeed needed.
George Novotny, Toronto
If Trump and Poilievre are elected
Can anyone calm my nerves of the thought that a re-elected Donald Trump might have eyes on getting Canadian provinces to join the U. S.? And can we be sure that a new Canadian Prime Minister, such as Pierre Poilievre, would not have plans to help facilitate Trump down the road?
Just bad dreams about two right-winged conservatives? Let's hope, eh?
John Cousins, Bowmanville
CAPTION: Police canvas a Toronto neighbourhood in December 2023, after an incident involving a bike resulted in person being taken to hospital with life threatening injuries.

Subject: Digital currencies; Public health; Speed limits; Cellular telephones; Voters; Profits; Ransomware; Injuries

Document 388 of 500

Solving the city's budget fiasco

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/solving-citys-budget-fiasco/docview/2917499624/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Solving+the+city%27s+budget+fiasco&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The dismal state of Toronto's finances hangs over the city like a malevolent cloud that won't go away. As municipal budget season rolls in, the stark reality facing Torontonians, at least according to city staff, is that without historic-scale (though investment-repelling) property tax increases and a lot of federal support, Toronto won't be able to balance its books.
Torontonians face a proposed tax hike of up to 16.5 per cent (10.5 per cent if the feds come through with $250 million for shelter costs). This moment should be recognized as a stark and powerful wake-up call.
For more than a decade, including in an alarming 2010 budget analysis "The Growing Chasm," Toronto Region Board of Trade has been warning about the city's unsustainable fiscal position. And we've proposed solutions that won't derail Toronto's business growth and our North American competitiveness, in the process dragging down our collective prosperity and quality of life, two qualities that have attracted people and businesses to Toronto over the years. These hallmarks are seriously fraying.
Which is why the yawning fiscal chasm can no longer be ignored. It is estimated to be a monumental $36 billion over the next 10 years, even after the historic "New Deal" clinched last year between Mayor Olivia Chow and Premier Doug Ford.
The reality is Toronto simply does not have the means to pay for the ever-expanding assortment of services it provides today without significantly eroding the quality of core municipal services.
But the real question is: should it?
Toronto is filling service gaps left by other governments. So many of these services - dental care, child care, long-term care, housing, shelter beds, mental health, nonpolice crisis response, and more have been piled on over the years by successive councils. That's not to say there hasn't been a need, indeed often a pressing one. But, $1 out of $5 collected through city property taxes funds a service that should be paid for by Ottawa or Queen's Park. Thanks to Toronto, they're off the hook.
The three-year "New Deal" has helped reduce the city's operating and capital pressures, but it should come with a brightly flashing warning sign. It's only the beginning of what will be needed to realign spending responsibilities and structurally right Toronto's sinking fiscal ship for the next generation.
Having experienced a massive and largely unfunded population surge over the last decade, Toronto is choking on its rapid growth. With federal policy fuelling this surge, Ottawa must also come to the table. Toronto businesses and residents shouldn't be footing their portion of the bill.
New revenues aren't an invitation for the city to do more, rather it's an opportunity to do better. The first requirement is to balance the books. This year's budget process should be the starting point for renewal.
City council needs to take greater accountability over Toronto's budget. It's time to create a strong expenditure management framework - a Toronto Treasury Board - as a strategic oversight mechanism to ensure municipal spending aligns with Toronto's long-term financial health. Presently, any member of council can introduce a motion for a new program or service that councillors vote on, often without staff analysis or consideration for how costs will escalate and impact the city's finances. A little horse-trading on council, and the motion is passed.
New York City's Office of Management and Budget manages America's largest city budget by evaluating the cost-effectiveness of city services and proposals. Toronto's budget is the seventh largest in Canada, larger than five provinces, all of which have a treasury board function. It only makes sense for Toronto.
The city also needs to control operating costs by restructuring service delivery and prioritizing high quality shared services, a point made by the Board of Trade in our 2020 "Shaping Our Future" report. And it should consider a variety of revenue options, such as leveraging existing assets from land, buildings and even utilities to reduce its chronic deficits.
Delivering high quality core services, like well-maintained transit and transportation systems, safe water and wastewater infrastructure and sufficient emergency services is Job 1 for the city. While the New Deal is critical for the city's long-term financial health, it is not an invitation to do "more with more." To address our fiscal chasm, we need to figure out how to do better with what we have.
Giles Gherson is president
and CEO of the Toronto Region Board of Trade.
CREDIT: Giles Gherson Contributor Giles Gherson is president and CEO of the Toronto Region Board of Trade.;

Document 389 of 500

Rural Ontario to get piece of housing pie: Ford letting smaller towns share in $1.2B program to boost construction

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/rural-ontario-get-piece-housing-pie/docview/2917499588/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Rural+Ontario+to+get+piece+of+housing+pie%3A+Ford+letting+smaller+towns+share+in+%241.2B+program+to+boost+construction&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford will allow more remote towns to share in the province's $1.2-billion "building faster fund" to speed up housing construction in rural Ontario.
Ford told the Rural Ontario Municipalities Association's annual conference in Toronto on Monday that he hopes even the smallest centres will take advantage of the new funds.
Announced last summer at the height of the Progressive Conservatives' $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal, the three-year, $1.2-billion program is a sweetener that rewards cities and towns that try to meet the housing construction targets the province has imposed upon them.
While $120-million of the program had been set aside for rural municipalities that were assigned provincial housing benchmarks, smaller towns and hamlets without such targets had been excluded from tapping into that money.
"I know many small, rural and northern municipalities have been waiting patiently to hear how they can access their share of the fund," Ford told hundreds of delegates at the Sheraton Centre Hotel conference.
"Friends, let me assure you we will not leave these communities behind.
"We will soon set up an application-based process that will allow municipalities which have not been assigned housing targets to access the building faster fund," said Ford.
"Successful applications will receive funding or important infrastructure projects like roads, water or wastewater that will help get more homes built in your communities," he said.
"With this infrastructure rural communities will have the tools to build for the future."
Under the program, unveiled last August at the Association of Municipalities of Ontario conference, towns and cities that reach 80 per cent of their target each year are eligible for funding based on their share of the government's overall goal of 1.5 million new homes by 2031.
The city of Toronto, for example, has a housing target of 285,000 new homes, which it is on track to exceed, having achieved 146 per cent of the goal so far.
But, mindful that "not every municipality has reached" their housing-construction target, Ford conceded "in many cases a lack of infrastructure is partly to blame."
"So moving forward our government will take unused funds from the building faster fund - potentially hundreds of millions of dollars - and make them available for housing-enabling infrastructure investments that all municipalities can apply for."
Ford used his speech to tout the actions his Tory government is taking to boost the economy in remote communities, including "building a homegrown EV supply chain connecting critical minerals in northern Ontario to clean steelmakers in Hamilton and Sault Ste. Marie to automakers and battery manufacturers across Ontario."
"Over the past three years, we've attracted more than $28 billion in auto and electric vehicle investments, including Volkswagen's $7 billion investment - the single largest auto investment in Canadian history - to build North America's largest battery plant in St. Thomas."
The premier also said that thanks to "our nearly $4 billion, multi-year investment, we're on track to bring high-speed internet access to every region in Ontario by the end of 2025, because in today's digital world, everyone needs access to reliable internet."
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Strategic materials; Rural areas; Infrastructure; Internet access; Scandals; Municipalities; Home building

Document 390 of 500

Ford's office denies claims of inconsistencies in testimony: NDP says emails show government was 'far more involved' in land removal

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-office-denies-claims-inconsistencies/docview/2917498490/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+office+denies+claims+of+inconsistencies+in+testimony%3A+NDP+says+emails+show+government+was+%27far+more+involved%27+in+land+removal&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is downplaying NDP accusations that internal emails reveal inconsistencies in government Greenbelt testimony to Ontario's ethics watchdog.
"I don't know anything about it," Ford told the Star at the new Bombardier business jet factory at Pearson International Airport on Monday.
Through a freedom of information request, the New Democrats obtained an email exchange the opposition party charged contradicts statements given under oath to the integrity commissioner last year.
As first reported by the Star, the issue revolves around an Oct. 17, 2022 email - on private accounts instead of government servers - between Patrick Sackville, then Ford's principal secretary and now his chief of staff, and Ryan Amato, at the time the top aide to then-municipal affairs minister Steve Clark.
The email "shows that the premier's office was far more involved than Mr. Ford claims in the selection of Greenbelt lands for removal," said NDP Leader Marit Stiles, who asked the integrity commissioner to "provide an opinion on whether it is consistent with Mr. Sackville's testimony to your office under oath."
According to the integrity commissioner, Sackville had testified "he did not discuss specific properties to be removed or removal criteria with Mr. Amato until the briefing that occurred on Oct. 27, 2022."
But Ford's officials said Monday there were no inconsistencies nor any "net-new information" in the recently discovered 15-month-old email from Amato to Sackville.
"Out of an abundance of caution and as part of year-end due diligence, our office conducted additional searches to ensure adherence with proper record keeping practices," the premier's office said.
"Through this process, we found one document, which does not include any net-new information from what has been previously disclosed and mirrors what was being briefed on at that time," it continued.
"Regardless, we have proactively provided this document to all relevant parties, including the integrity commissioner, the auditor general and anyone who made a relevant freedom of information requests."
Indeed, the email, which discussed "criteria" for removing land from the Greenbelt, was handed over to the NDP on Dec. 22, two days after Sackville forwarded it to cabinet office lawyers.
"This record was just discovered upon deeper review," the chief of staff wrote Dec. 20.
Asked why personal email accounts were being used to conduct official business, Ford's office said the government is still working on addressing concerns raised in the Aug. 9 auditor general's report into the Greenbelt affair.
The auditor noted "forwarding government information to personal accounts is not appropriate because of cybersecurity concerns" and that "using nongovernment resources to conduct government business is unacceptable."
In response, the Tories are tackling all 15 of the recommendations made in the report that probed the removal of 7,400 acres from the two-million-acre environmentally sensitive Greenbelt around the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area to build housing.
The most significant of those recommendations - cancelling the land swap - was addressed by Ford on Sept. 21 after a separate Greenbelt report on Aug. 30 by the integrity commissioner.
In their parallel probes, the auditor general and integrity commissioner found certain developers with Tory connections were "favoured" in a process supposedly led by Amato, who was Clark's chief of staff.
Amato, who has maintained he "acted appropriately," resigned on Aug. 22, while Clark, who remains the MPP for Leeds-Grenville-Thousand Islands and Rideau Lakes, quit Ford's cabinet on Sept. 4.
The premier has insisted he was not aware of how the 15 parcels of land were selected for removal from the Greenbelt, a rezoning change estimated to be worth $8.28 billion to landowners.
"I had nothing to do with the changes in the Greenbelt ... the auditor general cleared us, cleared my office, and the integrity commissioner cleared me and my office," Ford said Oct. 31.
But when he scrapped the land swap, which is now being investigated by the RCMP, he acknowledged "it was a mistake to open the Greenbelt."
"I made a promise to you that I wouldn't touch the Greenbelt. I broke that promise," the premier said last September.
The scandal also led Ford to part ways with Kaleed Rasheed, who was turfed as public and business service delivery minister and from the PC caucus, and Jae Truesdell, who was the premier's housing adviser.
That's because Rasheed and Truesdell were on a controversial 2020 trip to Las Vegas with Greenbelt developer Shakir Rehmatullah and Amin Massoudi, Ford's then principal secretary.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie and Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Testimony; Freedom of information

Location: Ontario Canada; Canada

People: Ford, Doug

Document 392 of 500

Liberals to hold summit over rise in auto thefts: Better co-ordination between agencies, governments sought

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-hold-summit-over-rise-auto-thefts/docview/2917404532/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+to+hold+summit+over+rise+in+auto+thefts%3A+Better+co-ordination+between+agencies%2C+governments+sought&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal government will convene a national summit to tackle auto theft across the country, after a surge in stolen vehicles in Toronto and much of the country sparked concerns the problem is funding organized crime abroad.
Arriving at a downtown Montreal hotel for two days of meetings with the federal Liberal cabinet, Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc said Sunday evening he wants national, provincial and municipal governments and police forces to attend the summit, which is set for Feb. 8 in Ottawa.
LeBlanc did not offer any new measures to immediately tackle the problem, but suggested the summit will help different levels of government, as well as police and border agents, co-ordinate on how to address the issue.
He also did not specify which governments or agencies have agreed to attend the summit.
However, LeBlanc said he spoke with Ontario Premier Doug Ford about the issue over the weekend, since the issue is a priority in Canada's biggest province.
Ford's office did not immediately respond to a request for comment Sunday evening.
"It's an important opportunity for us to work with partners across the country and take action with something that's increasingly becoming a concern in every part of the country," LeBlanc said.
Much of Canada has experienced a rise in auto thefts in recent years, including in Ontario and Toronto in particular. In announcing the summit, the federal government cited the Canadian Finance and Leasing Association, which reported three times as many vehicles were stolen in Toronto in 2022 than in 2015.
The Équité Association, a group funded by insurance agencies that tracks fraud and insurance crime, reported last year that vehicle thefts in the city jumped a further 34 per cent in the first half of 2023, compared with the same period one year earlier.
And that's after the city saw a 10-year high in auto thefts in 2022, with Toronto police reporting more than 9,700 stolen vehicles that year. The federal government, meanwhile, says vehicle thefts jumped 50 per cent in Quebec, 48 per cent in Ontario and almost 35 per cent in Atlantic Canada in 2022, compared with 2021.
The Équité Association blamed much of the problem on outdated Transport Canada regulations made before key fobs and remote starters were widespread.
The group's vice-president of investigative services - a former Ontario Provincial Police officer - also told the Star last autumn that the record level of auto theft is funding the trafficking of drugs and weapons by international organized criminals.
Last spring, the international policing agency Interpol named Canada as a major source for the global stolen vehicle trade that funds terrorism, human smuggling and the drug trade.
According to the federal government, "transnational" organized crime groups are suspected to be exporting stolen cars from Canada, but that "most vehicle thefts involve lower level" criminals, "with violent street gangs being the most prevalent."
With files from Peter Edwards and Jennifer Pagliaro
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall Toronto Star With files from Peter Edwards and Jennifer Pagliaro

Subject: Organized crime; Federal government; Automobile theft; Robbery; Vehicles

Location: Canada

Title: Liberals to hold summit over rise in auto thefts:   Better co-ordination between agencies, governments sought

Doug Ford promising more help for housing in rural Ontario

The premier also said that thanks to "our nearly $4 billion, multi-year investment, we're on track to bring high-speed internet access to every region in Ontario by the end of 2025, because in today's digital world, everyone needs access to reliable Internet."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford said he knows small municipalities have been waiting to hear how they can access their share of the fund.

Document 394 of 500

To reduce crime rates start by reducing poverty: letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/reduce-crime-rates-start-reducing-poverty-letters/docview/2917304883/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=To+reduce+crime+rates+start+by+reducing+poverty%3A+letters&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: To reduce crime rates start by reducing poverty Cutting police would make Toronto less safe, poll says, Jan. 19
Why do we think that increases to police budgets will increase public safety? Police react to situations. They normally cannot prevent them.
If we really want to reduce high crime rates, we need to start by reducing poverty and family breakdown, while increasing social cohesion and a sense of community.
Alan Trufal, Toronto
Where are the Liberal and NDP reactions to Ford's private surgical clinics?
It seems Premier Doug Ford can pretty much do whatever he wants with few limitations - even destroy public health care in Ontario by bringing in private surgical clinics that will introduce up-charges for patients while billing our publicly funded health-care system to line their pockets. This is the first step in initiating two-tiered medicine in Ontario and a U.S.-style system where profits rank first and patient care second.
What I would like to know is where are the Liberal and NDP reactions to Ford's announcement? It is time the opposing parties publicly state that if Ford brings in private clinics, they will immediately dismantle same when either party is elected. This will prevent these private surgical clinics from investing heavily in facilities, equipment and staff when they realize their venture may only have a two-year life span.
If Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie and NDP leader Marit Stiles remain passively silent on Ford's private surgical clinics, we can assume they are fully prepared to welcome them to Ontario at the expense of the public system.
J. Richard Wright, Niagara-on-the-Lake
The public wants action, not more studiesSome MPs want a study on cellphone bills, Jan. 20
The last thing we need is another study on cellphone bills just like we didn't need a study on grocery prices. What we need is action from the Liberal government. The Liberals are down in the polls and high cellphone and grocery prices are a gift to them to help them increase their popularity. Rogers and Loblaws are the bad guys: the consumers and the voters are the good guys. What could be easier for the Liberals? Corporations are making record profits and individuals and families are barely living paycheck to paycheck. Consumers and voters have been waiting patiently for some good news on these two important issues. Action not studies is what the public wants.
Charles Campisi, Oakville
SEC forced by courts to approve bitcoin ETFs
Bitcoin has just been legitimized in the U.S. Cue the ransomware criminals, Jan. 20
The recent U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission's approval of "several applications to launch exchange traded funds, tied to the spot price of bitcoin," is a bizarre decision made by U.S. courts.
The SEC was literally forced to approve bitcoin ETFs. This sad decision could well ruin the future of everyday Americans, who may succumb to the lure of the crypto-craze.
Star readers, and other well-informed Canadians, will surely concur with David Olive's warning about cryptocurrency's impact on fragile global economies: "What we can be sure of is that crypto will continue to be the financial instrument of choice for money launderers, terrorists and the ransomware criminals."
Reminds me of an eloquent sign in the front yard of a Funeral Home: "Drive carefully. We'll wait."
Rudy Fernandes, Mississauga
Harbourfront should continue to have something for everyone
Should Toronto's struggling Harbourfront Centre rethink what it offers? Here's why some say it's time to take a look, Jan. 14
It was a pleasure to see coverage of Harbourfront in Sunday's Insight section, together with provincial, national and international news; especially the picture and waterfront views of former Mayor David Crombie. His Honour was always a gifted wordsmith and analyst of urban social conditions.
As a former federal minister with urban responsibilities including Toronto's Harbourfront, two ideas quickly come to mind concerning any Harbourfront review.
First, don't throw the baby out with the bathwater. Second, keep in mind the Shakespeare success formula of something for everyone, both residents and visitors, continuing the public enjoyment of the Toronto shoreline.
Paul Cosgrove, former Scarborough Mayor, Metro Executive, M.P., and Federal Minister of Public Works
This is no way to look after vulnerable seniors
Seniors home to shut, not renovate, Jan. 22
If there was ever an argument to stop using private money to build and run seniors' homes, this is it.
We don't have hospital buildings in private hands (yet) because hospitals need to be there providing a service for generations of citizens and not to be abandoned to build condos.
This is the folly of private health care.
For profit business is simply that, for profit, and when there is a more lucrative or less burdensome opportunity the owners will take it.
That is no way to look after vulnerable seniors, and to add insult to injury the province is waiving the closing notice period.
Once again, Ford's false commitment to public health care exposed for what it is.
Peter Bradley, Mississauga
Cut speed limit on 400 series highways to east traffic jams and accidentsUrgent action needed for highway safety, Jan. 20
As letter writer Travis McDougall of the Truckers for Safer Highways states, urgent action for safety on our highways is desperately needed. I suggest the quickest, most effective, cost-efficient measure would be the reduction and enforcement of the speed limit for all vehicles on GTA 400 series highways to 80 kph. This would mean that a one-hour trip at 100 kph would only take an extra 12 minutes at 80 kph. However, the chances of not being stuck in massive traffic jams for accident cleanups would be much reduced. As well, there will be significant fuel savings which will translate into dollar savings and less carbon emissions. Our vehicles are certainly capable of much higher speed, but it's obvious that our drivers are not since the number of accidents is way too high. As well, injuries in an 80 kph incident will be less devastating.
Too many crashes are the result of driver distraction or inattention. Touchscreen controls, now so common in newer vehicles, take eyes off the road and add to the danger. Yes, urgent action is indeed needed.
George Novotny, Toronto
If Trump and Poilievre are elected
Can anyone calm my nerves of the thought that a re-elected Donald Trump might have eyes on getting Canadian provinces to join the U. S.? And can we be sure that a new Canadian Prime Minister, such as Pierre Poilievre, would not have plans to help facilitate Trump down the road?
Just bad dreams about two right-winged conservatives? Let's hope, eh?
John Cousins, Bowmanville

Document 395 of 500

Internal email suggests Doug Ford's office knew Greenbelt land swap details earlier than claimed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/internal-email-suggests-doug-fords-office-knew/docview/2917178478/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Internal+email+suggests+Doug+Ford%27s+office+knew+Greenbelt+land+swap+details+earlier+than+claimed&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A recently discovered internal email related to the $8.28-billion Greenbelt scandal suggests Doug Ford's office "was far more involved" in the controversial land swap than the premier has maintained.
The missive, obtained by the opposition New Democrats through a freedom of information request, contradicts testimony given under oath to the integrity commissioner last year.
At issue is an Oct. 17, 2022 email exchange - on their private accounts instead of on government servers - between Patrick Sackville, then the premier's principal secretary and now his chief of staff, and Ryan Amato, at the time the top aide to then-municipal affairs minister Steve Clark.
In a message from Amato to Sackville that day, the ministerial chief of staff shared a "list of criteria for removals" of 7,400 acres from the two-million-acre environmentally sensitive Greenbelt around the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area.
These include "infrastructure services," such as nearby roads, gas and water mains, hydro lines, police and fire services, among other things.
It also listed "potential offsets" such as the Paris Galt Moraine "green space our government has decided not to move on" and "options to go larger depending on executive interest."
The timing of the email is at odds with testimony made under oath to J. David Wake, the integrity commissioner, for his Aug. 30 report on the Greenbelt land swap.
"Members of the premier's staff also told us they were not involved in the selection of properties," wrote Wake.
"Mr. Sackville said he did not discuss specific properties to be removed or removal criteria with Mr. Amato until the briefing that occurred on October 27, 2022," the ethics watchdog continued.
"Mr. Sackville recalled being first briefed about the Greenbelt project at a meeting facilitated by the cabinet office on October 27, 2022. He told me that he recalled the intentions of the policy were discussed and that it should be 'programmatic,' meaning that it should be something that could be repeated and made standard."
But that was 10 days after the Oct. 17 email in which criteria for removal of the lands from the Greenbelt was addressed. Clark announced the changes on Nov. 4, 2022.
"This email ... shows that the premier's office was far more involved than Mr. Ford claims in the selection of Greenbelt lands for removal," said NDP Leader Marit Stiles.
"Conservatives very much want their Greenbelt scandal to go away, but it is glaringly clear they can't hide from what they've done," said Stiles, who has asked Wake to "provide an opinion on whether it is consistent with Mr. Sackville's testimony to your office under oath."
"With each new piece of evidence, we are seeing more cracks in the narrative Mr. Ford and his insiders have been trying desperately to get people to believe - that Ford was in the dark about this scheme to carve up prime farmland and green space for land speculators," she said.
Last week, the integrity commissioner advised Stiles he would "need some time to review the evidence, not only of Mr. Sackville, but other witnesses to determine whether there was an inconsistency in the evidence."
"If I determine that there was an inconsistency, I will provide you with my opinion as to what, if any, significance might attach to it. I will get back to you as soon as I have had an opportunity to review the matter," Wake wrote her on Jan. 15.
Sackville declined to comment when contacted by the Star.
But the email was shared with the NDP by cabinet office lawyers as soon as the now-chief of staff, who receives hundreds of messages daily, found and forwarded it.
"This record was just discovered upon deeper review," Sackville wrote Dec. 20 to cabinet office general counsel Don Fawcett.
Two days later, it was handed over to the New Democrats, who had filed a freedom of information request months earlier.
"To ensure all necessary due diligence was exercised, Patrick Sackville ... conducted additional searches within his personal email account," wrote Janet Dadufalza, senior freedom of information manager in cabinet office.
"From this search, an email that is considered to be responsive to your request was found. Partial access is granted," she wrote the NDP on Dec. 22.
But "access to the remaining record continues to be denied" because of cabinet privilege.
It is unclear why personal email accounts were being used to conduct official government business.
Amato resigned on Aug. 22. Clark, who remains the MPP for Leeds-Grenville-Thousand Islands and Rideau Lakes, quit Ford's cabinet on Sept. 4.
Through his lawyer, Kathryn Marshall, Amato declined to comment.
Ford has insisted he was out of the loop about the removal of 15 parcels of land from the Greenbelt that the auditor general estimated would be worth $8.28 billion to property owners.
"I had nothing to do with the changes in the Greenbelt ... the auditor general cleared us, cleared my office, and the integrity commissioner cleared me and my office," the premier said Oct. 31.
In separate reports last summer, the auditor general and integrity commissioner found certain developers with Tory connections were "favoured" in an unusual process supposedly led by Amato.
On Sept. 21, an embattled Ford finally cancelled the land swap, which is now being investigated by the RCMP.
"It was a mistake to open the Greenbelt. I'm very, very sorry," the premier said that day.
"I made a promise to you that I wouldn't touch the Greenbelt. I broke that promise. As a first step to earning back your trust, I'll be reversing the changes. We moved too quickly and we made the wrong decision ... it caused people to question our motives."
Other casualties of the imbroglio were Kaleed Rasheed, who was forced out as Ford's business minister and turfed from the Progressive Conservative caucus last September, and Jae Truesdell, who was Ford's housing adviser.
Rasheed and Truesdell were on a controversial 2020 trip to Las Vegas with Greenbelt developer Shakir Rehmatullah and Amin Massoudi, who was the premier's principal secretary at the time.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford listens as Steve Clark, thenOntario's minister of housing,speaks during a news conference in Mississauga on Aug. 11, 2023.

Subject: Testimony; Water mains; Freedom of information

Title: Internal email suggests Doug Ford's office knew Greenbelt land swap details earlier than claimed

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jan 22, 2024

Section: politics/provincial

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Document 397 of 500

Mad at Justin Trudeau? Tuning him out? The prime minister says he hears you - and he gets it

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mad-at-justin-trudeau-tuning-him-out-prime/docview/2916901853/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mad+at+Justin+Trudeau%3F+Tuning+him+out%3F+The+prime+minister+says+he+hears+you+-+and+he+gets+it&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau gets a lot of advice. It goes with the job of being prime minister.
But who does Trudeau seek out when times get tough, as they have so often over the past year?
"Define 'tough times,' because that sounds like Monday to me," Trudeau said when he sat down with me for a wide-ranging, one-on-one interview last week. "Every day is tough. We're running a country in a really, really complex time."
As 2024 gets under way, and as Trudeau prepares to huddle with his cabinet and the larger Liberal caucus to map out the year ahead, there's no let-up in sight for those tough times.
Canada is in the midst of a housing and affordability crisis, which Trudeau's critics - and there are many of them - are all too eager to pin on him. The prime minister has just come out of a round of year-end interviews in which he was asked repeatedly why he was staying on in the job.
Conservatives and their leader, Pierre Poilievre, are surging in the polls. A war in the Middle East is reverberating on the ground in Canada, dividing communities and Trudeau's own Liberal caucus. Ukraine is about to head into its third year under an illegal Russian invasion. Meanwhile, Canada's largest ally and neighbour, the United States, is flirting with the prospect of putting Donald Trump back in the White House, with all the collateral damage that could bring to Canada and the world.
Trudeau has said repeatedly that he doesn't intend to walk away from his job or any of these challenges, so we didn't waste a lot of time in this interview talking about whether he would - or should - pack it in.
We spoke instead about what all these tough times for Trudeau and his government reveal about larger strains in the fabric of Canadian society right now, and what, if anything, an eight-plus-year-old government can do about them.
Trudeau is concerned about where the Canadian consensus on immigration is headed, for instance, as people start to make connections between the influx of newcomers and demands on an already overloaded housing market and health-care system.
"I've long said that one of the most important responsibilities of any Canadian prime minister of government is to protect that consensus," he said. "We are facing a real challenge around that right now."
This is why Poilievre has been talking about tying immigration levels to housing, and why Immigration Minister Marc Miller has been talking about putting more stringent conditions on the inflow of foreign students and temporary workers.
However, Trudeau added, "One of the challenges around getting more housing built is having more construction workers. And one of the challenges we have around wait times in our health-care system is getting more health-care workers. We know that shutting down immigration would not even be a theoretical answer to that."
In other words, it's complicated.
Unfortunately for Trudeau, and progressive politicians and governments of all kinds these days, when times are tough, people aren't in the mood to hear that the problems have complicated causes or solutions. That's true about immigration, but also inflation and the cost of living or foreign interference in our democracy.
Last spring, Trudeau made a remarkable admission - or, at least, one you don't hear prime ministers make that often. He acknowledged that there are Canadians out there who aren't going to take his word for anything, no matter what he says.
So in this interview, I asked him what he was going to do about that - or whether, in fact, there was anything he could do about it.
"First of all, there are people who don't listen to anything any politician is going to say, and that is certainly increasing," he said. It goes hand in hand, Trudeau believes, with the fragmentation of the media landscape and the decline in traditional journalism. "If you don't believe politicians, you don't believe a prime minister, you don't believe all the assembled newspapers and media outlets that are recording what the prime minister is doing. It becomes harder and harder to break through."
A thoughtful long-time Liberal said to me last year that Trudeau and his team had a full grasp on all the antagonism out there toward them. How could they not? Trudeau is dogged by those angry protests everywhere he goes, and the prime minister talked about that at length when we sat down last year for an interview.
This Liberal, however, was worried Trudeau doesn't appreciate the apathy and indifference toward the government and how people may be tuning out whatever they say after eight long years in office.
"I certainly get the indifference," Trudeau said. "The challenge of the Liberal party as a centrist, a centre-left party, is that we don't have those fringes within the party on the far left, or on the far right, that are mobilized and activated about politics all the time."
It's the old dilemma of being in the middle - it's hard to fire people up about standing in the centre. Trudeau does believe, however, that Liberals are up against something relatively new in this climate, which he calls opinion-as-identity politics.
"I don't think that was a feature too much of other times in politics--where what you think about something actually creates the circles and the people that you actually associate with, and it defines who you are."
He also believes that there's a worldwide shift under way within conservatism and on the right, both in Canada and on the larger world stage. Traditional conservatism leaned a lot on respect for institutions and expertise and authority, Trudeau said, but "this particular brand of conservatism that seems to be spreading ... doesn't seem to be much about respecting institutions or protecting institutions ... It's almost anti-institutionalism; it's anti-expertise, it's anti-authority."
Is that what is happening in conservative-led provinces such as Saskatchewan and Alberta, where premiers such as Scott Moe and Danielle Smith have been openly musing about opting out of paying taxes or federal legislation they don't accept? Trudeau isn't sure it stretches that far.
"I don't think it's actually about the Constitution or division of powers. I think it's about trying to capture the mood of people ... It's always been the easiest thing for any provincial leader to do, is blame Ottawa, right? ... It's ever been thus, and it always will be."
But refusing to collect the carbon levy, as Saskatchewan is promising?
"Let's see where we actually go with that," Trudeau says. "Canada is a country of the rule of law."
Many prime ministers, especially the longer they serve in office, say they pay no attention to the media or the polls. Jean Chrétien, for instance, said he never watched the news before bed and Stephen Harper made no secret of his antipathy to the parliamentary press gallery.
Trudeau says he still is a consumer of the media, although he counsels all newcomers to politics to pay little attention to social media.
"But it really is important to be aware of how people are feeling out there ... because it does impact your ability to do the job - not how you feel about the job, but how you're able to actually succeed in the projects that you're putting forward."
He said he is not too fussed about all the talk of his unpopularity. "I think there's a lot of people who are just rightly grumpy at the world right now ... I think there's all sorts of things that are happening that are difficult, (so) it's got to be the prime minister's fault."
Trudeau also notes that he's had a lifetime of training in ignoring personal attacks - and flattery. "I grew up in a situation where I had to brush aside people who hated me because they didn't like my dad. But I also had to learn how to brush off people who loved me, because they loved my dad. Neither of those are necessarily real."
He says he was recently at a ceremony in Richmond Hill to commemorate the victims of the Ukraine International Airlines jetliner that was shot down by Iran four years ago. One long-time Liberal approached him and asked Trudeau how he was feeling. "It's tough," he said. The Liberal agreed, citing the polls and all the attacks on the prime minister.
"No," he said. "It's tough to be here with grieving families."
Another Monday, as he might put it. "The polls, the noise of politics ... that's just par for the course. It doesn't bug me. It's a tough time for Canadians."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau speaks with the Star's Susan Delacourt in Guelph on Jan. 12, 2024.

Subject: Institutionalism; Politics; Prime ministers; Immigration

Location: Saskatchewan Canada; Canada; Ukraine

People: Trudeau, Justin

Title: Mad at Justin Trudeau? Tuning him out? The prime minister says he hears you - and he gets it

Document 398 of 500

When every day is a tough day

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/when-every-day-is-tough/docview/2916872774/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=When+every+day+is+a+tough+day&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau gets a lot of advice. It goes with the job of being prime minister.
But who does Trudeau seek out when times get tough, as they have so often over the past year?
"Define 'tough times,' because that sounds like Monday to me," Trudeau said when he sat down with me for a wide-ranging, one-on-one interview last week. "Every day is tough. We're running a country in a really, really complex time."
As 2024 gets under way, and as Trudeau prepares to huddle with his cabinet and the larger Liberal caucus to map out the year ahead, there's no let-up in sight for those tough times.
Canada is in the midst of a housing and affordability crisis, which Trudeau's critics - and there are many of them - are all too eager to pin on him. The prime minister has just come out of a round of year-end interviews in which he was asked repeatedly why he was staying on in the job.
Conservatives and their leader, Pierre Poilievre, are surging in the polls. A war in the Middle East is reverberating on the ground in Canada, dividing communities and Trudeau's own Liberal caucus. Ukraine is about to head into its third year under an illegal Russian invasion. Meanwhile, Canada's largest ally and neighbour, the United States, is flirting with the prospect of putting Donald Trump back in the White House, with all the collateral damage that could bring to Canada and the world.
Trudeau has said repeatedly that he doesn't intend to walk away from his job or any of these challenges, so we didn't waste a lot of time in this interview talking about whether he would - or should - pack it in.
We spoke instead about what all these tough times for Trudeau and his government reveal about larger strains in the fabric of Canadian society right now, and what, if anything, an eight-plus-year-old government can do about them.
Trudeau is concerned about where the Canadian consensus on immigration is headed, for instance, as people start to make connections between the influx of newcomers and demands on an already overloaded housing market and health-care system.
"I've long said that one of the most important responsibilities of any Canadian prime minister of government is to protect that consensus," he said. "We are facing a real challenge around that right now."
This is why Poilievre has been talking about tying immigration levels to housing, and why Immigration Minister Marc Miller has been talking about putting more stringent conditions on the inflow of foreign students and temporary workers.
However, Trudeau added, "One of the challenges around getting more housing built is having more construction workers. And one of the challenges we have around wait times in our health-care system is getting more health-care workers. We know that shutting down immigration would not even be a theoretical answer to that."
In other words, it's complicated.
Unfortunately for Trudeau, and progressive politicians and governments of all kinds these days, when times are tough, people aren't in the mood to hear that the problems have complicated causes or solutions. That's true about immigration, but also inflation and the cost of living or foreign interference in our democracy.
Last spring, Trudeau made a remarkable admission - or, at least, one you don't hear prime ministers make that often. He acknowledged that there are Canadians out there who aren't going to take his word for anything, no matter what he says.
So in this interview, I asked him what he was going to do about that - or whether, in fact, there was anything he could do about it.
"First of all, there are people who don't listen to anything any politician is going to say, and that is certainly increasing," he said. It goes hand in hand, Trudeau believes, with the fragmentation of the media landscape and the decline in traditional journalism. "If you don't believe politicians, you don't believe a prime minister, you don't believe all the assembled newspapers and media outlets that are recording what the prime minister is doing. It becomes harder and harder to break through."
A thoughtful long-time Liberal said to me last year that Trudeau and his team had a full grasp on all the antagonism out there toward them. How could they not? Trudeau is dogged by those angry protests everywhere he goes, and the prime minister talked about that at length when we sat down last year for an interview.
This Liberal, however, was worried Trudeau doesn't appreciate the apathy and indifference toward the government and how people may be tuning out whatever they say after eight long years in office.
"I certainly get the indifference," Trudeau said. "The challenge of the Liberal party as a centrist, a centre-left party, is that we don't have those fringes within the party on the far left, or on the far right, that are mobilized and activated about politics all the time."
It's the old dilemma of being in the middle - it's hard to fire people up about standing in the centre. Trudeau does believe, however, that Liberals are up against something relatively new in this climate, which he calls opinion-as-identity politics.
"I don't think that was a feature too much of other times in politics - where what you think about something actually creates the circles and the people that you actually associate with, and it defines who you are."
He also believes that there's a worldwide shift under way within conservatism and on the right, both in Canada and on the larger world stage. Traditional conservatism leaned a lot on respect for institutions and expertise and authority, Trudeau said, but "this particular brand of conservatism that seems to be spreading ... doesn't seem to be much about respecting institutions or protecting institutions ... It's almost anti-institutionalism; it's anti-expertise, it's anti-authority."
Is that what is happening in conservative-led provinces such as Saskatchewan and Alberta, where premiers such as Scott Moe and Danielle Smith have been openly musing about opting out of paying taxes or federal legislation they don't accept? Trudeau isn't sure it stretches that far.
"I don't think it's actually about the Constitution or division of powers. I think it's about trying to capture the mood of people ... It's always been the easiest thing for any provincial leader to do, is blame Ottawa, right? ... It's ever been thus, and it always will be."
But refusing to collect the carbon levy, as Saskatchewan is promising?
"Let's see where we actually go with that," Trudeau says. "Canada is a country of the rule of law."
Many prime ministers, especially the longer they serve in office, say they pay no attention to the media or the polls. Jean Chrétien, for instance, said he never watched the news before bed and Stephen Harper made no secret of his antipathy to the parliamentary press gallery.
Trudeau says he still is a consumer of the media, although he counsels all newcomers to politics to pay little attention to social media.
"But it really is important to be aware of how people are feeling out there ... because it does impact your ability to do the job - not how you feel about the job, but how you're able to actually succeed in the projects that you're putting forward."
He said he is not too fussed about all the talk of his unpopularity. "I think there's a lot of people who are just rightly grumpy at the world right now ... I think there's all sorts of things that are happening that are difficult, (so) it's got to be the prime minister's fault."
Trudeau also notes that he's had a lifetime of training in ignoring personal attacks - and flattery. "I grew up in a situation where I had to brush aside people who hated me because they didn't like my dad. But I also had to learn how to brush off people who loved me, because they loved my dad. Neither of those are necessarily real."
He says he was recently at a ceremony in Richmond Hill to commemorate the victims of the Ukraine International Airlines jetliner that was shot down by Iran four years ago. One long-time Liberal approached him and asked Trudeau how he was feeling. "It's tough," he said. The Liberal agreed, citing the polls and all the attacks on the prime minister.
"No," he said. "It's tough to be here with grieving families."
Another Monday, as he might put it. "The polls, the noise of politics ... that's just par for the course. It doesn't bug me. It's a tough time for Canadians."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has acknowledged that there are Canadians who aren't going to take his word for anything, no matter what he says.Sean KilpatrickTHE CANADIAN PRESS file photo
