Document 399 of 500

Potholes and homelessness won't solve themselves: Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/potholes-homelessness-wont-solve-themselves/docview/2916700583/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Potholes+and+homelessness+won%27t+solve+themselves%3A+Letters&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Potholes and homelessness won't solve themselvesLiberal MPs are furious at Chow over property tax gambit, Jan. 15
Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre must take Torontonians for fools: "This is what you get when you elect NDP/Liberal politicians: massive tax hikes."
Toronto's financial problems are the result of Mike Harris, a Conservative premier, downloading provincial programs to the city, and the election ploy of Conservative mayors, beginning with Mel Lastman through John Tory, refusing to raise taxes enough to sustain the city's needs.
If Poilievre really wanted to help, he would address the problem of cities' primary source of revenue being property taxes. But he has not uttered a word about that.
Let's show him we understand potholes and homelessness won't solve themselves, and support Mayor Chow to build on Toronto's strengths.
Marcia Zalev, Toronto
Just as the feds helped big banks and big corporations in the past, municipalities should receive financial help, too
Liberal MPs are furious at Chow over property tax gambit, Jan. 15
Cities are under increasing financial strain as their revenue streams collapse and costs escalate. But increases to property taxes only shift the burden to already overextended homeowners. A better solution is for the federal government to distribute special grants that allow lower levels of governments to maintain normal services as well as cover those additional expenses relating to refugees.
The federal government can always access funds from the Bank of Canada which was established during the Great Depression to deal with systemic financial problems. For example, after the great financial crisis of 2008 when credit markets froze, the federal government bailed out financial institutions and corporations through a $200 billion Extraordinary Financing Framework.
Today provinces and municipalities should also receive direct help just as big banks, insurers, car manufacturers, and the issuers of credit cards received accommodation in the past. The central bank can and should be used primarily to benefit Canadians, and not just to ensure profits for economic elites.
Larry Kazdan, Vancouver, B.C.
Chow should focus on reducing costs rather than increasing revenueLiberal MPs are furious at Chow over property tax gambit, Jan. 15
No matter what the reasoning is by Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, she and others believe that a 16.5 per cent increase in tax is a valid move.
At the same time, we are inundated by economic news of consequences resulting from the necessary rise in interest rates required to drive inflation down to two per cent.
It makes one wonder why these politicians focus entirely on revenue as opposed to reducing costs.
Dave de Sylva, Markham
At least Mayor Chow understands municipal budgetsRefugee funding war heats up between Olivia Chow and feds, Jan. 15
The person misleading Torontonians is Liberal MP for Etobicoke Centre Yvan Baker, not Mayor Olivia Chow.
As Toronto asks for operations funding for refugees sent to Toronto by the federal government, Baker pretends billions of dollars to build new concrete tunnels somehow covers city shelter operations.
I'm glad we have Chow as mayor, at least she understands municipal budgets.
Cameron MacLeod, Toronto
MP Baker looking to score political points
Refugee funding war heats up between Olivia Chow and feds, Jan. 15
I am somewhat taken aback by Liberal MP for Etobicoke Centre Yvan Baker accusing Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow of being a liar, without listing the occasions when the federal government has come to the rescue of the city.
The federal and provincial governments have the possibility to borrow, the city does not. When former Ontario Premier Mike Harris took over funding education from the city, a predictable expense, he gave the city the responsibility for welfare, which is not predictable.
Many things are in danger of rapidly spiraling down if nothing is done: the TTC, the state of road surfaces, the state of repair of city housing for example.
In my opinion, Baker is just trying to score a political point, just like the person in the Jan. 17 editorial cartoon.
Christine de Groot, Toronto
Property taxes on monster houses must reflect the sizeHere's how property taxes work as Toronto mulls major hike, Jan. 17
If Toronto property taxes are to go up, I do hope that the taxes on the recently built, multimillion monster houses on my street that have five bedrooms and five bathrooms will be greater than the taxes on the original little bungalows, such as mine, that have two bedrooms and one bathroom.
The monster houses use far more city resources than the bungalows and the property taxes should reflect that.
The Municipal Property Assessment Corporation (MPAC) must conduct a thorough assessment to ensure that the taxes on monster houses reflect their size.
Mara Glebovs, Etobicoke

CAPTION: Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, budget committee chair Councillor Shelley Carroll and city manager Paul Johnson at a Toronto City Hall press conference regarding the 2024 budget.

Subject: Federal government; Mayors; Editorial cartoons; Funding; Politics; Cities; Property taxes; Tax increases

Document 400 of 500

Why did Doug Ford's government defeat a bill on sexual harassment?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-did-doug-fords-government-defeat-bill-on/docview/2916700547/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+did+Doug+Ford%27s+government+defeat+a+bill+on+sexual+harassment%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ford government voted down a bill that would allow local politicians found guilty of harassment to be kicked out of office - but with more than 200 municipalities now supporting the idea, there is renewed pressure for the province to act.
The Women of Ontario Say No, a non-partisan advocacy group, continues to garner more local council endorsements and is planning a meeting with staff from the office of the minister of municipal affairs and housing in the hopes of changing the government's mind.
"We're talking about people that have the most power in communities held to a lower standard than any other workers in Ontario," said Emily McIntosh. The Barrie-area resident is part of the advocacy group that last March had 30 local councils supporting such legislation, and now has 201.
The group will also have some high-profile help at next week's annual conference of the Rural Ontario Municipal Association, where both NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner will wear the group's new "Accountability Now" T-shirts or speak to the issue. Newly elected Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie is also onside.
McIntosh thought the issue would be a no-brainer, given a number of high-profile cases of harassment involving local politicians, "and I thought of course the government would move on it because it's (non-partisan) and it's a human rights issue. It's basic workplace safety, and it's about instilling public trust in publicly elected positions. But that's just not been the case at all."
Liberal MPP Stephen Blais (Orléans) has twice tried to introduce a private member's bill, and at one point had the unanimous support of the legislature, but last May his bill, the Stopping Harassment and Abuse by Local Leaders Act, didn't pass.
Blais - who served on Ottawa city council with former councillor Rick Chiarelli, who was found to have harassed female staffers over several years - was proposing that all municipal councillors fall under workplace harassment and violence policies, allowing them to be fired and even barred from running in the next election for violations.
His bill had the support of former premier Kathleen Wynne, who addressed councils in Richmond Hill, Orangeville and Mulmur about the importance of the legislation.
But in the legislature, PC MPP Charmaine Williams (Brampton Centre), who is associate minister of women's social and economic opportunity, noted when she was a councillor "allegations happened in my city as well."
"Our government has been absolutely crystal clear that we will not tolerate any workplace harassment or discrimination of any kind, full stop. We're not going to tolerate this activity by any councillor in the province," she added. However, "this bill wants to make integrity commissioners more powerful than an appointed judge. If you're accused of something illegal, you get a trial, but this bill wants to replace that and this bill wants to allow the integrity commissioner to remove an elected official without a trial."
Blais had said his bill addressed such concerns by stipulating that a judge would need to approve any integrity commissioner decision before ousting an elected official.
A spokesperson for Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra said last week that the government continues to believe "in the local democratic process and we trust voters to hold local politicians to account at the ballot box. Councillors and heads of council must carry out their duties as elected officials in an ethical and responsible manner."
But the Association of Municipalities of Ontario, Ontario Big City Mayors and the Rural Ontario Municipal Association have all agreed help is needed on code of conduct compliance.
Crombie, who was mayor of Mississauga before being elected leader of the Ontario Liberals, said the bill "would have strengthened municipal policies and processes around workplace harassment."
She called it "deeply upsetting to see the Ford government refuse to support this important initiative. Unlike the Conservatives, Ontario Liberals will pass the principles contained in Bill 5 into law when we form government."
McIntosh said she's heard from municipal workers and even deputy mayors who tells stories of being harassed.
"The problem is there's no incentive to report this behaviour because at the end of the day, the person can retain their position of power," McIntosh said. "We know that this legislation would be preventative in nature ... It's about creating environments where there is truly zero tolerance for violence and harassment."
Stiles, who has posted a video on social media in support of the legislation, called it "outrageous to think that any woman is going to work afraid of being harassed" or feeling unsafe.
"We need accountability, and we need it now."
CAPTION: Emily McIntosh is shown at the Barrie City Hall on March 18, 2023.

Subject: Legislation; Councils; Politics; Politicians; Accountability

Title: Why did Doug Ford's government defeat a bill on sexual harassment?

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jan 20, 2024

Section: politics/provincial

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Document 401 of 500

Municipalities back bill rejected by Ford's Tories: Sexual harassment legislation would see guilty local politicians kicked out of office

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/municipalities-back-bill-rejected-fords-tories/docview/2916625213/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Municipalities+back+bill+rejected+by+Ford%27s+Tories%3A+Sexual+harassment+legislation+would+see+guilty+local+politicians+kicked+out+of+office&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Blais had said his bill addressed such concerns by stipulating that a judge would need to approve any integrity commissioner decision before ousting an elected official.
A spokesperson for Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra said last week that the government continues to believe "in the local democratic process and we trust voters to hold local politicians to account at the ballot box. Councillors and heads of council must carry out their duties as elected officials in an ethical and responsible manner."
But the Association of Municipalities of Ontario, Ontario Big City Mayors and the Rural Ontario Municipal Association have all agreed help is needed on code of conduct compliance.
Crombie, who was mayor of Mississauga before being elected leader of the Ontario Liberals, said the bill "would have strengthened municipal policies and processes around workplace harassment."
She called it "deeply upsetting to see the Ford government refuse to support this important initiative. Unlike the Conservatives, Ontario Liberals will pass the principles contained in Bill 5 into law when we form government."
McIntosh said she's heard from municipal workers and even deputy mayors who tells stories of being harassed.
"The problem is there's no incentive to report this behaviour because at the end of the day, the person can retain their position of power," McIntosh said. "We know that this legislation would be preventative in nature ... It's about creating environments where there is truly zero tolerance for violence and harassment."
Stiles, who has posted a video on social media in support of the legislation, called it "outrageous to think that any woman is going to work afraid of being harassed" or feeling unsafe.
"We need accountability, and we need it now."
CAPTION: Emily McIntosh, a member of advocacy group The Women of Ontario Say No, says it's important to create "environments where there is truly zero tolerance for violence and harassment."Giovanni Capriotti file photo for the Toronto Star
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Zero tolerance; Violence; Legislation; Councils; Politics; Politicians; Advocacy; Accountability

Title: Municipalities back bill rejected by Ford's Tories:   Sexual harassment legislation would see guilty local politicians kicked out of office

Document 402 of 500

Politicians' homes off limits to protests

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/politicians-homes-off-limits-protests/docview/2916606614/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Politicians%27+homes+off+limits+to+protests&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's a rare day when politicians from rival parties can agree on anything in these troubled and intractable times.
But for one fleeting moment, MPs from all sides of the aisle were on the side of Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly.
Not over Canada's Middle East policy - they still disagree bitterly. All were agreed, however, on when - and where - those disagreements are utterly offside and absolutely off limits.
Joly's Montreal residence - not a constituency office, nor the House of Commons - was targeted for an organized protest last week after her home address was publicized on promotional posters. That so many protesters would converge on her private home - not the House where she is held accountable in the daily question period - was enough to rally MPs, right and left, to her side.
They knew a line in the sand had been crossed at the snow-covered lawn outside Joly's home.
Protesters had defiantly set up camp - erecting a pop-up canopy tent (courtesy of the Concordia Student Union), hoisting banners, unfurling flags, deploying megaphones and chanting anti-Israel slogans.
No mere political protest against a public official, this was an invasion of Joly's personal space. And an intrusion into the private lives of her partner and neighbours - none of them involved in the blood sport of political conflict in Canada, nor implicated in the bloodshed of military conflict abroad.
Does it matter what their protest was about? Palestinian rights, Israeli wrongs, Hamas right or wrong, genocide by either side? Can a cause be so undeniably right and one's righteousness so indisputable that the ends justify the means - whatever means necessary?
"This is appalling," NDP foreign affairs critic Heather McPherson wrote on social media. "People do not have to agree with politicians and elected representatives, but to harass them at their private homes is completely and utterly unacceptable."
"This is intimidation not protest," added Conservative deputy leader Melissa Lantsman. "Just like protesting in a predominantly Jewish neighbourhood in Toronto was. Find an office, a consulate, an embassy, a government building, a public square to protest all you want for what you believe in - not a private home."
Much has already been said about the spillover of the Israel-Hamas war onto the home front here in Canada. I've written before that it transgresses every Canadian norm to hide behind tortuous rationalizations while targeting Jewish-owned restaurants, Toronto's downtown Jewish Community Centre, or the Jewish neighbourhood Lantsman was referring to beside Avenue Road and the 401.
I covered the Israeli-Arab conflict on the ground long enough to know that the world needs more Canada, not more of the Middle East's worst excesses - and excuses. The more that Canadians emulate those hatreds, the more we will normalize dehumanization and demonization, antisemitism and Islamophobia.
But that's another argument for another day. On this day last week, a cabinet minister's personal space was violated.
Oh, but vilification is not violence, the protesters insist. And so elected representatives who willingly sign up to be heckled in the House are unwillingly hounded at home - with family and friends, children and elders treated as collateral damage.
Make no mistake, there is no shortage of protests for which protesters can persuade themselves that vigorous public debate extends to a politician's private driveway or sanctuary. The righteous can rationalize their rightness, left right and centre.
The focus on Canada's foreign minister is merely the most recent in an endless series of transgressions.
Former premier Kathleen Wynne was targeted by Black Lives Matter activists who showed up on her driveway.
The former Etobicoke home of the current premier, Doug Ford, has attracted protesters who thought he was fair game after hours - including one person who pulled a knife and had to be disarmed by an alert OPP protective detail.
"Every single day, my friends, there's protesters outside my house," Ford said, noting that his neighbours "didn't sign up for this."
Former health minister Christine Elliott faced protests at home by anti-vaxxers who faulted her for pandemic-era restrictions. So, too, did Education Minister Stephen Lecce.
The rise of far-right populism and decline of progressive politics can be explained by a lack of good ideas, but also an absence of good leaders. If we have a crisis of thoughtful leadership today, it's because politicians are exposed to so much abuse - online and now on their doorstep - that no one would think to give up their home life.
This isn't just about protecting politicians, it's about safeguarding our democracy. For the best way to chase worthy people out of politics is to make it not worth their while.
CREDIT: Martin Regg Cohn OPINION

Subject: Demonstrations & protests; Politics; Politicians; Diplomatic & consular services

Location: Middle East; Canada; Israel

People: Joly, Melanie

Company / organization: Name: Hamas; NAICS: 813940

Title: Politicians' homes off limits to protests

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: A.4

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jan 20, 2024

Section: News

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Here's what's truly awful about protesting at politicians' homes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/heres-whats-truly-awful-about-protesting-at/docview/2916720112/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here%27s+what%27s+truly+awful+about+protesting+at+politicians%27+homes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 404 of 500

Ontario expanding Hazel McCallion LRT: Plan to add to route in Mississauga, Brampton comes amid criticism over other delayed projects

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-expanding-hazel-mccallion-lrt/docview/2916356959/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+expanding+Hazel+McCallion+LRT%3A+Plan+to+add+to+route+in+Mississauga%2C+Brampton+comes+amid+criticism+over+other+delayed+projects&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is expanding the Hazel McCallion LRT into downtown Mississauga and Brampton four years after scaling back the transit line to cut costs.
In a Jan. 17 letter obtained by the Star, Transportation Minister Prabmeet Singh Sarkaria directed Metrolinx CEO Phil Verster to "bring forward a plan to build the downtown Mississauga loop and an extension into downtown Brampton."
The news comes amid heavy criticism over Metrolinx's other delayed LRT projects, including the embattled Eglinton Crosstown LRT, which was initially set to be complete by 2020 but as of now has no declared opening date.
The Finch West LRT, another major Metrolinx project, is set to open later this year after it was quietly delayed last summer.
The Hazel McCallion LRT, formerly the Hurontario-Main LRT, was initially set to stretch for 23 kilometres and 26 stops linking Mississauga's lakefront to Brampton's downtown.
But in a contentious 2015 vote, Brampton's city council rejected the plan by former premier Kathleen Wynne's Liberals to extend the transit line into downtown along Main Street, cutting the project short by four stops at Steeles Avenue.
In 2019, the LRT was once again on the chopping block, when Verster announced Metrolinx was shortening the transit line due to budget pressures, removing a proposed loop around Mississauga's city centre and eliminating three stops extending the LRT to Brampton.
Mississauga and Brampton leaders have pushed the government to reinstate the nixed loop and Brampton stops, with Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown calling the $4.6-billion LRT "half a project" to Mississauga News earlier this month.
Premier Doug Ford has previously said he would support reinstating the Mississauga loop, but this is the first indication his government would make it happen.
It's not clear how the new additions will affect the opening date for the Hazel McCallion LRT, which was set to be complete this year.
"When construction nears completion and we move into the testing and commissioning phase, we will be in a better position to provide a specific opening date," Metrolinx spokesperson Andrea Ernesaks told the Star.
"Peel is one of Canada's fastest-growing regions," Ministry of Transportation spokesperson Dakota Brasier said in a statement.
"Our government will continue to invest historic amounts in connected transportation there - and across the province - so that Ontarians can spend less time commuting and more time at home, doing what matters most to them."
At Queen's Park Thursday, Liberal leader and former Mississauga mayor Bonnie Crombie, who has long advocated for the expansion of the line, said she was "delighted that they are doing the right thing and considering putting the downtown loop back in the LRT."
Crombie, who stepped down as Mississauga mayor last Friday, was asked by reporters if the Tories were acting for political reasons over concerns they could lose Peel seats to her Liberals in the 2026 election. "I can't speculate to their motives, but they're doing the right thing," she said, welcoming the PC policy reversal.
"This is a commitment that the premier has made in the past ... and I'm delighted that is being considered to be put back in the plan."
In the letter, Sarkaria referenced the troubles with the Eglinton Crosstown LRT, which he blamed on Wynne's Liberals, who have not been in power since 2018.
"While projects like the Eglinton Crosstown started under the previous Liberals have faced years of delays, our government is doing things differently," Sarkaria wrote.
"Construction on the Hazel McCallion LRT, championed by Premier Ford, continues to make progress, including the near completion of the Operations and Maintenance Storage Facility for the project."
Sarkaria asked Verster to develop a business case and strategy for bids to build both the Mississauga and Brampton extensions, due to the Ministry of Transportation on Feb. 5.
With files from Robert Benzie
CREDIT: Lex Harvey Toronto Star With files from Robert Benzie

Subject: Light rail transportation; Mass transit

Document 405 of 500

Province slammed for use of temporary nurses: Agencies charge high rates amid staffing shortages

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-slammed-use-temporary-nurses/docview/2916356950/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+slammed+for+use+of+temporary+nurses%3A+Agencies+charge+high+rates+amid+staffing+shortages&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Pressure is building on the Ontario government to take action against temporary agencies that charge hospitals high rates for replacement nurses amid a worsening staff shortage and growing hospital deficits.
Citing "price gouging" among the concerns raised in the provincial auditor general's latest annual report, Liberal MPP Adil Shamji has asked the office to conduct a value-for-money audit on the impact of temporary staffing agencies in health care.
This is on top of NDP calls for Premier Doug Ford and Health Minister Sylvia Jones to cap rates charged to hospitals by the agencies and scrap Bill 124, which capped annual wage hikes for nurses and other public sector workers at one per cent for three years and has been blamed for high attrition rates.
"Temporary staffing agencies are holding our health-care system hostage," Shamji (Don Valley East), an emergency room physician, said Thursday.
"They engage in practices like rapid price gouging, with some agencies charging three to four times artificially suppressed public wages. We see dynamic surge pricing, the way you might see it with Uber and it's very busy on a Friday night."
The auditor's report in December found hospitals and nursing homes were over-reliant on temporary nurses.
"Worsening staffing shortages have forced hospitals to hire agency nurses at significantly higher hourly rates than permanent staff ... hospitals are spending millions of dollars on agency nurses," the report warned, noting the lack of a cap on rates agencies can charge.
In a statement, the health minister's office said agencies provide important flexibility.
"Hospitals have always had the tool to use agency nurses and did under the former Liberal government. In fact, the proportion of agency nurses has decreased from 3.8 per cent in 2017 to under three per cent in 2022-23," Jones spokesperson Hannah Jensen said.
But with costs of agency nurses rising, Shamji said a value-for-money audit by the auditor general would "reveal the full scope of the financial exploitation and catastrophe that Doug Ford is enabling."
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Workforce planning; Price gouging; Nurses; Labor shortages

Business indexing term: Subject: Workforce planning Price gouging Labor shortages

Title: Province slammed for use of temporary nurses:   Agencies charge high rates amid staffing shortages

Document 406 of 500

It's Big Tech versus little minds

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/big-tech-versus-little-minds/docview/2916356585/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=It%27s+Big+Tech+versus+little+minds&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Perhaps a slate board and a pencil sharpener would help in a time-travelling world?
Nah, there is no going back to the little schoolhouse classroom. Not even the classroom of a few years ago.
In this era of modern device entanglement, a student without a cellphone is practically starkers. Which is a fitting descriptor, given some of the soft porn selfies young people post on social media.
Well, get ready to strip down.
No time soon, of course, because the Toronto District School Board never does anything with alacrity - consultations, reviews, reports, stutter steps in nearly every undertaking. But likely coming down the pike by and by, given the tone of the board's discussion the other night on regulating cellphones in class.
This is hardly the TDSB out front and revolutionary. Many jurisdictions in Canada and around the globe are already clamping down on the annoyance and distraction presented by smarty-pants phones, with all the word at one's fingertips. If students were actually accessing real news, rather than surfing TikTok and the like, that might not even be entirely objectionable. An informed mind is preferable to ignorance. Instead, young people - all people actually - are being led astray by staggering misinformation whilst also getting groomed as consumers by Big Tech.
A UNESCO report last summer warned against the creeping deadheading of impressionable minds, issuing an "urgent call" for policymakers to prioritize student needs and craft enhanced guidance to navigate exploding technology. Excessive smartphone use, UNESCO emphasized, has impacted learning negatively and demonstrably. Even notifications popping up on a nearby phone can discompose a person, requiring up to 20 minutes to refocus.
Belgium, Spain and the United Kingdom, which have adopted restrictions, assert that limiting smartphone usage in schools has led to better academic outcomes, particularly for underachieving students who might already have problems concentrating, for all manner of reasons. In Ontario, boards were directed four years ago to update their codes of conduct - allowing cellphones in class only for educational, health and special needs purposes - and Education Minister Stephen Lecce recently told reporters he "strongly" supports tougher rules.
Students will wail that grown-ups are ganging up on them, and that young people's wishes are always given short shrift. But it seems clear that parents, at least those who've contacted the TDSB, are in favour of a more stringent policy. Though it can be argued that this responsibility rests firstly with mom and dad and maybe kids in Grade 3 shouldn't be issued cellphones in the first place.
To be fair, students are hardly the only culprits, eyeballs glued to phone screens. Everybody everywhere all the time is doing it, transfixed by X feeds and texts, the cacophony of imagery, whether at work, on the subway, while driving - bylaws notwithstanding - and during meals. Couples barely bother talking to each other in restaurants, so preoccupied are they with their devices.
Board chair Rachel Chernos Lin put forward the motion at Wednesday's confab of the governance and policy committee - accepted - arguing a clarified and sensible stand-alone policy, reaching with more exactitude beyond the provincial government mandate, is necessary for today's students. To this point, teachers have borne the brunt of enforcing whatever code of conduct exists and that, as the committee heard, might depend on a teacher's reputation, even size, standing up there, laying down the rules.
Conversely, the board has received complaints from some parents that phones confiscated in the classroom - set aside, in a basket maybe or a locker - have been stolen and demanding restitution. Which is a sad testament to classroom goings-on. Others contend they need to be in 24/7 contact with their progeny, a reflection of society's increasing obsession with instant and all-consuming connectivity. In fact, only matters of urgency or crisis demand prompt cue-the-kid and the system already accommodates those scenarios - phone call to the office and summons on the PA system.
There is plenty of data supporting harm caused by ever-present cellphones, especially on mental health. Bullying via social media has been acutely damaging with sometimes tragic results. Young people can't be buffeted by such deleterious content but surely the classroom should be a place of refuge from abuse and intimidation.
"A lot has changed since 2019," said Chernos Lin, to wide agreement. "While I don't envision us ever not allowing a mobile device that's used for a medical condition, that kind of thing, I do think it's time to revisit this idea and I think it's really crucial that we create an actual policy. Because one of the things that I've heard the most in the five years I've been a trustee and increasingly in the last two years, since the pandemic, is that parents and teachers are really concerned about the use of devices by students and its interruption to learning, and the impact it's having in the classroom."
Part of the concern, said Lin, is that it has been left to teachers to police. Trustees need to have their back.
"I envision a policy that's very clear, that sets a culture of what place the cellphone would have in a school. I've asked that we consider restrictions around social media. I would imagine this as setting the groundwork so that teachers feel supported.
"The time is right. Things have changed dramatically. We know so much more about the impact of cellphone use, cellphone addiction; mental health and well-being are at epidemic proportions."
There is, among educators, a "passion verging on thirst" to implement a policy that would elevate an educational environment impacted by cellphones.
"In terms of how difficult it was to deal with ... and the type of negative behaviours associated with cellphone usage," said Trustee Matias de Dovitiis of his recent conversations with middle school principals. "That was news to me."
Really? Then you haven't been paying attention.
Trustee Sara Ehrhardt, who seconded the motion, said that cellphones were the first topic raised at her first ward meeting.
"I'm a person who loves technology but ... I am increasingly learning and taking to heart the evidence that's coming in around our phones and devices and social media and their harms, particularly for children."
Which, Ehrhardt admitted, put her in the weird position of agreeing with Premier Doug Ford's government, at least on this specific item. "This is a rare circumstance where I'm in very much agreement with our provincial government of the day. This is an area where restrictions are warranted and where there is some kind of consensus. I think it's important that we leverage it and are very ambitious for the benefit of students and children and our broader society."
For starters, any new protocol would have to address likely different parameters for students depending on age. Just as crucial is instructing students - their parents as well - on device literacy, in the same way that media comprehension long ago became part of the curriculum.
Trustee Michelle Aarts stressed that point.
"I don't support bans of any kind for the sake of banning things. I would really have to see that this comes attached to education programs ... The conversations around social media, I don't think we get very far on that if we are not also creating education programs, especially at an early age, those primary and junior grades, about social media. Not waiting until students are already in the mix, they've already been given cellphones, they're already mired in social media and then we're fighting upstream."
Can't turn back the clock. But hit the alarm and wake up to the realities of a digital universe that never stops haranguing.
Get the kids while they're young, really young, or Big Tech will brainwash them first.
CREDIT: Rosie DiManno Toronto Star OPINION

Ford government slammed for Ontario's use of temporary nurses

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-slammed-ontarios-use-temporary/docview/2916476620/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+slammed+for+Ontario%27s+use+of+temporary+nurses&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Pressure is building on the Ontario government to take action against temporary agencies that charge hospitals high rates for replacement nurses amid a worsening staff shortage and growing hospital deficits.
Citing "price gouging" among the concerns raised in the provincial auditor general's latest annual report, Liberal MPP Adil Shamji has asked the office to conduct a value-for-money audit on the impact of temporary staffing agencies in health care.
This is on top of NDP calls for Premier Doug Ford and Health Minister Sylvia Jones to cap rates charged to hospitals by the agencies and scrap Bill 124, which capped annual wage hikes for nurses and other public sector workers at one per cent for three years and has been blamed for high attrition rates.
"Temporary staffing agencies are holding our health-care system hostage," Shamji (Don Valley East), an emergency room physician, said Thursday.
"They engage in practices like rapid price gouging, with some agencies charging three to four times artificially suppressed public wages. We see dynamic surge pricing, the way you might see it with Uber and it's very busy on a Friday night."
The auditor's report in December found hospitals and nursing homes were overreliant on temporary nurses.
"Worsening staffing shortages have forced hospitals to hire agency nurses at significantly higher hourly rates than permanent staff ... hospitals are spending millions of dollars on agency nurses," the report warned, noting the lack of a cap on rates agencies can charge.
In a statement, the health minister's office said agencies provide important flexibility.
"Hospitals have always had the tool to use agency nurses and did under the former Liberal government. In fact, the proportion of agency nurses has decreased from 3.8 per cent in 2017 to under three per cent in 2022-23," Jones spokesperson Hannah Jensen said.
But with costs of agency nurses rising, Shamji said a value-for-money audit by the auditor general would "reveal the full scope of the financial exploitation and catastrophe that Doug Ford is enabling."
"Cautious estimates suggest that they cost us at least $175 million more than necessary," he added.
The health minister's office said the government remains ready to work with hospitals and the Ontario Hospital Association on finances, with measures to date including a four per cent funding increase to the sector and $44 million to ease emergency room wait times.
"This includes communicating to the OHA and hospital corporations across the province that our government would provide financial support to hospitals facing financial restraint," it said.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Ford's government should pass legislation capping pay for temporary agency nurses at 10 per cent above hospital rates, follow British Columbia's example of setting a nurse-patient staffing ratio to ensure quality care, and drop its appeal of Bill 124, which a provincial court struck down as unconstitutional.
"Things are only getting worse," Stiles said in a statement. "The premier needs to stop pretending this crisis doesn't exist."
CAPTION: Health Minister Sylvia Jones speaks in Toronto on Jan. 16, 2023.

Subject: Workforce planning; Emergency medical care; Price gouging; Nurses; Labor shortages

Business indexing term: Subject: Workforce planning Price gouging Labor shortages

Ontario expanding Hazel McCallion LRT into downtown Mississauga and Brampton

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-expanding-hazel-mccallion-lrt-into/docview/2916060661/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+expanding+Hazel+McCallion+LRT+into+downtown+Mississauga+and+Brampton&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is expanding the Hazel McCallion LRT into downtown Mississauga and Brampton four years after scaling back the transit line to cut costs.In a January 17 letter obtained by the Star, Transportation Minister Prabmeet Singh Sarkaria directed Metrolinx CEO Phil Verster to "bring forward a plan to build the downtown Mississauga loop and an extension into downtown Brampton."The news comes amid heavy criticism over Metrolinx's other delayed LRT projects, including the embattled Eglinton Crosstown LRT, which was initially set to be complete by 2020 but as of now has no declared opening date. The Finch West LRT, another major Metrolinx project, is set to open later this year after it was quietly delayed last summer.The Hazel McCallion LRT, formerly the Hurontario-Main LRT, was initially set to stretch for 23 kilometres and 26 stops linking Mississauga's lakefront to Brampton's downtown. But in a contentious 2015 vote, Brampton's city council rejected the plan by former premier Kathleen Wynne's Liberals to extend the transit line into downtown along Main Street, cutting the project short by four stops at Steeles Avenue. In 2019, the LRT was once again on the chopping block, when Verster announced Metrolinx was shortening the transit line due to budget pressures, removing a proposed loop around Mississauga's city centre and eliminating three stops extending the LRT to Brampton.Mississauga and Brampton leaders have pushed the government to reinstate the nixed loop and Brampton stops, with Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown calling the $4.6 billion LRT "half a project" to Mississauga News earlier this month. Premier Doug Ford has previously said he would support reinstating the Mississauga loop but this is the first indication his government would make it happen.It's not clear how the new additions will affect the opening date for the Hazel McCallion LRT, which was set to be complete this year. Metrolinx did not immediately respond to a request for comment from the Star."Peel is one of Canada's fastest-growing regions," Ministry of Transportation spokesperson Dakota Brasier said in a statement. "Our government will continue to invest historic amounts in connected transportation there - and across the province - so that Ontarians can spend less time commuting and more time at home, doing what matters most to them."In the letter, Sarkaria referenced the troubles with the Eglinton Crosstown LRT, which he blamed on Wynne's Liberals, who have not been in power since 2018."While projects like the Eglinton Crosstown started under the previous Liberals have faced years of delays, our government is doing things differently," Sarkaria wrote. "Construction on the Hazel McCallion LRT, championed by Premier Ford, continues to make progress, including the near completion of the Operations and Maintenance Storage Facility for the project."Sarkaria asked Vertser to develop a business case and strategy for bids to build both the Mississauga and Brampton extensions, due to the Ministry of Transportation on February 5.
CAPTION: Metrolinx CEO Phil Verster.

While Canadians line up at food banks, corporate profits are never enough: letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/while-canadians-line-up-at-food-banks-corporate/docview/2915914498/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=While+Canadians+line+up+at+food+banks%2C+corporate+profits+are+never+enough%3A+letters&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: While Canadians line up at food banks, corporate profits are never enoughFood bank crisis warns of increasing inequality, Nov. 30
Avoidable injustices are forcing many Canadians to choose between essential needs.
Unrelenting food-price inflation is pushing more people to use food banks while giant-grocer corporate profits and payouts to corporate officers correspondingly inflate.
Yet, the more such corporations make, the more they want - nay, need - to make in the next quarter. It's never enough. Maximizing profits at the expense of those with so much less, or nothing, is a significant part of the nature of the big business beast.
Big businesses are getting unaccountably bigger, defying the very spirit of government rules established to ensure healthy competition by limiting concentrated ownership.
And while corporate officers shrug their shoulders and defensively say their job is only to protect shareholders' bottom-line interests, the shareholders shrug their shoulders while defensively stating that they just collect the dividends and that the big bosses are the ones to make the moral and ethical decisions.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, meanwhile, criticizes the Justin Trudeau Liberals for the unaffordability of housing as well as food, both of which are largely due to real-estate speculation or greedflation, respectively.
But Poilievre's Tories, like Trudeau's Liberals, are in bed with Big Business and their lobbyists. Mix in promised Conservative austerity measures with the unaffordability crisis, and you get a breeding ground for worsened economic conditions, thus human suffering.
Spared from this turmoil, of course, will be the well-to-do, which tend to side with the money-first minded Tories.
What will Poilievre do about the fact that the more giant-grocers and corporate officers make, the more they will go after in the next quarter?
It's never enough.
Frank Sterle Jr., White Rock, B.C.
Hire more nurses, especially RNs, and green light nurse practitioner-led clinics
'No one wants to spend 20 hours waiting': Canada's doctors say our ERs are in crisis, Jan. 11
Nurses echo what physicians are saying about the state of emergency rooms and the lack of access to primary care. The Registered Nurses' Association of Ontario (RNAO) has been vocal about these issues and provided solutions. Faster access to primary care and proper staffing of all health professionals in all sectors will serve Ontarians better and improve satisfaction and health outcomes.
Before the pandemic, Ontario already had the lowest registered nurses-per-capita ratio in the country. The province's RN deficit increased from nearly 22,000 to 25,000 during the pandemic. Although we're pleased that the Ontario government has hired more nurses, we must address the shortfall more quickly.
As for primary care, more than two million Ontarians do not have a primary care provider. Ontario must announce funding for dozens of not-for-profit nurse practitioner-led clinics. They are ready to open doors to serve Ontarians.
We owe it to Ontarians to deal with systemic issues facing our health system. The solutions are there: hire more nurses, especially RNs, and give the green light to more NP-led clinics.
Dr. Doris Grinspun, Registered Nurses' Association of Ontario, Toronto
OHIP funds only one ER doctor from 2 a.m. to 5 a.m.
How many people know that the reason for the long waits at emergency at hospitals is not enough funding for doctors?
My severely ill 92-year-old husband had to wait in emergency for seven hours before he even saw a doctor - when a second doctor came on shift at 7 a.m. We were there for 13 hours.
A nurse told us that OHIP funds only one doctor from 2-5 a.m. for all ambulatory patients and those for whom they manage to find a bed. At the same time the doctor is also catching up from the backlog during the day.
Hospitals in general have not been funded properly for decades even for inflation let alone the increase of patients, the numbers of aging patients, and the aging of infrastructure.
They are all in financial trouble.
The Ford government is using the situation to push private health care instead of funding our cherished universal care.
What a disaster that will be. Just look at the cost of care in the United States.
Ellie McCaig, Kitchener
Canada's ban on foreign collaborators is too broadCanada bans funding for some foreign-linked research, Jan. 15
I looked at the list of research areas in Mark Ramzy's piece on where Canadian scientists being forbidden to collaborate with partners from certain schools in China, Iran, and Russia.
It's too broad.
For example, DNA sequencing is on the list - but this is done in most hospitals and biology labs in Canada, especially for things like cancer research and treatment. Blocking collaborations using DNA sequencing makes no sense. And let's focus on one country: China.
Chinese researchers are prominent in the top ranks of science now. Looking at highlighted articles in the most recent issue of "Science," two of four have authors from Chinese universities, and one of these is on cancer treatment and involves DNA methods in the banned research areas list.
Checking one of the banned universities in China, five professors working on CO2 capture and storage, cancer, green hydrogen production, fuel cells, and cleaning of wastewater would be banned as collaborators.
Ottawa is using too broad a brush.
Clement Kent, Toronto

Fee structures appear to be based on doctor's incomes rather than medical outcomes
Many medical conditions require testing and assessing every three months, Jan. 17
The letter claims "It is bad medical practice to simply refill medications perpetually without 'laying eyes' on the patient for any condition."
If this is true then no doctor should be able to renew a prescription over the phone, fee or no fee.
In British Colombia, doctors started to renew prescriptions by phone during the pandemic without any catastrophic results being reported. For many conditions, the far greater risk is picking up an illness from all the sick people one is forced to share space with in a crowded waiting room.
While each case is different, the pros and cons of whether to renew prescriptions over the phone seems to have more to do with fee structures and doctor's incomes than medical outcomes.
Steen Petersen, Nanaimo B.C.
Reference to 'white men' inappropriate, unnecessary
Higher property taxes inevitable, Jan. 15
A letter writer states "the reason we find ourselves in this financial mess is mayors like Rob Ford and John Tory, both rich white men ..."
Is it assumed that white men are immune to racial prejudice? Replace the reference to "white men" with any other race and all hell would erupt. I found the reference to "white men" inappropriate, unnecessary, and an indication that racism is acceptable providing it targets a white male.
Robert Woodcock, North York

CAPTION: Numerous shopping carts are parked out front of the Fort York Food Bank on College Street in May 2023 where people line up for donated food.

Document 410 of 500

Private-clinic plan to begin in spring: Non-profit to oversee provincial program allowing companies to offer CT scans, MRIs and more

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/private-clinic-plan-begin-spring/docview/2915913479/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Private-clinic+plan+to+begin+in+spring%3A+Non-profit+to+oversee+provincial+program+allowing+companies+to+offer+CT+scans%2C+MRIs+and+more&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is forging ahead with its controversial plan to shorten wait times for patients by approving more privately owned clinics to provide CT scans, MRIs, and hip and knee replacements.
But the change - which critics fear will bleed the public health system of doctors, nurses and other resources at a time when patients face record waits in hospital emergency rooms - remains months away from taking shape.
The province won't begin taking applications for clinics until this spring, Health Minister Sylvia Jones said Wednesday, adding clinics will be prioritized for areas with long wait-lists.
"We aren't accepting a status quo that leaves too many people waiting too long for care," she added, saying services will "always be paid for with your OHIP card, never a credit card."
The initial plan had been to begin accepting applications from prospective clinics last summer.
To alleviate concerns about quality of care, new clinics and more than 900 already operating will be overseen starting April 1 by the independent, non-profit organization Accreditation Canada, which monitors hospitals and clinics across Canada and in 35 countries.
Chief executive Leslee Thompson pledged to "develop and deliver a high-quality oversight and assessment program" and said reports will be made public.
Critics said the expansion of privately owned clinics comes with the health-care system in a capacity crisis, as some hospital operating rooms are underused or closed and emergency rooms are swamped.
"There is very clear underinvestment in our hospitals," said Liberal MPP Adil Shamji (Don Valley East), an emergency room physician.
"By focusing on the private sector, they're going to make it worse," New Democrat MPP Peter Tabuns (Toronto-Danforth) predicted at a Queen's Park news conference.
Tabuns warned more privately owned clinics raised the spectre of patients being asked to pay for extras not covered by OHIP, despite assurances from the health minister.
Ontario's auditor general warned in 2021 that cataract surgery patients - who suffer blurry vision as they age - are the most vulnerable to "misleading sales practices" in some circumstance.
Jones said the new services in private clinics will also include gastrointestinal endoscopies, with hip and knee replacements to be the first orthopedic surgeries offered.Having more diagnostic services available more conveniently will speed treatment, she maintained.
Ontario currently has more than 900 private clinics in operation, mainly providing X-rays and other diagnostic services, and many of them predate Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative government. There are only a handful of surgery clinics. It's not known how many more will be licensed, but four new cataract surgery clinics have opened in the last year to meet demand, getting 14,000 patients off waiting lists.
While there has been fierce public debate about the merits of more private clinics, Windsor Regional Hospital has hailed the private Windsor Surgery Centre - staffed by ophthalmologists with hospital privileges - for taking on OHIP-funded cataract surgeries it could not handle.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said the looming call for more private clinics is "an open invitation for corporations to profit off our publicly funded health-care system."
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Emergency medical care; Cataracts; Bone surgery; Eye surgery; Medical waiting lists

Document 411 of 500

Trudeau can deal with Trump, but says he would prefer not to

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-can-deal-with-trump-says-he-would-prefer/docview/2915913457/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+can+deal+with+Trump%2C+but+says+he+would+prefer+not+to&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau insists he is not taking sides in this year's U.S. election, but he is also making it abundantly clear that neither Canada nor the world should be keen for a rerun of the Donald Trump years.
"I mean, the first time through four years of Donald Trump was a real challenge," the prime minister said in a one-on-one conversation with the Star. "Given what Mr. Trump has said about his intentions if he gets re-elected, it would certainly be challenging once again."
Does this mean Trudeau will be rooting for Joe Biden if the November vote comes down to a rematch between Biden and Trump, as it now seems?
"There's no question that when there is significantly more alignment, like there is on environment and human rights and international engagement with the current president, with Mr. Biden, I obviously would hope to continue to work with people who are more aligned."
On Monday night, Trump emerged from the Iowa caucuses with strong, almost inevitable momentum to become the Republican candidate for this year's presidential vote.
On Tuesday, during a question-and-answer session with the Montreal Chamber of Commerce, Trudeau was asked what he was doing to prepare the country for the possible re-election of Trump.
"We are going to be ready for the choice that the Americans are going to make in November," Trudeau said. But the prime minister, who was speaking in French, framed that choice in stark terms - one in which democracy is at stake.
"The choice that Americans are going to make: do they want to continue to be optimistic and committed to the future or are they going to choose to step back, out of nostalgia for a moment that never existed?"
Trudeau then went on to tell the crowd in Montreal that the next election in Canada - presumably pitting him against Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre - would be a "similar choice."
"It's a conversation that we are going to have."
On the one hand, it isn't surprising that Trudeau would be open about his views on Trump, who came to office determined to tear up the North American Free Trade Agreement and make Canada a casualty of his fierce, America-first protectionist policies.
Personally, Trump appeared to hold Trudeau in low regard, famously slamming him as "dishonest and weak" during a 2018 Twitter tantrum. Similarly, Trudeau's tolerance of Trump seemed stretched at times; notably, he was caught on camera in late 2019 laughing at the president with other world leaders at a Buckingham Palace reception.
As one of the longest-serving progressive leaders now on the world stage, Trudeau is one of the few who bears the battle scars of dealing with Trump, and he has spoken before of how he has had to explain to Biden how much America's reputation changed during the stormy Trump years.
Trudeau is also gambling that there's a domestic political payoff for taking on Trump, if it allows him to cast Poilievre and the Conservatives in the same "far-right" mould.
However, a new poll from Abacus Data shows that Canadians are more ambivalent about Trump than they were eight years ago - and while not as polarized as Americans, more divided on party lines than they once were.
Abacus CEO David Coletto reports that in his latest survey of opinions about the U.S. election, not all Canadians - especially Conservatives - share Trudeau's view that a Trump victory would be bad news.
Coletto writes that "66 per cent of Canadians favour Biden over Trump, with a notable divide among Conservative supporters - 57 per cent favouring Trump and 43 per cent Biden. On the other hand, overwhelming majorities of Liberals and NDP supporters prefer Biden. Strikingly, younger Canadians, particularly those under 30, show a higher inclination towards Trump compared to those over 60."
Using online panels, Abacus surveyed 1,500 Canadian adults between Jan. 4 and 9. Although opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 2.6 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
In an interview with me last Friday in Guelph, Trudeau said he's not daunted by the prospect of a Trump victory - he dealt with him once and he'll do it again, if he must. He said Canada survived Trump by convincing Americans at all levels that their relationship with their northern neighbour was in their self-interest.
"My job is to make sure that we are ready for whatever Americans decide in the next election," he said. "I was able to do that for four years, and I'm going to be able to do that into the future."
Still, I asked, doesn't this mean that this Canadian prime minister is taking a side in an American election?
"It's not my job to take a side," he said.
However, Trudeau added, "My biggest priorities ... (are) building an economy that is fighting climate change and creating good jobs in an inclusive way, and standing up for the international, rules-based order. Those are things that Mr. Trump has explicitly pushed back hard against, that Mr. Biden is very much more aligned with."
CREDIT: susan delacourt OPINION

Subject: Canadians; Prime ministers

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; United States--US; Canada

People: Trump, Donald J; Trudeau, Justin

Title: Trudeau can deal with Trump, but says he would prefer not to

Document 412 of 500

Amazon snaps up waterfront studio: Long-term Toronto lease will 'help ensure that the industry will continue to grow,' premier says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/amazon-snaps-up-waterfront-studio/docview/2915913152/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Amazon+snaps+up+waterfront+studio%3A+Long-term+Toronto+lease+will+%27help+ensure+that+the+industry+will+continue+to+grow%2C%27+premier+says&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Amazon MGM Studios is leasing prime space near Toronto's waterfront, the first long-term commitment it has made in Canada for film production.
News of the multi-year deal with Pinewood Toronto Studios, a sprawling property located on Commissioners Street, was announced Wednesday morning.
"This is a tremendous vote of confidence in our province and our workers," Premier Doug Ford told the crowd gathered at Pinewood, in the Port Lands area of the city.
Amazon will have use of a total of 160,000 square feet of space, including five sound stages, as well as workshops and office space.
It already produces a number of series in Canada, including "The Boys," "Reacher" and "The Handmaid's Tale," which are all shot in Ontario.
The long-term deal is "going to boost film and TV production and help ensure that the industry will continue to grow and flourish for years to come," Ford said, noting that "Ontario is one of the leading jurisdictions for film and TV production - not only in Canada, but around the entire world."
Film and television productions contribute more than $3 billion to Ontario's economy annually and support 46,000 jobs. Ford said the province's new goal is to boost that to $5 billion a year.
"There's so much in Ontario that fits the mould for what people want to do production-wise in film and television," Neil Lumsden, minister of tourism, culture and sport, said in an interview following the announcement.
"They're seeing the expertise here, and the talent here."
Although final figures for film and TV production for 2023 have yet to be released, they are likely to be down from the record year in 2022 - when the industry contributed $3.15 billion to Ontario's economy - because of recent, and lengthy, writers' and actors' strikes in the United States.
Lumsden doesn't believe those job actions will slow things down moving forward. "I think there's some momentum that comes out of that - there's a hurry up, because they have to get things done."
Film and TV productions, which benefit from provincial tax credits, not only boost provincial coffers but also help local businesses.
"Film and television production is a microcosm of a community," Tim Clawson, head of worldwide production for Amazon Studios, said at the announcement.
"We're using dry cleaners, hotels, restaurants, caterers," he said.
"We come in and really turbocharge the spending for them, but we rely on them to be here ... We always like to try to hire as local as we can."
Mayor Olivia Chow and Coun. Paula Fletcher (Toronto-Danforth), who chairs Toronto's film/television/digital media advisory board, also spoke at Wednesday's launch, noting the 35,000 jobs the sector contributes with $2.5 billion in production in the city.
Details of Amazon's lease agreement, including the number of years, were not released.
In 2022, domestic film and television production increased by 25 per cent, surpassing $3 billion for the first time.
The province was home to 419 productions, including the Amazon Prime superhero show "The Boys." The Academy Award-winning movie "Women Talking," directed by Canadian Sarah Polley and based on the novel by Manitoba-born writer Miriam Toews, was shot on a farm in Pickering and most of those working on the film were Ontarians.
The province has also been the top choice for Hallmark and other romance movies, with Mississippi Mills, near Ottawa, referred to as "Hollywood of the North" given the number of such films shot there.
Tax credits provide incentives, and last year the province extended tax credits for online productions.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Television production; Tax credits

Letters: 'No one wants to spend 20 hours waiting': Canada's doctors say our ERs are in crisis, Many medical conditions require testing and assessing every three months, Food bank crisis warns of increasing inequality, Canada bans funding for some foreign-linked research,

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/2915913131/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+%27No+one+wants+to+spend+20+hours+waiting%27%3A+Canada%27s+doctors+say+our+ERs+are+in+crisis%2C+Many+medical+conditions+require+testing+and+assessing+every+three+months%2C+Food+bank+crisis+warns+of+increasing+inequality%2C+Canada+bans+funding+for+some+foreign-linked+research%2C&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hire nurses to help fix access issues in Ontario
'No one wants to spend 20 hours waiting': Canada's doctors say our ERs are in crisis, Jan. 11
Nurses echo what physicians are saying about the state of emergency rooms and the lack of access to primary care. The Registered Nurses' Association of Ontario (RNAO) has been vocal about these issues and provided solutions. Faster access to primary care and proper staffing of all health professionals in all sectors will serve Ontarians better and improve satisfaction and health outcomes.
Before the pandemic, Ontario already had the lowest registered nurses-per-capita ratio in the country. The province's RN deficit increased from nearly 22,000 to 25,000 during the pandemic. Although we're pleased that the Ontario government has hired more nurses, we must address the shortfall more quickly.
As for primary care, more than two million Ontarians do not have a primary care provider. Ontario must announce funding for dozens of not-for-profit nurse practitioner-led clinics. They are ready to open doors to serve Ontarians.
We owe it to Ontarians to deal with systemic issues facing our health system. The solutions are there: hire more nurses, especially RNs, and give the green light to more NP-led clinics.
Dr. Doris Grinspun, Registered Nurses' Association of Ontario, Toronto
Hospitals, ERs being starved of proper funding
How many people know that the reason for the long waits at emergency at hospitals is not enough funding for doctors?
My severely ill 92-year-old husband had to wait in emergency for seven hours before he even saw a doctor - when a second doctor came on shift at 7 a.m. We were there for 13 hours. A nurse told us that OHIP funds only one doctor from 2-5 a.m. for all ambulatory patients and those for whom they manage to find a bed. At the same time the doctor is also catching up from the backlog during the day.
Hospitals in general have not been funded properly for decades even for inflation let alone the increase of patients, the numbers of aging patients, and the aging of infrastructure.
They are all in financial trouble.
The Ford government is using the situation to push private health care instead of funding our cherished universal care.
What a disaster that will be. Just look at the cost of care in the United States.
Ellie McCaig, Kitchener
Fees appear to be based on income, not outcomes
Many medical conditions require testing and assessing every three months, Jan. 17
The letter claims "It is bad medical practice to simply refill medications perpetually without 'laying eyes' on the patient for any condition."
If this is true then no doctor should be able to renew a prescription over the phone, fee or no fee.
In British Colombia, doctors started to renew prescriptions by phone during the pandemic without any catastrophic results being reported. For many conditions, the far greater risk is picking up an illness from all the sick people one is forced to share space with in a crowded waiting room.
While each case is different, the pros and cons of whether to renew prescriptions over the phone seems to have more to do with fee structures and doctor's incomes than medical outcomes.
Steen Petersen, Nanaimo, B.C.
Corporate profits still grow amid food insecurity
Food bank crisis warns of increasing inequality, Nov. 30
Avoidable injustices are forcing many Canadians to choose between essential needs.
Unrelenting food-price inflation is pushing more people to use food banks while giant-grocer corporate profits and payouts to corporate officers correspondingly inflate.
Yet, the more such corporations make, the more they want - nay, need - to make in the next quarter. It's never enough. Maximizing profits at the expense of those with so much less, or nothing, is a significant part of the nature of the big business beast.
Big businesses are getting unaccountably bigger, defying the very spirit of government rules established to ensure healthy competition by limiting concentrated ownership.
And while corporate officers shrug their shoulders and defensively say their job is only to protect shareholders' bottom-line interests, the shareholders shrug their shoulders while defensively stating that they just collect the dividends and that the big bosses are the ones to make the moral and ethical decisions.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, meanwhile, criticizes the Justin Trudeau Liberals for the unaffordability of housing as well as food, both of which are largely due to real-estate speculation or greedflation, respectively.
But Poilievre's Tories, like Trudeau's Liberals, are in bed with Big Business and their lobbyists. Mix in promised Conservative austerity measures with the unaffordability crisis, and you get a breeding ground for worsened economic conditions, thus human suffering.
Spared from this turmoil, of course, will be the well-to-do, which tend to side with the money-first minded Tories.
What will Poilievre do about the fact that the more giant-grocers and corporate officers make, the more they will go after in the next quarter?
It's never enough.
Frank Sterle Jr., White Rock, B.C.
Ban on foreign collaborators
is too broad
Canada bans funding for some foreign-linked research, Jan. 15
I looked at the list of research areas in Mark Ramzy's piece on where Canadian scientists being forbidden to collaborate with partners from certain schools in China, Iran, and Russia.
It's too broad.
For example, DNA sequencing is on the list - but this is done in most hospitals and biology labs in Canada, especially for things like cancer research and treatment. Blocking collaborations using DNA sequencing makes no sense. And let's focus on one country: China.
Chinese researchers are prominent in the top ranks of science now. Looking at highlighted articles in the most recent issue of "Science," two of four have authors from Chinese universities, and one of these is on cancer treatment and involves DNA methods in the banned research areas list.
Checking one of the banned universities in China, five professors working on CO2 capture and storage, cancer, green hydrogen production, fuel cells, and cleaning of wastewater would be banned as collaborators.
Ottawa is using too broad a brush.
Clement Kent, Toronto
CREDIT: ;

Subject: Cancer; Health care access; Emergency medical care; Bans; Corporate officers; Food; Stockholders; Funding; Pandemics; Medical research; Hospitals; Primary care; Nurses; Physicians

Business indexing term: Subject: Corporate officers Stockholders Hospitals Physicians

Location: Canada; China

Title: Letters:   'No one wants to spend 20 hours waiting': Canada's doctors say our ERs are in crisis, Many medical conditions require testing and assessing every three months, Food bank crisis warns of increasing inequalit y, Canada bans funding for some foreign-linked research,
