Document 414 of 500

With Bonnie Crombie now officially gone, who wants to be Mississauga's next mayor?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/with-bonnie-crombie-now-officially-gone-who-wants/docview/2915913095/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=With+Bonnie+Crombie+now+officially+gone%2C+who+wants+to+be+Mississauga%27s+next+mayor%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With the mayor's seat now officially empty in Mississauga, several current council members are saying they may run for the top job.
Councillors declared the mayoralty of Ontario's third-largest city vacant at a council meeting on Wednesday, a week after Bonnie Crombie resigned from the position to lead the Ontario Liberal party.
Though the election date has yet to be decided, the eventual winner will only be the city's third mayor since 1978 - due to a decades-long tenure by Hazel McCallion - and will lead Mississauga as it tries to shed its image as a sleepy suburb of Toronto.
But for any mayoral candidate, housing and affordability must be top of mind, said long-time Mississauga resident Moaz Ahmad, who keeps a close eye on city politics.
"Everything ties back to the cost of housing," said Ahmad, adding that both affordability and availability of housing in Mississauga has become a key issue for those who live in the city, and those who want to live there.
He said the Mississauga of the past will also look different than the city of the future.
"Mississauga has reached a stage where it has no more developable land, and it can no longer expand outwards but has to expand through infill and verticalization of communities," he said. "But the challenge has been that the city isn't ready to make the changes they need to do this."
Ahmad said the person who becomes mayor will have to be able to forcefully advocate for the city at higher levels of government, including Peel Region - which it will stay a part of after a provincial flip-flop last year - and the province.
Staff are expected to come back with a report at the next council meeting in Jan. 31, after which an election date will be finalized.
Some have floated the idea that local politicians like current MP Omar Alghabra may be among those in the running for mayor, but he did not respond to the Star's questions. Rookie ward councillor Alvin Tedjo said he was still undecided.
Though it's still early, here's a look at some of those who have said they are contemplating a run to be the city's next mayor:
Carolyn Parrish, Ward 5 councillor
Parrish is a veteran politician who has served both as Liberal MP and as local ward councillor, under McCallion and Crombie. During her decades in politics, Parrish has developed a reputation as a rare straight shooter, someone who always speaks her mind. It's a reputation she's happy to use to advocate for Mississauga.
"I do it ... even if it gets me in trouble," she said. "But I also have my city's priorities and needs at the front of my mind."
Among those, Parrish said are investing in housing, including efforts "beyond gentle infill" through policies to rezone land for more affordable housing, and converting underused plazas into mixed-use highrise housing and commercial space.
She said she will continue to advocate for better transit to make it "easier to leave cars home."
In polling released this week by Liaison Strategies for the National Ethnic Press and Media Council of Canada, Parrish had an early lead over her rivals. The poll of 983 people, with a margin of error of 3.06 19 times out of 20, had Parrish with 35 per cent of the vote. Nearly half of those polled said they were undecided.
Parrish said she also plans to give up her ward seat once the election is officially announced to commit to the role. She said she would avoid a costly second byelection by holding one for her ward at the same time as the mayoralty byelection. While she never considered a mayoral run before, she sees this as an opportunity to continue her commitment to the city: "It's a new horizon for Mississauga."
Stephen Dasko, Ward 1 councillor
Dasko, who is in his second term on council, has had a front-row seat to development pressures facing the city, with 40 per cent of new development in Mississauga coming to his ward along the waterfront.
He believes the most important priority for Mississauga residents is affordable housing. "We need to ensure that everyone has a place to live," he said, with a real focus on building the so-called "missing middle."
Dasko, who calls himself a lifelong resident, said he has been advocating to senior levels of government to enforce the "use it or lose it" clause to push developers to encourage them to get moving on the roughly 80,000 housing units that have been approved in the city but have not moved forward.
Dasko also said he will continue to push for efficiency in government: "Every single dollar comes from the taxpayer and we have to constantly be cognizant of that," he said.
Deepika Damerla, Ward 7 councillor
Damerla, who served as a provincial cabinet minister under Kathleen Wynne, made a run for local politics in 2018 and is now in her second term. Damerla said she is "seriously considering" a run, but is waiting to get the final election timelines before making her decision.
In the meantime, she said she is working on her campaign priorities, which include: smart growth, affordability and striving to get Mississauga the recognition from higher levels of government that it deserves.
"That recognition isn't there at senior levels of government around Mississauga's new status. We are Ontario's second-largest economy, third-largest city by population and we are not getting the funding we deserve."
She said she will be pushing for affordable taxes and advocating against a continued move by senior levels to download responsibilities - such as social services - onto homeowners
She said there needs to be a focus on growth, but added that it's important to get all "that new growth right so that we can harvest all the advantages of density ... but not lose livability."
Damerla said she's excited to run for mayor at a time when Mississauga is maturing into a cosmopolitan city: "Mississauga is nobody's suburb anymore."
CAPTION: Longtime local politician Carolyn Parrish is one of the leading contenders for the race to replace Bonnie Crombie. Mississauga council has not yet set a date for the mayoralty byelection.

Subject: Local elections; Mayors; Councils; Affordable housing; Politics

Business indexing term: Subject: Affordable housing

Title: With Bonnie Crombie now officially gone, who wants to be Mississauga's next mayor?

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jan 18, 2024

Section: news/gta

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document type: News

ProQuest document ID: 2915913095

Document URL: https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/with-bonnie-crombie-now-officially-gone-who-wants/docview/2915913095/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 415 of 500

Who wants to be Mississauga's mayor?: It's early, but there are a few politicians showing interest in running for the post

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/who-wants-be-mississaugas-mayor/docview/2915913067/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Who+wants+to+be+Mississauga%27s+mayor%3F%3A+It%27s+early%2C+but+there+are+a+few+politicians+showing+interest+in+running+for+the+post&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Deepika Damerla,
Ward 7 councillor
Damerla, who served as a provincial cabinet minister under Kathleen Wynne, made a run for local politics in 2018 and is now in her second term. Damerla said she is "seriously considering" a run, but is waiting to get the final election timelines before making her decision.
In the meantime, she said she is working on her campaign priorities, which include smart growth, affordability and striving to get Mississauga the recognition from higher levels of government that it deserves.
"That recognition isn't there at senior levels of government around Mississauga's new status. We are Ontario's second-largest economy, third-largest city by population and we are not getting the funding we deserve."
She said she will be pushing for affordable taxes and advocating against a continued move by senior levels to download responsibilities - such as social services - onto homeowners
She said there needs to be a focus on growth, but added that it's important to get all "that new growth right so that we can harvest all the advantages of density ... but not lose livability."
Damerla said she's excited to run for mayor at a time when Mississauga is maturing into a cosmopolitan city: "Mississauga is nobody's suburb anymore."
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Noor Javed Toronto Star

Subject: Local elections; Mayors; Councils; Affordable housing; Politics

Document 416 of 500

MPPs' salaries stuck at 2008 levels: Insiders worry wages may limit candidate pool

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mpps-salaries-stuck-at-2008-levels/docview/2915912917/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MPPs%27+salaries+stuck+at+2008+levels%3A+Insiders+worry+wages+may+limit+candidate+pool&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: MPPs, who have not had a pay raise in almost 16 years, will see their salaries frozen for the foreseeable future.
But the continuing freeze is raising questions about attracting the best and brightest to provincial politics if candidates can earn more federally or municipally.
Premier Doug Ford's government has confirmed there will be no change to the wages of provincial legislators, who last had an increase in 2008.
"We don't have any plans to lift the freeze," said Andrew Kennedy, a spokesperson for Attorney General Doug Downey, who oversees the pay legislation.
That means MPPs, who earn significantly less than Toronto city councillors or members of Parliament, will continue to fall behind their counterparts at other levels of government who receive annual raises.
Former Liberal premier Dalton McGuinty froze compensation for MPPs, ministers and opposition leaders in 2009 during the global financial crisis.
McGuinty said at the time it was a bid to "lead by example" and promote restraint while Queen's Park was dealing with public-sector unions seeking wage increases.
"In some ways, those of us within the public sector have been sheltered from the full ravages of this private-sector recession," the then premier said 15 years ago.
The base pay for an MPP remains $116,550 - that's the equivalent of $83,400 in 2008 dollars, according to the Bank of Canada.
If MPPs' salaries had kept pace with inflation, they would earn about $163,000 this year.
McGuinty's initial one-year freeze was extended in 2010, and again in 2012. His Liberal successor, premier Kathleen Wynne, continued the practice until she was defeated by Ford's Progressive Conservatives in 2018.
Although sources told the Star the Tories considered raising MPPs' pay after that election, it was felt that would not be politically palatable. Similarly, the governing party quietly looked at a hike after its successful 2022 re-election campaign, but again backed off.
"It's easy for Doug, because he's rich," groused one Tory insider, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations.
That's a reference to Ford, who makes $208,974 as premier, being the multimillionaire scion of a wealthy family.
Provincial ministers are paid $165,851, while NDP Leader Marit Stiles, who heads the Official Opposition, makes $180,886. Tory MPPs who serve as parliamentary assistants take home $133,217. Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, who is not an MPP, is being paid $185,000 by her party.
One MPP, who acknowledged they personally could afford to take a steep pay cut to run for office, said there are problems with not paying competitive salaries.
"You're either attracting people who are already well-off or people whose time is worth $116,550 a year," said the member, noting politicians at the federal and municipal level received annual increases.
Indeed, under city law, Mayor Olivia Chow, who makes $216,116, and city councillors, who earned $128,346 last year, are in line for 3.5-per-cent raises because their pay is tied to inflation.
Similarly, MPs, whose base pay was $194,600 last year, will receive an increase in April. MPPs' salaries were originally supposed to be tied to federal members' compensation, paying them 75 per cent of their counterparts in Ottawa. But now, an MPP makes less than 60 per cent of an MP's base.
Also getting bumps are Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, who makes $389,200, and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, who earns $287,400 as leader of the Official Opposition. Federal ministers are paid $287,400, while Liberal MPs who serve as parliamentary secretaries take home $213,400.
But NDP MPP Peter Tabuns (Toronto Danforth) said there is simply "no appetite" at Queen's Park to boost members' pay.
"People are hurting - and the thought that, when their salaries are stagnant or dropping, ours go up just makes people incredibly angry," Tabuns said Wednesday.
"I don't see how you address that in a way that doesn't make people angry," said the veteran MPP, who was first elected in 2006.
Tabuns said he's "not surprised" that Ford's Tories, like previous Liberal governments, are skittish about tackling pay.
"Again, just the frustration and anger that people have about it. We're a level of government that's relatively close to people and they see it and that would be a major headline," he said.
As things stand now, MPPs would only receive an increase if the province, which has a budget deficit, balances its books.
One person firmly convinced MPPs deserve a raise is the integrity commissioner. In his 2021-22 annual report, J. David Wake warned "continuing to tie the lifting of the freeze to achieving a balanced budget is problematic for all MPPs."
"After the next election, I respectfully suggest that the government consider lifting the freeze or re-establishing an independent arm's-length process to review MPP salaries," said Wake in 2022.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Wages & salaries; Raises; Legislation; Elections

Full text: MPPs, who have not had a pay raise in almost 16 years, will see their salaries frozen for the foreseeable future.
But the continuing freeze is raising questions about attracting the best and brightest to provincial politics if candidates can earn more federally or municipally.

If MPPs' salaries had kept pace with inflation, they would earn about $163,000 this year.

His Liberal successor, premier Kathleen Wynne, continued the practice until she was defeated by Ford's Progressive Conservatives in 2018.
While sources told the Star the Tories considered raising MPPs' pay after that election, it was felt that would not be politically palatable.
Similarly, the governing party quietly looked at a hike after its successful 2022 re-election campaign, but again backed off.
"It's easy for Doug, because he's rich," groused one Tory insider, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations.
That's a reference to Ford, who makes $208,974 as premier, being the multimillionaire scion of a wealthy family.

"You're either attracting people who are already well-off or people whose time is worth $116,550 a year," said the member, noting politicians at the federal and municipal level received annual increases.

Also getting bumps are Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, who makes $389,200, and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, who earns $287,400 as leader of the Official Opposition. Federal ministers are also paid $287,400 while Liberal MPs who serve as parliamentary secretaries take home $213,400.
But NDP MPP Peter Tabuns (Toronto Danforth) said there is simply "no appetite" at Queen's Park to boost members' pay.
"People are hurting - and the thought that, when their salaries are stagnant or dropping, ours go up just makes people incredibly angry," Tabuns said Wednesday.
"I don't see how you address that in a way that doesn't make people angry," said the veteran MPP, who was first elected in 2006.
Tabuns said he's "not surprised" that Ford's Tories, like previous Liberal governments, are skittish about tackling pay.
"Again, just the frustration and anger that people have about it. We're a level of government that's relatively close to people and they see it and that would be a major headline," he said.
As things stand now, MPPs would only receive an increase if the province, which has a budget deficit, balances its books.
After an expected $2.1-billion surplus in 2021-22 would have automatically triggered a $25,575 hike in MPP pay to $142,125, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy quickly amended the law to prevent that from happening as inflation was rising in September 2022.
One person firmly convinced MPPs deserve a raise is the integrity commissioner, the independent ethics watchdog.
In his 2021-22 annual report, J. David Wake warned "continuing to tie the lifting of the freeze to achieving a balanced budget is problematic for all MPPs."
"After the next election, I respectfully suggest that the government consider lifting the freeze or re-establishing an independent arm's-length process to review MPP salaries, similar to the one my office was involved in from 2001 to 2007," said Wake in 2022.
Some 18 months after that election, his concerns have not been addressed.
CAPTION: Ontario Attorney General Doug Downey holds a news conference at Queen's Park in Toronto on Oct. 24, 2023.

Document 418 of 500

War over refugee funding heats up as Liberal MP accuses Olivia Chow of 'lying to Torontonians'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/war-over-refugee-funding-heats-up-as-liberal-mp/docview/2915728800/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=War+over+refugee+funding+heats+up+as+Liberal+MP+accuses+Olivia+Chow+of+%27lying+to+Torontonians%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Toronto Liberal MP is going to war with Mayor Olivia Chow over her demand that the Trudeau government provide more money to house refugee claimants, accusing her of "lying to Torontonians."
Yvan Baker, a backbencher who represents Etobicoke Centre, in a Tuesday interview on Newstalk 1010 also accused Chow, who took office in July, of irresponsible spending and threatening a reckless property tax hike for 2024.
Baker was responding to questions from host John Moore about a Star story in which members of the 23-strong Toronto Liberal caucus said Chow's administration is unfairly threatening to call a six-per-cent tax hike - on top of a city staff-proposed 10.5-per-cent increase - the "federal impacts levy" unless Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government covers the $250 million Toronto expects to spend this year to take care of refugee claimants.
Saying he was speaking only for himself, Baker told Moore: "Olivia Chow, when she comes out and says the federal government needs to step up, Toronto MPs need to step up, that is misleading and she's lying to Torontonians about how supportive the federal government has been and how committed Toronto MPs have been in successfully fighting for Toronto."
Baker said the Trudeau government has given Toronto billions of dollars in recent years, adding the city staff-proposed tax hikes "completely disrespect Torontonians" and are the result of Chow boosting spending since taking office rather than looking for savings. "Olivia Chow's tax hike is outrageous, it's completely irresponsible," he said.
In an interview with the Star, Baker stood by his comments, adding that he needs to safeguard federal tax dollars and, amid changing funding demands from the city and "This can't be a shakedown." He declined to say how other Liberal MPs have reacted to his comments.
Chow has not endorsed the proposed tax hikes. The former NDP MP said last week that she wants to hear feedback from Torontonians and the city's budget committee before tabling her proposed budget and tax hike on Feb. 1, to be debated by city council on Feb. 14. She has, since taking office, urged the Trudeau government to help fund services for refugees, some of whom have been forced to sleep outside due to full homeless shelters.
Chow's office declined to respond to Baker's accusations. However, Gord Perks, council's planning and housing chair, told reporters at city hall: "I can't find words for how disappointed I am in the members of Parliament from the city of Toronto who think that the right outcome is someone fleeing violence to arrive in Toronto and sleep on a sidewalk in the winter.
"That's not Canadian values. They have to stop it. They have to pay up."
A spokesperson for Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland, the government's most senior Toronto MP, declined to comment on Baker's accusations, instead pointing to the Star to her comments at a news conference last week. She said then that her government has given Toronto more than $1.5 billion in direct support so far over the 2023-2024 fiscal year, including some for refugee costs.
"We are here for Toronto right now, and we will continue to be here for Toronto in the future, " Freeland said, refusing to say if her government will give the city more refugee funding.
City staff say Toronto in 2022 got $71.3 million in federal Interim Housing Assistance Program funding toward refugee claimant response
For 2023, "an agreement has been made for the first instalment of $35 million from the Federal government, which remains pending. In other words, the total amount owed to the City for refugee claimants is $165 million," city staff said, adding that the city has formally asked Ottawa for $250 million for 2024.
Michael Coteau, a former Ontario Liberal cabinet minister who is the federal Liberal MP for Don Valley East, questioned whether simply giving the city a lump sum to cover the costs of sheltering asylum seekers is the right idea.
Coteau believes it would be better for all three levels of government to work out a new system to support these newcomers so that they don't end up in Toronto's shelter system but can receive supports to help them start paying rent and landing jobs in the country.
"We need to rethink the entire way we deliver those services (to asylum seekers)," he said.
Cathy Crowe, a Toronto street nurse renowned for her advocacy for people experiencing homelessness, said in an email: "I absolutely think it is a federal responsibility to pay for shelter for refugees but I also think Toronto should not be using them as pawns," when the city could have done more to help asylum seekers since the summer.
"We have fantastic refugee services in the city," that are overstretched, she said, as volunteers struggle to help refugees seeking help at churches and outside the city's shelter intake office on Peter Street.
With files from Alex Ballingall.
CAPTION: A Toronto Liberal MP is going to war with Mayor Olivia Chow over her demand that the Trudeau government provide more money to house refugee claimants, accusing her of "lying to Torontonians."

Amazon MGM snaps up prime studio space near Toronto's waterfront for film production

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/amazon-mgm-snaps-up-prime-studio-space-near/docview/2915667157/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Amazon+MGM+snaps+up+prime+studio+space+near+Toronto%27s+waterfront+for+film+production&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Amazon MGM is leasing prime studio space near Toronto's waterfront for its burgeoning film production.
News of the multi-year deal with Pinewood Toronto Studios, a sprawling property located on Commissioners Street, was announced Wednesday morning with Premier Doug Ford speaking to the crowd.
"Amazon MGM's long-term commitment to new studio space in Toronto is a tremendous vote of confidence in our province and our workers," he said in a statement.
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow was also on hand.
The deal includes use of about 160,000 square feet of five state of the art stages and workshops in the Portlands area of the city.
This is a developing story.

Document 420 of 500

Pierre Poilievre is putting a new face on the Conservative party. Here are some of the people who want to help

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievre-is-putting-new-face-on/docview/2915667114/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre+is+putting+a+new+face+on+the+Conservative+party.+Here+are+some+of+the+people+who+want+to+help&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - A prominent conservative commentator wants to run as a Conservative MP in the next federal election, adding to the names being bandied about for who might govern alongside Pierre Poilievre if he wins.
The party currently holds 117 seats, and would need to win at least 172 to form a majority government. Who could fill out those spots is a question coming up more and more often in political conversations as polls suggest the Conservatives are on the road to power.
On Wednesday, the Star has learned, high-profile columnist Sabrina Maddeaux will announce she's seeking the party's nomination in the GTA riding of Aurora-Oak Ridges-Richmond Hill, one of the seats the Tories lost to the Liberals in 2021.
Maddeaux, whose work has appeared in the National Post and elsewhere, told the Star she's decided to try for office to give voice to a cohort - voters under the age of 40 - with which she says the Tories are already gaining traction.
"People continue to be surprised by polls that show millennials and Gen-Z stampeding to the right because of the outdated stereotype that conservatism isn't 'cool,'" said Maddeaux, 35, who grew up in the riding.
"But you know what's really not cool? Not being able to pay rent, buy groceries, or start a family because of Liberal policies that divided the country into economic winners and losers, with younger Canadians deliberately chosen as the disproportionate losers."
Polls point to younger voters - who were crucial to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals coming to power in 2015 - shifting to the Conservatives, as Poilievre keeps his message largely focused on housing and the cost of living.
While Maddeaux still needs to be selected by party members to carry the Tory banner, she's not the only one of her generation hoping to ride a youthful wave of conservative support into office.
An upcoming byelection in Durham will see Jamil Jivani, 36, try to hold onto the seat for the Conservatives following last year's resignation of former party leader Erin O'Toole.
Like Maddeaux, Jivani's conservative outlook and media profile have seen him elevated to a position of prominence in the conservative movement: She nabbed a rare one-on-one interview with Poilievre last fall, while Jivani earned a spot on the party's convention stage.
The Ivy League graduate turned community advocate and broadcaster told thousands of party members he feels his dreams are being dashed under Trudeau's Liberal government, noting he's a renter who can't afford a home.
"I feel the housing crisis in this country on a very personal level and so do many of my future constituents," Jivani told the party delegates.
If Maddeaux and Jivani are successful, they will stand as examples of how Poilievre is rebuilding the party since he launched his leadership bid nearly two years ago.
His campaign brought hundreds of thousands of new party members into the fold, creating a new class of volunteers and donors who continue to see the Tories outpace their rivals when it comes to raking in the cash.
After overhauling the party's inner workings, Poilievre's next step is to find like-minded Conservatives to run under his banner in the next election.
While the notion of finding "star" candidates is part of that conversation, more than one Tory insider has made the point to the Star that to Poilievre, candidates who might already have some public profile aren't as important as those who are willing to do the grunt work it takes to get elected.
That was the message sent late in 2023 when rumours flew that the Conservatives were courting Quebec Finance Minister Eric Girard as potential "star candidate."
As La Presse first reported, Conservative insiders shot down the notion that Girard would get a red carpet rolled out for him just because of who he was, particularly given that he and Poilievre are not aligned on policy ideas like eliminating the Liberals' carbon pricing program.
Poilievre has billed the coming campaign as a "carbon tax election," in which he'll focus on the Liberals' signature policy of a consumer price on carbon. The carbon levy, Liberals argue, is essential to help people curb their use of greenhouse gas emitting fuels; for Poilievre, it is ineffective and too costly.
He made that point crystal clear during an event last week in Northern Ontario, where the Conservatives have already chosen Gaétan Malette to run against NDP MP Charlie Angus in Timmins-James Bay.
There, the Conservatives unveiled an early sign - literally - of what a Poilievre campaign will look like: the party has purchased a billboard featuring Poilievre and Malette together on one side under the banner, "Axe the carbon tax," and NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh and Angus on the other, under the banner, "Hike the carbon tax."
Among those scouting candidates for the Tories is a mix of longtime party insiders and regional volunteers. While there have only been a few nomination votes so far, the pace is expected to pick up as the new riding boundaries - and in some cases new ridings - come into play later this spring.
The party declined to comment on nominations, saying they are an internal party matter.
While recruiting political newcomers is one avenue, not all of those seeking to run for Poilievre are new to elected life.
In Edmonton, former MP James Cummings is expected to try for the nomination in his old riding, which he lost to the Liberals in 2021.
Alberta is also getting three new seats out of boundary redistribution, providing an opportunity for three new Conservatives in that stronghold.
In B.C., the Conservatives are hoping both to pick up seats and win back ones they previously held. Former MP Nelly Shin is trying for a comeback there to win the nomination in her old stomping ground.
Another well-known conservative personality on the roster for a B.C. riding is Aaron Gunn, who has secured the nomination in North Island-Powell River, after a failed effort to run for leadership of a provincial party and a long career as an advocate for conservative causes.
In 2021, the People's Party of Canada took 4.6 per cent of the vote in the riding, possibly hiving off enough support from the Conservatives to allow the NDP to hold the seat.
The Conservatives want the riding, and it's not the only one they hope to flip by bringing back voters who turned away from them in 2021 in favour of the PPC, in part because of the party's stance on COVID-19.
In Kitchener South-Hespeler, the PPC captured 6.7 per cent of the vote in 2021 and the Tory candidate at the time only lost by two points to the Liberals.
Come the next election, the Conservative candidate will be Matt Strauss, a former interim medical health officer, whose pushback on some COVID-19 control measures made him a lightening rod for criticism during the pandemic.
Meanwhile, in York Centre, the Conservative candidate will be Roman Baber, the former Ontario MPP ousted from the Progressive Conservative caucus by Premier Doug Ford after they clashed over COVID-19 measures. He went on to run for leadership of the federal Conservatives in the contest won by Poilievre.
The Tories nearly won the seat in a 2020 byelection, and now Baber is seeking to turn his past victory in the riding provincially - he had wrested it away from provincial Liberals - into one that would see him defeat federal Liberal cabinet minister Ya'ara Saks.
How soon he - and other candidates - will get the chance to test their mettle against incumbents remains unknown. The next election might not be held until 2025.
CAPTION: National Post columnist Sabrina Maddeaux interviews Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre in a video posted to YouTube on Nov. 8, 2022.

Subject: Conservatism; Nominations; Leadership; Candidates; Voters; Carbon; COVID-19; Environmental tax; Tax elections

Why aren't we using nuclear fuel waste as energy?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-arent-we-using-nuclear-fuel-waste-as-energy/docview/2915666948/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+aren%27t+we+using+nuclear+fuel+waste+as+energy%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Why aren't we using nuclear fuel waste as energy?How Ontario's nuclear 'waste' can power our future, Jan. 14
It is with great interest that I read Peter Ottensmeyer's article on nuclear waste. If we already have the technology to turn nuclear fuel waste into noncarbon energy, what are the politicians waiting for?
Providing Ontario with energy for 5,000 years seems like a no-brainer to me.
Heidi Kudlak, Aurora
There are too many ugly buildings Toronto's ugliest intersection? Why beauty matters to a city, Jan. 13
I write to you as a retired Northern Ontario architect, living in Sudbury. My practice extended to most major points in Northern Ontario and sometimes Toronto.
I agree vehemently that there are too many ugly buildings in our built environment. I wasn't aware of the new renovations done to the Simpson/Hudson Bay tower; but it is truly a masterpiece of "cheap ... awkward ... beastly aircraft hanger" design, even if one could call it design.
In his column, Shawn Micallef recalls other particularly attractive world cities, including Mexico City and Hong Kong. I do agree with him having visited both a number of times. Missing from his list is the best of the bunch, in my humble opinion: Bangkok, Thailand, which I have visited more than the others put together, and if I wasn't so old, I would be on the plane tomorrow if given the opportunity to enjoy its "full of surprise ... sometimes a little messy; sometimes weird, never boring" convictions and approach to city design.
Arthur Peach, Sudbury
Enacted expanded MAID now
Decision to proceed with expansion of medically assisted dying law looms, Jan. 8
The opponents of extending MAID to the mentally ill contend that rather than assisted suicide, the mentally ill just need better access to support, housing and health care.
A person could conclude that less access to support, housing and health care would mean these opponents would then support expansion of MAID to include mental disorders.
We are being told that the Conservatives will win the next federal election, no doubt, bringing tax cuts and the corresponding cuts to services such as mental health. It is therefore urgent that the expanded MAID is enacted now, to save John Scully and others like him from a future with no options.
Clyde McDonald, Bracebridge
Call for Ontario's NDP and Liberal leaders to defeat Ford government
Congratulations to both Ontario's NDP leader Marit Stiles and Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie. Both are strong and capable leaders. Stiles has shown dogged persistence in exposing the underhandedness of Ontario Premier Doug Ford especially in regards the Greenbelt and the dismantling of our health care. Crombie has demonstrated effective municipal leadership for many years.
I challenge these two effective women to unite their strengths and their parties because Ford must be stopped, and to show that two effective leaders who just happen to be women, can truly refresh Ontario's plans for the future.
I challenge them to energize the voting public and help restore our faith in a fair system and a strong province.
Please put province before party and unite to defeat the dreadful current government.
Wendy McQuarrie, Mississauga
Toronto property taxes should be increased to the average of GTA municipalitiesToronto's 2024 budget may include double-digit tax hike, Jan. 9
Ever since amalgamation in 1998, Toronto has increased property taxes at or below the rate of inflation. Former mayor Mel Lastman actually froze property taxes during the first two years of his term, as did former mayor Rob Ford later during the first year of his term. At the same time other GTA municipalities were raising their property taxes at or above the rate of inflation. As a result, Toronto property taxes are now (often significantly) lower than other GTA municipalities.
Unsurprisingly, Toronto is broke and begging for a bail out from the provincial and federal governments. While a bail out may be necessary, it would only be fair that it be conditional on Toronto property taxes being increased to the average of all GTA municipalities, which I am sure will be a much larger increase than what is being proposed.
Scott Heaslip, Stouffville
Canada could exceed the U.S. in oil production
In 2023, the U.S. overtook Qatar and Australia to become the world's largest exporter of liquefied natural gas (LNG).
However, with another LNG pipeline and terminal like the nearing completion Coastal Gas Link/LNG Canada project, and with several other existing in-construction, in-development, and in-approval LNG projects, Canada could rival U.S. LNG exports.
Also, according to the American Petroleum Institute, "America is producing more oil than any other country in history."
If Alberta's oilsands, bitumen carbonate, and tight shale gas liquids resources were unleashed, Canada could even exceed the U.S. in oil production.
Mike Priaro, Calgary, Alta.
Many medical conditions require testing and assessing every three months
Ontario doctors charging fees for uninsured services, Jan. 16
It is part of medical practice to bill patients for non-OHIP services (including prescriptions without a visit) and has always been.
The letter writer refers to a litany of conditions she believes only need one yearly visit. There is a thing called "standard of care" that medical practitioners must follow to avoid negligence. Diabetes, hypertension and many more conditions require testing and assessing every three months.
It is bad medical practice to simply refill medications perpetually without "laying eyes" on the patient for any condition.
Dr. Sandra Marsetti, Toronto
Did our councillors never go tobogganing?
Toronto's tobogganing ban threatens to kill the thrill of the hill, Jan. 16
You have to give it to Toronto City Council - they are nothing if audacious. First, they name something after Rob Ford and now they take away a fundamental right from every child and adult that wants to remember what it was to be a child, by banning the exhilarating activity of tobogganing.
What next? Putting a stop to outdoor skating rinks!
Matthew Marosszeky, Aurora
Tobogganing is fun ... until someone gets hurt
Edward Keenan demeans Toronto's concerns for the risks of tobogganing. Instead, he describes the tumbles and lack of control while flailing down a hill as one of the delights of winter.
What Keenan omits, either from deliberate deception or complete ignorance, is the other end of the statistic: the child (or adult) who ends up with a serious spinal cord or traumatic brain injury as a result.
Allan J Fox, retired neuroradiologist, Toronto
CAPTION: A jogger runs along the beach past the Pickering Nuclear Generating Station, in Pickering in January, 2020. By using a different type of nuclear reactor, Ontario can transform its nuclear waste into 5,000 years of noncarbon energy, Peter Ottensmeyer wrote.

Document 422 of 500

Olivia Chow's tax hike gambit angered local Liberal MPs. That doesn't make her wrong

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/olivia-chows-tax-hike-gambit-angered-local/docview/2915525361/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Olivia+Chow%27s+tax+hike+gambit+angered+local+Liberal+MPs.+That+doesn%27t+make+her+wrong&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Look, I don't know if Mayor Olivia Chow's "federal impacts levy" surtax gambit trying to secure funding for refugee shelter needs is a good idea, politically. Whether the voters in Toronto and across Canada agree that the federal government should give Toronto the estimated $250 million to shelter asylum seekers in 2024 - or else take the blame for an added six per cent property tax increase to make up the difference - is for those voters to decide. Relatedly, whether Justin Trudeau and his government feel enough pressure to deliver the funding because of this public threat to raise taxes is a political calculation they're going to make without any help from me.
But it sure strikes me that morally, the mayor is on solid ground. The federal government has sole responsibility for immigration decisions, and has - for good reason, many of us might agree - ramped up the number of immigrants it admits, and given the scale of global conflict, especially ramped up the number of refugees it accepts. Toronto is the landing place for a disproportionate number of those newcomers and asylum seekers. And Toronto has no spare housing to put them in.
So many of those refugees who have fled hell on earth in the countries they're coming from have found a different kind of hell in the country that offered them shelter: sleeping on the street of a major city. Which means that, when it can find space, Toronto's emergency homeless shelter system has to take them in. The demand for spaces for refugees in the Toronto shelter system increased 500 per cent between September 2021 and May 2023. That's gratuitously expensive (as it is when sheltering the already large population of homeless people in Toronto, too). And the cost traditionally falls on Toronto's property tax payers.
That's immigration and refugee policy, clearly a federal responsibility. What about housing and shelter? That's a shared provincial and federal responsibility, as Toronto has been arguing for a few years now, as it has found the costs delegated to it by those other governments. In the "new deal" for Toronto negotiated by Chow with Premier Doug Ford, the province seems to have accepted part of the costs that come with that responsibility, agreeing to provide $200 million per year to cover shelter costs for the street homeless population. But both parties to that deal asked the federal government to cover the cost of sheltering refugees.
That seemed like a fair request to many of us well before the current mayor's property-tax-hike threat, and indeed before Chow was even elected. Back during the campaign, Liberal-affiliated strategist Scott Reid (an adviser to former prime minister Paul Martin, and now a CTV political analyst) told me he thought getting funding to help with that should be one of the biggest issues in the campaign. "We're getting a raw deal, and I think we should have candidates who are saying Toronto is getting a raw deal. We are being asked to do something that is outsized in proportion to the rest of the country. That doesn't mean we resent doing it. We celebrate doing it and we will do it," he said. "We're not going to say, 'We're going to boycott helping these refugees who come here, or helping to integrate new Canadians who arrive and any other of these social services.' But you do say, 'We're getting screwed and let's be crystal clear about it.'"
Now, I have heard from many readers whose response is that, in fact, Toronto should boycott sheltering refugees, and let those desperate people sleep in the street while shrugging off culpability on the federal government. That would be a different sort of political gambit, clearly, for a different kind of politician to pursue. Chow is not willing to do that. I think it is to the credit of our country that we offer asylum to those in need of it, and it is to the credit of our local politicians that they refuse to make those people suffer further to make a political and financial point.
But that doesn't mean that, while displaying a basic level of humanity, a mayor can't make the same point through financial and political means. Like, say, through the budget process and tax rates.
Which brings us to the whinging this week by mostly unnamed federal Liberal MPs from Toronto, who told the Star on Monday that this tax gambit by Chow trying to put the responsibility on them is "outrageous" and "ridiculously political."
According to them, Chow is "shirking the city's responsibility to cover the costs of asylum seekers." And as a hotheaded Etobicoke MP Yvan Baker ranted on NewsTalk 1010 Tuesday, she's not giving them enough credit for the funding they have already provided the city, including $97 million for refugee shelter needs last year.
Perhaps they have a point, politically, that the mayor could show more gratitude for the bills they have already helped with, and certainly their resentment at the mayor's attempt at a short squeeze here is driven by the reality that they face a tough slog in the next federal election. But none of that changes the truth that there are obviously still big bills to be paid, and these federal politicians are making the argument they shouldn't be expected to step up, or accept blame for the costs to others, if they refuse. Even though it is their decisions that are directly driving up the price on those bills.
Even leaving aside the moral arguments, I'm not sure this is the political message the federal Liberals are looking for. Because it sounds a lot like they're saying, "Our job is to make the decisions, someone else's job is to pay for them."
That's an attitude many of their opponents might already accuse them of displaying. I'd be surprised if it was a slogan they wanted to carry into the next election.
CAPTION: A Toronto Liberal MP is going to war with Mayor Olivia Chow over her demand that the Trudeau government provide more money to house refugee claimants, accusing her of "lying to Torontonians."

Subject: Federal government; Refugees; Tax rates; Costs; Voters; Funding; Politics; Homeless people; Immigration; Property taxes; Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Tax rates Costs Property taxes Tax increases

Title: Olivia Chow's tax hike gambit angered local Liberal MPs. That doesn't make her wrong

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jan 17, 2024

Section: opinion/star-columnists

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

ProQuest document ID: 2915525361

Document URL: https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/olivia-chows-tax-hike-gambit-angered-local/docview/2915525361/se-2?accountid=15090

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/federal-liberals-dont-have-moral-high-ground/docview/2915525209/se-2?accountid=15090

CAPTION: There are big bills that Mayor Olivia Chow is trying to get paid, Edward Keenan writes, and critical Liberal MPs are making the argument they shouldn't be expected to step up even though it is their decisions about immigration that are directly driving up the price on those bills.R.J. Johnston Toronto Star

Document 424 of 500

Here's what Justin Trudeau thinks about the coming U.S. election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/heres-what-justin-trudeau-thinks-about-coming-u-s/docview/2915525139/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here%27s+what+Justin+Trudeau+thinks+about+the+coming+U.S.+election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau insists he is not taking sides in this year's U.S. election, but he is also making it abundantly clear that neither Canada - nor the world - should be keen for a rerun of the Donald Trump years.
"I mean, the first time through four years of Donald Trump was a real challenge," the prime minister said in a one-on-one conversation with the Star. "Given what Mr. Trump has said about his intentions if he gets re-elected, it would certainly be challenging once again."
Does this mean Trudeau will be rooting for Joe Biden if the November vote comes down a rematch between Biden and Trump, as it now seems?
"There's no question that when there is significantly more alignment, like there is on environment and human rights and international engagement with the current president, with Mr. Biden, I obviously would hope to continue to work with people who are more aligned."
On Monday night, Trump emerged from the Iowa caucuses with strong, almost inevitable momentum to become the Republican candidate for this year's presidential vote.
On Tuesday, during a question-and-answer session with the Montreal Chamber of Commerce, Trudeau was asked what he was doing to prepare the country for the possible re-election of Trump.
"We are going to be ready for the choice that the Americans are going to make in November," Trudeau said. But the prime minister, who was speaking in French, framed that choice in stark terms - one in which democracy is at stake.
"The choice that Americans are going to make: do they want to continue to be optimistic and committed to the future or are they going to choose to step back, out of nostalgia for a moment that never existed?"
Trudeau then went on to tell the crowd in Montreal that the next election in Canada - presumably pitting him against Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre - would be a "similar choice."
"It's a conversation that we are going to have."
On the one hand, it isn't surprising that Trudeau would be open about his views on Trump, who came to office determined to tear up the North American Free Trade Agreement and make Canada a casualty of his fierce, America-first protectionist policies. Personally, Trump appeared to hold Trudeau in low regard, famously slamming him as "dishonest and weak" during a 2018 Twitter tantrum. Similarly, Trudeau's tolerance of Trump seemed stretched at times; notably, he was caught on camera in late 2019 laughing at the president with other world leaders at a Buckingham Palace reception.
As one of the longest-serving progressive leaders now on the world stage, Trudeau is one of the few who bears the battle scars of dealing with Trump, and he has spoken before of how he has had to explain to Biden how much America's reputation changed during the stormy Trump years.
Trudeau is also gambling that there's a domestic political payoff for taking on Trump, if it allows him to cast Poilievre and the Conservatives in the same "far-right" mould.
However, a new poll from Abacus Data shows that Canadians are more ambivalent about Trump than they were eight years ago - and while not as polarized as Americans, more divided on party lines than they once were.
Abacus CEO David Coletto reports that in his latest survey of opinions about the U.S. election, not all Canadians - especially Conservatives - share Trudeau's view that a Trump victory would be bad news.
Coletto writes that "66 per cent of Canadians favour Biden over Trump, with a notable divide among Conservative supporters - 57 per cent favouring Trump and 43 per cent Biden. On the other hand, overwhelming majorities of Liberals and NDP supporters prefer Biden. Strikingly, younger Canadians, particularly those under 30, show a higher inclination towards Trump compared to those over 60."
Using online panels, Abacus surveyed 1,500 Canadian adults between Jan. 4 and 9. Although opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 2.6 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
In an interview with me last Friday in Guelph, Trudeau said he's not daunted by the prospect of a Trump victory - he dealt with him once and he'll do it again, if he must. He said Canada survived Trump by convincing Americans at all levels that their relationship with their northern neighbour was in their self-interest.
"My job is to make sure that we are ready for whatever Americans decide in the next election," he said. "I was able to do that for four years, and I'm going to be able to do that into the future."
Still, I asked, doesn't this mean that this Canadian prime minister is taking a side in an American election?
"It's not my job to take a side," he said.
However, Trudeau added, "My biggest priorities ... (are) building an economy that is fighting climate change and creating good jobs in an inclusive way, and standing up for the international, rules-based order. Those are things that Mr. Trump has explicitly pushed back hard against, that Mr. Biden is very much more aligned with."
CAPTION: U.S. President Donald Trump, left, and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau arrive for a round table meeting during a NATO leaders meeting in Watford, Hertfordshire, England, onDec. 4, 2019.

Former PC minister in political limbo: Independent MPP must wait for RCMP Greenbelt investigation to finish before ethics review

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/former-pc-minister-political-limbo/docview/2915522498/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+PC+minister+in+political+limbo%3A+Independent+MPP+must+wait+for+RCMP+Greenbelt+investigation+to+finish+before+ethics+review&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: MPP Kaleed Rasheed, ejected from Premier Doug Ford's cabinet and caucus over the Greenbelt scandal, will be stuck in political limbo until the RCMP completes its investigation.
Insisting he did nothing wrong, Rasheed has been banking on Integrity Commissioner J. David Wake to exonerate him so he can return to the Progressive Conservative fold.
But Wake - author of the August report that triggered the dismissal due to a misremembered Las Vegas trip with a Greenbelt developer - has not yet even determined whether to take a second look at the Mississauga East-Cooksville MPP's case.
The ethics watchdog says his hands will remain tied as long as the RCMP criminal probe continues.
Both Rasheed, who wants to clear his name, and NDP Leader Marit Stiles, who seeks more details about what she has dubbed "the now-infamous" Las Vegas jaunt, have asked Wake to do another report.
"No decision has been made regarding the request from Marit Stiles, leader of the Official Opposition, for the integrity commissioner to provide an opinion on whether Kaleed Rasheed has contravened the Members' Integrity Act," said Michelle Renaud, spokesperson for the legislative officer.
"Given that the RCMP has launched a broadly worded investigation into a matter that could overlap with the subject matter of, and individuals named in, the request, the commissioner has informed Ms. Stiles, Kaleed Rasheed and Speaker of the legislative assembly, Ted Arnott, that he will not be proceeding with the request at this time," said Renaud.
Under the Members' Integrity Act, which governs MPPs' conduct, the integrity commissioner "shall suspend" any "inquiry until the police investigation or charge has been finally disposed of, and shall report the suspension to the Speaker."
That means Rasheed, a former public and business service delivery minister who currently sits as an Independent MPP, must await the RCMP probe, which could take years to resolve.
Rasheed did not return repeated messages from the Star seeking comment.
Rasheed's office provided the wrong dates for a winter 2020 trip to Las Vegas, where he encountered his developer friend Shakir Rehmatullah, who, Wake has concluded, was "more likely than not" tipped off by someone to the government's plans to remove lands from the Greenbelt.
Also on that vacation were Ford's then-principal secretary, Amin Massoudi, and Jae Truesdell, who departed as the premier's director of housing policy the same day as Rasheed was forced from caucus.
On Sept. 20, the premier's office said "if Mr. Rasheed can clear his name through the office of the integrity commissioner, he will be provided an opportunity to return to caucus."
But Ford, who was personally fond of the Mississauga MPP, was privately unhappy that Wake had been misled.
On Monday, Ford's office confirmed there has been "no change" in the MPP's status.
Wake's Aug. 30 report into the Greenbelt debacle found Rasheed was "close friends" with Rehmatullah and said he was "shocked" to see the developer in Las Vegas in December 2019 when they allegedly bumped into each other in a hotel.
It later emerged that the trip was postponed till January 2020 and that Rasheed, Massoudi and Rehmatullah had massages at the same time in the hotel.
Wake's report pointed out Rehmatullah was "the only developer who had lands removed from the Greenbelt who attended the premier's daughter's wedding."
In November 2022, Ford announced he would open up 7,400 acres of the two-million-acre Greenbelt around the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area to spur housing development.
Last summer, separate reports to the legislature by the auditor general and the integrity commissioner concluded "certain developers" were "favoured" in that process.
Amid public outcry and plunging poll numbers, the premier to finally change course on Sept. 21.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Criminal investigations; Scandals

Document 426 of 500

Notley's departure leaves big shoes to fill

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/notleys-departure-leaves-big-shoes-fill/docview/2915522450/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Notley%27s+departure+leaves+big+shoes+to+fill&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Rachel Notley, a politician who changed the face of Alberta politics as the province's first New Democratic premier and then as the most successful Opposition leader in the province's history, is stepping down.
Her departure won't just be felt in Alberta but potentially across Canada as one of the country's most influential progressive politicians exits the stage. She leaves a gaping hole in Alberta's official Opposition party and a crucial question: what is the future of Alberta's NDP without the charismatic and popular Notley as leader?
Her departure doesn't come as much of a shock; speculation about her future has been growing since she lost the provincial election to United Conservative Premier Danielle Smith last May.
In announcing her retirement Tuesday morning in a speech to NDP caucus members, Notley said she would stay on as leader while the party conducts what is expected to be a hotly contested leadership race.
In an interview with the Star before making the announcement, Notley wouldn't say if she'd stay on as an MLA after the leadership race. And she was coy about whether she might enter federal politics.
She said right now she is focused on helping a smooth transition of leadership power.
"I want to give whoever becomes the new leader enough time to put their own stamp on the party and to bring everybody together to make sure the party is election ready."
The next election isn't for another three years. But a new leader will indeed need that time to demonstrate they are a worthy successor to Notley, who has left Alberta's progressive politics in a much better shape than when she found it.
When first elected in 2008, Notley's NDP caucus had just two members.
When she became leader in 2014, the NDP had grown to four members. However, in the rollicking 2015 provincial election, she defeated the long-reigning Progressive Conservatives to form the province's first NDP government with a 54-seat majority.
As premier, she enacted sweeping social and environmental changes that included protecting health care and education, phasing out coal-fired power plants and introducing a $15-an-hour minimum wage.
But it was Notley's signature piece of legislation, a provincial carbon tax, that landed her in the most trouble as her Conservative opponents blamed her for hobbling Alberta's economy.
The real culprit, though, was an oil-price-driven recession that undermined Notley's attempts to find a balance between taking action on climate change while protecting Alberta's energy-based economy.
At times, she even bumped heads with her NDP allies, not just her Conservative opponents.
In February 2018, she instituted a boycott of British Columbia wines after the B.C. government tried to prevent the expansion of the Trans Mountain pipeline transporting Alberta's energy products to the West Coast for shipment to the U.S. and overseas.
In August 2018, she sparked a public fight with federal NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh over his opposition to Notley's partnership with the federal government to have Ottawa buy the pipeline.
The Trans Mountain expansion is still underway and might be completed this year.
Notley counts that among her political accomplishments.
"The reality is that there has been just one pipeline to tidewater built in over 50 years and it's the one that I got built," said Notley, who still defends her position as an environmentalist who supports pipelines. "The pipeline is not about increasing production. It's about increasing the rate of return to the people of Alberta, who own the resource."
When it comes to legacies, though, Notley's biggest achievement was transforming the face of Alberta politics. For pretty much a century, Alberta was a one-party state, where a succession of Conservative governments reigned for decades at a time.
That changed because of Notley, who led the NDP to victory in 2015 over the deeply divided Progressive Conservative and Wildrose parties.
After she lost the 2019 election to Jason Kenney, who had formed the United Conservative Party, Notley did not quit, as do most former premiers in Alberta.
"After the 2019 election, the conversation was rife with predictions that the NDP was done and that Alberta was going to forever revert back to a one-party Conservative province. In my mind, that was very irritating. It wasn't true."
Notley stuck around and transformed the NDP into a devastatingly effective Opposition party.
A year ago, Notley was the odds-on favourite to win the May general election. It was a closely and bitterly fought contest where the NDP failed by a whisker to win enough seats in Calgary to form government.
Notley, 59, said she realized she would have to quit after losing the last election but she wanted to play shepherd to her 38-member caucus, the largest official Opposition in Alberta's history - and one that not only won every seat in Edmonton but 14 of the 26 seats in Calgary.
Some of the names already popping to the surface include: Sarah Hoffman, a former health minister who is a powerhouse in Edmonton NDP circles; Kathleen Ganley, a former justice minister who won more votes (16,500) than any other candidate in Calgary in the 2023 election; and Rakhi Pancholi, a lawyer and relative newcomer from Edmonton who has proven herself an adept critic of government policies.
CREDIT: Graham Thomson Contributing Columnist

Subject: Provincial elections; Opposition parties; Progressivism; Coal-fired power plants; Political leadership; Pipelines

Document 427 of 500

Poilievre ponders power team: Conservative columnist among 'star candidates' being considered for Tories in next election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievre-ponders-power-team/docview/2915521387/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+ponders+power+team%3A+Conservative+columnist+among+%27star+candidates%27+being+considered+for+Tories+in+next+election&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A prominent conservative commentator wants to run as a onservative MP in the next federal election, adding to the names being bandied about for who might govern alongside Pierre Poilievre if he wins.
The party currently holds 117 seats, and would need to win at least 172 to form a majority government. Who could fill out those spots is a question coming up more and more often in political conversations as polls suggest the Conservatives are on the road to power.
On Wednesday, the Star has learned, high-profile columnist Sabrina Maddeaux will announce she's seeking the party's nomination in the GTA riding of Aurora-Oak Ridges-Richmond Hill, one of the seats the Tories lost to the Liberals in 2021.
Maddeaux, whose work has appeared in the National Post and elsewhere, told the Star she's decided to try for office to give voice to a cohort - voters under the age of 40 - with which she says the Tories are already gaining traction.
"People continue to be surprised by polls that show millennials and Gen-Z stampeding to the right because of the outdated stereotype that conservatism isn't 'cool,'" said Maddeaux, 35, who grew up in the riding.
"But you know what's really not cool? Not being able to pay rent, buy groceries, or start a family because of Liberal policies that divided the country into economic winners and losers, with younger Canadians deliberately chosen as the disproportionate losers."
Polls point to younger voters - who were crucial to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals coming to power in 2015 - shifting to the Conservatives, as Poilievre keeps his message largely focused on housing and the cost of living.
While Maddeaux still needs to be selected by party members to carry the Tory banner, she's not the only one of her generation hoping to ride a youthful wave of conservative support into office.
An upcoming byelection in Durham will see Jamil Jivani, 36, try to hold the seat for the Conservatives following last year's resignation of former party leader Erin O'Toole.
Like Maddeaux, Jivani's conservative outlook and media profile have seen him elevated to a position of prominence: She nabbed a rare one-on-one interview with Poilievre last fall, while Jivani earned a spot on the party's convention stage.
The Ivy League graduate turned community advocate and broadcaster told thousands of party members he feels his dreams are being dashed under Trudeau's Liberal government, noting he's a renter who can't afford a home.
"I feel the housing crisis in this country on a very personal level and so do many of my future constituents," Jivani told delegates.
If Maddeaux and Jivani are successful, they will stand as examples of how Poilievre is rebuilding the party since he launched his leadership bid nearly two years ago.
His campaign brought hundreds of thousands of new party members into the fold, creating a new class of volunteers and donors who continue to see the Tories outpace their rivals when it comes to raking in the cash.
After overhauling the party's inner workings, Poilievre's next step is to find like-minded Conservatives to run under his banner in the next election, possibly not until 2025.
While the notion of finding "star" candidates is part of that conversation, more than one Tory insider has told the Star that to Poilievre, candidates who might already have some public profile aren't as important as those who are willing to do the grunt work it takes to get elected.
That was the message sent late in 2023 when rumours flew that the Conservatives were courting Quebec Finance Minister Eric Girard as a potential "star candidate."
As La Presse first reported, Conservative insiders shot down the notion that Girard would get a red carpet rolled out for him, particularly given that he and Poilievre are not aligned on policy ideas like eliminating the Liberals' carbon pricing program.
Poilievre has billed the coming campaign as a "carbon tax election," in which he'll focus on the Liberals' signature policy of a consumer price on carbon. The carbon levy, Liberals argue, is essential to help people curb their use of greenhouse gas emitting fuels; for Poilievre, it is ineffective and too costly.
He made that point clear during an event last week in Northern Ontario, where the Conservatives have already chosen Gaétan Malette to run against NDP MP Charlie Angus in Timmins-James Bay.
Among those scouting candidates for the Tories is a mix of longtime party insiders and regional volunteers. While there have only been a few nomination votes so far, the pace is expected to pick up as the new riding boundaries - and in some cases new ridings - come into play later this spring.
The party declined to comment on nominations, saying they are an internal party matter.
While recruiting political newcomers is one avenue, not all of those seeking to run for Poilievre are new to elected life.
In Edmonton, former MP James Cummings is expected to try for the nomination in his old riding, which he lost to the Liberals in 2021.
Alberta is also getting three new seats out of boundary redistribution, providing an opportunity for three new Conservatives in that stronghold.
In B.C., the Conservatives are hoping both to pick up seats and win back ones they previously held. Former MP Nelly Shin is trying for a comeback there to win the nomination in her old stomping ground.
Another well-known conservative personality on the roster for a B.C. riding is Aaron Gunn, who has secured the nomination in North Island-Powell River.
The Conservatives want the riding back from the NDP, and it's not the only one they hope to flip by bringing back voters who turned away from them in 2021 in favour of the PPC, in part because of the party's stance on COVID-19.
In Kitchener South-Hespeler, the PPC captured 6.7 per cent of the vote in 2021 and the Tory candidate lost by two points to the Liberals.
Come the next election, the Conservative candidate will be Matt Strauss, a former interim medical health officer, whose pushback on some COVID-19 control measures made him a lightning rod for criticism during the pandemic.
In York Centre, the Conservative candidate will be Roman Baber, the former Ontario MPP ousted from Premier Doug Ford's caucus after they clashed over COVID-19 measures. He went on to run for leadership of the federal Conservatives in the contest won by Poilievre.
CREDIT: Stephanie Levitz Deputy Toronto Star

Subject: Conservatism; Nominations; Candidates; Voters; Carbon; COVID-19; Tax elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Tax elections

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Title: Poilievre ponders power team:   Conservative columnist among 'star candidates' being considered for Tories in next election
