Document 428 of 500

Rachel Notley is stepping down as Alberta NDP leader - and leaves a gaping hole

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/rachel-notley-is-stepping-down-as-alberta-ndp/docview/2915110564/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Rachel+Notley+is+stepping+down+as+Alberta+NDP+leader+-+and+leaves+a+gaping+hole&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Rachel Notley, a politician who changed the face of Alberta politics as the province's first New Democratic premier and then as the most successful Opposition leader in the province's history, is stepping down.
Her departure won't just be felt in Alberta but potentially across Canada as one of the country's most influential progressive politicians exits the stage. She leaves a gaping hole in Alberta's official Opposition party and a crucial question: what is the future of Alberta's NDP without the charismatic and popular Notley as leader?
Her departure doesn't come as much of a shock; speculation about her future has been growing since she lost the provincial election to United Conservative Premier Danielle Smith last May.
In announcing her retirement Tuesday morning in a speech to NDP caucus members, Notley said she would stay on as leader while the party conducts what is expected to be a hotly contested leadership race.
In an interview with the Star before making the announcement, Notley wouldn't say if she'd stay on as an MLA after the leadership race. And she was coy about whether she might enter federal politics.
She said right now she is focused on helping a smooth transition of leadership power.
"I want to give whoever becomes the new leader enough time to put their own stamp on the party and to bring everybody together to make sure the party is election ready."
The next election isn't for another three years. But a new leader will indeed need that time to demonstrate they are a worthy successor to Notley, who has left Alberta's progressive politics in a much better shape than when she found it.
When first elected in 2008, Notley's NDP caucus had just two members: herself and party leader Brian Mason.
When she became leader in 2014, the NDP had grown to four members. However, in the rollicking 2015 provincial election, she defeated the long-reigning Progressive Conservatives to form the province's first NDP government with a 54-seat majority.
As premier, she enacted sweeping social and environmental changes that included protecting health care and education, phasing out coal-fired power plants, and introducing a $15-an-hour minimum wage. All were opposed by Alberta's conservative parties: the PCs and the Wildrose.
But it was Notley's signature piece of legislation, a provincial carbon tax, that landed her in the most trouble as her Conservative opponents blamed her for hobbling Alberta's economy.
The real culprit, though, was an oil-price-driven recession that undermined Notley's attempts to find a balance between taking action on climate change while protecting Alberta's energy-based economy.
At times, she even bumped heads with her NDP allies, not just her Conservative opponents.
In February of 2018, she instituted a boycott of British Columbia wines after the B.C. government tried to prevent the expansion of the Trans Mountain pipeline transporting Alberta's energy products to the West Coast for shipment to the U.S. and overseas.
In August of 2018, she sparked a public fight with federal NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh over his opposition to Notley's partnership with the federal government to have Ottawa buy the pipeline.
The Trans Mountain expansion is still underway and might be completed next year.
Notley counts that among her political accomplishments.
"The reality is that there has been just one pipeline to tidewater built in over 50 years and it's the one that I got built," said Notley, who still defends her position as an environmentalist who supports pipelines. "The pipeline is not about increasing production. It's about increasing the rate of return to the people of Alberta, who own the resource."
When it comes to legacies, though, Notley's biggest achievement was transforming the face of Alberta politics. For pretty much a century, Alberta was a one-party state, where a succession of Conservative governments reigned for decades at a time.
That changed because of Notley, who led the NDP to victory in 2015 over the deeply divided Progressive Conservative and Wildrose parties.
After she lost the 2019 election to Jason Kenney, who had formed the United Conservative Party with the sole purpose of defeating her, Notley did not quit, as do most former premiers in Alberta.
"After the 2019 election, the conversation was rife with predictions that the NDP was done and that Alberta was going to forever revert back to a one-party Conservative province. In my mind, that was very irritating. It wasn't true."
Notley stuck around and transformed the NDP into a devastatingly effective Opposition party where, during the height of the pandemic, Notley and the NDP were routinely more popular than Kenney and the UCP.
A year ago, Notley was the odds-on favourite to win the May general election. It was a closely and bitterly fought contest where the NDP failed by a whisker to win enough seats in Calgary to form government.
Notley has accused the UCP under Danielle Smith of bribing and lying to Albertans to win the election, pointing to Smith's still-unfulfilled promise to give a tax break to lower-income Albertans and her refusal during the campaign to admit she had a plan for an Alberta Pension Plan.
Notley, 59, said she realized she would have to quit after losing the last election but she wanted to play shepherd to her 38-member caucus, the largest official Opposition in Alberta's history - and one that not only won every seat in Edmonton but 14 of the 26 seats in Calgary.
And now she'll stay on as leader to provide stability for the NDP as it makes plans to kick off a leadership race that, arguably for the first time in Alberta history, will generate interest and excitement for an Opposition party's choice.
Some of the names already popping to the surface include: Sarah Hoffman, a former health minister who is a powerhouse in Edmonton NDP circles; Kathleen Ganley, a former justice minister who won more votes (16,500) than any other candidate in Calgary in the 2023 election; and Rakhi Pancholi, a lawyer and relative newcomer from Edmonton who has proven herself an adept critic of government policies.
And what of Notley's future? Does she have any interest in federal politics?
She ducks the question.
"I am interested in successfully resigning," she said with a laugh. "I am interested in and supporting our caucus over the next while. I am interested in enthusiastically supporting the next leader."
That is not a "no."
Notley might have ended the speculation over her future as NDP leader in Alberta. But let the speculation over a possible future in federal politics begin.
CAPTION: Leader of the Alberta NDP Rachel Notley gives her concession speech in the provincial election on May 29 in Edmonton.

Subject: Provincial elections; Opposition parties; Progressivism; Coal-fired power plants; Political leadership; Pipelines

Document 429 of 500

City Hall must treat noise pollution as the menace it is: letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/city-hall-must-treat-noise-pollution-as-menace-is/docview/2915110256/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City+Hall+must+treat+noise+pollution+as+the+menace+it+is%3A+letters&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Noise must be treated as a menace Toronto releases long-awaited updates to noise bylaw, Jan. 4
Noise is the new second-hand smoke harming our hearts, minds, nervous systems and children. It ruins our hearing and diminishes our quality of life and productivity.
Like cancer and COVID, noise pollution is a public-health issue, not a business development one. Toronto's weak noise regulations allow a wealth transfer from our health care, productivity and well-being to noisy businesses.
The current bylaw allows lawn-care equipment to roar throughout our neighbourhoods until the trees are bare and infill construction noise without decibel limits or noise mitigation measures.
Nighttime private garbage collection abruptly jars thousands of residents out of a good night's sleep. This is dangerous public policy particularly as our public health system has crashed.
Toronto must do better.
New York City, for example, requires construction noise mitigation for all building sites. If New York can remain a "vibrant" city with noise mitigation, so can Toronto. It just takes courage and leadership to make Toronto quieter and more livable.
City hall must treat noise as a menace, strengthen its regulations and fund response enforcement.
Public education about noise and our health is essential to encourage people to reduce their noise footprint and protect themselves.
We must keep pushing city hall to make us safer. Powerful interests are pushing for the status quo.
Harold B. Smith, Toronto
M-86 Citizens' Assembly on Electoral Reform
There seems to be a lack of coverage on the upcoming parliamentary vote regarding the establishment of a Citizens' Assembly on Electoral Reform.
Bringing public attention to the M-86 Citizens' Assembly on Electoral Reform will enable citizens to write to their MPs and encourage them to vote on electoral reform. MPs will vote on M-86 early in 2024, according to Fair Vote Canada.
Given the extensive impact of electoral reform to all who reside in Canada presently and in the future, news organizations should be covering and impartially educating everyone about the National Citizens' Assembly on Electoral Reform.
Elizabeth Whitwell, St. Catharines
Need to treat cellphone withdrawal as an addiction Ontario government wants tougher cellphone rules in schools, Dec. 11
There could be good outcomes for both teachers and students, but this could be a big challenge for implementation.
Research has shown that where there is an addiction of any kind, just saying you are not allowed to do this anymore does not change the negative behaviours.
I suggest that before the cellphone ban takes place, there be discussions with parents about what can be started at home regarding time out for cellphones with their children who are addicted to using their phone frequently. Most students will require the help of a professional in the school to manage behavioural changes. Start planning and use smart goals for good outcomes.
This behaviour will not change overnight just by having the teachers inform students that their phones are no longer allowed in the classroom. Let's work together for good outcomes for families.
Norma Nicholson, Mississauga
No shortage of diverse candidates - being appointed a judge depends on who you know Judicial Vacancies put justice at risk, Jan. 13
I am tired of reading about vacancies and the view that there is "a lack of applicants from diverse groups." That is simply untrue. There are lots of qualified, capable candidates of diverse backgrounds that keep applying, some of them even being interviewed on more than one occasion, but unfortunately, they lack the political connections and are just simply overlooked.
Being appointed has very little to do with merit as it does about who you know.
Cynthia Hill, Brampton
Toronto can't support more refugees
Toronto churches close to refugees amid growing expenses, Dec. 27
It is completely irresponsible of Ottawa to be sending so many refugees to Toronto when they know we can't support them properly and we already have hundreds of homeless, poor and food bank users who need those resources. Also, it is an added burden on our health-care system and affordable housing which is in crisis right now.
Toronto's citizens should not be having to pay for refugees upkeep while so many are barely getting by already. Are you listening, Prime Minister Trudeau?
Jane White, Scarborough
Transit mess belongs to the province
It almost sounds like Olivia Chow is to blame for this transportation mess and should now clean it up. I see no mention at all of Premier Doug Ford and his "subways" mantra.
Actually, wasn't a replacement LRT planned, organized and paid for to be installed some time before the RT was to be retired? But then Ford nixed all that, like so much else no matter what the results and pushed for a subway, with two stops only and no plan for anybody's speedy, or any kind of travels for the many years in between.
This mess belongs to the province, where are Ford's planners? And have they not been collecting from all of us payments toward this construction since its conception?
Helga Weick, Toronto
Ontario doctors charging fees for uninsured services
Why are Ontario's doctors allowed to threaten to charge their patient clients $15 to $25 per prescription called in by pharmacies, if the patient has not visited them every three months?
All an investigator has to do is to visit doctors' clinics/offices to see such threatening notices featured prominently in patient clients' waiting rooms.
For patients with decades-long prescriptions for diabetes, high-blood pressure, maintenance for cardiac issues, psoriasis, eczema, maintenance of eye issues, water-retention, leg swelling issues, back issues, etc., a visit to the doctor to check on blood testing levels regarding all these long-time issues are only required once a year, maybe twice a year for some of it. Why should OHIP be charged for four visits when one is totally sufficient?
Small billings in the thousands amount to a lot of wasted money.
The billing processes needs to be examined immediately. What are our elected officials doing to stop dishonesty in health-care billing?
Antoinette Schokman-De Zilva, North York
Nothing beats a good night's sleep
Should we normalize Separate bedrooms? Jan. 15
My best gift for our 50th wedding anniversary was my own bedroom.
A good night's sleep equals a happier and healthier life.
Aileen Wong, Scarborough
Renters do pay property taxesSupport for Chow and blame for Ford and Tory on taxes, Jan. 15
In response to Aldona Satterthwaite assertion that renters do not pay property taxes and thus are not doing their part is very far from the truth. Apartment buildings are indeed taxed at a different, and usually higher, rate than single family homes. This cost is baked into their rents unless of course the landlord is absorbing the cost. Unlikely, but I suppose possible.
Kim Bennett, Toronto
CAPTION: A garbage truck and worker collecting waste on the east side of Yonge Street in Toronto.

Document 430 of 500

Toronto MP Carolyn Bennett to become Canada's ambassador to Denmark: Sources

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-mp-carolyn-bennett-become-canadas/docview/2915110183/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+MP+Carolyn+Bennett+to+become+Canada%27s+ambassador+to+Denmark%3A+Sources&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Outgoing Toronto Liberal MP Carolyn Bennett will soon be named Canada's next ambassador to Denmark, the Star has learned.
The appointment is expected to be announced in the coming days, sources say. The current ambassador, Denis Robert, was appointed to the role in 2021.
Bennett's office did not respond to a request for comment Tuesday morning.
The 73-year-old former cabinet minister announced in December that she was resigning her seat in Toronto-St. Paul's after more than 26 years in the House of Commons.
She will join other former Liberal cabinet ministers to whom Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has awarded positions aboard. In 2017, Trudeau named outgoing foreign affairs minister Stéphane Dion as ambassador to the European Union and Germany, while John McCallum - who was Trudeau's immigration minister from 2015 to 2017 - was appointed Canada's ambassador to China.
After Trudeau's Liberals won power in 2015, Bennett served as minister of Crown-Indigenous relations for six years. Trudeau shuffled her to the mental health and addictions portfolio in 2021, a role that is charged with helping to address Canada's toxic drug crisis, among other issues.
Last summer, a time of anxiety and frustration within Liberal circles about the government's performance as the party lags in national polls behind Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives, Bennett was among a group of veteran ministers who left Trudeau's cabinet after they said they wouldn't run for re-election. They included Mississauga Centre MP Omar Alghabra, Helena Jaczek, and Vancouver's Joyce Murray.
Bennett's departure comes as the electoral map in Canada's biggest city slowly takes shape for the next federal vote, which isn't scheduled until 2025, but could occur sooner if Trudeau's minority government falls to a confidence vote or if the prime minister decides to call the election earlier.
Running to replace Bennett as Liberal MP in Toronto-St. Paul's is Leslie Church, who was previously chief-of-staff to Deputy Prime Minister and Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland, who remains the MP for University-Rosedale. Former Ontario Health Minister Eric Hoskins was rumoured to be eyeing the nomination there, too, but told the Star Tuesday that he does not currently plan to enter the contest.
Neither the NDP nor the Conservatives have not announced a candidate in the riding.
Besides Bennett, Beaches-East York Liberal MP Nate Erskine-Smith has confirmed he won't seek re-election, after his unsuccessful run at the provincial Liberal leadership last year. Kevin Vuong, the Independent MP for Spadina-Fort York, told the Star recently that he intends to run again as an Independent in the next election. The Liberals - who held the riding along with every other seat in Toronto since 2015 until they turfed Vuong days before the last election over a dropped sexual assault charge - have not yet chosen a candidate to face him in the downtown riding.
Meanwhile, the electoral dynamic will be different as the city is slated to drop from 25 ridings to 24, thanks to redrawn boundaries that occur once a decade at the federal level. The current riding of Don Valley East, represented by former provincial cabinet minister Michael Coteau, is set to dissolve into neighbouring constituencies. Coteau did not immediately respond to questions from the Star on Tuesday about whether - or where - he intends to run again.
Another question mark is Independent MP Han Dong in Don Valley North, who left the Liberal caucus last year amid controversy over alleged Chinese government meddling in Canadian politics. Dong has said he was "vindicated" after a report concluded some allegations about his involvement were unfounded, but the Liberal government has not yet said he will return to its caucus.
Dong did not immediately respond when asked Tuesday whether he will run for re-election.
The NDP has decided on candidates in two Toronto ridings so far - Kingsley Kwok in Scarborough Rouge Park, and Clare Hacksel in Toronto-Danforth - while the Conservatives have chosen former MPP and unsuccessful Conservative leadership candidate Roman Baber to run in York-Centre.
- With files from Raisa Patel
CAPTION: Toronto MP Carolyn Bennett rises during question period in the House of Commons on May 30, 2023.

Document 431 of 500

Here's what made Ed Broadbent truly stand out

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/heres-what-made-ed-broadbent-truly-stand-out/docview/2915060214/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here%27s+what+made+Ed+Broadbent+truly+stand+out&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: He was the prime minister we never had, though we surely had the chance.
Ed Broadbent, who led Canada's New Democrats for 14 years - but never the country - died last week at 87.
He left his mark as an NDP leader and a thought leader. But what made Broadbent truly stand out was as much about personality as policy or party.
This unabashed social democrat exuded an ineffable grace and sophistication, a posh professor in tailored suits with a fondness for pipes and cigars, Bach and books, while fighting the good fight for the working class. Perhaps he embodied the quintessential Canadian campus lefty - progressive, professorial, purposeful - a worthy adversary facing off against prime ministers Pierre Trudeau, John Turner and Brian Mulroney.
Reading the accolades and obituaries, I looked back to see what I'd written about Broadbent in his political prime. As a young reporter on Parliament Hill, riding along on his chartered DC-9 campaign plane, I'd wondered if he might keep gaining altitude in the polls and land in the prime minister's office on voting day.
Ahead of the historic free trade election of 1988, Broadbent had emerged as the most popular leader by far, with the NDP on a roll at 41 per cent support in one survey, compared to 35 per cent for the Liberals and 23 per cent for the Progressive Conservatives. The Liberals were tired, the Tories tarnished and Broadbent's NDP trusted.
"This shows a real shift in attitudes away from seeing the NDP as a protest party," he told me at the time. "We're the new boy in town ... we have the new prominence, we're the new presence."
But it didn't last. In the final election day poll that counted, Broadbent's ambition to transform both his party and his country remained unfulfilled.
While he won a record 43 seats, his New Democrats were shut out of the Quebec (where he'd long plotted a breakthrough) and the Atlantic Provinces. The NDP ended up with a mere 20 per cent of the popular vote, a single percentage point above its 19 per cent showing in 1984.
The party's slogan had been, "This time, Ed Broadbent and the New Democrats." It was neither, and he knew it.
"Now I'm going to listen to Mozart and Bach and Billie Holiday, and I'd like to read a novel like ordinary people," he mused.
Within a year he was gone but not forgotten. He set up the Broadbent Institute that endures to this day, a progressive think tank inspired by the leader who had carried on the intellectual and ideological legacy of his NDP predecessors, David Lewis and Tommy Douglas.
Economic nationalism had been the building block of Broadbent's early political career. Yet free trade became his electoral undoing in the end.
The more the 1988 campaign turned on free trade, the more voters turned against Broadbent's New Democrats, whom Canadians still distrusted on economic matters. That's politics.
Growing up in Oshawa in the shadow of GM, the giant U.S. carmaker, Broadbent had always believed in repatriating economic control to Canada. A reluctant warrior, he had flirted at first with the party's far-left Waffle movement.
But after winning the leadership in 1975, he privately questioned the need for widespread economic interventionism and foreign isolationism. Broadbent was never quite able to bring New Democrats along, as the party faithful kept clinging to traditional policy orthodoxy calling for nationalization (taking over a bank, Bell and the CPR) and nationalism (withdrawing from NATO and Norad).
By the late 1980s, with the party rising in the polls and Broadbent suddenly a contender, New Democrats were on the defensive over their defence policies and economic ambitions. It would take years for the party to adapt to changing economic times.
Yet Broadbent persevered, describing himself to me as "a happy warrior by disposition" thanks to "the genes I inherited."
In truth, Broadbent's childhood had been fraught. The son of an alcoholic gambler, he disliked confrontation and took refuge in intellectual reflection and gentle persuasion.
He liked to tell the story of working in an Oshawa clothing store where the sales staff relegated the ugliest ties to a rack of leftovers and competed to see who could off-load them on customers. Broadbent won the contest every time, endowed with a deceptive ability to seal the deal.
But Broadbent overreached when he pitched his ultimate dream of eliminating the Liberals by transforming Canada into a two-party system.
"It would be healthy for Canada to evolve ... with one party that's left of centre ... like us ... and a conservative party," he mused in the 1988 campaign. "I would like to see it that way."
He quickly confided to his campaign aides that he'd blundered. Sure enough, Liberals rallied to the base, panicked by the idea of political extinction, relegating the NDP to third place once again.
CAPTION: Ed Broadbent speaks in Convocation Hall at the University of Toronto on Oct. 29, 2017.

Subject: Free trade; Political leadership; Prime ministers; Political parties

Business indexing term: Subject: Free trade

Location: Canada

Title: Here's what made Ed Broadbent truly stand out

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication date: Jan 16, 2024

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Subject: Electoral reform; Cellular telephones; Judicial appointments; Property taxes

Business indexing term: Subject: Property taxes

Location: Canada

Title: Letters:   Toronto releases long-awaited updates to noise bylaw, Jan. 4 Judicial vacancies put justice at risk, Jan. 13 Ontario government wants tougher cellphone rules in schools, Dec. 11 Toronto churches close to refugees amid growing expenses, Dec. 27 Should we normalize separate bedrooms? Support for Chow and blame for Ford and Tory on taxes,

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: A.10

Publication year: 2024

Document 433 of 500

Former NDP leader exuded an ineffable grace, sophistication

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/former-ndp-leader-exuded-ineffable-grace/docview/2915057964/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+NDP+leader+exuded+an+ineffable+grace%2C+sophistication&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: He was the prime minister we never had, though we surely had the chance.
Ed Broadbent, who led Canada's New Democrats for 14 years - but never the country - died last week at 87.
He left his mark as an NDP leader and a thought leader. But what made Broadbent truly stand out was as much about personality as policy or party.
This unabashed social democrat exuded an ineffable grace and sophistication, a posh professor in tailored suits with a fondness for pipes and cigars, Bach and books, while fighting the good fight for the working class. Perhaps he embodied the quintessential Canadian campus lefty - progressive, professorial, purposeful - a worthy adversary facing off against prime ministers Pierre Trudeau, John Turner and Brian Mulroney.
Reading the accolades and obituaries, I looked back to see what I'd written about Broadbent in his political prime. As a young reporter on Parliament Hill, riding along on his chartered DC-9 campaign plane, I'd wondered if he might keep gaining altitude in the polls and land in the prime minister's office on voting day.
Ahead of the historic free trade election of 1988, Broadbent had emerged as the most popular leader by far, with the NDP on a roll at 41 per cent support in one survey, compared to 35 per cent for the Liberals and 23 per cent for the Progressive Conservatives. The Liberals were tired, the Tories tarnished and Broadbent's NDP trusted.
"This shows a real shift in attitudes away from seeing the NDP as a protest party," he told me at the time. "We're the new boy in town ... we have the new prominence, we're the new presence."
But it didn't last. In the final election day poll that counted, Broadbent's ambition to transform both his party and his country remained unfulfilled.
While he won a record 43 seats, his New Democrats were shut out of the Quebec (where he'd long plotted a breakthrough) and the Atlantic Provinces. The NDP ended up with a mere 20 per cent of the popular vote, a single percentage point above its 19 per cent showing in 1984.
The party's slogan had been, "This time, Ed Broadbent and the New Democrats." It was neither, and he knew it.
"Now I'm going to listen to Mozart and Bach and Billie Holiday, and I'd like to read a novel like ordinary people," he mused.
Within a year he was gone but not forgotten. He set up the Broadbent Institute that endures to this day, a progressive think tank inspired by the leader who had carried on the intellectual and ideological legacy of his NDP predecessors, David Lewis and Tommy Douglas.
Economic nationalism had been the building block of Broadbent's early political career. Yet free trade became his electoral undoing in the end.
The more the 1988 campaign turned on free trade, the more voters turned against Broadbent's New Democrats, whom Canadians still distrusted on economic matters. That's politics.
Growing up in Oshawa in the shadow of GM, the giant U.S. carmaker, Broadbent had always believed in repatriating economic control to Canada. A reluctant warrior, he had flirted at first with the party's far-left Waffle movement.
But after winning the leadership in 1975, he privately questioned the need for widespread economic interventionism and foreign isolationism. Broadbent was never quite able to bring New Democrats along, as the party faithful kept clinging to traditional policy orthodoxy calling for nationalization (taking over a bank, Bell and the CPR) and nationalism (withdrawing from NATO and Norad).
By the late 1980s, with the party rising in the polls and Broadbent suddenly a contender, New Democrats were on the defensive over their defence policies and economic ambitions. It would take years for the party to adapt to changing economic times.
Yet Broadbent persevered, describing himself to me as "a happy warrior by disposition" thanks to "the genes I inherited."
In truth, Broadbent's childhood had been fraught. The son of an alcoholic gambler, he disliked confrontation and took refuge in intellectual reflection and gentle persuasion.
He liked to tell the story of working in an Oshawa clothing store where the sales staff relegated the ugliest ties to a rack of leftovers and competed to see who could off-load them on customers. Broadbent won the contest every time, endowed with a deceptive ability to seal the deal.
But Broadbent overreached when he pitched his ultimate dream of eliminating the Liberals by transforming Canada into a two-party system.
"It would be healthy for Canada to evolve ... with one party that's left of centre ... like us ... and a conservative party," he mused in the 1988 campaign. "I would like to see it that way."
He quickly confided to his campaign aides that he'd blundered. Sure enough, Liberals rallied to the base, panicked by the idea of political extinction, relegating the NDP to third place once again.
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Subject: Free trade; Political leadership; Prime ministers; Political parties; Deaths

Document 434 of 500

Mayor's tax ploy angers Liberal MPs: Feds, city hall spar over new funding for asylum seekers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mayors-tax-ploy-angers-liberal-mps/docview/2915057954/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mayor%27s+tax+ploy+angers+Liberal+MPs%3A+Feds%2C+city+hall+spar+over+new+funding+for+asylum+seekers&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA A fault line between the Toronto mayor's office and local Liberal MPs is opening up over a proposed property tax increase linked to a perceived shortfall in federal funding support.
Olivia Chow's office, in turn, is accusing Ottawa of dragging its feet when it comes to getting funds out the door and argues local MPs need to fight harder for their city.
At issue is the city's forecast that the cost to house asylum-seekers will rise to at least $250 million in 2024 - and its request for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government to pick up the tab.
Otherwise, Toronto's budget chief, Coun. Shelley Carroll, said she'll pitch adding an extra six per cent "federal impacts levy" onto the already planned property tax hike of 10.5 per cent.
"This is so ridiculously political," one Toronto Liberal MP, who was granted anonymity to discuss the intergovernmental dynamics at play, told the Star.
Those dynamics are indeed sensitive.
Tensions between city hall and Parliament Hill are a tale as old as political time.
But this isn't a great time for Trudeau. His party is being battered in the polls, with at least one pollster saying its projections suggest the Liberal stronghold in Toronto - where the party has dominated in the past three federal elections - is at risk of being breached.
Being blamed for a property tax hike at a time when cost-of-living issues are on everyone's minds is a hit the Liberals don't need.
Several told the Star they see Chow's move as "outrageous" on numerous fronts. They charge she's shirking the city's responsibility to cover the costs of asylum-seekers, not being transparent enough about the price tag and just expecting Ottawa to step in, figuring the federal Liberals have no political choice.
"It's political and it may be because she calculates we're weaker than (Premier Doug) Ford, but I think we can win on the strength of our record," a Liberal MP said.
Privately and publicly, Liberals point to the $5.47 billion in support their government has provided to Toronto since they took office, including $97 million this past summer towards housing costs for asylum-seekers.
Julie Dzerowicz, a Liberal MP who won the riding of Davenport by a whisker over the NDP in 2021, said her government isn't getting enough credit for its investments.
"We need to be friends," she told the Star. "We have tough times ahead."
Dzerowicz and others told the Star that meetings are ongoing between Ottawa and Toronto officials to figure out the next steps for funding. Complicating those conversations is a tension over who is actually responsible for the costs associated with asylum-seekers.
When a surge in people seeking asylum in Canada began just after Trudeau came to power, cities and provinces argued it was time for the federal government to start footing the bill because that surge was driven by federal policies that were beyond their control.
A fund was launched in 2017 to help, but what was supposed to be a short-term measure has become a long-term expectation from cities. Within the government, discussions are underway about how to manage that expectation, not just for this year but also in the future.
On Monday, Chow said she is having productive conversations - and also thanked the Liberals for the funding the city has received so far.
"I've been speaking to a lot of the members of Parliament from Toronto to answer the questions they have," she told reporters.
"It is a process, and I'm optimistic that we will find some kind of solution."
But her office said there is also too much talking.
A source close to Chow, who was granted anonymity because they are not authorized to speak to the media, said local MPs should be pressuring the Prime Minister's Office to help their city, not complaining about the mayor or city council making them look bad with a tax hike.
"The feds are always willing to take a phone call, respond to text messages, have a photo op, but it has been like getting blood from a stone when it comes to actually funding things," the source said, pointing to funding announcements on housing and even previous refugee funding commitments where the cash isn't flowing as fast as promised.
All the meetings in the world don't matter if the money doesn't get sent along, the source said.
"Nobody quite understands what their calculation is. It's almost like they've decided to give up on Toronto and that doesn't seem to be in anybody's best interest."
Criticism from Toronto that it's being ignored by Ottawa is hardly new. But the political context is very different in this round of fighting, as the Liberals consider what it will take to remain in government after the next federal election.
Abacus Data CEO David Coletto told the Star, however, that just as his national polls suggest the government is in trouble overall, his internal projections suggest a number of Toronto ridings are at risk, including Davenport, Eglinton-Lawrence, Etobicoke Centre, Etobicoke-Lakeshore, Spadina-Harbourfront, Willowdale and York Centre.
Some of those seats are very firmly in the Tories' sights, and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre moved swiftly to condemn Chow's proposed tax increases.
"This is what you get when you elect NDP/Liberal politicians: massive tax hikes," he posted to his social media account in the wake of Chow's budget reveal.
"Only common sense Conservatives will cut your taxes."
Some Liberals told the Star they see Chow's move as an effort to give NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh a bump - she's a former New Democrat MP and her late husband, Jack Layton, led the federal party.
Mélanie Richer, a former adviser to Singh, said she doesn't think that's what's happening, but acknowledged the federal New Democrats have struggled to break through in Toronto in recent years.
Chow's move opens a window, Richer suggested, for Singh to argue that federal Liberals are failing Toronto.
"A lot of what Mayor Chow is doing, there is an opportunity for the NDP to pick up on that because folks in Toronto are upset," she said.
Muhammad Ali, vice-president at Crestview Strategies and a veteran of Liberal campaigns, called Chow's decision to hold Trudeau responsible for a potential property tax increase "bold," but also likely to backfire.
"If I was Chow, I would have built up a few more big-city allies with lots of Liberal seats pushing the same message, demanding relief or face widespread big tax increases," he said. "But she didn't do that, and even (former mayor John) Tory did not win the battle demanding big money relief even leading into the 2021 election."
With files from Mahdis Habibinia
CREDIT: Stephanie Levitz Deputy Toronto Star David Rider Toronto Star Chief With files from Mahdis Habibinia

Subject: Local elections; Members of Parliament; Costs; Funding; Politics; Prime ministers; Cities; Property taxes; Tax increases

People: Chow, Olivia

Title: Mayor's tax ploy angers Liberal MPs:   Feds, city hall spar over new funding for asylum seekers

Document 435 of 500

Former Doug Ford cabinet minister in political limbo as RCMP probes Greenbelt scandal

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/former-doug-ford-cabinet-minister-political-limbo/docview/2915142999/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+Doug+Ford+cabinet+minister+in+political+limbo+as+RCMP+probes+Greenbelt+scandal&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: MPP Kaleed Rasheed, ejected from Premier Doug Ford's cabinet and caucus over the Greenbelt scandal, will be stuck in political limbo until the RCMP completes its investigation.
Insisting he did nothing wrong, Rasheed has been banking on Integrity Commissioner J. David Wake to exonerate him so he can return to the Progressive Conservative fold.
But Wake - author of the August report that triggered the dismissal due to a misremembered Las Vegas trip with a Greenbelt developer - has not yet even determined whether to take a second look at the Mississauga East-Cooksville MPP's case.
The ethics watchdog says his hands will remain tied as long as the RCMP criminal probe continues.
Both Rasheed, who wants to clear his name, and NDP Leader Marit Stiles, who seeks more details about what she has dubbed "the now-infamous" Las Vegas jaunt, have asked Wake to do another report.
"No decision has been made regarding the request from Marit Stiles, leader of the Official Opposition, for the integrity commissioner to provide an opinion on whether Kaleed Rasheed has contravened the Members' Integrity Act," said Michelle Renaud, spokesperson for the legislative officer.
"Given that the RCMP has launched a broadly worded investigation into a matter that could overlap with the subject matter of, and individuals named in, the request, the commissioner has informed Ms. Stiles, Kaleed Rasheed and Speaker of the legislative assembly, Ted Arnott, that he will not be proceeding with the request at this time," said Renaud.
Under the Members' Integrity Act, which governs MPPs' conduct, the integrity commissioner "shall suspend" any "inquiry until the police investigation or charge has been finally disposed of, and shall report the suspension to the Speaker."
That means Rasheed, a former public and business service delivery minister who currently sits as an Independent MPP, must await the RCMP probe, which could take years to resolve.
While he did not return repeated messages from the Star seeking comment, friends confide that he had hoped Wake would take on his cause.
"Kaleed was optimistic this could be resolved," said one Tory MPP, speaking confidentially in order to recount a private conversation.
Rasheed, an affable two-term member, remains in contact with many of his former caucus colleagues, who were shocked by his defenestration last September.
"I was sitting near him (in a Niagara Falls conference room) when (a top Ford official) came in and said, 'Kaleed, can I see you for a second?' We never saw him again," said another rattled PC MPP.
Rasheed "just wants to get this cleared up," a third Tory emphasized.
"He wants to get back into the PC caucus. He knows he made a mistake on the dates (of the trip) but he didn't do anything wrong and he doesn't want this hanging over him."
But Rasheed's office provided the wrong dates for a winter 2020 trip to Las Vegas, where he encountered his developer friend Shakir Rehmatullah, who, Wake has concluded, was "more likely than not" tipped off by someone to the government's plans to remove lands from the Greenbelt.
Also on that vacation were Ford's then-principal secretary, Amin Massoudi, and Jae Truesdell, who departed as the premier's director of housing policy the same day as Rasheed was forced from caucus.
On Sept. 20, the premier's office said "if Mr. Rasheed can clear his name through the office of the integrity commissioner, he will be provided an opportunity to return to caucus."
But Ford, who was personally fond of the Mississauga MPP, was privately unhappy that Wake had been misled.
"We expect people to remember the month they took a trip," a source close to the premier said at the time.
"There are generally a lot of questions not answered in light of what was said about what happened."
On Monday, Ford's office confirmed there has been "no change" in the MPP's status.
Rasheed, who has not been seen at Queen's Park since last summer, has maintained he stepped aside "so as not to distract from the important work of the government."
Wake's Aug. 30 report into the Greenbelt debacle found Rasheed was "close friends" with Rehmatullah and said he was "shocked" to see the developer in Las Vegas in December 2019 when they allegedly bumped into each other in a hotel.
It later emerged that the trip was postponed till January 2020 and that Rasheed, Massoudi and Rehmatullah had massages at the same time in the hotel.
Wake's report pointed out Rehmatullah was "the only developer who had lands removed from the Greenbelt who attended the premier's daughter's wedding."
In November 2022, Ford announced he would open up 7,400 acres of the two-million-acre Greenbelt around the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area to spur housing development.
Last summer, separate reports to the legislature by the auditor general and the integrity commissioner concluded "certain developers" were "favoured" in that process.
Amid public outcry and plunging poll numbers, the premier to finally change course on Sept. 21.
"It was a mistake to open the Greenbelt. I'm very, very sorry," Ford said at that time.
The Greenbelt fiasco also led to the resignations of former housing minister Steve Clark and a senior aide.
CAPTION: Mississauga East-Cooksville MPP Kaleed Rasheed, shown onSept. 1, 2021, is sitting as an Independent after resigning from the Progressive Conservative caucus in the wake of an integrity commissioner probe into the Ford government's Greenbelt land swap.

Subject: Criminal investigations; Scandals

Location: United States--US; Las Vegas Nevada

Title: Former Doug Ford cabinet minister in political limbo as RCMP probes Greenbelt scandal

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Document 436 of 500

'Outrageous': Privately, Justin Trudeau's Toronto MPs are furious at Olivia Chow over her property tax gambit

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/outrageous-privately-justin-trudeaus-toronto-mps/docview/2915142997/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Outrageous%27%3A+Privately%2C+Justin+Trudeau%27s+Toronto+MPs+are+furious+at+Olivia+Chow+over+her+property+tax+gambit&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Subject: Local elections; Members of Parliament; Costs; Funding; Politics; Prime ministers; Cities; Property taxes; Tax increases

People: Trudeau, Justin

Title: 'Outrageous': Privately, Justin Trudeau's Toronto MPs are furious at Olivia Chow over her property tax gambit

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jan 15, 2024

Section: politics/federal

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document 437 of 500

Young Ontario activists' climate lawsuit against province goes to Court of Appeal on Monday

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/young-ontario-activists-climate-lawsuit-against/docview/2914889870/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Young+Ontario+activists%27+climate+lawsuit+against+province+goes+to+Court+of+Appeal+on+Monday&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The young Ontarians who've spent years fighting the province's climate-change plan are gearing up for another day in court Monday.
The consortium of young Ontarians who, since 2019, have been pursuing legal action against the Ford government over its climate change plan, is headed to the Ontario Court of Appeal this week. These youths, many of whom launched this lawsuit when they were teens, have alleged the province's handling of the climate crisis is so "woefully inadequate" it breached their Charter rights.
In April, after years of effort, they won a Pyrrhic victory. The court agreed with many of their arguments, rejecting Ontario's contentions that the province's emissions have no effect on climate change and its climate plan is meaningless, rather than actual law.
Still, Justice Marie-Andrée Vermette ultimately sided with the province, ruling that while "young people are disproportionately impacted by climate change," that is not the fault of the Ontario government's climate laws.
One of the applicants, Shaelyn Wabegijig, told the Star she's excited to go before the triumvirate of judges in-person Monday after having had to appear virtually due to pandemic restrictions last go around.
She said she can't wait to look the province's representatives "in the eye" as her and her cohorts "fight for their future."
Wabegijig said she hopes these judges will be the ones to finally compel the government to base its emissions targets on "peer-reviewed climate science."
"Justice Vermette made a number of findings of fact that were extremely consistent with the theory of the case we put forward," said lawyer Nader Hasan of Stockwoods LLP in Toronto. "But we say she made some mistakes we're trying to get corrected."
Hasan, who will be representing the youths in court again this week, said he is optimistic about the appeal. But even if he were walking into another defeat, "these issues are so important that, regardless of chances of success, one has to fight."
"Our position is a simple one: Governments should not be above the law when it comes to confronting one of the most pressing and substantial challenges humanity has ever faced," he said. "If we're successful, it'll make it clear that the people have the ability to hold the government's feet to the fire."
The initial case focused on the climate legislation passed by the government of Premier Doug Ford, shortly after he was elected in 2018. The Cap and Trade Cancellation Act repealed more stringent emission reduction targets, passed by former premier Kathleen Wynne's government, and replaced them with targets that would allow 200 additional megatonnes of carbon to be emitted by 2030, the lawsuit alleged.
"Ontario's plan is to curb greenhouse gas emissions by 30 per cent by 2030," said Hasan. "That is far, far, far below what needs to happen on a global scale."
It's also not consistent, the lawsuit claimed, with Canada's Paris Agreement commitments to limit global warming to 1.5 degrees Celsius, which Ontario also pledged to uphold, constituting a breach of the Charter rights of the province's youth, who will bear the brunt of climate change.
"Young people have the most to lose," said Hasan. "And they've done nothing wrong. It's their parents and grandparents and great-grandparents who caused this harm, yet they're the ones who are going to suffer."
And so it's their hope that the Court of Appeal will overturn the lower court's findings and rule that Ontario's 2030 target is "unconstitutional and violates youth and future generations' rights to life, security of the person, and equality," said Danielle Gallant, a lawyer for environmental law charity Ecojustice, also working on the case.
"We are out of time to act," she said, "to prevent catastrophic climate change."
With files from Marco Chown Oved
CAPTION: Clockwise from top left: Sophia Mathur, Shelby Gagnon, Beze Gray,Madison Dyck,Shaelyn Wabegijig,Alex Neufeldt and Zoë Keary-Matzner, whose battle with Doug Ford's Ontario government enters a new phase on Monday.

Subject: Emissions; Climate change

Title: Young Ontario activists' climate lawsuit against province goes to Court of Appeal on Monday

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jan 15, 2024

Section: news/canada

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Document 438 of 500

Ontario Crombie's to discover, win

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-crombies-discover-win/docview/2914567835/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+Crombie%27s+to+discover%2C+win&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: So, which will it be: door No. 1 or door No. 2?
The question, of course, is one of the very first that confronts the newly elected leader of the Ontario Liberal party, Bonnie Crombie.
And what will the door she chooses tell us about how she intends to lead?
Until just this past week, Crombie was the mayor of Mississauga. But now that she is clear of this obligation and can fully turn her mind to defeating Premier Doug Ford and the Ontario PCs, Crombie must decide whether - and when - to seek a seat in the Ontario legislature.
And this is where those two doors come into play.
Many would advise that it is crucial for Crombie to get a seat as quickly as possible. Those who hold this view - door No. 1 - would argue that it is only there, in the people's house, that the new Liberal leader can effectively hold the government to account and demonstrate her ability to one day become premier.
They would further argue that it is from that green leather seat that the new leader can best form a relationship with the press gallery, which is an essential ingredient in a successful general election campaign.
And, finally, they would say it is respectful of our democratic process.
Others - and I am one of them - see things very differently and strongly believe Crombie's best choice is door No. 2.
Door No. 2 holds that she need not worry about getting a seat until the next election. Those who believe this is the best approach understand that, for her, sitting in the legislature is a waste of time.
We believe this for several reasons - both strategic and tactical. Throw in practical as well.
Let's start with the practical: the Liberals aren't considered, under the rules of the legislature, to be an official party. What does that mean? It means that Question Period is essentially the Marit Stiles and Doug Ford show, with Crombie looking like a not-ready-for-prime-time understudy. It means the Liberals are not guaranteed a question every day.
Now the tactical: the Liberals currently hold just nine seats. For Bonnie to be able to run in a byelection, one of those nine would have to resign. Whilst there is plenty of precedent for this approach, with a caucus of nine and a party that can charitably be called impecunious, it would seem to be unwise.
A lot of time and effort that would be more efficiently spent elsewhere would have to be directed to ensuring the leader's victory.
But now to the strategic: it simply doesn't matter. Not a whit.
Experience in a legislature? She already has it. Crombie sat as a federal Liberal MP from 2008 until 2011.
Relationships with the media? She's got those as well. Besides, long gone are the days when the gallery was the gatekeeper to communicating with the public.
But more than all of that, being free of the obligations of being tied to Queen's Park will allow Crombie to play to her strengths as a terrific retail politician.
Now, it is generally believed that incumbent governments have a structural advantage in elections, that incumbency allows them to hold most of the cards.
But it is her abilities as a retail politician where Crombie pulls even with the premier. Without question, Ford is the most accomplished retail politician of his generation. He's proven he can connect with Ontarians from all walks of life. It was central to his ability to lead Ontario through the pandemic. It remains his superpower.
That said, his responsibilities as premier, which keep him tied to the Pink Palace, don't let him show off those powers as much as he might like. And Crombie's freedom from the very same restrictions allow her to shine.
So while Ford's Conservatives will look to define the new leader with a multi-channel ad campaign, Crombie will be free to tour the province in a bus with her face splashed on all sides, giving real-life proof to another reality. Door No. 2 it is.
Jaime Watt is the executive
chair of Navigator Ltd.
and a Conservative
strategist. He is a freelance columnist for the Star.
CREDIT: Jaime Watt OPINION Jaime Watt is the executive chair of Navigator Ltd. and a Conservative strategist. He is a freelance columnist for the Star.;

Document 441 of 500

A hybrid proves a stepping stone to their EV for this couple

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hybrid-proves-stepping-stone-their-ev-this-couple/docview/2913705573/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+hybrid+proves+a+stepping+stone+to+their+EV+for+this+couple&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It turned out to be a life-changing experience.
Six years ago, Sue Pallotta and her husband Andy took a ride in a friend's Chevy Volt plug-in hybrid. That sparked their curiosity about hybrids and electric vehicles, and, in 2018, they leased a plug-in Mitsubishi Outlander hybrid. Last year, they made the move to a fully electric car, a Chevy Bolt SUV.
Pallotta, who lives in Port Perry, explains why she loves the Bolt, her year-round, everyday vehicle:
'In 2018, we ordered a Chevy Volt when the Ontario government offered a rebate (up to $14,000). When Doug Ford was elected, the rebate was cancelled. The Volt wasn't going to be delivered before the rebate ended, so we went to a Plug 'n Drive event in Toronto where we learned a lot about electric cars and (we) tried a Mitsubishi hybrid.
We cancelled the Volt, and leased a Mitsubishi Outlander plug-in hybrid off the lot before the rebate expired, and got $7,500 back. The Mitsubishi salesperson was very knowledgeable about electric cars, compared to what we found at many other dealerships. The Outlander wasn't exactly what we wanted, so we leased it and figured in two or three years the technology would be that much better and we could decide then if we wanted to purchase one.
When the lease was up, we bought the Mitsubishi out and used it as a trade-in. In late 2022, we were ready to go to a fully electric car. We felt the Mitsubishi technology hadn't advanced enough and they only had a plug-in hybrid. Our Mitsubishi hybrid had four-wheel drive and a lot of great features, but didn't go that far in electric mode ... maybe 45 kilometres in summer and less in winter.
We ordered a Chevy Bolt EUV in November, 2022 and got it in January, 2023. It was roomier and sportier-looking than the Mitsubishi. We bought it from a dealership not far from us. We, again, found most salespeople were not super knowledgeable about the electric cars they were selling. We knew more than they did. But we could take it to our local dealership to be serviced - that's one reason we didn't look at Tesla, because we couldn't get one serviced in our area.
Although reducing our carbon footprint mattered, we didn't want to spend a fortune on an electric car. The main reason we bought an electric vehicle was because you save a lot over time and they hold their value better than gas cars. We live on a country road, and they are heavier than gas cars, so it's very stable in winter.
We got a family and friends discount through GM and a $5,000-federal-government incentive to buy an electric car, so the Bolt cost us $43,000. Andy figures in 10 years, it will have paid for itself. I have no fuel costs, no oil changes, no exhaust and the brakes don't wear as quickly. (The car's electric motors help slow it).
I plug the Bolt in at 7 p.m. at night (off-peak hours) in our garage and unplug it at 7 a.m., although I don't plug it in every day. It gets 417 km per charge in summer and about 280 in winter, and I work only eight kilometres away. The only time I got range anxiety was when I had to attend a banquet in November in Oakville. It was cold, so the car didn't get the range it did in warmer weather and I didn't know where the charging stations were. But I didn't run out of charge. Also in November, I went to the Royal Winter Fair and was worried when it took an hour to get out of the parking lot. It only used 10 km of range during that hour, even though I had the heater running. If you are not moving, it hardly uses any charge.
Most malls have free charging stations, including the Oshawa Centre, where I often shop. Their stations are near the entrance, which is amazing. At home, we figure the car charging adds about $25 a month to our hydro bill. Andy figures it would cost $4.88 to fully charge it if it was plugged in every day, but as I'm only making a 16-km round-trip to work, I don't need to plug it in daily.
Andy drives a GMC Silverado and would love to get an electric truck. We use the truck to take my dressage horse to shows and electric trucks don't get good enough range if you're pulling a horse trailer for us to consider getting one at this time."
CAPTION: Sue Pallotta of Port Perry loves her 2023 Chevy Bolt EUV. She never has gas or oil change bills, and a full charge of its battery lasts her for days.
CREDIT: Tracy Hanes Special to the Star

Subject: Automobiles; Electric vehicles

Title: A hybrid proves a stepping stone to their EV for this couple

Document 442 of 500

Online survey is a horrible way for the city to determine what the average person wants

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/online-survey-is-horrible-way-city-determine-what/docview/2913705203/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Online+survey+is+a+horrible+way+for+the+city+to+determine+what+the+average+person+wants&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: What Torontonians want Olivia Chow to spend more on, Jan. 10
I was stunned to read the Star's coverage of city of Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow's online prebudget survey.
Conducting an online survey is a horrible way to determine what the average person wants, and both the mayor's office and the Toronto Star know this. The methodology of the survey was designed for bias, as the mayor's base is much more online than the average Torontonian and is also paying attention to missives from her office, including the invitation for this survey posted on Twitter.
The survey itself reveals further bias, having grouped affordable housing and shelters as a single category. While both are very important, they are clearly two entirely different issues. One applies directly to pretty much everyone in the city, and the other addresses the homeless.
It seems quite clear that the mayor conducted this survey because she wanted to get a boost for the programs she already supports.
Greg Stewart, Toronto
Toronto tax increases have been too low
Toronto budget chief warns of 16 per cent tax hike without Ottawa's help on refugees, Jan. 10
Many Torontonians are balking at the idea of a double-digit increase to their property tax rate.
Let's keep things in perspective.
In 2023, the city of Toronto had the lowest residential rate in the province at just 0.66 per cent. Compare that to Ottawa, 1.17 per cent, Brantford, 1.38 per cent, or Windsor with its eye-watering rate of 1.94 per cent.
Even after factoring-in Toronto's higher property values, homeowners in this city have no real cause to complain about the proposed increase.
Mick Welch, Toronto
Sell and move to Chatham!
If you can't afford a 10 per cent plus tax increase, plus the increases that will come every year going forward, perhaps it's time to sell your house that is worth millions in Toronto (or Vancouver) and move to southern Ontario, where jobs are going begging especially in the new and old car industry. Doctors, nurses -- all can have a better life here. Young people can even own a small farm or house.
Whether you are older, retired or young, house prices are well under $500,000 on average. You could buy acreage, or a small hobby farm and grow your own vegetables. Fewer people mean less hassle.
So, who will be the next overtaxed taxpayer to come on down to southern Ontario between Windsor and London?
Get here before the new bridge is completed to the United States and beat the boom that is bound to follow.
Wayne Robertson, Chatham
Toronto should expect an exodus
Canada's vacancy rate for major office markets continues to rise, presently showing at 17.7 per cent.Many large corporations are leaving centers such as Montreal, Toronto, Edmonton and Vancouver to resituated in smaller communities with more financial friendly governments, taxes and excess labor force.
At the same time many municipalities are facing financial challenges brought on by excessive spending before and during the pandemic. Using up their emergency funds to exhaustion levels. When it was good times financially, these municipalities did not bank their excess funds, often investing in financial schemes that did not work out as planned. Toronto may well increase some of its taxes well over 10 per cent. If land taxes increase to these levels, many overpriced homes may go onto the market, and those in the money may well relocate to friendlier municipalities nationwide.
Canada's housing starts may have increased, but not to the levels politicians hoped for, with inflation and interest rates remaining higher than previously, those with homes were not prepared to sell. With the increase in municipal taxes in places like Toronto this exodus may well happen.
The Green Party of Ontario has come out with a plan to increase housing starts, but this plan speaks of a new form of housing, down scaled in size and price. Furthermore, it speaks of doing away with regulations and possibly amalgamating these regulations into softer costs for the purchasers of homes, while at the same time, protecting the provinces wetlands and farms.
But progressive parties face a powerful sector unwilling to change its ways of developing and selling its housing starts.
The political parties of Ontario and most of Canada proclaim, "a revolution in housing," but there is no revolution, and their claims are merely propaganda with no teeth.
Perhaps the only "revolution" we will see is the relocation of those with money to other jurisdictions, leaving cities like Toronto with an empty downtown core. This happened before and will surely happen again.
Steven Kaszab, Bradford
CAPTION: Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow during a press conference at Queen's Park in Toronto, Nov. 27, 2023.
