Document 451 of 500

He 'believed in building a good society': Former Ontario MP led Oshawa riding for two decades, took helm of New Democrats in 1975

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/he-believed-building-good-society/docview/2913404951/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=He+%27believed+in+building+a+good+society%27%3A+Former+Ontario+MP+led+Oshawa+riding+for+two+decades%2C+took+helm+of+New+Democrats+in+1975&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ed Broadbent, the former Ontario MP who was the longest-serving leader of the federal New Democrats, has died at the age of 87.
The news was shared Thursday by the Broadbent Institute, a think tank the longtime New Democrat founded in 2011 to champion progressive policies.
"Our country has lost a fierce champion for ordinary Canadians, an intellectual who strongly believed in building a good society," the organization said in a statement.
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh said Broadbent was often "generous" with his time, offering advice and encouragement whenever Singh needed to hash out a policy idea or political strategy.
"I have often said that Ed was who I wanted to be when I grew up. He taught me about leadership and how to turn political principle into actions that helped improve the lives of Canadians. Ed knew that the struggle for justice and equality is long and there are many days when we are tempted to give in to cynicism," Singh wrote in a statement.
"He lived a long life of hope and he worked hard to turn that hope into reality."
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said Canada was "better off" because of Broadbent's political contributions to the country.
"He leaves behind an incredible legacy - one that will, no doubt, continue to inspire people across the country," Trudeau wrote on social media.
Former prime minister Jean Chrétien, meanwhile, remembered Broadbent as a good friend who was known for his civility.
Broadbent hailed from Oshawa and was the son of a General Motors office worker father and a mother he has credited with shaping his social democratic beliefs.
According to a story published in the Star after his ascent to party leader in 1975, Broadbent won his first political contest when he was elected student council president in Grade 11.
After working as a professor - a job he would return to later in his career - he was elected as an MP in Oshawa in 1968, clinching an extraordinarily narrow victory over his Progressive Conservative rival. He spent two decades representing the riding, becoming NDP leader in 1975 and serving in the role for 14 years.
In that time, he led the New Democrats through four elections. During the 1988 contest, the party won 43 seats in the House of Commons, then an all-time high.
But the party failed to form the official Opposition, with Broadbent announcing his resignation as leader in 1989 in a speech to an emotional audience at a Toronto hotel.
"Now is the time for renewal; now is the time to pick a new man or a woman to lead this party," he said.
Broadbent went on to relinquish his Oshawa seat at the end of that year. He became a Companion of the Order of Canada - which recognizes achievement of the highest degree - in 2002.
Yet in 2003, at the age of 67, the NDP titan decided to return to the political stage after Jack Layton won the party leadership, in an attempt to boost the NDP's profile ahead of the 2004 election.
Broadbent won his seat, this time in Ottawa Centre, in a historic race that saw Layton's New Democrats win 103 ridings to form the Official Opposition.
He left politics in 2005 to spend time with his wife, Lucille, who succumbed to breast cancer the following year.
Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Broadbent was "someone who never stopped learning and finding joy in life," while Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow said Canadians had lost "a fierce champion of ordinary people."
Chow said she'd recently had the pleasure of introducing Broadbent at the launch of his 2023 book, "Seeking Social Democracy." Holding a copy marked with Post-It notes, she read from a speech Broadbent made to the House of Commons in 1989 about ending child poverty.
His leadership on the topic helped inspire her to become the city's first child advocate when she became a councillor, Chow said.
Brian Topp, chair of board of the Broadbent Institute and a former national campaign director for Layton, said the veteran politician was instrumental in helping Layton chart his path to success.
"You could say that not only was Ed the leader in what has become the second best results (for the NDP), but he was also one of the architects of the best results," Topp said.
Broadbent also enjoyed a close relationship with Bob Rae, Canada's ambassador to the United Nations who previously served with both the federal and Ontario NDP before joining the Liberal party.
"Oh, he was not keen at all. He was very upset, and he let me know it," Rae said, reflecting on his 2006 decision to become a member of the federal Liberals.
For Timmins-James Bay NDP MP Charlie Angus, though, Broadbent was the person who made Angus, aged 18 when the pair first met, believe that politics was a noble thing to do.
"He liked passion and wasn't afraid to get in and really shake things up when things had to be shaken up. But he was also a skilled negotiator," he said.
With files from Alyshah Hasham
CAPTION: Ed Broadbent led the federal New Democrats through four elections between 1975 and 1989. Inthe 1988 vote, the party won 43 seats in the House of Commons,then anall-time high.Toronto Star file photo

CREDIT: Raisa Patel Toronto Star

Document 452 of 500

Small caucus but a big paycheque for Crombie: Liberal leader will make more than head of official Opposition despite just 9 MPPs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/small-caucus-big-paycheque-crombie/docview/2913404925/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Small+caucus+but+a+big+paycheque+for+Crombie%3A+Liberal+leader+will+make+more+than+head+of+official+Opposition+despite+just+9+MPPs&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Liberals will pay their new leader Bonnie Crombie - who steps down Friday as Mississauga mayor after nine years in the job - a salary of $185,000 a year, the Star has learned.
That will make Crombie the second-highest paid party leader in Ontario after Premier Doug Ford at $208,974 and ahead of Ford's cabinet ministers at $165,851.
Despite heading a small caucus of nine MPPs in third place at the legislature, the $185,000 is slightly more than the $180,886 pay packet earmarked for the head of the official Opposition, New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles.
"The way the party looked at this, it's pretty much commensurate with the opposition leader's salary," said a senior party source who spoke confidentially Thursday to discuss internal deliberations.
The $185,000, however, is a "considerable" pay cut, the source added, noting the money is coming from the party's fundraising efforts because Crombie does not have a seat in the legislature where MPPs earn a base salary of $116,550.
As mayor, Crombie earned $293,255 in salary and benefits in 2022 - the last full year for which figures are available - for her role on Peel Region council and for sitting on the board of Alectra Utilities.
She told reporters after winning the leadership Dec. 2 that compensation arrangements would have to be made with party officials and set a fundraising target of $1 million by the end of the year to boost the Liberal war chest for the 2026 provincial election.
Ford taunted the Liberals for that comment.
"Their number-one concern for their new leader is, 'Let's raise a million dollars because someone has to pay my salary' ... that shows me you picked the wrong leader," the premier said in the legislature's question period.
Crombie dismissed that and other attacks from Ford's Progressive Conservatives as "lame" and "desperate" attempts to distract voters after their flip-flop in the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal and subsequent criminal investigation by the Royal Canadian Mounted Police.
As a result of Crombie's fundraising appeal, the Liberals exceeded the goal and raised $1.2 million by Dec. 31, bringing to the total for last year to $6 million as they look to rebound after disastrous election defeats in 2018 and 2022 and close what she calls a "considerable" fundraising gap with the Conservatives.
More "ambitious" fundraising targets will be set within weeks with milestones for the rest of the year, the party source told the Star.
Crombie will be at Queen's Park for regular weekly caucus meetings with her MPPs, but is spending much of her time on the road building support for the party while keeping an eye out for MPP resignations that could present an opening for her get a seat in the legislature.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 453 of 500

Harsh reality in city budget

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/harsh-reality-city-budget/docview/2913404785/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Harsh+reality+in+city+budget&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A splash of cold water. That's one way to describe the attention-getting launch of Toronto's 2024 budget process this week.
A splash in the face of city homeowners who could be looking at 10.5 per cent hike in property taxes, a splash in the face of the federal government which has failed to live up to its financial obligations to the city.
The double-digit wake-up call is an indictment of past budgets and councils that put undue emphasis on keeping tax hikes low, even as city finances dictated otherwise. The result was a crowd-pleasing modest increase and the appearance of sound fiscal management even as the city's foundation was rotting away.
Indeed, until this year, the whole budget process had been about setting the increase first and then working the budget to make it all fit. But there was an uncomfortable truth in the significant financial shortfalls that were being kicked down the road.
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow made clear in her mayoralty campaign last year that wouldn't be her style and refused to bow to political rivals who demanded she put a number on what her tax increase would be. She would only commit that any increase would be "modest."
The size of the tax hike proposed by municipal staff - and remember, it's early days - would seem to stretch that election vow. But this year's financial plan - and the significant tax hike - make plain that budget chickens have come home to roost, to the tune of a $1.8 billion shortfall.
For the average home, such an increase would mean an extra $321 a year, plus another $53 for a special fund for housing and transit. Add in higher costs for waste management and water and it amounts to about $35 a month, not so onerous. But it comes at a time when household budgets are already strained with grocery prices and other rising expenses.
If residents are to be asked to pay more, Chow and councillors need to make the case how those tax dollars will be spent smartly to make life better.
But recall last summer's mayoralty election, when a decline in municipal services was lamented at almost every debate - overflowing garbage cans, water fountains that didn't work and locked park bathrooms. A series of disturbing crimes on the transit system highlighted underlying social problems on city streets.
It all pointed to what is put at risk through short-sighted penny-pinching. This budget proposes extra funding for transit safety and security, a welcome citywide expansion of a crisis service and more shelter beds.
The 2024 shortfall highlights too the extent of systemic financial challenges facing Canada's largest city. In addition to the costs of delivering typical municipal responsibilities, Toronto faces outsized fiscal demands to support refugees, an ever-growing shelter system, the country's largest transit system that serves commuters from across the region, and massive infrastructure bills.
As city manager Paul Johnson underscored during Wednesday's budget presentation, it's all too much for property taxpayers alone to pay. "The property-tax base just cannot generate the kinds of resources that are required to adequately fund the infrastructure of cities, to adequately fund the full breadth and depth of programs that cities are now delivering," he told reporters.
It speaks to the importance of Toronto reaching a new fiscal arrangement with senior governments. Credit to Doug Ford's government for a promising start on this front. The agreement reached last fall between Toronto and the province will see a $382 million contribution to city operations this year.
Getting the federal government to the table has proven more difficult.
The immediate priority is to get the federal government to pay for refugee support. The city has no say in how many refugees the country accepts or where they stay when they get here. Yet it's forced to pay the bills. Every night 4,300 shelter beds in Toronto are occupied by refugee seekers.
That's why this budget contains a strong dose of political brinksmanship. The city says it needs $250 million from Ottawa to reimburse the costs of supporting refugees. If the federal government refuses to pay, that could mean a further six per cent increase in property taxes, which will be known as the "federal impact levy." That most certainly has gotten Ottawa's attention.
There's a good chance that by the time council votes on the final budget in mid-February following weeks of discussion and debate, the actual tax hike will be lower. But there's a message underlying this budget - there's a price to be paid for city services. That bill has come due.
CREDIT: ;

Subject: Federal government; Refugees; Budgets; Cities; Property taxes; Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Budgets Property taxes Tax increases

Document 454 of 500

Some ServiceOntario locations will be shut down and moved inside Staples stores

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/some-serviceontario-locations-will-be-shut-down/docview/2913517077/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Some+ServiceOntario+locations+will+be+shut+down+and+moved+inside+Staples+stores&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You could soon be getting your driver's license or health card while surrounded by reams of paper and shiny new printers, instead of the usual grey and green of ServiceOntario's offices.
The Ontario government is shuttering the doors of an unknown number of ServiceOntario locations and will replace them with new counters within Staples stores.
While opposition parties have decried the move as further privatization of public services by Premier Doug Ford's government, many ServiceOntario locations has been privatized and franchised for years.
The new partnership with Staples was announced last month, on Dec. 7, "to help provide services where people already live, work and shop," public service delivery minister Todd McCarthy said in a press release.
However, the original press release from the government did not mention that several ServiceOntario locations would be closing because of the partnership. Doug Allingham, a spokesperson for McCarthy, confirmed to the Star that some ServiceOntario locations would be closing, but did not say how many or where.
"All ServiceOntario locations that are closing will have a new location open in select Staples Canada stores, not kiosks, and service will remain uninterrupted," Allingham said, while emphasizing that customers will see an increase in service thanks to Staples' longer hours.
"Many ServiceOntario providers are private, including those in communities where locations are moving to Staples Canada stores," he added. "Employees of impacted private services providers are also being provided opportunities to continue employment with Staples Canada."
ServiceOntario already has branches within Canadian Tire stores, including at its Yonge-Davenport and St. Clair-Old Stock Yards locations. The partnership began in 2010 under then-premier Dalton McGuinty.
Allingham said the government considered a number of factors when deciding to partner with Staples, including the size of stores, parking availability, and number of locations across Ontario.
NDP MPP Catherine Fife said the new partnership is "another attempt for Ford to quietly hand over our public services to private corporations," and lamented the "uncertainty" that some public servants would face if ServiceOntario locations were to close.
In the same vein, Green Leader Mike Schreiner took aim the "back room deals" that he alleges the Ford government has prioritized over everyday Ontarians, and questioned why Staples was chosen as the government's partner.
Previous governments had also tried introducing automated ServiceOntario kiosks, but those were permanently shut down in 2012 due to security concerns.
But, the human element hasn't been without its issues.
In December, several ServiceOntario employees were charged in Toronto police's Project Safari investigation and were alleged to have provided criminals with vehicle and driver information.
CAPTION: The partnership with Staples was announced in December "to help provide services where people already live, work and shop," according to the Ford government.

Conservatives will hold a nomination meeting there at the end of January with Ford expected to call a byelection soon after.

Former NDP leader Ed Broadbent dies at 87

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/former-ndp-leader-ed-broadbent-dies-at-87/docview/2913516899/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+NDP+leader+Ed+Broadbent+dies+at+87&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Ed Broadbent, the former Ontario MP who led the federal New Democrats from 1975 to 1989, has died at the age of 87.
"It is with the heaviest of hearts that the Broadbent Institute announces the passing of our founder," the think tank Broadbent founded in 2011 said in a statement Thursday.
"Our country has lost a fierce champion for ordinary Canadians, an intellectual who strongly believed in building a good society. Ed devoted decades of his life to fighting for justice and equality in Canada and around the world. As a member of Parliament, leader of the New Democratic Party, president of Rights & Democracy and beyond, he tirelessly advocated for all people, especially those on the margins of society."
More to come.
CAPTION: Ed Broadbent led the federal New Democrats from1975 to 1989.

Document 457 of 500

PM answers call for Hamas probe: Ministers exploring how to play role in investigation into sex abuse claims

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pm-answers-call-hamas-probe/docview/2913008474/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PM+answers+call+for+Hamas+probe%3A+Ministers+exploring+how+to+play+role+in+investigation+into+sex+abuse+claims&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Calls by women from across the political spectrum for Canada to play a role in investigating Hamas's use of sexual violence in its Oct. 7 attacks on Israel are finally being answered.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has ordered two key cabinet ministers to look into exactly how Canada could support a request from former Ontario premier Kathleen Wynne, former deputy Conservative leader Lisa Raitt and others to commit $1 million to support victims and survivors, and send the RCMP to Israel to aid in investigations.
But sources privy to Ottawa's deliberations say their first option - a repeat of the mechanism used to support a probe of Russia's use of sexual violence in Ukraine - faltered as that funding was delivered through the International Criminal Court, and Israel does not recognize the court's jurisdiction.
Now, Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly and Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc are exploring alternatives.
"Our hearts are with the victims and their families impacted by sexual violence," Trudeau wrote in a letter obtained by the Star that was sent to Wynne, Raitt, former Ontario NDP MPP Cheri DiNovo and others.
"Our government will always stand against sexual and gender-based violence and advocate for justice for all victims."
Ottawa's move comes as the United Nations is now sending an envoy to Israel to investigate reports of Hamas's sex crimes, according to statements from Israel's Foreign Affairs Ministry reported in The New York Times on Wednesday.
Pramila Patten, the special representative of the UN secretary-general on sexual violence in conflict, will take on her investigation as two other UN experts said the reports emerging from Hamas's Oct. 7 attacks suggest they may also qualify as crimes against humanity.
Allegations of sexual torture include gang rapes, sexual assaults, mutilations and gunshots to genital areas. Female bodies were found with their clothing pulled up to their waists, with underpants removed or torn or stained with blood, the two noted in a statement released this week.

A spokesperson for LeBlanc, who oversees the RCMP, told the Star that Canada is committed to seeking justice for victims.
"Our government unequivocally condemns the horrific attacks perpetrated by Hamas militants against innocent Israeli citizens on Oct. 7," Jean-Sebastien Comeau said.
"We continue to look at ways to support investigative efforts into instances of gender-based violence that took place on Oct. 7, and are committed to seeking justice for victims."
In Toronto, Wynne said she was "pleased that the government is seriously working on providing funding that can be used for investigation and support for victims and witnesses of these atrocities."
"It is not question of whether these crimes of rape and sexual assault took place at the hands of Hamas on Oct. 7. We know they did. It is a question now of holding the perpetrators to account," said the former premier.
"For generations the violation of women has been a reality of war that too often has been ignored or minimized because of the context. That cannot happen any longer."
Wynne's letter was followed by an opinion article in the Star from former B.C. premier Christy Clark, former interim Conservative leader Rona Ambrose, former Liberal Ontario education minister Liz Sandals and Laureen Harper, a prominent Conservative and wife of former prime minister Stephen Harper. They added their voices to a growing chorus demanding Ottawa take action, and pushed back against those who dismissed, downplayed or even denied that Hamas attackers had sexually assaulted Israelis.
Supporting Israeli women matters beyond Israel's borders, the leaders wrote.
"It does not just send a message to the women of Israel that they are believed, but to women who are victims of rape all around the world, that you are believed," they said.
"For those who were murdered and don't have a voice, we must be their voice."
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief Stephanie Levitz Deputy Toronto Star

Justin Trudeau responds to calls for probe into allegations of sexual assault by Hamas

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-responds-calls-probe-into/docview/2913200732/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+responds+to+calls+for+probe+into+allegations+of+sexual+assault+by+Hamas&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Calls by women from across the political spectrum for Canada to play a role in investigating Hamas's use of sexual violence in its Oct. 7 attacks on Israel are finally being answered.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has ordered two key cabinet ministers to look into exactly how Canada could support a request from former Ontario premier Kathleen Wynne, former deputy Conservative leader Lisa Raitt and others to commit $1 million to support victims and survivors, and send the RCMP to Israel to aid in investigations.
But sources privy to Ottawa's deliberations say their first option - a repeat of the mechanism used to support a probe of Russia's use of sexual violence in Ukraine - faltered as that funding was delivered through the International Criminal Court, and Israel does not recognize the court's jurisdiction.
Now, Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly and Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc are exploring alternatives.
"Our hearts are with the victims and their families impacted by sexual violence," Trudeau wrote in a letter obtained by the Star that was sent to Wynne, Raitt, former Ontario NDP MPP Cheri DiNovo and others.
"Our government will always stand against sexual and gender-based violence and advocate for justice for all victims."
Ottawa's move comes as the United Nations is now sending an envoy to Israel to investigate reports of Hamas's sex crimes, according to statements from Israel's Foreign Affairs Ministry reported in The New York Times on Wednesday.
Pramila Patten, the special representative of the UN secretary-general on sexual violence in conflict, will take on her investigation as two other UN experts said the reports emerging from Hamas's Oct. 7 attacks suggest they may also qualify as crimes against humanity.
Allegations of sexual torture include gang rapes, sexual assaults, mutilations and gunshots to genital areas. Female bodies were found with their clothing pulled up to their waists, with underpants removed or torn or stained with blood, the two noted in a statement released this week.
Three months ago, Hamas - which Canada has designated a terrorist organization - infiltrated Israel, killing an estimated 1,200 people and taking as many as 250 people hostage. Roughly half of the hostages have now been released.
Israel's retaliation is ongoing and the latest estimates from the Hamas-run government in Gaza say more than 23,200 people have been killed. Its death toll does not distinguish between combatants and civilians.
A spokesman for LeBlanc, who oversees the RCMP, told the Star that Canada is committed to seeking justice for victims.
"Our government unequivocally condemns the horrific attacks perpetrated by Hamas militants against innocent Israeli citizens on Oct. 7," Jean-Sebastien Comeau said.
"We continue to look at ways to support investigative efforts into instances of gender-based violence that took place on Oct. 7, and are committed to seeking justice for victims."
In Toronto, Wynne said she was "pleased that the government is seriously working on providing funding that can be used for investigation and support for victims and witnesses of these atrocities."
"It is not question of whether these crimes of rape and sexual assault took place at the hands of Hamas on Oct. 7. We know they did. It is a question now of holding the perpetrators to account," said the former premier.
"For generations the violation of women has been a reality of war that too often has been ignored or minimized because of the context. That cannot happen any longer,"
Wynne's letter was followed by an opinion article in the Star from former B.C. premier Christy Clark, former interim Conservative leader Rona Ambrose, former Liberal Ontario education minister Liz Sandals and Laureen Harper, a prominent Conservative and wife of former prime minister Stephen Harper. They added their voices to a growing chorus demanding Ottawa take action, and pushed back against those who dismissed, downplayed or even denied that Hamas attackers had sexually assaulted Israelis.
Supporting Israeli women matters beyond Israel's borders, the leaders wrote.
"It does not just send a message to the women of Israel that they are believed, but to women who are victims of rape all around the world, that you are believed," they said.
"For those who were murdered and don't have a voice, we must be their voice."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Kathleen Wynne, then Ontario's premier, are shown in Toronto on Oct. 17, 2017.

Banning cellphones a good first step but won't make classrooms safer: Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/banning-cellphones-good-first-step-wont-make/docview/2912702217/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Banning+cellphones+a+good+first+step+but+won%27t+make+classrooms+safer%3A+Letters&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Banning cellphones a good first step but won't make classrooms safer
Phones in classrooms aren't our only concerns, Jan.4
After reading this column, I am concerned that while unauthorized cellphone use in classrooms is disruptive, the school system needs to pay more attention to the unique challenges associated with student misbehaviour.
Why? During and after the pandemic, the surge in the number of students with mental-health issues has led to a growing number of students becoming apathetic, unruly, or violent towards their peers or educators.
The writer cites a report from the OECD indicating that reading, science, and particularly math scores have steadily declined in this country over the last two decades due to the overuse of technological devices. However, critics argue that overcrowded classrooms, cutbacks to resources, and a revolving door of teachers and educational assistants are destabilizing classrooms and eroding student learning.
Classroom distractions occur in most schools but it is the frequency and type of those interruptions that can prevent students from fully engaging with their teacher's instructions.
According to a global student survey released in 2020, one in five 15-year-old Canadian students reported that learning was lost to noise, distractions, and disorder. Surely, this disturbing data is tied to the call from Ontario principals warning of "enormous" staff shortages.
Banning the unauthorized use of cellphones and other devices in classrooms is a step in the right direction but not enough to make classroom environments safer and more conducive for learning.
Robert Ariano, Scarborough
Income disparity between heroes and homeless is shocking
William Nylander on verge of reaching deal with Maple Leafs, Jan. 8
With a provincial government that questions whether a nurse or a teacher deserves a one per cent pay raise, and with thousands of Ontarians going to food banks, it is an abomination that one hockey player can make $92 million (U.S.) (over eight years) while countless homeless are on sleeping on the streets of Toronto.
Something is very wrong about the income disparity between sports "heroes" and the rest of society.
Diane Sullivan, Toronto
Moving remains to develop graveyards is not new
Humane Society needs bigger home: What happens to the 650 pets buried in their cemetery? Jan. 7
It is not only pet cemeteries that are moved to a new location.
The village of Yorkville, at the north-west corner of Yonge and Bloor Streets, was home to nearly 7,000 human graves on a six-acre site extending to Bay Street and Cumberland Avenue. It was known by the name Pioneer Burial Ground, with burials between 1826 and 1855.
With the planned expansion of Yorkville, the cemetery was found to be an obstacle to development, so the families were invited to move the graves to another location. More than one thousand were reinterred at Mount Pleasant Cemetery. The remainder are probably lying under Yorkville properties.
Paul Epton, Toronto
Do pets have souls?
As an ordained minister and animal lover, I was intrigued by this article.
I am asked, occasionally, if animals have souls. As one who has interred several of my own pets, I have responded as follows:
The official answer is pets do not have souls. There is no indication in Scripture of such.
Unofficially my answer is if there are no dogs or horses in heaven, I don't want to go there.
Heretical, perhaps, but heartfelt, based on a lifetime love for God's creatures.
May the Humane Society find a good solution to their need.
The Rev. Mark McLennan, Woodstock ON
Ozempic shortage is another sign of our broken health-care system
I got a prescription for Ozempic, even though I shouldn't have qualified, Dec. 30
As a prediabetic senior, I've used Ozempic for three years without side effects other than reducing weight and obtaining healthy lab results.
For the last eight months, I've only been allowed one 28-day syringe each pharmacy visit because of the Ozempic "shortage" aggravated by people seeking Ozempic outside of legitimate prescriptions.
That brings me to the broader issues of lax oversight, privatization, greed and profits-only motivation which are increasing at an alarming rate in Canada as our health care - once envied around the world - decays.
Corporations are smelling profits as they use new pay online and telehealth and retail "health" centres.
I can no longer get a flu or COVID shot at my family doctor's office as they are now outsourced to private retail chains.
At least I am fortunate to have a doctor. Many do not.
The current Ontario government contributes to this climate as it continues its drive to starve and break public systems, declare a "crisis" and then propose only private, for-profit providers as "solutions."
Greg Kokko, Scarborough
I will be voting for the party that gives rather than takes
I have recently read Jonathan Karl's books "Betrayal" and "Front Row at the Trump Show." These books show howDonald Trump slandered the media as being fake news.
Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre is doing the same thing, rudely chomping on an apple during an interview. He insults journalists exactly like Trump.
Personally, I trust the mainstream media coverage. I read the Star and the Globe and Mail -- both accurate and honest. I believe the media holds politicians accountable through checks and balances. When the Star or the Globe print a story that is inaccurate you can call the media council of Canada to make things right.
I will be voting for the party that gives rather than takes. We pay taxes for a reason. I like dental coverage and a decent pension.
Conservatives will kill our social safety nets. And for how much savings?
Russ Young, Fort Erie

Moving science centre is like throwing a tomato at the Mona Lisa
A faulty case to move Ontario Science Centre, Dec. 21
The present provincial government is planning to move the Ontario Science Centre to Ontario Place at a great cost to taxpayers. The science centre was designed by a world-renowned architect and resident of Ontario, the late Raymond Moriyama. The building is a work of art and should not be torn down. Premier Doug Ford seems to be more interested in making money for developers than preserving a beautiful piece of architecture. Not really different from throwing a tomato at the Mona Lisa or vandalizing some other great works done by respected people.
Jane Rodman, Mississauga
Welcoming Palestinian refugees to Canada
Canada must expand eligibility to welcome Palestinians fleeing the 'Worst Place on Earth', Jan. 7
About 188,000 Ukrainians have been welcomed into Canada from a population base of about 46 million. Proportionately at least 9,000 Palestinians should be welcomed on a base of 1.9 million in Gaza.
Gary MacDonald, Toronto

Document 460 of 500

Doug Ford's flip-flop on wind turbines won't undo all the damage he caused

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-flip-flop-on-wind-turbines-wont-undo/docview/2912702212/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+flip-flop+on+wind+turbines+won%27t+undo+all+the+damage+he+caused&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Immediately upon winning power in 2018, Doug Ford's Tories unplugged wind and solar power.
Now, five years after ripping up so many signed contracts, there's a reckoning over renewable energy. Back in power after two election cycles, Ford is ready to rehabilitate the wind turbines he once dreamed of uprooting from Ontario.
New year, new policy. Another about-face from Ontario's seat-of-the-pants premier.
Fresh from reversing direction on the Greenbelt, Ford is reversing polarity on green energy. Energy Minister Todd Smith, who like the premier came to power huffing and puffing against wind turbines, is now boasting about bringing them back to the towns that ran them out of town.
All that said, Smith says the Tories will right the wrongs of the last Liberal government. He promises renewables will be restored to Ontario at roughly half of the old rates, and at a far lower political price.
That's because the cost of wind and solar has dropped steadily, thanks to innovation and competition. Smith notes that the Liberals made the mistake of overriding community objections, while the Tories are giving the final word to local governments before any wind turbines take root.
There's more. Ontario has belatedly resumed renewable energy swaps with Quebec, after quietly letting their bilateral deal lapse in late 2022. Smith had walked away from a signed agreement for hydroelectricity, negotiated by the Liberals in 2015, but recently signed a new 10-year deal with Quebec.
If all this good news sounds too good to be quite true, there's some truth to that. While it's a relief that Ford and Smith are finally seeing the error of their ways, on this as on other issues, that doesn't diminish the folly and futility of their first approach.
Where once electricity demand was seen as stalling, in line with a faltering economy, the latest projections suggest Ontario is falling behind its future needs for clean power. In short, after wasting the last five years as a bystander while the world passed them by, the Tories had to get back in the energy game to take account of long-term trends.
Even a stopped clock is accurate twice a day. But that's small consolation the rest of the time.
To be sure, the Liberals paid a price for being too far ahead of the curve with wind turbines. They tried to preposition the province for a new era of green jobs, overpaying for renewables contracts as a way of kick-starting the industry, handing the province a competitive advantage over its rivals.
The backlash came quickly. The idea soon took root that these generous incentive payments were responsible for the perceived rise in electricity rates - a political myth propounded by the Tories when they were in opposition.
In fact, renewables always made up such a small proportion of the total energy supply - a fraction of the increase in monthly utility bills. But those concerns were hyped into a pre-election issue in 2018 that magically vanished, postelection, from the political sphere.
By aborting the experiment in renewable power when they came to power in 2018, however, the Tories introduced hidden costs into the system:
First, the "opportunity cost" (as economists call it) of absenting Ontario from the latest currents in renewable energy over the past five years, while the world of electric vehicles (EVs) and battery manufacturing moved by leaps and bounds adds up a foregone opportunity to keep the province up to speed.
Second, there is the real damage to Ontario's reputation as a reliable place to do business after Ford so recklessly ordered the cancellation of 750 contracts. That he did so while simultaneously declaring, fatuously and hypocritically, that "Ontario is open for business," further eroded his credibility with private investors.
Yes, foreign investors are still coming to Ontario to make the most of EV demand, but at the expense of massive incentives from the federal and provincial governments. If Ontario's reputation for reliability on renewables were less chequered, the need for generous tax incentives might have been lessened for investors seeking a clean energy sanctuary with certainty. Either way, the province will have to pay a premium to compensate investors for the immeasurable risk of being sandbagged should Ford change his mind yet again.
Third, Ontario is falling farther behind its obligation to meet Ottawa's draft Clean Electricity Regulations that speak of a net-zero grid by 2030. We won't know for a while if willing hosts will be found for wind and solar to enable the Ford government to meet its new targets of 2,000 megawatts starting this year, plus an additional 3,000 megawatts in future years, let alone the more ambitious goals required for net-zero.
If the Liberals overdid the overriding of local sentiments, the Tories may find that an entirely hands-off approach won't get them where they need to go. But that, in the end, may be part of the plan - the subtext behind the minister's latest soothing statements.
By going through the motions of renewing renewables, Smith is artfully distracting critics from a more difficult debate about Ontario's excessive reliance on gas-fired power plants that will continue to emit undesirable levels of carbon. But that is increasingly in the nature of power politics in this province - an inability to level with ratepayers and taxpayers about the true cost levels, demand curves and supply lines.
Just ask the populist premier who talked of removing all of those renewables all those years ago.
CAPTION: While it's good news that Premier Doug Ford and his Energy Minister Todd Smith are finally seeing the error of their ways on renewable energy, it doesn't diminish the folly and futility of their first approach, Martin Regg Cohn writes.

Subject: Turbines; Electric rates; Competitive advantage; Electricity; Alternative energy sources; Renewable resources

Business indexing term: Subject: Competitive advantage Renewable resources

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

Title: Doug Ford's flip-flop on wind turbines won't undo all the damage he caused

Document 461 of 500

Antisemitic history echoing back

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/antisemitic-history-echoing-back/docview/2912267035/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Antisemitic+history+echoing+back&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ursula Schneider was a 16-year-old Jewish girl when she boarded the MS St. Louis in 1939, sailing out of Hamburg.
The Voyage of the Damned, as it became known. With 936 passengers, most of them Jews fleeing Nazi Germany, the liner crossed the Atlantic, docking first in Havana, where Ursula's father was waiting. Only 28 who had valid entry documents were allowed to get off. Ursula wasn't one of them. The ship sailed on to the Florida coast but the St. Louis was turned away by the United States.
Refugees turned pleading eyes to Canada, a country distinguished by its immigration welcome mat. But the government of prime minister William Lyon Mackenzie King, cleaving to a "none is too many" policy - those Jews - denied permission for the St. Louis to disembark in Halifax. The ship was forced to return to Europe, where the United Kingdom, Belgium, France and the Netherlands agreed to take the passengers in. More than 250 of those men, women and children were killed in the Holocaust, according to war records.
Ursula was among the fortunate refugees. The teenager, her mother and brother found sanctuary in England. Schneider later married and immigrated to Canada. She passed away at age 94.
Just over a week ago, her granddaughter Laura, born and raised in Toronto, was driving home when she came upon the pro-Palestinian demonstration that had occupied the overpass at Highway 401 and Avenue Road. She was infuriated. And she was scared. "I've lived here all my life and this is the first time I've actually felt fearful," Laura tells the Star. (She asked that we not use her surname out of anxiety that she, too, would face antisemitic wrath.)
Angry enough, and thinking of the grandmother she adored, Laura wrote a letter to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau. In it she reminded Trudeau of the formal apology he made five years ago to the St. Louis passengers, their families and the broader Jewish community for Canada's failure eight decades earlier. In the House of Commons, Trudeau said: "It is my sincere hope that we can shine a light on this painful chapter of our history and ensure that its lessons are never forgotten. Antisemitism, xenophobia and hatred have no place in this country, or anywhere else in this world."
Laura accuses Trudeau of forgetting.
"It appears that Canada and yourself have not learned from its mistakes. And it is happening again ... YOUR back is turning again," she wrote. "It appears that YOU used your apology to try to mask the antisemitism and antipathy that continues in our country. The mask has come off.
"You have shown little if any support for the people of Israel following the atrocities that happened to them by Hamas," she wrote, adding that a "senior Hamas leader thanked Canada for your support of the United Nations calling for a ceasefire. Being thanked by a terrorist group is appalling and scary. Thank goodness my grandmother isn't here to witness what is happening."
The antisemitism behind the shunning of the St. Louis is being echoed and reshaped in protests across the globe - no less right here in Toronto - that may have decreased in numbers over the past three months but have escalated in menace.
While doubtless a great many participants were expressing their revulsion for the warIsrael has conducted against Gaza, the horrors inflicted on helpless civilians, it has also become crystal clear that Jew hatred and demonizing of Israel is at the craven heart of a rabid rump of demonstrators that have been allowed to threaten, intimidate and break all the laws they wish, with police doing little to intervene and curtail.
Goodness, even bringing protesters trays of Tim Hortons coffee at the highway overpass, which they belatedly closed.
When agitators bring their hollers and jeers into primarily Jewish residential neighbourhoods, when bomb threats are made to synagogues, when Jewish schools have to be evacuated, when a Jewish-owned deli is firebombed, when Jewish businesses are vandalized, when senior citizens are harassed at a skating party at Nathan Phillips Square, when a hothead anti-Israel protester threatens to put an objecting man "six feet deep" - right in front of cops! - and nothing happens, nobody is arrested, then we have turned into a city, a country, where antisemitism will triumph, taken hostage by emboldened haters. The constitutionally enshrined right to protest peacefully has been made a mockery of, exploited to rationalize the unjustifiable.
However many protesters may attend these demonstrations out of solidarity with Palestinians, some don't even pretend to hide their support of Hamas, a designated terrorist group that unleashed mayhem with their Oct. 7 butchery, slaughtering some 1,200 Israeli men, women and children, thrilled in their rampage, and seizing at least 236 hostages. A total of 135 are still being held in Gaza but 21 are believed to have been killed in captivity.
Ursula told her granddaughter about how frightened and demoralized the St. Louis passengers had been when they were repeatedly rejected, begging for asylum. "What should have been a short time on this ship turned into a very long time. I'm usually a very quiet un-political person. But my grandmother came immediately into my mind when I saw this demonstration. What is going on in this country? I've been scared since Oct. 7 but now this was on-my-doorstep scared."
Trudeau, of course, was off enjoying a comped family vacation at a luxury Jamaica resort when Laura sent her letter.
"Not that I'm expecting a whole lot. Ideally, though, there should be some acknowledgment of how the Jewish people are being targeted. Why is this being allowed?" she asked. "Stand up and say that Jewish people deserve some protection as well. Show that you're supporting the Jewish people and not just paying lip service."
Laura was there when Trudeau delivered his long-awaited apology. It feels like she's come full circle. "Are we going to have another meaningless apology 30 years down the road for what's happening here now?"
She wrote as well to Mayor Olivia Chow and Premier Doug Four. "Ford was very supportive of the Jewish people early on but right now the silence is deafening."
The antisemitism Laura had experienced in her own life was mild, the typical bigoted jokes and cracks about money. She'd barely taken notice. But this is different, this surge of unambiguous loathing, often dressed up as anti-Zionism. The vilification has infected academia, campuses, unions, health-care workers and mainstream media.
"Maybe people were quietly antisemitic before and now people think it's OK what they wouldn't say out loud before, voicing views that were buried," says Laura. "There's also a lot of people who probably don't know the full story and are just jumping on the bandwagon. Young people looking for a cause without understanding any of the history.
"It's turned into not what happened in Israel but an attack on Jews around the world."
And right here.
CREDIT: Rosie DiManno Toronto Star OPINION

Subject: Antisemitism; Jewish people; Demonstrations & protests; Prime ministers

Document 462 of 500

Flip-flop won't undo damage by Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/flip-flop-wont-undo-damage-ford/docview/2912222611/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Flip-flop+won%27t+undo+damage+by+Ford&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Immediately upon winning power in 2018, Doug Ford's Tories unplugged wind and solar power.
Now, five years after ripping up so many signed contracts, there's a reckoning over renewable energy. Back in power after two election cycles, Ford is ready to rehabilitate the wind turbines he once dreamed of uprooting from Ontario.
New year, new policy. Another about-face from Ontario's seat-of-the-pants premier.
Fresh from reversing direction on the Greenbelt, Ford is reversing polarity on green energy. Energy Minister Todd Smith, who like the premier came to power huffing and puffing against wind turbines, is now boasting about bringing them back to the towns that ran them out of town.
All that said, Smith says the Tories will right the wrongs of the last Liberal government. He promises renewables will be restored to Ontario at roughly half of the old rates, and at a far lower political price.
That's because the cost of wind and solar has dropped steadily, thanks to innovation and competition. Smith notes that the Liberals made the mistake of overriding community objections, while the Tories are giving the final word to local governments before any wind turbines take root.
There's more. Ontario has belatedly resumed renewable energy swaps with Quebec, after quietly letting their bilateral deal lapse in late 2022. Smith had walked away from a signed agreement for hydroelectricity, negotiated by the Liberals in 2015, but recently signed a new 10-year deal with Quebec.
If all this good news sounds too good to be quite true, there's some truth to that. While it's a relief that Ford and Smith are finally seeing the error of their ways, on this as on other issues, that doesn't diminish the folly and futility of their first approach.
Where once electricity demand was seen as stalling, in line with a faltering economy, the latest projections suggest Ontario is falling behind its future needs for clean power. In short, after wasting the last five years as a bystander while the world passed them by, the Tories had to get back in the energy game to take account of long-term trends.
Even a stopped clock is accurate twice a day. But that's small consolation the rest of the time.
To be sure, the Liberals paid a price for being too far ahead of the curve with wind turbines. They tried to preposition the province for a new era of green jobs, overpaying for renewables contracts as a way of kick-starting the industry, handing the province a competitive advantage over its rivals.
The backlash came quickly. The idea soon took root that these generous incentive payments were responsible for the perceived rise in electricity rates - a political myth propounded by the Tories when they were in opposition.
In fact, renewables always made up such a small proportion of the total energy supply - a fraction of the increase in monthly utility bills. But those concerns were hyped into a pre-election issue in 2018 that magically vanished, postelection, from the political sphere.
By aborting the experiment in renewable power when they came to power in 2018, however, the Tories introduced hidden costs into the system:
First, the "opportunity cost" (as economists call it) of absenting Ontario from the latest currents in renewable energy over the past five years, while the world of electric vehicles (EVs) and battery manufacturing moved by leaps and bounds adds up to a foregone opportunity to keep the province up to speed.
Second, there is the real damage to Ontario's reputation as a reliable place to do business after Ford so recklessly ordered the cancellation of 750 contracts. That he did so while simultaneously declaring, fatuously and hypocritically, that "Ontario is open for business," further eroded his credibility with private investors.
Yes, foreign investors are still coming to Ontario to make the most of EV demand, but at the expense of massive incentives from the federal and provincial governments. If Ontario's reputation for reliability on renewables were less chequered, the need for generous tax incentives might have been lessened for investors seeking a clean energy sanctuary with certainty. Either way, the province will have to pay a premium to compensate investors for the immeasurable risk of being sandbagged should Ford change his mind yet again.
Third, Ontario is falling farther behind its obligation to meet Ottawa's draft Clean Electricity Regulations that speak of a net-zero grid by 2030. We won't know for a while if willing hosts will be found for wind and solar to enable the Ford government to meet its new targets of 2,000 megawatts starting this year, plus an additional 3,000 megawatts in future years, let alone the more ambitious goals required for net-zero.
If the Liberals overdid the overriding of local sentiments, the Tories may find that an entirely hands-off approach won't get them where they need to go. But that, in the end, may be part of the plan - the subtext behind the minister's latest soothing statements.
By going through the motions of renewing renewables, Smith is artfully distracting critics from a more difficult debate about Ontario's excessive reliance on gas-fired power plants that will continue to emit undesirable levels of carbon. But that is increasingly in the nature of power politics in this province - an inability to level with ratepayers and taxpayers about the true cost levels, demand curves and supply lines.
Just ask the populist premier who talked of removing all of those renewables all those years ago.
CAPTION: While it's good news that Premier Doug Ford, shown in 2018, and Energy Minister Todd Smith are finally seeing the error of their ways on renewable energy, it doesn't diminish the folly of their first approach, Martin Regg Cohn writes.Nathan Denette THE CANADIAN PRESS FILE PHOTO

CREDIT: Martin Regg Cohn Toronto Star OPINION

Phones in classrooms aren't our only concerns, A faulty case to move Ontario Science Centre, Canada must expand eligibility to welcome Palestinians fleeing the 'Worst Place on Earth',

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+I+got+a+prescription+for+Ozempic%2C+even+though+I+shouldn%27t+have+qualified%2C+Humane+Society+needs+bigger+home%3A+What+happens+to+the+650+pets+buried+in+their+cemetery%3F+Phones+in+classrooms+aren%27t+our+only+concerns%2C+A+faulty+case+to+move+Ontario+Science+Centre%2C+Canada+must+expand+eligibility+to+welcome+Palestinians+fleeing+the+%27Worst+Place+on+Earth%27%2C&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ozempic scarcity a sign of broken health system
I got a prescription for Ozempic, even though I shouldn't have qualified, Dec. 30
As a prediabetic senior, I've used Ozempic for three years without side effects other than reducing weight and obtaining healthy lab results.
For the last eight months, I've only been allowed one 28-day syringe each pharmacy visit because of the Ozempic "shortage" aggravated by people seeking Ozempic outside of legitimate prescriptions.
That brings me to the broader issues of lax oversight, privatization, greed and profits-only motivation which are increasing at an alarming rate in Canada as our health care - once envied around the world - decays. Corporations are smelling profits as they use new pay online and telehealth and retail "health" centres.
I can no longer get a flu or COVID shot at my family doctor's office as they are now outsourced to private retail chains.
At least I am fortunate to have a doctor. Many do not.
The current Ontario government contributes to this climate as it continues its drive to starve and break public systems, declare a "crisis" and then propose only private, for-profit providers as "solutions."
Greg Kokko, Scarborough
Voting for the party that gives rather than takes
I recently read Jonathan Karl's books "Betrayal" and "Front Row at the Trump Show," about how Donald Trump slandered the media as being fake news.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre does the same thing, rudely chomping on an apple during an interview. He insults journalists just like Trump.
Personally, I trust the mainstream media coverage. I read the Star and the Globe and Mail - both accurate and honest. I believe the media holds politicians accountable through checks and balances. When the Star or the Globe print a story that is inaccurate you can call the media council of Canada to make things right.
I will be voting for the party that gives rather than takes. We pay taxes for a reason. I like dental coverage and a decent pension. Conservatives will kill our social safety nets. And for how much savings?
Russ Young, Fort Erie, Ont.
Moving remains from graveyards is nothing new
Humane Society needs bigger home: What happens to the 650 pets buried in their cemetery? Jan. 7
It's not only pet cemeteries that are moved to a new location.
The village of Yorkville, at the north-west corner of Yonge and Bloor Streets, was home to nearly 7,000 human graves on a six-acre site extending to Bay Street and Cumberland Avenue. It was known by the name Pioneer Burial Ground, with burials between 1826 and 1855.
With the planned expansion of Yorkville, the cemetery was found to be an obstacle to development, so the families were invited to move the graves to another location. More than one thousand were reinterred at Mount Pleasant Cemetery. The remainder are probably lying under Yorkville properties.
Paul Epton, Toronto
Without dogs or horses in heaven, I don't want to go
As an ordained minister and animal lover, I was intrigued by this article. I am asked, occasionally, if animals have souls. As one who has interred several of my own pets, I have responded as follows:
The official answer is pets do not have souls. There is no indication in Scripture of such.
Unofficially my answer is if there are no dogs or horses in heaven, I don't want to go there.
Heretical, perhaps, but heartfelt, based on a lifetime love for God's creatures.
May the Humane Society find a good solution to their need.
The Rev. Mark McLennan, Woodstock, Ont.
Phone ban good but won't make classrooms safer
Phones in classrooms aren't our only concerns, Jan. 4
While unauthorized cellphone use in classrooms is disruptive, the school system needs to pay more attention to the unique challenges associated with student misbehaviour.
During and after the pandemic, the surge in the number of students with mental-health issues has led to a growing number of students becoming apathetic, unruly, or violent towards their peers or educators.
The writer cites a report from the OECD indicating that reading, science, and particularly math scores have steadily declined in this country over the last two decades due to the overuse of technological devices. However, critics argue that overcrowded classrooms, cutbacks to resources, and a revolving door of teachers and educational assistants are destabilizing classrooms and eroding student learning.
Classroom distractions occur in most schools but it is the frequency and type of interruptions that can prevent students from fully engaging with their teacher's instructions. According to a global student survey released in 2020, one in five 15-year-old Canadian students reported that learning was lost to noise, distractions, and disorder. Surely, this disturbing data is tied to the call from Ontario principals warning of "enormous" staff shortages.
Banning the unauthorized use of cellphones and other devices is a step in the right direction but not enough to make classroom environments safer and more conducive for learning.
Robert Ariano, Scarborough
Moving science centre an insult to a great design
A faulty case to move Ontario Science Centre, Dec. 21
The provincial government is planning to move the Ontario Science Centre to Ontario Place at a great cost to taxpayers. The science centre was designed by a world-renowned architect and Ontarian, the late Raymond Moriyama. The building is a work of art and should not be torn down. Premier Doug Ford seems to be more interested in making money for developers than preserving a beautiful piece of architecture. Not really different from throwing a tomato at the Mona Lisa or vandalizing some other great works done by respected people.
Jane Rodman, Mississauga
Welcoming Palestinians
to Canada
Canada must expand eligibility to welcome Palestinians fleeing the 'Worst Place on Earth', Jan. 7
About 188,000 Ukrainians have been welcomed into Canada from a population base of about 46 million. Proportionately at least 9,000 Palestinians should be welcomed on a base of 1.9 million in Gaza.
Gary MacDonald, Toronto

Document 464 of 500

Antisemitic history of the MS St. Louis is echoing in Toronto

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/antisemitic-history-ms-st-louis-is-echoing/docview/2912213856/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Antisemitic+history+of+the+MS+St.+Louis+is+echoing+in+Toronto&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ursula Schneider was a 16-year-old Jewish girl when she boarded the MS St. Louis in 1939, sailing out of Hamburg.
The Voyage of the Damned, as it became known. With 936 passengers, most of them Jews fleeing Nazi Germany, the liner crossed the Atlantic, docking first in Havana, where Ursula's father was waiting. Only 28 who had valid entry documents were allowed to get off. Ursula wasn't one of them. The ship sailed on to the Florida coast but the St. Louis was turned away by the United States.
Refugees turned pleading eyes to Canada, a country distinguished by its immigration welcome mat. But the government of Prime Minister William Lyon Mackenzie King, cleaving to a "none is too many'' policy - those Jews - denied permission for the St. Louis to disembark in Halifax. The ship was forced to return to Europe, where the United Kingdom, Belgium, France and the Netherlands agreed to take the passengers in. More than 250 of those men, women and children were killed in the Holocaust, according to war records.
Ursula was among the fortunate refugees. The teenager, her mother and brother found sanctuary in England. Schneider later married and immigrated to Canada. She passed away at age 94.
Just over a week ago, her granddaughter Laura, born and raised in Toronto, was driving home when she came upon the pro-Palestinian demonstration that had occupied the overpass at Highway 401 and Avenue Road. She was infuriated. And she was scared. "I've lived here all my life and this is the first time I've actually felt fearful," Laura tells the Star. (Asked that we not use her surname out of anxiety that she, too, would face antisemitic wrath.)
Angry enough, and thinking of the grandmother she adored, Laura wrote a letter to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau. In it she reminded Trudeau of the formal apology he made five years ago to the St. Louis passengers, their families and the broader Jewish community for Canada's failure eight decades earlier. In the House of Commons, Trudeau said: "It is my sincere hope that we can shine a light on this painful chapter of our history and ensure that its lessons are never forgotten. Antisemitism, xenophobia and hatred have no place in this country, or anywhere else in this world."
Laura accuses Trudeau of forgetting.
"It appears that Canada and yourself have not learned from its mistakes. And it is happening again ... YOUR back is turning again," she wrote. "It appears that YOU used your apology to try to mask the antisemitism and antipathy that continues in our country. The mask has come off.
"You have shown little if any support for the people of Israel following the atrocities that happened to them by Hamas," she wrote, adding that a "senior Hamas leader thanked Canada for your support of the United Nations calling for a ceasefire. Being thanked by a terrorist group is appalling and scary. Thank goodness my grandmother isn't here to witness what is happening."
The antisemitism behind the shunning of the St. Louis is being echoed and reshaped in protests across the globe - no less right here in Toronto - that may have decreased in numbers over the past three months but have escalated in menace.
While doubtless a great many participants were expressing their revulsion for the war Israel has conducted against Gaza, the horrors inflicted on helpless civilians, it has also become crystalized clear that Jew hatred and demonizing of Israel is at the craven heart of a rabid rump of demonstrators that have been allowed to threaten, intimidate and break all the laws they wish, with police doing little to intervene and curtail.
Goodness, even bringing protesters trays of Tim Horton's coffee at the highway overpass which they belatedly closed.
When agitators bring their hollers and jeers into primarily Jewish residential neighbourhoods, when bomb threats are made to synagogues, when Jewish schools have to be evacuated, when a Jewish-owned deli is firebombed, when Jewish businesses are vandalized, when senior citizens are harassed at a skating party at Nathan Phillips Square, when a hothead anti-Israel protester threatens to put an objecting man "six feet deep" - right in front of cops! - and nothing happens, nobody is arrested, then we have turned into a city, a country, where antisemitism will triumph, taken hostage by emboldened haters. The constitutionally enshrined right to protest peacefully has been made a mockery of, exploited to rationalize the unjustifiable.
However many protesters may attend these demonstrations out of solidarity with Palestinians, some don't even pretend to hide their support of Hamas, a designated terrorist group that unleashed mayhem with their Oct. 7 butchery, slaughtering some 1,200 Israeli men, women and children, thrilled in their rampage, and seizing at least 236 hostages. A total of 135 are still being held in Gaza but 21 are believed to have been killed in captivity.
Ursula told her granddaughter about how frightened and demoralized the St. Louis passengers had been when they were repeatedly rejected, begging for asylum. "What should have been a short time on this ship turned into a very long time. I'm usually a very quiet un-political person. But my grandmother came immediately into my mind when I saw this demonstration. What is going on in this country? I've been scared since Oct. 7 but now this was on-my-doorstep scared.''
Trudeau, of course, was off enjoying a comped family vacation at a luxury Jamaica resort when Laura sent her letter.
"Not that I'm expecting a whole lot. Ideally, though, there should be some acknowledgment of how the Jewish people are being targeted. Why is this being allowed?'' she asked. "Stand up and say that Jewish people deserve some protection as well. Show that you're supporting the Jewish people and not just paying lip service.''
Laura was there when Trudeau delivered his long-awaited apology. It feels like she's come full circle. "Are we going to have another meaningless apology 30 years down the road for what's happening here now?''
She wrote as well to Mayor Olivia Chow and Premier Doug Four. "Ford was very supportive of the Jewish people early on but right now the silence is deafening.''
The antisemitism Laura had experienced in her own life was mild, the typical bigoted jokes and cracks about money. She'd barely taken notice. But this is different, this surge of unambiguous loathing, often dressed up as anti-Zionism. The vilification has infected academia, campuses, unions, health care workers and mainstream media.
"Maybe people were quietly antisemitic before and now people think it's OK what they wouldn't say out loud before, voicing views that were buried,'' says Laura. "There's also a lot of people who probably don't know the full story and are just jumping on the bandwagon. Young people looking for a cause without understanding any of the history.
"It's turned into not what happened in Israel but an attack on Jews around the world.''
And right here.
CAPTION: Pro-Palestinian and pro-Israel protesters at the 401 overpass at Avenue Road on Dec. 30. Toronto police closed the overpass, keeping protestors on Avenue Road.

Subject: Antisemitism; Jewish people; Demonstrations & protests; Prime ministers; Truces & cease fires; Roads & highways

Location: Gaza Strip; Canada; Israel

Company / organization: Name: Hamas; NAICS: 813940

Title: Antisemitic history of the MS St. Louis is echoing in Toronto
