Bonnie Crombie smashes fundraising target as Liberals collect $1.2M

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bonnie-crombie-smashes-fundraising-target-as/docview/2909278662/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bonnie+Crombie+smashes+fundraising+target+as+Liberals+collect+%241.2M&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Rookie Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie has eclipsed her target of raising $1 million in a month for the third-place party.
Crombie, who won the Liberal crown on Dec. 2, said the Grits brought in more than $1.2 million during her inaugural weeks at the helm.
"On my first day on the job as Ontario Liberal Leader, I set an ambitious goal: to raise $1 million by our 2023 year-end deadline," she said in an email to contributors being sent out this week.
"Well, thanks to thousands of donors like you ... I am so thrilled to announce that we exceeded that goal, raising $1,213,181!"
The Mississauga mayor, who steps down from her municipal duties Jan. 12, emphasized the Liberals have their work cut out for them if they hope to topple Premier Doug Ford's two-term Progressive Conservatives in the next election.
"Ontario Liberals are back, and we're putting in the hard work necessary to defeat Doug Ford in 2026 and replace the scandal-plagued Conservatives with a government that will put the people of Ontario first," said Crombie.
"We know we have to close the considerable fundraising gap between us and the Conservatives, and I'm so grateful to everyone who contributed to doing exactly that last month," she said.
"We still have a lot of work to do between now and 2026 to grow our team, catch up to the Conservatives' fundraising and offer Ontario voters a plan they trust. This year-end fundraising total represents a strong start on our path to victory."
Her announcement comes as the Tories are stepping up a multimillion-dollar advertising blitz attacking the new Liberal chief.
The governing party, which views her as a threat in the seat-rich Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area, hopes to negatively define Crombie for voters using TV, radio and online spots.
In an interview with the Star two weeks ago, she said the Tory ads are to "distract people" from problems like the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal that is now under RCMP investigation.
Two of Ford's cabinet ministers and a pair of senior aides have resigned or been forced to quit in the wake of the debacle triggered by the premier's since-reversed scheme to open up 7,400 acres of environmentally sensitive land to housing development.
Last August, separate watchdog reports from the auditor general and the integrity commissioner concluded certain developers with PC connections were "favoured" in the land swap that ended up being scrapped in September.
The latest Abacus Data poll for the Star suggested Ford's party is weathering the Greenbelt fiasco after a rocky summer.
According to Abacus, the Tories were at 39 per cent support last month compared to 27 per cent for the Liberals, 24 per cent for Marit Stiles's Official Opposition New Democrats and six per cent for Mike Schreiner's Greens.
Using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform, Abacus surveyed 1,000 Ontarians Dec. 7-12. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
In the previous poll in November, the Tories were at 42 per cent support, the NDP at 24 per cent, the Liberals at 23 per cent and the Greens at seven per cent.
The Liberals, who governed Ontario under premiers Kathleen Wynne and Dalton McGuinty from 2003 until Ford's 2018 victory, now have only nine MPPs in the 124-member legislature.
That's three shy of official party status, meaning the Grits don't get regular questions to the government during the daily question period and have no standing on legislative committees.
The Tories have 79 MPPs, including Speaker Ted Arnott who does not caucus with them, while the New Democrats have 28 and the Greens have two. There are five Independents and one vacancy.
Nor do the Liberals receive public funding for staff and research, which is why Crombie, who is not an MPP, has made raising money a priority.
"This is the party's strongest year-end fundraising result ever under the new fundraising rules," she wrote to donors. "In fact, it triples our usual year-end fundraising results."
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie speaks at a news conference at Queen's Park on Dec. 5, 2023.

Subject: Scandals; Voters

Document 498 of 500

Budget test looms for Mayor Olivia Chow

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/budget-test-looms-mayor-olivia-chow/docview/2908780439/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Budget+test+looms+for+Mayor+Olivia+Chow&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If 2024 is anything like 2023, Torontonians should brace themselves for a wild ride because last year brought this city a world of surprise. Last February, just as political reporters were settling in to yet another year covering a predictable long-term mayor, the news offered a headline no one imagined possible except perhaps in a strange dream: "Mayor John Tory steps down from office after admitting he had relationship with staffer."
While many believed that Tory the popular politician could survive the office affair scandal it was clear that Tory the private person could not. He stepped away from the mayor's seat last winter leaving deputy mayor Jennifer McKelvie at the helm of a city facing gargantuan problems, among them: a massive budget hole, violent crimes on a struggling TTC and a housing crisis.
Cue one of Toronto's most competitive political races in recent memory: the 2023 June mayoral by-election served the city well, with a diverse choice of candidates and ideas.
Political veteran Olivia Chow beat the Tory-backed candidate and former city councillor Ana Bailão with 37 per cent of the vote on a platform that promised to build affordable housing and thwart Premier Doug Ford's controversial plans for Ontario Place.
Of course, all of it was easier said than done. And yet Chow has managed to do more in her short time in office than expected. Though many doubted that the progressive mayor could build bridges with the Progressive Conservatives at Queen's Park, Chow has proven to be a practical leader. In late 2023, the mayor's affordable housing plan passed with strong support; it calls for Toronto to build tens of thousands of rental housing units by 2030 (though how the city will pay for the plan is another story).
Additionally, Chow and Ford reached a historic "new deal" to have the province take on the costs of operating the Don Valley Parkway and the Gardiner Expressway, freeing up room in Toronto's budget for other priorities. Chow meanwhile will back off opposing Ford's spa plans at Ontario Place.
Though the new deal doesn't wash away the city's financial troubles, it will save Toronto several billion dollars and is a crucial step in establishing a new sustainable fiscal framework for the city. In other words, despite the chaos of Tory's resignation at the beginning of 2023 and the subsequent doomsday predictions about Toronto's financial situation, it is difficult to deny that the city finds itself in a brighter spot today. Brighter, however, isn't necessarily cheerful.
There is no question that Toronto will continue to face huge obstacles in 2024. The downtown core is struggling to recover amid the emergent reality of hybrid work. There is no quick fix for Toronto's intractable homelessness and refugee crisis. And it isn't just the homeless who are suffering in the city; a recent report found that roughly 1 in 10 Torontonians are relying on food banks.
Toronto's subway system desperately needs news cars for the Bloor-Danforth Line 2. And then there is the issue of getting the federal government on board to help with all of it. The "new deal" with the province remains a major victory for Chow, but elements are contingent on funding from the federal government.
Last year, Chow proved she could get the province to begin paying its fair share to Toronto, the economic engine of the province and nation. In 2024, she will have to convince the federal government to do the same. It was welcome news in December when the federal government announced nearly half a billion dollars in funding to the city to tackle the housing crisis.
But the city requires more than occasional monetary injections to prosper. It requires a new fiscal model that guarantees major, consistent funding from all levels of government.
Chow made headlines -- and drew heat from her mayoralty rivals -- for refusing to say exactly how much she would raise property taxes during the 2024 budget deliberations. She pledged only that it would be "modest." She said the past approach of starting with a tax hike and then figuring out the budget ignored the true financial needs of the city. Seeing the decline of municipal services in recent years, it's hard to argue.
But the coming budget debates will be a test for Chow and Toronto councillors as the city copes with outstanding bills for refugee support and transit investments as it awaits to hear whether Ottawa will pony up. Toronto has few options to make up the expected shortfall -- property tax hikes or service cuts.
Chow certainly hit the ground running in 2023: one poll from early in her term put her approval rating at 73 per cent. In 2024 we will learn if the mayor can carry that momentum and that good will forward.
CAPTION: Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, left, looks on as Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announces $471 million in federal funding for affordable housing last month. The city is hoping Ottawa will pledge other funding for refugee support and transit upgrades.

Subject: Federal government; Mayors; Scandals; Affordable housing; Funding; Politics; Homeless people; Elections; Tax increases

City budget test looms

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/city-budget-test-looms/docview/2908283830/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City+budget+test+looms&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 500 of 500

Chow's need for speed may spell trouble

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/chows-need-speed-may-spell-trouble/docview/2908282704/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chow%27s+need+for+speed+may+spell+trouble&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-01-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Olivia Chow had a heck of a year.
Twelve months ago, she didn't even know the job of mayor of Toronto would become available, much less that she'd end up getting it.
And given the scale of the challenges facing her, few would have bet that she'd have probably the most successful start of anyone to hold the gig in recent memory.
So yeah, for Chow, 2023 was a good year.
But 2024 will be worse.
I'm not trying to be a downer here, It's just that there's almost no avoiding it. The honeymoon period for newly elected politicians always comes to an end.
And for the mayor, there have been a few missteps during her first few months that could hasten that end.
Call them minor warnings, barely noticeable underneath the size of Chow's policy wins so far. But they're real - and they all relate to the mayor's need for speed.
In an interview conducted before the holidays, I asked Chow if she was satisfied with the rate at which things get done at city hall. She pointed to the recent turnaround time on a major report on a new housing strategy as a marker of success but acknowledged that sometimes she's had to push hard to get work done more quickly.
"It's just having performance standards," Chow said.
"Having clear priorities and the right amount of resources means you get the result. And I'm very clear what my priority is. So, you will see me driving it: 'This is my priority, and I want to get it done - and I want to get it done within this amount of time.'"
Then she added, with a laugh, "I'm the most impatient person."
By itself, that's not a bad thing. Not enough gets done at slowpoke Toronto city hall. In former mayor John Tory's final full term, between 2018 and 2022, for example, council and other city hall boards and committees requested 2,455 reports, according to a report request log maintained by the city. But just 1,179 of those requests were followed up on before the term expired.
That means the majority - 1,276 requests, or 52 per cent - were given the status "Not Carried Forward" and consigned to the dustbin of history.
Chow has a good reason to want to see better results than that.
But the danger - and the thing that could cause stormy waters for the mayor in 2024 - is if the mayor's impatience gives way to impulsiveness. And Chow has shown some worrying signs of some freewheeling so far.
In October, for example, Chow threw her support behind a surprise motion by midtown Coun. Mike Colle to immediately put a cap on the number of licences available for drivers working for ride-share services like Uber and Lyft.
The sudden move quickly had Uber threatening a lawsuit. And a later confidential report to Chow and council indicated that it was a lawsuit city hall would almost certainly lose. In response, the mayor withdrew the cap in December. (Chow has said she would take another swing at it in the next couple of months.)
Then there was the curious plight of TTC CEO Rick Leary. Later in October, Chow's appointed TTC chair, Scarborough Coun. Jamaal Myers, called a special meeting of the TTC Board that was almost entirely conducted behind closed doors. The Star's Ben Spurr later reported that Myers sought to put Leary on administrative leave while an investigation into alleged workplace misconduct took place.
But Myers was outvoted by the rest of the TTC board and Leary remains on the job. In the aftermath, Chow tried to distance herself from the surprise messy drama, but the fact that there was surprise messy drama at all is not a great omen.
And most recently, there was the mayor-brokered Sankofa accord, where in yet another surprise move Chow supported a last-minute agenda item brought to the December meeting of city council by downtown Coun. Chris Moise to rename Yonge-Dundas Square to Sankofa Square, bypassing a previously planned process to allow for a period of public consultation on a shortlist of new names.
It was supposed to be a grand compromise. Instead, it prompted sharp public criticism.
Don't get me wrong. These stories are about issues that do not compare to the scale of Chow securing a new deal with Premier Doug Ford worth billions, or her passage of a new housing plan through city council with minimal opposition. But there are some common elements that warrant some self-reflection as the mayor embarks on a new year: surprise last-minute moves made without a public process, that haven't really achieved the intended outcome.
Impatience can be a virtue, but impulsiveness is the kind of thing that gets mayors in trouble.
For Chow, the challenge of 2024 may be to find the right balance between moving fast and breaking things.
CREDIT: Matt Elliott OPINION
